BRIEF  OF  THE  APPELLANTS

JUDGMENT,  DECISION,  AND  OPINIONS  BELOW

The  majority,  concurring,  and  dissenting  opinions  and
order  of the  Court  of  Appeals  rendered  August  15,  1975,
are  not  yet  officially  reported.  They  are  printed  as
Appendix A to appellants’  Jurisdictional  Statement  filed  in
the appeal  from  the Court  of Appeals’  decision.

The judgment  of the  District  Court  entered  August  15,
1975,  is not  yet officially  reported.  It is printed  as  Appendix
A to appellants’  Jurisdictional  Statement  filed  in the appeal
from  the  District  Court’s  judgment.

2

JURISDICTION

.
The  Court  of Appeals  had  jurisdiction  of the  constitu-
tional  questions  certified  to  it  pursuant  to  2  U.S.C.  §
437h(a).  Jurisdiction  of the  appeal  from  the  decision  ren-
dered  by the  Court  of Appeals  is conferred  on  this  Court  by
2 U.S.C.  § 437h(b),  which  provides:

Notwithstanding  any  other  provision  of law,  any
decision  on  a matter  certified  under  subsection  (a)
shall  be  reviewable  by  appeal  directly  to  the
Supreme  Court  of the  United  States.”
Section  437h(c)  provides:
“It  shall  be the duty of the  court  of appeals  and  of
the  Supreme  Court  of  the  United  States  to  ad-
vance  on  the  docket  and  to  expedite  to  the
greatest  possible  extent  the  disposition  of  any
matter  certified  under  subsection  (a).”’
The United  States  District  Court  for  the  District  of
Columbia had jurisdiction  over  the constitutional  challenge
to  Subtitle  H by virtue  of 26 U.S.C.  § 9011(b)  and  28 USC
§§ 2282, 2284.  This  Court  has  jurisdiction  over  the  appeal
from  its judgment  by virtue  of 28 U.S.C.  §§ 1253,  2101.

Places  of Chusing  Senators.”

including  amendments  to  the  criminal  code  (all  referred  to

5

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED
1.  Did  the  Court  of  Appeals  correctly  conclude  that  the
limitations  imposed  by FECA  on  expenditures  by political
candidates  and  organizations  are  constitutional?
2.  Did  the  Court  of  Appeals  correctly  conclude  that  the
limitation  imposed  by FECA  on  expenditures  by any  person
relative  to a clearly  identified  candidate  are  constitutional?
3.  Did  the  Court  of  Appeals  correctly  conclude  that  the
limitations  imposed  by FECA  on  contributions  to  political
candidates  and  organizations  are  constitutional?
4.  Did the Court  of Appeals  correctly  conclude  that the dis-
closure  requirements  imposed  on_  political  candidates,
organizations  and  individuals  by FECA  are  constitutional?
5.  Did  the  courts  below  correctly  conclude  that  the  public
financing  provisions  of  FECA  and  Subtitle  H  of  the
Internal  Revenue  Code  are  constitutional?
6.  Did  the  Court  of  Appeals  correctly  conclude  that  the
method  provided  by FECA  for  appointing  members  of the
Federal  Election  Commission  is constitutional?
7.  Did  the  Court  of  Appeals  properly  decline  to  decide
whether  certain  powers  conferred  upon  the  Federal
Election  Commission  by FECA  are  constitutional?
8.  Are  the  powers  conferred  upon  the  Federal  Election
Commission  by FECA  constitutional?

A more  detailed  statement  of the  questions  presented,
as  certified  from  the  District  Court  to the Court  of Appeals,
is found  at pp.  1582-86  of Appendix A to the Jurisdictional
Statement  filed  in the appeal from  the decision  of the Court
of Appeals.

7

STATEMENT  OF THE  CASE

A.  History  of the Proceedings'

This  action  was  filed  by appellants  on  January  2, 1975,
the  first  day after  the  effective  date  of the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974.  The  First  Amended
Complaint  (App.  I-36-73  seeks  declaratory  and  injunctive
relief  against  certain  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  of 1971,  P.L.  92-225,  86 Stat.  3, 2 U.S.C.  §§
431  er  seq.  (hereinafter,  as  amended,  the  FECA),  the  Federal
Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of 1974.  P.L.  93-443,  88
Stat.  1263  (hereinafter  the  FECA  Amendments),  and  Subtitle
H of the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of 1954,  P.L.  92-178,  85 Stat.
562,  as  amended  by P.L.  93-53,  87  Stat.  138,  26  U.S.C.  §§
9001  et  seq.,  and  by the  FECA  Amendments  (hereinafter
Subtitle  H).  Appellants  requested  convocation  of a  three-
judge  court  under  28  U.S.C.  §§  2282  and  2284  and
certification  to the Court  of Appeals  under  2 U.S.C.  § 437h.
On  January  24,  1975,  District  Judge  Corcoran  denied
appellants’  request  for a three-judge  court  and  certified  the
action  to the Court  of Appeals,  stating:

‘The  Court  specifically  finds  that  the  plaintiffs
raise  substantial  constitutional  questions...  .”

B.  The  Challenged  Statutes

(1) Limits  on  Campaign  Expenditures

Candidates  for  nomination  for the  Presidency  may  not
spend  in  excess  of $10  million  in  pursuit  of that  nomina-
tion.  The  total  spent  in any  one  state  may  not  exceed  twice
the expenditure  limit  applicable  in that  state  to a candidate
for  nomination  for  election  to  the  Senate.  Jd.,  § 608(c)  (1)
(A).  The  Federal  Election  Commission  (FEC)  is to prescribe
rules under  which  expenditures  by candidates  for  nomina-
tion  for  the  Presidency  in  two  or  more  states  are  allocated
for  purposes of this  provision.  Jd.,  § 608(c)  (4).  No general-
election  candidate  for  President  may  spend  in excess  of $20
million.  J/d.,  § 608(c) (1) (B).

Candidates  for  nomination  for  election  to  the  Senate
may  not  make  expenditures  in excess  of 8 cents  multiplied
by the  state’s  voting-age  population  or  $100,000,  whichever
is  greater.  /d.,  § 608(c)  (1) (C).  No  general-election  candi-
date  for  the  Senate  may  spend  in excess  of  12 cents  multi-
plied  by  the  State's  voting-age  population  or  $150,000,
whichever  is greater.  Jd.,  § 608(c)(1)(D).

In  states  with  only  one  member  in  the  House  of
Representatives,  the  Senate  spending  limits  apply.  /d.,
§60&c)  (1) (C)(D).  In  all  other  House  elections,  no  candidate
may  spend  in excess  of $70,000  in pursuit  of the nomination
rd 4 oo of $70,000  in the  general  election.  Jd.,  § 608(c)

Candidates  may  spend  an  additional  20 percent  of the
raisi
linea

applicable  spending  limits  for  purposes  of  fund
Id..  § 591(f) (4) (H).

‘i

Expenditure  is  defined  as  ‘‘a  purchase,  payment
distribution,  loan,  advance,  deposit,  or  gift  of  money  or
anything  of value  .
.  made  for  the  purpose  of influencing

.

rs cit t

9

the  nomination  for  election,  or  election  ...  ,”” but  certain
enumerated  items  are  excepted,  id.,  § S91(f).

Section  9008  of the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954
(hereafter  I.R.C.)  limits  to  $2  million  the  expenditures  of
party  national  committees  with  respect  to each  presidential
nominating  convention.  The  FEC  may  authorize  an  excep-
tion  to this  limit  if it determines  that  ‘due  to extraordinary
and  unforeseen  circumstances”  greater  expenditures  ‘‘are
necessary  to  assure  the  effective  operation”  of such  con-
ventions,  I.R.C.  § 9008(d) (3).

The  national  committee  and  state  committees  of  a
political  party  may  make  expenditures  in  general  election
campaigns  for  federal  office.  National  committee  expendi-
tures  are  limited  to  2 cents  multiplied  by the  voting-age
population  of the  United  States  in the  general  election  for
President,  id.,  § 608(f)  (2).  A  national  or  state  committee
may  also  spend  up  to  2 cents  multiplied  by the  voting-age
population  of the  state  or  $20,000,  whichever  is greater,  in
an  election  to  the  office  of Senator  or  at-large  Representa-
tive.  Such  committees  may  also  spend  up  to  $10,000  in an
election  to the office  of Representative,  id.,  § 608(f) (3).

Limitations  on  expenditures  are  enforced  by criminal

sanctions.  18 U.S.C.  § 608(i);  I.R.C.  § 9012.

All  limits  on  expenditures  are  adjusted  to  changes  in
the  Consumer  Price  Index.  18  U.S.C.  § 608d);  I.R.C.  §
9008(b) (S).

(2)  Limits  on  Independent  Expenditures
Section  608(e),  18 U.S.C.,  prohibits  expenditures  in ex-
cess  of  $1,000  during  a  calendar  year  by  any  person
(defined  by id,  §591(g)  to  include  individuals,  committees
and  groups),  “‘relative  to a clearly  identified  candidate”’  and
‘advocating  the election  or defeat  of such  candidate.”

10

No  person,  as  defined  by  18  U.S.C.

contribute  in  excess  of $1,000  a any  poet  So Po

from  more  than  fifty  persons  and  have  contributed  to  five  or
more  candidates  for  federal  office  may  make  contributions
to  individual  candidates  for  federal
offi

each. Id.,  § 608(b) (3).

Candidates  may  not  spend  personal  funds  or  the funds
of their  immediate  families  in excess  of $50,000  in the  case
of a candidate  for  President,  $35,000  in the  case  of a candi-
date  for  the  Senate,  and  $25,000  in th
© case  of a  candidate
for the  House.  /d.,  § 608(a).

Contribution  is defined  as  “‘a  gift,  subscription,  loan
advance,  or  deposit  of money  or  anything  of value . .  made
for  the  purpose  of influencing  the  nomination  for  election
or  election  ....”’  All  bank  loans  made  “‘in  the  ordinary
course  of business”’  are  considered  a  loan  by each  endorser
or  guarantor  in  proportion  to  his  total  commitment  to  the
entire loan.  /d.,  § S91{e).  Exceptions  to  the  definition  of
contribution  are  enumerated  in id.,  § 591(e) (5).

(4)  Federal  Subsidies  in Presidential  Elections
|
Section  9006,  I.R.C.,  establishes  a  Presidential  Elec-
mes Campaign  Fund  composed  of  moneys  transferred  to
that  Fund  by the  Secretary  of the Treasury  not  in excess  of

the sum  of amounts  designated  by individuals  under the tax
checkoff  provided  in  Section  6096.  Individual  and  joint
taxpayers  may  designate  $1  and  $2,  respectively,  to  be
transferred  to  the  Fund.  Individuals  may  not  designate
either  their  party or their candidate  preferences.

The first  priority for the distribution  of federal  funds  is
the  subsidization  of the  nominating  conventions.  Amounts
remaining  are  used  to  provide  financial  support  to  candi-
dates  in the general  election.  Any amounts  which  are  avail-
able  and  are  not  expended  for  these  priority  purposes  are
distributed  to candidates  for the presidential  nomination  of
a  political  party.  §§ 9037(a),  9008(a).  The  Secretary  of the
Treasury  estimates  the  amount  of money  to  be  kept  aside
for the general  election.  § 9006(d).  Once  money  is put aside
for  the  nominating  conventions  and  the  general  election,
distribution  is  made  to  eligible  primary  candidates.  The
Secretary  must  make  an  “equitable  distribution’”’  among
candidates  for  nomination  of the  same  party,  taking  into
account  the  sequence  in which  candidates  become  eligible.
§ 9037(b).

Section  9008(b)  provides  $2  million  for  major-party
nominating  conventions  and  an  amount  to  minor  parties
(those  receiving  between  S and  2S  percent  of the  popular
vote  in the  preceding  election,  § 9002(7)),  proportionate  to
the popular vote  they received  in that election.

In  the  general  election,  the  subsidy  is divided  evenly
between  major-party  candidates,  each  receiving  $20  mil-
lion,  if available.  § 9004(a)  (1).  Minor-party  candidates  re-
ceive  amounts  proportionate  to  the  popular  vote  they  re-
ceived  in the  preceding  election.  § 9004(a)  (2).  New  parties’

>  The  statute  defines  “new  party”  to  include  not  only  parties  which
have  recently  been  created,  but  also  all  third  parties  previously  in
existence  which  received  less  than  5 percent  of the  popular  vote  in the
preceding  presidential  general  election.  I.R.C.  § 9002(8).  Appellants
will  use  the term  as the statute  defines  it.

12

are entitled  to  subsidies  after  a  general  election  in propor-
tion  to the vote  received  in that  election,  if over  5 percent.*  §
9004(a) (3).  To  be  eligible  for  any  funds,  candidates  must
qualify  to  have  their  names  (or  the  names  of  electors
pledged  to them)  on  the election  ballot  as  the candidate  of a
political  party  in 10 or  more  states.  § 9002(2).

A candidate  for  nomination  by  a  political  party  for
election  to  the  Presidency  is  eligible  to  receive  matching
primary  payments  if  he  raises  private  contributions  of
$5,000  from  residents  of  each  of  at  least  20  states  in
amounts  of $250 or  less.  §§ 9037,  9033(b).

(S)  Reporting  and Disclosure
Political  committees,  as  defined  by 2 U.S.C.  § 431(d),
must  keep  detailed  records  of  the  identities  of  all  indi-
viduals  who  contribute  more  than  $10  to  them,  subject  to
audit  by the  FEC.  /d.,  §§ 432(b)-(d)  and 438(a)-(d).  Political
committees  and  candidates  are  further  required  to  file
periodic  reports  disclosing  the  names,  occupation,  and
principal  places  of  business  of  those  who  contribute  in
excess  of $100.  /d.,  § 434(b)  (1)-(8).  Certain  resources  avail-
able  to  incumbent  officeholders,  enumerated  in  id.  §
434(d)  are  not,  however,  required  to be disclosed.

|

Every  person  who  contributes  or  expends  more  that
$100 per year  other  than  by contribution  to a political  com-
mittee  or  candidate  (including  persons  who  make  “‘inde-
pendent  expenditures,”’  see  p.  8,  supra,  and  volunteers
with  incidental  expenses  exceeding  $600)  must  also  file  a
report  with  the  FEC  disclosing  the  same  information  re-
quired  of political  committees  and  candidates.  /d..  § 434(e).
.

Criminal  penalties  are  provided  for  violation  of  the
disclosure  provisions.  /d.,  § 441.  Candidates  who  fail  to

*  Such  post-election  subsidies  are  apparently  limited  to  amounts

needed  to repay  outstanding  debts,  see  infra,  pp.  154-55n.  12

13

file  required  reports  are  barred  from  seeking  federal  office
for a specified  number  of years.  Id.,  § 456.

Persons  (other  than  individuals)  who  expend  funds  or
take  any  action  directed  at the public designed  to influence
the  outcome  of elections  or  voters’  choices,  or  who  publish
or  broadcast  any  material  referring  to  a  candidate  advo-
cating  his  election  or  defeat,  or  setting  forth  a candidate’s
voting  record  or  positions  on  public  issues,  or  who  take  any
action  otherwise  intended  to  influence  voters,  must  file
reports  with  the  FEC  as  if  such  persons  were  political
committees,  id.,  § 437a.6  Exempt  from  the  statute  are
certain  publications  or  broadcasts,  but  certain  other
publications  or broadcasts  are  not  exempted,  id.

(6)  Federal Election  Commission
The  Federal  Election  Commission  (FEC)  is composed
of six  members:  two  appointed  by the  president  pro  tem  of
the  Senate  on  the  recommendations  of the  Senate  majority
and  minority  leaders,  two  appointed  by the  Speaker  of the
House  on  the  recommendations  of the  House  majority  and
minority  leaders,  and  two  appointed  by the  President.  All
six  must  be  confirmed  by  a  majority  of  both  Houses.  2
U.S.C.  § 437c(a) (1).

The  FEC  has  broad  powers.  It may,  inter  alia:  (a) in-
vestigate  violations  and  issue  subpoenas;  (b)  exercise
primary jurisdiction  with  regard  to civil  enforcement  of the
FECA:  (c)  refer  violations  to  the  Attorney  General,  who
“shall  institute  a  civil  action  for  relief’;  (d)  formulate
general  policy  with  respect  to  the  administration  of  the
FECA,  including  certain  provisions  of the criminal  code;  (e)
render  advisory  opinions;  (f) make  rules  to  implement  the
FECA,  id.,  §§ 437d,  437g.

S  This  section  was  held  unconstitutional  by the Court  of Appeals  (op.

at  1549-60),  and  no  appeal  from  that  holding  has been  filed.

ny

14

15

a ee

Either  the  FEC  or  the  Attorney  General  may  bring
civil  actions  (including  proceedings  for  injunctions)  against
any  person  who  “‘has  engaged,  or  is about  to  engage  in any
acts  or  practices  which”’  violate  the  FECA,  including  cer-
tain  sections  of the Criminal  Code,  id.,  § 437g (a).

Any  proposed  rule  or  regulation  dealing  with  reports
or  statements  required  of  Senate  or  House  candidates  or
committees  supporting  such  candidates  must  be  trans-
mitted  to  the  Senate  or  the  House,  respectively.  If  the
House  or  the  Senate,  as  the  case  may  be,  does  not  disap-
prove  the  proposed  rule  or  regulation  within  30  legislative
days,  the  FEC  may  prescribe  it.  Rules  and  regulations
relating  to  required  reports  or  statements  by presidential
candidates  or  committees  supporting  such  candidates  go to
both  houses  and  either  may  disapprove  any  such  proposal
within  30  legislative  days.  Id.,  § 438(c).  If the  FEC  finds
that  a  candidate  for  federal  office  has  not  filed  required
reports,  such  person  shall  be disqualified  from  candidacy  in
future  federal  elections  until  one  year  after  the expiration  of
the term of the  office  he was  seeking.  Jd.,  § 456.

C.  The Appellants

1.  James  L. Buckley

James  L.  Buckley  ran  for  the  United  States  Senate
unsuccessfully  in  1968  on  the  New  York  Conservative  Party
ticket.  He ran  again  and  won  on  that  ticket  in  1970.  He pro-
poses  to  run  for  a  second  six-year  term  next  year.  Findings
III,  Buckley  1-4,  pp.  1-2;  App.  II-700-01.°

3.  Eugene J. McCarthy
-  Eugene  J.  McCarthy,  a  former
from
Minnesota, is an  independent  candidate  for the  Presidency
in 1976.  His  candidacy  has  no  tie  to  any  political
Findings  III, McCarthy  1, p.17:  App.  II-712-13..
.

In his  independent  candidac

y McCarthy  and  the

Senator

part

Com-

mittee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency—McCarthy  °76 (the

Committee),  which  is supporting  him,  are  seeking  to  place
his  candidacy  on  the  ballot  in all  SO states  and  the  District
of Columbia.  His  candidacy  involves  no  primary  elections,
caucuses,  or  conventions,  and  the  Committee  does  not  plan
to  hold  a  nominating  convention.  /d.,  McCarthy  2, p.  17;

In  1968,  when  he  ran  for  the  Democratic  presidential
nomination,  McCarthy  received  individual  contributions  in
excess  of  $1,000  and  support  from  many  volunteers  who
gave  freely  of their  time  and  effort,  meeting  most  of their
expenses  out  of their  own  pockets.  Jd.,  McCarthy  4, 11, pp.
18,  20;  App.  II-714-15.  He  has  received  individual  con-
tributions  in  excess  of $1,000  for  his  1976  campaign.  |
McCarthy  23, 31, pp.  22-23;  App.  II-717-18.

McCarthy  anticipates  considerable  informal  and  un-
structured  activity  by  volunteers  supporting  his  1976
candidacy.  Id.,  McCarthy  19, p. 22; App.  II-717.  In his current
campaign  individuals  have  hesitated  to  support  him  finan-
cially  for  fear  of  the  consequences  of  disclosure  of  their
names.  McCarthy  dep.  tr.  17-18,  67-70.  McCarthy  believes
that  the  Federal  Election  Commission,  because  of  its
method  of appointment  and  its bi-partisan  nature,  will  not
look  favorably  on  independent  campaigns.  McCarthy  aff.  9

Constitutional

Presi-
dency—McCarthy  °76  (the  Committee)  is  a  registered
political  committee  under  the  FECA.  It supports  the  presi-
dential  candidacy  of plaintiff McCarthy.  It plans to engage
in legal  challenges  to state  laws  which  prevent  independent
candidacies  from  listing  on  the  ballot.  The  time  and
expense  involved  are  problems  unique  to  candidates
running  outside  the  two  major  parties.  Findings  III.
Monroe  2-4, p. 24; App.  II-719-20.

19

The  Committee  anticipates  a  need  to  raise  in excess  of
$24  million  for  the  1976  presidential  campaign.  Monroe
aff.  § 13; Monroe  dep. tr.  44.  It believes  it is impeded  by the
Act's limitations  on  individual  and  organization  contribu-
tions,  Monroe  aff.  99 8, 9, on  campaign  expenditures,  id.,  9
11,  and  on  volunteer  expenses.  Jd.,  99  14-15.  The
Committee  believes  that  Subtitle  H  discriminates  against
its  candidate. Id.,  9§ 20-21.  The  Committee  desires  that  its
candidate  make  expenditures,  accept  contributions,  and
accept  the  assistance  of  volunteers  with  incidental  ex-
penses,  all  in excess  of the  Act’s  limits;  it further  desires  to
accept  contributions  without  making  disclosure  under  the
Act. It desires  that  persons  supporting  its  candidate  make
independent  expenditures  exceeding  those  permitted  by the
Act.  It believes  that  it will  be  harmed  by these  restrictions
and  by the  public-financing  provisions.  /d.,  99 8-17,  20-25.

5.  Stewart  R.  Mott

Stewart  R. Mott  has  since  1963  been  engaged  in
supporting  political  and  social  movements  and  certain
candidates  for political  office.  Findings
hoo.  Tt.
gs III, Mott  1-3,  p. 26;
.

III

-

Mott  was  particularly  active  in  the  Vietnam  protest
movement,  supporting  candidates  who  favored  extrication
of  the  United  States  from  Vietnam,  and  making  inde-
pendent  efforts  to affect  public  policy.  In  1968  Mott  worked
as  a volunteer  for  Eugene  McCarthy’s  nomination  for  Presi-
dent,  gave  over  $200,000  to  that  effort,  and  spent  several
thousand  dollars  of his own  unreimbursed  money  for travel
telephone,  etc.,  in connection  with  political  campaigns.  He
paid  for  newspaper  advertisements  to  encourage  Nelson
Rockefeller  to  present  himself  as  a  Republican  alternative
to  Richard  Nixon.  Mott  contributed  well  in excess  of $1,000
to  an  authorized  Rockefeller  presidential  committee.  Jd
Mott  4-8, pp.  26-27;-App.  II-721-22.
i

After  1968  Mott  became  a  member  of  the  National
Committee  for  an  Effective  Congress  (NCEC),  and  worked
with  that  group  to  elect  candidates  for  Congress.  In  1970
Mott  organized  “Peace,  Inc.,’’  and  donated  over  $30,000  to
Congressional  candidates  supporting  U.S.  withdrawal  from
Vietnam.  Beginning  in  1971  he  made  financial  contribu-
tions  to  George  McGovern  totaling  well  over  $350,000.
Mott  also  incurred  some  $50,000  for  personal  expenses  on
McGovern’s  behalf  in  1972.  Through  the  1972  campaign
Mott  financed  independent  political  activities  critical  of
Edmund  Muskie  and  Richard  Nixon.  In  1972  Mott
contributed  to  candidates  for  the  House  and  Senate,  both
of  these
non-incumbents.
incumbents
contributions  were  in excess  of $1,000.  Jd.,  Mott  9-18,  pp.
28-30;  App.  II-722-25.

Sixteen

and

In  future  elections,  Mott  desires  to  make  contribu-
tions,  to  make  incidental  expenses  as  a  volunteer,  and  to
make  independent  expenditures,  all  exceeding  the  Act’s
limits.  He believes  he and  his  political  goals  will  be harmed
by these  restrictions.  Mott  aff.  9 31.

6.  William  A.  Steiger

William  A.  Steiger  represents  Wisconsin’s  Sixth
Congressional  District  in the  House  of Representatives,  and
has  done  so  since  1967.  He  is considering  a  candidacy  for
federal  office  in  1976;  it is possible  that  he will  be  a candi-
date  for  the  U.S.  Senate.  Steiger  believes  that  the  Act  will
enhance  his  chances  of reelection  to the  House  and—by  the
same  token—will  severely  hinder  his ability to challenge  the
candidacy  of one  of his  state’s  incumbent  Senators.  Find-
ings III,  Steiger  1, p. 33; App.  II-727-28.

In  1966  Steiger  received  contributions  of $4,000  each
from  his  father  and  from  the  Wisconsin  Republican  Party.
Id.,  Steiger  19-20,  p. 39;  App.  II-732.  Steiger  desires  to spend
the  amounts  necessary  and  available  to  convey  his  views  to

20

21

the  citizens  meaningfully.  If unable  to  do  so,  he  believes
that  his  campaign  will  be  injured  and  the  political  efficacy
of his supporters  impaired.  Steiger  aff.  9 33.

7.  New  York  Civil Liberties  Union

New  York  Civil  Liberties  Union  (NYCLU)  is an  affili-
ate  of  the  American  Civil  Liberties  Union  (ACLU);  its
Executive  Director  is Ira Glasser.  Findings  III, Glasser  1, p.
41;  App.  II-733.

NYCLU,  as  a  principal  function,  disseminates  within
New  York  State  information  concerning  constitutional
rights.  NYCLU  is barred  by the  ACLU’s  constitution  from
endorsing  or  opposing  candidates  for  public  office.  How-
ever,
NYCLU,  among  other  activities,  frequently  criticizes
public  officials  whose  conduct  it believes  violates  civil  liber-
ties.  /d.,  Glasser  2-3,  p. 41;  App.  II-733-34.  NYCLU  takes  fre-
quent  and  controversial  positions  on  public  issues.  Glasser
aff.  9 3,  Glasser  dep.  tr.  120.  NYCLU  praises,  criticizes,
describes  and  rates  the  conduct  of clearly  identified  public
officials  who  may  be candidates  for  federal  office.  Findings
III,  Glasser  4,  pp.  41-42;  App.  II-734.  NYCLU  has  bought
newspaper  advertisements  costing  more  than  $1,000  criti-
cal  of  then-Governor  Rockefeller  with  respect  to  Attica,
critical  of  then-President  Nixon  with  respect  to  school
busing,  and  critical  of  President  Ford  for  pardoning  Mr.
Nixon.  Glasser  intends  to  publish  other  similar  ad-
vertisements.  Glasser  aff.  99 5-10;  Glasser  dep.  tr.  16,  92,
110,  117,  126.

NYCLU  published  a  membership  newsletter  ap-
proximately  six  times  a  year,  at  a  cost  exceeding  $1,000
per  issue,  delineating  NYCLU'’s  views  on  public  issues  and
the  positions  taken  on  such  issues  by public  officials,  some
of  whom  are  candidates  for  federal  office.  Findings  II,
Glasser  S-8,  p. 42;  App.  H-734-35.

8.  Mississippi  Repubican  Party

The  Mississippi  Republican  Party (the  Party) is part of
the  national  Republican  Party.  The  Party  has  nominated
and  intends  to  nominate  candidates  for  the  Presidency,
U.S.  Senator,  and  members  of the  U.S.  House  of Represen-
tatives.  Findings  III, Reed  1, p. 48;  App.  II-738-39.

In  1972  the  Party’s  State  Central  Committee  voted not
to solicit,  receive,  or  expend  money  on  behalf of  candid ates
for  federal  office  because  it  opposed  in  principle  the
requirements  imposed  by  the  FECA.  /d.  The  Central
Committee  will  not  contribute  over  $1,000  to  any  future
candidate  for  federal  office  in  order  to  protect  the  confi-
dentiality  of its contributors.  Jd.,  Reed  3, p.  49;  App. II-739
Many  Party  members  fear  that  they  would sufter adverse
consequences  from  disclosure  of  their  political  contribu-
tions.  Jd.,  Reed  8, p.  49;  App.  II-740.  One  consequence  has
been  that  the  Party  has  received  a  large  number  of  $99
contributions.  /d.,  Reed  11; Reed  dep. tr.  31-32.

Some  Mississippi  Republicans  have  contributed  more
than  $1,000  to  Party  candidates  for  federal  office. Prior  to
the  FECA,  the  Party  contributed  directly  to candidates  for
federal  office,  including,  in  some  cases,  contributions  in
excess  of $5,000.  Jd.,  Reed  10-13,  p. SO;  App.  II-740-41.  The
Party’s  basic  fund-raising  system,  accounting  for  two-thirds
of the funds  used  to run  its base  operations,  is  a bank-draft
system,  which  raises,  from  monthly  $10  contributions,
yearly individual  contributions  exceeding  $100.  Jd.,  Reed  7,
9, pp. 49-SO;  App.  II-740.

Only one  Party candidate  for federal  office  has  beaten
an  incumbent,  in  a  1964  House  race.  Jd.,  Reed  16,  p.  51;
App.  II-741-42.

In  future  campaigns,  the  Party  desires  to  make
expenditures  on  behalf  of its candidates,  to have its  candi-
dates  make  expenditures,  to  have  its  candidates  accept

22

23

contributions,  and  to  have  its  candidates  expend  personal
or  family  funds  all  in  excess  of the  Act's  limits;  it further
desires  that  it  and  its  candidates  accept  contributions
without  making  the  disclosure  required  by  the  Act.  It
desires  that  no  public  funds  be  spent  on  presidential  elec-
tions.  The  Party  believes  it will  be harmed  by the  provisions
of  the Act  mentioned  above.  Reed  aff.  99  10-12,  15-18,  20-
23, 27-44.

9.  Conservative  Victory  Fund

The  Conservative  Victory  Fund  (CVF)  is  a  registered
political  committee  under  the  FECA,  affiliated  with  the
American  Conservative  Union  (ACU).  CVF  and  ACU  have
most  of their  officers  in  common,  they  share  office  space,
and  they work  on  common  matters  in some  cases.  CVF  was
established  in  1970  to raise  funds  to aid  the election  of con-
servative  candidates  for  public  office.  CVF  itself  pays  no
salaries.  Findings  III,  Roberts  1-4,  p. 52; App.  II-742.

In  1970,  1972,  and  1974  CVF  contributed  in excess  of
$5,000  to  each  of  certain  candidates  for  federal  office,
including  both  challengers  and  incumbents.  In  these  three
election  campaigns  CVF  contributed  a  total  of  approx-
imately  $370,000  to  the  campaigns  of  179  candidates  for
the  House  and  Senate.  /d.,  Roberts  7-8,  pp.  53-54;  App.  II-
743-44.
CVF  is not  connected  to any  political  party,  and  it does
not  now  plan  to  form  a new  political  party.  Jd.,  Roberts  12,
15,  pp.  S4-SS;  App.  II-744.  However,  a  new  political  party  is
being discussed  among  conservatives,  and  if such  a  party  is
formed,  it is possible  that
CVF would  be involved  in raising
funds  for  a  nominating  convention  and  a  campaign  for
President.  Roberts  dep. tr.  42-43.

CVF  believes  that  the  limitations  the  Act  places  on
contributions  by political  committees,  single  and  aggregate
contributions  by  individuals,  expenditures  of  family  or
personal  funds  by a candidate,  and  overall  expenditures  by

a  candidate  will  harm  it.  CVF  desires  to  receive  the
maximum  amount  of money  it can  from  individuals,  and  to
continue  to  contribute  to  conservative  challengers  to
incumbent  officeholders.  Roberts  aff.  99 8, 16,  18; Roberts
dep. tr.  12-17,  34-35.

10.  American  Conservative  Union

The  American  Conservative  Union  (ACU),  established
in  1964,  has  made  contributions  to  candidates  for  federal
III,  Joy
office,  including  one  $2,500  contribution.  Findings
1, 3,  12,  pp.  59,  61;  App.  II-747-49.  ACU  desired  to  con-
tribute  to  that  candidate  as  much  as  its  resources  per-
mitted.  Joy dep. tr.  7-8,  11-12.

rnin

indings

ACU  has  engaged  in  lobbying  activities,  and  it
publishes  annual  ratings  of all members  of Congress. These
ratings  list  voting  records,  showing  positions  on  Issues  of
interest  to  ACU.  ACU  also  distributes  statements  on  its
positions  with  respect  to  public  issues  and  political  candi-
dates.  Findings  III, Joy 4-6, p. 60; App.  II-748.

ACU  believes  that  limitations  on  contributions  by
political  committees,  and  other  provisions  of  the  Act,
severely  decrease  ACU’s  ability  to  make  its  views  effective.
Joy aff.  9 4; Joy dep. tr.  21-22.

11.  HUMAN  EVENTS,  INC.

HUMAN  EVENTS,  INC.,  publishes  a  weekly  news-
paper  dealing  primarily  with  events  of political  importance
and  with  public  issues,  public  officials,  and  cardidates.
Findings  III, Winter  1-2, p. 56; App.  II-745.
HUMAN  EVENTS  each  election

year  discusses
selected  electoral  contests  of  interest  to  its  readers.
HUMAN  EVENTS  has  also  published  articles  on  various
candidates.  HUMAN  EVENTS  endorses
presidential

24

25:

explicitly  or  by  implication  certain  candidates  for  federal
office.  Jd.,  Winter  3-5,  8, pp.  56-58:  App.  II-745-47.

12.  Libertarian  Party

The  Libertarian

Party  (the  Party)  was  formed  in
December,  1971,  and  held  its  first  nominating  convention
in  June,  1972.  Its  nominee  for  President,  although  on  the
ballot  in  only  two  states,  traveled  extensively  and  received
one  vote  in  the  electoral  college.  The  ticket’s  popular  vote
was  approximately  10,000.  By  1974  the  Party  membership
had  grown  to  5,000  individuals.  Major  campaigns  were  run
in  1974  in  several  federal  elections.  The  Party  is  now
organized  in  40  states.  Findings  III,  Crane  2-5,  pp.  63-64;
App.  II-750-S1.

The  Party  estimates  that  it has  received  approximately
10
Ap-
individual
proximately  five  other  individuals  have  indicated  that  they
would  contribute  more  than  $1,000.  /d  ,  Crane  7-9,  pp.  64-
6S;  App.  II-751-52.

contributions

exceeding

$1,000.

In its future  campaigns  the  Party  desires  that  its candi-
dates  make  expenditures,  accept  contributions,  and  accept
the  assistance  of volunteers  with  incidental  expenses,  all  in
excess  of the  Act’s  limits;  it further  desires  that  its  candi-
dates  accept  contributions  without  making  the  disclosure
required  by the  Act.  It desires  that  persons  supporting  its
candidates  be  able  to  make  independent  expenditures
exceeding  the  Act’s  limits.  It believes  that  it will  be harmed
by these  restrictions  and  by the  public-financing  provisions.
Crane  aff.  99 10-11.

D.  Decision  of the Court  of Appeals

Relying  heavily  on  what  it  perceived  as  the  public’s
perception  of corruption  in government,  the majority  found
ample justification  for congressional  attempts  to reform  the
electoral  process.  In particular,  the majority  found  a  ‘‘com-
pelling  need”’  for  equalizing  the  power  of  citizens  to  in-
fluence  the course  and  outcome  of elections  and  for restric-
ting the political  influence  of wealth  (op. at  1510-20).

On  the  basis  of  such  considerations  the  majority,
without  further  significant  constitutional  discussion,  ap-
proved  the  limitations  on  contributions,  expenditures,  and
independent  expenditures  (op. at  1528-41).

The  majority  also  upheld  the  constitutionality  of the
disclosure  provisions  of the  challenged  legislation,  holding
that  the  disclosure  thresholds  are  not  too  low  and  that  the
showing  in the  record  of chilling  effect  on  and  harassment
of  minor  parties  and  their  contributors  is  insufficient  to
overcome  the  presumption  of constitutionality  of disclosure
as  applied  to  independent  and  minor-party  candidates  (op.
at  1541-49).  7

The  majority  held  that  the  Congress  may  con-
stitutionally  appropriate  public  funds  for  the  support  of
partisan  political  activities  in  the  conduct  of presidential
nominating  conventions  and  campaigns,  and  that  the  for-
mulas  for distribution  of the  moneys  chosen  by Congress  in
Subtitle  H  do  not  unconstitutionally  discriminate  against
minor  and  new  parties  and  independent  candidacies.  The
majority  further  held  that  four-year-old  voting  patterns
were  permissible  as  means  for  determining  the  entitlement
to federal  funds  (op. at  1560-70).

7  However,  the  Court  unanimously  invalidated  the  provision  for
disclosure  by  “‘certain  persons”  other  than  political  groups,  2 U.S.C.
§437a,  a ruling  from  which  no  appeal  has been  taken  by the appellees.

26

Finally,  the  majority  upheld  the  method  of  ap-
pointment  and  some  of .e  powers  of the  Federal  Election
Commission.  The  constitutionality  of some  of the  principal
enforcement,  supervisory,  and  investigatory  powers  of the
Commission  were  found  not  ripe  for  decision  (op.  at  1570-
80).

Chief Judge  Bazelon  dissented  with  respect  to the  con-
Stitutionality  of  disclosure  of  campaign  contributors  to
nascent  political  parties.  Judge  Bazelon  would  have  held
that  such  compelled  disclosure  by minor  and  new  parties
impermissibly  intrudes  upon  basic  rights  of  associational
privacy  as  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amendment  (op.  at
1593-97).

Judge  Tamm,  joined  by Judge  Wilkey,  dissented  from
the  Court’s  holdings  with  respect  to  the  constitutionality  of
contribution  and  expenditure  limitations,  the  financing  of
federal  elections  by  the  means  established  in  Subtitle  H.
and  the  powers  and  method  of appointment  of the  Federal
Election  Commission  (op. at  1598-1609).

In a separate  opinion  Judge  MacKinnon  also dissented
with  respect  to  the  powers  and  method  of appointment  of
the Commission  (op. at  1609-23).

27

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

The  challenged  statutes  are  the  most  far-reaching
regulation  of political  communication  in the  history  of the
republic.  The  Court  of Appeals  failed  to give the legislation
the  critical  scrutiny  requisite  under  long-accepted  First
Amendment  principles.  Erzoznik  v.  City of Jacksonville,  43
U.S.L.W.  4809  (June  23,  1975).  Especially  close  scrutiny  is
required  of these  statutes  under  the  accepted  tests  of con-
stitutionality  because  (1)  they  were  adopted  without
adequate  attention  to  their  consequences;  (2)  they  fun-
damentally  alter  the  political  process  —  the  core  of the  First
Amendment  —  and  thus  affect  the  rules  by  which  all
other  rules  are  made,  Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77,  96
Kramer  v.  Union  Free
(1949)  (Frankfurter,  J., concurring);
School District,  395  U.S.  621  (1969);  and  (3) they were  enac-
ted  by  legislators  who  faced  a  plain  conflict  of  interest,
since  the  statutes  in many  ways  affect  the  political  balance
between  challengers  and  incumbents.

The  Court  of  Appeals  incorrectly  applied  United
States  v.  O'Brien,  391  U.S.  367  (1968),  to  analyze  the
statutes’  restrictions  on  political  speech.  Since  virtually
every  political  communication  involves  the  expenditure  of
money,  expenditures  for political  purposes  are  protected  by
the  First  Amendment.  United  States  v.  Auto  Workers,  352
U.S.  567,  594  (1957)  (Douglas,  J., dissenting). Limiting the use
of  money  for  political  purposes  amounts  to  restricting  the
communication  itself.  Similarly,  contributions  for  political
purposes  are  both  a means  for contributors  to express  their
political  ideas  and  the necessary  prerequisite  for candidates
for  federal  office  to  communicate  their  views  to  voters.
Contributions  are  crucially  important  in  determining  the
level  of political  debate  and  in implementing  the freedom  of
association  guaranteed  by the  First  Amendment.

28

29

Political  contributions  and  expenditures  may  be
regulated  only  when  the  regulation  is limited  to  non-speech
elements  or  protects  a  governmental  interest  unrelated  to
freedom  of expression.  The  line  regulation  must  walk  is the
line  between  speech  assimilated  into  regulable  activity
(e.g.,  bribery)  and  speech  directed  at  persuading  voters  to
make  political  choices  (e.g.,  the  typical  political  ex-
penditure  or  contribution).  The  Court  of Appeals  failed  to
walk  this  line;  instead  it denigrated  political  campaigns  by
labeling  them  ‘‘Romanesque  political  extravagances,”’  thus
evaluating  the  content  of speech  and  dismissing  it as  un-
worthy  of  protection.  Police  Department  of  Chicago  v.
Mosley,  408  U.S.  92 (1972  )

Il.

The  Court  of  Appeals  offered  three  justifications  to
support  the  challenged  statutes,  none  of which  can  sustain
them.

(1) Allegations  about  the  ‘‘skyrocketing’’  costs  of
political  campaigns  are  unsupported  by the  facts,  and  lack
perspective  when  compared  to  increases  in  other  relevant
variables.  Particularly  when  compared  to  increases  in  the
perquisites  of office  that  incumbent  members  of Congress
appropriate  for  themselves,  campaign  costs  —  a  vital
element  of  self-government  —  are  modest.  If  anything,
Americans  spend  too  little  to  finance  the  process  by which
their  government  is chosen.

(2)  Private  campaign  financing  does  not,  as  the  Court
of  Appeals  believed,  foster  inequality  of  political  ex-
pression.  Large  contributions  are  made  on  behalf  of  the
whole  spectrum  of  political  persuasion.  They  frequently
benefit  society’s  least  powerful.  And  they  enable  political
candidates  to  overcome  other  inequalities  exacerbated  by
the  challenged  statutes,  such  as  differences  in  name
recognition,  the  perquisites  of incumbents,  and  inequality

of access  to the  media.  Cf. Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.
Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241  (1974).

(3) The  challenged  statutes  do nothing  to reduce  either
the  fact  or  the  appearance  of  corruption.  They  enhance
rather  than  diminish  the  power  of  so-called  ‘‘special  in-
terests,””  even  allowing  government  contractors  more  in-
volvement  in  campaign  contributions  than  prior  law  per-
mitted.  Properly  drafted  disclosure  statutes,  the  regulation
of cash  contributions,  and  other  precisely  tailored  measures
would  provide  measures  both  far  more  effective  against
corruption  and  far  less  intrusive  on  constitutional  rights
than  the challenged  legislation.

Il.

The  FECA’s  limits  on  the  expenditures  political  par-
ties,  committees  and  candidates  can  make  in election  cam-
paigns  are  in  flat  violation  of the  First  and  Fifth  Amend-
ments.  They are  explicit  limitations  on  political  speech,  and
will  result  in  an  unjustifiable  diminution  in  the  levels  of
campaign  activity  and  political  debate.  The  national  in-
terest  demands  full  and  robust  debate  during  political  cam-
paigns.  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S.  214  (1966).  Limiting
campaign  expenditures  unconstitutionally  closes  important
channels  of  political  communication,  and  restricts  the
ability  of the  average  citizen  to participate  effectively  in the
process  by which  his leaders  are  chosen.  The  highest  courts
expenditure
have  recently
of  two  states
Ore.
limitations  on  these  grounds.  Deras  v.  Myers,
.  535  P.2d  541  (1975);  Bare  v.  Gorton,  84  Wash.  2d.

380,  526  P. 2d 379 (1974).

Expenditure  limits  will  have  their  most  injurious  ef-
fects  in races  for truly contested  House  and  Senate  seats  by

competition.

30

31

Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968).  Victorious
challengers  must  typically  spend  in  excess  of  the  ex-
penditure  limits  in  order  to  overcome  the  enormous  re-
sources  available  to  incumbents  as  perquisites  of their  of-
fices.  Those  perquisites  have  a  political  impact  even  when
scrupulously  used  for  non-political  purposes.  Incumbent
officeholders  have  an  enormous  stake  in  their  own  re-
election.  They  have  been  dramatically  increasing  their  own
perquisites  in  recent  years  while  at  the  same  time  limiting
what  challengers  may  spend.

The  security  in  office  of  incumbent  members  of
Congress  has  been  steadily  increasing.  The  FECA  ex-
penditure  limits,  by making  it more  difficult  for candidates
espousing  change  to  win  elections,  will  make  American
politics  more  unresponsive  and  will  inevitably  lead  to  an  in-
crease  in alienation  and  apathy  —  two  ills the  statutes  were
supposed  to cure.

Independent  candidates  and  political  parties  without  a
national  committee  are  discriminated  against  by provisions
allowing  national  and  state  committees  of political  parties
to  spend  in  excess  of  the  candidate-expenditure  limits.

IV.

citizens

The  FECA  limits

in  spending  money,

to

process

This  provision  effectively  limits  participation  in  the
campaign
(1)  the
electoral
organizations  and  (2) the  mass  media.  While  these  groups
play  vital  and  important  roles  in  the  political  process,
enhancing  their  relative  power  by stifling  expression  by in-
dividual  citizens  and  independent  groups,  which  greatly
enriches  the public debate,  is constitutionally  indefensible.

candidates’

Vv.

The  FECA’s  limitations  on  contributions  to  political
candidates  by  individuals  and  groups  violate  the  con-
stitutional  rights  of  donors,  candidates,  and  volunteer
political  workers.  Limits  on  contributions  will  neither
reduce  corruption  nor  equalize  political  speech.

Contribution  limits  discriminate  invidiously  against
challengers  to  incumbents,  minor  parties  and  independent
candidates  by making  it more  difficult  for such  parties  and
candidates  to  compete  effectively  in  the  electoral  process.
Large  contributions  have  functioned  as  “seed”  money  for
groups  advocating  social  change  throughout  our  history,
and  they  play  the  same  role  in  political  campaigns.  In
closely  contested  races  in  particular,  large  contributions
received  relatively  early  in  the  campaign  provide  the
wherewithal  to  raise  larger  amounts  of money  (generally  in
smaller  contributions)  to be used  in the campaign.  Limiting
large  contributions  thus  lessens  political  participation  and
reduces  the level  of political  debate.

Limits  on  the  incidental  expenses  of volunteers  restrict
rights  of  speech  and  association,  Edwards  v.  South
Carolina,  372  U.S.  229  (1963),  yet  serve  no  compelling
en-
governmental
forcement.

and  invite  discriminatory

interest

32

—

33

Providing  substantially  higher  limits  on  contributions
by a candidate  or  his family than  on  contributions  by others
impermissibly  discriminates  against  candidates  without
personal  wealth.  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134 (1972).

The  requirements  that  political  committees  must  meet
before  they  may  contribute  to  candidates  violate  the  First
and  Fifth  Amendments  by placing  irrational  restrictions  on
the  exercise  of  protected  rights  of  political  expression  and
association.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51  (1973).
Moreover,  these  restrictions  enhance  the  power  of already
organized  and  established  interest  groups,  contrary  to  the
rationale  supposedly  justifying  the  statute.

recipients

Finally,  the  contribution  limits  are  overbroad.  The
same  limitations  apply  no  matter  what  federal  office  is
being  sought.  They  do  not  take  into  account  differences
among  potential
(e.g.,  differences  between
presidential
granted
funding  for
presidential  campaigns  and  those  denied  such  funding),
and  they  ignore  several  less  intrusive  means  such  as
properly  drafted  disclosure  statutes.  United  States  v.
Robel,  389  U.S.  258  (1967);  Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State,
378  U.S.  S00 (1964).

candidates

federal

VI.

The  federal  subsidy  provisions  of Subtitle  H violate  the
general-welfare  clause  of the  Constitution  by giving  money
directly  to  political  parties  and  candidates.  There  is  no
public  interest  in  relieving  candidates  of the  need  to  raise
money  to  finance  their  political  activities,  and  none  of the
legislative  rationales  supports  these  provisions.
Direct
financing  also  effectively  establishes  the  two  major  parties,
requires
which  _ necessarily
discriminates  invidiously  against  some  candidates,  leads  to
excessive  government  entanglement  in  the  affairs  of
political  parties.  and  encourages  continuous  struggles  in

allocation

formula

an

Congress  over  who  is to  have  what  political  power.  Meeks
v.  Pittenger,  43  U.S.L.W.  4596  (May  19,  1975);  Lemon  v.
Kurtzman,  403  U.S.  608  (1971).  Even  if some  method  of
federal  funding  is permissible,  far  less  intrusive  means  are
available.

subsidies)

Chapter  95  (general-election

allocates
federal  subsidies  in  a  discriminatory  and  to  some  extent
random  fashion  with  respect  to  third  parties  and  in-
dependent  candidates  by effectively  excluding  them  from
public  financing  because  they  are  thought  to  have  no  chance
to  win  office,  while  subjecting  them  to  the  limits  on  con-
tributions  because  they  might  be  corrupted.  Four  par-
ticularly  discriminatory  features  are:  (1) the  use  of a  prior-
vote  criterion  to determine  the  level  of funding;  (2) the  fact
that  major  parties  receive  the  full  subsidy  prior  to  the
November  election,  while  small  and  new  parties  and  in-
dependents  receive  nothing  until  after  the  election  (if
anything  even  then);  (3) only general-election  candidates  on
the  ballot  in  ten  or  more  states  are  eligible  at  all;  and  (4)
only  general-election  candidates  receiving  5  percent  or
more  of  the  popular  vote  are  eligible  at  all.  Williams  v.
Rhodes,  supra;  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354  U.S.  234
(1957).

Chapter  96  (pre-nomination  subsidies)  discriminates
against  candidates  who  raise  contributions  in  less  than
twenty  states,  those  who  seek  ballot  positions  through
petitions  (because  no  federal  funding  is  provided  for  ob-
taining  ballot  status),  and  those  who  lack  the  support  of
organized  interest  groups  with  state  affiliations  which  can
easily  make  certain  candidates  eligible  for  primary  match-
ing grants. Lubin  v. Panish,  415 U.S.  709  (1974).

34

Vil.

Although  disclosure  of  certain  contributions  enables
voters  to  make  informed  electoral  choices,  and  the  govern-
ment  may  legitimately  require  disclosure  to  that  end,  the
FECA  disclosure  requirements  are  unconstitutionally  over-
broad.  They  intrude  on  protected  rights  of  associational
privacy,  and  deter  individuals  from  making  political  con-
tributions  by subjecting  them  to  the  fear  of harassment  in
their  business  and  employment  relationships.  NAACP  v.
Alabama,  357  U.S.  449 (1958).

Minor-party  and  independent  candidates  face  par-
ticular  difficulties,  as  the  Court  of Appeals  recognized,  and
Chief  Judge  Bazelon  emphasized  in his dissent.  The various
interests  justifying  disclosure  do  not  support  the  sweep  of
the  FECA’s  disclosure  provisions  which  treat  all parties  and
candidates  indiscriminately.  Pollard  vy.  Roberts,  283  F.
Supp.  248 (E.D.  Ark.),  aff'd per curiam,  393  U.S.  14 (1968).

There  is  no  substantial  nexus  between  the  claimed
governmental  interests  and  the  disclosure  thresholds.
Disclosure  should  be  required  only  of those  contributions
whose  donors  may  seek  to  influence  governmental  action
unlawfully.  The  threshold  of  disclosure  should  be  much
higher,  and  calibrated  to the office  involved,  rather  than  set
at  a single,  arbitrary  threshold  for all federal  offices.  United
States  v.  Rumely,  345  U.S.  41  (1953).

The  provisions  imposing  disclosure  and  burdensome
reporting  requirements  on  individuals  who  make  in-
dependent  expenditures  of  more  than  $100  serve  no
legitimate  purpose  and  have a chilling  effect  on  political  ex-
pression.

35

Vill.

The  enforcement  provisions  of the  challenged  statutes
are  unconstitutional  in  two  major  respects.  First,  vesting
the power  to nominate  and  appoint  four  of the six members
of the  Federal  Election  Commission  (FEC)  in  the  major-
party  leadership  in Congress  violates  Article  II, Section  2 of
the  Constitution,  which  vests  the  power  of appointment  of
officers  of the  United  States  in  the  President.  FEC  mem-
bers  must  be appointed  by the  President  if the  FEC  has  any
substantial  powers  which  Congress  itself could  not  exercise,
and  the  FEC  plainly  does  have  such  powers.  Springer  v.
Government  of the Philippine  Islands,  277  U.S.  189 (1928).
Even  under  the test  used  by the  Court  of Appeals,  the  FEC
is vested  with  primarily  executive  and  judicial  duties  and
therefore  must  be appointed  by the  President.  Even  if some
of the  FEC’s  powers  have  not  yet  been  utilized,  the  ap-
pointment  issue  is ripe  for  adjudication,  since  the  issue  is
whether  Congress  may  constitutionally  create  the  com-
mission  it did,  not  whether  the  FEC  may  at  some  time  in
the future  employ  its full battery  of powers.

Second,  the  enforcement  provisions  give the  FEC  the
power  to  stand  astride  American  politics  in  a  fashion  far
more  intrusive  and  chilling  than  the  Constitution  permits.
Lamont  v.  Postmaster  General,  381  U.S.  301  (1965).  The
FEC  has virtually  unfettered  discretion  to enforce  statutory
provisons  that  are  often  hopelessly  vague.  The  possibility
for  discriminatory  enforcement  is  particularly  hi,  since
the  FEC  is  dominated  (by  virtue  of  several  statutory
provisions)  by incumbent  members  of Congress,  one of the
very  interests  the  FEC  is  supposed  to  regulate.  Similarly,
Subtitle  H  allows  the  FEC  and  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury  excessive  discretion  in allocating  federal  subsidies
to presidential  candidates.

The  FEC’s  power  to  disqualify  citizens  from  federal
candidacy  plainly  conflicts  with  constitutional  provisions
governing  qualifications  for  federal  office.  Powell  v.  Mc-
Cormack,  395 U.S.  486 (1969).

36

ARGUMENT

I.

The Court  of Appeals  Failed  To Subject the Challenged
Legislation  to the Critical  Scrutiny  Necessary  When
Fundamental  Rights  Are at Stake.

The  challenged  legislation  massively  reorders  the
American  political  process.  It  avowedly  and  drastically
regulates  peaceful  political  communication.  It  subjects
candidates  and  their  supporters  to  a  panoply  of sanctions,
including  criminal  penalties,  for  what  since  the  beginning
of the  republic  have  been  customary  campaign  activities.  It
envelops  every  participant  in the  political  process  in a com-
plex, burdensome,  often  ambiguous  maze  of regulation.

This  legislation  is beyond  question  the  most  far-reach-
ing regulation  of political  communication  in the  history  of
the  republic.  It  touches  upon  rights  indispensable  to  the
preservation  of our  other  freedoms.  In  addition,  it deals  en-
tirely  with  matters  in which  those  who  enacted  the  law  have
a considerable  self-interest.  As  a result,  the  legislation  does
not  carry  the  normal  presumption  of  constitutionality.
Rather,  it  requires  an  exceptionally  critical  exercise  of
judicial  review.

A.  Both  Principles  General  to  First  Amendment  Cases
and  Factors  Unique  to  This  Legislation  Require  Far More
Exacting  Scrutiny  Than  the Court  of Appeals Accorded.

The  Court  of Appeals  failed  to give this  legislation  ade-
quate  scrutiny.  It  wholly  failed  to  examine  the  actual
impact  of the  statutes  on  the  political  process.  It  uncriti-
cally  deferred  to  legislative  judgments  as  to  what  means
were  appropriate,  and  did  not  examine  whether  there  was  a
rational—let  alone  necessary—relationship  between  the
ends  sought  and  the means  chosen.

37

Before  upholding  a  law  imposing  restrictions  on
speech,  the  courts  must  determine,  at  the  very  least,  that:
(1) the  governmental  interest  in the  restriction  is unrelated
to  the  suppression  of free  expression;  (2) the  governmental
non-speech  interest  is  legitimate  and  compelling;  (3)  the
threat  to this  interest  is real  and  not  merely  speculative;  (4)
the  restriction  on  speech  is  incidental  or  indirect;  (S)  the
means  employed  are  rationally  and  precisely  related  to  the
protection  of the  governmental  interest  and  are  the  least
intrusive  available;  and  (6)  the  specified  enforcement
process  is impartial,  well  monitored  and  well  suited  to  the
highly  sensitive  task  involved.  See  Erznoznik  v.  City  of
Jacksonville,  43  U.S.L.W.  4809  (June  23,  1975);  New  York
Times  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713  (1971);  Kramer  v.
Union  Free  School  District,  395 U.S.  621  (1969);  United  States
v.  O'Brien,  391  U.S.  367,  377  (1968);  United  States  v.
Robel,  389  U.S.  258  (1967);  Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State.
378  U.S.  500  (1964);  New  York  Times  v.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.
254  (1964);  Sherbert  vy.  Verner,  374  U.S.  398  (1963);
Speiser  vy.  Randall,  357  U.S.  513  (1958);  Thornhill  v.
Alabama,  310 U.S.  88 (1940).

Plaintiffs  will  demonstrate,  as  to  each  section  of  the
challenged  statutes,  that  one  or  more—usually  several—
elements  of this  test  cannot  be met.  At this  point,  we  identify
those  factors  unique  to this  legislation  which  suggest  that  the
closest  scrutiny  under the tests  are  enumerated  is warranted.

(1)  This  action  challenges  the  constitutionality  of laws
enacted  under  the  banner  of  reform  in  the  wake  of
America’s  worst  political  scandal.  History  demonstrates,
however,  that  claims  that  government  and  the  political
process  are  being  subverted,  whether  by a foreign  power,  a
totalitarian  ideology  or  powerful  economic  interests,  often
generate  demands  for  protective  action  which  may  lack
discrimination.  Not  infrequently,  the fear of subversion  and
zeal  for new  law  are  far  more  substantial  than  the  evidence

———   —

generating  them.  And,  not  infrequently,  the  measures  taken
are  adopted  without  adequate  attention  to  all  their  con-
sequences  and  implications.

Some  public-opinion  polls  relied  upon  by  appellees
and  the  Court  of Appeals  (op. at  1514)  tend  to  demonstrate
that  public  alienation  and  mistrust  of the  political  process
are  at  a  high  level,  as  is the  belief that  government  is run  by
a  few  big interests.  Findings  I, 133  and  149-55,  pp.  114-28;
App.  II-159-89,  206-53.  Even  assuming  that  election  prac-

even  assuming  that  the  challenged  legislation  will  operate
to  lower  that  alienation  and  mistrust  (an  assumption  that  is
surely  incorrect,  see  infra,  pp.  67-75),  the  implication  that

view  of the Constitution.’

(2)  Where  legislation  infringes  on  First  Amendment
political  rights,  and  especially  where  one  group  limits  the
political  freedom  of others,  searching  and  critical  scrutiny
is  essential  and  a  broad  presumption  of constitutionality
unjustifiable.  Thus  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter,  even  while
denying  the  “preferred  position’’  of  First  Amendment
rights,  nevertheless  declared  that  ‘those  liberties  of  the
individual  which  his  sry  has  attested  as  the  indispensable
conditions  of an  open  as  against  a  closed  society  come  to
this  Court  with  a momentum  for  respect  lacking  when  ap-
peal  is made  to  liberties  which  derive  merely  from  shifting
economic  arrangements.”  Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77,
95 (1949) (concurring  opinion).

and  the  First  Amendment  59-64  (1966).  As  Professor

First  Amendment  as  an  Absolute,”’  1961  Sup.  Ct.  Rev.  245,
255.  Judicial  scrutiny  is thus  at  its  greatest  when  there  is a
