The  First  Amendment  by its  very  terms  suggests  that
our  primary  national  policy  is no  regulation  in  the  area  of
political  communication.
See  Columbia  Broadcasting
Svstem,  Inc.,  y.  Democratic  National  Committee,  412  U.S.
94,  126  (1973).  This  is not  a costless  policy,  for  unhampered
expression  may  well  be  harmful—for  example,  through  the
circulation  of  falsehoods  against  a  candidate.  It  may  also
by  permitting
physical  confrontation
risk
controversial,  insulting,  and  provocative  demonstrations,
or  seem  untair  by permitting  the  exclusion  of certain  points
of  view  by  private  publications.  But  freedom  of  any  kind
imposes  costs,  and  the  First  Amendment  dictates  at  the
very  least  that  such  costs  are  presumptively  less  than  those
entailed  in  restrictive  control  by  law.  See  Miami  Herald
Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241  (1974);  New  York
Times  y.  Sullivan,  supra;  Edwards  vy.  South  Carolina,  372
U.S.  229  (1963).  As  Mr.  Justice  Brandeis  said:  “‘{flear  of
serious  injury  cannot  alone  justify  suppression  of  free
speech  and  assembly....  Those  who  won  our
inde-
pendence  were  not  cowards.  They  did  not  fear  political
change.  They  did  not  exalt  order  at  the  cost  of  liberty.”
Whitney  vy.
357,  376-77  (1927)
(concurring  opinion).

California,

274  U.S.

The  freedom  to  engage  in  peaceful  political  com-
munication  not  only  is  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amend-
ment,  although  that  would  be  sufficient,  but  inheres  in
every  constitutional
provision  relating  to  democratic,
representative  government.  Without  that  freedom,  the
plain  intent  and  effect  of those  other  provisions  might  easily
be  vitiated.  Stromberg  v.  California,  283  U.S.  359,  369
(1931).  If candidates  or  other  citizens  are  not  free  to  dis-
seminate  political  information,  to  criticize  officials,  to  en-
gage  in political  advocacy—and  to  spend  the  money  that  it
costs  to  do  so—the  competition  for  elective  office  which
makes  democratic  government  responsive  government  will
be  gravely  weakened.  Constitutional  provisions  protecting
the  integrity  of elections  and  guaranteeing  the  right  to vote
will  become  abstractions  having  no  relation  to  the  actual
functioning  of the  organs  of power.  An  honest  mistake  in
legislative  judgment  in  this  area  carries  with  it  conse-
quences  potentially  far  more  dangerous  than  in  areas  such
as  economic  or  safety  regulation.  See  United  States  v.
Carolene  Products  Co.,  304  U.S.  144,  152,  n.  4  (1938)?
Mistakes  here  go  to  the  heart  of political  freedom,  and  be-
cause  they  may  permanently  change  the  political  pro-
cess—the  very  process  counted  on  to  correct  error—they
may  well  be  irredeemable.  Thus  Mr.  Justice  Cardozo  des-
cribed  freedom  of speech  and  thought  as  ‘‘the  matrix,  the

*  Although  the  Court  of  Appeals  chided  the  appellees  for  asserting
that  the  standards  of review  applicable  to  economic  regulation  should
be  used  to  judge  the  challenged  statutes  (op.  at  1519),  the  court  itself
substantially  adopted  that  theory.  Citing  cases  upholding  the  Alabama
Unemployment  Compensation  Law,  Steward  Machine  Co.  v.  Davis,  301
U.S.  548  (1937),  and  the  Federal  Social  Security  Act  of  1935,  49  Stat.
620,  Carmichael  v.  Southern  Coal  Co.,  301  U.S.  49S (1937),  the court  in
effect  declined  to  apply  the  “least  intrusive  means”’  test  once  it decided
that  some  limitation
on,  for  instance,  contributions  was  within
Congress’  power  (op. at  1518).

42

43

indispensable  condition  of  nearly  every  other  form  of
freedom.”  Palko  v.  Connecticut,  302  U.S.  319,  327 (1937).

The  constitutional  approach  to  legislation  infringing
on  political  liberties  differs  in other  essentials  from  that  of
economic  regulation.  In  the  latter  case,  legislatures  are
permitted  to  make  distributional  judgments  between
groups  as  to  the  benefits  of  regulation,  Daniel  vy.  Family
Sec.  Life Ins.  Co.,  336  U.S.  220  (1949),  on  the  basis  of data
which  the  courts  will  rarely  second-guess,  Williamson  v.
Lee  Optical,  348  U.S.  483  (1955).  Indeed,  it is the essence  of
a  pluralistic  democracy  that  the  results  of group  interplay
with  respect  to  economic  regulation  not  be  upset  by a  non-
elected  branch.

Where  political  liberties  are  involved,  however,  gross
distributional  judgments  among  groups  are  not  permitted
because  individual  rights  which  attach  to  every  citizen  are
at  stake.  Courts  cannot,  in  those  circumstances,  show  great
deference  to  legislative  judgment,  for  political  liberty  can
be  protected  only  by a highly  particularistic  inquiry  into  the
need  for  the  legislation  in  question  and  its  impact  on  par-
ticular  individuals  and  groups.  Whereas  the  grossest
correlation  of  ends  and  means  can  save  regulation  of  an
economic  nature,  Railway  Express  Agency  v.  New  York,
336  U.S.  106  (1949),  only  the  strongest  interest  of the  state
can  justify  stilling  even  a  highly  individualistic  act  of anger
so  long  as  it implicates  political  communication.  See  Street
v.  New  York,  394  U.S.  576  (1969);  Schneider  v.  Irvington,
308  U.S.  147,  161  (1939).

(3)  Searching  judicial  scrutiny  is  necessary  for  yet
another  reason:  those  controlling  the  political  branches  of
government  have  an  enormous  stake  in  how  the  political
dialogue  is carried  on,  and  cannot  be  expected  to  regulate
that  dialogue  with  a  fine  impartiality.  Officeholders  are,  of
necessity  and  quite  legitimately,  concerned  with  the  pre-
servation  and  enlargement  of  their  own  and  their  sup-

porters’  political  power.  It  merely  recognizes  fact,  rather
than  casts  aspersion,  to  acknowledge  that  this  concern
must  affect  the  content  of any  regulation  of political  com-
munication.

History  has  not  been  kind  to  legislation  ‘‘cleansing”
the  political  process  of disfavored  activities  of opponents  of
those  in power.  The  challenge  to the  Sedition  Act  of 1798,  1
Stat.  596,  for  example,  while  never  heard  by  this  Court,
“has  carried  the  day  in  the  court  of  history.’’  New
York  Times  v.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.  at  276.  That  it  has  no
doubt  stems  from  a  recognition  that  “‘it  is  a profound  tenet
of  democracy  that  no  government  official  has  the  legal
power  to silence  such  commentary  about  himself.”  Kalven,
The Negro and the First Amendment  15 (1966).

This  Court  has  held  that  the  presumption  of constitu-
tionality  draws  its  strength  from  the  assumption  that
legislative  structure  provides  fair  representation  of ‘‘all  the
people’  and  that,  when  the  constitutional  issue  entails  a
challenge  to  that  assumption,  the  presumption  is  weak-
ened.  Kramer  v.  Union  Free  School  District,  39S  U.S.  621,
628 (1969).  Claims  such  as  those  made  here,  that  a majority
is trying  to  limit  the  political  communication  of a  minority
or  that  incumbents  are  attempting  to  restrict  the  political
activities  of challengers,  are  just  such  an  attack  upon  the
assumption.  This  point  in  fact  is  the  simple  proposition
that  great  deference  to legislative  decisions  cannot  be justi-
fied  in the face  of a plain conflict  of interest.

The  court  below  upheld  extensive  and  unprecedented
restrictions  on  political  communication  on  the grounds  that
“(t]he  wiles  of ambitious  office  seekers  and  their  supporters
are  not  easily cabined”’  (op. at  1518).  It should  have  recalled
that  every  one  who  voted  for this  legisiation  is such  an  office
seeker.

Notwithstanding  these  reasons  unique  to  this  legisla-
tion  which  should  have  prompted  the  closest  scrutiny,  the

44

45

court  below  failed  to give the challenged  legislation  even  the
normally  careful  scrutiny  required  when  First  Amendment
rights  are  at  stake.*  Rather,  it  relied  upon  the  expressed
intent  of  the  sponsors  of  the  legislation  to  eliminate  cor-
ruption,  to  redress  inequality,  to  reduce  the  power  of spe-
cial  interests  by  eliminating  the  “‘corrosive  influence’’  of
campaign  money,  and  to  prevent  another  Watergate  (op. at
1510-16).  Whether  the  record  really  suggests  the  baleful
state  of  affairs  described  by  the  sponsors,  whether  the
legislation  may  indeed  aggravate  rather  than  cure  the
identified  abuses,  and  whether  far  less  intrusive  means  are
available  received  only  cursory  examination  to  the  extent
addressed  at  all.

B. The  Expenditure  or  Contribution  of  Money  for
Political Purposes  Is  Protected  by the First Amendment  and
May  Be  Regulated  Only  in  Carefully  Defined  Cir-

cumstances.

The  primary  reason  for  the  failure  of the  court  below
to  afford
the  necessary  scrutiny  was  its  conclusion,
premised  upon  United  States  v.  O'Brien,  supra,  that  all
contributions  and  expenditures  for political  purposes  are  so
infected  with  “non-speech  elements”’  that  virtually  un-
limited  regulation  is permissible.  Indeed,  it not  only upheld
the  extensive  restrictions  of the  present  legislation  but  went
on  to say that  they “may well  be not  enough”  (op. at  1582).

O'Brien  does  not  sustain  such  an  analysis.  In O'Brien,
this  Court  held  that  Congress  may  prohibit  the  destruction
of a  draft  card  even  when  that  destruction  serves  as  the  sym-
bolic  expression  of  an  idea.  The  Court  said  no  more  than
that  speech  incidental  to regulable  conduct  might be limited;  it
did  not  in  any  sense  endorse  the  wholesale  supression

*  The  Court  of Appeals’  majority  opinion  acknowledged  elements  of
the  proper  standard  of review  (op.  at  1516-20),  but  the  opinion  comes
nowhere  near  the  searching  and  critical  inquiry  that  the  court  itself
agreed  was  necessary.

of  speech.  It  did  not,  for  example,  hold  that  all  speech
opposing  the draft  might  be suppressed  since  some  of it en-
tailed  the burning  of draft  cards.

That  campaign  expenditures  and  contributions  have
the  status  of protected  speech  does  not  mean  that  certain
contributions  or  expenditures  may  not,  because  of the  par-
ticular  circumstances  surrounding  them,  be  regulated
because  they  entail  minimal  elements  of protected  speech
or  seem  to entail  an  immediate  and  high  danger  of a quid
pro  quo.  Drawing  any  such  lines,  however,  must  follow  a
study  of  the  nature  of  the  abuses  demonstrated  in  the
record  and  of  the  actual  impact  of  the  challenged
legislation  on  the political  process.

(1)  Expenditures  for Political  Purposes  Are  Protected

by the First Amendment.

Expenditures  for  political  purposes,  whether  by an  indi-
vidual  or  a  group  on  behalf  of a cause  or  of a candidate,  or
by  candidates  on  behalf  of  themselves,  are  essential  to
political  speech  and  are  protected  by the First  Amendment.

As Mr.  Justice  Douglas  once  noted:

“The  making  of a  political  speech  up  to  now  has
always  been  one  of the  preferred  rights  protected
by the First  Amendment.  It usually  costs  money  to
communicate  an  idea  to  a large  audience.  But  no
one  would  seriously  contend  that  a  limitation  on
the  expenditure  of  money  to  print  a  newspaper
would  not  deprive  the  publisher  of  freedom  of
the  press.  Nor  can  the  fact  that  it costs  mroney  to
make  a  speech—whether  it  be  hiring  a  hall  or
purchasing  time  on  the  air—make  the speech  any
the  less  an  exercise  of First  Amendment  rights.”

46

47

United  States  vy.  Auto  Workers,  352  U.S.  567,  594
(1957)  (dissenting  opinion).

Virtually  every  political  communication  involves  the
expenditure  of  money.  Even  the  paradigm  case  of  the
speaker  in  Hyde  Park  involves  transportation  and  a  soap-
box.  Demonstrations,  see  Edwards  v.  South  Carolina,  372
U.S.  229 (1963),  require  that  people  be moved  and  concen-
trated  in one  place.  Handbills,  see  Talley  v.  California,  362
U.S.  60  (1960),  require  a  printing  press,  paper,  and  some
means  of distribution.  In  bad  weather,  speeches  require  a
hall.  Bull  horns,  pens,  pencils,  and  even  picket  signs  cost
money.  Similarly,  litigation  to  preserve  one’s  rights  can
entail  substantial  expenses.  NAACP  y.  Button,  371  U.S.
415  (1963).

Virtually  every  form  of political  activity  thus  requires
either  out-of-pocket  expenditures  or  at  least  an  “‘oppor-
tunity  cost’’  (in  economic  terms,  the  foregoing  of  oppor-
tunities  to  earn  money).  Volunteers  need  a  paid  staff  to
supervise  them  and  may  have  to  sacrifice  income  to  devote
their  time  to the campaign.

These  examples,  moreover,  involve  the  less  costly  and
often  less  efficient  means  of political  communication.  Tele-
vision  speeches,  direct  mailings,  transportation,  a  staff  to
research  issues,  consultants,  and  newspaper  advertisements
are  all essential  to effective  political  communication  and  in-
volve  substantial  financial  outlays.

It mocks  the  purpose  of the  First  Amendment  to  argue
that,  while  such  communication  cannot  be  restricted,  the  un-

*  Mr.  Justice  Dougias  would  have  found  18 U.S.C.  § 610  (prohibiting
corporate  and  labor-union  contributions)  unconstitutional;  the  passage
quoted  in the  text  is part  of his  opinion  on  that  point.  The  majority  did
not  reach  the  constitutional  question  but  instead  remanded  the case  for
trial.

derlying  expenditures  can.  Under  this  view,  a limitation  on
how  much  newspapers  spend  on  publication—to  hire
reporters,  investigate  stories,  and  print  editions—would  not
be  a regulation  of the  newspaper  itself.  But  see  Grosjean  v.
American  Press  Co.,  297  U.S.  233 (1936).  Political  activities
require  the  expenditure  of  resources.  Prevent  the  use  of
resources  and  the  activities  will  end.  Prevent  the  use  of
money  to  obtain  the  resources  and  the  same  result  is ob-
tained.

Expenditures  for  political  communication  fall  within
what  this  Court  has  called  ‘pure  speech’”’  under  the  First
Amendment,  Edwards  v.  South  Carolina,  372  U.S.  229
(1963).  Since  protection  of such  speech  is a ‘‘major  purpose
of the  amendment,”  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S.  214,  218
(1966),  expenditures  for  political  communication  have  the
same  stature  as  the  communication  itself.  “Liberty  of cir-
culating  is  as  essential  to  [freedom  of  speech  and  of  the
press]  as  liberty  of publishing;  indeed,  without  the  circula-
tion,  the  publication  would  be  of  little  value.”’  Lovell  v.
Talley  v.
Griffin,  303  U.S.  444,  452  (1938);  accord,
California,  362  U.S.  at 64.

Decisions  of this  Court  clearly  support  these  proposi-
tions.  Not  only  do  they  derive  from  the  cases  cited  above,
but  New  York  Times  vy.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.  254  (1964),
directly  held  that  it was  “immaterial”  for First  Amendment
purposes  that  the  advertisement  there  in question  had  been
purchased.  Accord,  Bigelow  y.  Virginia,  43  U.S.L.W.  4734
Indeed,  one  important  function  of
(June  16,  1975).
‘freedom  of the  press”  is  to  preserve  avenues  of  citizen
communication  through  the  medium  of  political  ad-
vertising.

It makes  no  difference  whether  the  expenditure  is on
behalf of an  issue  or  on  behalf of a candidate.  In either  case
those  making  the  expenditure  seek  to  communicate  with
the  public  to  promote  their  views  on  governmental  policy.

48

49

The  First  Amendment  does  not  distinguish  between
praising  unionism  in  the  abstract  and  tnviting  people  to
join  a  particular  union.  Thomas  v.  Collins,  323  U.S.  516
(1945S).  Nor  does  it distinguish  between  espousing  a  cause
and  supporting  the political  candidates  who believe  in it.

(2)  Contributions for Political  Purposes  Are  Protected

by the First

Amendment.

Contributions  for  political  purposes  are  a means  of es-
pousing  political  ideas  and  pursuing  political  goals.  They
are  not,  as  the  court  below  suggested  (op.  at  1517),  in  any
sense  the  functional  equivalent  of votes.  When  put to cam-
paign  purposes,  contributions  enable  candidates  to  com-
municate  their  views  to  the  voters  who  then  make a free
choice.  Contributions  thus  serve  to  increase  the  level  of
political  communication.

The

sole  argument

for  distinguishing

between
expenditures  and  contributions  for  similar  ends  is that,  in
the  case  of the contribution,  the  donor  gives  up control  over
the  manner  in  which  the  money  is ultimately  expended.  So
long  as  contributions  are  used  for campaign  purposes,  that
attempted  distinction  fails,  for  the  choice  between  an
expenditure  and  a contribution  is one  made  by donors  as  to
the  most  efficient  means  of espousing  their  political  ideas
and  pursuing  their  political  goals.

At  ‘me  point  in  the  history  of  a  movement,  it  may
make  sense  for  adherents  to  spend  money  acting  in-
dependently  in  its  support,  e.g.,  newspaper  advertise-
ments,  bumper  stickers.  At  other  times  giving  to  organiza-
tions  which  espouse  the  underlying  ideas  but  do  not
endorse  or  support  candidates  may  seem  most  sensible.  At
yet  other  times  it may  be  more  appropriate  to  give directly
to candidates  who  espouse  or  symbolize  that  cause,  or  to do
all  these  things.  The  ultimate  goal,  after  all,  is to  see  one’s

views  become  government  wlicy.  Electing  the  right  candi-
dates  is central  to achieving  that  end.

To  restrict  donors  in their  choice  of means  of expres-
sion  is  directly  to  limit  the  articulation  of  their  political
goals,  the  effectiveness  of their  pursuit  of those  goals,  and
their  right to associate  in combination  with  others  to  make
that effectiveness  greater.

The  1968  presidential  campaign  of appellant  Eugene
McCarthy  is  a  paradigm  case  of  why  contributions  to  a
candidate  are  protected  by  the  First  Amendment.  Indi-
viduals  who  contributed  to  the  McCarthy  campaign  in
order  to  further  the  cause  of  terminating  the  war  in
Southeast  Asia  were  able  to pool their  resources  in the most
effective  way  available  at  that  time.  if each  had  been  re-
quired  to  act  only  as  a  volunteer,  or  to  contribute  only  to
‘issue’  organizations  not  supporting  a candidate,  not  only
would  the  McCarthy  campaign,  which  was  heavily  de-
pendent  on  campaign  money.  have  been  far less  successful,
see  infra,  p.  135,  but  all  contributors  as  a  result  would
have  been  far  less  able  to  further  the  cause  in  which  they
believed  so  strongly.  Campaign  contributions  were  thus  a
means  of exercising  the  freedom  of association  as  well  as
the freedom  of expression.

Similarly,  many  persons  intensely  committed  to  the
cause  of ending  the  war  made  the  quite  rational  decision
that  only  by extending  their  influence  beyond  their  voting
districts  would  they be able to further  that  cause  effectively.
It  is  too  constricted  a  notion  of  the  political  process  to
suggest  that  persons  ought  not  to be able  to propagate  their
views  effectively  to  voters  in  other  voting  districts.  As  was
the  case  with  the  McCarthy  New  Hampshire  primary  cam-
paign,  events  in  one  district  can  have  an  enormous
influence  on  issues  affecting  all  voters,  and  political
freedom  is not  possible  if citizens  are  not  free  to  attempt  to

50

51

influence  the  electoral  process  wherever  it  affects  them.
Pierce  v.  Kohler,  163  Wisc.  615,  158  N.W.  696 (1916).

The  contribution  of  money  to  a  candidate,  in  con-
junction  with  other  individuals  making  similar  contribu-
tions  or  contributions  of their  time  and  energy,  is precisely
the  sort  of  associational  freedom  protected  in  NAACP  v.
Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958),  and  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,
414  U.S.  51  (1973).  Unless  the  First  Amendment  right  of
freedom  ‘“‘to  petition  .
.  for  a  redress  of grievances”  is to
be reduced  to  a shadow,  the  rights  of individuals  to join to-
gether  to  support  like-minded  candidates  with  financial
contributions  must  be preserved.

.

Nor  can  one  distinguish  between  contributions  and
other  political  acts  because  of  any  “obligation”  that  is
created.  The  volunteer  lawyer,  whose  contribution  of time  is
not  limited  by  the  statute,  and  the  contributor  of  money
stand  on  the  same  footing  so  far  as  that  is concerned,  as  do
other  kinds  of  protected  activities  such  as  a  New  York
Times  editorial  endorsement  or  favorable  coverage  by
Time  or  Newsweek.  Indeed,  the  latter  are  probably  far
more  valuable,  and  thereby  create  a  more  compelling
“obligation,’’  than  any  contribution  received  by  a  candi-
date.  No  one  would  suggest  that,  because  issues  affecting  the
press  such  as  shield  laws  or  postage  rates  are  subject  to
congressional  action,  the  press  may  be  barred  from  en-
dorsing  a  candidate  on  the  ground  that  it  might  be  pur-
Suing  its “special  interests.”

Of course,  those  who  contribute  money  to  a candidate
hope  to  further  their  political,
social  and  economic
views—just  as  those  who  engage  in  demonstrations  and
rallies,  write  books  on  political  issues,  organize  like-minded
citizens,  draft  and  circulate  petitions,  buy  advertisements,
publish  periodicals  or  newspapers,  make  speeches,  sponsor
seminars  or  teach-ins,  or  engage  in  litigation  hope  to
further  their  political,  social  and  economic  views.  Such

activities  are  the  hallmark—as  well  as  the  indispensible
precondition—of a free  society.  See Eastern  Railroad  Presi-
dents’  Conference  v.  Noerr  Motor  Freight,  365  U.S.  127,
139  (1961).

These  principles  have  been  clearly  recognized  in  the
decisions  of this  Court.  In Bates  v.  City of Little  Rock,  361
U.S.  516  (1960),  and  United  States  v.  Rumely,  345  U.S.  41
(1953),  this  Court  recognized  that  contributions  are  pro-
tected  by the  First  Amendment.  In  NAACP  v.  Alabama,
supra,  the  Court  indicated  that  the  individual’s  right  of
expressio.1,  not  the  association’s,  was  at  stake.  The  First
Amendment  applies  to  financial  contributions  to  political
candidates  and  organizations  dealing  with  public  issues.
ACLU  vy.  Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041  (D.D.C.  1973),
vacated  as  moot  sub  nom.  Staats  v.  ACLU,  43  U.S.L.W.
vy.  National  Committee  for
5043  (1975);  United  States
Impeachment,  469  F.2d  1135  (2d  Cir.  1972);  Pollard  v.
Roberts,  283  F.  Supp.  248  (E.D.  Ark.),  affd  per  curiam,
393  U.S.  14 (1968).

(3)  The  Permissible  Zone  of Regulation  of Campaign

Contributions  and Expenditures  Is Narrow.

Although  campaign  contributions  and  expenditures
are  protected  by  the  First  Amendment,  there  is  a per-
missible  zone  of regulation  recognized  in  the  decisions  of
this  Court.  That  zone  is narrow;  it is limited  to the  regula-
tion  of non-speech  elements  or  the  protection  of a  govern-
mental  interest  unrelated  to  freedom  of  expression.  The
Court  has  held  that  “when  ‘speech’  and  ‘non-speech’
elements  are  combined  in  the  same  course  of conduct,  a
sufficiently  important  governmental  interest  in  regulating
the non-speech  element  can  justify incidental  limitations  on
First  Amendment  freedoms.”  United  States  v.  O'Brien,  391
U.S.  367,  376  (1968).  Where  that  principle  applies,  the
regulation  must  be  carefully  tailored  to  fit  the  cir-
cumstances  which  called it into being.

$2

$3

Under  this  Court’s  decisions,  advocacy  which  is  not
likely  or  intended  to  incite  or  produce  illegal  actions,
Brandenburg  v.  Ohio,  395  U.S.  444  (1969),  peaceful,  non-
obstructionist  demonstrations,  Edwards  vy.  South  Carolina,
372  U.S.  229  (1963),  and  non-disruptive  activity  such  as
wearing  arm  bands,  Tinker  v.  Des  Moines  Independent
Community  School  District,  393  U.S.  503  (1969),  cannot  be
labeled  *‘non-speech."’  But  where  the  activity  brings  about
results  other  than  those  which  stem  from  persuasion,  such
as  the  obstruction  of thoroughfares  or  coercive  pressure  on
a  judicial  system,  see  Cox  v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.  536,  379
U.S.  559 (1965),  the  government  may  regulate  the  attendant
conduct  with  narrowly  drawn  provisions  directed  at  the
harm  to be eliminated.

Where  the  money  is  given  on  the  understanding  that
specific  governmental  action  will  be taken  as  a result  there-
of,  or  as  a  reward  therefor.  Congress  may  regulate  the
conduct.  See  United  States  v.  Brewster.  408  U.S.  S01
(1972).  Neither  persuasion  nor  the  usual  exercise  of politi-
cal  power  is  involved.  Rather,  the  official  has  abandoned
freedom  of decision.  That  and  the  acts  inducing  it are  not
protected,  because  the  speech  involved
is  necessarily
assimilated  into  validly  regulable  activity.  See  also  Pitts-
burgh  Press  Co.  y.  Pittsburgh  Commission  on  Human
Relations,  413  U.S.  376  (1973)  (aiding  the  formation  of an
illegal  contract).

The  case  is  different,  however,  when  a  campaign
expenditure  is  made  in  the  hope  of  persuading  voters  to
cast  their  ballot  for  a  particular  candidate,  and  when,  with-
out  the  quid  pro  quo  mentioned  above,  a  contribution  is
made  to  a  candidate  to  use  in  his  campaign  to  communi-
cate  with  voters.  To  be  sure,  those  who  make  such
contributions  or  expenditures  hope  their  interests  or  views
will  be  advanced  by the  election  of the  candidate.  What  is
involved  is  no  more  (assuming  the  politician  takes  the
contribution  of  expenditure  into  account  at  all)  than  the

kind  of  political  judgment  any  politician  makes  as  to
decisions  which  affect  those  who  have  in the  past  aided  his
en-
volunteer
candidacy
dorsements,  contributions  or  otherwise)  or  might  do  so  in
the  future.  It is no  more  subject  to limitation  by the state.

(by  votes,

editorial

work,

The  line  between  these  polar  cases  is  the  line  which
regulation  of expenditures  and  contributions  must  walk;  it
must  be  directly  and  precisely  aimed  at  control  of conduct
with  minimal  speech  elements  while  intruding  as  little  as
possible  on  the exercise  of associational  rights  and  freedom
of speech.

In  walking  this  line,  there  is no  reason  to  distinguish
between  groups  making  expenditures  in the  hope  of elect-
ing candidates  who  will  take  governmental  action  favorable
to their  economic  interests  and  groups  whose  expenditures
appear  more  ideological.  As  Mr.  Justice  Black  stated  for
the Court  in  Eastern  Railroad  Presidents’  Conference  v.
Noerr  Motor Freight,  365  U.S.  at  139:

“It  is neither  unusual  nor  illegal  for people  to
seek  action  on  laws  in  the  hope  that  they  may
bring  about  an  advantage  to  themselves  and  a
disadvantage  to  their  competitors  ...[T]o  dis-
qualify  people  from  taking  a  public  position  on
matters  in  which  they  are  financially  interested
would  deprive  the  government  of  a  valuable
.  deprive  the  people
source  of information  and  .
of their  right  to  petition  in  the  very  instances  in
which  that  right may  be of the  most  importance  to
them.”

.

Because  expenditures  and  contributions  for  political
purposes  have  the  same  constitutional  stature  as  political
speech,  moreover,  they  may  not  be  regulated  because  of
their  content,  Schacht  v.  United  States,  398  U.S.  58 (1970),
or  because  they  are  too  persuasive,  Mills  vy.  Alabama,  384

54

U.S.  214  (1966),  or  because  access  to  communication  is
unequal,  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418
U.S.  241  (1974).  Neither  may  they be regulated  in the  name
of the  anti-democratic  purpose  stated  in the  House  Report
accompanying  this  legislation,  to  protect  the  electorate
from  *‘‘a  sophisticated  advertising  program.’  H.  Rep.  No.
93-1239,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  3 (1974).

In  the  present  case,  the  court  below  made  no  attempt
to  isolate  the  non-speech  elements  but  flatly  held  that  the
speech  itself  could  be  prohibited  by  proscribing  access  to
the  necessary  resources.  For  every  practical  purpose  it held
that  the  contribution  and  expenditure  of  money  for  cam-
paign  purposes  are  simply  not  protected  by  the  First
Amendment.  Indeed,  it denigrated  political  campaigns  by
labeling  them  “Romanesque  political  extravagances,”  a
judgment  as  to  the  content  and  quality  of  the  speech
involved.  With  all  due  respect.  the judiciary’s  responsibility
under  the  Constitution  is to  protect  speech,  not  to  evaluate
it  and  dismiss
it  as  unworthy  of  protection.  Police
Department  of Chicago  v.  Mosley,  408  U.S.  92,  95 (1972).

55

Il.

The  Legislation  Is Not Responsive  to  a Demonstrated  Need,
and  Aggravates  Rather  than  Cures  the  Abuses  Asserted  as
Its Rationale.

A.  The  Statute  Cannot  Be Sustained  on  the  Basis  of the

Justifications  Offered  To Support  It.

has  repeatedly

The  challenged

been
legislation
defended  in  the  name  of  eliminating  corruption,  re-
ducing  inequality,  preventing  “special  interests’  from
exercising  undue  influence  over  government,  and  pre-
venting  another  “‘Watergate.”  The  suggestion  repeatedly
made  is  that  those  who  attack  the  FECA  are  attacking
these  goals.  The  great  tragedy,  however,  is  that  the
legislation  as  framed  is unresponsive  to  these  goals  and  in
The  most
some  respects
suspect  contributions  are  left  untouched,  the  power  of
special  interests  is  increased,  and  the  danger  of  a  recur-
rence  of an  affair  such  as  “‘Watergate”’  is enhanced.

actually  counterproductive.

The  theory  upon  which  the  challenged  legislation  was
enacted  by Congress  and  upheld  by the  Court  of Appeals  is
based  on  three  essential  allegations.

First,  it is claimed  that the costs  of political  campaigns
have  ‘“‘skyrocketed”  at  an  unacceptable  rate  (op.  at  1512).
The  Senate  Report  spoke  of ‘‘the  task  of halting  escalating
campaign  costs,”  S. Rep. No.  93-170,  93rd  Cong.  Ist  Sess.  1
(1973),  while  the House  Report  declared,

“Under  the  present  law  the  impression  persists
that  a  candidate  can  buy  an  election  by simply
spending  large  sums  in  a  campaign....  The
electorate  is  entitled  to  base  its  judgment  on  a
straightforward  presentation  of  a  candidate's
qualifications  for  public  office  and  his  programs
for  the  Nation  rather  than  on  a  sophisticated

56

$7

advertising  program  which  is encouraged  by the
infusion  of vast  amounts  of money.”  H.  Rep.  No.
93-1239,  93-1239,  supra,  at  3.

The  second  allegation  asserts  that  traditional  methods
of campaign  financing  give the  ‘‘wealthy  few  .
.  a stronger
political  voice  than  the  unwealthy  many  because  they  are
able  to give and  spend  more  money  ..

’’  (op. at  1517).

.

.

.

The  third  allegation  is  that  such  financing  creates  a
‘link  .
.  between  campaign  contributions  and  preferential
treatment  by  politicians  ....’’  Hearings  on  S.  372  Before
the  Subcommittee  on  Privileges  and Elections  of the  Senate
Committee  on  Rules  and  Administration,  93rd  Cong.,  Ist
Sess.  149  (1973).  The  claim  is that  campaign  contributors
frequently  solicit  and  receive  special  governmental  action  in
return  for their  contributions.

The  evidence  alleged  to  support  the  first  two  of these
three  rationales  for  the  legislation  fails  to  demonstrate  a
compelling  need.  At  best,  it is speculative;  at  worst,  it flies
in the  face  of common  observation.  And  while  the  third  ra-
tionale,  the  threat  of corruption,  reflects  concern  about  a
genuine  problem,  the  evidence  demonstrating  it in no  sense
calls  for  this  particular  legislative  scheme,  which,  as
demonstrated  infra,  pp.  63-78,  leaves  the  most  suspect
campaign  finance  practices  entirely  untouched  while  out-
lawing vast  areas  of legitimate  activity.

It  must  be  emphasized  that  the  FECA  is  not  par-
ticularized  legislation  aimed  at  specific  suspect  practices.
Rather,  it  is  based  on  a  general  condemnation  of private
campaign  financing.  This  generally  negative  view  of private
campaign  financing  taken  by the  sponsors  of this  legisla-
tion  finds  little  or  no  support  in  the  works  of scholars  who
have  studied  the  subject  over  the  last  generation.
Numerous
scholars,  while  not  denying  that  private  financing  has  some
negative  aspects,  have  concluded  that  it performs  counter-
vailing  valuable  functions.  Cole,  Campaign  Financing  in

Senate  Elections,  passim  (1975);  Alexander,  “‘Links  and
Contrasts  Among  American  Parties  and  Party  Sub-
systems,”  in  Heidenheimer  ed.,  Comparative  Political
Finance  103-04  (1970);  Adamany,  Financing  Politics  230-
33 (1969);  Polsby  and  Wildavsky,  Presidential  Elections  39-
40 (1968);  Key, Politics,  Parties  and  Pressure  Groups  495-
513  (Sth  Ed.  1964);  Heard,  The  Costs  of Democracy  6
(1960);  Overacker,  Money  in Elections  5-14 (1932).

Effective  disclosure  provisions,  properly  drawn,  might
well  be  expected  further  to  illuminate  the  role  private
financing  plays  in  elections.  Unfortunately,  the  FECA
effective
Amendments  came  so  soon
disclosure  provisions  were  enacted  that  additional  informa-
tion  on  the  role  of campaign  money  provided  by disclosure
was  neither  assembled  nor  digested  by  students  of  the

there  time  to  ascertain  fully  whether  operation  of the  dis-

after  the  first

(1)  “Skyrocketing’’  Costs  of Campaigns
Claims  about  skyrocketing  campaign  costs  lack  per-
spective.  Costs  have  of course  increased  over  the years.  But
that  increase  is comparable  to or  less than  changes  in other
relevant  variables.  The rise  in campaign  costs  between  1952
and  1972  (almost  300%)  is almost  exactly  matched  by the
increase  in total  expenditures  for advertising  of commercial
products  (305%  between  1950 and  1972),  Findings  I, 13, pp.
7-8,  App.  II-10-11,  and  by  the  increase  in  the  Gross
National  Product  (310%  between  1952  and  1972),  Jd.  1, 90,
p. 54; App.  II-S2.

Absolute  costs,  moreover,  are  not  so  high  as  some
think.'  In fact,  the  total  1968  campaign  bill  was  16 percent

'  Political  spending  in the  United  States  is low  compart  d to  that  of
other  countries.  Findings  I, 54,  p.  25;  App.  II-26.  Sweden,  for  example,
(continued)

58

59

less  than  the  total  spent  that  year  on  advertising  for tobacco
products.  The  total  cost  of  all  elections  in  1972,  $400
million,  was  less  than  that  spent  on  advertising  by the  two
largest  commercial  advertisers,  id.  I, 92.  In  addition,  per-
centage  increases  in  the  federal  budget  over  a  comparable
period  have  far  outstripped  any  increase  in  the  costs  of
elections,  id.  1, 96, p. S6;  App.  II-53-54.

If the  costs  of campaigns  are  skyrocketing,  then  the
financial  advantages  available  to  incumbent  officeholders
are  rising  at  the  speed  of  light.  While  campaign  costs
increased  300%  between  1952  and  1972,  the  cost  of franked
mail  increased  600%  between  1954  and  1970,  id.  I, 35,  p.
21;  App.  II-23.  The  following  demonstrates  the  incredible
increase  in more  recent  years:

Cost  of the  Frank

1966
1973
1975
1976  (budgeted)

$7.5  million
23.0  million
38.0 million
46.1  million

Id.  1, 36,  p.  21;  App.  II-23;  Wall  Street  Journal,  June  2,
1975,  at  5, col.  1.

Of  course,  use  of  the  frank  throughout  a  Congressman’s
term  benefits  him.  And  since,  as  all parties  have  agreed  and
the  District  Court  found,  “‘the  peak  mailing  periods  for  use
of franked  mail  are  just  prior  to  the  dates  of primary  and
general  elections”  (Findings  I,  34,  p.  21,  App.  II-23),  it  is
unrealistic  to  consider  the  rise  in  campaign  costs  as  an  in-
de,endent  phenomenon.

'  (continued)

spends  four  times  as  much  per  capita  as  the  United  States,  Adamany
and  Agree,  Political  Money:  A  Strategy  for  Campaign  Financing  in
America  9,  221  (forthcoming  this  month  from  Johns  Hopkins  U.
Press).

Increases  in other  perquisites  of incumbency,  id.  I, 24-
33,  pp.  15-21,  App.  II-16-23,  have  increased  to  match  the  as-
tonishing  pace  set  by  the  frank,  to  the  point  where
Americans  for  Democratic  Action  now  estimates  that  an
incumbent  member  of the  House  seeking  re-election  has  a
$976,000  advantage  per  term  over  a  challenger  solely  be-
cause  of  these  perquisites.  Washington  Post,  Aug.  26,
1975,  at  A3,  col.  1. Since  tie  start  of 1974  alone,  the  value
of these  advantages  has  increased  by 30 percent.  /d.

The  development  of  new  and  costly  telecommunica-
tions  technology  increases  the amounts  spent  on  campaigns
but  also  dramatically  increases  the  numbers  of  voters
reached  and  the  amount  of political  information  they  re-
ceive.  Heard,  supra,  at 403-0S.

So too  does  an  increase  in the number  of primary  elec-
tions  and  local  caucus  meetings  increase  the cost  of politics.
Between  1968  and  1972  the  number  of primaries  doubled,
and  comparable  changes  in rules  governing  the selection  of
delegates  in  non-primary  states  also  occurred.  Findings  I,
91,  p.  55;  App.  II-S3.  We  cannot  have  increased  political
activity  and  more  widespread  participation  in  the  demo-
cratic  process  without  increased  costs.  Alexander,  “The
Impact  of  Election  Reform  Legislation  on  the  Political
Party  System,”  27  (delivered  at  the  1975  meeting  of the
American  Political  Science  Association,  Sept.  2-5,  1975).

Similarly,  the  increased  incidence  of ticket  splitting,
id.  1, 38-40,  p.  22,  App.  II-24,  forces  individual  candidates  to
rely  less  on  party  tickets  and  fading  party  loyalties  and  to
run  and  spend  more  on  individual  campaigns.

The  allegation  of  “skyrocketing”  campaign  costs  is
based  entirely  on  the  fact  that  campaign  costs  rise  more
quickly  than  the  Consumer  Price  Index.  However,  the
parties  have  agreed  and  the  District  Court  found  that
‘‘  ..  campaign  items  tend  to  be  those,  historically,  whose
prices  increase  faster  than  the  inflation  rate,  in particular,

60

61

items  such  as  wages  and  other  payments  for  personal
services,  and  advertising  costs.”’  Findings  II,  B 47,  p.  202;
App.  II-S81.  Not  only  does  this  destroy  the  “skyrocketing”’
costs  argument;  it  also  means  that  limitations  on  expendi-
tures  linked  only  to  the  Consumer  Price  Index  will  in  real
terms  become  more  restrictive  as  time  goes  by.

Finally,  the  Court  of Appeals  was  concerned  about  the
time  and  energy  presidential  candidates  spend  on  fund
raising  under  the system  of unlimited  private  financing.  For
support  it  relied  solely  upon  two  statements  made  in
congressional  hearings  (op.  at  1513),  each  of which  seems
exaggerated  and  self-serving  and  is nowhere  supported  in
the  literature  describing  political  campaigns.  Moreover,  the
effect  of  the  legislation  on  contribution  size  makes  fund
raising  far  more  onerous  and  time-consuming  than  before.
By  the  time  the  court  below  decided  this  case,  it  was
abundantly  apparent  that  the  effect  of  the  challenged
legislation  has  forced  members  of  Congress  with  presi-
dential  ambitions  to  curtail  their  legislative  responsibilities
in order  to  engage  in full-time  fund  raising  well  over  a year
and  a half before  the  presidential  election.  Alexander,  ‘“The
Impact  of  Election  Reform  Legislation  on  the  Political
Party  System,”’  supra,  at  27;  Washington  Post,  Sept.  15,
1975,  at  Al,  Col  1;  Los  Angeles  Times,  Aug.  15,  1975,  at
126, col.  1; New  York  Times,  July 21,  1975,  at  10, col  3.?

It  may  well  be  that  too  little  rather  than  too  much  is

?  In  addition,  the  legislation  demands  not  inconsiderable  expendi-

with  voter-contributors.

““...We  pay  only  a  small  sum  for  competitive
campaigns  that  pose  choices  of officials,  clarify
the  programs  of  candidates,  and  expose  the

Similarly,  Herbert  Alexander  has observed  that

“(political  costs  need  to  be  considered  in  per-
spective.  Considered  in  the  aggregate,  politics  is
not  overpriced.
It  is  underfinanced....The
important  consideration  is not  the  costs  as  such,
but  whether  they  are  essential  for  a  competitive
and  responsive  political  system.’’  Hearings  on  S.
372, supra,  at 219.

(2) Inequality  of Political  Voice

As  for  the  second  rationale—that  private  campaign
financing  fosters  inequality  of political  expression  as  be-
tween  the  rich  and  the  poor—the  court  below  simply
assumed  that  such  inequality  necessarily  means  that  the
views  and  interests  peculiar  to  the  wealthy  will  receive  dis-
porportionate  favor  (op. at  1513).

This  assumption  is neither  intuitively  nor  empirically
correct.  Political  newcomers  who  lack  an  established  politi-
cal  base  and  high  name  recognition  need  large  contribu-
tions  to  make a start,  a  point  demonstrated  in more  detail
infra,  pp.  124-38.  Large  contributions  may  also  offset  other
inequalities  such  as  access  to  the  media,  the  perquisites  of
incumbent  officeholders,  and  the  unfamiliarity  of  one’s
cause.  See  infra,  pp.  128-31.  Recipients  of large  contribu-
tions  may  be,  and  often  have  been,  among  society’s  least
powerful.  See  infra,  pp.  121-24.  Merely  as  one  example,

62

63

Julian  Bond’s  campaign  manager,  in  ending  Bond's
presidential  candidacy,  blamed  the  challenged  legislation
for  his  failure  to  raise  adequate  ‘“‘seed  money”’  to mount  an
effective  campaign.  See infra,  p.  132.  As one  scholar  put it,
‘...  the traditional  fat cats  are  not  all of one  species,  allied
against  common  adversaries.  Big  givers  show  up  im-
portantly  in  both  parties  and  on  behalf  of many  opposing
candidates,’’  Heard,  supra,  at 6 (emphasis  in original).

Indeed,  campaign  contributions  tend  to  be  less  con-
centrated  in  a  small  percentage  of  the  population  than
other  forms  of  political  activity.  More  people  contribute
than  work  actively  for  a  candidate  or  participate  in  demon-
strations.  Findings  I,  107,  pp.  97-8;  App.  II-147.  The  court
below  relied  upon  the  fact  that  1 percent  of the  people  ac-
count  for  90  percent  of political  contributions  and  that  2
to  3 percent  of the  people  are  responsible  for  95 percent  of
financing  for  congressional  elections  (op.  at  1513).  In  fact
these  figures  demonstrate  that  contributing  to campaigns  is
one  of the  most  widespread  form  of political  participation
and  expression  today,  for  3  percent  of  the  population
amounts  to  well  over  seven  million  people.  If seven  million
people  participated  in  ownership  and  control  of  the  in-
stitutional  press,  the  allegations  of  media  concentration
found  inadequate  in Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  vy.  Tor-
nillo,  supra,  to  justify  inhibitory  legislation  would  never
even  have  been  made.

The  court  below  suggests  that  increased  expenditures
lead  to  increased  reliance  on  a  small  number  of large  con-
tributors  (op. at  1513).  There  is no  support  in the record  for
this  assertion.  The  popular  base  of campaign  contributions
has  been  broadening  in  recent  years,  as  demonstrated  by
the  increasing  proportion  of income  to  national-level  com-
mittees  derived  from  individual  contributions  in  sums  of
less  than  $500  in the presidential  years  1948-1968,  Findings
I, 45,  pp.  23-24,  App.  II-25,  and  the  experience  of the  1968
McCarthy  campaign  and  the  1972  McGovern  effort.  Both  of
these  campaigns,  and  many  others  as  well,  began  with

“‘seed’”’  money  from  a small  number  of large contributions,
and  used  that  money  to  amass  a large  campaign  fund  from
large and  small  contributors  alike,  see  infra,  pp.  135-36.

(3)  The  ‘‘Corruption"’  Rationale

This  legislation  was  as  much  a  response  to  the  sus-
picion  of corruption  as  to  the  fact  itself.  John  Gardner,
speaking  on  behalf of Common  Cause,  testified  that  ‘‘[o}ne
can  rarely  nail  down  the  causal  relationship  between  cam-
paign  gifts  and  later  political  acts,  but  the  pattern  of
political  giving  creates  a  cloud  of suspicion  that  can  only
deepen  the cynicism  of the average  citizen.’’  Hearings  on  S.
372, supra,  at 92. This  theme  was  repeated  again  and  again
by supporters  of the FECA  Amendments.’

>  Senator  Scott,  a co-sponsor,  said,

“Regardless  of the  motives  of the  donor,  or  the intentions  of the
recipient  candidate,  there  appears  to be an  appearance  of impropriety.
I regret  that  this  is so,  because  such  charges  are  usually  unfounded,  but
like  the proverbial  wife  of Julius  Caesar,  we  must  be  above  and  beyond
reproach.”  Hearings  before  the  Subcommittee  on  Privileges  and  Elec-
tions  of the  Committee  on  Rules  and  Administration,  on  S.  1103,  S.
1954,  S. 2417,  S. 3, Yd Cong.,  Ist Sess.  33 (1973).

Senator  Abourezk:

“It  is  really  the  appearance  of corruption  that  harms  us,  | think,
more  than  any  corruption  that  does  go  on,  because  there  are  certain
controversial  amendments  up  today  that  I would  like  to  vote  for,  but
because  I received  contributions  from  daily  farmers  around  the  coun-
try,  | am  almost  afraid  to vote  for them  because  it is set  up  now  in the
press  that  anybody  who  does  vote  for them  who  has  taken  money  from
the dairy farmers  has been  bought  off.” Hearings  on  S. 23, S. 372 et  al.,
before  the  Subcommittee  on  Privileges  and  Elections  of  the  Senate
Committee  on  Rules  and  Administration,  93d  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  207
(1973).

Senator  McGovern:

‘“{TJhose  of us  who  run  for  office  can  profess  that  the  campaign
contributions  we  receive  do not  in any  way  control  our  votes,  but  I ven-
ture  to say that  not  many  believe  it."’  120 Cong.  Rec.  S. 4553  (daily  ed.
March  27, 1974).

64

The  record  demonstrates  a  limited,  though  plainly
serious,  amount  of corruption  in the  sense  of proven  or  sus-
pected  quid pro  quo  arrangements  involving  campaign  con-
tributions  and  government  action.  At  most,  the  record
shows:

(1)  A number  of illegal  corporate  contributions  which
may  or  may  not  have  involved  such  quid pro  quos,  Findings
I, 109-17,  pp.  98-106,  App.  II-148-53,  but  which  are  untouched
by the  FECA  Amendments  since  they were  illegal  under  prior
law.  These  provide  support  for  the
FECA  Amendments
only  in  that  effective,  but  narrowly  drawn,  disclosure
provisions  (which  appellants  support)  will  make  such
crimes  more  easily detectable  in the future.

(2)  A  few  apparently  unsuccessful  attempts  to  influ-
ence  governmental  action,  id.  1, 118,  160,  164,  pp.  106,  171-
72;  App.  11-153,  254-55.

(3)  Attempts  by the  milk  producers  to  influence  gov-
ernmental  action,  id.  I, 158,  165,  pp.  171-73;  App.  II-254-55,

(4)  The  conferring  of several  ambassadorships  in  ar-
guable  or  apparent  exchange  for  large  contributions,  id.  I,
121-23,  pp.  107-08;  App.  II-154-55.

The  record  also  demonstrates  two  categories  of con-
tributions  with  a  high  potential  for  improper  motive:  first,
contributions  made  to  opposing  condidates;  second,  con-
tributions  before  April  7, 1972  (the  effective  date  of the dis-
closure  provisions)  or  so  late  in  the  campaign  as  not  to  be
subject  to effective  disclosure.

These  cases  represent  only a fraction  of total  campaign
contributions.  That  the  evidence  should  be  so  meager  is
highly  significant  in light  of the  fact  that  it was  gathered  in
the  midst  of a  major  political  scandal,  at  a time  of unusual
public  receptivity  and  interest,  when  evidence  of  official
corruption  was  uniquely  and  unprecedentedly  available,

65

and  in  the  course  of a  vigorous  investigation  by an  inde-
pendent  special  prosecutor.  While  any  substantial  level  of
corruption  must  be  dealt  with,  the  particularized  abuses
demonstrated  are  subject  to particularized  remedies  and  do
not  call  for  wholesale  and  unnecessary  limitations  on
political  communication.  Indeed,  bans  on  labor-union  and
corporate  contributions,  18 U.S.C.  §§ 610,  611,  the  source
of much  that  was  illegal  in the  1972  elections,  and  attempts
at  bribery  and  giving  rewards  for  government  action  were
illegal, id.,  §§ 201,  597, long before  Watergate  or the  FECA
Amendments.  The  other  abuses  identified  are  untouched
by this legislation.

There  is a serious  lack  of precision  in the charges  made
about  the “corrosive  influence”  of private campaign  financing.
Such  rhetoric  fails  to  distinguish  between  quid  pro
quo  arrangements  and  giving to candidates  who  are  already
predisposed  to the  donor’s  positions  on  public  issues.  Thus
Sen.  Kennedy  argued  on  behalf of the legislation,  “‘is there
any  doubt  that  the  AMA  money  will be used  in the fall elec-
tions  to  subvert  national  health  insurance  and  to  support
candidates  who  oppose  health  reform?”  120  Cong.  Rec.  S
5275  (daily ed.  April  4, 1975).  And  the record  in this case  is
thick  with  detailed  lists  of  campaign  contributions  by
“special  interests,”  Findings  I, 101-03,  pp.  57-96;  App.  II-54-
=  The  evidence  indicates,  however,  that  contributions
generally  go to  candidates  already  disposed  to  the  donor’s
point  of view,  and  that  ideological  lines  are  usually  clearly
drawn  well  in advance  of the  campaign.  Comparing  the  25
most  expensive  House  races  in  1972  and  1974,  Findings  II,
A 8, and  10, pp.  76-81,  84-87,  App.  II-422-33,  435-40,—races
in which  large contributions  were  of critical  importance  to the
candidates—with  the endorsements  the candidates  received
from  various  political  groups  with  well-defined  policy  stan-
ces,  30 Cong.  Q. Wkly.  Rep.  2967  (Nov.  11,  1972);  32 Cong.
Q.  Wkly.  Rep.  3077  (Nov.  9, 1974),  confirms  the  common-

66

67

sense  proposition  that  campaign  contributions  generally
flow  to  those  candidates  who  have  clearly  defined  positions
and  with  whom  donors  already  agree.

Once  “‘corruption”  or  ‘‘corrosive  influence”  is defined
mechanically  to  embrace  all  contributions  intended  to  help
political  allies  communicate  with  the  voters,  the  case  against
private  campaign  financing  is  not  hard  to  make.  Coming
from  a  different  Senator  in  1968,  the  statement  quoted
supra,  p. 65,  might  have  been,  “‘is  there  any  doubt  that  anti-
war  money  will  be  used  in  the  fall  elections  to  subvert  the
war  in  Southeast  Asia  and  to  support  candidates  who  op-
pose  it?”

The  court  below  relied  heavily  upon  the  existence  of
contributions  by what  it and  the  appellees  label  as  “‘special
interests’’  (nowhere  defined)  to  justify  the  proscriptions  of
the  FECA  (op.  at  1513-16).  However,  the  vast  majority  of
such  “special  interest”  contributions  are  small,  and  in  the
aggregate  constituted  no  more  than  15.7  percent  of total
contributions  to  congressional  candidates  in  1972  and  11.6
percent  in  1974.  Findings  I, 101-03,  pp.  57-96,  App.  II-54-146,
and  II,  A  85-96,  pp.  114-120;  App.  I1-460-64.  To  single  out
these  contributions  as  unjustifiable  because  of the  views  they
represent,  moreover,  comes  perilously  close  to  regulating
the  content  of political  speech.  In  any  event,  the  actual  im-
pact  of  the  FECA  is  to  strengthen  the  relative  power  of
these  groups  to support  candidates  they favor,  see  infra,  pp.
68-72.

Although  the  sporsors  of  the  FECA  Amendments
made  much  of ‘‘Watergate”’  as  a demonstration  of the  evils
of campaign  financing,  the  reforms  which  in  fact  speak  to
the  break-in  and  subsequent  cover-up  (e.g..  prohibiting  cash
contributions  in excess  of $100,  18 U.S.C.  § 615)  constitute
only  a  fraction  of the  legislation  and  are  not  challenged  by
appellants.  To  the  extent  that  private  campaign  money
financed  the  affair—in  fact,  public  money  financed  much

of the  cover-up—it  was  through  contributions  made  prior
to effective  disclosure  requirements.  The  unraveling  was  in
fact  accomplished  in  large  part  by tracing  these  contribu-
tions,  Findings  I, 128,  p.  110;  App.  II-156.  Private  contribu-
tions  or  campaign  expenditures,  if  subject  to  properly
drawn  disclosure  requirements,  are  thus  hardly  well  suited
to  illegal  or  covert  purposes.  If ‘“Watergate’’  were  truly the
target  of the  FECA  Amendments,  they would  have  stopped
with  disclosure  and  the  severe  limitation  of  cash  con-
tributions.

B.  The  Impact  of the  Challenged Legislation  Will Be  To

Aggravate  Rather  Than  To Cure  the Specified Abuses.

This  legislation  cannot  be  upheld  merely  upon  the
repeated  intonation  of  “corruption”,  ‘special  interests”,
“inequality”,  and  ‘‘Watergate.”  Scrutiny  rather  then
rhetoric  is called  for  when  legislation  trenches  upon  First
Amendment  rights,  and  here  that  scrutiny  must  be directed
to  the  impact  of the  FECA  on  this  nation’s  political  life.
That  impact,  aside  from  the  basic  impact  of  severely
inhibiting  free  expression,  most  assuredly  will  be  to  in-
crease  the  power  of  “special  interests,’  to  increase  the
power  of those  who  are  ‘‘in”  while  decreasing  that  of those
who  are  “out,”  and  to  leave  untouched  the  chances  of a
recurrence  of some of the kinds  of incidents  which  fit under
the  penumbra  of the  term  ‘‘Watergate.”  The  great  tragedy
of the  challenged  statutes  is that  they do  not  reform  those
problems  which  legitimately  concerned  the court  below,  but
rather  aggravate  them,  and  do  so  by  trenching  on  First
Amendment  rights.

One  effect  of the  statute  will  be to reduce  competition
between  candidates,  by making  it more  difficult  for  chal-
lengers  to  unseat  incumbents.  As  demonstrated  more  fully
infra,  pp.  87-104,  challengers  need  to  spend  at  relatively
high  levels  to offset  the  high  name  recognition  of incumbents
and  their  extensive  use  of  their  perquisites  of  office,

68

69

e.g.,  the  frank.  To  become  viable,  moreover,  challengers
need  ‘‘seed  money,”’  see  infra,  pp.  130-38,  which  is made
virtually  impossible  to raise  by the  limits  on  contributions.

The  FECA  simply  does  not  limit  political  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  equally.  It limits  a// expenditures  by
challengers  but  only  some  expenditures  by  incumbents.
And  it limits  al/  contributions  to  challengers  but  only some
contributions  to  incumbents.  Incumbent  officeholders  can
by their  own  vote  increase  the  resources  at  their  disposal  in
ways  which  are  the  equivalent  of very  large  contributions.
See  infra,  pp.  130-38.  The  actual  impact  of the  legislation,
therefore,  will  necessarily  make  campaigns  by challengers
less  likely to succeed.

Another  effect  will  be to  increase  the  relative  power  of
organized  economic  interest  groups,  which  the  court  below
believes  are  already  too  powerful.  Under  18 U.S.C.  § 608 (b)
(2),  a political  committee  may  give up to  $5,000  to each  can-
didate.  There  is  no  restriction  upon  the  number  of  such
committees  which  may  represent  a  particular  ‘‘interest.”’
Any individual  may  give $25,000  to a single  such  committee
so  long  as  none  of  it  is  earmarked  for  a  particular  can-
didate.

Under  18  U.S.C.  §§ 610  and  611,  moreover,  corpora-
tions  and  unions  may  solicit  contributions  to  “segregated
funds”  which  make  contributions  to  candidates  and  politi-
cal  committees  and  may  pay  for  the  maintenance  and  ad-
ministration  of such  funds.  Unions,  business  interests,  and
professional  groups  need  only  create  several  political  com-
mittees  rather  than  one  to  continue  their  past  practices,
subject  only  to  the  “‘earmarking’’  limitation  and  the
$25,000  limit  on  individual  contributions.

Thus,  while  the  statute  severely  limits  contributions  by
ideologically  motivated  individuals  and  spontaneously  or-
ganized  committees,  it allows  the  practices  of organized  in-
terest  groups  to continue  unfettered.

The  legislation  leaves  untouched,  for  example,  the
contributions  of the dairy industry,  which  figured  largely  in
the  argument  of  appellees  and  the  opinion  of  the  court
below.  Every  county  organization  of  the  dairy  producers
can  have  a political  committee  entitled  to give up to  $5,000
apiece  to  an  unlimited  number  of candidates.  In  effect,  dairy
interests  can  give  unlimited  amounts  to  a  candidate,  can  con-
tribute  to  opposing  candidates  and  can  time  these  con-
tributions  to  avoid  effective  disclosure.  And  what  is true  of
dairy interests  is true  of all organized  economic  interest  groups.

The  lack  of even  a whisper  of protest  from  such  groups

is surely significant.  As one  commentator  has stated:

‘Special  interest  groups  such  as  labor  union,
business,  education,  health  and  dairy  political-
action  committees  are  relatively  unaffected  by the
new  contribution  limits.  The  reason  is that  they
have  strong  local  bases  and  can  funnel  their  con-
tributions  through  state  and  local  affiliates,  thus
making  smaller  multiple  contributions  instead  of
one  large  contribution  to  their  favorite  can-
didates.

“It  is ironic  and  instructive  that  labor,  one  of
the  groups  the  law  would  be  expected  to  check,
had  no  complaints  with  the  $5,000  contribution
limit  on  political  committees.  Neal  Gregory,  for-
mer  co-director  of the  Center  for  Public  Financing
that  lobbied  for  the  law’s  passage,  has  repor-
ted  that  the  $5,000  limit  was  checked  with  the
AFL-CIO  before  it was  put  in the  bill.  “There  was
no  opposition  to  it,’  he  said.”  Freed,  ‘‘How  to
Sneak
Campaign-Reform

It has  already  been  reported  that  “(t]he  predicted  up-
surge  in corporate  political  activity  has  begun  .
.  Twenty
apparently  new  political  committees  have  been  formed  by
companies  seeking  to take  advantage  of the  clarification  of
section  610,  prohibiting  corporate  campaign  gifts  except
through  a  separate,  segregated  fund.”  2 Campaign  Prac-
tices  Rep.  No.  3 (Aug.  25,  1975),  at  3.  See  also  Walters,
‘New  Wrinkles  in  Corporate  Fund  Raising,”  National
Journal  Reports  (Sept. 6,  1975),  1283.

.

.

The  20-state  matching  grant  requirement  for  federal
subsidization  of primary  expenses  under  Subtitle  H, see  in-
fra,  pp.  165-68,  also  increases  the  leverage  of organized  in-
terests  with  state  or  local  affilitates.  Not  only can  they make
sizeable  contributions  but  they  can  be  of enormous  aid  in
helping  a candidate  become  eligible  for matching  grants.

Notwithstanding  all  the  drumbeating  about  eliminat-
ing  the  “lavish  contributions’  by  “special  interests,”  the
legislation  in fact  increases  their  relative  power.  It does  not
in  any  sense  respond  to  the  specified  abuses.  It  does
nothing  to limit  the  size  of ‘‘special  interest’’  contributions,
nothing  to  prevent  contributions  by  such  interests  to op-

72

73

posing  candidates,  nothing  to  control  contributions  made
too  late  to  be subject  to effective  disclosure,  and  nothing  to
curb  the  ability  of winners,  even  after  victory  at the  polls, to
seek  contributions  for  purposes  other  than  campaigni
See infra,  p. 77.

iesiitee

The  FECA  thus  avoids  and  indeed  frustrates  remedies
for  the  very  problems  which  the  court  below  believed  to  be
the  worst  abuses  of private  financing.  While  the  McCarthy
campaign  of 1968  would  have  died  before  the  New  Hamp-
shire  primary  had  the  FECA  been  in  effect,  see  infra,  pp.
97,  135,  the  contributions  of  the  dairy  industry  in  1972
would  have  been  unaffected.

Beyond  that,  the  FECA  subjects  the  political  com-
munication  of candidates  and  their  supporters  to  the  dis-
cretionary  judgment  of a  regulatory  agency  staffed  by ap-
pointees  of  the  President  and  the  congressional  party
leadership.  Question  after  question  will  come  before  the
FEC calling upon  it to  decide  how  much  speech  particular
candidates  or  groups  may  engage  in.  Myriad  decisions  by
the  FEC,  down  to  the  choice  of which  campaigns  to  audit,‘

To  the  extent,  therefore,  that  Watergate  involved
abuses  of  presidential  power,  the  FECA  is  counterpro-
ductive.  It increases  the  ability  of incumbent  Presidents  to
win  re-election,  gives  them  some  control  over  the  distribu-
tion  of  matching  grant  primary  money  to  potential  op-
ponents,  and,  by  centralizing  control  over  political  com-

The  tide  of informed  commentary  has  turned  against
this  legislation  as  time  has  permitted  careful  scrutiny  of its
actual  impact  on  American  politics.  Commentator  after
commentator,°  as  well  as  the  American  Bar  Association’
