C.  Less  Intrusive  and  More  Effective  Means  Were  Avail-
able  to Congress.

The  “‘less  intrusive  means’’  doctrine  is usually  invoked
in circumstances  in which  this  Court  must  balance  losses  in
the  efficiency  of regulation  of unprotected  conduct  against
attendant  gains  in the  protection  of speech.  In the  present
case,  no  such  balancing  is necessary,  for the  least  intrusive
means  are  actually  far  more  efficient  regulations  of  the

col.  1;  Kilpatrick,  ‘““Campaign-Law  Challenge,’  Washington  Star-
News,  Dec.  27,  1974,  at  A 6, col.  1; Alsop,  ‘“The  Effects  of Campaign
Finance  Reform,”  Washington  Post,  Nov.  25, 1974,  at A 21, col.  1.

Space  forbids  quotation  of  but  a  few  representative  comments.
David  Broder  wrote  that  “‘[ijn its haste  to pass some  kind  of reform  bill,
Congress  has  unleashed  an  unguided  missile  against  the  American
.  this  law  we  have  on  the  books  begins  to  look  like  a
political  system.  .
menace.”  Broder,  supra,  April  30,  1975,  at  A  17,  col.  6.  One  com-
mentator  has  noted  that  “*. .
.  bribes  will  continue  to pass  and  influence
will  still be peddled,  despite the naive  expectation  that  the bad  guys will
dutifully  line  up  and  file their  reports  along  with  the  rest  of us.  At the
same  time,  however,  in  the  dubious  name  of  ‘election  campaign
reform,’  we  have managed  to make  a monkey  out  of the idea of free  and
democratic  elections,’’  Freedman,  supra.  Another  said,“*.  .
.  what  oc-
curred  was  that  Congress,  in  its  laudable  rush  to  purify  politics,  may
have  anesthetized  it,”” Roche,  supra.

’  The  American  Bar  Association  Special  Committee  on  Election
Reform  recently  issued  its Report  (hereafter  cited  as  “‘ABA  Report’’),  in
substantial  measure  adopting  appellants’  views.

*  The  American  Civil  Liberties  Union’s  recently  issued  “Questions
and  Answers  About  Campaign  Finances”  (hereafter  cited  as  ‘ACLU
Report”)  argues  on  constitutional  grounds  against  every  challenged
provision  of the statutes.

76

77

abuses  relating  to  the  potential  for corruption  identified  by
appellees  and  the court  below.’

There  is  a  class  of cases  in  which  the  involvement  of
non-speech  elements  seems  particularly  high  and  which  are
not  adequately  covered  by bribery  and  similar  statutes.

For  example,  prior  to  the  passage  of this  so-called  ‘‘re-
form”  legislation,  government  contractors  were  barred
from  making  any  direct  or  indirect  contribution  to  candi-
dates  for  federal  office.  Now,  18  U.S.C.  § 611  has  been
clarified  so  that  such  contractors  may  facilitate  and  support
contributions  from  their  employees  so  long as  they establish
‘separate,  segregated  funds.”  Jd.  Before  its  “reform,”  this
section  was  carefully  directed  at  situations  rife  with  con-
flicts  of interest  and  the  danger  of corruption,  cf. Civil Ser-
vice  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548  (1973).
Thus  the  challenged  legislation  eliminates  a precise  remedy
that  already  existed  for a precise  problem.

The  appointment  of large  contributors  to  high  office
involves  the  danger  of quid  pro  quo  arrangements  which
can  be avoided  by precise  measures.  Many such  officers,  in-
cluding  ambassadors,  are  presently  subject  to  Senate
confirmation;  carefully  drawn  disclosure  provisions  would
provide  the  Senate  with  the  facts  it  needs  to  determine
whether  appointments  have  been  improperly  influenced.  As
to  offices  not  presently  subject  to  confirmation,  Congress
could  require  that  any  potential  appointee  who  contributed
in  excess  of a  certain  amount  to  the  President’s  campaign,
or  the  campaigns  of  his  party’s  candidates,  must  be
confirmed  by the  Senate.  This  would  enable  the  Senate  to
scrutinize  on  a  case-by-case  basis  whether  or  not  the  ap-
pointment  was  based  on  merit  or  on  campaign  contribu-
tions.  Congress  could  go  further  if it wished,  and  prohibit
any  appointment  of contributors  of more  than  a  specified
amount.

*  For  additional  discussions  of the  application  of the  “‘less  intrusive

means”  test,  see  infra,  pp.  151-52,  163,  184,  188-89.

Another  category  may  be the  contributing  of funds  to
opposing  candidates.  Absent  special  circumstances,  the
protected  speech  elements  in helping  to  persuade  voters  to
choose all of several  alternatives  seem  low.  A law directed  at
such  practices,  if carefully  drafted,  would  be  less  intrusive
than  the  present  law,  which  encourages  such  multiple
giving.  33  Cong.  Q. Wkly Rep.  1961,  1962 (Sept.  13,  1975).

Late  contributions,  above  a certain  size,  may  be timed
to  avoid  effective  disclosure  prior  to  the  election.  A  law
which  either  limited  such  late  contributions  or  required
instantaneous  disclosure  to  permit  the  timely  informing  of
the voters  would  be directed  at a particular  evil.

Congress  clearly  would  have  the  power  to  require  that
contributions  be actually  spent  on  campaigns  and  thus  not
be of value  for purposes  other  than  persuading  voters,  i.e.,
for  non-speech  purposes.  For  when  contributions  can  be
put  to  virtually  unlimited  uses,  the  danger  of  improper
conduct  is clearly  high,  as  is the temptation  to  raise  money
not  needed  for  political  communication.  The  FECA,  how-
ever,  not  only  fails  to  deter  this  abuse  but  actively  en-
courages  it. See 2 U.S.C.  § 439a.

Finally,  disclosure  of contributions  large enough  to at-
tract  a candidate’s  attention  is the least  intrusive  regulation
and,  even  more  importantly,  the  most  democratic.  Whether
a contribution  is too  large or  in furtherance  of a bad  special
interest  rather  than  a good common  cause  is better  decided
in  every  case  by  the  voters  than  by  wholesale  legislative
fiat.  Similarly,  if  a candidate  is failing  to  address  substan-
.  ex-
tial  issues  but  merely  putting  on  “Romanesque  .
travagances”’  (op.  at  1581),  the  electoral  process  provides  a
direct,  effective,  democratic  remedy.

.

The  Court  of Appeals  asserts  that  the  events  of 1972,
id.,  and  the failure  of earlier  disclosure  statutes,  id.  at  1592-
93, demonstrate  that  disclosure  is inadequate.  With  regard
to  the  failures  of disclosure  statutes  prior  to  the  1971  Act,
appellants  in no  way  contest  the  conclusion  that  they  were

78

both  ineffective  and  inadequately  enforced.  Indeed,  they
believe  that,  but  for  the  problems  discussed  infra,  pp.  170-
92,  the  1971  disclosure  requirements  of  the  FEC  are  a
much-needed  reform  in this  area.  But  the  Court  of Appeals
ignores  the  fact  that  the  1971  FECA,  which  went  into  effect
in  the  middle  of the  1972  campaign  and  was  amended  by
the  FECA  Amendments  prior to  the  1974  election,  was  not
even  tested  for  one  full  campaign  period.  The  Court  of Ap-
peals’  reference  to  ‘‘the  failure  of piecemeal  regulation  to
preserve  the  integrity  of federal  elections’  (op.  at  1593)  is
unpersuasive  in  view  of Congress’  failure  to  give  the  1971
disclosure  provisions  a  reasonable  chance  and  to  assess  the
need  for  still  further  legislation  in the  light  of the  effects  of
those  provisions.

The  record  as  to  1972,  indeed,  strongly  indicates  that
disclosure  is an  effective  remedy.  The  rush  to beat  the  April
7,  1972  effective  date  of  the  disclosure  provisions,  and  the
prosecutions  and  exposures  effected  in the  aftermath  of the
1972  elections,  Findings  I, 126-27,  p. 110,  App.  II-156,  demon-
Strate  the  salutary  impact  disclosure  must  have.  These
events  demonstrate  not  that  disclosure  is  ineffective,  but
that  the  political  liability  it can  impose  is swift  and  com-
plete,  as  is the  concern  rational  politicians  must  give  to the
sources  of their  campaign  money  in the future.

Justified  by rhetoric  invoking  the  spectre  of  “special
interests,”  ‘‘corruption,”  “inequality,”  and  ‘“Watergate,”’
this  legislation  itself  increases  the  very  dangers  those  words
symbolize.  Attention  to  what  the  challenged  provisions  ac-
tually  say  and  what  they  actually  do,  as  well  as  to  the  far
more  effective  and  far  more  democratic  remedies  which
were  and  are  available,  demonstrates  that  beyond  question.
If  society  is  presently  afflicted  with  alienation  and  mis-
trust,  this  legislation  is  a  nostrum  which,  even  if it briefly
restored  confidence  at  the  price  of freedom,  would  result  in
quick  measure  in  the  revival  of  an  overwhelming — and
quite justified  —  cynicism.

79

The  FECA  Limitations  on  Campaign  Expenditures  by
Political  Candidates,  Parties,  and  Committees  Violate  the
Constitution.

A.  Limitations  on  Campaign  Expenditures  by Political
Candidates,  Parties  or  Committees  Are  Unconstitutional
Per Se.

The  FECA’s  expenditure  limits,  18 U.S.C.  § 60&(c),  are
a  direct  abridgement  of the  rights  of speech  and  associa-
tion.  The  speech  which  they abridge  is that  most  central  to
the  objectives  and  essence  of the  First  Amendment  and  of
any  democratic  society—political  discussion  by candidates.
Speech  in the  political  arena  may  be unheard  unless  widely
and  effectively  distributed.  To  limit  the  expenditure  of
money,  therefore,  is to limit  the  speech  itself.  The  abridge-
ment  challenged  here  is  so  fundamental  that  how  the
Framers  would  have  regarded  it is beyond  question.  It  is
palpably  unconstitutional.

A single  example  will  illustrate  the  point.  Consider  the
dilemma  of the  candidate  for  federal  office  running  in  a
district  in which  the  only newspaper  is implacably  opposed
to  his  candidacy  and  at  every  opportunity  praises  his  op-
ponent.  What  coverage  the  candidate  gets  is  sparse  and
negative.  Other  media  are  for  the  most  part  either  located
in  other  districts  or  find  a  substantial  audience  in  them.
They  give  some  coverage  to  his  campaign  and  that  of his
opponent,  but  cannot  afford  too  much  in view  of the  inter-
ests  of their  audience  as  a  whole.  The  candidate  wants  to
purchase  advertising,  but  the  newspaper  declines  to  accept  it
or  places  it  on  undesirable  pages.  Media  time  is  very
expensive  relative  to  the  number  of local  voters  reached,
since  the candidate  must  in effect  pay to reach  a large audi-
ence  outside  the  district.  If he  purchases  any  substantial
amount  of media  time,  in  fact,  the  expenditure  limits  will
force  him  to  lay off his  staff.  He  will  no  longer  be  able  to

we oe
Ane
a ee ane TE TAO
Neen

80

81

hold  events  to  recruit  volunteers  and  will  otherwise  have  to
restrict  campaign  activities,  such  as  mailings,  etc.

This  is not  an  unrealistic  hypothetical.  Indeed,  the  na-
tion’s  first  presidential  primary  is held  in  a  similar  place.
The  hypothetical  does  not  include  such  other  likely  facts  as
the  opponent’s  being  an  incumbent  with  high  name  recog-
nition  who  has  made  extravagant  use  of the  frank,  and  who
takes  full  advantage  of his  ability  to  generate  media  cover-
age.  What  it does  make  clear,  however,  is that  candidate
expenditure  limitations  cannot  help  but  have  a  baleful
influence  on  that  speech  which  is at  the  core  of freedom  of
expression.

Political  campaigns  provide  competition  central  to the
democratic  process,  Findings  I,  6-7,  p.  5;  App.  II-8.  If
political  candidates  are  not  to  be  protected  in their  run  for
office,  protecting  the  angry  acts  of  individuals,  Street  v.
New  York,  394  U.S.  576  (1969),  cannot  save  freedom  of ex-
pression.

The  Report  on  the  House  predecessor  of  the  FECA
Amendments  defended  limits  on  candidate  expenditures  so
the  public  would  not  be deceived  by a  “‘sophisticated  adver-
tising  campaign.”  See  pp.  55-56,  supra.  As  the  floor
manager  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  Sen.  Cannon,
declared,  limiting  the  amount  a  candidate  can  spend  “‘cer-
tainly  is  going  to  limit  the  amount  of  persuasiveness  one
can  put  forward  to  the  public.”  120  Cong.  Rec.  S  18535
(daily  ed.  October  8,  1974).  The  issue  of free  discussion  of
governmental  affairs  is thus  squarely  posed.  Congress  lacks
the  power  to say when  a candidate  for federal  office  has  had
“too  much”’  communication  with  the  voters  or  to dictate  to
such  candidates  the  strategic  timing  or  the  means  by which
that  communication  is to take  place.

Political  candidacies  rely  on  the  consumption  of  re-
sources  and  the  expenditure  of money.  Even  an  activity  like
a  candidate  ‘‘walk’’  requires  advance  men  to  invite  news
personnel  and  general  citizens  to  meet  the  candidate  and

the  ability  to  provide  news  outlets  with  tape,  film,  pictures
and  press  releases  along the way.  Cole,  Campaign  Spending
in Senate  Elections  159 (1975).

The  Court  of Appeals  suggested  that the limitations  on
contributions  and  expenditures  are  harmless  because  they
are  evenhanded:  the  same  limits  apply  to  everyone  (op.  at
1518).  This  is surely  a novel  view  of freedom.  Until  now,  no
one  had  thought  that  the  right  to  speak  could  be erased  so
long as  everyone  else’s  rights were  extinguished  at the  same
time.  Indeed,  the suggestion  is that  the  First  Amendment  is
satisfied  only if the deprivation  is massive  enough.  In a free
society  individual  rights  are  protected  so  the  public  will  be
exposed  to  a robust  and  uninhibited  debate  about  political
matters,  free  of  governmental  regulation,  no  matter  how
reasonable  or  evenhandedly  that  regulation  may  be thought
to  affect  that  debate.  That  is the  teaching  of both  Miami
Herald  v.  Tornillo  and  Mills  vy.  Alabama,  supra,  where  this
Court  held  that  statutes  which  were  evenhanded  and  might
reasonably  be said  to equalize  the  opportunity  to  influence
voters,  were  invalid  because  they  lowered  the  level  of po-
litical  communication.  The  difference  between  the  FECA
and  the legislation  in those  cases  is that  the  FECA  attempts
the  same  result  in a massive,  indiscriminate  fashion  where-
as  the others  were  more  narrowly  focused.

In  any  event,  the  FECA’s  evenhandedness  is  illusory,
for  its  operation  inevitably  works  great  discrimination
against  certain  candidates  and  certain  causes.  Candidates
and  political  movements  do  not  begin  from  positions  of
equality.  The  amount  a candidate  needs  to spend  to wage  a
viable  campaign  will  turn  on  a wide  variety  of factors.  As  a
result,  no  limits  on  spending  can  ever  be functionally  even-
handed.  For  example,  a  candidate  with  low  name  recogni-
tion  or  poor  press  coverage  running  against  a  well-known
opponent  can  increase  name  recognition  only  through  his
own  campaign.  How  much  or  how  little  the  opponent
spends  is far  less  important  to  him  than  how  much  he can

82

83

spend.  Cole  dep.  tr.  378;  Crane  dep.  tr.  14-20;  Mahoney
dep.  tr.  59-63;  Wertheimer  dep.  tr.  50-51,  53-54.

Public  concerns  at  the  beginning  of a campaign,  more-
over,  will  necessarily  affect  the  relative  position  of  the
candidates.  Some  messages  will  require  more  spending
than  others  to  get  across.  Thus,  in  the  1972  Michigan
Senate  campaign  between  State  Attorney  General  Kelley
and  Sen.  Griffin,  key  Kelley  campaign  personnel  be-
lieved  they could  not  divert  the voters  from  the busing  issue,
on  which  Kelley  was  weak,  to  economic  issues,  on  which  he
was  thought  to  be  strong,  without  spending  considerable
sums  of money.  Findings  II, B 12, p.  194;  App.  II-575.  That
strategy  depended  on  what  one  side  could  spend  without  re-
gard to the  actions  of the opponent.

Campaign  activity  and  debate  will  be  reduced  by
expenditure  limits,  and,  as  a result,  the  public  will  have  less
information  about  issues  of public  importance  at  precisely
the  time  when  such  information  is  most  crucial  and  voter
interest  is at  its  peak.  Jd.  II,  B 57,  p.  204;  App.  II-583.  Not
only  will  campaigns  provide  less  information,  but  a  de-
crease  in  campaign  activity  will  likely  reduce  the  extent  of
media  coverage.  Jd.  II,  B  57-59  pp.  204-05;  App.  II-583.  A
limit  on  expenditures  will  also  reduce  citizen  participation
in political  campaigns.  It costs  money  to  recruit  and  super-
vise  volunteers  effectively,  id.  II,  B 15,  39,  pp.  195,  200,
App.  II-576,  580,  and  a  spending  limit  will  tempt  cam-
paigns  to  put  more  of their  resources  into  television  spots
and  the  like  than  into  participatory  activities,  a  result  in-
fringing  on  the  associational  activities  of a candidate’s  sup-
porters.

If Congress  can  limit  candidate  expenditures,  there  is
no  kind  of political  advocacy  that  it cannot  limit.  If it can
set  a maximum  on  the  amount  of political  communication
in  which  a  candidate  can  engage,  then  it can  put  a  max-
imum  on  the  number  of people  who  engage  in a demonstra-
tion,  see  Edwards  vy.  South  Carolina,  supra,  or  on  the

number  of handbills  which  may  be distributed.  See  7a/lley
v.  California,  supra.
Indeed,  this  very  statute  moves
radically  in  that  direction,  for  it  prohibits  independent
expenditures  in excess  of $1,000  “‘relative  to”’  a candidate,  a
limit  which  would  affect  a distributor  of handbills  or  the or-
ganizers  of a demonstration.’

The  expenditure  limit  itself  creates  pressure  for
further  regulation.  To the extent  that candidates  are  unable
to  communicate  with  voters  in  ways  they  regard  as  ade-
quate,  they  will  feel  more  and  more  dependent  upon  the
media  for  exposure.  To  the  extent  that  such  dependence
grows  <9  too  will  complaints  of unfairness,  see  Findings  II,
B 58, p. 204,  App.  II-583,  and  Statement  of Jim  McClellan,
Hearings  on  S.  372,  supra,  at  252-56,  and  demands  for
more  and  more  regulation  of the  media.  If politics  is to be-
come  a  regulated  industry,  regulation  of the  media  is inevi-
table.  The  only  way  to  preserve  the  values  identified  in
Miami  Herald  vy.  Tornillo,  supra,  Mills  vy.  Alabama,  supra,
v.  Democratic
and  Columbia
National  Committee,  supra,  is to  leave  candidates  free  to
communicate  with  voters  in  the  ways  they  believe  best.’

Broadcasting

System

'  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  opposes
expenditure  limits  of any  kind.  ABA  Report  at  5.  The  ACLU  agrees
that  there  should  be no  limits  on  candidate  expenditures:

“Our  interest  in  eliminating  corruption  should  not  make
us  reduce  discussion  of public  affairs.  That  is the  inevitable
.  Limitations  on
consequence  of limits  on  expenditures  .
expenditures  by  candidates  directly  restrict  their  right  to
speak  to  the  electorate  and  the  public.  If candidates  are
limited  in what  they spend,  they will  generate  less campaign
activity,  which  in  turn  will  mean  less  press  interest  and
reporting  and,  thereby,  less  information  to  the  electorate.”
ACLU  Report  at  18-19.

.

.

?  Although  since  United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.S.  299  (1941),
federal  power  to regulate  primary elections  has  not  been  disputed,  there
(continued

84

85

This  danger  is  underlined  by the  fact  that  the  drafters  of
the  FECA  Amendments  thought  it was  necessary  in effect
to  bar  the  owners  of newspapers  from  running  for  federal
office  by  including  within  the  definition  of  expenditure
news  stories,  commentaries,  and  editorials  in a paper  owned  by
a candidate.  18 U.S.C.  §591(fX4XA).

One  feature  of the  FECA  which  has  a uniquely  radical
effect  on  American  presidential  politics  is the  limitation  on
how  much  money a presidential  aspirant  may  spend  in any
one  state  in both  primary  and  general  elections.  The  FECA
prohibits  spending  more  than  twice  the  applicable  Senate
limits  in  any  particular  state.  It is one  thing  to  place  total
limits  on  expenditures  by presidential  hopefuls;  it is quite
another  to mandate  how  and  where  those  expenditures  may
be made.

This  limitation

is  tantamount  to  controlling  the
strategy  of presidential  campaigns  and  thus  the  content  of
what  the  presidential  candidate  is trying  to communicate  to
his  party  and  to  the  public.  Presidential  primaries  in  par-
ticular  states  may  serve  crucial  functions  to  a  candidate’s
campaign,  well  beyond  the  voting  strength  or  population  of
that  state.  One  example  will  suffice.  The  turning  point  of
John  F.  Kennedy’s  quest  for  the  Presidency  was  his  defeat
of  Hubert  Humphrey  in  the  1960  West  Virginia  presi-
dential  primary,  which  enabled  Kennedy  to rebut  the  claim
that  a Roman  Catholic  could  not  win  in a  Protestant  state.

?  (continued)

is still  much  to  be said  for  maximizing  a  party’s  autonomy  when  its ad-
herents  choose  their  candidates.  To  the  extent  that  expenditure  and
contribution  limits  and  federal  financing  in  presidential  nominating
contests  interfere  with  this  autofiomy,  they may  well  upset  or  distort  the
shifting  and  often  fragile  coalitions  which  comprise  at  least  the  two
major  parties.  This  risk  imposed  by the challenged  statutes  ought  not  to
be ignored.  Cf: Cousins  v.  Wigoda,  419  U.S.  477  (1975).

Had  the  FECA  been  in effect  in  1960,  John  Kennedy  would
likely not  have  been  elected  President.’

The  limitation  on  presidential-candidate  spending  in
any  one  state  in the  general  election  may  have  equally  pro-
found  and  unwarranted  effects  on  national  politics.  In  a
close  presidential  election,  where  the  outcome  may  depend
on  the  result  in one  or  two  major  states,  and  one  of the  two
major party candidates  comes  from  that  state,  his opponent
will  be severely  handicapped  in his  ability  to overcome  that
enormous  advantage.  The  result  of the statute’s  restrictions
may  be to dictate  which  candidate  will  be elected  President.
No governmental  interest  can  sustain  that  result.

What  is  true  of  candidates  is  also  true  of  political
parties  and  committees.  Limitations  on  the  amounts  spent
by these  organizations  also  violate  the  First  Amendment.
To  the  extent  that  national  conventions  are  a  means  of
communicating  with  the  public  as  well  as  of  nominating
candidates,  any  provision  limiting  the  amount  that  can  be
spent  on  conventions  falls  within  the  reasoning  above.  Cf.
Cousins  v.  Wigoda,  419 U.S.  477 (1975).

The  highest  courts  of  two  states  have  recently  con-
sidered  expenditure  limitations,  and  in both  cases  the chal-
lenged  statutes  were  stricken  down,  Deras  v.  Myers,  535
P.2d  541  (Ore.  1975);  Bare  v.  Gorton,  526  P.2d  379 (Wash.
1974).  The  Deras  court  explicitly  rejected  the  argument
that  Watergate-related  evils  justified  the  Oregon  state
expenditure  limits,  stating  ‘[w]e  fail  to  see  how  these  evils
are  relevant  to  the  need  for  controlling  campaign  expendi-
tures,”  535 P.2d  at 546.  The court  concluded  that:

>  Appellant  Eugene  McCarthy's  campaign  in  the  1968  New  Hamp-
shire  presidential  primary  and  George  McGovern’s  victory  in the  1972
California  primary  provide  similar  examples.

86

87

se

..  the  limiting  legislation  closes  or  impedes
important  channels  of communication  on  public
issues  and  thus  denies  citizens  freedom  of expres-
sion  where  the  protection  of  that  constitutional
right  is the  most  necessary  to  preserve  our  system
of government.”  /d.  at  548.

We  submit  that  the  same  conclusion  here  is inescapable.

B.  There  is No  Compelling  Governmental  Interest  Justi-

fying Limitations  on  Expenditures.

Two  alleged  governmental  interests  have  been  ad-
vanced  to justify  expenditure  limitations:  (1) the  need  to re-
duce  the  “‘skyrocketing”’  cost  of elections  and  (2) the  need
to  eliminate  the  allegedly  corrupting  reliance  of candidates
on  private  financing.  See pp.  55-57,  supra.

As demonstrated  supra,  pp.  57-61,  the  “skyrocketing”
costs  rationale  is simply contrary  to fact.  Even  if it were  not,
the  idea  that  “too  much”’  is being  spent  on  elections  is an
impermissible  rationale.  It reflects  a  mistrust  of the  voter’s
capacity  to  evaluate  what  the  House  Report  called
‘sophisticated  advertising  campaign{s].’’  H.R.  Rep.  No.  93-
1239,  supra  at  3. There  are  few  means  of persuasion  which
might  not  be  deemed  somehow  too  persuasive  or  somehow
likely  to  fool  the  public.  Moreover,  the  House  Report  itself
states  only  that  “‘under  present  law  the  impression  persists
that  a  candidate  can  buy  an  election  by simply  spending
large sums  in a campaign  ...
.”’ Jd.  (emphasis  added).  ‘‘Im-
pressions”  are  not  sufficient  to  justify  infringements  on
First  Amendment  rights.

The  rationale  of the  court  below  —  that  limitations  on
expenditures  are  necessary  to reduce  the incentive  to violate
contribution  limits  (op.  at  1538)  —  iustifies  a  massive  in-

grounds.

trusion  on  political  communication  on  thin  and  un-
persuasive
Marginal  gains  in  enforcement
authority  do  not  outweigh  pervasive  restraints  on  freedom
of  expression.  The  statute,  moreover,  does  not  limit  ex-
penditures  only by candidates  who  have  received  large con-
tributions.  It limits  expenditures  by all candidates,  no  mat-
ter how small  the contributions  he receives.

In  any  event,  limits  on  expenditures  will  on  the  whole
reduce  candidate  interest  in  small  contributions.  Because
small  contributions  cost  proportionately  more  to raise  than
large  contributions,  Findings  II,  B  41,  pp.  200-01,  App.
II-S80,  Cole  dep.  tr.  384-86,  a  limit  on  expenditures,  includ-
ing expenditures  for fund  raising,  will  induce  candidates  to
raise  as  much  as  possible  in  contributions  as  large  as  the
law  allows,  thus  reducing  the costs  of fund  raising.

The  FECA,  moreover,  permits candidates  to use  excess
funds  in  ways  which  will  induce  them  to  raise  as  much
money  as  they can  regardless  of limits  on  expenditures.  An
incumbent  is able  to  use  excess  campaign  funds  to  defray
office  expenses,  2 U.S.C.  §439a,  or  for  “any  lawful  purpose,”
id.,  which  apparently  includes  personal  use—an  ironic  re-
form  to emerge  from  Watergate  and the Agnew  affair.  Such
funds  can  also  be  put  aside  to  use  as  seed  money  for a re-
election  effort,  thus  increasing  the  advantages  of  incum-
bency  and  making  the candidate  independent  of the  public
for  financial  support  in  the  next  election.  The  FECA
Amendments  thus  in  no  way  discourage  candidates  from
raising  large amounts  of money.

C.  The  Limitations  on  Expenditures  Are  Generally  So
Low  as  To Lack  a  Rational  Basis,  Are  Impermissibly  Over-
broad,  and Have More  Than  an  Incidental Effect on  Speech.
The  particular  limitations  on  candidate  expenditures
embodied  in  the  FECA  lack a rational  basis,  unless  they
were  deliberately  intended  to  be  so  low  as  to  disadvantage

88

89

challengers.  See infra,  pp.  94-104.  They do not  take  into  ac-
count  variables  such  as  incumbent  use  of perquisites  of of-
fice  or  the  local  cost  of media  time.  They  are,  moreover,  far
below  the  level  of expenditures  in  closely  contested  cam-
paigns*  and  will  thus  have  a substantial  impact  on  political
communication.

The  effect  of the  limits  will  be  “marginal”  or  “‘inci-
dental’’  on  political  communication  only  in  contests  for
“‘safe’’  seats.  It wili  be drastic,  on  the  other  hand,  on  races
for  truly  contested  seats,  that  is,  on  those  races  which  in
fact  are  crucial  to  a genuinely  competitive  electoral  system.
The  FECA  will  thus  affect  not  only  the  nature  of  fund

*  As  a  political  rule  of thumb,  a  “‘closely  contested  election”  is any
race  where  the  winner  receives  less  than  SS  percent  of the  total  vote.
Those  Senate  seats  or  House  districts  where  the  winner  does  receive
more  than  SS  percent  of the  vote  are  typically  labelled  “‘safe’’  seats.
Conversely,  the  Senate  seats  or  House  districts  where  the  winner  re-
ceives  less  than  SS percent  of the  vote  are  typically  labelled  “swing”  or
“marginal”  seats.

This  assumption  may  understate  the  opposition  in  general
elections  since  a few  losers  receiving  less  than  4S percent  may  have  been
realistically  regarded  as  serious.  On  the  other  hand,  the  assumption
overstates  the  opposition  in  primaries  without  runoffs,  since  a  51-
percent  victor  over  ten  opponents  may  well  have  been  a sure  winner.

“Open”  seats  appear  where  the  previous  incumbent  is elected  to
another  office,  retires  or  dies.  A  seat  previously  considered  safe  may
become  a  swing  seat  when  the  previous  incumbent  leaves.  The  out-of-
power  party  may  perceive  that  its  chances  have  considerably  increased
when  the  party  holding  the  seat  may  no  longer  run  the  weil-known  and
popular  incumbent.  In  such  a  case,  both  parties  may  well  regard  the
seat  as  having  become  marginal.  When  a particular  party  has  a  firm
hold  on  a  particular  seat,  however,  even  the  departure  of the  previous
incumbent  may  still  not  be enough  to  move  the  seat  from  the  “‘safe”’  to
the  “swing”  category.

As  a  matter  of practical  politics,  the  two  n.ajor  parties  concentrate
heavily  on  swing  seats,  attempting  to  hold  those  won  in  the  previous
election  (and  if possible  increase  the  margin  of victory),  and  attempting
to  unseat  the  incumbent  in swing seats  held  by the other  party.

raising  but  also  will  dramatically  restrict  political  com-
munication  in the most  crucial  electoral  contests.

The  different  statistics  available  in the  record,  and  the
assumptions  underlying  them,  are  explained  in Appendix  A
hereto.  Table  1 on  p.  90 clearly  demonstrates  that  in  1972
and  1974  large  numbers  of  candidates  for  the  House  of
Representatives  exceeded  the expenditure  limits.  The  same
conclusion  obtains  in Senate  races.  In  1972,  on  the  basis  of
estimates  for  general-election  expenditures  (see  Appendix
A, pp.  A-2  -  A-4,  infra),  33 Senate  candidates  exceeded  the
expenditure  limits,  Findings  II, B 19, p.  196;  App.  II-577.  (If
inflation  between  1972  and  1974  were  considered,  this
figure  would  be  even  higher.)*
In  fact,  the  expenditure
limits  would  have  eliminated  one-third  of total spending for
the  Senate  in  1972.  Id.  Il,  B 20, p.  196;  App.  II-S77.  Even
taking  the  ‘combined  overall  expenditure  limit”
primary  limits  plus  general-election  limits  —  and  using
Common  Cause  figures  unadjusted  for  inflation,  18 Senate
candidates  exceeded  this  limit  in  1972,  Findings  II,  A 47,
pp.  99-100,  App.  II-450,  and  17 exceeded  it in  1974,  id.  II,
A 28, p. 93; App.  I1-445.

overall

expenditure

92

In  presidential  elections,  the  limits  are  set  far  below
the  amount  spent  by Sen.  McGovern  in  1972  even  when  no
adjustments  are  made  for  inflation.  In  presidential  cam-
paigns,  moreover,  the  need  to  expend  money  in  excess  of
the  challenged  limits  is  especially  crucial.  Given  the
importance  of the  President’s  role,  and  the  history  of ex-
penses  in  past  presidential  campaigns,  Findings  I, 89,  pp.
52-53,  App.  II-49-51,  limiting  expenditures  in  those  cam-
paigns  must  drastically  alter  the  interplay  of  forces  af-
fecting  presidential  elections.  See, e.g.,  Findings  II, A 71, p.
110;  App.  II-457.

The  statutory  limits  are  so  low  as  to  lack  a rational
basis.  Thus,  the  parties  have  agreed  that  in the  1972  cam-
paigns  it  cost  approximately  20¢  to  make  one  mail  or
telephone  contact  with  a  voter.  Findings  II,  B 40,  p.  200;
App.  II-580.  The  Senate  limit  is only  8¢ in the  primary  and
12¢  in the  general  election,  plus  20 percent  for fund  raising.

The  court  below  argues  that  great  deference  must  be
shown  to  Congress’  judgment  on  this  matter  (op.  at  1541).
Quite  apart  from  the  fact  that  such  deference  in the context
of this  case  is a  virtual  abandonment  of the  First  Amend-
ment,  since  any  limit  on  speech  could  then  turn  on  “‘facts”’
conclusively  determined  by the  legislature,  there  is  a way  to
test  that  judgment.  By use  of the frank  Congress  has  shown
its judgment  as  to  what  are  reasonable  costs  of communica-
tion  with  voters.  In  the  year  1974,  over  $38  million  was
spent  on  the  congressional  frank,  Findings  I,  36,  p.  21,
App.  II-23,  while  major-party  challengers  spent  in all primary
and  general-election  campaigns  a  total  of  $20.2  million,
Findings  II, A 14, 24, pp.  89, 92, App.  II, 442,444,  for all their
activities  such  as  media,  mailings,  staff,  rent,  telephones,  tran-
sportation,
$46.1
million  has  been  budgeted  for  the  frank,  while  all  the
campaign  activities  of challengers  are  now  subject  to  the
FECA  limits.  Given  the  interest  of incumbent  members  of
Congress  in limiting  their  challengers’  expenditures,  while

printing,

1976,

year

For

etc.

the

93

stead
increasing  their  own  expenditures  which  are  not
subjec  o the  limits  but  produce  political  effects,  to  show
great  deference  to  congressional  judgment  in  this  case
would  be intolerable.

Not  only  will  the  limitations  on  expenditures  have  a
dramatic  rather  than  an  incidental  effect  on  political  com-
munication;  they  lack  rational  basis  in  other  ways.  For
example,  the  Federal  Election  Commission  has  ruled  that  a
candidate  may  spend  up to the  limit  in a  completely  unop-
posed  contest  for  nomination.  FEC  Notice  1975-27,  40  Fed.
Reg.  36242  (Aug.  19,  1975).  That  a  House  candidate  can
spend  $70,000  to win  a completely  unopposed  nomination,
but  only  $70,000  in  a  hotly  contested  general  election,  is a
bizarre  result  of the  statute.  Moreover,  the  statute  creates
an  incentive  to  spend  up  to  the  limit  in  unopposed
primaries,  in  order  for  a  candidate  to  increase  his  ex-
penditures  having  general-election  impact  beyond  the
general-election  limit.  33 Cong.  Q.  Wkly.  Rep.  1242  (June
14,  1975).  Total  spending  may  thus  be  increased  in  some
campaigns  as  a  result  of the  statute.  Indeed,  this  Court  is
asked  to  sustain  legislation  which  is  based  upon  the
“skyrocketing”’  cost  of campaigns,  but  which  says  that  a
Senator  in  New  York  can  spend  —  and  indeed  is given  a
new  incentive  to  spend  —  a  million  dollars  seeking  a  com-
pletely  unopposed  nomination.  A candidate  who  faces  stiff
opposition  in a primary  will  clearly  be disadvantaged  if his
general-election  opponent  can  spend  up  to  the  limit  in  an
unopposed  primary  which  for all practical  purposes  is spen-
ding  for  general-election  purposes.  The  magnitude  of this
disadvantage  will  largely  depend  on  the  wholly  fortuitous
variable  of  state-established  primary  dates;  the  later  the
primary  —  and  some  are  as  late  as  September  ——  the
greater the effect  on  the general  election.

The  FECA  limits  take  no  account  of  differences  in
primary  dates  or  differences  in  state  laws  as  to  how  a

94

95

nomination  is won.  Thus,  in a  state  where  primaries  result
only if a party’s  convention  nominee  is challenged,  the limit
cannot  safely  be  spent  ahead  of the  convention  and  not  at
all after  it if no  challenge  occurs.  See,e.g.,  Conn.  Gen.  Stats.
Ann.  §§ 9-381  -  9-452,  (1958).

Finally,  the  limits  take  no  account  of  the  countless
variables  which  affect  the  cost  of elections.  See  Findings  I,
4, p. 2; App.  II-S-6.  From  district  to district  and  from  state  to
state  there  are  enormous  differences  in media  coverage  and
media  cost.  Jd.  I, 86,  pp.  50-51;  App.  II-49.  In  some  districts
and  states  there  may  be concentrated  centers  of population,
while  in  others  voters  may  be  dispersed.  Some  candidates
may  find  their  greatest  sources  of  strength  in  rural  reas
while  others  find  theirs  in  urban  areas,  and  their  relative
Yet  the
costs
challenged  statutes  irrationally  tie  all  campaigns  to  the
same  Procrustean  bed  in  complete  disregara  of  the  com-
plexities  of  the  political  process.  Such  an  exercise  is  not
only  irrational;  it  is  potentially  fatal  to  free  political
discourse.

of  communication  may  vary

widely.

D.  The  Expenditure  Limitations  Are  Unconstitutional
Because  They  Discriminate  Generally  Against  Challengers
and  Generally  in Favor  of Incumbents.

If it can  legitimately  be  said  that  the  FECA  limits  on
expenditures  prevent  “‘high’’  spending,  it is a  fact  beyond
challenge  that  successful  challengers  to  incumbents  have
historically  been  ‘“‘high’’  spenders.°  Challengers  are  likely to

*  The  Court  of  Appeals  stated  that  the  District  Court  ‘properly
declined”  to  find  “undwe  discrimination  in  favor  of incumbents”  (op.
at  1540).  This  statement  is a misrepresentation  of the  fact-finding  pro-
cedure  below.  In fact,  the  initial  requests  for  findings,  which  included
substantial  requests  for  findings  of a  conclusory  nature  by both  sides,
were  never  ruled  upon.  It could  be just  as  easily said  that  the  District
Court  properly  declined  to  find  that  the  statutes  were  justified  by a
compelling  governmental  interest,  a finding offered  by appellees.

have  low  name  recognition  and  other  disadvantages  which
can  be  overcome  only  by a  vigorous  and  inevitably  costly
campaign,  no  matter  what  the  incumbent  spends.  The
peryuisites  available  to  incumbents,  moreover,  entail  govern-
rental  financial  support  of  incumbent  campaigns,  e.g..
the  frank.  Limits  only  on  direct  campaign  expenditures
thus  constitute  a  gross  imbalance  in  ability  to  reach  the
voter,  an  imbalance  without  precedent  in American  history.

Amendments:  The  Shortcomings  of  Good  Intentions,”
1975  Duke  L.J.  851,  878-89  (1975)  (hereinafter,  ““The  1974
FECA  Amendments’’).

To  the  extent  that  the  Court  of Appeals  addressed  the  statistics  in
the  record  as  to  campaign  spending  in previous  years,  it relied  entirely
on  comparisons  with  the  so-called  “combined  overall  expenditure
limitations,’’  which  are  misleading  for  a number  of reasons.  See  Ap-
pendix  A, pp.  A-3  -  A-4, infra.

96

97

In  1972,  according  to  the  same  figures,  the  successful
challengers  spent  an  average  of  $148,115.  This  spending,
moreover,  almost  surely  was  mostly  in the  general  election,
for  in  1972  only  2 of the  10  winning  challengers  ran  in  a
primary  and  received  less  than  55%  of the  vote.  In  1974,  of
the  29 successful  challengers  who  spent  over  $70,000,  only
10 ran  in a primary  and  received  under  55%  of the vote.  Jd.

In  the  1972  Senate  general  elections,  five  challengers
defeated  incumbents.  If adjustments  are  made  for inflation
between  1972  and  1974  and  general-election  spending  is es-
timated  by the  Cole  method,*  all five  spent  at  least  14.4¢  or
$180,000;  in  short  all  exceeded  the  limits  set  by the  FECA
Amendments.  In  1974,  two  Senate  challengers  won.  Of
these,  one  spent  more  than  the  overall  combined  expendi-
ture  limit,  according  to  Common  Cause  figures.  Findings
II,  A 30,  p.  94;  App.  II-445.  The  other  reported  spending
substantial  sums  after  his  primary  date.  September  12,
1974,  which,  if allocated  to  the  general  election,  exceeded
the  1974  limit.  Jd.  II, C 3, pp.  210-13;  App.  II-587-94.

The  facts  thus  are  clear.  Successful  challengers  to
incumbents  tend  to  be  “high”’  spenders.  (This  is  so  even
when  the  1974  races  are  taken  into  account,  races  in which
Republican  incumbents  were  severely  disadvantaged  by the
Watergate  scandal  and  presidential  and  vice-presidential
resignations.)  Successful  campaigns  for  change  require
substantial  financial  expenditures  if  they  are  not  to  be
buried  under  the status  quo.

Since  the  limits  imposed  on  spending  are,  according  to
agreed  facts,  well  below  even  one  contact  per  voter  (for  the
Senate,  12c  per  voter  against  a  stipulated  20c  cost  of one
contact,  Findings  II,  B 40,  p.  200,  App.  II-580),  a  candidate
who  begins  with  a low name-recognition  factor  (challenger)  will
have  grave  difficulty  competing  with  one  with  a high  name-

*  See  Appendix  A, pp.  A-2  -  A-4, infra.

recognition  factor  and  many  previous  voter  contacts  (in-
cumbent).

There  is thus  a  “‘floor’’  of spending  which  challengers
must  reach  to  be  “‘viable,”’  to  obtain  the  voter  recognition
that  enables  a  campaign  to  attract  votes.  The  Senate
challengers  or  campaign  managers  interviewed  by Roland
Cole  in  1972  all said  they needed  a certain  amount  for their
campaign,  regardless  of  what  was
to  the
incumbent  opponent.  Findings  II, B 7, p. 192; App. II-574.

available

The  marginal  utility of initial  spending  is much  greater
to  challengers  than  to  incumbents.  Each  dollar  spent  by
challengers  below  the  ‘floor’  of  viability  brings  them  a
much  greater  return  than  that  received  for  the  same
amount  by incumbents.  Once  the  challenger  reaches  that
““floor’’  he has  a viable  candidacy.  A limit  can  thus  prevent
challengers  from  ever  gaining  viability.  Indeed,  since  cam-
paign  money  is subject  to  the  law  of diminishing  marginal
returns,  high-spending  challengers  may  win  even  though
outspent  by their  incumbent  opponents.

The  most  dramatic  example  of  high  spending  as  a
force  for  change  is the  McCarthy  New  Hampshire  primary
campaign.  Although  dubbed  the  “‘Children’s  Crusade”’  and
portrayed  by  the  media  as  largely  a  volunteer  effort,
McCarthy  in fact  spent  $12  per vote  received  ($18  adjusted
for inflation),  Findings  I, 43, p. 23; App.  II-25.  (Richard  Nixon
spent  67¢  per  vote  in  the  1968  general  election.)  A  con-
siderable  television  and  radio  effort  at the  end  of the  cam-
paign,  moreover,  was  used  to  increase  McCarthy’s  name
recognition.  Alexander,  Financing  the  1968  Election  39
(1971).  Without  that  spending,  his  efforts  and  those  of his
supporters  would  have  been  in vain.

In  the  economic  marketplace,  a  high  level  of  adver-
tising  correlates  with  a  high  level  of introduction  of  new
product  brands,  Telser,  Advertising  and  Competition,
Journal  of  Political  Economy  (Dec.  1964),  at  537.  The

98

99

newcomer  obtains  more  value  out  of  its  initial  spending
than  the  familiar  product  does  by  spending  an  equal
amount  on  advertising.  Few  would  doubt  that  a limit  on  ad-
vertising  for  automobiles,  restaurants,  or  movie  theaters
would  hamper  newcomers.  Similarly  in politics,  a low  limit
on  expenditures  will  generally  work  to  the  advantage  of
incumbents  and  discriminate  against  challengers.

This  phenomenon  has  been  widely  recognized  by  a
wide  variety  of commentators  on  campaign  financing,  in-
cluding  appellees  in  statements  made  before  this  litiga-
tion  was  commenced:  Herbert  Alexander  of  the  Citizens
Research  Foundation,  Hearings  on  S.  372,  supra,  at  223-
24;  Russell  Hemenway  of the  National  Committee  for  an
Effective  Congress,  Hearings  on  S.  23,  et  al.,  supra,  at  75;
appellee  John  Gardner,  Hearings  on  H.R.  7612  and  S.372
hefore  the  Subcommittee  on  Elections  of the  House  Com-
mittee  on  Administration,  93d  Cong.,  1st  Sess.  388  (1973),
and
Fred  Wertheimer  of  appellee  Common  Cause,
Hearings  on  S.  23,  et  al.,  supra,  at  269.  It  has  also  been
widely  recognized  in  Congress.  Among  supporters  of  the
FECA  Amendments  who  have  stated  their  belief  that  the
challenged  expenditure  limits  generally  work  to  the  ad-
vantage  of incumbents  are  Rep.  Frenzel,  H.R.  Rep.  No.  93-
1239,  supra,  at  151-52,  and  120  Cong.  Rec.  H.  7846  (daily
ed.  Aug.  7, 1974);  Rep.  Brown,  id.,  H 7831;  Sen.  Clark,  id.,
S  18529  (daily  ed.  Oct.  8,  1974);  Sen.  Biden,  id.,  S 18534:
and  Sen.  Cannon,  id.,  S 18535.

That  incumbents  frequently  outspend  their  opponents
is no  answer  to the  argument  that  expenditure  ceilings  gen-
erally  discriminate  against  challengers.  Incumbents  with-
out  serious  opposition  quite  typically  are  easily  able  to out-
spend  their  opponents.  Thus,  in the  chart  reflecting  House
expenditures  printed  supra,  p.  91,  it  is  clearly  demon-
strated  that  the  gap  in  average  spending  by winners  and
losers  decreases  with  the  closeness  of  the  election.  Thus
where  the  winning  percentage  is  70  percent  or  greater,
spending  is  relatively  low  and  winners  outspend  losers

roughly  S to  1. Where  the  winning  percentage  is 55 percent
or  less,  however,  spending  is much  higher  and  the  gap  vir-
tually  disappears.  Since  most  big winners  are  incumbents
and  the  limits  do  not  seriously  affect  those  races,  the
principal  impact  of  limits  will  be  on  those  close  races  in
which  usually  spending  is  already  fairly  equal.  Permitting
spending  in  unopposed  primary  campaigns,  moreover,
clearly  disadvantages  challengers  since  they  are  unlikely  to
have  substantial  funds  available  at early dates.

Although  the  issue  of ‘“‘skyrocketing’’  campaign  costs
is presumably  a matter  for  all to be concerned  about,  the  idea
that  each  House  of Congress  should  set  its own  expenditure
limits  permeated  the  debate  over  the  FECA  Amendments.
Particularly  in  the  Senate,  supporters  of S 3044  admonished
its opponents  that  it was  the  “‘prerogative’’  of the  House  of
Representatives  to  “write  its own  ticket.’’  Remarks  of Sen.
Kennedy,  /20  Cong.  Rec.  S 4470 (daily ed.  March  26,  1974).
See  also  remarks  of  Sen.  Cannon,  id.,  S 4548;  at  S 4930
(daily ed.  April  1, 1974),  stating  that  if the  House  wished  to
lower  its expenditure  limitation  to  $50,000,  ‘‘that  would  be
perfectly  all right  with  us’’;  and  at  S 5273  (daily ed.  April  4,
1974).

~

Moreover,  Sen.  Cannon,  as  Chairman  of  the  Senate
Committee  on  Rules  and  Administration  and  a  floor
manager  of  S  3044,  and  of  the  subsequent  Conference
Committee  bill,  also  admitted  that  he  did  not  believe  “the
precise  question  was  raised  [in the  hearings]  as  to  whether
the  incumbent  ought  to  get  less  than  the  person  who  is the
challenger.”  Sen.  Cannon  then  stated,  ‘“‘but  in  simple
frankness,  if we  wrote  into  the  bill  a  different  figure,  we
would  kill  the  bill.’’  120  Cong.  Rec.  S 4940  (daily  ed.  April
1,  1974)  (emphasis  added).  See  also  remarks  of  Rep.
Pritchard,  id.,  H 7801  (daily ed.  Aug.  7, 1974).

No  wonder.  Not  only  do  incumbents  have  political  ad-
vantages  which  can  be overcome  only by spending,  but  they

100

101

receive  direct  financial  support  from  tax  money  which  is
not  affected  by limits  on  either  contributions  or  expendi-
tures.

Although  difficult  to  quantify,  the  direct  tangible
support  provided  by  the  government  to  each  House  in-
cumbent  is  almost  $1  million  per  term.  See  p.  51,  supra.
Incumbents  have  paid  staff,  free  telephone,  office  space  in
Washington  and  three  offices  in  their  home  districts,  sub-
sidies  for  stationery  and  office  supplies,  access  to  inex-
pensive  television  and  recording  facilities,  use  of the  frank,
and  many  other  benefits,  all in addition  to the  normal  bene-
fits  of incumbency  such  as  high  visibility  in  their  districts
and  the  ability  to  make  news.  Findings  I, 24-33,  pp.  15-21;
App.  II-16-23.

The  FECA  provides,  moreover,  that  use  of  the  tele-
vision  and  recording  facilities  in non-election  years  is not  to
be treated  as  an  “‘expenditure,”’  2 U.S.C.  § 434(d),  see,  e.g.,
Findings  III,  Steiger  11-12,  p. 37; App.  II-730.  This  permits
the  incumbent  to do his  filming  in the year  prior to  an  elec-
tion,  and  leave  only the cost  of editing  to be counted  toward
the  spending  limits.

Obviously,  all  of these  services  are  frequently  used  for
deliberate  political  purposes.  Even  when  they  are  not,  the
most  scrupulous  Congressman  will  obtain  a political  advan-
tage  from  them.  /d.  III,  Steiger  7, 9-17,  pp.  36-38;  App.  II-
729-32.  Appellants  by no  means  contend  that  perquisites  of
incumbents  which  enable  them  to  serve  and  communicate
with  their  constituents  are  unjustified.  But  communication
by  challengers  plays  as  critical  a  part  in  the  political
process.

munications  by challengers  must  be paid  for and  are  a cam-

issues  and  write  policy  papers  on  them.  Challengers  can  do
the  same  only  by  expending  campaign  resources.  Chal-

benefits.

Similar

lengers  must  rent  offices.  If a  challenger  wants  to  call  a
meeting,  he  must  normally  hire  a  hall  and  advertise  it,
while  an  incumbent  member  of Congress  may  be  able  to
hold  a  well-publicized  hearing  in  his  home  district.  In
raising  small  contributions,  an  incumbent  has  an  ad-
vantage  in  that  he  has  mailing  lists  which  are  regularly
used,  while  the  challenger  must  normally  purchase  or
develop  them  on  his own.  Findings  II, B 63, p. 205;  App.  II-
584.

Consider  just  the  effect  of the  frank.  In  the  first  two
weeks  of October,  1974,  the  House  and  Senate  mailed  21.8
million  pieces  of franked  mail.  During  the  same  two-week
period  in non-election  year  1973,  only  10.5  million  pieces  of
franked  mail  were  sent  out.  The  average  cost  for the  use  of
the  frank  per member  of Congress  per term  is equivalent  to
$104,000,  and  rising.  Expenditures  on  the  frank  in election
the  total  amount  spent  by
years
congressional  challengers  on  all  their  campaign  activities.
This  ratio  may  be  increasing,  since  $46.1  million  has  been
budgeted  for  use  of the  frank  in  fiscal  1976,  $7.4  million
more  than  in  fiscal  1975,  while  challengers  must  spend
within  the  FECA  limits.  Wall  Street  Journal,  June  2, 1975,
at 5, col.  1.

almost  twice

are

In  addition,  the  use  of  direct-mail  specialists  and
mass-mailing  techniques  applied  to  franked  mail  during
election  years  clearly  provide  electoral  benefits.  They
enable  incumbents  to build  up mailing  lists  and  experience
in  direct  mail  which  are  invaluable  in  raising  small
contributions.

These  perquisites,  moreover,  are  entirely  within  the
control  of incumbents,  who  can  and  do  raise  them  at  will.
Indeed,  the  same  House  committee  which  passed  on  the
challenged  statutes  also  has  control  over  the  levels  of these
incumbent  resources.

In  the  last  five  years,  the  number  of  congres-
sional  employees  has  grown  from  10,000  to  17,000.  Find-

7

102

103

ings  I, 26, pp.  18-19;  App.  II-21.  Office  staffs  of House  mem-
bers  have  been  increased  from  16  to  18  (an  increase  of
870  employees  at  a  cost  of  nearly  $10  million);  the  per-
mament  staffs  of thirty  House  committees  have  been  in-
creased  from  12  to  30;  and  the  House  Rules  Committee
(which  passed  on  the  FECA  Amendments)  recently  voted  to
give each  of its members a legislative  assistant,  thus  tripling
the  size  of the  committee’s  staff.  All  committee  chairmen
and  ranking  minority  members  are  to be entitled  to another
staff member  in addition  to the total  allocated  to their  com-
mittees.  Washington  Post,  June  1,  1975,  at  A9,  col.  1. The
Senate  Rules  Committee  recently  voted  in  favor  of  a
proposal  that  would  provide  every  Senator  with  a staff
member  for  every  committee  on  which  he sits,  unless  he al-
ready  has  one  designated  (an  increase  for  the  entire  Senate
of  approximately  100  staff  members).  Washington  Post,
June  4, 1975,  at  A8, col.  1.

In  addition,  the  House  has  increased  the  number  of
paid  trips  to  a  member’s  district  from  21  to  32,  and  has
allotted  tax  money  to  pay  for the  printing  of two  newsletters
per  member.  One  editorial  accurately  described  these  in-
creases  as  ‘re-election  firepower.’’  Wall  Street  Journal,
June  3, 1975,  at  15, col.  9.

it is like  starting  a  basketball  game  with  one  team  leading
SO  to  0,  and  then  “‘evenhandedly”’  limiting  both  teams  to
the  same  number  of shots  at  the  basket.  Given  the  ample
resources  which  incumbents  put  at  their  own  disposal,  the
FECA  is not  even  facially  evenhanded.  What  it limits  are
all  expenditures  by challengers  and  some  expenditures  by
incumbents.

In  no  sense,  therefore,  do  the  FECA  Amendments
“open  up  the  system.’”’  They  are  ‘incumbents’  protection’”’
legislation  which  will  freeze  the  status  quo  and  further  de-
crease  the  responsiveness  of  government  to  the  people.
Indeed,  they  will  thus  aggravate  what  may  be  the  primary
ill of the  American  political  system:  the  security  of incum-
bent  officeholders.  Political  scientists  are  in  agreement
that  since  World  War  II  incumbents  have  become  much
more  likely  to  be  reelected.  Kostroski,  Plaintiffs’  April  3
“Documents,”  Ex.  K,  at  1217-18.  Erikson,  id.,  Ex.  F,  at
1240.  See  also  Cowart,  id.,  Ex.  B,  at  852;  Cummings,  id.,
Ex.  C, at 69;  and  Mayhew,  id.,  Ex.  N, at  313-15.

where  an  overriding  issue  is present,  there  is little  excuse  for

themselves  from  the  Treasury,  as  did  the  court  below,  is to

which  incumbent  office-holders

*  David  Broder  has  made  the  point cogently  and  concisely:

“If you  are  an  incumbent  in office,  you  are  in a strong  posi-
tion  to  publicize  yourself  and  your  views.  So  when  you  set
limits—and  I think  this  applies  to  questions  of new  parties
versus  established  parties,  challenging  candidates  versus
incumbent  officeholders,  establishment  views  versus  nones-
tablishment  views—in  every  instance,  when  you  limit
expenditures  on  an  across-the-board  basis,  what  you  are
really  doing  is saying  ‘We  are  systematically  going  to tilt  the
thing  in  favor  of  those  who  already  hold  power  and  their
points  of  view."  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Campaign

104

105

defeat.”’  120  Cong.  Rec.  S 4938  (daily ed.  April  1, 1974).  In
1966,  25 out  of 26 incumbents  who  ran  for reelection  in the
Senate  won,  20 out  of 24  in  1968,  23 out  of 26  in  1970,  20
out  of 25 in  1972  and  21 out  of 25 in  1974.  In the House,  the
figures  are  even  more  dramatic.  In  1966,  362 of the 402 who
ran  won,  396  of the  405  in  1968,  379 of the  390  in  1970,  366
of the  379  in  1972  and  343  of the  383  in  1974.  The  1974
turnover,  of  course,  followed  the  unprecedented  resigna-
tions  of a  President  and  Vice  President.  Of the  40 who  lost,
36  were  members  of their  party.  Findings  I, 17,  pp.  9-11;
App.  II-12-14.  See  also  id.,  I, 19-23,  pp.  12-14;  App.  II-14-
16.  Indeed,  between  1954  and  1968  only  8 percent  of the
House  members  and  15  percent  of  Senators  running  for
reelection  were  defeated.  It is precisely  because  incumbents
are  so  secure  that  challengers  have  little  chance  to  win
unless  they can  spend  substantial  amounts.

There  can  be  no  question  that  a  law  restricting  po-
litical  communication  in  a  way  that  discriminates  against
challengers  and  favors  incumbents  is  unconstitutional,
Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968);  Gould  v.  Grubb,
536  P.2d  1337,  1345  (Cal.  1975).  There  are  no  coun-
such
tervailing
discrimination.  If the  First  Amendment  protects  anything,
it  protects  political  communications  by citizens  trying  to
unseat  those  in  power,  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  supra,
354  U.S.  at 250-51.

interests

justify

to

E.  The  Limits  Imposed  by  the  FECA  Amendments
Violate  the First  and Fifth Amendments  Because  They Dis-
criminate  Against  Certain  Candidates  and Parties.

The  majority  below  declared  that  the  FECA  was
‘without  discrimination  based  on  party...”  (op. at  1518).
In  fact,  the  statute  on  its  face  prevents  independent  candi-
dates  from  spending  as  much  as  party-sponsored  candi-
dates,  prevents  some  party-sponsored  candidates  from
spending  as  much  as  others  and  permits  parties  with  na-

tional  committees  to  spend  more  in  presidential  elections
than  those  without.  The  court  below  never  addressed  these
discriminations.  No  compelling  interest
is  offered  to
support  them.  There  is  none  to  offer.  They  are  demon-
strably  inconsistent  with  every  tenet  of a free society.

The  FECA  allows  national  or  state  political  parties  to
expend  2¢  per  adult  on  behalf  of each  of their  candidates
for  federal  office.  18  U.S.C.  § 608(f).  Independent  candi-
dates,  lacking  party  structures,  are  not  entitled  to  receive
such  additionai  support.  This  leaves  independents  unable
to  spend  as  much  as  their  opponents,  a  patent  violation  of
the  Constitution.  (The  Republican  Party  is  planning  to
raise  and  spend  $20  million  in private  funds  in  1976,  twice
as  much  as  ever  before.  Broder,  ‘“The  GOP’s  Financial
‘Loophole,’  ””  Washington  Post,  Sept.  14,  1975,  at  B 6, col.
3.) This  discrimination  cannot  be cured  simply  by requiring
independents  to  establish  a  party  on  “‘paper’’;  much  of an
independent’s  appeal,  as  Sen.  McCarthy  believes,  is lack  of
connection  with  a  party,  an  important  asset  at  a  time  of
alienation  from  the  major  parties.  McCarthy  dep.  tr.  8-10;
Monroe  aff.  9 7.

The  constitutional  rights  of party  candidates  are  also
infringed  by limiting  their  spending  while  giving  their  state
and  national  committees  an  option  as  to whether  to support
them  with  the  permitted  extra  expenditure.  If  for  some
reason  a  candidate  has  alienated  those  who  control  the
committees  or  does  not  receive  the  money  for other  reasons,
he will  face  an  opponent  whose  expenditure  limit  is greater
than  his.  This  also  is  a  patent  violation  of  the  Consitu-
tion,  serving  no  rational  purpose,  let  alone  a  compelling
governmental  interest.

In  addition,  parties  such  as  the  Conservative  Party  of
New  York,  which  do  not  have  a  national  committee,  Find-
ings  III,  Mahoney  2, p.  11, App.  II-708,  are  not  entitled  to the
extra  expenditure  on  behalf of their  candidate  for President

106

as  are  parties  with  a  national  committee.  Such a restriction
demonstrably  harms  the  Conservative  Party,  its candidates
and  its  supporters,  whether  it  nominates  a  candidate  for
President  nominated  by  one  of  the  two  major  parties  or
someone  else,  Mahoney  aff.  99 7-8.

These  distinctions  between  various  kinds  of party com-
mittees,  other  formal  political  committees,  and  informal
associations  are  unprecedented  in  American  election  law
and  serve  no  purpose  other  than  discrimination.  As  such,
they infringe  the  rights  of speech  and  association,  and  work
an  invidious  discrimination  among  different  kinds  of
political  movements.

107

IV.

The  Limitation  »n  Independent  Expenditures
Violates  the Constitution.

Title  18  U.S.C.  § 608(e)  prohibits  persons  or  groups
completely  independent  of  any  candidate  from  spending
more  than  $1,000  per  year  “relative  to  a  clearly  identified
candidate”  and  “advocating  the  election  or  defeat  of such
candidate.’  This  statute  makes  it  a  federal  criminal  of-
fense  for  a  citizen  to  place  a  one-quarter  page  ad-
vertisement  in  the  New  York  Times  or  the  Washington
Post  urging  his  fellow  citizens  to  vote  for  (or  against)  the
President  of the  United  States.  Findings  I, 65,  pp.  32-33;
App.  II-32.  As  such,  it  is an  extraordinarily  explicit  restric-
tion  on  individual  political  activity  and  one  without
precedent  in the  history  of the  republic.  Section  608(e)  and
the  First  Amendment  cannot  stand  together.  One  or  the
other  must  fall.

A more  sweeping  intrusion  upon  free  political  expres-
sion  is difficult  to  imagine.  No  person  or  group,  no  matter
how  independent  of any  candidate,  may  spend  more  than
$1,000  to  speak,  so  long  as  that  speech  is  construed  as
‘‘advocating  the election  or defeat  of’ a candidate.’

'  2 U.S.C.  § 434e)  imposes,  on  all  those  who  make  such  expendi-
tures  in  excess  of  5/00  per  year,  onerous  disclosure  and  reporting
requirements.  See  pp.  189-92,  infra.  The  Act  thus  captures  in-
dependent  political  speech  coming  and  going.

*  The  history  of  Section  60&e)  itself  suggests  its  unconstitu-
tionality.  It was  designed  to avoid  the  problems  raised  by the candidate-
certification  requirement  of  Title  | of  the  FECA  of  1971,  which  re-
quired  that  the  media,  before  accepting  advertisements  ‘‘on  behalf  of”
candidates,  obtain  certifications  from  candidates  that  the  adver-
tisements  purchased  would  not  cause  the candidate  to exceed  the media
spending  limit.  In  the  case  of advertisements  derogating  a  candidate,
the  media  had  to determine  a lack  of any  connection  with  the opposing

(continued)

108

The  limitation  on  independent  expenditures  applies  no
matter  how  critical  is  the  choice  between  candidates  or
issues.  Unforeseen  events  may  vastly  change  the  stakes  in
an  election  or  raise  new  and  critical  issues  near  the  end  of
the  campaign,  which  an  individual  or  group  may  wish  to
address.  Yet,  once  the  $1,000  is  expended,  any  further
speech  is a federal  crime.

The  limit  is  not  to  be  adjusted  to  changes  in  the
Consumer  Price  Index.’  Not  only  is the  limit  low,  and  get-
ting  lower  because  of inflation,  but  it takes  no  account  of
differences  in costs  between  areas,  media  outlets,  and  kinds
of activities  suited  for particular  issues,  id.  I, 86, pp.  SO-51;
App.  II-49.

The  Senate  Report  justifies  the  drastic  limiting  of
independent  expenditures  as  ‘‘the  best  compromise  of com-
peting  interests  in  free  speech  and  effective  campaign
regulation.”  S.  Rep.  No.  93-689,  93rd  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  19
(1974).  The  Report  also  states  that  “‘the  bill  [S.  3044]  pre-
serves  to  everyone  some  right  of political  expression...  ,”

?  (continued)

candidates.  When  the  New  York  Times  refused  to  accept  an  adver-
tisement  offered  by the  American  Civil  Liberties  Union  and  appellant
NYCLU,  which  listed  the  members  of  Congress  who  voted  against
President  Nixon's  busing  proposals  but  was  not  accompanied  by the re-
quired  certifications,  the  statute  was  challenged  and  held  unconstitu-
tional,  ACLU  v. Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041  (D.D.C.  1973),  vacated  as
moot  sub  nom.  Staats  v. ACLU,  43 U.S.L.W.  5043 (1975).

To  avoid  the  unconstitutional  prior  restraint  of  the  candidate-
certification  mechanism,  Congress  instead  enacted  a  flat  monetary
ceiling  on  the same  speech.

>  Since  expenditures  by  candidates  and  parties  are  so  adjusted,
the  discrepancy  between  what  campaigns  can  do  and  what  private  citi-
zens  can  do  in  the  way  of political  speech,  already  great,  will  become
steadily  greater.  No  reason  for  this  irrational  discrimination  has  to  our
knowledge  ever  been  suggested.

AG od I Ft eR CHD OR FX

109

id.  (emphasis  added).  See  also  H.R.  Rep.  No.  93-1239,  93rd
Cong.,  2d Sess.  6 (1974).

Preserving  “‘some  right  of political  expression”’  is not
sufficient  to  pass  constitutional  muster.  ‘Some  right  of
political  expression’’  would  be  preserved  if  Mills  v.
Alabama,  supra,  Miami  Herald  v.  Tornillo,  supra,  or  even
New  York  Times  v.  Sullivan,  supra,  had  gone  the other  way.
The  preservation  of ‘‘some  right  of political  expression”  is
simply  not  what  the  First  Amendment  is about.

For  Section  608(e)  to be sustained,  it would  have  to be
assumed  that  individual  citizens  and  independent  groups
have  nothing  to  add  to  the  public  debate.  Under  Section
608(e),  virtually  all  political  activity  must  now  be  absorbed
by,  and  emanate  from,  candidates’  campaigns  and  party
committees  and  the  institutional  press.  Issues  not  ade-
quately  debated  by the  campaigns,  and  not  deemed  signifi-
cant  by  the  institutional  press,  are  simply  not  to  be  dis-
cussed  except  in the  abstract.  This  is  no  trivial  matter,  for
candidates  frequently  avoid  delicate  issues,  and  the  only
way  such  issues  can  be  discussed  is through  independent
activities  by  persons  and  groups  hoping  to  influence  the
campaign  and  the election.

While  the  institutional  press  certainly  contributes  in
major,  essential  ways  to  public  debate,  and  its  power  to do
sO  must  remain  completely  untrammeled,  see,  e.g.,  Miami
Herald  y.  Tornillo;  Mills  vy.  Alabama,  supra,  the  First
Amendment’s  protection  of  freedom  of  expression  com-
prehends  much  more  than  the  rights  of  the  institutional
press.  Paid  advertisements,  like  the humblest  flyer or  leaflet
expressing  views  on  public  issues—“‘every  sort  of publica-
tion  which  affords  a  vehicle  of information  and  opinion,”
Lovell  v.  Griffin,  303  U.S.  444,  452  (1938)—are  part  of the
press  themselves.  Accord,  New  York  Times  vy.  Sullivan,
(1943);
supra;
Branzburg  v.  Hayes,  408  U.S.  663,  704  (1972);  Bigelow  v.

319  U.S.

Struthers,

Martin

141

y.

a  ng  en

110

Virginia,  supra.  Paying  for  the  publication  of  such  ma-
terials  is  the  principal  type  of  independent  expenditure
prohibited  by the  statutes.  The  FECA’s  restriction  on  inde-
pendent  expenditures  thus  undercuts  a  primary  forum  for
the  expression  of opinion  on  public  questions,  namely,  the
paid  ‘editorial’  advertisement  which  ‘“‘communicate(s]
information,  express[es]  opinion,  recite[s]  grievances,  pro-
test[s]  claimed  abuses...”  or  seeks  financial  support  for
important  social  movements,  New  York  Times  v.  Sullivan,
376  U.S.  at  266.  There,  the  Court  ruled  that  to  deny  such
advertisements  First  Amendment  protection  because  they
are  paid  for  ‘“‘would  discourage  newspapers  from  carrying
editorial  endorsements  of this  type,  and  so  might  shut  off
an  important  outlet  for  the  promulgation  of  information
and  ideas  by persons  who  do  not  themselves  have  access  to
publishing  facilities—who  wish  to exercise  their  freedom  of
speech  even  though  they are  not  members  of the  press,”’  id.

While  the legislation  exempts  most*  newspaper  stories,
commentaries  and  editorials  from  the  limitations  on  in-
dependent  expenditures  (18  U.S.C.  § 591(f)  (4) (A)),  this
explicit  protection  of the  organized  press  cannot  serve  as  a
kind  of constitutional  trade-off justifying  the suppression  of
independent  speech  and  press.  It  turns  the  First  Amend-
ment  on  its  head  to  suggest  that,  because  a  newspaper
publisher  remains  free  editorially  to  endorse  a  candidate
for  President,  it can  constitutionally  be  made  a  crime  for
anyone  else  to  take  out  an  advertisement  in the  same  news-

*  Even  newspapers  and  other media-exearabraced  by the statute  if
“owned  or  controlled  by  any  political  party,  political  committee,  or
candidate,”’  18  U.S.C.  § S91(fX4KA).  Matter  in  such  organs  deemed
favorable  to  a  candidate  is  thus  subject  to  the  expenditure  and
independent-expenditure  limitations  of  the  Act.  The  Framers,  who
were  intimately  familiar  with  press  organs  controlled  by  political
parties  or  candidates—a  connection  typical  in  their  time,  see  Mott,
American  Journalism  124  (1950),  and  frequent  throughout  our  his-
tory—would  have  been  astonished  at  the  notion  that  such  organs  did
not  enjoy  full  First  Amendment  protection.

8 OD Ls stn el i ee

paper  which  does  no  more  than  set  forth  the  text  of the
editorial.  Freedom  for news  stories  and  editorials  is a neces-
sary  prerequisite  for  a  free  society,  see,  e.g.,  Grosjean  v.
American  Press  Co.,  297  U.S.  233  (1936);  Estes  v.  Texas,
381  U.S.  532  (1965),  but  it  is  by no  means  the  only  pre-
requisite.  See Police  Department  of Chicago  v.  Mosley,  408
U.S.  92 (1972),  striking  down  on  equal-protection  grounds
a  city  ordinance  prohibiting  labor  picketing  near  schools
while  other  picketing  was  prohibited.

By the  same  token,  institutional  political  parties  play
a vital  role  in the  American  political  process,  shaping  poli-
cies  and  candidacies.  And  restrictions  on  such  activities
Kusper  v.  Pon-
would  also  be  unconstitutional.  See,  e.g.,
tikes,  supra;  Jennes  v.  Fortson,  supra.  But  protecting,  and
indeed  enhancing,’  the  power  of political  parties  cannot  be
a  constitutional  substitute  for  the  right  of  independent
political  speech  and  press.

The  freedom  to  make  independent  expenditures
relating  to elections  is particularly  important  for groups  not
committed  to  political  parties  or  political  candidates.  They
may  well  disfavor  the  positions  of all  the  candidates  but  on
balance  prefer one  to the others,  as  in Professor  Rosenthal’s
example  of  the  1968  campaign  button  “Reluctantly  for
Humphrey.”’  Rosenthal,  Federal  Regulation  of Campaign
Finance:  Some  Constitutional  Questions  35,  n.  75  (1971)
(hereinafter  Federal  Regulation).  Such  activities,  like  ap-
pellant  Mott’s  ‘negative  research”’  project  on  the sources  of
presidential  candidate  Muskie’s  campaign  contributions,
Findings  III,  Mott  14,  p.  29,  App.  Il-724,  may  well  force  a
candidate  ‘‘off  the  dime,”  thus  raising  the  level  of  cam-
paign  debate.

*  A group  of political  scientists  recently  concluded  that  one  of the
major  effects  of the  FECA
is to  magnify  the  power  of party  committees
and  structures  because  of their  ability  to  make  additional  expenditures
on  behalf  of  their  candidates.  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election
Reform,  Symposium,  supra,  passim.
