The  court  below  held  that  the  limitations  on  expendi-
tures  and  contributions  will  not  be  fully  effective  if indi-
viduals  and  groups  can  make  independent  expenditures
(op.  at  1530).  See  also  S.  Rep.  No.  93-689,  supra,  18.  That
logic,  however,  compels  a  complete  prohibition  of the  dis-
cussion  of  candidacies  outside  the  control  of  campaigns.
For  it can  hardly  be contested  that  demonstrations,  rallies,
newspaper  articles,  television  coverage,  and  the  like—let
alone  explicit  editorial  endorsements  of a  candidacy—also
tend  to  undercut  spending  and  contribution  limits  to  the
extent  they  benefit  one  candidate  over  another.  And  often
that  benefit  is far more  valuable  than  a $1,000  expenditure.

That  the  prohibition  of Section  60&e)  is “flatly  unconsti-
tutional,’  Alexander  M.  Bickel,  in  Rosenthal,  Federal  Reg-
ulation,  supra,  at  66,  seems  beyond  doubt.®  See  Hallihan,
“The  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendment  of  1974:
The  Constitutionality  of Limiting  Political  Advertising  by the
Non-Candidate,”  3  Fla.  St.  U.L.  Rev.  266,  282-89  (1975):
Fleishman,  “‘Freedom  of  Speech  and  Equality  of Political
Opportunity:  The  Constitutionality  of the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  of  1971,”’  51  No.  Car.  L.  Rev.  389,  473-77
(1973)  (hereinafter  ‘‘Fleishman,  The  FECA  of  1971’’);
Redish,  ‘““Campaign  Spending  Laws  and  the  First  Amend-
ment,”  46  N.Y.U.  L.  Rev.  900,  920-21  (1971);  Emerson,
639  (1970).
The  System  of  Freedom  of  Expression

Section  608(e)  cannot  be  saved  by  the  Court  of
Appeals’  view  that  it  does  not  forbid  “‘issue’’  activities
which  are  not  “relative  to”  particular  candidates  or  do  not
advocate  their  election  or  defeat  (op. at  1530-31).  Even  as  so

*  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  stated  that
.  limits  on  independent  citizens  and  groups  present  substantial
.  .
legal  questions  because  of  their  potential  curtailment  of  First
Amendment  rights.’’  ABA  Report  at  S. The  ACLU  similarly  opposes  a
limit  on  independent  expenditures.  ACLU  Report  at  12-13  (1975)

limited,  the  statue  is  flatly  unconstitutional  for  all  the
reasons  set  forth  above.

The  majority’s  construction,  moreover,  raises  a  host
of other  problems.  Speech  on  public  issues  can  rarely  go
forward  without  mentioning  the  conduct  of public  officials;
and  if those  officials  are  candidates  for  federal  office,  the
restrictions  of  the  section  potentially  arise.  As  one  com-
mentator  asked,  ‘‘[w]here  does  advocacy  of a  principle  end
and  support  of a candidate  begin?’’  Rosenthal,  ““Campaign
Financing  and  the  Constitution,”  9 Harv.  J. Legis.  359,  393
(1972) (hereinafter,  ““Campaign  Financing’’).’

In  its  effort  to  narrow  the  statute’s  reach,  the  Court
of  Appeals  added  additional  language  (‘‘clearly’’  ad-
vocating  the election  or defeat  of a candidate,  as  opposed  to
relating  “‘primarily’’  to  issues  of public  policy)  which  can
only  confuse  the  already  vague  and  sweeping  contours  of
the  section.’  In  such  circumstances,  the  vagueness  has
“some  of the  evils  of a  continuous  and  effective  restraint.”

”  George  Agree  posed  the  following  scenario  as  a graphic  example

of the  intractable  nature  of the problem:

“In  1968,  I got  some  money  together  for  a  group  of black
ieaders  who,  late  in  October,  addressed  a  message  in a full
page  ad  in the  New  York  Times,  to Gene  McCarthy.  And  it
said,  ‘Please  do  not  turn  your  back,  Senator  McCarthy.’
And  it went  on,  from  black  people,  with  a  long text  on  why
he should  come  out  in favor  of Humphrey.  Now,  to whom  do
you  attribute  that  expenditure.  These  were  black  people
who  felt  that  a  public  personality  was  not  properly
cognizant  of  the  issues  of  concern  to  them,  and  who  ad-
dressed  a  message  to  him  in  the  open.  What  do  you  do
about  that?’’  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Campaign  Re-
form,  Symposium,  supra,  at  100.

*  A  continuous  stream  of  questions  will  arise  as  to  whether  lan-
guage  such  as  ‘Jn  Your  Heart,  You  Know  He's  Right’  or  ‘Goldwater
for Halloween”  advocates  an  issue  or  a candidate.

114

115

United  States  v.  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612,  633 (1954) (Douglas,
J., dissenting).

As  a  consequence,  groups  such  as  the  NYCLU  or  the
Impeachment  Committee  will  still  have  to  guess  whether
their  issue  advertisements  and  statements  come  within
Section  608(e)  as  amended  by the  Court  of  Appeals,  and
will  still  have  to  draft  every  such  pronouncement  with  an
eye  toward  how  the  staff  and  members  of  the  FEC  will
interpret  it.  Independent  groups  and  individuals  will  thus
face  a  triumvirate  of  unacceptable  choices—drastically
tailoring  their  message  to  avoid  the  statute’s  reach,
publishing  their  message  at  the  risk  of criminal  penalties,
or  seeking  the  FEC’s  approval.

Moreover,  the  “‘loophole”’  is  not  closed  if  ““X  means
nuclear  holocaust;  Y  means  peace  and  prosperity”  is  not
prohibited.  All  that  will  happen  is that  some  lawyers  will
turn  from  drafting  bond  indentures  to  drafting  political
slogans.

In any  event,  present  case  law  gives special  recognition
to  the  preferred  position  of  speech  about  public  officials
and  political  candidates.  Indeed,  “...it  can  hardly  be
doubted  that  the  Constitutional  guarantee  has  its  fullest
and  most  urgent  application  precisely  to  the  conduct  of
campaigns  for  political  office,’”’  Monitor  Patriot  Co.  v.
Roy,  401  U.S.  265,  272  (1971).  Into  this  area  of uniquely
vital  discourse  comes  now  Section  608(e),  with  the  result
that  the  Constitution  protects  the  right  of  individuals  to
make  relatively  outrageous  statements  about  candidates
while  Section  608(e)  would  prohibit  them  from  making  very
many  statements  about  them  of any  kind.

The  difficulties  of enforcing  Section  608(e)  must  also
cause  serious  constitutional  questions.  Because  it  will
necessarily  entail  highly  individualized  inquiry  by the  FEC
and  the  courts  into  the  actions  of  individual  citizens,  the

and

discriminatory

of  particularized

likelihood
en-
forcement  is  considerable.  Close  scrutiny  of  those  who
make  independent  expenditures  will  be  necessary,  or  else
Section  608(e)  will  soon  become  ‘‘more  loophole  than  law.”’
Surely  a newspaper  knowingly  publishing  an  advertisement
which  violates  Section  608(e)  is  aiding  and  abetting  the
crime,  and  an  FEC  regulation  requiring  that  newspapers
send  to the  FEC  all  political  advertisements  with  the  name
of  the  purchaser  and  the  amount  paid  would  vastly  aid
enforcement  efforts.  Even  now,  2 U.S.C.  § 434(e),  compels
anyone  making  independent  expenditures  of  more  than
$100  to  file  detailed  reports  with  the  FEC,  a  chilling
invasion  of individual  privacy.

There  is  no  precedent  whatsoever  for  such  a  drastic
limitation  on  individual  political  activity.  In  Civil  Service
Commission  vy.  National  Association  of Letter  Carriers,  413
U.S.  548  (1973),  this  Court  upheld  certain  Hatch  Act
limitations  on  political  activities  by government  employees.
The  Court  took  care  to  indicate  that  the  limitations  could
be justified,  if at  all,  only  by the  nature  of public  employ-
ment:  “‘the  government  has  an  interest  in  regulating  the
conduct  of  its  employees  that  differ[s]  significantly  from
those  it  possesses  in  connection  with  regulation  of  the
speech  of the  citizenry  in  general,”  id.  at  564.  The  Hatch
Act,  moreover,  allows  the  option  of leaving  public  employ-
ment,  a sacrifice  to be sure,  but  considerably  less  than  that
needed  to  avoid  Section  608(e)—leaving  the  United  States.
The  ‘‘judgment  of history,”’  id.  at  567,  that  the  federal  civil
service  and  partisan  political  activity  should  not  be  inter-
twined  finds  no  parallel  here.  American  citizens  are  not
servants  of the state.

116

V.

The  FECA  Limits  on  the  Size of Individual
Contributions  Are  Unconstitutional.

A.  Wholesale  Limits  on  Individual  Contributions  Vio-

late  the First Amendment.

The  court  below,  in  two  paragraphs,  held  that
Congress  has  virtually  unlimited  power  to  limit  contribu-
tions  to candidates  (op. at  1528-29).  Such  a pervasive  ruling
disregards  the  impact  of  such  stringent  prohibitions  on
donors,  candidates,  supporters  of  candidates,  volunteers
and  on  political  communication  generally.  Because  the
Court  mistakenly  assumed  that  equality  in fact,  rather  than
merely  in  the  abstract,  would  be  furthered,  it endorsed  a
statutory  scheme  which  enhances  the  power  of organized
economic  interests  by suppressing  the  political  activity  of
others.

The  FECA  Amendments  (a) limit  contributions  by in-
dividuals  and  most  groups  to  candidates  for  federal  office
to  $1,000  per  election;  (b)  limit  to  $25,000  the  aggregate
contributions  to  candidates  by an  individual  per  election;
and  (c) limit  to  $5,000  contributions  by political  committees
which  have  been  registered  for  six  months  and  have  re-
ceived  contributions  from  over  fifty  persons.  18  U.S.C.  §
608(b).
travel
Incidental  expenses  of  volunteers,  e.g.,
expenses,  become  a  contribution  when  in  excess  of  $500.
Id..  § S91(e)  (S).  The  expenditure  of  personal  or  family
tunds  by candidates  is  limited  to  $25,000  in  House  races,
$35,000  in  Senate  races,  and  $50,000  in  the  case  of  the
Presidency.  /d.,  § 608(a).

Two  basic  arguments  were  employed  by appellees  and
the  court  below  to justify  these  proscriptions:  (1) the  need  to
avoid  the  reality  and  the  appearance  of corruption,  and  (2)
the  need  to  avoid  the  reality  and  appearance  of  “undue
influence,’  particularly  by  ‘‘special  interests,’’  with  the

117

wealthy  having  a  “stronger  political  voice  than  the
unwealthy”’  (op. at  1515-17).

In  either  case,  concern  over  “appearances’’  cannot
justify  such  legislation.  If  it  ‘“‘appears’’  that  corruption
abounds  or  that  the  wealthy  have  too  great  a  voice,  the
voters  have  an  easy  and  effective  remedy  so  long  as  there  is
proper  disclosure.  To  assert  that  a  blanket  proscription  is
necessary  to  avoid  an  ‘“‘appearance”’  is  to  say  that  voters
cannot  be  rational  and  cannot  react  to  appearances  as  well
as  the  sponsors  of this  legislation.  Civil  Service  Comm'n  vy.

The  reality  of corruption  is serious,  but  not  a compelling
justification  for the  particular  FECA  restrictions.  Appellees
and  the  court  below  rely heavily  on  instances  of illegal  cor-
porate  or  union  contributions,  Findings  I,  112-21,  pp.  99-
107,  App.  II-148-54,  evidenced  by  numerous  indictments
handed  down  under  prior  law  (18  U.S.C.  §610).  New
legislation
not
challenged  by  appellants,  e.g.,
18  U.S.C.  §615,  adds
nothing  to  previous  law  in  this  area  is  not  a  responsive
remedy.  The  failure  to  enforce  old  laws  is no  justification
for  regulating  unrelated  conduct  which  was  previously
lawful.  It would  have  to  be shown,  at  the  least,  that  the  old
statutes  had  proven  unenforceable  even  though  diligent  at-
tempts  at  enforcement  had  been  made,  and  that  nothing
short  of the  new  approach  would  be  effective.  None  of this
has  even  been  alleged,  let alone  demonstrated.

provisions

If  a small  minority  of political  contributions  are  given
to  secure  appointments  for  the  donors  or  some  other  quid
pro  quo,  that  cannot  serve  to  justify  prohibiting  all  large
contributions,  the  vast  majority  of which  are  given  not  for

118

119

any  such  purpose  but  to  further  the  expression  of political
views  which  the  candidate  and  donor  share.  Where  First
Amendment  rights  are  involved,  a  blunderbuss  approach
which  prohibits  mostly  innocent  speech  cannot  be  held  a
means  narrowly  and  precisely  directed  to the  governmental
interest  in the  small  minority  of contributions  that  are  not
innocent.  Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State,  supra;  United
States  v.  Robel,  supra.  Moreover,  there  are  available  a host
of narrower  regulations,  which  the  majority  below  did  not
address,  which  would  limit  or  eliminate  improper  contribu-
‘ions  while  leaving  genuine  political  advocacy  untouched.

To  the  extent  a  remedy  is called  for,  properly  drafted
disclosure  provisions  provide  it. Value  judgments  about  the
merits  of a cause  are  for the voters.  If they believe,  after  the
full  facts  are  made  available,  that  a  candidate  is  unduly
swayed  by  the  influence  of  “special  interests,’’  they  can
demonstrate  their  displeasure  effectively.  That  disclosure  is
effective  is  strongly  suggested  by  the  ultimate  result  of
Watergate.’

The  court  below  suggested  that  limits  on  contributions
can  be  upheld  in  the  name  of equality  of  political  voice
(op.  at  1517).  That  contention  is  at  odds  with  the  law  of
this  Court.  In  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  vy.  Tornillo,
supra,  this  Court  held  that  a  state  may  not  compel  a
newspaper  to  print  a  reply  to  an  editorial  attacking  a
political  candidate.  If such  a  legal  requirement  were  im-

'  As  the  ACLU  has  noted,  ‘“‘[b]ig  contributions  may  have  a  cor-
rupting  influence  on  one  campaign  and  an  indispensable  role  in getting
a dissenter  started  in another.  Limits  on  contributions  are  only defensi-
ble  if the  price  we  wish  to  pay for reducing  corruption  is favoritism  for
the  status  quo.  If, instead,  we  want  to  limit  corruption  while  placing  no
greater  obstacles  in the  way  of dissenters  than  exist  anyway,  the  proper
course  is to  require  disclosure  of potentially  corrupting  contributions.
That  way,  voters  can  refuse  to  elect  candidates  because  of their  obliga-
tions,  but  the  government  can’t  step  in  to  prevent  a  candidate  from
being  heard.’’  ACLU  Report  at  17.

posed,  the  Court  reasoned,  newspapers  would  be  reluc-
tant  to  write  controversial  editorials  or  print  other  con-
troversial  matter.  The  Court  explicitly  recognized  that  its
decision  permitted  papers  to  attack  candidates  who  have
little  recourse.  Similarly,  the  Court  struck  down  a  bar  on
last-minute  election  editorials  by newspapers,  even  though
an  adequate  response  by an  attacked  candidate  might  not
be possible.  Mills  v. Alabama,  supra.

In both  these  cases,  the  challenged  statute  sought  to
bring  about  evenhandedness  and  equality  in  political  de-
bate.  This  Court,  however,  struck  them  down  because  they
would  have  reduced  the  level  of  communication  by
deterring  the  publication  of  controversial  material,  even
though  it  was  assumed,  arguendo,  that  the  challenged
legislation  would  enhance  equality  of  “political  voice.”’
These  cases  stand  foursquare,  therefore,  for the proposition
that government  may  not  attempt  to bring about  equality  of
political  speech  by reducing  the  total  level  of political  com-
munication.

The  cases  relied  on  by the court  below,  invalidating  the
poll  tax,  Harper  vy.  Virginia  Board  of Elections,  383  U.S.
663  (1966),  large filing fees,  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134
(1972),  and  malapportionment,  Reynolds  v.  Sims,  377  U.S.
533  (1964),  are  perfectly  consistent  with  the  Tornillo  and
Mills  principle  and,  if anything,  demonstrate  the  invalidity
of the  FECA.

Each  case  cited  by the  Court  of Appeals  involved a dis-
advantage  or  disability  directly  imposed  by governmental
action  which  was  on  its  face  inconsistent  with  the  main-
tenance  of a  high  level  of political  communication.  Each,
moreover,  might  well  be thought  to favor  incumbent  office-
holders.  In  the  case  of reapportionment,  for  example,  the
steadfast  refusal  of  incumbents  to  correct  even  the  most

= et ee eT ee ee ee

<0 Owe

re

eee

120

121]

patent  inequities  was  well  known.’  Finally,  the  dis-
advantage  to  those  excluded—denial  of the  right  to  be  a
candidate,  to  vote,  or  to  cast  an  undiluted  vote—was
beyond  question.

By contrast,  private  campaign  financing  does  not  in-
volve  governmental  action;  rather  it  is  the  FECA  which
constitutes  the  act  of  government  that  intrudes  on  an
open  and  freely operating  political  process.  In addition,  the
FECA  effects  a deliberate  reduction  in the  level  of political
communication.  Further,  the  FECA  aids  incumbents
by,  inter  alia,  limiting  expenditures,  supra,  pp.  79-106,
limiting  contributions,  infra,  pp.  127-40,  vesting  the  power
of appointment  of two-thirds  of the  FEC  commissioners  in
Congress,  infra,  pp.  193-210,  and  subjecting  the  exercise  of
FEC  discretion  to  congressional  control,  infra,  pp.  208-10.
These  considerations  clearly  suggest  the  unconstitutionality
of the  FECA  under  Harper,  Bullock,  and Reynolds.

One  who  is prevented  from  voting  by a  poll tax  is dis-
advantaged  b:  government  in  a  way  that  is simply  not  the
case  where  the  non-contributor  is concerned.  Contributions
increase  the  level  of political  information  at  the  voters’  dis-
posal  by increasing  the  level  of communication.  The  First
Amendment  embodies  a  policy  which  places  a  high  value
on  political  debate,  and  contributions  plainly  work  to  that
end.

The  ‘inequality’  rationale  assumes  that  non-contribu-
tors  are  disadvantaged  by  the  campaign  contributions  of
others.  That  is not  the  case:  those  who  opposed  the  war  in
1968  were  in  no  conceivable  sense  disadvantaged  by  the
fact  that  Sen.  McCarthy  was  enabled  by  large,  private
contributions  to  run  a viable  campaign  in New  Hampshire.

?  Cf.  San  Antonio  Ind.  Schoo!  Dist.  v.  Rodriguez,  411  U.S.  1, 17-
18  (1973)  (Bullock  v.  Carter  described  as  involving  a state  filing  fee
which  “effectively  barred  all  potential  candidates  who  were  unable  to
pay  the  required  fee’).

*

ee ee ee a ar re

Non-contributors  who shared  Sen.  McGovern’s  views  about
the  redistribution  of income  were  not  disadvantaged  by the
$636,882  Sen.  McGovern  received  by the  time  of the  1972
New  Hampshire  primary  but  which  the  FECA  would
outlaw.  Those  who  favored  the cause  of civil  rights were  not
disadvantaged  by President  Johnson’s  ability  to raise  funds
in  1964.  Large contributors  are  thus  often  functional  repre-
sentatives  of other  people  who  are  not  themselves  contribu-
tors.’

Contributions  run  to  all  kinds  of candidates  and  all
kinds  of  causes.  The  record  demonstrates  beyond  any
possible  doubt  that  campagn  money  knows  no  ideological
limits.  That  is patently  true  of presidential  races.  And  cer-
tainly  the  notion  that  private  campaign  financing  runs  in
large  amounts  only to those  who  champion  the  interests  of
the  wealthy  is  a myth.  Among  the  candidates  in  the  most
expensive  twenty-five  House  races  in 1972  were:

Equality  seems  furthered  by  limits  on  contributions
only  if the  rhetoric  employed  focuses  solely  on  the  con-
tributor  and  does  not  afford  a true  perspective  of the  role  of

*  As  Prof.  Rosenthal  has  succintly  stated,  ‘*.  .

.  inequality  in  the
power  to  persuade  voters  (whether  for  financial  or  other  reasons)  is not
the  same  as  inequality  in  the  right  to  vote.’  Rosenthal,  “Campaign
Financing,”  supra,  at  376.

122

large  contributions.  Large  contributions  must  also  be
viewed  in  terms  of  the  recipients  and  supporters  of  the
recipients,  and  they are  often  society’s  underdogs.*

“Fat  cats’’  like  Robert  Morris  and  Haym  Saloman
financed  the  American  revolution,  Steeg,  Robert  Morris:
Revolutionary  Financier  169  (1954);  Barnard,  This  Great
Triumvirate  of Patriots  43,  67  (1971).  In  the  next  century
wealthy  northerners  like  Arthur  and  Lewis  Tappan,  Francis
Jackson,  and  Richard  Fletcher  sustained  the  abolitionist
movement  in its early  years.  Lader,  The  Bold  Brahmins  71-
72, 82 (1961).

After  the  Civil  War,  organizations  like  the  NAACP
relied  on  the  contributions  of Oswald  Garrison  Villard  and
Julius  Rosenwald  (who  spent  $60-$70  million  in  helping
minorities)  and  others.  Eventually  the  NAACP  expanded  its
financial  base,  but  in  1914,  for  example,  “more  than  half
the  income  of  the  NAACP  came  from  eighteen  indi-
viduals.”  Kellogg,  NAACP,  Volume  I:  1909-1920  106
(1967).  The  Urban  League’s  history  is similar.  In  Chicago,
as  elsewhere,  “during  the  opening  period  several  large
pledges  were  obtained  to  provide  the  initial  impetus  for the
campaign.”’  Strickland,  History  of  the  Chicugo  Urban
League  97 (1966).

*  The  simple  way  to avoid  disadvantaging  low-income  groups  is to
encourage  the  dissemination  of more  political  information,  not  less.
When  less  information  is  available  to  voters,  low-income  citizens  are
the chief victims.  As one  scholar  explained:

“.  ..  the  cost  of information  is harder  for  low-income  citizens
to  bear;  hence  more  of  them  are  likely  to  be  uncertain
because  they  lack  information.  Since  uncertainty  reduces
the  returns  from  voting,  a  lower  proportion  of low-income
groups  would  vote  even  if voting  costs  were  equally  difficult
for  everyone  to  bear.’  Downs,  An  Economic  Theory  of
Democracy  273 (1957).

0 i

123

Other  examples  abound.  Albert  DeSilver  sustained  the
ACLU  for  many  years;  the  early  twentieth-century  peace
movement  depended  on  Andrew  Carnegie  and  Edward
Ginn,  and  Bernard  Baruch  sponsored  slum  clearance  and
hospitals.  ACLU  1924  Annual  Report;  ACLU  1928-1929
Annual  Report;  Coit,  Mr.  Baruch  197,  454  (1957).  Today,
huge foundations  continue  as  patrons  of unpopular  causes.
The  messages  of such  causes  began  as  unpopular  whispers
shunned  by the  press  and  misunderstood  by most  people.
No  legislature  would  have  ever  voted  them  funds.  In  order
to  join  the  national  debate,  all  of  these  reform  groups
looked  successfully  to wealthy  patrons.

The  history  of  certain  of  the  appellee  organizations
also  shows  the  need  for initial  large contributions.  Appellee
Center  for  Public  Financing  of Elections  received  in  1973,
for  example,  $68,751  from  23 contributors  (an  average  per
contributor  of almost  $3,000).  Findings  I, 69,  p.  33;  App.
A  member  of  appellee  Common  Cause  Board  of
II-32.
Directors  testified  that  large  contributions  were  crucial  to
the  organization’s  creation.  Palevsky  dep.  tr.  76-77.  Com-
mon  Cause  has  received  approximately  $70,000  from  the
Rockefeller  family  alone,  including  $55,000  from  John  D.
Rockefeller  III  and  $1,500  from  Nelson  Rockefeller,  id.  87,
p. 51; App.  II-49.

Much  of this  history  is misunderstood  because  it is as-
sumed  that  each  dollar  is of the  same  value  to every  move-
ment  and  that  any  difference  in  fund-raising  ability  is  an
intolerable  inequity.  That  is not  the  case.  The  financial  re-
sources  available  to  NAACP  and  the  National  Association
of Manufacturers  (NAM)  have  been  disparate  throughout
this  century,  but  one  has  brought  about a social  and  legal
revolution  while  the  other  has  been  fighting  a  largely
rearguard  action.  Had  there  been  no  private  financing,  the
positions  of the NAM  would  have  nevertheless  received  sub-

124

125

stantial  exposure  and  support,  because  it  represents  the
status  quo,  while  those  of the  NAACP  wou.  i have  survived,
if at  all, as  a largely  unheard,  underground  movement.  The
value  of  each  dollar  to  the  well-publicized  status  quo
position  is always  less  than  to a nascent  movement  pressing
society to move  in a new  direction.

What  is true  of organizations  is also  true  of political
candidates.  The  challenger,  the  underdog,  and  the  candi-
date  espousing  change  need  large  contributions  to  get
started  if a  serious  challenge  is  to  be  made.  Thus,  in  the
following  section,  pp.  127-38,  infra,  we  will  demonstrate  how
candidates  espousing  new  ideas  or  challenging  incumbents
needed  large  amounts  of money  initially  to  wage  a  viable
campaign.

Finally,  equality  of  political  communication  is  not  a
goal  which  law  can  pursue  without  great  peril.  Short  of
silence—the  only  state  in  which  communication  is in  fact
equal—legal  restraints  are  likely  to  bring  about  more
rather  than  less  inequality.  The  distribution  of political  re-
sources  is in  no  way  “equal”  and  is simply  not  subject  to
“equalizing”  without  drastically  reducing  the  amount  of
political  speech.  As  David  Broder  has  pointed  out,  “‘[I]egis-
lation  that  eliminates  the  dollar  influence  on  politics
automatically  enhances  the  influence  of  those  who  can
provide  manpower  or  publicity  for  the  campaign.”’  120
Cong.  Rec.  H 7813  (daily ed.  August  7, 1974).

The  statute  distinguishes  between  the  volunteering  of
personal  services  and  the  contribution  of  money  used  to
purchase  those  services,  18  U.S.C.  § 591(e).  It  thus  dis-
tinguishes  between  the  lawyer  who  contributes  his  services
to  a  candidate  to  aid  him  in  complying  with  the  complexi-
ties  of the  FECA,  and  his contribution  of money  because  he
has  fallen  ill and  is temporarily  disabled.  As with  economic
resources,  political  resources  are  often  fungible.  To  the
participants  (whether  they  be  donors,  recipients  or  sup-

porters),  the  contribution  of services  and  the  contribution
of money  are  functionally  similar  activities.

similarity

This  functional

not  only  permits  the
extension  of  limitations  to  other  activities  but  seems
actually  to mandate  it, as  the  FECA  provisions  demonstrate
in  the  limitation  on  independent  expenditures  and  dis-
closure  of those  that  are  made  if greater  than  $100,  and  in
the  limitation  of  incidental  expenses  of volunteers.  Large
contributors  tend  to  be  activists  in  many  ways,  most  of
which  involve  substantial  expenditures.  Thus  appellant
Mott,  a  heavy  financial  contributor  to  various  campaigns,
has  also  contributed  to  a  number  of  other  organizations
and  causes  and  views  all  these  contributions  as  essentially
similar.  Findings  III,  Mott  1-21,  pp.  26-32;  App.  II-721-26.
Another  example  is  Martin  Peretz,  a  heavy  contributor  to
congressional  campaigns,  id.  II,  A 117,  p.  139-41,  App.  II-
478-81,  who  has  also  funded  the  SDS  and  Ramparts
magazine  and  has  bought  The  New  Republic,  id.  1, 138,  p.
129;  App.  11-190.  All  such  activities  further  the  political
goals  and  purposes  of the  individuals  involved,  as  do  cam-
paign  contributions.

There  are  at  least  thirty  ways  of  participating  in  the
political  process,  /d.  I, 5, p.  2-4,  App.  II-68,  only  three  of
which  (v, x  and  y) are  regulated  by the  challenged  statutes,
even  though  many  others  are  of comparable  or  greater  ef-
fectiveness  than  campaign  contributions  and  involve  the
use  of money  for  political  purposes.  Some  people  have  con-
free  time;  the  media  involve  concentrated
siderable
economic  unity and  wield  enormous  political  influence;  and
incumbent  officeholders  possess  numerous  resources  which
benefit  them  politically.  None  of these  are  touched  by the
statutes.

Limiting  financial  contributions  to  candidates  only
magnifies  all  these  other  inequalities.  Indeed,  the  effect  of
the  $1,000  limitation  may  well  discriminate  in  favor  of the
very  wealthy.  While  the  moderately  wealthy  must  forgo  a

126

127

$3,000  contribution,  the  very  wealthy  form  study  groups,
own  newspapers  and  magazines,  or  engage  in  extensive
political  activity  because  of their  resources  and  free time.

The  rhetoric  of  equality  of  speech,  moreover,  is
rhetoric  which  must  in  the  end  sweep  away  free  political
communication.  It is a  short  step  to  apply  that  rhetoric  to
the  media,  especially  to  such  large  economic  entities  as  the
television  networks,  Washington  Post,  the  New  York
Times,  and  the  Chicago  Tribune.  Findings  I, 60-64,  pp.  30-
32;  App.  II-30-32.  One  already  sees  such  stirrings.  Phillips,
Mediacracy  24 et seq.  (1975).  Indeed,  logic itself—not  to mem-
tion  equality—suggests  it  is  totally  irrational  to  permit
William  Loeb,  publisher  of the  Manchester  Union  Leader,
broad  freedom  to  employ  economic  resources  to  attack  a
candidate  while  restricting  the  right  of  that  candidate’s
supporters  to  employ  economic  resources  in  defense.  The
concern  of the  court  below  that  the  “wealthy  few’’  not  have
‘‘a  stronger  political  voice  than  the  unwealthy  many...”
(op.  at  1517)  is  a  concern  which  strikes  as  directly  at  the
press  and  electronic  media  as  at campaign  contributions.

Limits  on  individual  contributions  will,  moreover,
induce  potential  political  contributors  to  donate  funds
instead  to  ‘‘issue’’  groups.  That,  in turn,  may  create  addi-
tional  pressure  for  Congress  and  the  courts  to  see  that
‘issue’  organizations  also  are  regulated  in  the  way  that
political  campaigns  are  —  a  clearly  unconstitutional  ap-
proach,  op.  at  1549-60,  ACLU  vy.  Jennings;  United  States
v.  National  Committee  for Impeachment,  supra.  The  Con-
ference  Report’s  suggestion  that  disclosure  be  extended  to
“issue”  groups  because  “‘they  use  their  resources  for
political  purposes”’  is a  chilling  reminder  of just  how  slip-
pery  the  slope  is.  S.  Rep.  No.  93-1237,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.
83 (1974).

Finally,  the  eyuality  problem  is  democratically  re-
solved  by disc!»s  .ce  of large  contributions  with  a  potential
for  influenc..  If a  candidate  is  taking  “‘too  much”  money

from  large contributors,  the citizens  have  swift  and  effective
recourse.  And  that,  rather  than  the  restriction  of political
communication,  is the remedy  that  should  be relied  upon  in
a democratic  nation  which  trusts  its voters.

B.  The  FECA  Limits  on  Contributions  Discriminate
Against  Challengers  to  Incumbent  Officeholders  and
Against  Minor  Parties  and  Independent  Candidates  in
Violation  of the First  and Fifth

Amendments.

‘[Llower  limits  on  campaign  contributions,  by
themselves,  would  serve  to  increase  the  ad-
vantages  incumbents  presently  have  in  fund
raising.”

This  bald,  unequivocal  statement  is  from  the  Senate
Report  accompanying  the  FECA.  S.  Rep.  No.  93-689,
supra,
10  (emphasis  in  original).  Because  the  original
Senate  version  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  S.  3044,  pro-
vided  public  funding  for  all  federal  (not  just  presidential)
elections,  the  Senate  Committee  on  Rules  and  Administra-
tion  could  be  candid  about  the  impact  of the  FECA  limits
As  passed,  the  FECA
on  the  size  of  contributions.
Amendments  contained  no  public  financing  for  congres-
sional  elections.  The  Committee  statement  thus  stands  as  a
flat  admission  that  the  contribution  limits  discriminate  in
favor  of incumbents.  Rep.  Anderson,  a  leading  sponsor  of
the  FECA  Amendments,  stated  a  similar  proposition  in
the  House  debate  on  H.R.  16090,  120  Cong.  Rec.  H.  7924
(daily ed.  Aug.  8, 1974).

Why  the  Report’s  frank  admission  is  demonstrably
true  is illustrated  by Congress’  refusal  to provide  services  to
challengers.  For  example,  it would  be  a  simple  matter  to
allow  use  of the  franking  privilege  to  any  candidate  on  the
ballot.  This  was  rejected  in the  Senate  on  the  grounds  that
it was  far too  complicated,  a  pitifully  inadequate  excuse.  S.
Rep.  No.  93-689,  supra,  at S.

_—

128

129

We  have  already  shown  (pp.  86-104,  supra)  the  many
advantages  incumbents  have  in election  campaigns  and  the
extent  to which  these  advantages  are  enhanced  by a limit  on
expenditures.  The  limits  on  the  size  of  contributions
operate  to  the  same  end.  In  effect,  incumbents  can  vote
themselves  the  equivalent  of large contributions  in the  form
of more  staff,  offices,  franked  mail,  etc.,  while  challengers
are  severely  hampered  in  the  fund  raising  which  is  neces-
sary  to  purchase  those  items.  The  statute  in  effect  limits,
therefore,  some  contributions  to  incumbents  and  all  con-
tributions  to challengers.

Appellees  contended  below  that,  because  the  per-
centage  of money  received  in  contributions  over  $1,000  is
greater  for  incumbents  than  for  challengers  when  the
figures  for  all  congressional  elections  are  aggregated,  the
statute  will  not  have  any  harmful  effect  on  challengers.
Findings  II,  A 55,  p.  103,  App.  II-452;  compare  id.,  A  115,
p.  133  at  p.  135;  App.  Il-473,  475.  By its  own  terms,  this
argument  is true  only if the  marginal  value  to the candidate
of a  dollar  contributed  to  a  challenger  and  of a  dollar  con-
tributed  to  an  incumbent  is the  same.  Since  the  marginal
utility  of  money  is  greater  to  challengers  than  to  in-
cumbents,  appellees’  figures  themselves  demonstrate  that
the  limitation  on  individual  contributions  discriminates
against  challengers.  Cole  dep.  tr.  376-79.  Because  limits  on
contributions  reduce  the  amount  of  campaign  money
available  to  challengers,  they  have  precisely  the  same  effect
as  limits  on  expenditures.

Even  on  an  a  priori  basis,  appellees’  theory  is  er-
roneous.  The  limits  on  contributions  will  necessitate  new
fund-raising  methods,  principally  greater  reliance  on  di-
rect-mail  techniques.  The  agreed  facts  establish,  moreover,
that  incumbents  have  advantages  over  challengers  in
raising  small  contributions.  They  are  well  known;  they  are
experienced  in  dealing  with  their  constituents  through  the
mail;  they  have  mailing  lists  (both  from  use  of the  frank

and  from  previous  fund-raising  efforts)  which  may  be  used
with  some  assurance  of success  and  which  require  less  in
reporting  costs  because  the  required  information  is on  the
list;  and  they  can  utilize  the  mailing-list  facilities  of  the
Senate  computer.  Findings  I,  24  and  37,  pp.  15-18,  21;
App.  II-16-20,  23-24.  Direct  mail  is also  a time-consuming
process  which  incumbents  can  generally  begin  earlier,
using  their  offices  as  a  base,  while  challengers  are  still
seeking  nomination.  /d.  I, 24,  p.  16,  App.  II-16-20;  Keene
dep. tr.  14-15.

Appellees’  evidence,  moreover,  is totally  misleading,  in
that  it  aggregates  all  congressional  elections  without  any
regard  to  the  enormous  variances  among  such  elections,
and  is  thus  virtually  useless  in  analyzing  particular  cam-
paigns.  Appellants  have  compiled  figures,  Findings  II,
C 9, p. 257,  App.  II-679,  on  the amount  of “‘overage’”’®  in House
races  in  which  challengers  to  incumbents  made  a  serious
run  and  received  45 percent  of the vote  or  more.

When  closely  contested  campaigns  are  considered,  it is
clear  that  the  viability’  of many  challengers  is  derived  in
large  part  from  contributions  which  the  FECA  would
prohibit.  These  figures,  derived  from  Common  Cause’s
statistics,  Findings  II,  C  6,  p.  249,  App.  II-665,  demon-
strate  beyond  any  question  the  role  large  contributions
can  play  in  making  challengers  viable.  In  such  races,
incumbents  start  with  cash  on  hand  while  challengers  for
the  most  part  do  not.  Challengers  also  rely  much  more
heavily  than  do  incumbents  on  loans,  which  are  not
included  in the  aggregate  figures.  Compare  id.  II, A 52, 60,

*  “Overage’’  is  defined  as  amounts  received  in  contributions,
including  loans,  in  excess  of  $1,000  per  individual  and  in  excess  of
$5,000  per political  committee.

*  A  particular  campaign  is considered  politically  ‘viable’  if it is

able  to mount  an  effective  (whether  or  not  successful)  challenge.

130

131

pp.  101-05,  App.  I1-451-54,  with  II A 115,  pp.  133-39;  App.
I1-473-78.  The  amount  of overage  raised  by challengers  in
these  races  is  also  greater.  Finally,  for  those  challengers
who  had  total  overage  exeeding  $3,000,  the  large  con-
tributions  or  loans  were  generally  received  earlier  in  the
campaign  than  the  overage  received  by the  incumbents  they
faced,  indicating  that  such  early,  large  contributions  per-
formed  the  important  function  of “‘seed  money.”’

‘Seed  money”  or  ‘“‘up-front  money”  was  defined  by
.  the  critical  mass  of money
the  Court  of Appeals  to  be  “‘.  .
to  be  applied  at  the  outset  of  a  campaign  to  establish  a
‘viable’  candidacy.  For  a  campaign  that  intends  to  rely
upon  small  contributions  solicited  by  direct  mail,  for  ex-
ample,  the  seed  money  would  be the  cost  of an  initial  direct
mailing  and  associated  expenses”’  (op.  at  1532  n.85).  Ap-
pellees  deny  the  validity  of this  definition  and  claim  there  is
no  evidence  that  seed  money  in large  contributions  is an  es-
sential  component  of a viable  campaign.  They  are  wrong.

In  1974,  incumbent  Senators  seeking  re-election  had
$1,402,083  in  cash  on  hand  on  September  1,  1973.  Chal-
..  lengers  had  $21,676,  id.  II,  C  15,  p.  268;  App.  II-697.  In
Senate  races  in  which  challengers  received  at  least  45  per-
cent  of the  vote,  challengers  had  received  53.8  percent  of
their  overage  by October  1, 1974,  while  the  incumbents  had
received  only  31.8  percent  of theirs  by the  same  date.  /d.  II,
C6,  p. 249;  App.  II-66S.

In  the  1974  House  races  in  which  challengers  received
45  percent  of  the  vote  or  more,  incumbents  had  $398,097
cash  on  hand  while  challengers  had  $68,051  as  of September
1,  1973,  id.  II, C 10,  p.  267;  App.  II-697.  If one  excludes  Rep.
Price  of  Texas,  an  incumbent  who  borrowed  and  repaid
$74,000,  incumbents  reported  $66,913  in  repaid  loans,
while  challengers  had  $136,936.  Incumbents  had  $110,798
in  overage  while  challengers  had  $181,253.  In  the  forty
races  in  which  challengers  defeated  an  incumbent,  incum-

:
;

bents  had  $72,723  in  overage,  while  challengers  had
$110,894.  Repaid  loans  are  not  included  in  the  overage
figures.  Jd.  II,  C 10-13,  p. 267;  App.  II-697.

In the  nineteen  1974  House  races  in which  a challenger
received  over  45  percent  of the  vote  and  had  over  $3,000  in
overage,  56.0  percent  of  that  overage  was  on  hand  by
October  1,  1974,  and  thus  represented  vitally  necessary
seed  money,  while  incumbents  had  received  only  35.5  per-
cent  by  that  date.  In  twelve  of  the  nineteen  contests  in
which  the  challengers  won,  70.8  percent  of the  challengers’
overage  was  received  by October  1, while  incumbents  had
only 44.7  percent.  Jd.  II, C 6, p. 249;  App.  II-665.

Early  money  in  contributions

exceeding  $1,000,
therefore,  is  important  to  challengers  in  establishing  via-
bility.  Indeed,  it  is  well  known  in  the  political  world  that
seed  money  plays  a  terribly  important  role  for  those  chal-
lenging  incumbents.  Findings  III,  Buckley  11-23,  pp.  3-7,
App.  II-701-02;  Mahoney  S5, p.  12,  App.  11-709;  McCarthy
4-8,  pp.  18-19,  App.  II-714-15;  Wertheimer  2-5,  pp.  45-46,
App.  II-737.  Incumbents  have  high  name  recognition  and
other  political  advantages,  id.  I, 31-32,  pp.  20-21;  App.  II-
22-23.  Additionally,  incumbents  have  an  entire  term  in of-
fice  to  raise  money  for  the  next  campaign,  and  many  of
them  are  thus  able  to  begin  it with  a  substantial  amount  of
cash  on  hand  as  well  as  the  perquisites  of office.

The  court  below  recognized  the  need  for  seed  money
and,  indeed,  relied  on  this  need  to  uphold  the  higher  ceil-
ings on  personal  and  family expenditures.

Record

im-
evidence  demonstrating  the
portance  of ‘‘seed  money”’  to  challengers’  campaigns  show
the following:

critical

132

133

Julian
Bond,
Presidential  Nomination

1975  Campaign  for  the  Democratic

Bond  is  the  first  candidate  for  a  party’s  1976  presi-
dential  nomination  to  abandon  his  campaign.  He  did  so
explicitly  because  he was  unable  to raise  seed  money:

“One  candidate  who  thinks  his  inability  to
raise  seed  money  is hurting  his  1976  campaign  is
Julian  Bond,  the  Georgia  state  senator  who  has
said  he  wants  to  run  in selected  black  districts  to
collect  delegates  for  bargaining  purposes  at  the
Democratic  convention.  Atlanta  City  Council
member  James  G.  Bond,  the  candidate’s  brother
and  campaign  manager,  said  in an  interview  that
the  limit  on  individual  contributions  ‘is a disaster
as  far as  our  campaign  is concerned.  As a result  of
this  law,  Julian’s  probably  not  going  to  be able  to
run.

James  Bond  said  that  Morris  Dees,  the
Democratic  direct  mail  specialist  who  raised
funds  for  George  McGovern  in  1972,  ‘told  Julian
he could  raise  several  hundred  thousand  dollars  if
he could  raise  $20,000  to start.  Several  people  said
they  were  willing  to  lend  it  to  him,  but  that’s
illegal.’  He  said  that  the  requirement  that  candi-
dates  get  their  seed  money  in small  contributions
works  hardest  against  people  who  represent  poor
constituencies.  ‘Julian’s  base  is  the  black  com-
munity,  and  it’s  not  a  rich  community,’  Bond
said.’  Malbin,  “New  Campaign  Finance  Law
Faces  Legal,  Political  Tests,”  National  Journal
Reports  (July  12,  1975),  1011,  at  1018.

Thomas  Bradley,  1973 Los

Angeles  Mayoralty  Campaign

Bradley  was  victorious  in the  1973  Los  Angeles  election
for  mayor.  Max  Palevsky  stated  under  oath  in  the  present
case:

“In  1973,  I ran  the  campaign  to  elect  Tom
Bradley  Mayor  of Los  Angeles.  The  records  of the
Bradley  campaign  show  that  of the  approximately
$1.5  million  raised,  virtually  all  of  the  critical
early  money  came  from  large  contributions  in
excess  of  $5,000.  In  the  Bradley  campaign  the
central  issue,  in my  opinion,  was  electing  a  black
in a city which  is predominantly  white.  In order  to
deal  with  the  fears  and  apprehension  of some  of
the  population,  it  was  imperative  that  Mr.
Bradley  be seen  as  often  as  possible  in the  media.
Mr.  Bradley’s  constituency  was  not  in  an  eco-
nomic  position  to  provide  the  funds  needed  for
this  effort.  The  funds  could  only  be  raised,  in my
opinion,  from  wealthy  contributors.  Of the  initial
half  million  dollars  raised,  over  85%  came  in
contributions  in  excess  of $1,000.  Without  these
contributions,  in  my  opinion,  Mr.  Bradley  could
never  have  won  either  the  primary  or  the  run-off
election  for mayor.”’  Palvesky  aff.  9 7.

James  L. Buckley,  1970 Senate  Campaign

Buckley  defeated  an  incumbent  Senator,  but  he would
not  even  have  run  had  he  not  had  assurances  that  at  least
$100,000  in  seed  money  could  be  raised  quickly.  Findings
III,  Buckley  11-13,  p. 3, App.  I1-701-02.  The  Buckley  cam-
paign  began  with  a  $50,000  loan  from  a  personal  friend.
Id.,  Buckley  14, p. 4, App.  II-702.

The  Buckley  race  is typical  of the  crucial  importance
of early  large contributions,  and  the  uses  to  which  they  are
put.  Sen.  Buckley  required  a campaign  headquarters  and  a
telephone  bank  (for  contacting  voters).  Because  landlords
and  utilities  in  New  York  City  require  advance  deposits,
Sen.  Buckley  could  not  even  begin  his  campaign  without
substantial  sums  of  money,  Findings  III,  Buckley  20-23,
“pp.  6-7;  App.  II-704-0S.  The  District  Court  found  that  “ijn

134

135

fact,  the  agents  for the  building  in which  Senator  Buckley's
campaign  sought  and  finally  rented  space  would  not  even
talk  to  campaign  officials  unless  they  appeared  with  a cer-
tified  check  for  the  entire  amount  they  desired,”  id.,
Buckley  22,  p.  6;  App.  II-704.  The  amounts  involved  were
$18,000  for  rent  and  security  deposit  for  the  headquarters
and  $18,000  in deposits  for the telephone  bank,  id.

Much  of the  early  $50,000  loan  went  into  a direct-mail
campaign  that  eventually  raised  $650,000.  More  than
40,000  persons  contributed  to  the  campaign,  and  35  per-
cent  of those  gave  less  than  $20  apiece,  id.,  Buckley  17-18,
p.  5;  App.  II-703.  Without  the  early  large  contributions,
however,  Sen.  Buckley  believes  that  his  campaign  would
have  been  doomed  from  the  outset,  much  as  his  un-
successful  1968  campaign  was,  id.,  Buckley  15-16,  19,  pp.
4-5;  App.  II-702-04.

Ramsey  Clark,  1974  Senate  Campaign

Clark,  a well-known  former  Attorney  General,  publicly
associated  with  a  number  of  causes  with  substantial
constituencies  in  New  York  State,  relied  entirely  on
contributions  of  $100  or  less.  He  was  able  to  raise  ‘‘seed
money”’  of between  $12,000  to $15,000  frorn  friends  and  ac-
quaintances  to  launch  his  appeals  for  small  contributions.
His  response  on  direct  mail  was  between  S and 7 percent,  or
considerably  better  than  the  normal  2 or  3 percent.  Never-
theless,  because  he  relied  on  small  contributions  he  could
not  get  money  when  it  was  needed,  and  could  not  even
make  a  rough  estimate  as  to  how  much  would  be  available
for  the  general-election  budget  until  a  month  after  the
primary.  The  cost  of raising  money  thus  greatly  reduced  the
amount  that  he  had  available  for  campaign  purposes,
Findings  I, 182-90,  pp.  180-83;  App.  II-260-62.

Indeed,  Clark  stated  that  Sen.  Javits  appeared  to  out-
spend  him  by a ratio  of 3 to  2 when,  in fact,  because  of the
extra  cost  of raising  small  contributions,  he  was  really  out-

spent  2 to  1. Clark  received  only  38 percent  of the  vote.  Jd.
I, 185,  191,  pp.  181-83;  App.  II-261-62.

Walter D.

Huddleston,

1972 Senate  Campaign

The  Huddleston  campaign  for  Senator  from  Kentucky
began  with  a  $50,000  loan  from  the  state  party  and  a
$200,000  bank  loan.  /d.  II, B 17, p.  196;  App.  II-S76.  Hud-
dleston  was  elected,  in a race  between  two  nonincumbents.

Eugene  J.  McCarthy,  1968  New  Hampshire  Presidential-
Primary  Campaign

Sen.  McCarthy  did  unexpectedly  well  in  the  New
Hampshire  primary,  and  his  performance  was  a major  fac-
tor  in  causing  an  incumbent  President  not  to  run  for  re-
election.  Sen.  McCarthy  received  at least  $71,000  (when  ad-
justed  for  inflation  since  1968,  the  total  is over  $90,000)  in
contributions  which  would  have  been  illegal  under  the
FECA,  Findings  III,  McCarthy  4, p. 18;  App.  II-714;  id.  I,
181,  p.  179;  App.  260.

Max  Palevsky’s
Manckiewicz's
$150,000  salary).  Between  February  1 and  March  10,  1972,

McGovern  would  have  lost  another  $152,000.  Indeed,  from

direct-mail  techniques  require  that  much  of the  return  in

these  large  contributions  were  critical  to  McGovern’s  early

Charles  McC.  Mathias,  1974  Senate  Campaign

Sen.  Mathias,  like  Ramsey  Clark,  limited  contribu-
tions  to  his  campaign  to  $100  or  less.  However,  he  had
$50,000  already  on  hand  when  he  imposed  this  limitation
ana  had  already  spent  money  on  unspecified  pre-campaign
obligations.  He  was,  moreover,  a  well-known  and  popular
Senator  with  a  high-name  recognition  and  the  other  per-
quisites  of  incumbency,  Findings  I,  172-79,  pp.  175-79;
App.  II-257-60.

Edmund  S. Muskie,  1972 Presidential  Campaign

Sen.  Muskie’s  unsuccessful  campaign  for  the  Demo-
cratic  presidential  nomination  began  with  contributions
from  40  contributors  totaling  approximately  $600,000.  33
Cong.  Q.  Wkly.  Rep.  1239 June  14,  1975).

What  experience  demonstrates,  common  sense  con-
firms.  Direct-mail  campaigns  are  necessarily  very  ex-
pensive,  and  cannot  begin  without  substantial  seed  money.
One  contact  by a  candidate  with  one  voter  using  the  mails
costs  20 cents,  Findings  II, B 40, p. 200,  App.  II-S80,  and  the
initial  returns  from  direct-mail  fund  raising  are  often  small.
Indeed,  the  total  cost  of  raising  campaign  money  in  this
fashion  is  stipulated  to  be  around  40  percent  of the  total
eventually  raised.  /d.,  B SO, p. 203;  App.  I1-S82.  If challengers
cannot  obtain  large  contributions  initially,  their  campaigns
are  effectively  strangled  at  birth.  Appellees  strenuously
maintain  that  there  is no  evidence  demonstrating  the  neces-
sity  for  seed  money.  They  have  never,  however,  revealed
where  the  money  for  initial  mailing  will  come  from.  Far
from  closing  the  political  system,  large  early  contributions

ee ee

ee te A A ce T A ad SD ai Oe oe

are  truly  the  “‘seeds’’  that  enable  candidacies  to  flourish
and  become  viable.’

The  seed-money  phenomenon  applies  to  referenda  as
well  as  partisan  campaigns.  Harold  Willens,  a member  of
the  Board  of  Directors  of  appellee  Common  Cause,  des-
cribed  how  the  funding  of the  campaign  in  favor  of Cali-
fornia  Ballot  Proposition  9 ( a  state  law  with  contribution
and  expenditure  limits  similar  to  those  contained  in  the
FECA)  was  organized  in 1974:

“...1  estimated  we  would  nm  d  about  four
hundred  thousand  dollars.  i then  approached  a
number  of people  with  whom  I have  worked  and
whom I have  learned  to  respect  and  admire  be-
cause  their  affluence  is matched  by their  social
concern.  From  this  handful  of people  I got  about
forty thousand  dollars  which  was  our  seed  money,
or  start-up  fund.  We  were  then  able,  with  this
money,  to do some  other  things that  generated  the
rest  of the  money  we  needed.”’  ‘““The  Campaign
for  Proposition  9,"  The  Center  Magazine  (Nov.-
Dec.,  1974),  at 43.

presidential

candi-
of  current
dates—except  for  the  incumbent—demonstrate  that  the
cost  of  raising  small  contributions  is  even  higher  than
anticipated.  As of July  20,  1975,  Sen.  Jackson  had  on  hand
SO  percent  of  the  contributions  he  had  raised.  All  other
candidates  had  much  less:  Wallace  40 percent,  Bentsen  33
percent,  Udall  10  percent,  Harris  4 percent,  Carter  2 per-

’  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  expressly  de-
clined  to  take  a  position  regarding  the  appropriate  amount  of limita-
tions  on  contributions.  It  did,  however,  believe  that  such  limitations
*...  should  be  sufficiently  high  to  permit  meaningful  expression  of
support  ...  "  without  allowing  undue  influence.  ABA  Report  at  5.  As
the  discussion  in  the  text  demonstrates,  the  FECA’s  limits  are  far  too
low  to allow  such  a  ‘meaningful  expression  of support.”

138

139

cent,  Sanford  less  than  zero.  New  York  Times,  July  21,
1975,  p.  10, col.  3. During  this  early  stage  of the  campaign.
virtually  all expenditures  are  for fund  raising.

Finally,  limits  on  large  contributions  are  highly  dis-
criminatory  against  minor  parties  and  independent  candi-
dates.  Appellant  McCarthy,  for  example,  is not  permitted
to  receive  federal  funding  on  the  rationale  that  his  chances
of winning  are  too  insignificant  for the  government  to waste
its  resources  on  him.  He  is, on  the  other  hand,  prohibited
from  receiving  contributions  in excess  of $1,000  from  indi-
viduals  because  his  official  decisions  when  in  office  might
thereby  be corrupted.

The  $25,000  aggregate  limit  on  individual  contribu-
tions  to  candidates,  18  U.S.C.  § 608(1)  (3),  is also  likely  to
reduce  the  contributions  available  to  third-party  or  inde-
pendent  candidates.  Once  the  aggregate  amount  an
individual  can  contribute  is  limited,  donors  are  likely  to
want  to  make  their  limited  contributions  to  major-party
candidates  with  better  chances  of  winning  elections,  and
less  likely  to give them  to third-party  or  independent  candi-
dates.  McCarthy  dep. tr.  101;  Crane  aff.,  9 11B.

C.  The  Limits  on  Incidental  Expenses  of  Volunteers

Violate  the First

Amendment.

The  FECA  Amendments  limit  the  expenses  of volun-
teers  by  defining  them  either  as  a  contribution  or  an
expenditure,  with  a  $500  exception  for  travel  and  certain
other  expenses,  18  U.S.C.  § 591(e),  (f).  No  provision  of the
FECA  Amendments  better  typifies  its repressive  spirit  than
this.  It  starkly  demonstrates  the  gross  intrusion  of  this
Statute  into  the political  activities  of American  citizens.

It  applies  to  widely  varied  forms  of  typical  political
advocacy—the  use  of space  in  a  house,  the  use  of personal
phones,  travel  by  car,  plane  or  train—and  thus  restrains
all  kinds  of  hitherto-commonplace  First  Amendment

activity—demonstrations,  handbilling,  doorbell  ringing.
See Edwards  v.  South  Carolina,  supra;  Talley  v.  California,
supra.  The  vistas  of enforcement  this  provision  opens  are
chilling.  Individuals  will  be  subjected  to  close  audits  of
their  personal  finances  because  they have  engaged  in politi-
cal  activity  unregulated  before  the  FECA  Amendments.
Moreover,  the  possibilities  of discriminatory  enforcement
(as  with  the  enforcement  of limits  on  independent  expendi-
tures)  are  endless.

ae eke ee
ae Cee
© amar
ete

ee

Although  personal  services  contributed  by volunteers
are  exempted,  18  U.S.C.  § 591(eXSXMA),  the  effect  is neces-
sarily to reduce  such  services  whenever  some  expenditure  of
money  is  connected  with  them.  A  lawyer  contributing
volunteer  services  to  a  Senate  candidate  in an  effort  to  aid
compliance  with  this  statute  might  well  have  extensive
phone  bills,  travel  or  office  expenses.  By limiting  the  ex-
penses,  the  law  limits  the  services.  The  limit,  moreover,  be-
comes  more  restrictive  every  day  because  it does  not  take
inflation  into  account,  compare  18  U.S.C.  § 608(d).  If
Cor  gress  can  do  that,  what  kind  of political  activity  can  it
not  limit  or  forbid  entirely?  The  limit  on  volunteer  expenses
is unconstitutional  on  its face.

D.  The  FECA  Limits  on  Contributions  Discriminate
Against  Candidates  Without  Family  Wealth  in  Violation  of
the First  and Fifth Amendments.

The  limitations  on  contributions  are  likely  to  make  it
more  difficult  for  individuals  without  personal  or  family
wealth  to  run  for  office.  A candidate  for  the  House,  for  in-
stance,  who  has  personal  wealth  can  contribute  $25,000  to
his  own  campaign,  18  U.S.C.  § 608(a),  thereby  providing
seed  money.  On  the  other  hand,  candidates  without  per-
sonal  wealth  are  not  permitted  to raise  individual  contribu-
tions  above  $1,000.  The  result  is  that  candidates  from

140

14]

wealthy  families  will  have  far  greater  advantages  than  at
present.'®

The  court  below  suggested  that  the  higher  limits  for
family or  personal  expenditures  are  permissible  because  the
chance  of  corruption  is  less  in  such  cases  (op.  at  1533).
Clearly,  however,  that  is not  the  case,  for  history  abounds
with  charges  that  the  families  of  public  officials  have
profited  by their  relationship.

The  court  also  suggested  that  the  personal  expendi-
tures  could  be  used  as  “‘seed  money”  and  that  the  number
of  individuals  which  constitutes  a  family  is  so  large  that
“even  a  candidate  in  modest  circumstances  obtains  the
flexibility  to  raise  useful  sums  ..  .”’ (op. at  1533).  Surely  the
court  did  not  mean  to  suggest  that  only  those  candidates
whose  close  relations  can  contribute  $25,000  to  $50,000  are
entitled  to  raise  seed  money.  Yet  that  is precisely  the  result
of  the  statutory  provisions  and  the  Court  of  Appeals’
rationale.

By  distinguishing  between  what  wealthy  and  non-
wealthy  candidates  are  permitted  to  do  (i.e.,  because  the
latter  are  barred  from  receiving  contributions  in  excess  of
$1,000  from  wealthy  supporters)  in terms  of the  exercise  of
First  Amendment  rights,  the  interrelationship  of 18 U.S.C.
§ 608(a)  and  § 608(b)  creates  a  clear  discrimination  based
on  wealth  which  impinges  on  interests  expressly  guaranteed
by  the  Constitution.  As  such,  they  are  unconstitutional.
Bullock  y.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1972);  cf.  Harper  v.
Virginia  Board  of Elections,  382  U.S.  663  (1966).

'°  The  candidate-contribution  limits  for  the  Senate  ($35,000)  and
the  Presidency  ($50,000)  are  greater  than  the  $25,000  aggregate
contribution  limitation  imposed  on  all  other  individuals,  18  U.S.C.  §
608(b)  (3).

E.  The  Requirements  for  Becoming  a  Political  Com-
mittee  Which  Can  Contribute  $5,000  to  a  Candidate
Violate  the First  and Fifth Amendments.

As  in  the  case  of  limits  on  expenditures,  the  FECA
makes  distinctions  between  kinds  of political  committees.
Political  committees  which  have  received  contributions
from  more  than  fifty  persons,  and  which  have  been
registered  for  six  months,  are  permitted  to  contribute  to
candidates  up to  $5,000  per election,  18 U.S.C.  § 608(bX2).
The  six-month  limitation  restricts  the  ability  of citizens  to
engage  in  political  activity  which  is important  and  timely.
Interested  groups  and  individuals  will  be unable  to take  ad-
vantage  of the  $5,000  limitation  to  respond  to  events  oc-
curring  withing  six  months  of an  election  if they  have  not
previously  organized  or  registered  as  a political  committee.
Important  associational  freedoms  are  thus  severely  restric-
ted.  Ad  hoc  organizations,  for  example,  are  greatly  disad-
vantaged.

There  is no  reason  having  to do with  notions  of either
corruption  or  political  equality  which  justifies  this  kind  of
restriction  on  the  timing  of  political  activities,  and  the
legislative  history  of the  political-committee  section  reveals
For  all  that  appears,  the  statutory
no  justification.
provisions  are  a  deliberate  measure  to  protect  organized
interest  groups  which  have  the  means  to support  a national
network  of political  committees  on  a  continuing  basis.  For
this  among  other  reasons,  organized  economic  interest
groups  have  not  viewed  this  legislation  as  in  any  way  a
threat  to their  interests.  See supra,  pp.  69-71.  The  state  law
upset  in Mills  vy.  Alabama,  supra,  which  sought  to  prevent
last-minute  editorials  to  which  an  effective  response  could
not  be  made,  could  be  characterized  as  eminently  sensible
compared  to this  gross  restriction  on  the  timing  of political
speech.  See  also  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51  (1973),
which  held  that  a  state  may  not  “lock  in”  votes  by

142

143

prohibiting  persons  from  voting  in the  primary  of one  party
until  23  months  after  they  had  voted  in  the  primary  of
another  party.  The  main  effect  of  such  a  restriction,
moreover,  will  be  to  increase  the  power  of organized  and
established  interests  over  other  groups  because  such  in-
terests  can  more  easily  proliferate  and  maintain  committees
for the  period  required.  Requiring  contributions  from  more
than  fifty  persons  is also  arbitrary,  and  unjustified  by any
evidence  in the legislative  history.

F.  The  Limits  on  Contributions  Violate  the  First  and

Fifth Amendments  in  That  They Are  Overbroad.

The  Constitution  demands  that  regulation  touching
upon  the  sensitive  areas  protected  by the  First  Amendment
be  precisely  tailored  and  carefully  related  to the  evil  sought
to  be  remedied.  Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State,  supra;
United  States  vy.  Robel,  supra.  The  limitations  on  contribu-
tions  contains  in  the  FECA  are  a frontal  assault  on  this
stricture.

The  “corruption”  rationale  cannot  meet  the  constitu-
tional  test  when  applied  to  legislation  which  forbids
thousands  of  innocent,  ‘‘pure  speech’’  contributions  for
every  one  attempt  at corruption  which  it may  reach.  See  pp.
67-78,  supra.

Moreover,  the  $1,000  and  $5,000  contribution  limita-
tions  apply  no  matter  what  office  is being  sought.  Whether
the  goal  is  elimination  of  corruption  or  an  increase  in
political  equality,  such  limits  make  no  sense  in the  case  of
campaigns  for  the  Presidency  or  Senate  races  in  large
states.  Candidates  who  must  raise  millions  of  dollars
cannot  be  corrupted  by  contributions  in  these  amounts.
Where  small  states  or  House  races  are  involved,  $1,000  may
or  may  not  be a large sum  depending  on  the total  cost  of the
campaign.  Neither  appellees  nor  the  court  below  has  even
attempted  to  rationalize  the  overall  $1,000  limit.

The  limitations,  moreover,  leave  no  room  for  any
adjustment  depending  on  the number  of variables  which  af-
fect  the  cost  of elections,  Findings  I, 2-4,  pp.  1-2;  App.  II-5-6.
They  are,  moreover,  getting  smaller  daily  because  of infla-
tion.  If $1,000  is in  fact  a  rational  point  at  which  to  limit
contributions,  its real  impact  should  not  be left  to the vicis-
situdes  of the  economy.  Yet  Congress,  while  fully providing
for  cost-of-living  adjustments  to  the  expenditure  limita-
tions,  18 U.S.C.  § 608(d),  inexplicably  failed  to  include  any
similar  provision  with  respect  to contributions.

The  limitations  on  personal  expenditures  are  thus
pulled  more  from  the  air  than  from  any  kind  of rational
consideration  of the  cost  of elections.  The  $35,000  limit  on
personal  and  family  expenditures  in  Senate  races,  for
example,  is  only  $10,000  more  than  that  permitted  in
House  races,  although  the  cost  of  some  Senate  elections
may  well  be, even  with  spending  limits,  many  times  greater.
So too,  a limit  of $50,000  in the  case  of the  Presidency,  with
a  spending  limit  of  $20,000,000,  is  irrational  when  com-
pared  to  the  $25,000  limit  on  personal  expenditures  in
House  races,  where  the  spending  limit  is  $70,000.  Where
political  rights  are  concerned,  quantitative  limitations  es-
tablished  by the  state  must  be  related  to  a  legitimate  state
interest  rather  than  simply  arbitrary  figures  arrived  at  in a
random  fashion.  See Kusper v.  Pontikes,  supra.

Nor  do the  limits  on  contributions  differentiate  among
donors.  If, for  example,  there  is concern  that  some  groups
are  receiving  subsidies  from  the government  and  then  using
those  funds  to  lobby  the  government  itself  for  more  sub-
sidies,  precise  provisions  relating  to  political  contributions
by such  groups  might  be  upheld.  See  United  States  Civil
Service  Commission  vy.  National  Association  of Letter  Car-
riers,  413  U.S.  548  (1973).  Similarly,  if there  is  fear  that
people  are  buying  federal  offices  through  large  contribu-
tions,  the  proper  remedy  lies  in regulating  contributions  by
appointed  officials.  The  Senate,  in the exercise  of its advise-

144

and-consent  power,  could  easily  inquire  into  the  political
contributions  of  nominees  for  federal  office,  especially
when  aided  by properly  drafted  disclosure  requirements.  If
in  its  collective  judgment  the  Senate  believes  a  particular
nominee  has  ‘‘bought”’  the  nomination,  it can  simply  refuse
tu confirm  that  nomination.

Nor  do the  limitations  take  differing  recipients  into  ac-
count.  Appellant  McCarthy  may  not  solicit  contributions
over  $1,000  even  though  his  major-party  opponents  in  the
presidential  campaign  will  each  receive  $20,000,000  in
federal  funding.  There  is simply  no  way  to  justify  denying
him  both  federal  funding  and  the  right  to solicit  large  con-
tributions,  particularly  when  the  denial  of federal  funding
rests  on  the  legislative  judgment  that  candidates  like  Mc-
Carthy  have  no  chance  of being elected.

Appellants  have  suggested,  supra,  pp.  75-78,  a number
of statutory  schemes  directed  with  precision  at  the  kind  of
contributions  which  may  legitimately  be  said  to  involve
‘“‘non-speech”’  or  improper  elements.  Neither  appellees  nor
the  court  below  have  suggested  why  such  measures  are  not
fully  adequate  or  why  the  broadside  FECA  approach  is
necessary.  Rather,  they  have  merely  deferred  to  Congress’
judgment.  More  is demanded  where  the  First  Amendment
is at stake.

The  best  solution  is disclosure  of large  contributions.
The  First  Amendment  means  that  political  truth  is  a matter
for  individual  decision  in  a  free  marketplace  of  ideas.
Abrams  vy.  United  States,  250  U.S.  616,  630 (1919) (Holmes,
J., dissenting).  The  FECA,  on  the other  hand,  reflects a dis-
trust  that  voters,  even  when  fully  informed,  will  reject  can-
didates  who  abuse  campaign  financing.  The  two  ap-
proaches  cannot  stand  together.

145

VI.

The  Federal  Subsidy  Provisions  Violate  Article  I, Section  8,
and  the First  and  Fifth  Amendments  to the Constitution.

A.  Funding  Campaign  Activities  by Direct  Payment  to

em ES
a ot Ne el ie EET

Candidates  or Parties  Is  Unconstitutional.

Direct  government  subsidies  to candidates  and  parties

are  contrary  to the  ‘‘general  welfare,’’  U.S.  Const.,  Art.  I, §
8, because  they reduce  the  amount  of influence  that  citizens
have  over  their  representatives,  and  neuter  the  political  pro-
cess  by  eliminating  an  important  means  by  which  voters
can  express  intensely  held  views  on  important  public  issues.
None  of  the  stated  rationales  for  this  legislation—the
elimination  of corruption,  the  reduction  of election  costs,  or
the diminishing  of inequality  of political  resources—,even  if
fully  demonstrated  in  the  record,  leads  to  the  conclusion
that  candidates  should  be relieved  of the  need  to  raise  cam-
paign  money.  Cf.  Opinion  of the Justices,  197  N.E.  2d  691
(Mass.  1964);  Note,  Payment  of  State  Funds  to  Political
in  Meeting  Campaign
Use
Party  Committees
Expenses  Lacks  a  Public  Purpose,  78  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1260,
1262-63  (1964).  That  result  decreases  political  communica-
tion  and  political  participation  and  relieves  politicians  of an
important  burden  of their  office.’

for

Grave  dangers  to the  future  of democratic  government
result  from  direct  payments  to  parties  and  candidates.
Democracy  depends  largely  on  free  political  competition
and  the  freedom  to  form,  join  or  leave  political  organiza-
tions.  Once  a  party  becomes  officially  sponsored  by  the
government  or  government  begins  to  determine  the  alloca-
tion  of political  resources,  that  freedom  is endangered.  See
Lemon  vy.  Kurtzman,  403  U.S.  602,621  (1971):  “the  history

'  A  decline  in  voter  interest  in  party  activities  was  one  result  of
Puerto  Rico’s  public  subsidy  program,  Committee  for  Economic  De-
velopment,  Financing  a  Better  Election  System,  48 (1968).

of government  grants  of a continuing  cash  subsidy  indicates
that  such  programs  have  almost  always  been  accompanied
by varying  measures  of control  and  surveillance.’”

First  Amendment  decisions  dealing  with  the establish-
ment  and  freedom  of  exercise  of  religion  clauses  are
relevant  by analogy  here.’  The  First  Amendment  was  de-
signed  to  protect  freedom  of  conscience,  Freeman,  ‘‘A
Remonstrance  for  Conscience,’’  106  U.  Pa.  L.  Rev.  806
(1958).  As  Thomas  Jefferson  said,  in  arguing  against  forc-
ing citizens  to support  even  ministers  of their  own  religion:

consists  in the one  case  in the  multiplicity  of sects.

a ee ee eee Ce er ee

In  1784-85,  the  Virginia  assembly  considered  a  tax
check-off  scheme  analogous  to  Subtitle  H,  though  with
voluntary  features  absent  here.  ‘“That  bill  levied  a  tax  for
the  support  of Christian  churches,  leaving  to each  taxpayer
the  choice  as  to  ‘what  society  of Christians’  he  wanted  the
tax  paid.’’  Walz  v.  Tax  Commission,  397  U.S.  664,  704
(1970)  (Douglas,  J.,  dissenting).  By  his  ‘Memorial  and
Remonstrance  Against  Religious  Assessments,’’  Madison
was  able  to defeat  the  proposal.  Madison  wrote:

**...  the  same  authority  which  can  force a citizen
to  contribute  three  pence  only  of his  property  for
the  support  of any  one  establishment,  may  force
him  to  conform  to  any  other  establishment  in  all
cases  whatsoever.”  /d.  at  721.

Madison  thus  recognized  the dangers  inherent  even  in a so-
called  ‘‘voluntary’”’  tax-designation  system.  He  further
noted:

.
