The  establishment  clause  and  the  free-exercise  clause
are  closely  related,  Zorach  v.  Clauson,  343  U.S.  306  (1952).

Oe ee Oe ee ee wee ae

a

ee eee

148

149

Just  as  the  establishment  of a religion  or  religions  upsets
the  marketplace  of religious  ideas,  the  establishment  of a
political  party  or  parties  interferes  in  the  political  market-
place  and  infringes  the  free  exercise  of political  beliefs.  Cf.
Lemon  v.  Kurtzman,  supra,  at 628.

In  the  establishment-clause  cases,  three  requirements
must  be  met  before  legislation  will  pass  constitutional
muster:  (1)  the  legislative  purpose  must  be  primarily  non-
sectarian;  (2)  the  principal  effect  must  be  neither  to  ad-
vance  nor  to  inhibit  particular  organizations  or  movements;
and  (3) excessive  entanglement  in  religious  affairs  must  be
avoided.  Meek  vy.  Pittenger,  43  U.S.L.W.  4596  (May  19,
1975);  Abington  School  District  v.  Schempp,  374  U.S.  203
(1963).  By analogy  Subtitle  H meets  none  of these  tests.  (1)
Its  plain  purpose  is political.  (2)  It clearly  advances  some
and  inhibits  other  political  movements,  see  infra,  pp.  152-
64.  (3) It not  only  involves  but  actually  demands  excessive
government  entanglement  in political  affairs.

activities  and  certain

(1)  Subtitle  H’s  prime  purpose  is neither  nonpartisan
nor  apolitical  but  in  fact  the  direct  financing  of  certain
political  organizations.
political
Indeed,  there  is  no  nonpartisan,  apolitical  rationale  to
justify  relieving  political  candidates  or  parties  from  having
to  raise  funds.  Neither  excessive  spending,  fear  of corrup-
tion  nor  desire  to  reduce  political  inequality  justifies  direct
financing  of political  parties  and  political  candidates.

The  priorities  establishing  Subtitle  H,  according  to
which  the  available  funds  are  to  be  distributed,  demon-
strate  its  partisan  and  political  ends.  First  are  the  national
nominating  conventions;  second,  general-election  party
candidacies;  finally,  matching  grants  to  individuals  seeking
a  nomination  by traditional  party  routes.  I.R.C.  §§ 9008(a),
9037(a).  The  supports  of  organized  party  activities  was
clearly  uppermost  in  the  minds  of Congress.  Independent
candidates  such  as  appellant  McCarthy  are  excluded.

Throughout  Subtitle  H there  are  continued  references
to political  parties.  The very  term  ‘‘candidate”’  is defined  as
“the  candidate  of  a  political  party.”  1.R.C.  § 9002(2XB).
Similarly,  to be eligible  for matching  grants  one  must  be  a
candidate  seeking  nomination  by a political  party.  I.R.C.  §
9033(bX2).  In  the  other  parts  of the  statute,  which  place
restrictions  on  candidates  rather  than  conferring  benefits
upon  them,  candidates  are  defined  broadly  as  individuals
2 U.S.C.  § 431(b);  18
seeking  nomination  or  election.
U.S.C.  § 591(b).

(2)  Subtitle  H  inhibits  and  discriminates  among
various  parties  and  candidates,  a point  discussed  infra,  pp.
152-69,  as  an  independent  ground  of unconstitutionality.  It
should  be noted  here,  however,  that  direct  grants  to parties
or  candidates  necessarily  involve  allocative  formulas,  but
no  formula  can  ever  be  fair  to  everyone.  Under  every  for-
mula,  some  candidates  or  parties  will  be relieved  of the bur-
den  of raising  funds  while  others  competing  with  them  will
continue  to  sustain  that  burden.  And  the  government  will
have  in effect  declared  to  the  voter  that  some  parties  are
more  worthy  of adherence  than  their  competitors,  and  that
some  are  so  unimportant  as  to be ignored.

involve

subsidies

(3)  Direct

necessarily

excessive
entanglement.  They  necessitate  an  annual  or  biennial
struggle  in  Congress  as  to  the  allocation  of  political  re-
sources  among  political  groups.  There  are  simply  no
standards  to  guide  such  a  debate  other  than  self-interest.
Cf.  Meeks  v.  Pittenger,  supra,  at  4603.  Congress  will  thus
continuously  debate  the  question  of who  is  to  have  what
political  power.  Subtitle  H presently  allocates  $20,000,000

150

1S]

to  each  major-party  candidate,  a  result  reached  when  the
Presidency  and  Congress  were  controlled  by different  politi-
cal  parties.  Were  the  nation  to elect  a President  with  a large
majority  of his  own  party  in  the  Congress,  a  new  formula
basing  allocations  to  major  parties  on  past  performance
might  be enacted,  just  as  Subtitle  H bases  the  allocation  to
minor  or  new  parties  on  past  performance.‘

Other  forms  of wholly  undesirable  entanglement  will
occur.  The  statute  permits  party  committees  to  spend
2¢  per  voter  aiding  some  congressional  candidates.  18
U.S.C.  § 608(fX3).  Those  party  committees  may  well  avoid
supporting  candidates  challenging  members  of  Congress
with  particular  power  over  federal  funding  legislation,  such
as  committee  chairmen.

Entanglement  is increased  by other  provisions,  which
are  essential  to  the  entire  scheme,  for  the  continual
auditing  of committees  and  candidates.  2 U.S.C.  § 43&a)
(8);  I.R.C.  §§ 9007(a),  9008(g),  9038(a).  Furthermore,  the
FEC  and  incumbent  Presidents  (through  the  Secretary  of
the Treasury)  are  empowered  to exercise  discretion  as  to the
allocation  of funds  among  the various  categories  mentioned
in terms  of priorities  and  as  to disbursements  of matching-
grant  money  to  candidates  for  nomination  of their  own  or
9008(a),
another  party.  I.R.C.  §§  9004(aX3),  9006(d),
9037(a),  (b).

*  “Major”  parties  are  defined  as  those  parties  which  in the preceding
presidential  election  received  2S percent  or  more  of the total  number  of
popular  votes  cast.  I.R.C.  § 9002(6).  “Minor”  parties  are  defined  as
those  parties  which  in  the  preceding  presidential  election  received  5
percent  or  more  but  less  than  2S percent  of the total  number  of popular
votes  cast.  Jd..  § 9002(7).  ‘“‘New”’  parties  are  defined  as  those  parties
which  are  neither  major  nor  minor  parties.  /d..  § 9002(8).  The  term
new"  party  thus  includes  not  only  parties  created  since  the  last  elec-
tion,  but  also  all  other  third  parties  which  received  less  than  S percent
of the  vote  in the  preceding  election.

If the exercise  of political  rights  is to be kept free  from
government  pressure,  the  courts  must  strike  down  any  di-
rect  subsidizing  of political  parties  or  candidates.  Other-
wise,  government  by the  people  would  be in grave  danger of
becoming  government  by the government.

While  no  one  quarrels  with  the  necessity  of effective
action  against  corruption,  it does  not  follow  that  it is in the
“general  welfare”  to  relieve  candidates  from  the  need  to
raise  private  money.  For  example,  many  of the  difficulties
created  by Subtitle  H, see  infra,  pp.  152-69,  can  be avoided  by
a  “voucher”  scheme.  See,  e.g.,  S.  1390,  94th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.
(1975).  A voucher  system  places  the  subsidy  money  directly
in the  hands  of the  taxpayers—to  be distributed  as  they see
fit—and  allows  a  large  number  of  individuals  to  make
small  contributions.  Candidates  would  have  to attract  these
funds  by  addressing  issues  of importance  to  the  citizens,
rather  than  receiving  them  automatically  from  the  govern-
ment  or  through  a matching-grant  system.  The  court  below
felt
‘some
choice  ..  .  without  going  all  the  way  to  permit  each  tax-
payer  complete  authority  over  the  disposition  of  his
dollar...  ’’  (op.  at  1561-62).  But  choice  is the  essence  of
democracy,  and  if  Congress  is  to  use  tax  money  at  all,
maximization  of citizen  choice  is an  appropriate  safeguard.

Congress

properiy

provide

could’

A “‘voucher”’  scheme  would  not  necessarily  clearly  pass
constitutional  muster  or  be a  panacea.  However,  no  reason
for  the  public  financing  of  elections  justifies  Congress’
choosing  the  alternative  it did,  which  is  in  fact  the  most
intrusive  means.  As  Sen.  Baker  wisely  remarked,  ‘“‘there  is
something  politically  incestuous  about  the  Government
financing  and, I believe,  inevitably  then  regulating,  the day-
to-day  procedures  by which  the  Government  is selected.”
120  Cong.  Rec.  S  4457  (daily  ed.  March  26,  1974).°  A

*  Indeed,  five  of the  seven  members  of the  Senate  Select  Committee
on  Watergate  voted  against  federal  subsidies.  120  Cong.  Rec.  S 4560
(daily ed.  March  27,  1974);  id.  S 4708  (daily ed.  March  28,  1974).

1$2

153

“voucher”  plan  also  easily  avoids  the  discrimination
against  minor  and  new  parties  and  independent  candidates
which  Subtitle  H embodies,  and  which  we  now  discuss.

B.  Chapter  95  Violates  the  First  and  Fifth  Amendments
Because  It  Discriminates  Against  Minor  and  New  Parties
and Independent  Candidates.

Chapter  95 of Subtitle  H, which  establishes  a system  of
federal  subsidies  for candidates  in presidential  general  elec-
tions,®  favors  the  Republican  and  Democratic  parties  while
operating  in  a  discriminatory  and  to  some  extent  random
fashion  with  respect  to  all  others.  New  parties  and  inde-
pendent  candidates  are  severely  disadvantaged  when  they
begin  their  existence  and  most  need  financial  support.’  A
third  party  which  passes  the  statutory  threshold,  on  the
other  hand,  may  be  artificially  preserved  after  its  actual
voter  support  has  dissipated.  For  all of the elaborate  defini-
tions  of  ‘“‘major,”’  “‘minor,”  and  ‘“‘new”’  parties,  the  stark
fact  remains  that  in  1976  only  the  Democratic  and
Republican  parties  will  receive  any  pre-election  funding.

The  discriminatory  provisions  include  the following:

(1)  Most  grievous  is the  use  of prior-vote  criteria  to de-
termine  the  level  of federal  funding  in general  elections.®  A
four-year-old  performance  record  bears  no  necessary  rela-

*  See  pp.  10-12,  supra,  for a description  of its provisions.

’  As  Senator  Brock  said,  had  the challenged  statutes  been  in effect  at
the  time  of  the  emergence  of  the  Republican  Party,  ‘the  grave  of
Abraham  Lincoln  would  have  been  just another  burial  plot in the ceme-
tery.""  120 Cong.  Rec.  S 4552  (daily ed.  March  27,  1974).

*  A  Colorado  statute  enacted  in  1909  granting  parties  funds  in
proportion  to  their  voting  strength  was  declared  unconstitutional  by
that  state’s  Supreme  Court.  McDonald  v.  Galligan,  Col.  Sup.  Ct.  1910
(unreported),  described  in  Merriam  and  Overacker,  Primary  Elections
306 (1928).

tionship  to  current  strength.’  Such  a  formula  irrationally
overcompensates  declining  parties  and  undercompensates
those  on  the  rise.  See  Rosenthal,  “‘Campaign  Financing,”
supra,  at 412.  George  Wallace  would  have  received  nothing
in  1968,  while  John  Schmitz  would  have  received  $6.3
million  in 1972,  had  Chapter  95 been  law in those  years  (op.
at  1606,  dissent).

Prior-performance  criteria  make  qualification  and
an  entitlement  determination  for  major  parties  simul-
taneous:  achieving  25 percent  of the  vote  entitles  the  party
both  to  future  subsidization  and  receipt  of the  maximum
amount.  Minor  parties  upon  qualification  receive  an  en-
titlement  based  on  their  past  performance  in relation  to  an
“average  major  party  vote”  standard,  although  major
parties  receive  the  full  $20 million  entitlement  regardless  of
their  comparative  performance.  Compare  I.R.C.  § 9004(a)
(1) with  § 9004(a)  (2) and  (a) (3).  A party  with  only  25.1  per-
cent  of  the  vote  at  the  last  election  receives  the  full  en-
titlement,  even  though  it  may  be  slipping  into  an  even
greater  decline;  a  minor  part’  which  increases  its  strength
in  the  interim  will  obtain  financial  recognition  only  after
the  next  election.'®  There  are  bizarre  results  if Party  A won

*  The  ABA's  Special  Committee  on

Election  Reform  stated
‘[fJunding  should  be determined  on  the  basis  of current  voter  support.
Although  past  support  at the polls is  a proper  method  of recognizing  es-
tablished  major  and  minor  parties,  the  ability  of new  parties  and  inde-
pendents  to  gain  support  would  be  hampered  by a  process  that  may
lock  the system  into  the status  quo.’  (Emphasis  added.)  ABA  Report  at
6.

'°  If one  of the  two  major  parties  is deeply  divided  at  its convention
and  splits  into  separate  wings  each  fielding  a  candidate,  those  two
candidates  might  each  receive  less  than  2S  percent  of  the  vote.  The
other,  united  major  party  might  obtain  the  rest  of the  popular  vote,
leaving  under  the  statutory  definition  on/y one  major party.  In the  next
election  the  operation  of Subtitle  H  would  be  such  as  to  increase  the
likelihood  that  the  country  would  be left  permanently  with  a one-party
system.  In  1912,  the  Progressive  (Bull  Moose)  Party  received  27.4  per-

50 percent  of the  prior vote,  Party  B 25.1  percent  and  Party
C  24.9  percent.  Parties  A  and  B receive  $20  million  each,
and  Party  C  receives  $13.3  million.''  No  conceivable
justification  appears  for the discrimination  in favor  of Party
B and  against  Party  C.

(2)  Major-party  candidates  are  entitled  to  receive  the
full  subsidy,  and  minor-party  candidates  their  partial  sub-
sidy,  well  prior  to  the  November  election,  I.R.C.  §§ 9003-
9006.  New  parties  and  independents,  however,  receive
nothing  until  after that  election,  id.'*?  They have  no  alterna-

'©  (continued)

cent  of the  vote  and  the  Republican  Party  23.2  percent.  The  Progres-
sives,  although  not  an  ongoing  party  in  any  major  sense,  would  under
Subtitle  H have  been  entitled  to the full  major-party  allotment  for  the
1916  election,  while  the  Republican  Party  would  have  been  relegated  to
minor-party  status.  Had  Wilson  drawn  slightly  more  votes  away  from
the  Bull  Moosers,  only  the  Democratic  Party  would  have  been  a  major
party  under  Chapter  95S in the  1916  election.

In  addition,  one  of the  two  major  parties  has  been  close  to  the  25-
percent  cutoff  point  on  two  other  occasions.  In  1860  the  Democratic
candidate  (Stephen  A.  Douglas)  received  only  29.4  percent  of  the
popular  vote,  and  in  1924  the  Democratic  candidate  John  W.  Davis)
received  only  28.4  percent.  Only  a  very  small  voting  shift  in  either  of
those  years  would  have  consigned  the  Democrats  to  minor-party  status
under  Chapter  9S.

''  The  $13.3  million  figure  is derived  as  follows:  The  percentage  of
the  votes  of Parties  A and  B are  averaged  to create  the  ‘‘average  major
party  vote”  (here  37.5%).  Then,  it  is  determined  what  percent  of
the  average  major  party  vote  is the vote  received  by Party  C (37.5%  divided
by  24.9%).  The  resulting  percentage  (here  66.4%)  is  then  multiplied  by
the  amounts  received  by Parties  A and  B ($20  million)  to  arrive  at  the
$13.3  million  figure to which C is entitled.

‘?  Indeed,  it may  well  be that  unless  their  contributions  come  in the
form  of  loans,  new  parties  and  independents  are  conceivably  not
entitied  to  any  reimbursement  after  the  election.  A careful  reading  of
I.R.C.  §§ 9003(c)  (2)  and  9004(b)  (1)  indicates  that  post-election  pay-

(continued)

tive  by which  they  can  demonstrate  current  popular  sup-
port,  a  disability  that  is simply  not  rational.  Schwartz,  Public
Financing  of Elections:  A  Constitutional  Division  of the
Wealth  64  (1975).  They  are  thus  forced  to  divert  people
and  resources  into  fund  raising during  the  campaign,  while
major  parties  face  no  simiiar  burden.  The  post-election
subsidy  is  a  very  poor—indeed  almost  no—substitute  for
pre-election  payments.'?  Cf.  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  supra,  at  32.
Struggling  parties  have  a greater  need  for,  and  more  difficulty
obtaining,  money  than  do  established  parties.  Indeed,  these
difficulties  are  aggravated  by FECA’s  contribution  limits,  see
Note,  Developments  in the  Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.
1111,  1269  (1975).  If such  parties  do not  receive  money  before
the election,  paying them  afterwards  will  achieve  nothing."*

(3)  Any  general-election  candidate  on  the  ballot  in
fewer  than  ten  states  receives  no  federal  funding,  I.R.C.  §
9002(2XB).
This  obviously  damages  any  party  lacking
funds  for  getting  on  the  ballot  in  ten  or  more  states  but
which  nevertheless  hopes  to exert  influence  either  by taking

'*  (continued)

ments  to  new  parties  and  to  minor  parties  can  be  made  only  to  defray
campaign  expenses  which  have  not  been  paid  for  by  contributions,
unless  such  contributions  were  loans.

Since  the terms  “‘loan”’  and  ‘‘contribution”’  are  nowhere  defined  in
Subtitle  H (and  since  the  only analogous  definition,  18 U.S.C.  § 591  (e),
includes  loans  within  the  definition  of contribution),  it  appears  that
new  parties  and  minor  parties  which  increase  their  popular  vote  are
faced  with  a dilemma.  Either  they must  raise  contributions  primarily  in
the form  of loans,  or  they must  forego post-election  subsidies.

'  Moreover,  a  new  party  failing  to win  S percent  of the  vote  receives
no  subsidy;  a  subsidized  party  failing  to  meet  the same  standard  is not
required  to pay funds  back,  I.R.C.  § 9007(b).

'*  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  stated  that
[funds  should  be  made  available  to  all  candidates  and  parties  simul-
taneously  before  the  election.’’  ABA  Report  at  6.  Adamany  andAgree,
supra,  at  148,  strongly  concur.

he ee Demt

1S6

the  electoral  votes  of a  few  states  or  by affecting  the  results
in those  states.  It is a classic  means  of influencing  policy for
minor  parties  or  independent  candidates  to play a “‘balance
of  power’  role.'*  Chapter  95  ignores,  and  discriminates
against,  this  legitimate  means  of pursuing  political  goals  and
deprives  of any  federal  funding  parties  hoping  to  play such  a
role.  Cf, Anderson  v.  United States,  417 U.S.  211, 227 (1974).

The  ten-state  requirement  has  no  rational  relationship
with  a  candidate’s  chances  of success  in  the  electoral  col-
lege.  There  is  no  requirement  that  the  ten  states  have  a
significant  number  of electoral  votes.  Thus,  assuming  all
other  conditions  were  met,  a candidate  on  the ballots  of the
ten  smallest  states  (which  have a total  of 34 electoral  votes)
would  receive  federal  subsidies,  but  a  candidate  on  the
ballots  of the  nine  largest  states  (which  have a total  of 245
electoral  votes)  would  not.  Schwartz,  supra,  at 62.

(4)  Excluding  from  funding  parties  receiving  less  than
S percent  of the  vote  also  equates  seriousness  of purpose
with  the  ability  to  win,  an  approach  which  ignores  the  roles
of  third  parties  in  our  history.  Such  parties  and  inde-
pendents  often  desire  to  influence  the  political  process  not
by winning  presidential  elections  but  by affecting  the  public
debate.  Such  was  the  strategy  of both  Henry  A.  Wallace
and  J.  Strom  Thurmond  in  1948,  and  inspired  the  candi-
dacies  of  Eugene  V.  Debs  in  1908,  1912,  and  1920.  Yet
Chapter  95  studiously  denigrates  the  serious  contributions
of such  campaigns  to the  national  political  debate.'®

'S  In  the  elections  of 1836,  1844,  1884  and  1916,  “a change  in the
outcome  in  one  state  would  have  given  the  election  to  the  losing  candi-
date,’’  Schwartz,  supra,  at  59.

'©  With  respect  to  points  3 and  4  in  the  text,  the  ABA  Special
Committee  on  Election  Reform  stated  ‘‘[t}he  role  of  minor  parties
should  be  recognized  as a role  distinct  from  that  of major  parties  and

(continued)

157

**  cannot  be  overemphasized  that  at  stake  in  the
allocation  of federal  subsidies  is the  ability  of the  candidate
and  his  supporters  to  carry  their  views  to  the  electorate.
Under  Chapter  95,  minor  parties,  new  parties,  and
independents  bear  an  even  more  onerous  burden  in  con-
vincing  voters  that  they  are  serious  contenders  than  they
have  previously—a  burden  vastly  aggravated,  moreover,  by
the  new  contribution  limitations.  Chapter  95 thus  ‘‘tend[s]
to  silence  one  of the  nation’s  most  fruitful  sources  of new
ideas.”  Rosenthal,  “Campaign  Financing,”  supra,  at 413.

This  Court  has  made  clear  that  legislators  may  not
lock  in the  two  major  parties  by exclud:ag  all  competition
from  new  or  minor  parties.  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354
U.S.  234 (1957);  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23 (1968).  As
the  Court  said  in Sweezy:

“fall  political  ideas  cannot  and  should  not  be
channeled  into  the  programs  of  our  two  major
parties.  History  has  amply  proved  the  virtue  of
political  activity  by  minority,  dissident  groups,

programs  were  ultimately  accepted.”  Jd.  at  2S0-

ballot-access

Chapter  95,  however,  impinges  on  the  very  process  by
which  the  voters  become  interested  and  ultimate  election
results  are  obtained—communication  with  and  persuasion  of
the voters.  Subtitle  H is thus  a clear  intrusion  into  the  “robust”’
debate  at  the  heart  of the  First  Amendment,  and  not  merely
a  narrowly  drawn  “‘time,  place  or  manner”  restriction  on
how  the  results  of  that  debate  are  registered,  as  are  the
ballot-access  requirements.  As  Prof.  Rosenthal  has  said
regarding  the  effective  exclusion  of third  parties  from  fed-
eral  subsidies:  “It’s  worse  not  to  have  money  than  it is not
to  be  on  the  ballot,  because  [while]  write-in  candidates
sometimes  get  in,  unfinanced  candidates  never  get  in.”’
ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform,  Symposium,
supra,  258 (1975).

Chapier  95 involves  funding  of the  underlying  political
debate.
As  this  Court  said  in  Lubin  vy.  Panish,  415
U.S.  709,  716  (1974),  legitimate  governmental  interests
‘must  be  achieved  by a means  that  does  not  unfairly  or  un-
necessarily  burden  either  2  minority  party’s  or  an  individual
candidate’s  equally  important  interest  in  the  continued

emasculates  voter  choice  by altering  the  level  of political

To the  extent  the  ballot-access  cases  are  relevant.  they
support  appellants’  position.  In  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.
431  (1971),  the  goal  of the  prospective  candidate-plaintiffs
was  to  obtain  a  position  on  Georgia’s  general-election
ballot.  Under  Chapter  95 the  candidate’s  goal  is to  obtain
the  general-election  federal  subsidy  at  a  time  when  he  can
use  it to  advance  his  candidacy.  Under  the  statute  at  issue
in  Jenness  vy.  Fortson,  supra,  there  were  two  alternative
routes  to  reach  the  goal.  If a  prospective  candidate  wished
to obtain  the  benefit  of automatic  ballot  placement,  he had  to
incur  the  burden  of seeking  the  nomination  of a  party  en-
titled  to  such  automatic  placement,  thus  risking  a  primary
fight  for that  nomination.  If, on  the  other  hand,  a prospec-
tive  candidate  wished  to  obtain  the  benefit  of  remaining
independent  of the  established  parties,  he  had  to  incur  the
burden  of a petition  drive  that  would  accumulate  the  signa-
tures  of 5S percent  of the total  eligible  electorate.  Prospective
candidates  under  either  route  thus  had  an  opportunity  to
reach  the  goal  of a  position  on  Georgia’s  general-election
ballot,  ‘neither  .
.  [of which  could]  be  assumed  to  be  in-
herently  more  burdensome  than  the other.”’  /d.  at 441.

.

Under  Chapter  9S,  there  is only  one  route  by which  a
prospective  cairdidate  can  reach  ihe  goal  of obtaining  gen-
eral-election  fec  »ral  subsidy  money  before  the  November
election—the  only  time  at  which  it can  possibly  assist  his
candidacy.  If a  candidate  wishes  to  obtain  the  benefit  of
such  subsidy  money  in the  amount  of $20,000,000,  he must
incur  the  burden  of seeking  the  nomination  of a  party  en-
titled  to  the  automatic  payments.  If, on  the  other  hand,  a
candidate  wishes  to  obtain  the  benefit  of remaining  inde-

160

161]

pendent  of  the  major  parties,  there  is  no  corresponding
burden  he can  choose  to  incur  in order  to obtain  the benefit
of automatic  subsidy  money  in  the  amount  of $20,000,000
available  before  the  November  election.  He is simply denied
federal  subsidies  altogether  until  after  the  election,  and
even  then  he is not  guaranteed  the  full  $20,000,000  but  only
an  amount  based  on  the  ratio  of  his  vote  to  that  of  the
major-party  candidates,  and  nothing  at  all  if his  vote  is less
than  S percent.  I.R.C.  § 9004(a).

The  courts  below  confused  the  goals  at  stake  in  the
ballot-access  cases  and  under  Subtitle  H with  the  benefits
and  burdens  of seeking  the  nomination  of a  major  party  or
running  outside  of them.  They  thus  misconstrued  the  im-
port  of Jenness  v.  Fortson,  supra,  and  other  ballot-access
cases,  Storer  v.  Brown  415  U.S.  724 (1974); American  Party
of Texas  v.  White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974).  Although  under
Chapter  9S  a  new-party  or  independent  candidate  may  ob-
tain  some  federal  funding  (less  than  $20,000,000)  at  some
time  (after  rather  than  before  the  election),  his  candidacy  is
necessarily  less  viable  because  he can  never  achieve  the  goal
above  stated.  Accordingly,  in a  self-fulfilling  prophecy,  the
candidate  will  attract  less  support  and  in  fact  almost  cer-
tainly  will  never  obtain  even  the  partial  funding  Chapter  95
purports  to offer.

The  coup  de  grace  is  administered  by  contribution
limits,  which  make  it  more  difficult  for  new  and  minor
parties  to raise  private  funds  and  thus  to compete  with  their
publicly  funded  opponents.'’  Thus,  the  totality  of  the

Williams  v.  Rhodes,  supra,  at  34.  Far  from  benefiting  third

challengers,  see  supra,  pp.  127-38,  than  to  incumbents.

That  Congress  had  little  use  for  third  parties  is made
clear  by the  Senate  report  which,  in  defending  Subtitle  H,
said  that  a  proliferation  of  “splinter  parties’’  and  inde-
pendent  candidates  ‘‘would  undermine  the  stability  pro-
vided  by  a  strong  two  party  system  and  could  polarize
voters  on  the  basis  of a  single  volatile  issue.’’  S.  Rep.  No.
93-689,  supra,  at 8.'*

Rep.  Conyers,  who  voted  for  final  passage  of the  bill,
argued  that  the  S-percent  popular-vote  threshold  re-
quirement  for  new  and  minor  parties  would  preclude  them
from  getting  started.  Rep.  Hays  replied,  “I  personally
would  like  to  do  anything  I can  to  protect  the  two  party
system  ...  .”  120  Cong.  Rec.  H  7808  (daily  ed.  August  7,
1974).  The  bill  as  passed  demonstrates  clearly  how  success-
ful Rep. Hays and his like-minded  colleagues  were.

'*  The  legal  and  political  experience  of other  nations  subsidizing
political  activity  demonstrates  the  contrary.  Public  subsidies  to  parties
were  introduced  in Germany  with  only those  parties then  represented  in
the national  and  local  Parliaments  receiving  public  funds.  The  German
Constitutional  Court  declared  the  subsidies  invalid,  holding  that  the

Rep.  Conyers’  amendment  to  delete  the  5-percent

ties  and  independent  candidates  solely  on  the  basis  of the
popular  vote  the  candidate  received,  was  defeated  in  an
unrecorded  vote,  id..
H  7919.  Rep.  Hays,  opposing  the
Conyers  amendment,  stated  that  the  S-percent  figure  was
“arbitrary,”  an  unchallenged  characterization,  id.,  H 7916-
17.  Rep.  Frenzel  concurred  that  “we  have  never  been  able
to  establish  a  matching  formula  that  would  do  justice  to

formation  leading  from  the  people  to  the  organs  of the  state,  not  in the
reverse  direction,”’  and  defining  the  parties  as  ‘‘free  competing  groups
acting  on  their  own  behalf  and  independently  of the  state.’’  Quoted  in
Heidenheimer,  ed.,  Camparative  Political  Finance.  45 (1970).

In response,  the  German  parties  agreed  on  a plan that  would  allow
subsidization  of campaign  expenditures.  After  yet  another  court  chal-
lenge.  the  threshold  requirement  was  lowered  to 0.5 percent  of the  total
popular  vote.

The  present  German  law  thus  contains  a  threshold  requirement
one-tenth  the  size  of that  contained  in  Subtitle  H. Moreover,  Germany
presently  enjoys,  as  it has  since  World  War  II,  a  stable  “two  and  one-
half  party”  system,  and  does  not  appear  in  danger  of proliferation  of
parties.

23-49.

and

See

Scholars  examining  other  public-subsidy  schemes  uniformly
believe  that  they  entrench  the  established  parties.  This  entrenchment
has  been  found  in  Scandinavian  countries:  Andren,  “Partisan  Moti-
vation  and  Concern  for  System  Legitimacy  in  the  Scandinavian
Deliberations  on  Public  Subsidies."”  id.  at  50-67;  Canada:  Paltiel,
‘Contrasts  Among  the  Several  Canadian  Political  Finance  Cultures,”
id..  119-30;  Puerto  Rico:  Anderson,  Party  Politics  in  Puerto  Rico,
28-29  (1965);  and  Italy:  Sassoon,  ‘“The  Funding  of  Political  Parties  in
Italy.”’  46 The  Political  Quarterly  94 (1974).

Se

The  major-party  bias  permeating  Subtitle  H was  clear-
ly demonstrated  by the debate  over  the tax  “‘checkoff”  pro-
vision,  I.R.C.  § 6096.  In response  to Sen.  Mathias’  proposal
that  taxpayers  be allowed  to designate  their choice  of candi-
dates  or  parties,  Sen.  Pastore  replied,  “That  would  not  be
fair.  What  we  are  trying to do is to give both  sides  the  same
amount  of money...  .”  117  Cong.  Rec.  S 18891  (daily  ed.
November  17,  1971).

In  a  remarkable  statement,  Sen.  Long  further  argued
that  his  amendment  preventing  taxpayer  designation  of
parties  would  ‘“‘quit  confusing  people.  Rather  than  saying
both  Democrats  or  Republicans  are  the  party  of  one’s
choice,  the  taxpayer  just  makes  a  check  on  the  tax  re-
turn...  .’’ 119Cong.  Rec.  S 12196  (daily ed. June  27, 1973).

Although  it is uncertain  whether  or  not  a tax  checkoff
system  that  would  allow  taxpayers  to designate  their  choice
of candidates  or  parties  would  be permissible,  it would  cer-
tainly be far less discriminatory  than  the  present  system.  So
would  the  voucher  proposal;  see  Brief  of  Sen.  Meicalf  as
amicus  curiae.  Either  of these  devices  would  ensure  that
subsidies  were  allocated  in  accordarce  with  current  voter
support  rather  than  with  arbitrary  and  discriminatory  per-
centage  cut-offs  and  irrelevant  past  achievements.  Such
means  would  be  better  indicators  of present  popular  sup-
port  and  would  thereby  be less destructive  of First  and  Fifth
Amendment  values.  Those  values  therefore  dictate  that  the
more  intrusive  means  represented  by  Chapter  95  be
stricken.

Chapter  95  clearly  gives  ‘“‘the  two  old,  established
parties  a decided  advantage  over  any  new  parties  struggling
for existence  and  thus  places substantially  unequal  burdens
on  both  the  right  to  vote  and  the  right  to  associate,”
Williams  vy.  Rhodes,  supra,  393  U.S.  at  31.  See  Barrow,
“Regulation  of  Campaign  Funding  and  Spending  for
Federal  Office,”  S Mich.  J.L.  Reform  159,  187-89  (1972).

164

165

Although  one  of the  rationales  for  this  legislation  was  the
diminution  of  the  power  of  “special  interests,”’  two  such
interests  have  carried  the  day  with  Chapter  95—the
Democratic  and  Republican  parties.  There  can  be no  com-
pelling  governmental  interest  in  distorting  the  substantive
political  debate  in  such  an  invidiously  discriminatory
fashion.

C.  Chapter  96  Violates  the First  and  Fifth  Amendments

Because  It Discriminates  Against  Certain  Candidates.

Chapter  96 establishes  a  system  of federal  funding  for
candidates  for  party  presidential  nominations,  imposing
what  the  Court  of Appeals  termed  “‘stringent  threshold  re-
quirements”  for  eligibility  (op.  at  1527).  See  pp.  11-12,  supra,
for a description  of these  provisions.

A key requirement  of eligibility  for  payments  is that  a
presidential  candidate
‘‘is  seeking  nomination  by  a
political  party,”’  I.R.C.  § 9033(1),  (2).  Moreover,  candidates
must  be  participating  in  “primary  elections.’’  a  term  spe-
cifically  defined  to be an  event  or  series  of events  “‘held  by a
political  party,”  id.,  § 9032  (7).  Chapter  96 thus  deprives  of
substantial  rights  all  candidates,  and  particularly  inde-
pendent  candidates  such  as  appellant  McCarthy,  who
achieve  a  place  on  ballots  by petition  rather  than  by party
nomination.  Obtaining  ballot  status  in  the  various  states
requires  extensive  expenditures  of  funds,’’  whether  the
route  be  by  petition  or  otherwise.  Because  Chapter  96
denies  any  money  to  candidates  who  choose  that  route
rather  than  the  party  nomination  route,  it  discriminates
invidiously  against  them.  Storer  v. Brown,  supra,  at 746.

'*  Leaders  of  George  Wallace's  1968  presidential  campaign  es-
timated  that  the  organizational  and  legal  efforts  to  get  Wallace  on  the
ballot  in SO states  cost  $3 million.  Findings  I, 42, p. 23; App.  I-25.

The  courts  below  ignored  the  fact  that  under  the
ballot-access  cases  independent  candidates  at  least  had  an
opportunity  to  place  their  names  on  the  ballot,  albeit  by a
different  method  than  those  obtaining  party  nominations.
Chapter  96 provides  no  such  alternative  route.  It flatly  pro-
hibits  independent  candidates  from  ever  obtaining  any  fed-
eral  subsidy  money  under  any  circumstances.  There  is no
way  that  such  discrimination  can  be  constitutionally  justi-
fied.

Characterizing  the  effects  of  denying  funds  to  inde-
pendents  as  “‘incidental,”’  as  did  the  Court  of Appeals  (op.
at  1568),  drastically  understates  the  advantages  that  the
major  parties  gain.  Moreover,  the Court  of Appeals  implied
that  independents  should  be  eligible  for  general-election
subsidies  (op.  at  1569-70),  yet  it  gave  no  reason  why  the
logic  of that  implication  does  not  also  apply  to  subsidies
under  Chapter  96.

American  Party  of  Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  767
(1974),  relied  on  below,  is completely  inapposite.  First,  the
primary  expenses  defrayed  by Texas  were  not  the  costs  of
campaigning,  but  the  costs  of the  mechanics  of primary-
day  ballot  machines,  ballot  counting,  etc.  Second,  Texas
simply  took a slightly  different  route  to  funding  election-
day  expenses  than  other  states,  which  meet  such  expenses
out  of general  tax  revenues.  Upholding  Chapter  96  on  the
basis  of American  Party  is  like  saying  that  because  the
federal  government  pays  for  the  upkeep  of Lafayette  Park,
it may  also  defray  the  costs  of organizing,  publicizing,  and
holding  some  (but  not  all) of the demonstrations  and  rallies
that  occur  there.

The  matching-grant  formula  further  discriminates
unconstitutionally  by  requiring  applicants  for  matching
funds  to  become  eligible  by raising  $100,000  in  contribu-
tions  of $250  or  less,  in 20 states,  not  exceeding  $5,000  per
state,  I.R.C.  § 9033(b)  (3)  and  (4).  This  formula  has  no  rela-

166

167

tionship  whatsoever  to  the  manner  in which  candidates  are
nominated  or  elected  to  the  Presidency.  Such  a  require-
ment  discriminates  against  a  candidate  with  very  substan-
tial  regio:  al backing  who  is denied  the  opportunity  to  ex-
pand  his «  ipport  because  of the  lack  of matching  grants.  It
is, moveover,  in flat conflict  with  Moore  v.  Ogilvie,  394  U.S.
814  (1969),  which  held  unconstitutional  an  Illinois  statute
requiring  independent  nominating  petitions  to  contain
25,000  signatures,  including  200 each  trom  S0 of the  state’s
102 counties.

The  Court  of  Appeals’  statement  that  the  ‘‘20-state
requirement  simply  reflects  the  realities  of  Presidential
campaigning”  (op.  at  1568)  is  unsound.”  Presidential
candidates  win  by obtaining  a majority  of the electoral  vote,
not  by winning  in  a  majority  of the  states.  Chapter  96  is
even  more  burdensome,  and,  if anything,  less  rational  than
the  Illinois  statute  in Moore.  The  states  obviously  are  not
equal  in  their  electoral  vote,  but  they  are  treated  equally
(and  irrationally)  by Chapter  96, just  as  the  Illinois’  statute
in Moore  treated  the state’s  counties  equally  for purposes  of
nominating  petitions.  New  York  and  California  are  con-
sidered  no  differently  than  Nevada  and  Alaska*'  under
Chapter  96’s  presidential-candidate  licensing  system.””

2°  The  electoral  votes  of the  20 smallest  states  do  not  constitute  a

majority  of the electoral  college,  Findings  I, 52, p. 25; App.  I1-26.

**  New  York  and  California  together  have  more  electoral  votes
than  the  twenty  smallest  states  combined,  Findings  I, 50,  p.  24,  App.
I1-26,  and  twice  as  many  voters  as  the  20 smallest  states  combined,  id.
1, 49,  p. 24;  App.  I1-26.

*?  The  20-state  requirement  also  discriminates  against  ‘‘favorite
sgp"’  candidates,  especially  governors,  who  begin  with  a  narrow  re-
Boa candidacy,  but  attempt  to  expand  it as  the  nomination  nears.
Such  candidacies  will  die  aborning  under  Chapter  96.  See  Adamany
and  Agree,  supra,  at  149.

Chapter  96 is, moreover,  analogous  to the  filing-fee  re-
quirement  stricken  down  in Bullock  vy.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134
(1972),  and  Lubin  vy.  Panish,  supra.  As  succinctly  sum-
marized  by one  commentator,  the  vice  of matching-grant
formulae  is that  they  “‘rely  on  a wealth  criterion  in order  to
determine  eligibility.”  Fleishman,  ‘“‘The  FECA  of  1971,”
supra,  at  405.  Moreover,  wealth  is  the  only  criterion  by
which  matching  grant  funds  can  be  obtained—no  other
alternative  is  available.  Eligibility  should  be  based  on
popular  support,  not  wealth,  if Chapter  96  is  to  be  even
minimally  consistent  with  the  rest  of  the  challenged
legislation.”*

The  formula  is almost  openly  designed  to  increase  the
power  of ‘‘special  interests’  and  the  wealthy.  For  example,
a  candidate  supported  by  organizations  having  affiliates
nation-wide  would  clearly  be  in  a  better  position  to  garner
the  necessary  private  funds  than  would  be  a  candidate
without  such  ties.  The  matching  grant  device  also  gives
wealthy  people  a  considerable  advantage,”*  and  is  clearly
discriminatory,  Harper  v.  Virginia  Board  of Elections,  383
U.S.  663  (1966);  Bullock  vy.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1972).  A
wealthy  couple  can  contribute  $500  and  deduct  $200  from

>  In  Georgia  Socialist  Workers  Party  v.  Fortson,  315  F.  Supp.
1035,  1041  (N.D.Ga.  1970)  aff'd  on  other  grounds  sub  nom.  Jenness  v.
Fortson,  supra,  the  trial  court  said:  “[wJhile...  it  may  be  true  that
serious  candidates  traditionally  attract  money  for  their  candidacy,  we
cannot  say  as  a matter  of law  that  one’s  candidacy  is not  serious  or  that
he  does  not  have  the  right  to  run  merely  because  he  does  not  have  or
has thus  far failed  to attract  a certain  amount  of money.”

**  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  states  that
‘“...  amy  system  of  eligibility  and  distribution  should  attempt  to
minimize  the  importance  of private  contributions  in  attracting  public
funds.’’  ABA  Report  at  6. Chapter  96 does  precisely  the  opposite.  One
contributor  of $250  in effect  commands  matching  funds  ‘‘checked  off”
by up  to  250  other  citizens.  The  contributor’s  voice  is thus  magnified,
while  the taxpayers  are  rendered  mute  by I.R.C.  § 60%.

168

169

income  on a joint  return  because  of tax  credit  provisions,
I.R.C.  § 218.  If they  are  in  the  70  percent  bracket,  their
income-tax  liability  would  be reduced  by $140.  The  govern-
ment  then  adds  $500,  so  the  effect  is  a  contribution  of
$1,000  at  a  cost  of only  $360.  Chapter  96 thus  permits  the
wealthy  and  the  “‘special  interests’’  to continue  to give sub-
stantial  amounts  but  at a lower  cost  to them  than  under  the
present  system  of  private  financing.  See  Note,  Develop-
ments,  supra,  at  1269-70.  No amount  of drumbeating  about
eliminating  corruption  can  constitutionally  justify  this  kind
of provision.

Although  one  reason  for  sustaining  federal  subsidies
was  Said  to be the  need  to  save  candidates’  time  and  energy
by  reducing  the  need  to  raise  funds  (op.  at  1513),  the
complex  requirements  of  Chapter  96  create  exactly  the
opposite  result.  Because  contributions  needed  to qualify  for
federal  matching-grants  are  small  (under  $250),  it  is  dif-
ficult  if not  impossible  for  a  “‘late’’  entrant  to accumulate  a
sufficient  amount  of such  contributions  in a short  period  of
time.  Particularly  since  contributions  are  often  difficult  to
raise  without  an  announced  and  active  candidate,  would-be
presidential  nominees  have  entered  the  race  and  begun
active  electioneering  and  fund  raising  well  before  the  tradi-
tional  starting  dates.  Otherwise,  their  direct-mail  fund-
raising  efforts  (which  involve  a  long  and  costly  process
before  finally  resulting  in a  “‘profit’’)  would  have  almost  no
chance  of success.

Chapter  96  deprives  voters  of  rights  as  well  as
candidates.  To  the  extent  that  a  candidate’s  message  is
muted  because  of  the  discriminatory  allocation  of  public
funding,  that  necessarily  deprives  voiers  of information  they
might  otherwise  receive  in  a  free  market  place  of  political
ideas.  Lubin  vy.  Panish,  supra,  at  716.  Fleishman,  ‘‘The
1974  FECA  Amendments,”  supra,  at  889.  Particularly
when  such  alternatives  as  the  voucher  system  are  available
as  less  intrusive  means,  there  is no  reason  to  resort  to  the
crazy-quilt  structure  of Chapter  96.

D.  The  Formula  for  Federal  Funding  of Conventions

Violates  the First  and Fifth Amendments.

9008,

I.R.C.,

Section

providing  for  funding  of
nominating  conventions,  is  unconstitutional  for  the  same
reasons  that  the  prior-vote  criterion  for the  funding  of gen-
eral-election  presidential  candidates  is  unconstitutional,
see  supra,  pp.  152-54.  Major  parties  receive  equal  amounts;
minor  parties  lesser  proportional  amounts.  New  parties
such  as  appellant  Libertarian  Party  and  independents  such
as  appellant  McCarthy  receive  no  federal  funds  at  all,  not
even  a post-election  subsidy  based  on  performance.

quite

apart

considerable

The  courts  below  were  barely  concerned  with  this  dis-
crimination  (op.  at  1565).  Moreover,  political  conventions
have
from
responsibilities
nominating  a  presidential  ticket.  As  party  governing
bodies,  they  resolve  philosophical  disputes  and  chart  the
party’s  future  course  by writing  party  platforms  and  deal-
ing  with  credentials  disputes.  All  of  these  are  legitimate
and  crucially  important  functions,  cf.  Cousins  vy.  Wigoda,
supra.  Yet  new  parties  and  older  third  parties  receiving  less
than  5S percent  of the  popular  vote  receive  nothing  to  help
defray their  expenses.

How  a  court  that  can  complain  about  ‘quadrennial
Romanesque  political  extravagances’”’  can  then  uphold
blatantly  discriminatory  federal  financing  of  major  party
conventions  (which  are  often  mainly  exercises  in  political
publicity,  particularly  when  the  renomination  of an  incum-
bent  President  is involved)  is unclear.  What  is clear  is that
federal  subsidies  will  help  widen  the  gap  between  major
parties  and  all  other  political  groups.  American  Party  of
Texas  v.  White,  supra,  invoked  by the  court  below  (op.  at
1565),  can  in no  way  support  such  an  infringement  of First
Amendment  rights.

periodic  reports  disclosing  the  name,  occupation,  and
principal  place  of  business  of  all  those  contributing  in
excess  of  $100.  Jd.,  § 434(b)  (1)-(8).  Every  person  who  con-
tributes  or  expends  more  than  $100  other  than  by  con-
tribution  to  a  political  committee  or  candidate  (includ-
ing  volunteers  with  incidental  expenses  exceeding  $600)
must  also  disclose  his  contributions  to  the  FEC.  /d.,  §
434(e).  Certain  resources  available  to  incumbent  office
holders,  enumerated  in id.,  § 434(d),  need  not,  however,  be
disclosed.  Criminal  penalties  are  provided  for  violations  of
the foregoing.  Jd.,  § 441.

These  disclosure  requirements  are  unconstitutional
not  because  any  such  requirements  would  be  per  se
unconstitutional,  but  rather  because  they  indiscriminately
intrude  upon  associational  privacy  interests  in  a  manner
that  is  not  limited  to  securing  only  those  governmental
interests  which  are  served  by disclosure.  More  particularly,
the  statutes  here  are  unconstitutional  in  the  scope  of  their
application  to  minor-party  and  independent  candidates
because  in such  situations  the  valid  governmental  purposes
served  by  disclosure  are  attenuated  while  the  harm  to
associational  interests  is  severe.  Similarly,  they  are  over-
broad  because  the  threshold  amounts  of $10  and  $100  that
trigger  the  record-keeping  and  reporting  requirements,  re-
spectively,  are  excessively  low,  reaching  well  beneath  the

17]

level  at  which  the  governmental  interests  outweigh  the
claims  of associational  privacy.

Appellants  recognize  that  disclosure  requirements  dif-
fer  in  two  respects  from  expenditure  and  contribution
limitations.  First,  properly  drawn  disclosure  requirements
do  not  involve  the  same  direct  restrictions  on  political
activity  or  speech.  Second,  disclosure  of certain  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  can  advance  other  constitutional
values  by  providing  voters  with  information  relevant  to
their  choice  of candidates.  Indeed,  appellants  believe  that
narrowly  drawn  disclosure  requirements  are  the  proper
solution  to virtually  all the  evils  Congress  sought  to remedy
by  passing  the  challenged  statutes.  However,  this  Court
must  determine  whether  the  challenged  statutes  are  the
means  least  destructive  of  associational  interests  and
precisely  related  to  achieving  governmental  interests.  Ap-
theker  v.  Secretary  of State,  supra;  New  York  Times  v.
Sullivan,  supra;  Sherbert  v.  Verner,  supra.

A.  The  Act's  Disclosure  Requirements  Intrude  Upon

First Amendment  Rights  of Associational  Privacy.

It  is well  settled  that  the  constitutional  protection  of
associational  privacy  is not  limited  to advocacy  which  takes
the  form  of litigation  or  public  education,  e.g.,  NAACP  v.
Alabama,  supra,  but  fully  protects  those  v hose  association
is  for  the  purpose  of  political  and  electo  al  activity  and
51  (1973);
advocacy.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,
American  Party  of Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974).  As
the Court  observed  in Kusper:

414  U.S.

‘*There  can  no  longer  be any  doubt  that  free-
dom  to  associate  with  others  for  the  common  ad-
vancement  of political  beliefs  and  ideas  is a  form
of  ‘orderly  group  activity’  protected  by the  First
and  Fourteenth  Amendments.  .
.  The  right to  asso-
ciate  with  the  political  party  of one’s  choice  is an

.

172

173

integral
freedom.”  414  U.S.  at 56-57  [citations  omitted].

constitutional

of  this

basic

part

Moreover,  those  associational  rights  are  afforded  the  same
protection  against  indirect  restraints  as  they are  against  di-
rect  and  heavy-handed  intrusions.  NAACP  vy.  Alabama,
357  U.S.  at  461;  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  supra.
supra,
Accordingly,  statutes  which  inhibit,  even  indirectly,  the
right  to  associate  for the  advancement  of political  purposes
can  be  justified  only  if  designed  to  achieve  compelling
governmental  interests  that  can  be  furthered  in  no  less
intrusive  a  manner.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  at  56-60;
American  Party of Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  at  780-81.

That  the  disclosure  of political  affiliations  which  are
manifested  through  political  contributions  can  have  a deep
and  intrusive  inhibiting  effect  on  such  association  is  not
only  manifested  by the  record,  but  made  plain  by common
experience.

While  disclosure  requirements  may  serve  to buffer  the
dangers  of corruption  of governmental  officials,  they  can
also  have  a  significant  negative  effect  on  contributions  to
political  candidates,  parties,  and  committees.  This  occurs
because  some  potential  contributors  do  not  want  their
names  disclosed,  any  more  than  most  individuals  would
want  their  election-day  ballot  choices  disclosed.

presidential

to  Democratic

Substantial  reasons  exist  to  justify  such  fears.  The
celebrated  “‘enemies  list’?  was  compiled  in  part  from  lists
of  contributors
contenders.
Hearings  on  Watergate  and  Related  Activities  Before  the
Select  Committee  on  Presidential  Campaign  Activities,
U.S.  Senate,  93rd  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.,  Book  4, 1732-35  (1973).
Other  disclosure  requirements  have  typically  been  directed
at  the  members  of  unpopular  groups  or  the  adherents  of
unpopular  causes:  suspected  Communists  in  the  1950’s,
proponents  of  integration  in  ihe  1960’s,  and  members  of

the  anti-Vietnam-war  movement  in  the  late  1960’s  and
early  1970’s.'

There  are  considerable  fears  of private  retribution  as
well.  Disclosure  of  contributions  could  damage  employ-
ment  and  business  relationships.  A  public-school  teacher  in
Alabama  may  oppose  George  Wallace  but  fear  to  support
his  opponent  because  school  board  members  committed  to
Wallace  will  find  out.  A teacher  in  New  York  may  support
Wallace  and  fear  to contribute  to his campaign  because  the
parents  of his students  may  call him a bigot.

To be sure,  the  record  here  does  not  reflect  the  kind  of
focused  and  insistent  harassment  of contributors  and  mem-
bers  that  existed  in the NAACP  cases.’  But  the  record  does
show  the  clearly  articulated  fears  of  individuals,  well
experienced  in  the  political  process,  who  have  stated  that
the  systematic  disclosure  of political  contributions  required
by the statute  would  lessen  the  amounts  of contributions  re-
ceived  by their  political  organizations.  See,e.g.,  Reed  dep.
tr.  50,  66-69;  Reed  aff.  99 29-32;  McCarthy  dep.  tr.  17-18,
67-70,  106-07;  Weil  dep.  tr.  27-28;  Crane  aff.  9 11G;  Crane
dep. tr.  38.

'  Recent  revelations  indicate  that  the  Internal  Revenue  Service,
under  both  Democratic  and  Republican  administrations,  gave  special
attention  to  the  tax  returns  and  contributors  to  tax-exempt  groups  on
the  right  and  on  the  left.  And  it has  also  been  recently  disclosed  that
agencies  of the  government  maintain  a computer  network,  with  the dis-
arming  acronym  “IMP,”  with  information  on  the  political  activities
millions  of  Americans,  much  of  it  gathered  from  lists  of  campaign
contributions.  In  light  of these  practices,  a  potential  contributor  to  a
dissident  political  party  will  surely  get the  message  from  the  fiyer which
rehearses  the  statutory  legend:  “A  copy  of our  report  is filed  with  the
Federal  Election  Commission  . .

174

175

The  Mississippi  Republican  Party,  for  instance,  is
fearful  of disclosure  because  of those  party  members  who
do  not  want  their  political  contributions  publicly  known.
Findings  III,  Reed  8,  p.  49;  App.  II-740.  The  Party  relies
heavily  on  contributions  in excess  of $100  to finance  its op-
erations.  Approximately  two-thirds  of  the  funds  used  to
finance  such  operations  come  from  contributors  of $10  per
month,  made  on  a  bank-draft  system,  id.,  Reed  7, p.  49;
App.  II-740.  The  long-time  dominance  of the  Democratic  Par-
ty in  that  state  made  it hazardous  for  individuals  to  be  pub-
licly  known  as  supporters  of the  Republican  Party.  These
$120-per-year  contributors  and  many  others  could  remain
anonymous  under  the  Mississippi  disclosure  statute,  which
requires  reporting  only  of contributions  in excess  of $500,
Miss.  Code  Ann.  § 23-3-41  (1972),  and  avoid  possible
harassment  based  on  their  association.  Under  the  FECA,
these  contributors  are  not  longer  protected.

There  is  uncontradicted  testimony  by  Sen.  Buckley’s
1970  Finance  Chairman  that  contributions  to  that  cam-
paign  were  a  very  sensitive  matter  to  many  individuals  in
New  York,  Weil  dep.  tr.  28,  when  the  candidate  was  the
nominee  of only the Conservative  Party.

There  is  further  uncontradicted  testimony  from  Mr.
Weil  that  many  persons  with  Republican  affiliations  were
reluctant  to  contribute  to  a  third-party  candidacy  if that
fact  would  be  publicly  known.  At  that  time  the  option  was
open  to  such  individuals  to  contribute  through  ‘‘  Wash-
ington  committees.”’  Those  committees  were  not  subject  to
the  federal  or  New  York  reporting  and  disclosure  re-
quirements,  id.  Under  the  FECA,  that  option  is no  longer
open  to  them,  and  Conservative  Party  candidates  will  not
be  able  to  receive  contributions  without  disclosing  the
donors’  names.  Sen.  Long,  a  supporter  of the  challenged
legislation,  explicitly stated  that  ‘‘[t]he  disclosure  provisions
really  have  in fact  made  it difficult  for challengers  to  chal-
lenge  incumbents.”  120  Cong.  Rec.  S  18544  (daily  ed.
October  8, 1974).

And  thus,  as  Sen.  Long  implied,  contributions  which
otherwise  would  have  been  made  are  simply  not  made.  It
would  be the  rare  contributor  indeed  who  informed  a party
official  that  he did  not  intend  to contribute  because  of fear
of  disclosure.  Instead,  the  would-be  contributor  simply
would  remain  silent,  neither  contributing  nor  explaining
why  he  did  not  intend  to  do  so,  but  seeking  to  avoid
potential  harassment.  The  fact,  therefore,  that  there  is little
evidence  of  harassment  or  reprisals  should  not  be  sur-
prising.  Rosenthal,  ‘““Campaign  Financing,”  supra,  at  406-
07.  The  chilling  of  political  activity,  though  not  easily
measurable,  is nonetheless  real.  Nothing  else  explains  the
large  number  of  $99  contributions
received  by  the
Mississippi  Republican  Party.  Reed  aff.  § 11;  Reed  dep. tr.
31-32.  See  120  Cong.  Rec.  S 4570  (daily  ed.  March  27,
1974).  It would  be  ironic  if one  result  of campaign  reform
were  to  discourage  citizen  involvement  in  politics.’  See  H.
Rep.  No.  93-1239,  supra,  at  120.

*  The  result  in  Pichler  v.  Jennings,  347  F.  Supp.  1061  (S.D.N.Y.
1972),  has  been  strongly  criticized  precisely  because  the  court  there
“sought  to avoid  the conceptual  problem  of defining  minor  party  status
by converting  the  issue  into  a question  of fact:  could  the  plaintiffs  show
that  participants  in, or  contributors  to the Conservative  Party had  been
in  the  past?’’  Note,  Developments  in  the
subject  to  ‘reprisals’
Law—Elections,  supra,  at  1248.  The authors  continue:

“That  approach  is tenuous.  First,  proof of reprisal  is a dif-
ficult  matter,  as  the  sanctions  are  often  subtle.  Second,  if
disclosure  truly  deters,  contributors  fearful  of reprisal  will
not  place  themselves  in  a  position  to  suffer.  Last,  even  if
deterrence  is not  complete,  there  may  be great  injury  to  the
organization  before  the  [Pichler]  test  can  be  met.”  Jd.  at
1248-49.

The  same  Note  also  criticized  the  decision  in Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379  F.
Supp.  704 (N.D.  Ga.  1974),  for  its insensitivity  to the  “especially  severe
surdens  imposed,  and  the  diminished  importance  of  state  interests.
‘hen  disclosure  is required  of unpopular  candidates  and  parties,’  id.
‘  1250.  Indeed,  the  major  effect  on  a  new  party  might  be a failure  to
‘ow,  an  effect  impossible  to prove  yet very  real.

176

177

people  are  harassed,  lose their jobs, receive  threats  or suffer
acts  of  violence.  Its  protection  arises  instead  from  the
judicial  recognition  that  the  prospect  of  public  identi-
fication  with  groups  engaged  in  advocacy  will  deter  indi-
viduals  from  such  association.  That  is why  this  Court  has
characterized  it  as  “‘hardly  a  novel  perception  that  com-
pelled  disclosure  of  affiliation  with  groups  engaged  in
advocacy”’  may  constitute  an  effective  restraint  on  freedom
of association  and  has  “recognized  the vital  relationship  be-
tween  freedom  to  associate  and  privacy  in  one’s  associa-
tions  ....’"”  NAACP  v. Alabama,  357  U.S.  449,  462  (1958).
Indeed,  in numerous  cases,  that  right has been  protected  on
the  basis  of that  assumption  alone  and  in the  absence  of a
detailed  showing  of harassment.  United  States  v.  Rumely,
34S  U.S.  41  (1953);  Lamont  vy.  Postmaster  General,  381
U.S.  301  (1965);  Pollard  vy.  Roberts,  supra;  see  also ACLU
v.  Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041  (D.D.C.  1973),  vacated  as
moot  sub  nom.  Staats  y.  ACLU,  43  U.S.L.W.  5043  (1975).
The  proper  rule  was  stated  by Chief  Judge  Bazelon,  dis-
senting  below:  “‘The  Court  has  never  varied  the  rigor of its
scrutiny  depending  on  the  presence  or  absence  of a  record
of harassment  and  chill.  Rather,  whenever  a disclosure  re-
quirement—or  any  other  law—has  posed  a  reasonable
danger  of chilling  the  exercise  of First  Amendment  rights,
the  Court  has  applied  its  most  exacting  review’  (op.  at
1596)  (footnotes  omitted).  As we  will  now  show,  the  majority
below  failed  to apply such  scrutiny.

The  court  in  Pollard  v.  Roberts,  283  F.  Supp.  248
(E.D.  Ark.  1968),  affd  per  curiam,  393  U.S.  14  (1968),
clearly  understood  this  phenomenon.  There a local  prose-
cutor  investigating  alleged  election-law  violations  sought
access  to  the  Arkansas  Republican  Party’s  bank  account,
the  effect  of which  would  be to  reveal  individual  contribu-

as  a violation  of associational  privacy:

‘While  there  is no  evidence  of record  in this  case
that  any  individuals  have  as  yet been  subjected  to
reprisals  on  account  of the  contributions  in ques-
tion,  it would  be  naive  not  to  recognize  that  the
disclosure  of the  identities  of contributors  to cam-
paign  funds  would  subject  at  least  some  of them

Recognizing  these  phenomena,  this  Court  has  not  re-
quired  the  right  of associational  privacy  to turn  solely  upon
a  detailed  showing  that  a  certain  minimum  number  of

*  Although  the court  in Pollard  acknowledged  the merit  of general
political-committee  disclosure  requirements,  its  holding  was  premised
upon  a  recognition  that  a  particular  application  of  disclosure  may  so
intrude  on  associational  interests  as  to be unconstitutional.

178

179

B.  Application  of  The  Acts  General  Disclosure  Re-
quirements  to  Minor-Party  and  Independent  Candidates
Serves  No  Compelling  Governmental  Interest  Sufficient  To
Override  the Associational  Rights  at Stake.

The  disclosure  requirements  cause  particular  difficulty
to  new  and  minor  parties  and  independent  candidates.°
Organizations  such  as  the  Libertarian  Party  and  the  Com-
mittee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency—McCarthy  '76  are
often  viewed  as  “‘fringe’’  groups.  Individuals  who  share
their  views  might  wish  to contribute  to them,  but  would  be
reluctant  to  do  so  if their  contributions  were  made  public.
Contributing  to a new  party  or  an  independent  candidate  is
considerably  different  from  contributing  to  an  established
party  in most  instances.  See,  e.g.,  Crane  aff.  99  11E,  11G;
$22.  Minor  parties  tend  to take  more  well-
Wertheimer  aff.
defined  positions  and  to adhere  to them  more  strongly  than
do  major  parties.  Sorauf,  Political  Parties  in  the American
System  33-34  (1964).  Indeed,  a  recognized  function  of
minor  parties  is  to  create  a  climate  of  opinion  in  which
major  parties  will  adopt  their  stands  on  issues,  not
necessarily  to elect  their  candidates  to office,  Sweezy  v.  New
Hampshire,  supra,  at  251.

*  One  law-review  comment  has observed:

“Yet,  because  minor  parties  often  advance  unpopular
political  positions,  and  because  their  candidates  usually
have  no  expectations  of victory,  their  contributors  may  feel
more  endangered  by  disclosure  requirements  than  would
supporters  of the  major  parties.  To  the  extent  that  effective
advocacy  of a  political  position  in contemporary  public  life
requires  organizing  a  political  group  and  collecting  large
amounts  of  money,  this  burden  may  stifle  an  unpopular
point  of view  permanently.  While  the  primary  impact  of dis-
closure  statutes  on  the  major  parties  may  be  to  reduce  the
volume  of their  speech,  the  impact  on  minor  parties  could
be  to  eliminate  them  entirely.""  Note,  Developments  in  the
Law—Elections,  supra,  at  1247.

In  the  often-quoted  words  of  Chief  Justice  Warren:
“History  has  amply  proved  the virtue  of political  activity  by
minority,  dissident  groups  who  innumerable  times  have
been  in the vanguard  of democratic  thought  and  whose  pro-
grams  were  ultimately  accepted.”’  /d.  at 251.

The  majority  below  recognized  that  disclosure  might
work  some  harm  on  unpopular  minor  parties,  and  ‘‘does
indeed  infringe  their  First  Amendment  freedom  to  asso-
ciate  to  advocate  unorthodoxy,”’  but  concluded  on  balance
that  such  rights  were  subject  to  ‘reasonable  restrictions  for
reasons  of  compelling  state  interest...”  (op.  at  1548).
That  conclusion  cannot  be sustained.

Although  this  Court  has  not  addressed  or  analyzed
associational-privacy  claims  in  the  context  of campaign-
finance  disclosure  laws,  it has  recognized  such  claims  in  a
variety  of  other  situations.°  This  Court  has  also  made  it
tlear  that  the  constitutional  guarantee  of  associational
orivacy  fully protects  those  whose  association  is for the  pur-
dose  of electoral  activity,
Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51
1973);  American  Party  of Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  767

*  The  right  of associational  privacy  has  been  upheld  as  overriding
claimed  governmental  interests  in probing  subversive  activities  within  a
state,  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354  U.S.  234,  255  (1957)  (concurring
opinion);  in  regulating  corporations  doing  business  in  the  state,  e.g..
NAACP  vy.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958);  in  preventing  false  or
fraudulent  political  or  other  advertising  by  requiring  that  handbills
identify  their  individual  sponsors,  Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.  60
(1960);  in  scrutinizing  the  fitness  of teachers,  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364
U.S.  479  (1960);  in  probing  possible  subversive  influence  in race  rela-
tions,  Gibson  v.  Florida  Legislative  Investigation  Committee,  372  U.S.
539  (1963);  in  regulating  foreign  travel  for  espionage  purposes,
Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State,  supra;  in screening  Communist  political
propaganda  from  abroad,  Lamont  v.  Postmaster  General,  381  U.S.  301
(1965);  in  protecting  defense  industries,  United  States  v.  Robel,  389
U.S.  258 (1967);  and  in assessing  the character  and  fitness  of applicants
for admission  to the  bar,  e.g..  Baird  v.  Arizona,  401  U.S.  1 (1971);  In re
Stolar,  401  U.S.  23(1971).

180

18]

(1974);  accord,  Pollard  v.  Roberts,  supra.  Accordingly,  any
inhibit‘on  of such  association  can  be justified  only by refer-
ence  .o  the  compelling-interest  analysis  identified  in  the
associational-privacy  cases.

That  is why the  two  decisions  of this  Court  relied  on  by
the  court  below  do  not  support  the  constitutionality  of
the  FECA  disclosure  provisions.  Although  the  decision  in
Burroughs  v.  United  States,  290  U.S.  534 (1934),  upheld  the
disclosure  provisions  of the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act
of  1925,  that  decision  only  established  that  Congress  has
authority  under  Article  I of the  Constitution  to  enact  such
legislation.  No  First  Amendment  issues  were  raised  or
decided  in  that  case.  Moreover,  the  deference  concerning
means  to  achieve  goals  accorded  to  legislative  choices  in
that  case,  id.,  at  547-48,  is  no  longer  the  law  when  First
Amendment  rights  are  at  stake,  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  supra;
Aptheker  vy.  Secretary  of State,  supra;  United  States  v.
Robel,  389  U.S.  at 258 (1967).

Similarly,  although  United  States  y.  Harriss,  347  U.S.
612  (1954),  involved  no  direct  associational-privacy  claims,
the  Court  nevertheless  gave  an  extremely  narrow  reading  to
the  federal  lobbying  statute,  confining  its  reach  to  those
persons  or  contributions  the  main  purpose  of which  was  to
engage  in  core  lobbying  efforts  through  direct  representa-
tions  to  members  of  Congress.  Harriss  and  its  approving
citation  of Burroughs  do  not  sustain  the  sweep  of the  dis-
closure  provisions  here.  And  the  holding  of Harriss  must  be
read  together  with  United  States  v.  Rumely,  supra,  where
the  Court  rejected  a congressional  attempt  ‘‘to  inquire  into
all  efforts  of private  individuals  to  influence  public  opinion
through  books  and  periodicals,  however  remote  the  radia-
tions  of influence  which  they  may  exert  upon  the  ultimate
legislative  process  ...  .’’  345  U.S.  at  46.  The  distinction
between  Harriss’  recognition  of power  to  regulate  through
disclosure  those  who  bring  direct  pressures  to  bear  at  the
core  of the  legislative  process  and  Rumely’s  denial  of power

to  regulate  those  remote  radiations  of  influence  is  highly
relevant,  for  it suggests  the  distinction  between  disclosure
of the funding  of those  major-party  candidates  with  present
access  to governmental  power  and  disclosure  in the context
of  new,  unorthodox,  minor-party  or  independent  candi-
dacies  whose  present  access  to  governmental  power  is
normally  remote.’

The  court  below  saw  three  interests  served  by sweeping
the

independent  and  minor-party  candidates
FECA’s  general  disclosure  requirements.

within

First,  the  Court  thought  that  disclosure  of the  identity
of  those  ‘‘angels’’  who  occasionally  support  minor-party
candidates  would  help ensure  enforcement  of the  contribu-
tion  limitations  and  other  prohibitions  in the  FECA.  How-
ever,  there  is  virtually  no  showing  in  the  record  of  such

’  The  decision  in United  States  v.  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect
the  President,  507  F.2d  1194  (D.C.  Cir.  1974),  relied  on  below,  does  not
preclude  this  Court’s  consideration  of the  over-breadth  challenge  here.
There,  the  conviction  of  the  Committee  for  failing  to  report  the
$200,000  cash  contribution  from  Robert  Vesco  was  upheld  against  a
claim  that  the  1971  Act’s  disclosure  requirements  infringed  First
Amendment  freedoms.  Since  the  conduct  involved  there—a  $200,000
cash  contribution  to  an  incumbent  President’s  re-election  campaign—is
the  paradigm  case  for disclosure,  it was  hardly  an  appropriate  occasion
to  employ  the  overbreadth  doctrine  to  overturn  the  conviction  on  the
basis  of the  Act’s  improper  reach.  See,  e.g.,  Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,
413  U.S.  601  (1973).  Second,  there  was  no  attempt  to  make  a factual
showing of harm,  because  the issue  was  not  even  raised  in the  District  Court,
.  not  an  appropriate  medium”  for  a
rendering  “the  barren  record  .
challenge  to  a  statute  whose  general  validity  had  been  upheld  forty
years  earlier  in Burroughs.  In  this  case,  however,  in  a  factual  context
and  involving  parties  far  from  the  core  of any  valid  legislative  purpose
requiring  disclosure,  careful  scrutiny  of the  reach  and  imprecision  of
the disclosure  provisions  is most  appropriate.

.

Similarly  inapposite  is Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379  F.  Supp.  704  (N.D.
Ga.  1974) (three-judge  court),  where  the challenge  to a similar  state  dis-
closure  statute  was  made  by a candidate  in a major-party  primary.

182

183

violations  sufficient  to  warrant  inclusion  of all  minor-party
and  independent  candidates  within  the  FECA’s  sweep.
Conversely,  the  record  does  show,  as  the  dissent  noted,  that
minor  parties  generally  receive  little  money  and  have  few
large contributors.*®

Second,  the  Court  found  persuasive  the  public’s  inter-
est  in  knowing  “‘the  composition  of the  angel’s  wings  and
what  makes  them  fly,”  so  that  “‘voters  are  not  required  to
vote  blind”’  (op. at  1545).  However,  there  is no  indication  in
the  record  that  members  of the  public  or  of the  news  media
are  significantly  interested  in  contribution  information
about  minor-party  candidates.  In  addition,  since  the  views
and  programs  of minor  parties  tend  to  be  well!  established
and  well  known,  there  is a diminished  public  need  to  know
the identity  of the supporters  of such  a party  for purposes  of
evaluating  their  programs,  platforms,  or  candidates.  But
most  importantly,  since  the  “radiations  of influence’’  which
minor  parties  generally  exert  upon  the  electoral  process  are
“remote,”  cf.  United  States  v.  Rumely,  supra,  345  U.S.  at
146,  the  compelling  interest  in  identifying  the  sponsors  of
such  activity  is remote  as  well.  Indeed,  the electoral  activi-
ties  of minor-party  and  independent  candidates  are  much
more  directed  at  influencing  public  opinion  than  at  achiev-
ing  electoral  power.  To  that  extent,  those  activities  are
much  more  similar  to  the  functions  of  independent  “‘issue”

*  Only  6  percent  of  the  contributions  to  minor  parties  are  in
amounts  over  $1,000.  Findings  II,  A  56,  73,  pp.  103,  110-11;  App.  II-
453,457.  Spending  by minor-party  and  independent  candidates  for  federal
office  normally  constitutes  approximately  2 percent  of all  federal  political
spending.  See, e.g.,  Findings  II, A 13, p. 89; App.  II-441.  To be sure,  minor-
party  and  independent  candidates  sometimes  are  successful,  and  may  be so
in the  future  if the blatant  discriminations  against  them  in many  sections  of
the challenged  statutes  are  held  unconstitutional.  That  possibility  could
easily be handled  by requiring  post-election  disclosure  by any  minor-party  or
independent  candidate  who  managed  to  win  office;  that  would  be sufficient
to prevent  undue  influence  by large contributors  on  elected  officials.

organizations  which  the  court  below  afforded  constitu-

interest  in  learning  the  identity  of  contributors  to  issue

Third,  though  recognizing  that  any  connection  be-
tween  preventing  official  corruption  and  disclosure  of
minor-party  contributions  was  less  substantial,  the  court
below  thought  that  since  it was  not  impossible  that  such
candidates  might  win  office,  the  rationale  had  some  force.
But,  obviously,  any  potential  for  corruption  exists  only
where  those  who  hold  governmental  power  can  exercise
favoritism.  Libertarian  Party candidates  have  never  been  in
a position  to raise  milk  price supports  to advantage  special-
interest  contributors.  The  Conservative  Party  of New  York
has  never  nominated  an  ambassador  to  reward  a  large
contributor.  The  Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presi-
dency—McCarthy  ’76 has  never  controlled  the  awarding  of

contracts.

The

Finally,  common  to all three  interests  was  the  Court  of
Appeals’  concern  that  disclosure  of contributors  to  minor
parties  was  necessary  to  prevent  the  ‘‘stalking  horse’’  role,
whereby  a  minor  party  is created  or  funded  by one  of the
two  major  parties.  But  there  is not  a bit  of evidence  in the
record  to  show  that  the  stalking-horse  problem  is real  or
substantial,  or  that  the  conspiracy  required  to  effectuate
such  a strategy  could  easily  form,  or  that  the  Congress  was
even  concerned  with  such  a  problem.  In  any  event,  dis-
closure  of the  expenditures  of major-party  candidates  or
committees  is an  effective  deterrent.  The  extremely  remote
possibility  of this  problem  is a  grossly  inadequate  basis  to

184

185

justify  the  very  real  harm  that  wholesale  disclosure  may
impose  on  minor-party  or  independent  candidates.

These  various  interests  cannot  sustain  the  indiscrimin-
ate  sweep  of the  FECA’s  disclosure  provisions.  ‘“‘Sometimes
the  grossest  discrimination  can  lie  in  treating  things  that
are  different  as  though  they  were  exactly  alike.’’  Jenness  v.
Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  441-42  (1971).  The  same  statute
which  treats  candidates  and  parties  with  sharp  and
unconstitutional  distinctions  when  it comes  to  disbursing
benefits  in the  form  of public  financing  makes  no  such  dis-
tinctions  in imposing  the  burdens  on  associational  rights  of
reporting  and  disclosure  requirements.  Nor,  similarly,  can
it be  contended  that  securing  the  general  interests  in  dis-
closure  cannot  be  achieved  by less  drastic  means.  In  addi-
tion  to  the  distinctions  employed  in  the  public  financing
provisions,  Congress  could  have  defined  as  minor  parties,
and  spared  from  disclosure,  those  not  qualifying  for  auto-
matic  ballot  access  under  state  law,  thus  incorporating  the
lower  thresholds  employed  by  the  states.  See  Note,  De-
velopments  in  the  Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1111,
1124  n.11  (1975).  Instead,  as  with  the  Communist  Party
membership  cases,  Congress  indiscriminately  cast  the  net
of  disclosure  ‘‘across
a  broad  range  of  associational
activities,”  United  States  v.  Robel,  supra,  389  U.S.  at  265-
66,  without  regard  to  the  widely  varying  interests  at  stake.
See  also Aptheker  v.  Secretary  of State,  378  U.S.  500 (1964).
As  in  those  cases,  the  disclosure  provisions  here  are  defec-
tively overbroad  and  improperly  applied  to the  appellants.

C.  The  Disclosure  Thresholds  Lack  a  Substantial  Nexus

with  the  Claimed  Governmental  Interests  Advanced.

The  first  major  federal  legislation  requiring  disclosure
of  contributions  to  political  candidates  or  committees,
passed  in  1910,  36  Stat.  822,  required  disclosure  of certain
contributions  to  candidates  of  $100  or  more.  Under  the
FECA,  contributions  in  excess  of $100  are  required  to  be

reported.  The triggering  threshold  for  automatic  reporting
of  contributors  is  only  one  penny  higher  than  that  first
enacted  in 1910.

Neither  the  1910  legislation,  nor  the  Federal  Corrupt
Practices  Act  of  1925,  nor  the  FECA  of  1971  has  any
legislative  history  revealing  the  rationale  for the  $100  trig-
gering  threshold.  The  Consumer  Price  Index  rose  397  per-
cent  between  1913  (the  first  year  in  which  statistics  are
available)  and  1974.°  Even  without  considering  the  in-
crease  in  the  cost  of  political  campaigns  since  1910,  a
present  figure  comparable  to  the  1910  threshold  would
have  to be  at least  $397.  The  $100  figure  has  no  greater  ra-
tionality  than  that  it is  a round  number.  Requiring  detailed
record-keeping  of all contributions  in excess  of $10,  subject
to  government  inspection,  is even  more  difficult  to justify.'°  It
constitutes  a  monitoring  of virtually  all  political  contribu-
tions  to  federal  candidates.  To  suggest  that  such  record-
keeping  advances  any  countervailing  interest  in preventing
cerruption  is ludicrous."

‘©  The  ACLU  has  noted  this  difficulty  and  stated  that  ‘‘[s}mall
contributions  which  cannot  purchase  corruption  should  not  be  dis-
closed  ....  It  is  ridiculous  to  suggest  that  contributions  of this  [$10
and  $100] size have  a corrupting  influence  on  presidential  campaigns  or
even  on  congressional  campaigns.  But  while  the  disclosure  of  such
contributions  is irrelevant  to corruption  it seriously  intrudes  on  privacy
since  it provides  a ready-made  list  for employers  to check  on  the  politi-
cal activities  of their employees.’’  ACLU  Report  at  14.

't  One  of the  supervisory  officers  charged  with  enforcing  the  1971
Act,  the  Secretary  of the  Senate,  suggested  that  eliminating  record-
keeping  of all transactions  of less than  $100  would  be a sensible  change
endangering  no  valid  governmental  interests.  Hearings  Before  the  S:  o-
Committee  on  Privileges  and  Elections  and  the  Senate  Committee  on
Rules  and  Administration,  S. 23, et al.,  93d  Cong.,  Ist Sess.  63-64.
