186

187

The  FECA  Amendments  were  enacted.  moreover,
before  one  full  election  cycle  had  taken  place  after  the
effective  date  (April  7,  1972)  of  the  1971  FECA.  The
1974  Act  made  only  minor  changes  in the  1971  Act’s  pro-
visions  relating  to  disclosure  (e.g.,  the  disclosure  threshold
was  raised  from  $100  to  $100.01,  2 U.S.C.  § 434(b)).  There
was  thus  no  opportunity  to  evaluate  the  full  impact  of the
existing  disclosure  requirements,  with  regard  to  modifying
them  or  with  regard  to  any  need  for  more  drastic  legisla-
tion.

Although  Burroughs  v.  United  States  upheld  the  pre-
decessor  disclosure  statute  and  was  cited  with  approval  in
United  States  vy.  Harriss,  the  associational-privacy  cases
decided  since  then  require  much  closer  scrutiny  of whether
the  present  disclosure  requirements  directly  and  precisely
serve  the  interests  claimed  for  them  than  was  afforded
below.”?

The  court  below,  relying  on  the  perceived  absence  of a
showing  of  harm  from  disclosure  in  the  record,  felt  that
there  was  no  need  to  re-examine  Burroughs  and  Harriss  in
that  fashion  and  that  the  interests  served  by disclosure  were
generally  compelling.

It is conceded  that  there  are  a variety  of circumstances
where  disclosure  of the  identity  of contributors  serves  com-

'*  (continued)

The  court  below  ruled  that  the challenge  to the  $10 record-keeping
requirements  and  the  audit  provision  was  “‘unripe’’  since  there  was  no
indication  that  the  FEC  would  automatically  disclose  the identity  of any
such  small  contributors  learned  during  an  audit.  Based  on  the lack  of a
provision  authorizing  public  disclosure  of such  records  by the  F x. the
court  simply  assumed  they had a confidential  status.

'?  The  lobbying  statute  in Harriss  required  disclosure  only of those
contributors  of  more  than  $500  per  year,  a  threshold  significantly
higher  than  the  threshold  here.  In  1954,  $500  could  be far more  “‘influ-
ential’  than  $101  (or $500) could  ever  hope to be today.

naan To

pelling  governmental  interests.  But  even  when  the  govern-
mental  interest  is deemed  compelling,  the  nexus  between
disclosure  and  the  governmental  interest  advanced,  and  the
precision  with  which  the  particular  disclosure  requirements
promote  these  interests,  are  as  important  as  the  degree  of
harm  which  would  result  from  disclosure.'?  Absolutely  no
such  connection  appears  anywhere  in the legislative  history,
or  in  any  of the  evidence  in this  case.  There  is no  evidence
that  any  federal  officials  or  candidates  for  federal  office
have  been  corrupted  by amounts  in the range  of $100.'*  Nor
can  there  be;  common  sense  dictates  that  even  the  most

'?  See,  e.g.,  Gibson  v.  Florida  Legislative  Investigations  Comm'n,
372  U.S.  539  (1963);  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479  (1960);  Bates  v.
Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516 (1960).

Surely,  for  example.  invalidating  a  statute  requiring  disclosure  of
organizational  affiliations  by each  person  writing  a  partisan  letter  to
the  editor  or  a  guest  editorial,  or  a  statute  requiring  voters  to  disclose
how  he or  she  voted,  would  not  turn  on  a showing  that  such  disclosure
would  cause  a given  number  of people  to  lose  their  jobs.  Such  statutes
would  be  infirm,  among  other  reasons,  because  of the  lack  of any  con-
nection  between  a governmental  interest  and  such  disclosure  sufficient
to  warrant  burdening  such  fundamental  First  Amendment  activities.
They would  also  be invalid  because  such  information  is presumed  to be
none  of the government's  business.

So  too  for  the  right  to  associate  with,  contribute  to  and  support
political  causes  and  candidates  whose  views  one  shares,  unless  the  re-
quired  disclosure  is  precisely  tailored  to  those  circumstances  and
amounts  involving  the  possibility  of corruption.  Writing  a  check  to  a
candidate  is a  very  fundamental  political  act.  One-third  of those  sur-
veyed  in  an  opinion  poll  had  contributed  financially  to  a  political
campaign.  Other  studies  show  that,  next  to  actually  voting,  making  a
political  contribution  ranks  with  wearing  a  political  button  or  dis-
playing  a  political  sticker  as
a  common  form  of  political  activity.
Findings  I, 107,  p.97;  App.  II-147.

'*  Indeed,  the  Common  Cause  information  on  contributors  to  the
Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  does  not  even  contain  a
category  for  contributors  of less  than  $5,000.  Findings  II,  A 119,  pp.
157-81;  App.  II 508-64.

188

189

corrupt  Members  of Congress  will  not  be  “bought”  for  so
small  an  amount.'*  Sen.  Humphrey,  a  supporter  of  the
challenged  legislation,  made  precisely  that  point,  120 Cong.
Rec.,  S 5540 (daily ed.  April 9, 1974).

The  NAACP  cases  show  that  the  substantial-con-
nection  element  requires  not  merely  a  general  connection
between  the  governmental  interest  (preventing  corruption)
and  the  means  employed  to achieve  that  interest  (disclosure
of contributions),  but  rather  a specific  relationship  between
the  achievement  of the  general  interest  and  the  particular
disclosure  requirement.  See,  e.g.,  Bates  v.  Little  Rock,
supra,  at  524-25.  Thus,  the  substantial-nexus  test  is  not
whether  disclosure  is  generally  valid,  but  whether  it  is
specifically  necessary.  Here,  for the  reasons  set  forth  above,
application  of the  requirements  to many  of these  appellants
and  other  minor  parties  like  them  does  not  meet  the  sub-
stantial-connection  test.

The  court  below  found  disclosure  necessary  to prevent
the  secrecy  ‘‘so  essential  to  the  appearance  and  reality  of
influence  dealing’  (op.  at  1547).  Disclosure  of  some
contributions  will  have  these  salutary  effects,  but  the
challenged  statutes  reach  well  beyond  that.

The  $10  and  $100  thresholds  apply  to  all  elections  for
federal  office.  In  a  clear  example  of legislative  failure  to
evaluate  with  precision  the  means  necessary  to  attain  the
goal  of  removing  corruption  from  the  electoral  process,
Congress  made  no  effort  to  calibrate  the  disclosure  thres-
hold  requirements  with  respect  to  different  federal  offices,

'S  The  ABA  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform  endorsed
disclosure,  but  it  did  mot  endorse  the  FECA’s  triggering  thresholds.
Indeed,  its  Report  explicitly  stated  that  ‘‘.  .
.  disclosure  should  be  re-
quired  of  those  amounts  which  could  tend  to  improperly  influence
office  holders  and  policy."  ABA  Report  at  S. As  the  text  indicates,  no
one  could  argue  that  $10  or  $100  are  amounts  that  could  tend  to
influence  improperly  candidates  for federal  office.

as  it did  with  expenditure  limits,  see  18 U.S.C.  § 608.  That
there  was  no  similar  effort  to  arrive  at  different  disclosure
thresholds  indicates  that  Congress  did  not  seriously  con-
sider  either  the  impact  or  the  intent  of the  $100  threshold
and  clearly  did  not  employ the least  drastic  means  to attain
its goals.  In Shelton  v.  Tucker,  supra,  this Court  held:

ities — ———

“...even  though  the  governmental  purpose  be
legitimate  and  substantial,  that  purpose  cannot
be  pursued  by means  that  broadly  stifle  funda-
mental  personal  liberties  when  the  end  can  be
more  narrowly  achieved.  The  breadth  of legisla-
tive  abridgement  must  be  viewed  in  the  light  of
less  drastic  means  for  achieving  the  same  basic
purpose.”  364 U.S.  at 488.

See  aiso  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  supra,  at  56-60;  American
Party  of Texas  vy.  White,  supra,  at  780-81.  These  standards
require  closer  scrutiny  than  the  ‘reasonable  latitude  as  to
where  to  draw  the  line”  which  the  court  below  afforded  to
Congress.

A  more  carefully  drafted  system  of disclosure,  fixing
the  disclosure  thresholds  at  levels  which  truly  protect  the
integrity  of the  electoral  process,  would  be constitutionally
permissible.  The  FECA,  however,  has  not  attempted  to
draw  such  lines  with  precision.

D. Requiring  Disclosure  by Individuals  Who Make  Inde-
pendent  Expenditures  Unconstitutionally  Chills  Freedom
of Speech.

Every  person  (other  than  a  political  committee  or
candidate)  who  makes  contributions  or  expenditures  other
than  by contribution  to  a  political  committee  or  candidate
in excess  of $100  per calendar  year  must  file with  the FEC  a
statement  giving  his  full  name,  address,  occupation  and
principal  place  of business,  and  periodic  reports  giving  the
same  information  required  in  reports  by  political  com-

190

19]

mittees.  That  includes  identifying  each  person  to  whom  an
expenditure  is made,  the  amount,  date  and  purpose  of each
such  expenditure,  and  an  allocation  of expenditures  among
candidates  if  more  than  one  was  involved.  2  U.S.C.  §§
434(bX9),  434(e).

The  requirement  is the same  as  that  imposed  on  politi-
cal  committees,  but  with  one  vital  difference.  Section  434(e)
requires  individual  citizens  to  report  political  activity
costing  more  than  $100  directly  to  the  FEC,  upon  pain  of
criminal  penalties  if they fail to do so.  Jd.,  § 441."*

In  a  one-paragraph  discussion  of  this  section,  the
Court  of Appeals  said  only that,  if the  limit  on  independent
expenditures  is constitutional,  so  too  is  a requirement  that
such  expenditures  be  disclosed  (op.  at  1549).  The  court
below  did  not  even  mention,  let  alone  distinguish,  the  one
case  that  is squarely  on  point,  and  squarely  contradictory  to
Section  434e)}—Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.  60 (1960).

Here,  as  in  Talley,  the  challenged  statute  clearly
encompasses  the distribution  of handbills  and  leaflets  deai-
ing with  politica!  subjects.  Here,  as  in Talley,  the  statute  is
not  limited  to  “*...  providing  a  way  to  identify  those  re-
sponsible  for fraud.  false  advertising  and  libel.”’  362  U.S.  at
64.  Here,  as  in Talley,  ‘‘[t}here  can  be no  doubt  that  such  an
identification  requirement  would  tend  to  restrict  freedom
to  distribute  information  and  thereby  freedom  of  ex-
pression,”’  id.

In  several  respects,  Section  434(e)  is  actually  more
burdensome  and  chilling  than  the  municipal  ordinance
stricken  down  in  Talley.  There,  the  only  requirement  was
that  handbills  and  leaflets  had  to contain  on  their  face  cer-

‘*  It appears  from  the  statute  and  the  FEC’s  forms  that,  once  the
reporting  requirement  has  been  triggered  by  the  making  of  one
expenditure  in excess  of $100,  a/l expenditures  by that  individual  must
be reported  whether  in excess  of $100 or  not.

tain  information,  362  U.S.  at  60-61.  Section  434(e),  how-
ever,  affirmatively  requires  individual  citizens  to  report
their  expenditures  directly  to  a  governmental  agency.
Immense  chilling effects  are  created  by requiring  persons  to
write  to the  FEC  and  inform  it of their  political  activities.

The  Tallcy  decision  did  not  require  proof  of harass-
ment  or  reprisals.  Rather,  it  declared  the  Los  Angeles
ordinance  void  on  its face,  362  U.S.  at  65,  because  as  Mr.
.  it will  not  do for the  State  simply
Justice  Harlan  said,  **.  .
to say that  the circulation  of all  anonymous  handbills  must
be suppressed  in order  to  identify  the  distributors  of those
that  may  be  of an  obnoxious  character.””  362  U.S.  at  66
(Harlan,  J., concurring).  Accord,  Zwickler  vy.  Koota,  290  F.
Supp.  244  (E.D.N.Y.  1968),  rev'd  on  other  grounds  sub
nom.  Golden  v. Zwickler,  394 U.S.  1031  (1969).

If the  important  role  played  by handbills,  leaflets,  and
brochures—all  of which  are  forms  of spontaneous  political
communication—is  to be preserved,  Section  434e)  must  be
declared  unconstitutional.  It  is  flatly  inconsistent  with
Talley  v.  California,  supra,  and  its effects  will  be even  more
serious  than  the  other  disclosure  provisions  challenged
herein.  If the  “breathing  space,’””  NAACP  vy.  Button,  371
U.S.  415,  433  (1963),  necessary  to  protect  the  First  Amend-
ment  is to be preserved,  Section  434e) must  fall.

Finally,  apart  from  the statute’s  direct  conflict  with  the
protection  of political  anonymity  recognized  in  Talley,  the
very  real,  practical  burdens  of the  reporting  requirements
themselves  are  certain  to  deter  individuals  from  making
expenditures  for  their  independent  political  speech.  Core
First  Amendment  activities  cannot  be  thus  burdened.  In
Thomas  v.  Collins,  323  U.S.  516  (1945),  a Texas  statute  re-
quired  labor  organizers  simply  to  register  with  a local  offi-
cial  in  order  to  give  a  speech.  This  Court,  though  recog-
nizing  that  such  requirements  might  be  justifiable  where
solicitations  of funds  or  subscriptions  were  concerned,  held
to the contrary  where  speech  alone  was  involved:

192

“If one  who  solicits  support  for the cause  of labor
may  be  required  to  register  as  a  condition  to  the
exercise  of his  right  to  make  a  public  speech,  so
may  he who  seeks  to  rally  support  for  any  social,
business,  religious  or  political  cause.  We  think  a
requirement  iat  one  must  register  before  he
undertakes  to  make  a  public  speech  to  enlist
support  for  a  lawful  movement  is  quite  incom-
patible  with  the requirements  of the  First  Amend-
ment.’’  323  U.S.  at 540.

is

‘“‘quite

If  a  simple  registration  requirement
in-
compatible”  with  the  First  Amendment  when  imposed  as
the  precondition  of making  a speech,  then  the far more  de-
tailed  and  burdensome  reporting  requirements  of Section
434(e)  can  surely  not  be sustained  solely  on  the ground  that
they  will  help  enforcement  of  a  provision  which  is  itself
thoroughly  suspect.

193

Vill.

The  Enforcement  Provisions  of the Challenged  Legislation
Violate  the Constitution.

A.  The Method  of Appointment  of Members  of the FEC

Is Unconstitutional.

Two  members  of  the  Federal  Election  Commission
(FEC)  are  appointed  by the  President  pro  tem  of the  Senate
upon  the  recommendation  of  the  majority  and  minority
leaders;  two  are  appointed  by the  Speaker  of the  House
upon  the  recommendation  of  the  majority  and  minority
leaders;  and  two  are  appointed  by  the  President.  All
nominees  must  be  confirmed  by both  Houses.  2 U.S.C.  §
437c(a)."

The  Constitution  explicitly  vests  the  appointment

power  in the President:

“{The  President]  shall  nominate,  and  by and
with  the  advice  and  consent  of the  Senate,  shall
appoint  ambassadors,  other  public  ministers  and
consuls,  judges  of  the  Supreme  Court,  and  all
other  officers  of the United  States,  whose  appoint-
ments  are  not  herein  otherwise  provided  for,  and

'  Rep.  Frenzel,  one  of the  strongest  supporters  of the  FEC,  stated
after  the  passage  of  the  FECA  Amendments  that  the  FEC  was
“unique”  among  federal  agencies  because  of  its  members’  mode  of
appointment.  120 Cong.  Rec.  H 10908 (daily ed.  November  20,  1974.)

Congress  has,  on  many  occasions,  exercised  the  power  of appoint-
ment.  In no  case,  however,  has  it attempted  to exercise  this  power  with
respect  to a department,  agency  or  board  with  the vast  powers  vested  in
the  FEC.  The  Rock  Creek  Park  Committee  and  the Committee  to Cele-
brate  100  Years  of Peace  and  Independence  are  of a  wholly  different
dimension.  And  even  these  exercises  of the  appointment  power  have
never  been  approved  by judicial  decision.

194

195

which  shall  be  established  by  law;  but  the
Congress  may  by law vest  the appointment  of such
inferior  officers,  as  they think  proper,  in the  Presi-
dent  alone,  in the  courts  of law,  or  in the  heads  of
departments.”’  Article  II, Section  2.

The  six  FEC  members  are  “‘officers  of the  United  States.”
They  clearly  have  executive  and  judicial  functions  to  per-
form.  The  FEC  has  the  power  to  investigate  violations  of
the  FECA,  to  exercise  primary  jurisdiction  over  civil
enforcement,  to render  advisory  opinions,  to prescribe  rules
and  regulations  implementing  the  Act,  to  determine  eligi-
bility  for  public  financing,  and  to disqualify  a person  from
running  for  federal  office.  2 U.S.C.  §§ 437d,  437f,  437g,
and  456.  This  enormous  executive  and  judicial  power  is
magnified  because  the  provisions  of the  FECA  are  so  vague
and  delegate  so  much  power  to the  FEC.  The  holding  below
that  persons  wielding  such  power  are  not  “‘officers  of the
United  States’’  most  seriously  distorts  the  separation  of
powers  embodied  in the Constitution.

(1)  The  Appointments  Clause  Is  an  Important  and

Carefully  Considered  Part  of the Constitutional  Scheme.

The  history  of the  appointments  clause  demonstrates
beyond  doubt  that  the  constitutional  language  was  deliber-
ately  chosen.  At the  time  of the  Constitutional  Convention,
all  state  constitutions  save  that  of New  York  vested  the  ap-
pointing  power  in  the  legislature.  See  The  Federalist,  No.
47,  Cooke  ed.  1961,  p.  323;  Thach,  The  Creation  of the
Presidency,  1776-1789,  33 et  seq.  (1969).  The  Framers  were
clearly  aware  of this  fact,  for  the  locus  of the  appointment
power  was  a  matter  of heated  debate  in the  states.  In  1778,
for  example,  a  proposed  Massachusetts  constitution  failed
of ratification  partly  because  of its  weak  executive.  Many
Massachusetts  towns  submitted  written  reasons  for  re-
jecting  that  constitution.  Among  these,  the  important
‘Essex  Result”  emphasized  that  the  proposed  Constitution

’

— a nr

did  not  fix the  appointment  power  in the executive  branch.
Thach,  supra,  at 47.

At  the  federal  level,  the  vesting  by  the  Articles  of
Confederation  of  the  power  of  appointment  in  Congress
was  a  major  source  of dissatisfaction.  For  instance,  ‘“‘fac-
tional  differences  kept  important  offices  unfilled  for  long
periods.”  Thach,  supra,  at 73; op.  at  1572.

At  the  Constitutional  Convention  the  appointment
power  was  highly controversial:  some  wanted  it to remain  in
the  legislative  branch  while  others  desired  to  entrust  it
exclusively  to  the  executive.  The  ultimate  resolution,  in
language  as  carefully  and  deliberately  drafted  as  any  in the
Constitution,  was  a compromise:  the power  of appointment
would  be lodged  in the  executive,  but  the  Senate  must  give
its  advice  and  consent.  2 Farrand,  Records  of the  Federal
Convention  535-39  (1934  ed.);  3 id.  at 99.

This  compromise  was  intended  to  exclude  the  legisla-
tive  branch  from  the  power  to  nominate  officers  of  the
United  States,  as  both  the  explicit  consitutional  language
and  the  statements  of the  Framers  make  clear.  Hamilton
wrote,

“It  will  be  the  office  of  the  President  to
nominate,  and,  with  the  advice  and  consent  of the
Senate,  to  appoint.  There  will,  of course,  be  no
exertion  of choice  on  the  part  of the  Senate.  They
may  defeat  one  choice  of the  executive  and  oblige
him  to  make  another  but  they cannot  themseives
choose.  They can  only ratify or  reject the choice  he
may  have  made.  They  might  entertain  a  prefer-
ence  to  some  other  person  at  the  very  moment
they  were  assenting  to the  one  proposed,  because
there  might  be no  positive  ground  of opposition  to
him;  and  they  could  not  be  sure,  if they withheld
their  assent,  that  the  subsequent  nomination

196

197

would  fall  upon  their  own  favorite,  or  upon  any
other  person  in their  estimation  more  meritorious
than  the  one  rejected.’’  The  Federalist,  No.  66,
Cooke  ed.  1961,  p. 449 (emphasis  in original).

In  The  Federalist,  No.  47,  Madison  wrote:

“The  accumulation  of all  powers,  legislative,
inthe  same  hands,
executive,  and  judiciary,
whether  of  one,
a  few,  or  many,  and  whether
hereditary,  self-appointed,  or  elective,  may  justly
be  pronounced  the  very  definition  of  tyranny.”
Cooke  ed.  1961,  p. 324.

He  recognized  the  particular  pertinence  of these  principles
to the appointment  power:

“each  department  should  have  a  will  of its  own,
and  consequently  should  be  so  constituted  that
the  members  of each  should  have  as  little  agency
as  possible  in  the appointment  of the  members  of
the  others."”  The  Federalist,  No.  51,  Cooke  ed.
1961,  p. 348.

During  the  first  Congress  Madison  said,  ‘I conceive  that  if
any  power  whatsoever  is  in  its  nature  executive,  it  is  the
power  of appointing,  overseeing  and  controlling  those  who
execute  the  laws,’’  1 Annals  of Congress  481  (1789),  and
that  ‘if  the  legislature  determines  the  powers,  the  honors,
and  emoluments  of an  office  we  should  be  insecure  if they
were  to  designate  the  officer  also,”  id.  at  581-82  (emphasis
added).’

In  The  Federalist,  No.  76,  Hamilton  made  the  point
even  clearer  when  he argued  that  the  Senate’s  effective  veto
on  appointments  would  be beneficial:

*  See  generally  The  Federalist,  No.  51,  Cooke  ed.  1961,  pp.  347-

SO; id.,  No.  73, at 494-95,

“...the  necessity  of  their  concurrence
would  have  a powerful,  though  in general, a silent
operation.  It would  be  an  excellent  check  upon  a
spirit  of  favoritism  in  the  President,  anc  would
tend  greatly  to  prevent  the  appointment  of unfit
characters  from  state  prejudice,  from  family  con-
nection,  from  personal  attachment,  or  from  a view
to popularity.’’  Cooke  ed.  1961,  p. 513.

Finally,  Jefferson  stated  in a letter:

is  an

“Nomination

executive
function.  To give it to the  legislature  as  we  do is a
violation  of the  principle  of separation  of powers.
It swerves  member

tion  to  contract  fc  office  for  themselves  and  to

People,  3 I.  79,  141  (1839).

What  case  law  there  is strongly  supports  these  proposi-
tions.  In Springer  v.  Government  of the Philippine  Islands,
277  U.S.  189 (1928),  the  Governor  General  of the  Philippines
challenged  the  validity  of  an  act  of  the  Philippines
legislature  creating  the  National  Coal  Company  and  the
Philippine  National  Bank.  That  legislation  provided  that
the  Board  of Control  would  be  composed  of the  Govenor
General,  the  President  of the  Philippines  Senate,  and  the
Speaker  of  the  Philippines  House.  The  President  and
Speaker  were  to  elect  the  directors  of the  company.  This

*  See  also  Cutler  and  Johnson,  ‘Regulation  and  the  Political
Process,”  84  Yale  L.J.  1395,  1411  (1975):  “The  President  is the  only
nationally  elected  officer  and  thus,  at  least  arguably,  our  most  politi-
cally accountable  official.”

198

199

Court  held  that  the organic  act  of the  Philippines  vested  the
Governor  General  with  executive  power,  including  the
power  to  appoint  governmental  officials.  The  legislation
was  held  invalid  on  the  ground  that  the  legislature  could
not  confer  an  executive  power  on  some  other  body  or  per-
son.

In  United  States  vy.  Ferreira,  54  U.S.  39 (1851),  Chief
Justice  Taney,  referring  to  commissioners  empowered  to
decide  claims  arising  under  a treaty  with  Spain,  wrote  that:

power  of  appointment

‘“...the
is  in  the
President,  by and  with  the  advice  and  consent  of
the  senate;  and  Congress  could  not  by  law,
designate  the  persons  to  fill  these  offices.’’  Jd.  at
50-51.

The  limited  nature  of  the  Article  I],  § 2,  cl.  2  grant  of
participation  by the  Congress  in the  appointment  of execu-
tive  officers  was  made  plain  in Myers  v.  United  States,  272
U.S.  52,  164  (1926):  “...the  provisions  of  the  second
section  of Article  II,  which  blend  action  by the  legislative
branch,  or  by part  of it,  in  the  work  of the  executive,  are
limitations  to  be  strictly  construed  and  not  to  be  extended
by implication.””  See  also  Humphrey's  Executor  v.  United
States,  295  U.S.  602,  625-26  (1935),  noting  that  the  Federal
Trade  Commission  is independent  of the  executive  “except
in its selection’  (emphasis  in original).*

Scholars  have  similarly  concluded  that  “Congress  has
no  authority  to appoint  persons  to the  Federal  Government
or  to  indicate  the  persons  to  be  appointed  by the  Execu-
tive.’’  2 Antieau,  Modern  Constitutional  Law  § 13:14,  at
514  (1969);  see  also  Mechem,  Public  Offices  and  Officers,
§§  102-06,  at  42-44  (1890).  Two  Attorneys  General  have

*  See  also  Ponzi  v.  Fessenden,  258  U.S.  254,  262  (1922);  J.W.
Hampton,  Jr.  &  Co.  v.  United  States,  276  U.S.  394,  406  (1928);
O'Donoghue  v.  United  States,  289 U.S.  516,  530 (1933).

concurred  in this  view.  37 Ops.  Att’y Gen.  56, 58 (1933);  39

(President  Arthur);  H.  Doc.  No.  529,  72nd  Cong.  2d  Sess.,  76

7Sth  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.,  81  Cong.  Rec.  4779  (1937)  (President

VII,  1634-35 (1971) (President  Nixon).

(2)  The  FEC'’s  Members  Are  Officers  of  the  United

States  and Must  Therefore  Be  Appointed  by the President.

The  Court  of  Appeals  stated  that  ‘Congress  cannot
constitutionally  appoint  the  members  of a  cormmission  or
agency  that  performs  primarily  executive  or  judicial  func-
tions’’  (op.  at  1575).  That  is an  incorrect  statement  of the
proper  constitutional  standard.  A// federal  officials  meeting
certain  tests  of tenure,  duration,  emoluments,  and  duties
are  officers  of  the  United  States,  and  therefore  must  be
appointed  by  the  President,  the  courts  or  the  head  of  a
department  pursuant  to  the  Constitution.  The  only
exception  is  that,  when  subordinate  employees  of  the
legislative  and  judicial  branches  perform  solely  functions
which  those  branches  could  perform  directly,  it necessarily
follows  that  they may  be appointed  by those  branches.  Thus
the  proper  test  is  not  how  to  characterize  the  ‘‘primary”’
functions  of  an  agency—inevitably  a  difficult  and  sub-
jective  test—but  whether  the  agency  is charged  with  per-
forming  any  substantial  functions  which  under  the  con-
stitutional  scheme  are  to  be  performed  by officers  of the
United  States.  Under  that  test,  there  can  be no  doubt  of the
outcome  here.°

ee DP ee oe

ee

*  See  pp.  204-07,  infra,  for our  alternative  argument  that  even  if the
Court  of Appeals’  standard  is correct,  the  FEC does  perform  ‘‘primarily
executive  or  judicial  functions,"’  and  accordingly,  on  the  Court  of Ap-
peals’ own  reasoning,  its members  must  be appointed  by the  President.

200

201

In  United  States  v.  Hartwell,  73  U.S.  385,  393  (1867),

this  Court  said:

“An  office  is  a  public  station,  or  employment,
conferred  by the  appointment  of government.  The
term  embraces  the  ideas  of  tenure,  duration,
emolument,  and  duties.”’

The  Court  held  that  a  Treasury  clerk  was  an  officer,
noting  that  his duties  ‘‘were  continuing  and  permanent,  not
occasional  or  temporary,”’  id.  The  FEC  members  are  clearly
officers  under  the  Hartwell  test.  Contrast  United  States  v.
Germaine,  99  U.S.  508,  512  (1878).  In  18  Ops.  Att’y  Gen.
409  (1886),  the  Attorney  General  stated  that  the  President
must  appoint  the  Chief  of  Examiners  of the  Civil  Service
Commission,  saying:

“The  examiner  is certainly,  under  this  act,  an  of-
ficer,  as  distinguished  from  a  mere  agent,  clerk,
or  employee.  His  station  embraces  the  ideas  of
tenure,  duration,  emolument,  and  duties,  and
then  becomes  an  office.”’  See  also  11  Ops.  Att’y
Gen.  209 (1865).

Finally,  in  United  States  vy.  Maurice,  26  Fed.  Cas.
1211,  1214  (Cir.  Ct.  D.  Va.  1823),  Chief  Justice  Marshall
FEC
stated  a  similar  test  for  ‘‘officership,”’  also  met  by the
members:

“Although  an  office  is  ‘an  employment’  it  does
not  follow  that  every  employment  is an  office.  A
man  may  certainly  be employed  under  a contract,
express  or  implied,  to  do  an  act.  or  perform  a
service,  without  becoming  an  officer.  But  if a duty
be  a  continuing  one,  which  is  defined  by  rules
prescribed  by  the  government,  and  not  by  con-
tract  which  an  individual  is appointed  by govern-
ment  to  perform,  who  enters  on  the  duties  apper-
taining  to  his  station,  without  any  contract  de-

fining  them,  if those  duties  continue,  though  the oO

person  be  changed;  it seems  very  difficult  to  dis-
tinguish  such  a  charge  or  employment  from  an
office,  or  the person  who  performs  the duties  from
an  Office.  If it may  be converted  into  a contract,  it
must  be  a  contract  to  perform  the  duties  of the
office  of agent  of fortifications,  and  such  an  office
must  exist  with  ascertained  duties,  or  there  is no
standard  by which  the  extent  of the  condition  can
be measured.”’

See  also  Mechem,  supra,  §§ 1-20,  at  1-9.

The  congressional  attempt  to appoint  a majority  of the
members  of the  FEC  is flatly at odds  with  the language,  his-
tory  and  case  law  of the  Constitution.  See  Watson,  ‘“‘Con-
gress  Steps  Out;  A  Look  at  Congressional  Control  of
the  Executive,’’  63  Cal.  L.  Rev.  983,  1037-43  (1975).  Were
the  Court  to  permit  this  usurpation  of  executive  power,
there  would  be no  principled  constitutional  bar  to congres-
sional  appointment  of the  members  of the  Securities  and
Exchange  Commission  or  the  National  Labor  Relations
Board.  Cf.  Gravel  vy.  United  States,  408  U.S.  606,  624-25
(1972).°

The  majority  below  apparently  believed  that  appel-
lants’  arguments  would  preclude  congressional  appoint-
mcnt  of its own  inferior  officers  (op.  at  1572).  This  is plainly
incorrect,  for  such  inferior  officers  are  not  “officers  of the
United  States’’  subject  to  presidential  appointment.  The
power  of the  legislative  and  judicial  branches  to select  their
own  inferior  officers  to assist  them  in their  day-to-day  func-
tioning  (¢.g.,  the  Secretary  of the  Senate  or  the  Doorkeeper
of the  House)  is  wholly  consistent  with  the  separation  of
powers,  and  indeed  necessarily  follows  from  that  very

*  Indeed,  Congress  is now  considering  a  proposal  that  would  vest
the  appointment  of several  Governors  of the  Postal  Service  in Congress.
H.R.  2445,  94th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  (1975) § 8.

202

203

principle.  Thus,  such  ‘‘officers  of the  court”  as  bailiffs  are
not  “‘officers  of the  United  States’’  and  are  appointed  by
judges  of federal  courts.  United  States  v.  Swift,  139  F. 225,
227 (1st  Cir.  1905S),  The  same  principle  applies  to the Secre-
tary  of the  Senate  and  similar  positions.  If it were  other-
wise,  the  executive  would  be  able  to  encroach  into  the
legislative  and judicial  spheres,  just as  in the  FEC’s  mode of
appointment  Congress  has  encroached  into  the  executive
sphere.

Clearly,  bailiffs  (as agents  of the courts)  and  the  House
Doorkeeper  and  others  (as agents  of the Congress)  perform
only  judicial  and  legislative  functions  respectively.  Every-
thing  they  do  could  be done  by a judge  or  justice  or  by the
Congress.  That  is  not  the  case  with  the  FEC.  Congress’
function  is  to  enact  laws,  not  to  render  advisory  opinions
about  the  effects  of statutes  already  passed,  not  to set  gen-
eral  policy  with  respect  to such  statutes,  and  certainly  not  to
enforce  such  statutes  through  civil  actions.

Indeed,  the  Court  of Appeals  agrees  that  “‘the  Com-
mission  has...  been  delegated  various  quasi-judicial  and
quasi-executive  powers”’  (op.  at  1575).  Because  it did  not
address  these  powers  on  grounds  of lack  of ripeness,  it de-
clined  to  ‘invalidate  on  its  face  a  statutory  provision  that
confers’’  such  powers,  id.  at  1576.  Judge  MucKinnon’s  dis-
sent  provides  the complete  answer  to this contention:

.

.

“In  light of the deep and  pervasive  involvement  of
the  Commission  in  the  present  and  anticipated
political  affairs  of the plaintiffs  .

the  normal  statutory  enforcement  procedure

.  I would  hold  that  [Questions

Judge  Tamm’s  dissent  underlined  the  practical  reasons  for
considering  these  issues  ripe:  “‘It  is clear  that  all  planning
in  this  political  season  must  be  made  with  FECA  and  the
Commission  in  mind”’  (op.  at  1607).  Finally,  the  FEC  has
now  begun  promulgating  rules,  including  rules  relating  to
Title  18,’  see,  e.g.,  40 Fed.  Reg.  32951  (Aug.  5, 1975)  (FEC
Notice  1975-18).  Political  candidates,  parties  and  com-
mittees  must  follow  these  rules  in their  daily  activities,  and
their  actions  are  inevitably  affected  thereby.

The  court  below  also  relied  on  the  suggestion  made  by
the  FEC’s  Special  Counsel  during  oral  argument  that  the
FEC  “would  not  utilize  in [sic]  its civil  enforcement  power
but  would  instead  refer  appropriate  cases  to  the  Depart-
ment  of  Justice.  ..””  (op.  at  1576).  The  court  specifically
stated  that  the civil-enforcement  power  raised  ‘‘very  serious
constitutional  questions,”’  but  believed  that  the  issue  posed
by that  power  was  not  ripe  for  adjudication  since  no  party
had  been  joined  in  a  civil-enforcement  action  initiated  by
the  FEC, id.

Yet by letter  of August  28, 1975,  FEC  Chairman  Curtis
abandoned  the  position  taken  by the  FEC  at oral  argument
and  threatened  appellant  Committee  for  a  Constitutional

Moreover,  since  appellants  make a facial  attack  on  the
mode  of appointment  of the  FEC  it is of no  consequence
whether  or  not  the  FEC  may  use  or  has  used  all  its  pow-
ers.  The  question
writ-
ten—and  which  beyond  question  grants  such  pow-
ers—stands  or  falls  as  a  violation  of  the  separation  of
powers  and  due  process  doctrines.  Even  if the  court  below
was  correct  that  the  constitutionality  of as-yet-unexercised
powers  of the  FEC  is not  yet ripe for review  as  distinct  ques-
tions,  a  quite  different  issue  is presented  by appellants’  at-
tack  on  the  method  of  appointment—an  issue  no  one
claims  is  unripe.  In  determining  whether  the  Com-
missioners  are  officers  of  the  United  States,  this  Court
should  look  not  only to powers  they have  thus  far exercised,
but  to  all  the  powers  Congress  gave  them,  for  the  question
is whether  the  offices  which  Congress  created  and  intended
to  be  performed  are  in  their  nature  such  that  only  the
President  can  constitutionally  make  appointments  to them.

Judge  MacKinnon’s  dissenting  view  that  the  advisory-
opinion  power  interferes  with  the  judgments  of  federal
prosecutors  interpreting  the  criminal  statutes  (op. at  1579).
The  advisory-opinion  power  is in a sense  judicial®  and  in a
stronger  sense  executive;  what  is  clear  is  that  it  is  in  no
sense  legislative.  From  every  practical  standpoint  it is the
most  important  of  the  FEC’s  powers.  Thus  an  agency
possessing  that  power,  and  exercising  it on  a massive  scale,
cannot  possibly  be  held  to  be  “‘primarily  legislative”  in  its
functions.

respecting

Imagine  the  situation  that  might  exist  if a  legislature

could
opinions
previously  enacted.  Fear  of favoritism  and  undue  influence
is one  of the  stated  rationales  for  much  of the  challenged
legislation.  The  ability  of  Congress  to  render  advisory
opinions,  especially  in an  area  such  as  electoral  law,  would
open  unprecedented  vistas  of favoritism.  Because  advisory
opinions  contemplate  the  application  of  statutory  pro-
visions  to  specific  and  concrete  factual  situations,  they
clearly  fall  outside  the category  of legislative  decisions.  The
Court  of Appeals  erred  in holding  that  the  FEC’s  advisory-
opinion  power  was  legislative.

The  issue  of the  FEC’s  mode  of appointment  is ripe for
review,  and  requires  a  decision  that  that  mode  of appoint-
ment  is facially  unconstitutional  as  an  abridgement  of the
President’s  power  to  appoint  “officers  of  the  United
States.”

(3)  The  FEC  Performs  Primarily  Executive  and
Judicial  Functions;  Its  Members  Must  Therefore  Be  Ap-
pointed  by the President.

The  court  below  considered  only  one  of  the  FEC’s
powers,  the  power  to  render  advisory  opinions;  it declined
to  address  the  rest  of the  powers  on  the  ground  they  were
not  ripe  for  review  (op.  at  1576).  Even  as  to  advisory
opinions,  however,  the  majority  below  did  not  address

In  support  of its  contention  that  the  FEC  may  be  ap-
pointed  by Congress,  the  Court  of Appeals  compares  some
of the  FEC’s  functions  to  the  Comptroller  General's  cer-
tification  and  audit  functions.  But,  as  Judge  MacKinnon
points  out  in dissent,  the  Comptroller  General  is appointed
by the  President,  31  U.S.C.  § 42,  op.  at  1615.  The  Comp-
troller  General,  like  the  FEC,  performs  functions  which  are
partly  legislative  and  partly  executive,  United  States  v.
Stewart,  234  F.  Supp.  94,  99 (D.D.C.),  aff'd,  339  F2d  753
(D.C.  Cir.  1964).

The  FEC’s  other  powers  are  even  more  clearly  execu-

tive  or  judicial.  The  civil-enforcement  power,  2 U.S.  C.  §

*  See  United  States  v.  M’Cabe,  129 F. 708,  711  (1st  Cir.  1904).

206

207

437g,  which  includes  receiving  complaints,  investigating
violations,  attempting  to  correct  violations  by conciliation
and  persuasion,  and  instituting  civil-enforcement  actions,
is executive  beyond  question.  To emphasize  the  importance
of the  civil-enforcement  power,  Section  437c(b)  grants  the
FEC  primary  civil  enforcement  power  over  the  FECA  and
certain  enumerated  sections  of  Title  18.  And  if the  FEC
requests,  the  Attorney  General  “‘shall”  institute  civil-
enforcement  actions  and  “‘shall’’  report  to  the  FEC  within
60 days as  to the action  he has taken.  § 437g(a) (7), (b).

The  legislative  sponsors  clearly  envisaged  a  vigorous
enforcer,  not  one  that  would  abdicate  its responsibilities  at
the  first  challenge.  See  remarks  of Rep.  Frenzel,  a key sup-
porter  of the  FEC,  120  Cong.  Rec.  H 10908  (daily ed.  Nov.
20,  1974).  Similarly,  the  FEC’s  powers  “to  formulate
general  policy,”  2 U.5.C.  § 437d(a)  (9),  and  to  make  rules,
id.,  § 437d(a)  (8),  are  quintessentially  policy-making  func-
tions  falling within  the executive  sphere.

Although  in  its  brief  filed  in  the  Court  of  Appeals
(FEC  Brief  at  5S),  the
FEC  attempted  to  disavow  any  rule-
making  power  with  respect  to  Title  18,  U.S.C.,  in  its  first
action  dealing  with  an  actual  election  (the  special  New
FEC  issued  sweeping  ‘‘interim
Hampshire  Senate  race)  the
_  guidelines”  replete  with  rules  referring  to  and  interpreting
sections  of  Title  18,  FEC  Notice  1975-34,  40  Fed.  Reg.
40668-71  (Sept.  3,  1975S).  The  rulemaking  power  has  thus
now  been  exercised,  and  its  validity  is  plainly  ripe  for
decision.

With  respect  to  the  operation  of Subtitle  H,  the  FEC
has  numerous  powers,  all  of  which  are  plainly  executive.
The  FEC  must  certify  the  eligibility  to  receive  payments  of
candidates  during  the  nominating,  national-convention,
and  general-election  stages  of  the  campaign,  §§  9005,
9008(g),  9036.  The  FEC  must  determine  whether  circum-
stances  warrant  repayments  from  candidates  or  parties

back  to  the  Treasury,  §§ 9007,  9008(h),  9038.  The  FEC  is
authorized  to  issue  such  regulations  as  it deems  necessary
to  carry  out  its  duties  and  functions  under  Subtitle  H, §§
9009,  9039.  And  finally,  it has  civil-enforcement  power  to
implement  the federal  subsidy  provisions,  §§ 9011,  9040.

The  sum  effect  of the  FEC’s  battery  of powers  is that
the  FEC  is  the  enforcer  of  the  challenged  statutes.  Its
record-keeping  functions,  2 U.S.C.  § 438  (deemed  to  be
legislative  by the  court  below,  op.  at  1616-17),  are  merely
supportive  of its  enforcement  powers.  To  hold  to  the  con-
trary—that  the enforcement  provisions  are  merely  adjuncts
of the  record-keeping  duties—would,  as  the  court  below
said  in a different  context  (op. at  1582),  be ‘“‘Aesopian  in the
sense  of the  dog losing  his  bone  going  after  its  deceptively
larger reflection  in the water.”

Nt ee mk RE ne

Especially  in  view  of  the  vagueness  of  many  of  the
statutory  provisions  and  the  great  discretion  vested  in  the
FEC,  the  enforcement  provisions,  and  particularly  the  ad-
visory-opinion  power,  are  plainly  the  FEC’s  primary  func-
tion.  As  a  practical  matter,  most  questions  arising  under
these  statutes  will  be  resolved,  as  they  are  being  resolved
today,  by the exercise  of the advisory-opinion  power.  Where
that  fails,  the  FEC  has  ample  further  powers  to  compel
compliance  with  its wishes.  To hold  that  powers  of this  sort,
because  joined  with  record-keeping  functions,  may  be exer-
cised  by a  congressionally  appointed  agency  is to  do  drastic
violence  to the constitutional  plan.  Indeed,  in the context  of
these  statutes,  even  the  record-keeping  functions  are  not
properly  classified  as  legislative,  because  their  primary
purpose  is not  to  enable  Congress  to  consider  new  legisla-
tion  but  to  enable  the  FEC  to collect  the  data  and  conduct
the  investigations  on  the  basis  of which  the  enforcement
provisions  are  to be exercised.

208

209

(4)  Congressional  Usurpation  in  the Electoral  Area  Is
Particularly  Unacceptable  Given  the  Sensitive  Issues  and
Conflicts  of  Interest  Involved.

The  powers  of  Congress  are  quite  precise:  it creates
offices,  and  officers  of the  United  States  assume  their  posi-
tions  only  after  the  Senate  advises  and  consents  to  their
appointment  by  the  President.  The  appointment  power,
however,  rests  with  the executive.

The  FEC  flagrantly  violates  these  principles.  By
entrusting  the  appointment  power  to  the  majority  and
minority  leaders  of both  Houses,  as  well  as  providing  for
confirmation  by  both  Houses,  Congress  sought  to  create,
not
an  independent  Commission,  but  a  Commission
dependent  on  Congress  and  thus  responsive  to its will.  Thus
it  is  that  Congress  controls  the  majority  of  the  appoint-
ments,  the  salaries,  and  appropriations  and,  through  the
legislative  veto,’  the manner  in which  much  of the  Act  is ad-
ministered  by the  FEC.  2 U.S.C.  § 438.

tno!
a + 00th
a eae

*  Under  2  U.S.C.  § 438&(c),  rules  relating  to  reporting  and  dis-
closure  requirements  promulgated  by the  FEC  are  svbject  to  veto  by
one  or  both  houses  of Congress.  The  history  of the veto  parallels  that  of
the  appointment  power.  Thach,  The  Creation  of the  Presidency,  1776-
1789,  3S et  seq.  (1969);  Ginnane,  ‘The  Control  of Federal  Adiministra-
tion  by Congressional  Resolution  and  Committees,”’  66  Harv.  L.  Rev.
569,  576  et  seq.  (1953).  Vesting  veto  power  in  Congress  is  just  as
unconstitutional
as  vesting  appointment  power  there.  Watson,
“Congress  Steps  Out:  A  Look  at  Congressional  Control  of  the
Executive,’’  63  Cal.  L.  Rev.  983  (1975);  Harris,  Congressional  Control
of  Administration
Legal
Opinion,”  66  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1353  (1953).  Congress’  retention  of  the
legislative  veto  is additionally  unconstitutional  because  it is performed
without  the  participation  of the  President,  as  the  Constitution  requires.
See  Statement  of Antonin  Scalia,  Assistant  Attorney  General,  Office  of
Legal  Counsel,  on  Executive  Agreements  (S.  1251  and  S. 632), before  the
Subcommittee  on  Separation  of Powers,  Committee  on  the  Judiciary,
U.S.  Senate  May  1S,  1975,  at  11-23.  Yet  a  further  unconstitutional
feature  is that  Congress  may  not  delegate  legislative  power  to one  of its
Houses,  as  the  veto  provision  does.  The  Federalist,  No.  51  (Madison),
Cooke  ed.  1961,  p. 350; Scalia,  id.  at  18.

(1964);  Jackson,  “A  Presidential

241

acts.’’  Opportunities  for  self-serving

It is naive  to expect  an  agency  subject  to the control  of
a single branch  to act  independently  of the will of those  who
control  that  branch.  Indeed,  as  Watson,  supra,  at  1043,
points ou:  “‘...  the denial  to Congress  of the power  to name
public  officers  [was]  intended  to  prevent  opportunities  for
acts
self-serving
abound  under  the  FECA,  demonstrating  yet again that  it is
an  incumbent-protection  statute.  The  FEC  was  created  by
Congress,  staffed  by Congress,  and  draws  its  sustenance
and  direction  from  Congress.  This  is so  even  though  it exer-
cises  numerous  executive  and  judicial  functions,  e.g.,
rendering  advisory  opinions,  law  enforcement,  rulemaking,
2 U.S.C.  §§ 437c(b),  437d(a)  (6)  and  (9),  437g{a)  (S) and  (7),
and  similar  functions  with  respect  to  Subtitle  H, §§ 9005,
9007-09 , 9011,  9036,  9039,  and  9040.  The  Constitution  for-
bids  the  creation  of such  an  agency.’°  If the  FEC  is to exer-
cise  such  functions,  it must  be appointed  pursuant  to  Arti-
cle  II. Otherwise,  it must  be divested  of those  functions.

The  answer  of the  Court  of Appeals  is to rely on  cases
upholding  Congress’  power  under  Article  I, § 4, cl.  1 of the
Constitution  to regulate  federal  elections.  But  the  power  to
regulate  elections  is no  different  from  the other  substantive
powers  of Congress  (the  power  to  tax,  the  power  to  regulate
interstate  commerce,  etc.),  and  the  court  below  makes  no
effort  to distinguish  it. Like  all Congress’  other  substantive
powers,  the  power  to  regulate  elections  is to  be  exercised
“by Law,”  as  Article  I, § 4, cl.  1 expressly  provides.  Congress’
function,  with  respect  to elections  as  to other  matters,  is to
legislate,  not  to administer.  If what  Congress  has done  here
can  be  justified  by  its  substantive  power  over  federal
elections,  then  Congress  could  equally  vest  in  itself  the
appointment  of  all  the  independent  regulatory  agencies,
and  even  agencies  of the executive.

©  Cf,  United  States  v.  Nixon,  418  U.S.  683,  693  (1974);  Marshall
vy.  Gordon,  243  U.S.  521,  536  (1917);  Kilbourn  v.  Thompson,  103  U.S.
168,  190-91  (1881);  Confiscation  Cases,  740  U.S.  454,  457-9  (1869).

210

211

— Sm ee ee 8 te ee ee

There  is much  language  in  the  legislative  history,  ap-
pellees’  briefs  below,  and  the  Court  of  Appeals’  decision
(op.  at  1519)  about  the  need  for  an  “independent  agency”
to administer  the challenged  statutes.  Appellants  agree  that
whatever  regulation  Congress  may  constitutionally  enact  in
the  most  sensitive  area  of  electoral  politics  is  best  per-
formed  by  an  independent  agency.  But  ‘‘independent”’  is
exactly  what  this  Commission  is not;  it is wholly  subordin-
ate  to the  legislative  branch.  A truly  independent  agency  is,
of  course,  created  by the  time-‘ionored  process:  appoint-
ment  by the  President  for  fixed  terms  enacted  by law,  with
confirmation  by the  Senate.  Congress’  unwillingness  to  use
that  process  here  is  plainly  a  reflection  of  its  desire  that
electoral  regulation  be  conducted  not  independently,  but
under  its own  tight and  continuous  control.

As this  Court  stated  in affirming  the fundamental  doc-
trine  of separation  of powers  in Marbury  v.  Madison,  5 U.S.
(1 Cranch)  137,  176  (1803).

‘The  powers  of  the  legislature  are  defined  and
limited;  and  that  those  limits  may  not  be  mis-
\aken  or  forgotten,  the  constitution  is written.  To
what  purpose  are  powers  limited,  and  to  what
purpose  is that  limitation  committed  to writing,  if
these  may,  at  any  time,  be  passed  by  those  in-
tended  to be restrained?”

The  FEC  is  an  instance  where  Congress  has  exceeded  its
constitutional  powers.  What  it  has  done  cannot  be  sus-
tained  by  the  judiciary,  which,  at  least  since  Marbury  v.
Madison,
has  been  committed  to  “maintaining  the
constitutional  plan  of separation  of powers.’’  Glidden  Co.
v.  Zdanok,  370  U.S.  530,  536  (1962)  (opinion  of  Justice
Harlan);  Massachusetts  v.  Mellon,  262  U.S.  447,  488
(1923).

B.  The  Challenged  Legislation  Is  Too  Vague  and Leaves

Excessive  Discretion  to the Administering  Authority.

renders

criminal

The  challenged

an
legislation
enormous  variety  of political  acts,  2 U.S.C.  § 441,  18 U.S.C.
§ 608i),  I.R.C.  §§ 9012,  9042.  It is, however,  a  morass  of
uncertainty  as  to  which  activities  are  permitted  and  which
lead  to  a  federal  prison.  The  sponsors  of  the  legislation
made  no  effort  to deny  this,  but  rather  sought  to  avoid  the
problem  by delegating  power  to the  FEC.  The  court  below
took  the same  view  (op. at  1581).  The criminal  provisions  of
the  FECA  Amendments,  in  particular,  do  not  convey
“sufficiently  definite  warning  as  to the  proscribed  conduct
when  measured  by  common  understanding  and  prac-
tices.’  Jordon  v. DeGeorge,  341  U.S.  223,  231-32  (1951).

For  example,  an  incumbent  Senator  running  for  re-
election—not  yet  nominated,  but  unopposed  in  the
primary—authorizes  an  expensive,  in-depth  poll  of  his
constituents’  views  on  public  issues.  Is that  an  expenditure
under  18  U.S.C.  § 608(c)?  If so,  is it to  be allocated  to the
“campaign’”’  for nomination  or  to the general  election?  The
answers  to  these  questions  may  determine  whether  the
candidate  spends  six years  in the  New  Senate  Office  Build-
ing or  one  year  in Lewisburg.  The  legislation,  however,  con-
tains  not  a  glimmer  of  guidance  to  the  most  scrupulous
candidate.

For  those  not  authorized  to  seek  advisory  opinions
(anyone  who  neither  holds  federal  office  nor  is a candidate
or  a political  committee,  2 U.S.C.  § 437(f)),  the legislation  is
plainly  so  vague  as  to  give  no  adequate  guidance.  Political
activities  must  either  be  avoided  or  undertaken  at  the  risk
that  prosecutors  or  courts  will  decide  after  the  event  that  a
crime  has been  committed.  For those  entitled  to seek  an  ad-
visory  opinion,  the  situation  is little  different.  The  legisla-
tion  is so  vague  that  persons  acting  in  full  good  faith  may
well  think  that  no  reasonable  person  would  believe  that  a

212

213

particular  act  was  unlawful,  e.g.,  a poll as  an  expense  of the
contest  for  nomination,  yet  learn  only  too  late  that  others
disagree.  There  is, in short,  insufficient  notice  even  as  to the
circumstances  in  which  an  advisory  opinion  should  be
sought.

If  the  presence  of  the  FEC  is  thought  to  cure  the
vagueness  problem,  that  presence  merely  renders  the
statute  unconstitutional  in  another  way.  In  Speiser  v.
Randall,  357  U.S.  513  (1958),  this  Court  held  that  tax-
assessment  procedures  which  shift  the  burden  of  proof  to
the  taxpayers  are  not  adequate  where  First  Amendment
issues  are  at stake.  The  Court  said  there  that  “the  operation
and  effect  of the  method  by which  speech  is sought  to be re-
strained  must  be  subjected  to  close  analysis  and  critical
judgment  in  the  light  ofthe  particular  circumstances  to
which  it is  applied.”’  Jd  at  520.  And  this  Court  has  held
unconstitutional  in  a  long  and  important  line  of cases  the
delegation  of excessive  discretion  to  administering  officers.
See,  e.g.,  Saia  v.  New  York,  334  U.S.  558  (1948);  Shut-
tlesworth  v.  Birmingham,  394  U.S.  197 (1969).

This  Court  has  repeatedly  emphasized  that  “standards
of permissible  statutory  vagueness  are  strict  in  the  area  of
free  expression...  .  Because  First  Amendment  freedoms
need  breathing  space  to  survive,  government  may  regulate
in  the  area  only  with  narrow  specificity.’  NAACP  v.
Button,  371  U.S.  415,  432-33  (1963).  If  citizens  are  not
clearly  informed  of what  is  unlawful,  they  will  necessarily
“steer  far  wider  of the  unlawful  zone,”’  Speiser  v.  Randall,
supra,  at  526.  Indeed,  this  Court’s  characterization  of the
statute  struck  down  in Keyishian  v.  Board  of Regents,  385
U.S.  589,  604  (1967),  accurately  applies  here:  ‘‘Vagueness
of  wording  is  aggravated  by  prolixity  and  profusion  of
statutes,  regulations,  and  administrative  machinery,  and  by
manifold  cross-references  to  interrelated  enactments  and
rules.”

When  both  criminal  penalties  and  First  Amendment
rights  are  involved,  loose  methods  of enforcement  tolerable
elsewhere
well-
monitored  and  fair  procedures  must  be  provided,  and  the
enforcing  tribunal  must  be  guided  by  legislation  which
provides  adequate  direction.

insufficient.

Impartial,

plainly

are

The  FEC’s  power  to  make  rules  and  general  policy
and  to  issue  advisory  opinions  constitutes  a system  of prior
restraint.  Bantam  Books,  Inc.,  y.  Sullivan,  372  U.S.  58
(1963).  Any  system  of prior  restraint  ‘“‘comes  to  this  Court
bearing  a heavy  presumption  against  its constitutionality.”’
Id.  at  70.  Such  a  system  “avoids  constitutional  infirmity
only  if it takes  place  under  procedural  safeguards  designed
to  obviate  the  dangers  of a  censorship  system.”’  Freedman
v.  Maryland,  380  U.S.  51,  58 (1965).  The  statute  here  fails
to meet  these  standards  in several  respects.  It empowers  the
Commission  to  issue  regulations,  rulings  and  advisory
opinions  that  impose  a system  of prior restraint  on  political
activity  and  speech  without  any  judicial  determination,  id.
at  58.  Moreover,  the  statute  fails  to  meet  those  procedural
requirements  that  this  Court  has  repeatedly  identified:
“first,  the  burden  of  instituting  judicial  proceedings,  and  of
proving  that  the  material  is unprotected,  must  rest  on  the
censor;  second,  any  restraint  prior to judicial  review  can  be
imposed  only  for  a  specified  brief  period  and  only  for  the
purpose  of preserving  the  status  quo;  third,  a  prompt  final
judicial  determination  must  be  assured.”’  Southeastern
Promotions,  Ltd.,  vy.  Conrad,  43 L. Ed.  2d 448,  460 (1975).

The  legislation  under  attack  is  a  mountain  of unan-
swered  questions  as  to  what  is legal  and  what  is illegal."'

''  For  example,  Rep.  Frenzel  candidly  described  the  statutory
definition  of “contribution”  as  ‘‘rather  vague  and  nebulous.”’  He hoped
that  the  FEC  would  clarify  the  definition  “‘on  a case-by-case  basis"’  by
means  of  advisory  opinions.  120  Cong.  Rec.  H  10910  (daily  ed.
Nov.  20,  1974).

(continued)

Indeed,  the  very  authorization  of  advisory-opinion  and
rulemaking  power  demonstrates  that  Congress  deliberately

The  following  lengthy  but  by no  means  complete  summary  sug-
gests  how  many  important  sections  of  the  challenged  statutes  are
impermissibly  vague:

(1)  The  definitions  of  “‘candidate,"’  2  U.S.C.  § 431(b);  18  U.S.C.  §
591(b);  I.R.C.  §§ 9002(2),  9032(2).  Obviously,  since  so  much  of the  law
turns  on  who  is and  who  is not  a  candidate,  this  definition  should  be
precise.  Yet,  note  particularly  2 U.S.C.  § 431(b)  (2)  and  18  U.S.C.  §
591(b)  (2):  do these  subsections  embrace  a preliminary  poll to  ‘‘test  the
waters”  for  a  possible  candidacy?  If so,  is it to  be allocated  to  the  can-

(2)  The  definitions  of  ‘‘political  committee,"’  2  U.S.C.  § 431(d);  18
U.S.C.  § S91(d);  L.R.C.  §§ 9002(9),  9032(8).  Does  this  include  even  an
informal  gathering  of  a  few  individuals  who  make  the  preliminary
soundings  and  policy  formulations  described  inthe  preceding  para-
graph?  Does  it, for  instance,  encompass  a  group  analyzing  a  potential
candidate's  chances?

(3)  The  definitions  of ‘‘contributions,””  2 U.S.C.  § 431(e);  18  U.S.C.  §
591(e);  I.R.C.  § 9032(4),  and  “expenditure,””  2  U.S.C.  § 431  (f);  18
U.S.C.  § S91(f);  L.R.C.  § 9032(9).  The  definition  of  “expenditure”’
appears  to  include  all  contributions.  If it does,  the  consequences  are
enormous,  since  an  individual  then  could  not  make  both  a contribution
and  an  independent  expenditure  if the  aggregate  amount  exceeded  the
expenditure  limit.  There  is considerable  additional  lack  of clarity  in
these  sections.  Are  repaid  loans  to  candidates  to  be  considered
campaign  expenditures?  When  are  the  facilities  of  a  newspaper,
magazine,  or  broadcast  station  “controlled”  by a political  candidate?  2
U.S.C.  § 431(f)  (4) (A);  18  U.S.C.  § S91(f)  (4) (A).  See  also  2 U.S.C.  §
434e).

(4)  Candidate  expenditure  limits.  How  are  contributions  and  expendi-
tures  to  be  allocated  with  respect  to  primary  and  general  elections
under  the limits  imposed  by 18 U.S.C.  § 608?

(S)  Title  2 U.S.C.  § 437a.  The  vagueness  and  other  unconstitutional  as-
pects  of this  section  were  sufficient  for  it to  be  declared  unconstitu-

(6)  The powers  of the  FEC,  2 U.S.C.  § 437d.

(9)  The  provision  on  contribution  “earmarking,”’  18  U.S.C.  § 60&b)
(6).  The  vagueness  of this  subsection  is palpable,  and  the  possibilities

apply here.

ae

217

tions  will  in every  case  decide  the  level  of communication  in
which  candidates  and  their  supporters  may engage.

In effect,  Congress  delegated  to the  FEC  the  power  to
make  the  delicate  judgments  between  regulation  and  free
political  communication—the  power  frequently  to  decide
who  will  win  an  election.

The  breadth  of the  FEC’s  power  and  the  virtually  un-
checked  discretion  it  enjoys  give  it  dramatic  power  over
what  kind  of and  how  much  speech  particular  candidates
for  federal  office  may  engage  in.  Merely  as  a few of literally
hundreds  of possible  examples  of issues  that  have  already
come  or  will  come  before  the  FEC:

e  Are  private  contributions  eligible  for  matching
government  subsidies  under  Chapter  96  without
regard  to  the  costs  incurred  raising  them,  and
without  regard  to whether  any  benefit  is conferred
on  the contributor  in exchange  therefor?  See  FEC
Notice  1975-40,  40  Fed.  Reg.  41933  (Sept.  9,
1975).

e  How  should  expenditures  made  by one  candi-
date  for federal  office  incurred  while  campaigning
on  behalf of a candidate  for another  federal  office
be  allocated  between  those  candidates?  See  FEC
Opinion  of Counsel  letter  1975-48.

e  What  is the  applicable  spending  limit  for a can-
didate  running  for  two  federal  offices  simul-
taneously?  See  FEC  Notice  1975-44,  40  Fed.  Reg.
42839 (Sept. 16, 1975).

e  How  are  the costs  of a public-opinion  poll made
by  a  candidate  before  the  primary  election,  but
testing  issues  applicable  in  both  the  primary  and
the general  election,  to be allocated?

e  How  are  the  costs  of printing  brochures  advo-
cating  the  election  of several  canuidates  for  dif-
ferent  federal  offices  to  be  allocated?  What  if
such  brochures  also  include  candidates  for  state
or  local  office?

e  In  presidential  elections,  how  are  costs  to  be
allocated  when  expenditures  are  made  that  have
an  effect  on  more  than  one  state?

e  If, as  appellees  contend,  repayments  of loans  do
not  count  as  expenditures,  can  the  contributor
who  made  the  loan  make  another  contribution
after  the  loan  is repaid  if the  total  of the  loan  and
contribution  together  exceed  the statutory  limit?

Quite  apart  from  issues  of statutory  vagueness,  see  supra,
pp.  211-16,  the questions  the FEC  must  decide—almost  neces-
sarily  on  an  ad  hoc  basis—will  affect  election  results,  no
matter  what  answers  it ultimately  provides.

The  FEC  can  investigate  any  citizen  who  engages  in
any  political  activity.  Many  of  the  statutory  provisions,
e.g.,  18  U.S.  C.  § 608(e),  create  unprecedented  vistas  for
discriminatory  and  partisan  enforcement.  See supra,  p. 213
n.  11.  Clearly  not  all  volunteer  or  independent  activity  can
be  monitored.  Picking  and  choosing  is  necessary,  and
unwitting  violation  inevitable.  The  discretion  granted  is so
great  and  timing  in  politics  so  important  that  judicial
review  will  in most  cases  simply  not  afford  effective  protec-
tion  against  mistakes  which  will  deprive  some  citizens  of
their  rights.  Corrupt  or overzealous  commissioners  can  turn
these  enormous  powers  to  improper  uses  which  stagger  the
imagination.  Politicians  eager  to  punish  their  enemies  will
no  longer  look  to the tax  laws.  They will  look  to the election
“reform”’  law instead.

If it is  unconstitutional  for  a  municipality  to  require
that  anyone  desiring  tc  use  a  loudspeaker  obtain  per-

218

219

mission  of the  police  chief,  Saia  v.  New  York,  supra,  it is
surely  a  fortiori  unconstitutional  to  delegate  to  the  FEC
vast  discretion  to regulate  virtually  all political  communica-
tion  by  federal  candidates  and  their  supporters.’’  If
political  freedom  is  to  be  regulated  like  economic  ar-
rangements,  the  “‘hands  off”  strictures  of the  First  Amend-
ment  will  be lost completely.

Clearly,  the  enforcement  mechanisms  do  not  meet  the
exacting  standards  of  the  First  Amendment.  The  model
upon  which  the  FEC  was  purportedly  created  is that  of the
“independent”  administrative  agencies,  to which  consider-
able  economic  regulating  is  delegated.  These  agencies
perform  policy-making  functions  and  are  more  politically
responsive  than  courts  because  their  members’  terms  are
fixed  by statute.

The  independent  agencies  are  at  the  very  moment  the
subject  of  intense  criticism  for  being  ‘“‘captured’’  by  the
interests  they  were  supposed  to  regulate.  See  Cutler  and
Johnson,  supra,  at  1397.  The  Senate  recently  passed a bill  to
establish  an  Agency  for  Consumer  Advocacy  (to  act  as  an
advocate  for  consumers  before  federal  agencies),  S.  200,
94th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  (1975),  the  entire  raison  d'etre  of
which  is that  only  producer  interests  are  fully  represented
before  the other  agencies.

What  role  the  independent  agencies  should  play in the
area  of economic  regulation  is  a  matter  left  primarily  to
legislative  judgment.  Different  standards  apply,  however,

'?  FEC  Chairman  Curtis  has  outlined  the  dangers:  “If  this
Election  Commission  doesn't  show  wisdom  and  understanding  it could
go  too  far,  or,  even  more  practical,  they  could  build  upon  this  new
federal  agency  and  give  it  more,  and  more,  and  more  power,  which
would  be the  great  danger,  I would  think,  but  right  now  I don’t  believe
it’s actually  a danger,  and  hopefully  we  have  the judgment  not  to go this
far.’  Transcript  of ‘“‘Martin  Agronsky'’s  Evening  Edition,”’  prepared  by
the  FEC,  at  16-17.

when  political  speech  is  the  subject  of  regulation.  See
supra,  pp. 41-42.

If independent  agencies  have  not  fulfilled  the  hopes
placed in them,  far less  can  be expected  of the  FEC.  Here,
the  appointment  power  is given to some  of the very  interests
supposed  to be regulated,  creating  an  agency  necessarily  re-
sponsive  to  those  in power  in Congress.  Given  the  manner
of  the  appointment,  the  shortness  of  term,  reliance  for
appropriations,  and  the  legislative  veto,  the
FEC  seems  de-
signed,  not  to be “‘captured”’  by the regulated,  but to repre-
sent  them  from  the very start.

Bipartisan  election  commissions  in  the  states  have  a
sad  history  of unfair  enforcement  of state  election  laws,  see
Comment,  “Bipartisan
Election  Administration—New
York  City:  Pruning  the  Electoral  Thicket,”  84 Yale  L.J.  61
(1974).  The  court  below  expressly  viewed  the  mode  of
appointment  as  being  on  a  bipartisan  (rather  than  a  non-
partisan)  basis  (op.  at  1519).  The  FEC  is an  even  greater
danger  because  of the  vast  powers  it exercises  in this  most
sensitive  area.'’  In  the  long  run,  the  regulations  promul-
gated  by the  FEC  will  reflect  a responsiveness  to those  who
hold  power,  contrary  to the exacting  standards  called  for by
the  First  Amendment.  Speiser  v.  Randall,  supra;  see  also
Tumey  v.  Ohio,  273 U.S.  510 (1927)."*

There  may,  indeed,  be  constitutional  leeway  for  an
independent  agency  to  perform  administrative  tasks,  such
as  determining  what  reporting  and  disclosure  forms  are

'?  As the  ACLU  has  said,  “*.  .

.  bi-partisanship  in the makeup  and
operation  will  naturally  tend  to  favor  the  interests  of  the  two  major
parties  and  their  nominees.’’  ACLU  Report  at 24.

FEC
'*  Proposals  now  before  Congress,  moreover,  seek  to give the
vast  power  over  communications  between  citizens  and  governmental
officials  and  criminal  enforcement  powers  as  well.  See, e.y..  S. 776,  94th
Conry,.,  Ist Sess.  (1975).

220

221

proper  and  acting  as  a  custodian  for  the  reports  of candi-
dates  and  political  committees.  But  the  regulation  of
conduct  falling  within  areas  protected  by the  First  Amend-
ment  calls  for  legislative  exactitude  so  that  those  affected
will  have  adequate  notice  of what  the law  permits  and  what
it  prohibits,  and  to  guide  those  who  must  administer  it.
There  is,  therefore,  no  room  for  great  administrative  dis-
cretion  or  for  an  enforcement  process  controlled  by  the
legislative  branch  itself.

Like  the  postal  statute  in  Lamont  v.  Postmaster
General,  381  U.S.  301  (1965),  which  “sets  administrative
officials  astride  the  flow  of mail  to  inspect  it,  appraise  it,
write  the  addressee  about  it,  and  await  a  response  before
dispatching  the  mail,”  id.  at  306,  here  the  Act  sets
administrative  officials  astride  the  flow  of politics  in Ameri-
ca,  inspecting  the  campaign  of this  candidate,  appraising
the  expenditures  of that,  writing  this  candidate  about  his
campaign  activity  and  awaiting  a  response  before  allowing
it to  go forward.  This  Court  must  surely  conclude,  as  it did
in Lamont,  that  ‘‘[t]he  regime  of this  Act  is at  war  with  the
‘uninhibited,  robust,  and  wide  open’  debate  and  discussion
that  are  contemplated  by the  First  Amendment.”  381  U.S.
at  307.

C,  Subtitle  H Unconstitutionally  Grants  Too  Much  Dis-

cretion  to  the FEC and  to Incumbent  Administrations.

Subtitle  H’s  allocation  of  federal  funding  will  neces-
sarily  affect  the  outcomes  of presidential  elections,  and  the
provision  or  non-provision  of money  is an  act  seriously  af-
fecting  First  Amendment  rights.  The  directions  given  those
administering  these  statutes,  therefore,  must  be precise  and
detailed.

Subtitle  H  provides  no  such  standards.  For  example,
the  FEC  is permitted  to  increase  the  limit  on  spending  for
national  conventions  with  virtually  no  criteria  other  than  its

judgment  whether  an  effective  convention  can  be  held  at
the  prescribed  level  of spending,  I.R.C.  § 9008(d)  (3).  The
construction  of  the  courts  below  (op.  at  1580)  does  not
eliminate  this vagueness.

Subtitle  H is similarly  excessively  vague  in the  amount
of discretion  that  it vests  in  incumbent  administrations  to
determine  the  allocation  of  federal  funding,  I.R.C.  §§
9006(c)-(d),  9008(b)  (3),  and  9037.  These  sections,  not  dis-
cussed  by  the  courts  below,  permit  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury,  in effect,  to govern  the  allocation  of funds  among
candidates  eligible  for  primary  financing  by  making
decisions  about  the  likely  success  of  third  parties  in  the
general  elections.  The  Secretary  must  make  an  “equitable”
distribution  among  primary  candidates  of  each  party,
taking  into  account  the  sequence  in  which  they  have
applied,  id.,  § 9007(b).  The  Secretary  in  1976  must  thus
allocate  between  the  incumbent  President  whom  he  serves
and  the  President’s  opponents  in  his  own  party.  The
Secretary  must  also  allocate  funds  to  candidates  for
nomination,  in  situations  where  there  are  widely  varying
numbers  of candidates  within  the  two  major  parties.  The
exercise  of  that  power  by  an  incumbent  administration
could  determine  the  outcome  of  both  the  nominating
process  and  the  general  election.  Such  a grant  of discretion
is  not  only  a  perverse  sort  of  remedy  to  emerge  from
Watergate;  it  is  also  flatly  unconstitutional.  Cox  v.  New
Hampshire,  312 U.S.  569 (1941);  Saia  v. New  York,  supra.

D.  The  Candidate-Disqualification  Provision  Is  Uncon-

stitutional.

The  FEC  has  the  power  to  disqualify  individuals  from
becoming  candidates  for federal  office  if it finds  that  those
individuals,  while  candidates,  ‘‘failed  to  file  a  report  re-
quired  by Title  III”’  of the  FECA  (relating  to reporting  and
disclosure  requirements).  The  period  of  disqualification
extends  from  the  date  of the  FEC  finding  until  one  year

222

223

after  the  expiration  of  the  term  of  the  federal  office  for
which  the individual  was  .  candidate,  2 U.S.C.  § 456.

The  language  of  this  section  is  extremely  imprecise,
and  accordingly  vests  the  FEC  with  enormous  discretion.
What  does  “failed  to  file  a  report  required  by Title  III”
mean?  Does  it mean  that  the  FEC  can  find  such a failure  if
the  required  report  is  one  day  late?  If  the  report  omits
required  information?  If the  report  is  not  written  in  the
proper  form?  Is there  a requirement  of scienter?  These  and
many  other  questions  are  unanswered  by the statute.

The  Court  of  Appeals  deemed  this  issue  unripe,  but
none  of the  opinions  below  indicated  any  support  for  Sec-
tion  456.  Quite  the  contrary,  the  majority  expressed  serious
doubts  about  its  constitutionality  (op.  at  1576).  The  court
below  did  not  consider,  however,  that  the in terrorem  effect
of  the  candidate-disqualification  section  is  considerable.
Candidates  will  necessarily  spend  more  of their  time  worry-
ing over  the  correctness  of their  required  reports  and  less
time  campaigning.  Volunteers  who  help  compile  the  re-
quired  data  will  face  the  realization  that  an  error  by them
could  disqualify  their  candidate  from  future  candidacies.
The  constitutionality  of Section  456  is ripe for adjudication,
and  it should  be invalidated.

Although  this  Court  has  not  recognized  an  explicit
“right”  to  be  a  candidate,  it recently  stressed  that  ‘t]he
right  of a  party  or  an  individual  to  a  place  on  the  ballot  is
entitled  to  protection  and  is intertwined  with  the  rights  of
voters,”  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709,  716  (1974);  see  also
Bullock  y.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1972);  Williams  vy.
Rhodes,  393  U.S.  123 (1968).  Lower  federal  courts  have  af-
forded  considerable  protection  to  candidates,  while  recog-
nizing  that  the  government  can  impose  requirements  such
as  age,  residence,  and  citizenship,  Shakman  v.  Democratic
Org.  of Cook  County,  435  F.2d  267  (7th  Cir.  1970);  White
v.  Snear,  313  F.  Supp.  1100  (E.D.  Pa.  1970);  Stapleton  vy.
Clerk  for  City  of Inkster,  311  F.  Supp.  1187  (E.D.  Mich.
1970);  Zeilinga  v.  Nelson,  4  Cal.  3d  716,  484  P.2d  578
(1971).

This  Court  need  not  decide  the issue  of  a  “right  to
candidacy,’’  however,  since  Section  456  is unconstitutional
not  only  as  an  abridgement  of the  separation  of powers  but
also  as  a  prior  restraint  on  the  content  of peaceful  political
activity.  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States;  ACLU  v.
Jennings,  supra.  It should  be declared  invalid  now  without
waiting  until  some  unfortunate  candidate  in  the  future  is
tarred  by  allegations  based  on  it,  only  to  receive  almost
certain  constitutional  vindication  later.

Section  456  does  not  purport  to  give the

FEC  power  to
cemove  a  victorious  candidate  for  federal  office  from  that
office,  a power  which  would  be  in  flat  conflict  with  Powell
v.  McCormack,  395  U.S.  486  (1969).  Even  as  writien,  how-
ever,  the  section  <.ill  conflicts  with  the  theory  of Powell,  as
supporters  of the  legislation  recognized.  Remarks  of Rep.
Frenzel,  120  Cong.  Rec.  H  7193  (daily  ed.  July  25,  1974).
The  quali. cations  for  office  expressed  in the  Constitution,
see,  e.g.,  Article  I, §§  2,  5,  were  held  in  Powell  to  limit
office.
exclude
Congress’
Disqualification  from  being  a  candidate  has  the  same  ef-
fect.  See  Rosenthal,  ‘Campaign  Financing,”  supra,  at  394.

persons

ability

from

to

224

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  this  Court  should  declare
that  the  challenged  statutes  enumerated  in  the  questions
presented  for  review,  supra,  p.  S, are  unconstitutional  and
should  permanently  enjoin  their  enforcement.

Respectfully  submitted,
