A-|

APPENDIX  A

Note  on  the  Use of Election  Statistics  in the  Record

The  record  contains  two  basic  sets  of campaign-financ-
ing  statistics:  (1)  statistics  on  expenditures  in  the  1972
Senate  general-election  campaigns  compiled  by Roland  J.
Cole  for  a  Ph.D.  dissertation  at  the  Kennedy  School  of
Government  at  Harvard  University,  Campaign  Spending  in
Senate  Elections  (1975)  (hereinafter  ‘‘Cole  figures’’);  (2)
Statistics  compiled  by  the  Common  Cause  monitoring
group  on  1972  and  1974  congressional  elections  and  the
1972  presidential  election  (hereinafter  “Common  Cause
figures’).

The  proper  use  of  the  statistics  depends  on  a  clear
understanding  of the  different  assumptions  on  which  each
set  was  made  and  the  strengths  and  weaknesses  of those
assumptions.

For  example,  appellees  have  criticized  the  Cole  figures
because  (1) loan  repayments  are  treated  as  expenditures,  (2)
all  expenditures  are  treated  as  general-election  expendi-
tures  where  the candidate  was  unopposed  for  the  nomina-
tions,  and  (3)  in  all  other  cases  general-election  ex-
penditures  are  estimated  and  segregated  from  primary  ex-
penditures.

(1)  As  to  the  loan-repayment  issue,  the  FECA  provides
no  clue  as  to  whether  such  loan  repayments  are  to  be  in-
cluded  in expenditure  totals  or  not,  and  the  FEC  has not  yet
ruled  on  the  issue.  Moreover,  the  point  is  academic;  the
table  in  Cole,  supra,  at  398-99,  which  compares  Cole’s
figures  with  the  Common  Cause  figures,  makes  it possible
quickly  to discern  which  candidates’  expenditures  might  be
substantially  inflated  by the  inclusion  of loan  repayments.
Such  repayments  do  not  affect  the  conclusions  reached  in
this  brief.  For  example,  the  Cole  figures  are  relied  on  by
appellants  to  analyze  the  five  1972  Senate  campaigns  in

A-2

A-3

wiich  challengers  defeated  incumbents.  In  only  one  such
case—Haskell  vs.  Allott  in  Colorado—were  loan  repay-
ments  of a relevant  size,  and  there  only marginally  relevant.

(2)  Cole’s  treatment  of  all  expenditures  by  candidates
unopposed  for nomination  as  general-election  expenditures
is consistent  with  both  common  sense  and  experience.

(3)  Appellees  make  much  of  the  fact  that  Cole
estimated  general-election  spending  on  the  basis  of  the
available  data.  To  be  sure,  that  data  does  not  distinguish
between  general-election  and  primary-election  expendi-
tures,  and  absolute  accuracy  is  not  obtainable.  But  the
FECA  itself  requires  the  distinction,  and  some  attempt  at
differentiating  the  two  kinds  of spending  has  to  be  made  if
statistics  from  prior elections  are  to be at all useful.  Indeed,
agreed  findings  explain  the  process  by  which  Cole  dis-
tinguished  between  general-election  and  primary  expenses,
based  on  the  date  each  expenditure  was  made.  Findings  II
A 2, pp. 4-7;  B 2, pp.  186-88;  App.  II-266-69,  568-70.

To be sure,  absolute  accuracy  is not  attained  and  is not
claimed.  ‘‘It  is possible  to segregate  expenditure  figures  for
such  elections  with  reasonable  accuracy,  but  not  with  certain-
ty,” id.  II, B 2(c),  p.  187;  App.  11-569.  That  the distinction  bet-
ween  primary  and  general-election  spending  is  difficult
to  make  on  the  basis  of existing  reports  is a  peculiar  argu-
ment  for  appellees  to  make,  since  it  is  a  distinction
employed  by the  challenged  legislation  to  be  enforced  by
criminal  sanctions.  If the  distinction  can  not  be  rationally
made,  18  U.S.C.  § 608  is  unconstitutional  on  its  face.
Indeed,  at  this  very  moment,  the  FEC  is  issuing  guidelines
allocating  spending  to  particular  candidates  and  particular
elections.  See, e.g.,  FEC Opinion  of Counsel  letter  1975-48.

The  act  does  not  permit  candidates  to  aggregate
primary  and  general-election  money.  Candidates  are  not
allowed  to  spend  primary  money  for  activities  after  the
primary.  And  if  expenditures  are  to  be  allocated  to  a

primary  rather  than  to a general  election,  some  effort  must
be  made,  even  in  an  unopposed  primary,  to  make  it seem
that  the spending  is to influence  the primary.

The  Common  Cause  figures—upon  which  the Court  of
Appeals  placed  exclusive  reliance  (op. at  1540  n.  105)—are
thus  misleading  in the opposite  direction;  they make  no  at-
tempt  whatsoever  to distinguish  between  primary  and  gen-
eral-election  spending.  They  give  only  spending  totals  for
both  elections  lumped  together,  regardless  of  whether
either  or  both  were  serious  contests,  and  measure  these
against  the  ‘combined  overall  expenditure  limit,”  that  is,
the  total  of limits  for  both  elections  plus  the  fund-raising
allowance.  It is plainly  wrong  to compare  aggregate  spend-
ing  in  previous  years  with  a  statutory  limit  which  permits
spending  only  at  certain  inappropriate  times.  This  tech-
nique  creates  the  appearance  that  the  spending  limits  are  far
higher  relative  to  past  spending  then  they  really  are,  since
the  vast  majority  of candidates  had  a  serious  contest  in  at
most  one  election.  See  supra,  p.  90.  At  the  very  least,  the
statute  severely  regulates  the  timing  of campaign  expendi-
tures  and  thereby  their  eect  on  elections.  While  reference
to  the  ‘‘overall  combined  limit’’  seems  to  enhance  candi-
dates’  ability  to spend,  it does  so  only by requiring  them  to
make  expenditures  far  ahead  of  the  general  election.
Money  spent  in  June  will  affect  far  fewer  general-election
votes  than  money  spent  in October.

In  addition,  in  some  cases  the  primary  is  the  only
important  race  and  substantial  general-election  spending
is superfluous.  In  1972,  challenger  Elizabeth  Holtzman  de-
feated  long-time  incumbent  Emmanuel  Celler  in  the
Democratic  primary  in  New  York’s  Sixteenth  Congres-
sional  Distric:.  She  easily  defeated  her  Republican-general
election  opponent,  receiving  65.6  percent  of the  vote  to  his
22.9  percent.  The  Republican  reported  spending  no  money
at  all  for  either  primary  or  general  election,  whereas  Rep.
Holtzman’s  total  was  $104,318.  Findings  II,  A  3,  p.  19;

A-4

A-5

contributions  to  raise,  id.  II,  B 41,  pp.  200-01;  App.  II-S80;
Cole  dep.  tr.  384-86.  The  raising  of  small  contributions  is
very  expensive  and,  when  direct-mail  techniques  are  used,
runs  on  the average  of about  40 percent,  id.  II, B SO, p. 203;
App.  II-582.  The  20-percent  allowance  for  fund  raising  in
the  Act,  therefore,  is  wholly  inadequate  and  can  by  no
means  be  treated,  as  appellees  treat  it,  as  a  genuine  in-
crease  in the  limits.  Since  the  whole  purpose  of direct  mail
is to reach  likely contributors—that  is, persons  already  con-
vinced  of a  candidate’s  merit—fund-raising  mailings  have
only  a  marginal  campaign  spin-off  in  contacting  un-
committed  swing  voters.  The  most  valuable  mailing  list  is
one  which  contains  only  names  of  already  committed
people.

App.  II-299.  Clearly  the  bulk  of  her  funds  was  spent  to
defeat  Rep.  Celler  in  a  very  close  race,  although  the  Com-
mon  Cause  figures  misleadingly  suggest  that  it was  spread
over  two  campaigns.

The  Common  Cause  figures  suffer  from  other  diffi-
culties.  For  example,  even  though  loans  are  clearly  con-
tributions  under  the  provisions  of the  FECA  Amendments,
the  Common  Cause  figures  do  not  include  them  as
contributions,  Findings  II,  A 2(e),  p. 6; App.  11-268.  Loan
statistics  are  available  in  the  agreed  findings.  /d.  II,  C 7,
pp.  249-54;  App.  II-665S-75.

Expenditure  figures  for  previous  elections,  moreover,
are  not  fully  comparable  to  expenditures  under  the  FECA
Amendments.  For example,  the  Consumer  Price  Index  rose
18  percent  between  1972  and  1974.  See  Findings  II,  C
4, S pp.  223-48;  App.  II-618-64  (charts  demonstrating  the
impact  of  inflation  on  1972  races,  moreover,  include  no
amounts  expended  prior to  April  7, 1972,  the effective  date
of the  1971  Act.  /d.  Il, A 2, p. 4; App.  II-267.

The  FECA  Amendments  also  vastly  increase  the  costs
of campaigning  and  the need  to make  larger expenditures  than
in prior campaigns.  First,  the need  for accounting  and  legal
help  is  increased  by the  disclosure  requirements  and  the
complexity  of  the
FECA  Amendments.  In  1972,  for  in-
stance,  the  Percy  and  Tower  campaigns  used  computers  to
produce  contributor  or  expenditure  reports—the  cost  of
compliance  with  FECA  disclosure  requirements  in Tower's
case  being  at  least  $30,000,  Findings  II, B 13,  p.  195;  App.
11-575.  Compliance  with  the  disclosure  provisions  costs  the
same  no  matter  what  the size  of the contribution.  Increased
reliance  on  small  contributions  thus  means  increased  costs.

In  addition,  the  limitation  on  individual  contributions
to $1,000  increases  the costs  of raising  money  because  small
large
proportionately
contributions

more

than

cost

APPENDIX  B

FEDERAL  ELECTION  COMMISSION

WASHINGTON,  DC 20463

August  28,  1975

Mr.  Ronald  Cocome
Chairman
Committee  for Constitutional
Presidency—McCarthy  ’76
1223  Connecticut  Avenue,  N.W.
Washington,  D.C.  20016

Dear  Mr.  Cocome:

This  is in response  to your  letter  dated  August  22,  1975
and  to  confirm  the  subsequent  telephone  conversation  be-
tween  Mr.  Peter  Roman,  Chief  of the  Commission’s  Audit
and  Ivestigation  Division,  and  Ms.  Mary  Monroe,  your
Committee  Treasurer.  As  Mr.  Roman  arranged  with  your
Committee,  the  Commission  will  begin  an  audit  of  your
records  on  Tuesday,  September  2, at  10:00  AM.

Title  2  of  the  United  States  Code  requires  the
Commission  to  conduct  audits  of  reports  and  statements
filed  under  provisions  of the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act,  as  amended.  The  Act  also  assigns  civil  jurisdiction  to
the  Commission  of all  apparent  violations  of the  Act  and
Sections  608,  610,  611,  613,  615,  616,  and  617 of Title  18 of
the  United  States  Code.

In  addition,  the  Commission  is specifically  charged  to
correct  any  apparent  violations  “‘.  .
.  by informal  methods
of conference  conciliation and persuasion.”’  As  part  of the
Commission’s  continuing  efforts  to  encourage  voluntary
compliance,  the  Commission  has  authorized  the  Audit  and
Investigation  Division  to  audit  all  reports  and  records  of
Presidential  candidates  and  their  principal  campaign com-

B-2

mittees.  While  part  of this  audit  is, as  your  letter  notes,  de-
signed  as  a  “preliminary  step towards  certifying  candidates
eligible  for  matching  Federal  funds’’,  it  is  designed  to
insure  compliance  with  Federal  election  law  and  all  of the
interim  guidelines  issued  by  the  Commission.  In  that
regard,  I note  with  regret  that  your  letter  makes  reference
to  but  one  of our  interim  guidelines,  out  of the  numerous
guidelines  issued  to  date  dealing  with  both  certification  for
matching  payments  and  the  disclosure  and  reporting
requirements  of Federal  election  law.

If you  have  any  further  questions  or  comments  con-
cerning  the  Commission’s  planned  audit,  I invite  you  to
direct  them  to  Mr.  John  G.  Murphy,  Jr.,  our  General
Counsel.

Sincerely,
/s/

Thomas  B. Curtis
Chairman

21

Gu the Supreme Court of the Gnited  States

OcToOBER  TERM,  1975

Nos.  75-436  anp  75-437

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY,  ET  AL.,  APPELLANTS

v.

Francis  R.  VALEO,  ET  AL.

ON  APPEALS  FROM  THE  UNITED  STATES  COURT  OF  APPEALS  FOR
THE  DISTRICT  OF  COLUMBIA  CIRCUIT  AND  THE  UNITED  STATES
DISTRICT  COURT  FOR  THE  DISTRICT  OF  COLUMBIA

BRIEF  FOR  THE  ATTORNEY  GENERAL  AND  THE  FEDERAL
ELECTION  COMMISSION  '

OPINION  BELOW

The  opinions  of  the  court  of  appeals  (J.S.  App.
1505-1623 *), adopted  in  relevant  part  by  the  three-
judge  court  (J.S.  App.  No.  75-437,  la),  is  reported
at  519  F.2d  821.

‘The  Attorney  General  joins  this  brief  as  a  party.  The  At-
torney  General  has  also  joined  as  a  party  in  the  separate  brief
for  the  United  States  to  the  extent  that  brief  discusses  the  law
enforcement  and  interpretive  rule-making  powers  of  the  Federal
Election  Commission.  The  Attorney  General  is  participating  as
counsel  on  both  this  brief  and  the  separate  brief  for  the  United
States.

*  Unless  otherwise  noted,  “J.S.°  refers  to  the  jurisdictional

statement  in No,  75-436.

(1)

2

JURISDICTION

The  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals  with  respect
to  the  whole  case  (J.S.  App.  1582-1586)  and  the
judgment  of  the  three-judge  district  court  with  re-
respect  to  the  issues  arising  under  Chapters  95  and  96
of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  (J.S.  App.  No.  75-437,
la—5a)  were  both  entered  on  August  15,  1975.  Notices
of  appeal  to  this  Court  were  filed  on  August  21,  1975.
Probable  jurisdiction  was  noted  on  October  6,  1975.
The  jurisdiction  of  this  Court  to  entertain  the  appeal
from  the  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals  rests  on  2
U.S.C,  487h(b).  The  jurisdiction  of  this  Court  to  en-
tertain  the  appeal  from  the  judgment  of  the  three-
judge  district  court  rests  on  26  U.S.C.  9011(b)  (2)  and
28  U.S.C.  1253.  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter

Curriers,  413  U.S,  548.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

In  accordance  with  2 U.S.C.  487h  and  the  order  of
the  court  of appeals  (J. App.  74-75),  the  district  court
certified  28  constitutional  questions  to  the  court  of ap-

*  Because  it  is  not  clear  whether  the  reference  in  2  U.S.C.
437  to  “this  Act”  includes  new  Chapters  95  and  96  of  the  In-
ternal  Revenue  Code,  a  three-judge  district  court  was  con-
vened  to  consider  the  constitutional  challenge  to  these  provi-
sions.  That  procedure  seems  to  be  mandated  by 26  U.S.C.  9011
(b)(2)  with  respect  to  Chapter  95.  Although  there  is  no  com-
parable  provision  in  Chapter  96  (see  26  U.S.C,  9040-9041),  the
same  course  would  be  required  by  28  U.S.C.  2282.  Out  of
abundance  of  caution,  the  court  of  appeals  also  ruled  on  the
constitutional  questions  arising  under  Chapters  95  and  96.  Since
hoth  courts  reached  identical  results  and  this  Court  has  juris-
diction  on  appeal  from  both  judgments,  we  submit  it  is  wnnec-
essary  to decide  which  court  had  jurisdiction.

3

peals  (J.S.  App.  1582-1586).  The  questions  before  this
Court  may  be summarized  as  follows:  *

1.  Whether  the  particular  requirements  in the  chal-
lenged  statutes  providing  for  recording  and  disclo-
sure  of  the  source  and  amount  of  contributions  to
candidates  for  federal  elective  office  and  expenditures
by or  on  behalf  of such  candidates  violate  the  rights  of
one  or  more  of the  plaintiffs  under  the  First,  Fourth,
or  Ninth  Amendment  or  the  Due  Process  Clause  of
the  Fifth  Amendment.’

2.  Whether  any  statutory  limitation,  or  the  particu-
lar  limitations  in  the  challenged  statutes,  on  the
amounts  that  individuals  or  organizations  may  con-
tribute  in  connection  with  elections  for  federal  oftice
violate  the  rights  of  ene  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs
under  the  First,  Fifth,  or  Ninth  Amendment  or  the
Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  of  the
Constitution  of the  United  States.

3.  Whether  any  statutory  limitation,  or  the  partic-
ular  limitations  in  the  challenged  statutes,  on  the
amounts  that  candidates  for  elected  federal  office  may
expend  in their  campaigns,  or  on  the  amounts  that  may

*  This  brief  does  not  address  any  of  the  issues  arising  out  of
the  law  enforcement  powers  of  the  Commission.  With  respect  to
those  questions,  the  Commission  is separately  represented  by Spe-
cial  Counsel,  who  have  filed  a  further  brief  on  behalf  of the  Fed-
eral  Election  Commission.  The  Attorney  General  has  filed  a
separate  brief,  which  is  combined  with  the  amicus  brief  of  the
United  States  (see  note  1,  supra),  discussing  those  questions.
*Insofar  as  this  question  involves  a  challenge  to  2  U.S.C.
437a,  that  issue  has  been  eliminated  by  the  decision  of  all  ce-
fendants  not  to  appeal  the  ruling  below  striking  down  that  sec-
tion  as  unconstitutional.

4

be expended  by other  persons  or  organizations  for their
henefit,  violate  the  rights  of one  or  more  of the plaintiffs
under  the  First  or  Ninth  Amendment  or  the  Due  Proc-
ess  Clause  of the  Fifth  Amendment.

4.  Whether  any  statutory  provision  for  the  public
financing  of  political  conventions  or  campaigns  for
nomination  or  election  to  the  Presidency  or  Vice  Pres-
idency,  or  the  particular  provisions  for  public  finane-
ing  in  the  challenged  statutes,  violates  the  rights  of
one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  under  the  First  or  Ninth
Amendment,  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth
Amendment,  or  Article  I,  Section  8,  clause  1, of  the
Constitution  of the  United  States.

STATUTES  INVOLVED

The  statutes  involved  are  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  of  1971,  P.L.  92-225,  86  Stat.  3, 2  U.S.C.
(Supp.  IIL)  431  et  seq.;  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal
Revenue  Code  (Chapters  95  and  96,  also  known  re-
spectively  as  the  Presidential  Election  Campaign
Fund  Act  and  the  Presidential  Primary  Matching
Payment  Account  Act),  P.L.  92-178,  85  Stat.  562,  as
amended,  26  U.S.C.  (Supp.  If1)  9001  et  seq.;  and  the
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974,
P.L.  93-443,  88  Stat.  1263.  The  full  text  of  those
statutes  (together  with  other  related  provisions  not  at
issue  in  these  proceedings)  is  reproduced  in  a  gov-
ernment  pamphlet  entitled  “Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Laws,’’  which  is  reprinted  in  Vol.  IIT  of  the
joint appendix.’

i We  note  one  obvious  misprint  in the  pamphlet:  in the  fourth
line  of  26  U.S.C.  9004(a)  (2)(B),  the  word  “received”  should
be  substituted  for  “required”  (p.  64).

5

STATEMENT

A.  THE  CHALLENGED  LEGISLATION

Putting  aside  provisions  concerning  the  enforce-
ment  and  interpetive  rule-making  powers  of  the  Fed-
eral  Election  Commission,  the  legislation  in  suit  has
four  principal  features:  (1)  it requires  recordkeeping
and  disclosure  of campaign  contributions  and  expendi-
tures;  (2)  it limits  the  size  of  contributions  from  in-
dividuals  and  groups;  (3)  it places  ceilings  on  expend-
itures  by,  or  for  the  benefit  of, candidates  for  elective
federal  office;  and  (4)  it provides  public  financing  for
Presidential  campaigns.  We  describe  each  of  these  as-
pects  briefly,  subject  to  further  elaboration  of certain
points  in  the  Argument.

1.  RECORDKEEPING  AND  DISCLOSURE

The  initial  requirement  is that  political  committees
supporting  candidates  for  federal  office  record,  with
the  name  of  the  donor,  every  contribution  received  in
excess  of  $10  and  every  expenditure  made,  regardless
of  amount.  2 U.S.C.  432(¢)  ; see,  also,  2 U.S.C.  482(b).
Additionally,  periodic  reports  must  be  filed,  directly
or  indirectly  with  the  Commission,  which,  among  other
things,  disclose  the  source  of  every  contribution  ex-
ceeding  $100  and  the  recipient  and  purpose  of  every
expenditure  over  the  same  amount.  2  U.S.C.  432(f)
(2)-(3),  434(a)-(e).  That  obligation  is  imposed  on
individuals  as  well  as  committees.  2  U.S.C.  434(e).
The  last  pre-election  reports  must  be  complete  as  of
two  weeks  before  the  election  and  must  be  filed  not
later  than  10 days  before  the  event.  2 U.S.C.  434(a)  (1)

6

(A) (i).  But  any  last-minute  contribution  of  $1,000  or
more  must  be  reported  within  48  hours.  2 U.S.C.  434
(a)(1).  Finally,  such  reports  are  promptly  made
available  for  public  inspection  and  copying.  2  U.S.C.
38 (a) (4).

2,  LIMITATIONS  ON  CONTRIBUTIONS

Contributions  are  broadly  defined  to  include  any
eift  or  loan  of  money  or  anything  else  of  value.  18
U.S.C.  591(e).  But  volunteered  personal  services  are
not  included  (18  U.S.C.  591(e)(5(A));  nor  are  cer-
tain  advantages  volunteered  by  a  supporter  up  to  a
value  of  $500.  18  U.S.C.  591(e)(5)(B)-(D).  The
amount  of contributions  is limited  as  follows:

(a)  A  multi-candidate  political  committee
that  satisfies  certain  conditions  may  contribute
up  to $5,000  to any  single  candidate  in any  given
primary  or  election;  there  is, however,  no  limit
on  the  number  of  candidates  who  may  receive
such  a  contribution  from  the  committee  and
therefore  no  total  ceiling  on  the  contributions
of  such  a  committee.  18  U.S.C.  608(b)  (2);  see
also,  18  U.S.C.  608(b)  (5).

(b)  Other  political  committees  or  groups  are
limited  to  $1,000  in  contributions  per  election
(or  primary)  with  respect  to  any  particular
candidate;  but,  again,  there  is no  overall  limit
on  contributions.  18  U.S.C.  608(b)  (1).

(c)  Individuals  are  likewise  restricted  to
$1,000  per  election  (or primary)  to any  particu-
lar  candidate  (ibid.);  they  are,  moreover,  re-
stricted  to  an  aggregate  limit  of  $25,000  with
respect  to  any  election  period.  18  U.S.C.  608
(b) (3).

7

3.  EXPENDITURE  CEILINGS

The  new  law  imposes  limitations  on  spending  in
political  campaigns.  First  and  foremost,  there  are
ceilings  on  what  the  candidate  himself  (or  his  princi-
pal  canipaign  committee  or  other  authorized  repre-
sentative)  may  spend.  These  are:

(a)  In  the  case  of  a  candidate  for  the  Presi-
dency,  $10  million  in  the  primary  and  $20  mil-
lion  in  the  general  election  campaign.  18  U.S.C.
608(c)(1)(A)-(B).

(b)  In  the  case  of  a  candidate  for  the
Senate,  or  for  the  House  of  Representatives
in  a  state  which  is  entitled  to  only  one  Repre-
sentative,  the  greater  of  $100,000  or  8  cents
per  voting  age  resident  at  the  primary  and
the  greater  of  $150,000  or  12  cents  per  voting
age  resident  at  the  general  election.  18  U.S.C.
608(¢)(1)(C)-(D).

(c)  In  the  case  of any  other  candidate  for  the
House  of  Representatives,  $70,000  at  the  pri-
mary  and  the  same  amount  at  the  general  elec-
tion.  18  U.S.C.  608(¢)(1)(E).

These  ceilings  are  subject  to  upward  adjustment  on
two  bases.  First,  up  to  20  per  cent,  the  cost  of  collect-
ing  contributions,  is  exempted  from  the  expenditure
limits.  18  U.S.C.  591(f)  (4) (1).  Second,  the  ceilings
themselves  are  increased  each  year  to  reflect  the  in-
creased  costs  of living.  18 U.S.C.  608(d).

There  are  restrictions  on  what  a  candidate  may
expend  from  his  personal  or  family  funds:  $50,000
for  a  Presidential  candidate;  $35,000  for  a  Sena-
torial  candidate  or  House  candidate  in  a  single  Rep-

595-404—75——_2

8

9

resentative  State;  $25,000  for  all  other  House  candi-
dates.  18  U.S.C.  608(a).

Ceilings  also  apply  with  respect  to  the  expendi-
tures  of  individuals  or  groups  acting  independently
and  national  or  State  political  party  committees  in
support  of  a  candidate—none  of  which  is  counted
against  his  allowance.  18  U.S.C.  608(e )-(f).  For
individuals  and  groups,  the  ceiling  is  $1,000  per  can-
didate  per  election,  with  no  overall  limit.  18  U.S.C.
608(e).  For  political  party  committees,  the  limits

are:

(a)  For  a  Presidential  candidate,  2  cents
multiplied  by  the  voting  age  population  of  the
country.  18  U.S.C.  608(f)  (2).

(b)  For  a  candidate  for  the  Senate,  or  for
the  House  in  a  single  Representative  State, the
greater  of  $20,000  or  2  cents  per  voting  age
resident  of  the  State.  18  U.S.C.  608(f)  (3) (A).
(c)  For  any  other  House  candidate,  $10,000.

18  U.S.C.  608(f)  (3) (B).

Finally,  a  ceiling  is  imposed  on  party  expenditures
with  respect  to  Presidential  nominating  conventions.
26  U.S.C.  9008(d).  The  limit  is  $2  million  for  a
‘major  party”  (one  whose  candidate  received  25  per-
cent  or  more  of  the  votes  cast  at  the  previous  Presi-
dential  election,  26  U.S.C.  9002(6)).  26  U.S.C.  9008
(b)(1)  and  (d)(1).  For  a  “minor  party”  (one  whose
candidate  received  between  5  and  25  percent  of  the
votes  cast  at  the  last  election,  26  U.S.C.  9002(7)),
the  limit  is  such  portion  of  $2  million  as  the  votes
received  by  that  party’s  candidate  at  the  previous
election  bear  to  the  average  votes  received  by  the

major  party  candidates  at the  same  previous  election.
26  U.S.C.  9008(b)  (2)  and  (d)(2).  In  “extraordinary
and  unforeseen  circumstances,’’  however,  the  Federal
Election  Commission  is  authorized  to  increase  these
ceilings.  26  U.S.C.  9008(d)  (3).

4.  PUBLIC  FINANCING  OF  PRESIDENTIAL  ELECTIONS

The  legislation  provides  that  taxpayers  may  desig-
nate  $1  of  their  taxes  to  be  contributed  to  the  Presi-
dential  Election  Campaign  Fund.  26  U.S.C.  6096.
Three  accounts  are  established  within  the  Fund,  one
for  Presidential  nominating  conventions  (26  U.S.C.
9008(a)),  one  to  finance  the  general  election  cam-
paign  of  eligible  candidates  (26  U.S.C.  9006),  and
one  out  of  which  matching  payments  are  made  to
eligible  candidates  during  the  Presidential  primaries
(26  U.S.C.  9037(g)).  Provision  is  made  for  an  equi-
table  distribution  of  moneys  should  the  Fund  be  in-
sufficient  to  satisfy  all  claims.  26  U.S.C.  9006(d),
9007(b).  In  all  cases,  the  subsidy  must  actually  be
spent  and  does  not  increase  the  applicable  contribu-
tions  and  expenditure  ceilings.  26 U.S.C.  9003  (b)-(c),

9034(b),  9038(b).

Briefly,  the  scheme  is as  follows:

(a)  Any  person  who  seeks  nomination  as  the
Presidential  candidate  of  a  political  party  who
has  received  private  contributions  exceeding
$5,000  (counting  only  the  first  $250  of each  con-
tribution)  in  each  of  20  States  is  eligible  for
matching  payments  to  defray  his  “primary”

10

1]

campaign  expenses.  26  U.S.C.  9033(b).  Pay-
ments  from  the  Fund  match  only a  maxinuun  of
$250  from  any  private  contributor.  26  U.S.C.
9034(a).  The  total  subsidy  to  any  candidate
may  not  exceed  $5 million.  26 U.S.C.  9034(b).

(b)  The  national  committee  of  a_  political
party  whose  candidate  has  received  at  least  »
percent  of  the  votes  cast  at  the  previous  Presi-
dential  election  is  entitled  to  public  funds  to
defray  the  expenses  of its Presidential  nominat-
ing  convention,  up  to  the  total  spending  ccil-
ings  already  noted,  supra.  26  U.S.C.  9008(h)-
(c).

(ec)  Every  candidate  of  a  political  party  who
has  qualified  for  the  Presidential  ballot  in  10
States  and  who,  at  the  current  election  or
the  previous  election,  or  whose  party  at  the
previous  election,  has  received  5 percent  of  the
total  vote  cast,  is  entitled  to  public  moneys
toward  his  general  election  campaign  expenses.
26  U.S.C.  9002(2)(B),  9004(a).

With  respect  to  the  general  election,  the  amount
of  the  payment,  subject  to  the  availability  of sufficient
moneys  in  the  Fund,  is $20  million  for  a  major  party
candidate  and  such  portion  thereof  for  any  other  eligi-
ble  candidate  as  the  votes  he  or  his  predecessor  re-
ceived  represents  when  measured  against  the  average
votes  for  the  major  party  candidates.  26  U.S.C.  9004
(a)(1)-(2).  Minor  and  new  party  candidates  eligi-
ble  for  a  grant  on  the  basis  of  past  performance  are
entitled  to  a  further  payment  if they  achieve  a  higher
vote  in  the  current  election.  26  U.S.C.  9004(a) (3).
These  payments,  like  those  to  candidates  eligible  only

on  the  basis  of  current  performance,  are  necessarily
made  after  the  election.

B.  THE  PROCEEDINGS  BELOW

These  proceedings  were  begun  in  the  district  court
on  January  2, 1975.  The  plaintiffs  are  a  United  States
Senator  (James  L.  Buckley),  a  former  Senator  and
announced  candidate  for  the  Presidency  (Eugene  J.
McCarthy),  a  Member  of  the  House  of  Representa-
tives  (William  A.  Steiger),  a  substantial  contributor
to  candidates  for  federal  elective
office  (Stewart
Rawlings  Mott),  Senator  McCarthy’s  campaign  com-
mittee  (Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency—
McCarthy  °76),  three  minor  political  parties  (the  Con-
servative  Party  of  the  State  of  New  York,  the  Mis-
sissippl  Republican
and  the  Libertarian
Party,
Party),  one  chapter  of  a  national  civil  liberties  orga-
nization  (New  York  Civil  Liberties  Union,  Inc.),  two
political  action  organizations  (American  Conservative
Union  and  Conservative  Victory  Fund),  and  one  pub-
lication  (Human  Events,  Ine.).  All  sought  to  enjoin
the  implementation  of the  legislation  in  suit  as  uncon-
stitutional.  J.S.  App.  1508-1509.

Initially,  the  district  judge  certified  the  case  to  the
court  of  appeals,  as  required  by  2  U.S.C.  437h.  J.S.
App.  1509-1510,  However,  on  April  14,  the  court  of
appeals,  sitting  en  banc,  remanded  the  matter  to  the
district  court  with  instructions  to  develop  a  record
and  particularize  the  constitutional  questions  pre-
sented,  J.S.  App.  1510,  1587.  On  remand,  the  district
judge  received  and  adopted  certain  proposed  findings
submitted  by  the  parties.  J.S.  App.  1588.  These  are

ae

12

13

referred  to  hereinafter  as  “Findings  I,  { —,”  or
“Findings  IT,  { —.”  J.  App.  1-698.  There  were,  in
addition,  a  number  of  depositions  and  affidavits,
which  the  district  court  summarized,  ‘‘Findings  ITI.”
J.  App.  699-753.  Finally,  the  court  certified  28  consti-
tutional  questions.  J.  App.  754-760.  By  memorandum
and  order  of  May  19,  the  case  was  returned  to  the
court  of  appeals.  J.  App.  1.

Pursuant  to  the  order  of  the  court  of  appeals,  a
three-judge  district  court  was  convened  (consisting
of  District
Judge  Corcoran  and  two  circuit  judges).
That  court  and  the  court  of  appeals  en  bane,  sitting
together,  received  briefs  and  heard  oral  argument.  On
August  15, 1975,  both  courts  rendered  judgments  (J.S.
App.  1582-1586;  J.S.  App.  No.  75-437,  la-5a),  sup-
ported  by a  lengthy  per  curiam  opinion  of  the  court
of  appeals  (J.S.  App.  1505-1593),  which  the  district
court  adopted  in  relevant  part  (J.S.  App.  No.  75-
437,  1a).  In  effect,  both  courts  rejected  the  challenges
to the  legislation,  except  with  respect  to  2 U.S.C.  437a,
which  requires  disclosure  by  certain  organizations
which  expend  funds  or  do  acts  directed  to  the  public
for  the  purpose  of  influencing  the  election  or  advo-
cating  election  or  defeat  of  candidates,  which  was  de-
clared  unconstitutional  and  its  enforcement  enjoined.
Several  questions  relating  to the  composition  and  pow-
ers  of  the  Federal  Election  Commission  were  left
undecided  as  ‘‘not  presently  ripe  for  adjudication”
(J.S.  App.  1572).’

*Chief  Judge  Bazelon  dissented  only  from  the  majority  de-
cision  upholding  the  disclosure  requirements  as  applied  to  minor
parties.  J.S.  App.  1593-1597.  Judge  Tamm,  joined  by  Judge

None  of  the  defendants  or  intervening  defendants
having  appealed  from  the  ruling  invalidating  2 U.S.C.
437a,  the  case  is  now  before  this  Court  on  the  plain-
tiffs’  appeal  from  the  judgments  sustaining  the  leg-
islation  in all  other  respects.

ARGUMENT

INTRODUCTION  AND  SUMMARY

1.  Several  preliminary  remarks  are  appropriate  in
this  unusual  case.  Appellants’  lengthy  brief  challenges
on  innumerable  grounds  the  constitutionality  of  vir-
tually  every  provision  of  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  Amendments.  In  order  to  keep  our  submis-
sion  within  reasonable  length,  we  have  focused  upon
the  major  issues  and  have  dealt  only  briefly  or  not  at
all  with  many  of  appellants’  minor  points  which  we
deem  insubstantial.

To  a  considerable  extent,  appellants’  arguments  are
directed  to  the  wisdom  of  the  legislation  rather  than
its  constitutionality.  Again  and  again  they  contend
that  the  statute  will  not  accomplish  the  objectives  that
Congress  sought  to  achieve,  and  that  other  methods  of

Wilkey,  would  have  invalidated  the  provisions  establishing
ceilings  On  contributions  and  expenditures  as  well  as  those  pro-
viding  for  public  financing  of  Presidential  elections;  his  opin-
ion  also  condemns  the  method  of  appointment  of  the  Federal
Election  Commission.  J.S.  App.  1598-1609.  Judge  MacKinnon,
while  otherwise  joining  in  the  majority’s  decision,  would  have
invalidated  several  provisions  relating  to  the  powers  and  com-
position  of  the  Commission,  most  of  which  the  majority  of
the  court  of  appeals  deemed  not  ripe  for  adjudication.  J.S.
App.  1609-1623.

14

15

dealing  with  the  problem  of excessive  campaign  spend-
ing  would  be  preferable.  A  recurring  theme  through-
out  their  brief  is  that  disclosure  statutes  would  be
more  appropriate  than  the  present  limitations  upon
contributions  and  expenditures,  the  requirements  for
record-keeping  and  disclosure,  and  the  public  financ-
ing  of  Presidential  elections.

For  example,  appellants  state  (Br.  126-127)  that
“disclosure  of  large  contributions  with  a  potential  for
influence  *  *  *  rather  than  the  restriction  of  political
communication,  is  the  remedy  that  should  be  relied
upon  in  a  democratic  nation  which  trusts  its  voters.”
They  also  state  that  ‘‘[t]he  best  solution’’  to the  prob-
lem  of  contributions  “is  disclosure  of  large  contribu-
tions”  (Br.  144)  and  that  “appellants  believe  that  nar-
rowly  drawn  disclosure  requirements  are  the  proper
solution  to  virtually  all  the  evils  Congress  sought  to
(Br.
remedy  by  passing  the  challenged  statutes”
171).

Appellants’  brief  thus  in  large  measure  consists  of
policy  arguments  why  Congress  should  not  have  en-
acted  this  legislation.  Many  of  the  arguments  have
considerable  support  in  the  writings  of  political  com-
mentators  and  experts  on  government,  and  presum-
ably  they  were  vigorously  and  effectively  made  to
Congress  when  this  legislation  was  under  considera-
tion.  Congress,  however,  found  the  arguments  uncon-
vineing,  and  instead  adopted  the  procedures  and  tech-
niques  contained  in  the  Federal  Election  Campaign
of  those
Act  Amendments.  The  constitutionality

Amendments  is  the  sole  question  before  the  Court,
and  their  validity  is not  undermined  by the  fact  that
there  is a  substantial  body  of  opinion  which  believes
that  Congress  should  have  utilized  other  methods  of
dealing  with  the  problem.

The  claim  is  that  the  legislation  is  unconstitutional
on  its  face.  Many  of  the  specific  challenges,  however,
are  based  upon  speculation  about  the  effects  of  the
statute  as  applied  to  various  situations  which  have  not
securred  and  may  not  arise.  Although  the  implication
of  First  Amendment  rights  may  justify  examination
of  the  facial  validity  of legislation  which  allegedly  has
a  chilling  effect  upon  the  exercise  of  those  rights,  not
every  claim  of  facial  invalidity  need  be  resolved  mere-
ly because  it  is  made  within  the  rubric  of  protecting
First  Amendment  rights.  ‘‘The  delicate  power  of  pro-
nouncing  an  Act  of Congress  unconstitutional  is not  to
be  exercised  with  reference  to  hypothetical  cases  thus
imagined”’  (United  States  v.  Raines,  362  U.S.  17, 22).
As  the  court  of  appeals  correctly  ruled  in  this  case
and  as  we  argue  in  this  brief,  many  of  appellants’
attacks  upon  the  constitutionality  of the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Campaign  Act  are  essentially  premature.  Since
it is impossible  at  this  time  to  predict  how  the  statute
will  operate  in  numerous  respects  when  it  is  actually
applied  to  the  nominating  and  electoral  processes,  a
large  part  of  appellants’  constitutional  attacks  on  the
legislation  is  not  now  meet  for  judicial  resolution.

2.  With  these  introductory  comments,  we  turn  to  a
very  abbreviated  summary  of our  submission.  It would
unduly  burden  an  already  long  brief  to  attempt  more.

16

Our  discussion  begins  (Point  I,  infra,  pp.  17-37)
with  a  consideration  of  the  objectives  which  underlay
the  legislation.  We  identify  three:  (1)  reducing  elec-
toral  corruption,  in  the  form  of  undue  influence  by
financial  supporters  on  candidates;  (2)  partially  re-
storing  publie  confidence  in  representative  govern-
ment  by  allaying  popular  suspicions  that  large  mon-
eyed  interests  control  too  many  elected  officials;  and,
finally,  (3)  increasing  democracy  in  federal  elections
hy widening  the  meaningful  participation  of  ordinary
citizens  in electoral  campaigns.  We  conclude  that  each
of  these  ends  is  legitimate,  indeed  compelling.  And
we  submit  that  they  are  furthered  by the  several  fea-
tures  of  the  campaign  reform  statutes:  strict  record-
keeping  and  disclosure  requirements;  limits  on  money
contributions  to  candidates;  ceilings  on  campaign  ex-
peditures,  whether  made  directly  by the  candidate  and
his  agents  or  indirectly  by others  for  his  benefit;  and
optional  public  financing  of  Presidential  primaries,
conventions  and  general  elections.

We  next  examine  (Point  II,  infra,  pp.  37-69)  the
First  Amendment  objections  interposed  against  the
legislation.  We  focus  primarily  on  the  two  issues  that,
in  our  view,  are  the  major  First  Amendment  ques-
tions  presented:  (1)  whether  the  disclosure  require-
ments  impermissibly  inhibit  free  association,  and  (2)
whether  the  expenditure  ceilings  “cut  off”  free  speech
in  violation  of  the  Amendment.  After  full  examina-
tion  of  the  relevant  precedents,  we  conclude  that
the  impact  of  the  legislation  on  the  exercise  of  these
rights  is  minimal  and  that,  at  all  events,  the  impor-

ee
oe 2 ete

17

tance  of  the  governmental  objectives  (including  the
promotion  of First  Amendment  values)  outweighs  any
incidental  adverse  effect  on  free  association  and  politi-
cal  speech.

Finally  (Point  III,  infra,  pp.  69-83),  we  address
the  claim  that  the  new  law  discriminates  against  chal-
lengers  and  the  candidates  of  minor  and  new  parties.
We  consider  first  the  effect  of  the  contribution  and
expenditure  ceilings  and  then  the  public  financing
scheme  for  Presidential  elections.  In  both  cases,  we
show  that  the  attack  is  speculative  in  the  absence  of
actual  experience  under  the  new  provisions.  Although
the  challenged  legislation  will  not  eliminate  all  the
present  advantages  enjoyed  by incumbents  and  by the
candidates  of  established  major  parties,  its  probable
effect  will  be  to  reduce,  rather  than  enhance,  those
disparities.  Accordingly,  we  conclude  that  the  charge
of  discrimination  is  without  foundation.

I

THE  LEGISLATION  IS  RESPONSIVE  TO  LEGITIMATE  AND

COMPELLING  GOVERNMENTAL  CONCERNS

on

grounds

constitutional

The  threshold

question  whenever  legislation

is
challenged
is  always
whether,  in  the  words  of  Chief  Justice  Marshall,  “the
end  [is]  legitimate’’  and  the  “means  *  *  *  are  appro-
priate  *
*  [and]  plainly  adapted  to  that  end.”’
McCulloch  v.  Maryland,  4 Wheat.  316,  421.  We  turn
first,  therefore,  to  the  congressional  objectives  and
the  mechanisms  selected  to  implement  them—leaving
for  later  consideration  the  objection  that  the  means

*

18

19

“are * *  *  prohibited.”’  Ibid.  While  we  do  not  now
address  the  First  Amendment  claims,  our  discussion
of  the  legitimacy  of  the  congressional  objectives  and
the  appropriateness  of  the  means  selected  must  take
into  account  that  the  legislation  significantly  affects
First  Amendment  interests;  we  accordingly  show  that
the  legislation  is responsive  to  a  “compelling’’  govern-
mental  interest.  See  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391
U.S.  367,  376-377.

A.  LEGITIMACY  OF  THE  OBJECTIVES

The  challenged  federal  election  campaign  laws  were
intended  to  serve  at  least  three  objectives:  (1)  to  les-
sen  the  danger  of  money  corruption  in  electoral  cam-
paigns;  (2)  to  restore  public  confidence  in representa-
tive  government;  and  (3)  to  democratize  elections  by
diminishing  the  power  of  wealthy  interests  and  en-
couraging  wider  citizen  participation.  Each  of  these
goals  is  a  proper  predicate  for  congressional  action
and  any  one  is sufficient  to  justify  the  legislation.  We
consider  them  seriatim.

1.  Money  corruption  of  candidates  and  elected  officials

The  principal,  and  the  most  vocalized,  objective  of
the  legislation  was  to  remove,  or  diminish,  the  risk
that  certain  individuals  or  groups  will  “buy”  or  play
an  inordinate  role  in  the  election  of  selected  candi-
dates  for  federal  office  and  that,  once  elected,  these
officials  will  be  corrupted  by a  tie  with  large  campaign
contributions.

a.  That  congressionzl  power  exists  to  combat  such
an  evil  is not  debatable...  As  long ago  as  Ex  parte  Yar-
brough,  110  U.S.  651,  687,  the  Court  expressly  recog-
nized  authority  to  deal  with  “the  free  use  of money  in
elections,”  and  that  dictum  was  confirmed  in  Bur-
roughs  and  Cannon  v.  United  States,  290  U.S.  534,
sustaining  the  constitutionality  of  the  Federal  Cor-
rupt  Practices  Act  of  1925  as  applied  to  Presidential
Holm,  285  U.S.  355,  366-
elections.  See,  also,  Smiley  v.
367;  Roudebush  v.
Hartke,  405  U.S.  15, 24-25.  Whether
the  power  is  “implied”  or  “inherent”  in  the  con-
stitutional  scheme,  or  derives  frem  Article  I,  Sec-
tion  4, of  the  Constitution  or  from  the  Necessary  and
Proper  Clause  of  the  Same  Article  (Section  8, clause
18),  may  be  unclear.  Compare  Ex  parte  Yarbrough,
supra,  110  U.S.  at  658,  and  Burroughs  and  Cannon  v.
United  States,  supra,  290  U.S.  at  545,  with  Smiley  v.
Holm,  supra,  285  U.S.  at 366-367,  and  United  States  v.
Classic,  313  U.S.  299,  314-321.  And  see  Oregon  v.
Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  119-124  (Opinion  of  Black,
J.).  But  there  is no  doubt  that  Congress  may  legislate
to  this  end.

Nor  can  it be  disputed  that  the  goal  of freeing  elec-
tions  from  the  corrupting  influence  of  money  is  not
only  a  legitimate  subject  of  legislation,  but  a  com-
pelling,  indeed,  a  vital  governmental  concern.  The  im-
portance  of  voting,  the  ‘‘fundamental  political  right,
hecause  preservative  of  all  rights”  (Yick  Wo  v.  Hop-
kins,  118  U.S.  356,  370),  has  been  too  often  restated  by
this  Court  to  require  elaboration  here.  See,  e.g.,  Wes-

20

berry  v.  Sanders,  376  U.S.  1,  17;  Reynolds  v.  Sims,
377  U.S.  533,  562;  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23, 31;
Kramer  v.  Union  School  District,  395  U.S.  621,  626;
Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709,  714.  So  has  the  corol-
lary,  that  there  is a  compelling  governmental  interest
in  keeping  elections  free  from  the  corrupting  influ-
ence  of  “big  money.”  Ex  parte  Yarbrough,  supra;
Smiley  v.  Holm,  supra;  United  States  vy.  Auto  Work-
ers,  302  U.S.  567,  570-589.

b.  So  much  is,  we  assume,  unchallenged.  But,  say
the  appellants,  however  legitimate  it  might  be  (First
Amendment  objections  aside)  for  Congress  to  address
such  an  evil  if it were  real,  there  is no  present  danger
of  money  corruption  in  federal  elections,  and,  even
if there  were,  the  legislation  is not  appropriate  to  deal
with  it.

The  assertion  that  the  corrupting  influence  of
wealthy  interests  is  not  a  real  threat  to  the  electoral
process  and  representative  government  insults  the  in-
telligence,  and  the  integrity,  of  successive  Congresses
who  for  three  quarters  of  a  century  have  been
wrestling  with  the  problem.  That  history,  which  in-
cludes  statutes  enacted  in  1907  (34  Stat.  864),  1910
(36  Stat.  822),  1911  (37  Stat.  25),  1925  (43  Stat.
1070),  1940  (54  Stat.  767),  1943  (57  Stat.  163,  167)
and  1947  (61  Stat.  136,  159),  is  fully  recited  in  the
opinion  below  (J.S.  App.  1511-1513,  1589-1593)  and
in  United  States  vy.  Auto  Workers,  supra,  352  U.S.
at  570-583.  See,  also,  United  States  y.  C.I.0.,  335  U.S.
106,  113-115;  Pipefitters  v.  United  States,  407  U.S.

21

385,  402-412.  What  is  revealed  is  a  continuing  legis-
lative  concern  with  “the  corroding  effect  of  money
employed  in  elections  by  aggregated  power.”  United
States  v.  Auto  Workers,  supra,  352  U.S.  at  582.  The
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  and  the  1974
Amendments  challenged  here  represent  merely  the
culmination  of that  long  effort.

Against  this  background  and  the  ample  legislative
history  recounted  in  the  opinion  below  and  in  Auto
Workers,  supra,  it  would  be  pointless  to  indulge  in
a  debate  over  what  informed  the  congressional  mind
when  it  enacted  the  statutes  in  suit.  It  is hardly  sur-
prising  that  the  record  is not  replete  with  confessions
of  corrupt  influence.  There  is, however,  quite  enough
to  require  acceptance  of the  legislative  judgment  that,
absent  regulation,  there  would  be a  real  risk  that  large
contributions  would  corrupt  both  the  electoral  process
and  some  of  our  elected  officials.  See,  e.g.,  H.R.  Rep.
No.  93-1239,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  3 (1974)  ; 8S. Rep.  No.
93-689,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  4  (1974);  Findings  I,
"{  105,  108,  110-127,  146,  156-165;  J.  App.  147,  148-
156,  203,  253-255.  See,  also,  Findings  I, {1  101-103;
J. App.  54-146.

2.  Public  confidence  in  elected  government

Appellants  correctly  note  (Br.  63)  that  congres-
sional  speakers  often  expressed  a  concern  about  public
suspicion  of  the  improper  influence  of  “big  money,”
rather  than  actual  corruption.  What  is  demonstrated,
however,  is  no  more  than  that  this  was  an  additional
consideration  behind  the  enactment.  And  that  is  en-
tirely  natural.  From  the  beginning,  statutory  restric-

22

tions  on  the  unlimited  use  of  money  in  political  cam-
paigns  have  responded,  in  part,  to  public  suspicions
and  a  concern  to  avoid  the  appearance  of  evil.  Thus,
this  Court  described  the  1907  law  banning  most  cor-
porate  political  contributions  as  ultimately  provoked
by  ‘‘popular  feeling  that  aggregated  capital  unduly
influenced  politics,  an  influence  not  stopping  short  of
corruption.””  United  States  v.  Auto  Workers,  supra,
352  U.S.  at  570.  Surely,  no  one  doubts  that  the  elec-
torate  of  today  shares  those  views.  See  Findings  [,
©  133,  144-151,  153-155,  171:  J.  App.  159-189,  192-
209, 214-253,  257.

Nor  is there  anything  improper  in  legislating  to  re-
store  public  confidence  in  elected  government.  On  the
contrary,  that  is  a  wholly  legitimate  objective,  and,
indeed,  a  critical  one  in  times  of deep  public  suspicion
and  apathy  grounded  in  the  citizens’  belief  that
of
‘their’?
wealthy  special  interests.  If  authority  is  needed  to
support  so  self-evident  a  proposition,  we  cite  Civil
Service  Commission  Vv.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548,
565,  expressly  invoking  congressional  concern  for  ap-
pearances  in  support  of  the  Hatch  Act  ban  on  active
electioneering  by government  employees:

representatives

“captives’’

often

are

There  is  another  consideration  in  this  judg-
ment:  it is not  only  important  that  the  Govern-
ment  and  its  employees  in  fact  avoid  practicing
political  justice,  but  it  is  also  critical  that  they
appear  to  the  public  to  be  avoiding  it,  if  con-
fidence  in  the  system  of  representative  Govern-
ment  is not  to  be  eroded  to  a  disastrous  extent.

23

3.  Move  democratic  elections

Congress  was  also  attempting,  by  limiting  the  in-
fluence  of  wealthy  supporters,  placing  a  ceiling  on  ex-
penditures  and  providing  public  funds,  to democratize
federal  elections.  Fears  of  corruption  aside,  the  ob-
jective  was  to  lessen  the  disproportionate  advantage,
the  distorting  effect,  of wealthy  special  interest  groups,
and  to  increase  opportunities  for  meaningful  partici-
pation  by  ordinary  citizens,  as  voters,  supporters
and  candidates.  Appellants,  it  seems,  both  question
the  propriety  of  such  a  goal  and  doubt  whether  the
legislation  will  accomplish  it.

a.  Assuming  the  congressional  objective  was  not  to
‘‘freeze”  the  status  quo,  but,  rather,  to  open  up  fed-
eral  elections  to  wider  participation,  is  that  a  legiti-
mate  goal?  The  question  would  seem  to  answer  itself.
Indeed,  First  Amendment  objections  aside  {see  Point
II, infra),  what  can  be wrong  under  our  constitutional
system  with  an  attempt  to  assure  that  access  to  fed-
eral  elective  office  is made  available  to  a  greater  num-
ber  and  the  influence  of  supporters  and  contributors
is  made  more  equal?  The  consistent  jurisprudence  of
the  Court  has  approved  that  trend,  expressly  con-
demning  restrictive  rules  for  political  participation
that  discriminate  against  the  poor.  Lubin  v.  Panish,
supra;  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134;  Harper  v.
Virginia  Bd.  of  Elections,  383  U.S.  663.  See,  also,
Cipriano  Vv.  City  of Houma,  395  U.S.  701;  Phoeniz  v.
Kolodziejski,  399  U.S.  204;  Police  Jury  of Vermillion
v.  Herbet,  404  U.S.  807.

595-040-—75—-—-3

24

To  be  sure,  as  in  most  things,  enlarging  the  oppor-
tunities  of  the  many  diminishes  the  prerogatives  of
the  few.  But,  in  a  constitutional  system  involving  the
egalitarian  principle  of “one  person,  one  vote”  (Gray  Vv.
Sanders,  372  U.S.  368, 381;  Wesberry  v.  Sanders,  supra,
376  U.S.  at  7-8,  18:  Reynolds  vy.  Sims,  supra,  377  U.S.
at  561-568),  that  is no  objection,  so  long  as  no  one  is
excluded  from  participation.  The  inflated  voice  of
large  contributors,  the  head-start  of  wealthy  candi-
dates,  the  entrenched  position  of  the  major  political
parties,  are  not  vested  rights  which  enjoy  constitu-
tional  immunity  from  legislative  efforts  to  open  the
political  arena  more  widely.  One  might  as  well  argue
that  it  was  unconstitutional  to  deprive  those  who
satisfied  property  or  literacy  qualifications  for  voting
of  their  monopoly  when  the  Constitution  itself  did
not  mandate  it.  Cf.  Oregon  v.  Mitchell,  supra.

At  one  time  it  would  have  been  said  that  there  ts
a  difference  between  withdrawing  an  “artificial’’  pre-
rogative  or  advantage  which  the  state  itself  has
conferred  and  interfering  with  the  “natural’’  advan-
tage  that  wealth  carries.  But  that  argument  has  bec!
foreclosed  at  least  since  the  300th  volume  of  the
Court’s  Reports.  See,  e.g.,  West  Coast  Hotel  Co.  v.
Parvish,  300  U.S.  3879;  United  States  v.  Darby,  312
U.S.  100;  Ferguson  v.  Skrupa,  372  U.S.  726.  To
paraphrase  Mr.  Justice  Holmes  (Lochner  v.  New
York,  198  U.S.  45,  75),  the  Constitution  does  hot
enact  the  “laissez  faire”  philosophy  that  exalts  “‘rug-
eed  individualism’?  and  the  “free  market’’  over

25

every  other  value  and  forbids  all  governmental  inter-
ference  to  redress  gross  imbalance  or  to  protect  the
consumer  against  deceit.  If  it  were  so,  the  antitrust
laws,  the  pure  food  and  drug  laws,  the  minimum  wave
laws,  the  social  security  laws,  the  fair  employncut
Jaws,  and  a  myriad  of  other  regulatory  statutes,  state
and  federal,  not  to  mention  the  progressive  income
tax,  would  be  invalid.

It  will  be  said  that  cases  dealing  with  economic
regulation  have  nothing  to do  with  our  subject-matter.
But  that  is,  at  best,  only  a  half-truth.  One  of  the
major  concerns  of  the  present  law  is  the  influence  of
undue  economic  power  upon  the  electoral  processes.
It  is  not  irrelevant  that  the  Court’s  recitation  of  the
history  of  restrictions  on  political  contributions  hy
ageregated  wealth  in  United  States  y.
Auto  Workers,
supra,  352  U.S,  at  570,  begins  with  the  antitrust  laws
and  the  progressive  income  tax.  There  are,  to  be  sure,
First  Amendment  inhibitions  here.  But,  as  we  shall
argue  in  Point  IT,  infra,  the  Amendment  does  not
preclude  government  action  to  deal  with  the  problem
that  large  contributors  may  be  able  to  monopolize
or  grossly  distort  the  political  process,  even  absent
any  danger  of  actual  corruption.

Nor  is  this  a  radical  new  idea.  Two  decades  aco,
this  Court  noted,  with  apparent  approval,  that  the
first  federal  law  restricting  campaign  contributions
(the  Act  of  1907)  was  aimed  “not  merely  to  prevent
the  subversion  of  the  integrity  of  the  electoral  proc-
ess’?  but  that  its  “underlying  philosophy  was  to  sis-
tain  the  active,  alert  responsibility  of  the  individual

26

27

citizen  in  a  democracy  for  the  wise  conduct  of
government.’’  United  States  v.  Auto  Workers,  supra,
352  U.S.  at  575.  See,  also,  id.  at 570.  Even  the  staunch-
est  guardians  of  the  First  Amendment  on  this  Court
have  recognized  that  it may  be  ‘‘an  evil  for  organized
eroups  to  have  unrestricted  freedom  to make  expendi-
tures  for  directly  and  openly  publicizing  their  political
views  and  information  supporting  them”;  such  unre-
stricted  freedom  is  “subject  to  some  curtailment,  in
the  interest  of  preventing  grossly  unbalanced  presen-
tations.”  United  States  v.  C.I.O.,  supra,  335  U.S.  at
145,  146  (Rutledge,  J.,  concurring).  More  recently,
Mr.  Chief  Justice  Warren,  Mr.  Justice  Black  and
Mr.  Justice  Douglas  have  said  that  ‘*[i]f  Congress  is
of the  opinion  that  large  contributions  by labor  unions
to  candidates  for  office  and  to  political  parties  have
had  an  undue  influence  upon  the  conduct  of  elections,
it  ean  prohibit  such  contributions.”  United  States  v.
Auto  Workers,  supra,  352  U.S.  at  598,  n.  2  (Douglas,
J.,  dissenting).

The  need  for  regulatory  action  to  ensure  that  fed-
eral  elections  are  not  unduly  influenced  by wealthy  in-
terests  has  increased  with  the  years.  With  the  shift
from  old-fashioned  face-to-face  electioneering  to  radio
and  television  appearances,  the  extended  duration  of
campaigns,  the  massively  increased  size  of  political
organizations,  and  the  spiralling  costs  of  all  ingredi-
ents  of the  campaign  process,  the  importance  of finan-
cial  support  has  multiplied  and  with  it the  power  of
wealthy  interests.  See  Findings  I, {1  12, 88, 89, 93-95,
G8,  100;  J.  App.  9-10,  49-51,  53,  54.  Those  realities  of

today  fully  justify  governmental  intervention  to  pre-
serve  democracy  in  national  elections.

B.  THE  APPROPRIATENESS  OF  THE  MEANS

What  has  been  said  sufficiently  demonstrates  that
there  were  compelling  needs  that  Congress  might  ad-
dress.  It  remains  to  show  that  the  challenged  legis-
lation  was  in fact  tailored  to meet  those  needs.

One  ean  object  that,  however  prevalent  is  the  un-
due  influence—or  the  appearance  of undue  influence—
of  big money  in  federal  elections  and  however  sincere
the  congressional  intention  to  deal  with  that  prob-
lem  and  to democratize  the  electoral  process,  the  pres-
ent  legislation  is unresponsive  to the  stated  objectives.
Indeed,  after  denying  the  existence  of  the  evil,  ap-
pellants  say  as  much.

The  claim  is  extravagant.  Of  course,  one  can  argue
about  the  particular  dollar  figures  selected  to  trigger
recordkeeping  or  disclosure  requirements  or  which
establish  ceilings  on  contributions  or  expenditures,
and  about  the  specific  formulae  used  to  determine
eligibility  for  public  funds.  But,  absent  a  compelling
demonstration  that  the  mechanisms  chosen  are  gross-
ly  disproportionate  to  the  problems  addressed,  it  is
no  part  of  the  judicial  function  to  second-guess  the
legislative  judgment.

In  Burroughs  and  Cannon  vy.  United  States,  supra,

290  U.S.  at 547-548,  the  Court  said:

The  power  of Congress  to protect  the  election
of  President  and  Vice  President  from  cor-
ruption  being  clear,  the  choice  of means  to  that
