28

end  presents  a  question  primarily  addressed  to
the  judgment  of Congress.  If  it can  be seen  that
the  means  adopted  are  really  calculated  to  at-
tain  the  end,  the  degree  of  their  necessity,  tlie
extent  to  which  they  conduce  to  the  end,  the
closeness  of  the  relationship  between  the  means
adopted  and  the  end  to be  attained,  are  matters
for  congressional  determination  alone.

It  may  be  that  the  test  is  somewhat  stricter  today,
especially  in the  face  of a  First  Amendment  challenge.
But  no  decision  of  this  Court  remotely  suggests  that
constitutionality  depends  on  a  perfect  scheme  or  a
choice  of  what  judges  believe  would  be  the  best  for-
mula.  On  the  contrary,  in  sustaining  the  Hatch  Act,
the  Court  only  recently  rejected  the  argument  that
Congress  should  have  selected  a  different  remedy  in
these  words  (Civil  Service  Commission  vy.  Letter  Car-
riers,  supra,  413  U.S.  at  567):

Perhaps  Congress  at  some  time  will  come  to
a  different  view  of  the  realities  of  political  life
and  Government  service;  but  that  is its  current
view  of  the  matter,  and  we  are  not  now  in  any
position  to dispute  it.

Congress  was  not  writing  on  a  clean  slate.  As  we
have  noted,  for  almost  three-quarters  of  a  century
there  had  been  piecemeal  efforts  to  deal  with  the  prob-
lem  of  the  undue  influence  of  money  in  federal  elec-
tions.  But,  as  the  court  of  appeals  remarked  (J.S.
App.  1512),  “[t]he  achievements  of  the  statutes  were
overmatched  by  what  proved  to  be  wholesale  circum-
vention.”  “‘{T]he  failure  of  piecemeal  regulations  to
preserve  the  integrity  of federal  elections’’  (J.S.  App.

29

1593)—a  fact  hardly  debatable  in  view  of  what  the
court  below  characterized  as  “[t]he  excesses  revealed
by this  record”’  (J.S8.  App.  1581)—prompted  Congress
to  act  more  vigorously  and  more  comprehensively.
That  is, of  course,  a  relevant  consideration  in  assess-
ing  the  appropriateness  of  the  means  now  selected,
each  feature  of  which  must  be  seen  as  an  integral
part  of  a  comprehensive  attempt,  after  long  forbear-
ance,  to  correct  a  perceived  blight  on  our  democratic
processes.  Cf.  South  Carolina  v.  Katzenbach,  383
U.S.  301,  308-315,  328,  337.

With  this  preface,  we  turn  to  the  legislation  to  de-
scribe  how  its  several  features  are  calculated  to  con-
tribute  to  the  announced  goals—reserving  for  later
discussion  specific  constitutional  objections  to  partic-
ular  provisions.

1.  Recordkeeping  and  disclosure  requirements

The  virtue  of  public  disclosure  in  this  area  is  too
obvious  te  require  extended  discussion.  Indeed,  ap-
pellants  do  not  challenge  the  requirement  that  cam-
paign  expenditures  be  disclosed  (see  Br.  170).  They
do,  however,  object  to  compulsory  disclosure  of  all
contributions  over  $100.  Yet,  that  has  been  a  require-
ment  of  federal  law  since  1910.  See  United  States  v.
Auto  Workers,  supra,  352  U.S.  at  575-576,  The  im-
portance  of  such  a  rule  to  deter  corrupt  givers  and
discourage  receivers  from  being  corrupted  by  contri-
butions  is  self-evident.  As  this  Court  concluded  with
respect  to  the  lke  requirement  in  the  Federal  Cor-
rupt  Practices  Act  of  1925,  “[t]he  verity  of  this  con-

30

31

clusion  reasonably  cannot  be  denied.’’  Burroughs  and
Cannon  v.  United  States,  supra,  290 U.S.  at 548.

Nor  is  that  all.  Fears  of  an  improper  tie  between
contributor  and  candidate  aside,  disclosure  serves  an
informing  function:  the  knowledge  of  who  is  backing
which  candidate  aids  the  voter  in  appraising  the  con-
testants  and  determining  what  each  really  stands  for.
a.  The  suggestion  that  only  successful  candidates
be  required  to  disclose  their  contributions  (Appel-
lants’  Br.  n.  8, p.  182)  ignores  the  importance  of  the
‘bought’?  or  ‘‘stalking  horse”  candidate  who,  though
unsuccessful,  may  deflect  votes  from  others  whom  the
electorate  might  prefer  if they  knew  the  facts.  Wisely,
the  law  requires  disclosure  in  primaries  as  well  as
general  elections,  and  before  the  voting  day.  See  2
U.S.C.  434(a)(1)(A) (i);  2  U.S.C.  434(e).  Drawing
on  the  analogy  the  Court  itself  made  in  United  States
v.  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612,  625,  it is important  “to  main-
tain  the  integrity  of  a  basic  governmental  process”
that  the  voters  know,  with  respect  to  every  candidate,
“who  is putting  up  the  money,  and  how  much,”’  so  that
they  may  better  evaluate  him.

These  considerations  apply  to  all  candidates—suc-
cessful  or  unsuccessful—including  those  that  are  en-
tirely  legitimate.  As  the  court  of  appeals  noted,  com-
menting  on  the  “angels’’  who  often  support  minor
party  candidates,  ‘‘the  voting  population  has  an  in-
terest  in  knowing  the  composition  of the  angels’  wings
and  what  makes  them  fly.”’  J.S.  App.  1545,  n.  114.
Not  surprisingly,  besides  the  Congress,  the  legisla-

tures  of  34  States  have  also  reached  that  conclusion
and  require  disclosure  of  campaign  contributions.  See
Developments  in the Law—Elections,  88 Harv.  L. Rev.
1111,  1241-1242  (1975).

b.  Appellants’  further  objection  that  the  $100  limit
for  undisclosed  contributions  is  unrealistically  low
(Br.  170,  174-175,  184-189)  invites  impermissible
speculation.  See  United  States  vy.  Finance  Commit-
tee  to  Re-elect  President,  507  F.  2d  1194,  1200
(C.A.D.C.).  It is the  same  ceiling  selected  by Congress
in  the  1925  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act*  and  the
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  and  equal
to  or  greater  than  that  adopted  by  some  20  States.
See  Developments  in  the  Law—Elections,  supra,  88
Hary.  L.  Rev.  at  1241-1242,  A  $100  contribution  may
well  be  sufficient  to  “attract  the  attention  of  the  can-
didate.’’  Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379  F.  Supp.  704,  713
(N.D.  Ga.).  At  all  events,  the  $100  level  serves  to
uncover  the  common  situation  in  which  many  people
associated  with  a  single  enterprise,  or  occupation,
pool  their  contributions  to make  an  impact.

c.  Like  considerations  underlie  the  requirement  that
contributions  exceeding  $10 be  recorded  with  the  name
of  the  donor.  If  this  were  not  done,  it  would  be  pos-
sible  to evade  the  disclosure  requirement—and,  indeed,
the  maximum  contribution  limitation—by  successively
contributing  many  small  unidentified  amounts.

*In  the  1910  statute  reporting  was  required  of  contributions
which  aggregated  $50  per  year.  See  United  States  vy.  Auto  Work-
ers,  supra,  352  U.S.  at 575-576.

32

2.  Ceiling  on  contributions

Despite  their  challenge  to  disclosure  of any  or  most
contributions,  appellants  argue  (Br.  117-118,  126-
127)  that  disclosure  is  the  only  appropriate  remedy
for  corruption  through  large  donations.  That  is, how-
ever,  an  overly  optimistic,  if  not  naive,  notion.  It
the  consistent  congressional  judgment
contradicts
since  the  turn  of  the  century  that  disclosure,  alone,
cannot  deter  the  improper  influence  of  aggregated
wealth.  Beginning  with  the  outright  ban  on  corporate
campaign  contributions  in  1907,  there  have  been  re-
peated  attempts  to  limit  the  size  of  contributions  or
the  total  expenditure  of  candidates,  or  both.  Unfortu-
nately,  prior  statutes  contained  so  many  loopholes
that  they  had  little  effect.  See  Findings  I, {/] 130,  131,
137,  139-145;  J.  App.  156-158,  190-203.  And  so,  in
the  light  of  recent  experience,  Congress  determined
to  deal  with  the  problem  more  comprehensively.

a.  Setting  a  ceiling  on  contributions  from  any  sin-
gle  source  is  the  most  direct  way  of  preventing  the
visk  of  electoral  corruption  by  big  money.  The  ob-
jection  cannot  be  that  such  an  approach  is ineffective:
the  argument  must  be that  the  weapon  is unnecessarily
lethal.  But  is it?

If  the  object  is  to  reduce  the  risk  that  candidates,
successful  and  unsuccessful,  will  be  too  closely  tied—
or  appear  to  be—to  large  moneyed  interests,  how  else
is  that  goal  to  be  accomplished?  Certainly,  the  pros-
pect  of  exposure  will  often  deter  the  corrupt  trans-
action  and  exposure  after  the  fact  may  appreciably

33
weaken  a  corrupt  link.  As  we  have  seen,  those  are
good  reasons  for  disclosure  provisions.  But  experience
convinced  Congress  that  publicity  is  not  a  cure-all.
Too  often,  revelations  of undue  influence  by rich  spon-
sors  are  simply  shrugged  off  or  produce  a  cynical  in-
difference  in  a  citizenry  that  despairs  of  reforming
its  politicians.  What  is  more,  reporting  laws  are  not
always  obeyed.  The  risk  of  corruption  is  manifestly
reduced  when  both  parties,  contributor  and  recipient,
are  subject  to  eriminal  penalty  for  exceeding  the
limitation.

.

There  is,  of  course,  a  more  radical  alternative:
exclusive  public  financing  of  political  campaigns  for
federal  office.  Indeed,  the  optional  scheme  of the  pres-
ent  law,  as  we  shall  see,  serves  that  end  by  freeing
Presidential  candidates  from  total  dependence  on  pri-
vate  contributions.  But,  unless  public  financing  is com-
bined  with  a  prohibition,  or  a  limitation,  on  private
financing  of  campaigns,  it cannot  eliminate  the  temp-
tation  of  wealthy  interests  to  finance  candidates  who
they  believe  will  be  sympathetic  to  their  interests  and
perhaps  provide  reciprocal  favors.  Appellants  cannot
have  it both  ways:  so  long  as  private  contributions  to-
ward  the  election  of  federal  officials  are  permitted,  it
is  necessary  to  restrict  the  amount  from  any  single
source.

b.  What  is  more,  ceilings  on  contributions  impor-
tantly  serve  the  distinct  objective  of  widening  active
participation  in  the  electoral  process.  They  operate  in
several  ways  to make  elections  more  democratic.  The

34

candidate,  no  longer  able  to rely on  a few  wealthy  sup-
porters,  must  now  invite  the  ordinary  citizen  to  take
an  active  part  in  the  campaign.  The  citizen,  now  as-
sured  that  every  candidate  is anxious  for  his  help,  will
be  more  inclined  to  take  a  real  interest,  selecting  more
carefully  and  contributing  more  freely,  in  the  knowl-
edge  that  his  support  matters.  And,  finally,  eliminat-
ing  the  huge  advantage  of  those  who  could  depend  on
an  established  list  of  large  contributors  will  allow
new  challengers  to  enter  the  lists  with  a  fair  chance  of
successfully  appealing  to  the  electorate  at  large.

ce.  Once  the  principle  of  limiting  the  size  of  cam-
paign  contributions  is  conceded,  the  task  is  to  devise
rules  which  are  fair  and  effective.  That  is the  special
achievement  of  the  present  legislation,  which  plugged
most  of the  notorious  loopholes  of prior  law.  See  Find-
ings  I, £137;  J.  App.  190.  Now  a  ceiling  of  $1,000  is
placed  on  individual  contributions  to,  or  earmarked
for,  a  single  candidate,  18  U.S.C.  608(b) (1),  (4) (A)
and  (6),  and  an  overall  contribution  limit  of  $25,000
is  imposed  with  respect  to  any  election  period.  18
U.S.C.  608(b)(3).  These  provisions,  combined  with
the  $5,000  ceiling  on  contributions  to  a  particular
candidate  by subsidiary  political  committees,  18 U.S.C.
608(b)(2),  and  the  broad  definition  of  what  consti-
tutes  a  contribution,  18  U.S.C.  591(e),  are  carefully
designed  to  prevent,  so  far  as  possible,  any  undue  fi-
nancial  link  between  candidate  and  contributors.  Each
of  these  ceilings  complements  the  others  and  each  is
necessary  to  implement  the  objective.

35

3.  Ceiling  on  expenditures

a.  One  provision  of  the  new  law,  the  $1,000  limita-
tion  on  independent  expenditures  in  support  of  a
particular  candidate,  18  U.S.C.  608(e),  has  attracted
special  criticism.  Yet,  it  is  a  necessary  ceiling  if  the
restriction  on  large  contributions  is not  to  be  evaded.
Indeed,  but  for  such  a  provision  an  individual  or
organization  could  massively  support  a  favored  can-
didate  by  spending  directly  on  his  behalf,  rather
than  giving  a  money  contribution  to  the  candidate  for
him  to  spend.  Cf.  United  States  v.  C.I.0O.,  supra,  335
U.S.  at  115;  United  States  vy.  Auto  Workers,  supra,
352  U.S.  at  579-587.  And  see  Findings  I,  © 136;  J.
App.  190.

A  different  approach  to  the  problem  was  attempted
in  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971.  See
American  Civil  Liberties  Union  v.  Jennings,  366  F,
Supp.  1041  (D. D.C.),  vacated  as  moot  sub  nom.  Staats
v.  American  Civil  Liberties  Union,  422  U.S.  1030.
The  new  law  has  avoided  the  charge  of  “censorship”’
levelled  at  the  repealed  provision.  Publications  and
broadcasters  are  no  longer  required  to  obtain  the
candidate's  agreement  to  have  the  cost  of  a  “commer-
cial”  counted  against  his  expenditure  limit:  under
present  law,  the  sponsor’s  expenditure,  though  limited,
is subject  to no  veto  and  the  candidate  is not  penalized.
One  can  readily  appreciate  the  congressional  pref-
erence  for  the  present  solution.  But  some  restriction
on  independent  campaign  expenditures  in support  of a
particular  candidate  is  necessary  if  the  limitations
on  campaign  contributions  are  to  work.

36

37

b.  The  rationale  of  the  other  ceilings  on  campaign
expenditures  is perhaps  less  self-evident.  It is arguable
that  the  risk  of  undue  influence  by aggregated  wealth
is  fully  met  by  ceilings  on  contributions  and  inde-
pendent  expenditures.  The  fact,  however,  is that  when
campaign  spending  is  unlimited,  the  pressures  for
filling  the  void  with  large  donations  becomes  irre-
sistible.

The  expenditure  ceilings  importantly  contribute
to  lessening  the  opportunities  and  the  temptations  for
money  corruption.  That  has  been  the  congressional
judgment  since  1911.

c.  What  is more,  limiting  the  amount  each  candidate
may  spend  tends  to  curb  the  exaggerated  role  of
money  in  our  elections.  It  also  is  likely  to  encourage
participation  as  candidates  by many  who  in  the  past
remained  inactive  on  the  ground  that,  inevitably,  they
could  not  compete  against  the  established  fund-raiser.
The  limit  placed  on  a  candidate’s  expenditure  from
his  personal  or  family  funds  (18  U.S.C.  608(a))  con-
tributes  to  the  same  end,  by decreasing  the  advantage
a  wealthy  candidate  would  otherwise  enjoy—especially
under  a  regime  of  strict  contribution  ceilings—and
thereby  widening  the  opportunities  for  successful  can-
didacy.  The  combined  effect  of  these  provisions  also
must  be  substantially  to  democratize  federal  elections.

4. Public  financing  of Presidential  elections

Many  of  the  foregoing  considerations  support  the
provision  of public  funds—within  the  ceilings  already
established—in  Presidential  elections.  We  need  not

. ———=_

.

belabor  the  point  that  the  availability  of public  money
lessens  the  candidate’s  dependence  on  contributions
from  wealthy  groups  and  individuals.  To  be  sure,
federal  funds  are  not  given  to  a  candidate  who  does
not  prove  himself  a  serious  contender.  We  later  dis-
cuss  the  claim  that  the  scheme  is discriminatory.  But,
for  present  purposes,  it  is  enough  that  the  public
funding  provisions  obviously  further  the  objective
of  diminishing  the  danger  of  corruption  through
dependence  on  large  campaign  contributions,  and,
equaliy  important,  they  widen  the  opportunities  of
ordinary  citizens  to  participate  actively  in  electoral
campaigns,  both  as  candidates  and  as  supporters.

II

THE  LEGISLATION  DOES  NOT  IMPERMISSIBLY  TRENCH  ON

FIRST  AMENDMENT  RIGHTS

We  have  shown  that  the  challenged  legislation  re-
sponds  to legitimate  and  compelling  governmental  con-
cerns  and  that  it  implements  powers  reserved  to  the
national  Congress  by the  Constitution.  That  would  be
the  end  of  the  inquiry  were  it not  for  the  claim  that
the  new  law  impermissibly  discriminates  (see  Point
III,  infra)  and  trenches  on  First  Amendment  rights.
We  now  discuss  the  latter  objection.

A.  THE  DISCLOSURE  REQUIREMENTS

We  have  already  discussed  the  compelling  objec-
tives  served  by  disclosure  of  political  contributions
and  expenditures,  and  explained  why  it  is  necessary

38

to  fix  the  disclosure  limit  low  and  to  insist  that  con-
tributions  be  revealed  by  all  candidates  before  the
voting  occurs  (supra,  pp.  29-31).  We  now  discuss
the  arguments  that  the  disclosure  requirements  vio-
late  the  First  Amendment.

While  the  arguments  overlap,  it  will  be  useful  to
distinguish  two  propositions:  (1)  the  claim  that  indi-
viduals  have  an  unconditional  right  to  anonymous
speech,  regardless  of  any  association  with  others  for
a  common  purpose;  and  (2)  the  dishaket  claim  of
associational  privacy,  independent  of  any  connection
with  speaking  or  writing.  The  first  argument  pri-
marily  rests  on  Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.  60;
the  second  largely  relics  on  the  N.A.A.C.P.  cases,
N.A.A.C.P.  vy.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449;  Bates  v.  Little
Rock,  361  U.S.  516;  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479;
Louisiana  v.  N.A.A.C.P.,  366  U.S.  293;  N.A.A.C-.P.
v.  Button,  371  U.S.  415;  Gibson  v.  Florida  Legislative
Comm.,  372  U.S.  539.  We  deal  with  the  claims  in  that
order.

1.  The  right  to  speak  or  write  anonymously
a.  It  is far  from  clear  how  the  right  to  speak  freely
came  to  include  a  right  to  speak  anonymously.  As  Mr.
Justice  Clark  has  noted,  “[t]he  Constitution  says
nothing  about  freedom  of  anonymous  speech.”’  Talley
v.  California,  supra,  362  U.S.  at  70  (dissenting  opin-
ion).  Presumably,  the  only  rationale  for  recognizing
such  a  right  is  a  concern  that  requiring  disclosure  of
the  true  identity  of  the  speaker  or  writer  may  expose
him  to reprisal  and  thereby  deter  him  from  expressing

39

his  views.  But  one  may  well  question  whether  that
consideration  justifies  First  Amendment  insulation  for
all  speech  which  is  unlabelled  or  mislabelled  as  to
source,  regardless  of  any  showing  that  the  author  is
truly  in  danger  of  retaliation.  As  Mr.  Justice  Black
himself  wrote  in  support  of  a  requirement  that  for-
eign  agents  disclose  their  political  propaganda  activi-
ties  (Viereck  v.  United  States,  318  U.S.  236,  251  (dis-
senting  opinion) ):

Resting  on
the  fundamental  constitutional
principle  that  our  people,  adequately  informed,
may  be  trusted  to  distinguish  between  the  true
and  the  false,  the  bill  is intended  to  label  infor-
mation  of  foreign  origin  so  that  hearers  and
readers  may  not  be  deceived  by the  belief  that
the  information  comes  from  a  disinterested
source.  Such  legislation  implements  rather  than
detracts  from  the  prized  freedoms  guaranteed
by the  First  Amendment.

That  has  been  the  predominant  view:  absent  very
unusual  circumstances,  a  mere  requirement  that  the
speaker  or  sponsor  be  identified  has  been  sustained,
whether  its  purpose  was  to  inform  the  readers  of  a
newspaper  (Lewis  Publishing  Co.  v.  Morgan,  229  U.S.
288),  local  governmental  authorities  (New  York  ex
rel.  Bryant  v.  Zimmerman,  278  U.S.  63),  the  voters
(Buroughs  and  Cannon  v.  United  States,  supra),  the
Members  of  Congress  (United  States  v.  Harriss,
supra),  or  the  public  at  large  (Communist  Party  v.
Control  Board,  367  U.S.  1).  See,  also,  Rabinowitz  y.
Kennedy,  376  U.S.  605.  That  principle  is the  predicate
of  many  State  and  federal  statutes  (see  Communist

595—-040—75——-4

40

41

Party  v.  Control  Board,  supra,  367  U.S.  at  97-101),
including  provisions  in  the  United  States  Code  (18
U.S.C,  612  and  47  U.S.C.  317)  and  in  the  laws  of  43
(see  Developments  in  the  Law—Electtons,
States
supra,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.  at  1286-1287,  and  Talley  v.
California,  supra,  362  U.S.  at  70,  n.  2  (Clark,  J., dis-
senting))  requiring  disclosure  of  the  sponsors  of
political  campaign  literature.

b. Against  all  this,  appellants  invoke  (Br.  190-191)
this  Court’s  decision  in  Talley  v.  California,  supra,
striking  down  a  city  ordinance  that  banned  the  dis-
tribution  of  anonymous  handbills  as  violating  the
First  Amendment.  Because  that  ruling,  at  first  blush,
seems  at  odds  with  all  that  went  before,  we  must  con-
sider  it with  special  care.

Some  passages  of  the  Talley  opinion  do,  indeed,
suggest  an  absolute  and  wholly  individual  right  of
anonymous  speech.  But  that  is  not  the  actual  holding
of  the  case,  which  involved  an  ordinance  prohibiting
“all  handbills  under  all  circumstances  anywhere  that
do not  have  the  names  and  addresses  printed  on  them.”
362  U.S.  at  64.  Indeed,  the  Court’s  opinion  expressly
reserved  the  question  of  “the  validity  of an  ordinance
jumited  to  prevent  [fraud,  false  advertising  and  libel]
or  any  other  supposed  evils.”  Ibid.  See,  also,  362  U.S.
at  66-67  (Harlan,  J.,  concurring).  And,  in  citing
Talley  in  later  cases,  the  Court  has  characterized  it
as  condemning  a  regulation  of  speech  that  was  “over-
broad”  in  that  it  swept  unnecessarily  widely  to  deal
with  the  particular  problem  addressed.  See,  e.g.,  Shel-
ton  v.  Tucker,  supra,  364  U.S.  at  489.

In sum,  what  Talley  establishes  is this,  and  nothing
more:  that  the  First  Amendment  protects  anonymous
writings  against  governmental  insistence  on  disclosure
when  the  purpose  is  insufficiently  important  or  the
means  are  not  carefully  tailored  to  achieve  only legiti-
mate  purposes.

At  all  events,  the  proposition  that  anonymity  is
an  absolute  right  is  foreclosed  by  later  decisions  of
the  Court.  We  need  only  cite  Communist  Party  v.
Control  Board,  supra,  and  Branzburg  v.  Hayes,  408
U.S.  665.  See,  also,  United  States  v.  Nixon,  418  U.S.
683;  Procunier  v.  Martinez,  416  U.S.  396,  413-414.
Nor  is there  any  basis  in the  Court’s  cases  for  a  claim
that  anonymous  political  speech  in  particular  enjoys
First  Amendment  immunity.  On  the  contrary,  the
Court  has  been  reluctant  to  apply  Talley  so  as  to  con-
demn  laws  which  require  the  sponsors  of  campaign
literature  to  reveal  themselves.  See  Golden  v.  Zwick-
ler, 394  U.S.  103;  cf.  Hal  v.  Printing Industries  of the
Gulf  Coast,  No.  74-456,  decided  June  30,  1975.  That
hesitation  presumably  reflects  recognition  of  the  un-
usually  compelling  interest  in  assuring  that  voters
know  who  is  backing  which  candidate—so  that  they
may  better  appraise  the  candidate  and  not  be  misled
by a  false  assumption  as  to the  interests  he represents.
c.  But  the  present  case  does  not  even  involve  a  re-
quirement  that  all  who  speak  or  write  in  support  of
a  particular  candidate  identify  themselves.  Because
the  focus  of  the  challenged  legislation  is  the  undue
influence  of money,  disciosure  is confined  to  paid  com-

| ES one ee

ee EE ES.
