43

mercials.  What  is  more,  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  imposes  no  immediate  identification  re-
quirement  with  respect  to  spoken  or  written  adver-
tisements.  No  doubt,  in  some  instances  it will  be  pos-
sible  to  determine  from  the  reports  on  expenditures
filed  after  the  fact  what  group  or  individual  sponsored
a  particular  commercial.  But,  even  then,  all  that  can
be  discovered  is  the  identity  of  the  payor,  normally
the  candidate  himself  or  his  campaign  committee.
This  is  a  much  lesser  interference  with  anonymous
speech  than  a  contemporaneous  labelling  requirement.
To  be  sure,  disclosure  of the  identity  of contributors
to  a  particular  candidate  connects  them  indirectly
with  his  campaign  speeches  and  commercials.  But
the  link  is  far  too  tenuous  to  justify  any  claim  that
the  contributor’s  speech  is  involved.

affects

speech.

anonymous

incidentally

The  upshot  is that  the  challenged  jaw  only  remotely
and
Its
objective  is  not  to  prevent  pseudenymous  political
writing.  Indeed,  nothing  in  the  new  legislation  would
require  the  authors  of  a  modern  series  of  Federalist
Papers  to  reveal  their  identities.  What  is  aimed  at  is
secret  money,  not  anonymous  writing.  The  law  is
narrowly  drawn  to  accomplish  that  objective.  In  those
circumstances,  it  is  not  a  sound  objection  that,  una-
voidably,  there  is  an  incidental,  albeit  very  limited,
effect  on  anonymity.

2.  The  right  of associational  privacy

Appellants  also  charge  (Br.  171-184)  that  the
challenged  legislation,  by requiring  disclosure  of polit-
ical  contributions  and  expenditures,  impinges  on  the

SONS ee
A NED

- EO ee TE

right  of  free  association.  The  First  Amendment  pro-
tects  political  association  as  an  aspect  of  freedom  of
assembly—whether  or  not  speech,  spoken  or  written,
is  directly  involved.  See  Cousins  v.  Wigoda,  419  U.S.
477,  487-488,  and  cases  there  cited.  Not  only  flat  pro-
hibitions  on  such  association,  but  also  ‘“‘significant
encroachment[s],”  implicate  the First  Amendment  and
will  not  be  sustained  if  there  is  “a  less  drastic  way
of  satisfying  *  *  *  legitimate  [governmental]  inter-
ests.”’  Kusper  vy.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51,  58-59.  Our
submission  is that  the  present  law  has  no  such  “‘signifi-
cant”’  effect  and that, if it does, compelling  needs justify
that  incidental  consequence.

a.  The  Court  has  never  suggested  that  secret  mem-
bership  in  any  organization  is always  an  absolute  con-
stitutional  right.  On  the  contrary,  in  Communist
Party  vy.  Control  Board,  supra,  compelled  disclosure
of  membership  was  upheld,  and  no  Member  of  the
Court  in  dissent  objected  to  the  requirement  that  the
Party  register  and  list  its  members  if  membership
were  neither  stigmatized  nor  made  potentially  crim-
inal.  See  367:  U.S.  at  136-137  (Warren,  C.J.,  dis-
senting),  138  (Black,  J.,  dissenting),  190  (Douglas,
J.,  dissenting),  191  (Brennan,  J.,  joined  by Warren,
C.J.,  dissenting).  Nor  has  the  Court  ever  held  that
compelled  disclosure  of membership  is always,  or  usu-
ally,  an  “encroachment’’  on  freedom  of  association
which  calls  for  special  justification.  Indeed,  the  deci-
sions  suggest  the  opposite:  that  the  First  Amendment
shields  secret  membership  only  when  there  is  a  real
danger  of exposure  to reprisal.  See  N.A.A.C.P.  y.  Ala-

44

45

bama,  supra,  357  U.S.  at  462-463;  Bates  vy.  Little
Rock,  supra,  361  U.S.  at  523-524;  N.A.A.C.P.  v.
Button,  supra,  371  U.S.  at 485-436;  Gibson  vy.  Florida
Legislative  Comm.,  supra,  372  U.S.  at  556-557.

b.  But,  even  assuming  that  all  restrictions  on  pri-
vacy  of  association  implicate  the  First  Amendment,
sufficiently  compelling  governmental  interests  will
vindicate  some  intrusions.  The  test  of constitutionality
must  take  into  account  both  the  importance  of  the
legislative  objective  and  the  degree  of  the  invasion
of  privacy,  bearing  in  mind  that  only  the  least  in-
trusion  necessary  to  accomplish  a  legitimate  end  is
permissible.  We  consider  the  challenged  legislation  in
that  light.

We  have  already  noted  the  important  congressional
concerns  Which  underlie  the  disclosure  requirements
of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments
and  the  care  taken  to  select  mechanisms  that  would
be  effective  without  sweeping  too  broadly.  See  supra,
pp.  29-31,  41-42.  It  remains  only  to  emphasize  how
little  these  provisions  trench  on  associational  rights.
First,  anonymous  membership  in groups,  even  pure-
ly  political  associations,  is  not  prevented.  The  chial-
lenged  law  requires  no  one  to  say  (or  have  said  about
him)  whether  he  belongs  to  the  Republican  Party,  the
Democratic  Party,  the  Conservative  Party,  the  Pro-
gressive  Party,  or  indeed,  the  Communist  Party.  Com-
pare  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354  U.S.  234.  Indeed,
so  long  as  membership  involves  no  greater  annual
contribution  than  $100,  it  may  remain  secret,  what-

ever  the  organization.  That  is  because  the  Campaign
Act  is  concerned  with  money,  not  membership.

But  it  does  not  follow  that  by  contributing  more
than  $100  to  an  organization,  the  donor  exposes  his
membership.  The  law  reaches  only  contributions
‘‘made  for  the  purpose  of  influencing  the  nomina-
tion  for  election,  or  election,  of any  person  to  Federal
office  *  *  *.”?  2 U.S.C.  431(e)(1)(A).  Obviously  ex-
cluded  are  gifts,  dues  or  subscriptions  paid  to  non-
political  associations,  as  well  as  to  non-partisan  polit-
ical  organizations.  What  is more,  contributions  to  less
neutral  political  groups  need  be  disclosed  only  if they
are  earmarked  (expressly  or  impliedly)  for  one  or
more  specific  candidates.  The  challenged  statute  ¢x-
poses  only  contributions  which  the  giver  intends  shall
be  used  for  a  particular  candidate  or  slate  of  candi-
dates.’

A  disclosure  requirement  so  confined  has  only  a
marginal  effect  on  membership  in  existing  organiza-
tions  and  associations.  While  it is arguable  that  those
who  support  a  particular  candidate  are  thereby  jour
ing  in  a  new  “association,”  that  is a  strained  concept.
to
Especially  when  contributions  are  limited  as
amount  and  the  number  of  givers  is  increased,  it  is
almost  as  unreal  to  characterize  all  contributors,  for
the  most  part  unknown  to  each  other  and  in  no  way
organized  together,  as  an  “asociation’’  as  it  would

®The  Commission  has  rendered  an  interim  ruling  that  con-
tributions  to  an  established  political  party  which  intends  to
field  candidates  in  the  upcoming  election  are  subject  to  the
limitations,  even  though  the  identity  of  those  candidates  is
FEC  AO  1975-4,  in 40 Fed.  Reg. 29793-29794.
not  yet determined.

46

47

so  view  those  who  eventually  vote  for  the  candidate.
Nor  is  the  relationship  between  most  contributors
and  the  candidate,  although  more  meaningful  than  the
abstract  link  among  contributors  themselves,  the  kind
of  association  traditionally  embraced  by the  constitu-
tional  concept  of ‘‘free  association.’’  Although  the  can-
didate  will  have  his  close  campaign  associates  who
may  also  be  contributors,  their  identity  is  no  secret
and  their  direct  association  with  the  candidate  will
not  be  affected  by  a  disclosure  requirement.  So,  also,
large  contributions  may  create  a  close  tie,  corrupt  or
otherwise,  between  giver  and  receiver;  but  that  special
relationship,  based  on  financial  dependence,  is  now
eliminated.  The  bulk  of  supporters,  on  the  other  hand,
will  have  no  real  contact  with  their  candidate,  and  he
himself  will  not  even  know  their  names.  It  does  not
deprecate  the  practical  importance  of  mass  support,  or
the  national  interest  in more  democratic  electoral  c¢am-
paigns,  to  recognize  that  the  “association’’  between  the
individual  supporter  and  the  candidate  is very  distant
kin  to  the  right  of  assembly  which  the  First  Amend-
ment  protects.

ce.  But,  even  viewing  the  supporter’s  choice  of  a
particular  candidate  as  in  some  sense  an  exercise  of
the  right  of  free  association,  the  challenged  law  cre-
ates  only  the  mildest  deterrent,  if  any,  to  that  exer-
cise.  In  the  climate  of  today,  in  which  extreme  views
are  regularly  uttered,  without  restraint,  it  is  doubt-
ful  that  many  are  significantly  discouraged  from  con-
tributing  because  the  gift  may  become  known.  What

is  more,  disclosure  is  not  contemporaneous  with  the
gift,  and  it  will  be  a  rare  case  where  an  individual
contribution  of  $1,000  or  less  will  be  deemed  worth  re-
porting  by the  press.

There  is  no  substantial  showing  that  the  limited
disclosure  requirements  of  the  challenged  statute  will
deter  any  speech  or  any  association.  The  court  of  ap-
peals  correctly  characterized  the  evidence  on  this  score
as  “scant,”  ‘“‘skimpy”  and  “thin.’’  J.S.  App.  1546  and
n.  115.  The  truth  of  the  matter  is  that  the  disclosure
provisions  of  the  new  law  touch  only  tangentially
upon  contributors’  rights  of  free  speech  or  free  asso-
ciation.  At  all  events,  the  intrusion  is so  slight  that  it
provides  no  basis  for  invalidating  an  important  part
of the  regulatory  scheme.

B.  CEILINGS  ON  EXPENDITURES

Appellants  contend  (Br.  79,  107)  that  the  expendi-
ture  ceilings  are  particularly  offensive  to  the  First
Amendment  because  they  directly  and  intentionally
“eut  off’?  speech,  ‘‘flatly  prohibiting’  further  speech
after  the  allowance  is spent.  We  examine  that  charge,
first  in  its  most  extreme  form:  that  any  spending
limit  is  unconstitutional.  We  then  consider  the  par-
ticular  expenditure  ceilings  the  Act  imposes.

1.  The  principle  of spending  ceilings

a.  We  cannot  too  often  stress  that  the  legislation
in  suit  is  concerned  with  money,  not  speech.  That  is
not  the  same  thing,  even  today.  Many  campaign  ex-
penditures  are  unrelated  to  speech  and  there  are  still
ways  of  communicating  that  do  not  involve  spending.

— ee ee ee

48

49

os

But,  more  important,  what  is  regulated  is  one  of  the
means  for  communication,  not  speech  itself.  There  is
no  censorship  whatever:  the  candidate  and  his  sup-
porters  may  say  what  they  like.  What  is  more,  they
remain  entirely  free  to  choose  their  medium  of  com-
munication,  The  law  does  not  limit  the  total  number
of  hours  a  candidate  can  speak  or  the  number  of
words  that  can  be  written  on  his  behalf.  The  evil
aimed  at  is  not  prolixity,  but  the  undue  influence  and
the  gross  disparities  resulting  from  unlimited  spend-
ing in electoral  campaigns.

b.  So  saying,  we  do  not  blink  the  fact  that  the  effect
of  spending  limits—at  least  limits  low  enough  to  have
a  bite—must  be  to  restrict  some  political  speech.  But
is  that  enough  to  invalidate  the  law?  Let  us  examine
a  few  hypothetical  examples.

Consider  a  regulation  that  restricts  speakers  in  a
public  park  to  a  single  “Speaker’s  Corner’’  and  then,
in  the  interest  of  fair  access,  limits  any  single  speaker
to  one  two-hour  speech  a  week.  The  soap-box  orator
who  has  more  to  say  is  literally  ‘‘cut-off’’?  and,  we
may  assume,  has  no  practical  alternative  forum.  Is
such  a  reasonable  rule  good  only  for  Hyde  Park  in
London  but  not  for  Lafayette  Park  in  Washington?
And  what  of  a  regulation  that  bans  door-to-door  can-
Vassing  after  midnight  or  electioneering  at  the  polls?
Is  it  permissible  to  close  courthouses  and  government
offices  to  political  campaigning ?

The  answer  has  long  since  been  given.  As  the  Court
recently  summarized  its  prior  holdings,  ‘‘reasonable
‘time,  place  and  manner’  regulations  may  be  neces-
sary  to  further  significant  governmental  interests,
and  are  permitted.”  Grayned  vy.  City  of  Rockford,

408  U.S.  104,  115  and  eases  cited  at  n.  30.  Of  course,
speech  cannot  be  wholly  suppressed  in  the  guise  of
“regulation.”  But,  almost  necessarily,  every  restric-
tion  of  time  and  place  in  a  finite  world  imposes  a
quantitative  limitation,  it  “cuts  off’?  some  speech.
Given  a  sufficient  governmental  interest,  such  a  neu-
tral  (as  to  the  content  of  speech)  restriction  does  not
violate  the  First  Amendment.

C,  Appellants  repeatedly  cite  (i.e.,  Br.  47, 53, 81, 83,
199,  119,  141)  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S.  214,  which
invalidated  a  state  law  as  applied  to  a  newspaper
editor  who  was  prevented  from  publishing  on  election
day  an  editorial  urging  voters  to  cast  their  ballots  in
a  particular  way.  As  with  Talley  v.  California,  supra,
the  opinion  perhaps  sweeps  more  broadly  than  the
holding  and  we  must  examine  the  case  with  some  care,
Mills  can  be  read  as  saying  that  ‘‘no  test  of  reason-
ableness’’  can  justify  a  curb  on  press  freedom,  384
U.S.  at 220.  No  such  ruling  was  necessary  to the  result,
since  the  Court  also  found  a  “fatal  flaw”’  in the  justi-
fication  tendered  by  the  State  (cbid.):  a  sufficient
ground  for  reversal  was  that  the  statute  was  “wholly
ineffective’?  in  accomplishing  its  alleged  objective.

In  any  event,  the  statement  in  Mills  does  not  con-
trol  here.  The  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act—unlike
the  Alabama  statute—in  no  way  restricts  the  freedom
of  “a  newspaper  editor  to  *  *  *  urge  people  to  vote
one  way  or  another  in  a  publicly  held  election.”  Ibid.
As  we  have  already  noted  (supra,  pp.  47-48),  our  case
has  nothing  to  do  with  freedom  of  the  press,  which  is
left  wholly  unfettered.

Under  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.

50

51

254,  266,  and  Bigelow  v.  Virginia,  No.  73-1309,  de-
cided  June  16,  1975,  slip  op.,  pp.  8-16,  a  paid  edi-
torial  or,  indeed,  any  advertisement  containing  in-
formation  of  “public  interest’?  published  in  a  news-
paper  enjoys  the  same  protection  as  the  editor’s  own
views  printed  on  the  editorial  page.  But  nothing  in
Sullivan  or  Bigelow,  or  in  any  other  case,  suggests.
that  a  restriction  imposed  on  the  sponsor  of  an  ad-
vertisement,  before  publication,  must  be judged  by the
same  standard  that  governs  attempted  regulation  of
the  editor’s  freedom  to  print  what  he  chooses.  On  the
contrary,  the  Court  has  consistently  viewed  the  press
as  occupying  a  particularly  protected  position  under
the  First  Amendment.  See,  e.g.,  Miami  Herald  Pub-
lishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241;  New  York  Times
Co.  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713;  Pittsburgh  Press
Human  Rel.  Comm’n,  413  U.S.  376,  381-382,
Co.  v.
391.  Indeed,  Madison’s  original  draft  of  the  First
Amendment  singled  out  freedom  of  the  press  as  alone
“inviolable.”  See  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United
States,  supra,  403  U.S.  at 716  (Black,  J., concurring).
The  sponsor  of  a  paid  political  message  (whether
candidate  or  independent  supporter)  can  be  denied
all  access  to  the  medium  of  his  choice,  not  only  by
private  editors,  Columbia  Broadcasting  v.  Democratic
Comm.,  412  U.S.  94;  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.
Tornillo,  supra,  but,  at  least  in  some  circumstances,  by
government  itself,  Lehman  v.  City  of Shaker  Heights,
418  U.S.  298;  47  U.S.C.  (Supp.  IIT)  399(a).  Cf.  Nat.
Broadcasting  Co.  v.  United  States,  319  U.S.  190,  226-
227.  Other  would-be  sponsors  of  a  partisan  political
message  are  wholly  excluded  by law  from  every  public
— Civil  Service  Commission  vy.  Letter  Carriers,

supra;  Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601.  Simi-
larly,  although  the  press  ordinarily  cannot  be  stopped
from  publishing  information  which  prejudices  a  crimi-
nal  trial,  the  “sources”  of  that  information  can  be
enjoined  from  supplying  it.  Sheppard  v.  Maxwell,  384
U.S.  333,  359,  361-363.  And  it  would  seem  that  in
some  circumstances  even  a  United  States  Senator  can
be enjoined  from  delivering  material  to  a  private  pub-
lisher  that  the  latter  probably  could  not  be  prevented
from  printing.  Compare  Gravel  vy.  United  States,  408
U.S.  606,  with  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States,
supra.  See,  also,  Klcindienst  v.  Mandel,  408  U.S.  753.
The  most  recent,  and  the  clearest,  statement  of  the
principle  appears  in  Bigelow  v.  Virginia,  supra.  In
condemning  the  application  to  a  newspaper  editor  of  a
State  statute  prohibiting  the  publication  of  an  adver-
tisement  encouraging  abortion,  the  Court  expressly
noted  (slip  op.  at  18):

The  strength  of  appellant’s  interest  was  aug-
mented  by the  fact  that  the  statute  was  applied
against  him  as  publisher  and  editor  of a  news-
paper,  not  against  the  advertiser  or  a  referral
agency  or  a  practitioner.  The  prosecution  thus
incurred  more  serious  First  Amendment  over-
tones.

d.  We  have  already  discussed  the  important  con-
cerns  that  underlie  both  the  overall  limitation  on  cam-
paign  spending  and  the  ceiling  on  “unauthorized”  or
‘‘independent”’  expenditures  in  support  of  a  particu-
lar  candidate  (supra,  pp.  35-36).  Those  reasons,  we
submit,  are  sufficient  to  support  the  indirect  restraint
on  speech  which  the  spending  limitations  impose.  Un-
like  the  situation  in  Sullivan  and  Bigelow,  where  the
applicability  of  the  restriction  on  speech  depended  on

52

53

the  content  of  the  message  being  delivered,  the  Act’s
restrictions  apply  neutrally  to  all  excessive  campaign
expenditures  (regardless  of  the  particular  message
they  seek  to  support  and  whether  that  message  is
critical  or  laudatory  of  any  individual).  No  message
is  prohibited  beeause  of  its  content;  all  persons  are
free  under  the  Act  to  say  what  they  wish,  within  the
limits  of  the  Act’s  monetary  ceilings.  The  Act’s  re-
strictions  thus  are  more  akin  to  limitations  on  the
time,  place,  and  manner  of  speech  (already  discussed  )
or  to  the  similarly  neutral  regulation  applied  under
the  “fairness  doctrine”  to  the  broadcast  media.  See
Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.  Federal  Communica-
lions  Commission,  395  U.S.  367.  With  these  general
considerations  in  mind,  we  now  turn  to  examine  the
particular  limitations  in  the  light  of the  First  Aimend-
ment.

2.  Overall  limitations  on  candidate  cx pendilures

Having  shown  that  the  First  Amendment  does  not
bar  all  limitations  on  campaign  spending,  we  now  coti-
sider  the  overall  limitations  imposed  on  a  candidate's
campaign  expenditures  (15  U.S.C.  608(¢)),  the  hmita-
tion  on  his  personal  contribution  to  that  total  (18
U.S.C.  608(a)),  and  the  limitation  on  additional  ex-
penditures  on  behalf  of  a  candidate  by official  political
party  committees  (18  U.S.C.  608(f)).

|

Each  of  these  provisions  represents  an  accommoda-
tion  between  two  competing  sets  of  considerations.  On
the  one  hand,  the  ceilings  serve  the  objective  of  reduc-
ing  the  undue  influence  of  money  in  elections  and  pre-
venting  the  exaggerated  advantage  that  great  wealth—
whether  of the  candidate,  his  party  or  his  supporters—

presently  enjoys.  On  the  other  hand,  they  recognize
the  importance  of  allowing  as  much  freedom  as  pos-
sible  within  the  boundaries  of  effective  regulation  and
respect  the  reality  that  political  campaigns  cost  money
and  that  the  ability  to  raise  funds  is, in  an  important
degree,  a  reflection  of  the  candidate’s  public  appea!
(when  individual  and  group  contributions  are  severely
limited).  We  submit  that  in  these  provisions  Congress
struck  a  reasonable  and  justifiable  balance  which  tlic
courts  should  accept.

We  do  not  claim  that  each  of  the  ceilings  is fixed  at
the  only  defensible  level.  Indeed,  experience  may  sue-
gest  alteration  of one  limitation  or  another,  or  even  an
entirely  new  formula.  But  the  Constitution  does  not
require  perfection  at  the  very  inception  of  an  experi-
ment.  It  is enough,  at  this  stage,  to  know  that  the  law
does  not  intentionally  discriminate  (see  Point  III,
infra)  and  does  not  shackle  the  existing  electoral
system.

We  stress  two  aspects.  The  first  is that  the  expendi-
ture  limitations  are  deliberately  devised  to  avoid  de-
stroying  the  party  system.  That  is  accomplished  by
allowing  national  and  state  committees  to  make  ex-
penditures  which  are  not  counted  against  the  candi-
date’s  allowance.  Of  course,  it  was  necessary  to  im-
pose  a  limit  on  those  party  expenditures  in  support  of
a  particular  candidate,  but  the  ceilings  are
not
niggardly.

The  other  significant  feature  of  the  system  is  the
ample  allowance  fixed  for  spending  by  the  candi-
date  (or  his  campaign  committee).  An  important  ain
of  the  ceilings  is  to  curb  the  continuously  upward
experience
spiralling

of  ecampaigns—which,

cost

54

55

teaches,  is  inevitable  when  the  sky  is  the  limit—and
the  candidates’  consequent  dependence  on  wealthy
interests  and  practical  elimination  of  the  financially-
disadvantaged  candidate.  But  the  fact  is  that,  in  the
most  recent  elections,  the  overwhelming  majority  of
candidates  for  the  House  of  Representatives  and
the  Senate  did  not  spend  more  than  the  amounts  now
allowed.  See  Findings  ITA,  1118-19,  28-29,  40-41,
47-48;  J.  App.  442-445,  445,  448-449,  450.  Every  in-
dication  thus  is that  the  present  limits  will  cut  off  no

dehate.

3.  The  ceiling  on  independent  expenditures

Given  the  twin  premises  that  there  must  he  an
overall  ceiling  on  what  a  candidate  may  spend  to  win
election  and  that  there  must  be  a  limit  on  what  any
individual  or  group  may  contribute  to  that  end,  the
question  Congress  faced  was  how  to  avoid  cireumven-
tion  of  these  ccilings  by those  who  decide  to  help  the
candidate  by directly  spending  on  his  behalf—whether
by  sponsoring  printed  or  broadeasted  commercials  or
otherwise.

The  problem  is  a  difficult  one.  There  is  a  triple
dilemma:  if  the  candidate  is  allowed  to  screen  the
inaterial  he  deems  worthwhile  because  the  expenditure
consumes  a  portion  of  his  allowance,  independent
supporters  are  subjected  to censorship;  if, on  the  other
hand,  the  candidate  has  no  veto  and  unauthorized
commercials  are  counted  against  his  expenditure  ceil-
ing,  he  may  lose  control  of his  own  election  campaign ;
and,  finally,  if independent  expenditures  are  unlimited
and  unaccounted,  the  spending  and  giving  limitations
are  seriously  compromised,

There  is  no  perfect  solution.  But,  on  balance,  Con-
wress  has  chosen  a  realistic  compromise  which,  while
effective,  trenches  least  on  First  Amendment  values.
The  scheme  embodied  in  18  U.S.C.  608(e)  allows,  but
limits,  “independent”  or  “unauthorized’’  expendi-
tures  in  support  of  a  particular  candidate,  exempting
them  from  the  candidate’s  spending  ceiling  and  from
his  control.  It  thus  avoids  any  issues  of  censorship
or  of  the  candidate’s  freedom  to  spend  his  allowance
as  he  chooses.  The  only  question  remaining  is whether
the  limitation  on  individual  and  group  expenditures
itself  involves  an  impermissible  curtailment  of speech.
The  answer  is negative.  As  we  have  seen  (supra,  p.
22),  the  problem  of  the  “indirect”  contribution  has
plagued  all  prior  legislation  and  rendered  it  largely
ineffective.  Unless  no  effort  is  to  be  made  to  reduce
the  undue  influence  of  big  money  in  federal  elections,
there  must  be  some  limit  on  independent  expenditures.
And,  because  those  expenditures  are  not  counted
against  the  candidate’s  spending  ceiling,  that  limit
must  be  low.

Of  course,  an  allegation  of  necessity  does  not  auto-
matically  establish  constitutionality.  Such  an  argu-
ment,  for  instance,  would  probably  not  sustain  a  re-
striction  on  the  news  media’s  own  editorializing  in
favor  of  a  candidate  and  the  present  law  accordingly
exempts  that  kind  of  support.  But,  as  we  have  just
shown,  the  immunity  of  sponsors  of  paid  commercials
is  not  the  same.  Here  we  deal  with  a  very  limited  re-
triction  that  is  covered  by  the  principle,  articulated
above  (supra,  p.  44),  that  a  sufficiently  compelling
need  will  vindicate  a  partial  restraint  on  a  would-be
sponsor’s  desire  to  promote  his  candidate.

595-040-755

56

If  the  risk  of  improper  pressures  on  governmental
employees  and  the  “unseemliness”  of their  electioneer-
ing  to  maintain  their  employers  in  power  is  enough
wholly  to  disable  several  million  citizens  from  active
participation  in  electoral  campaigns—as  does  the
Hatch  Act  which  this  Court  upheld  in the  Civil  Serv-
ice  Commission  Vv.  Letter  Carriers,  supra—a  concern
for  preserving  the  integrity  of  the  political  process
and  opening  it  up  meaningfully  to  more  people  is  a
sufficient  predicate  for  merely  limiting  the  money  any
citizen  can  spend  on  behalf  of  a  particular  candidate.

C.  APPELLANTS’  OTHER  FIRST  AMENDMENT  CHALLENGES

1.  Preliminary  comments

Two  preliminary  comments  are  in order.
a.  This  case  does  not  involve  the  doctrine  barring
prior  restraints  on  speech.  The  legislation  makes  no
attempt  to  censor;  it is entirely  neutral  about  the  con-
tent  of political  advocacy.  The same  rules  apply whether
the  candidate  or  the  supporter  is  a  Republican
or  Democrat  or  Liberal  or  Conservative  or  Inde-
pendent  or  Communist;  whether  what  is said  or  writ-
ten  is  polite  or  rude,  patriotic  or  seditious,  true  or
false;  whether  the  cause  espoused  is  deemed  healthy
or  subversive.  That  the  law  authorizes  injunctive  re-
lief  (2  U.S.C.  487g(a)(5)  and  (7)  is  therefore
irrelevant.

Literally,  of  course,  any  bar  to  speech  enforceable
by  injunction  is  a  “prior  restraint.’’  But  that  is  not
the  point  of  the  ‘‘prior  restraint”  doctrine.  It  reflects
an  abhorrence  of censorship,  an  attempt  to control  con-

57

tent.  Near  v.  Minnesota,  283  U.S.  713-719;  Pitts-
burgh  Press  Co.  v.  Human  Rel.  Comm’n.,  supra,
413  U.S.  at  389-390.  The  doctrine  has  no  appli-
cation  to  a  clear-cut  prohibition  or  limitation  un-
related  to  content.  The  challenged  statute  no  more  im-
poses  a  forbidden  “prior  restraint”  than  would  a  law
authorizing  injunctions  to  enforce  a  ban  on  the  sale
and  distribution  of  newspapers  on  throughways  or,
indeed,  a  rule  fixing  the  total  time  for  television  com-
mercials  on  any  given  channel  at two  hours  per  day—
whatever  other  First  Amendment  objections  might
prevail  against  such  restrictions.

b.  There  is likewise  no  issue  of  press  freedom  here.
The  legislation  is  at  pains  to  avoid  any  restrictton
whatever,  qualitative  or  quantitative,  or  temporal  (see
Mills  v.  Alabama,  supra),  on  what  publishers  and
broadcasters  may  say  about  election  issues  or  candi-
dates.  Expressly  excluded  from  the  definition  of  “ex-
penditure”  is  “any  news  story,  commentary,  or  edi-
torial  distributed  through  the  facilities  of  any  broad-
casting  station,  newspaper,  magazine,  or  other  period-
ical  publication.”’  2  U.S.C.  431(£)(4)(A);  18  U.S.C.
591(£) (4) (A).  Nor  is what  such  a  publisher  or  broad-
caster  writes  or  says  treated  as  a  ‘‘contribution,”  how-
ever  openly  he  supports  a  particular  candidate.  See
120  Cong.  Rec.  H10330  (daily  ed., Oct.  10, 1974)  ; H.R.
Rep.  No.  93-1239,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  4  (1974) ; opin-
ion  below,  J.S.  App.  1536-1537.  No  obligation  of  re-
porting  or  disclosure  is  imposed  on  the  press  or  the
electronic  media.

58

It  may  be  that  publishers  and  broadcasters  are  in-
directly  affected  by the  ceilings  on  political  giving  and
spending  in that the total  volume  of paid political  com-
mercials  will  decrease.  But  any  such  injury  is too  in-
substantial  and  remote  to  have  constitutional  signifi-
cance.  In  its  impact  on  the  press,  this  statute  bears  no
resemblance  to the  direct  tax  struck  down  in  Grosjean
v.  American  Press  Co.,  297  U.S.  233,  on  the  ground
that  it had  “the  plain  purpose  of  penalizing  the  pub-
lishers  and  curtailing  the  circulation  of  a  selected
group  of  newspapers.”  Id.  at  251.

The  constitutional  guarantee  of  freedom  of  the
press  does  not  merely  condemn  governmental  action
that  “takes  money  from  the  pockets’’  of  publishers
and  broadcasters,  but  exists  for  the  benefit  of  the  citi-
zenry.  Id.  at  250;  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,
supra,  376  U.S.  at  266;  Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.
vy.  Federal  Communications  Commission,  395  U.S,  367,
390.  But  the  interest  of  viewers  and  listeners  and
readers,  “paramount”  as  it is, does  not  require  news-
papers  or  broadcasters  to  accept  any  paid  politica!
Democratic
message.
Comm.,  supra;  47  U.S.C.  315(a)  ; Miami  Herald  Pub-
lishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  supra.  Indeed,  so  long  as  they
do  not  discriminate,  even  governmentally-owned  or
assisted  channels  of  communication  are  constitution-
ally  free,  at  least  in  some  circumstances,  to  ban  all
political  advertisements.  47  U.S.C.  (Supp.  I11)  399
(a);  Lehman  v.  City  of Shaker  Heights,  supra;  and
see  Columbia  Broadcasting  vy.  Democratic  Comm.,

Broadcasting

Columbia

v.

59

supra,  412  U.S.  at  121-132  and  146-147  (White,  J.,
concurring).  Nor  does  the  First  Amendment’s  concern
for  the  widest  debate  prevent  a  flat  ban  on  active
electioneering—including  the  sponsoring  of  commer-
cials—by  several  millions  of  government  employees.
Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers,  supra;
Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601.  There  is,
therefore,  no  valid  basis  for  complaint  that  the  pres-
ent  legislation  “deprives”  the  public  of  some  political
speech.

Moreover,  no  real  risk  exists  that  any  speech  will
go  uncommunicated.  To  be  sure,  18  U.S.C.  608(e)
prevents  any  independent  individual  or  group  from
placing  a  full-page  advertisement  supporting  a  single
candidate  in  a  major  daily  newspaper  or  sponsoring
more  than  a  modest  television  commercial.  See  Find-
ings  I,  £165,  86;  J.  App.  32,  49.  But  the  provision
does  not  bar  the  expression  of  that  support  in  other
ways,  nor  does  it  affect  the  freedom  of  the  candidate
or  his  campaign  committee  from  using  contributions
to  this  end.

Whatever  the  impact  of Section  608(e)  on  the  spon-
sor’s  right  of  free  speech  (see  supra,  pp.  47-52),  it
does  not  work  any  measurable  abridgement  of the  pub-
lice’s  interest  in  a  free  press.  Given  the  “equal  time”’
provision  of  the  Communications  Act,  47  U.S.C.  315,
the  Fairness  Doctrine  (including  the “personal  attack”
and  “editorial-reply’’  rules),  and  the  complementary
Cullman  doctrine,  there  is no  reason  to anticipate  that
the  challenged  legislation  will  deprive  the  audience  of
any  point  of  view.  See  Columbia  Broadcasting  v.

60

Democratic  Comm.,  supra,  412  U.S.  at  122-125;  Red
Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.  Federal  Communications
Commission,  supra,  395  U.S.  at  386-395,  On  the  con-
trary,  the  object  is  to  encourage  more  voices  to  be
heard  by  subduing—but  not  suppressing—the  dis-
proportionate  influence  of  the  wealthy  few.  Cf.  Citi-
zen  Publishing  Co.  v.  United  States,  394  U.S.  131,
139-140;  Associated  Press  v.  United  States,  326  U.S.
1,  20.  That  result  serves,  rather  than  defeats,  the
premise  of press  freedom.

2.  Contribution  ceilings  and  freedom  of speech

Limitations  on  direct  political  contributions—as  op-
posed  to  expenditures—do  net  violate
the  First
Amendment.  For  this  proposition,  we  need  say  little
more  than  that  it  has  been  endorsed  by  Mr.  Chief
Justice  Warren,  Mr.  Justice  Black  and  Mr.  Justice
Douglas.  As  already  noted,  the  three  Justices,  dis-
senting  in  United  States  vy.  Auto  Workers,  supra,  352
U.S.  at  593-598,  joined  in  an  opinion  holding  the
total  ban  on  union  expenditures  for  a  political  broad-
cast  offensive  to  the  First  Amendment  but  expressly
stated  that  Congress  “can  prohibit  *  *  *  large  con-
tributions  by labor  unions  to  candidates  for  office  and
to  political  parties.’’  Jd.  at  598,  n.  2.

a.  To  conclude  otherwise  is  to  equate  money  and
speech  or  money  and  political  association.  This  would
turn  the  decision  in  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,
supra,  on  its  head.  That  case  held  that  paid  political
expression  remains  protected  speech  notwithstanding

SL — IS | A Cs te we

ee

61

that  it  is  paid  for:  it  did  not  remotely  suggest  that
the  giving—or  indeed  the  spending—of  money  for  po-
litical  purposes  is  itself  a  First  Amendment  activity.
Nor  does  any  decision  of  this  Court  hold  that  the
mere  pooling  of  financial  contributions  for  political
purposes  is  protected  “assembly”  or  “association”.
The  focus  of  the  First  Amendment  is  on  the  right  of
citizens  to  join  together  in  body  or  mind,  in  speech
or  action,  for  a  common  political  cause;  meeting  and
membership  are  protected,  not  the  mingling  of money.
See  N.A.A.C.P.  v.  Alabama,  supra,  357  U.S.  at  460;
Bates  v.  Little  Rock,  supra,  361  U.S.  at  522-523;
N.A.A.C.P.  vy.  Button,  supra,  371  U.S.  at  430-431;
Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  supra,  414  U.S.  at  56-57.

So  saying,  we  do  not  ignore  that  “[i]t  usually  costs
money  to  communicate  an  idea  to  a  large  audience,”’
United  States  vy.  Auto  Workers,  supra,  352  U.S.  at 594
(Douglas,  J.,  dissenting),  and  that  political  parties
and  groups  cannot  be  effective  without  substantial
funds.  But  we  must  be  wary  of equating  contemporary
Cf.  Felia
“reality’’  with  constitutional
Frankfurter  Reminisces
(Anchor  1962).
The  present  importance  of  money  in  federal  elections
is  not  a  constitutional  imperative:  there  are  ways  of
diminishing  that  dependence  and,  to  a  modest  extent,
the  new  law  works  in that  direction.

necessity.

339-340

Nevertheless,  it  would  presumably  offend  the  First
Amendment  rights  of  free  expression  and  free  asso-
ciation  to  prohibit  all  spending  by  individuals  and
groups  for  political  purposes.  But  expenditures  and

62

63

contributions  must  be  distinguished.  The  nexus  be-
tween  expression  and  expenditures  for  political  ‘‘com-
mercials”  is closer  than  it is with  respect  to  contribu-
tions,  which  may  or  may  not  be  used  for  written  or
spoken  statements  supporting  the  candidate  and,  at
best,  will  not  directly  finance  the  contributor’s  own
message.  If  adequate  public  funds  were  made  avail-
able  on  a  non-discriminatory  basis  to candidates,  polit-
ical  parties  and  other  political  organizations,  there
would  be  no  constitutional  inhibition  to  a  complete
ban  on  direct  political  contributions  from  private
sources,  The  fact  is  that  private  contributions  are
not  essential  to  freedom  of  expression  or  freedom  of
association.

b.  At  all  events,  merely  restricting  the  amount  of
contributions  to,  or  earmarked  for,  specified  political
candidates  does  not  violate  the  First  Amendment,  The
challenged  provisions  do  not  inhibit  the  freedom  of
citizens  to  form  a  political  group  or  to join  an  already
existing  party.  Indeed,  as  long  as  the  organization  is
not  the  express  agent  of  a  single  candidate,  any  in-
dividual  remains  free  to  contribute  up  to $25,000,  and,
if  the  organization  is  not  actively  committed  to  any’
particular  candidate,  no  limit  applies  to  contributions
made  by committees  that  do  not  go  to  individual  can-
didates.  There  can  be  no  claim  that  political  associa-
tions  are  starved  of  necessary  funds.  Nor  are  they
rendered  impotent.  While  qualifying  political  com-
mittees  are  limited  to  a  $5,000  direct  contribution  to
any  single  candidate  (18  U.S.C.  608(b)(2)),  their

total  contributions  are  unrestricted,  and  official  party
committees  may,  in  addition,  spend  substantial  sums
on  behalf  of any  candidate  (18 U.S.C.  608(f) ).

The  citizen  also  retains  his  individual  voice,  He  re-
mains  free  meaningfully  to  give  support,  in  his  own
name,  to  one  or  more  candidates.  A  contribution  of
$1,000—-especially  when  no  one  can  make  a  larger
one—is  not  derisory.  He  may,  moreover,  spend  an-
other  $1,000  per  candidate  (with  no  total  limit)  in
support  of  his  choices  (18  U.S.C.  608(e),  discussed
Demi pp.  47-52),  plus  $500  in incidental  expenses  (18
U.S.C,  591(e)  (5),  591(1)  (4) ). Only  relatively  few  will
he  affected  by these  limits  and  even  they  are  far  from
silenced.

——

~~

As  we  have  seen  (supra,  pp.  23-27),  one  objective  of
the  new  law  is  to  encourage  more  citizens  to  become
politically  active  by assuring  the  less  affluent  that  their
donations  will  not  be  superfluous  or  so  relatively  in-
significant  as  to  go  unnoticed.  The  legislation,  far  from
stifiing  the  expression  of  any  point  of  view,  or  dis-
couraging  political  association,  tends  to  open  political
campaigns  to  every  opinion  and  thereby  serves  the
central  goal  of  the  First  Amendment.

3,  Public  financing  and  the  First  Amendment

I:xcept  insofar  as  the  claim  of  invidious  discrimi-
nation  against  challengers  and  new  or  minor  parties
(with  which  we  deal  in  Point  III,  infra)  rests  on  the
First  Amendment  as  well  as  the  Fifth,  publie  finane-
ing  of  federal  election  campaigns  involves  no  First
Amendment  violation.  The  suggestion  that  the  scheme

64

impermissibly  “involves”  the  government  in  the  area
of  political  expression  is  without  substance.

a.  The  dispositive  fact  is  that  the  First  Amend-
ment  includes  no  “Establishment  Clause’’  with  re-
spect  to  speech  or  press  or  assembly.  The  “‘church-
and-state”  analogy  (Appellants’  Br.  146-150)  is there-
fore  inapt.  Government  may  not  censor  speech,  but
it  may  subsidize  or  build  or  operate  or  allocate  the
means  of  communication—activities  prohibited  with
respect  to  religion.  One  need  only  consider  the  United
States  Postal  Service,  the  licensing  and  regulation
of  commercial  broadcasting,  the  substantial  grants  to
educational  radio  and  television  stations  (47  U.S.C.
390-399),  the  expenditures  made  by State  and  Federal
governments  to  conduct  elections,  the  preferential
postal  rates  (39  C.F.R.  132.2)  and  antitrust  exemp-
tions  (15  U.S.C.  1801-1804)  for  newspapers,  the
comprehensive  regulation  of  telephone  and  telegraph
operations  and  charges  (47  U.S.C.  201-223),  and  the
establishment  and  maintenance  of  public  libraries,
halls  and  parks,

To  be  sure,  one  can  argue,  as  a  matter  of  policy  or
political  philosophy,  that  it  is  “dangerous”?  to  make
federal  elections  dependent  on  public  money,  because
the  government—presumably  the  incumbent  Admin-
istration—will  then  be  in  a  position,  albeit  illegally,
to  manipulate  the  outcome.  We  could  retort  that  it is
safer  to  place  that  power  of  the  purse  in  govern-
mental  hands  than  in  the  hands  of  aggregated  private
wealth.  But,  constitutionally,  those  considerations  are
irrelevant.  In  the  eye  of  the  Constitution,  there  being

65

no  prohibition,  it  is  enough  that  public  funds  are
appropriated  for  objectives  that  Congress  (as  we
have  seen)  has  power  to  further.

b.  Given  the  ends  served  by  public  financing  of
Presidential  elections,  the  necessary  moneys  can  be
raised  by taxes  levied  in  the  promotion  of  the  “gen-
eral  Welfare  of  the  United  States.”  Const.,  Art.  I, -
Section  8, clause  1; see  United  States  v.  Butler,  297
U.S.  1, 66.  As  this  Court  recently  observed,  “it  is dif-
ficult  to  single  out  any  [governmental  function  sup-
ported  from  general  revenues]  of a  higher  order  than
the  conduct  of  elections  at  all  levels  to  bring  forth
those  persons  desired  by their  fellow  citizens  to  gov-
ern.”  Bullock  y.  Carter,  supra,  405  U.S.  at  148-149.
Nor  is  government  limited  to  providing  election  ma-
chinery  and  supervisory  staff:  it  may  directly  subsi-
dize  or  reimburse  candidates  or  their  political  parties.
American  Party  of Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  767,  791-
794.

In  our  view,  there  could  be  no  objection  if  the
moneys  needed  were  directly  appropriated  from  the
Treasury  without  express  taxpayer  approval,  so  long
as  the  funds  are  not  committed  to  a  partisan  cause  or
to  favor  a  particular  group  of  candidates.  Compare
Machinists  v.  Street,  367  U.S.  740;  Lathrop  v.  Dono-
hue,  367  U.S.  820.  That  some  of those  whose  money  is
used  disapprove  of  all  the  beneficiaries  is no  sounder
an  objection  than  that  some  taxpayers  disapprove  of
a particular  government  spending  objective.  But,  at all
events,  there  can  be  no  infirmity  in  a  scheme  that  em-

on at 8 Oe we eee

66

ploys  only  funds  voluntarily  contributed  for  the  pur-
pose.

Of  course,  there  were  alternative  solutions.  It  was
suggested  that  the  taxpayer  be  allowed  to  designate
the  party  or  candidate  he  wished  to  have  receive  his
contribution.  That  scheme  has  obvious  deficiencies,
however:  it  excludes  any  independent  candidate  who
hecomes  known  only  after  the  tax  returns  are  filed;
it  freezes  the  subsidy  of  each  beneficiary  at  an  early
date  in  the  campaign;  it compels  voters  to  choose  he-
fore  they  have  heard  much  of  the  debate,  and,  under
present  practice,  before  the  nominees  of  the  major
parties  have  been  chosen;  and  it unnecessarily  reveals
to  the  government  the  citizen’s  political  preference.

Nor  is  the  “voucher”  system  advocated  by  Senator
Metcalf  (see  Senator  Metcalf’s  amicus  brief,  pp.  46-
51, and  Appellants’  brief,  pp.  151-152,  163)  without  its
dangers.  The  potential  for  abuse,  through  coercive
pressures  on  voucher  holders  and  ‘‘black  market”
purchases  at  cut-rate  prices,  is evident  enough.  Again,
unless  the  citizen  is to  hold  back  his  voucher  contribu-
tion  until  it  is  too  late  to  help  his  candidate  much,
he  will  have  committed  himself  without  benefit  of  the
campaign  discussion,

But,  at  all  events,  the  existence  of  other  possible
schemes  does  not  invalidate  the  present  congres-
sional  choice.  The  Constitution  does  not  mandate  a
single  solution:  it only  forbids  some.

c.  There  is, in  our  view,  no  other  First  Amendment
question  with  respect  to  the  public  financing  provi-

67

sions  of the  challenged  legislation.  The  suggestion  that
substituting  public  money  for  private  contributions
will  estrange  the  Presidential  candidate  from  his  sup-
porters,  or  the  elected  President  from  the  citizenry  at
large,  misses  the  mark.  Of  course,  dependence  on
campaign  donations,  especially  from  moneyed  inter-
ests,  will  end.  But  that  is  no  loss.  Nor  is  the  link  be-
tween  the  candidate  and  his  audience  severed:  he  must
still  win  votes  to gain  election.

d.  Of  course,  public  funds  cannot  be  allocated  in
a  way  that  is intended  to  favor  one  candidate  or  polit-
ical  party  or  disadvantage  any  political  aspirant.
Government  must  be  neutral.  That  concern  has  led
to  many  provisions  of  law  inhibiting  “interference”
by  the  government  or  its  agents  in  political  activity.
See,  e.g.,  the  Hatch  Act,  5  U.S.C.  7524(a);  47  U.S.C.
326;  47  U.S.C.  398.  The  First  Amendment  would  con-
demn  any  scheme  that  grossly  discriminated  on  its
face  (e.g.,  O’Brien  Vv.  Skinner,  414  U.S.  524),  or  which
invited  such  discrimination.  See  e.g.,  Shuttlesworth  v.
Birmingham,  394  U.S.  147.  Because  the  charge  of
discrimination  is  in  essence  the  same  one  advanced
under  the  equal  protection  principle  embodied  in  the
Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment,  we  dis-
cuss  it below  (see  Point  III,  infra).

4.  Vagueness

Appellants  contend  (Br.  113-114,  213-215,  220-221)
that  certain  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  Amendments  are  so  imprecise  or  ambiguous
as  to  require  their  invalidation  for  ‘‘vagueness,”  a

68

doctrine  of special  application  when  First  Amendment
rights  are  implicated.

The  answer  is  supplied  by  Civil  Service  Commis-
sion  v.  Letter  Carriers,  supra,  413  U.S.  at  568-581.
There  the  Court  was  confronting  the  argument  that
the  Hatch  Act  prohibition  on  government  employees
taking  “an  active  part  in  political  management  or  in
political  campaigns’’  was  void  for  vagueness.  To  be
sure,  that  broad  generality  was  given  more  concrete
content  by  congressional  adoption  in  1940  of  prior
Civil  Service  Commission  rulings.  But  the  Court  rec-
ognized  that,  even  so,  it  was  necessary  to  evolve  “a
more  refined  definition  of  what  conduct  would  or
would  not  violate  the  statutory  prohibition”  and  that
it was  entirely  proper  for  Congress  to leave  that  task
to  the  Commission  by  a  process  that  would  continue
“down  through  the  years.”  Id.  at  575.  The  Court  also
noted  that  ‘“‘the  Commission  has  established  a  proce-
dure  by which  an  employee  in  doubt  about  the  valid-
ity of  a  proposed  course  of  conduct  may  seek  and  ob-
tain  advice  from  the  Commission  and  thereby  remove
any  doubt  there  may  be  as  to  the  meaning  of the  law,
at least  insofar  as  the  Commission  itself  is concerned.”
Id.  at  580,  footnote  omitted;  see,  also,  Federal  Trade
Commission  v.  Colgate-Palmolive  Co.,  380  U.S.  374,
394.

Those  principles  govern  here.  Congress  expressly
directed  the  Federal  Election  Commission  to  resolve
ambiguities
in  the  Campaign  Act  Amendments
through  rules  and  regulations  (2 U.S.C.  438(a)  (10) )

69

and  advisory  opinions  at  the  request  of  any  federal
office  holder,  candidate  or  political  committee  (2
U.S.C.  437f).  There  is  thus  no  serious  risk  that  any
provision  will  remain  unclear,  except  only  insofar  as
“‘there  are  limitations  in  the  English  language  with
respect  to  being  both  specific  and  manageably  brief.”’
Letter  Carriers,  supra,  413  U.S.  at  578-579.

Nor  is there  any  cause  to  fear  that  clarifying  inter-
pretations  will  be  so  long  delayed  as  to produce  a  sub-
stantial  ‘‘chilling”  of  activity  protected  by the  First
Amendment  in  the  meantime.  An  affected  party  can
obtain  a  prompt  ruling  for  his  case  by requesting  an
advisory  opinion.  Indeed,  many  requests  for  such
opinions  have  already  been  submitted.  See  40  Fed.
Reg.  26660-26666,  28044-28045,  28944-28945,  30258-
30259,  and  as  of  October  14,  1975,  the  Commission  has
issued  more  than  20  of  them.  See  40  Fed.  Reg.  29792--
29794,  31315-31217,  34084-34085,  36092-36094,  36242-
36243,  36746-36747,  40673-40675,  42838-42840,  44040),
45292-45293,  45295-45296,  47691.

Itl

THE  LEGISLATION  DOES  NOT  INVIDIOUSLY  DISCRIMINATE

AGAINST  CHALLENGERS  OR  CANDIDATES  OF  MINOR  OR

NEW  PARTIES

Appellants  contend  (Br.  94-104,  127-138,  152-169)
that  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments
invidiously  discriminate  against  challengers  to  incum-
bents  and  against  candidates  of minor  or  new  parties.
The  first  answer  is  that  the  challenge  is essentially
premature.  So  saying,  we  invoke  no  jurisdictional  doce-

Se
Re ene en oe

70

71

trines  of  “ripeness”  or  “prematurity.”  We  mean  only
that,  until  an  electoral  campaign  is held  under  the  new
rules,  no  one  really  knows  precisely  what  their  prac-
tical  impact  will  be.  It  is  impossible  to  predict  with
assurance  who  will  be  helped  and  who  will  be  hurt.
Accordingly,  we  urge  that  the  Court  should  proceed
cautiously  in  assessing  the  claim  of  discrimination.

Of  course,  it would  be proper  to  entertain  any  claim
that  the  new  rules  will  inevitably  grossly  prejudic
one  group  or  another.  But,  to  prove  such  a  charge,
appellants  carry  a  heavy  burden.  Since  the  subject-
matter  is  one  with  respect  to  which  the  Members  of
Congress  are  peculiarly  knowledgeable,  it  is  difficult
to  say  that  the  law  works  gross  injustice  without
thereby  imputing  improper  motives  to  its  framers.

What,  then,  are  the  main  complaints?  We  can  sum-
marize  them  under  two  heads:  (1)  that  the  contribu-
tion,  and  especially  the  expenditure,  ceilings  grossly
favor  incumbents  and  major  party  candidates,  and
(2)  that  the  public  financing  provisions  for  Presi-
dential  primaries  and  elections  grossly  favor  the  major
party  candidates.

A.  THE  CONTRIBUTION  AND  EXPENDITURE  CEILINGS

By  imposing  contribution  and  expenditure  limits,
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  are
said  to  discriminate  against  two  groups:  challengers
and  minor  party  or  independent  candidates.  Although
the  situations  often  coincide,  it is appropriate  to  treat
the  claims  separately.  We  first  look  at  the  effect  of the

ea

new  law  insofar  as  it  is  alleged  to  entrench  incum-
bents  against  primary  challengers  (usually  major
party  candidates),  and  then  consider  the  position  of
minor  party  and  independent  candidates.

1.  Challengers

It  is  a  well-known  fact  of  American  political  life
that,  in  most  cases,  the  incumbent  seeking  reelection
enjoys  a  substantial  advantage  over  a  new  challenger.
There  are  many  reasons:  the  incumbent  is  usually
the  better  known  figure;  he  can  claim  the  benefit  of
experience  in  the  particular  office;  he  has  a  longer
opportunity  to  know  his  constituency  and  his  con-
stituents;  he  is  in  a  better  position  to  “make”  news
and  thereby  win  free  publicity;  without  serious  check,
he  has  the  services  of  a  staff  paid  with  public  funds;
and,  finally,  he  can  use  the  franking  privilege  to  com-
municate  with  his  constituents  during  the  campaign
(but  see  2 U.S.C.  439b).

If  one  looks  at  the  history  of  the  Presidency,  the
“momentum’’  of  ineumbency  seems  almost  insur-
mountable.  In  this  century,  no  sitting  President  who
wanted  it has  ever  failed  to  gain  renomination  by his
party  and,  with  only  two  very  special  exceptions,”
he  has  won  reelection  as  well.  Congressmen  and  Sen-

“e

1°  President  Taft  was  defeated  in  1912  when  a  faction  of  his
party  supported  former  President  Roosevelt  and  Woodrow  Wil-
son  was  elected  with  only  42  percent  of  the  popular  vote.
75;  J.  App.  40.  President  Hoover  was  defeated
by  Franklin  Roosevelt  in  1932,  the  depth  of  the  worst  depres-
sion  in the  Nation’s  history.  Findings  I, § 85; J. App.  41.

indings  I,

595-040 —75——_8

72

they  can  be  defeated
ators  are  somewhat  less  secur
by  age,  scandal,  apparent  estrangement  from  their
constituents,  or  simply  by an  irresistibly  charismatic
challenger.  But  the  tradition  remains  that  here,  too,
the  incumbent  is usually  returned  to  office.  Thus,  only
a  total  of  57  incumbents  were  defeated  in  the  1972
and  1974  elections.  Findings  ITA,  {| 3-6;  J. App.  269-
394.

In  the  light  of  these  realities  (so  much  stressed  by
appellants,  Br.  94-104,  127-129),  one  may  wonder
whether  the  new  federal  election  campaign  statutes
ean  do  any  harm.  The  charge,  however,  is  that  the
law  aggravates  the  plight  of  challengers  by  prevent-
ing  them,  henceforth,  from  attempting  to  overcome
their  disadvantage  by  receiving  larger  contributions
and  spending  more  than  incumbents.  To  impose  the
same  ceilings  on  both  incumbent  and  challenger,  it  is
said,  is  to  ensure  that  the  former,  with  a  big  head
start,  will  prevail.

The  argument  has  a  superficial  logic.  But  there  is
a  fundamental  flaw:  what  is  ignored  is  that,  among
the  advantages  incumbents  usually  enjoy,  is  a  sub-
stantially  greater  ability  to  raise  campaign  funds,
especially  large  contributions.  Thus,  if  there  were
no  limit  on  the  size  of  contributions,  and  no  ceiling
on  total  expenditures,  one  would  expect  the  threat-
ened  incumbent  to  be  able  to  win  the  fund-raising
contest  and  to  outspend  the  challenger.  Not  surpris-
ingly,  that  is  precisely  what  actual  experience  shows.
Thus,  in the  two  most  recent  congressional  elections

73

of  1972  and  1974,  incumbents  on  average  outspent
their  major  party  opponents  by  nearly  two  to  one
(Findings  ITA,  1114,  24,  37,  45;  J.  App.  442,  444,
447-448,  449),  and  more  than  twice  as  many  incum-
bents  as  challengers  spent  more  than  would  be allowed
by  present  law.  Findings  ITA,  {{  18,  28,  40,  47;  J.
App.  442-443,  445,  448,  450.  It  is  true  that  of  the  57
races  in  1972  in  which  incumbents  were  defeated,  they
were  outspent  in  34  instances.  Findings  IIA,  {1  3-6,
20,  30,  42,  49;  J.  App.  269-394,  443,  445,  449,  450.
But  in all  but  9 cases  the  successful  challenger  did  not
exceed  the  present  spending  limits.  Findings  ITA,  {1
19,  20,  29,  30,  41,  42,  48,  49;  J.  App.  443,  445,  448-
449,  450.  The  upshot  is  that  the  expenditure  ceilings
would  apparently  hurt  incumbents  as  a  class  more
than  challengers  as  a  class.

Nor  is  there  any  indication  that  the  contribution
ceilings  now  imposed  will  disadvantage  challengers.
In  the  1974  congressional  elections  in  which  mafor _  “7
party  candidates  challenged  incumbents,  a  $1,000  lim-
itation  on  individual  contributions  would  have  yielded
for  major  party  challengers  almost  exactly  the  same
percentage  (just  over  one-half)  of  the  funds  raised
by  incumbents,  the  latter  losing  substantially  more
dollars.  See  Findings  ITA,  11  55,  57-58.  J.  App.  452,
453.  With  respect  to  the  most  recent  Presidential
election,  the  major  party  challengers,  it seems,  would
have  been  relatively  much  better  off.  See  Findings
ITA,  £74;  J. App.  458.

The  claim  (Appellants’  Br.  129-138)  that  chal-
lengers,  unlike  incumbents,  have  a  special  need  to

74

,

75

raise  ‘‘seed  money”  through  large  contributions  is
simply  unproven.  No  doubt  some  challengers  have
received  large  donations  at  the  start  of  a  campaign,
as  have  incumbents.  But  there  is no  evidence  that  this
is necessary  to  a  viable  contest.  Indeed,  the  experience
of  Ramsey  Clark,  who  successfully  raised  more  than
$850,000  in  contributions  limited  to  $100  (see  Find-
ines  I,  {{  182,  185-186;  J.  App.  260,  261),  sug-
gests  that  a  $1,000  limit  will  not  seriously  prejudice
challengers.”

2.  Minor  party  and  independent  candidates

The  claim  that  the  giving  and  spending  limits  im-
posed  by  the  new  law  discriminate  against  minor
party  and  independent  candidates  has  even  less  foun-
dation  than  the  attack  made  on  behalf  of  major  party
challengers.  The  bald  fact  is  that  only  a  single  minor
party  or  independent  candidate  in  the  1972  and  1974
congressional  elections  exceeded  the  present  spend-
ing  limit.  Findings  ITA,  £! 7, 19,  29,  41,  48;  J.  App.
395-422,  443,  445,  448-449,  450.  And,  of  course,  none
came  ¢lose  to  the  ceiling  in  the  most  recent  Presiden-
tial  election.  Finding  ITA,  £51;  J.  App.  451.  When
this  is  contrasted  with  the  showing  that  77  major
party  candidates  for  Congress  in  1972  and  1974  spent

1  Also  indicative  is  the  fact  that,  in  the  1972  Presidential
race,  Senator  McGovern  accumulated  some  $24  million  in  con-
tributions  not  exceeding  $1,000.  Findings  ITA,  § 72;  J.  App.
457.  And  see  the  amounts  raised  as  of  the  end  of  1974  by  1976
Presidential  candidates  in  contributions  up  to  $1,000.  Findings
I, © 196;  J.  App.  263-264.

more  than  the  present  law  permits  (Findings  IIA,
“f18,  28,  40,  47;  J.  App.  442-443,  445,  448,  450)  and
that  both  major-party  candidates  for  the  Presidency
did  so  in  1972  (Findings  ITA,  £71;  J.  App.  457),
it  is evident  that  minor  party  and  independent  candi-
dates  will  be  relatively  better  off  under  the  expendi-
ture  limits  fixed  by  the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act  Amendments.

Nor  is  there  any  indication  that  the  new  contribu-
tions  limits  will  disadvantage  those  who  do  not  rep-
resent  a  major  party.  On  the  contrary,  it appears  that
in  the  1972  Presidential  elections  minor  party  and
independent  candidates  would  have  relatively  bene-
fitted  from  the  new  contributions  ceilings  (Findings
ITA,  £175;  J.  App.  458),  and  congressional  candi-
dates  in  1974  would  not  have  suffered.  See  Findings
ITA,  SS 59-60,  115;  J.  App.  453-454,  473-474.

Appellants  have  failed  to  demonstrate,  as  is  their
burden,  that  the  challenged  contributions  and  expen-
diture  ceilings  will  invidiously  discriminate  against
challengers  or  against  minor  party  and  independent
candidates.  To  the  contrary,  those  provisions  are  likely
to  reduce  the  present  advantages  enjoyed  by  incum-
bents  and  major  party  candidates  by making  it  more
difficult  for  entrenched  office  holders  to  overcome  any
serious  challenge  by  raising  more  money  and  out-
spending  their  opponents.  The  net  effect  is  to  afford
outsiders  a  better  chance,  even  if  still  very  unequal.
That  the  balance  is not  fully  redressed  is no  basis  for
a  charge  of  discrimination.

B.  PUBLIC  FUNDING  OF  PRESIDENTIAL  ELECTIONS
The  charge  (Appellants’  Br.  152-169)  that  the  pub-
lie  funding  provisions  of  the  new  law’  discriminate
against  minor  and  new  parties  and  independent  can-
didates  is  entirely  conjectural,  if  not  a  little  unreal,
since  no  candidate  not  of  a  major  party  has  remotely
approached  the  Presidency  since  Theodore  Roosevelt’s
“Bull  Moose”  defection.  Once  again,  it  is  no  ground
for  faulting  the  Campaign  Act  that  it does  not  so  radi-
cally  change  the  status  quo  as  fully  to  even  the  odds
for  the  candidate  of  an  entirely  new  party  as  against
the  nominees  of the  two  traditional  major  parties.  Our
submission  is  that  the  new  law  helps  new  challengers.
But,  in  answer  to  the  appellants’  charge,  it is enough

ae  al

isting  disparities.

S

We  examine  first  the  eligibility  conditions  for  en-
titlement  to  any  public  funds,  leaving  to  later  con-
sideration  the  allocation  formulae.

1.  Eligibility  provisions

Although  public  funds  are  not  made  available  to
every  would-be  candidate  for  the  Presidency,  no  mat-
ter  how  frivolous,  that  is  as  it  should  be.  The  Court
has  expressly  approved  various  State  election  rules
that  seek  to  restrict  the  number  of  candidates  who
enter  the  lists,  noting  that  “a  State  has  an  interest,
if  not  a  duty,  to  protect  the  integrity  of  its  political
processes  from  frivolous  or  fraudulent  candidacies.”
Bullock  vy.  Carter,  supra,  405  U.S.  at  145.  Beyond
that,  it  has  been  recognized  as  proper  to  take  meas-
ures  to  discourage  “splintered  parties  and  unre-

‘toe oe

77

strained  factionalism”  (Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.
724,  736),  and  “to  prevent  the  clogging  of  [the]  elec-
tion  machinery,  avoid  voter  confusion,  and  assure  that
the  winner  is  the  choice  of  a  majority,  or  at  least  a
strong  plurality,  of  those  voting.”  Bullock  v.  Carter,
supra,  405  U.S.  at 145.

If  these  interests  may  be  served  by denying  a  place
on  a  ballot  to  those  who  do  not  first  demonstrate  sub-
stantial  popular  support,  as  the  Court  has  repeatedly
stated  (Williams  v.  Rhodes,  supra,  393  U.S.  at  31-33;
Jenness  Vv.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  442;  Bullock  vy.
Carter,  supra,  405  U.S.  at  145;  Lubin  v.  Panish,
supra,  415  U.S.  at  718-719;  Storer  v.  Brown,  supra,
415  U.S.  at  736;  American  Party  of Texas  v.  White,
supra,  415  U.S.  at  781),  it must  follow,  a fortiori,  that
the  same  considerations  will  justify  the  lesser  deter-
rent  of  withholding  a  public  subsidy.  Thus,  the  ques-
tion  is  not  whether  it  is  permissible  to  restrict  eligi-
bility  for  public  funds,  but  whether  the  mechanisms
selected  are  too  restrictive.

a.  We  begin  with  the  Presidential  primary  match-
ing  fund  scheme.  Subject  to  the  overall  $10  million
spending  limitation’  and  to  the  availability  of  suffi-
cient  moneys  in  the  Presidential  Election  Campaign
Fund,”  every  eligible  candidate  who  enters  Presi-

*  No  candidate  may  receive  public  funds  exceeding  one-half
the  Presidential  primary  spending  ceiling.  26  U.S.C.  9034(b).
**  Matching  payments  for  primaries  have  the  last  claim  on  the
Fund  (26  U.S.C.  9037(a)),  and,  in  the  event  the  Presidential
Primary  Matching  Account  is insufficient  to  satisfy  all  demands,
the  Secretary  of  the  Treasury  is  directed  to  “seek  to  achieve
an  equitable  distribution,”  taking  into  consideration  the  time
priority  of claims.  26  U.S.C.  9037(b).

78

79

dential  primaries  is  entitled  to  a  matching  payment
up  to  $250  for  every  private  contribution  he  receives.
No  distinction  is  made  between  candidates:  all  must
meet  the  same  eligibility  test  and  all  receive  funds
according  to  the  same  formula.

Nor  is  qualification  unduly  restrictive.  To  be  eligi-
ble,  the  candidate  must  raise  $5,000  (counting  only
the  first  $250  of  any  contribution)  in  each  of  20
States.  That  requires  no  more  than  20  relatively
modest  contributions  of  $250  in  as  many  States  and
a  total  campaign  fund  of  $100,000.  It  is  a  test  which
any  serious  Presidential  contender  should  be  able
to  meet,

Matching  payments  are  made  only  to  those  who  are
engaged  in  ‘‘seeking  nomination  by  a  political  party
for  election  to  the  office  of  President  of  the  United
States.”  26  U.S.C.  9033(b)(2).  But  that  is  no  more
than  a  recognition  that  it  is  the  expense  of  winning
nomination  that  is  being  reimbursed  in  part  out  of
public  funds.  Those  who  incur  no  such  costs  are  prop-
erly  excluded.  There  is  no  discrimination  between  as-
pirants,  whether  the  party  whose  nomination  is sought
is established  or  new,  major  or  minor.  Nor  is the  term
‘‘nolitical  party”  restrictive:  although  not  defined  in
the  Internal  Revenue  Code,  it  presumably  embraces
any  ‘‘association,  committee  or  organization”  that
sponsors  a  Presidential  candidate,  See  2  U.S.C.  431
(m);  18  U.S.C.  591(i),  And  it makes  no  difference  by
what  method  the  party  nominee  is chosen,  whether  by

primary  election,  by convention,  or  by caucus.  See  26
U.S.C.  9032(7).*

In  sum,  the  matching  fund  scheme  is  as  all-cim-
bracing  as  possible,  more  so  than  the  law  upheld  in
American  Party  of  Texas  v.  White,  supra,  415  U.S.
at  791-794.  At  all  events,  this  financing  law  is no  ‘‘ex-
clusionary  mechanism.’’  Jd.  at  794.  It  does  not  offend
the  equal  protection  principle  of  the  Fifth  Amend-
ment.

b.  The  public  financing  of  Presidential  nominating
conventions  likewise  involves  no  impermissible  dis-
crimination.  Of  course,  only  parties  holding  such  con-
ventions  are  eligible  for  the  grant,  but  the  new  law  in
no  way  dictates  which  parties  should  follow  that  pro-
cedure.  Compare  American  Party  of Texas  v.  White,
supra.

In  order  to  receive  funding  for  its  convention  an
organization  must  be  a  party  which  has  established  a
footing  in  the  political  arena  | y winning  for  its  can-
didate  in  the  previous  Presidential  election  5 percent
of  the  votes  cast.  See  26  U.S.C.  9008(b),  9002(7).
That,  however,  is  well  within  the  rule  that  public
funds  may  properly  be  reserved  for  serious  candidacy.
As  the  Court  has  said,  merely  because  government  de-
frays  some  election  expenses,  it is not  required  also  to
“finance  the  efforts  of  every  nascent  political  group

**  Despite  the  title  of  the  Act  (see  26  U.S.C.  9031),  the  opera-
tive  provisions  of  the  matching  payment  statute  nowhere  re-
quire  that  nomination  be  by  “primary”  election.  But,  even  if
that  be  implied,  the  cited  section  defines  “primary  election”
comprehensively,  as  we  have  indicated  above.

80

*  **  American  Party  of Texas  v.  White,  supra,  415
U.S.  at 794.

c.  The  most  important  aspect  of public  financing  in-
troduced  by the  new  law  is the  provision  for  grants  to
the  candidates  at  the  general  election  for  the  Presi-
dency.  Appropriately,  special  efforts  have  been  made
here  to  avoid  excluding  any  serious  contender,  while
at  the  same  guarding  against  encouragement  of  friv-
olous  candidacy.  A just compromise  between  those  two
objectives  has  been  achieved.

There  are  two  basic  eligibility  requirements.  The
first  is  that  the  candidate  must  show  that  he  pre-
sumptively  represents  5  percent  of  the  voters.  This
condition  can  be  satisfied  in  several  ways.  It is enough
if  the  applicant  is the  nominee  of  a  party  whose  can-
didate  at  the  previous  Presidential  election  received
that  percentage  of  the  popular  vote.  26  U.S.C.  9002
(6)  and  (7),  9004(a)(1)  and  (2)(A).  Or  the  candi-
date  himself  may  have  achieved  such  a  past  record,  in
which  event  he  qualifies  notwithstanding  that  he  may
now  be  sponsored  by a  new  party.  26  U.S.C.  9004(a)
(2)(B).  Or,  finally,  although  neither  the  present  can-
didate  nor  his  party  received  the  necessary  votes  at
the  last  election,  he  will  still  be  entitled  to  the  subsidy
if  he  now  wins  5  percent  of  the  popular  vote.  26
U.S.C.  9004(a)(3).  This  is  a  fair  attempt  to  afford
every  serious  aspirant  a  share  in the  scheme,  imposing
only  a  reasonable  test  of  past  or  current  support.

There  is  a  further  requirement  that  the  candidate
must  have  his  name  (or  that  of  electors  pledged  to

81

him)  on  the  Presidential  ballot  in  10  States.  26  U.S.C.
9002 (2) (B).  This  is to assure  a national,  and  not  mere-
ly local,  appeal  in  those  who  receive  public  funds.  Ex-
perience  tells  us  that  such  a  requirement  will  exclude
no  aspirant  with  any  real  prospect  of  success,  consid-
ering  that  victory  in  the  electoral  college  requires  the
votes  of the  11  most  populous  Siaies  or  40  of the  least
populous.  Findings  I,  £  51;  J.  App.  26.  Compare
Moore  v.  Ogilvie,  394  U.S.  814.

The  public  financing  provisions  of  the  new  law  ex-
clude  no  substantial  Presidential  contender.  It remains
only  to  answer  the  charge  that,  among  eligible  candi-
dates,  the  apportionment  of  funds  is  discriminatory.

2.  Allocation  form ulae

We  have  already  noted  that  the  primary  matching
payment  scheme  treats  every  eligible  candidate  iden-
tically.  There  are,  however,  different  allocation  for-
mulae  applicable  to  grants  for  nominating  conven-
tions  and  for  the  general  election.  Thus,  ‘“‘major”  par-
ties  automatically  receive  a  full  subsidy  for  their  con-
ventions,  as  do  their  nominees,  whereas  other  parties
and  candidates  are  only  entitled  to  a  pro  rata  share
based  on  their  performance  at  the  previc  .s  or  cur-
rent  election.  This  is said  to be discriminatory.

It  goes  without  saying  that  any  demarcation  line
between  “major”,  “minor’’  and  “new”  parties  is neces-
sarily  a  matter  of  judgment.  There  is,  however,  no
present  basis  for  faulting  the  particular  percentage
figure.  selected,  assuming  that  some  distinction  is

82

83

proper.  Our  submission  is  that  it  was  permissible  to
take  account  of  the  “obvious  differences  in  kind  be-
tween  the  needs  and  potentials  of  a  political  party
with  historically  established  broad  support,  on  the  one
hand,  and  a  new  or  small  political  organization  on  the
other.’’  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  supra,  403  U.S.  at  441.  Of
course,  it would  be  improper  to  use  public  financing  to
assure  “a  complete  monopoly”  to “two  particular  par-
ties’?  (Williams  v.  Rhodes,  supra,  393  U.S.  at  32).
But,  as  long  as  the  status  quo  is not  frozen,  there  is no
objection  to  ‘‘encourag[ing]  compromise  and  political
stability”  (id.  at  31-32)  by incentives  to  relatively  uni-
fied  large  political  parties.  See  Jenness  v.  Fortson,
supra,  403  U.S.  at  489-440.  That  is what  the  law  does.
The  public  financing  provisions  are  carefully  de-
signed  to  enable  any  party  to  win  ‘“‘major”  party
status.  First,  the  boundary  line  is  fixed  relatively
low—at  25  percent  of  the  votes  cast—and  no  distine-
tion  is  made  above  that  level.  Second,  the  formula
for  grants  to  minor  and  new  parties  relates  to  the
average  of  the  votes  received  by  the  major  party
candidates—not  those  received  by  the  winner—with
the  effect  of  increasing  financing  for  lesser  candidates.
Third,  the  grants  for  minor  candidates  are  strictly
proportional  to  votes  received,  thereby  reflecting  the
growth  of those  parties.  Finally,  while  the  initial  allo-
cation  of  funds  is made  on  the  basis  of  past  perform-
ance,  changes  in  support  are  recognized  by providing
additional  grants  (or  first  grants  in  the  case  of  new
parties)  on  the  basis  of  current  performance.  The

statutory  scheme,  without  wasting  public  money  and
unduly  encouraging  factionalism,  effectively  reflects
and  respects  the  “‘potential  fluidity  of American  polit-
ical  life’?  (Jenness  v.  Fortson,  supra,  403  U.S.  at
439).

Ideally,  it would  be  preferable  to  give  all  candidates
their  share  of  public  funds  before  the  election.  That
is  done  with  respect  to  the  nominees  of  all  parties
which  have  a  proven  record,  as  well  as  candidates  who
have  personally  achieved  the  required  vote  in  the
most  recent  election.  But,  unless  every  claimant,  no
matter  how  frivolous,  is  to  be  given  $20  million  for
the  asking,  there  must  be  a  formula  that  tests  the
seriousness  of  candidates,  and,  for  those  who  have
not  run  before  and  who  represent  new  parties,  the
first  available  record  is  the  current  election.

It  is  a  disadvantage  for  them  to  have  to  borrow
and  receive  reimbursement  only  if  they  demonstrate
their  support  at  the  election.  Yet  that  is the  best  that
can  be  done,  and  the  Constitution  does  not  require
the  impossible.  It  would  not  be  reasonable  to  deny
everyone  else,  of  proven  record,  his  share  during  the
obviously  appropriate  pre-election  period  simply  to
avoid  this  disparity.  That  would  indeed  be  allowing
the  tail  to wag  the  dog.

The  general  election  financing  scheme  is  imperfect
only  insofar  as  is  unavoidable.  On  the  whole,  it
reaches  a  fair  accommodation.  There  is  no  invidious
discrimination  here.

84

CONCLUSION

The  judgments  of  the  court  of  appeals  and  of  the

district  court  should  be affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted.

Epwarp  H.  Levt,

Supreme  Court  of the  United  States

IN THE

OcroserR  TERM,  1975.

Nos.  75-436  anp  75-437.

ee

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY  ert  AL.,

Appellants,

v.

HON.  FRANCIS  R.  VALEO  ert  AL.,

Appellees.

On  APPEAL  FROM  THE  UNITED  StTaTes  CourT  OF  APPEALS
FOR  THE  District  or  COLUMBIA  AND  THE  UNITED  STATES
Districr  CourT  FOR  THE  DisTtrRICT  OF  COLUMBIA

BRIEF  OF  THE  FEDERAL  ELECTION
COMMISSION.

QUESTION  PRESENTED.

The  Commission’s  separate  brief  is  addressed  to  the
issues  embraced  in  Question  8 of  the  certified  questions,
reading  as  follows:  '

8.  Do  the  provisions  in  the  challenged  statutes  con-
cerning  the  powers  and  method  of  appointment  of  the
Federal  Election  Commission  violate  the  rights  of one  or
more  of the  plaintiffs  under  the  constitutional  separation
Fourth,  Fifth,  Sixth,  or  Ninth  Amend-
of powers,  the First,
ments,  Article  I, Section  2, Clause  6, Article  I, Section  5,
Clause  1, or  Article  III?

1.  The  Commission’s  position  with  re
fied  questions  is  set  forth  in  the  brief  filed
Department  of  Justice.

to  the  other  certi-
on  its  behalf  by  the

Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

|

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

3

(a)  Does  2  U.  S.  C.  § 437(a)  violate  such
rights  by the  method  of appointment  of the  Fed-
eral  Election  Commission?

(b)  Do2  U.S.  C.  §§ 437d  and  437g  violate
such  rights,  in  that  they  entrust  administration
FECA  to  the  Federal
and  enforcement  of  the
Election  Commission?

(c)  Does  2 U.  S.  C.  § 437g(a)  violate  such
rights,  in  that  it empowers  the  Federal  Election
Commission  and  the  Attorney  General  to  bring
civil  actions  (including  proceedings  for  injunc-
tions)  against  any  person  who  has  engaged  or
who  may  engage  in  acts  or  practices  which  vio-
late  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act,  as
amended,  or  § 608,  610,  611,  613,  614,  615,  616,
or  617  of Title  18?

(d)  Does  2  U.  S.  C.  § 438(c)  violate  such
rights,  in  that  it empowers  the  Federal  Election
Commission  to  make  rules  under  the  FECA  in
the manner  specified  therein?

(e)  Does  2 U.S.C.  § 456 violate  such rights,
in that  it imposes  a  temporary  disqualification  on
any  candidate  for  election  to  federal  office  who
is found  by the  Federal  Election  Commission  to
have  failed  to  file  a  report  required  by Title  III
of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act,  as
amended?

(f)  Does  § 9008  of  the  Internal  Revenue
Code  of  1954  violate  such  rights,  in  that  it em-
powers  the  Federal  Election  Commission  to  au-
thorize  expenditures  of  the  national
»mmittee
of a  party  with  respect  to presidential  no.uinating
conventions  in  excess  of  the  limits  enumerated
therein?

STATUTE  INVOLVED.

The  provision  governing  the  establishment  and  com-
position  of  the  Commission  (2 U.  S.  C.  § 437c)  reads  as
follows:

There  is  established  a  commission  to  be
known  as  the Federal  Election  Commission.  The
Commission  is composed  of the  Secretary  of the
Senate  and the Clerk  of the House  of Representa-
tives,  ex  officio  and  without  right  to  vote,  and  6
members  appointed  as  follows:

(A)  two  shall  be  appointed,  with  the
confirmation  of a majority  of both  Houses  of
the  Congress,  by the  President  pro  tempore
of the  Senate  upon  the  recommendations  of
the  majority  leader  of  the  Senate  and  the
minority  leader  of the  Senate;

(B)  two  shall  be  appointed,  with  the
confirmation  of a majority  of both  Houses  of
the  Congress,  by the  Speaker  of the  House
of  Representatives,  upon  the  recommenda-
tions  of the majority  leader  of the House  and
the  minority  leader  of the  House;  and

(C)  two  shall  be  appointed,  with  the
confirmation  of a majority  of both  Houses  of
the Congress,  by the  President  of the  United
States.

A  member  appointed  under  subparagraph  (A),
(B),  or  (C)  shall  not  be affiliated  with  the  same
political  party  as  the  other  member  appointed
under  such  paragraph.

|

The  various  provisions  bearing  on  the  powers  and
functions  of the  Commission  are  set  forth  in  the  statutory
pamphlet  (“Federal  Election  Campaign  Laws”),  copies  of
which  have  heen  lodged  with  the Court.
