Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

5

rr,

STATEMENT.

The  history  of  these  proceedings  is  set  forth  in  the
brief  filed  by the  Department  of Justice.  We  confine  our-
selves  here  t«:  a summary  statement  of the court  of appeals’
rulings  on  tiie issues  embraced  in Question  8.

Addressing  itself  to  the  question  whether  the  Com-
mission  is  unlawfully  constituted  because  only  two  of  its
members  are  appointed  by the  President,  the  court  below
first observed  (J. S. 1508)  that the Commission  is “essential
to the effective  functioning  of the overall  scheme,”  that  the
area  in  which  it is operating  is “novel  and  complex,”  and
that  its  existence  as  an  agency  ought  not  to  be  terminated
unless  “wholly  without  legitimacy  in  the  very  terms  in
which  it is brought  into  being.”  The  court  ruled  further
that  Article  II,  Section  2, of the  Constitution  does  not  bar
the  appointment  of inferior  civil  officers  by the  Legislative
Branch  when  the  officers  will  be  engaged  in  the  perform-
ance  of dominantly  legislative  functions;  that  Congress  has
broad  and  pervasive  responsibilities  with  respect  to  the
conduct  and control  of federal  elections  and  the prevention
of  corruption  in  the  electoral  process;  and  that  Congress
“draws  sufficient  authority  from  the  necessary  and  proper
clause  of the  Constitution”  to  create  a Commission  to  aid
it  in  the  execution  of  those  responsibilities  (J.  S.  1573).
“We  thus  conclude,”  the  court  stated  (ibid.),  “that  Con-
gress  has  the  constitutional  authority  to  establish  and  ap-
point  the  Federal  Election  Commission  to  carry  out
appropriate  legislative  functions.”

Recognizing  that,  in addition  to  its  primary  legislative
functions,  the  Commission  is  authorized  by certain  of the
statutory  provisions  to  perform  tasks  that  may  be  char-
acterized  as  quasi-executive  or  quasi-judicial,  the  court
reasoned  that  this  alone  does  not  render  those  provisions
It must  be asked  whether  the  particular  function
invalid.

is  sufficiently  related  to,  or  is  ancillary  to,  an  appropriate
legislative  function.
For  the  most  part,  the  court  found
plaintiffs’  challenges  to  specific  powers  of the  Commission
not  ripe for adjudication.  This  litigation,  it observed  (J. S.
1576 ), “does  not  present  the  court  with  the  concrete  facts
that  are  necessary  to  an  informed  decision.”  The  reach  of
the Commission’s  powers  in numerous  particulars,  it noted,
has  not  been  administratively  determined.
Powers  argu-
ably  granted  are  still  unexercised  and  no  one  is  adversely
affected.
.
may  never  reach  a  stage  at  which  they  would  be  ripe  for
adjudication”  (ibid. ).

.  [S]ome  of the  issues  presented  to  us.  .

“.  .

The  court  did  find,  however,  that  two  issues  relating
to  the  exercise  of  delegated  powers  were  sufficiently  ripe
for consideration.  It considered  and  upheld  (see Question
8(b))  the  Commission’s  authority  to  issue  advisory
opinions  (“We discern  no  constitutional  barrier  that  would
prevent a legislative  agency  from  informing  the  public  of
its  interpretation  of  the  statutory  provision  it  must  ad-
minister”  (J. S.  1579) ).  It likewise  adjudicated  the  claim
(see  Question  8(f))  that  the  Commission  is  granted  ex-
cessive  discretion  insofar  as  it  is  permitted  to  authorize
nominating  convention  expenditures  in excess  of $2 million.
Construing  the  statute  to  authorize  excess  amounts  only
where  “due  to  extraordinary  and  unforeseen  circumstances
.  the  national  committee  is  unable  to  provide  the
services  actually  needed  to  run  the  convention  without
exceeding  the  statutory  limit”  (J. S.  1580),  the  court  held
that  there  were  sufficient  guidelines  to  withstand  a  consti-
tutional  challenge  of vagueness.

Finally,  we

four  of  the  six  appointments  to  the  Commission  on  a  bi-

Before  reaching  the  merits,  however,  there  is  an  im-

private  appellants  may  challenge  the  composition  and  au-

dent’s  appointive  prerogatives  have  been  impinged,  when

General  did  express

actually  would  perform  in  the  area  of  enforcement  and  that  the
question  was  therefore  premature.

The  Attorney

6

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

INTRODUCTION  AND  SUMMARY.

This  brief  defends  the legitimacy  of the means  chosen
by Congress  to establish  the  Federal  Election  Commission
(the method  of appointment)  and the propriety  of granting
it various  powers  and functions  delegated  by the campaign
legislation.
Appellants  contend  that  the  scheme  of  ap-
pointment  impinges  on  the  President’s  powers  of appoint-
ment  as  set forth in Article  II, § 2, cl. 2 of the Constitution.’
They  also  urge  that  various  of the  Commission’s  activities
will  violate  the  principle  of separation  of powers  because
the  agency  is called  upon  to  perform  quasi-executive  and
quasi-judicial,  as  well  as  quasi-legislative,  functions.*

We  argue,  in  response,  as  follows.

Firstly,  we  point
out  that Article  II, Section  2, of the Constitution  is not  pre-
clusive  of  Congressional  authority  to  exercise  appointive
power  when  the functions  of the appointees  are  dominantly
or substantially  related  to the performance  of Congressional
responsibilities.
Secondly,  we  demonstrate  that  the  Con-
stitution  confers  upon  the  Congress  broad  and  pervasive
responsibilities  in  relation  to  the  federal  electoral  process

2.  “[The  President]  shall  nominate,  and  by  and  with  the
Advice  and  Consent  of the  Senate,  shall  appoint  Ambassadors,
other  public  Ministers  and  Consuls,  Judges  of  the  Supreme
Court,  and  all  other  Officers  of  the  United  States,  whose  Ap-
intments  are  not  herein  otherwise  provided  for,  and  which
shall  be  established  by  Law:  but  the  Congress  may  by  Law
vest  the  Appointment  of  such  inferior  Officers,  as  they  think
proper, in  the  President  alone,  in  the  Courts  of  Law,  or  in  the

eads  of  Departments.”

3.  We  agree  with  the  court  of  appeals’  conclusion  that  most
of  the  —  addressed  to  the  Commission’s  powers  are  pre-
mature,  and  that  the  courts  ought  not  attempt  to  adjudicate  the
powers  in  advance  of  concrete  action
reach  of  the  Commission’s
taken  by the  Commission  affecting  the  complaining  litigants.  To
the  extent  that  we  deal  with  issues  relating  to  the  Commission’s
powers  not  reached  by the  court  below,  we  do  so  because  of  the
possibility  that  this  Court  may  adopt  somewhat  differing  views  as
to which  of the numerous  issues  raised  by appellants  are  sufficiently
ripe to  warrant  consideration.

ARGUMENT.

I,

Appellants  Have  No  Cognizable  Interest  in  Congress’  Al-
leged  Impingement  Upon  Presidential  Powers.

Appellants  urge  that  Congress  has  trenched  upon  the
President's  powers  under  Article  II  of the  Constitution  to
appoint  inferior  officers,  although  the  President  signed  the
legislation  which  provided  for  the  appointment  of  the
members  of  the  Federal  Election  Commission.
Since
there  is  no  basis  for  any  claim  that  Congress’  appointees
to  the  Commission  lack  the  qualifications  prescribed  by
the  legislation,  the  situation  is one  in  which  private  plain-
tiffs  are  seeking  to  litigate  the  validity  of a provision  which
goes  solely  to  the  allocation  of  the  appointive  power  be-
tween  the  Executive  and  the  Legislative  Branches.
The
constitutional  provision  which  appellants  invoke  was  not
enacted  for  their  benefit  or  protection,  and  its  alleged  vio-
lation  here  threatens  them  with  no  injury,  either  immedi-
ate  or  remote.
Insofar  as  appellants  claim  that  the  Presi-
dent's  powers  are  infringed,  they  are  asserting  that  they
“suffer  in  some  indefinite  way  in  common  with  people
generally.”  Frothingham  v.  Mellon,  262  U.  S.  447,  488,
reaffirmed  in  United  States  v.  Richardson,  418  UV.  S.  166,
173.  They  would  “employ  a  federal  court  as  a  forum  in
which  to  air  .
.  generalized  grievances  about  the  conduct
of  government  or  the  allocation  of  power  in  the  Federal
System,”  Flast  v.  Cohen,  392  U.  S.  83,  106.
As  Flast
further  explains  (392  U.  S.  at  99-100),  “the  question  is
whether  the  person  whose  standing  is  challenged  is  a
proper  party  to  request  an  adjudication  of  a  particular
issue  and  not  whether  the  issue  itself  is justiciable.”

also  Tileston  v.  Ullman,  318  U.  S. 44, to the effect  that one

sonal  stake  in  the  outcome  of  the  controversy’  serves  to
assure  “that  concrete  adverseness  which  sharpens  the
presentation  of  issues  upon  which  the  court  so  largely
depends  for  illumination  of  difficult  constitutional  ques-

A  reading  of  the  records  of  the  Constitutional  Con-
vention  shows  that  the  differences  of opinion  with  respect
to  the  power  of  appointment  focused  on  the  question  of
the degree  to which  Congress  should  control  or  participate
in the selection  of executive  officers.  Under  the Articles  of
Confederation,  Congress  alone  had  the  power  to  appoint
“committees  and  civil  officers  as  may  be  necessary  for
managing  the  general  affairs  of the  United  States.  .
.”
(Article  9).*°  Some  of  the  members  of  the  Convention,
highly  conscious  of  the  then  prevailing  practice,  were  of
the  view,  expressed  by Gouverneur  Morris,  that  the  legis-
lature  is  a body “too  numerous”  to continue  to exercise  that
authority."  James  Wilson  was  of the  opinion  that  “there
can  be  no  good  Executive  without  a  responsible  appoint-
ment  of officers  to  execute”  but  believed  that  the  nomina-

9.  Article  I,  § 2 does  provide,  “The  House  of  Representatives

their  other  Officers.  .  .”  Read  in  context,  however,  these  provi-
sions  appear  to  contemplate  official  posts  to  be  occupied  by  the
Representatives  and  Senators  themselves.  The  records  of the  con-

Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

tions  should  be made  by a Council  of Advice.”*  He further
defined  executive  powers  as  “those  of executing  the  laws,
and  appointing  officers  not  appertaining  to  and  appointed
by the  Legislature.”  **  Mr.  Sherman  took  the  position  that
it was  “proper  that  many  officers  in  the  Executive  Depart-
ment”  should  be  appointed  by  the  President  but  that
“many  ought  not,  as  general  officers  of  the  Army  in  time
of peace,” because  of the abiding  danger  that the Executive
“may  set  up  an  absolute  government.”  **  In essence,  there
were  two  opposing  pressures:  one  exerted  by those  who
felt  that  the  Congress  should  not  be  deeply  implicated  in
the  selection  of  executive  officers  because  responsibility
should  be centered;  the  other  exerted  by those  who  feared
that  centralization  of  appointive  authority  could  lead  to
despotism.

Article  II,  § 2,  cl.  2,  as  it  finally  evolved,  reflects  a
compromise.
The  President’s  power  to  appoint  ambas-
sadors,  public  ministers  and  consuls,  and  Judges  of  the
Supreme  Court  is  made  subject  to  Senate  confirmation.
The  President  may  appoint  other  officers  only  when  the
positions  are  “established  by Law.”  **  These  appointments
are  also  subject  to  Senate  confirmation  except  that  Con-
gress  has  discretion  to  vest  the  sole  power  of appointment
of “inferior  Officers”  in  the  President,  the  heads  of depart-
ments  or  the  courts  of  law.  Congress,  moreover,  is  free
to  establish  the  criteria  for  filling  any  vacancy."

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

15

The  important  point  for present  purposes  is that  Con-
gress  was  concerned  to  give  the  President  ample  power  to
select  his  own  officers  to  execute  the  laws  while  retaining
an  effective  degree  of control—in  the  words  of Gouverneur
Morris,  to  give  the  President  “responsibility”  while  retain-
ing  in  the  Congress  sufficient  control  to  provide  “secu-
rity.”*’  There  is not  the  slightest  hint  in  the  proceedings
of the  Convention  that  the  Framers,  who  were  of  course
highly  sensitive  to  the  dangers  of executive  authority  and
who  debated  at  length  the  appropriate  limitations  to  be
imposed  upon  the President’s  appointive  authority,  had any
thought  to  deprive  the  Congress,  which  was  to  exercise
those  controls,  of  the  power  to  appoint  its  own  inferior
officers  for purposes  of executing  such  business  as  was  ap-
propriately  related  to  Congressional  functions.
To  do  so
would  have  relegated  Congress,  which  contemporaneously
exercised  the  appointive  power  exclusively,  to  a  position
below  that  of the  co-equal  branches  (the  Framers  having
also  provided  that  the  power  to  appoint  inferior  officers
might  be  delegated  to  the  courts  of law **).

It can  hardly  be disputed,  then,  that  Congress,  at  the
least,  has  inherent  power  to  appoint  inferior  officers  or

16.  (Cont'd. )

which  Congress  has  traditionally  “restrict{ed]  the  exercise  by the

President  of  the  power  of  nomination”  ( e. 269).
number  of  statutes  that  “confined  the
President’s  selection  to  a
small  number  of  persons  to  be  named  by others”  (p.  274).

to  appoint  their  respective  clerks.

17.  Farrand,  p.  539.

delegated  to  the  Supreme  Court  and  the  district  courts  the  author-
ity
Ruling  in  In  Re  Hennen,
13  Pet.  230,  that  this  carried  with  it  the  power  to  remove  the
clerk  in  the  court’s  discretion,  this  Court  observed  that  the  power
to  appoint  inferior  officers,  as  spelled  out  in  the  Constitution,  “was
no  doubt intended  to  be  exercised  by the  department  of  the  gov-
ernment  to  which  the  officer  to  be  appointed
most  appropriately
belonged”  (13 Pet.  at  258).  As  Hennen  indicates  and  as  the  court
below  assumed,  officers  of  the  three  departments  are  unquestion-
ably  officers  of  the  United  States.

17

employees  for the  discharge  of its  own  responsibilities  and
the  conduct  of  its  own  business.
If  it  were  necessary  to
look  for  explicit  Constitutional  authority  to  support  the
view  that  Congress  may  make  appointments  of that  char-
acter,  the  grant  of power  embraced  by the  “necessary  and
proper’  clause  (Art.  1, § 8, cl.  18)  would  surely  provide  it.
With  these  propositions,  all  members  of  the  court  below
were  in apparent  agreement.”®

B.  Moreover,  the  practice  prevailing  from  the  earliest
days  of  the  Republic  fully  supports  the  conclusion  that
Congress  does  not  lack  appointive  power.
This  is  not  to
say  that  Congress  may  make  the  appointment  to  any  office
that  it creates.  We  assume  that  the power  is limited  to  the
situation  where  the  function  performed  by the  appointee
bears  a  significant  relationship  to  Congressional  responsi-
bilities.  Our  positon,  elaborated  in the  section  of the brief
that  follows,  is  that  Congress  has  a  special  and  pervasive
role in relation  to the electoral  process—a  constitutional  role
which  plainly  involves  the  performance  of  quasi-judicial
and  quasi-executive  tasks  as  well  as  the  conventional  legis-
At  this  juncture,  we  point  out  that,  quite
lative  function.
apart  from  its  role  in  the  electoral  process,  Congress  has
from  the  beginning  exercised  an  appointive  power  in  a
wide  variety  of situations  (in some,  with  less  obvious  justi-
fication  than  is  present  in  the  instant  case)  and  that  the

legitimacy  of  these  actions  has  been  unquestioned.

it

instances  are  so  numerous  that  we  confine  ourselves  to  il-
lustrative  examples.

Virtually  the  first  act  of the  House  of Representatives

was  to  appoint  a  Clerk  of the  House.”

In  1792,  the  Second  Congress,  a  body  largely  in-
fluenced  by men  who  were  among  the  Framers,  enacted  a
statute  (1 Stat.  282)  which  authorized  a  commission  con-
sisting  of  the  President  of  the  Senate,  the  Chief  Justice,
the  Secretary  of State,  the  Secretary  of  the  Treasury  and
the  Attorney  General  “to  purchase  the  debt  of the  United
States,  at its  market  price,  if not  exceeding  the  par  or  true
value  thereof...  .”

The  same  Congress  passed  a  Succession  Act  (1 Stat.
240)  providing  that  “in  case  of removal,  death,  resignation
or  inability  both  of the  President  and  Vice  President  of the
United  States,  the President  of the Senate  pro  tempore,  and
in  case  there  shall  be no  President  of the  Senate,  then  the
Speaker  of the House  of Representatives  for the  time  being
shall  act  as  President  of  the  United  States  until  the  dis-
ability  shall  be removed  or  a  President  shall  be  elected”—
thus  exercising  the  power  of  appointment  by designating
the  occupant  of an  office.”

Also  in  1792  the  House  of  Representatives  assumed
authority  to  create  and  staff  an  investigating  committee
with  power  to  “call  for  such  persons,  papers  and  records
as  may  be necessary  to assist  their  inquiries.”  *

Although  the  Constitution  contains  no  express  provision  au-
thorizing  Congress  to  make  investigations  and
exact  testimony—
much  less  to  appoint  investigators—a  broad  power  to  conduct  in-
vestigations  through  legislators  and  their  appointees  had  been
frequently  exercised  by  the  British  Parliament  and  the  colonial

Congress  has  designated  those  who  are  to  serve  as
members  of  various  quasi-public  associations  or  corpora-
tions,  e.g.,  the  Washington  National  Monument  Society
(11  Stat.  386),  the  National  Military  and  Naval  Asylum
(13 Stat.  509),  the  American  Printing  House  for the  Blind
(20  Stat.  467},  the  American  Historical  Association  (25
Stat.  640),  the  Carnegie  Institution  (33 Stat.  575)  and  the
American  National  Red  Cross  (33 Stat.  599).

In  various  contexts,  it  has  appointed  individuals  to
serve  in public employment  or  office,  e.g.,  Morse  to oversee
experiments  with  telegraph  (5 Stat.  618);  regents  of  the
Smithsonian  Institution  appointed  by Joint  Resolution  (a
practice  instituted  in  1846,  9  Stat.  115,  and  continued
thereafter ); managers  of  the  National  Asylum  appointed
by Joint  Resolution  (a practice  instituted  in  1867,  15  Stat.
1,  and  continued  thereafter);  Green  to  supervise  the
Library  of  Congress,  29  Stat.  470;  Colum  to  be  Commis-
sioner  of  the  Lincoln  Memorial,  37  Stat.  731;  Goethals  to
investigate  designated  claims,  38  Stat.  388;  the  President
to  send  a  named  person  to  Alaska  to  construct a territorial
In innumerable  instances  Congress
railroad,  38  Stat.  772.
has  directed  the  President  to  appoint  a  named  person  to  a
particular  military  rank.
See  Wood  v.  United  States,  107
U.  S. 414.

As  noted  above,  as  early  as  1792  Congress  appointed
a  commission  which  performed  an  administrative  or  quasi-
executive  task:  the  purchase  of outstanding  instruments  of
debt.
There  have  been  numerous  similar  instances  in
which  quasi-executive  or  quasi-judicial  duties,  as  well  as
rule-making  or  recommendatory  functions,  have  been  per-

formed  by a  committee,  commission  or  board  whose  mem-

Territorial  governor  and  judges  to  lay out  a  town  (2 Stat.

420).

It  is  also  notable  that  for  many  years  Congress  has
followed  the  practice  of sharing  with  the  President  the  re-
sponsibility  for  appointing  members  to  commissions  en-

gaged  in fact  gathering,  study,  research  and  the  prepara-

Ill.

Congress’  Fundamental  Constitutional  Role  in  the  Con-
duct  of the  Federal  Electoral  Process  Embraces,  in  Addi-
tion  to  Its  Legislative  Power,  the  Performance  of  Quasi-
Judicial  and  Quasi-Executive  Functions.

In this  section  of the  brief,  we  note  initially  the  vari-
ous  sources  of Congress’  constitutional  powers  in  relation
to  the  federal  electoral  process.  We  then  indicate  the
breadth  and  character  of its  legislative  authority—a  matter
presumably  not  in dispute  but one  which  sheds  light upon
the  extent  of  Congress’  auxiliary  powers  to  do  what  is
“necessary  and  proper.  We  examine,  next,  the  judicial
duties  with  which  it is charged.  Finally,  we  examine  the
administrative  or  quasi-executive  functions  that  are  appro-

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

21

priate  to  carrying  out  the  foregoing  and  how  Congress  has
traditionally  performed  those  functions  through  its  own
delegates,  with  particular  emphasis  upon  the  administra-
tion  of antecedent  corrupt  practices  and  federal  financing
legislation.

A.  In  addition  to  the  general  legislative  powers
vested  in it by the  Constitution  (Art.  I, § 1), Congress  has
enumerated  responsibilities  bearing  on  the  conduct  of fed-
eral  elections.

Article  I, Section  4, cl.  1, provides:

The  Times,  Places  and  Manner  of  holding
Elections  for  Senators  and  Representatives,  shall
be  prescribed  in  each  State  by  the  Legislature
thereof;  but  the  Congress  may  at  any  time  by
Law  make  or  alter  such  Regulations,  except  as
to  the  Places  of chusing  Senators.

Article  I, Section  5, declares  that  “Each  House  shall
be  the  Judge  of the  Elections,  Returns  and  Qualifications
of  its  own  Members...  .”  It  states  further  that  each
House  may  “punish  its  Members  for disorderly  Behaviour,
_, with the  Concurrence  of  two  thirds,  expel  a  Mem-

r.

So  far  as  Presidential  elections  are  concerned,  the
Twelfth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  provides  that  the
certificates  of  the  votes  of  the  electoral  college  shall  be
transmitted  sealed  to  the  seat  of government,  “directed  to
the President  of the  Senate,”  and  that the “President  of the
Senate  shall,  in  the  presence  of the  Senate  and  House  of
Representatives,  open  all  the  certificates  and  the  votes
shall  then  be  counted.”  (Analogous  provisions  were  orig-

22

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

inally within  Art.  II, § 1, cl. 3.)  It was  in execution  of this
responsibility  to  judge  the  bona  fides of returns  that  Con-
gress  devised  the  procedures  (discussed  infra)  utilized  in
adjudicating  the  disputed  Hayes-Tilden  contest  of  1876.”
Congress  additionally  has  the  authority  to  implement
by  legislation  the  provisions  of  the  Fourteenth  and
Fifteenth  Amendments  (an authority  not  implicated  in the
present  case)  and  the  powers  conferred  by the  “necessary
and  proper’  clause.

B.  Congress’  power  to  regulate  the  times,  places  and
manner  of holding  elections  has  been  broadly  interpreted
and  widely  implemented.  In Chief  Justice  Hughes’  words,
speaking  for  a  unanimous  Court  in  Smiley  v.  Holm,  285
U.  S. 355,  366,  “[i]t cannot  be doubted  that  these  compre-
hensive  words  embrace  authority  to  provide  a  complete
code  for  congressional  elections,  not  only  as  to  times  and
places,  but  in  relation  to  notices,  registration,  supervision
of  voting,  protection  of  voters,  prevention  of  fraud  and
corrupt  practices,  counting  of  votes,  duties  of  inspectors
and  canvassers,  and  making  and  publication  of  election
returns...  .*

24.  For  present
purposes,  we  shall  not  elaborate  upon  the
procedures  prescribed
by  the  Amendment  if  no  candidate  has  a
mana of  the  electoral  votes  and  the  choice  is  thrown  into  the

ouse.

25.  Throughout  most  of  our  history,  Congress  has  left  the
actual  conduct  of  federal  elections  in  the  hands  of  State  officials,
though  subject  to  federal  standards,  requirements  and  sanctions.
The  principal  exceptions  were  during  the  Reconstruction  Era.
Thus  the a  Reconstruction  Act  of  March  23,  1867,  15
Stat.  3,  provided  that  the  commanding  generals  of  the  military
districts  embracing  the  former  Confederate  States  should  assume
control  of registration  and  elections  in  their  areas.  More  broadly,
Congress  provided  in  1870  and  1871  that,  upon  citizens’  petition,
the  judges  of  the  United  States  Circuit  Courts  should  appoint
supervisors  to  oversee  registration  and  polling  in  connecti.n  with
any  election  at  which  representatives  or  delegates  to  Congress  are
to  be  chosen.  The  relevant  provisions  are  set  forth  in
Ex  parte
Siebold,  100  U.  S. 371,  which  upheld  the  exercise  of this  appointive

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

23

Subsequently,  in  United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.  S.
299,  318,  the  Court  ruled  that  Congress’  power  to  safe-
guard  the  integrity  of  elections  extends  to  primary  elec-
tions  where  “the  state  law  has  made  the  primary  an  in-
tegral  part  of  the  procedure  of  choice,  or  where  in  fact
the  primary  effectively  controls  the  choice  ..  .  .”

While  it is true  that Article  II, § 1, commits  the power
of appointment  of Presidential  electors  and  the  manner  of
their  appointment  to  the  States,  there  is no  question  of the
power  of Congress  “to  preserve  the  purity  of presidential
and  vice  presidential  elections”  as  well  as  of Congressional
elections.  Burroughs  v.  United  States,  290  U. S. 534, 544.*°
“To  say  that  Congress  is  without  power  to  pass  appropri-
ate  legislation  to  safeguard  such  an  election  from  the  im-
proper  use  of money  to  influence  the  result  is  to  deny  to
the  nation  in  a  vital  particular  the  power  of  self  protec-
tion.  Congress,  undoubtedly,  possesses  that  power,  as  it
possesses  every  other  power  essential  to  preserve  the  de-
partments  and  institutions  of the  general  government  from
impairment  or  destruction,  whether  threatened  by  force
or  by corruption.”  Id. at 545.”"

25.  (Cont'd. )

870-71  legislation  remained

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

25

Finally,  of course,  it is part  and  parcel  of the  legisla-
tive  power  to secure  such  information  and data  as  is neces-
sary  or  useful  in  the  performance  of  the  legislative  role—
a  point  we  discuss  more  fully  infra  in  connection  with  the
delegation  to  the  Election  Commission  of  information-
gathering  functions.

C.  The performance  of judicial  duties  by the Congress
is  necessitated  in  part  by the  direction  that  each  House
shall  pass  upon  the  elections,  returns  and  qualifications  of
its own  members  as  well  as  by the provision  governing  the
expulsion  of members.  Thus,  in Reed  v.  County  Commis-
sioners,  277  U. S. 376, in adverting  to the power  of a Senate
Committee  to  take  ballots  and  ballot  boxes  from  State  offi-
cials  in  order  to  investigate  the  “means  used  to  influence
the nomination  of candidates  for the  Senate”  (p. 386),  the
Court  reasoned  that  the  Senate,  as  “judge  of the  elections,
returns  and  qualifications  of  its  members”  is  “fully  em-
powered,  and  may  determine  such  matters  without  the  aid
of  the  House  of  Representatives  or  the  Executive  or
Judicial  Department”  (p.  388).  “By  means  of  its  own
process  or  that  of its  committee  .
.  [it has]  authority  to
take  such  steps  as  may  be  appropriate  and  necessary  to
secure  information  upon  which  to  decide  concerning  elec-
tions”  (ibid.).  The  Court  noted  further  that  “Congress
has passed  laws  calculated  to facilitate  such  investigations”
(ibid. ).

.

In a case  arising  at a later  stage  of the same  Senatorial
inquiry,  the Court  ruled  that exercise  of the Senate’s  powers
as  the judge of elections  “necessarily  involves  the ascertain-
ment  of facts,  the attendance  of witnesses,  the examination

27.  (Cont'd. )

opinions  of  the  monty”

district  courts  in  United  States  v.
anning,  215  F.  Supp. 272,  283-9  (W.  D.  La.,  1963)  and  United
hts  of  Ku  Klux  Klan,  250  F.  Supp.  330,
.

States  v.  Original
350-5  (E.  D.

La.,  1

965

of  such  witnesses,  with  the  power  to  compel  them  to
answer  pertinent  questions,  to  determine  the  facts  and
apply  the  appropriate  rules  of law,  and,  finally,  to  render
a  judgment...  .  .”  Barry  v.  United  States  ex  rel.  Cun-
ningham,  279  U.  S. 597,  613.*  See  also  In  re  Loney,  134
U.  S. 372;  In re  Chapman,  166  U.  S. 661.

Congress’  role  in  a  contested  Presidential  election  is
vividly  illustrated  by the  events  of  1876,  when  conflicting
electoral  returns  were  received  from  a  number  of  States.
In  lieu  of determining  the  contest  itself,  Congress  passed
a statute  creating  an  electoral  commission  to which  it dele-
gated  its  investigatory  and  adjudicatory  functions  (19
Stat.  227).  The  judgment  of  the  Commission  was  to  be
binding  unless  overridden  by both  Houses.”  The  mem-
bership  of the  Commission  was  prescribed  by the  statute
and  was  to  consist  of  five  Representatives  appointed  by
the  House,  five  Senators  appointed  by the  Senate  and  the
Associate  Justices  of  the  Supreme  Court  assigned  to  the
First,  Third,  Eighth,  and  Ninth  Circuits.  The  four Justices
were  then  to  appoint a fifth  Associate  Justice  to  complete
the Commission  of Fifteen.

D.  As  observed  above,  Congress  has  plenary  legisla-
tive  power  to  establish  regulations  governing  the  federal
It  also  has  the  responsibility  to  judge
electoral  process.
elections,  returns  and  qualifications—a  role  which  includes
the authority  to engage  in such  administration,  fact-gather-
ing and  investigation  as  may  be appropriate  to the making

analogous  to  the  Federal Rules of  Civil  Procedure,  as  to  the  pro-
cedures  to  be followed  in cases  of contested  elections.  The  current
rules  are  codified  in  2  U.  S.  C.  §§ 381-396.
Earlier  rules  were
codified  in 2  U.  S.  C.  § 201 et seq.  re  ,

Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

of its  judgments.  There  is no  reason,  however,  to  confine
these  auxiliary  powers  to  a  situation  in  which  the  dispute
has  crystallized  and  a  challenge  has  already  been  pre-
Thus,  the  responsibility  to  pass  on  qualifications
sented.
carries  with  it  the  authority  to  impose  and  administer
ongoing  requirements  calling  for  the  disclosure  and  pro-
duction  of such  information  as  may  be relevant  to  qualifi-
cation.  Such  requirements  are  but  prophylactic  measures
to  assure  compliance  with  the  law  and  to  minimize  the
areas  and  instances  of dispute.  The  same  is of course  true
of Congress’  responsibility  to  deal  with  corruption  or  with
fraud  upon  the  electoral  process.

It  is  true,  to  be  sure,  that  the  administration  of  on-
going  requirements  to report  and  disclose  information  that
will  reveal  compliance  or  failure  of  compliance  with  the
law  may  fairly  be characterized  as  a  quasi-executive  func-
tion.  The  point,  however,  is that  it is intimately  related  to
Congress’  ultimate  responsibilities—responsibilities  that  go
well  beyond a purely  legislative  role.

That  the  nature  of the  business  to  be conducted  may
justify,  if not  require,  some  blending  of  the  rule-making,
adjudicatory  and  administrative  functions  is hardly  a novel
concept  in a  society  which  has  long conducted  much  of its
vital  business  through  boards  and  commissions.
Cf.
Humphrey's  Executor  v.  United  States,  295  U.  S.  602.%

An  examination  of Corrupt  Practices  legislation  ante-
cedent  to  the  1974  legislation  at  issue  in  this  case  (to
which  we  now  turn)  shows  that  Congress  and  its  agents
have  long  exercised  functions  of  the  very  kind  and  de-
scription  that  have  now  been  delegated  to  the  Federal
Election  Commission.

sional  regulation  of federal  election  campaigns:  (1)  The

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

Commission  is given  broad  supervisory  authority  over  ad-
ministration  of the  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements
for  political  contributions  and  expenditures  in  federal
elections  contained  in  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
of  1971,  as  amended,  2 U.  S.  C.  §§ 431  et  seq.;  (2)  The
Commission  also plays an  important  role  in the administra-
tion  of the  provisions  for  public  financing  of  Presidential
primary  and  election  campaigns  contained  in  the  Presi-
dential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  and  the  Presidential
Primary  Matching  Payment  Account  Act,  26  U.  S.  C.
(3) The  Commission  has  a
§§ 9001  et  seq.,  9031  et  seq.;
limited  role  in the administration  of the limitations  on  con-
tributions  and  expenditures  contained  in  Chapter  29  of
the Criminal  Code,  18 U.  S. C.  §§ 608  et seq.”

Appellants  urge  that  the  delegation  of some  or  all  of
these  functions  to  a body  chosen  in part  by the  leadership
of the  Houses  of Congress  violates  the  constitutional  sepa-
ration  of powers.  They  suggest  that  only members  of the
Executive  Branch  of the  Government,  nominated  and  ap-
pointed  pursuant  to  Article  II,  Section  2 of the  Constitu-
tion,  may  exercise  the  functions  which  Congress  has  given
to  the  Election  Commission.
In  this  portion  of  the  brief
we  show  that,  quite  to  the  contrary  of  appellants’  argu-
ment,  Congress  has  in the  past  assigned  the  functions  now
given  to  the  Commission  not  to  Executive  Branch  officers
and  agencies,  but  to  officers  and  arms  of  the  Legislative
Branch.

32.  In  addition  to  these  three  main  areas  of  responsibility,  the
Commission  is  directed  to  be  a  general  clearinghouse  for  informa-
tion  relating  to  the  administration  of  elections  and  is  specifically
empowered  to  contract  for  studies  into  the  administration  of  elec-
tions  and  to  publish  such  studies.
In  view
of the  extensive  and  heretofore  unchallenged
practice,  summarized
supra,  of congressional  appointment  of  federal  study  commissions,
we  assume  that  no  substantial  constitutional  question  is  presented
with  respect  to  this  activity.

2 U.  S.  C.  §438(b).

Brief  of the  Federal  Election  Commission

29

1.  The  earliest  examples  of  congressional  statutory
regulation  of the  federal  election  process  did  not,  for  the
most  part,  involve  separate  administrative  or  enforcement
machinery.  The  Enforcement  Act  of  1870,  16  Stat.  144,
the  constitutionality  of  which  was  upheld  in  Ex  parte
Siebold,  supra,  100  U.  S. 371,  made  criminal  certain  desig-
nated  election  practices,  including  voting  fraud  and  other
interference  with  the  free  exercise  of the  franchise.  The
Tillman  Act  of  1907,  34  Stat.  814,  was  the  first  modern
exercise  of  congressional  power  to  regulate  contributions
in election  campaigns.  Banks  and  corporations  were  pro-
hibited  from  making  any  monetary  political  contributions
in  federal  election  campaigns.
Criminal  sanctions  were
utilized  and  no  separate  administrative  or  enforcement
mechanism  was  established.  The  Tillman  Act  was  broad-
ened  and  incorporated  into  subsequent  legislation.
See
35  Stat.  1088  (1909),  57  Stat.  163  (1943)  and  the  Taft-
Hartley  Act,  61  Stat.  136,  159  (contributions  by organized
labor),  and  the Corrupt  Practices  Act  of 1925,  infra.

2.  When  Congress  in  the  Corrupt  Practices  Act  of
1910,  36 Stat.  822,  first  attempted  a  comprehensive  regula-
tion  of campaign  contributions  and  expenditures  in federal
elections,  it  utilized  a  legislative  officer—the  Clerk  of the
House  of  Representatives—to  administer  the  regulatory
system.  The  duties  of  the  House  Clerk  under  the  1910
Act  were  similar  to  those  duties  of  the  present  Election
Commission  relating  to  the  reporting  and  disclosure  re-
quirements  of 2 U.  S.  C.  §§ 431  et  seq.  Under  the  1910
Act,  political  committees  were  required  to  keep  records  of
all  contributions  and  expenditures  and  to  file  the  records
with  the  Clerk  of the  House  after  the election.  The  Clerk
33.  As  noted  above  (p.  22,  note),  the  1870  legislation  did
provide  that  in  certain  circumstances  federal  judges  might  ap-
of E
rt
en

int  election  supervisors.

See,  also,  the  discussion

Siebold,  infra,  p.  68.

30

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

was  to  make  the  filings  available  to  the  public.  Certain
campaign  contributors  were  also  required  to  file  reports
with  the  Clerk.  Criminal  penalties  were  provided  for im-
proper  filing.

One  year  after  its  initial  passage,  the  1910  Act  was
broadened  to  include  primary  elections  in  its  coverage,  to
require  the  filing of records  of contributions  by candidates
as  well  as  by political  committees,  and  to  impose  spending
limitations  on  both  House  and  Senate  election  campaigns.
37  Stat.  25  (1911).  The  date  for  filing  required  records
of contributions  and  expenditures  was  advanced  so  as  to
require  receipt  before  the  relevant  election,  and  a  second
legislative  officer—the  Secretary  of the Senate—was  brought
into  the  process  of  administering  the  reporting  and  dis-
Aspects  of  the  1910  Act,  as
closure  requirements.
amended,  were  upheld  over  constitutional  objections  in
United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.  S. 299.

3.  The  Corrupt  Practices  Act  of  1925,  43  Stat.  1070,
elaborated  the  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  of
the  1910  Act,  which  it  replaced,  re-imposed  expenditure
limitations  in House  and  Senate  campaigns,  and re-enacted
the  Tillman  Act  prohibitions  on  contributions  by certain
Again,  the  Clerk  of the  House  and  the
types  of entities.
Secretary  of  the  Senate  administered  the  system  of  re-
quired  filings  of reports  of contributions  and  expenditures.
The  number  of required  filing  dates  for  political  commit-
tees  was  increased  to  five  pre-election  filings  as  well  as  a
post-election  summary.  Candidates  were  required  to  file
records  of contributions  and  expenditures  with  the  appro-
priate  legislative  officer  once  before  and  once  after  each
election,  and  certain  contributors  were  also  made  subject
to  these  filing  and  disclosure  requirements.
In  Nader  v.
Kleindienst,  375  F.  Supp.  1138  (D. D.  C.  1973),  aff'd,  497
F. 2d 676  (D. C. Cir.  1974),  the  court  held  that  the  1925

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

31

Act  imposed  no  affirmative  duty on  the Clerk  of the House
or  the  Secretary  of the  Senate  to refer  violations  of the  Act
to  the  Attorney  General  for  criminal  prosecution,  but  the
court  assumed  that  the Clerk  and  Secretary  had  the power
to  make  such  referrals.
See,  also,  United  States  v.  Foote,
42  F.  Supp.  717  (D.  Del.  1942),  upholding  the  constitu-
tionality  of the  1925  Act.

4.  Federal  financing  of  Presidential  election  cam-
paigns  was  first  provided  for  in  the  Presidential  Election
Campaign  Fund  Act  of  1966,  80  Stat.  1957,  which  estab-
lished  a  fund  to  receive  the  proceeds  of a  one-dollar  fed-
eral  income  tax  checkoff  system.
Disbursements  were  to
be  made  from  this  fund  to  political  parties  by the  Secre-
However,  Congress  vested  signifi-
tary  of  the  Treasury.
cant  adininistrative  and  enforcement  powers  with  respect
to  the  federal  financing  system—functions  similar  to  those
vested  in the  Election  Commission  under  the  1974  Act—in
non-Executive  Branch  officers.  The  Comptroller  General
was  given  the  function  (exercised  by  the  Commission
under  the  1974  Act)  of  certifying  to  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury  what  amounts  were  due  to  be  paid  from  the
fund  and  to  whom.  The  Comptroller  General  was  also
explicitly  given  rule-making  and  investigatory  powers  in

public  financing  under  the  1966  Act  was  delayed  by Con-

gress  pending  its  adoption  of  legislative  guidelines.

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

Brief  of the Federal  Election  Commission

33

Stat.  57.  Such  guidelines  were  not  enacted  until  passage
of the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  of  1971,
infra,  which  replaced  most  of the  1966  Act.

5.  The  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  of
1971,  85  Stat.  560,  substituted  for  the  1966  Act  a  more
comprehensive  system  for the public  financing  of Presiden-
tial  election  campaigns.
As  in  the  1966  Act,  the  Comp-
troller  General  was  responsible  for  certification  of entitle-
ment  to payments.**  He  was  to be assisted  by an  Advisory
Board  similar  to  the  one  created  under  the  1966  Act,
except  that  the  membership  of  this  Board  was  expanded
to  include,  in  addition  to  nominees  of the  political  parties
and  their  nominees,  the  two  major  party  leaders  in  each
House  of  Congress  (as ex  officio  members  of  the  Board).
The  1971  Campaign  Fund  Act  required  at  least  five  pre-
election  reports  to  be  filed  with  the  Comptroller  General;
it gave  the  Comptroller  General  general  investigatory  and
record  keeping  powers  (§ 9009),  as  well  as  the specific  duty
to  audit  and  examine  campaign  expenses  (§ 9007);  and  it
provided  criminal  penalties  for violating  reporting  require-
ments  or  exceeding  specified  expenditure  and  contribu-
tion  limitations  (4 9012).  The  Comptroller  General  was
given  broad  rulemaking  authority  to  implement  the  Act
(§ 9009)  and regulations  were  issued  by him, see  11 C. F. R.
1.1  et  seq.  (1974).  In  addition,  § 9010  of the  1971  Cam-
paign  Fund  Act  gave  the  Comptroller  General  the  inde-
pendent  power  to  institute  suit  seeking  injunctive  relief
to  enforce  the  Act,  the  power  to  sue  to  recover  overpay-
ments,  and  the  power  to  defend  his  actions  on  appeal.
The  Comptroller  General's  duties  under  the  1971  Cam-
paign  Fund  Act  were  thus  essentially  similar  to  the  Elec-

34.  Payments  were  never  made  under  the  1971  Campaign
because  the  Act  was  not  made  applicable  to  the  1972
Fund  Act
Presidential  election  and  it has  since  been  superseded  by the  1974

tion  Commission’s  duties  under  the  public  financing  pro-
visions  of  the  1974  Act,  and  these  duties  were,  indeed,
expressly  transferred  to  the  Commission  by the  1974  Act,
§ 404  of  the  Act,  88  Stat.  1292-93  (to  be  codified  at  26
U.  S. C.  § 9001  et seq.).

6.  A second  1971  statute,  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  of  1971,  86  Stat.  3, dealt,  not  with  public  fund-
ing  of  Presidential  campaigns,  but  with  the  subjects  cov-
ered  by earlier  corrupt  practices  legislation.  Superseding
the  Corrupt  Practices  Act  of  1925,  it added  to  prior  cor-
rupt  practices  legislation  in three  main  ways:  It established
limits  on  broadcast  media  spending  by  candidates;  it
amended  the Criminal  Code  (18 U. S. C. § 608)  to provide
for  limitations  on  the  amounts  that  candidates  for  federal
office  could  spend  from  personal  family  resources;  and  it
broadened  the  disclosure  requirements  for  campaign-re-
Primary  responsi-
lated  contributions  and  expenditures.
bility  for  administration  and  enforcement  of the  1971  Act
was  placed  in three  “supervisory”  officers—the  Clerk  of the
House  of  Representatives  (for  matters  related  to  House
campaigns),  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  (for  matters  re-
lated  to  Senate  campaigns)  and  the  Comptroller-General
Duties  of
(for  matters  related  to  Presidential  elections).
these  supervisory  officers  included  comprehensive  super-
vision  of  the  reporting  and  filing  requirements;  auditing
and  field  investigations;  general  rulemaking  authority  in
the  realm  of required  reports;  receiving  and  investigating
complaints  and  recommending  to  the  Attorney  General  in
appropriate  cases  the  institution  of civil  (§ 308(d)(1))  or
criminal  (§ 311) proceedings.  In addition,  the Comptroller
General  was  given  authority  to  make  interpretative  rules
relating  to  the  media  spending  limitations  and  was  desig-
nated  to  act  as  a  national  clearinghouse  for  electoral  in-
formation.
The  1974  Act  which  is  at  issue  in  this  case

ee -

34

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

explicitly  transferred  the  duties  of the  supervisory  officers
under  the  1971  Act  to  the  Federal  Election  Commission,
§ 208(c)  of  the  Act,  88  Stat.  1286  (to  be  codified  at  2
U.  S. C.  431  et  seq.).

To  summarize  the  foregoing  history:  In  statutes  prior
to  the  1974  FECA  Amendments,  Congress  repeatedly  del-
egated  administrative  and  enforcement  responsibilities  in
connection  with  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements
for  election  expenditures  and  contributions,  in  connection
with  public  funding  of  Presidential  election  campaigns,
and  in  connection  with  limitations  on  permissible  elec-
tion  expenditures  and  contributions.
Except  for  the  au-
thority  to  institute  criminal  prosecutions,  which  has  always
resided  in  members  of  the  Executive  Branch  (and  which
continues  so  to  reside  under  the  1974  Act),  these  delega-
tions  vested  responsibilities,  not  in  executive  officers  and
agencies,  but  in  legislative  officers  or  agencies.  Functions
similar  to  those  presently  exercised  by the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Commission  have  previously  been  performed  by the
Secretary  of the  Senate,  the  Clerk  of the  House  of Repre-
sentatives,  advisory  boards  selected  outside  of  the  provi-
sions  of  Article  II,  §2  of  the  Constitution,  and  by  the
Comptroller  General.

The  Comptroller  General  and  the  General  Accounting
Office,  we  emphasize,  are  “a part  of the  legislative  branch
of the  Government.”  Reorganization  Act  of  1949,  § 7, 63
Stat.  205.  Appointed  for a fifteen  year  term  (31  U.  S.  C.
§ 43),  the  Comptroller  is  “independent  of  the  executive
departments”  (31 U.S.  C.  § 41)  and  may  be removed  only
by  joint  resolution  of  Congress  or  by  impeachment  (31
U.S.C.  § 43).  Congress  may  act  through  him  as  it would
through  its own  committees.  United  States  v.  Stewart,  234
F. Supp.  94, 99  (D. D.  C.),  affd,  339  F. 2d 753  (D. C.  Cir.

35.  See,  also,  31  U.  S.  C.  65(d)  (noting  that  the  Comptroller
performs  his  auditing  functions  “as  an  agent  of  the  Congress”).

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

35

1964).  See  also,  Schwartz  and  Jacoby,  “Litigation  With
The  Federal  Government”  (1970),  p.  161;  Mansfield,  “The
Comptroller  General”  (1939),  pp.  247-8.

Throughout  the lengthy  history  of close  Congressional
regulation  of  the  financial  aspects  of  federal  election
campaigns  no  one,  it  appears,  has  previously  seen  fit  to
challenge,  as  a violation  of the Constitutional  separation  of
powers,  the  delegation  of administrative  and  non-criminal
enforcement  responsibilities  in  this  area  to  legislative  offi-
cers  and  agencies.  A  number  of  cases  have  considered
constitutional  attacks  on  aspects  of the prior legislation,  but
in none  is there  a suggestion  of any impropriety  by virtue  of
the delegation  of authority  to legislative  officials.  See  Bur-
roughs  v.  United  States,  290  U.  S. 534  (upholding  require-
ment  of reporting  contributions  to the Clerk  of the House);
Rumely  v.  United  States,  197  F.  2d  166,  187  (D.  C.  Cir.
1952),  aff'd, 345  U. S. 41  (“no one  would  seriously  contend
that  the  requirements  for disclosure  under  the  [1925]  Cor-
rupt  Practices  Act  are  offensive  to  the  Constitution”);
United  States  v.  Harris,  347  U .S. 612  (upholding  the  Fed-
eral  Regulation  of  Lobbying  Act,  2  U.  S.  C.  §§ 261-270,
under  which  the  defendant  was  charged  with  failure  to
report  lobbying  expenditures  to  the  Clerk  of the  House);
Pichler  v.  Jennings,  347  F.  Supp.  1061  (S. D.  N.  Y.  1972)
(challenge  to  provisions  of  1971  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  requiring  disclosure  of  contributors);  United
States  v.  National  Committee  for Impeachment,  469  F.  2d
1135  (2d Cir.  1972)  (challenge  to  reporting  requirements
of  1971  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act);  ACLU  v.  Jen-
nings,  366 F. Supp.  1041  (D. D. C.  1973),  appeal  dismissed
as  moot,  95  S.  Ct.  2646  (1975)  (same);  Nader  v.  Klein-
dienst,  375  F.  Supp.  1138  (D. D.  C.  1973),  aff'd, 497  F. 2d
676  (D. C.  Cir.  1974)  (power  of House  Clerk  and  Senate
Secretary  to  refer  violations  of  Corrupt  Practices  Act  for
prosecution ); United  States  v.  Finance  Committee  to  Re-

36

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

Elect  the  President,  507  F.  2d  1194  (D.  C.  Cir.  1974)
(affirming  conviction  for failure  to report  under  1971  Fed-
eral  Election  Campaign  Act).

IV.
The  Specific  Functions  Entrusted  to  the  F ederal  Election
Commission  Are  Duties  Which  Can  Constitutionally  Be
Delegated  to Officers  Serving  as  an  Arm  of the Legislature
and  Appointed  in  Part  by the  Congress.

As  noted  above,  the  functions  delegated  by Congress
to  the  Federal  Election  Commission  are  of three  principal
types:  (1)  supervision  of the  reporting  and  disclosure  re-
quirements  relating  to  contributions  and  expenditures  in
federal  elections;  (2)  duties  in  connection  with  federal
financing  of Presidential  primaries  and election  campaigns;
and  (3)  duties  in  connection  with  limits  imposed  on  per-
missible  contributions  and expenditures  in federal  elections.
We  here  describe  these  functions  in some  detail  and  show
how,  as  to  each  type  of function,  the  duties  delegated  are
appropriately  vested  in  a Commission  serving  as  an  arm  of
After  describing  the  duties  of  the  Com-
the  legislature.
mission  in  the  context  of  the  Congressional  scheme,  we
deal  further,  and  more  specifically,  with  various  objections
raised  by appellants  with  respect  to  the  delegation  of par-
ticular  powers  to  the  agency.

If the  FEC  were  an  agency  performing  a  dominantly
executive  role,  we  assume  that  its  full  membership  would
be required  to be appointed  under  the provisions  of Article
II.  The  fundamental  point  of this  aspect  of  the  current
litigation,  however,  is that  the  Commission  serves  not  ina
dominantly  executive  capacity,  but as  an  immediate  arm  of
It  performs  functions  which  the  Congress
the  Congress.
could  perform  itself  or  through  its  legislative  committees
or  staff.  These  functions  are  directly  related  to  and  in aid

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

37

of continuing  congressional  responsibility  over  the  federal
electoral  process.
In  these  circumstances,  Congress  may
we  submit,  select  the individuals  who perform  these  dele.
gated tasks,  just as  it may  select  committee  chairmen,  com-
mittee  staff  personnel,  the  House  Clerk  and  the  Senate
Secretary.  The  Executive  has  no  constitutional  preroga-
tives  in  the selection  of personnel,  such  as  the  members  of
the F ederal  Election  Commission,  whose  primary  task  is
to  aid  Congress  in  the  performance  of its  responsibilities.

A.  Duties  in  Connection  With  Reporting  and  Dis-
closure  of Contributions  and  Expenditures.
One of the Commission’s  primary  roles  under  the  1974
Act  is  to  act  as  Congress’  delegate  in  supervising  the
gathering  of extensive  information  relating  to the financing
of federal election  campaigns.  Under  the  Act,  the  Com-
mission  is  designated  as  the  recipient,  on  behalf  of  Con-
gress, of  a  variety  of  statements  and  reports  relating  to
contributions  and  expenditures  in  federal  election  cam-
paigns.  (In the case  of reports  and  statements  required  to
be filed by candidates  for the House  and  Senate,  the filings
are  initially  to  be  made  with  the  Clerk  of  the  House  of
Representatives  and  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate,  respec-
tively,  but  these  officers  receive  such  filings  as  “custodian”
for  the  Commission.  See  2 U.  S.  C.  §438(d).)  The  in-
formation  thus  required  to  be  filed  with  the  Commission
includes  statements  of organization  of political  committees
(2 U. S.C.  § 433);  reports  of receipts  and  expenditures  by
political  committees  and  candidates  (§ 434);  reports  from
certain  contributors  to  political  campaigns  (§ 434(e);
§ 437(a)  ); and statements  relating  to  the financing  of  and
expenditures  in  connection  with,  Presidential  nominating
conventions  (§ 437).

The  Commission’s  affirmative  duties  in  connection
with  reports  of  election  contributions  and  expenditures

38

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

are  closely related  to its function  as  the gatherer of election
financing  information  sought  by Congress.  With  regard
to  the  reporting  and  filing  process  itself,  the  Commission
is to  develop  and  furnish  the  forms  on  which  reports  and
statements  are  to  be made  (§ 438(a)(1);  § 434);  to  make
rules  for the  reporting  of unusual  items  such  as  petty  cash
disbursements  ( § 437b(b)  ) and debts  and promises  to con-
tribute  (§ 436(c)  ); to prepare  a manual  setting out uniform
methods  of bookkeeping  and  reporting  to  be  used  in  the
filing  process  (§ 438(a)(2));  to  develop  indexing  systems
for the filed material  ( § 438(a) (3) (6) ); to preserve  reports
and  statements  filed  with  it and  to  make  them  available  to
the public  (§ 438(a)(4)(5));  and  to  designate  the period
of time  for which  persons  must  preserve  copies  of filed  re-
ports  and  statements  (§ 436).  In  order  to  insure  the  in-
tegrity  of the information-gathering  system  the Commission
is required  to  perform  audits  and  field  investigations  with
respect  to filed  reports  and possible  failures  to file required
statements  or  reports  (§ 438(a)(8)).  It must  also  deter-
mine  the  periods  of time  during  which  receipted  bills  and
accounts  must  be preserved  (§ 432(d));  and  it is to  make
rules  for  the  establishment  of  campaign  depositories  by
Presidential  candidates  (§ 437b(c)  ).  To  provide  needed,
although  limited,  flexibility  in  the  reporting  system,  the
Commission  is  empowered  to  waive  certain  filing  dates
and  requirements  (§§ 434(a)  (3);  436(b)),  and  it  may
render  advisory  opinions  with respect  to the meaning  of the
filing  requirements  as  applied  to  specific  transactions  or
activities  so  as  to  protect  persons  who,  in  good  faith,  at-
tempt  to comply with  those  requirements ( § 437E£).

In  addition  to  its  auditing  functions,  the  Commission
has  a further  role  in seeking  compliance  with  the  informa-
tion-gathering  system  that  Congress  has  established.  The
Commission  is  to  receive  complaints  of  violations  of  the
filing  and  reporting  requirements  and  is either  to  investi-

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

39

gate  these  itself  (through  hearings  or  otherwise)  or  refer
apparent  violations  directly  to  the  Attorney  General
(§ 437g).  If the Commission  investigates  an  alleged  viola-
tion  and  determines  that  applicable  reporting  require-
ments  have  not  been  or  are  not  being  complied  with,  it
may  attempt  to  correct  the  default  through  conciliation
(§ 437g(a)(5)).  If  these  methods  fail,  the  Commission
may  institute  a  civil  action  for injunctive  or  other  relief  to
correct  such  violations  (§ 437g(a)(5)).  Alternatively,  it
may ask the Attorney  General  to institute  such  a civil  action
(§ 437g(a)(7)),  and the Commission  is further  directed  to
refer  apparent  violations  of criminal  provisions  to  the  ap-
propriate  law enforcement  authorities  (§ 437g(a)(6)).  As
an  “additional  enforcement  authority”  the  1974  Act  pro-
vides  that  a  Commission  determination,  after  notice  and
hearing,  that  a candidate  for federal  office  has  failed  to  file
a  required  report,  has  the  effect  of disqualifying  the  can-
didate  for  election  to  federal  office  for  a  limited  period.
As  with  all  other  Commission  determinations  of violation
of  the  filing  requirements,  such  a  Commission  finding  is
subject  to  judicial  review  (§ 456).

Although  the  Commission  is given  the  general  power
to  formulate  rules  for  the  proper  effectuation  of  the  re-
porting  and  disclosure  requirements  of  the  1974  Act
(§§ 438d; 437(a)(8)(9)  ), such rules  cannot  be finally pro-
mulgated  by the  Commission  itself.
In  recognition  of the
Commission’s  role  as  the agent  of Congress,  proposed  rules
formulated  by the  Commission  must  be transmitted  to  the
Congress  and  may  take  effect  only  if not  disapproved  by
either  House  within  thirty  legislative  days  (§ 438).  The
Commission  is also  obliged  to make  annual  reports  to  each
House  of Congress  and  to  the  President  concerning  its  ac-
tivities  in  carrying  out  its  duties,  together  with  recom-
mendations  for  legislative  or  other  action  it deems  appro-
priate.

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

41

As  shown  in  Point  E of the  preceding  section  of  this
brief,  there  is direct  historical  precedent  for  vesting  virtu-
ally  all  of  the  above-described  duties  relating  to  the  in-
formation-gzthering  process  in  officers  or  arms  of  the
Congress  rather  than  in  executive  or  quasi-executive  offi-
cials  chosen  under  the  appointment  provision  of  Article
Thus,  the  Clerk  of the
II,  Section  2 of the  Constitution.
House  and  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  have  been  the
designated  recipients  and  custodians  of  reports  relating
to  election  financing  and  expenditures  since  the  Corrupt
Practices  Act  of  1910.  The  power  to  refer  violations  of
reporting  requirements  for  prosecution  was  apparently  as-
sumed  to  rest  in the Clerk  and  the  Secretary,  at least  since
the  1925  Corrupt  Practices  Act.  See  Nader  v.  Kleindienst,
supra.  Under  the  1971  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
the  designated  “supervisory  officers”  were  the  Clerk,  the
Secretary,  and  the Comptroller  General.  These  legislative
officers  had,  in  addition  to  their  functions  under  prior
legislation,  the  power  to  make  audits  and  field  investiga-
tions,  to receive  and  investigate  complaints,  to  recommend
the institution  of civil or  criminal  proceedings,  and to make
rules  relating  to  the  required  reports  and  filings.  While
the  supervisory  officers  did  not  themselves  have  the  power
under  the  1971  Campaign  Act  to  institute  civil  actions  to
obtain  compliance  with  the  Act,  such  power  was  vested  in
the Comptroller  General  under  the  1971  Presidential  Elec-
tion  Campaign  Fund  Act,  which  also  contained  reporting
requirements  regarding  expenditures  and  contributions.

Aside  from  the  historical  precedents,  there  is  ample
basis  to  conclude  that  Comgress  may  appropriately  vest
information-gathering  duties  relating  to  the  financing  of  _
Such  information
federal  elections  in  its  own  delegates.
is  directly  related  to  two  constitutional  functions  of  Con-
gress—that  of formulating  legislation  governing  the federal
election  process  and that of judging the elections  and quali-

fications  of  its  own  members  and  of  supervising  the
legitimacy  of the  returns  in  Presidential  elections.  Under
Article  I,  Section  8  of  the  Constitution,  Congress  may
enact  “all  Laws  which  shall  be  necessary  and  proper  for
carrying  into  Execution”  these  functions.  The  gathering
of comprehensive  and  accurate  information  about  election
financing  is  quite  obviously  both  relevant  and  necessary
to  the  continuing  legislative  oversight  which  Congress,
through  repeated  corrupt  practices  and  campaign  funding
legislation,  has exercised  over  the federal  electoral  process.
The  legislative  process  can  hardly  be  properly  informed
without  the availability  of such  information,  and  Congress
plainly  has  the power  to gather  the required  data,  if neces-
sary  by compulsory  process.
Sinclair  v.  United  States,  279
U.  S. 263;  Hutcheson  v.  United  States,  369  U.  S. 599.  With
regard  to  Congress’  adjudicatory  role  over  election  returns
and  the  qualifications  of  its  members,  the  information
gathered  by the  Election  Commission  serves  both  to  deter
fraudulent  practices  which  would  be a basis  for disqualifi-
cation  and  to provide  a  basis  for disqualification  itself  (or
for expulsion ) when  the filed information  shows a violation
of laws  regulating  financial  campaign  practices.  As  to the
undoubted  power  of Congress  to gather information—inde-
pendently  of the  Executive—for  its  adjudicative  purposes
see  Reed  v.  County  Commissioners,  supra,  277  U.  S. at
388,  recognizing  the  Senate’s  authority,  “by  means  of  its
own  process  or  that of its committee  .
.  .to  take  such  steps
as  may  be appropriate  and  necessary  to secure  information
upon  which  to  decide  concerning  elections.”
See  also
Barry  v.  United  States  ex  rel.  Cunningham,  supra;  In  re
Loney,  supra;  In re  Chapman,  supra.

An  additional  practical  consideration  of great  weight
supports  Congress’  partial  use  of its  own  delegatzs,  rather
than  exclusive  reliance  upon  Presidential  nominees,  to

tn Es 6 Ee le es rene

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

42
gather  information  relating  to  its  legislative  and  adjudica-
tive  roles  in the federal  electoral  process.  As recent  events
have  shown,  the  President,  both  as  head  of his  party  and
as  candidate  for  his  own  re-election,  not  only  has  a  dis-
tinctly  partisan  interest  in  election  financing  matters,  but
may  even  be  implicated,  either  personally  or  through  his
own  campaign  staff,  in  actual  wrongdoing  or  in  suppres-
sion  of  information  about  federal  campaign  financing
practices.  The  1974  Act  was  passed  by Congress  in the
shadow  of  these  events.  To  say  that  Congress,  in  these
circumstances,  must  rely  exclusively  upon  Presidential
nominees  to  secure  information  about  federal  election
financing  which  it  requires  to  perform  its  constitutional
responsibilities  would  impair,  rather  than  vindicate,  the
The
constitutional  principle  of  separation  of  powers.
Election  Commission,  a  bipartisan  body  nominated  in
equal parts  by the President  and  the  leadership  of the  two
Houses  of  Congress,  appears  a  plainly  appropriate  re-
sponse  to  the  distinct  danger  that  partisan  political  bias
may  affect  the  accuracy  and  completeness  of the  informa-
tion  which  Congress  requires  in  order  to  perform  its  con-
stitutional  duties.”

36.  To  hold,  as  we  urge  the  Court,  that  Congress  has  properly
functions  to  a  Commission
delegated  broad  information-gathering
by the  President,  would  not
selected  in  part  by itself  and  in  part
of  course  foreclose  all  future  separation-of-powers  challenges  to
all  aspects  of  the  Commission’s  information-gathering  role  under
the  1974  Act.  While  it  seems  clear  that  at  least  the  vast  majority
duties  are  properl
of  the  Commission's  information-gathering
vested  in  an  arm  of the  legislature,  particular  functions,  as  anal
cally  applied  in  concrete  situations,  may  conceivably  be  so  domi-
nantly  executive  or  judicial  in  nature  as  to  raise  more  substantial
questions.  Such  2  contention  mi  ht,  for  example,  be  raised  as  to
a  future  exercise  of the  power  of the  Commission,  under  2 U.  S. C.
§ 456,  to  make  determinations  (subject,  however,  to  judicial  re-

candidates  from  seeking  federal  office  for  limited  periods  of  time.
A  challenge  to  this  Commission  function  is  obviously  not  ripe  at
the  present  time.  Should  such  a  challenge  to  a  discrete  Commis-

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

43

B.  Duties  in  Connection  With  Federal  Financing  of
Presidential  Elections  and  Primary  Campaigns.
The  Federal  Election  Commission  is  the  principal
administrator  of  the  system  for  federal  funding  of  Presi-
dential  election  and  primary  campaigns  contained  in
Chapters  95  and  96  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code,  26
U.  S. C.  §§ 9001  et seq.  and  9031  et  seq.  (the  Presidential
Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  and  the  Presidential
In  general,
Primary  Matching  Payment  Account  Act).
the  Commission’s  duties  under  this  legislation  are  (1)  to
certify  that  only those  who  comply  with  the  statutory  con-
ditions  for  eligibility  receive  funds  in  the  statutorily  pre-
scribed  amounts,  and  (2)  to  see  that  overpayments  or
improperly  spent  funds  are  returned  to the Treasury.  This
is precisely  the kind  of function  that  the Comptroller  Gen-
eral,  a  legislative  officer,  normally  performs  where  federal
disbursements  are  involved.  We  submit  that,  just  as  with
the  Commission's  information-gathering  functions  relating
to  the  financing  of federal  election  campaigns,  the  task  of
monitoring  disbursements  authorized  by Congress  is  one
which  may  constitutionally  be  delegated  to  a  body  some
of whose  members  are  appointed  by Congress  rather  than
by the President.  This  is especially  so,  we  believe,  because
the  disbursements  here  are  to  be  used  to  finance  Presi-
dential  election  and  primary  campaigns.
In  view  of  an
incumbent  President's  potentially  partisan  role  in  such
campaigns  (whether  or  not  he  is  himself  a  candidate  for
re-election  ), Congress,  we  believe,  did  not  act  inappropri-
ately  in  reserving  to  itself  the  power  to  select  (on  a  bi-
partisan  basis)  a  portion  of the  body  charged  with  insur-
ing that the federal  funds  are  properly  allocated  and  spent.

36.  (Cont'd. )

power  ever  prove  successful  in  a  justicabl

sion
wal, of  course,  be  to  delete  the  seslieules feamenar | BB  “ot
wer  from the  Commission's  duties,  rather  than  to  void  the  entire
delegation  of preponderantly  valid  functions.
Note,  in  this  connec-
tion,  the explicit  separability  provision  contained  in 2 U. S. C.  § 454.

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

45

eee. ee

A principal  function  of the  FEC  under  the  Presiden-
tial  election  financing  provisions  is  the  duty  of certifying
In
candidates  as  eligible  for  receipt  of  federal  funds.
order  for a Presidential  candidate  to be eligible  to  receive
such  funds,  he must  agree  in writing  to  furnish  the  Com-
mission  with  evidence  of his  campaign  expenses,  to  keep
and  furnish  records,  to  submit  to  Commission  audit,  and
26
to  make  any  required  repayments  where  necessary.
U.  S. C.  § 9003(a).  Presidential  candidates  seeking  fed-
eral  financing  must  also  certify  to  the  Commission  that
they  will  not  incur  campaign  expenses  in  excess  of  the
amount  of federal  funding  received  and  that  they  will  not
accept  private  financing.  The  Commission  is to  designate
the  time  when  such  certification  must  be  made  (§ 9003
(b)(c)).  If candidates  submit  the  required  agreements
and  certifications,  the  Commission  is,  in  turn,  to  certify
their  eligibility  for  payment  to  the  Secretary  of Treasury,
who  is  charged  with  the  actual  disbursement  of  the  au-
thorized  funds  (§§ 9005,  9006(c)).  The  Commission  has
no  role  in  determining  the  amounts  paid  to  eligibile  can-
didates,  such  amounts  being  fixed  by  statute  (§ 9004),
The  1974  legislation  also  provides  for  federal  financ-
ing  of Presidential  nominating  conventions  and  of  Presi-
dential  primary  campaigns.  The  Commission’s  certifica-
tion  duties  with  respect  to  these  payments  are  similar  to
its  duties  relating  to  Presidential  campaigns  (§§ 9008(g),
9033,  9036,  9037).

Once  payments  of  federal  funds  are  made  pursuant
to  the  above  provisions,  the  Commission  has  the  respon-
sibility  of auditing  the expenses  of Presidential  candidates
and others  who  receive  these  funds  (§§ 9007(a),  9008(g),
9009(b),  9038(a),  9039(h)),  and  may,  to  this  end,  re-
quire  the  keeping  of  appropriate  records  (§§ 9009(b),
9039(b)).  If the  Commission  determines  that  any  Presi-
dential  candidate  received  or  spent  more  than  was  allow-

able  under  the  applicable  statutes,  it is required  to  notify
the recipient,  who is under  a duty to repay  the  improperly
received  or  expended  amounts  to  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury  (§§ 9007(b),  9008(h),  9038(b)).  If repayment
is not  made,  the  Commission  may  institute  suit  to  recover
the  amounts  due  the  Secretary  (§§9010(b),  9008(h),
9040(b))  and  it  may  generally  seek  injunctive  relief  to
compel  compliance  with  the  pertinent  statutory  require-
ments  (§§9011,  9040(c)).
Criminal  sanctions  are  im-
posed  for  violations  of  the  federal  financing  statutes
(§§ 9012,  9042),  but  the  Commission  has  no  direct  role
in respect  to  the  enforcement  of these  penalties.

As  is  the  case  with  the  information-gathering  provi-
sions  of Title  2, U.  S.  C.  (discussed  in  Section  A  of  this
part of our  brief ), the Commission  has  general  rulemaking
authority  regarding  the  federal  financing  provisions  for
Presidential  elections  and  primaries.
Just  as  in  the  infor-
mation-gathering  area,  however,  such  rules  may  only  be
proposed  by the  Commission.  They  become  effective  if
not  disallowed  by either  House  of Congress  within  thirty
legislative  days  (§§ 9009(c),  9039(b)).
The  Commis-
sion  may  waive  certain  expenditure  limitations  relating
to  nominating  conventions  when  it  determines  that,  due
to  extraordinary  and  unforseen  circumstances,  additional
expenditures  are  necessary  to  ensure  an  effective  conven-
tion  (§ 9008(d)),*’  and  it  may  render  advisory  opinions
so  as  to protect  persons  who,  in good  faith,  seek  to  comply
with  the  statutory  requirements  (2 U.  S. C.  § 437f).  The
Commission  is  to  make  reports  to  the  Congress  of  the
37.  This  provision  has  been  narrowly  (and,  we  believe

-

rectly)  interpreted  by  the  court  below  to  refer  to  situations  of
emergency
(such  as  a  strike  or  breakdown  of  equipment)  that
require  “eh  measures  in  order  to  keep  the convention
functioning.  J.  5.  1580.  So  interpreted,  the  provision  authorizing
the  allowance  of additional  expenditures  to  meet  situations  falling
= = limited  category  easily  withstands  appellants’  void-for-

s

46

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

expenses  incurred  by Presidential  candidates  and  parties,
amounts  certified  by it for payment,  and  amounts  required
to  be repaid  (§§ 9009(a),  9038(a) ).

As  the  above  description  shows,  the  Commission  s
function  with  regard  to  the  federal  financing  of Presiden-
tial  campaigns  under  the  1974  legislation  is  a  ministerial
It certifies  when  candidates  and  par-
and  watchdog  role.
ties  have  met  the  statutory  requirements  for  eligibility  for
funds,  it audits  the  accounts  of  those  who  receive  funds,
and  it is charged  with  attempting  to  obtain  repayment  of
The
improperly  received  or  improperly  spent  funds.
Commission  has  no  power  to  set  policy  regarding  the  ex-
tent  or  allocation  of federal  financing,  these  matters  being
The  Commission’s  role  in
fully  prescribed  by  statute.
this  area  is  thus  quite  similar  to  that  exercised  by  the
Comptroller  General  with  regard  to  federal  appropriations
generally.  The  Comptroller  General  audits  substantially
all government  accounts  (see 31 U. S. C.  §§ 41, 44, 60, 67)
and  certifies  amounts  for  payment  (31,U.  S.  C.  §§ 47,  71,
72,  74).  The  Commission’s  role  in  enforcing  repayment
of  overpaid  or  misspent  amounts  has  precedent  in  the
Comptroller's  power  to  set  off  claims  by the  Government
against  persons  seeking  to recover  from  the  United  States,
and  to  bring  suit  to  enforce  such  set-offs  (31  U.  S.  C.
§ 227).*  Indeed,  in  the  area  of federal  funding  of Presi-
dential  elections,  under  the  legislation  immediately  pre-

rivate

;

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

47

ceding  the  1974  Act  (The  Presidential  Election  Cam-
paign  Fund  Act  of 1971  (discussed  in  Point  III-E  of this
brief,  supra) ), the  Comptroller  General,  assisted  by  an
Advisory  Board  chosen  outside  the  appointment  provi-
sions  of  Article  II  of  the  Constitution,  exercised  all  the
principal  functions—including  the  duty  to  sue  to  recover
overpayments  and  to seek  injunctive  relief—now  vested  in
the  Commission  by  the  1974  Act.

The line between  those  administrative  functions  which
are  so  dominantly  executive  or  quasi-executive  that  they
may  only be exercised  by officials  selected  according  to the
appointment  provisions  of Article  II, Section  2 of the Con-
stitution,  and  those  functions  so  related  to  the  legislative
role  that  they  may  be performed  by legislatively  selected
officers  acting  as  legislative  agents  is, as  discussed  in  Point
II of this  brief,  supra,  necessarily  indistinct  at  the  fringes
of the respective  authorities.  The  Commission’s  functions
of certifying  payments,  auditing  accounts  and  recovering
overpayments  or  misspent  amounts  fall  within  an  area
which  appears  to  relate  as  closely  to  legislative  as  to  ex-
ecutive  duties.
As  the  numerous  instances  of  legislative
selection  of officials  detailed  in  Point  II of this  brief  indi-
cates,  Congress  has in the past,  in several  instances  at least,
picked  officers  of  its  own  choosing  to  administer  the  ex-
penditures  of  congressionally  appropriated  funds,  and  in
some  of these  cases  the  officers  so  selected  have  possessed
a discretion  as  to amount  and  recipient  of funds  which  the
Election  Commission  here  totally  lacks.  When  one  con-
siders,  as  well, that the Comptroller  General—an  arm  of the
legislature  rather  than  of the executive—has  long exercised
certifying,  auditing  and  set-off  roles  regarding  federal  ex-
penditures,  it appears  firmly established  that Congress,  if it
so  cl ooses,  generally  has  the  constitutional  prerogative  of

48

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

designating  the  personnel  to  oversee  the  integrity  of  its
appropriations.

In  the  present  instance  the  considerations  supporting
congressional  use  of  an  auditing  and  certifying  agency
whose  members  are  not  entirely  selected  by the  President
are,  moreover,  of much  greater  strength  than  is  the  case
with  the  supervision  of  federal  appropriations  generally.
Once  having  determined  to  create  a  separate  agency  to
monitor  the federal  financing  of Presidential  election  cam-
paigns  (a decision  surely  within  Congress’  power  to make )
it would  be a strange  situation  indeed,  especially  in light of
recent  events  showing  the potentialities  for abuse  of Presi-
dential  power,  if Congress  were  required  to  vest  in an  in-
cumbent  President—himself  a candidate  for re-election  and,
as  such, both a primary  beneficiary  of the federal  funds  and
a  political  competitor  with  others  entitled  to  claim  such
funds—the  exclusive  power  to  nominate  the  members  of
Congress,  we  believe,  was  entitled  to  take
that  agency.
steps  to insure  both  the apparent  and  actual  fairness  of the
financing  process.”

tures  in federal  election  campaigns.  These  sections  provide

The  Election  Cummission’s  role  with  regard  to  these
substantive  limits  and  their  enforcement  is a  narrow  one.
It may  receive  and  investigate  complaints  about  violations
and  may  refer  apparent  violations  to  the  Department  of
Justice  for  prosecution  or  civil  relief  (2  U.  S.  C.  § 437g
(a)(b)(7)).  The Commission,  however,  is given  no  inde-
pendent  enforcement  authority  with  respect  to  these  pro-
visions.
(See  2 U.  S.  C.  § 437g¢(5),  restricting  the  Com-
mission’s  independent  enforcement  authority  to  violations
of  “this  Act,”  ie.,  the  information-gathering  provisions,
as  compared  with  2 U.  S. C.  § 437g¢(7),  which  authorizes
the  Commission  to  refer  to  the  Attorney  General  viola-
tions  of both  “this  Act”  and  the  Criminal  Code  provisions
in  18 U.  S. C.  $§ 608-617).

The  Commission  may  issue  advisory  opinions  as  to
the meaning  of §§ 608-617  (2 U.  S. C.  § 437f)  and  it may
institute  actions  for declaratory  judgment  to  test  their  con-
stitutionality  (2  U.  S.  C.  §437h).“°  The  Commission  is
given  no  general  substantive  rulemaking  authority  with
regard  to  the  Title  18  spending  and  contribution  limita-
tions,  although  it is authorized  to  make  such  rules  in  one
limited  area,  that of allocating  primary  campaign  expendi-
tures  as  between  States  when  such  expenditures  are  made
for use  in two  or  more  States  (§ 608(c)(4))."
