50

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

If, as  we  argue  above,  the  Commission  has  properly
been  delegated  broad  responsibilities  in  gathering  infor-
mation  about  election  financing  for use  by Congress  and in
monitoring  the  disbursement  of federal  funds  to  be  used
to  finance  Presidential  primaries  and  election  campaigns,
its  narrow  additional  functions  relating  to  the  Criminal
Code  limitations  on  expenditures  and  contributions  would
appear  to  raise  no  substantial  additional  problems  regard-
ing  the  appropriate  separation  of powers.  The  Commis-
sion’s  limited  functions  with  regard  to  the  Criminal  Code
provisions  are  intimately  and  naturally  related  to  its  other
Thus,  the  Commission's  exercise  of  its
responsibilities.
auditing  and  investigating  functions  with  respect  to  the
required  reporting  of campaign  contributions  and expendi-
tures  and  the  uses  made  of federal  financing  funds  might
well  reveal  apparent  violations  of the  Criminal  Code  con-
tribution  and expenditure  limitations.  Congress  can  hardly
have  overstepped  constitutional  bounds  in  permitting  the
Commission's  investigations  full  sweep  and  in  requiring
the Commission  to notify  the appropriate  law enforcement
authorities  of  such  violations,  should  they  be  discovered.
Again,  the  intimate  relationship  between  the  required  re-
porting of expenditures  and contributions,  on  the one  hand,
and  the  substantive  limits  on  the  amount  and  source  of
such  expenditures  and  contributions,  on  the  other,  make
it  appropriate  for  the  Commission's  advisory  opinion  re-
sponsibility  to cover  both  subjects.  This  is especially  so  as

With  the  exceptions  noted  in  the  Statement,  supra,  the
court  below  found  those  objections  premature.  It noted,
for example,  that  although  appellants  challenged  the Com-
mission’s  rule-making  authority,  no  rules  had  become  ef-
fective.  To  be  sure,  some  of the  Commission's  activities
may  be at  a  more  advanced  stage  by the  time  this  Court
has  occasion  to  act.  Nonetheless,  it remains  true  that  the
Court  has no  pleading before  it that challenges  a particular
action  taken  by the Commission,  much  less  a showing  that
a definitive  action  taken  by the Commission  has  caused  an

“re |  > a  person  before  the  Court.  As a result,  appel-

Appellants’  reasoning  on  the  merits  is  that  the  mem-
bers of the F. E. C., not  having been  appointed  exclusively
by the  President,  cannot  perform  any  quasi-executive  or
quasi-judicial  functions.  Our  basic  response  is  rooted  in
the contentions  already  elaborated:  that  Congress’  respon-
sibility  in  relation  to  federal  elections  goes  well  beyond
the  mere  legislative  prohibition  of  fraud  or  corruptive
practice  (its role  encompassing  a  duty  to  secure,  examine
and audit relevant  information,  both  in order  to judge elec-
tions  and  qualifications  and  in  order  to  deter  the  exertion
of corruptive  influence;  a  duty  to  control  and  oversee  the
expenditure  of public  monies  made  available  to  those  en-
gaged  in  the  electoral  process;  and  a  duty  to  initiate  ap-
propriate  measures  with  respect  to  violations  of law  that
come  to its attention ); that, although  the functions  entailed
by this pervasive  responsibility  have  quasi-executive  and
quasi-judicial  aspects,  they  are  functions  appropriate  to
the  Legislative  Department  in  light  of  its  constitutional
role;  and  that  accordingly  they may  appropriately  be per-
formed  by a legislative  commission  in  aid  of the  Legisla-
ture.

.  within  the  executive

sphere.”  Addressing  the matter  in the abstract,  one might

essentially”  a  legislative  function.
Davis’  words,  it is “the  part  of the  administrative  process

government  make  rules,  whether  formally  or  informally.
The  real  question  is  whether  we  are  correct  in  our  basic
argument  that  Congress’  constitutionally  permissible  role
in  relation  to  the  federal  electoral  process  is considerably
broader  than  the bare  legislation  of statutory  prescriptions.

45.  This  is not  to  deny  that  a  rule  adopted  by the  Commission

ity.  Any  such  claim,  however,  must  obviously  await  a  concrete
case  or  controversy.

U.  S.  134,  140,  the  Court,  commenting  on  an  interpreta-
tion  of the  Fair  Labor  Standards  Act  promulgated  by the
Administrator,  observed:

We  consider  that  the  rulings,  interpretations
and opinions  of the Administrator  under  this  Act,
while  not  controlling  upon  the  courts  by reason
of  their  authority,  do  constitute  a  body  of  ex-
perience  and  informed  judgment  to  which  courts
and  litigants  may  properly  resort  for  guidance.
The  weight  of  such  a  judgment  in  a  particular
case  will  depend  upon  the  thoroughness  evident
in  its  consideration,  the  validity  of its  reasoning,
its  consistency  with  earlier  and  later  pronounce-
ments,  and  all  those  factors  which  give  it power
to  persuade,  if  lacking  power  to  control.

The same,  the Court  notes  (ibid.),  is true  of administrative
interpretations  by  “other  bodies.”

It  is  of  course  commonplace  that  a  statutory  inter-
pretation  adopted  by an  independent  regulatory  agency
will have  relevance  to a legal question  arising  in a  criminal
proceeding  brought  by the  Attorney  General  to  enforce
the  same  statute.*7  That  has  never  been  thought  to  inter-
fere  with  the  prerogatives  of  the  Attorney  General,  even
though  it  is  obviously  true  of  other  commissions,  as  it  is
of  the  F.  E.  C.,  that  their  members  are  not  subject  to
provisions  of  the  Securities  Act  of  1933,  15
U.  S.C.  TT a the  Securities Exchange  Act  of  1934,  15  U.  S.  C.

57

executive  control,  either  by way  of  day-to-day  direction

Having  firs’  contended  that  rule-making  authority
gives  the  Commission  excessive  authority,  appellants  next
object  (Br.  208)  that  “rules  relating  to  reporting  and  dis-
closure  requirements  promulgated  by  the  F.  E.  C.  are
subject  to  veto  by one  or  both  houses  of Congress”  pur-
suant  to  § 438(c),  a  provision  plainly  designed  to  keep
the  Commission  from  straying  from  Congress’  purposes.
This  provision  is questioned  on  the  ground  that  the “legis-
lative  veto”  does  not  involve  Presidential  participation.
But  a  disapproval  does  not  involve  new  legislation;  it
leaves  the law  unchanged.  The  procedure  in question  has
the  implicit  approval  of  this  Court.
In  1934,  Congress
delegated  to this Court  the formulation  and prescription  of
civil rules  for the district  courts.  In a subsequent  litigation
raising  the  question  whether  certain  rules  that  had  been
adopted  and  become  effective  were  beyond  the  scope  of
the delegation,  the  Court,  sustaining  the  rules  in question,
stated  (Sibbach  v.  Wilson  &  Co.,  312  U.  S.  1,  14-15):
“Moreover,  in  accordance  with  the  Act,  the  rules  were
submitted  to the Congress  so  that that body might examine
them  and  veto  their  going  into  effect  if contrary  to  the
policy  of the  legislature.” *°

Recognizing
Though  not  binding,  it  provides  guidance.
that  secret  rulings  might  in some  circumstances  become  an

role in the electoral  process.  In the court  of appeals’  words

gation  of  powers  was  inexorably  applied  (in  theory  at  least)

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

59

vision  it  must  administer.
In  general,  indeed,
disclosure  to  the public  of how a statute  pertain-
ing to election  campaigns  will  be administered  is
viewed  as  wholesome,  and  in any  event  naturally
related  to  the  function  of  administration.
The
fact  that  advisory  opinions  are  usually  issued  in
connection  with  executive  functions,  or  quasi-
judicial  functions,  does  not  assign  this  technique
exclusively  to the executive  or  judicial  branch.

The  court  below  also  responded  to  the  concern  ex-
pressed  in  Judge  MacKinnon’s  dissent,  namely,  that  the
“good faith”  provision  (i.e.,  that one  who  acts  in good  faith
in accordance  with  an  advisory  opinion  shall  be presumed
to be in compliance  with  the law)  may  clash  with  the  At-
torney  General's  prerogatives.
Acknowledging  that  the
language  of presumption  is “rather  ambiguous,”  the  major-
ity pointed  out  that  the  relevant  legislative  history  makes
plain  that  the presumption  is rebuttable  and  does  not  con-
stitute  a grant  of immunity.  Ibid.  Thus,  the agency  is not
in  the  position  of administering  provisions  of the  criminal
law.
At  the  same  time,  the  provision  for  rendition  of
advisory  opinions  does  serve  as  a measure  of protection  for
the citizen  who  seeks  assurance  that  his  proposed  conduct
conforms  to  the  law’s  requirements—a  protection  that  de-
rives  not  merely  from  the  statutory  words  of presumption,
but  from  the  natural  inference  that  one  who  acts  con-
sistently  with  official  advice  lacks  criminal  intent.
It  is
difficult  to see  how  appellants  can  claim  that  they are  prej-
udiced  by a  procedural  device  that  makes  such  guidance
available.”

(2) Enforcement  and Investigation.

Appellants  also  challenge  the  F.  E.  C.’s  enforcement
powers  under  the  1974  legislation,  urging  that the  power
of enforcement  is “executive  beyond  question”  and,  hence,
inappropriate  for a legislative  agency.  In fact, se
ment  powers  delegated  to  the F. E. C. are  quite
scope  and,  as  limited,  are  well  within  the  proper  preroga-
tives  of an  arm  of the legislature.  In addition, as  the court
of appeals  held  and  as  the Attorney  General  in  his amicus
brief  correctly  urges,  any  challenge  to  the  F.  E.  C.s  -
forcement  powers  at  this  time  is  wholly  premature  an

this case.  The  1974  statute,  entitled the  Federal Election
Campaign  Act  Amendments  of 1974,  is divided  into  ~_—
Part  1 consists  o
main  parts.
amendments  to  §§ 608  et seq.  of the Criminal  Code  ( Title
18, U.S.C.)  (see 88 Stat.  1263-1272).  These  amendments
place  substantive  limits  on  contributions  and  expenditures
in federal  election  campaigns.  Part  2 of the  1974  Act  con-
sists  of amendments  to the Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
of 1971,  2 U.S.  C.  § 431  et seq.  (see 88 Stat.  1272-1290).
These  amendments  create  the  F. E. C., describe its  duties,
and  impose  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  on
political  committees,  candidates,  contributors,  etc.  Part  3,
the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  and  the
sent  hard-

egg oy ep  oy “Laundry & Cleaning Council v.

|

Presidential  Primary  Matching  Payment  Account  Act,  con-
sists  of  amendments  to  the  Internal  Revenue  Code,  26
U.  S. C.  §§ 9001  et  seq.  (see 88 Stat.  1291-1304).  These
amendments  provide  for  federal  funding  of  general  and
primary  Presidential  election  campaigns.

The  F. E. C.’s  main  enforcement  powers  with  relation
to  the  provisions  of Titles  2 and  18  of  the  United  States
Code  are  set  out  in 2 U.  S. C.  § 437g,  a provision  of Part  2
of  the  1974  legislation—i.e.,  that  part  which  consists  of
amendments  to  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of
1971.  The  language  of  Section  437g  imposes  a critical
limitation  with  respect  to  the  F.  E.  C.’s  enforcement
powers.  Where  § 437g  refers  to enforcement  of “this  Act”
—that  is,  where  it refers  to  enforcement  of  the  reporting
and disclosure  requirements  contained  in the  amendments
to  the  1971  Act  (2 U.  S.  C.  §§ 431  et  seq.)—it  gives  the
F.  E.  C,  full  independent  power  to  go  to  Court  to  obtain
compliance  with  those  information-gathering  requirements.
See  subsection  (5)  of § 437g,  giving  the F. E.  C. power  to
seek  civil  judicial  enforcement  as  against  acts  “which  con-
stitute  or  will  constitute  a  violation  of this Act”  (emphasis
added).  That  “this  Act”  means,  not  the  entire  group  of
1974  amendments,  but  only  those  amendments  relating  to
the  information  gathering  provisions  of  the  1971]  Federal
Election  Campaign  Act,  is  confirmed  by footnote  1 to  2
U.  S.  C.  § 434,  as  set  forth  in  the  statutory  pamphlet  en-
titled  Federal  Election  Campaign  Laws  which  has  been
lodged  with  the  Court:  “‘This  Act’  refers  to  the  Federal
Election  Campaign  Act  of 1971  .
.  as  amended  by...
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974.
This  will  be  true  whenever  the  term  ‘this  Act’  appears.”
But  where  § 437g refers  to  civil  enforcement  of the  Crimi-
nal  Code  provisions  of Title  18—provisions  which  set  sub-
stantive  limits  on  contributions  and  expenditures—it  does
not  give  the  F. E. C.  independent  enforcement  power,  but

the  Congress.  They  do  not  relate  to  the  enforcement  of
the  substantive  limits  on  contributions  and  expenditures
contained  in  the  Criminal  Code."  We  submit  that  it  is

the  Attorney  General,  on  behalf  of the  United
tute  a  civil  enforcement  proceeding.  We  agree  with  the  court
below  that  “there  is  no  showing  in  this  case  of  a  convincing  legis-
lative  history  that  would  enable  us  to  conclude  that  ‘shall’  was  in-
tended  to  be  the  ‘language  of  command.”  To  our  knowledge
Congress  has  never  attempted  to  deprive  the  Attorney  General  of
his  ordinary  discretion  to  decline  to  institute  legal  proceedings.

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission
63
entirely  clear  that  Congress  may  thus  vest  in  a  legislative
agency  staffed  by personnel  of its own  choosing  the power
to  gather information  on  behalf  of  Congress  relevant  to
Congress’  own  responsibilities—here  the  responsibility  of
exercising  legislative  oversight  into  the  federal  election
process  as  well  as  the  responsibility  of judging  the  qualifi-
cations  of  its  own  members  in  terms  of their  compliance
with  federal  election  finance  laws.
As  the  court  below
noted  (J. S. 1575):  “It is certainly  within  the sphere  of ap-
propriate  legislative  activity  for  Congress  to  gather  and
audit  information  to aid in the process  of ‘preventing  fraud
and  corruption’  in  federal  elections  and  in  formulating
legislation  to  accomplish  that  goal”  (citing  Barenblatt  v.
United  States,  360  U.  S.  109,  111  (1959)  (“[t]he  scope
of the  power  of inquiry  is  .
.
.  as  penetrating  and  far-
reaching  as  the  potential  power  to  enact  and  appropriate
under  the  Constitution)  and  McGrain  v.  Dougherty,  273
U.  S. 135,  175  (1927)  (“[a]  legislative  body  cannot  legis-
late  wire Fn yor
in  the  absence  of  information
3  ec dag  hecee  yy
respecting
the  legislation  is  intended

the conditions  which

i

And  where  Congress  has the power  to  gather  inf
-
tion,  it also  has  the  power,  through  its  ion agents, raed
voke  compulsive  sanctions  against  persons  who  do  not
comply  with  its  legitimate  reporting  and  disclosure  re-
quirements.
“[T]he  power  of  inquiry—with  process  to
enforce  it—is  an  essential  and  appropriate  auxiliary  to  the
legislative  function.”  McGrain  v.  Dougherty,  273  U.  S.
at  174.  Congress  may,  indeed,  apply  coercive  sanctions
directly,  without  resort  to  the  Judicial  Branch.
See,  e.g.,
In  re  Chapman,  166  U.  S.  661;  United  States  v.  Bryan
339  U.  S. 323;  Watkins  v.  United  States,  354  U.  S.  178,
206-207;  McGrain  v.  Dougherty,  supra.  A fortiori,  it may
authorize  its  agents  to  use  the  judicial  process  to  compel
compliance  with  its proper  requests  for  information.
See

64

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

e.g.,  Eastland  v.  United  States  Servicemen’s  Fund,  95  S.
Ct.  1813;  Senate  Select  Committee  v.  Nixon,  498  F.  2d
725  (D.  C.  Cir.  1974).  We  note  in  passing  that  the  in-
dependent  administrative  agencies  also  routinely  exercise
the  independent  enforcement  power  of going  to  court  in
aid  of their  information-gathering  processes  (see,  e.g.,  15
U.  S. C.  § 49  (F. T.  C.);  15  U.  S. C.  § 78  (S. E.  C.);  29
U.  S.  C.  § 161  (N.  L.  R.  B.)),  and  that  the  same  power
has  been  given  to  legislative  study  commissions  whose
membership,  like  that  of the  F.  E. C., is appointed  in part
by Congress  outside  of  the  provisions  of  Article  II,  Sec-
tion  2  (see,  e.g.,  84  Stat.  938.  § 806  (Commission  to  Re-
view  National  Policy  Toward  Gambling);  80  Stat.  868
(Commission  on  Political  Activity  of  Government  Per-
sonnel ) ).

It  is  not  clear  from  appellants’  brief  which  aspects
of the  F. E. C.’s  enforcement  authority  they mean  to  chal-
lenge beyond  those  conferred  by §§ 437g(5)  and  (7).  The
power  to  receive  complaints  and  to  investigate  alleged
violations  of the  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  of
Title  2  (“this  Act”)  is  a  necessary  part  of  the  power  to
enforce  compliance  with  those  requirements.  Since  viola-
tions  of  the  duties  to  report  contributions  and  expendi-
tures  will  often  be  linked  with  violations  of the  substan-
tive  limits  on  those  contributions  and  expenditures,  it was
completely  natural,  we  believe,  for  Congress  to  broaden
the  F.  E.  C.’s  investigative  authority  to  cover  these  sub-
stantive  violations  as  well,  subject  to  the proviso,  as  noted
above,  that  if  the  F.  E.  C.  should  uncover  an  apparent
substantive  violation  it  may  not  commence  enforcement
proceedings  itself,  but  must  refer  the  case  to  the  Depart-
ment  of Justice.  Separation  of powers  principles  are  surely
not  violated  by requiring  legislative  agencies  to refer  cases
to  the  Executive  when  their  investigations  uncover  viola-
tions  of law.

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

65

As noted  in Point  IV-B  of this brief, supra,  the F.  E. C.
also  has  independent  enforcement  power,  under  the  In-
ternal  Revenue  Code  provisions  of  the  1974  legislation,
to insure  that presidential  candidates  and  others  receiving
federal  funds  in  connection  with  presidential  primaries,
nominating  conventions  and  general  elections  do  not  re-
ceive  or  spend  more  than  is  allowable  under  the  law.
These  enforcement  duties  were  exercised  by  the  Comp-
troller  General—a  conceded  legislative  officer—under  the
legislation  immediately  preceding  the  1974  amendments.
In view  of the  traditional  role  of the  Comptroller  General
in  the  area  of  appropriations,  there  can  hardly  be  any
serious  doubt  of  the  legislature’s  power  to  use  its  own
officers  to  monitor  the  use  of funds  which  it appropriates.
Finally  (and  without  modifying,  in  any  respect,  our
view  that the enforcement  powers  delegated  to the F. E. C.
are entirely  appropriate  powers  to  be  vested  in a legisla-
tive  agency)  we  wish  to  emphasize  our  agreement  with
both  the  court  below  and  the  Attorney  General  as  amicus
in  this  Court  that  appellants’  challenges  to  the  F.  E.  C.’s
limited  enforcement  responsibilities  are  entirely  prema-
ture.  Questions  of ripeness  in  constitutional  adjudication
are  often  close  and  intricate.
This  ripeness  question  is
not.
It is, quite  frankly,  difficult  to  conceive  of any  basis
for the  view  that  resolving  the  legitimacy  of the  F.  E.  C.’s
enforcement  responsibilities  at  this  time  will  affect  the
conduct  of any  interested  party  in any  respect  whatsoever.
The  F.  E.  C.  has  begun  no  enforcement  proceedings
against  anyone.”  Indeed,  since  the  F.  E.  C.  is  given  the

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

67

option  in  all  cases  of  referring  enforcement  suits  to  the
Department  of  Justice,  the  F.  E.  C.  may  never  exercise
the  challenged  powers  at  all.  The  questions  urged  by
appellants  may  thus  not  only be unripe,  but may  be wholly
Moreover,  all  of  the  violations  as  to
academic  as  well.
which  the  F.  E. C.  has  any  potential  enforcement  role  are
subject,  whatever  the  speculative  possibility  of an  F. E.  C.
enforcement  proceeding,  to  criminal  sanctions  as  well  as
to  civil  enforcement  proceedings  instituted  by  the  Attor-
ney  General  (see  2 U.  S.  C.  § 441).  It  therefore  cannot
be credibly  asserted  that  the  F. E. C.’s  enforcement  power
bears  in  the  least  upon  the  present  conduct  of the  appel-
lants.™

V.
The  Method  of  Appointment  Chosen  by  Congress  Is
Neither  Incongruous  Nor  Violative  of  the  Doctrine  of
Separation  of  Powers.

We  have  elaborated  our  reasons  for  believing  that
Congress  may  exercise  authority  to  appoint  officers  when
the  functions  delegated  to  those  officers  are  substantially

related to  the  performance  of Congressional  responsibili-
ties;  that  Congress’  constitutional  role  in  the  federal
electoral  process  entails  the  performance  of quasi-judicial
and quasi-executive  functions  as  well  as  exercise  of its legis-
lative  power;  and that when,  as  here, Congress  delegates  to
a  Commission  functions  of a  kind  that  it is authorized  to
perform  for  itself,  it may  assuredly  choose  (or participate
in the choice  of ) its surrogates.

So  understood,  the  argument  involves  no  clash  with
the concept  of separation  of powers.  Since  the  Commis-
sion  is to perform  tasks  of a  kind  and  character  that  Con-
gress  could  appropriately  perform  (and  has  long  per-
formed ) through  its own  staff  and  officers,  it is in no  sense
an executive  agency.”  By  the  same  token,  there  is  no
invasion  of  any  prerogative  reserved  to  the  Executive
Branch.
As  stated  in  Humphrey's  Executor  v.  United
States,  295  U.  S.  602,  628,  with  reference  to  the  Federal
Trade  Commission,  “To  the  extent  that  [the  Commission]
exercises  any  executive  function—as  distinguished  from
executive  power  in  the  constitutional  sense—it  does  so  in
the  discharge  and  effectuation  of  its  quasi-legislative  or
quasi-judicial  powers  .
.  .”

.

68

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

Brief of the Federal  Election  Commission

69

:

We  submit  that  it  avoids,  rather  than  creates.  the  sug-
gestion  of impropriety.

As noted  earlier,  the Commission  is bipartisan.  A
from its two  ex  officio members,  the Saat of the jo
and  the Clerk  of  the  House,  it  consists  of  two  members
nominated  in the Senate,  two  nominated  in the House.  and
two  nominated  by the  President.
Since  the  Commission's
responsibilities  relate  to  Presidential  elections  and  to
elections  of the  members  of both  Houses,  the  scheme  can
hardly be regarded  as  incongrous.

On  the  other  hand,  one  might  well  question  the  wis-
dom  of  confining  to  the  President  alone  the  selection  of
those  who  are  to  be  engaged  in  the  supervision  of  the
electoral process.  The  President  is not  only Chief  of State:
he is  Chief  of his  Party.  He  may  be,  in  addition,  an  an-
nounced  candidate  for reelection.  To  divide  the  authority
to  select  the  members  of the  Commission  is  to  minimize
potential  conflicts  of  interest,  to  lessen  the  dangers  of
partisanship,  and  to  strengthen  public  confidence  that  the
law will  be administered  diligently  and  even-handedly.

ments.”  Rather,  it reflects  the  precept  that,  as  a  general
rule,  each  branch  of government  should  have,  and  be  free
to  exercise,  those  powers  that  are  appropriate  to  its
functions.

The  point  was  strikingly  made,  almost  a  century  ago,
in  Ex  parte  Siebold,  100  U.  S.  376,  a  case  which,  like  the
instant  litigation,  involved  control  over  the federal  electoral
process.  Congress  had provided  in the Enforcement  Act  of
1870  that  in  certain  circumstances  federal  judges  should
appoint  supervisors  to  oversee  elections  being  conducted
in  the  States.  The  supervisors  were  to  attend  at  places  of
registration  and  at  elections,  and  were  accorded  authority
to  challenge  any  person  proposing  to  register  or  vote  un-
lawfully,  to  witness  the  counting  of votes,  and  to  identify
by their  signatures  the  registration  of voters  and  the  elec-
tion  tally  sheets.  Addressing  the  claim  that  it was  consti-
tutionally  impermissible  for judges  to be charged  with  the
duty  of appointing  officials  who  would  perform  functions
of  a  quasi-executive  character,  the  Court  ruled  that  the
separation  doctrine  is not  an  “absolute”  (100 U.  S. at 397 ),
and  that  it was  neither  incongrous  nor  improper  for  Con-
gress,  in  its  discretion,  to  vest  the  power  to  appoint  the
officers  in question  in the  federal  judiciary  rather  than  the
President  (id. at 398).

ae

70

Brief of the  Federal  Election  Commission

CONCLUSION.

The  Commission  is  an  arm  of  the  Congress  and  the
method  by which  its  members  are  selected  is both  consti-
tutional  and appropriate.  The powers  and  functions  which
it has  been  delegated  are  likewise  appropriate  to  its  role.

Respectfully  submitted,

166

a

Nos.  75-436,  75-437

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY,  et  al.,

Appellants,

Ve

FRANCIS  R.  VALEO,  et  al.,

Appellees.

On  Appeal  from  the  United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the
District  of  Columbia  Circuit  and  the  United  States
District  Court  for  the  District  of  Columbia

BRIEF  FOR  APPELLEES  CENTER  FOR  PUBLIC
FINANCING  OF  ELECTIONS,  COMMON  CAUSE,
LEAGUE  OF  WOMEN  VOTERS  OF  THE
UNITED  STATES,  et  al.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

1.  Have  appellants  proven  a  sufficient  likelihood  of
unconstitutional  harm  to  themselves  or  others  to  warrant
invalidating  major  congressional  legislation  before  it  has
had  a  chance  to  function?

2

3

2.  Does  the  First  Amendment  forbid  Congress  to  im-
pose  any  limits  on  campaign  contributions  and  expendi-
tures,  even  where  necessary  to  serve  compelling  interests
in  protecting  the  integrity  of  the  electoral  process?

3.  Have  appellants  proven  that  the  particular  limits
chosen  by  Congress  will  so  inevitably  have  unconstitu-
tional  effects  that  the  limits  should  be  voided  before  they
have  any  chance  to  operate?

4.  Have  appellants  proven  that  the  disclosure  provi-
sions  of  these  laws  are  overbroad  in  requiring  disclosure
of  small  contributions  and  contributions  to  third  party
candidates?

5.  Does  the  Constitution  forbid  Congress  to  provide
any  financial  assistance  for  the  campaigns  of  persons
seeking  election  to  federal  office?

6.  Have  appellants  proven  that  the  particular  public
financing  provisions  of  these  statutes  discriminate  invidi-
ously  against  them?

7.  Are  the  provisions  of  these  statutes  concerning  the
powers  and  method  of  appointment  of  the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Commission  ripe  for  review,  and  if so  do  they  violate
the  constitutional  separation  of  powers? '

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

This  action  arises  out  of  a  complaint  filed  in  the  United
States  District  Court  for  the  District  of  Columbia  on
January  2,  1975,  seeking  a  declaration  that  appellants’
constitutional  rights  are  violated  by two  federal  statutes:

‘We  do  not  address  this  question  but  respectfully  submit  that
the  decision  of  the  court  of  appeals  with  respect  to  it  should  be
affirmed,  for  the  reasons  stated  in  the  Brief  of  Special  Ccunsel  to
the  Commission  and  also  for  the  broader  reasons  stated  in  -he  Brief
for  Amici  Curiae  Senators  Scott  and  Kennedy.

—the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971,
Public  Law  92-225,  86  Stat.  3,  2 U.S.C.  §§ 431  et  seq.
(“FECA”),  as  amended  by  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974,  Public  Law
93-443,  88  Stat.  1263  (“FECA  Amendments”);  and

—Subtitle  H of the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954,
Public  Law  92-178,  85  Stat.  562,  26  U.S.C.  §§ 9001
et  seq.  (“Subtitle  H”),  as  amended  by  Public  Law
93-53,  87  Stat.  188,  and  by  the  FECA  Amendments.

The  complaint  also  asked  that  the  enforcement  of  these
statutes  be  enjoined.

Appellants  are  an  assortment  of  federal  officeholders,
candidates  for  federal  office,  political  organizations  sup-
porting  those  candidates,  one  chapter  of  a  national  civil
liberties  organization,  one  publication,  and  one  donor  to
candidates  for  federal  office.  Appellants  make  three  basic
challenges  to  the  new  laws:
First,  they  deny  that  Con-
gress  has  authority  to  impose  any  limits  on  campaign
contributions  or  expenditures  or  to  provide  any  public
financing  to  campaigns  for  election  to  federal  office.  Sec-
ond,  they  attack  the  specific  contribution,  expenditure,
public  financing,  and  disclosure  provisions  Congress  en-
acted  on  the  ground  that  they  will  necessarily  operate
discriminatorily  and  have  other  impermissible  effects.
Third,  they  attack  the  composition  and  powers  of  the
Federal  Election  Commission  (FEC)  as  a  violation  of
the  separation  of  powers.

The  defendants  named  in  the  complaint  are  the  Secre-
tary  of  the  United  States  Senate,  the  Clerk  of  the  United
States  House  of  Representatives,  the  Comptroller  General
of  the  United  States,  the  Attorney  General  of  the  United
States,  and  the  FEC.

4

5

Appellees  Center  for  Public  Financing  of  Elections,
Common  Cause,  League  of  Women  Voters  of  the  United
States,  et  al.  intervened  as  defendants  with  the  permis-
sion  of  the  court  of  appeals.  They  are  three  nonprofit
organizations  that  have  as  their  aim  the  protection  of  the
integrity  of  the  electoral  process,  and  eight  individual
voters  active  in  these  organizations.

Pursuant  to  Section  315(a)  of  the  FECA  as  amended,
(Corcoran,  J.)  on
2  U.S.C.  §487h,  the  district  court
January  27,  1975  certified  the  proceeding  to  the  court  of
appeals.  On  April  14,  that  court  en  bane  granted  the  de-
fendants’  and  intervening  defendants’  motion  to  remand,
and  ordered  the  district  court  to  identify  constitutional
issues  in  the  complaint,  take  evidence,  make  findings  of
fact,  and  certify  constitutional  questions  to  the  court  of
appeals.  The  April  14  Order  also  directed  that  the  public
financing  issues  raised  by  Subtitle  H  be  argued  both  to
the  court  of  appeals  and  to  a  three-judge  district  court,
so  as  to  eliminate  any  doubt  as  to  the  correct  method  of
reviewing  Subtitle  H.

On  May  19,  1975  the  district  court  completed  its  as-
signed  tasks  and  transmitted  its  findings  of  fact  and  its
certified  constitutional  questions  to  the  court  of  appeals.
The  district  court’s  findings—which  are  based  largely  on
stipulations  by  the  parties  and  which  appellants  do  not
‘challenge—detail  a  history  of  campaign  financing  abuses
proving,  as  the  court  of  appeals  found,  that

“the  Government  has  a  clear  and  compelling  interest
in  safeguarding  the  integrity  of  elections  and  avoid-
Ct.  App.  Op.
ing  the  undue  influence  of  wealth.”
1517.’

? The  court  of  appeals’  decision  is  reproduced  in  Appendix  A  to
the  jurisdictional  statement  in  No.  75-436.  Page  citations  herein
are  to  the  page  numbers  of  the  slip  opinion,  which  are  identical
to  the  page  numbers  in  that  appendix.

As  the  court  of  appeals  concluded,  the  findings  contain
only  “scant”  and  “skimpy”  evidence  to  support  appel-
lants’  allegations  of  harm  from  the  challenged  statutes.
Id.  at  1546.°  The  district  court’s  findings  are  discussed
in  more  detail  in  the  Argument  below.

The  court  of  appeals  heard  oral  arguments  en  banc  on
June  18,  1975,  sitting  together  with  the  three-judge  dis-
trict  court,  and  both  courts  announced  their  decisions  on
August  15,  1975.  They  upheld  all  major  provisions  of
the  statutes.  With  respect  to  certain  provisions  dealing
with  the  powers  of  the  FEC,  they  held  that  appellants’
challenge  was  premature.
The  court  of  appeals  invali-
dated  one  peripheral  disclosure  provision  as  overbroad.‘
The  court  of  appeals  explained  its  decision  in  an  88-page
per  curiam  opinion,  joined  on  most  issues  by  six  of  the
eight  judges.  The  three-judge  district  court  did  not  issue
a  separate  opinion  but  relied  on  the  per  curiam  opinion
of  the  court  of  appeals.

Appellants  appealed  to  this  Court  from  the  decisions  of
both  lower  courts.  All  appellees  supported  the  request  to
note  probable  jurisdiction,  and  the  Court  noted  probable
jurisdiction  on  October  6,  1975.

6

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

Prematurity  and  Failure  of  Proof.

Section  315  of  the
FECA  did  not  and  could  not  empower  an  Article  LII
court  to  render  an  advisory  opinion.  Despite  the  broad
sweep  of  appellants’  claims,  few  of  the  effects  of  the
challenged  provisions  are  ready  for  judicial  review.  The
provisions  have  not  yet  had  a  chance  to  function  in  a
single  regular  election  year.  The  FEC  is  only  beginning
to  interpret  and  apply  them  to  concrete  situations,  and
none  of  its  interpretations  or  regulations  is  now  before
the  Court  for  review.

Appellants’  hypothetical  assertions  of  injury  and  dis-
crimination  have  not  actually  occurred  in  fact,  either
to  appellants  or  to  anyone  else,  and  there  is  no  proof
that  they  ever  will.  As  the  courts  below  found,  appellants’
own  deposition  testimony  and  the  record  of  past  elections
either  belies  their  predictions  or  is  at  worst  equivocal.
As  plaintiffs  attacking  the  constitutionality  of  a  new
statute,  appellants  have  failed  to  carry  their  burden
of  proving  that  the  mere  existence  of  the  statute  has
produced  or  thieatens  immediately  to  produce  the  in-
juries  they  claim.

This  Court’s  treatment  of  the  constitutionality  of  the
Hatch  Act  (5  U.S.C.  § 7324)  suggests  the  appropriate
judicial  response.  In  United  Public  Workers  v.  Mitchell,
330  U.S.  75  (1947),  the  Court  had  before  it  a  similarly
broad  challenge  to  that  statute’s  ban  on  political  ac-
tivities,  filed  soon  after  its  enactment.
The  Court  re-
jected  the  broad  challenge  on  the  ground  that  the  statute
served  compelling  public  interests  and  posed  only  a
“hypothetical  threat”  to  the  constitutional  rights  asserted
by  plaintiffs.  The  Court  resolved  only  the  narrow  issue
one  plaintiff  properly  posed,  saying  “[i]t  would  not  ac-

7

cord  with  judicial  responsibility  to  adjudge”  the  broader
issues  until  “definite  rights  appear  upon  the  one  side
and  definite  prejudicial  interferences  upon  the  other.”
Id.  at  90  (footnote  omitted).

Twenty-five  years  later,  another  challenge  to  the  Hatch
Civil  Service  Commission
Act  came  before  the  Court.
v.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548  (1973).
The  Court
reviewed  the  Commission’s  administration  of  the  Act
since  1940  and  the  detailed  interpretive  regulations  it had
issued  and  upheld  the  constitutionality  of  the  statute  and
the  regulations  “as  actually  applied  in  practice.”  Jd.  at
575.

Similar  principles  of  judicial  responsibility  apply  here.
To  the  extent  appellants  argue  that  the  challenged  pro-
visions  are  unconstitutional  on  their  face  because  Con-
gress  may  not  set  any  limits  on  campaign  contributions
and  expenditures  and  may  not  provide  any  public  financ-
ing  for  presidential  campaigns,  their  claims  can  and
should  be  adjudicated  now.
But  their  claims  should  be
rejected  to  the  extent  their  arguments  turn  on  actions
the  FEC  may  or  may  not  take  at  some  future  date,
or  on  their  unprovable  predictions  and  speculations  as
to  how  the  particular  statutory  limits  and  public  fi-
nancing  provisions  may  or  may  not  work  in  a  variety
of  presently  hypothetical  circumstances.

The  Basic  Congressional  Power.  Congress  has  the  ex-
press  constitutional  duty  to  make  laws  regulating  the
“Times,  Places  and  Manner”  of  electing  members  of  the
Senate  and  House  (Article  I,  §4),  and  the  same  im-
plied  power  for  presidential  elections.
Burroughs  v.
United  States,  290  U.S.  534  (1934).  “To  say  that  Con-
gress  is  without  power  to  pass  appropriate  legislation
to  safeguard  such  an  election  from  the  improper  use  of

8

9

money  to  influence  the  result  is  to  deny  to  the  nation
in  a  vital  particular  the  power  of  self-protection.”
Jd.
Since  the  central  purpose  of  the  challenged
at  545.
provisions  is  precisely  to  “safeguard”  federal  elections
“from  the  improper  use  of  money,”  they  are  clearly
within  the  lawmaking  power  unless  they  infringe  on
other  constitutional  rights.

The  Relationship  of  Money  to  Speech.  Appellants  ar-
gue  that  any  monetary  limits  on  campaign  giving  and
spending  have  impermissible  effects  on  political  speech.
The  challenged  provisions  place  no  limit  whatever  on  the
content  or  quantity  of  political  speech.  The  harms  they
seek  to  remedy  are  not  the  effects  of  unlimited  speech
on  the  integrity  of  the  political  process,  but  the  effects
These  laws  do  not  touch,  nor  are
of  unlimited  money.
their  objectives  frustrated  by,  any  actions  that  increase
the  reach  of  political  speech  without  further  escalating
the  cost  of  campaigns.’

Of  course,  it  is  true  that  the  more  money  a  candidate
has  available,  -the  more  he  might  try  to  disseminate
his  message.
If  unlimited  money  were  available,  some
candidates  would  spend  it  ali,  just  as  the  Pentagon  would
“fortify  the  moon.”  It  is only  in  this  sense  that  the  regu-
lation  of  campaign  money  has  any  effects  on  political
expression  and  other  constitutional  values  protected  by
the  First  Amendment.  We  submit  that  when  such  effects
are  entirely  incidental  to  the  purpose  of  the  statute—

*  For  example,  the  Federal  Communications  Commission’s  recent
reinterpretation  of  the  “equal  time”  rule  will  have  the  effect  of
expanding  the  reach  of  candidate  speech  at  no  cost  to  candidates
or  their  supporters.  But  it will  not  frustrate  the  objectives  of  these
laws,  because  the  harms  at  which  they  aim  do  not  arise  out  of  the
fact  that  candidates  speak  or  that  their  speech  reaches  many  voters.
See  pp.  89-91,  infra.

as  is true  here—they  can  be  justified  by compelling  needs
of  at  least  equal  constitutional  importance,  so  long  as
the  result  does  not  go  beyond  what  Congress  has  reason-
ably  determined  to  be  the  least  restrictive  means  of  meet-
ing  those  needs.  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367
(1968).  We  also  agree  that  the  congressional  judgments
as  to  the  existence  of  the  needs  and  the  selection  of  the
remedies  demand  close  judicial  scrutiny.  We  submit  that
these  very  strict  standards  are  met  by the  statutory  pro-
visions  now  ripe  for  review.

The  Compelling  Needs.  Compelling  public  needs—all
of  them  closely  related  to  First  Amendment  as  well  as
other  constitutional  values—sustain  the  validity  of  the
statutes  challenged  here.  The  primary  compelling  need
is  to  curb  the  undue  influence  of  a  wealthy  few  on  can-
didate  positions  and  on  government  actions—and  the  cor-
rosive  effect  of  both  the  fact  of  such  influence  and  its
appearance  on  the  public’s  confidence  in  the  integrity  of
its  elected  government.

The  compelling  need  to  check  public  cynicism  and  re-
store  public  confidence  in  the  integrity  of  government  has
been  rising  in  urgency  for  more  than  a  decade.  By  1974,
it  could  not  have  been  plainer.  Unlimited  campaign  giv-
ing  and  spending  had  created  a  powerful  magnetic  at-
traction  between  candidates  and  wealthy  individuals  and
groups.  It had  also  created  a  pervasive  and  in  some  cases
accurate  public  belief  that  candidates  who  seek  and  ob-
tain  the  disproportionate  support  of  the  wealthy  grant
government  favors  and  relieve  government  pressures  in
gratitude  or  exchange.  As  the  courts  below  found,  the
record  is  full  of  such  instances:
the  sordid  tale  of  the
milk  contributions  and  the  consequent  shift  in  govern-
mental]  policy  on  milk  price  supports;  the  outright  sale
of  ambassadorships;  the  dunning  of  businessmen  and

10

11

labor  leaders  by or  on  Yehalf  of  the  members  of  congres-
sional  committees  with  oversight  over  the  particular  in-
terests  of  those  solicited;  the  contributors  who  frankly
admitted  they  gave  te  those  with  governmental  power
over  their  special  interests  in  order  to  advance  or  defend
those  interests;  and  the  numerous  contributors  who  gave
to  opposing  candidates  running  for  the  same  office.

As  this  Court  noted  in  Civil  Service  Commission  V.
Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548,  565  (1973),  “it  is  not
only  important  that  the  Government  and  its  employees
in  fact  avoid  practicing  political  justice,  but  it  is  also
critical  that  they  appear  to  the  public  to  be  avoiding  it,
if  confidence  in  the  system  of  representative  government
is  not  to  be  eroded  to  a  disastrous  extent.”  What  applies
lo  the  apparent  behavior  of  civil  servants  applies  with
equal  force  to  the  apparent  behavior  of  our  most  im-
portant  elected  officials  and  of  candidates  for  these  high
elective  offices.

An  ancillary  compelling  need  is the  same  constitutional
interest  that  underpins  the  apportionment  and  voting
rights  eases—to  equalize  as  far  as  practicable  the  relative
ability  of  all  voters  to  affect  electoral  choices.  Nothing
much  can  be  done  about  some  differences  among  voters,
such  as  their  degree  of  political  interest,  their  skill  in
advocacy,  or  their  willingness  to  engage  in  campaign
work.  But  something  can  be  done  about  the  ability  of
the  wealthy  few  to  make  large  contributions  and  the
extreme,  almost  drug-like  dependence  of  candidates  on
obtaining  these  large  contributions  in  order  to  match
It  was  this  same
the  expenditures  of  their  opponents.
compelling  need  that  persuaded  this  Court  to  strike  down
the  large  state  filing  fees  held  unconstitutional  in  Bul-
lock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  144  (1972),  because  they

created  “disparity  in  voting  power  based  on  wealth”
between  the  “wealthy  few”  and  the  “unwealthy  many.”
See  Ct.  App.  Op.  1517.

The  courts  below  found  that  in  the  1974  congres-
sional  elections,  90  per  cent  of  all  campaign  contribu-
In
tions  came  from  less  than  1  per  cent  of  the  voters.
the  1972  elections,  the  1254  largest  contributors—less
than  0.001  percent  of  the  voting  age  population—ac-
counted  for  over  $51  million  in  campaign  contributions.
A  mere  153  individuals—less  than  one  two-thousandth
of  one  percent  of  those  who  voted  for  Mr.  Nixon—con-
tributed  some  $20  million  or  about  a  third  of  the  money
raised  in  his  campaign.

As  the  courts  below  found,  the  record  shows  that
candidates  must  spend  a  disproportionate  amount  of  their
time  and  effort  to  solicit  gifts  from  these  large  donors,  and
must  pay  a  disproportionate  amount  of  attention  to  their
views.  There  is  plainly  a  compelling  constitutional  need
to  prevent  the  wealthy  few  from  having  a  stronger  po-
litical  voice  per  vote  than  the  unwealthy  many—not  be-
cause  of  the  persuasiveness  of  their  ideas  or  how  many
other  voters  agree  with  them,  but  solely  because  they
give  and  spend  more  money  than  most  citizens  can
afford.

Yet  another  compelling  constitutiona!  interest  is served:
to  more  nearly  equalize  the  opportunity  of  all  interested
citizens  to  become  candidates  for  elective  federal  office,
and  to  reduce  the  barrier  that  lack  of  personal  wealth
or  access  to  the  wealth  of  others  now  presents.

As  the  courts  below  correctly  found,  the  skyrocket-
ing  political  “arms  race”  has  far  outstripped  the  pace
In  presidential  campaigns,  expenditures
of  inflation.

1Z

13

rose  from  $27  million  in  1960  to  $38  million  in  1964
to  $56  million  in  1968  to  $94  million  in  1972—an  in-
crease  of  210  percent  in  12  years,  five  times  the  41.3%
In  congressional
increase  in  the  consumer  price  index.
(Pp.  67-68,
races  expenditures  rose  at  comparable  rates.
infra).  A  political  analogue  of  Gresham’s  Law  has  been
at  work:  the  more  each  candidate  has  raised  and  spent,
the  more  his  opponent  has  raised  and  spent,  until  the
entire  process  has  been  cheapened  and  sheer  cost  has
become  a  major  barrier  to  political  entry.
Bullock  v.
Carter,  supra,  stands  squarely  for  the  constitutional
importance  of  lowering  this  barrier.

Some  reasonable  limits  on  individual  and  organizational
contributions  are  plainly  essential  if  we  are  to  moderate
the  undue  influence  of  large  contributors  on  candidates,
reduce  the  pernicious  appearance  of  such  influence,  and
expand  participation  in  the  political  process.  Any  restric-
tive  impact  of  equal  evenhanded  giving  and  spending
limits  on  freedom  of  expression  is  very  indirect.  Nothing
in  these  limits  touches  the  content  of  speech.  Giving  and
spending  money  are  not  speech.  Action,  however,  inter-
twined  with  speech,  is  not  entitled  to  the  full  and  special
protection  of  speech  itself.  Cox  v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.
559  (1965);  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367
(1968) ; Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77  (1949).  As  Pro-
fessor  Freund  has  said:

“The  right  to  speak  is,  I submit,  more  central  to
the  values  envisaged  by  the  First  Amendment  than
the  right  to  spend.  We  are  dealing  here  not  so  much
with  the  right  of  personal  expression  or  even  associa-
tion,  but  with  dollars  and  decibels.  And  just  as  the
volume  of sound  may  be limited  by law,  so  the  volume
of dollars  may  be limited,  without  violating  the  First
Amendment.”

Contribution  Limits.  The  most  significant  and  rep-
resentative  contribution  limit  in  the  new  statutes  is  the
$1,000  limit  on  individual  gifts  to  a  single  candidate.
Appellants  have  presented  no  convincing  proof  that  this
limit  is  unreasonable  on  its  face.  The  relatively  few
larger  than  $1,000
persons  who  make  contributions
have  had  disproportionate  influence  over  the  conduct  of
political  campaigns  and  the  actions  of  public  officials
after  their  election.  Contributions  of  the  scale  previously
permitted  create  the  appearance  and  widespread  public
belief  of  such  misconduct.
Stewart  Mott,  the  sole  ap-
pellant  who  has  contributed  and  intends  to  contribute
more  than  $1,000  per  candidate,  himself  attempted  to
exercise  such  influence  by  dangling  the  prospect  of  $1
million  in  contributions  if  a  presidential  candidate  would
endorse  views  compatible  with  Mott’s  own.

Moreover,  contrary  to  appellants’  allegations,  neither
challengers  nor  minor  parties  depend  more  on  contribu-
tions  over  $1,000  than  the  incumbents  or  major  parties
As  the  courts  below  found,  the  record
they  oppose.
shows  that  if  the  excess  over  $1,000  of  all  individual
contributions  were  eliminated  from  the  totals  raised  in
the  1972  and  1974  elections,  challengers  and  minor  party
and  independent  candidates  would  have  been  financially
better  off  or  at  least  no  worse  off  relative  to  incumbents
and  major  party  candidates  than  they  were  without
the  limitation.  The  $1,000  limitation  has  not  been  shown
to  discriminate  against  any  class  of  which  an  appellant
is  a  member,  invidiously  or  otherwise.

Expenditure  Ceilings.  The  expenditure  ceilings  in  the
challenged  statutes  serve  compelling  constitutional  needs.
As  the  courts  below  found,  they  reinforce  the  contribu-

14

15

tion  limits  and  help  to  prevent  their  wholesale  evasion.
They  also  put  an  effective  stop  to  the  skyrocketing  po-
litical  money  race,  and  lower  the  barrier  to  entry  of
additional  candidates  that  the  increasing  cost  of  cam-
paigning  now  raises.  They  do  not  touch  the  content  of
candidate  speech  or  prevent  any  candidate  from  speaking
as  much  as  he  wants.
True,  candidates  will  be  equally
limited  in  the  number  of  dollars  they  can  spend  to
amplify  their  voices,  just  as  contributors  are  limited  in
the  dollars  they  may  give.  But  in  Professor  Freund’s
phrase,  the  spending  ceilings  deal  merely  with  “dollars
and  decibels,”  not  with  the  content  or  quantity  of  speech
itself.

Appellants  have  not  shown  that  these  equal  spending
ceilings  will  deny  ample  opportunity  for  candidates  to
speak  freely  and  to  conduct  fully  effective  campaigns.
As  the  courts  below  found,  in  only  a  small  fraction  of
the  1972  and  1974  congressional  races  did  one  or  both
candidates  exceed  the  spending  limits  that  will  apply  to
In  the  1975  special
future  races  under  the  new  laws.
Senate  election  in  New  Hampshire,  conducted  under  the
new  laws,  both  candidates  lived  within  the  spending
limits  and  managed  to  turn  out  a  vote  much  higher  than
they  turned  out  in  the  1974  election  when  the  new  spend-
ing  limits  did  not  apply.  The  new  presidential  limits
were  exceeded  in  1972,  but  the  very  excesses  of this  cam-
paign  are  what  most  shocked  the  nation  and  spurred
Congress  to  provide  a  remedy.  And  Mr.  Nixon’s  own
Finance  Chairman  has  testified  that  in  his  judgment  an
amount  within  the  new  limits  would  have  been  sufficient
to  conduct  the  1972  campaign.  P.  70,  infra.  Moreover,
as  shown  below,  the  presidential  spending  limits  are  im-
portant  to  the  public  financing  portion  of  the  Act.

Ironically,  one  basis  of  appellants’  attack  is  that  the
ceilings  are  totally  evenhanded.
Appellants  argue  that
this  equality  is  unconstitutional  because  challengers  need
to  spend  more  than  incumbents,  and  minor  party  and
independent  candidates  need  to  spend  more  than  major
party  candidates.  But,  as  the  courts  below  found,  nothing
in  the  record  begins  to  prove  that  in  practice  equal  ceil-
ings  will  disfavor  any  appellant  or  any  class  such  as
challengers  for  office  or  third  party  candidates.  To  the
contrary,  in  view  of  the  fact  that  incumbents  and  major
party  candidates  generally  have  outspent  challengers  and
minor  party  and  independent  candidates,  spending  ceil-
ings  will  tend  to  reduce  the  spending  advantages  of  the
former  classes.  Appellants  have  shown  no  unreasonable
restriction  or  invidious  discrimination  in  the  expenditure
ceilings  to  outweigh  the  important  First  Amendment  and
other  constitutional  values  they  serve.

As  the  courts  below  found,  the  $1,000  limit  on  “in-
dependent”  expenditures  by  an  individual  to  promote  the
election  or  defeat  of  a  candidate  stands  on  the  same
constitutional  footing  as  a  limitation  on  contributions.
Indeed,  it  is  essential  to  close  an  otherwise  yawning
gap  in  the  limitations  on  contributions,  since  a  donor
could  otherwise  spend  as  much  as  he  wished  for  the
creation  and  broadcasting  of  candidate  “commercials”
instead  of  giving  the  same  amount  for  the  same  purpose
to  the  candidate’s  campaign  committee.  This  Court  should
uphold  the  provision  on  its  face  and  decline  to  rule  on
any  hypothetical  applications  of  it  that  are  not  now  pre-
sented  in  a  concrete  case  or  controversy,  for  the  reasons
stated  herein  and  in  the  Brief  for  Amici  Curiae  Senators
Scott  and  Kennedy.

Disclosure  Requirements.

stitutional  problems.

Disclosure  presents  few  con-
In  the  oft-quoted  phrase  of  Mr.

16

17

Justice  Brandeis,  sunlight  is  still  “the  best  of  disin-
fectants.”  Citizens  have  a  clear  First  Amendment  right
to  know  who  contributes  substantial  amounts  to  each
candidate’s  campaign  and  how  much  each  candidate  raises
and  spends.  And  the  availability  of  this  information
helps  the  press  to  be  an  effective  watchdog  of government.

Nothing  but  speculation  supports  appellants’  suggestion
that  disclosure  will  “chill”  contributions.  Although  dis-
closure  requirements  for  contributions  over  $100  have
been  on  the  statute  books  since  1910,  appellants  have
produced  no  significant  example  of  an  actual
“chil-
Courts  have  repeatedly  rejected  such  at-
ling”  effect.
tacks  on  campaign  disclosure  laws.  E.g.,  United  States
v.  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President,  507  F.2d
1194  (D.C.  Cir.  1974).  The  reporting  requirements  were
adopted  for  an  express  and  proper  public  purpose,  not
for  some  ulterior  and  improper  deterrent  purpose,  as  in
NAACP  v.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958).  They  are
fundamental  protections  reasonably  related  to  the  pub-
lic’s  First  Amendment  right  to  know  and  the  need  to
enforce  the  remainder  of  this  reform  legislation.

As  the  courts  below  found,  the  record  provides  no
basis  for  the  Court  to  second-guess  the  judgment  of  Con-
gress  in  setting  the  disclosure  floor  at  contributions  of
more  than  $100,  particularly  since  the  record  does  demon-
strate  that  disclosure  of  relatively  small  contributions
ascists  in  detecting  cumulative  contributions  from  closely
associated  persons.

Similarly,  the  record  provides  no  basis  for  concluding
that  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  should  be
exempt  from  the  requirements  of  disclosure.
Citizens
have  no  less  need  for  such  information  merely  because  a
party’s  or  candidate’s  adherents  are  not  numerous.  Minor

party  and  independent  candidates  often  have  an  ap-
preciable  impact  on  elections.
Moreover,  any  such  ex-
emption  would  create  an  enormous  loophole  and  a  strong
incentive  toward  party  fractionalization  and  instability.

Least  Restrictive  Means.  Appellants  suggest  that  laws
against  bribery  and  laws  requiring  the  disclosure  of  large
contributions  to  the  major  parties  are  sufficient,  and
that  the  provisions  of  the  challenged  statutes  limiting
contributions  and  expenditures  and  requiring  broader
disclosure  amount  to  unconstitutional  legislative  overkill.
But  anti-bribery  and  disclosure  laws  have  been  on  the
books  for  most  of  the  last  fifty  years  without  solving
the  evils  they  address.  They  were  in  effect  throughout
the  1972  campaign,  but  they  did  little  to  lessen  the  de-
pendence  of  candidates  and  officeholders  on  large  con-
tributors,  to  prevent  large  contributors  from  wielding  or
appearing  to  wield  undue  influence  over  government
actions,  or  to  slow  the  accelerating  upward  spiral  of
candidate  expenditures  and  the  barrier  it  creates  to
entry  by  new  candidates.

This  Court  has  upheld  laws  more  restrictive  of  po-
litical  expression  than  those  challenged  here,  where  neces-
sary  to  meet  similarly  compelling  needs.
The  Hatch
Act’s  direct  prohibitions  of  campaign  activity  by  civil
servants,  including  many  activities  well  within  the  um-
brella  of  the  First  Amendment,  have  been  upheld  be-
cause  of  the  compelling  need  to  serve  the  constitutional
Civil
value  of  impartiality  in  public  administration.
Service  Commission  Vv.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548
(1973).  And  despite  many  opportunities  to  rule  on  the
point,  this  Court  has  never  struck  down  the  statutes
directly  prohibiting  campaign  contributions  and  expendi-
tures  by corporations  and  labor  unions—prohibitions  that
certainly  affect  First  Amendment  rights.  Many  opinions

——

18

19

of  this  Court  recognize  the  compelling  need  to  prevent
the  aggregated  interests  of  such  large  special  interest
groups  from  improperly  influencing  the  outcome  of  fed-
eral  elections.  See  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter’s  opinion  in
United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567  (1957),  and  the
dissenting  opinion  of  Mr.  Justice  Douglas  at  598  n.  2.

The  elected  members  of  Congress  are  certainly  as
expert  as  any  branch  of  government  in  determining  the
measures  necessary  to  regulate  the  financing  of  elections
so  as  to  prevent  corruption,  to  remove  “disparities  in
voting  power  based  on  wealth”  (Bullock  v.  Carter,  405
U.S.  134,  144  (1972)  ), and  to  maintain  public  confidence
in  the  integrity  of the  electoral  process  and  of  elected  offi-
cials.  Congress  has  tried  varying  combinations  of  laws
over  the  past  50  years  and  has  learned  from  experience
what  does  and  does  not  work.  With  the  grim  record  of
the  1972  and  earlier  election  scandals  before  it,  Congress
devised  the  comprehensive  and  complementary  set  of
measures  now  challenged.  As  we  show  and  as  the  court
of  appeals  properly  concluded,  neither  the  facts  of  record
nor  the  wisdom  of  judicial  experience  affords  any  basis
for  concluding  that  Congress  selected  means  more  re-
strictive  than  necessary  to  meet  the  compelling  needs  it
faced.

Public  Financing  of  Presidential  Campaigns.  The  pub-
lic  financing  provisions  of  the  new  law  are  a  creative
step  toward  eliminating  the  dependence  of  presidential
candidates  on  private  money  to  fuel  their  campaigns.
Financial  assistance  to  campaigns  for  federal  office  is  a
proper  use  of  the  congressional  power  to  raise  and  spend
money  to  provide  for  the  general  welfare.  It  effectuates
the  purposes  of  Articles  I and  I]—as  well  as  the  First
Amendment—by  permitting  candidates  to  reduce  their  de-
biitating  dependence  on  large  private  contributions.

Appellants  asserting  interests  in  presidential  campaigns
have  failed  to  prove  that  the  public  financing  provisions
will  injure  them  in  any  way,  or  that  they  will  tilt  the
balance  of  political  power  against  any  class  of  which  ap-
pellants  are  a  part.  While  the  provisions  draw  distinctions
between  major,  minor,  and  new  parties,  a  failure  to  draw
such  distinctions  would  violate  common  sense.  “Sometimes
the  grossest  discrimination  can  lie  in  treating  things
that  are  different  as  though  they  were  exactly  alike.”
Jenness  Vv.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  481,  442  (1971).  Com-
pelling  interests  justify  awarding  differential  assistance
to  different  types  of  parties  based  on  their  electoral
support,  and  denying  assistance  to  parties  and  non-party
candidates  that  have  not  established  “a  significant  modi-
cum  of  support.”  Jd.  at  442.  The  distinctions  in  the
statute  are  objective  and  nondiscriminatory  on  their  face.
They  leave  all  parties  free  to  move  from  one  level  of
subsidy  to  another  on  the  basis  of  their  demonstrated
popular  support.  The  statute  in  no  way  impairs  “the
potential  fluidity  of  American  political  life.”  Jd.  at  439.
Virtually  identical  distinctions  have  been  upheld  in  laws
defining  the  rights  of  candidates  to  be  named  on  the
ballot  in  Jenness,  supra;  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724
(1974);  and  American  Party  v.  White,  415  U.S.  767
(1974).

Under  the  challenged  provisions,  candidates  of  parties
which  in  the  previous  election  achieved  more  than  the
five  percent  “significant  modicum  of  support”  approved
in  Jenness  will  receive  public  financing  during  the  cam-
paign  itself.  Since  such  funding  would  be  impracticable
for  new  parties  and  parties  which  achieved  less  than  a
“significant  modicum”  in  the  previous  election,  those
parties  remain  free  to  obtain  and  spend  private  con-
tributions  before  the  election;  in  addition,  they  are  pro-

20

21

convention  financing  provisions  has  been  shown  to  dis-
criminate  invidiously  against  any  appellant.

*

*

*

«

The  Constitution  does  not  condemn  the  nation  to  con-
tinue  suffering  old  abuses  that  reached  new  extremes  in
the  1972  campaign.  As  the  court  of  appeais  concluded,
“these  latest  efforts  on  the  part  of  our  government  to
cleanse  its  democratic  processes  should  at  least  be  given
a  chance  to  prove  themselves.  Certainly  they  should  not
be  rejected  because  they  might  have  some  incidental,  not
clearly  defined,  effect  on  First  Amendment  freedoms.  To
do  so  might  be  Aesopian  in  the  sense  of  the  dog  losing
his  bone  going  after  its  deceptively  larger  reflection  in  the
water.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1582.

vided  public  financing  after  the  election  on  the  basis
of  the  support  they  have  proven.
Equal  protection  of
the  laws  cannot  require  more  equality  than  the  circum-
stances  make  possible.

Appellants’  theoretical  arguments  about  discrimination
are  refuted  by  the  facts  of  previous  elections.  The  chal-
lenged  statutes  impose  a  ceiling  of  $20  million  on  the
amount  that  any  presidential  candidate  may  spend,
whether  or  not  he  accepts  public  financing.  In  1972,  well
before  the  election,  both  major  party  candidates  had
easily  raised  funds  in  excess  of  the  new  ceilings,  even
when  the  excess  over  $1,000  of  large  contributions  is  ex-
cluded  from  the  calculation.
There  is  no  showing  that
absent  the  challenged  legislation  they  would  fail  to  do  so
in  1976.  Pre-election  entitlements  of public  funds  in  1976
will  therefore  not  increase  the  financial  advantages  of
major  party  presidential  candidates  over  other  candidates.
To  the  contrary,  the  public  financing  and  expenditure
limits  of  the  new  laws  will  reduce  those  advantages.

The  new  laws  also  provide  public  financing  for  party
conventions  and  assistance  to  all  candidates  in  party  pri-
maries  through  matching  of  small  private  contributions.
Public  financing  for  the  costs  of  conducting  these  early
stages  of  the  electoral  process  has  already  been  upheld.
American  Party  v.  White,  supra.  The  matching  mecha-
nism  used  to  allocate  primary  assistance  is  a  traditional
feature  of  federal  assistance  programs,  and  provides  a
valid  means  of  assessing  a  candidate’s  popular  support.
The  statute’s  reasonable  eligibility  requirements  for  as-
sistance  in  the  primaries,  as  in  the  general  election,  are
plainly  necessary  to  avoid  waste  of  public  money  on  the
frivolous  candidacies  that  would  arise  in  the  absence
of  such  requirements.  No  feature  of  these  primary  and

22

ARGUMENT

I.  APPELLANTS  HAVE  SHOWN  NO  REASON  WHY
THIS  COURT  SHOULD  INVALIDATE  THE  CHAL-
LENGED  LEGISLATION  BEFORE  IT  HAS  BEEN
GIVEN  A  CHANCE  TO  WORK

Appellants  filed  this  suit  the  day  after  the  FECA
Amendments  took  effect,  in  an  effort  to  wipe  the  chal-
lenged  statutes  off  the  books  before  they  could  be  tested
in  actual  operation.  Indeed,  appellant  Buckley,  who  un-
successfully  opposed  the  laws  in  Congress,  introduced
an  expedited  judicial  review  provision  to  facilitate  his
design  to  challenge  the  congressional  decision  in  the
courts.°

Appellants  rushed  to  court  with  three  basic  attacks
First,  they  argue  that  Congress  has
on  the  statutes.
no  power  to  regulate  campaign  contributions  and  expendi-
tures  or  to  provide  public  financing  for  campaigns—under
any  compelling  circumstances  and  regardless  of  the  spe-
cific  statutory  provisions.  Congress  may  not,  appellants
argue,  impose  any  limit  on  the  size  of  contributions  to
federal  candidates.  It  may  not  impose  any  ceiling  on  a
candidate’s  spending  to  seek  federal  office.  And  it  may
not  provide  any  form  of  public  financing  to  reduce  can-
didates’  dependence  on  large  private  contributions.  Ap-
pellants  do  concede  that  Congress  may  impose  some  re-
quirements  to  disclose  the  identity  of contributors.

Second,  appellants  argue  that  the  particular  provisions
of  these  statutes  are  constitutionally  infirm.  They  assert
that  the  contribution  and  expenditure  limits  are  too  low

to  permit  “effective”  campaigns.  They  argue  that  the

*  Buckley  Deposition  Tr.  at  38-39;  120  Cong.  Rec.  S5702-08  (daily

ed.  April  10,  1974).

23

limits—which  are  facially  evenhanded—are  intended  to
favor  and  will  in  fact  favor  incumbents  over  challengers
and  major  party  candidates  over  independent  and  minor
party  candidates.  They  also  argue  that  the  public  financ-
ing  mechanism  has  discriminatory  effects  and  that  the
disclosure  thresholds  are  set  so  low  as  to  restrict  the
right  of  political  association.

Third,  they  attack  the  composition  and  powers  of  the
FEC—an  attack  that  is  answered  by  the  FEC  itself
and  the  Amici  Curiae  Senators  Scott  and  Kennedy  and
that  we  do  not  address.

This  case  requires  the  Court  to  make  much  less  new
constitutional  law  than  appellants  suggest.  Some  purely
legal  issues  seem  ripe  for  decision—such  as  those  raised
by  appellants’  claim  that  their  rights  are  threatened
because  any  limits  on  contributions  or  expenditures  and
any  public  financing  are  unconstitutional.
The  Court
should  reject  appellants’  extreme  position  on  those  issues,
as  did  six  of  the  eight  judges  on  the  court  of  appeals.
Appellants’  contentions  about  the  future  consequences  of
the  specific  statutory  provisions  should  be  rejected  as
speculative  and  unproven.  Precedent  and  sound  judicial
policy  dictate  that  major  congressional  enactments  should
be  tested  on  their  actual—not  hypothetical—results.

A.  Appellants’  “Proof”  of  Harm  is Nothing  More  Than
Allegations  of  Their  Own  Beliefs  and  Speculation
About  Possible  Effects  of  the  Statutes

Appellants  attack  virtually  every  provision  of  these
statutes,  alleging  that  each  provision  will  cause  con-
But  ap-
stitutional  harm  to  one  or  more  appellants.
pellants  bring  to  this  Court  little  more  than  theories  and
beliefs  to  throw  against  congressional  judgments  based
on  a  decade  of  legislative  inquiry  and  over  fifty  years

of  electoral  abuses.  Appellants’  Br.  14-24."  Appellants’

by  the  courts  below,  appellants’  “proof”  is  speculative
and  insubstantial,  particularly  in  comparison  to  the  com-
pelling  interests  the  challenged  statutes  serve.

718.

Appellants  also  challenge  the  $5,000  limit  on  contribu-
tions  by  political  committees,  yet  they  produce  no  evi-
dence  that  they  will  be  harmed  by this  limit.  Senator  Mc-
Carthy  does  not  know  how  many  contributions  in  excess
of  $5,000  he  received  in  1968;  nor  does  he  have  in-
stitutional  support  for  his  1976  race.  Findings  III,  Mc-
Carthy  979,  23,  25,  IIB  J.A.  715,  717-18.  Congressman
Steiger  received  less  than  $5,000  from  his  party  in
1966.  Findings  III,  Steiger  § 20,  IIB  J.A.  732.

Appellants  have  also  failed  to  demonstrate  their  re-
liance  on  contributions  in  excess  of  $1,000  in  their  ca-

calendar  year.  Findings  III,  Buckley  §§ 37,  39,  IIB  J.A.  707.

or  the
been  candidates  for  election  to  federal  office,

appears  to  have  exceeded  what  would  have  been  the

3.  The  “Harm”  Allegedly  Caused  by
Disclosure

Disclosure  of contributions  over  $100  has  been  required
since  1910.  Yet,  as  the  court  of appeals  found,  the  record
contains  “only  scant  evidence  tending  to  show a  ‘chilling
effect’  resulting  from  disclosure,  and  only  a  skimpy  indi-
cation  of  harassment  of  those  whose  contributions  were
disclosed  in  the  1972  and  1974  elections.  ..  .”  Ct.  App.
Op.  1546  (footnote  omitted).

The  First  Amended  Complaint  is  virtually  devoid  of
any  allegation  that  any  appellant  will  cease  making  po-
litical  contributions  because  of  the  disclosure  provisions
of  the  challenged  statutes.  On  the  contrary,  the  First
Amended  Complaint,  appellants’  affidavits,  and  appellants’
deposition  testimony  demonstrate  that  appellants  have
contributed  and  intend  to  contribute  reportable  amounts
in  the  future  despite  the  disclosure  requirements.

Appellant  Mott  testified  that  he  favors  strong  report-
ing  and  disclosure  requirements  such  as  those  contained
in  the  statute  he  challenges.  Mott  Deposition  Tr.  at  30-
31.  Indeed,  Mott’s  description  of  his  past  contributions
in  his  affidavit  demonstrates  that  disclosure  has  no  de-
terrent  effect  whatsoever  on  him.

Furthermore,  the  appellant  candidates  and  parties  have
failed  to  prove  that  the  statute  will  deter  contributions  to
them.  The  district  court  was  unable  to  find,  with  one  in-
consequential  exception,  any  facts  tending  to  prove  that  a
potential  contributor  has  been  deterred  from  contribut-
ing  by  disclosure  or  that  a  contributor  has  been  sub-
jected  to  any  reprisal  in  consequence  of  disclosure.’*  Ap-

©The  single  exception  was  based  on  hearsay.  Two  persons
solicited  by  a  Ms.  Wertheimer  for  a  contribution  to  a  Libertarian
Party  gubernatorial  campaign  in  New  York  purportedly  refused
to  contribute  because  they  were  unwilling  to  have  their  names

33

pellant  Buckley  testified  that  he  has  never  heard  that
any  individual  has  refused  to  contribute  to  his  cam-

campaigns  because  of  disclosure.  Buckley  Deposition  Tr.
at  71-72.  See  also  Findings  III,  Buckley  {{ 26,  33,  IIB
J.A.  705,  706.

that  any  individual  whose  name  had  been  disclosed  as  a
contributor  to  his  party  had  been  subjected  to  harass-

4.  The  “Harm”  Allegedly  Caused  by  Public
Financing  of Presidential  Campaigns

The  district  court’s  findings  show  that  no  appellant
is  in  a  position  to  be  harmed  by  the  public  financing
provisions.  McCarthy  is  an  independent  or  “new  party”
candidate”  and  testified  he  will  not  accept  public  fi-

18  See  pp.  223-25,  infra.

34

35

nancing  for  his  presidential  campaign  even  if  he  re-
ceives  enough  votes  to  qualify  for  it  after  the  election.
Findings  III,  McCarthy  930,  IIB  J.A.  718.  Appellant
Libertarian  Party  will  also  refuse  public  funds  if  it
qualifies  after  the  1976  presidential  election.
Crane
Deposition  Tr.  at  32-33,  62-63.

Appellant  Conservative  Party  has  never  nominated  a
candidate  for  President  who  was  not  also  the  presidential
candidate  of  one  of  the  two  major  parties  (Findings  III,
Mahoney  { 15,  IIB  J.A.  711;  Mahoney  Deposition  Tr.  at
39-40)  and  thus  independently  entitled  to  the  maximum
amount  of  public  financing.  26  U.S.C.  § 9004(a)  (1).

B.  There  Are  No  “Unique  Factors”  That  Render  These
Laws  Constitutionally  Suspect

Appellants  devote  much  of  their  brief  to  the  contention
that  the  court  of  appeals  “failed  to  give  this  legislation
adequate  scrutiny.”  E.g.,  Appellants’  Br.  36.  There  is  no
dispute  in  this  case  about  the  standard  of  review.  These
statutes  have  incidental  effects  on  vital  First  Amendment
interests  and  must—like  any  statutes  affecting  similar
interests—be  shown  to  serve  “compelling  needs”  and  to
be  no  more  restrictive  than  necessary  to  serve  those
The  court  of  appeals  expressly  applied  those
needs.
standards.
It  agreed  with  appellants  that  “strict  ju-
dicial  scrutiny  of  the  challenged  provisions  is  appropri-
ate.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1519.

The  court  of  appeals  “closely  scrutinized  Plaintiffs’
contentions”  and  concluded  that  these  statutes  serve  “a
clear  and  compelling  interest  in  safeguarding  the  in-
tegrity  of  elections  and  avoiding  the  unnecessary  in-
fluence  of  wealth”  and  that  “the  statute  taken  as  a
whole  affirmatively  enhances  First  Amendment  values.”

@ EMO
Beer
Gh rman

Id.  at  1517.  As  we  show  in  Part  IV,  these  conclusions
are  correct.

Appellants  say  that  Congress  in  enacting  campaign
reform  laws  faces  “a  plain  conflict  of  interest,”  because
such  laws  may  “affect  the  political  balance  between
challengers  and  incumbents.”  Appellants’  Br.  27.  They
suggest  repeatedly  that  these  laws  are  an  attempt  by
incumbents  to  guarantee  their  own  reelection.  That  sug-
gestion  defames  the  national  legislature  and  is  unsup-
ported  by the  record.

It  is  settled  that  “this  Court  will  not  strike  down  an
otherwise  constitutional  statute  on  the  basis  of  an  alleged
illicit  legislative  motive.”  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391
U.S.  367,  383  (1968);  Arizona  v.  California,  283  U.S.
423,  456  (1931)
(Brandeis,  J.).  The  Court  said  in
O’Brien:

“Inquiries  into  congressional  motives  or  purposes
are  a  hazardous  matter.  When  the  issue  is  simply
the  interpretation  of  legislation,  the  Court  will  look
to  statements  by  legislators  for  guidance  as  to  the
purpose  of  the  legislature,  because  the  benefit  to
sound  decision-making  in  this  circumstance  is thought
sufficient  to  risk  the  possibility  of  misreading  Con-
gress’  purpose.  It is entirely  a  different  matter  when
we  are  asked  to  void  a  statute  that  is,  under  well-
settled  criteria,  constitutional  on  its  face,  on  the  basis
of  what  fewer  than  a  handful  of  Congressmen  said
about  it.  What  motivates  one  legislator  to  make  a
speech  about  a  statute  is  not  necessarily  what  moti-
vates  scores  of  others  to  enact  it,  and  the  stakes  are
sufficiently  high  for  us  to  eschew  guesswork.”  391
U.S.  at  383-84  (footnote  omitted).

These  statutes  are  completely  evenhanded  as  between  in-
cumbents  and  challengers  and  among  candidates  of  all

36

37

points  of view.  The  available  evidence  suggests  they  will
help,  not  hinder,  challengers  in  their  attempt  to  match
See  pp.  112-29,  143-51,  infra.
incumbents’  spending.
Thus,  even  if  some  incumbent  congressmen  had  hoped
to  benefit  themselves,  that  hope  would  be  no  ground  for
invalidating  the  laws.

In  any  event,  the  members  of  Congress  did  not  conceive
and  enact  these  laws  to  promote  their  own  financial  ad-
Since  incumbents  have
vantages  in  future  campaigns.
always  had  a great  advantage  in fund-raising,  inertia  and
