sentatives  that  a  vote  against  campaign  financing  reform
would  anger  their  constituents.”

19  Appellants’  insinuation  that  incumbents  passed  these  laws  to
protect  themselves  is  countered  by  appellants’  own  argument,  made
elsewhere,  that  the  laws  result  from  the  overreaction  of  politicians
to  intense  public  sentiment  arising  out  of  Watergate.  Appellants’
Br.  37-38.

20  Public  sentiment  increasingly  favors  these  laws.  See  Findings
I,  7 133,  IIA  J.A.  180-89.  Appellants  argue  that  “the  latest  Harris
poll”  is  not  a  guide  to  interpreting  the  First  Amendment.  Appel-
lants’  Br.  38-39.  We  agree.  However,  the  growing  majority  of  the
American  public  that  supports  these  laws  refutes  appellants’  claim
that  Congress  enacted  them  in  its  own  interest  rather  than  that
of  the  public.  It  also  refutes  appellants’  irrelevant  assertion  that
“Ttjhe  tide”  has  turned  against  this  legislation.  Jd.  at  74.

Major  newspapers  across  the  country  continue  to  voice  strong
editorial  support  for  these  laws.  E.g.,  “Hope  for  the  Election  Reform
Act,”  The  Louisville  Times,  Aug.  19,  1975;  “A  Chance  to  Prove
Itself,”  Philadelphia  Bulletin,  Aug.  20,  1975;  “Good  News  on  the
Campaign  Front,”  Baltimore  Sun,  Aug.  19,  1975;  “Election  Reform
Upheld,”  Albuquerque  Journal,  Aug.  17,  1975;  “A  Right  to  Spend?,”
Des  Moines  Register,  Sept.  7,  1975;  “Free  Speech,  Big  Money,”

AE Ree ree ee ee ne

Moreover,  reality  contradicts  appellants’  notion  that
Congress  is  a  monolithic  group  of  incumbents,  all  of
whom  share  the  identical  interest  in  the  reelection  of  all.
Congress  consists  of  535  individuals,  each  with  his  own
constituency,  ideology,  party—and  political  problems.  The
self-interest  of  an  individual  Congressman  depends  on
such  highly  variable  factors  as  his  personal  wealth,  his
access  to  wealthy  contributors,  and  his  perception  of  how
his  next  political  rival  will  fare  on  those  counts.  There
is  no  reason  for  this  Court  to  assume  that  these  facially
evenhanded  statutes  are  an  ingenious  attempt  by  con-
gressional  incumbents  to  pull  the  ladder  up  after  them-
selves,  and  that  incumbent  appellants  Buckley  and  Steiger
stand  alone  in  virtuous  self-denial.

Under  our  Constitution,  Congress  is  the  source  of  all
legislation.  To  suggest  that  campaign  reform  laws  are
inherently  suspect  because  they  will  affect  the  members
who  vote  on  them  is  to  argue  that  the  Constitution  per-
mits  no  legislative  remedy  for  abuses  of  the  electoral
process.  That  nihilistic  proposition  answers  itself.

C.  Settled

Judicial  Policy  Dictates  That  These  Statutes
Should  Be  Given  a  Chance  to  Operate  as  Congress
Intended

After  years  of intensive  study,  Congress  concluded  that
the  evils  plaguing  the  electoral  process  present  a  com-
pelling  need  for  an  effective  remedy.  Congress  studied

The  New  York  Times,  Aug.  16,  1975;  “The  Election  Reform  Act
in  Court,”  Sacramento  Bee,  Aug.  31,  1975;  “A  Boost  for  Campaign
Reform,”  Christian  Science  Monitor,  Aug.  21,  1975;  “Costly  Cam-
paigns  and  Free  Speech,”  The  Washington  Post,  Aug.  21,  1975;
“Money  and  Politics,”  Newark  Star  Ledger,  Aug.  19,  1975.

Congress  is  preparing  to  take  a  further  step  by  extending  public

financing  to  congressional  campaigns.  See  note  113,  infra.

38

and  tried  many  alternatives  and  decided  that  the  reforms
achieved  by  the  challenged  statutes  will  work  without
producing  the  harmful  effects  that  appellants  predict.
Congress  is  in  a  far  better  position  than  are  either  the
appellants  or  this  Court  to  make  these  essentially  practi-
cal,  factual  assessments  of  the  probable  effects  the  laws
will  have.  At  least  until  the  actual  effects  can  be  seen,
the  Court  should  defer  to  those  legislative  judgments.

“TI)t  is clear  to  us  that  proper  regard for  the  special
function  of  Congress  in  making  determinations  of
legislative  fact  compels  this  Court  to  respect  those
determinations  unless  they  are  contradicted  by  evi-
dence  far  stronger  than  anything  that  has  been  ad-
duced  ....”  Oregon  Vv.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  240
(1970)  (Brennan,  White  &  Marshall,  JJ.).”

The  Court  followed  such  a  course  in  upholding  the  Vot-
ing  Rights  Act  of  1965  in  South  Carolina  v.  Katzenbach,
383  U.S.  301  (1966).  In  that  case,  a  sweeping  and  inno-
vative  Act  of  Congress  was  challenged  on  the  ground  that
it  abridged  constitutional  rights  as  fundamental  as  the
First  Amendment  rights  asserted  here.  The  Court  sus-
tained  the  law,  placing  much  reliance  on  congressional
findings  and  judgments  very  similar  to  those
that
The  Court
prompted  Congress  to  pass  these  statutes.
said:

“The  constitutional  propriety  of  the  Voting  Rights
Act  of  1965  must  be  judged  with  reference  to  the
historical  experience  which  it reflects.”  Jd.  at  308.

21  See  also  California  Bankers  Ass'n  Vv.  Shultz,  416  U.S.  21,  75-76
(1974);  Golden  v.  Zwickler,  394  U.S.  103,  110  (1969);  United
States  v.  Finance  Comm.  to  Re-Elect  the  President,  507  F.2d  1194,
1200  (D.C.  Cir.  1974) ; Gomez  v.  Wilson,  477  F.2d  411,  416  (D.C.
Cir.  1973)  ; Cox,  The  Role  of Congress  in Constitutional  Determina-
tions,  40  U.  Cin.  L.  Rev.  199,  209  (3971).

The  Court  accepted  the  congressional  judgment  about  the
need  for  the  law,  emphasizing  two  points:  that  “Congress
felt  itself  confronted  by an  insidious  and  pervasive  evil,”
and  that  “Congress  concluded  that  the  unsuccessful  reme-
dies  which  it had  prescribed  in  the  past  would  have  to  be
replaced  by sterner  and  more  elaborate  measures.”  Id.  at
309-19.  The  situation  is  identical  here.

Many  of  appellants’  arguments  attack  congressional
judgments  about  where  the  lines  should  be  drawn  to
achieve  effective  reform.  Congress  is peculiarly  well  suited
to  make  those  judgments,  especially  where,  as  here,  the
permissibility  of  the  lines  drawn  turns  less  on  abstract
constitutional  principles  than  on  the  practical  impact  of
the  laws  in  operation.  Courts  traditionally  eschew  sec-
ond-guessing  Congress  in  such  situations,  even  where  the
lines  drawn  by  Congress  are  alleged  to  violate  First
Amendment  or  other  fundamental  rights.”

.

.  contingent  events  .

The  Court  has  applied  this  principle  in  First  Amend-
ment  litigation  strikingly  similar  to  the  case  at  bar.  When
a  challenge  to  the  Hatch  Act  first  came  here,  it was  based
on  allegations  of  hypothetical  First  Amendment  injury
comparable  to  those  of  the  present  case.  The  Court  re-
fused  to  declare  the  Act  unconstitutional:

“The  power  of courts,  and  ultimately  of this  Court,
to  pass  upon  the  constitutionality  of  acts  of  Congress
arises  only  when  the  interests  of litigants  require  the
use  of  this  judicial  authority  for  their  protection
against  actual  interference.  A  hypothetical  threat  is
not  enough.  We  can  only  speculate  as  to  the  kinds  of
political  activity  the  appellants  desire  to  engage  in or
as  to  the  contents  of their  proposed  public  statements
or  the  circumstances  of  their  publication.
It  would
not  aecord  with  judicial  responsibility  to  adjudge,  in
a  matter  involving  constitutionality,  between  the  free-

which  was  not  available  when  the  legislature  acted.”

The  subsequent  history  of  Hatch  Act  litigation  proves
the  wisdom  of  the  Court’s  rejection  of  the  First  Amend-
ment  attack  in  Mitchell.  When  the  issue  returned  here  in
Civil  Service  Commission  Vv.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548
(1973),  the  Court  was  presented  with  the  actual  or  threat-
ened  enforcement  of  the  Act  against  specific  conduct,
against  the  backdrop  of  years  of  experience  with  its
operation.  See  id.  at  551-53,  574-81.  The  Court  said:

“The  Commission  was  to  issue  notice,  hold  hearings,
adjudicate,  and  enforce.  This  process,  inevitably  and
predictably,  would  entail  further  development  of the
.  and  would  be  productive  of  a  more  refined
law  .

the  Act  is  unconstitutionally  vague  and  overbroad.”

In  this  context,  the  Court  upheld  the  law.  We  submit
that  the  same  principle  of  judicial  responsibility  applies

II.  CONGRESS  HAS  POWER  TO  REGULATE
FEDERAL  CAMPAIGN  FINANCING

As  the  court  of  appeals  held,  “[t]he  constitutional
power  of  Congress  to  regulate  federal  elections  (includ-
ing  the  pre-election  stages)  is  extensive.”  Ct.  App.  Op.
This  power  arises  from  the  express  provision
1516-17.
of  Article  I authorizing  Congress  to  regulate  the  “Man-
ner”  of  conducting  congressional  elections  **  and  from  the
implied  authorization  in  Article  II to  regulate  presidential
elections.
If  Congress  were  powerless  to  assure  the  fair-
ness  and  integrity  of  the  elections  by  which  national
officers  are  chosen,  the  very  legitimacy  of  the  Government
would  be  jeopardized.”

In  a  series  of  decisions  beginning  in  179,  the  Court
has  held  that  the  authority  of  Congress  under  Article
I  encompasses  any  measures  Congress  reasonably  be-
lieves  will  help  to  ensure  that  elections  are  fairly  con-
ducted  and  provide  the  electorate  an  effective  means  to
choose  its  representatives.*”
The  Court  has  held  that
Article  II  confers  an  equally  broad  implied  power  to

2“The  Times,  Places  and  Manner  of  holding  Elections  for
Senators  and  Representatives,  shal]  be  prescribed  in  each  State  by
the  Legislature  thereof;  but  the  Congress  may  at  any  time  by  Law
make  or  alter  such  Regulations,  except  as  to  the  places  of  chusing
Senators.”  U.S.  Const.,  art.  I, §4.

existence  and  breadth  of  that  power  are  now  beyond

Congress  has  great  latitude  in  choosing  means  to  pro-
tect  the  electoral  process.  As  the  Court  said  in  Bur-
roughs:

“The  power  of  Congress  to  protect  the  election  of
President  and  Vice-President  from  corruption  being
clear,  the  choice  of  means  to  that  end  presents  a
question  primarily  addressed  to  the  judgment  of
Congress.”  290  U.S.  at  547.

Congress  may,  as  it  repeatedly  has,  directly  regulate
individual  conduct  in  order  to  prevent  practices  injurious
to  the  electoral  process.  Or  it  may  use  its  taxing  and
spending  powers  to  promote  that  same  end—just  as  it
may  to  achieve  any  other  permissible  goal.**  The  legis-
lation  challenged  in  this  case  takes  both  those  routes.

III.  CONGRESS  FOUND  A  COMPELLING  NEED  TO
REMEDY  SERIOUS  ABUSES  OF THE  ELECTORAL
PROCESS

Popular  election  of  Congress  and  the  President  anchors
the  American  system  of  government.  But  age-old  abuses

stages  of  the  electoral  process—including  primaries  and  conven-
tions—are  within  the  reach  of  Congress’  power.  See,  e.g.,  Smith  v.
Allwright,  321  U.S.  649  (1944);  Terry  v.  Adams,  345  U.S.  461
(1953)  ; ef.  Developments  in  the  Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.
1111,  1182-84  (1975).

;

82  See  U.S.  Const.  art.  I.,  §8,  cl.  1;  United  States  v.  Butler,  297

and  newer  manifestations  of  the  role  of  money  have
For
impeached  the  integrity  of  the  electoral  process.
more  than  half  a  century,  Congress  has  enacted  various
measures  to  deter  these  evils,**  but  they  persist.  The
1972  election  and  its  aftermath  shocked  the  nation,  and
the  public  demanded  that  Congress  take  further  cor-
rective  measures.  Congress  responded  by  enacting  these
laws  to  end  the  debilitating,  almost  drug-like  dependence
of  candidates  on  large  campaign  contributions.

Congress  acted  after  considering  a  formidable  body
of  evidence  about  the  consequences  of  unfettered  private
financing  of  federal  election  campaigns.  A  familiarity
with  that  evidence  is  critical  to  judicial  review  of  the
remedies  Congress  enacted.  As  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter
instructed  with  respect  to  an  earlier  campaign  reform
statute:

“Appreciation  of  the  circumstances  that  begot  this
statute  is  necessary  for  its  understanding,  and  un-
derstanding  of  it  is  necessary  for  adjudication  of
the  legal  problems  before  us.
Speaking  broadly,
what  is involved  here  is  the  integrity  of our  electoral
process,  and,  not  less,  the  responsibility  of  the  indi-
vidual  citizen  for  the  successful  functioning  of  that
process.  This  case  thus  raises  issues  not  less  than
basic  to  a  democratic  society.”
United  States  v.
UAW,  352  U.S.  567,  570  (1957).

The  legislative  record  shows  that  campaign  financing
abuses  have  threatened  the  very  legitimacy  of  federal
elections  and  that  reform  was  imperative.

47

A.  Congress  Found  that  Dependence  on  Large  Cam-
paign  Contributions  Has  Exposed  Candidates  and
Elected  Officials  to  Improper  Influence

Congress  rightly  concluded  on  the  basis  of  voluminous
evidence  that  the  dependence  of  candidates  on  large  con-
tributions  is  one  of  the  cardinal  flaws  in  past  cam-
paign  financing  practices.  Congress  found  and  the  dis-
trict  court  confirmed  that  the  need  to  finance  expensive
campaigns  forces  candidates  and  their  solicitors  to  seek
large  contributions  from  wealthy  individuals  and  groups.
Because  governmental  decisions  often  vitally  affect  the
interests  of  those  individuals  and  groups,  large  contribu-
tions  are  often  made  to  further  private  interests  by
facilitating  access  to  government  officials  or  influencing
governmental  decisions.
In  return,  elected  officials  have
tended  to  afford  special  access  and  treatment  to  large
contributors.**  Appellants’  image  of  the  disinterested  con-
tributor  motivated  by  general  rather  than  personal  in-
terests  may  sometimes  be  true  but  generally  does  not
mirror  reality.”

The  relationship  between  candidates  and  potential  large
contributors  is  symbiotic:
The  candidates  depend  on
monied  and  special  interests  to  make  the  large  gifts  they
costly  campaigns;
believe  necessary
monied  and  special  interests  depend  on  the  decisions  made
by  elected  officials  to  advance  their  private  goals.  The
“dangers  and  pressures  of  conflict”  and  abuse  on  both
sides  of  this  relationship  are  obvious.“  The  appearance

to  finance

their

priety  and  illegality  was  demonstrated  in  1972  beyond
the  point  of  surfeit.

The  record  before  Congress  was  replete  with  specific
examples  of  improper  attempts  to  obtain  governmental
favor  in  return  for  large  campaign  contributions.
See
Findings  I,  {] 159-64,  IIA  J.A.  254-55.
For  example,
the  Senate  Select  Committee  on  Presidential  Campaign
Activities  learned  that  dairy  organizations  attempted  to
gain  access  to  government  officials  of  both  parties  and
to  influence  governmental  decisions  affecting  them  by
making  large  legal  and  illegal  campaign  contributions.
Findings  I,  1158,  IIA  J.A.  254.  One  dairy  executive
testified  that  his  organization  decided  to  contribute  $100,-
000  in  illegal  corporate  funds  to  Herbert  Kalmbach,  a
Nixon  fund-raiser,  in  1969—after  the  election—because

ers.”  Id.  at  592.

The  dairy  industry  pledged  to  contribute  $2,000,000
to  the  1972  Republican  presidential  campaign,  a  pledge
of  which  the  President  and  other  White  House  officials
were  aware.
Jd,  at  612-14,  616.  On  March  23,  1971,
after  a  meeting  with  dairy  organization  representatives,
the  President  decided  to  increase  milk  price  supports,
thereby  reversing  a  decision  of  his  Secretary  of  Agricul-
ture.  During  the  next  two  days,  before  this  decision
was  announced,  White  House  officials  and  Kalmbach  held
a  series  of  meetings  with  dairy  organization  officials.
In  describing  these  meetings  to  the  Senate,  Kalmbach
admitted  the  relationship  between  the  decision  and  the
pledge  of  large  contributions  as  follows:

“(M]y  understanding  was  simply  ..  .  that  the  price
support  would  be  announced  the  next  day;  and  that
in  view  of  that  fact,  ...  the  pledge  [by  the  dairy

Similar  improprieties  occurred  in  connection  with  am-
bassadorial  appointments.  Over  $1.7  million  in  contribu-
tions  to  the  winning  1972  presidential  campaign  can  be
attributed  in  whole  or  in  part  to  persons  who  received
ambassadorial  appointments  from  the  incumbent  Admin-
9124,  IIA  J.A.  155.
istration.
To  recite  only  a  few:  the  former  Ambassador  to  Great
Britain  contributed  $254,000;  the  former  Ambassador  to
Switzerland,  $100,000;  the  Ambassador  to  Luxembourg,
$300,000;  the  Ambassador  to  Belgium,  $115,100;  the  Am-
bassador  to  The  Netherlands,  $100,900;  the  former  Am-
bassador  to  Austria,  $100,500;  the  former  Ambassador  to
France,  $303,000;  the  former  Ambassador  to  Jamaica,
$103,500;  and  the  former  Ambassador  to  Trinidad  and
Tobago,  $100,500.  Jd.  It is well  known  that  Presidents  of
both  parties  have  long  named  large  contributors  to  am-
bassadorships.

In  confirmation  of  what  this  pattern  suggests,  Herbert
Kalmbach  pleaded  guilty  to  promising  a  European  ambas-
sadorship  to  one  J.  Fife  Symington,  Jr.,  in  return  for  a
$100,000  contribution,  and  testified  that  he  had  reached
a  similar  arrangement  with  another  ambassadorial  can-

didate.”  As  Kalmbach  described  it  to  the  Senate,  tite
Symington  affair  involved  the  clearest  possible  quid  pro
quo:

“He  [Symington]  said  ...  I will  contribute  $100,-
000  to  the  President  ...  [b]ut  I want  to  make  it

Congress  learned  of  other,  similar  efforts  to obtain  gov-
ernment  favor  by  offering  large  campaign  contributions.
Congress  heard  testimony  that  in  1972,  one  person  offered
a  $100,000  contribution  only  because  he  thought  it would
cause  the  lifting  of  a HUD  suspension  on  his  construction
business.  The  witness  testified:

“A.

...  1 asked:  Do  I have  any  reassurance  here
that  I am  going  to  get  anything  for  my  money?
I  mean  I  did  not  make  the  contribution  for
any  other  reason  than—

“Q.  You  were  not  seeking  to  make  a  contribution

to  the  election  of  the  President?
No,  sir,  I really  was  not  interested  in  making
that  contribution.”  **

*?  Mott  Deposition,  Ex.  5, confirmed,  id.  Tr.  at  13-14,  34.  In  1971,
when  Senator  Jackson  was  seeking  the  Democratic  presidential
nomination  for  1972,  M.  T.  Mehdi  of  The  Action  Committee  on
American-Arab  Relations  wrote  to  Jackson:

“The  Arab  Americans  and  American  Arabists,  viewing  you  as
a  serious  man  ..  .  have  decided  to  offer  you  the  opportunity  to

That  large  contributions  buy  access  to  candidates  and
elected  officials—and  thus  the  oppertunity  to  attempt  to
influence  them—has  been  the  common  experience  of  elec-
tive  politics.  As  Senator  Charles  Mathias  testified  with-
out  dispute  in  this  action:

“Candidates  usually  know  and  remember  the  names
of  individuals  who  make  large  contributions.  An
official  may  not  change  his  or  her  vote  solely  to  ac-
commodate  the  views  of  such  contributors,  but  often
officials,  including  myself,  will  agree  to  meet  with
an  individual  who  made  a  large  contribution  so  the
official  can  hear  the  contributor’s  concerns  and  make
the  contributor  aware  these  concerns  have  been  con-
sidered.  .
.  Since  an  elected  official  has  only  so
much  time  available,  the  inevitable  result  of  such
special  treatment  for  the  large  contributor  is  that
other  citizens  are  denied  the  opportunity  they  other-
wise  would  have  to  confer  with  the  elected  official.”  *
The  burden  of  the  evidence  is  inescapable.  What  other
inference  adequately  accounts  for  the  common  practice  of

.

wage  your  campaign  for  nomination  as  a  freer  man  and  with
a  greater  possibility  to  succeed.

“More  specifically,  we  are  offering  you  our  financial  support.
Presently,  there  are  pledges  ranging  from  $800,000  to  $1
million.  ...  Furthermore,  I am  happy  to  advise  you  that  we  are
ready  to  offer  you  at  least  twice  as  much  financial  support  as
the  Jewish  funds  you  are  seeking  and  you  can  realistically
expect.”

Mehdi  later  said  about  the  offer:  “While  it  is  true  that  this  is  not
done  in  public,  in  reality  this  is  done,  so  why  should  we  be  so
hypocritical?”  Making  the  offer,  he  said,  was  “as  American  as
apple  pie.
.  We  are  looking  for  one  great,  strong  politician  to
stand  on  his  feet  and  say,  ‘To  hell  with  Israel.’  4 National  Journal

many  large  donors  of  contributing  to  both  candidates  for

registered  interest  groups  made  contributions  on  more
than  450  occasions  to  two  or  more  candidates  for  the
same  office,  for  a  total  of  more  than  $1.3  million.*

For  example,  the  political  arm  of  the  Teamsters  Union
in  Alaska  contributed  to  two  Democratic  candidates  and
one  Republican  candidate  in  the  1974  Alaska  Senate  race.

Jackson,  and  Congressman  Mills.  Findings  I, { 156,  IIA

The  contributions  of  the  American  Medical  Association
(AMA)  to California  candidates  in  1974  further  illustrate
that  many  donations  are  made  for  reasons  other  than
ideology.  The  AMA  gave a total  of  $151,165  to  29  of  the
37  California  House  incumbents  who  faced  major  party
opposition  in  1974.  They  included  17  Republicans  and  12
Democrats,  ranging  on  the  ideological  spectrum  from  con-
servatives  John  Rousselot  and  Barry  Goldwater,  Jr.  to
liberals  Ron  Dellums  and  John  Burton,  Jr.  Two  chal-
lengers  received  AMA  contributions,  but  in  both  cases
the  AMA  gave  larger  contributions  to  the  incumbents

+  Findings  IIA,  §{  104,  111,  IIB  J.A.  467,  471-72;  Affidavit  of
John  J.  Conway,  Ex.  E-IV  to  Proposed  Findings  of  Fact  of  De-
fendants  and  Intervening  Defendants  Center  for  Public  Financing
of  Elections,  League  of  Women  Voters  of  the  United  States,  et  al.
(hereinafter  “Proposed  Findings  of  Defendants  and  Intervening
Defendants”),  { 15.

against  whom  the  challengers  were  running.
In  three
open  House  races—races  not  involving  an  incumbent—
both  the  Democratic  and  Republican  nominees  received
AMA  contributions;  the  AMA  also  gave  significant  con-
tributions  to  both  the  Democratic  and  Republican  nomi-
nees  for  the  Senate.  Findings  IIA,  § 110,  IIB  J.A.  470.

Moreover,  interest  groups  give  heavily  to  legislators
from  both  parties  who  are  members  of  the  congressional
committees  that  regulate  their  industries.  For  example,
during  the  1974  campaign,  political  committees  of  mari-
time-related  unions  contributed  $22,200  to  Representative
Frank  Clark,  Chairman  of  the  House  Merchant  Marine
Subcommittee,  and  $22,000  to  Senator  Russell  Long,
Chairman  of the  Senate  Merchant  Marine  Subcommittee—
who  was  unopposed  in  the  general  election  and  won  his
primary  with  75  percent  of the  vote.  Findings  IIA,  § 108,
IIB  J.A.  469;  see  id.,  7} 93-94,  105-07,  110-11;  IIB  J.A.
462-63,  467-69,  470-72.

Wealthy  individuals  with  special  business  or  personal
interests  follow  similar  practices.  H.  Ross  Perot  is  the
chairman  of  one  of  the  largest  suppliers  of  data  process-
ing  to  the  Government  for  medicare  and  medicaid  pro-
grams.  The  Ways  and  Means  Committee  has  jurisdiction
in  the  House  over  those  programs.  During  the  period
from  September  1,  1973  through  December  31,  1974,
Perot  made  personal  contributions  to  12  members  of  the
House  Ways  and  Means  Committee,  on  both  sides  of  the
aisle,  totaling  $31,900.  Two  of  the  contributions  were
made  after  the  election  to  members  of the  Committee  who
had  been  unopposed  in  the  primary  or  general  elections—
Rep.  Omar  Burleson,  who  received  $5,000,  and  Rep.  Joe
Waggoner,  who  received  $5,000.  In  addition,  Perot  con-
tributed  $2,500  to  Rep.  James  Jones  and  $500  to  Rep.
James  Martin  on  December  30,  1974—almost  two  months

56

57

after  the  election  and  approximately  two  weeks  after
these  two  Congressmen  had  been  named  to  serve  on  the
Ways  and  Means  Committee  during  the  upcoming  Con-
gress.  Perot  also  contributed  $2,500  to  Rep.  Dan  Flood,
who  served  as  Chairman  of  the  House  Appropriations
Subcommittee  for  the  Department  of  Health,  Education,
and  Welfare,  which  administers  medicare  and  medicaid
programs.

In  the  Senate,  the  Finance  Committee  has  jurisdiction
over  medicare  and  medicaid  programs.  Perot  contributed
$16,000  to  Democratic  and  Republican  members  of  the
Senate  Finance  Committee,  including  $5,000  each  to  Sena-
tors  Gravel,  Packwood,  and  Talmadge,  and  $1,000  to
Senator  Russell  Long,  Chairman  of  the  Finance  Commit-
tee.  In  sum,  Perot  contributed  $53,400  to  19  members  of
Congress  associated  with  either  the  House  Ways  and
Means  Committee,  the  House  Appropriations  Subcom-
mittee  for  HEW,  or  the  Senate  Finance  Committee.  Find-
ings  IIA,  7 93-95,  IIB  J.A.  462-64.

The  reasons  for  such  lavish  giving  are  unmistakable.

Indeed,  Congressmen  acknowledge  them:

“The  link  that  so  often  exists  between  campaign
contributions  and  preferential  treatment  by  politi-
cians  was  summed  up  by  Edward  Garmatz,  former
Maryland  Congressman  who  had  served  as  chairman
of  the  Merchant  Marine  Committee.  When  ques-
tioned  recently  about  the  heavy  political  contribu-
tions  he  received  from  the  maritime  industry,  he
said  ‘Who  in  the  hell  did  they  expect  me  to  get  it
from—the  post  office  people,  the  bankers?  You  get
it from  the  people  you  work  with,  who  you  helped  in
some  way  or  another.  It’s  only  natural.’  Congress-
man  Chet  Holifield  expressed  it  another  way,  in
(as  quoted  in
speaking  of  lobbyists.  He  asserted
the  Los  Angeles  Times)  that  if  they  didn’t  help  fill

campaign  purses  ‘the  power  of  lobbyists  would  be
practically  nil.’”  *

In  this  vein,  Senator  Biden  earnestly  complained:

“All  I know  is that  I have  felt  that  pressure...  ,
and  I  think  that  it  is  in  the  public  interest  to  see
to  it  that  we  who  are  running  for  public  office  can
get  about  the  business  of  telling  the  people  what  we
think  and  not  have  to  worry  that  if we  take  a  posi-
tion  that  is in opposition  to any  major  interest  group,
whether  it  be  labor  or  big  business,  that  we  are  go-
ing  to  have  campaign  funds  cut  off  that  we  need  to
get  our  case  before  the  public.”  *

As  the  court  of  appeals  noted,  Senator  Long  was  even
more  blunt:

“(When  you  are  talking  in  terms  of large  campaign
contributions  .
.  the  distinction  between  a  campaign
contribution  and  a  bribe  is  almost  a  hair’s  line  dif-
ference.  ...

.

“For  example,  I  recall  an  election  in  my  State
where  one  man  put  up  almost  a  quarter  of  a  million
dollars.  He  was  a  very  fine,  high  type  man  and  we
passed  a  big  tax  that  cost  him  a  fortune.  But  we
did  not  do  that  without  first  talking  to  him  and  say-
ing,  we  think  this  is  necessary  [and]  we  hope  you
will  pardon  us  for  doing  it.  Frankly  if  he  said,  no,
it  just  cannot  be  done,  I  do  not  know  whether  we
would  have  done  that  or  not.

“.  .

.  One  sweet  woman  was  on  the  opposite  side
and  thought  they  were  going  to  lose  and  came  charg-

Pressures  bear  also  on  potential  contributors.

The
cost  of  the  escalating  campaign  “arms  race,”  detailed
below,  forces  candidates  to  seek  ever  greater  amounts
of  financial  support.  Dependent  on  ever-increasing  sup-
plies  of  money,  candidates  must  solicit  contributions  in
ways  and  under  circumstances  that  compromise  both
donors  and  donees.  Both  courts  below  found,  as  Con-
gress  did,  that  “big-spending  campaigns  pull  like  a  mag-
netic  field”—“a  magnet  for  large  contributions,  and  for
circumventions.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1516,  1541.

The  testimony  of  corporate  executives  before  Congress
confirms  that  many  donors  feel  they  must  respond  or
risk  suffering  unfavorable  treatment  at  the  hands  of
“Extortion,  or  practices  that  differ
their  Government.
only  in  being  more  genteel,  are  the  Janus  face  of  undue
George  Spater,  former
influence  by  contributors.”**
Chairman  of  American  Airlines,  told  Congress  that:

“T  believe  that  the  present  system  places  unfair
pressures  both  on  the  candidates  and  on  corporate

terrifying  than  fear  of  the  known.  I think  this  is
a  very  large  element  in  the  picture.” @

“We  didn’t  expect  those  points  of  view  to  be  ac-
cepted,  but  only  from  the  point  of view  of being  able
to  express  them  and  that  was  our  thinking  or  re-

The  disclosures  about  the  1972  elections  are  painful
reminders  of the  extent  of  these  problems.  One  high  cam-
paign  official,
a  former  Cabinet  member,  pleaded  guilty
to  receiving  large  illegal  contributions  from  major  corpo-
rations.  Findings  I, { 112a,  IIA  J.A.  148.  A  former  high
official  in  the  Humphrey  campaign  was  recently  con-
victed  of  willfully  receiving  such  contributions.  Twenty-
two  individuals  pleaded  guilty  to  making,  conspiring  to
make,  or  aiding  and  abetting  illegal  corporate  contribu-
tions  to  both  major  parties  (Findings  I, ‘!{  113-14,  117h-k,
IIA  J.A.  148-50,  152-53) ; 17  corporations  pleaded  guilty
to  the  same  offense  or  conspiring  to  commit  that  of-

Three  individuals  associated  with  large  companies  have
pleaded  guilty  to  concealing  the  identity  of  large  indi-
vidual  contributors.  Findings  I, 9117,  IA  J.A.  151-53.

These  abuses  and  questionable  practices  were  not  re-
stricted  to  the  1972  election,  nor—as  the  record  plainly
shows—to  any  one  party.  Recent  studies  have  chronicled
similar  practices  dating  from  the  Pierce  and  Buchanan
administrations  down  through  the  1960’s.""  For  example,
in  the  1896  presidential  campaign,  before  corporate  con-
tributions  were  outlawed,  one  campaign  manager  simply
“assessed”  major  corporations  at  the  rate  of  one-fourth

of  one  percent  of  their  capital;  they  gladly  contributed
the  assessed  amounts."  In  1946,  Truman’s  nomination  of
a  former  campaign  treasurer  for  a  high  post  faltered
when  the  nominee  was  accused  of  trying  to  influence
governmental  decisions  in  favor  of  oil  companies  who
were  large  contributors.
Truman’s  military  aide  also
was  accused  of  collecting  political  gifts  in  exchange  for
government  favors.**  Major  American  corporations  such
as  Goodyear  Tire  and  Rubber,  Ashland  Oil,  Gulf  Oil,
Minnesota  Mining  and  Manufacturing,  and  Phillips  Pe-
troleum  have  been  responding  to  solicitations  from  and
making  corporate  contributions  to  both  parties,  at  all
levels  of  politics,  for  many  years.”

Indeed,  the  legislative  history  of  the  Federal  Corrupt
Practices  Act  of  1925  reads  as  if  it had  been  written  in
the  past  year.  During  debate  on  that  law,  Senator  Rob-
inson  said:

*¢  Findings  I,  9  119,  120,  ITA  J.A.  153-54;  Exs.  F-XXXIV  and
F-XXXV  to  Proposed  Findings  of  Defendants  and  Intervening
Defendants.

A  report  filed  with  the  Securities  and  Exchange  Commission  by
Ashland  Oil  lists  illegal  corporate  contributions  over  a  five-year
period.  They  include  very  large  gifts  to  the  Democratic  presi-
dential  campaign  in  1968  and  the  Republican  presidential  campaigns
in  1968  and  1972  and  gifts  of  $5,000  each  channeled  through  Carl
Arnold  to  10  Senate  candidates,  7  Democrats  and  3  Republicans.
Arnold  also  channeled  $50,000  to Wilbur  Mills  for  use  in  Democratic
congressional  campaigns.  Report  of  the  Special  Committee  to  the
Board  of  Directors  of  Ashland  Oil,  Inc.  (filed  Aug.  9, 1975).

Appellants  argue  that  the  new  law  would  do  nothing  additional  to
curb  already  illegal  corporate  contributions.  Appellants’  Br.  29,  64.
However,  as  the  Ashland  report  illustrates,  such  contributions  have
commonly  been  disguised  as  large  individual  gifts.  See  also  Find-
ings  ITA,  § 119,  IIB  J.A.  508-64.  The  $1,000  limit  on  individual
gifts  will  severely  restrict  this  method  of  circumventing  the  ban  on
corporate  contributions.

In  1962,  President  Kennedy’s  Commission  on  Campaign

Costs  issued  a  report  which  found  that:

“We  agree  with  President  Kennedy,  former  Presi-
dent  Eisenhower,  and  other  leaders  of  both  parties
that  the  existing  system  of  presidential  campaign
finance  poses  serious  problems.
It  is  not  desirable
to have  candidates  for  high  office,  especially  for  Pres-
ident  and  Vice  President,  dependent  on  individuals
or  organizations  with  special  interests  who  are  will-
ing  to  make  large  contributions  in  the  form  of  cash
or  campaign  services.
As  President  Kennedy  has
stated,  ‘it  is  not  healthy  for  the  democratic  process
—or  for  the  ethical  standards  in  our  government—

63

to  keep  our  national  candidates  in  [the  present]  con-
dition  of  dependence.’

“Under  present  practices,  the  parties  encounter
enormous  difficulty  in  raising  adequate  funds  at  the
proper  time.  Given  the  erratic  flow  of  funds  and
the  periodic  crises  in  campaign  finance,  individuals
and  organizations  providing  substantial  gifts  at criti-
cal  moments  can  threaten  to  place  a  candidate  in
moral  hock.  In  consequence,  a  disturbing  and  funda-
mental]  failure  of present  practices  is the  widespread
Fs  about  the  democratic  system  they  pro-

uce,”’  *°

Finally,  Congress  was  spurred  by  another  strong  con-
sideration—it  found  “a  definite  need  for  effective  and
comprehensive  legislation  in  this  [campaign  finance]  area
to  restore  and  strengthen  public  confidence  in  the  integ-
rity  of  the  political  process”—confidence  that  had  de-
clined,  in  significant  part,  because  of  campaign  financing
abuses.”  A  survey  done  for  the  Senate  Subcommittee  on
Intergovernmental  Relations  concluded:

“Public  confidence  in  most  government  institutions
.
.  has  declined  drastically  over  the  past  six  years.

.

.

.

.  [W]hen  asked  to  explain  their  concern,  one
quarter  of  those  sampled—the  largest  segment—
volunteered  the  belief  that  ‘government  leaders  are
corrupt  and  immoral.’

A  public  opinion  poll  prepared  for  the  Republican  Na-
tional  Committee  and  released  in  January  1975  showed
that  those  who  believe  that  government  is  conducted  for
the  “benefit  of  all  the  people”  had  declined  from  64  per-
cent  in  1964  to  a  low  of  21.3  percent  in  1974,  while  the
percentage  of  people  polled  who  believe  that  government
is  “pretty  much  run  by  a  few  big  interests  looking  out
for  themselves”  increased  from  29  percent  in  1964  to  a
high  of  69.9  percent  in  1974.  Findings  I,  7155;  HA
J.A.  252-53;  see  id.  9] 149-59,  171,  IIA  J.A.  206-53.  The
percentage  of  people  who  think  that  “hardly  any”  of  the
“people  running  the  government  are  a  little  crooked”
declined  from  18.2  percent  in  1964  to 9.8  percent  in  1974,
while  the  percentage  who  think  that  “quite  a  lot”  are
“crooked”  increased  from  28  percent  in  1964  to  49.3  per-
cent  in  1974—nearly  a  majority  of  the  electorate.  Find-
ings  I,

7155,  ITA  J.A.  252-53.

Louis  Harris,  the  expert  public  opinion  analyst,  testi-
fied  that  “Public  confidence  in  government  generally  must
be  reported  as  being  lower  than  a  constituent  democracy
can  afford.”  Congress  found  that  lack  of  respect  un-

dermines  the  legitimacy  of  the  Government  in  the  public

As  President  Ford  said  when  he  signed  the  FECA
Amendments,  “The  unpleasant  truth  is  that  big  money
influence  has  come  to  play  an  unseeming  role  in  our  elec-
toral  process.  This  bill  will  help  to  right  that  wrong.” ”

B.  Congress  Found  that  the  Ability  of  the  Wealthy
Few  To  Make  Large  Campaign  Contributions  Has
Fostered  Inequality  Among  Citizens  in  the  Ability
To  Affect  Electoral  Choices

With  no  effective  limits  on  campaign  gifts,  a  tiny
fraction  of  the  population  has  furnished  a  grossly  dis-
proportionate  share  of  the  money  used  in  campaigns.
The  power  to  affect  electoral  choices  is  thereby  con-
centrated  disproportionately  in  the  wealthy  few—not  be-
cause  of the  power  of their  ideas  but  because  of the  power
of  their  purses.’  A  mere  one  percent  of  the  people  con-
tribute  90  percent  of  the  money  candidates  receive  for
their  campaigns.  Findings  I, 7100,  IIA  J.A.  54.  Dona-
tions  from  only  2-3  percent  of  the  wealthiest  people  in
the  country  provide  about  95  percent  of  the  financing  for
congressional  elections.“
In  1972,  the  1254  largest  con-
tributors  accounted  for  over  $51  million  of  campaign

66

67

funds.  And  in  the  1972  presidential  race  alone,  153
large  contributors  accounted  for  almost  $20  million  of
the  incumbent’s  campaign  funds—that  is,  one-third  of
his  financial  support  was  furnished  by less  than  one  two-
thousandth  of  one  percent  of  the  people  who  voted  for
him  and  less  than  one  ten-thousandth  of  one  percent  of
the  population.”  By  contrast,  in  1975  alone,  more  than
82  million  individuals  have  chosen  to  help  finance  the
1976  presidential  campaigns  by  contributing  $1  through
the  tax  checkoff.

These  statistics  lend  substance  to the  popular  conception
that  electoral  politics  has  been  a  “rich  man’s  game”  and
that  the  ordinary  citizen  can  have  little  effect  on  elections
that  are  dominated  by wealthy  contributors.  That  popu-
lar  understanding  discourages  persons  of  modest  means
from  participating,  either  through  their  small  contribu-
tions  or  by more  direct  means.  Senator  Mathias  testified
that  many  persons  have  told  him  they  “feel  the  average
American  has  no  significant  role  in  the  political  process,
and  ...  they  therefore  do  not  participate.”  Mathias  Affi-
davit,  supra  note  43,  /11(b);  Findings  I, {/] 170-71,  ITA
J.A.  174-75.  Limits  on  campaign  giving  and  spending  are
designed  to  reverse  that  tendency.  Like  earlier  campaign
reform  statutes,  their  goal  is

“not  merely  to  prevent  the  subversion  of  the  integ-
rity  of  the  electoral  process.
[Their]  underlying
philosophy  [is]  to  sustain  the  active,  alert  responsi-
bility  of  the  individual  citizen  in  a  democracy  for
the  wise  conduct  of  government.”  United  States  v.
UAW,  352  U.S.  567,  575  (1957).

C.  Congress  Found  that  the  High—and  Rising—Cost
of  Campaigning  Has  Tended  To  Close  Politics  to
Candidates  and  Parties  Who  Lack  Access  to  Large
Sources  of  Wealth

Congress  found  that  federal  election  campaigns  have
become  enormously  expensive  and  that  the  costs  of  con-
ducting  such  campaigns  are  increasing  at  an  “alarming”
rate.”  An  estimated  $400  million  was  spent  in  1972  for
nomination  and  election  campaigns—a  33 percent  increase
over  1968  and  almost  a  300  percent  increase  since  1952.”
Over  the  same  periods,  the  consumer  price  index  rose
only  20.3  percent  from  1968  to  1972  and  only  57.6  per-
cent  from  1952  to  1972.°

In  1972,  presidential  campaign  spending  alone  totaled
$94.4  million—up  85  percent  from  $56.4  million  in  1968;
up  147  percent  from  $38.1  million  in  1964;  and  up  210
percent  from  $27.2  million  in  1960.  Findings  IIA,  { 51,
IIB  J.A.  451;  Findings  I, 9 89,  IIA  J.A.  49-51.  During
the  same  12-year  period,  the  consumer  price  index  rose
only  41.3  percent.  Findings  I,  11,  IIA  J.A.  9.  In  House

{| 88,  89,  93-95,  IIA  J.A.  49-51,  53.

The  GNP
total  value  of  goods  and  services  produced,  which  reflects  changes

measures  the  increases  in  the  prices  of  a  “standard  market  basket”
of  goods  and  services—and  is  the  standard  used  by the  Department
of  Labor  and  the  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics  to  measure  inflation.

70

Candidates  feel  they  must  try  to  match  spending  in-
creases  by  their  opponents  or  suffer  a  competitive  dis-
advantage.  Each  side  escalates  in  an  “arms  race”  that
neither  can  control.  A  good  example  of  the  tendency  of
a  campaign  to  raise  and  spend  as  much  as  possible—
even  more  than  is  necessary  to  win—is  presented  by
President  Nixon’s  1972  reelection  effort.  Maurice  Stans,
Chairman  of the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  Pres-
ident,  testified  before  the  Senate  that  in  his  opinion  the
1972  Nixon  campaign  spent  more  than  was  necessary:

.

“TI  thought  .
.  originally  that  in  the  President’s
position  he  could  be  reelected  for  25  or  30  million
dollars  and  when  they  came  to  me  with  budgets  of
40  million  dollars  which  were  incomplete,  and  higher
amounts,  I objected  very  strenuously.  ! insisted  that
there  was  overkill  in  the  budget  in  the  sense  that
they  were  spending  money  for  massive  amounts  of
direct  mail,  massive  amounts  of  telephoning,  massive
amounts  of  advertising  that  just  were  duplicating
each  other.”

Congress  found  that  as  a  result  of  rising  campaign
expenditures,  much  of  the  electorate  has  come  to  believe
that  candidates  can  “buy”  an  election  by  simply  out-
spending  their  opponents  in  a  campaign,  and  that  can-
didates  with  wealthy  or  special  interest  supporters  there-
fore  have  a  decided  advantage  in  federal  elections.”
Obviously,  where  such  an  impression  exists,  public  con-
fidence  in  elected  representatives  deteriorates.  See  Find-
ings  I, 7] 149-55,  ITA  J.A.  206-53.

7  Hearings  Before  the  Senate  Select  Comm.  on  Presidential

Campaign  Activities,  supra  note  41, at 715.

™  H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239,  supra  note  56, at 3.

71
Candidates  must  raise  larger  and  larger  sums  of money
in  order  to  match  the  ever  more  expensive  campaigns
of  their  opponents.  Findings  I, 197,  IIA  J.A.  54.  Con-
gress  found  that  in  order  to  raise  these  sums,  candidates
from  all  parties  have  increasingly  relied  on  large  con-
tributions  from  monied  and  special  interests.”  Potential
candidates  who  lack  access  to  large  sources  of  wealth—
or  who  are  unwilling  to  incur  obligations  to  large  con-
tributors—face  an  extreme  competitive  disadvantage.  The
testimony  of  a  former  presidential  campaign  finance
chairman  illustrates  this  phenomenon:

“(We  know  there  was  fund  raising  being  done  at
the  local  level  by  the  state  committees,  through  the
store-front  offices  and  women’s  clubs  and  all  that
sort  of  thing.

“Now,  from  my  1968  experience,  we  knew  that
there  was  absolutely  no  possibility  of  raising  $40
million  by  that  kind  of  effort  because  the  average
contribution  that  came  through  those  programs  was
less  than  $25,  we  had  to  find  ways  of tapping  larger
sources  and  this  meant  to  people  who  had  means  to
contribute  more.

a

*

@

e

“.  ..  [W]here  we  had  to  look  for  the  major  source
of  money  was  to  the  people  of  wealth,  the  people
of  means,  who  were  willing  and  able  to  contribute
more  of what  they  had  in  proportion  to  their  wealth.

“It  was  by that  means  that  we  were  able  to  reach
something  like  75  people  who  gave  $100,000  or
more.”  ™

This  rising  thirst  for  money  has  forced  candidates
to  divert  time  and  energy  to  fund-raising  activities  and
away  from  other  activities,  such  as  addressing  the  sub-
stantive  issues,  that  do  not  fill  campaign  coffers."*  Joseph
Cole,  who  was  National  Finance  Chairman  of  the  Demo-
cratic  National  Committee,  vividly  recounted  the  effects
of the  pressure  to  raise  money  on  candidates  in  a  passage
quoted  by  the  court  of  appeals:

..  I have  been  close  to  four  or  five  Presidential
“
campaigns  ....  1 have  sat  next  to  the  Presidential
candidate,  who  was  tired,  who  was  weary  and  con-
cerned  with  the  issues  and  not  able  to  handie  them,
not  able  to  prepare,  not  able  to  think  about  them
because  he  has  to  go  downstairs  at  7  in  the  morning
to  shake  hands  with  a  guy  from  whom  he  may  get
a  large  contribution.

“Tt  goes  on  all  day  long  and  all  night  long,  and
I was  asked  at  the  Senate  hearings  how  much  time
do  you  think  a  Presidential  candidate  spends  on
fund-raising?  And  I said  at  least  70  percent  of  his
time,  and  I  think  all  of  his  waking  hours.
It  is
really  demeaning,  demeaning  to  go  through  it.”  ”

disgustine,  demeaning,
debilitating
experience  of a  politician’s  life.  It’s  stinky,  it’s  lousy.
I just  can’t  tell  you  how  much I hate  it.  I’ve  had  to

break  off  in  the  middle  of  trying  to  make  a  decent,

The  high  price  of  campaigning  thus  has  become  a
barrier  to  the  candidacies  of  persons  who  lack  great
wealth  or  access  to  it.”  Congress  concluded  that  the
range  of  potential  candidates  for  federal
office  was
steadily  being  reduced  to  the  friends  of  the  wealthy  and
the  very  wealthy  themselves.  Candidates  who  could  pump
tremendous  personal  and  family  fortunes  into  their  cam-
paigns  have  become  increasingly  predominant  in  federal
elections.  Vice  President  Rockefeller’s  stepmother  alone
contributed  almost  $1.5  million  to  his  1968  Republican
presidential  nomination  effort.”  “In  the  Nation’s  seven
largest  States  in  1970,  11  of  the  15  major  senatorial
candidates  were  millionaires.
The  four  who  were  not
millionaires  lost  their  bid  for  election.”  117  Cong.  Rec.

cedures  89  (1972);  1970  Cong.  Q.  2059-63  (Aug.  14,
1970).

“allow  candidates—incumbents  and  challengers  alike

IV.  THE  FIRST  AMENDMENT  PERMITS  CONGRESS
TO  IMPOSE  SOME  REASONABLE  LIMITS  ON
CAMPAIGN  CONTRIBUTIONS  AND  EXPENDI-
TURES

Appellants  argue  that  the  First  Amendment  forbids
Congress  to  impose  any  limits  on  campaign  giving  and
spending.  They  further  argue  that,  even  assuming  the
power  to  set  some  limits,  the  particular  limits  Congress
chose  are  unreasonable  and  discriminatory.  We  deal
here  with  appellants’  broad  denial  of congressional  power
in  this  area.  In  Part  V,  we  answer  their  attack  on  the
specific  limits  Congress  set.

Contribution  and  expenditure  limits  are  aimed  at  the
adverse  effects  of  unlimited  money,  not  at  any  effect  of
unlimited  speech.  Of  course,  to  restrict  what  candidates
and  their  supporters  may  spend  to  win  elections  can  have
incidental  effects  on  political  speech  and  association.  How-
ever,  that  conclusion  does  not  end  the  analysis,  but  only
begins  it.

In  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367  (1968),  this
Court  summarized  the  test  for  determining  the  permissi-
bility  of  laws—such  as  these—that  do  not  have  the  pur-
pose  of  limiting  speech  but  that  incidentally  impinge  on
First  Amendment  interests:

“To  characterize  the  quality  of  the  governmental  in-
terest  which  must  appear,  the  Court  has  employed  a

cogent;

paramount;

variety  of  descriptive  terms:  compelling;  substan-
tial;  subordinating;
strong.
Whatever  imprecision  inheres  in  these  terms,  we
think  it  clear  that  a  government  regulation  is  suffi-
ciently  justified  if  it  is  within  the  constitutional
power  of  Government;  if it furthers  an  important  or
substantial  governmental  interest;  if  the  governmen-
tal  interest  is  unrelated  to  the  suppression  of  free
expression;  and  if  the  incidental  restriction  on  al-
leged  First  Amendment  freedoms  is  no  greater  than
is  essential  to  the  furtherance  of  that  interest.”  Jd.
at  376-77  (footnotes  omitted).

ee eS ee es

See  also  Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  611-12
(1973).  Each  element  of  the  test  is  clearly  met  in  this
case,

ae
Ab A Oe ee! ee

I tm

We  have  shown  that  regulation  of  federal  elections  is
clearly  “within  the  constitutional  power  of  Government.”
Part  II, supra.

The  interests  served  by campaign  giving  and  spending
limits,  described  in  Part  III,  are  the  most  compelling
interests  in  a  representative  democracy.  Foremost  is  the
interest  in  curbing  the  corrupting  influence  of  large  con-
tributions  on  candidates  and  elected  officials  and  elimi-
Ancillary  to
nating  the  appearance  of  such  influence.
that  are  the  related  needs  to  reduce  the  disproportionate
power  of  the  wealthy  to  affect  electoral  choices,  and  to
reduce  financial  barriers  to  candidacy.

These  public  interests  plainly  are  “unrelated  to the sup-
pression  of  free  expression.”  Appellants  assert  that  the
evils  attacked  by giving  and  spending  limits  grow  out  of
“too  much  communication”  and  that  the  limits  achieve
their  goals  by restricting  communication.  Appellants’  Br.
79-86,  116-27;  see  United  States’  Amicus  Br.  26-27.  That
view  is  based  on  a  fundamental  misconception  of  the

76

ways  in  which  money  has  harmed  the  electoral  process
and  the  ways  in which  the  limits  will  remedy  those  harms.
We  will  show  that  the  harms  result  directly  from  unlim-
ited  money,  not  from  unlimited  speech,  and  that  the
limits  will  achieve  their  goals  wholly  apart  from  any
effect  on  communication.

Finally,  we  will  show  that  giving  and  spending  limits
are  not  unnecessarily  restrictive  means  of  achieving  the
legislative  goals.

A.  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Serve  Compelling  First
Amendment  and  Other  Interests  in  Protecting  the
Integrity  of  the  Electoral  Process

The  compelling  need  to  remedy  campaign  financing
abuses  derives  not  only  from  Articles  I and  II  but  also
from  the  First  Amendment.  That  amendment  protects  a
cluster  of  related  and  sometimes  competing  values.  Ac-
commodation  among  those  competing  values  is  essential
See
to  preserve  the  matrix  of  our  democratic  system.
A.  Bickel,  The  Morality  of  Consent  (1975);  T.  Emerson,
supra  note  31,  at  3-20,  627-30.  We  believe  that  giving
and  spending  limits  clearly  promote,  rather  than  infringe,
the  values  embodied  in  the  First  Amendment.

This  Court’s  decisions  affirm  that  the  First  Amendment
is  not  a  simple  absolute.  “Neither  the  right  to  associate
nor  the  right  to  participate  in  political  activities  is  abso
lute...  .”  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers,
413  U.S.  548,  567  (1973).  Even  where  First  Amendment
interests  are  pitted  against  wnrelated  governmental  inter-
ests,  this  Court  has  made  clear  that  accommodation  of
the  competing  interests  is  necessary.  If  the  countervail-
ing  governmental  interest  is  compelling,  laws  that  inci-
dentally  impinge  on  free  expression  may  be  upheld.  E.v.,
United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367,  376-77  (1968) ;
Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  611-12  (1978).

77

The  need  for  accommodation  is  much  more  obvious
where,  as  here,  First  Amendment  values  lie  on  both  sides
of the  case.  There  may  be  a  First  Amendment  interest  in
allowing  a  reasonable  amount  of  campaign  giving  and
spending,  but  there  is an  equally  compelling  First  Amend-
ment  interest  in  preventing  the  excessive  use  of  money
from  distorting  and  defeating  the  democratic  process.  In
Professor  Freund’s  words,

“We  are  dealing  here  on  the  one  hand  with  the  right
to  speak  fully  and  freely  and  on  the  other  with  the
right  of  equality,  the  right  of  access  to  the  media,
the  right  of  the  electorate  not  to  have  their  repre-
sentative  or  their  president  unduly  beholden  to  a
particular  interest
of  campaign  obliga-
PP

because

The  issue  posed  by  legislation  “designed  to  purify  the
democratic  process”  is,  according  to  Professor  Emerson,

“not  the  reconciliation  of  freedom  of expression  with
another  kind  of  interest  but  the  reconciliation  of
opposing  interests  within  the  system  of  free  expres-
sion  itself.”  T.  Emerson,  Toward  a  General  Theory
of  the  First  Amendment  103-04  (1966).”

Emerson  concludes  that  the  First  Amendment  permits
Congress  to  impose  “certain  restraints  on  expenditure  of
money  in political  campaigns  or  in direct  contacts  with  the
legislature.”  Jd.  at  104-05.

”  Freund,  supra  note  31,  at  72-73.  See  also  Note,  Campaign
Spending  Controls  Under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of
1971,  8 Colum.  J.L.  &  S.P.  285,  302  (1972)  ; Comment,  Free  Speech
Implications  of Campaign  Expenditure  Ceilings,  7 Harv.  Civ.  Rights-
Civ.  Lib.  L.  Rev.  214,  223-24  (1972).

®°  See  T.  Emerson,  supra  note  31,  at  629;  Redish,  Campaign
Sue taal Laws  and  the  First  Amendment,  46  N.Y.U.L.  Rev.  900,

1971).

78

79

A  primary  purpose  for  the  First  Amendment’s  protec-
tion  of  free  expression  is  to  make  democracy  practicable.
The  goal  is  “producing  an  informed  public  capable  of
conducting  its  own  affairs.”  Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.
v.  FCC,  395  U.S.  367,  392  (1969).  As  Alexander  Bickel
has  said:

“The  social  interest  that  the  First  Amendment  vin-
dicates  is...  the  interest  in  the  successful  operation
of  the  political  process,  so  that  the  country  may  bet-
ter  be  able  to  adopt  the  course  of  action  that  con-
forms  to  the  wishes  of  the  greatest  number,  whether
or  not  it  is  wise  or  is  founded  in  truth.”  A.  Bickel,
supra  p.  76,  at  62.

Self-expression  as  an  aid  to  self-government  is  “the  cen-
tral  meaning”  of  the  First  Amendment“  and  a  keystone
of  our  democratic  system.

Many  federal  laws—such  as  the  laws  forbidding  vote-
buying,  18  U.S.C.  § 597,  and  intimidation  of  voters,  18
U.S.C.  § 594—are  designed  to  prevent  subversion  of  the
democratic  process.  State  laws  constitutionally  forbid  elec-
tioneering  at  or  adjacent  to  the  polls.  All  such  laws  re-
strict  behavior  that  might  be  called  speech,  assembly,  or
petition  and  considered  protected  by  the  First  Amend-
ment;  but  they  do so  to achieve  the  First  Amendment  goal
of choosing  our  elected  representatives  through  a  free  and
informed  suffrage.

Without  such  laws,  we  might  have  utter  political  license
but  we  would  not  have  a  working  representative  democ-

81  See,  e.g.,  Kalven,  The  New  York  Times  Case:  A  Note  on  “The
Central  Meaning  of  the  First  Amendment,”  1964  Sup.  Ct.  Rev.
191;  Meiklejohn,  The  First  Amendment  is  an  Absolute,  1961  Sup.
Ct.  Rev.  245.

racy.  Laws  such  as  these—including  the  challenged  stat-
utes—bring  the  reality  of  the  electoral  process  closer  to
its  ideal.  They  do  not  hinder,  they  promote  the  funda-
mental  goals  of  the  First  Amendment.

Such  laws  also  promote  other  constitutional  values.
Articles  I  and  II  establish  the  legislative  and  executive
branches  of  the  Government  and  provide  for  the  methods
of  selection  of  Senators,  Congressmen,  and  the  President
and  Vice  President.  The  provisions  prescribing  the  man-
ner  of  choosing  these  federal  officers  have  been  modified
and  supplemented  by  the  Twelfth,  Fourteenth,  Seven-
and  Twenty-Fifth
Twenty-Third,
teenth,
Amendments.  The  Fifteenth,  Nir  >teenth,  Twenty-Fourth,
and  Twenty-Sixth  Amendments  have  successively  ex-
panded  the  franchise.  Each  of  these  constitutional  provi-
sions  presupposes  that  Congress  can  and  will  enact  the
laws  necessary  to  assure  that  the  elections  in  which  legis-
lative  and  executive  officers  are  chosen  will  be  fair  and
open.

Twentieth,

The  challenged  laws  are  such  laws.  They  will  help  to
make  elections  in  practice  what  they  are  in  constitutional
text  and  theory:  a  means  by which  the  voters  can  choose
their  leaders  and  the  directions  their  Government  will
take,  with  each  voter  having  an  equal  say  in  the  choices.

1.  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Will  Protect  Candi-
dates  and  Elected  Officials  Against  Improper
Influence

The  cardinal  objective  of  giving  and  spending  limits  is
to  reduce  the  temptation  for  candidates  to  seek  the  favor
of  large  contributors,  and  thus  to  do  away  with  the  im-
proper  influence  of  large  campaign  donors  on  those  who
win  elections.  Our  elected  representatives  will  be  better
able  to  respond  to  the  views  and  petitions  of  all  their

80

81

constituents  if  they  are  less  dependent  on  monied  special
interests  to  win  or  retain  their  seats.”

Without  giving  and  spending  limits,  few  politicians  can
afford  to  end  their  courtship  of  large  contributors.  The
limits  in  the  new  laws  give  candidates  this  freedom.  The
candidates  are  now  freed  to  turn  much  of  their  own  time
.  one  individual  for  days  or  months  in
from  “courting  .
the  hope  of  getting  $15,000  or  $20,000”  to  the  substance
of  the  campaign,  while  their  staffs  canvass  more  broadly
for  small  contributions.**  Candidates  are  also  freed  to
frame  their  campaign  positions—and  their  policies  once
they  are  elected—to  suit  the  views  of  all  constituents,  not
merely  a  few  large  givers.

.

Moreover,  even  if  politicians  were  able  to  resist  the
temptation  to  favor  their  large  financial  backers,  this
Court  has  recognized  the  public  interest  in  preventing
the  appearance  of  such  favor—in  “protect[ing]  the  [elec-
toral]  process  from  being  misjudged  in  the  minds  of  the
public.”**  The  Court  has  said:

“I]t  is  not  only  important  that  the  Government
and  its  employees  in  fact  avoid  practicing  political
justice,  but  it  is  also  critical  that  they  appear  to  the
public  to  be  avoiding  it,  if  confidence  in  the  system
of  representative  Government  is  not  to  be  eroded  to
a  disastrous  extent.”  *

2.  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Will  Help  To
Equalize  the  Ability  of  All  Citizens  To  Partici-
pate  Effectively  in  Elections

Giving  and  spending  limits  will  also  help  to  equalize
the  ability  of  all  citizens  to  participate  in  elections  and
affect  the  choices  available  in  them.  Our  constitutional
system  is  based  on  the  precept  that  citizens  of  unequal
wealth  are  entitled  to  an  equal  say  in  electing  national
leaders  and  in  urging  their  views  on  those  elected  after
they  take  office.  “Wealth  .
.  is  not  germane  to  one’s
.
ability  to  participate  intelligently  in  the  electoral  proc-
ess.”  Harper  Vv.  Virginia  Board  of  Elections,  383  U.S.
663,  668  (1966).  Of  course,  individuals  are  unequal  in
traits  other  than  wealth—they  are  not  equally  endowed
with  intelligence,  persuasiveness,  lung-power,  or  free
time.
But,  unlike  wealth,  there  are  natural  limits  on
these  other  advantazes:

“Men  may  not  oe  equal  in  lung-power,  but  even
Senate  filibusters  reveal  that  the  capacity  of  any
man  to  speak  is finite.  However,  the  things  that  un-
limited  sums  of  money  can  buy—recordings,  sound
trucks,  the  speeches  of  others,  radio  and  television
programs—are  subject  to  no  comparable  human
limit.”  *

The  political  inequality  produced  by  great  disparities
in  wealth,  if  left  unregulated,  would  upset  the  premises
on  which  our  democracy  is  based.*’  There  is  a  marked

Appellants  assert  that,  since  not  all  rich  people  think  alike,  rich
people  can  be relied  on  to be  the  “representatives”  of  the  unwealthy

82

83

correlation  between  the  decline  in  citizen  participation
in  the  political  process  and  the  increase  in  citizen  belief
that  government  is  controlled  by  monied  and  special  in-
terests.**  Democratic  government  is  endangered  if  the
many  stop  participating  because  they  believe  the  wealthy
few  determine  what  the  choices  will  be.

In  limiting  the  size  of  contributions,  Congress  took  a
historic  step  toward  disestablishing  wealth  as  a  basis  for
political  power  and  preference.  It  has  not  removed  the
disparities  of  wealth,  but  it  has  broken  the  link  of
mutual  attraction  between  great  wealth  and  candidates
for  elected  office  that  gives  the  possessors  of  such  wealth
a  disproportionate  impact  on  the  course  of  public  affairs.

That  such  congressional  action  is permissible  is shown  by
this  Court’s  decisions  abolishing  other  voting  inequalities.
Direct  restrictions  on  the  right  to  vote  based  on  wealth—
such  as  poll  taxes  *  or  property  qualifications  *°°—are  un-

and  to  promote  their  interests.  Appellants’  Br.  61-62,  121-22.  John
Stuart  Mill  answered  that  assertion  in  1861:

“[I}n  order  to  support  the  claim  of  all  to  participate  in  the
sovereign  power[,]  [w]e  need  not  suppose  that  when  power
resides  in  an  exclusive  class,  that  class  will  knowingly  and
deliberately  sacrifice  the  other  classes  to  themselves:  it  suffices
that,  in  the  absence  of its  natural  defenders,  the  interest  of  the
excluded  is  always  in  danger  of  being  overlooked;  and,  when
looked  at,  is  seen  with  very  different  eyes  from  those  of  the
persons  whom  it  directly  concerns.”

Considerations  on  Representative  Government,  in  The  Philosophy
of  John  Stuart  Mill  409-10  (M.  Cohen  ed.  1961).  Our  nation  was
founded  on  the  principle  that  even  the  unwealthy  are  entitled  to
elect  those  who  will  be  their  representatives.

ziejski,  399  U.S.  204  (1970).  “Any  unjustified  discrimination  in

constitutional.  Even  where  the  right  to  vote  is  not  ac-
tually  denied,  laws  that  create  inequalities  in  voting
power  have  been  held  unconstitutional.  The  reapportion-
ment  decisions  protect  equality  of  voting  power  against

And
the  Court  held  in  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1972),
that  laws  creating  “disparity  in  voting  power  based  on
wealth”  also  are  impermissible  under  the  Equal  Protec-
tion  Clause.

Bullock  was  a  challenge  to  a  state  election  law  that
required  candidates  in  primary  elections  to  pay  large
fees—ranging  up  to  $8,900—to  have  their  names  placed
on  the  ballot.
The  Court  unanimously  invalidated  the
state  law,  holding  that  it  impermissibly  increased  the
political  power  of  wealthy  candidates  and  wealthy  voters:

“Many  political  office  seekers  lacking  both  personal
wealth  and  affluent  backers  are  in  every  practical
sense  precluded  from  seeking  the  nomination  of their
chosen  party,  no  matter  how  qualified  they  might  be,
and  no  matter  how  broad  or  enthusiastic  their  pop-
ular  support.  .
.  [T]here  is  the  obvious  likelihood
that  this  limitation  would  fali  more  heavily  on  the
less  affluent  segment  of the  community,  whose  favor-
ites  may  be  unable  to  pay  the  large  costs  required
by  the  Texas  system.  To  the  extent  that  the  system
requires  candidates  to  rely  on  contributions  -from
voters  in  order  to  pay  the  assessments,  ..  .  it tends

.

.  under-
determining  who  may  participate  in  political  affairs  .
mines  the  legitimacy  of  representative  government.”  Kramer  Vv.

to  deny  some  voters  the  opportunity  to  vote  for  a

The  Court  struck  down  the  law  on  the  ground  that  it
impermissibly

“utilizes  the  criterion  of ability  to  pay  as  a  condition
to  being  on  the  ballot,  thus  excluding  some  candi-
dates  otherwise  qualified  and  denying  an  undeter-
mined  number  of  voters  the  opportunity  to  vote  for
candidates  of  their  choice.”

Jd.  at  149.

The  court  of  appeals  found  those  decisions  apposite

here:

“It  would  be  strange  indeed  if,  by  extrapolation
outward  from  the  basic  rights  of  individuals,  the
wealthy  few  could  claim  a  constitutional  guarantee
to  a  stronger  political  voice  than  the  unwealthy  many
because  they  are  able  to  give  and  spend  more  money,
and  because  the  amounts  they  give  and  spend  can-
not  be  limited.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1517.

Appellants  argue  that  these  decisions  are  irrelevant
because  they  held  unconstitutional  government-imposed
disabilities  based  on  wealth.  Appellants’  Br.  119-20.  But
we  do  not  argue  that  the  inequality  flowing  from  large
contributions  is unconstitutional—only  that,  as  these  cases
show,  laws  reducing  that  inequality  help  to  effectuate  the
First  Amendment.
The  Constitution  does  not  require
Congress  to  eliminate  disparities  in  political  power  re-
sulting  from  disparities  in  wealth,  but  it  permits  Con-
gress  to  do  so.

“If  financial  barriers  to  the  right  to  vote  are  con-
stitutionally  forbidden,  there  is  a  legitimate  argu-
ment  that  reduction  of  financial  barriers  to  the
right  to  influence  voters  is  constitutionally  permit-
ted.  And  if  the  Constitution  requires  that  each
man’s  v
2  count  equally,  may  not  that  fact  be
deemed  pertinent  in  consideration  of  the  validity  of
measures  intended  to  reduce  inequalities  in  men’s
opportunities  to  affect  the  vote.” ”

Giving  and  spending  limits,  by  reducing  the  power  of
“big  money,”  stimulate  political  participation  by  or-
dinary  citizens.  The  record  in  this  case  shows  how  ef-
fective  the  challenged  statutes  already  have  been  in  that
regard.  In  past  years,  only  a  tiny  fraction  of  Americans
have  contributed  most  of  the  money  used  in  political
campaigns.  Pp.  65-66,  supra.  These  limits  have  begun
to  change  that  phenomenon.

Ordinary  people  have  been  encouraged  to  make  gifts
that  will  not  be  dwarfed  by  huge  contributions.
Can-
didates  who  voluntarily  limited  gifts  to  their  campaigns
before  the  legal  limits  became  effective  received  many
contributions  from  persons  who  had  never  before  given
to  a  candidate.”  And  this  new  interest  of  small  givers

3.  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Will  Lower  the
Financial  Barrier  to  the  Entry  of  Additional
Candidates  and  Parties

The  barrier  to  entry  posed  by  high  campaign  costs
harms  not  only  the  candidates  who  are  excluded.  It  also
impoverishes  the  nation’s  political  discourse,  harming  all
who  might  have  responded  to  a  new  political  voice.  Giv-
ing  and  spending  limits  will  help  to  increase  both  the

be  dominated  by  the  very  rich  and  the  corrupt.  I am  enclos-
ing  my  check  for  $50.00  to  advance  this  cause.  The  only  stipu-
lation  which  I place  on  this  contribution  is  that  it  be  returned
to  me  in  the  event  that  Senator  Mathias  abandons  these  guide-
lines  during  the  campaign.  In  return  I pledge  that  should  you
notify  me  that  this  1974  campaign  is  in  financial  difficulty,  I
shall  contribute  an  additional  $50.00.”

“Your  action  concerning  fund  raising  for  your  coming  cam-
paign  is  most  impressive  and  a  giant  step  in  the  right  di-
rection.
It  is  also  an  excellent  example  for  your  colleagues.
Therefore,  for  the  first  time  in  my  life,  I am  making a political
contribution.”  Mathias  Affidavit,  supra  note  43,  § 18.

%  Findings  I, { 166  (Rep.  Morris  K.  Udall);  id.  { 196,  IIA  J.A.

263-64;  see  pp.  122-23,  infra.

number  and  the  diversity  of  voices  heard  in  politics  by
removing  access  to  wealth  as  a  qualification  for  office.

This  Court  confirmed  the  importance  of  that  objective
in  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1972).  As  noted
above,  it held  that  one  impermissible  feature  of  the  large
filing  fees  was  that  unwealthy  candidates  were  “in  every
practical  sense  precluded  from  seeking  the  nomination
of  their  chosen  party.”  Id.  at  1438.  High  campaign  costs
are  just  as  preclusive.
“[T]jhe  task  of  fund  raising
frequently  appears  hopeless  to  well-qualified  candidates
who  wish  to  advance  a  program  or  to challenge  the  record
of  an  incumbent.”  D.  Adamany  and  G.  Agree,  supra
note  48,  at  21.°  Applying  Bullock,  the  Court  has  held
that  a  state  may  not  exclude  an  indigent  candidate  from
the  ballot  for  failure  to  pay  even  a  modest  filing  fee.
Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709  (1974).

Spending  limits  are  the  only  way  to  slow  the  meteoric
rise  of  campaign  costs.
The  FECA  of  1971  was  in
effect  for  the  1972  and  1974  elections  but  did  not  halt
H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239,  supra
the  campaign  arms  race.
note  56,  at  3;  S.  Rep.  No.  170,  supra  note  59,  at  1-2.
Limits  on  spending  that  apply  equally  to  all  candidates
in  a  race  will  free  the  candidates  from  monetary  one-
upmanship  and  open  the  field  to  a  greater  number  of
candidates.  The  Wisconsin  Supreme  Court  relied  on  these
considerations  in  upholding  that  state’s  spending  ceilings
for  state  candidates.”

*  Indeed,  the  record  in  this  case  suggests  that  two  of  the  appel-
lant  political  parties  consider  a  potential  candidate’s  access  to
wealth  as  a  factor  in  their  decision  to  nominate  him.  See  Mahoney
Deposition  Tr.  at  35-36  (Conservative  Party  of  the  State  of  New
