88

89

The  first  salutary  results  can  already  be  seen  in  the
unusual  number  of  candidates  actively  seeking  the  Presi-
dency  in  1976.  See  pp.  122-23,  infra.  At  least  one  of
them  has  described  the  new  giving  and  spending  limits  as
making  it  possible  for  members  of  the  House  of  Repre-
sentatives  to  compete  effectively  with  the  Senators  and
governors  who  have  traditionally  dominated  presidential
elections.  Affidavit  of  Morris  K.  Udall,  § 7.

In  sum,  far  from  suppressing  political  activity,  giving
and  spending  limits  are  a  political  Magna  Carta  for  the
many  persons  whose  political  activity  and  influence  was
previously  eclipsed  by  the  spending  power  of  the  wealthy
few.”

B.  Reasonable  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Will  Not

Restrict  Freedom  of  Expression

Appellants  argue  that  Congress  may  not  limit  campaign
giving  or  spending  because  such  activities  are  “pure
speech.”  They  reason  that  some  forms  of  political  speech
require  that  money  be  spent  for  drafting,  printing,  mail-
ing,  or  broadcasting,  and  that  any  limits  whatever  on
the  amounts  spent  for  such  purposes  unconstitutionally
abridge  the  right  of free  speech  itself.  Appellants’  Br.  48.
Similarly,  the  United  States  as  Amicus  asserts  that  giv-
ing  and  spending  limits  are  restrictions  on  speech  be-
cause,  “Many  if  not  most  of  the  benefits  attributed  to

*®  The  American  Bar  Association  Special  Committee  on  Election
Reform  has  adopted  a  Resolution  and  Report  endorsing  legislation
.  to  reasonable  amounts.”
to  limit  “[c]ampaign  contributions  .
Resolution  125A  (Aug.  1975).  Appellants  assert  that  the  Special
Committee  “substantially  adopt[s]  appellants’  views.”  Appellants’
Br.  75  n.7.  That  is  not  true.  The  Resolution  and  Report  endorse
contribution  limits,  disclosure,  the  independent  Federal  Election
Commission,  and  the  concept  of  public  financing.

.

FECA  stem  directly  from  its  impact  upon  communica-
tion.”  United  States  Amicus  Br.  26.

Those  arguments  misconceive  the  manner  in  which
untrammeled  money  has  harmed  the  electoral  process.
They  also  misconceive  the  means  by  which  giving  and
spending  limits  will  accomplish  their  purposes.

Giving  and  spending  limits  are  aimed  at  the  harmful
effects  of  unlimited  money—effects  that  occur  regardless
of  whether  the  money  is  spent  to  disseminate  speech.  The
influence  of  large  contributions  on  candidates  and  elected
officials  occurs  without  regard  to  any  speech  of  the  donor
or  the  recipient.  Such  contributions  influence  their  re-
cipients  not  because  they  create  communication  but  be-
cause  they  create  an  improper  obligation  in  favor  of  the
donors—in  appellants’  words,  “the  activity  brings  about
results  other  than  those  which  stem  from  persuasion.”
Appellants’  Br.  52.  Candidates  and  elected  officials  favor
their  large  contributors  simply  because  they  dare  not  bite
the  hand  that  feeds  them.  That  conflict  of interest  thwarts
the  electoral  process  by preventing  candidates  and  officials
from  responding  to  the  views  of  the  many  constituents
who  are  not  large  donors.

As  Professor  Freund  has  said,  large  contributors  “are
in  a  position  of  exerting  influence  through  wealth...  .
They  are  operating  vicariously  through  the  power  of their
purse,  rather  than  through  the  power  of  their  ideas”—an
activity  that  ranks  “lower  in  the  hierarchy  of  First
Amendment  values”  than  speech  itself.’

Similarly,  large  donors  wield  disproportionate  power
over  elections  not  because  their  speech  or  their  viewpoint
is  more  persuasive  but—as  the  Government  as  Amicus

100  Freund,  supra  note  31,  at  74.

90

91

recognizes—“because  it  has  the  most  money  behind  it.”
United  States  Amicus  Br.  32.  And  rising  campaign  costs
are  harmful  not  because  they  create  communication  but
because  they  create  an  incentive  to  obtain  ever  greater
amounts  of  contributions  and  a  barrier  to  the  entry  of
otherwise  qualified  candidates.**
In  short,  none  of  the
harms  at  which  these  limits  aim  arises  from  the  fact
that  the  person  regulated  is  communicating,  or  from  the
effect  of  his  message  on  his  audience.  The  ill  effects  to
be  prevented  would  arise  even  if  the  giver’s  or  spender’s
conduct  had  no  communicative  element  whatever.

Giving  and  spending  limits  correct  these  evils  by  re-
moving  the  corrosive  effects  of  money  itself.  They  are
not  censorial—any  effect  on  communication  is  incidental,
not  the  means  by which  the  limits  accomplish  their  goals.
An  illustration  of  this  fact  is  provided  by  the  Federal
Communications  Commission’s  recent  ruling  that  the
“equal  time”  rule  of  the  Communications  Act  does  not
apply  to  candidate  press  conferences  or  debates  between
candidates.**  By  removing  an  obstacle  to  broadcast  cov-
erage  of  those  events,  the  ruling  will  produce  a  wider
reach  for  candidate  speech  on  electoral  matters  at  no
cost  to  candidates  or  their  supporters.  It  will  do  so  with-
out  in  any  way  frustrating  the  objectives  of  the  new
giving  and  spending  limits.  Thus,  it  illustrates  that  the
limits  do  not  depend  on  curtailment  of  speech  to  accom-
plish  their  goals.

101  See  Comment,  Free  Speech  Implications  of Campaign  Expendi-
ture  Ceilings,  supra  note  79,  at  216;  Note,  Campaign  Spending
Regulation:  The  r  wilure  of  the  First  Step,  8 Harv.  J.  Legis.  640,
644  (1971).

102  In  re  Petitions  of  Aspen  Institute  Program  on  Communica-
tion  &  Society,  et  al.,  FCC  75-1090  (Mem.  Op.  &  Order,  Sept.  25,
1975),  pet’n  for  review  filed,  No.  75-1951,  D.C.  Cir.,  Sept.  26,  1975.

Congress  was  not  concerned  about  the  consequences  of
anyone’s  words  or  ideas  but  about  the  effects  of  large
By  removing
donations  and  rising  costs  themselves.
those  effects,  the  limits  free  candidates  and  elected  of-
ficials  to  be  more  responsive  to  the  ideas  of  all,  accord-
ing  to  their  merit.  Monetary  limits  attack  the  effects  of
money  at  their  source;  as  the  Government’s  Amicus  Brief
says  of  the  draft-card  law  in  O’Brien,  “every  one  of
.  could  be  achieved  without  regard  to
[their]  goals  .
any  effect  upon  communication.”  United  States  Amicus
Br.  27."

.

Thomas  v.  Collins,  323  U.S.  516  (1945),  and  Eastern
Railroad  Presidents  Conference  v.  Noerr  Motor  Freight,
Inc.,  365  U.S.  127  (1961),  cited  in  the  Government’s
Amicus  Brief  at  32-34,  are  therefore  inapposite.  Both
cases  disapproved  prohibitions  against  speech  itself,  which
were  based  on  the  “bad”  economic  motivations  of  the
speakers.  Limits  on  campaign  giving  and  spending  for-
bid  no  speech;  they  leave  everyone  free  to  speak  his  mind
in  campaigns,  regardless  of his  motivations.  They  merely
limit  the  use  of  money  in  campaigns—regardless  of
whether  it  is  used  in  connection  with  speech  and  again
regardless  of  motivations—because  of  the  proven  evils
attributable  to  the  money  itself.**

1083  See  Ely,  Flag  Desecration:  A  Case  Study  in  the  Roles  of
Categorization  and  Balancing  in  First  Amendment  Analysis,  88
Harv.  L.  Rev.  1482,  1496-97  (1975).

106  The  Government’s  Amicus  Brief  (at  37-41)  suggests  that  the
limits  are  intended  to  prevent  people  from  being  “annoyed  by
‘excessive’  costly  communication”  because  it  is  “noisy”  or  “drowns
out”  other  speakers—an  interest  that  might  be  viewed  as  relating
to  the  suppression  of  at  least  the  quantity  of  communication  itself.
Likewise,  appellants  assert  that  the  limits  are  designed  to  prevent
“too  much  communication.”  Appellants’  Br.  80.  However,  reducing
“noisy”  or  “excessive”  communication  is  not  a  goal  of  these  limits.

As  the  legislative  record  shows,  Congress  was  concerned  about
the  effects  of  money,  regardless  of  its  relation  to  communication.

92

relevance

also  misjudge  the

For  similar  reasons,  appellants  and  the  Government’s
Amicus  brief
of  this
Court’s  decisions  striking  down  restrictions  on  the  press.
Appellants’  Br.  47,  81,  118-19;  United  States  Amicus  Br.
12-13,  16,  44,  47.  The  prohibition  of  election-day  cam-
paign  editorials  voided  in  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S.
214  (1966),  was  a  direct  restriction  on  expression  by the
press.  Appellants  assert  that,  if  it  had  been  a  ban  on
spending  money  to publish  election-day  editorials,  it would
be  indistinguishable  from  the  spending  limits  challenged
here.  The  analogy  is  only  superficial.  A  spending  ban
imposed  for  a  purpose  that  could  only  be  achieved  by
suppressing  the  editorials  might  be  as  invalid  as  the  law
struck  down  in  Mills.  But  the  spending  limits  in  these
laws  can  accomplish  their  ends  without  regard  to  whether
they  keep  anyone  from  speaking.  They  attack  ill  effects
of  spending  unrelated  to  speech.  And,  as  noted  below,
they  are  carefully  drafted  to  avoid  any  restriction  on  the
press.

Grosjean  V.  American  Press  Co.,  297  U.S.  233  (1936),
is  equally  inapposite.  The  Court  there  voided  a  special
tax  on  newspapers  which  had  “a  long  history  of  hostile
misuse  against  the  freedom  of  the  press”  and  was  a  “de-
liberate  and  calculated  device  in  the  guise  of  a  tax  to
limit  the  circulation  of  information  to  which  the  public
is entitled  in  virtue  of  the  constitutional  guaranties.”  Jd.
at  250.  In  effect,  the  tax  was  a  regulation  of  content,
applying  almost  wholly  to  those  newspapers  in  Louisiana
which  were  opposed  to  the  political  regime  then  in

And  the  court  of  appeals  never  mentioned  “noisiness”  as  a  justi-
fication  for  the  limits.  Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77  (1949),  dis-
cussed  infra,  is  relevant  not  because  these  statutes  share  its  con-
cern  about  the  “noisiness”  of  speech  but  because  Kovacs  illustrates
the  permissibility  of  regulating  speech-related  conduct  to  serve
legitimate  goals  unrelated  to  the  suppression  of  ideas.

eo

- a ew

93

power.**  The  Court  stressed  that  newspapers  may  be
taxed  for  other,  legitimate  purposes  whose  achievement
does  not  depend  on  suppression  of  speech,  even  though
such  a  tax  would  reduce  the  money  available  for  publish-
ing.  In  fact,  a  year  later,  the  Court  dismissed  an  appeal
from  a  decision  upholding  the  application  to  newspapers
of  a  nondiscriminatory  privilege  tax.  Giragi  v.  Moore,
301  U.S.  670  (1937).  Similarly,  the  wages,  prices,  trade
practices,  and  supplies  of  the  press  may  be  regulated  in
an  evenhanded  manner  for  purposes  unrelated  to  speech,
even  though  this  may  also  reduce  the  resources  available
for  publishing.  Associated  Press  v.  United  States,  326
U.S.  1  (1945).  In  the  same  way,  campaign  giving  and
spending  limits  may  be  imposed  to  serve  purposes  that
are  not  achieved  through  suppression  of  speech,  even  if
they  incidentally  impinge  on  campaign  expression.

Moreover,  decisions  such  as  Mills,  Grosjean,  and  Miami
Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241  (1974),
reflect  in  part  the  special  role  of  the  press  in  our  system
of  freedom  of  expression.  The  critical  importance  of  a
free  press  in  our  history  requires  that  the  most  extreme
necessity  be  shown  to  justify  any  direct  restriction  on
its  freedom  to  publish.  E.g.,  New  York  Times  Co.  V.
Sullivan,  376  U.S.  254  (1964);  “Or  of  the  Press,”  Ad-
dress  of  Justice  Potter  Stewart,  Yale  Law  School  Ses-
quicentennial  Convocation  (Nov.  2, 1974).  Compare  New
York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713  (1971),
with  Gravel  v.  United  States,  408  U.S.  606  (1972).
The  decisions  placing  some  limits  on  the  rights  of  re-
porters  to  gather  news,  Pell  v.  Procunier,  417  USS.
817  (1974);  Branzburg  v.  Hayes,  408  U.S.  665  (1972),
do  not  erode  that  principle.  In  light  of  the  special  role

105  Alfange,  Free  Speech  and  Symbolic  Conduct:  The  Draft-Card

Burning  Case,  1968  Sup.  Ct.  Rev.  1,  33-34.

94

95

of  the  press,  laws  regulating  campaign  practices  must
scrupulously  respect  its  editorial  freedom—as  these  laws
clearly  do.  T.  Emerson,  supra  note  31,  at  631-40,  667-
71;  see  pp.  158-60,  infra.

Moreover,  the  spending  of  large  amounts  of  money  is
conduct,  not  speech.  This  Court  has  repeatedly  held  that
nonspeech  conduct  is  not  entitled  to  the  same  protection
as  speech,  even  when  engaged  in  for  purposes  of  expres-
sion.  For  example,  in  Cow  v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.  559,
564  (1965),  it  upheld  a  statute  banning  demonstrations
in  or  near  a  courthouse:

“.  .

.  We  are  not  concerned  here  with  such  a  pure
form  of  expression  as  newspaper  comment  or a  tele-
gram  by a  citizen  to  a  public  official.  We  deal  in
this  case  not  with  free  speech  alone,  but  with  ex-
pression  mixed  with  particular  conduct  ....

“We  hold  that  this  statute  on  its  face  is  a  valid
law  dealing  with  conduct  subject  to  regulation  so
as  to  vindicate  important  interests  of  society  and
that  the  fact  that  free  speech  is  intermingled  with
such  conduct  does  not  bring  it  constitutional  protec-
tion.”

The  Court  in  Coz  held  that  the  statute  was  justified  by
the  state’s  interest  in  preventing  its  courts  from  being
subjected  to  improper  influences—an  interest  similar  to
that  served  by these  statutes:

“It  is,  of  course,  true  that  most  judges  will  be
influenced  only  by  what  they  see  and  hear  in  court.
However,  judges  are  human;  and  the  legislature  has
the  right  to  recognize  the  danger  that  some  judges,
jurors,  and  other  court  officials,  will  be  consciously
or  unconsciously  influenced  by  demonstrations  in  or

near  their  courtrooms  both  prior  to  and  at  the  time
of  the  trial.  A  State  may  also  protect  the  judicial
process  from  being  misjudged  in  the  minds  of  the
public...  .”  Id.  at  565.

These  considerations  were  held  to  justify  “a  legislative
determination  based  on  experience  that  such  conduct  in-
herently  threatens  the  judicial  process.”
Jd.  See  also
Cameron  v.  Johnson,  390  U.S.  611  (1968).  Surely  Con-
gress  in  this  case  had  no  less  power  to  remove  the  danger
that  elected  officials  ‘“‘will  be  consciously  or  unconsciously
influenced”  by  massive  campaign  contributions,  and  to
“protect  the  [political]  process  from  being  misjudged  in
the  minds  of  the  public.”

Conduct  that  merely  amplifies  the  effects  of  speech
can  also  be  regulated  to  serve  a  non-censorial  purpose.
In  Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  386  U.S.  77  (1949),  the  Court  up-
held  a  city  ordinance  forbidding  the  use  of  loudspeakers
emitting  “loud  and  raucous  noises”  on  the  public  streets.
It  affirmed  the  conviction  of  a  person  who  had  used  a
sound  truck  to  publicize  his  views  on  a  pending  labor  dis-
pute,  holding  that  his  First  Amendment  right  to  speak

106  See  also  Giboney  v.  Empire  Storage  &  Ice  Co.,  336  U.S.  490
(1949);  Zemel  v.  Rusk,  381  U.S.  1,  16-17  (1965);  Broadrick  v.
Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  614  (1973).

Mr.  Justice  Black,  who  believed  that  the  First  Amendment’s
protection  of  speech  is  absolute,  nonetheless  consistently  recognized
that  conduct  merely  related  to  speech  is  entitled  to  a  lesser  degree
of  protection.  E.g.,  Giboney  v.  Empire  Storage  &  Ice  Co.,  supra;
Street  v.  New  York,  394  U.S.  576,  609-10  (1969)  (dissenting  opin-
ion)  ; Tinker  v.  Des  Moines  School  Dist.,  393  U.S.  503,  515  (1969)
(dissenting  opinion).

Mr.  Justice  Douglas  has  expressed  the  same  view.  E.g.,  Com-
munist  Party  v.  Subversive  Activities  Control  Board,  367  U.S.  1,
173-74  (1961);  Teamsters  Local  695  v.  Vogt,  Inc.,  354  U.S.  284,
295  (1957)  (dissenting  opinions).  See  also  United  States  v.  CIO,
335  U.S.  106,  139-40  (1948)  (Rutledge,  Black,  Douglas,  Murphy,
J.J.,  concurring).

96

97

did  not  prevent  reasonable  regulation  of  place,  time,  and
volume.  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter,  concurring,  explained:

“Only  a  disregard  of  vital  differences  between  natu-
ral  speech,  even  of  the  loudest  spellbinders,  and  the
noise  of  sound  trucks  would  give  sound  trucks  the
constitutional  rights  accorded  to  the  unaided  human
voice.  Nor  is  it  for  this  Court  to  devise  the  terms
on  which  sound  trucks  should  be  allowed  to  operate,
if  at  all.  These  are  matters  for  the  legislative  judg-
ment  controlled  by  public  opinion.  So  long  as  a  leg-
islature  does  not  prescribe  what  ideas  may  be  noisily
expressed  and  what  may  not  be,  nor  discriminate
among  those  who  would  make  inroads  upon  the  pub-
lic  peace,  it  is  not  for  us  to  supervise  the  limits  the
legislature  may  impose  in  safeguarding  the  steadily
narrowing  opportunity  for  serenity  and  reflection.
Without  such  opportunities  freedom  of  thought  be-
comes  a  mocking  phrase,  and  without  freedom  of
theught  there  can  be  no  free  society.”  Jd.  at  96-97.

The  public  interests  here  are  different  from  those  in
Kovacs—not  protection  against  noise,  but  against  the
corrosive  effects  of  money.  Much  more  clearly  than  in
Kovacs,  these  laws  can  achieve  their  goals  regardless
of  their  effect—or  lack  of  effect—on  the  volume  or  reach
of  speech  itself.  Campaign  money  may,  of  course,  be
used  as  a  means  of  amplifying  the  reach  of  “the  un-
aided  human  voice.”
But  reasonable  and  evenhanded
limits  on  giving  and  spending  serve  purposes  unrelated  to
money’s  usefulness  in  this  respect.  They  suppress  no  one’s
ideas  or  speech.  Any  incidental  effect  in  reducing  the
amplification  of expression  by the  wealthy  does  not  offend
the  First  Amendment.  As  Professor  Freund  has  said:

“The  right  to  speak  is,  I submit,  more  central  to
the  values  envisaged  by  the  First  Amendment  than

the  right  to  spend.  We  are  dealing  here  not  so  much
with  the  right  of  personal  expression  or  even  asso-
ciation,  but  with  dollars  and  decibels.  And  just  as
the  volume  of  sound  may  be  limited  by  law,  so  the
volume  of  dollars  may  be  limited,  without  violating
the  First  Amendment.”  *”

Professors  Alexander  Bickel  and  Abraham  Rosenthal
have  expressed  the  identical  view.’”

Moreover,  appellants  are  wrong  in  contending  that
reasonable  giving  and  spending  limits  will  necessarily
lower  the  overall  “level  of  political  communication.”  Ap-
pellants’  Br.  48.  Appellants’  stress  on  the  amounts  given
or  spent  in  the  past  misses  the  point—because  it  as-
sumes  that  what  has  been,  must  and  should  be.  If  large
contributions  and  expenditures  have  played  a  big  role  in
some  recent  campaigns,  it  does  not  follow  that  future
campaigns  will  be  unable  to  adapt  to  these  laws  and  to
accomplish  ample  “political  communication”  through  dif-
ferent  means.  Indeed,  such  adaptation  is  a  goal  of  these
laws.

Despite  appellants’  protests  to  the  contrary,  money  is
The  stump  has  not
not  all  there  is  to  campaigning.
wholly  been  replaced  by the  cash  register.  Citizens  can—
and  do—participate  in  politics  in  myriad  ways  that  do
not  involve  giving  or  spending  large  sums  of  campaign
money.  Findings  I, { 5,  IIA  J.A.  6-8.  Citizen  activities
that  can  affect  the  political  process  include  joining  or
forming  an  organization  dedicated  to  one’s  goals;  organ-
izing  and  attending  rallies;  distributing  handbills;  lobby-

107  Freund,  supra  note  31,  at  72-73.

108  See  Bickel,  supra  note  31,  at  65  (“I  agree  that  regulating  the
giving  of  money  is  not  the  same  as  regulating  speech”);  A.  Rosen-
thal,  supra  note  31,  at  21  (“Spending  money  is  not  the  same  as

98

99

ing  for  or  against  legislation;  writing  letters  to  office-
holders  or  newspaper  editors;  drafting  and  circulating
petitions;  making  speeches;  and  contributing  personal
services  to  candidates  for  office.  Such  personal  services
can  often  replace  services  for  which  candidates  would
otherwise  have  to  pay.’  The  vast  majority  of  citizens
who  participate  in  these  activities  do  so  without  spending
significant  amounts.*”®

To  the  extent  that  giving  and  spending  limits  re
strict  some  avenues  of  political  activity,  the  result  is
most  likely  to  be  an  increase  in  these  and  other  ac-
tivities,  rather  than  the  silencing  of  any  expression.  The
district  court  has  found  that:

“Difficulty  in  engaging  in  one  kind  of  activity  [af-
fecting  the  political  process]  may  induce  an  indi-
vidual  to  engage  instead  in  a  different  activity.”
Findings  I, 7 5, IIA  J.A.  7-8.

This  Court  has  stressed  that  the  availability  of  multiple,
unrestricted  avenues  of  expression  is  significant  in  de-

10@  Even  appellants’  affiant  Cole  concluded  that  only  about  40
percent  of  all  campaign  money  has  been  spent  for  purposes  directly
related  to  expression.  R.  Cole,  Campaign  Spending  in  Senate  Elec-
tions  30,  100-01  (1975).  The  remainder  goes  to  pay  staff  salaries,
travel  expenses,  the  gathering  of  intelligence,  or  even,  as  the  Water-
gate  experience  indicated,  “dirty  tricks”  (Findings  I,  § 128,  IIA
J.A.  156)—activities  only  indirectly  related  to  the  writing  or  dis-
semination  of  political  speech.  Appellants  have  not  shown  that  any
reduction  in  campaign  spending  will  necessarily  come  out  of  speech-
related  expenses.

110  Appellants  claim  that  “campaign  contributions  tend  to  be  less
concentrated  in  a  small  percentage  of  the  population  than  other
forms  of  political  activity.”  Appellants’  Br.  62.  But  the  facts  show
that  14  percent  of  the  population  have  worked  actively  for  a  candi-
date  (Findings  I,  § 107,  IIA  J.A.  97),  compared  to  the  mere  one
percent  of  the  people  who  contribute  90  percent  of  the  money  all
eee  receive  for  their  campaigns,  Findings  I, § 100,  IIA  J.A.

termining  the  permissibility  of  regulations  that  inci-
dentally  impinge  on  one  mode  of  expression:

.

“That  more  people  may  be  more  easily  and  cheaply
reached  by  sound  trucks  .
.  is  not  enough  to  call
forth  constitutional  protection  ...  when  easy  means
.  [The  challenged  loud-
.
of  publicity  are  open.  .
speaker  law  places]  no  restriction  upon  the  commu-
nication  of  ideas  or  discussion  of  issues  by  the  hu-
man  voice,  by newspapers,  by pamphlets,  by dodgers.”
Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77,  88-89  (1949).™

Moreover,  since  giviny  and  spending  limits  apply  only
to  candidate-related  activities,  an  individual  can  give  or
spend  as  much  money  as  he  likes  to  promote  issues  and
to  help  issue-oriented  groups  to  achieve  goals  other  than
the  immediate  election  of  candidates.  These  laws  impose
no  limits  whatsoever  on  giving  or  spending  to  promote
ideas  or  causes,  only  on  giving  or  spending  to  promote
personal  candidacies  for  office.  They  make  the  same  dis-
tinction  between  partisan  campaigning  and  other  political
activity  as  do  the  Hatch  Act  restrictions  upheld  in
United  Public  Workers  v.  Mitchell,  330  U.S.  75,  99-100
(1947),  and  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers,
413  U.S.  548,  556  (1973).

Appellants’  brief  itself  highlights  the  significance  of
noneandidate  political  activities.
Appellants  recall  suc
cessful  political  movements—some  of  which  have  changed
history—that  relied  to  some  extent  on  large  contribu-
tions.  Appellants’  Br.  122-23.  But  the  movements  they
cite  accomplished  their  ends  largely  outside  the  electoral

Appellants’  concessions  demonstrate  how  limited  is  the

Such  limits  do  not
They  merely  prevent—at  the
stifle  political  activity.
source—the  abuses  that  flow  from  candidates’  dependence
on  large  private  contributions.  If  set  at  reasonable  lev-

myriad  political  activities  that  do  not  involve  giving
large  sums  of  money  to  candidates  for  federal  office.  As

112  Appellants  acknowledge  that  there  are  “at  least  thirty  ways  of
participating  in  the  political  process,  ...  only  three  of  which...
are  regulated  by  the  challenged  statutes,  even  though  many  others
are  of  comparable  or  greater  effectiveness  than  campaign  contribu-
tions  and  involve  the  use  of  money  for  political  purposes.”  Jd.  at
125  (emphasis  added).

43  Congress  is  now  considering  bills  to  provide  public  financing
for  congressional  campaigns.  H.R.  9100;  S.  564.  Lust  year,  the
Senate  passed  a  congressional  public  financing  bil.
120  Cong.
Rec.  55852  (daily  ed.  Apr.  11,  1974).  This  year,  in  the  House,  the
bill  already  is  co-sponsored  by  221  Congressmen,  more  than  a  ma-
jority  of  the  House  membership.  121  Cong.  Rec.  H8048  (daily  ed.

appellants  admit,  “None  of  these  are  touched  by  the
statutes.”  Appellants’  Br.  125.

Even  if  we  were  wrong,  and  the  limits  did  depend  for
their  effectiveness  on  limiting  the  quantity  of  campaign
speech,  the  limits  would  be  sustainable.  Equal  limits  in
a  debating  context  are  common  in  our  free  and  open
society  and  have  never  been  thought  to  impose  unconsti-
tutional  limits  on  speech—even  political  speech.
Court
rules  fixing  equal  page  limits  for  briefs  and  equal  time
limits  for  oral  argument  have  never  been  thought  to  in-
fringe  the  First  Amendment.  In  any  structured  debate,
rules  fixing  equal  time  are  accepted  as  necessary  methods
of  achieving  focus  and  good  order.  Like  the  restrictions
on  the  time,  place,  and  manner  of  speech  repeatedly  up-
held  by  this  Court,’*  they  are  entirely  neutral  as  be-
tween  speakers  and  points  of  view.  When  the  debate  is
conducted  under  governmental  auspices,  as  in  a  judicial
proceeding  or  an  election  for  public  office,  reasonable  and
evenhanded  rules  that  fix  equal  limits  do not  automatically
transgress  the  First  Amendment.

C.  Reasonable  Giving  and  Spending  Limits  Are  Not
Unnecessarily  Restrictive  Means  To  Accomplish
Congress’  Goals

Although  giving  and  spending  limits  touch  incidentally
on  First  Amendment  activities,  they  do  so  no  more  than
reasonably  necessary  to  preserve  the  integrity  of  the  elec-
toral  process."*  This  Court  has  recently  reaffirmed  that

14  F.g.,  Grayned  Vv.  Rockford,  408  U.S.  104  (1972);  Cameron  v.
Johonson,  370  U.S.  611  (1968);  Adderly  v.  Florida,  385  U.S.  39
(1966) ; Cow  v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.  559  (1965).

115  As  noted,  we  deal  in  Part  V  with  appellants’  complaints  about
the  particular  monetary  limits  contained  in  these  laws  and  show
that  appellants  have  failed  to  prove  any  palpable  restrictions  on
their  political  activity.

102

103

laws  in  this  area  must  take  the  least  restrictive  course
that  will  achieve  Congress’  goals.  In  American  Party  V.
White,  415  U.S.  767,  781  (1974),  a  nearly  unanimous
Court  held  that  electoral  regulations  are  “constitutionally
valid  measures”  if  “reasonably  taken  in  pursuit  of  vital
state  objectives  that  cannot  be  served  equally  well  in
significantly  less  burdensome  ways.”  Where  a  variety  of
equally  effective  means  are  available  to  achieve  compel-
ling  public  interests,  Congress  may  not  choose  those
which  are  significantly  more  intrusive  on  First  Amend-
ment  interests.
Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479,  488
(1960);  see  Note,  Less  Drastic  Means  and  the  First
Amendment,  78  Yale  L.J.  464  (1969).  However,  the  fact
that  less  intrusive  measures  can  be  imagined  does  not
warrant  a  finding  of  unconstitutionality  unless  it  is
clear  that  those  measures  can  accomplish  the  congres-
sional  goals  with  equal  effectiveness.
“The  Constitution
does  not  require  the  state  to  choose  ineffectual  means  to
achieve  its  aims.”  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724,  736
(1974);  id.  at  761  (Brennan,  J.,  dissenting);  Rosario  v.
Rockefeller,  410  U.S.  752,  762  n.10  (1973).

It  need  not  be  demonstrated  that  every  conceivable
application  of  the  statute  is  necessary  to  further  the
statutory  objectives.  Procunier  v.  Martinez,  416  U.S.  396,
414  (1974);  United  Public  Workers  v.  Mitchell,  330  U.S.
75,  100-01  (1947).  This  Court  upheld  the  prohibition  on
courthouse  picketing  in  Cox  v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.  559
(1965),  to  protect  the  integrity  of  the  judiciary,  even
though  that  law  might  apply  in  individual  instances
where  such  picketing  would  in  fact  have  no  coercive
effect.  A  statute  is  not  unnecessarily  restrictive  merely
because  it  may  affect  certain  innocent  transactions—as

long  as  the  statute  is  “generally  necessary”  to  achieve  the
Government’s  vital  ends.  Procunier  v.  Martinez,  416  U.S.
at  414.

Congress  determined  after  exhaustive  inquiry  that  giv-
ing  and  spending  limits,  disclosure,  public  financing,  and
independent  enforcement  together  are  necessary  to  pre-
serve  the  integrity  of  the  electoral  process  because  other
measures  cannot  achieve  that  purpose  with  equal  effec-
tiveness.  For  half  a  century,  Congress  has  experimented
with  various  combinations  of  disclosure  requirements,*”®
contribution  limits,’’’  expenditure  limits,***  public  financ-
ing,’  bribery  laws,**®  and  improper  influence  laws,’
but  those  laws  did  not  solve  the  urgent  problems  pre-
sented  by the  abuse  of  money  in  electoral  politics.

Appellants  urge  that  the  only  means  constitutionally
available  to  Congress  to  regulate  the  use  of  money  in
elections  are  minimal  disclosure  requirements  and  a  pro-
hibition  against  bribery.  Appellants’  Br.  77.  That  sug-
gestion  mocks  the  gravity  and  complexity  of  the  problems
Disclosure  and  bribery  laws
that  confronted  Congress.

tion  Campaign  Act  of  1971,  86  Stat.  11,  2  U.S.C.  §§ 431-454.

The  conclusion  that  lesser  means  had  not  sufficed  was
amply  justified  by  the  historical  record  before  Congress.
Appellants  have  produced  nothing  that  would  justify  this
Court’s  substituting  a  different  reading  of  history.  This
is not  a  case  in  which  the  Court  can  identify  alternative
measures  plainly  equivalent  in  effectiveness.  E.g.,  Martin
v.  City  of Struthers,  319  U.S.  141  (1943).  As  the  court
of  appeals  correctly  concluded:

105

“We  see  no  principled  basis  on  which  a  court  can
displace  the  legislative  judgment  that  reporting  and
disclosure  are  necessary,  but  not  sufficient,  remedies
to achieve  the  objective  of  trammeling  ‘the  pernicious
influence  of  “big  money”  campaign  contributions.’  ”
Ct.  App.  Op.  1518-19,  quoting  United  States  v.  UAW,
352  U.S.  567,  572  (1972)  (footnotes  omitted).
|  This  Court  has  reached  the  same  conclusion  in  deci-
sions  upholding  laws  more  restrictive  than  these,  to  serve
interests  certainly  no  more  compelling.

1.  This  Court  Has  Upheld  More  Restrictive
Limitations  on  Direct  Political  Activities
The  Court  has  upheld  laws  that  restrict  not  mere  po-
litical  spending  but  direct  political  participation  itself.  In
Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.
548  (1973),  it  upheld  the  Hatch  Act  provision  forbid-
ding  federal  employees  from  taking  “an  active  part  in
political  management  or  in  political  campaigns.”  Hatch
Act  §9(a),  5  U.S.C.  § 7324(a)  (2).  On  the  same  day,
it  held  that  states  may  also  restrict  partisan  activity  by
their  employees—as  all  50  states  have  done.  Broadrick
Vv.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  604-05  n.2  (1978).

Restrictions  on  partisan  activities  by  public  employees
serve  two  purposes,  similar  to  the  purposes  of  the  laws
challenged  here:  They  protect  (1)  the  public’s  right  to
have  its  civil  servants  “enforce  the  law  and  execute  the
programs  of  the  Government  without  bias  or  favoritism
for  or  against  any  political  party  or  group  or  the  mem-
bers  thereof,”  413  U.S.  at  565,  and  (2)  the  employees’
right  not  to  be  pressured  by their  superiors  to  engage  in
partisan  politics.

This  Court  affirmed  the  appropriateness  of  these  gov-
ernmental  objectives  in  United  Public  Workers  v.  Mit-

106

107

chell,  330  U.S.  75  (1947).  In  Letter  Carriers  and  Broad-
rick,  it  “unhesitatingly  reaffirm[ed]”  that  even  the  Hatch
Act’s  broad  prohibition  of  any  partisan  campaign  activi-
ty by public  employees  is  permissible  to  accomplish  these
It  rejected  pre-
important  objectives,  413  U.S.  at  556.
cisely  the  overbreadth  attack  made  by  appellants  here:
that  Congress  could  only  prohibit  corruption  itself,  not
take  broader  prophylactic  measures.  The  Court  said:

“(T]he  joint  judgment  of  the  Executive  and  Con-
gress  has  been  that  to  protect  the  rights  of  federal
employees  with  respect  to  their  jobs  and  their  politi-
cal  acts  and  beliefs  it  is not  enough  merely  to  forbid
one  employee  to  attempt  to  influence  or  coerce  an-
other.  ..  .  Perhaps  Congress  at  some  time  will  come
to  a  different  view  of  the  realities  of  political  life
and  Government  service;  but  that  is  its  current  view
of  the  matter,  and  we  are  not  now  in  any  position
to  dispute  it.  Nor,  in  our  view,  does  the  Constitution
forbid  it.”  Id.  at  566-67.’*°

The  Court  in  Letter  Carriers  also  recognized  that,  in
order  to  avoid  the  appearance  of  corruption  or  bias,  it
may  be  necessary  for  Congress  to  act  more  broadly  than
merely  to  prohibit  the  undesired  practices.

“(I]t  is not  only  important  that  the  Government  and
its  employees  in  fact  avoid  practicing  political  jus-
tice,  but  it  is  also  critical  that  they  appear  to  the
public  to  be  avoiding  it,  if  confidence  in  the  system

123  As  the  dissenters  in  Broadrick  noted,  the  appellants  there
conceded  “that  the  State  could  prohibit  the  conduct  with  which
they  were  charged  [partisan  campaigning  for a local  official]  with-
out  infringing  the  guarantees  of  the  First  Amendment”;  the  dis-
senters  found  the  statute  to  be  insufficiently  precise  in  accomplish-
ing  that  goal.  413  U.S.  at  621-22,  628-29  (Brennan,  J.,  joined  by
Stewart  &  Marshall,  J.J.);  see  id.  at  597  (Douglas,  J.).  By  con-
trast,  these  laws  set  precise  dollar  limits  and  provide  for  clarifica-
tion  of  any  ambiguities  by  the  FEC.

of  representative  Government  is  not  to  be  eroded  to
a  disastrous  extent.”  Jd.  at  565.

Of  course,  the  Hatch  Act  restrictions  on  direct  politi-
cal  activities  would  be  unconstitutionally  broad  if  ap-
plied  to  the  whole  electorate.  But  Letter  Carriers  pro-
vides  strong  support  for  the  proposition  that  where  les-
ser  measures  have  failed,  Congress  has  latitude  to  im-
pose  stronger  ones  in  order  to  meet  compelling  needs.
The  monetary  limits  challenged  here  are  far  less  restric-
tive  than  the  Hatch  Act  strictures,  and  there  has  been
no  demonstration  that  they  go  further  than  necessary
to  meet  related  and  equally  compelling  needs.

2.  The  Courts  Have  Upheld  More  Restrictive  Limi-

tations  on  Political  Giving  and  Spending

The  courts  have  also  upheld  regulations  of  political
giving  and  spending  that  are  more  restrictive  than  the
limits  in  these  laws.  Federal  law  for  many  years  has
prohibited  corporations  (since  1907)  and  unions  (since
1943)  from  making  any  contributions  to  federal  electoral
campaigns.  18  U.S.C.  § 610;  Note,  Statutory  Regulation
of  Political  Campaign  Funds,  66  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1259
(1953).  Since  1947,  that  law  has  also  forbidden  corpo-
rate  or  union  expenditures  aimed  at  influencing  voters  at
large.  A  corporation  or  union  may  spend  only  to  ex-
press  its  views  to  its  stockholders  or  members  or  to  en-
courage  them  to  make  voluntary  contributions  to  a  sepa-
rate  segregated  fund  managed  by the  corporation  or  un-
ion  for  political  purposes.’**

es
ant
eames

12418  U.S.C.  §610.  The  Government’s  Amicus  Brief  (at  35-36,
74)  seriously  misreads  these  laws  to  allow  such  separate  funds  to
make  campaign  contributions  and  expenditures  without  limit  from
the  money  voluntarily  donated  by stockholders  or  members.  In  fact,
the  funds  are  political  committees  subject  to  the  same  contribution
limits  as  other  committzes  and  to  the  $1,000  “independent  expendi-

108

The  prohibitions  in  Section  610  serve  two  purposes:
to  prevent  undue  exertion  of  the  political  power  that  cor-
porations  and  unions  possess  because  of  their  aggregated
wealth,  and  to  protect  minority  stockholders  or  members
who  do  not  wish  to  contribute  to  causes  of  the  organi-
zation’s  choosing.  The  first  of  these  purposes  is  closely
related  to  the  goals  of  the  laws  challenged  here.

This  Court  has  never  been  required  to  rule  on  the  con-
stitutionality  of  Section  610  or  its  predecessors.  How-
ever,  it  has  dealt  with  the  statute  on  several  occasions
and  has  recognized  the  legitimacy  of  its  legislative  pur-
poses.  See  Cort  v.  Ash,  48  U.S.L.W.  4773  (1975);  Pipe-
fitters  Union  v.  United  States,  407  U.S.  385,  402,  413-17
(1972);  United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567,  593
(1957);  United  States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.  106,  113-15
In  UAW,  Mr.  Justice  Douglas,  dissenting
(1948).%°
from  the  majority  decision  not  to  pass  on  the  statute’s
constitutionality  as  applied  to  a  union-financed  radio
broadcast,  expressed  the  view  of  three  Justices  that,  “If
Congress  is  of  the  opinion  that  large  contributions  by

ture”  limit  of  Section  608(e).  The  exceptions  in  Sections  610  and
608(e)  (2)(B),  to  which  the  Government’s  Amicus  Brief  refers,
merely  codify  this  Court’s  ruling  in  United  States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.
106  (1948),  that  organizations  subject  to  Section  610  may  spend
to communicate  with  their  members  and  to solicit  contributions  from
them  to  a  voluntary  fund.

128  The  CIO  decision  construed  Section  610  to  allow  corporations
and  unions  to  spend  to  communicate  with  their  stockholders  or
members  about  federal  candidates.  Four  Justices,  concurring,
thought  the  statute  forbade  such  communications  and  that  it  was
unconstitutional  in  that  respect  only.  However,  they  added:

“Other  applications  are  not  in  question.  There  can  be  little
doubt  of  Congress’  power  to  regulate  the  making  of  political
contributions  and  expenditures  by  labor  unions,  as  well  as  by
other  organizations  and  individuals,  in  the  interest  of  free  and
pure  elections  and  the  prevention  of  official  corruption,  by  ap-
propriate  measures  not  trenching  on  those  basic  rights.”  335
U.S.  at  139  (emphasis  added).

ee es

109

labor  unions  to  candidates  for  office  and  to  political  par-
ties  have  had  an  undue  influence  upon  the  conduct  of
elections,  it  can  prohibit  such  contributions.”  Id.  at  598
n.  2  (emphasis  added).'”

The  District  of  Columbia  Circuit  and  the  Eighth  Cir-
United  States  v.
cuit  have  both  upheld  Section  610.
Boyle,  482  F.2d  755,  cert.  denied,  414  U.S.  1076  (1978);
United  States  v.  Pipefitters  Local  562,  434  F.2d  1116
(8th  Cir.  1970),  rev’d  on  other  grounds,  407  U.S.  385
(1972).
Accord,  United  States  v.  Painters  Union,  79
F.  Supp.  516  (D.  Conn.  1948),  rev’d  on  other  grounds,
172  F.2d  854  (2d  Cir.  1949);  United  States  v.  United
States  Brewers’  Ass’n,  239  Fed.  163  (W.D.  Pa.  1916).
The  Eighth  Circuit  has  also  upheld  the  Public  Utility
Holding  Company  Act’s  ban  on  political  contributions  by
registered  public  utility  holding  companies.
Egan  v.
United  States,  187  F.2d  369  (8th  Cir.  1943),  cert.  de-
nied,  320  U.S.  788  (1943).  Appellants  do  not  challenge
the  constitutionality  of  those  laws.

Bans  on  political  contributions  by  federal  employees
to  other  federal  employees  have  long  been  recognized  as
constitutional.  Congress  in  1876  prohibited  federal  em-
ployees  from  giving  money  to  or  receiving  it  from  other
This  Court  up-
federal  employees  for  political  purposes.
held  the  law  as  a  proper  measure  “to  promote  efficiency
and  integrity  in  the  discharge  of  official  duties.”  Ex
parte  Curtis,  106  U.S.  371,  373  (1882).  The  Court  also
upheld  Section  312  of  the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act,
which  prohibited  any  member  of  Congress,  any  candidate
for  Congress,  or  any  federal  employee  from  giving  to,  or

126  Obviously,  appellants  are  wrong  in  saying  that  in  UAW  “Mr.
Justice  Douglas  would  have  found  18  U.S.C.  §610  (prohibiting
corporate  and  labor-union  contributions)  unconstitutional.”  Appel-
lants’  Br.  46  n.5.

110

111

receiving  from,  any  other  such  person  any  contribution
for  a  political  purpose.  United  States  v.  Wurzbach,  280
U.S.  396  (1930).  The  Court  there  sustained  the  indict-
ment  of  a  Congressman  for  receiving  campaign  contribu-
tions  from  other  federal  employees,  saying:

“It  hardly  needs  argument  to  show  that  Congress
may  provide  that  its  officers  and  employees  neither
shall  exercise  nor  be  subjected  to  pressure  for  money
for  political  purposes,  upon  or  by  others  of  their
kind,  while  they  retain  their  office  or  employment.”
Id.  at  398-99.

Successors  to  those  statutes  are  found  in  18  U.S.C.
§§ 602  and  607,  punishing  both  the  giving  and  receiving
of  contributions  among  federal  employees.  Appellants  do
not  challenge  their  constitutionality.

Unlike  those  total  prohibitions  on  political  activities,
contributions,  or  expenditures,  the  giving  and  spending
limits  in  the  challenged  statutes  leave  every  person  free
to  contribute  or  spend,  where  otherwise  legal,  up  to  the
monetary  limits  in  18  U.S.C.  § 608.  They  leave  to  each
donor  or  spender  an  equal  opportunity  to  participate  in
elections  through  the  expenditure  of  money.  And  they
leave  all  candidates  an  equal  right  to  use  money  to  en-
hance  the  impact  of  their  political  messages.  They  are
clearly  less  restrictive  than  the  total  prohibitions  pre-
viously  approved  by  the  courts.

Even  the  concurring  Justices  in  United  States  v.  CIO,
supra,  who  would  have  struck  down  the  total  prohibition
on  corporate  and  union  spending  insofar  as  it  prevented
such  entities  from  expressing  their  views  to  their  mem-
bers,  said  that  a  reasonable  monetary  limitation  would  be
less  objectionable  under  the  First  Amendment.  335  U.S.

at  145-46.'*"  Congress  exercised  sound  judgment  in  con-
cluding  that  the  evils  of financial  excesses  in  campaigning
necessitated  imposition  of  reasonable  monetary  limita-
tions  applicable  to  all  contributors  and  candidates.”

V.  APPELLANTS  HAVE  FAILED  TO  PROVE  THAT
THE  PARTICULAR  CONTRIBUTION  AND  EX-
PENDITURE  LIMITS  IN  THESE  STATUTES  UN-
CONSTITUTIONALLY  RESTRICT  THEIR  FIRST
OR  FIFTH  AMENDMENTS  RIGHTS

A.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Partic-
ular  Contribution  Limits  Chosen  by  Congress  Are
Unreasonable  or  Discriminatory

In  addition  to  attacking  any  limits  on  contributions,
appellants  also  urge  that  the  particular  contribution  lim-
its  Congress  established  violate  the  Constitution  because
they  discriminate  against  challengers  to  incumbents—and

127  The  inference  drawn  from  their  opinion  by  the  Government's
Amicus  Brief  (at  34-35  n.  16),  that  giving  and  spending  limits
impermissibly  prevent  the  expression  of  “bloc  sentiment,”  is  there-
fore  wrong.  Reasonable  monetary  limits  prevent  no  group  from
expressing  its  views,  as  the  four  concurring  Justices  recognized.

#8  Appellants  cite  two  recent  state  court  decisions  voiding  laws
that  set  spending  limits.  Appellants’  Br.  85-86.  In  both  cases  the
candidate  spending  limits  were  intertwined  with  other  provisions
not  contained  in  these  laws  that  impermissibly  subjected  both  can-
didate  and  non-candidate  expenditures  to  the  same  ceiling.  The
Oregon  statute  invalidated  in  Deras  v.  Myers,  ——  Ore.  ——,  535
P.  2d  541  (1975),  prohibited  any  expenditure  in  support  of  or
opposition  to  a  candidate  without  the  candidate’s  permission.  Under
the  Washington  statute,  Bare  v.  Gorton,  84  Wash.  2d  380,  526  P.2d
379  (1974),  a  wealthy  person  or  group  acting  without  the  candi-
date’s  consent  could  have  spent  the  entire  statutory  maximum,
thereby  precluding  expenditures  by  the  candidate  himself.  Both
statutes  thus  resembled  former  Title  I of  the  FECA,  held  invalid  in
ACLU,  Inc.  Vv.  Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041  (D.D.C.  1973),  vacated
as  moot,  43  U.S.L.W.  3673  (1975).  As  we  show  below  (pp.  158-60),
ap
carefully  drafted  these  laws  with  the  Jennings  problem
in  mind.

112

113

minor  party  and  independent  candidates—by  denying
them  the  large  gifts  allegedly  needed  to  wage  “effective”
campaigns  and  to  obtain  the  “seed  money”  allegedly
needed  to  support  a  “viable”  campaign.

1.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Con-
tribution  Limits  Prevent  Any  Class  of  Candi-
dates  from  Conducting  “Effective”  Campaigns

Appellants  assert  that  incumbency  benefits  a  candidate
and  that  major  party  candidates  have  advantages  over
minor  party  and  independent  candidates.
They  sug-
gest  that  one  means  by  which  the  disadvantaged  can
overcome  their  handicaps  is  to  outspend  their  opponents;
but  they  complain  that  such  outspending  is  impossible  if
the  size  of  contributions  is limited  and  if ceilings  on  total
expenditures  are  established.’  Such  assertions  are  mis-
taken  in  fact  and  are  beside  the  point.

Of  course  incumbents  have  inherent  advantages  over
challengers  that  derive  from  the  holding  of  office.  They
may  also  have  disadvantages  that  derive  from  voter
perception  of  their  conduct  in  office.’”  In  any  event,  these
advantages  and  disadvantages  are  not  created  or  in-
creased  by  the  challenged  contribution  limits.  Where  in-
cumbents  use  their  offices  for  partisan  purposes,  that
practice  itself  can  be  remedied.

Appellants  appear  to  believe  that  incumbents  exploit
the  perquisites  of  federal  elective  office  to  give  them-
selves  a  financial  advantage  over  challengers,  and  that
challengers  must  be  allowed  to  raise  more  large  contribu-

128  We  deal  here  with  the  alleged  discriminatory  effects  of  the
contribution  limits.  In  Part  V-B,  infra,  we  deal  with  the  ceilings
on  candidate  expenditures,  and  in  V-C  with  the  ceilings  on  “inde-
pendent”  expenditures.

130  See  D.  Dunn,  supra  note  34,  at  7-8.

tions  to  overcome  this  fact.  If  incumbents  do  make  im-
proper  partisan  use  of  the  perquisites  of  their  offices,
remedies  are  available  to  prevent  that  practice.  For  ex-
ample,  a  suit  is  currently  in  progress  challenging  the
alleged  political  abuse  of  the  frank.  Common  Cause  v.
Klassen,  C.A.  No.  1887-73  (D.D.C.).  The  proper  legis-
lative  or  judicial  remedy  for  any  such  abuse  is  not  to
invalidate  these  neutral  campaign  reform  measures,  but
to  attack  the  abuses  directly.

Appellants’  attack  fails  to  recognize  that  the  need  for
money  in  politics  is  relative  and  that  the  contribution
limits  will  apply  equally  to  incumbents  and  challengers,
major  parties  and  minor  parties,  alike.
The  FECA
Amendments  permit  individuals  to  contribute  $1,000  per
candidate  per  election  (and  not  more  than  $25,000  to  all
candidates  per  year).
Certain  organizations  are  per-
mitted  to  contribute  $5,000  per  candidate  per  election.
These  limits  are  evenhanded  and  neutral  on  their  face.
They  apply  regardless  of the  identity  or  views  of the  donor
or  the  recipient.  The  lines  drawn  by  Congress

“are  not  aimed  at  particular  parties,  groups,  or
points  of  view,  but  apply  equally  to  all.  They  dis-
criminate  against  no  racial,  ethnic,  or  religious  mi-
norities.  Nor  do  they  seek  to  control  political  opin-
ions  or  beliefs,  or  to  interfere  with  or  influence  any-
one’s  vote  at  the  polls.”  Civil  Service  Commission  v.
Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548,  564  (1973).

The  constitutionality  of  a  statute  so  neutral  on  its
face  does  not  become  doubtful  because  it  may  adversely
affect  some  proportion  of  challengers  or  minor  party  and
independent  candidates  in  a  random  fashion,  just  as  it
may  adversely  affect  some  proportion  of  incumbents  and
major  party  candidates.  A  showing  that  the  law  will
sometimes  operate  in  favor  of  one  faction  and  sometimes

114

»  115

in favor  of another  is insufficient  proof  that  constitutional
guarantees  have  been  violated.  See  Hunter  v.  Erickson,
(Harlan,  J.,  concurring).
393  U.S.  385,  394  (1969)
Rather,  it  is  necessary  to  show  that  the  statute  neces-
sarily  disadvantages  challengers  or  minor  party  candi-
dates  and  favors  incumbents  or  major  party  candidates
as  a  class.  See  Gordon  v.  Lance,  403  U.S.  1,  7  (1971);
Jefferson  V.  Hackney,  406  U.S.  535,  548-49  (1972);  cf.
James  V.  Valtierra,  402  U.S.  187,  141-43  (1971).  The
record  before  this  Court  shows  no  such  disadvantaged
class.

Appellants  carry  the  burden  as  plaintiffs  of  proving
that  the  contribution  limits  have  a  “real-life  impact”  of
discriminating  against  challengers  or  minor  party  and
independent  candidates  as  discrete  classes  of  candidates.
Cf.  Whitcomb  v.  Chavis,  403  U.S.  124,  146  (1971).  Their
statistical  demonstrations  fall  far  short  of  meeting  that
burden.  As  the  court  of  appeals  concluded:

“We  have  considered  the  charge  that  the  limits  set
by  Congress  work  an  undue  discrimination  in  favor
of  incumbents.  Although  plaintiffs  sought  findings
in  support  of  this  theory,  Judge  Corcoran  properly
declined  to  provide  such  findings  in  light  of  the
speculative  nature  of  the  assertions.
The  material
available  to  the  court  looks  both  ways.  The  political
scientists  draw  diametric  conclusions  from  the  avail-
able  information.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1540-41  (footnote
omitted).

The  record  is  at  worst  indecisive.

It  permits  only  the
conclusion  that  the  impact  of  contribution  limits  on  elec-
tions  varies  in  each  case,  along  with  the  innumerable
other  interacting  variables  of  the  political  vortex.
in-
deed,  the  record  evidence  refutes  appellants’  claim  that
the  contribution  limits  necessarily  discriminate  against

a  class  comprised  of  all  or  even  most  challengers,  or
against  a  class  of  all  or  even  most  minor  party  and  inde-
pendent  candidates.

The  best  available  data  of  record,  taken  from  the  con-
tribution  and  expenditure  records  of  all  1972  and  1974
federal  elections,  **  show  that  there  is  no  basis  for  the
assertion  that  contribution  limits  will  reduce  the  rela-
tive  ability  of  challengers  or  minor  party  and  independ-
ent  candidates  to  raise  campaign  money,  as  compared  to
their  opponents.  Most  incumbents  and  major  party  candi-
dates  have  always  outstripped  their  opponents  in  the
ability  to  raise  and  spend  campaign  funds,  particularly
in  the  ability  to  raise  large  contributions.
This  is  one
advantage  of  incumbency  that  appellants  studiously  ig-
nore.  The  contribution  limits  on  the  whole  bite  deeper
into  the  traditional  sources  of  financial  support  for  in-
cumbents  and  major  parties  than  any  other  putative
class.**

131  The  data  are  found  in  Part  IIA  of  the  district  court's  find-
ings  (cited  as  “Findings  IIA”)  and  are  derived  from  computer
studies  by  the  Common  Cause  Campaign  Finance  Monitoring  Proj-
ect  based  on  official  reports.  The  court  of  appeals  relied  properly
on  these  findings  exclusively  in  discussing  the  1972  and  1974  elec-
tions.  Ct.  App.  Op.  1540,  n.  105.

Appellants  also  make  use  of  these  findings,  but  in  certain
instances  they  use  figures  drawn  from  a  study  by  one  of  their
affiants,  Roland  J.  Cole.  Appellants  cite  the  Cole  materials  as  “Find-
ings  II,  B—,  p.  ——,  App.  ——.”  Appendix  A  to  this  brief  de-
scribes  the  Common  Cause  and  Cole  studies  and  explains  why  this
Court  should,  like  the  court  of  appeals,  avoid  reliance  on  the  Cole
materials.

‘82  Tf  the  $1,000  individual  contribution  limit  had  been  in  effect
for  the  1974  congressional  elections,  incumbents  would  have  given
up  11%  times  as  much  as  challengers.  Findings  IIA,  § 115  ({ 55
recomputed),  IIB  J.A.  475.  If  the  $5,000  limit  on  political  com-
mittees  had  been  in  effect  for  the  1974  elections,  incumbents  would
have  given  up  three  times  as  much  as  challengers.  Findings  IIA,
/ 80,  IIB  J.A.  459.

and  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  on  the  whole
would  have  been  in  approximately  the  same  or  a  better
position  relative  to  incumbents  and  major  party  candi-
dates  if  the  contribution  limits  had  been  in  effect.  The
following  table  indicates  the  amounts  raised  by  major

major  party  incumbents  in  the  1972  presidential  and

ever  large,  are  considered,  and  (2)  if only  the  first  $1,000
of  any  contribution  from  an  individual  is  considered:  **

We  recognize,  of  course,  that  if  contributions  by  individuals  over

have  differed  somewhat  from  the  actual  amounts  raised  less  the

would  have  differed.  But  any  differences  are  purely  speculative.
The  1972  and  1974  facts  of  record  provide  the  best  available  evi-
dence  of  what  would  have  happened  in  1972  and  1974  under  the
contribution  limits  of  the  challenged  legislation  and  what  is  likely
to  happen  in  1976.

tive  financial  position  of  the  challenger  in  the  1972  presi-

dates  opposing  major  party  candidates  in  the  1974  con-

134  Tf  a  $1,000  individual  contribution  limit  had  been  in  effect  in

If  a  $1,000  individual  contribution  limit  had  been  in  effect  in
the  1972  presidential  race,  only  $66,000  in  contributions  to  minor
party  and  independent  candidates  would  have  been  eliminated—six
percent  of  their  total  contributions.  In  contrast,  $13.2  million—or
34  percent—of  McGovern’s  contributions  and  $33.1  million—or  55
percent—of  Nixon’s  contributions  would  have  been  eliminated  by
the  limit.  Findings  IIA,  § 73,  IIB  J.A.  457.  Thus,  the  facts  clearly
show  that  “patrons  or  large  donors”  do  not  “disproportionately
aoe  small  or  struggling  parties.”  Cf.  United  States  Amicus

r.

61,

118

119

gressional  races,  and  a  strengthening  of  minor  party  and
independent  candidates  in  the  1972  presidential  race.

These  past  election  statistics  demonstrate  that  the  chal-
lenged  contribution  limits  are  not  likely  to  reduce—and
may  increase—the  fund-raising  ability  of  challengers  and
minor  party  and  independent  candidates,  relative  to  in-
cumbents  and  major  party  candidates.  As  the  court  of ap-
peals  concluded:

“In  a  broad  sense,  the  statute  may  favor  relative
newcomers,  by  limiting  the  access  of  incumbents  to
large  donations.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1541.

These  are,  of  course,  cumulative  figures  for  the  uni-
verse  of  incumbents  and  various  classes  of  challengers.
Within  that  universe,  there  were  inevitable  exceptions.
But  when a statute  is  attacked  as  unconstitutional  on  its
face,  the  plaintiffs  must  demonstrate  an  intentionally  or
necessarily  discriminatory  effect  on  definable  classes  of
affected  persons:

“In  reversing  the  District  Court  we  should  empha-
size  that  the  equal-protection  claim  below  was  based
upon  an  alleged  irfirmity  that  attaches  to  the  statute
on  its  face...  .  It  might  well  be  that,  designedly
or  otherwise,  a  multi-member  constituency  apportion-
ment  scheme,  under  the  circumstances  of  a  particu-
lar  case,  would  operate  to  minimize  or  cancel  out
the  voting  strength  of racial  or  political  elements  of
the  voting  population.  When  this  is  demonstrated  it
will  be  time  enough  to  consider  whether  the  system
still  passes  constitutional
This  question,
however,  is  not  presented  by  the  record  before  us.”
Fortson  v.  Dorsey,  379  U.S.  483,  438-39  (1965).

muster.

Appellants  argue  that  the  impact  of  the  challenged
contribution  limits  is  most  pronounced  in  “closely  con-

tested”  campaigns—according  to  appellants’  definition,
those  in  which  challengers  received  at  least  45  percent  of
the  vote.  Appellants’  Br.  129.  Appellants  have  yet  to  dem-
onstrate  the  truth  or  relevance  of  this  suggestion.  But
accepting  arguendo  appellants’  premise,  the  facts  suggest
that  even  when  attention  is  narrowed  to  the  races  ap-
pellants  define  as  “close,”  the  challenged  contribution
limits  may  actually  improve  the  financial  position  of chal-
lengers  relative  to  incumbents.  In  the  six  1974  Senate
elections  defined  by  appellants  as  “close,”  incumbents
raised  $225,948  in  amounts  over  the  new  contribution
limits,  while  challengers  raised  only  $95,395  over  those
limits.  Findings  IIC,  § 8,  IIB  J.A.  676-78.
In  the  81
House  races  in  1974  defined  by  appellants  as  “close,”
only  3.5  percent  of  the  total  contributions  received  by
challengers  would  have  been  eliminated  if  a  $1,000  indi-
vidual  contribution  limit  had  been  in  effect.  See  Findings
IIC,  971,  9,  IIB  J.A.  586,  679-96.
In  71  of  these  81
races,  only  five  percent  or  less  of  the  challengers’  total
contributions  would  have  been  eliminated;  in  30  of  these
71  races,  no  contributions  at  all  would  have  been  elimi-
nated.  Id.

Moreover,  the  contribution  limits  will  have  another
salutary  effect  from  the  perspective  of  challengers—they
will  tend  to  decrease  the  significant  advantage  which  in-
cumbents  enjoy  in  raising  special  interest  group  money.
Findings  IIA,  {] 77-90,  93-98,  105,  110-11,  IIB  J.A.  459-
61,  462-65,  467-68,  470-72.  And  a  study  performed  by
Congressman  Anderson  suggests  that  such  contributions
play  a  more  critical  role  in  close  elections.***  Thus,  con-
trary  to  appellants’  hypotheses,  the  net  impact  of  the
contribution  limits  may  be  to  improve  the  relative  posi-

136  Hearings  on  H.R.  7612  and  S.  372  Before  the  Subcomm.  on

Elections,  supra  note  75,  at  203,  219.

120

tion  of challengers  and  minor  party  or  independent  candi-
dates  in  close  elections  as  well.

2.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Con-
tribution  Limits  Prevent  Any  Class  of  Candi-
dates  From  Obtaining  “Seed  Money”

Even  though  the  overall  contribution  figures  for  1972
and  1974  indicate  no  discriminatory  effect  against  any
putative  class,  appellants  argue  that  the  contribution
limits  will  nevertheless  discriminate  against  challengers
and  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  because
those  candidates  have  a  special  need  for  “seed  money”  to
launch  their  candidacies  and  can  obtain  that  money  only
from  large  contributions.  Again,  the  theory  is  unproven.
The  evidence  in  this  case  demonstrates  that  any  neces-
sary  “seed  money”  can  be  raised  in  contributions  per-
mitted  by  the  challenged  statutes.

Appellants  attempt  to  prove  their  assertions  concerning
“seed  money”  by reference  to the  amounts  of contributions
received  before  October  1,  1974—-which  they  label  “seed
money”  for  purposes  of  the  1974  House  and  Senate  elec-
tions.  Appellants’  Br.  130-31.  What  appellants  are  treat-
ing  as  “seed  money”  is  all  monies  raised  prior  to  the
Since  the  term  “seed
last  five  weeks  of  an  election.
money”  is  commonly  understood  to  refer  to  funds  raised
by a  candidate  “at  the  outset  of  the  campaign”  (Ct.  App.
Op.  1532  n.  85;  Appellants’  Br.  130),  the  proffered  data
are  utterly  unrelated  to  their  assertions.

Appellants  simply  assume  the  critical  fact  on  which
their  “seed  money”  argument  depends—that  early  or
“seed  money”  can  be  raised  only  in  the  form  of  large
contributions.
Appellants  cite  Senator  Buckley’s  1970
race,  Senator  McCarthy’s  New  Hampshire  primary  race,

121

and  other  examples  to  support  their  “seed  money”  con-
Appellants’  Br.  132-37.  These  examples  have
tentions.
First,  since  large  con-
limited,  if  any,  probative  force.
tributions  were  permitted  during  the  cited  races,  the
candidates’  reliance  on  such  contributions  proves  only
that  they  raised  money  in  what  was  for  them  the  easiest
way  then  permitted—going  to  a  few  big  donors.  It  does
not  tell  what  these  candidates  would  or  could  have
done  had  contribution  limits  been  in  effect.  Second,  appel-
lant  Buckley  admits  that  he  made  no  effort  to  raise  “seed
money”  in  small  amounts,  and  appellant  McCarthy  ad-
mits  that  he  has  no  knowledge  whether  his  campaign
Buckley  Deposition  Tr.  at  7-9;
made  any  such  effort.
Findings  III,  McCarthy  7 4, 9, IIB  J.A.  714,  715.  More-
over,  appellants  have  failed  to  show  what  percentage  of
the  early  money  in  these  and  other  campaigns  was  in
fact  received  in large  contributions.**’  These  circumstances
reduce  the  statements  of  appellants  Buckley  and  Mc-
Carthy  to  mere  self-serving  opinions  which  merit  little
attention.’

187  For  example,  the  Treasurer  of  appellant  ACU  testified  that
in  his  experience  large  contributions  usually  are  made  late  in
a  campaign,  not  at  the  beginning.  Joy  Deposition  Tr.  at  37,  39,  40.

138  The  district  court  certified  Senators  Buckley  and  McCarthy
as  expert  witnesses  despite  the  fact  that  they  had  not  been  offered
or  qualified  as  such.  As  appellants  in  this  action,  their  potential
bias  is  a  factor  in  deciding  what  weight  to  accord  their  testimony.
Grain  Dealers  Mut.  Ins.  Co.  v.  Farmers  Union  Cooperative  Elev.
&  Shipping  Ass'n,  377  F.2d  672,  679  (10th  Cir.  1967).  Moreover,
neither  Buckley  nor  McCarthy  when  questioned  during  his  deposi-
tion  could  testify  to  his  own  past  fund-raising  practices  in  any
detail  whatsoever.

The  court  found  these  men  to  be  experts  because  of  their  experi-
ence  as  campaigners  and  Members  of  Congress.  According  to  that
reasoning,  all  535  Members  of  Congress  are  also  experts  on  this
subject.  Since  the  FECA  and  FECA  Amendments  were  passed
by  large  majorities,  the  weight  of  “expert”  opinion  overwhelmingly
contradicts  appellants  McCarthy  and  Buckley  on  the  need  for  large
contributions  to  begin  a  campaign.

122

In  contrast  to  these  speculative  opinions  stands  for-
midable  record  evidence  that  candidates  can  and  have
raised  substantial  sums  of  “seed  money”  well  in  advance
of  an  election  under  the  FECA  contribution  limits  or
more  stringent  self-imposed  restrictions.  The  large  ar-
ray  of  1976  presidential  hopefuls,  many  of whom  are  rela-
tively  unknown  nationally,  have  raised  substantial  “seed
money”  from  small  contributions.

Thus,  more  than  13  months  before  the  1976  presidential
election,  $7.8  million  in  “seed  money”  had  already  been
raised  under  the  new  contribution  limits.  And  six  candi-
dates  had  already  raised  more  than  $500,000,  including
a  once  and  possibly  future  third-party  candidate,  Gov-
ernor  Wallace,  and  three  relatively  unknown  challengers,
Senator  Bentsen,  Congressman  Udall,  and  former  Gov-
ernor  Carter.

As  of  October  11,  1975,  eight  candidates  have  an-
nounced  that  they  have  qualified  for  primary  matching
(Bentsen,  Carter,  Ford,  Harris,  Jackson,  Shriver,  Udall
and  Wallace).  Each  of  these  will  receive  a  substantial
sum  in  federal  money  for  campaign  use  on  January  1,
1976,  and  will  remain  eligible  to  qualify  for  additional
matching  funds  in  1976.  Public  financing  thus  provides
a new  source  of “seed  money”  to presidential  candidates—
who  require  more  than  congressional  candidates—well  in
This  money  will  substantially
advance  of  the  election.
augment  funds  already  raised  and  spent  campaigning  in
1975.  Matching  funds  thus  become,  as  they  were  de-
signed  to  become,  a  new  form  of  “seed  money.”  **

141  As  appellants  note,  Bond  has  dropped  out  of  the  race.  Appel-
lant’s  Br.  132.  Although  Bond  cited  lack  of  funds  as  requiring  this
action,  such  was  often  the  case  with  Presidential  candidates  before
imposition  of  contribution  limits.  See,  e.g.,  in  1972,  30  Cong.  Q.
Weekly  Rep.  928  (Muskie);  id.  at  587  (McCloskey);  id.  at  726
(Hartke).

142  Appellants  may  argue  that  much  of  the  money  raised  by
presidential  candidates  thus  far  has  already  been  spent  on  fund-
raising,  and  that  few  candidates  have  accumulated  large  balances.
Even  if  this  were  the  case,  these  monies  have  earned  substan-
tial  matching  funds  from  the  public  treasury,  which  the  candi-
dates  will  receive  beginning  January  1, 1976  without  additional  cost
to  them.  The  candidates  furthermore  have  benefited  in  disseminat-
ing  their  views  on  the  issues  to  the  electorate  while  raising  this

124

125

In  addition,  Senator  Buckley—a  third  party  candidate
and  an  appellant  who  challenges  these  contribution  lim-
its—is  evidently  encountering  no  difficulty  in  raising
early  “seed  money”  for  his  1976  reelection  race.  Between
July  1  and  September  30,  Buckley  reported  receipts  of
$142,631,  for  a  total  raised  to  date  of  $376,325.  His  two
opponents  together  raised  $27,160  during  the  same  period,
for  a  total  to  date  of  $52,121.’

Appellants’  “seed  money”  argument  is  also  contrary  to
experience  under  earlier,  voluntary  contribution  limita-
tions.  Ramsey  Clark  voluntarily  imposed  a  limit  of  $100
—one-tenth  of  the  challenged  limit—on  all  contributions
to  his  1974  challenge  to  a  three-term  incumbent  senator.
He  was  able  nevertheless  to  raise  sufficient  “seed  money”
to  launch  a  serious  campaign.  Clark  began  his  fund-
raising  by  sending  personal  letters  to  400  friends  and

money  in  small  contributions.  As  one  of  the  country’s  recognized
experts  in  direct-mai!  fund-raising  has  recently  confirmed:

“Direct  mail  is  a  form  of  advertising,  the  same  as  radio,  tele-
vision  and  newspapers.  Wallace  will  have  communicated  with
maybe  30  million  voters  out  there  by  his  mailings,  and  it’s  a
lot  more  effective  than  radio  or  television.”

Interview  with  Richard  A.  Viguerie,  The  Washington  Post,  July  27,
1975,  at  A-1,  A-12.  Viguerie  went  on  to  say  that  another  benefit
of  direct-mail  fund-raising  is  that  it  gives  a  candidate  a  chance  to
develop  volunteers  and  active  supporters  in  every  area  of  the
country:

“[W]hen  he  [McGovern]  went  into  the  primaries,  he  was  going
in  with  the  names  of  1,000  or  2,000  supporters  in  every  con-
gressional  district,  and  these  people  had  an  investment  in  his
campaign,  they  could  be  contacted  for  manning  the  polls,
getting  the  vote  out,  you  name  it.  Contrast  that  with  Humphrey
r Muskie—he  had  them  outnumbered  through  his  direct  mail.”

See  also  D.  Rosenbloom,  Electing  Congress:  The  Financial  Dilemma

Similarly,  Senator  McGovern  financed  the  first  na-
tional  direct-mail  appeal  in  his  1972  presidential  cam-
paign  with  $27,813,  comprised  of  a  $10,000  loan  and  44

$1,250,000."

These  examples—reflecting  the  experiences  of  challen-
gers  and  incumbents  and  one  third  party  candidate—re-
fute  appellants’  contentions  that  “seed  money”  must  be
raised  in  large  contributions  and  that  this  is  particularly
More-
true  for  challengers  and  third  party  candidates.

145  “Before  Senator  Mathias  announced  these  limitations,  his  cam-
paign  organization  had  raised  $118,745  from  approximately  546

$93,700.  The  average  contribution  was  $217.”  Findings  I,  {| 174,
IIA  J.A.  258.

14  Affidavit  of  Marian  R.  Pearlman,  supra  note  139,  "* 7-12.
Pearlman  was  Treasurer  of  McGovern’s  campaign;  it is her  opinion
that:

“  ..  [I]t  is possible  for  a  candidate  for  the  presidential  nomi-
nation  of a  major  party,  having  little  national  name  recognition
at  the  outset,  to  begin  and  conduct  a  successful  campaign  for
that  nomination  without  heavy  reliance  on  large  contributions
from  private  individuals.  By  using  techniques  such  as  direct-
mail  solicitation,  it  is  possible  for  a  candidate  to  solicit  and
receive  substantial  amounts  of  money  to  finance  such  a  cam-
paign  while  relying  largely  or  entirely  on  small  contributions
from  a  large  number  of  individuals.  Moreover,  also  on  the  basis
of  my  experience  with  the  McGovern  for  President  Campaign,
I have  concluded  that  it  is  possible  to  finance  a  serious  cam-
paign  for  election  as  President  of  the  United  States  primarily
or  solely  on  the  basis  of  relatively  small  contributions  from
a  large  number  of  individuals.”  Jd.,  { 4.

over,  there  is  no  showing  that  the  contribution  limits

in  raising  “seed  money.”  Appellants  have  not  shown
that  the  relative  abilities  of  incumbents  and  challengers
to  raise  small  contributions  for  “seed  money”  differ.
