At  bottom,  appellants  argue  that  the  Constitution  for-
bids  a  remedy  for  improper  influence  in  the  situations
that—by  appellants’  own  contention—-present  the  greatest
If  appellants  are  correct,
potential  for  such  influence.
the  candidate  in  need  of  “seed  money”  may  be  the  one
most  utterly  dependent  on  the  benevolence  of  one  or  a
small  handful  of  political  “angels”  and  may,  therefore,
be  forever  obligated  to  them.  Appellants  gain  little  by
marshaling  examples  where  a  generous  few  have  financed
“worthy”  causes;  they  are  overwhelmed  by  the  counter-
examples  evidencing  the  threat  and  appearance  of  perni-
cious  influence.  See  Part  IIIA,  supra.

Precisely  because  it  was  so  seductively  easy  to  raise
“seed  money”  in  large  contributions,  candidates  did  so.
That  fact,  even  if  proven,  is  not  evidence  that  start-up
financing  cannot  be  obtained  under  the  challenged  limits.
And  the  experience  to  date,  discussed  above,  belies  appel-
lants’  fears.
If  some  challengers  or  minor  party  candi-
dates  remain  who  have  problems  raising  early  money,
the  reason  may  be  lack  of  popular  support.
Professor
Bickel,  who  believed  that  it  would  be  “both  wise  and
lawful”  for  Congress  to  impose  contribution  limits,  pro-
posed  to  deal

“with  the  problem  of the  remaining  inequality  among
candidates  by  recognizing  its  legitimacy  insofar  as
it  reflects  inequality  in  support,  in  initial  support.

“TI  think  the  political  system  ought  not  to  be  em-
barrassed  by  that  kind  of  inequality.  That  is  the
heart  of  it.  Inequality  of  support  is  what  finally
decides  elections,  and  inequality  of  initial  support
embarrasses  me  no  more.”

3.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  that  the  $500
Exclusion  for  Incidental  Expenses  of  Volunteers
Violates  Their  First  Amendment  Rights

18  U.S.C.  §591(e)(5)(A).

The  challenged  legislation  carefully  excludes  from  the
definition  of  “contribution”  (and  thus  permits  without
limitation)  personal  services  of  all  kinds  provided  by
volunteers.
Certain  inci-
dental  expenses  which  volunteers  may  incur,  such  as
unreimbursed  travel  expenses,  are  deemed  contributions
to  the  extent  they  exceed  $500.  18  U.S.C.  § 591(e)  (5).
Appellants  suggest  that  this  $500  exclusion  “typifies”  the
“repressive  spirit”  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  their
“gross  intrusion  .
.  into  the  political  activities  of  Amer-
ican  citizens.”
Appellants’  Br.  138.  But  the  exclusion
actually  has  the  effect  of  allowing  additional  financial
contributions  to  a  campaign  on  top  of  the  $1,000  contri-
bution  limit.  As  the  court  of  appeals  held,  some  limit
must  be  placed  on  the  exclusion  as  a  “necessary  loophole-
closing  provision  designed  to  prevent  contributions  by
indirection.”  This  provision  allows  “sufficient  flexibility
for  effective  volunteer  campaign  assistance,  without  open-
ing  the  door  to  wholesale  circumvention  of  contribution
and  expenditure  limits.”

Ct.  App.  Op.  1534.

.

148  Commentary,  supra  note  31,  at 67.

130

4.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the
Limit  on  Contributions  by Political  Committees
Is  Unreasonable  or  Discriminatory

Appellants  make  two  contradictory  attacks  on

the
provisions  of  the  challenged  statutes  relating  to  con-
tributions  by political  committees.  Appellants  first  argue
that  the  FECA  has  increased  the  relative  influence  of
special  interest  groups  by  permitting  any  group  of  in-
dividuals  that  qualifies  as  a  “political  committee”  under
Section  608(b)  (2)  to  contribute  $5,000  to  a  candidate
and  by  allowing  organized  interest  groups  such  as  labor
unions  and  corporations  to  proliferate  political  com-
Appellants  claim  that
mittees.
the  legislation  does  not  effectively  dampen  the  ability
of  economic  interest  groups  such  as  the  dairy  industry
to give  unlimited  amounts  to  candidates,  because  “|[e] very
county  organization”  can  have  a  political  committee  en-
titled  to  give  up  to  $5,000  apiece  to  an  unlimited  num-
ber  of  candidates.  Jd.  at  69.

Appellants’  Br.  68-71.

To  the  contrary,  the  appellants  have  provided  no  evi-
dence  that  these  provisions  permit  circumvention  of  the
individual  or  group  contribution  limits;  the  statutory
scheme  itself  precludes  the  result  appellants  fear.
To
qualify,  a  political  committee  must  have  been  registered
with  the  FEC*”  for  at  least  six  months,  have  received

149  2 U.S.C.  § 433  requires  political  committees  to  file  a  statement
of  organization  with  the  FEC,  a  statement  designed  to  ensure  that
political  committees  are  in  truth  separate  organizations.  For  ex-
ample,  the  statute  requires  a  statement  of  “the  names,  addresses,
and  relationships  of  affiliated  or  connected  organizations,”  § 433(b)
(2),  and  a  statement  of  the  continuing  nature  of  the  committee
and  the  planned  disposition  cf  residual  funds  in  the  event  of  the
committee’s  dissolution.  Section  433(b)  (7),  (8).  Furthermore,  Sec-
tion  434  requires  political  committees  to  report  receipts  and  ex-
penditures  to  the  FEC;  included  in  each  report  must  be  the  name
and  address  of  each  political  committee  or  candidate  to  or  from
which  the  committee  has  made  any  transfer  of  funds.  2  U.S,C.
§ 454(b)  (4).

131

contributions  from  more  than  50  persons,  and  (except
for  any  state  political  party  organization)  have  made
contributions  to  five  or  more  candidates  for  federal  office.
18  U.S.C.  § 608(b)  (2).

The  legislative  history  of  Section  608(b)  manifests
Congress’  intent  that  only  truly  autonomous  political
committees  receive  the  benefit  of  the  $5,000  limit.  For
example,  the  Conference  Report  contains  the  following
language:

“A  ...  question  was  raised  ..  .  regarding  the  pos-
sibility  of  circumventing  the  limit  on  contributions
by  political  committees  where  a  national  committee
of  a  political  organization  may  contribute  the  maxi-
mum  allowable  amount  to  a  candidate  and  a  state
or  local  sub-unit  or  subsidiary  of  that  committee
may  also  contribute  to  the  same  candidate.
It  was
the  intent  of the  House  Committee  to  allow  the  maxi-
mum  contribution  from  each  level  of  the  organiza-
tion  if  the  decision  or  judgment  to  make  such  con-
tributions  is independently  exercised  within  the  sepa-
rate  levels  of the  organization.  However,  if the  sub-
sidiary  or  sub-unit  organizations  are  under  the  con-
trol  or  direction  of  the  parent  organization  with
respect  to  their  contributions  to  specific  candidates,
then  the  organizations  acting  in  concert  would  con-
stitute  one  political  committee  for  the  purpose  of  the
contribution  limits  included  in  the  House  amendment
—

Moreover,  appellants’  tidy  theoretical  argument  is con-
trary  to  the  facts.  Appellants  focus  on  the  dairy  in-

dustry,  because  of its  prominent  role  in  recent  elections,’*
and  assert  that  “dairy  interests
can  give  unlimited
amounts  to  a  candidate’  through  county  organizations.
Appellants’  Br.  69  (emphasis  in  original).
In  practice,
however,  the  power  of  dairy  interests  in  making  political
contributions  stemmed  from  the  fact  that  a  handful  of
individuals  controlled  contributions  in vast  amounts  raised
from  across  the  country.  On  June  30,  1975,  the  single
most  important  dairy  committee,  “the  political  action
arm  of  the  Associated  Milk  Producers,  Inc.,”  had  a  cash
balance  on  hand  of  $1,813,060;  two  other  dairy  com-
The
mittees  had  balances  in  excess  of  $300,000  each.
Washington  Post,  Sept.  21,  1975,  at  A-2,  cols.  3,  7.  Had
the  challenged  contribution  limits  not  been  enacted,  these
committees  would  today  be  free  to  give  the  entire  $2.4
million  to  a  single  presidential  candidate—with  or  with-
out  the  benefit  of  a  meeting  in  the  oval  office.  Under  the
limits  appellants  attack,  these  three  committees  may  give
no  more  than  a  total  of  $15,000  to  a  single  candidate.

Second,  appellants  attack  (Br.  141-42)  as  an  undue
burden  on  political  activity  the  six-month  and  50-person
requirements  for  qualified  political  committees—provisions
obviously  designed  to  avoid  the  very  artificial  prolifera-
tion  of committees  that  appellants  fear.  The  requirements
prevent  individuals  or  groups  from  making  cumulative
contributions  by  forming  and  dissolving  new  political
They  also  inhibit
committees  throughout  a  campaign.
the  formation  of  small  committees  that  exist  solely  for
the  purpose  of  evading  the  contribution  limits.  See  Ct.
App.  Op.  1536.  Together  with  the  requirement  that  a
qualified  committee  support  five  or  more  candidates,  these
requirements  serve  to  ensure  the  general  legitimacy  of

181  See  Findings  I, f/f] 103,  158,  IIA  J.A.  101-07,  254.

political  committees  without  preventing  groups  from  ex-
pressing  their  collective  political  views.  And  of  course,
groups  of  individuals  who  cannot  meet  the  requirements
for  qualified  political  committess  are  still  free  to pool  their
resources  by  forming  a  non-qualifying  committee  that
makes  contributions  subject  to  the  general  $1,000  limit
applicable  to  contributions  by any  “person”  other  than  a
qualified  political  committee.

18  U.S.C.  § 608(b)  (1).

The  six-month  requirement  for  the  duration  of  a  po-
litical  committee  is  not  an  impermissible  burden  on  the
ability  of  citizens  to  engage  in  political  activity.
The
six-month  rule  is  merely  a  condition  on  the  availability
of  the  favorable  contribution  limit  for  bona  fide  organi-
zations.  Even  where  an  actual  restriction  is  involved,
the  Court  has  upheld  such  reasonable  time  limitations
if,  as  here,  they  are  tied  to  a  particularized  legiti-
mate  legislative  purpose.
See  Rosario  v.  Rockefeller,
410  U.S.  752  (1973);  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51
(1973)  ; Marston  v.  Lewis,  410  U.S.  679  (1973).

5.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the
Limits  en  Expenditures  from  a  Candidate’s  Per-
sonal  or  Family  Funds  Are  Discriminatory

Appellants  assert  that  the  limitations  on  the  expendi-
tures  from  a  candidate’s  personal  or  immediate  family
funds
(18  U.S.C.  §608(a)),  invidiously  discriminate
against  candidates  without  wealthy  families.  Appellants’
Br.  139-40.  However,  Section  608(a)  decreases  the  ad-
vantages  already  possessed  by  wealthy  candidates.  Prior
to  its  enactment,  candidates  pumped  tremendous  family
fortunes  into  their  election  effects.  See  p.  73,  supra.  The
Constitution  permits  ameliorative  legislation  to  eradicate
existing  inequality  one  step  at  a  time.  Katzenbach  v.
Morgan,  384  U.S.  641,  657  (1966);  see  also  Williamson

134

135

v.  Lee  Optical  Co.,  348  U.S.  483,  489  (1955).  Moreover,
there  is  no  evidence  that  any  appellant  would  have  been
harmed  in  the  past  or  expects  to  be  harmed  in  the  future
by  the  limits  in  the  section.  See  supra  p.  25.

6.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  The
$25,000  Aggregate  Limit  on  Individual  Contri-
butions  Is  Discriminatory

Appellants  assert  that  the  $25,000  limit  on  aggregate
individual  contributions  to  all  candidates  in  a  given  elec-
tion  (18  U.S.C.  § 608(b)  (3)  ) discriminates  against  third
party  and  independent  candidates  because  donors  will
prefer  to  give  their  allotted  amount  to  major  party
candidates  who  they  believe  have  better  chances  for
victory.  Appellants’  Br.  138.  Yet  the  Chairman  of  ap-
pellant  Libertarian  Party  has  admitted  that  prior  to
January  1,  1975,  large  contributors  gave  to  major  party
candidates  anyway.  Crane  Deposition  Tr.  at  25-27.  Not
one  contribution  made  in  1974  by an  individual  who  gave
$25,000  or  more  in  contributions  of  $500  or  more  went
to  a  minor  party  or  independent  candidate.  See  Findings
IIC,  76,  IIB  J.A.  665.  Appellant  McCarthy—the  only
independent  candidate  before  this  Court—is  unable  to
state  whether  the  $25,000  aggregate  limit  might  harm
his  campaign.  Findings  III,  McCarthy  § 26,  IIB  J.A.
718.  Appellants  have  not  proven  their  case.

B.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Par-
ticular  Expenditure  Ceilings  Chosen  by  Congress
Are  Unreasonable  or  Discriminatory

As  with  contribution  limits,  appellants’  attack  on  ex-
First,  they  assert  that
penditure  ceilings  is  twofold:
the  First  Amendment  forbids  Congress  to  establish  any

ceiling  on  candidate  expenditures.  We  answer  that  argu-
ment  in  Part  IV,  above.

Second,  appellants  assert  that  the  particular  ceilings
imposed  by  Congress  are  (1)  too  low  to  permit  candi-
dates  to  conduct  “effective”  campaigns,  and  (2)
dis-
criminatory  in  their  effects,  favoring  incumbents  and
major  party  candidates  over  challengers  and  minor  party
and  independent  candidates.  As  demonstrated  below,  ap-
pellants  have  failed  to  prove  either  part  of  this  conten-
tion.

1.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Ex-
penditure  Ceilings  Prevent  Any  Class  of  Candi-
dates  from  Conducting  “Effective’  Campaigns

This  Court  is  in  no  position  on  the  present  record  to
tell  Congress  that  it should  have  chosen  different  expendi-
ture  limits  in  Section  608(c).  The  choice  of  ceilings  that
will  achieve  the  statutory  objectives  without  preventing
effective  campaigns  is  peculiarly  a  legislative  function.
Congress,  not  the  judiciary,  is  equipped  to  collect  and
review  the  wide-ranging  evidence  bearing  on  that  choice.
It  has  studied  the  problems  of  campaign  finance  for
years,  held  months  of  hearings,  and  debated  the  practical
questions  at  length.  On  such  a  matter  of  line-drawing,
the  case  for  judicial  deference  to  legislative  judgment  is
most  powerful.  See  pp.  38-39,  supra.

Moreover,  nothing  in  this  record  suggests  that  the  ceil-
ings  are  unconstitutionally  low.  Appellants  have  sued
before  it  is possible  to  see  the  results  of even  one  regular
election  year  in  which  the  ceilings  were  in  effect.  Ac-
cordingly,  the  best  available  data  are  the  spending  figures
for  the  1972  and  1974  elections,  set  forth  in  the  district
court’s  findings.  They  show  that  the  ceilings  are  not  out
of  line  with  past  expenditures.

136

137

In  choosing  limits,  Congress  had  different  objectives
with  respect  to  congressional  and  presidential  campaigns.
For  congressional  races,  the  limits  are  designed  merely
to  slow  further  increases  in  spending,  while  for  presi-
dential  races,  it  was  essential  to  roll  back  the  amount
candidates  could  spend,  because  spending  in  presidential
races  had  risen  intolerably—to  a  point  far  beyond  the
needs  of  an  “effective”  campaign.  The  1972  and  1974
figures  suggest  that  these  are  exactly  the  effects  the  ceil-
ings  will  have  on  future  races.

In  1972,  out  of  1,009  candidates  who  ran  in  the  gen-
eral  election  for  House  seats,  only  21,  or  2.1  percent,
reported  total  spending  higher  than  $168,000  (the  com-
bined  primary  and  general  election  ceiling,  including
fund-raising  costs,  for  House  candidates  under  Section
608(c)).%  In  1974,  of  the  1,056  House  candidates  who
ran  in  the  general  election,  only  22,  or  2.1  percent,  spent

1582  Findings  IIA,  9] 40-41,  IIB  J.A.  448-49.  Section  608(c)  per-
mits  House  candidates  to  spend  $70,000  in  the  primary  and  $70,000
in  the  general  election.  Section  591(f)  (4) (H)  permits  an  additional
20  percent  of  the  total  for  fund-raising.  Under  the  statute,  all  ex-
penditure  ceilings  are  adjusted  in  each  election  year  for  changes
in  the  consumer  price  index.  The  legislative  history  makes  clear
that  a  candidate  can  spend  the  full  amount  of his  primary  allowance
regardless  of  whether  he  faces  primary  opposition,  see  Appendix
A  at  2a-3a,  and  the  FEC  has  so  ruled.  Advisory  Opinion  1975-9,
40  Fed.  Reg.  36242  (Aug.  19,  1975).

These  findings  compare  total  reported  spending  in  1972  and
1974  to  the  combined  limits  for  primaries  and  general  elections.
This  is  the  only  meaningful  and  accurate  comparison  that  can  be
made  from  the  reports  filed  in  1972  and  1974  under  the  FECA  of
1971.  Separate  comparisons  for  primaries  and  general  elections
in  1972  and  1974  cannot  be  made  in  any  meaningful  way  because
it  is  impossible  to  determine  from  the  reports  filed  for  those  years
whether  an  expenditure  was  made  to  influence  a  primary  or  gen-
eral  election.  See  Appendix  A at 4a-5a.

1 Lips Rae a Sali AE,

-}

a
-

more  than  the  combined  spending  ceiling  in  Section  608
(ec).  Findings  IIA,  J 18-19,  IIB  J.A.  442-43.  Thus  the
overwhelming  majority  of  House  candidates  spent  less
than  the  ceiling  in  conducting  their  campaigns,  winning
and  losing.

The  figures  for  Senate  elections  support  the  same  con-
clusion.
In  1972,  of  the  106  senatorial  candidates  who
ran  in  the  general  election,  only  18,  or  17  percent,  ex-
ceeded  the  combined  primary  and  general  election  spend-
ing  limit  (9.6  cents  per  voting  age  person  in  a  primary
and  14.4  cents  in  a  general  election,  including  fund-
raising  costs).  Findings  IIA,  §] 44,  47-48,  IIB  J.A.  449-
50.  In  1974,  of  the  105  senatorial  candidates  who  ran
in  the  general  election,  only  17,  or  16  percent,  exceeded
the  combined  spending  limit.  Findings  IIA,  {J 23,  28-29,
IIB  J.A.  444-45.

Adding  the  figures  for  House  and  Senate  races  in
1972,  only  39  out  of  1115  candidates,  or  3.5  percent,  ex-
ceeded  the  new  limits.  Adding  the  figures  for  1974,  only
39  out  of  1161  candidates,  or  3.4  percent,  exceeded  these
limits.***

In  the  1972  presidential  election,  both  major  party
candidates  exceeded  the  combined  spending  ceiling  ($10
million  in  primaries  and  $20  million  in  the  general  elec-
tion  plus  fund-raising  costs).***  Former  President  Nixon
reported  spending  approximately  $55  million,  and  Senator
McGovern  reported  spending  approximately  $38  million.
Findings  IIA,  $51,  IIB  J.A.  451.  The  excessive  fund-
raising  and  spending  in  that  race,  and  the  abuses  that

agree  that  one-half  of  campaign  expenditures  are  wasted,  but  no

‘

138

139

resulted,  were  significant  factors  in  the  public  demand
and  the  congressional  decision  to  impose  ceilings  lower
than  those  amounts.  The  ceilings  Congress  selected  were
higher  than  the  spending  in  any  presidential  campaign
before  1972.  Findings  I, 7 12,  89,  IIA  J.A.  9-10,  49-51.
_  Moreover,  they  were  higher  than  what  President  Nixon’s
own  finance  chairman  testified  would  have  been  sufficient
in  1972.  See  p.  70,  supra.

Further  confirmation  that  the  new  limits  permit  “ef-
fective’  campaigns  comes  from  the  first  Senate  race
held  under  the  new  laws,  the  Durkin-Wyman  New  Hamp-
shire  special  election  in  September.  Both  candidates  re-
ported  expenditures  within  the  new  ceilings;  yet  they
succeeded  in  turning  out  almost  40,000  more  votes  (some
18  percent)  above  what  the  same  two  candidates  polled
in  their  1974  race  without  spending  limits.’*

Appellants  attempt  to  show  the  impossibility  of  “ef-
fective”  campaigns  under  the  ceilings  by  describing  an
elaborate  hypvthetical—admittedly  based  on  New  Hamp-
shire—in  which  the  only  newspaper  in  the  district  is
“implacably  opposed”  to  the  challenger,
other  media
mostly  reach  an  audience  outside  the  district,  and  the
challenger  faces  a  long-time  incumbent  Appellants’  Br.
79-80.
The  Wyman-Durkin  race  shows  the  fallacy  of
looking  to  hypotheticals  rather  than  facts.

154  Appellants  incorrectly  claim  (Br.  84)  that  the  Act  limits
the  portion  of  general  election  funds  a  presidential  candidate
may  spend  in  any  one  state.  Only  primary  spending  is  limited  to
twice  the  Senate  limit  for  that  state.  Compare  Section  608(c)  (1)
(A)  with  Section  608(c)  (1)  (B).

155  1975  Cong.  Q.  Weekly  Rep.  1995  (Sept.  20,  1975)  (based  on
unofficial  spending  figures;  the  official  reports  were  not  available
at  this  printing).

— _ ee ee ee alates

Wyman  was  endorsed  by  the  only  major  newspaper,
The  Manchester  Union  Leader.  He  was  assisted  by cam-
paign  visits  from  the  incumbent  President  and  the  Presi-
dent’s  chief  rival  for  the  1976  Republican  nomination.
He  was  a  5-term  Congressman  from  one  of  the  state’s
two  congressional  districts,  while  Durkin’s  only  previous
offices  were  New  Hampshire  insurance  commissioner  and
moderator  of  a  Massachusetts  town.  Yet  Durkin  won
with  53.6  percent  of  the  vote.  The  ceilings  necessarily
caused  some  adjustments  in  the  campaigns,  but  they
certainly  did  not  prevent  “effective”  campaigning.’”

Appellants  unsuccessfully  attempt  to  draw  contrary
conclusions  from  expenditures  in  selected  past  races.  They
assert  that,  “on  the  basis  of  estimates  for  general  elec-
tion  expenditures  in  the  Senate”  in  1972,  33  candidates
exceeded  the  general  election  ceilings  and  the  ceilings
“would  have  eliminated  one-third  of  total  spending.”  Ap-
pellants’  Br.  89.  Those  comparisons  are  based  entirely
on  the  Cole  study,  whose  unreliability  we  have  demon-
strated  in  Appendix  A  and  which  the  court  of  appeals
properly  disregarded.  Since  the  errors  in  Cole’s  method-
ology  overstate  the  1972  spending  in  relation  to  the  lim-
its,  they  render  his  conclusions  useless  in  predicting  what
effects  the  limits  will  have.  In  particular,  Cole  errone-

156  Td.  at  1933-94  (Sept.  6, 1975),  1995  (Sept.  20,  1975);  7 Nat'l.

J.  Rep.  1339  (Sept.  20,  1975).

Moreover,  as  noted  above  (p.  90),  the  “effectiveness”  of  the  1976
and  later  campaigns  may  well  be  enhanced  by  an  extraneous  fac-
tor,  the  recent  Federal  Communications  Commission  ruling  permit-
ting  radio  and  television  coverage  of  candidate  press  conferences
and  candidate  debates,  free  of  the  “equal  time”  restriction  of  Sec-
tion  315  of  the  Communications  Act.
Until  the  results  of  this
important  change  are  fully  known,  no  one  can  predict  with  confi-
dence  the  minimum  expenditure  necessary  to  conduct  an  “effective”
campaign.

140

141

ously  counted  as  general  election  spending  all  spending
during  primary  periods  where  the  candidate  had  no
serious  primary  opposition  or  was  unopposed.  See  Ap-
pendix  A  at  3a-4a.  Thus  Cole  has  incorrectly  compared
the  total  amount  spent  over  the  full  course  of  an  election
—including  the  primary  and  general  election  periods—
with  the  ceilings  in  the  challenged  statutes  for  just  one
period:  the  general  election.

Appellants  attempt  to  justify  this  methodology  by  as-
serting,  as  did  Cole,  that  candidates  who  “had  serious
opposition  in  no  more  than  one  election”  aimed  “‘the
overwhelming  bulk”  of  their  spending  at  the  more  con-
tested  election.  Appellants’  Br.  89.  As  we  show  in  Ap-
pendix  A,  there  is  no  proof  that  this  assertion  is  true.
Even  if  it  were  true  about  past  elections,  candidates  in
such  situations  in  the  futwre  will  not  be  limited  to  spend-
ing  only  half  the  combined  ceiling.  These  laws  permit
even  a  candidate  who  has  no  primary  opposition  to  spend
up  to  the  primary  ceiling  during  the  primary  period
without  counting  it  toward  his  general  election  ceiling.
Thus,  even  if some  candidates  in  past  years  have  bunched
their  spending  in  the  general  election  period,  they  will
be  free  to  adjust  their  spending  to  take  advantage  of
the  combined  ceilings.  Appellants’  comparisons  of  total
spending  against  partial  ceilings  say  nothing  about  how
the  statutes  will  actually  operate.

Appellants  argue  that  for  those  who  run  in  “fully  con-
tested”  races  the  combined  overall  spending  limits  will
“dramatically  restrict”  campaigns.  Appellants’  Br.  89.
They  have  produced  a  table  which  simply  indicates  that
of  the  2,065  candidates  who  ran  for  the  House  in  1972
and  1974,**’  66  spent  in  excess  of  the  FECA  limits  (ad-

157  Findings  IIA,  9] 18-19,  40-41,  IIB  J.A.  442-43,  448-49.

justed  for  inflation).
Jd.  at  90.  And  having  defined  a
class  of candidates  they  consider  so  important—those  who
had  “serious  opposition  in  both  primary  and  general  elec-
tions”—appellants  can  find  only  20  such  candidates  who
exceeded  the  overall  combined  spending  limits  in  all  1972
and  1974  House  races.

Appellants  offer  a  second  table  to  prove  that  “  ‘high’
spending  occurs  in  the  most  competitive  races.”  Id.  at
90-91.  But  the  table  itself  shows  that,  even  in  the  most
hotly  contested  House  races  in  1972  and  1974  (those  in
which  the  winner  received  55  percent  or  less  of the  vote),
the  average  spending  of  the  winners  was  far  below  the
$168,000  limit.  For  example,  according  to  the  table  in
1974  the  average  total  expenditures  by  both  winners
($95,544)  and  losers  ($85,606)  were  each  approximately
one-half  of  the  overall  House  spending  limit  ($168,000).
And  appellants’  use  of  average  figures  has  little  meaning
in  analyzing  Senate  races,  since  the  spending  limit  for
Senate  races  varies  with  the  population  of  each  state;
thus  the  average  figure  has  no  relation  to  the  limit  for
any  given  state.

Appellants’  third  table  (Br.  95)  is  of  minimal  value
because  the  sample  of  candidates  is  so  small.  For  exam-
ple,  in  1972  only  10  House  challengers  (out  of  323)
beat  an  incumbent.  Thus  the  40  percent  figure—repre-
senting  successful  challengers  in  1972  who  spent  in
excess  of  the  overall  limit  adjusted  for  inflation—is  in
reality  four  individuals.  In  1974  almost  half  of  the  win-
ning  challengers  (18  out  of  40)  spent  less  than  $84,000.
Only  one  winning  challenger  spent  more  than  the  $168,-
000  overall  limit.  Findings  IIA,  773,  5,  IIA  J.A.  269-
318,  326-86.  In  sum,  none  of  appellants’  tables  provides
the  slightest  support  for  appellants’  arguments  about  dis-

142

143

crimination  against  challengers  or  minor  party  or  inde-
pendent  candidates.

Appellants  cite  only  one  additional  piece  of  evidence
to  show  that  the  limits  will  prevent  “effective”  cam-
paigning—Cole’s  conclusion  that  in  the  1972  Senate  cam-
paigns  one  phone  call  or  letter  cost  approximately  20
cents.  Comparing  that  figure  to  the  combined  ceiling
of  24  cents  per  eligible  voter,  appellants  conclude  that
the  “statutory  limits  are  so  low  as  to  lack  a  rational
basis.”  Appellants’  Br.  92.  The  comparison  proves  noth-
ing.  No  candidate,  so  far  as  we  know,  has  ever  thought
that  telephoning  or  writing  every  voter  was  an  effective
way  to spend  campaign  money.**  In  1972  and  1974,  when
spending  was  not  limited  and  candidates  to  whom  the
money  was  available  presumably  spent  what  was  neces-
sary  for  “effective”  campaigns,  all  but  a  handful  of
House  and  Senate  candidates  spent  below  the  new  limits.
Pp.  136-37,  supra.  Their  spending  proves  the  irrelevance
of  appellants’  20-cent  figure.

Appellants  next  argue  (Br.  94)  that  because  the  costs
of  campaigning  differ  from  state  to  state  and  among
congressional  districts,  it  was  irrational  for  Congress  to
impose  identical  spending  ceilings  for  all  House  races
and  identical  per-voter  ceilings  for  all  Senate  races.’
They  assert  that  Congress  was  obliged  to  adopt  a  tailor-
made  ceiling  for  each  race  to  reflect  the  many  factors
that  determine  the  relative  cost  of  effective  electioneering
in  different  areas.

158  Appellants  analyze  McCarthy’s  and  Nixon’s  spending  in  1968
on  the  basis  of  money  spent  per  vote  received  by  them.  Appellants’
Br.  97.  Such  a  comparison  is  of  no  relevance  in  analyzing  expendi-
ture  limits  expressed  in  terms  of  money  spent  per  eligible  voter.

159  As  noted  above,  House  races  in  states  having  only  one  Repre-
sentative  are  treated  the  same  as  Senate  races  by  Section  608(c).

Such  a  requirement  would  demand  impossible  precision
of  Congress  and  would  create  dangerous  opportunities
for  gerrymandering  of  the  ceilings.  The  statute  books
are  full  of  laws  with  numerical  ceilings  that  apply
throughout  the  nation—such  as  the  personal  exemption
from  income  tax,  the  minimum  wage,  and  license  and
filing  fees.  Appellants’  argument  would  require  each  of
these  to  be  revised  to  reflect  local  conditions.  This  Court
has  consistently  held  that  Congress  may  prescribe  a  gen-
eral  remedy  for  an  evil  that  arises  in  various  forms
or  degrees,  where  it would  not  be practicable  or  desirable
to  deal  with  each  specific  problem  individually.  William-
son  V.  Lee  Optical  Co.,  348  U.S.  488,  487-88  (1955).

The  choice  of  ceilings  is  a  legislative  judgment  en-
titled  to  judicial  deference.
This  is  particularly  true
where  Congress,  after  careful  deliberation,  has  made
such  a  judgment  on  a  matter  uniquely  within  its  own
field  of  expertise.  This  Court  has  no  warrant  to  invali-
date  the  ceilings  before  they  have  had  a  chance  to  work,
unless  appellants  can  show  that  Congress’  choice  was  man-
ifestly  unreasonable.
Nothing  in  the  record  supports
such  a  conclusion.

2.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Ex-
penditure  Ceilings  Will  In Practice  Discriminate
In  Favor  of  Incumbents  and  Major  Party  Can-
didates

Appellants  make  the  paradoxical  claim  that,  because
the  spending  ceilings  apply  equally  to  all  candidates  in
any  race,  they  will  be  discriminatory  in  their  practical
effect.  As  with  contribution  limits,  their  theory  is  that
candidates  who  lack  the  advantages  of  incumbency  or
major  party  sponsorship  need  some  way  to overcome  their

ce.

crimination  against  challengers  or  minor  party  or  inde-
pendent  candidates.

to  show  that  the  limits  will  prevent  “effective”  cam-
paigning—Cole’s  conclusion  that  in  the  1972  Senate  cam-

cents.  Comparing  that  figure  to  the  combined  ceiling

sary  for  “effective”  campaigns,  all  but  a  handful  of

2.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Ex-
penditure  Ceilings  Will  In  Practice  Discriminate
In  Favor  of  Incumbents  and  Major  Party  Can-
didates

Appellants  make  the  paradoxical  claim  that,  because
the  spending  ceilings  apply  equally  to  all  candidates  in
any  race,  they  will  be  discriminatory  in  their  practical
effect.  As  with  contribution  limits,  their  theory  is  that
candidates  who  lack  the  advantages  of  incumbency  or
major  party  sponsorship  need  some  way  to  overcome  their

i
i
|
|
|

ee

144

145

handicaps,  and  that  they  are  constitutionally  entitled  to
do  so  by outspending  their  opponents—and  exceeding  the
expenditure  ceilings.”

Appellants’  argument  would  not  make  out  a  claim
of unconstitutional  discrimination  even  if they  could  show
that  challengers  and  minor  party  candidates  had,  on  the
average,  outspent  their  rivals  in  the  past  as  a  way  to
overcome  other  disadvantages.  Every  evenhanded  law  af-
fects  some  people  more  than  others,  because  the  circum-
stances  of  life  are  varied.  Absent  a  showing  that  a  law,
equal  on  its  face,  will  affect  only  or  predominantly  a
single  group,  minor  variations  in  the  incidence  of  the
law  do  not  show  unconstitutional  discrimination.  And
Congress  need  not  provide  exceptions  from  evenhanded
laws  for  every  person  or  group  who  may  labor  under
handicaps  unrelated  to  the  laws.  See  pp.  113-14,  supra.

160  Under  the  authority  of  the  Act,  the  FEC  is  acting  to  admin-
ister  the  law  in  such  a  way  that  political  activities  of  incumbents,
such  as  the  use  of  private  office  accounts,  are  treated  in  the  same
way  as  political  activities  of  non-incumbent  candidates.  See  FEC
Notice  1975-18,  40  Fed.  Reg.  32951  (Aug.  5,  1975);  S.  Rep.  No.
409,  94th  Cong.,  1st  Sess.  13  (1975).  The  Senate  recently  rejected
two  of  the  Commission’s  regulations  on  office  accounts,  but  not  on
the  ground  that  incumbents’  advantages  were  reduced  by  the  pro-
posed  regulations.  The  Senate  concluded  that  equality  between  in-
cumbents  and  non-incumbents  would  be  better  promoted  by  impos-
ing  identical  treatment  on  all  federal  officeholders  and  non-office-
holders,  and  that  the  proposed  regulations  did  not  adequately  ac-
complish  this  end.  120  Cong.  Rec.  S  17872-88  (daily  ed.  Oct.  8,
1975);  S.  Rep.  No.  409,  supra,  at  2-4.  The  Commission  intends  to
propose  a  revised  regulation  taking  into  account  this  concern.  The
Washington  Post,  Oct.  10,  1975,  at  A-3,  col.  1.  Senator  Buckley,  who
opposes  incumbents’  advantages  in  this  lawsuit,  was  the  deciding
vote  in  a  48  to  47  vote  to  reject  the  proposed  regulations.  We  as-
sume  he  would  not  have  voted  against  the  proposed  regulations  if
to  do  so  served  to  promote  incumbents’  advantages.

In  any  event,  appellants  have  failed  to  show  that  the
ceilings  will  impinge  any  more  on  challengers  or  minor
party  and  independent  candidates  than  on  their  oppon-
ents.  On  the  contrary,  the  statistics  fy  m  the  1972  and
1974  elections  show  that  challengers  and  minor  party
and  independent  candidates  generally  have  been  outspent
in  the  past  by  incumbents  and  major  party  candidates.
Thus,  they  will  be  no  worse  off—and  in  a  number  of
cases  better  off—in  relative  terms  under  the  ceilings
than  they  would  have  been  in  their  absence.

Incumbents  have  demonstrated  their  ability,  in  the  ab-
sence  of  ceilings,  to  raise  and  spend  more  money  than
challengers.
In  the  1972  and  1974  House  and  Senate
elections,  with  no  ceilings,  incumbents  outspent  their
major  party  challengers  by  about  $62.4  million  to  $36.5
million.  Findings  IIA,
14,  24,  37,  45,  IIB  J.A.  442,
444,  447-48,  449.  In  those  elections,  more  challengers
would  have  been  helped  than  hurt  if  the  expenditure
ceilings  had  been  in  effect.
In  all  1972  and  1974  con-
gressional  races,  the  ceilings  would  have  operated  to  re-
duce  the  spending  of  32  incumbents  but  only  15  chal-
lengers—and  of  these  15  challengers,  six  were  outspent
by  their  incumbent  opponents.
Findings  IIA,  {9 5,  8,
28,  47,  ITA  J.A.  326-86,  IIB  J.A.  422-31,  445,  450.
Thus,  only  nine  challengers  in  the  approximately  700
congressional  races  in  1972  and  1974  involving  incum-
bents  and  challengers  spent  more  than  the  ceilings  and
also  outspent  their  incumbent  opponents.  Findings  IIA,

161  In  1974,  incumbents  outspent  their  major  party  challengers
in  21  of  22  Senate  races  and  252  of  323  House  races.  Findings
ITA,  {/{[ 21,  31,  IIB  J.A.  443,  445.  In  1972,  incumbents  outspent
their  major  party  challengers  in  19  of  25  Senate  races  and  249  of
321  House  races.  Findings  IIA,  {/f] 43,  50,  IIB  J.A.  449,  450.

146

147

1 14,  24,  37,  45,  IIB  J.A.  442,  444,  447-48,  449.  In  the
more  numerous  cases  where  incumbents  exceeded  both
the  ceilings  and  their  challengers’  spending,  the  ceilings
would  have  benefited  the  challengers.’”

Appellants  say  that  it  is  in  “close”  races  that  the
limits  most  discriminate  against  challengers.  Appellants’
Br.  98-99.  To  the  contrary,  the  expenditure  ceilings  may
help  challengers  when  the  races  are  close.  For  example,
in  1974  there  were  nine  Senate  races  in  which  the  win-
ner  received  less  than  55  percent  of  the  vote  in  the  gen-
eral  election.  Eight  were  won  by  incumbents  and  one
by  a  challenger.  The  eight  winning  incumbents  all  sub-
stantially  outspent  their  major  party  challengers,  and  five
of  the  eight  would  have  exceeded  the  new  ceilings.  Find-
ings  ILA,  7 33,  IIB  J.A.  446-47.  For  example,  Senator
Henry  Bellmon  in  1974  spent  $622,480  compared  with
$195,429  spent  by  his  challenger  Ed  Edmundson.  The
new  ceiling  for  Oklahoma  is  $450,960.  Bellmon  received
49.4  percent  of  the  vote  compared  with  48.9  percent  re-
ceived  by  Edmundson.  Findings  IIA,  { 33,  IIB  J.A.  446.
In  1974  Representative  Sam  Steiger  spent  $203,899  as
opposed  to  $68,203  spent  by  his  challenger,  Pat  Bosch.
The  combined  ceiling  for  House  races  is $168,000.  Steiger
received  51.1  percent  of  the  vote  compared  with  48.9  per-
cent  received  by  Bosch.  Findings  IIA,  § 5, IIA  J.A.  328.
At  the  very  least,  these  facts  of  record  establish  the
failure  of  appellants  to  prove  that  the  new  ceilings  will
make  challengers  as  a  group  worse  off,  relative  to  in-
cumbents  as  a  group,  than  they  would  have  been  if  the
ceilings  had  never  been  enacted.

162  Appellants’  affiant  Cole  conceded  this  fact.

The  1972  and  1974  results  are  even  more  striking  in
what  they  show  about  the  relative  spending  power  of
minor  party  and  independent  candidates  vis-a-vis  major
party  candidates.  Minor  party  and  independent  candi-
dates  were  grossly  outspent  by  major  party  candidates
in  the  1972  and  1974  congressional  elections,  when  no
ceilings  were  in  effect.  They  were  outspent  by 40  to  1 in
1972  and  65  to  1 in  1974.  Findings  IIA,  {9 12,  35,  IIB
J.A.  441,  447.
In  1972  and  1974,  only  one  of  the  42
House  candidates  who  exceeded  the  ceilings  was  a  minor
party  or  independent  candidate,  former  Representative
Allard  Lowenstein  of  New  York.’
None  of  the  35
Senate  candidates  who  exceeded  the  ceilings  was  a  minor
party  or  independent  candidate.’

Considering  all  1972  and  1974  races  together,  the  new
ceilings  would  have  reduced  the  spending  of  76  major

163  Lowenstein  ran  as  a  Liberal  in  the  1972  general  election  after
losing  in  a  Democratic  primary  and  a  court-ordered  second  primary
against  a  Democratic  incumbent.  His  total  spending  was  $285,475.
Of  the  192  other  minor  party  and  independent  House  candidates
in  1972,  only  18  spent  more  than  $10,000.  Findings  IIA,  {]{] 40-41,
IIB  J.A.  448-49.

In  1974,  the  highest  amount  spent  by  any  of  the  246  minor
party  and  independent  House  candidates  was  $85,680  by Don  Elliott
of  New  York.  Elliott  ran  as  a  Liberal  in  the  general  election  fol-
lowing  his  defeat  in  the  Democratic  primary  by  the  incumbent.  Of
the  remaining  245  minor  party  and  independent  candidates,  only
15  reported  spending  more  than  $10,000.  Findings  IIA,  {{ 18-19,

McKeithen,  an  independent  candidate  in  Louisiana.  Only  one  other

A.  450.

In  1974,  the  highest  amount  spent  by  any  of  the  40  minor  party
and  independent  Senate  candidates  was  $192,462  by  Barbara  A.

other  such  candidates  spent  over  $20,000.  Findings  IIA,  §{] 28-29,

party  candidates  but  only  one  minor  party  candidate.
Clearly,  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  as  a
group  will  be  better  off  in  spending  ability  vis-a-vis
major  party  candidates  than  they  would  have  been  if the
ceilings  had  not  been  enacted.’  At  the  very  least,  the
contrary  has  not  been  proved.

In  any  event,  no  firm  relationship  has  been  established
between  outspending  one’s  opponent  and  winning  elec-
tions.  While  there  were  occasional  instances  in  which  a
challenger  outspent  a®  incumbent  and  also  won  the  elec-
tion,  there  were  other  instances  in  which  an  outspending
challenger  lost,  or  in,  which  a  challenger  won  without
outspending  the  incumbent.
For  instance,  even  if  ap-
pellants’  figures  were  accepted,  of  the  five  challengers
who  outspent  an  incumbent  in  the  1972  Senate  general
elections,  two  won  and  three  lost.  Findings  IIB,  {ff 4-6,
IIB  J.A.  573-74.  In  1972  and  1974  congressional  races,
there  were  a  total  of  57  races  in  which  a  challenger
defeated  an  incumbent  in  the  general  election.  The  chal-
lenger  outspent  the  incumbent  in  34  of  these  races  and
was  outspent  by  the  incumbent  in  23  of  these  races.
Findings  IIA,  {{ 34,  36,  IIB  J.A.  447.  Even  if  the  cor-
relation  between  spending  and  winning  were  much  closer,
causation  would  not  be  shovn;  the  correlation  might  in-
dicate  that  money  flows  to  lixely  winners,’”  or  that  both

165  The  1972  presidential  election  shows  the  same  pattern.  Both
major  party  candidates  exceeded  the  new  spending  ceiling  for
presidential  candidates.
The  eight  minor  party  candidates  and
three  independent  candidates  altogether  reported  spending  $1,005,-
000—only  one  percent  of  the  total  spending  reported  by  the  two
major  party  candidates.  Findings  IIA,  § 51,  IIB  J.A.  451.  Obvi-
ously,  none  of  the  minor  party  or  independent  candidates  even
approached  the  ceiling;  all  will  thus  be  better  off  vis-a-vis  major
party  candidates  under  the  new  ceilings.

money  and  votes  flow  to  candidates  with  other  traits  that

Appellants  seek  to  avoid  the  force  of  these  statistics
by  focusing  on  selected  individual  races  and  proffering
hypothetical  and  unsubstantiated  theories.  They  argue
that  there  is  a  “floor”  of  spending  that  a  challenger
must  reach  “to  be  ‘viable,’  to  obtain  the  voter  recogni-
tion  that  permits  a  campaign  to  attract  votes.”  Ap
pellants’  Br.  97.  If  any  such  “floor”  exists,  appellants
have  not  shown  that  the  ceilings  will  prevent  candidates
from  reaching  it.  Of  the  seven  challengers  who  beat
Senate  incumbents  in  the  1972  and  1974  general  elec-
tions,  only  one  spent  more  than  the  new  ceiling  of  24
cents  per  eligible  voter.  Findings  IIA,  {{ 30,  47,  IIB
J.A.  445,  450.  In  the  five  1972  Senate  races  appellants
characterize  as  “high-spending”  (Br.  96),  three  of  the
victorious  challengers  spent  (with  an  inflation  adjust-
ment)  less  than  $300,000—the  lowest  combined  ceiling
for  the  Senate  in  any  state—and  another  exceeded  it
by less  than  $10,)00.  Findings  IIA,  7 4, ITA  J.A.  319-25.
These  results  suggest  that  the  ceilings  will  not  prevent
Senate  candidates  from  reaching  the  “floor,”  if  any  floor
exists.

The  statistics  on  House  elections  point  the  same  way.
Of  the  50  challengers  who  beat  House  incumbents  in
the  1972  and  1974  general  elections,  only  two  spent  more
than  the  new  ceiling  of  $168,000.  Findings  IIA,  {¥ 20,
21,  42,  43,  IIB  J.A.  443,  449.  Challengers  in  1974  House
races  defeated  incumbents  while  spending  as  little  as
$15,846  (David  Evans),  $33,064  (Gary  Myers),  $36,227

faced  contested  primaries  before  the  general  election.
House  candidates  also  won  open  races  not  involving  an

(Hall),  $29,965

Appellants  hypothesize  a  comparative  “marginal  utility
of  initial  spending”  theory  to  support  their  paradoxical
claim  that  incumbents  are  favored  over  challengers  by
Appellants’
any  ceilings  that  apply  equally  to  hoth.
Br.  97-98.  They  assert  that,  because  challengers  often
start  as  relative  unknowns,  they  get  more  impact  than
incumbents  do from  their  initial  campaign  spending,  until
they  “obtain  the  voter  recognition  that  enables  a  cam-
paign  to  attract  votes.”  Jd.  at  97.

Even  if  it  were  sound,  the  theory  would  not  impugn
It  would  support  only  an  argument  that
these  ceilings.
the  ceilings  should  be  high  enough  to  permit  challengers
to  reach  a  hypothetical  “floor”—which,  as  we  have  shown,
these  ceilings  clearly  do.  But  the  theory  is  not  sound,
because  it  disregards  incumbents’  unquestioned  ability
to  raise  and  spend  more  money.  The  net  result  would
vary  with  the  circumstances  of  each  case—which  renders
the  theory  useless  in  proving  discrimination  between

groups.

In  sum,  appellants’  contention  that  the  expenditure  ceil-
ings  will  favor  incumbents  rests  on  inconclusive  data  and
unsupported  hypotheses.  As  the  court  of  appeals  con-
cluded:

“Plaintiffs  contend  that  only  by  immense  expendi-
tures  can  an  unknown  candidate  compete.  This  may
be  correct  for  some  candidates,  in  some  situations.
But  it  also  is  true  that  at  other  times  a  newcomer
has  achieved  political  momentum  without  large  con-
tributions.  In  a  broad  sense,  the  statute  may  favor
relative  newcomers,  by  limiting  the  access  of  in-
cumbents  to  large  donations.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1541.

Such  a  state  of  proof  does  not  rise  to  the  dignity  of
an  unconstitutional  discrimination.
Professor  Freund’s
analysis  is  apposite:

“Turning  to  a  limit  on  expenditures,  the  claim  is
made  that  a  candidate  should  have  the  liberty  to
attract  all  the  material  support  he  can  and  allocate
it  as  he  deems  best...  .

“Much  is  argued  about  the  favor  that  would  be
bestowed  upon  a  competitor  as  against  an  incumbent.
It  seems  to  me  very  problematic  which  would  be
helped,  and  in  that  event  there  is no  serious  problem.
The  incumbent  may  be  helped  by  a  limitation  on
expenditures  since  he  is better  known  and  needs  less
exposure.  On  the  other  hand,  a  limit  on  expendi-
tures  may  encourage  entry  into  the  field  by competi-
tors,  and  in  that  situation  you  have  a  standoff,  cer-
tainly  not  so  clearly  a  situation  of  favoritism  be-
stowed  by law  as  would  call  for  the  invocation  of  a
constitutional  prohibition.”

3.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  the  Ex-
penditure  Ceilings  Discriminate  Against  Inde-
pendents  or  Single-State  Political  Parties

Appellant  McCarthy  makes  a  further  argument:  that
these  statutes  discriminate  against  independents  by per-

mitting  national  or  state  party  committees  to  spend  ad-
ditional  amounts  not  charged  against  a  candidate’s  ceil-
ing  (18  U.S.C.  §608(b))—a  provision  from  which
only  a  party-sponsored  candidate  can  benefit.  Appellant
Conservative  Party  of  New  York  also  complains  that
since  it  does  not  have  a  national  committee  it  cannot
gain  the  advantage  of  the  expenditures  allowed  national
committees.
Appellants’  Br.  104-06.  Again,  appellants’
grievances  are  aimed  at  the  facts  of  political  life  and
not  at  any  aspect  of  the  challenged  statutes.

A  candidate  nominated  by a  party—major  or  minor—
obtains  advantages  but  also  bears  extra  burdens.
He
obtains  the  party’s  support,  financial  and  otherwise,
but  loses  some  of  his  freedom  to  stake  out  his  own  posi-
tions  and  usually  obliges  himself  to  support  the  party’s
other  candidates.
In  short,  “the  political  party  and  the
independent  candidate  approaches  to  political  activity  are
entirely  different...  .”  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724,
745  (1974).  Appellant  McCarthy  readily  admits  that  the
group  supporting  his  candidacy,  appellant  Committee
for  a  Constitutional  Presidency—McCarthy  ’76,  chose  not
to  organize  as  a  political  party  in  order  to  dramatize  the
differences  between  party-nominated  and  independent  can-
didates;  he  considers  his  independent  status  an  “impor-
tant  asset”  in  his  “appeal.”  ***  Appellant  Conservative
Party  has  chosen  to  confine  its  activities  to  one  state.

16°  Appellants’  Br.  105;  see  McCarthy  Deposition  Tr.  at  66-67;

Monroe  Affidavit,  { 7.

McCarthy  nevertheless  qualifies  as  the  candidate  of  a  “party”
for  purposes  of  receiving  public  financing  for  his  candidacy.  See
p. 224,  infra.

By  permitting  a  party  committee  to  spend  money  to
support  its  candidates,  the  challenged  statutes  merely
allow  parties  to  continue  to  perform  one  of  their  tra-
To  provide  otherwise  would  be  to
ditional  functions.
outlaw,  in  part,  “the  political  party  .
.  [approach]  to
.
Instead,  the  statutes  leave  to  each
political  activity.”
candidate  the  decision  whether  to  seek  a  party  nomina-
tion,  and  to  each  party  the  decision  to  organize  as  it
chooses.  Appellant  McCarthy,  who  has  sought  and  re-
ceived  party  nominations  in  the  past,  is  free  to  seek
them  in  the  future  if  he  decides  that  the  benefits  out-
weigh  the  disadvantages.  Appellant  Conservative  Party
can  expand  geographically  if it chooses.

Appellant  McCarthy  also  complains  that  the  expendi-
ture  ceiling  harms  him  because  it  “prevents  independent
candidates  from  spending  as  much  as  party-sponsored
candidates,”  apparently  with  reference  to  the  prenomina-
tion  period,  while  he  is  gaining  access  to  the  ballot  as
an  independent.**  We  believe  that  appellants  have  mis-
read  the  challenged  legislation.  The  legislative  history
makes  it clear  that  McCarthy,  as  an  independent,  will  be
allowed  to  spend  $10  million  (plus  the  fund-raising  al-
lowance)  during  the  period  before  the  primary  elections
of  the  major  parties.  See  Appendix  A  at  3a-4a.  And  in
a  virtually  indistinguishable  case,  the  FEC  has  already
ruled  that  a  party  candidate  can  spend  up  to the  primary
election  ceiling  even  if he  is  unopposed  in  the  primary.’”

In  summary,  the  recent  election  statistics  do  not  sup-
port  appellants’  claims  of  unconstitutional  discrimina-
tion.  Nothing  else  in  the  record  does  so.  In  their  attack
on  the  face  of  the  statutes  before  the  laws  have  operated
in  even  one  regular  election  year,  appellants  are  obli-
gated  to  present  convincing  evidence  that  the  statutes
will  inevitably  have  the  effects  they  allege.  They  have
presented  no  such  evidence,  and  their  claims  of  discrimi-
nation  fail  utterly  for  lack  of  proof.

C.  The  Independent  Expenditure  Limit  Is  a  Reason-
able  Means  To  Prevent  Evasion  of  Giving  and
Spending  Limits

Section  608(b)  limits  to  $1,000  the  contributions  a
person  may  make  to  a  candidate  in  a  federal  election.
Section  608(e)  imposes  an  identical  limit  on  any  ex-
penditure  by  a  person  “relative  to  a  clearly  identified
.  advocating  the
candidate  during  a  calendar  year  .
election  or  defeat  of  such  candidate.”  The  statute  nar-
rowly  defines  the  words  “clearly  identified.”
18  U.S.C.
§ 608(e)  (2)  (A).

.

Thus,  in  an  election  for  federal  office,  an  individual
may  give  up  to  $1,000  to  any  candidate  and  may  also
make  “independent  expenditures”  up  to  the  same  limit
to  promote  the  candidate’s  cause  or  to  defeat  his  op-
ponent.
In  addition,  he  may  incur  entertainment  or
travel  expenses  to  the  same  end,  the  first  $500  of  each
of  which  are  not  treated  as  an  “expenditure.”  18  U.S.C.
§ 591(f).

As  the  court  of  appeals  recognized,  the  limit  on  in-
dependent  expenditures  is  necessary  to  eliminate  an  ob-
Ct.
vious  means  of  evading  the  limit  on  contributions.
App.  Op.  1530.  Congress  was  concerned  that  wealthy
individuals  might  spend  vast  sums  of  money  for  satellite

campaigns,  formally  unconnected  to  a  candidate’s  cam-
paign  but  supportive  of  it all  the  same:

“{S]uch  controls  are  imperative  if  Congress  is
to  enact  meaningful  limits  on  direct  contributions.
Otherwise,  wealthy  individuals  limited  to  a  [$1,000]
direct  contribution  could  also  purchase  one  hundred
thousand  dollars’  worth  of  advertisements  for  a  fa-
vored  candidate.  Such  a  loophole  would  render  direct
contribution  limits  virtually  meaningless.”  *”

Obviously,  such  spending  would  also  frustrate  the  limits
on  expenditures  by  candidates.

This  congressional  concern  finds  ample  basis  in  the
history  of  evasion  of  prior  campaign  finance  laws.  Giv-
ing  and  spending  limits  are  not  new  to  the  1974  legisla-
tion.  They  have  been  on  federal  statute  books  since  1940.
Amendments  to  the  Hatch  Act  in  that  year  imposed  a
$5,000  limit  on  contributions  to  any  interstate  campaign
committee  and  a  $3  million  ceiling  on  the  receipts  and
expenditures  of  such  a  committee.’  Both  of  those  limits
were  ingeniously  evaded  and  became  dead  letters.  The
1940  law  was  read  by  political  fund-raisers  and  their
counsel  as  merely  limiting  contributions  to  a  single  com-
mittee,  and  as  containing  nothing  to  prevent  multiple
gifts  to  multiple  committees  even  when  the  multiple  com-

72S.  Rep.  No.  689,  supra  note  35,  at  71.  This  is  a  judgment
that  Congress  has  repeatedly  and  consistently  emphasized.
See
H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239,  supra  note  56,  at  6-7,  17,  51;  S.  Rep.  No.  310,
93d  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  12-13,  43  (1973);  S.  Rep.  No.  1237,  93d  Cong.,
2d  Sess.  4, 56-57  (1974)  (Conference  Report) ; H.R.  Rep.  No.  1438,
93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  4,  56-57  (1974)
(Conference  Report);  119
Cong.  Rec.  S  14636,  14857-58  (1973)  (consideration  of  provision
comparable  to  § 608(e)  in  earlier  version  of  Senate  bill).

™8  Act  of  July,  1940,  54  Stat.  767-72,  enacting  Hatch  Act  §§ 13,

Ne et le et ial

20.

eee

156

157

mittees  were  under  common  control.’*  Also,  it  was  read
as  inapplicable  to  committees  operating  within  a  single
state.  Campaign  solicitors  and  contributors  showed  no
reluctance  to  exploit  these  loopholes,  proving  the  statute
to  be,  in  President  Johnson’s  phrase,  “more  loophole  than
law.”  See  Ct.  App.  Op.  1530.

An  even  more  direct  antecedent  exists  in  the  history
of  Section  610,  which  until  1947  prohibited  only  corporate
and  union  “contributions.”  That  word  was  construed  to
include  only  direct  gifts  or  payments  to—not  expendi-
tures  independent  of—a  candidate’s  formal  campaign.
The  result  was  massive  evasion,  as  the  district  court
found.  Findings  I, 1136,  IIA  J.A.  190.  The  experience
is  recounted  in  detail  in  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter’s  opin-
ion  for  the  Court  in  United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.
567,  570-84  (1957).

After  the  1944  elections,  Congress  investigated  the
evasion  of  Section  610  and  concluded  that  the  term  “con-
tribution”  had  been  wrongly  interpreted  to  exclude  ex-
H.R.  Rep.  No.  2739,  79th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.
penditures.
It  amended  the  statute  in  Section  304
40,  46  (1946).
Senator
of  the  Taft-Hartley  Act  to  correct  the  error.
Taft  explained  the  purpose  of  the  amendment:

“'T)he  words  of  [Section  610]  have  been  somewhat
changed  in  effect  so  as  to  plug  up  a  loophole  which
obviously  developed,  and  which,  if  the  courts  had
permitted  advantage  to  be  taken  of  it,  as  a  matter
of  fact,  would  absolutely  have  destroyed  the  prohibi-
tion  against  political  advertising  by corporations.  If

1"*  This  reading  may  have  been  incorrect,  see  Common  Cause  V.
Democratic  Nat'l  Comm.,  333  F.  Supp.  803  (D.D.C.  1971);  Com-
mon  Cause  Vv.  Finance  Comm.  to  Re-Elect  the  President,  Civil
No.  1780-72  (D.D.C.  1973),  but  it  was  never  challenged  by  the
Department  of  Justice.

‘contribution’  does  not  mean  ‘expenditure,’  then  a
candidate  for  office  could  have  his  corporation  friends
publish  an  advertisement  for  him  in  the  newspapers
every  day  for  a  month  before  election.
I  do  not
think  the  law  contemplated  such  a  thing,  but  it  was
claimed  that  it did,  at  least  when  it  applied  to  labor
organizations.”  93  Cong.  Rec.  6439  (1947).

This  Court  has  recognized  the  force  of that  logic.  It has
said  that  “it  was  obvious  that  the  statute  as  construed
[before  1947]  could  easily  be  circumvented  through  in-
direct  contributions.”
United  States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.
106,  115  (1948).  See  also  United  States  v.  UAW,  supra.
The  Court  construed  Section  610,  as  amended,  to  leave
corporations  and  unions  free  to  spend  money  to  com-
municate  with  their  stockholders  and  members.
United
States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.  at  121-24.  However,  it has  never
cast  doubt  on  either  the  need  for  or  the  constitutionality
of  prohibiting  other  corporate  and  union  expenditures
designed  to  influence  federal  elections.
See  Pipefitters
Union  v.  United  States,  407  U.S.  385  (1972).

The  independent  expenditure  limit  in  Section  608  is
not  a  total  prohibition  but  a  ceiling  permitting  expendi-
tures  up  to  $1,000  per  candidate;  and  it  limits  only
the  spending  of  money,  leaving  individuals  free  to  ex-
press  their  views  in  any  forum  at  any  time.'**  Moreover,
independent  expenditures  for  or  against  candidates  are
not  a  traditional  means  of  political  expression;  their

178  Compare  Civil  Service  Comm'n  v.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.

activities  that  the  district  court  found  available  to  a  citizen  wishing
to  participate  in  and  affect  the  political  process;  the  vast  majority
of  these  activities  involve  little  or  no  expenditure  of  money.

er
wise

158
amount  has  been  almost  negligible.’  As  the  history
of  Section  610  reveals,  if  such  expenditures  increase,  the
increase  will  almost  certainly  be  because  they  offer  a
means  to circumvent  the  other  limits.

In  writing  Section  608(e),  Congress  conscientiously  fol-
lowed  the  guidance  furnished  by the  courts  in  invalidat-
ing  an  earlier  provision  aimed  at  the  circumvention
problem.  Title  I  of  the  FECA  as  enacted  in  1971  im-

159

the  candidate’s  ceiling,  and  to  give  the  candidate  a  vir-
tual  veto  power  over  everything  said  in  his  favor  or
against  his  opponent  in  a  paid  advertisement.  Further-
more,  the  scheme  was  enforceable  by  penalties  against
the  communications  media  for  unauthorized  publications.
That  enforcement  scheme  was  held  by  a  three-judge
district  court  to  be  an  unconstitutional  prior  restraint
on  the  press.  ACLU,  Inc.  v.  Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041
(D.D.C.  1973),  dismissed  as  moot,  43  U.S.L.W.  5043
(1975).  The  district  court  observed  that  the  candidate
expenditure  ceilings  themselves  were  “presumably  valid,”
wd.  at  1050,  and  that  Title  I’s  “constitutional  infirmities
—its  prior  restraints  and  criminal  sanctions  directed  to
the  press—[are]  inherent  in  the  enforcement  structure
that  it  creates.”  Jd.  at  1054.  The  Court  also  noted  that
“{t}]he  aims  of  the  Act  [are]  clearly  of  prime  national
concern.”  Jd.  Congress  responded  by  repealing  Title  I
and  substituting  the  contribution  and  independent  ex-
penditure  limits  here  under  review.*”

The  present  law  is  free  of  its  predecessor’s  flaws.  An
individual  may  contribute  directly  to  a  candidate,  incur
independent  expenses  in  the  candidate’s  support,  or  both.
In  each  case,  he  is  subject  only  to  a  statutory  maximum
amount.  No  candidate  has  control  over  what  may  be
said  about  him  or  his  opponent  by  others,  and  no  re
strictions  or  prohibitions  run  against  the  communications
media.  Congress  has  seen  the  defects  of  its  previous
approach  and  manifestly  found  a  better  way,  guided  by

78  In  1973,  the  Senate  passed a bill

uiring

of  all  independent  expenditures  (over  $1,000) A a
that  the  1971  law  required  approval  of  media  expenditures.

Jennings  decision  intervened  and  in  the  next  session  Congress
changed  its  approach  by passing  the  challenged  statutes.

the  decision  in  Jennings.  See  S.  Rep.  No.  689,  supra
note  35,  at  18-19.*"°

As  the  court  of  appeals  stressed,**°  Section  608(e)  is
carefully  delimited  to  cover  only  expenditures  that  would
be  direct  evasions  of  the  contribution  limits.  Only  ex-
penditures  “advocating  the  election  or  defeat”  of a  “clear-
ly identified  candidate”  are  subject  to  the  monetary  limit.
“Clearly  identified”  is  explicitly  and  narrowly  defined.
18  U.S.C.  § 608(e)(2)(A).  Funds  spent  to  propagate
one’s  views  on  issues  without  expressly  calling  for  a
candidate’s  election  or  defeat  are  thus  not  covered.

The  boundaries  of  Section  608(e)  are  not  so  vague
that  groups  and  individuals  will  have  to  guess  as  to  the
statute’s  reach.  Nor  is  the  likelihood  of  discriminatory
enforcement  which  appellants  foresee  (Br.  114-15)  more
than  speculation.  In  determining  the  scope  of  permissible
federal  regulation,  the  courts  have  had  no  trouble  dis-
tinguishing  between  discussions  of  general  political  is-
sues  and  partisan  electoral  campaigning.  See,  e.g.,  ACLU,
Inc.  v.  Jennings,  supra;  United  States  v.  National  Com-
mittee  for  Impeachment,  469  F.2d  1135  (2d  Cir.  1972) ;
United  States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.  106  (1948);  Pipefitters
Union  v.  United  States,  407  U.S.  385  (1972)  ; Civil  Serv-
ice  Commission  Vv.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548  (1973).

179  Appellants  quote  Professor  Bickel  as  saying  that  a  provision
such  as  Section  608(e)  is  “flatly  unconstitutional.”  Appellants’  Br.
112.  Professor  Bickel  said  no  such  thing.  The  quoted  remark
refers  to  a  provision,  like  old  Title  I of  the  FECA,  designed  “to  put
the  candidate  in  control,  make  him  accountable  for,  and  allow  him
to  control,  the  expenditures  that  are  made  in  his  behalf.”  Bickel,
supra  note  31,  at  65-66.  Professor  Bickel  went  on  to  say  that  a
law  limiting  contributions  would  be  “both  wise  and  lawful,”  but  did
not  express  any  view  on  an  independent  expenditure  limit  such  as
Section  608(e).  7d.

A.  Disclosure  Serves  a  Compelling  Public  Need  and

Promotes  First  Amendment  Values

1.  Disclosure  Deters  Political  Corruption  and  Re-
duces  the  Appearance  of  Such  Corruption

Congress  was  aware  that  pernicious  abuses  associated
with  campaign  financing  have  plagued  the  electoral  pro-
cess.  Corruption  of public  officers—and  the  appearance  of
such  corruption—have  seriously  weakened  public  confi-
dence  in  the  integrity  of  the  Government.  There  can  be
little  doubt  that  Congress  and  the  district  court  correctly
concluded  that  secrecy  in  campaign  contributions  and  ex-
penditures  fosters  these  abuses.  Findings  I,  $135,  IIA
J.A.  190.

The  following  testimony  before  the  Senate  about  the
execution  of  an  illegal  corporate  contribution  depicts  the
predilection  for  secrecy:

“Mr.  Dash.
Were  you  told  anything  concerning
whether  or  not  that  date,  April  6,  was  relevant,  why
the  contribution  had  to  be  made  then?
“Mr.  Clark  [of  American  Shipbuilding  Corporation}.
Yes;  the  intent  of  writing  the  check  on  April  6  was
because  of  the  disclosure  law  going  into  effect  on
April  7.
“Mr.  Dash.  So  that  having  given  the  contribution
on  April  6  would  mean  that  it  did  not  have  to  be
disclosed?

“Mr.  Clark.

Yes.”

An  official  of  Ashland  Oil  gave  similar  testimony  con-
cerning  his  company’s  illegal  $100,000  corporate  contri-
bution:

—

Furthermore,  the  district  court  has  found  that  persons
who  contribute,  solicit,  receive,  and  expend  campaign
contributions  with  improper  motives  have  preferred  to  do
so  secretly;  that  a  substantial  percentage  of  the  illegal
corporate  campaign  contributions  described  in  Part  III,
supra,  were  made  prior  to  April  7, 1972,  the  effective  date
of  the  disclosure  provisions  of  the  FECA  in  1971;  and
that  a  substantial  number  of  the  largest  contributions
from  individuals  as  well  as  organizations  were  made
prior  to  April  7.’**

Secrecy  encourages  impropriety  not  only  in  the  raising
of  campaign  contributions,  but  also  in  campaign  spend-
ing.  Donors  wishing  to  conceal  their  identity  often  make
their  contributions  in  cash.  The  Senate  Select  Committee

18?  Hearings  Before  the  Senate  Select  Comm.,  supra  note  41,  at

5441;  see  id.  at  5336,  5448.

1**  Findings  I,  9  126,  127,  146,  IIA  J.A.  156,  203;  see  id.  I,
€ 14,  IIA  J.A.  203-05;  id.  IIA,  § 119,  IIA  J.A.  508;  Final  Report,
supra  note  38, at 445.

165

on  Presidential  Campaign  Activities  found  that  the  “Wa-
tergate  break-in”  and  certain  campaign  “dirty  tricks”
undertaken  by  Donald  Segretti  were  funded  entirely  by
such  secret  campaign  contributions."  Only  secret  cam-
paign  contributions  were  used  to  finance  the  “Watergate
coverup  conspiracy.”  *”  A  secret  $5,000  contribution  to
the  Nixon  campaign  was  diverted  by  a  member  of  the
White  House  staff  to  pay  for  the  illegal  entry  into  the
offices  of  Daniel  Ellsberg’s  psychiatrist."  Fifty  thousand
dollars  in  secret  cash  contributions  were  used  falsely
and  illegally  to  inflate  the  proceeds  of  a  campaign  din-
ner.’**

In  view  of  the  nexus  between  secrecy  and  campaign
corruption,  the  congressional  conclusion  that  disclosure
is  a  reasonable  means  of  achieving  reform  can  hardiy  be
assailed.  In  the  famous  phrase  of  Mr.  Justice  Brandeis,

“Publicity  is  justly  commended  as  a  remedy  for
social  and  industrial  diseases.  Sunlight  is  said  to  be
the  best  of  disinfectants;  electric  light  the  most  effi-
cient  policeman.”  Other  People’s  Money  92  (1914).

Campaign  contribution  disclosure  laws  are  hardly  new.
This  Court  has  long  since  upheld  them:

“Congress  reached  the  conclusion  that  public  dis-
closure  of  political  contributions,  together  with  the
names  of  contributors  and  other  details,  would  tend

Disclosure  has  many  beneficial  effects.  As  the  district
court  found,  disclosure  will  deter  illegal  contributions  and
other  contributions  considered  corrupt  by the  community.
Findings  I, 17  125,  135,  IIA  J.A.  156,  190.  Public  of-
ficials  will  shrink  from  affording  special  benefits  to  cam-
paign  contributors  where  the  electorate  knows  or  has  the
means  of  obtaining  information  about  the  contributor’s
largesse.  Disclosure  also  helps  in  enforcing  related  laws
frequently  violated  during  the  conduct  of  political  cam-
paigns.  As  the  district  court  found,  discovery  of the  per-
sons  responsible  for  the  Watergate  break-in  was  facili-
tated  by  tracing  the  campaign  contributions  used  to  fin-
ance  the  operation.  Findings  I,  {  128,  IIA  J.A.  156;  see
Ct.  App.  Op.  1544-45.  Judicially  compelied  disclosure  of
previously  secret  lists  of  pre-April  7, 1972  campaign  con-
tributions  enormously  facilitated  the  investigations  of
the  Watergate  Special  Prosecution  Force  and  resulted  in
a  large  number  of  guilty  pleas  and  convictions  for  mak-
ing  and  seeking  illegal  contributions.  Watergate  Special
Prosecution  Force  Report  71-72  (Oct.,  1975).  The  record-
keeping  and  disclosure  provisions  are  also  essential  to
the  enforcement  of  many  other  parts  of  the  challenged
It  would  be  impossible  to  determine  whether
statutes.
committees,  candidates,  and  contributors  were  adhering
to  other  provisions  of  these  statutes  without  the  require-
ment  that  records  of  certain  receipts  and  expenditures
be  maintained  and  reported  periodically.

188  Disclosure  alone,  however,  is  not  sufficient  to  achieve  these

results.  See  pp.  103-05,  supra.

ent em

—

2.  Disclosure  Promotes  First  Amendment  Values
by Informing  the Electorate

The  periodic  pre-election  reporting  of  campaign  con-
tributions  and  expenditures  prescribed  by  these  laws  as-
sists  voters  to  make  informed  assessments  of  candidates
before  they  vote.  Thus,  as  the  district  court  found,  dis-
closure  of the  sources  of  campaign  contributions  improves
some  voters’  ability  to  evaluate  the  candidates.  Findings
I,  § 134,  IIA  J.A.  190.  By improving  the  flow  of informa-
tion  to  the  electorate,  disclosure  provisions  facilitate  the
intelligent  exercise  of  the  foremost  among  constitutional
freedoms—the  right  to  vote—and  thereby  promote  one  of
the  central  purposes  of  the  First  Amendment.’  More
broadly,  informed  public  opinion  is  a  precondition  to  ef-
fective  citizen  participation  in  and  control  of government.
As  Mr.  Justice  Stewart  has  observed,  “an  informed  and
critical  public  opinion  .
.  protect  the
values  of  democratic  government.”  *  The  dissemination
of  campaign  finance  information—which  is  indisputably
relevant  to  the  conduct  of  public  affairs—thus  nourishes
the  cardinal  First  Amendment  goal  of  “producing  an  in-
formed  public  capable  of  conducting  its  own  affairs.”
Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.  FCC,  395  U.S.  367,  392
(1969).  Hence,  disclosure  sustains  active,  alert,  and  re-
sponsible  citizenship.  See  United  States  v.  UAW,  352
U.S.  567,  575  (1957).

.  alone  can  .

.

.

exposing  to  public  scrutiny  the  expenditures  of  political
advocates.  It  has  upheld  against  First  Amendment  at-

tack  a  disclosure  statute  much  like  the  FECA—the  Fed-
eral  Reeulation  of  Lobbying  Act.  United  States  v.  Har-
riss,  347  U.S.  612  (1954).  The  Court  reasoned  that  the
American  ideal  of  government  by  elected  representatives
depends  on  the  representatives’  ability  to  evaluate  com-
peting  choices,  and  that  information  about  the  employers
of  lobbyists  helps  Congress  properly  evaluate  the  posi-
tions  they  advance.  Jd.  at  625.  The  American  ideal  of
government  no  less  depends  on  the  electorate’s  ability  to
evaluate  candidates  who  seek  to  represent  them,  and  such
information  is no  less  valuable  in  helping  the  public  make
its  choices.  See  Burroughs,  supra.

3.  Disclosure  Promotes  First  Amendment  Values
by  Assisting  the  Press  to  Perform  Its  Consti-
tutional  Role

ee “a en =

The  disclosure  requirements  assist  the  press  in  perform-
ing  its  vital  First  Amendment  functions—the  “organized,
expert  scrutiny  of  government,”  and  the  dissemination
of  information  to  the  public  about  its  government.”
The  press  plays  an  indispensable  role  in  fostering  the
flow  of  information  critical  to  maintaining  an  informed
.  have  shed  and  continue  to
public.  “{NJewspapers  .
.  affairs  of  the  nation
shed,  more  light  on  the  public  .
than  any  other  instrumentality  of  publicity...  .”  Gros-
jean  v.  American  Press  Co.,  297  U.S.  238,  250  (1936).
In  particular,  the  press  plays  a  crucial  and  unique  role
in exposing  government  misconduct.  As  Mr.  Justice  Black
has  said,  “Only  a  free  and  unrestrained  press  can  ef-
fectively  expose  deception  in  government.”  *”

.

.

1%  “Or  of  the  Press,”  Address  of  Potter  Stewart,  supra  p.  93.

1"t  New  York  Times  Co.  vy.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713,  717

(1971)  (concurring  opinion).

The  press  has  applied  its  “expert  scrutiny”  to  cam-
paign  finance  disclosure  reports  with  salutary  results.
The  Secretary  of  State  of  Florida  has  said  about  the
Florida  disclosure  statute:

“I  do  not  wish  to  exaggerate,  but  I  frequently  had
to  shove  reporters  out  of  the  way  before  I could  get
the  papers  [campaign  treasurer’s  pre-election
re-
port]  filed  .
.  because  they  [the  reporters]  would
grab  them...  ."°”

.

B.  Even  If  the  Disclosure  Provisions  Had  Some  Inci-
dental  Effect  on  First  Amendment  Rights,  They
Would  Nevertheless  Be  a  Constitutional  Exercise
of  Congress’  Power  To  Regulate  in  This  Area
We  fully  recognize  the  scope  of  First  Amendment  pro-
tection  of  freedom  of  association  for  the  advancement  of
beliefs  and  ideas.  We  do  not  gainsay  that,  at  least  in
theory,  the  requirement  of  public  disclosure  of  associa-
tions  may  dampen  an  individual’s  freedom  to  associate
and  to  express  his  views  through  organizational  ties.

However,  as  discussed  more  fully  in  Part  IV,  supra,
this  Court  has  recognized  that  a  process  of  constitutional
accommodation  is  necessary  and  appropriate  where—as
here—countervailing  interests  compete  with  First  Amend-
ment  values.
The  need  for  accommodation  is  acutely
present  here  because  important  First  Amendment  values
lie  on  both  sides  of the  issue.

Judicial  decisions  involving  similar  laws  have  uni-
formly  upheld  their  constitutionality.
In  Burroughs  v.
United  States,  290  U.S.  534  (1934),  this  Court  upheld
in  sweeping  terms  the  similar  disclosure  provisions  of

**Roady,  Ten  Years  of  Florida’s  “Who  Gave  It—Who  Got  It”

Law,  27  Law  &  Contemp.  Prob.  434,  438-39  (1962).

170

171

the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act  of  1925.  The  Court  in
Burroughs  did  not  have  occasion  to  address  First  Amend-
The  Court’s  reasoning,  however,  fore-
ment  questions.
shadowed  First  Amendment  analysis,
for  the  Court
first  identified  the  compelling  public  need  for  the  legisia-
tion  and  then  concluded  that  disclosure  was  an  appropri-
ate  means  of  achieving  that  end.  See  Stoner  v.  Fortson,
379  F.  Supp.  704,  713-14  (N.D.  Ga.  1974)  (three-judge
In  any  case,  Burroughs  at  the  very  least  fore-
court).
closes  further  dispute  about  the  need  for  and  utility  of

ing  data.  Pichler  v.  Jennings,  347  F.  Supp.  1061,  1068
(S.D.N.Y.  1972).

When  the  Court  upheld  the  Federal  Regulation  of
Lobbying  Act  against  First  Amendment  attack  in  United
States  v.  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612  (1954),  it  relied  on  and
approved  Burroughs:

.

.  merely  pro-
“Toward  that  end,  Congress  has  .
vided  for  a  modicum  of  information  from  those  who
for  hire  attempt  to  influence  legislation  or  who  col-
lect  or  spend  funds  for  that  purpose.  It  wants  only
to  know  who  is  being  hired,  who  is  putting  up  the
money,  and  how  much.  It  acted  in  the  same  spirit
and  for  a  similar  purpose  in  passing  the  Federal
Corrupt  Practices  Act—to  maintain  the  integrity  of
a  basic  governmental  process.”  Jd.  at  625.”

In  Harriss,  the  Court  rejected  the  argument  that  dis-
closure  will  deter  political  activity,  in  terms  equally  ap-
plicable  to  appellants’  contentions  in  this  case:

‘8°  See  Communist  Party  v.  Subversive  Activities  Control  Bd.,
367  U.S.  1,  97-99  (1961);  id.  at  173-74  (Douglas  J.,  dissenting:
“Criminal  sanctions  for  failure  to  report  and  to  disclose  all  con-
tributions  made  to  political  parties  are  permitted,”  citing  Bur-
roughs).

“But,  even  assuming  some  such  deterrent  effect,  the
restraint  is  at  most  an  indirect  one  resulting  from
self-censorship,  comparable  in  many  ways  to  the  re-
straint  resulting  from  criminal  libel  laws.  The  haz-
ard  of  such  restraint  is  too  remote  to  require  strik-
ing  down  a  statute  which  on  its  face  is  otherwise
plainly  within  the  area  of  congressional  power  and
is  designed  to  safeguard  a  vital  national  interest.”
Id.  at  626  (footnote  omitted) .*”

The  lower  courts  have  consistently  upheld  the  constitu-
Ct.
tionality  of  campaign  finance  disclosure  statutes.
App.  Op.  1541-49;  United  States  v.  Finance  Committee
to  Re-Elect  the  President,  507  F.2d  1194,  1200  (D.C.
Cir.  1974)  (upholding  the  similar  original  FECA  dis-
closure  provisions:  “We  reject  the  attack  on  this  statute
on  First  Amendment  grounds  on  the  present  record—
in  the  context  of  Burroughs  &  Cannon  and  Harriss,  and
in  the  absence  of  a  foundation  in  fact,  as  contrasted
with  speculation”) ; Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379  F.  Supp.  704,
(upholding
713  (N.D.  Ga.  1974)
“This  court  has  no  doubt
Georgia  disclosure  statute:
that  the  disclosures  sought  bear  a  substantial  connection
to  preventing  corruption  in  politics,  to  preventing  influ-
