200  This  Court  has  sustained  a variety  of other  disclosure  require-
ments  against  First  Amendment  attacks.  In  Lewis  Publishing  Co.
v.  Morgan,  229  U.S.  288  (1913),  it upheld  a  statute  requiring  news-
papers  using  the  mails  to  publish  certain  information  about  their
management  and  ownership.  In  Viereck  v.  United  States,  318  U.S.
236  (1943),  the  disclosure  provisions  of  the  Foreign  Agents  Reg-
istration  Act  withstood  attack.  See  also  id.  at  251  (Black,  J.,
dissenting);  Communist  Party  v.  Subversive  Activities  Control
Bd.,  supra  note  199;  New  York  ex  rel.  Bryant  v.  Zimmerman,
278  U.S.  63  (1928).

These  cases  would  establish  the  facial  validity  of  the
challenged  disclosure  provisions  even  if  appellants  had
shown  some  modicum  of  harassment  or  “chilling  effect.”
On  the  present  record  they  are  conclusive,  for  appellants
have  utterly  failed  to  establish  a  factual  foundation  for
In
their  claim  of  “chilling  effect.”
this  Court,  instead  of  urging  the  same  factual  claims
rejected  below,  appellants  now  offer  only  “clearly  articu-
lated  fears’”—carefully  phrased  in  the  subjunctive—as  to
the  effect  the  statute  “can,”  “would,”  or  “might”  have.
Appellants’  Br.  172-73,  178.

Ct.  App.  Op.  1546.

Appellants  seek  to  justify  their  failure  of  proof  by
stressing  the  evanescent  quality  of  evidence  of  harass-
ment  or  deterrence.  By  so  doing,  they  ask  in  effect  for
a  rule  of  per  se  unconstitutionality  wherever  disclosure
of  association  is  required.
Adoption  of  any  such  per
se  rule  would  radically  conflict  with  this  Court’s  wise
and  cautious  development  of  the  law  on  disclosure,  Bur-
roughs,  supra;  NAACP  v.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958),
and  with  First  Amendment  doctrine  in  general,  see
e.g.,  Near  Vv.  Minnesota,  283  U.S.  697  (1931);  Branden-
burg  v.  Ohio,  395  U.S.  444  (1969).

On  the  present  record,  NAACP  vy.  Alabama,  supra,  and
related  cases  are  inapposite.**
In  NAACP,  there  was  a
proven  threat  of  direct  and  immediate  retaliation.**  As
the  court  of  appeals  said:

cion,  and  other  manifestations  of  public  hostility.”

We  accept  Judge  Bazelon’s  statement  below  that  “[t]jhe
Court  has  never  varied  the  rigor  of  its  scrutiny  depend-
ing  on  the  presence  or  absence  of  a  record  of  harassment
Ct.  App.  Op.  1596  (footnote  omitted);  Ap-
and  chill.”
pellants’  Br.  177.  We  agree  that  “compelling  interest”

added);  Bates  v.  City  of  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516  (1960);  see

Similarly  inapposite  are  those  cases  (1)  where  the  state  had
advanced  no  compelling  interest  for  the  required  disclosure:  Gibson
v.  Florida  Legislative  Comm.,  372  U.S.  539,  557  (1963)  ; NAACP  v.
Button,  371  U.S.  415,  444  (1963);  Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.

ment  of  the  state’s  interest  and  disclosure  of  the  information:
Bates  v.  City  of  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  at  525;  Shelton  v.  Tucker,
364  U.S.  479  (1960).  These  problems  neither  inhere  in  nor  result
from  the  challenged  disclosure  provisions.

-  C.  The  Threshold  Amounts  for  Disclosure  Are  Not

Unconstitutionally  Overbroad

Appellants  attack  the  disclosure  provisions  on  the  theory
that  the  disclosure  of  some  contributions  greater  than
$100  but  less  than  some  unidentified  amount  is  unneces-
sary  to  deter  political  corruption.  Appellants’  Br.  184-
Appellants  further  suggest  that  these  threshold
89.°
levels  are  arbitrary  because  they  are  not  calibrated  for
Id.  at  188-89.  Apparently,  ap-
different  elective  offices.
pellants  contend  that  Congress  was  constitutionally  re-
quired  to  legislate  an  assumption  that  Representatives
can  be  improperly  influenced  more  cheaply  than  Sena-
tors,  and  Senators  more  cheaply  than  Presidents.

In  focusing  solely  on  the  anti-corruption  values  served
by  disclosure,  appellants  wholly  ignore  the  informational
role  disclosure  plays.  Apart  from  the  significance  of  a
contribution  in  excess  of  $100  by itself,  the  disclosure  of
many  such  contributions  from  common  or  related  sources
can  identify  the  nature  of  a  candidate’s  financial  “con-
stituency.”  The  possibility  that  such  pooling  can  produce
significant  aggregate  contributions  is  illustrated  by  the
reports  of  one  Senator,  which  disclosed  that  he  had  re-
ceived  on  one  day  247  individual  contributions  from  one
corporation’s  employees—totalling  $28,025—for  his  re-
election  campaign.*”

Appellants’  theory  that  the  thresholds  require  disclos-
ure  of  contributions  too  low  to  have  any  relation  to

20¢The  United  States  Amicus  Brief

incorrectly
states  that  disclosure  is  required  for  contributions  of  $100.
In
fact,  disclosure  is  required  only  for  contributions  “in  excess  of
$100.”  2  U.S.C.  § 434(b)  (2).

(at  86-87)

public  corruption  has  been  rejected  by  the  courts.
In
Stoner  v.  Fortson,  supra,  the  three-judge  court  recog-
nized  that:

“(A]  cut-off  point  had  to  be  established  somewhere.
.  ..  This  court  views  the  $101  or  more  threshold  as
a  rational  attempt  ..  .  to  establish  a  level  of  contri-
bution  that  will  attract  the  attention  of the  candidate
and  thus  begin  to  satisfy  the  goals  of  the  statute.”
379  F.  Supp.  at  712-3  (citations  omitted) .°”

Appellants  present  no  new  factual  basis  for  this  Court
to  hold  that  the  threshold  chosen  by  Congress  is  so  un-
reasonable  as  to  be  unconstitutional.
In  United  States
Vv.  Finance  Committee,  supra,  the  court  rejected  an  argu-
ment  similar  to  appellants’  in  precisely  these  circum-
stances:

“Appellant  says  the  allowance  of  secrecy  only  to
gifts  of  $100  and  under  would  chill  many  campaign
gifts  [that]  present  no  conceivable  menace  to  the  in-
tegrity  of  the  electoral  process,  presumably  those
over  $10(  and  under  some  other  unknown  figure.  In
the  absence  of  data,  we  are  in  no  position  to  make
a  merely  speculative  determination  as  to  the  degree
of  chill  to  be  expected  from  publicity  on  donations
of  any  particular  amount,  nor  as  to  how  large  a
gift  is  necessary  to  undermine  the  integrity  of  a
candidate.  The  $100  figure  is,  it would  appear,  high
enough  for  a  political  campaign  to  enjoy  substantial
financial  support  from  secret  gifts  alone.”  507  F.2d
at  1200.

206  The  court  in  Stoner  supported  its  conclusion  by  reference  to
the  thresholds  of  the  FECA  of  1971  (in  excess  of  $100)  and  FCPA
of 1925  ($100),  and  to  court  rules  that  require  federal  judges  to
disclose  gifts  over  $100.  Jd.  at  712-13.  It  is  noteworthy  that  Con-
gress  chose  to  permit  tax  deductions  for  political  contributions  up
to  $100.  26  U.S.C.  § 218(b).

Congress’  factual  and  practical  judgment  in  deciding
where  the  line  should  be  drawn  is  entitled  to  great
respect.  Burroughs  v.  United  States,  supra,  290  U.S.
at  548;  United  States  v.  Finance  Committee,  supra,  507
F.2d  at  1200;  Stoner  v.  Fortson,  supra,  379  F.  Supp.
at  712-18;  see  Pell  v.  Procunier,  417  U.S.  817,  827
(1974).  As  was  said  in  upholding  the  $100  disclosure
threshold  in  Burroughs:

“Tf  it can  be  seen  that  the  means  adopted  are  really
calculated  to  attain  the  end,  the  degree  of  their
necessity,  the  extent  to  which  they  conduce  to  the
end,  the  closeness  of  the  relationship  between  the
means  adopted  and  the  end  to  be  obtained,  are  mat-

ters  for  congressional  determination  alone.”

Independent  Candidates

Appellants  contend  that  the  challenged  disclosure  pro-
visions  are  unconstitutionally  overbroad  as  applied  to
Appellants’
minor  party  and  independent  candidates.
Appellants’  premise  for  this  argument  is

that  the  “present  access”  of  such  candidates  to  “govern-
mental  power  is normally  remote”—they  have  little  chance

Appellants  are  again  myopic  in  focusing  exclusively
on  one  of  the  purposes  served  by  disclosure—the  pre-
Minor  party  and  independent
vention  of  corruption.
candidates—like  major  party  candidates—hold  themselves

no  constitutional  reason  why  voters  should  have  to  as-
sess  some  candidates  in  greater  darkness  than  others
merely  because  the  one  candidate’s  established  following
is not  yet  numerous.

might  break  off  from  the  Republican  Party  to  form  a
new  “More  Republican  Party”  (“MRP”).
Since  the
MRP  has  little  established  following,  it  would  be  a  new
party  under  the  statute  and  under  appellants’  test.
It
would—if  appellants’  arguments  are  accepted—be  per-
mitted  to  raise  and  expend  funds  in  total  secrecy,  and
it might  raise  $1 million  each  from  30  millionaires.  Such
secrecy  would  subvert  the  enforceability  of  the  statute’s
Indeed,  if  appellants’  full  argument
other  provisions.
—that  contribution  and  expenditure  limits  should  not
be  applied  to  minor  party  and  independent  candidates—
is  accepted,  the  MRP  could  raise  and  expend  much  more
than  the  $36  million  limit  imposed  on  other  presidential
candidates.  The  result  would  be  a  non-fringe  party  with
potential  “present  access  to  governmental  power”  that
could  operate  wholly  in  disregard  of  the  challenged
statutes,  and  therefore  at  a  great  advantage  over  the
major  parties.

Similarly,  the  applicability  of  the  challenged  statutes
to  Governor  Wallace’s  campaign  would  depend  under
appellants’  theory  on  whether  or  not  Governor  Wallace
decides  to  run  as  a  Democrat—a  choice  he  has  not  yet
made.  During  any  period  before  or  after  running  as  a
Democrat,  Governor  Wallace  could  amass  and  spend
a  campaign  war  chest  unaffected  by  the  campaign  fi-
nance  laws  applicable  to  the  activities  of  other  serious

——- a

candidates—simply  by  disassociating  himself  from  the
Democratic  Party.

These  examples  suggest  the  problems  inherent  in Judge
Bazelon’s  attempt  to  distinguish  Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379
F.  Supp.  704  (N.D.  Ga.  1974),  as  a  case  involving  a
“controversial”  major  party  candidate  rather  than  a
“minor  party”  candidate.
Ct.  App.  Op.  1596  n.28.
Surely,  the  application  of  First  Amendment  principles
cannot  be  made  to  turn  on  the  name  of  the  party  under
whose  banner  the  controversial  candidate  chooses  to  cam-
paign.

Appellants  concentrate  their  argument  on  the  im-
probability  of  victory  for  a  minor  party  or  independent
candidate.  Appellant  Buckley  and  Amicus  Curiae  Long-
ley  graphically  demonstrate  that  minor  party  and  in-
dependent  candidates  sometimes  win  elections.
In  any
event,  appellants  ignore  the  very  practical  effects  non-
major  parties  have  on  the  electoral  process—even  when
they  do  not  win.  Numerous  examples  need  not  be  cited
to  demonstrate  the  increasing  frequency  with  which
third  party  and  independent  candidates  have  played  a
“balance  of  power”  role  in  an  election,  or  where  a  third
party  candidate  has  siphoned  off  sufficient  votes  to  affect
the  outcome  of  an  election  or  the  candidates’  positions
on  issues  *°-—roles  whose  importance  appellants  elsewhere
stress  heavily.
The  voters
are  entitled  to know  who  fuels  such  campaigns.

Appellants’  Br.  120-123.%°

2  It  is  the  stated  intent  of  appellant  Conservative  Party  of  the
State  of  New  York  to  demonstrate  its  capacity  to  take  votes  away
from  major  party  candidates  in  order  to  influence  their  policies.
Deposition  Tr.  at  47-51.

220  The  “stalking-horse”  threat  is  a  real  danger.  During  Presi-
dent  Nixon’s  1972  campaign,  the  Committee  for  the  Re-Election
of  the  President  considered  the  secret  funding  of  La  Raza  Unida

.

:
/

;
.

180

181

Appellants  thus  prove  nothing  with  their  asserted
paradox  that  the  disclosure  provisions  (and  giving  and
spending  limits)  apply  to  minor  parties  on  the  premise
that  they  can  win,  while  the  distribution  formula  for
general  election  public  financing  is  premised  on  their
inability  to  win.  The  application  of  the  disclosure  pro-
visions  (and  the  contribution  and  expenditure  limits)
to  minor  parties  is  premised  not  on  their  probability  of
victory,  but  on  the  practical  reality  that  they  seek  and
often  do  affect  the  electoral  process;  consistent  with
this  premise,  their  eligibility  for  public  financing  is
properly  gauged  on  the  degree  of  their  probable  effect
on  that  process.

Appellants’  suggestion  that  only  winning  minor  party
or  independent  candidates  should  be  required  to  disclose
their  contributions  would  prevent  disclosure  from  play-
ing  any  pre-election  role.
Post-election  disclosure  can-
not  provide  voters  with  information  relevant  to  the  ex-
ercise  of  the  franchise—a  role  appellants  recognize  as

in  an  attempt  to  draw  the  vote  of  Spanish-speaking  Americans
away  from  the  Democratic  presidential  candidate  in  several  key
states.  Hearings  Before  the  Senate  Select  Comm.,  supra  note  41,  at
5533-34,  5677-80.  In  1970,  aides  to  President  Nixon  attempted  to
destroy  the  viability  of  Governor  Wallace  as  a  third-party  presi-
dential  candidate  in  1972  by  funneling  $400,000  in  secret  funds  to
his opponent  in the  1970  primary  election  for  the  Democratic  Party’s
gubernatorial  nomination.  Testimony  of  Herbert-W.  Kalmbach,  id.
at  2144-45.  A  1973  investigation  by  the  Secretary  of  State  of
California  revealed  that  during  1972,  California  Republican  organ-
izations  secretly  paid  the  filing  fees  and  contributed  other  cam-
paign  funds  to  thirteen  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  candidates  in
key  state  assembly  races.  Sacramento  Bee,  July  4,  19,  1973.  As  a
result  of  the  investigation,  the  secret  contributions  and  expendi-
tures  were  set  forth  in  amended  campaign  statements  by  several
Peace  and  Freedom  candidates.  See  Socialist  Workers  1974  Cali-
fornia  Campaign  Committee  v.  Brown,  2d  Civ.  No.  44922  (Cal.  Ct.
App.,  filed  Sept.  9,  1974),  Respondents’  Memorandum  in  Opposition
to  Petition  for  Writ  of  Prohibition  32.

important.
Appellants’  Br.  171.  Nor  can  post-election
disclosure  effectively  deter  corrupt  campaign  practices
or  aid  in  the  enforcement  of  the  other  provisions  of
the  challenged  statutes.

Similarly,  no  judicially  administrable  line  can  be drawn
to  effectuate  appellants’  suggestion  (Br.  182-83),  echoed
by some  amici,  that  Congress  segregate  certain  candidate-
supporting  minor  parties  for  treatment  as  issue-oriented
rather  than  candidate-oriented  groups.  Ct.  App.  Op.  1549-
60;  see  United  States  v.  National  Committee  for Impeach-
ment,  469  F.2d  1135  (2d  Cir.  1972).  Who  can  decide
whether  appellant  McCarthy  is seeking  election  or  merely
the  dissemination  of his  political  philosophy?

In  sum,  the  application  of  the  challenged  disclosure
provisions  to  all  candidate-supporting  parties  is  neither
indiscriminate  nor  overbroad.  The  statute  is  neutral  in
language  and  application;  it  is  not  aimed  at  and  does
not  discriminate  against  any  particular  group  because
of  the  content  of  its  speech  or  the  composition  of  its
members.  It  applies  equally  to  all.  Civil  Service  Com-
mission  V.  Letter  Carriers,  413  U.S.  548,  564  (1973).
Appellants  have  wholly  failed  to  prove  any  substantial
“chilling  effect.”  If  a  minor  party  or  independent  candi-
date  can  prove  harm  from  the  application  of  the  dis-
closure  statutes,  the  Federal  Election  Commission  and
ultimately  this  Court  sit  viligant  to  protect  his  rights.

VII.  APPELLANTS  HAVE  FAILED  TO  SHOW  THAT
THE  CHALLENGED  PUBLIC  FINANCING  PRO-
VISIONS  DENY  THEIR  FIRST  OR  FIFTH  AMEND-
MENT  RIGHTS

ee Yee ee
wore

To  meet  the  compelling  need  to  improve  the  electoral
process,  Congress  decided  on  a  combination  of  related

182

188

remedies.  Public  financing  of  presidential  election  cam-
In
paigns  is  the  most  innovative  of  those  remedies.
adopting  these  provisions,  Congress  took  the  first  step
toward  ending  the  dependence  of  candidates  for  public
office  on  private  wealth.*”  Someone  must  pay  the  cost  of
election  campaigns;  as  Senate  Majority  Leader  Mans-
field  said:

“Shall  the  piper  be  the  whole  of the  American  people
through  a  system  of  public  participation  in  cam-
paign  financing?  Or  shall  the  tune  be  called  more
and  more  in  the  beat  of  special  interests  and  great
wealth?”  113  Cong.  Rec.  12165  (1967).

Appellants  mount  a  wholesale  attack  on  this  reform.
They  claim  that  the  Constitution  precludes  Congress
from  adopting  any  type  of  public  financing  for  presi-
dential  elections.  Appellants’  Br.  145-52.  They  also  con-
tend  that  the  particular  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  abridge
their  freedom  of  speech  and  discriminate  invidiously
against  them.  Jd.  at  152-69.  Both  attacks  are  unfounded.

A.  The  Statutory  Provisions

These  laws  contain  two  groups  of  provisions  for  the
financing  of  presidential  election  campaigns.
Chapter
95  of  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  estab-
(the
lishes

Election  Campaign  Fund

a  Presidential

211  A number  of  foreign  countries  have  already  taken  such  steps.
See,  e.g.,  Casper,  Williams  v.  Rhodes  and  Public  Financing  of  Po-
litical  Parties  under  the  American  and  German  Constitutions,  1969
Sup.  Ct.  Rev.  271,  288-302  (German  law);  D.  Adamany  & G.  Agree,
supra  note  48,  ch.  9.  Conditions  more  or  less  unique  to  American
politics  make  it difficult  to  generalize  from  the  foreign  experience.
See,  e.g.,  id.  at  181.

For  examples  of  state  public  financing  laws,  see  American  Party
v.  White,  415  U.S.  767,  791-92  (1974)  (Texas  law);  statutes
collected  in  Developments  in  the  Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.
1111,  1266  &  n.176  (1975).

“Fund”)  in  the  Treasury,  made  up  of  amounts  desig-
nated  by  individual  taxpayers  on  their  income  tax  re-
turns  under  the  voluntary  $1  tax  checkoff  provision,  26
U.S.C.  § 6096.  Payments  will  be  made  from  the  Fund
to  finance  the  general  election  campaigns  of  eligible
presidential  candidates  as  well  as  party  conventions  at
which  the  candidates  are  nominated.  Chapter  96  of  the
same  subtitle  authorizes  federal  matching  payments  from
the  Fund  to  help  finance  presidential  primary  cam-
paigns.

Chapter  95  provides  that  the  candidates  of  major
parties  (parties  whose  candidates  received  25  percent
or  more  of  the  popular  vote  in  the  previous  presidential
election)  are  entitled  to  equal  payments  from  the  Fund
The  payments
for  their  general  election  campaigns.
to  each  major  party  candidate  may  not  exceed  the
expenditure  ceiling  for  presidential  candidates  under
Section  608(c)(1)(B)  of  Title  18  ($20  million,  plus
an  adjustment  for  inflation).
&§ 9002(6),
9004 (a) (1).

26  U.S.C.

The  candidate  of  a  minor  party  (a  party  whose  can-
didate  received  5  to  25  percent  of  the  vote  in  the  pre
ceding  presidential  election)  is  entitled  to  the  proportion
of  $20  million  that  its  candidate’s  vote  in  the  preceding
presidential  election  bore  to  the  average  of  the  votes
received  by  the  candidates  of  the  major  parties.
Id.
§§ 9002(7),  9004(a)  (2).  In  addition,  if  a  minor  party
candidate  performs  better  in  the  current  election  than
his  party’s  candidate  did  in  the  preceding  election,  he
may  receive  an  additional  payment  after  the  election  up
to  the  proportion  that  his  vote  bears  to  the  average  of
the  votes  received  by the  major  party  candidates  in  the
current  election.  Id.  § 9004(a)  (3).

184

185

The  candidate  of  a  new  party  (any  party  that  is
neither  a  major  nor  a  minor  party)  will  receive  no
payments  before  the  election.  However,  if  he  wins  five
percent  or  more  of  the  vote,  he  is  entitled  to  receive  a
payment  after  the  election  according  to  the  same  formula
applicable  to  minor  parties.  Id.  §§ 9002(8),  9004(a)  (3).

A  candidate  of  a  minor  or  new  party  must  qualify
to  be  on  the  ballot  in  ten  or  more  states  in  order  to
receive  assistance.  Jd.  § 9002(2)  (B).

A  major  party  candidate  who  accepts  public  financ-
ing  may  not  accept  private  contributions  that  would
cause  him  to  exceed  the  $20  million  expenditure  ceiling.
Other  candidates  receiving  federal  financing  remain  free
to  solicit  and  receive  private  contributions,  subject  to
Jd.
the  contribution  limits  and  the  expenditure  ceiling.
€ 9003(b)  (2),  (ce)  (2).

Chapter  95  also  authorizes  payments  from  the  Fund
to  the  national  committees  of  major  and  minor  parties
to  finance  their  presidential  nominating  conventions.  A
major  party  is  entitled  to  payments  up  to  $2  million,
adjusted  for  inflation.  A  minor  party  is  entitled  to
the  proportion  of  this  amount  that  its  vote  in  the  pre-
ceding  presidential  election  bears  to  the  average  vote  re-
ceived  by the  major  party  candidates.  Federal  payments
for  conventions  may  not  be  used  to  pay  the  expenses
of  any  candidate  or  delegate  at  a  convention,  but  only
to  pay  convention  expenses  incurred  by  the  national
committee.  Every  party  is  forbidden  to  spend  more  than
$2  million  for  its  convention,  except  that  the  FEC  may
authorize  additional  expenditures  if  necessitated  by  ex-
traordinary  and  unforeseen  circumstances.  Id.  § 9008.

Chapter  96  authorizes  payments  from  the  Fund  to
match  private  contributions  raised  by  presidential  pri-
mary  candidates,  up  to  $250  per  contribution.  Jd.  § 9034.
To  become  eligible  to  receive  matching  payments,  a
candidate  must  raise  $5,000  in  contributions  (counting
only  the  first  $250  of  contributions  from  any  individual)
in  each  of  20  states.  Id.  § 9038.  Since  private  contribu-
tions  are  matched  dollar-for-dollar  up  to  the  $250  maxi-
mum,  a  candidate  may  receive  no  more  than  50  per-
cent  of his  spending  ceiling  for  primaries  from  the  Fund.
Id.  § 9034 (b).

If  the  Secretary  of  the  Treasury  were  to  determine
that  the  amount  in  the  Fund  might  not  satisfy  the  full
entitlements  of  all  eligible  candidates,  he  would  be  re-
quired  to  withhold  from  candidates  the  amount  necessary
to  assure  that  each  candidate  receives  his  pro  rata  share
of  his  full  entitlement.
Jd.  § 9006(b),  (d).  However,
the  Vice-Chairman  of  the  FEC  recently  estimated  that
the  Fund  will  contain  $100  million  before  the  1976  elec-
tions.  He  said  that  “it  is safe  to  anticipate  that  sufficient
funds  will  be  available  to provide  all  of  the  public  financ-
ing  required  under  the  new  campaign  financing  legisla-
tion.”  **
Each  year  a  growing  number  of  taxpayers
choose  to  participate  in  financing  the  presidential  elec-
tion  by designating  $1  of  their  tax  liability  for  that  pur-
pose;  in  1975  alone,  more  than  $32  million  was  added
to  the  Fund.  Jd.  In  1976,  the  taxpayers,  by their  volun-
tary  decisions—rather  than  large  campaign  donors—will
for  the  first  time  finance  the  process  by which  the  nation
chooses  its  President.

212  See  Federal  Election  Commission  News  Release,  Oct.  15,  1975.

See  also  Findings  I, § 74,  ITA  J.A.  34.

B.  Public  Financing  of  Campaigns  Is  a  Proper  Use
of  the  Spending  Power  that  Will  Promote—Not
Restrict—First  Amendment  and  Other  Fundamental
Constitutional  Values

.

The  financing  of campaigns  for  federal  office  is a  valid
and  appropriate  use  of  public  money.  Congress  is  au-
thorized  by  the  Constitution  to  raise  and  spend  money
.  general  welfare  of  the  United
to  “provide  for  the  .
States.”  *  This  Court  has  held  that  “the  power  of  Con-
gress  to  authorize  expenditure  of  public  moneys  for
public  purposes  is  not  limited  by  the  direct  grants  of
legislative  power  found  in  the  Constitution.”  **  It  is  up
to  Congress  to  decide  what  expenditures  will  promote
the  general  welfare.

There  could  scarcely  be  a  more  legitimate  use  of public
Public  fi-
money  than  to  reform  the  electoral  process.
nancing  will  help  to  free  candidates  from  dependence  on
large  campaign  contributions,  reduce  the  fact  and  ap-
pearance  of  undue  influence  by large  contributors,  equal-
ize  the  ability  of  all  voters  to  participate  in  elections,
and  remove  wealth  or  access  to  wealth  as  a  qualification
for  election  to  office.  Further,  by  diminishing  the  pres-
sure  to  obtain  private  campaign  funds,  public  financing
will  promote  compliance  with  the  vital  disclosure  require-
ments  and  giving  and  spending  limits.*”

Federal  and  state  governments  already  pay  many  of the
expenses  of elections,  recognizing  that  without  polling  and
vote-counting  machinery  and  all  the  related  expenses  there

Federal  financing  of  campaigns  no  more  offends  the
First  Amendment  than  any  of  the  other  federal  expendi-
tures  necessary  to  make  elections  and  government  work.
Congress  wrote  these  statutes  with  care  to  avoid  coerced
expression  or  coerced  political  association.*°  The  amount
that  goes  into  the  Fund  is  determined  by  the  voluntary
In  this
designations  of  taxpayers  on  their  tax  returns.
way,  the  public  indicates  directly  how  much  public  money
should  be  used  to support  the  1976  presidential  campaigns.
Public  financing  of election  campaigns  places  no  restric-
tion  at  all  on  First  Amendment  rights.  No  candidate  is
compelled  to accept  public  financing.  Appellants  McCarthy
and  Libertarian  Party  have  already  announced  they  will
refuse  any  such  assistance.  Findings  III,  McCarthy  § 30,
IIB  J.A.  718;  Crane  Deposition  Tr.  at  32-33,  62-63.  The
opportunity  to  accept  public  financing  in  no  way  restricts
any  person’s  freedom  of  speech,  assembly,  or  petition;  in-

Appellants  cite  Opinion  of  the  Justices,  197  N.E.2d  691  (Mass.
1964),  holding  that  the  Massachusetts  constitution  prohibits  state
financing  of  political  campaigns  because  state  funds  may  be  spent
only  by  state  officers.  That  ruling  has  been  criticized  as  “dubious”
and  having  “little  inherent  logic.”  Rosenthal,  supra  note  218,  at
411  n.188;  Note,  78  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1260,  1261  (1965).  Moreover,
the  state  law  involved  was  strikingly  partisan  and  discriminatory :
Had  it  been  in  effect  for  the  1964  Massachusetts  gubernatorial
election,  the  Democrats  would  have  received  80  percent  of  the  state
funds.  Jd.  at  1261.  Finally,  the  Massachusetts  ruling  has  no  appli-
cation  to  the  federal  Constitution;  federal  programs  are  numerous
in  which  subsidies  are  given  to  persons  who  are  not  federal  officials
in  order  to  promote  public  purposes.  E.g.,  7  U.S.C.  § 1281  et  seq.
(agricultural  subsidies).

220  Compare  International  Association  of  Machinists  v.  Street,

367  U.S.  740  (1961);  Lathrop  v.  Donohue,  367  U.S.  820  (1961).

stead,  it  facilitates  political  expression  by  ensuring  that
qualified  candidates  will  have  the  resources  necessary  to
conduct  effective  campaigns.

tie es
~ Sai

C.  Appellants’  “Establishment”

Argument  Funda-
mentally  Misconceives  the  Role  of  Government  in
Fostering  Free  Expression

Appellants  lack  authority  for  the  sweeping  assertion
that  no  form  of  public  financing  of  election  campaigns  is
permissible  under  the  Constitution.  They  would  have  this
Court  fill  the  void  by  “analogy”  to  the  establishment  of
religion  clause  of  the  First  Amendment.

The  argument  begins  by  assuming  its  conclusion.  The
Government  has  not  “declared  to  the  voter  that  some
parties  are  more  worthy  of  adherence  than  their  competi-
tors.”  Appellants’  Br.  149.  No  political  party  has  be
come  “officially  sponsored  by  the  government.”
Jd.  at
145.  There  is  no  evidence  here  of  a  “scheme  to  establish
political  parties.”  Jd.  at  146  n.8,  148.  Nor  can  any  such
scheme  be  concocted  from  the  remains  of  defeated  floor
amendments  (id.  at  146  n.2)  and  easy  speculation  as  to
“a  new  formula”  that  “might  be  enacted.”  Jd.  at  150.2"
Appellants’  “establishment”  argument  must  stand  or  fall
on  the  strength  of  the  “analogy”  asserted.

The  First  Amendment  deals  with  religious  freedom  by
marking  two  boundaries:  “Congress  shall  make  no  law
respecting  an  establishment  of  religion,  or  prohibiting  the
free  exercise  thereof.”  These  clauses,  in  the  classic  analy-
sis  of  Professor  Kurland,  “should  be  read  as  stating  a
single  precept:  that  Government  cannot  utilize  religion
as  a  standard  for  action  or  inaction  because  these  clauses,

221To  the  extent  these  statements  are  simply  an  alternative
formulation  of  appellants’  “discrimination”  contentions,  we  re-
spond  in  Subparts  D-F  below.

. ee

a ON a
ede

j
& 2

4
4

190

read  together  as  they  should  be,  prohibit  classification  in
terms  of  religion  either  to  confer  a  benefit  or  to  impose  a
burden.”  Kurland,  Of Church  and  State  and  the  Supreme
Court,  29  U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  1, 96  (1961).  In  short,  the  Gov-
ernment  may  neither  favor  one  religion  over  another  nor
promote  religion  generally.

The  First  Amendment  forbids  only  “abridging”  the
freedom  of  speech  or  of  the  press;  there  is  no  speech  or
press  equivalent  of  the  establishment  clause.  The  Con-
stitution  does  not  forbid  the  Government  to  foster  free
expression.  Indeed,  it  is  difficult  to  understand  how  the
framers  could  have  intended  to  prevent  the  Government
from  spending  money  to  inform  and  educate  the  public,
communicate  new  and  controversial  ideas,  and  promote
expression  by private  citizens  as  well.

In  a  variety  of  ways,  the  Government  recognizes  that

“educational”  material  (39  C.F.R.  § 135.2(a)(4),  for-
merly  39  U.S.C.  4554(a)).

Professor  Zechariah  Chaffee,  Jr.  summarized  the  affir-
mative  role  of  Government  in  supporting  free  expression
as  follows:

192

“Just  as  unrestricted  freedom  of  locomotion  would
be  useless  without  streets  and  roads,  so  when  com-
munication  passes  beyond  a  conversation  in  a  small
group,  somebody  must  provide  means  for  reaching
hearers,  readers,  or  other  consumers.  Often  the  state
or  some  other  governmental  agency  is  best  fitted  to
supply  the  essential  facilities.”  **’

Through  Subtitle  H,  the  Government  provides  “essential
facilities”  which  it  is  “best  fitted  to  supply”—the  cam-
paign  funds  that  will  enable  presidential  candidates  to
communicate  their  views  free  of  the  problems  associated
with  large  private  contributions.

Appellants  contend—agair.,  “by  analogy”  to  the  estab-
lishment  clause—that  federal  financing  will  “necessarily
Appellants’  Br.  149.
involve  excessive  entanglement.”
Again,  that  statement  is premised  on  a  series  of  sweeping
speculations,  such  as  the  assertion  that  “Congress  will
thus  continuously  debate  the  question  of  who  is  to  have
what  political  power.”  Jd.  As  the  United  States  points
out,  “the  Court,  of  course,  must  determine  how  likely
these  events  are  and  whether,  if  they  did  occur,  a  judi-
cial  remedy  at  that  time  would  offer  a  solution.”  Amicus
Br.  97.

Moreover,  the  concept  of  “entanglement”  is  singularly
appropriate  in  analyzing  questions  of  religion  because
the  Constitution  preciudes  both  restrictive  and  affirmative
action  by the  Government  in  that  area—including  affirma-
tive  action  that  is  evenhanded  among  competing  re-
ligions.***  Because  the  Constitution  dictates  the  strictest
neutrality—because  the  Government,  in  Professor  Kur-

223  7%.  Chafee,  Jr.,  Government  and  Mass  Communications  479
(1947,  Repr.  ed.  1965).  Accord,  T.  Emerson,  supra  note  31,  ch.
XVIII.

224  E.g.,  Meck  v.  Pittinger,  95  S.  Ct.  1753  (1975);  Lemon  Vv.

Kurtzman,  403  U.S.  602  (1971).

193

land’s  phrase,  “cannot  utilize  religion  as  a  standard  for
action  or  inaction”—any  involvement  must  be  scrutinized
for  evidence  that  the  wall  of separation  has  been  breached.
But  no  similar  concern  exists  when  the Government  fosters
political  communication,  any  more  than  when  it  assists
newspapers  and  news  services,  public  broadcasting,  or
the  arts  and  humanities  or  otherwise  promotes  the  devel-
opment  and  communication  of  ideas.

Of  course,  when  the  Government  undertakes  to  provide
such  assistance,  it  must  do  so  in  an  evenhanded  manner.
West  Virginia  State  Board  of Education  v.  Barnette,  319
U.S.  624  (1943);  Hague  v.  CIO,  307  U.S.  496  (1939).
Public  financing  of  elections  must  be  undertaken  only  in
such  a  manner  as  will  not  “freeze  the  political  status
quo,”  Jenness  Vv.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  438  (1971);
Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23,  32  (1968).  We  there-
fore  turn  from  apepllants’  sweeping  assertions  about  the
nature  of  all  public  financing  to  their  allegations  that
the  particular  provisions  before  this  Court  are  uncon-
stitutionally  discriminatory.

D.  In  Providing  Financial  Assistance  For  Presidential
Election  Campaigns,  Congress  May—and  Must—
Make  Rational  Differentiations  Among  Political
Movements

Chapters  95  and  96  are  ameliorative  in  character,  de-
signed  to  reform  the  electoral  process  by  removing  the
evils  that  flow  from  dependence  on  large  private  contri-
butions.  As  such,  they  are  subject  to  this  Court’s  state
ment  in  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan  that

“the  principle  that  calls  for  the  closest  scrutiny  of
distinctions  in  laws  denying  fundamental  rights...
.
is  inapplicable  [to  a  statutory  formula  that  serves]
only  as  a  limitation  on  a  reform  measure  aimed  at

195

eliminating  an  existing  barrier  to  the  exercise  of the
franchise.  Rather,  in  deciding  the  constitutional  pro-
priety  of  the  limitations  in  such  a  reform  measure
we  are  guided  by the  familiar  principles  that a ‘stat-
ute  is  not  invalid  under  the  Constitution  because  it
might  have  gone  farther  than  it  did’...  that  a  leg-
islature  need  not  ‘strike  at  all  evils  at  the  same  time,’
...  and  that  ‘reform  may  take  one  step  at  a  time,
addressing  itself  to  the  phase  of  the  problem  which
seems  most  acute  to  the  legislative  mind’...  .”  384
U.S.  641,  657  (1966).

Chapters  95  and  96  “should  be  scrutinized  under  judicial
principles  sensitive  to”  the  fact  that  their  “thrust  .
.  is
affirmative  and  reformatory,”  even  though  “every  reform
that  benefits  some  more  than  others  may  be  criticized  for
what  it fails  to  accomplish.”  **°

.

Any  system  of  campaign  financing  must  deal  with  the
structural  differences  among  parties  and  political  move
ments  and  the  relative  degrees  of  support  they  have  ob-
tained.  Chapters  95  and  96  contain  allocation  formulas
that,  like  any  such  formulas,  may  be  capable  of producing
questionable  results  in  hypothetical  or  extreme  circum-
stances.“
However,  that  mere  possibility  provides  no
basis  for  invalidating  the  statute  before  it  has  had  a
chance  to  work.

Congress  need  not  give  public  money  to  every  person
who  calls  himself  a  candidate  for  the  Presidency,  but
may  limit  assistance  to  candidates  who  have  demonstrated

225  San  Antoniv  School  Dist.  v.  Rodriquez,  411  U.S.  1,  39  (1973).

226  Appellants  acknowledge  that  direct  payments  “necessarily  in-
volve  allocative  formulas”  (Br.  149),  but  draw  from  this  fact  the
conclusion  that  the  Constitution  prohibits  any  federal  financing,
on  the  ground  that  “no  formula  can  ever  be  fair  to  everyone.”  Id.
(Emphasis  in  original.)  On  that  theory,  a  good  part  of  the  laws
of  the  United  States  must  fall.

“a  significant  modicum  of  support.”  ***  Such  a  limitation
is  obviously  needed  to  prevent  waste  of  the  taxpayers’
money,  to  permit  the  fair  and  effective  administration  of
the  electoral  process,  and  to  avoid  the  confusion  that
would  result  if every  self-declared  candidate,  whatever  his
support,  were  given  substantial  financial  backing.  The
Court  has  recognized  that

“a  State  has  a  legitimate  interest  in  regulating  the
number  of  candidates  on  the  ballot.  Jenness  v.  Fort-
son,  403  U.S.,  at  442;  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.
at  32.
In  so  doing,  the  State  understandably  and
properly  seeks  to  prevent  the  clogging  of  its  election
machinery,  avoid  voter  confusion,  and  assure  that
the  winner  is  the  choice  of  a  majority,  or  at  least
a  strong  plurality,  of  those  voting,  without  the  ex-
pense  and  burden  of  runoff  elections.  .
.  Moreover,
a  State  has  an  interest,  if not  a  duty,  to  protect  the
integrity  of  its  political  processes  from  frivolous  or
fraudulent  candidacies.”  **

.

Various  statutory  tests  can  and  have  been  used  to
accomplish  these  purposes.  Every  state  imposes  qualifica-
tions  that  a  candidate  must  meet  to  be listed  on  the  ballot.
State  laws  generally  allow  candidates  of  established  par-
ties,  which  have  received  substantial  support  in  past  elec-
tions,  to  be  listed  on  the  basis  of  their  past  electoral  sup-
port.  Independent  candidates  and  candidates  of  new  par-
ties  may  generally  obtain  ballot  position  by showing  their
support  in some  other  way,  such  as  submission  of petitions

Indeed,  an  additional  compelling  interest  is  present  in
the  case  of  public  financing  that  does  not  come  into  play
when  only a ballot  position  is at  stake.  If public  financing
were  available  to  every  potential  candidate,  regardless  of
his  chance  of  success,  it  would  create  an  artificial  and
non-neutral  stimulus  to  the  proliferation  of  splinter  par-
ties,  endangering  the  stability  and  effectiveness  of  the
two-party  system  that  has  evolved  without  government

The  Court  has  recognized  similar  interests  in  holding
that  states  may  require  voters  to  be  party  members  for
some  period  of  time  before  a  primary,  Rosario  v.  Rocke-
feller,  410  U.S.  752,  760-62  (1973);  id.  at  765,  768-69
(dissenting  opinion).  The  Court  has  suggested  that  there
is a  compelling  interest  in  protecting  “a  two-party  system
in  order  to  encourage  compromise  and  political  stability,”
as  long  as  a  statute  does  not  impermissibly  “favor  twe
particular  parties.”  °*”

Subtitle  H  does  not  affirmatively  favor  either  the  two-
party  system  or  any  particular  parties;  its  formula  would
work  equally  well  if, for  example,  there  were  three  major
parties.
After  considering  the  constitutional  issues  at
length,***  Congress  carefully  avoided  altering  the  political
balance  either  by  freezing  out  third  party  movements  or
by  artificially  inducing  them.**  To  hold  that  Congress
cannot  make  distinctions  for  that  purpose  “would  utterly
violate  common  sense.”  ***  “Sometimes  the  grossest  dis-
crimination  can  lie  in  treating  things  that  are  different
as  though  they  were  exactly  alike.”  Jenness  v.  Fortson,
403  U.S.  at  442.

(“It  seems  undeniable  that  the  politica]  stability  that  a  two-party
system  fosters  ...  is  a  compelling  state  interest.”).  See  also  Affi-

E.  Appellants  Have  Failed  To  Prove  That  Chapter  95

Invidiously  Discriminates  Against  Them

Appellants  allege  that  the  system  of  public  financing
for  presidential  general  election  campaigns  established
by Chapter  95  is  unconstitutional  because  it discriminates
against  third  parties.  But  in  fact  Chapter  95  provides
for  differential  treatment  based  on  an  objective  formula
that  is  reasonably  designed  to  avoid  artificial  distortion
of  third  party  strength  and  that  leaves  the  way  open
for  a  new  or  minor  party  to  become  a  major  party  in  the
next  election  if  it  attracts  enough  popular  support.

Assuming  that  the  Fund  contains  a  sufficient  amount
from  designations  by  taxpayers  under  Section  6096  (as
is  virtually  certain  for  1976),  a  major  party  candidate
will  receive  $20  million  for  his  general  election  cam-
paign.
(For  simplicity,  this  discussion  disregards  the
effect  of  the  inflation  adjustment  under  18  U.S.C.  § 608
(d).)  Since  he  is  not  permitted  to  spend  more  than  that
amount,  he  will  not  be  allowed  to  accept  private  cam-
paign  contributions.**
A  minor  party  candidate  will
receive  a  proportionate  amount  based  on  his  party’s
nerformance  in  relation  to  the  average  of  the  major
parties  in  the  last  election.

A  new  party  will  receive  federal  assistance  only  if  it
establishes  significant  public  support  by  obtaining  five
If
percent  or  more  of  the  vote  in  the  current  election.
it  does  so,  or  if  a  minor  party  betters  its  previous  per-
formance  vis-a-vis  the  average  of  the  major  parties,  the
new  or  minor  party  will  receive  additional  payments

296  26  U.S.C.  § 9003(b).  Of  course,  a  major  party  candidate  may
refuse  to  accept  federal  financing.  If  he  does  so,  he  will  be  free  to
solicit  and  receive  contributions  and  to  make  expenditures  up  to
the  $20  million  ceiling  in  § 608(c),  plus  20  percent  for  fund-raising
costs.

|
1

:

‘

199

based  on  its  electoral  showing.  Since  that  showing  can-
not  be  known  until  the  votes  are  counted,  the  addi-
tional  payments  can  only  be  made  after  the  election.
Such  a  party  will  be  free  to  raise  and  spend  private
contributions  up  to  the  ceiling.

If  a  minor  or  new  party  receives  25  percent  of  the
vote  in  a  presidential  election,  it  will  be  a  major  party
in  the  next  election  and  receive  $20  million  of  assistance.
Moreover,  if  its  vote  matches  the  average  of  the  major
parties’  votes,  its  post-election  payments  for  the  current
election  will  bring  it up  to  the  major  parties’  $20  million
level  of  assistance.

These  formulas  represent  a  conscientous  effort  by Con-
gress  to  be  evenhanded  in  the  treatment  of  different
types  of  political  movements  without  improving  the  lot
of  any  at  the  expense  of  others.  Major  parties—that  is,
those  with  broad  past  support—receive  equal  assistance
in  amounts  greater  than  those  received  by minor  parties.
In  giving  the  major  parties  equal  amounts,  Congress
recognized  that  each  of  them  is  a  vehicle  for  political
expression  by  a  sizeable  portion  of  the  population  and
attempts  to  appeal  to  a  majority  of  the  electorate.
To
give  greater  resources  to  either,  based  on  its  success
in  the  preceding  or  the  current  election,  might  increase
the  advantage  of  the  incumbent  party  and  make  it more
difficult  for  a  new  majority  favoring  the  opposition
to  replace  the  party  in  power.  As  a  practical  matter,
the  voters’  only  real  alternative  to  the  party  in  power
is  often  a  major  party  out  of  power,  and  the  challenged
legislation  does  not  weaken  that  alternative.  Neither  of
the  present  major  parties  challenges  this  equal  treat-
ment  as  a  plaintiff  here.

The  differential  treatment  of  minor  parties  is  permis-
sible  in  view  of  the  “obvious  differences  in  kind  between

200

201

tS TR Oe

the  needs  and  potentials  of  a  political  party  with  his-
torically  established  broad  support,  on  the  one  sand,  and
a  new  or  small  political  organization  on  the  other.”  Jen-
ness  V.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  at  441.  A  minor  party’s
strength  is  reasonably  measured  by  comparison  to  the
average  of  the  major  parties.*”

The  statute  in  no  way  enshrines  any  particular  parties;
it  leaves  all  parties  free  to  move  from  one  category
to  another  on  the  basis  of  demonstrated  public  support.**
A  major  party  will  become  a  minor  party  if  it  fails  to
attract  25  percent  of  the  vote  in  a  presidential  election.
Minor  or  new  parties  are  equally  free  to  move  upward
as  they  gain  public  support.  Thus,  the  statute  does  not
impair  the  “potential
of  American  political

“In  short,  minor  parties  will  retain  their  present

As  the
spread  and  not  a  transient  phenomenon.
United  States  Supreme  Court  has  noted,  the  legiti-
mate  interest  in  preserving  the  benefits  of  two  major
parties  does  not  justify  laws  which  would  choke  off

“The  bill  complies  with  this  requirement.  It  does
not  prevent  minor  parties  from  placing  candidates
on  the  ballot  or  from  organizing  resources  to  sup-
port  them.  It does  not  freeze  the  political  status  quo.
Compare  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968),
with  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  481  (1971).”  S.
Rep.  No.  689,  supra  note  35,  at  8-9.

As  the  court  of  appeals  observed,  “the  legislative  his-
tory  indicates  that  Congress  had  in  mind  the  Supreme
Court’s  specific  approval  in  Jenness  of  a  system  which
bases  differential  treatment  on  ‘demonstrated  relative
strength’  in  prior  elections.”
(Ct.  App.  Op.  1564)  Indeed,

‘the  development  of  public  financing  legislation  closely
tracks  the  guidance  provided  in  this  Court’s  decisions.
The  original  public  financing  measure  for  presidential
elections  was  adopted  in  1966,  when  this  Court  had  pro-
vided  little  illumination  of the  area.  Presidential  Election
Campaign  Fund  Act  of  1966,  Pub.  L.  No.  89-909,  80
Stat.  1587-90.  The  1966  Act  placed  parties  rather  than
candidates  in  control  of  funds,  required  minor  parties
to  poll  over  seven  percent  of  the  votes  in  the  previous
election  in  order  to  receive  any  financing,  and  failed  to
provide  any  payments  whatsoever  based  on  performance
in  the  present  election.**  In  1967,  after  substantial  de-
bate,  Congress  suspended  the  operation  of  the  1966
statute.  81  Stat.  57.  The  Court’s  decision  the  following
year  in  Williams  v.  Rhodes  raised  additional  questions
about  the  kinds  of  distinctions  incorporated  in  the  sus-
pended  1966  Act.  It  was  not  until  this  Court’s  unani-
mous  decision  in  Jenness  on  June  21,  1971,  that  clear
guidelines  became  available  on  the  permissible  scope  of
differentiation  in  providing  public  financing  for  both
major  and  minor  parties.  Five  months  after  that  de-
cision,  Senator  Pastore  introduced  the  provision  that  be-
came  Chapter  95.***  This  Court’s  later  decisions  in  Bul-
lock,  Storer,  and  American  Party  were  promptly  re-

flected  in  congressional  debate  and  reports.  Thus  there

2.  The  Treatment  of Minor  Parties  is Not
Invidiously  Discriminatory

Appellants  attack  the  formula  for  assistance  to  minor
parties,  asserting  that  various  hypothetical  events  might
Appellants’  Br.
yield  results  they  find  unsatisfactory.
But  Congress  cannot  be  expected  to  act  with
153-54.
mathematical  precision.  The  Constitution  does  not  re-
quire  precise  adherence  to  an  equal-dollars-per-vote  stand-
ard  or  any  other  arbitrary  rule.**  If Congress  adopts  an
objective  standard  that  reasonably  reflects  relative  elec-
toral  performance,  as  it  has  in  Chapter  95,  the  require-
ments  of  equal  protection  are  satisfied.  The  “procedure
may  not  always  be  a  completely  precise  or  satisfactory
barometer  of  actual  community  support  for  a_  political
party,  but  the  Constitution  has  never  required  the  States
to  do  the  impossible.”  American  Party  v.  White,  415
U.S.  at  786-87.

Besides,  it  would  be  premature  and  unwise  for  the
Court  to  test  Chapter  95’s  formula  for  assistance  to
minor  parties  on  the  basis  of  speculative  and  hypothetical
future  events,  rather  than  on  the  facts  before  it  and
the  experience  of  history.  Appellants  ignore  the  signifi-

243  Even  if  dollars-per-vote  were  the  appropriate  constitutional
Be-
standard,  appellants’  hypotheticals  show  no  discrimination.
cause  minor  party  financing  is  determined  by  the  average  showing
of  the  major  parties,  minor  parties  will  receive  fewer  dollars  per
vote  than  the  lower  ranking  major  party,  but  more  dollars  per
vote  than  the  winning  party.  It  is  difficult  for  a  minor  party  to  al-
lege  discrimination  when  it has  been  benefited  (on  the  dollars-per-
vote  standard)  more  than  the  victor.

205

cant  forces  that  have  been  shown  to govern  the  emergence
of  third  parties  in  this  country.

“The  emergence  of  significant  third  parties  de-
pends  on  the  coincidence  of  four  factors:  severe  na-
tional  conflict  over  a  few  very  important  issues,  a
period  of  “crisis  politics”;  division  of  the  electorate
on  one  or  more  of  these  issues  into  at  least  one  in-
tense and estranged  minority  and  a  broad  majority;
rejection  or  avoidance  of the  position  of  the  minority
by both  major  parties,  causing  alienation  of  the
minority;  and  a  politician  or  political  group  willing
to  exploit  the  situation  by  initiating  a  new  party.
Such  a  combination  assures  an  appreciable  third-
party  vote.

“The  conditions,  however,  are  cumulative;  each  is
necessary  for  the  next  to  operate.  Hence,  except  in
a  period  of  crisis,  politically  active  minorities  will
usually  be absorbed  by the  major  parties;  seldom  will
they  be drawn  into  a  third  party.  If  during a crisis
the  major  parties  take  opposing  positions,  little  lee-
way  remains  for  a  third  party.  Lacking  a political
crisis  and  an  intense  minority  to  appeal  to,  few
politicians  of  repute  will  venture  to  lead  a  third-
party effort.  But  in  a  period  of crisis  when  a  minor-
ity  emerges  and  the  major  parties  ignore  it,  signifi-
cant  third  parties  appear.  Together,  the  four  condi-
tions  are  sufficient  to
i
produce  a  large  third-party

ird-

It  is highly  unlikely  that  a  movement  with  such  political
force  will  be  unable  to  raise  sufficient  private  financing
to  communicate  its  views  effectively—or  that  its  emer-
gence  will  be  deterred  by  the  prospect  of  differential
treatment  in  federal  campaign  financing.

For  example,  appellants  assert  that  “had  the
ments.
challenged  statutes  been  in  effect  at  the  time  of  the

raham  Lincoln  would  have  been  just  another  burial  plot

Metcalf  Amicus  Br.  30.  But  the  Republican  party  did

Moreover,  only  once  in  modern  history  have  we  faced
a  situation  remotely  resembling  those  hypothesized  by
appellants—when  the  Bull  Moose  party  split  from  the  Re-
publican  Party  in  1912.  Even  here,  appellants’  hypotheti-
cal  examples  are  addressed  not  to  the  effect  of  the  for-
mula  in  an  election  such  as  1912,  but  to  its  effect  in  the
next  ensuing  election.  History  suggests  appellants’  con-
cern  is  misplaced.  Almost  invariably,  a  “major  party
actively  courts  the  disaffected  group  and  wins  its  con-
fidence,  spelling  doom  for  the  third  party.”  **  For  ex-
ample,  in  1916  the  Progressives  again  nominated  Theodore
Roosevelt;  the  Republicans  nominated  the  progressive
Charles  Evans  Hughes,  which  led  Roosevelt  to  withdraw
in  favor  of  Hughes  and  eliminated  a  separate  Progres-

245

J.  Sundquist,  supra  note  244,  at  289-90.  See  also  id.,  chs.  4-5;

207
sive  ticket.’  Although  the  prospect  of  public  financing
for  the  third  party  in  a  forthcoming  election  might  in-
crease  its  resistance  to  such  courtship,  it  would  also  en-
hance  the  major  party’s  incentive  to  bring  the  disaffected
group  within  its  fold.

But  most  importantly,  no  such  question  is  presently
before  this  Court.  The  major  parties  each  received  over
25  percent  and  no  other  party  received  between  five  and
25  percent  of  the  vote  in  the  1972  election.  Neither  the
Bull  Moose  phenomenon  nor  any  of  its  variants  can  affect
the  operation  of  the  formula  in  the  1976  election,  and
no  present  or  potential  candidate—let  alone  any  ap-
pellant—presently  qualifies  for  minor  party  treatment
under  Chapter  95  in  the  1976  election.
If  a  Bull  Moose
phenomenon  occurs  in  1976,  it  will  be  time  enough  then
for  Congress  to  make  any  necessary  adjustments  for
1980,  and  if  it  fails  to  do  so,  for  this  Court  to  judge
the  constitutionality  of  the  formula  in  such  a  context.

3.  The  Treatment  of  New  Parties  Is  Not
Invidiously  Discriminatory

Appellants  contend  that  Chapter  95  invidiously  dis-
criminates  against  new  parties  by denying  them  financial
assistance  for  an  election  campaign  because  of  their  fail-

247

J.  Sundquist,  supra  note  244,  at  165.  Whether  or  not  Roose-
velt  had  withdrawn,  application  of  the  current  formula  to  the  1916
election  would  not  have  produced  an  unreasonable  financial  result.
The  Democratic  candidate  and  any  Progressive  candidate  would
each  have  received  an  equal  amount,  and  the  Republican  candidate
would  have  received  67  percent  of  that  amount.  See  Findings  I,
§ 85,  IIA  J.A.  40.  Hughes,  the  Republican  candidate,  would  almost
certainly  have  been  able  to  raise  the  remaining  33  percent  from
private  contributions,  as  the  formula  permits,  and  thus  to  spend
the  same  amount  as  President  Wilson.

A  further  example  is  provided  by  the  absorption  of  the  People’s
Party,  which  gained  8.5  percent  of  the  popular  vote  in  1892,  into
the  Democratic  party  by  the  time  of  the  1896  election.  See  J.
Sundquist,  supra  note  248,  ch.  7.

a te em 8A

ee eT

eee
eee

208

209

ure  to  receive  five  percent  of  the  vote  in  the  preceding
presidential  election  or  in  the  current  presidential  elec-
tion.  We  disagree.

Recent  decisions  of  this  Court  make  clear  that  there
is  nothing  unconstitutiona!  about  denying  federal  assist-
ance  to  parties  that  failed  to  gain  a  significant  vote  in
the  preceding  election.
In  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.
431  (1971),  the  Court  unanimously  upheld  a  statutory
formula  indistinguishable  from  Chapter  95,  which  was
applied  by  Georgia  to  determine  which  candidates  were
entitled  to  positions  on  the  ballot  for  elective  state  offices.
The  Georgia  law  entitled  any  political  organization
whose  candidate  had  received  20  percent  or  more  of  the
vote  in  the  last  gubernatorial  or  presidential  election  to
have  its  candidate  automatically  placed  on  the  ballot
in  the  next  election.  Any  other  political  organization
could  have  its  nominee  placed  on  the  ballot  only  by  filing
a  nominating  petition  signed  by  five  percent  or  more  of
those  eligible  to  vote  at  the  last  election  for  the  office
sought.  The  Court  rejected  the  claim  that  these  require-
ments  operated  “to  freeze  the  political  status  quo”:

“  ..  Georgia  in  no  way  freezes  the  status  quo,  but
implicitly  recognizes  the  potential  fluidity  of  Ameri-
can  political  life.  Thus,  any  political  body  that  wins
as  much  as  20%  support  in  an  election  becomes  a
‘political  party’  with  its  attendant  ballot  position
rights  and  primary  election  obligations,  and  any  ‘po-
litical  party’  whose  support  at  the  polls  falls  below
that  figure  reverts  to  the  status  of  a  ‘political  body’
with  its  attendant  nominating  petition  responsibili-
ties  and  freedom  from  primary  election  duties.  We
can  find  in  this  system  nothing  that  abridges  the
rights  of  free  speech  and  association  secured  by  the
Jd.  at
First  and  the  Fourteenth  Amendments.”
439-40.

This  reasoning  applies  precisely  to  the  treatment  of  new
parties  in  Chapter  95.

The  Court  in  Jenness  also  rejected  the  argument  that
the  need  of  some  candidates  to  obtain  signatures  of  five
percent  of  the  electorate  invidiously  discriminated  against
those  candidates.  The  Court  pointed  out  that  the  nomi-
nation  of  a  major  party  is  difficult  to  obtain.
It  re
fused  to  assume  that  it is  “inherently  more  burdensome”
for  a  candidate  to gather  the  signatures  of  five  percent  of
the  electorate  than  it is to  win  the  nomination  of  a  party
whose  broad  support  in  the  last  election  entitles  its  nomi-
nees  automatically  to  ballot  positions.

“The  fact  is, of course,  that  from  the  point  of view
of one  who  aspires  to elective  public  office  in  Georgia,
alternative  routes  are  available  to  getting  his  name
printed  on  the  ballot.  He  may  enter  the  primary  of
a  political  party,  or  he  may  circulate  nominating
petitions  either  as  an  independent  candidate  or  under
the  sponsorship  of  a  political  organization.  We  can-
not  see  how  Georgia  has  violated  the  Equal  Protec-
tion  Clause  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  by making
available  these  two  alternative  paths,  neither  of which
can  be  assumed  to  be  inherently  more  burdensome
than  the  other.”  Jd.  at  440-41.

The  Court  upheld  the  five  percent  support  requirement,
stating  that:

“There  is  surely  an  important  state  interest  in
requiring  some  preliminary  showing  of  a  significant
modicum  of  support  before  printing  the  name  of  a
political  organization’s  candidate  on  the  ballot—the
interest,  if  no  other,  in  avoiding  confusion,  decep-
tion,  and  even  frustration  of  the  democratic  process
at  the  general  election.”  Jd.  at  442.

210

211

The  five  percent  support  requirement  for  federal  fi-
nancing  in  Chapter  95  is  valid  for  the  same  reasons.
There  is  no  ground  for  assuming  that  it  is  necessarily
more  burdensome  for  a  candidate  to  obtain  five  percent
of  the  vote  in  a  general  election  than  it  is  to  win  the
nomination  of  one  of  the  established  political  parties.
An  aspirant  for  the  Presidency  may  seek  that  office  by
alternative  routes:  working  within  an  established  party
or  forming  a  new  political  organization.  Appellant  Mc-
Carthy,  having  tried  the  former  route  in  1968,  has
now  chosen  to  try  the  latter.  Chapter  95  holds  out  the
prospect  of  federal  assistance  in  the  general  election
to  a  candidate  who  qualifies  either  by  winning  a  major
or  minor  party  nomination  or  by  winning  five  percent
or  more  of  the  general  election  vote.  Congress  did  not
deny  equal  protection  of  the  laws  “by  making  available
these  two  alternative  paths.”

Chapter  95  is  no  more  discriminatory  if  considered
from  the  viewpoint  of  a  new  party—like  the  Libertarian
Party—rather  than  its  candidate.  Obviously,  a  new  party
would  be  better  off  if,  despite  its  lack  of  a  track  record
of  public  support,  Chapter  95  treated  it  as  favorably
as  it  does  parties  with  a  record  of  electoral  success.
However,  Congress  is  not  forbidden  to  require  that  a
party  establish  “a  significant  modicum  of  support,”  id.
at  442,  in  one  way  or  another.  Nor  is  the  five  percent
figure  chosen  by  Congress  an  “impossible  dream.”  Over
the  past  century,  a  minor  party  candidate  achieved  over
five  percent  in  four  of  the  25  presidential  elections,  or
16  percent  of  the  time.***  The  choice  of  that  percentage

248  See  Findings  I, 1 85,  ITA  J.A.  38-40  (James  B.  Weaver,  Peo-
ple’s  Party,  1892;  Theodore  Roosevelt,  Progressive  Party,  1912;
Robert  M.  LaFollette,  Progressive  Party,  1924;  George  C.  Wallace,
American  Independent  Party,  1968).

———

Nam we
Oh ET ETT

figure,  which  is  the  same  as  the  one  upheld  in  Jenness,
is  obviously  a  permissible  legislative  judgment.  As  Mr.
Justice  Holmes  said  in Louisville  Gas  Co.  v.  Coleman,  277
U.S.  32,  41  (1928):

“(When  it is seen  that  a  line  or  point  there  must  be,
and  that  there  is  no  mathematical  or  logical  way
of  fixing  it  precisely,  the  decision  of  the  legislature
must  be  accepted  unless  we  can  say  that  it  is  very
wide  of  any  reasonable  mark.”  **

24°  Tf  the  Court  should  nevertheless  conclude  that  Chapter  95  in-
vidiously  discriminates  against  new  parties  by  permitting  them  to
receive  assistance  only  if  they  win  five  percent  of  the  vote  in  the
preceding  or  the  current  election,  it  should  not  invalidate  all  of
Chapter  95.  26  U.S.C.  §7852(a)  (separability  clause).
It  should
hold  the  statute  unconstitutional  only  to  the  extent  that  Section
9004(a)  (3)  bars  proportional  pre-election  financing  to  a  party  that
received  some  votes  but  less  than  five  percent  in  the  preceding
presidential  election  and  to  the  extent  that  it  bars  proportional
post-election  financing  to  a  party  that  receives  some  votes  but  less
than  five  percent  in  the  current  election.

The  fact  that  striking  the  exclusions  for  new  parties  would  ex-
pand  the  coverage  of  Chapter  95  is  not  an  obstacle  to  severability.
It  is  well  established  that  a  court,  on  holding  a  statute  unconstitu-
tional  in  part,  can  expand  the  scope  of  the  statute  if the  expansion
will  cure  its  legal  infirmity  and  promote  the  major  purpose  of  Con-
gress.  As  Mr.  Justice  Harlan  said,  concurring  in  Welsh  v.  United
States,  398  U.S.  333,  361  (1970):

“Where  a  statute  is defective  because  of  underinclusion  there
exist  two  remedial  alternatives:  a  court  may  either  declare  it a
nullity  and  order  that  its  benefits  not  extend  to  the  class  that
the  legislature  intended  to  benefit,  or  it may  extend  the  cover-
age  of  the  statute  to  include  those  who  are  aggrieved  by  ex-
clusion.  Cf.  Skinner  v.  Oklahoma,  316  U.S.  535,  543  (1942);
Iowa  Des-Moines  National  Bank  v.  Bennett,  284  U.S.  239
(1931)”.

See  also  Developments  in  the  Law—Equal  Protection,  82  Harv.  L.
Rev.  1065,  1136-37  (1969).  The  Court  has  applied  this  principle  in
many  cases,  cost  recently  in  Weinberger  v.  Wiesenfeld,  43  U.S.L.W.
4393  (March  19,  1975)  (extending  social  security  benefits  to  widow-
ers  after  holding  that  their  exclusion  war  unconstitutional).  See
also  Neal  v.  Delaware,  103  U.S.  370  (1880)  ; Sweatt  v.  Painter,  339
U.S.  629  (1950).  In  this  case,  the  legislative  purpose  would  clearly

212

4.  Chapter  95 Provides  Realistic  Alternative  Means
of  Financing  Third  Party  Presidential  Cam-
paigns

Appellants  argue  that  receiving  money  before  the  elec-
tion  is  better  than  receiving  it  after  the  election.  How-
ever,  if—as  the  Court  held  in  Jenness—electoral  success
may  be  used  as  a  measure  of  public  support,  it  is  im-
possible  to  assist  a  new  party  on  the  basis  of  such  a
measure  until  afte:  the  votes  have  been  counted.  Con-
gress  has  provided  that  both  major  and  minor  parties
will  receive  assistance  before  the  election  to  the  extent
that  their  entitlements  can  be  calculated  on  the  basis  of
past  election  results.  The  same  obviously  cannot  be  done
for  a  new  party.  Nor  is  it  feasible  for  this  purpose  to
use  such  alternative  measures  of  support  as  petition  sig-
natures  or  public  opinion  polls,  as  the  court  of  appeals
(Ct.  App.  Op.  1563-64)  and  the  United  States  (Amicus
Br.  101  n.63)  have  agreed.  The  best  that  can  be  done  is
to  provide  assistance  after  the  election  based  on  the  new
party’s  performance,  in  order  to  enable  the  party  to  re-
pay  loans  and  contributions—thus,  incidentally,  building
further  goodwill  with  its  supporters.  Equal  protection  of
the  laws  cannot  require  greater  equality  than  is  feasible.

Appellants  attempt  to  distinguish  this  Court’s  ballot
They  assert  that  “there  is  only  one
access  decisions.
route  by  which  a  prospective  candidate  can  reach  the
goal  of  obtaining  general  election  federal  subsidy  money
before  the  November  election”  (Br.  159),  and  conclude
that  the  absence  of  “two  alternative  routes  to  reach  the
goal”  violates  the  equal  protection  standards  established
by  this  Court  in  Jenness  and  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.
709  (1974).  The  purported  distinction  is  fallacious.

be  better  effectuated  by extending  payments  proportionately  to  new
parties  than  by  invalidating  the  entire  system  of  financing  presi-
dential  general]  election  campaigns.

213

.

First,  what  this  Court  said  in  Jenness  and  Lubin  was
that  the  state  must  provide  a  realistic  alternative  means
of  access  to  the  ballot  where  the  state  has  restricted  such
access,  by  requiring  a  specified  previous  electoral  per-
formance  or  filing  fee.  Chapter  95,  on  the  other  hand,
provides  only  affirmative  assistance;  since  it  does  not
restrict  electoral  opportunities,  it  need  not  provide  alter-
native  routes  to  surmount  any  restrictions.  The  provi-
sions  of  Chapter  95  in  no  way  “burden  either  a  minority
party’s  or  an  individual  candidate’s  .
.  important  in-
terest  in  the  continued  availability  of  political  opportu-
nity.”  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  at  716.  Chapter  95
can  be  regarded  as  restrictive  only  if  one  mistakes  the
objective  of  presidential  candidates.
Appellants  allege
that  “the  candidate’s  goal  is  to  obtain  the  general  elec-
tion  federal  subsidy  ...  .”  Br.  159.  But  federal  money
is  no  more  an  objective  of  presidential  candidates  than
‘any  particular  means  of  ballot  access  was  an  objective
of the  Georgia  candidates  who  brought  Jenness.  The  Geor-
gia  candidates  wanted  access  to  the  ballot  by  whatever
means,  just  as  presidential  candidates  want  access  to
sufficient  funds,  by  whatever  means,  to  fuel  an  effective
campaign.  Chapter  95  does  not  reduce  the  number  of
paths  to  that  goal;  it  adds  one—federal  assistance.

Second,  Congress  has  provided  alternative  paths  even
to  obtain  federal  assistance.  They  are  the  same  “alter-
native  routes”  specifically  provided  in  Jenness.  408  U.S.
at  440.  A  candidate  may  choose  either  to  fight  and  win
the  nomination  of a  major  party  or  to  fight  independently
to  gain  the  support  of  five  percent  or  more  of  the  elec-
torate—“neither  of  which  can  be  assumed  to  be  inher-
ently  more  burdensome  than  the  other.”  Jd.  at  441.  A

214

215

candidate  who  chooses  the  latter  route  receives  federal
payments  at  the  earliest  opportunity  after  he  has  demon-
strated  sufficient  support.  And  the  prospect  of  federal
assistance  after  the  election  should  be  very  useful  to  a
new  party  that  has  a  realistic  prospect  of  getting  a
sizable  vote.  Governor  Wallace’s  13.5  percent  showing  in
the  1968  presidential  election  did  not  come  as  an  election-
day  surprise;  it  had  been  predicted  by  public  opinion
polls  and  political  experts  well  before  the  election.  A
new  party  with  such  prospects  of  success  will  find  its
ability  to  obtain  commercial  credit  and  loans  to  finance
a  campaign  enhanced  by  the  prospect  of  federal  assist-
ance  in  repaying  the  loans  after  the  election.
Political
supporters  can  be  asked  to  contribute  by  making  loans
up  to  $1,000  for  individuals—$5,000  for  qualified  com-
mittees—repayable  after  the  election.  In  addition,  ordi-
nary  bank  loans  are  available  without  regard  to  the
contribution  limits  in  Section  608.*°  Therefore,  even  for
a  new  party,  Chapter  95  provides  a  measure  of  financial
assistance  before,  and  not  merely  after,  an  election.

There  is  a  final  and  decisive  point  with  respect  to  the
“discrimination”  allegedly  resulting  from  the  alternative

250  The  challenged  statutes  expressly  permit  candidates  to  receive,
without  limitation,  any  “loan  of  money  by  a  national  or  State  bank
made  in accordance  with  the  applicable  banking  laws  and  regulations
and  in  the  ordinary  course  of  business.”  18  U.S.C.  §591(e).  Such
loans  are  also  excluded  from  the  prohibition  of  any  “contribution”
by a  national  bank.  18  U.S.C.  § 610.  The  legislative  history  of  these
provisions  indicates  that  Congress  intended  all  parties,  including
new  parties,  to  be  free  to  finance  their  campaigns  by ordinary  bank
loans  in  any  amount,  except  that  loans  made  only  on  an  endorse-
ment  or  guarantee  are  considered  to  be  contributions  by  the  en-
dorser  or  guarantor,  subject  to  the  contribution  limits.  See  S.  Rep.
No.  229,  92d  Cong.,  lst  Sess.  59,  121  (1971);  117  Cong.  Rec.  43385-
86  (1971).  Even  before  these  provisions  were  inserted  in  Sections

paths  created  by  the  public  financing  provisions.  When
public  financing  is  considered  together  with  the  other
features  of  these  laws,  it  is  clear  that  minor  and  new
parties  will  find  their  financial  positions  enhanced  rela-
tive  to  major  parties.  As  the  court  of  appeals  said,  “it
should  be  borne  in  mind  that  smaller  parties  are  likely
to  be  the  major  beneficiaries  of another  part  of the  statu-
tory  scheme,  the  expenditure  ceilings.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1564.
They  will  also  benefit  from  the  contribution  limits.  See
Part  V, supra.

In  the  1972  presidential  election,  each  of  the  major
party  candidates  had  no  trouble  raising  more  than  $20
million  of  private  funds  before  the  election.  The  Demo-
eratic  candidate  raised  $38  million;  the  Republican  candi-
date  raised  $60  million.  Findings  IIA,  J 71,  IIB  J.A.  457.
Even  if  the  excess  over  $1,000  of  each  contribution  had
been  eliminated,  the  Democratic  and  Republican  candi-
dates  would  each  have  raised  over  $24  million.  Jd.  § 72,
IIB  J.A.  457.  Minor  and  new  party  candidates  in  the
aggregate  raised  $1,043,000  in  1972;  eliminating  the  ex-
cess  over  $1,000  of  each  contribution,  they  would  have
raised  $977,000.**  That  is  only  one  percent  of  the  $98
million  raised  by  the  major  party  candidates.

In  1976,  neither  the  Republican  nor  the  Democratic
candidate  may  spend  more  than  $20  million  in  the  general

591(e)  and  610,  bank  loans  were  made  to finance  political  campaigns.
See  e.g.,  United  States  v.  First  National  Bank,  329  F.  Supp.  1251
(S.D.  Ohio  1971).

election,  whether  in  public  or  private  money.  If  minor
and  new  party  candidates  raise  only  the  same  amounts
in  1976  as  they  did  in  1972,  they  will  be  proportionately
much  better  off  financially  compared  to  the  major  par-
ties.  Their  spending  as  a  percent  of  the  $40  million  the
major  parties  may  spend  in  the  general  election  will  be
2.5  percent,  or  more  than  double  the  1972  figure.  The
additional  fund-raising  ability  they  should  have  because
of  the  possibility  of  receiving  federal  assistance  after
the  election  may  enable  them  to  do  even  better.  The  re-
sult  appears  to  bear  out  the  prediction  of  Professor
Rosenthal:

“If  [third  parties]  were  treated  generously  even
though  not  equally,  it  might  be  shown  that  they
were  in  fact  accorded  a  better  chance  to  convey  their
views  to  the  voters  than  if  all  parties  were  depend-
ent  solely  on  private  contributions.”  **

Thus,  despite  appellants’  protestations,  the  court  of
appeals  rightly  concluded  that  “third  party  and  inde-
pendent  candidates  are  now  in  a  better  position  to  ap-
Ct.
proach  the  spending  levels  of  the  major  parties.”
App.  Op.  1564.  Appellants  have  failed  to  show  that  the
alternative  routes  to  presidential  campaign  financing  pro-
vided  by  Chapter  95  “discriminate”  against  minor  or
new  parties.

5.  No  Other  Financing  Mechanism  Has  Been
Shown  to  Provide  a  “Less  Restrictive  Means”

Appellants  suggest  that  there  are  less  restrictive  means
of  providing  public  financing.  Of  course,  they  carefully

252  Rosenthal,  supra  note  218,  at  416.  See  also  Affidavit  of  James

ee ee =

MacGregor  Burns,  supra  note  58,  § 11.

refrain  from  endorsing  any  of  the  alternatives  they  ad-
vance.
Moreover,  they  offer  their  alternatives  without
meaningful  elaboration  or  analysis.

.

Alternatives  are  always  offered  when  a  new  and  com-
plex  legislative  program  is  debated.  Adherents  of  par-
ticular  proposals  always  assert  that  their  method  is  the
most  effective,  the  least  restrictive,  or  the  sole  constitu-
tional  means  of  attaining  the  legislative  objective.  For
example,  appellants  assert  that  “many  of  the  difficulties
created  by subtitle  H  .
.  can  be  avoided  by  a  ‘voucher’
scheme,”  in  which  the  Government  would  distribute  sub-
sidy  vouchers  directly  to  individuals,  who  would  in  turn
contribute  the  vouchers  to  the  candidates  of  their  choice.
Appellants’  Br.  151.  Appellants  ignore  the  obvious  defects
of such  a  scheme.***  The  voucher  system  could  focus  enor-
mous  political  power  in  organized  groups  able  to  collect
vouchers  quickly  and  efficiently,  tempting  such  groups  to
use  coercion  or  other  unacceptable  means  to  aggregate
their  voucher  strength.
Since  vouchers  would  be  the
equivalent  of  money,  a  “black  market”  might  develop  in
which  vouchers  would  be bought  from  voters  at  a  discount
by political  candidates  or  committees  or  by intermediaries.
A  voucher  system  would  impose  an  enormous  administra-
tive  and  financial  burden,  estimated  by one  proponent  at
~$50  million  if  public  financing  were  extended  to  all  fed-
eral  elections.**  A  voucher  system  would  not,  like  direct
'  federal  grants,  permit  a  candidate  to  know  when  he  will

253  See,  e.g.,  American  Bar  Association  Special  Committee  on
Election  Reform,  Symposium  on  Campaign  Financing  Regulation
103-14  (1975)  ; J.  Kirby,  Congress  and  the  Public  Trust,  Report  of
the  Association  of  the  Bar  of  the  City  of  New  York  Special  Com-
mittee  on  Congressional  Ethics  131  (1970).  The  Report  concludes
that  “the  direct  Federal  subsidy  seems  preferable  to  the  other
choices.”  Jd.

25¢  ABA  Special  Committee  Symposium,  supra  note  253,  at  103-04.

218
receive  the  federal  aid;  the  timing  would  hinge  on  the
decisions  of  millions  of  voucher  holders.

The  leading  congressional  advocate  of the  voucher  plan,
Senator  Metcalf,  has  filed  a  brief  amicus  curiae  of  which
one  paragraph  (Br.  48)  is devoted  to  these  frequent  criti-
cisms,  most  of  which  had  been  raised  below.  He  asserts
.
that  many  of  the  difficulties  could  be  “averted  by  ..
harsh  criminal  penalties.”  As  to  the  randomness  in  the
timing  of  receiving  vouchers,  this  “presumably  is  a  mat-
ter  over  which  the  candidate  enjoys  some  control.”  Id.
Other  proponents  of  the  voucher  plan  have  responded  to
these  criticisms  by  formulating  increasingly  complicated
schemes.  The  latest  is  a  “modified  voucher  system”  sup-
plemented  by  “private  giving”  and  involving  both  “flat
D.  Adamany  &  G.  Agree,
and  proportional  grants.”
Despite  its  novelty,  this  pro-
supra  note  44,  at  199.
T.  Schwartz,
posal  has  already  itself  been  criticized.
Public  Financing  of  Elections:  A  Constitutional  Division
of  the  Wealth  87  (1975).  The  current  dispute  over  the
voucher  system  illustrates  the  difficult  policy  questions
underlying  the  selection  of  the  most  effective,  least  re
strictive  system  of  public  financing—and  offers  further
