The  other  alternative  suggested  by  appellants  is  “a
tax  checkoff  system  that  would  allow  taxpayers  to  desig-
nate  their  choice  of  candidates  or  parties...  .”  Appel-
lants’  Br.  163.  Congress  rejected  that  alternative  because,
at  the  time  when  income  tax  returns  are  filed  on  April
15,  it  is  impossible  to  know  who  the  nominees  will  be,
and  accordingly  such  a  provision  would  discriminate  pri-
marily  against  third  parties.  As  Senator  Pastore  ex-
plained:

“So,  what  happens?  When  you  attempt  to  desig-
nate  your  party,  you  are  doing  that  by  April  15,

219

1972."  That  is  the  last  day  to  file  the  income  tax
return.  How  do  you  know  if  McCarthy  will  run?
How  do  you  check  him  off?  What  do  you  do  for
him?  ....  But  let  the  Senator  tell  me  how  we  han-
dle  the  situation  where  the  known  candidate  will  get
the  checkoff  and  those  not  yet  known  cannot  get  the
checkoff  because  the  taxpayer  does  not  know  who  he
is  yet  or  what  party  he  will  represent  yet.  What
do  we  do  about  that?

“What  concerns  me  is what  do  we  do  about  miror-
ity  parties?  Take  the  Wallace  candidacy.  Whether
you  like  him  or  not,  whether  you  vote  for  him  or
not,  he  has  a  right  as  an  American  to  run.  He  has
a  right  to  be  treated  like  everyone  else.  That  is
what  you  are  trying  to  do.  By the  same  token,  some-
one  else  might  come  along  and  file  in  a  certain  num-
ber  of  States  whereby  he  becomes  qualified  on  the
presidential  ballot.  He  is  a  candidate  no  one  ever
heard  of  on  April  15  because  he  does  not  file  until
May,  June  or  July,  and  he  was  out  of  the  picture
completely  then.  What  happens  to  him?

“Where  is  the  equity?  That  is  what  disturbs  me.”

117  Cong.  Rec.  42376  (1971).**

Thus  appellants’  “far  less  discriminatory”  system  (Br.
163)  may  in  fact  be  more  discriminatory.

have  been  worse  than  in  1972.

221

The  United  States  as  Amicus  asks  whether  a  system
of  matching  funds  similar  to  that  provided  for  presiden-
tial  primary  elections  under  Chapter  96,  discussed  below,
could  be  used  for  general  elections  as  well.**’  But  match-
ing  grants  in  the  general  election  might  act  as  an  artifi-
cial  incentive  to  entry  by  additional  candidates,  after
the  party  primary  and  nominating  processes,  thus  en-
couraging  the  splintering  and  proliferation  of  parties.
In  primaries,  multiple  candidacies  have  been  a  common
facet  of  American  political  life;  in  general  elections,  they
have  been  much  less  so.  Congress  had  reason  to  fear  that
a  new  incentive  for  multiple  parties  in  general  elections
might  threaten  the  nation’s  political  stability.
Because
of  the  inherent  differences  between  primary  and  general
elections,  Congress  adopted  differing  financing  mecha-
nisms  in  order  to  prevent  the  distortion  of  either  proc-
ess.
See,  e.g.,  S.  Rep.  No.  689,  supra  note  35,  at  5-9.
Moreover,  a  matching  system  is  subject  to  some  of  the
same  uncertainties  in  timing  associated  with  the  voucher
plan;  the  court  of  appeals  concluded  it was  “a  more  cum-
Ct.
bersome  system”  than  the  approach  of  Chapter  95.
App.  Op.  1564.

Even  more  importantly,  a  matching  system  would  not
accomplish  the  congressional  objective  of  taking  the  first
experimental  step  toward  eliminating  dependence  on  pri-
vate  contributions  in  federal  election  campaigns.
See,
e.g.,  5S. Rep.  No.  689,  supra  note  35,  at  4-5.  And  there
is  powerful  logic  for  the  compelling  need  to  attain  this
goal:

“The  last  decade  has  made  ever  clearer  the  de-
termination  of  the  Court  to  insure  that  political  op-

**?  United  States  Amicus  Br.  100-01.  Appellants  are  presumably
constrained  from  suggesting  such  a  proposal  by  their  view  (Br.
164-68)  that  this  system  is  unconstitutional  as  well.

portunity  and  infiuence  are  allocated  equally  among
all  our  citizens.  Any  reasonable  legislative  policies
which  breathe  reality  into  that  opportunity,  and
which  diminish  inequalities  in  the  exercise  of  politi-
cal  influence,  serve  vital  constitutional  goals,  re-
peatedly  sanctioned  by  the  Court.

“The  attainment  of  those  goals  depends  primarily
on  eliminating  the  use  of  privately  donated  money
in  campaigns.  In  no  other  way  can  government  be
opened  to  participation  by  all  segments  of  the  popu-
lation,  giving  talented  poor  people  for  the  first  time
a  genuine  chance  to  compete  for  office.  In  no  other
way  can  public  policy-making  be  insulated  from
skewing  by  private  interest.
In  no  other  way  can
the  electoral  balances  be  immunized  from  the  effects
of  differential  wealth  distribution  in  the  society  as
a  whole.”  ***

An  alternative  such  as  matching  grants,  which  moves
only  part  way  toward  the  elimination  of  dependence  on
private  money—and  which  Congress  expressly  rejected  as
inappropriate  for  presidential  general  elections—cannot
be  regarded  as  a  “less  restrictive’  solution  to  the  com-
pelling  need.

In  this  case,  there  simply  is  no  readily  apparent  less
restrictive  means  of  accomplishing  the  legitimate  legisla-
lative  ends.  This  is  not  a  case  in  which  the  legislative
objectives  could  be  met  by  straightforward  and  plainly
workable  adjustments  such  as  reducing  the  durational

econor  .  has  termed  these  public  financing  provisions  “the  most
impe  ant  law  passed  in  1974.”  A.  Okun,  Equality  and  Efficiency:

residency  requirement  **  or  shortening  the  period  of  time

of  legislative  policy  already  resolved  by the  Congress.  We
agree  with  the  United  States  as  Amicus  that:

the  interest  in  protecting  the  Treasury  against  raids

choice  of  Congress  must  be  respected.”  United  States
Amicus  Br.  102.

6.  The  Ten-State  Eligibility  Requirement  Is  Not

Invidiously  Discriminatory

Chapter  95  gives  assistance  to  a  minor  or  new  party
candidate  who  has  established  a  broad  base  of  public
support  by  qualifying  to  have  his  name  listed  on  the
26  U.S.C.  § 9002(2)(B).
ballot  in  ten  or  more  states.
This  eligibility  requirement  serves  an  important  interest
in  reserving  federal  resources  for  candidates  who  appeal
to  a  national  rather  than  a  local  constituency  and  are
thus  serious  contenders  for  the  Presidency.

While  a  “favorite  son”  or  other  candidate  having
strictly  local  appeal  may  serve  a  variety  of  legitimate

with  Burns  v.  Fortson,  410  U.S.  686  (1973);  and  Marston  v.  Lewis,
410  U.S.  679  (1973).

26°  Compare  Kusper  Vv.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51,  59  (1973);  with
Rosario  Vv.  Rockefeller,  410  U.S.  752  (1973);  see  Storer  v.  Brown,
(dissenting  opinion  of  Brennan,  J.,
415  U.S.  724,  761  (1974)
joined  by  Douglas  and  Marshall,  JJ.).

political  functions,  Congress  may  permissibly  conclude  *’
that  those  functions  are  not  of  sufficient  national  impor-
tance  to  entitle  a  candidate  to  a  federal  subsidy.  Con-
gress  thus  required  that  a  candidate  meet  the  qualifica-
tion  requirements  of ten  states  before  receiving  assistance.
There  has  been  no  showing  that  this  ten-state  require-
ment  is  unreasonably  high  or  discriminatory.  Victory  in
the  electoral  college  requires  the  votes  of  the  11  largest
states  or  the  40  smallest  states.  Findings  I,  751,  IIA
J.A.  26.  Thus,  Congress  has  not  even  required  a  candi-
date  to  show  that  he  has  support  in  a  group  of  states
that  constitute  a  majority  of  the  electoral  college,  as  it
might  have  done.***  The  ten-state  figure  merely  requires
a  candidate  to  show  that  his  support  has  some  substan-
tial  breadth.

Absent  a  showing  that  this  requirement  works  undue
hardship  on  any  candidate,  such  a  matter  of line  drawing
is  reserved  for  legislative  judgment.**  No  appellant  has
alleged  or  shown  that  he  will  even  be  affected  by  this
requirement.  McCarthy  is  the  only  appellant  who  is  a
presidential  candidate,  and  it  is  far  too  early  to  tell
whether  he  will  qualify  in  ten  or  more  states.

7.  “Independent”  Candidates
General  Election  Funding

Are  Not  Denied

Appellants  asserted  below  that  Chapter  95  provides
assistance  only  to  candidates  of  “political  parties,”  while
appellant  McCarthy  is an  independent  candidate  affiliated
with  no  party.  Even  if correct,  that  assertion  would  show

Moreover,  appellants  misread  the  statute.

It  is  true
that  the  general  election  funding  provisions  speak  of  as-
sistance  only  to  candidates  of  major,  minor,  or  new
“parties.”  26  U.S.C.  §§ 9003,  9004.  But  Subtitle  H  con-
tains  no  definition  of  the  term  “party.”  “Major”  and
“minor”  parties  are  defined  by  their  success  in  the  last
election,  and  a  “new  party”  is  “a  political  party  which
is  neither  a  major  party  nor  a  minor  party.”  Jd.  § 9002
(6),  (7),  (8).

committee,

The  FECA  and  FECA  Amendments,  companion  legis-
lation  to  Subtitle  H,  define  the  term  “political  party”  to
include  “any  association,
or  organization
which  nominates  a  candidate  for  election  to  any  federal
office  whose  name  appears  on  the  election  ballot  as  the
candidate  of  such  association,  committee  or  organization.”
2  U.S.C.  § 481(m);  18  U.S.C.  § 591i).  Relying  on  that
definition,  the  court  of  appeals  held  that  there  is  “ample
room  for  the  Commission  or  the  officials  in  charge  of
disbursing  funds  to  find  that  even  informal  committees
formed  to  support  independent  candidates  for  President
constitute  political  parties  for  the  purposes  of  Chapter
95.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1570.%'  MeCarthy’s  group  may  thus
qualify  as  a  “party”  if  he  obtains  a  ballot  position  under
state  law.  As  the  court  of  appeals  said:

264  McCarthy’s  “informal  committee,”  the  Committee  for  a  Consti-
tutional  Presidency—McCarthy  ’76,  is  already  a  registered  political
committee  under  the  FECA.  Findings  III,  Monroe  3,  IIB  J.A.
719.  And  the  Commission  is  specifically  addressing  the  definition  of
“new  party”  in  its  pending  rulemaking  proceeding.  40  Fed.  Reg.
23833  (June  2,  1975)  (Item  II-G).  Cf.  Appendix  A,  p.  2a,  infra.

“Until  it  is  shown  that  a  narrower  definition  is
being  applied,  to  the  detriment  of  independent  candi-
dates  who  would  otherwise  qualify,  we  are  in  no
position  to  invalidate  the  public  financing  provisions
of  the  statute.”  Ct.  App.  Op.  1570.

nate  against  him  because  he  obtained  the  nomination  of

A  political  movement  formed  around  one  person,  as
McCarthy’s  apparently  is,  plays  a  different  role  from
either  a  major  party  or  a  minor  party  that  has  shown

265  Congress  found  that:

“Although  many  of  the  campaign  finance  abuses  have  occurred
in  connection  with  presidential  general  elections,  the  influence
or  potential  influence  of  big  money  is  as  great  in  all  phases
of  presidential  elections.

“For  example,  major  political  parties  have  come  to  rely  heavily
on  large  corporations  and  labor  unions  to  pay  for  advertise-
ments  in convention  programs  to  meet  the  expenses  of  nominat-
ing  conventions.”  H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239,  supra  note  56,  at  14.

227

substantial  support  and  fields  candidates  for  a  number
of  different  offices.  The  same  is  true  of  the  Libertarian
Party,  which  has  held  one  convention  in  the  past,  at  an
expense  of  $1,423.91.  Findings  III,  Crane  § 11,  IIB  J.A.
752.  As  shown  above,  the  Court  has  repeatedly  held  that
election  laws  may  make  reasonable  distinctions  based  on
these  differences  in  political  life.

For  example,  the  Texas  law  upheld  in  American  Party
v.  White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974),  required  parties  of  a
certain  size  to  hold  primary  elections,  all  parties  to  hold
conventions,  and  independent  candidates  to  file  nominat-
In  upholding  that  law.  the  Court  heid
ing  petitions.
that  a  state  does  not  violate  the  First  Amendment  or
deny  equal  protection  by enacting  regulations  that  recog-
nize  the  inherent  differences  among  types  of  political
movements.  Chapter  95  is  less  restrictive  than  that  law.
Parties  remain  free  to  choose  their  methods  of  nominat-
ing  candidates,  and  candidates  remain  free  to  enter
primaries,  compete  at  conventions,  or  form  their  own
political  organizations.
There  is  nothing  discriminatory
in  recognizing  the  public  functions  traditionally  served
by  conventions  and  primaries  and  providing  assistance
for  them  without  similarly  compensating  independent
candidates  who  choose  to  eschew  these  traditional  ave-
nues  to  election,  or  parties  that  have  failed  to  demon-
strate  substantial  public  support.

Indeed,  American  Party  puts  to  rest  appellants’  dis-
crimination  arguments  with  respect  to  convention  fund-
ing  under  Chapter  95  and  primary  funding  under  Chap-
ter  96,  discussed  below.
Besides  regulating  party  or-
ganization,  the  Texas  law  upheld  there  provided  state
financing  for  the  conduct  of  primary  elections,  which  only
parties  above  a  certain  size  were  permitted  or  required

to  hold.  No  financing  was  provided  for  conventions,  which
all  parties  were  required  to  hold  and  at  which  smaller
415  U.S.  at  793.
parties  nominated  their  candidates.
The  Court  rejected  the  argument  that  financing  only
primaries  discriminated  against  the  smaller  parties.
It
said:

“(Wie  cannot  agree  that  the  State,  simply  be-
cause  it  defrays  the  expenses  of  party  primary  elec-
tions,  must  also  finance  the  efforts  of  every  nascent
political  group  seeking  to  organize  itself  and  un-
successfully  attempting  to  win  a  place  on  the  general
election  ballot.”  415  U.S.  at  794.

Appellants  seek—as  they  must—to  distinguish  Ameri-
can  Party.  They  assert  (Br.  165)  that  the  Texas  statute
was  different  because  it  financed  election  costs  rather
than  campaigning  costs,  and  because  Texas  simply  took
a  “different  route  to  funding  election-day  expenses  than
other  states...  .”  But  the  public  financing  of  primaries
in  American  Party  was  of  undoubted  assistance
in
generating  publicity,  strengthening  party  loyalty  and
support,  and  otherwise  laying  the  groundwork  for  a
successful  general  election  campaign.  And  it  permitted
the  benefited  parties  to  husband  their  funds,  while  the
“minority  parties  .
.  must  undergo  expenses  ..  .  in
holding  their  conventions  and  accumulating  the  necessary
signatures  to  qualify  for  the  ballot...  .”  415  U.S.  at
793-94.  Nevertheless,  this  Court  was  “not  persuaded  that
the  State’s  refusal  to  reimburse  for  these  expenses  is
any  discrimination  at  all  against  the  smaller  parties
and  if  it  is,  that  it  is  also  a  denial  of  the  equal  pro-
tection  of  the  laws  within  the  meaning  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment.”  Id.  at  794.

.

F.  The  Primary  Matching  Provisions  of  Chapter  96

Are  Likewise  Constitutional

Chapter  96  provides  federal  assistance  to  candidates
in  presidential  primaries.
The  problem  of  determining
which  candidates  should  receive  federal  assistance  is
different  in  the  case  of  primaries  than  it  is  in  general
elections.  Each  candidate  in  a  general  election  has  sought
and  obtained  the  nomination  of  his  party  or  has  formed
his  own  organization  outside  the  existing  parties.  There-
fore,  the  tests  used  to  determine  who  will  receive  as-
sistance  and  how  much  he  will  receive  can  be  cast  in
terms  of  the  electoral  support  enjoyed  by  the  parties—
either  in  the  preceding  election  or  (where  that  is  not
possible)  in  the  current  election.  However,  the  range  of
primary  candidates  is  virtually  unlimited,  and  any  test
of  the  seriousness  of  a  candidacy  must  be  applied  to
the  candidate  himself  rather  than  his  party.  See  S.  Rep.  .
No.  689,  supra  note  35,  at  5-6.

The  mechanism  Congress  devised  to  allocate  primary
assistance  to  candidates  who  have  sufficient  popular  sup-
port  is  federal  matching  of  small  private  contributions.
A  candidate  who  satisfies  the  initial  eligibility  require-
ments,  discussed  below,  is  entitled  to  federal  assistance
based  on  the  amount  he  is  able  to  raise  privately  in  con-
tributions  of  $250  or  less.
This  mechanism  allows  in-
dividual  citizens  to  indicate  by  their  contributions  which
candidates  are  most  worthy  of federal  assistance.

1.  Chapter  96  Does  Not  Enhance  but  Greatly  Re-
duces  the Disproportionate  Power  of the  Wealthy
To  Influence  Primary  Elections

Since  the  Government  will  match  only  the  first  $250
of  any  contribution,  the  matching  mechanism  does  not
permit  the  wealthy  to  have  disproportionate  influence

in  choosing  the  recipients  of  federal  assistance.
Appel-
lants  assert  that  federal  matching  of  contributions  up
to  $250  “gives  wealthy  people  a  considerable  advantage.”
Appellants’  Br.  167.  But  Subtitle  H  replaces  a  system  of
wholly  private  presidential  campaign  financing  in  which
contributions  of  $250,000  or  more  were  not  uncommon.
In  1972,  194  individuals  contributed  $50,000  or  more  each
to  presidential  candidates,  for  a  total  of  $27  million."
As  we  have  shown,  Congress  did  not  deny  equal  protec-
tion  by  remedying  the  major  part  of  the  problem.

2.  Matching  Is

a  Permissible  Mechanism

To

Allocate  Federal  Assistance

The  Constitution  permits  Congress  to  employ  a  match-
ing  mechanism.  The  same  compelling  interests  that  jus-
tify  using  a  reasonable,  objective  formula  to  determine
how  much  assistance  candidates  will  receive  in  the  gen-
eral  election  apply  equally  in  the  case  of  primaries.  The
eases  discussed  above  confirm  that  Congress,  which  may
deny  assistance  altogether  to  candidates  who  do  not
evidence  “a  significant  modicum  of  support,”  **  may  also
provide  differential  assistance  to  candidates  based  on  an
objective  measure  of  their  support  among  the  electorate.
The  amount  raised  by  a  candidate  in  small  contributions
provides  such  an  objective  measure.
Since  only  small
contributions  are  matched,  the  amount  thus  raised  evi-
dences  the  degree  of  seriousness  of  support  that  Congress
is  entitled  to  demand.

Matching  grants,  of  course,

are  a  common  means
of  allocating  assistance  in  other  federal  subsidy  pro-

267  H.  Alexander,  supra  note  63,  at  648.

268  Jenness  V.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  442  (1971).

230

231

grams.*”  As  used  in  such  programs,  the  matching  mecha-
nism  serves  the  same  function  it  does  in  Chapter  96:
to  ensure  that  the  project  is  sufficiently  worthy  to  have
attracted  other  support  before  federal  assistance  is  pro-
vided.

In  this  respect,  matching  payments  under  Chapter
96  will  be  functionally  identical  to  the  credits  and  de-
ductions  for  political  contributions  authorized  by  the
Internal  Revenue  Code.*”?  When  an  individual  who  has
contributed  to  a  campaign  receives  a  credit  or  deduction
that  reduces  his  income  tax  liability,  the  Government  in
effect  makes  part  of  the  contribution  through  its  loss
of  tax  revenue.  The  Internal  Revenue  Code  gives  such
credits  or  deductions  for  a  wide  range  of  purposes—
even  in  areas  where  the  Government  could  not  subsidize
directly,  such  as  contributions  to  religious  organizations.*”
Such  tax  incentives  are  of  unquestionable  constitution-
ality.**  A  matching  mechanism,  like  a  tax  incentive,
has  the  advantage

“that  it  permits  the  realities  of  the  campaign—the
relative  importance  of  the  major  versus  the  minor
candidates—to  be  reflected  through  the  separate  de-
cisions  of  millions  of  taxpayers,  thus  relieving  the
government  of  the  necessity  and  the  onus  of  making
those  decisions  itself.”  *”

ment  Act);  42  U.S.C.  §1857(c)  (1970)  (Clean  Air  Act).

Matching  has  the  additional  advantage  of  being  more
progressive  than  tax  incentives,  because  small  private
donations  are  matched  dollar  for  dollar  regardless  of  the
donor’s  income,  while  the  benefits  of  a  tax  incentive  de-
pend  on  the  donor’s  tax  bracket.

3.  The  Eligibility  Requirements  Serve  a Compelling
Interest  in  Limiting  Assistance  to  Serious  Na-
tional  Candidates

Chapter  96  gives  assistance  to  a  primary  candidate
who  has  established  a  broad  base  of  public  support
by raising  $5,000  in  contributions  (counting  only  the  first
$250  of  contributions  from  any  person)  in  each  of  20
states.
This  eligibility  requirement
serves  two  functions.

26  U.S.C.  § 9033.

First,  it  requires  a  candidate,  before  receiving  any
federal  assistance,  to  establish  that  he  has  a  substantial
level  of  public  support—at  least  400  individuals  willing
to  donate  $100,000  in  contributions  of  $250  or  less.
The  right  of  Congress  to  impose  such  a  requirement  in
order  to  avoid  stimulating  an  artificial  proliferation  of
candidates  and  to  prevent  waste  of  federal  revenues  is
established  by Jenness,  American  Party,  and  Storer,  dis-
cussed  above.

Second,  like  the  ten-state  requirement  for  general  elec-
tion  assistance  under  Chapter  95,  it limits  federal  financ-
ing  to  candidates  who  have  shown  popular  support  in  a
number  of  states.
It  serves  an  important  interest  in
reserving  federal  resources  for  candidates  who  appeal  to
a  national  constituency  and  are  thus  serious  contenders
for  the  Presidency.  Absent  a  showing  that  an  unreason-
able  burden  is  imposed  on  any  candidate,  Congress  may
require  that  candidates  show  support  in  20  states—

232

233

equivalent  to  a  mirimum  of  89  electoral  votes—before
receiving  assistance  in  presidential  primaries.

Moore  v.  Ogilvie,  394  U.S.  814  (1969),  relied  on  by
appellants.  held  only  that  an  Illinois  statute  requiring
independent  candidates  to  obtain  200  petition  signatures
in  each  of  50  counties  was  invalid  where  52  percent  of
the  state’s  voters  lived  in  a  single  county  and  93  percent
of them  lived  in  the  49  most  populous  counties.  Jd.  at  816.
The  law  had  the  effect  of  preventing  the  93  percent  of
the  voters  who  lived  in  those  49  counties  from  forming
a  new  political  party  and  placing  its  candidates  on  the
ballot,  thereby  improperly  diluting  their  political  power.
Moore  does  not  apply  to  the  requirement  in  Chapter
96  that  candidates  for  a  presidential  nomination  establish
For  one  thing,  the  extreme  dis-
support  in  20  states.
crepancies  in  population  among  counties  in  Illinois  are
not  matched  by  the  variations  among  the  populations
of  the  50  states.***  More  important,  the  candidates  in
Moore  were  seeking  statewide  offices  that  they  could  win
by  obtaining  a  majority  of  the  statewide  vote.  By  con-
trast,  the  President  is  not  elected  by  nationwide  popular
vote  but  is  chosen  by  the  electoral  college,  where  the
states  award  their  electors  on  a  winner-take-all  basis.**°
Therefore,  “[c]ampaign  strategy  must  be  aimed  at  carry-
ing  states,  not  simply  at  increasing  popular  vote  totals.”
Ct.  App.  Op.  1567.  Since  no  candidate  can  win  the  Presi-
dency  without  attaining  a  majority  of  the  electoral  col-
lege,  Congress  is  entitled  to  reserve  federal  assistance

2°  California,  the  largest  state,  has  only  9.8  percent  of  the  na-
_  tion’s  population,  U.S.  Census  Bureau,  1970  Census  of  Population,
Number  of  Inhabitants,  Table  8,  at  I-48,  while  the  largest  single
county  in  Illinois  has  more  than  50  percent  of  the  Illinois  voters.

275  U.S.  Const.  art.  II,  §1,  amends.  XII,  XXIII;  see  Develop-
ments  in  the  Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1111,  1196-97  (1975).

i re

for  candidates  who  show  support  in  a  number  of  states.
See  Bode  v.  National  Democratic  Party,  452  F.2d  1302,
1308-09  (D.C.  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  404  U.S.  1019  (1972).

Moreover,  Chapter  96  is  not,  like  the  Illinois  statute
in  Moore,  a  restriction  on  access  to  the  ballot.  Rather,  as
noted  above,  it is an  amelioratory  law  designed  to  ensure
that  all  nominees  of  serious  national  political  movements
will  have  the  resources  to  run  effective  campaigns.  Con-
gress  has  not  forbidden  narrowly  based  presidential  can-
didates  to  run;  it has  simply  determined  that  such  candi-
dates  do  not  serve  a  sufficient  national  function  to  merit
federal  financial
Congress  has  substantial
latitude  in  setting  “limitation{s]  on  a  reform  measure
aimed  at  eliminating  an  existing  [financial]  barrier  to
[effective  candidacies}.”  *”*

assistance.

The  20-state  requirement  has  invigorated  the  early
stages  of  presidential  campaigns.  Instead  of  quietly  vis-
iting  a  few  financial  “angels,”  prospective  candidates  for
1976  are  touring  the  country  seeking  small  contributions
from  large  numbers  of  voters.  And  the  money  is  coming
in.  At  least  eight  presidential  candidates  have  already
reported  qualifying  for  matching  funds.
See  p.  123,
supra.  To  date,  the  requirement  has  proven  to  be  bene-
ficial,  not  invidious  as  appellants  contend.*”’

man,  supra  note  218,  at  387.

277  If  the  Court  should  nevertheless  conclude  that  the  20-state

For  these  reasons,  the  decisions  of  the  court  of  appeals

and  the  district  court  should  be  affirmed.

234

4.  Financing  of  Primaries  Does  Not  Discriminate
Impermissibly  Against  Candidates  Seeking  Nom-
ination  by  Another  Route

Appellants  argue  that  federal  financing  of  primary
campaigns  discriminates  invidiously  against  candidates,
like  appellant  McCarthy,  who  seek  election  by  a  route
other  than  the  primary.  Like  their  attack  on  convention
financing,  this  argument  asks  the  Court  to  rule  that
Congress  must  be  blind  to  the  differences  among  routes
to  general  election  victory.  Appellants’  argument  is based
on  the  absence  of  federal  financing  to  assist  in  defraying
the  “extensive  expenditures  of funds”  required  in  seeking
“ballot  status  in  the  various  states.”  Appellants’  Br.  164.
Precisely  this  contention  was  rejecte?  in  American  Party
v.  White,  415  U.S.  at  792  n.  24.  Indeed,  Chapter  96  goes
farther  than  the  Texas  law  upheld  there,  for  it  assists
candidates  who  seek  nomination  at  a  “nominating  con-
vention  or  caucus”  as  well  as  in  a  primary  election.  26
U.S.C.  § 9032(7).

Congress  did  not  invent  primaries  or  conventions  as
a  means  of  conferring  unfair  advantages  on  those  who
participate  in  them.  Both  primaries  and  conventions  are
traditional  means  by  which  American  political  parties
have  long  chosen  their  nominees.
Federal  law  neither
requires  nor  prohibits  any  primary  election  or  conven-
tion.  By  assisting  candidates  who  choose  to  run  in  pri-
maries,  Chapter  96  merely  recognizes  the  public  func-
tion  served  by  primary  elections  **  and  attempts  to  ease
the  burden  of  participating  in  them.

278  See  United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.S.  299  (1941);  Smith  v.
Allwright,  321  U.S.  649  (1944);  Terry  v.  Adams,  345  U.S.  461
(1953).

APPENDIX  A

The  Courts  Below  Properly  Refused  to  Rely  Upon
The  Statistical  Evidence  Offered  By  Appellants

The  district  court  has  made  a  series  of  findings  based
on  statistical  studies  submitted  by  both  the  appellants
and  the  nonfederal  appellees
(intervening  defendants
below).  Both  studies  analyzed  contribution  and  expendi-
ture  data  extracted  from  the  reports  filed  under  the
FECA  of  1971.  We  submitted  studies  of  the  1972  presi-
dential  election,  all  1972  House  and  Senate  elections,  and
all  1974  House  and  Senate  elections,  conducted  by  the
Common  Cause  Campaign  Finance  Monitoring  Project
(the  “Common  Cause  study”).  Findings  IIA,  II  J.A.
264-564.

Appellants  submitted  a  study  limited  to  expenditures
in  the  1972  Senate  elections  conducted  by  a  graduate
student,  Roland  J.  Cole,  for  purposes  uf  obtaining  his
dissertation’

J.A.  565-86.

(the  “Cole  study”).

ently  ...”  (id.  at  277),  and  that  “more  is needed  before  anyone  wiil

The  Cole  study  is based  on  a  series  of  crucial  mistaken

history—would  distort  the  practical  effect  of  the  expendi-

Ex.  3  to  Response  of  Defendants  and  Intervening  Defendants  to
Plaintiffs’  Proposed  Findings  of  Fact,  { 6.  The  district  court  found
that  several  fundamental  “reservations”  attached  to  Cole’s  state-
ment.  Findings  IIB,  { 73,  IIB  J.A.  585.

ture  limits  in  a  way  Congress  neither  intended  nor  en-
acted.
For  example,  if  a  candidate  borrows  $20,000,
spends  that  money  in  the  campaign,  and  then  raises
$20,000  to  repay  the  loan,  he  has  had  $20,000  available
for  use  in  the  campaign—not  $40,000.
Cole  uses  the
$40,000  figure  in  determining  campaign  spending,  and
there  is  no  question  that  it  is  the  $20,000  figure  that
applies  for  purposes  of  the  spending  limit.

Second,  Cole  counted  as  general  election  expenditures
amounts  that  would  be  counted  as  primary  expenditures
under  the  new  law.  Cole  defined  general  election  spend-
ing  as  including  expenditures  during  the  primary  period
where  the  candidate  was  unopposed  in  the  primary,  or
where  the  candidate’s  opponent(s)  received  less  than  ten
percent  of  the  primary  vote.  Findings  IIB,  {2(a),  IIB
J.A.  568.  However,  the  legislative  history  is  very  clear
that  an  unopposed  candidate  in  a  primary  can  charge
all  his  expenditures  during  the  primary  period  against
his  primary  spending  limit  and  not  count  them  against
his  general  election  limit.

When  8.  3044,  the  Senate  version  of the  FECA  Amend-
ments  of  1974,  was  being  debated  in  the  Senate,  it  pro-
vided,  in  proposed  Section  504(c),  that:  “[n]o  candidate
who  is  unopposed  in  a  primary  or  general  election  may
make  expenditures  in  connection  with  his  primary  or
general  election  campaign  in  excess  of  10  percent  of  the
limitation”  on  expenditures.  Senator  Cranston  introduced
an  amendment,  No.  1125,  to  strike  the  words  “primary
or”  from  this  section  of  the  bill.  He  argued  at  length
that  it  would  be  unfair  for  an  unopposed  candidate  in
a  primary  election  to  be  denied  the  opportunity  to  make
the  same  expenditures—and  thus  gain  the  same  level  of
recognition  looking  toward  the  general  election—as  his

4a

5a

opponents  facing  a  contested  primary  in  another  political
party.  Senator  Dole  agreed  with  Senator  Cranston,  as
did  the  Chairman  of  the  Rules  Committee,  Senator  Can-
The  amendment  was  accepted  without  objection,
non,
120  Cong.  Rec.  $5701-02  (daily  ed.  April  10,  1974),  and
no  later  version  of  the  legislation  contained  such  a  re-
striction.  See  18  U.S.C.  § 608(c).

The  FEC  has  confirmed  this  reading  of  the  statute.
FEC  Advisory  Opinion  1975-9,  40  Fed.  Reg.  36242  (Aug.
19,  1975).*  This  means  that  Cole  has  included  substantial
amounts  in  his  calculation  of  general  election  spending
which  will  be  treated  as  primary  spending  for  purposes
of  the  new  law,  and  his  general  election  spending  figures
are  thus  erroneously  inflated.

Third,  Cole  attempts  to  segregate  the  amount  spent
during  a  primary  from  the  amount  spent  in  a  general
election  based  on  the  reports  filed  under  the  1971  FECA.

gation  cannot  be  performed  with  any  accuracy  under  the
1971  Act,  because  (1)  it  is  impossible  to  determine  from
reports  under  the  1971  FECA  whether  payments  made
before  or  after  the  primary  date  were  made  for  services
provided  before  the  primary,  and  (2)  the  timing  of  the
reports  leaves  a  period  immediately  before  and  after  the
primary  ‘date  in  which  it  is  impossible  to  allocate  re-
ported  expenditures.  Cole  himself  admits  that  the  1971
reports  do  not  permit  one  to  determine  accurately  which
expenditures  made  between  a  month  before  and  a  month

after  a  primary  were  made  for  the  general  election,  and
that  candidates  tended  to  report  an  expenditure  on  the

Cole  Deposition  Tr.  at  50-55,  63-67.  Appellants  concede
as  much.  Appellants’  Br.  at  A2.

The  Common  Cause  study  on  which  we  rely  avoids
Cole’s  mistaken  assumptions  in  the  following  ways.
First,  where  loans  are  received  by  the  candidate  or  his
political  committees,  the  use  of  the  proceeds  of  these
loans  is  considered  a  campaign  expenditure,  but  the  re-
payment  of  that  loan  is  not  considered  a  separate  and
additional  campaign  expenditure.  Findings  IIA,  § 2(a),
ITA  J.A.  267.  This  avoids  the  inflated  double-counting
contained  in  the  Cole  study.

Second,  the  Common  Cause  study  follows  the  legisla-
tive  history  and  the  Commission  ruling  establishing  that
a  candidate  is free  to  spend  the  full  expenditure  limit  for
the  primary  period,  regardless  of  whether  he  faces  pri-
mary  opposition.

Third,  the  Common  Cause  study  does  not  attempt  to
segregate  primary  expenditures  from  general  election
expenditures,  since  this  cannot  be  done  with  any  accuracy
from  the  reports  filed  under  the  1971  FECA.  Findings
Instead,  it  compares  total
IITA,  {2(b),  ITA  J.A.  267.
campaign  expenditures  for  each  candidate  to  the  com-
bined  primary  and  general  election  expenditure  limits
under  the  new  laws.  This  is  the  only  meaningful  and
accurate  comparison  that  can  be  made  from  the  reports
filed  in  1972  and  1974  under  the  FECA.

Appellants  argue  that  the  combined  overall  limit  is  a
misleading  measure  because  some  candidates  had  only
Appellants’  Br.  at  A2-4.  The  fact
one  serious  contest.
that  some  candidates  had  only  one  serious  contest  does

6a
not  make  it  reasonable  to  conclude,  as  appellants  have,
that  in  such  races  the  total  amount  spent  during  both
election  periods  must  be  compared  with  the  spending
limit  for  just  one  period.  The  example  appellants  cite  |
In  1972,  as  appellants
in  fact  belies  their  reasoning:
note,  challenger  Holtzman  spent  about  $104,000  defeat-
ing  a  House  incumbent  in  the  primary  and  a  major
party  opponent  in  the  general  election.  Appellants  specu-
late  that,  since  the  primary  was  a  closer  race  than  the
general  election,  Holtzman  may  have  made  almost  all  of
her  expenditures  in  the  primary—thus  exceeding  the
$84,000  primary  limit.  On  the  contrary,  Holtzman’s  re-
ports  reveal  that  her  campaign  made  expenditures  of
over  $43,000  after  September  1,  1972—ten  weeks  after
the  primary.  If  even  half  of  that  $43,000  was  spent  to
influence  the  general  election,  then  her  primary  spending
was  below  the  $84,000  limit,  and  the  Common  Cause
figures  mislead  no  one.

Furthermore,  as  we  have  explained,  the  new  law  per-
mits  a  candidate  with  no  primary  opposition  to  spend
up  to  the  primary  ceiling  during  the  primary  period
without  counting  it  toward  his  general  election  ceiling.
Even  if  some  candidates  in  past  election  years  have
bunched  their  spending  in  the  general  election  period,
they  will  be  free  to  adjust  their  spending  to  take  advan-
tage  of  the  combined  ceilings.  Thus  the  overall  limits
are  not  misleading  measures  of  the  effect  the  FECA
Amendments  will  have  on  candidate  expenditures,  but
are  rather  the  best  available  measure  to  predict  their
effects.

Appellants  at  one  point  questioned  the  treatment  of
loans  in  connection  with  the  Common  Cause  study.’  Ac-

’The  questions  did  not  involve  the  double-counting  defect  of

the  Cole  study  referred  to  above.

7a

cordingly,  certain  important  sections  of  this  study,  Find-
ings  ITA,  {\] 52-61,  91-92,  IIB  J.A.  451-54,461-62  were  re-
computed  using  the  loan  assumptions  suggested  by  ap-
pellants;  the  additional  computations  show  no  material
See  Findings  IIA,  115,
changes  in  relative  impact.
IIB  J.A.  473-74.
(Br.  A4)  has
thus  been  mooted.

Appellants’  criticism

Appellants  attack  the  Common  Cause  expenditure  fig-
ures  as  misleading  on  one  other  ground—that  the  figures
are  low  as  a  guide  to  future  spending  because  the
reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  in  these  laws  will
Jd.  They  give  the
increase  the  cost  of  fund-raising.
example  of  Senator  Tower’s  expenditure  of  $30,000  for
computer  processing  of  contributions  and  expenditures.
However,  appellants  fail  to  state  that  Senator  Tower
spent  $2.3  million  in  his  1972  campaign  and  that  the
$30,000  fund-raising  cost  is  a  mere  1.3  percent  of  that
huge  amount.  Findings  IIB,  {\] 3,  16,  IIB  J.A.  570,  576.

Thus,  the  Common  Cause  studies  are  based  on  accurate
assumptions  that  make  them  the  best  available  for  pur-
Moreover,  in  both  1972  and  1974,
poses  of  this  case.
congressional  candidates  were  given  the  opportunity  to
review  and  correct  the  campaign  finance  totals  set  forth
for  them  by  the  Common  Cause  Monitoring  Project  (in-
cluding  their  total  contributions  and  total  expenditures).
The  Monitoring  Project  has  made  public  an  enormous
amount  of  campaign  finance  data  during  the  past  three
years,  dealing  with  thousands  of candidates  and  hundreds
of  registered  interest  groups;  it  has  rarely  received  any
requests  for  correction  of  such  campaign  finance  data
following  publication.  Findings  IIA,  § 1, IIA  J.A.  264-66.

The  Common  Cause  study,  which  is  the  basis  of  all
findings  in  Part  IIA  of  the  district  court’s  order  (cited

8a

as  “Findings  IIA”),  therefore  represents  the  most  com-
plete  and  best  available  collection  of  data.  The  court  of
appeals  was  correct  in  relying  exclusively  on  the  findings
based  on  that  study.  Ct.  App.  Op.  1540  n.105.

REPLY  BRIEF  OF  THE  APPELLANTS

INTRODUCTION

While  this  reply  brief  deals  shortiy  with  most  aspects  of.  p45..¢5

the  litigation,  our  treatment  of the  téea@ surrounding  the
appointment  and  powers  of  the  Federal  Election  Com-
mission  is considerably  more  extensive  for  two  reasons:  First,
there  have  been  important  developments  respecting  the
FEC  and  its  activities  since  the  Court  of Appeals  opinion,
and  even  since  our  main  brief,  which  bear  with  particular
sharpness  on  both  appellees’  ‘‘ripeness’’  arguments  and  on
the  substantive  questions.  Second,  in  the  court  below  ap-

2

3

pellees  took  varying  positions  vith  respect  to  the  Com-
mission-related  issues.  We  deferred  full  consideration  of
'  the  various  possible  defenses  in  order  not  to  burden  this
Court  with  material  that  might  later  become  superfluous.
The  briefs  filed  in  this  Court  by appellees  and  amici  have
justified  this  prudence.*

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

I.

Appellants  clearly  have  the  requisite  standing  to  bring
this  action,  notwithstanding  the  unclear  arguments  and
highly  selective  distillation  of the  record  presented  by in-
tervening  appellees  to  the  contrary.  Steffel  vy.  Thompson,
415  U.S.  452  (1974).  This  Court  should  decide  the  con-
stitutionality  of the  challenged  legislation  now,  rather  than
allowing  its abridgment  of essential  freedoms  to  work  their
profound  and  pernicious  effects  on  the  American  political
system.  Dombrowski  v.  Pfister,  380  U.S.  479, 491  (1965S).

Since  limiting  the  contribution  and  expenditure  of
money  for  political  purposes  clearly  implicates  First  Amend-
ment  freedoms,  and  given  the  positions  asserted  by the  ap-
pellees,  this  Court  is called  upon  to decide  whether  the  rule

*  We  will  cite  the  other  briefs  filed  in  this  case  as  follows:  Brief  for
the  Attorney  General  as  Appellee  and  for  the  United  States  as  Amicus
Curiae  —  “AG  &..  I’;  Brief  for  the  Attorney  General  and  the  Federal
Election  Commission  —  “AG  Br.  II";  Brief  of  the  Federal  Election
Commission  —  “FEC  Br.”;  Brief  for  the  Intervening  Defendants-
Appellees  —  “IA  Br.”*;  Brief  of  Senators  Hugh  Scott  and  Edward  M.
Kennedy.  Amici  Curiae  —  “SK  Br.”’;  Brief  for  United  States  Senator
Amicus  Curiae  —  ‘Metcalf  Br.”’;  the  other  amici  briefs  as
Lee  Metcalf.
appropriate;  and  our  own  main  brief in this  Court  —  “A.  Br.”

of such  cases  as New  York  Times  ¥. Sullivan,  376  U.S.  254
(1964),  which  protect  free  and  robust  political  debate,  or
the  result  in  such  cases  as  Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.
FCC,  395  U.S.  367  (1969),  and  United  States  v.  O'Brien,
391  U.S.  367  (1968),  which  had  previously  been  thought
distinguishable  from  the  Suliivan  rule  because  of  their
special  circumstances,  shall  govern  omnibus  regulation  of
the  political  process.  The  principles  declared  in O'Brien  it-
self plainly  require  a different  result  here;  here  speech  and
conduct  are  not  separable  and  the  governmental  interest  is
not  “unrelated  to  the  suppression  of free  expression.”  391
U.S.  at 367.

Il.

Nothing  appellees  have  brought  forward  indicates  that
the  challenged  legislation
will  have  any  substantial
remedial  impact  on  the  abuses  to  which  it  is  supposedly
directed.  Appellees  have  failed  to  show  that  ‘‘special  in-
terests’’  have  been  restrained;  they have  failed  to show  that
corruption  will  be  diminished  to  any  significant  degree;
they  have  failed
are
“skyrocketing’’;  and  they  have  failed  to  show  that  other
remedies,  more  effective  and  less  intrusive  on  con-
stitutionally  guaranteed  freedoms,  were  not  available.

to  show  that  campaign  costs

Appellees  fail  to  show  why  indiscriminate  campaign  ex-
penditure  limits  are  not  unconstitutional.  Nor  are  appellees
able  to  rebut  our  contention  that  the  expenditure  limits
discriminate
against
challengers;  their  argument  that  our  proof is “speculative”
is all  but  a  concession  by them  of what  is basically  a  com-
mon-sense  proposition.  Appellees’  inadequate  use  of elec-
tion-financing  statistics  and  their  unwillingness  to  accept
marginal-utility  analysis  only  further  reveal  the  weaknesses

of the  positions  they  espouse.  Expenditure  limits  are  in ef-

V.

Despite  their  effort  to  read  paid  political  advertisements
out  of the  First  Amendment's  protection  of press  freedoms,

their  specious  attempt  to distinguish  between  “personal ac:
tivity’  and  other  kinds  of partisan  political  actions;  their
argument  moreover  defeats  itself,  since  they  admit  the  in-

Congress  had  no  evidence  before  it that  any  “loophole”

Vi.

Appellees  fail  to  justify  their  extreme  position  that
Congress  has  virtually  limitless  power  to  regulate  con-
tributions  to  political  candidates.  Appellees’  paternalistic
arguments,  if accepted,  would  drastically  reduce  the  elec-
toral  opportunities  of  candidates  challenging  incumbent
federal  officeholders  and  newcomers  to  the  political
process.  The  pitifully  inadequate  evidence  appellees  adduce
on  the  ‘‘seed-money”’  point  cannot  sustain  their  arguments.

discriminatory  effects  of the  challenged  legislation  in  view
of  incumbent  access  to  enormous  perquisites  of  office.

to  disprove

have

they

Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241
(1974); Police  Dep't of Chicago  v. Mosley,  supra.

vil.

The  inadequacy  of appellees’  response  to our  “‘establish-
ment’’  argument  concerning  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal
Revenue  Code  (A.  Br.  145-52),  and  the  contradictions  and
obvious  fallacies
in  their  own  briefs  concerning  the
discriminations  against  minor  and  new  parties  and  in-
dependent  candidates,  fully demonstrate  the dangers  of un-
constitutional  government  entanglement  in  politics  when
direct  federal  payments  to  parties  and  candidates  are  em-
ployed.  Meek  v.  Pittenger,  421  U.S.  349  (1975).  If Subtitle
H is not  stricken  down,  Congress  will continually  debate  the
problem  of  ‘‘who  gets  what,  when,  and  how”  in  federal
political  subsidies  in  concrete  situations  involving  the
highest  potential  for partisan  abuse.

While  no  system  of allocating  funds  directly  to  parties
and  candidates  can  be truly equitable,  I.R.C.  Chapter  95 is
one  of the  more  irrational  and  discriminatory  methods  that
could  be  selected.  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23
(1968).  Minor  parties  will  be  thwarted  at  their  inception
or  preserved  after  their  popular  support  has  dissipated.
I.R.C.  Chapter  96  is  not  having  the  salutary  effects
hypothesized  by Congress;  quite  to  the  contrary,  it makes
money  more  rather  than  less  critical  in  pre-convention
presidential  campaigning.  Finally,  appellees  have  failed  to
rebut
that
un-
constitutional  discretionary  power  to  the  Secretary  of the
Treasury  in the  allocation  of federal  subsidies;  indeed,  ap-
pellees  themselves  anticipate  that  that  power  will  be  exer-
cised  in  a  way  that  may  deny  minor  parties  and  in.
dependent  candidates  even  the  post-election  pittances
which  the statute  purports  to guarantee  them.

Subtitle  H  grants

contention

our

7

such  argument.  Powell  v. McCormack,  395 U.S.  489 (1969).
An  agency  charged  with  enforcing  a  critical  area  of sub-
stantive  federal
civil  and  criminal
law  through  in-
terpretative and  substantive  rulemaking,  advisory  opinions,
investigations,  hearings  and  civil-enforcement  actions  con-
sists  of officers  of the  United  States;  thus  presidential  ap-
pointment  and  Senate  confirmation  are  constitutionally
required.  See  Watkins  v.  United  States,  354  U.S.  178 (1957)
and  Quinn  v.  United  States,  349  U.S.  155,  161  (1955).
Astonishingly,  Commission  counsel  disavows  powers  which
the  Commission  has  already  exercised  and  is  exercising
today.  And  Congress’  vigorous  use  of  its  veto  power  to
protect  incumbent  advantages  vividly  demonstrates  why an
arm  of  Congress  may  not  be  crowned  czar,  with  vast
discretionary  power,  over  the political  process.

Only  an  agency  not  under  the  thumb  of Congress  —  a
truly  independent  agency  —  can  constitutionally  enforce
whatever  federal  election  law  may  remain  in force  when  this
case  is over.  None  of the  blatantly  political  arguments  ad-
vanced  by the  FEC  can  justify  perverting  the  Constitution
in  the  indefensible  manner  attempted  here.  The  proper
remedy  is to terminate  the  Commission's  existence,  leaving
Congress  free  to  create  a  constitutionally  proper  en-
forcement  agency  if it so  chooses.

6

Vill.

of

the

lists

membership

Freedom  of association  clearly  encompasses  financial  con-
tributions  to  political  campaigns.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414
U.S.  51, 56-57  (1973).  This  Court  has  protected  lists  of con-
tributors  from  unwarranted  disclosure,  as  it has  similarly
controversial
protected
organizations.  Bates  v.  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516,  519
(1960).  Appellees  have  wholly  failed  to  show  that  this  case
in. olves  insufficient  ‘‘chilling”  of protected  First  Amend-
ment  rights.  Nor  have  they  even  attempted  to  defend  the
sweeping  overbreadth  of the  FECA  disclosure  requirements.
See  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  265-66  (1967).
Appellees  stand  wholly  mute  as  to  the  uniquely  burdensome
and  intrusive  requirements  of 2 U.S.C.  § 434e),  requiring
disclosure  and  reporting  by individuals  directly  to the  F EC
when  they  make  independent  expenditures.  See  Talley  v.
California,  362  U.S.  60 (1960).

IX.

Since  appellants,  as  active  participants  in  the  political
process,  are  directly  subject  to  FEC  regulation  and  affected
by it,  and  since  the  Commission’s  impact  on  politics  has
already  been  dramatic,  appellants’  challenge  to  the  FEC’s
mode  of  appointment  is  clearly  ripe,  and  they  have  the
requisite  standing  to  make  it.  Glidden  Co.  v.  Zdanok,  370
U.S.  530  (1962);  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  vy.  Tornillo,
418  U.S.  241,  247 (1974).

The  Commission,  because  of  its  method  of  appointment
and  Congress’  reservation  of  veto  power  over  its  rules,  is
concededly  an  ‘‘arm  of Congress’’  and  can  constitutionally
do  nothing  Congress  could  not  do  directly.  But  Congress
has  no  plenary  power,  exercisable  other  than  “by  Law,”  with
regard  to  the  federal  electoral  process,  nor  do the  so-called
historical  “precedents”  offered  by  the  FEC  support  any

:
}
H :
:

8

ARGUMENT
I.

Appellees’  Attempt  To Persuade  This  Court  To Forgo  Fully
Deciding  The  Constitutionality  of the  Challenged  Statutes
Has  No  Foundation  In  Law,  and  It  Invites  Disastrous
Consequences.

A.  Appellants  Have  Conclusively  Demonstrated  That

They Are Harmed  by the Challenged  Legislation.

pp.

86-90.

appellees

Intervening

Appellants’  standing  to  bring  this  action  is  challenged
only with  respect  to  the  issues  concerning  the Federal Elec-
tion  Commission  (“‘FEC’’);  that  challenge  is  dealt  with  in-
do,  however,
fra,
present  a highly  selective  distillation  of the  record  in  an  ef-
fort  to  show  that  appellants  are  not  “harmed”  by  the
challenged  legislation  (IA  Br.  23-24).  The  point  of  their
argument  is unclear.  Intervening  appellees  did  not  appeal
the  lower  court’s  ruling  that  appellants  have  standing  to
bring this  action,  op.  at  1528,  1582,’  nor  do they raise  stan-
ding  as  a  jurisdictional  issue  before  this  Court.  The
originality  of  intervening  appellees’  doctrine  is  thus  ex-
ceeded  only  by its  pointlessness.  And  it is clear  that  under
the  existing  precedents  appellants  are,  as  the  Court  of Ap-
peals  correctly  held,  sufficiently  directly  affected  by  the
challenged  legislation  that  they  may  bring  this  suit.  Steffel
v.  Thompson,  415  U.S.  452  (1974);  Civil  Service  Comm n v.
Letter  Carriers.  413  U.S.  548,  551-53  (1973);  Roe  v.  Wade,
410  U.S.  113,  124-25  (1973);  Baker  v.  Carr,  369  U.S.  186,
204 (1962).

Intervening  appellees’  incomplete  version  of the  record
includes  a  number  of  serious  distortions  and  outright

'  The  opinion  of the  Court  of Appeals  has  now  been  reported at 519
F.  2d 821.  For consistency,  appellants  will  in this  reply  brief  continue  to
cite  to slip opinion  pages.

— Oe eee ee ee

:
'
|

9

errors.  Throughout  their  discussion  of  appellants,  they
refer  to  the  allegedly  few  times  that  one  or  more  of  ap-
pellants  have  taken  action  which  would  today  violate  the
challenged  statutes.  (See, e.g.,  [A Br. 26, 27, 29, 30, 32.) But
the  contribution,  expenditure  and  disclosure  provisions  of
the  challenged  legislation  need  only  be  violated  once  to
send  to jail the  person  who  chooses  thereby  to  exercise  his
constitutional  rights.  Appellees  also  suggest  that  Eugene

ceptional,  is far from  unique.  Even  if he were,  that  would  be

10

a reason  to  accord  him  First  Amendment  protection,  not  to
deprive  him  of it; the  First  Amendment  protects  the  speech
not  only  of the  majority  but  of those  who  present  different,
controversial,  and  minority  viewpoints.  See  Cantwell  v.
Connecticut,  310  U.S.  296 (1940).

In  their  attempt  to  minimize  the  effect  of expenditure
limitations  upon  the  future  campaigns  of  appellants
Buckley  and  Steiger,  appellees  have  used  past  campaign
figures  uncorrected  for  inflation  and,  in  the  case  of
Buckley.  fictitious  figures  urged  by  them  on  the  District
Court  but  expressly  rejected  by it in  its  findings  (IA  Br.  28-
30).  The  inflation  rate  from  1966  to  1974  is  S2  percent.*
Therefore,  Representative  Steiger’s  1966  expenditure  of ap-
proximately  $64,000  is, in  1974,  equivalent  to  $97,000.  This
is  substantially  in  excess  of the  $84,000  limitation’  in  18
U.S.C.  § 608(c)  (1) (E).  The  inflation  rate  between  1970  and
1974  is  27  percent.*  Senator  Buckley  raised  approximately
$1.8  million,  Findings  III,  Buckley  17, p. 5, App.  I B-703.
not  the  $1.4  million  claimed  by  appellees  (IA  Br.  28).
Corrected  for  inflation  the  $1.8  million  becomes  $2.29
million.  The  expenditure  limitation  for  candidates  for
United  States  Senator  from  New  York,  based  on  1970
allowances  per  eligible  voter,  is approximately  $1.5  million.
Thus  Senator  Buckley  obviously  exceeded  the  FECA  ex-
penditure  limitations  in his  1970  race.

It  is thus  clear  that  the  challenged  statutes  prohibit  ac-
tivity  in which  the  appellants  have  engaged  in the  past  and

B.  Settled  Judicial  Policy  Dictates  That  This  Court

Should  Consider  the Challenged Legislation  Now.

12

What  precisely  they  would  have  this  Court  do,  however,
is far  from  clear.  They  do  not  specifically  ask  for  a holding
that  certain  portions  of the  challenged  legislation  are  not
ripe  for  decision.  If appellees  were  seriously  advancing  a
ripeness  argument,  they would  obviously  have  an  obligation
to  identify  to  this  Court  with  particularity  what  challenges
to  which  statutory  provisions  were  unripe;  this  they  never
do.  Their  lack  of specificity  indicates  that  their  contentions
are  mere  rhetorical  flourishes,  or  perhaps  an  attempt  to
camouflage  the  weakness  of their  substantive  arguments.

It may  be that  appellees  are  asking  the  Court  to rule  that
Congress  has  some  power,  for instance,  to limit  political  ex-
penditures  and  contributions,  but  to refrain  from  ruling  on
the  specific  limits  contained  in the challenged  statutes  until
some  unspecified  period  of  ‘‘experience’’  under  them  has
been  endured.  If that  is their  suggestion,  it verges  on  the
irresponsible;  they  would  be  asking  this  Court  to  permit
one  or  more  Congresses  and  Presidents  to  be  elected  pur-
suant  to  what  may  later  be  determined  to  be  an  un-
constitutional  statute  and  regulatory  system.  Appellees
elsewhere  in  their  briefs  express  great  concern  for  public
confidence  in  the  American  government.  (See,  e.g.,  IA  Br.
64.) They  nowhere  mention  the  effects  on  public  confidence
of a  perception  that  elections  are  being  conducted  under  a
scheme  that  protects  incumbent  officeholders  from  ef-
fective  challenge  by disaffected  citizens,  or  of a  discovery
some  years  from  now  that  our  national  legislature  and
Executive  have  been  elected  under  unconstitutional  laws
which  may  well  have  affected  the results.

vastus

The  invitation  to abstain  from  deciding  the critical  issues
raised  by this  case  is  mischievous  and  indefensible.  The
Court  of  Appeals,  quoting  Mr.  Justice  Frankfurter  in
United  States  y.  United  Auto  Workers,  352  U.S.  567,  570
(1957),  described  the issues  presented  here  as  “‘not  less than
basic  to a democratic  society”  (op. at  1510).  The  federal  ap-
pellees  themselves  have  declared  that  “these  appeals
present  substantial  questions  which  warrant  plenary  con-
sideration  by this  Court.’  To avoid  decision  now  would  be
to  ensure  that  when  the  issues  do  come  before  this  Court
again  —  as  they  surely  will  —  they  will  come  in  a  piece-
meal,  erratic,  and  disorganized  fashion.  If even  one  per-
son’s  candidacy  is jeopardized  by uncertainty  over  whether
these  statutes  are  constitutional,  that  is  one  person  too
many.  Moreover,  the  future  challenges  will  undoubtedly
arise  during  the  heat  of an  election  campaign,  with  all  the
attendant  pressures  for  a  hurried  decision  before  the  im-
pending  election  or  convention.  Cf.  Williams  v.  Rhodes.
393  U.S.  23  (1968);  O'Brien  v.  Brown,  409  U.S.  1 (1972):
ACLU  vy.  Jennings,  366  F.  Supp.  1041  (D.D.C.  1973),
vacated  as  moot  sub  nom.  Staats  v.  ACLU,  43  U.S.L.W.
5043 (1975).

As  this  Court  said  in  Dombrowski  v.  Pfister,  380  U.S.
479,  491  (1965S),  “[wle  believe  that  those  affected  by  a
Statute  are  entitled  to  be  free  of the  burdens  of defending
prosecutions,  however  expeditious,  aimed  at  hammering
vut  the  structure  of  the  statute  piecemeal,  with  no
likelihood  of  obviating  similar  uncertainty  for  others.”
More  recently,  this  Court  held  that:

“[w]here  the  inevitability  of  the  operation  of  a
Statute  against  certain  individuals  is patent,  it is

case  presents  difficult  questions  close  to  the  core  of our  system  of gov-
ernment.”  (AG  Br.  I 1. See also id.  at  93.)

The  instant  case  provides  a  far  more  immediate  situa-
tion  for  judi  «1  action  than  did  the  Regional  Rail
Reorganization  Act  Cases.  The  requirements  of  United
Public  Workers  vy.  Mitchell,  330  U.S.  75 (1947),  cited  by in-
tervening  appellees,  IA  Br.  40-41,  are  also  more  than  met
here.  The  challenged  statutes  are  now  in operation.’®  These
appellants  and  all other  participants  in the political  process
must  today  observe  their  strictures,  and  incur  the  chilling
effects  both  of their  prohibitions  and  their  vagueness,  at the
risk  of  criminal  prosecution.  Pronouncements  from  the,
FEC  now  comprise  almost  an  inch-thick  sheaf  of Federal
Register  pages.  Copious  evidence,  cited  throughout  the
briefs  of  all  parties,  indicates  the  profound  impact  the
challenged  legislation  is already  having  on  our  national  life.
Every  commentator  on  this  legislation  has  spoken  of the
drastic  effects  it  is  having  on  the  political  process  at  this
very  moment.

That  Expenditures
Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Demonstrate
Or Contributions  For  Political  Purposes  Are  Not  Protected
By The  First  Amendment.

Appellants  argue  that  the  contribution  or  expenditure  of
money  for  political  purposes  is  protected  by the  First
Amendment  under  such  cases  as  New  York  Times  Co.  v.
Sullivan.  376  U.S.  254  (1964);  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S.
214  (1966):  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418
U.S.  241  (1974),  and  the  other  cases  cited  at  A.  Br.  44-45.
Appellees  contend  to  the  contrary  that  such  contributions
and  expenditures  are  broadly  regulable  under  such  cases
as  United  States  v.  O.Brien,  391  U.S.  367  (1968),  and  Red
Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.  FCC,  395  U.S.  367  (1969)  (IA  Br.
74-78;  AG  Br.  II 18, 52).

As  the  Attorney  General  has  pointed  out,  cases  like
Sullivan,  Mills,  and  Miami  Herald  form  a body  of case  law
“that  shows  political  expression  to  be  at  the  center  of the
unbridgeable  freedom  of  speech,  that  protects  paid-for
political  speech,  that  fears  the deterrence  caused  by punish-
ment  after  speech  and  abhors  a  restraint  that  prevents
speech,  and,  finally,  a  case  law  that  protects  speech  even
when  self-seeking  and  deceptive”  (AG  Br.  I 14).  These  cases
have  been  thought  by all  to  state  the  general  rule  governing
political  debate.

It  is clear  from  their  arguments  and  the  breadth  of the
FECA  prohibitions  that  appellees  would  have  this Court
reject  the  rule  of Sullivan,  Miami  Herald,  and  Mills  in  favor
of Red  Lion  and  O'Brien,  cases  that  until  now  have  been
distinguished  from  the  general  rule  because  of the  special
circumstances  they  presented.  Thus,  intervening  appellees
candidly  argue  that  they  believe  that  political  debate
throughout  the  nation  must  be  “‘structured”’  by law  much
as  club  regulations  structure  a  college  debate,  with  a
panoply  of rules  about  equal  time,  equal  limits  and  the  like
(IA  Br.  101).  And  the  Scott-Kennedy  brief  argues  that

ee ee RR

17

Congress  may  regulate  candidate  expenditures  by  deter-
mining  what  level  of expenditures  affords  candidates  ‘‘an
adequate  opportunity  to  present  [their  views]  (SK  Br.  68).
It is clear  from  the  face  of these  arguments  that  appellees
would  have  this  Court  adopt  the  model  of regulated  speech
represented  by  Red  Lion,  and  reject  the  “uninhibited,
robust,  and  wide-open”  debate  called  for  by New  York
Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  supra,  376  U.S.  at  270.  Even  under
O'Brien,  however,  the  challenged  statutes  must  fall,
because  no  interest  “unrelated  to the  suppression  of free  ex-
pression,”  391  U.S.  at  367,  calls  for  such  overbreadth  (A.
Br. 44-45).

Appellees  seek  to  have  it both  ways.  On  the  one  hand,
they  and  the  supporting  amici  argue  that  candidates  for
federal  office  have  an  “almost  drug-like  dependence”  on
campaign  funds  (IA  Br.  45).  On  the  other  hand,  they  also
argue  that  “‘no  firm  relationship  has  been  established  be-
tween  out-spending  one’s  opponent  and  winning  elections”
(IA  Br.  148).  If, as  is agreed  by the  parties  and  found  by the
District  Court,  Findings  II, B 73,  p.  207;  App.  II B 585,  a
firm  relationship  between  spending  and  votes  has  not  been
established,  the  dependence  of candidates  upon  campaign
contributions  can  be  explained  in  only  one  way:  money  is
essential  to political  communication  with  voters.

Appellees  cannot  consistently  claim  that  political  can-
didates  need  to seek  campaign  money  and  at the same  time
argue  that  the  FECA  regulation  of campaign  contributions
and  expenditures  can  be distinguished  from  the  regulation
of political  communication.  To  say  “they  merely  limit  the
use  of money  in campaigns  ...  .”,  IA  Br.  91,  is identical  to
saying  ‘‘they  merely  limit  political  communication  in cam-
paigns.’’  Campaign  contributions  and  expenditures  are  the
vocal  cords  of  political  communication.  Without  the  for-
mer,  the latter  cannot  exist.

me eee
OE RR ry HEE

Appellees  note  that  in  Cox  vy.  Lousiana,  379  U.S.  559
(196S),  a  statute  banning  demonstrations  in  or  near

18

19

courthouses  was  upheld,  and  they  quote  this  Court  (IA  Br.
94) to the effect  that:

«|  We  are  not  concerned  here  with  such  a pure
form  of expression  as  newspaper  comment  or  a
telegram  by a citizen  to  a  public  official."’  Id.  at
564 (emphasis  added).

At  last  report,  newspapers  could  not  be  published  without
cost,  nor  telegrams  sent  at  no  charge.  Such  “pure  speech
obviously  requires  the  expenditure  of money  or  some  other
valuable  resource.  Prevent  the  expenditure  of the  money,
and  the  speech  is prevented  as  well.  Here,  a contribution  of
money  to  a  political  candidate,  party  or  committee  has
exactly  the  same  function  as  a  telegram  to  a  public  official
might  have,  or  as  the  paid  political  advertisement  held  in-
violable  in  New  York  Times  v.  Sullivan,  supra,  had.  It
registers  support  for  one  candidate,  and  opposition  to  his
opponents.

Appellees’  indifference  to  the  impact  of  the  FECA  on
political  communication  is  starkly  demonstrated  by their
position  on  the  issue  of  ‘‘seed  money”  (IA  Br.  120-29).
Whether  in beginning  a campaign  for a political  issue  or  for
a  political  movement,  ‘‘up-front’’  money  is needed  to  pur-
chase  those  initial  resources  —  offices,  telephones,  the
items  necessary  for  an  initial  mailing  —  to  get  the
movement  or  candidacy  off the  ground  (A.  Br.  121-24,  129-
38).  The  record  fully  demonstrates  this  proposition  as  to
issues  and  candidates  which  seek  change  and  which  thus  do
not  have  the  benefits  of media  attention  or  incumbency  in
political  office.  ‘Seed  money”  never  guarantees  that  either
the  idea  or  the  candidate  will  gain  public  acceptance.  It
merely  permits  the  public  to  be  exposed  to  new  ideas  and
new  candidates  and  to have  an  opportunity  to evaluate  their
message.

Seed  money  is  terribly  difficult  to  raise  through  small
contributions  both  because  of the  cost  of mailings  (agreed
by the  parties  and  found  by the  District  Court  to be 20 cents

_—

aN Teens

ee

a ee ee ros

. I OC ee

—— —— a

per contact  even  before  the  impending  postal  rate  increase)
and  because  small  contributions  follow  rather  than  precede
public  acceptance  (A.  Br.  136-38).  Appellees  would  cheer-
fully  sacrifice  the  presentation  of new  ideas  and  new  can-
didates  rather  than  permit  money  to be used  to increase  the
alternative  ideas  and  candidates  exposed  to the  public.

‘‘Merely”’  limiting  the  use  of money  for political  purposes
thus  affects  political  communication  as  much  as  “‘merely”’
severing  the  vocal  cords  affects  personal  conversation.  The
assertion  that  the  public  is  thus  saved  from  candidates
laden  with  improper  obligations  is clearly  overdrawn;  that
the  public  will  be  ‘‘saved”’  from  a robust  and  free  debate  is
certain.

Indeed,  appellees  and  supporting  amici  do  not,  in  the
last  analysis,  deny  that  the  remedies  they offer  rely heavily
on  lessening  the level  of political  communication.  Thus,  the
Scott-Kennedy  brief  admits  the  “‘full  awareness  that  the
volume  of political  debate,  the  size  of a  candidate’s  audi-
ences  and  the  frequency  with  which  they  are  reached  may  be
substantially  affected  by the  amounts  of money  that  he  is
allowed  to raise  and  spend  for campaign  purposes”  (SK  Br.
30).

Appellees  also  complain  that  the  appellants  unjustifiably
discuss  the merits  of the  FECA  (IA  Br.  39).  In First  Amend-
ment  cases,  discussion  of  the  congressional  rationale  is
necessary  since  legislation  trenching  on  political  com-
munication  must  be  justified  by compelling  governmental
interest.  A foolish  or  frivolous  statute,  or  a statute  which  in
fact  does  not  cure  the evils  it claims  to remedy,  or  one  which
is  only  partially  consistent  with  its  rationale,  cannot  be
based  on  a compelling  governmental  interest.  Erznoznik  v.
City  of Jacksonville,  43  U.S.L.W.  4809,  4810-13  (June  23,
1975);  Bigelow  v.  Virginia,  43  U.S.L.W.  4734,  4739-40
(June  16,  1975);  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo.
supra;  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258 (1967).  To some
extent,  therefore,  both  the  rationale  of  the  underlying

20

political  com-
legislation  and  its  actual  impact  on
munication  must  be  examined,  at  the  very  least  to  deter-
mine  whether  the  goal is compelling,  the  means  chosen  are
reasonably  related  to  that  goal,  and  that  subtle  but  highly
discriminatory  distinctions  are  not  involved.

The  Challenged  Legislation  Will  Not  Eliminate  the

Appellants  have  demonstrated  not  only that  the  challenged
legislation
a  compelling  govern-
mental  interest,  but  that  it in  fact  aggravates  rather  than
cures  most  of the  ills  alleged  to be its rationale  (A.  Br.  55-78).
Appellees  have  no  answer  for  these  arguments,  preferring

proper  influence  over  official  decisions  sought  or  wielded  by

challenged  legislation  has  little  impact."'

Although  there  is now a ceiling  on  aggregate  individual
contributions,  18 U.S.C.  § 608 (b) (3), the effect  of this  limit

''  Senator  Pastore,  Chairman  of  the  Senate  subcommittee  that  con-
sidered  S.  372  and  a  Senate  floor  manager,  has  recently  agreed  that

in  that  bill  .

22

23

will  be  dissipated  by the  numerous  other  statutory  provisions
that  allow  organized  economic  interests  to continue  to  con-
tribute  in very  large amounts.  Under  18 U.S.C.  § 608 (b) (2),
certain  kinds  of political  committees  are  permitted  to make
substantially  larger  contributions  than  either  individuals  or
other  committees.  The  statute  requires  that  such  com-
mittees  be registered  for more  than  six months,  receive  con-
tributions  from  50 or  more  persons  and  give to  five  or  more
candidates.  There  are  no  limits  on  how  many  such  com-
mittees  may  represent  particular  interests.

This  provision  thus  permits  organized  economic  interests
such  as  the  dairy  industry  or  the  AMA,  see  IA  Br.  47-50,  to
give more  money  to candidates  than  other  groups,  since  the
six-month  requirement  is  tailored  to  groups  which  are
already  well  organized  and  have  the  finances  to  remain  in
continuous  operation  and  contact.  Whereas  a  six-month
requirement  will  hamper  philosophically  motivated  groups
with  widely  dispersed  members  who  want  to  respond  to  an
issue  as  it arises,  or  who  coalesce  only  near  election  time,
organized  interest  groups  can  easily  adjust  to it.

Organized  interest  groups  will  also  be  better  able  to
‘proliferate’  committees  and  thus  expand  their  per-
missible  giving  to  candidates  more  than  will  ‘‘issue”’
groups.  Thus,  medical  associations  and  dairy  interests  can
organize  their  political  committees  at the  county  level,  each
county  organization  being  able  to  give  $5,000.  Civil-rights
groups,  anti-busing  groups,  groups  seeking  to  expand  the
welfare  state  or  groups  promoting  gun  control  will  also  have
‘six  month”  committees,  but  rarely  will  they  be  represent-
ed  by more  than  one  at  any  particular  time.  Because  their
in  continuous
adherents  are  dispersed  and  are
professional  contact,  only  one  or  two  such  committees  will
have  sufficient  name  recognition  to  attract  contributions.
The  six-month  requirement  thus  deters  proliferation  only
among  those  less  well-organized  philosophical  groups  and
not  among  organized  economic  interests.  The  examples  in

not

ee ee a

ere eeertees

appellees’  brief  of ‘special  interest’  contributions  are  ac-
tually  examples  of  activities
left  untouched  by  the
challenged  legislation.

Appellees  make  virtually  no  attempt  to answer  this  point
other  than  to  quote  a  wholly  inapposite  passage  from  the
legislative  history of the Act (IA Br.  131).  The  portion  of the
Conference  Report  quoted  therein  was  obviously  directed  to
organizations  with  a hierarchical  structure  and  intended  to
limit  the  activity  of political  committees  formed  vertically
therein.  In  the  case  of both  the  dairy  interest  and  AMA,
however,  horizontal  committees  representing  geographical
areas  within  the  states  are  involved  and  are  simply  unaf-
fected  by either  the provisions  of the statute  or  its legislative
history.  To be sure,  the  present  organization  consisting  of a
few  large  committees  will  have  to  be  changed  to  increase
the  number  of potential  $5,000  donors,  but  the  FECA  does
not  restrict  that.  The  need  to  avoid  the  status  of “‘affiliated
committees” (which  are  to be treated  as  one  committee  sub-
ject to a $5,000 contribution  limit)  is at most  a marginal  im-
pediment  to knowledgeable  ‘‘special  interests.”
