political  committees,  e.g.,

In  the  case  of  unions  and  corporations  the  challenged
legislation
financial
participation  in  campaigns.  They  are  also  free  to  pro-
liferate
local  union
might  have  one  committee  while  each  corporation  might
have  a stockholder  committee  and  an  employee  committee.
The  challenged  legislation,  moreover,  for the  first  time  per-
mits  corporations  which  are  government  contractors  —  the
largest  corporations  —  to  take  advantage  of the  so  called
“segregated  fund’’  provisions  previously  available  only  to
unions  and  corporations  without  government  contracts.  18
U.S.C.  § 611.  These  funds  can  be administered  by union  or
corporate  money,  subject  to  no  statutory  limits  whatsoever.
and  the  amounts  raised  can  be  directed  to  candidates
through  *‘six-month”’  political  committees.  Such  funds  are
not  required  to let their  contributors  have  any  say  about  the

24

25

candidates  to  whom  the  committees  contribute.  It  is thus
possible  for  a  union  or  corporation  to  raise  $100,000  by
spending  $100,000  from  its treasury.  As  a result,  the  temp-
tation  to  make  illegal  corporate  contributions  —  which  ap-
pellees  emphasize  so  heavily  —  is relieved,  since  corporate
money  can  now  be  used  to  raise  money  for  contributions.
The  illegal  conduct  relied  on  to  justify  the  FECA  is  thus
“reformed”  by providing  a functionally  identical  but  lawful
alternative.  Thus,  although  individuals  such  as  H.  Ross
Perot  may  not  now  directly  make  contributions  in excess  of
$25,000,  see  IA  Br.  55-56,  they need  not  use  personal  funds
for contributing  but  can  have  their  companies  raise  and  ad-
minister  ‘‘segregated  funds”  and,  through  them  make
unlimited  expenditures  from  the  corporate  treasury  to
raise  funds  for  candidates.  The  challenged  legislation  thus
facilitates  rather  than  limits  the  use  of campaign  money  by
organized  economic  interests.

The  chairperson  of the  Republican  National  Committee
recently  exhorted  the  National  Association  of  Manufac-
turers  to  take  advantage  of the  provision  of the  law  per-
mitting  corporations  to  communicate  with  their  stockhold-
ers  and  their  families.  She  declared:

..  under  the  new  law  corporations  can,  for  the
‘*
first  time,  set  side  separate,  segregated  funds  to
establish  and  administer  political  action  com-
mittees.  While  corporate  money  still  can  not  be
put  into  the  committee  fund,  corporate  money
may  be  spent  to  administer  the  fund.  So  far,
through  the  end  of  September  99  corporations
have  registered  political  action  committees  —
there  should  be  999,  or  9.999  —  and  still  it would
be  only  a  beginning.”’  Speech  of  Mary  Louise
Smith  to  National  Association  of  Manufacturers
Public  Affairs  Conference,  Oct.  23,  1975.

EE

While  the  challenged  legislation  carefully  protects  and
fosters  campaign  spending  by economic  interest  groups,  it
does  nothing  to end  such  abuses  as  giving  to opposing  can-
didates  or  making  large  late  contributions  which  avoid  ef-
fective  disclosure.  What  the  challenged  legislation  does
regulate,  and  with  a  vengeance,  are  truly  ideological  con-
tributions  —  those  very  contributions  which  fall  most
clearly within  the term  “pure speech.”

B.  Other  Remedies  Are  More  Effective  and  Less  In-

trusive.

We  have  previously  enumerated  a  number  of  less  in-
trusive  and  more  efficient  remedies  for the  specified  abuses
(A.  Br.  75-78).  Appellees,  preferring  the  condemnation  of
corruption  in  the  abstract  to  the  rigorous  examination  of
the  FECA,  have  not  responded  to  these  suggestions  or
shown  why these  will  not  effectively  remedy  the  problems.

Nor  have  they  convincingly  responded  to  appellants’
Suggestion  that,  apart  from  carefully  drafted  remedies
responding  precisely  to  a demonstrated  abuse,  disclosure  to
the  voter  is not  only  an  adequate  remedy  but  the  one  most
consistent  with  democratic  values.  In their  brief,  for exam-
ple,  they  emphasize  that  $20,000,000  was  received  by
President  Nixon  in  1972  in  large  contributions  from  153
people  (IA  Br.  65-66).  It  is  also  true,  however,  that
$16,000,000  was  raised  by the  Nixon  campaign  from  large
contributors  before  April  7, 1972  —  obviously,  given  the  un-
usually  early  date,  in  the  belief  that  the  contributions  would
not  be  disclosed.  That  strongly  suggests  that  disclosure  to
the  voter  is the  best  deterrent  to  improper  conduct  or  “‘ex-
cessive’’  reliance  on  large gifts.

26

C.  Appellees  Have  Not  Show  Any  Undue  Increase  in

the Cost  of Campaigning.

Appellees  again  rely entirely  upon  a comparison  with the
cost-of-living  index  to demonstrate  an  ‘arms  race”  in  cam-
paign  costs.  As  we  demonstrated  earlier,  the  increases  in
campaign  costs  are  readily  explainable  on grounds  not
suggesting  any  crisis  or  illness  in the body politic (A.  Br.  S7-
61).  Merely  as  one  example,  advertising  expenditures  have
increased  at  a  rate  greater  than  that  of  campaign  ex-
penditures  (A.  Br.  57).  To the  extent  that  candidates  for of-
fice  must  compete  with  commercial  and  other  advertisers
for  the  resources  employed  in  advertising,  they  necessarily
have  to  pay  more.  That  demonstrates  only  that  a  limit  on
campaign  expenditures  will  necessarily  limit  speech.

Moreover,  if the  ‘“‘sky-rocketing”’  increase  in  the  cost  of
campaigning  continues  to  be  relied  upon  by appellees,
surely  they  are  under  some  obligation  to  explain  why  the
cost  of the  frank  has  risen  at  twice  the  rate  of the  cost  of
campaigns  generally  (A.  Br.  57-58)  and  yet  is not  limited  by
the  challenged  legislation.

27

IV.

Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Demonstrate  That
the  Limitations  on  Candidate  Expenditures Are
Constitutional.

A.  Appellees  Have  Demonstrated  No  Compelling  Need

Justifying  Limitations  on  Candidate  Expenditures.

Appellants  have  shown  that  expenditure  limits  are  un-
constitutional  per se  and  serve  no  compelling  state  interests
(A.  Br.  79-87).  Indeed,  expenditure  limits  effectively  reduce
competition  for  federal  elective  office  and  are  contrary  to
the  pervasive  national  interest  in  full  and  robust  political
debate."”

Appellees’  response  is confused  at  best.  They  argue  first
that  limitations  on  expenditures  will  aid  the enforcement  of
limitations  on  contributions  by reducing  the  temptation  to
raise  large  contributions  (IA  Br.  79-80).  Even  if that  were
true,  a  marginal  increase  in enforcement  efficiency  cannot
justify  such  drastic  limitations  on  political  speech. See,  e.g..
United  States  v. Robel,  389 U.S.  258,  262-67  (1967).

Appellees  next  argue  that  “‘equalizing”’  expenditures  will
equalize  political  opportunity  (IA  Br.  81-86,  AG  Br.  II  23-
27).  As  we  demonstrate  in  our  main  brief,  94-107,  and  in

.

‘*  Senator  Muskie,  a  strong  proponent  of the  FECA  Amendments.
has  recently  voiced  his  support  for our  position:  “  .
.  since  passage  of
the  act  last  year,  I  have  had  increasing  doubts  about  the  con-
stitutionality  and  wisdom  of [expenditure  limits]...  .  The  right  to
communicate  one’s  views  to the voters,  and  the right of voters  to make  a
choice  among  candidates  based  upon  informed  judgment  of  what  a
candidate  stands  for,  is basic  to  our  democratic  process.  .
.  Spending
limits  which  restrict  that  access  may,  in  my  opinion,  be  inconsistent
with  the  First  Amendment  freedoms  embodied  in  the  Constitution”
121  Cong.  Rec.  S 17888  (daily ed.  Oct.  8, 1975).

.

een
- EO eee

28

29

the  next  subsection,  the  particular  limits  on  expenditures
are  by  no  means  equal  in  view  of  their  exclusion  of  the
many  financial  and  other  factors  favoring  incumbents.  In
any  event,  New  York  Times  Co.  vy.  Sullivan,  Mills  v.
Alabama,  and  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  vy.  Tornillo,
supra,  stand  four-square  in the  path  of any  attempt  to  ap-
ply  Red  Lion,  supra.  to  the  entire  political  process,  for
‘equalizing’  speech  does  not  reflect  a  legitimate  “‘non-
speech”’  interest  of the  government.

Appellees  claim  that  “‘reducing  ‘noisy’  or  ‘excessive’
communication  is not  a  goal  of these  limits”  (IA  Br.  91  n.
104).  That  is flatly  inconsistent  with  one  of their  principal
justifications  for  the  Act,  just  discussed:  that  it  will
“equalize”  opportunities  for  political  success.  That  means,
if it  means  anything,  that  the  political  speech  of some  is
‘excessive’  and  must  be  curtailed  in  the  interest  of
equality.  It  is  understandable  that  appellees  are  uneasy
with  their  own  argument,  since  such a legislative  purpose  is
plainly  not  “unrelated  to  the  suppression  of  free  ex-
pression.”  United  States  v.  O'Brien,  391  U.S.  367,  377
(1968),  and  thus  constitutionally  is wholly  illegitimate.

Appellees  strenuously  argue  that  the  FECA  limits  do not
regulate  the  content  of speech.  (See,  e.g..  IA  Br.  91.)  That
contention  is plainly  wrong,  since  the  statute  on  its face  ap-
plies  only  to  expenditures  for  speech  which  advocates  the
election  or  defeat  of a candidate.  See  Gore  Newspapers  Co.
v.  Shevin,  397  F.  Supp.  1253,  1256-57  (1975).  The  statute
thus  depends  upon  a distinction  between  speaking  for  or
against  candidates  and  speaking  on  issues  or  non-political
subjects  —  a  distinction  based  squarely  on  content.  Cf.
Thomas  vy.  Collins,  323  U.S.  516 1945).

The  common  thread  running  throughout  appellees’  con-
tentions  is their  expansive  view  of congressional  power  over
political  communication.  They  and  the  supporting  amici
argue  that  Congress  may  regulate  expenditures  without

limit  so  long  as  it leaves  candidates  for  federal  office  ‘‘an
adequate  opportunity  to  present  themselves  to  the  public”
(SK  Br.  68).  What  opportunity  is  ‘‘adequate”  is  to  be
decided  by  Congress,  and  its  judgment  is  to  be  shown
deference  by this  Court.'*

Central  to  appellees’  argument  is  their  attempt  to
misstate  our  position.  We  carefully  defined  the  cir.
cumstances  in  which  the  First  Amendment  bars  the
regulation  of campaign  expenditures  (A.  Br.  45-48),  and  we
also  defined  the  narrow  permissible  zone  of regulation  (id.
51-54).  Appellees  blur  this  distinction  by asserting  that  we
deny  any  congressional  power  to  regulate  the  expenditure
of campaign  funds  (IA  Br.  74).  To the contrary,  we  explicitly
argued  that  Congress  can  enact  laws  limiting  the  ex-
penditure  of  campaign  funds  for  non-political,  ie.  non-
speech,  purposes  (A.  Br.  77).  The  FECA  cuts  sharply  in the
opposite  direction:  while  limiting  expenditures  for  speech

'»  The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  contends,  citing  Communist  Party  v.
Subversive  Activities  Control  Board,  367  U.S.  1, 94-95,  169-70  (1961)
and  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  271,  285  (1967),  that  this
Court  ‘‘will  not  review  congressional  findings  of fact even  in cases  under
the  First  Amendment”  (SK  Br.  49).  Both  of those  cases  involved  the
Communist  Party  and  the  security  of this  country.  Even  so,  in Robel.
the  statute  was  invalidated,  and  the  majority  opinion  stated  that  “the
concept  of national  defense  cannot  be deemed  an  end  in itself justifying
any  exercise  of legislative  power  designed  to  promote  such a goal."’  389
U.S.  at  264.  See  also  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.
713,  719(1971)  (Black,  J., concurring);  729-30 (Stewart,  J., concurring).

In  United  States  v.  United  States  District  Court,  407  U.S.  297,  320
(1972),  this  Court  stated  with  regard  to  the  facts  at  issue  there:  “{wle
cannot  accept  the  Government's  argument  that  internal  security  mat-
ters  are  too  subtle  and  complex  for judicial  evaluation.  Courts  regularly
deal  with  the  most  difficult  issues  of our  society."’  In  truth,  therefore.
this  Court  does  examine  facts,  particularly  in  First  Amendment  cases.
where  necessary  to  safeguard  fundamental  liberties.  To  do  otherwise
would  simply  result  in  abdication  of  the  judiciary's  responsibility  to
protect  the Constitution.

30

purposes  in campaigns,  it legitimizes  (and  places  no  limits
on)  the  use  of campaign  funds  for  non-speech  purposes.  2
U.S.C.  § 439a.

Since  voter  persuasion  is  at  the  heart  of freedom  of ex-
pression,  and  expenditures  are  essential  for  political  com-
munication,  Congress  may  not  limit  such  speech  in the  ab-
sence  of  a  compelling  need,  and  in  no  event  unless  ‘‘the
governmental  interest  is  unrelated  to  the  suppression  of
free  expression,”  United  States  vy.  O'Brien,  supra,  391  U.S.
at  377,  which  is not  the  case  here.'*  We  urge  this  Court  to
reject  appellees’  extreme  view  of  a  virtually  unlimited
congressional  regulatory  power  over  political  campaigns.

B.  The  Particular  Limitations  on  Candidate  Expendi-

tures  Are  Discriminatory  and  Unconstitutional.

Appellants  have  demonstrated  that  the  FECA  on  its face
systematically  discriminates  against  challengers  to  in-
cumbent  officeholders  (A.  Br.  94-104).  As  we  showed
in  our  main  brief,  incumbents  have  enormous  financial
resources  in  the  form  of  the  frank,  office,  staff,  etc.,
available  to  them  in sums  which  exceed  by far  the  amounts
spent  by challengers  on  all  campaign  expenses  (A.  Br.  99-
103).  Even  when  scrupulously  employed,  these  perquisities

'*  Appellees’  attempts  to  distinguish  Deras  v.  Myers,  535  P.2d  S41
(Ore.  1975)  and  Bare  v.  Gorton,  526  P.2d  379  (Wash.  1974)  from  the
present  case  are  futile,  as  even  a casual  reading of those  cases  will  show.
See  Note,  State  Campaign  Spending  Restriction  Violates  the  First
Amendment,  49  Tulane  L.  Rev.  1146  (1975).  Certainly  there  were
provisions  in those  statutes  not  involved  here,  but  the courts  did  directly
and  specifically  address  the  question  of  limits  on  campaign  ex-
penditures.  They  held  such  limits  unconstitutional.

31

give  congressional  incumbents  immense  financial  ad-
vantages  over  challengers.'*

Appellants  have,  moreover,  shown  that  the  use  of these
perquisites  often  performs  campaign  functions.  It  is  well
known,  for example,  that  staff members  frequently  perform
essentially  political  tasks,  cf.  Findings  I, 28, p.  19,  App.  II
A  21,  and  it  is  agreed  by  the  parties  and  found  by  the
District  Court  that  peak  periods  of use  of the  frank  are  just
before  elections,  id.,  34, p. 21, App.  IIA  23.  In the  first  two
weeks  of October,  1974,  for example,  21.8  million  pieces  of
congressionally  franked  mail  were  sent  out.  Wall  Street
Journal,  June  2,  1975,  at  S, col.  1.  None  of this  is a  cam-
paign  expenditure  under  the  FECA,  while  every  piece  of
mail  sent  by  a  challenger  comes  within  the  expenditure
limits.  It  cannot  be  seriously  contended  that  these  21.8
million  pieces  of  franked  mail  do  not  involve,  in  amici’s
phrase,
[in-
cumbents]  to the public”  (SK  Br.  68).

Although  appellees  make  much  of  their  fear  of  the
corruption  inherent  in  private  financing,  they  are  virtually
silent  on  this  flagrant  violation  of principles  of elemental
fairness.  Indeed,  only  two  wholly  transparent  arguments
are  made  to  support  the  gross  unfairness  of  the  FECA
limits  on  candidate  expenditures.  First,  appellees  ask  this
Court  to  ignore  the  vast  perquisites  of congressional  office
since  they  are  not  part  of the  FECA  itself  (IA  Br.  112-13).
They  thus  view  the  limitations  on  expenditures  in isolation
from  all  other  laws  or  appropriations  passed  by  Con-

of  the  Conservative  Party  of  New  York,  the  Mississippi  Republican

office
as  challengers  at  every  level.

gress,  no  matter  how  functionally  similar  and  no  matter
what  discriminations  are  thus  created.'®  This  ostrich-like

Second,  appellees  suggest  there  are  other  remedies  for
the  gross  imbalance  in campaign  resources  dictated  by the
FECA,  id.  These  remedies  remain  unspecified,  apart  from
the  suggestion  that  a  lawsuit  might  be  brought  by a  can-
didate  challenging  his  incumbent  opponent’s  use  of  the
frank  (IA  Br.  113).  This  is  a  totally  inadequate  response.
Appellants’  contention  is  not  that  incumbent  perquisites
are  ‘“‘abused”’  but  that  the  FECA  itself,  by subjecting  every
expenditure  by  challengers  to  the  limits,  affirmatively
discriminates,  since  only some  expenditures  by incumbents
are  covered.  Incumbent  perquisites  perform,  quite  in-
escapably,  a  dual  function:  they  are  perfectly  legitimate
necessities  in  helping  Congressmen  do  their  jobs  properly;
but  they  a/so  have  inevitable  political  effects  and  in  that
sense
functionally  interchangeable  with  expenses
challengers  must  make  from  contributions  they  can  raise

are

‘©  Even  beyond  the  frank,  congressional  staff  and  other  incumbent
benefits  expressly  excluded  from  the  expenditure  limits  (see,  e.g..  39
U.S.C.  § 3210(f)  ), the  FECA  does  not  on  its  face  regulate  what  are
euphemistically  called  congressional  ‘‘office  accounts.'’  These  accounts
finance  many  of the  activities  by which  incumbent  office  holders  main-
tain  contact  with  their  constituents.  Private  individuals  and  interest
groups  are  currently  free  to  give  unlimited  and  undisclosed  amounts  to
congressional  incumbents  for  these  ‘‘accounts.”’  There  can  be  little
doubt  that  such  accounts  have  a  political  impact,  for  the  Senate,  in
rejecting  FEC  regulations  which  would  have  treated  them  as  campaign
funds,  clearly  indicated  that  they were  of value  in campaigns,  121  Cong.
Rec.  17676  (Senator  Bell);  17880  (Senators  Hatfield  and  Stevens):
(Senators  Pell  and  Ford)  (daily  ed.  Oct.  8,  1975).  The  extent  to  which
FECA  will  be  construed  to  regulate  “office  accounts”  is thus  solely  in
the  hands  of the  FEC  and  incumbent  Members  of Congress.  Because
all  contributions  to  a  non-officeholding  challenger  are  treated  as  cam-
paign  contrhutions.  the  challenged  legislation  is  unconstitutionally
discriminatory  on  its  face.

and  must  charge  against  their  ‘“equa!l”’  expenditure  limits.
Victims  of  discriminatory  legislation  trenching  on  First
Amendment  freedoms  should  not  be  required  to  institute
actions  of  doubtful,  if not  frivolous,  merit  against  other
statutes  in  the  hope  that  the  discrimination  will  be
lessened.'’  Nor,  of  course,  do  we  argue  that  “the  Con-
stitution  requires  the  government  to  provide  challengers
with  some  offsetting  method  of attracting  ballots’  (SK  Br.
65);  we  do  argue  that  the  Constitution  forbids  government
to  prevent  challengers  from  using  self-help  in  employing
offsetting  methods  —  i.e.,  expenditures  equalling  or  ex-
ceeding,  or  at least  coming  closer  than  the  limits  permit  to,
the incumbent’s  real  expenditures  —  if they can.

Appellees  and  supporting  amici  ask  this  Court  to  reject
our  claim  of  discrimination  against  challengers  on  the
grounds  that  it is  ‘‘speculation’’  requiring  ‘‘elaborate  fac-
tual  inquiries’  for  which  the  Court  is unsuited  (SK  Br. 47).
We  argue  that  when,  for  example,  there  is a  disparity  be-
tween  candidates  in  name  recognition,  the  candidate
without  high  name  recognition  must  be  able  to  spend  at  a
high level  if that  candidate  is to have  a viable  chance  of vic-
tory,  regardless  of  what  his  opponent  spends.  The  name
recognition  of challengers  is typically  lower  than  that  of in-
cumbents,  and  for that  reason  —  and  because  of the  finan-
cial  perquisites  available  to  incumbents  which  surely  in-
crease  name  recognition  —  limitations  on  spending  will
generally  hurt  challengers.  It  can  hardly  be  denied,  for
example,  that  challengers  seeking  to  defeat  men  with  the
high  name  recognition  of amici  Senators  Scott  and  Ken-

nedy  would  have  to spend  at  a high level  merely  to gain the

That  is an  a  priori,  common-sense  proposition.  It is no
more  “‘speculation”  than  the  proposition  that  an  “equal
space”  requirement  will  deter  the  printing  of controversial
material.  See  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,
supra;  see  also  Mills  vy. Alabama,  supra.  Indeed,  those  very
cases  were  decided  on  the  basis  of  generalized  “‘factual’”’
propositions  unsupported  by record  evidence.  That  a  sup-
porting  record  exists  in this  case  merely  adds  to the force  of
appellants’  claims.

Appellees  further  argue  that  deference  should  be  shown
to  Congress’  judgment  about  whether  a particular  limit  is
discriminatory  against  challengers.  That,  of  course,  asks
.  [its]  own
Congress  to  be  accepted  as  the  ‘‘judge  in  .
cause,”  The  Federalist,  No.  10,  Cooke  ed.  1961,  p. 59,  and
imperils  the  First  Amendment  by permitting  government  to
regulate  speech  on  the  basis  of dubious  findings  of “Tact.

.

‘8  Appellees  assert  that  Dr.  Roland  Cole  agreed  that  “where  in-
cumbents  exceeded  both  the  ceilings  and  their  challengers’  spending.
the  ceilings  would  have  benefitted  the  challengers”  (IA  Br.  146  n.  162).
This  is  a complete  misrepresentation  of what  Dr.  Cole  said.  He  agreed
only to  the obvious  arithmetical  fact  that  in the situation  just described,
if both  challenger  and  incumbent  were  limited  to  and  spent  the  same
amount  of money.  then  the  difference  in  the  amount  of expenditures
between  the  two  candidates  would  have  been  narrowed.  Cole  dep.  tr.
304-05.

This  miscitation  is  typical  of appellees’  contir.ued  unwillingness  to
understand  marginal-utility  analysis.  The  fact  that  two  candidates
spend  the  same  amounts  where  in other  circumstances  they would  have
spent  different  amounts  quite  obviously  tells  us  nothing  about  the
marginal  value  of each  additional  dollar  of expenditures  to  those  two
candidates.  As  we  have  stressed.  the  marginal  value  of extra  spending
to  less-well-known  challengers  is  plainly  greater  than  the  marginal
value  of the  same  amount  spent  by well-known  incumbents.  See  A.  Br.
97.

35

Any case  arising  under  the  First  Amendment  can  be made
a  “‘fact’’  case.  Thus,  the judgment  as  to whether  requiring
newspapers  to  give  equal  space  to  those  they  editorially
criticize  outweighs  the  deterrent  effect  on  the  printing  of
controversial  matter  would  be,  under  appellees’  position,
a  “‘factual’’  judgment  to  be  made  by the  legislature.  But
see Miami Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  supra.

In  any  event,  Congress’  actual  judgment  as  to  the
political  impact  of the  use  of their  perquisites  not  regulated
by the  FECA  is  abundantly  demonstrated  by the  debate
over  “‘office  accounts,”  a  debate  which  demonstrated  the
firm  conviction  of the  Senate  that  such  accounts  affect  the
results  of elections.  See p. 32 n.  16, supra.

The  evidence  fully and  amply  establishes  the  proposition
that  expenditure  limits  discriminate  against  challengers.
Neither  appellees  nor  supporting  amici  rely  on  any  statistics
other  than  the  so-called  “‘overall  combined  limitation,”’
which  has  no  relevance  to  the  actual  impact  of the  stat-
ute.’  All  parties  agree  that  the  FECA  does  not  permit
candidates  to  aggregate  primary  and  general-election
spending,  and  to  use  the  overall  limit  as  a  measure  of

'*  Appellees  criticize  Cole,  Campaign  Spending  in  Senate  Elections
(1975).  (See  IA  Br.  1a-8a.)  The  criticism  this dissertation  received  at the
Kennedy  School  was  no  more  than  is  typically  directed  at  any  such
scholarly  effort  as  part  of the  academic  exercise.  The  criticism  did  not.
moreover,  in any way  relate  to the materials  utilized  by appellants.

The  vociferous  objection  voiced  by appellees  to  Cole's  inclusion  of
repaid  loans  in expenditures  (IA  Br.  Appendix  A,  2a)  has already  been
addressed  by the  FEC.  The  Cole  method  was  upheld,  and  the  Common
Cause  method  rejected.  See  FEC  Notice  1975-51,  40  Fed.  Reg.  44698.
44699,  § 100.7(aX1XiXAX1)  (September  29,  1975).  The  Common  Cause
figures  thus  understate  expenditures.  Appellees’  denunciation  as
“surely,impossible’’  (IA  Br..  Appendix  A.  2a) of a  classification  which
the  Commission  has  found  required  by the statute  suggests  also  that  the
Statute  is noi  without  serious  problems  of vagueness.  (See  A.  Br.  211-
20.)

|

36

37

comparision  with  spending  in  prior  elections  is  thus
utterly  misleading.  The  Act  clearly  and  unequivocally
distinguishes  between  primary  spending  and  general-
election  spending  and  dues  not  permit  candidates  to spend
up  to  the  ‘‘overall  limit’’  in  the  crucial  time  just  before  a
general  or  primary  election.  18  U.S.C.  § 608(c).  Congress
has  mandated  the  primary-general  election  distinction;  and
candidates  subject  to its terms  must  be concerned,  not  with
the  overall  combined  limit,  but  with  what  they can  spend  in
the  particular  election  they face.”°

Even  using  the  overall  combined  limitation  as  a  guide,
however,  it is apparent  that  successful  challengers  do spend
at  a high  level.  Once  any  attempt  is made  by any  method  to
segregate  primary  and  general-election  spending,  the  fact
that  successful  challengers  are  ‘‘high’’  spenders  (relative  to
the  limits)  becomes  clear  beyond  peradventure.  (See  A.  Br.
94-99.)

Indeed,  it is hard  to  understand  how  the  proposition  that
low  limits  will  discriminate  against  challengers  can  be
disputed.  The  proposition  was  widely  accepted  in  the
Congress  and  embraced  by such  enthusiastic  supporters  of
the  legislation  as  John  Gardner  and  Fred  Wertheimer  of
Common  Cause  and  Senators  Clark,  Biden,  and  Cannon.
(See  A.  Br.  98.) Only  now,  in the  context  of this  litigation,  is
the proposition  disputed.

A  less  frivolous  argument  is that,  even  if spending  is of
marginally  greater  value  to challengers  than  to  incumbents,
the  balance  of  advantage  in  a  system  of  unlimited  ex-
penditures  may  still  lie  with  incumbents  if they  are  able  to

©  Appellees  baldly  state  that  it is not  possible  to  segregate  primary
from  general-election  expenditures  in  past  elections  ‘with  reasonable
accuracy”  (SK  Br.  32  n.  11;  IA  Br.  136  n.  152).  They  simply  ignore  the
agreed  finding  that  it  is  possible  to  do  precisely  that  —  ‘with
reasonable  accuracy.”  Findings  II.  B2(c).  p.  187;  App.  II  B 569;  see  A.
Br..  Appendix  A.  A-2.

raise  amounts  which  substantially  exceed  that  raised  by
challengers  (e.g.,  AG  Br.  II  72).  Actual  experience,
however,  shows  that  the  relatively  great  advantage  in-
cumbents  have  in  fundraising  is in  those  races  which  are
never  close.  In  1974,  challengers  in  69  House  races  were
able  to  spend  more  than  the  FECA’s  $70,000  limit.  The
average  spending  in  those  races  by  challengers  was
$113,094  while  the  average  spending  by  incumbents  was
$106,230.  Findings  II,  A-S,  pp.  32-58;  App.  II  A  327-86.
The  impact  of the  FECA  ceiling  on  expenditures  will  be  in
those  very  races  in  which  fund-raising  capacity  is already
about  equal or  actually  favors  challengers.”'

The  low  limits  have  been  made  even  more  onerous  by the
FEC’s  recent  surprising  decision  that  the  large lawyers’  and
accountants’  fees  necessary  to  ascertain  the  meaning  of the
Act  and  to  attempt  to  comply  with  it  must  be  counted
against  the  expenditure  limits.  FEC  Advisory  Opinion
1975-27  (Oct.  22,  1975).  The  bizarre  result  is that  the  hard-
er  a  candidate  tries  to  comply  with  the  manifold  com-

*"  The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  suggests  that  the  limits  imposed  by the
FECA  permit  a candidate  ‘‘an  adequate  opportunity  to  present  himself

rate  increase,  for  example,  one  mail  or  phone  contact  with  a  voter,  as

against  a  12-cents-per-person  general-election  limit  in  Senate  races.

tributions  by  direct  mail,  as  necessitated  by  the  Act,  runs  at  least

18  U.S.C.  §
S91(fX4KH).)  Volunteers  have  to  be  supervised  by a  paid  staff  and  the
disclosure  provisions  of the  FECA  also  impose  substantial  costs  to  be
counted  as  campaign  expenditures.  Yet  these  are  only  the  most  basic
organizational  costs  for challengers.

Intervening  appellees  chide  us  for  not  giving  1972  spending-per-
eligible-voter  figures  for  appellant  McCarthy's  New  Hampshire  cam-
paign  and  President  Nixon's  general-election  campaign.  The  figures  in
question  are  $1  and  36.9¢  respectively  —  both  far in excess  of the limits.

plexities  of the  statute,  the  more  he is penalized  and  his less
conscientious  opponent  is  advantaged.  Commissioners
Aikens  and  Tiernan  dissented  from  the  opinion  for
illuminating  reasons:

‘‘The  Chairmen  of  the  Republican  Senatorial
and  Democratic  Senatorial  Campaign  Com-
mittees  in their  comments  regarding  this  Request
stated.

‘The  burdens  of complying  with  the  Federal
campaign  laws  and  Commission  regulations
are  far  in  excess  of  that  anticipated  by
Congress  when  the  1974  Amendments  were

‘‘They  went  on  to  note,  ‘The  difficuities  of com-
pliance  are  particularly  acute  for  non-incumbent

paign  official,  and  the  modestly  funded  candidate
should  not  be  dissuaded  from  entering  the  elec-
toral  arena  because  of difficulties  in  interpeting
the  Act;  once  involved  in  a  pclitical  campaign,

expressions  because  of uncertainty  about  the  con-
sequences  of every  deed.”  /d.

The  limits  are  both  stringent  and  heavily  discriminatory
against  the  unknown  newcomer.  They  are  in  effect  a
regulation  of  the  content  of  speech  which  will  inevitably
silence  the  challenger  with  low  name  recognition  and
limited  access  to  the  media.  See  Police  Dep't  of Chicago  v.
Mosley,  supra.  To  say  they  are  “‘neutral”’  or  ““evenhanded”
is to exalt faaem over  substance.

form

Appellees  claim  that  the  recent  New  Hampshire  Senate
election  proves  that  the  expenditure  limits  are  not  too  low
because  the  voter  turnout  was  greater  than  in  1974  (IA  Br.
138).  Obviously  special  factors  such  as  the  great  publicity,
the  closeness  of the  prior  vote  and  presidential  campaign-
ing were  not  unrelated  to the voter  turnout.  The  real  signifi-
cance  of the special  election  lies elsewhere:

“John  A.  Durkin,  the  Democratic  victor  and  new
Senator,  spent  less  of the  overall  $220,000  cam-
paign  ceiling  than  did  Louis  C.  Wyman,  the
Republican  loser,  but  Mr.  Durkin  was  able  to
target  his  spending  more  effectively  because
thousands  of dollars  worth  of  his  labor  support
fell outside  the ceiling.

**  *

“There  are  over  45,000  union  households  in
New  Hampshire,  and  labor  agents  working  for
Mr.  Durkin  telephoned  each  one  several  times,
mailed  literature,  distributed  absentee  ballots  and
even  worked  house-to-house  in  some  working
class  districts  on  the day of the election.

“Mr.  Wyman  had  the benefit  of volunteers  who
did  many  of the  same  things,  but  the  expense  of
maintaining  telephone  banks  and  of printing  and
mailing  literature  was  charged  against  his  cam-
paign  spending  ceiling,  reducing  the  amount  he
could  devote  to other  activities.

“As  a  result,  although  Mr.  Durkin  spent  about
$25,000  less  than  his  Republican  opponent,  he
could  afford  $20,000  worth  of television  spots  and
$35,000  in  radio  advertising  while  Mr.  Wyman
spent  $22,000  on  radio  and  nothing  on  television
at  all.”  New  York  Times,  September  22.  1975,  at
12. col.  2.

40

If this  were  not  devastating  enough  to  appellees’  position,
one  of  Mr.  Durkin’s  fund-raisers  recognized  that  the
spending  limit  would  have  benefited  the  incumbent  had  it
not  been  for the  unique  circumstances:

‘*  Because  this  was  a  rerun  of last  November’s  tie
election,’  Mr.  Hemenway  said,  ‘both  candidates
had  very  high  public  recognition.  If we  had  been
starting  from  scratch  trying  to  establish  our  can-
didate  with  a  $180,000  ceiling,  Wyman  would
have  beatetl Durkin’,” Id.

The  New  Hampshire  election,  so  far  from  supporting  ap-
pellees’  position,  provides  an  ominous  foretaste  of  the
vicious  discrimination  which  the  expenditure  limits,  if sus-
tained,  will  spread  throughout  our  political  system  next
November.

C.  Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Demonstrate  That  Other

Discriminatory  Distinctions  Are  Constitutionally  Justified.

Appellees  do  little  by  way  of  explaining,  much  less
justifying,  other  patently  discriminatory  provisions  relating
to expenditures.  (See  A.  Br.  104-06.)

For  example,  although  the  role  of political  parties  calls
for  permitting  them  to make  expenditures  on  behalf of can-
didates,  it  hardly  follows  that  the  total  expenditures  per-
mitted  to  independent  candidates  should  not  equal  those  of
party-supported  candidates.  Yet  the  FECA  enacts  just
such  a  disparity,  18  U.S.C.  § 60&f).  It is surely  no  answer  to
argue,  as  appellees  do,  that  former  Senator  McCarthy  can
avoid  such  discriminations  by running  as  a  party  candidate
(IA  Br.  153);  the  right  to  speak  may  not  be  so  conditioned.
Nor.  obviously,  would  political  parties  be  “‘outlaw[ed]”’  (id. )
if independent  candidates  were  allowed  increased  limits  to
equal  what  party-supported  opponents  can  spend.

4]

V.

Appellees  Have  Presented  No  Constitutional
Justification  for  Limitations  on  Independent  Ex-
penditures.

Appellants  have  explained  why  the  FECA’s  limit  on  in-
dependent  expenditures,
is  un-
constitutional,  and  why the  “‘loophole-closing”’  rationale  of
the  court  below  is incorrect  (A.  Br.  107-16).  Appellees  seek
to  justify  Section  608(e)  by  narrowing  the  meaning  of
freedom  of the press.

18  U.S.C.  §  608(e),

A critical  function  performed  by the  press  is the  printing
of  paid  advertising  on  public  affairs,  including  ad-
vertisements  advocating  the election  or defeat  of candidates
for federal  office.  That  the press  cannot  be compelled  to ac-
cept  such  advertising,  see,  e.g.,  Miami  Herald  Publishing
Co.  v.  Tornillo,  supra,  418  U.S.  at  254-56,  in  no  way  de-
tracts  from  the proposition  that  paid political  advertising  is
essential  to  a  system  in  which  a  multitude  of tongues  are
heard.  Both  the  right  to  reject  advertising  and  the  right  to
purchase  it reflect  a policy of non-interference  by the gover-
nment.  Indeed,  the word  “‘press”’  can  in no  sense  be limited
to  the  institutional  press  but  must  be  taken  to  refer
generally  to  all  kinds  of  publishing,  including  the  pur-
chasing  of  political  advertisements.  Lovell  v.  Griffin.  303
U.S.  444,  452  (1938);  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan.
supra;  Bigelow  v.  Virginia,  supra.  First  Amendment  rights
would  otherwise  turn  on  an  irrational  distinction  between
buying  a  printing  press  and  hiring  someone  who  owns  one.
Section  608(e)  is  thus  a  direct  restraint  on  publishing  as
well  as on  speech.”?

**  In  any  event.  Section  608&e)  does  regulate  the  institutional  press
both  through  potential  criminal  liability  and  through  surveillance  (A.
(continued)

43

The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  seeks  to  distinguish  between
“personal  activity’’  and  other  kinds  of political  activity  in
support  of candidacies  (SK  Br.  59-60).  This  is not  a distinc-
tion  which  has  ever  commanded  consideration,  much  less
acceptance,  by  this  Court.  Individuals  concerned  with
public  issues  commonly  sign  advertisements  drafted  by
others  which  support  candidates.  No  one  had  ever  thought
to suggest  that  the  author  was  entitled  to greater  protection
than  those  who  joined  in  the  advertisement.  The  entire
argument  of amici,  however,  rests  on  the  validity  of such  a
distinction  and  the  necessity  to  eliminate  some  “‘personal”
activity  in  order  to  regulate  the  “non-personal,’’  even
.  [an  in-
though  admittedly  that  “proximately  affect[s]  .
dividual’s]  personal  enjoyment  of opportunities  to  publish

Br.  115).  Moreover,  while  appellees  repeatedly  misstate  the  statute  as
exempting  from  the  limits  all  news  stories,  editorials,  etc.  (e.g..  IA  Br.
161  n.181),  in fact  such  press  expression  is subjected  to the expenditure
and  independent-expenditure  limits  when  the  source  is ‘owned  or  con-
trolled  by  any  political  party,  political  committee  or  candidate,”’  18
U.S.C.  § S9I(FX4K A); see  A.  Br.  110  n.4.  Thus,  for  example,  if a  can-
didate  who  owns  a  newspaper  had  reached  his  expenditure  limit,  his
newspaper  would  be  thereafter  barred  from  editorially  endorsing  his
candidacy,  and  presumably  from  printing  any  coverage  which  might  be
considered  favorable  thereto;  it would  also  be barred  from  endorsing  or
favoring  other  candidates  if  the  expenditure  in  question  exceeded
$1,000.

Democratic

Ownership  or  control  of news  media  by political  parties  is far  from
unusual  in  American  history,  dating  back  to  the  beginning  of  the
nation  when  the  First  Amendment  was  framed,  Mott,  American  Jour-
nalism  124  (1950).  Horace  Greeley,  Democratic  presidential  candidate
in  1872,  owned  and  published  the  New  York  Herald-Tribune.  James  M.
also  owned
Cox.
newspapers,  as  did  Senator  William  Knowland  and  Representative
Willian  Randolph  Hearst.
his
brother's  National  Review?  The  Republican  Party  certainly  controls  its
publication  First  Monday.  Thus  substantial  segments  of the  press  are
made  directly  subject  to  the  expenditure  and  independent-expenditure
limits  and  to the  prior restraints  which  they impose.

1920,

in

his  own  thoughts”  (SK  Br.  56).  Since  both  types  of activity
fall  within  the  protection  of the  First  Amendment,  no  such
effect  can  be  justified.  Amici’s  argument  is no  more  per-
suasive  than  a  claim  that  a  campaign  oration  is bereft  of
First  Amendment  protection  if it is ghost written.”°

Amici  also  argue  that  the invasion  of this  “‘personal  liber-
ty’’ is less  than  appears  because  only a small  fraction  of the
population  can  afford  to exercise  it (id.  61).  This  also  cuts  a
wide  swath  through  what  have  long been  considered  protec-
ted  activities.  People  frequently  sign  advertisements  which
advocate  candidacies  but  which  are  not  purchased  on  a pro
rata  basis.  Until  now,  the  ‘‘free  riders’’  were  thought  to  be
engaging  in  protected  political  activity.  Amici’s  extreme
position  should  be rejected  out  of hand.

But  there  are  more  fundamental  objections  to  the  ap-
pellees’  and  amici's  position.  The  very  instinct  of the  First
Amendment  rejects  the  concept  that  otherwise  perfectly
lawful,  peaceful  —  indeed,  vital  —  speech  may  be cut  off in
the  service  of  governmental  purposes,  particularly  where
those  purposes  are  themselves  constitutionally  suspect.  To
be  sure,  it  is  possible  that  some  wealthy  contributors,
prohibited  from  making  direct  contributions  to  the  can-
didates  of their  choice,  may  instead  spend  money  to  com-

**  Intervening  appellees  suggest  that  we  argue  “that  the  most  dif-
ficult  constitutional  questions  would  arise  if the  section  were  applied  to
advertisements  containing  the  purchaser's  own  speech"  (IA  Br.  161).
We  make  no  such  argument;  we  think  the  distinction  both  illusory
and  irrelevant.  It was  intervening  appellees  themselves  who  made  this
argument  in  the  Court  of Appeals,  urging  the  court  ‘‘to  construe  the
words  of Section  608 to exclude  individual  expenditures  to publicize  the
spender’s  own  speech,  should  that  be  necessary"  (Brief  of Intervening
Defendants  in the  Court  of Appeals,  p.  123).  “*‘We  admit  .
.  that  there
is  a  bigger  question  when  we  are  talking  about  speech  of  the  con-
tributor  himself...  .  We think  that  presents  a problem”  (Court  of Ap-
peals,  tr.  of oral  arg..  June  13,  1975,  p.  63  (Mr.  Cutler)  ). These  views
have  not  thus  far appeared  in intervening  appellees’  presentation  to this
Court.

Appellees  suggest  that  the  Court  should  refrain  from  in-
validating  the  law  until  it can  be seen  how  it functions  (IA
Br.  37-42).  Surely,  in the case  of independent  expenditures,
Congress  should  have  taken  the  risk  and  waited  to  see
whether  indeed  there  would  be such  a loophole  before  enac-
ting  this  unprecedented  limit  on
free  political  com-
munication.  The  First  Amendment  demands  no  less:

.

“Those  who  won  our  independence  by revolution
were  not  cowards.  They  did  not  fear  political
change.  They  did  not  exalt  order  at  the  cost  of
liberty.  .
.  If there  be  time  to  expose  through
discussion  the  falsehood  and  fallacies,  to avert  the
evil  by the  processes  of education,  the  remedy  to
be  applied  is more  speech,  not  enforced  silence."’
Whitney  vy.  California,  274  U.S.  357,  377  (1927)
(Brandeis,  J., concurring).

No  legitimate  justification  has  been  offered  for  the  ‘‘en-
forced  silence”  of  Section  608(e).  It  should  be  declared
flatly unconstitutional.

municate  their  political  choices  directly  to the  public  in the
form  of  paid  advertisements  or  other  partisan  corn-
munications.  Of course,  there  is no  indication  in the  record
or  elsewhere  how  likely this  is to occur,  given  the  fact  that  it
is  far  more  cumbersome  than  simply  making  a  con-
tribution.  But  even  assuming  such  activity  may  occur  with
some  regularity,  can  the  mere  possibility  of that  ‘‘loophole”’
—  before  there  has  been  any  development  of a  problem  —
justify  a flat  restriction  on  speech?

We  submit  the  Court’s  cases  say  that  it cannot.  Only  last
term  this  Court  again  stressed  that  the  genius  of the  First
Amendment  and  its  ban  against  prior  restraints  is  the
notion  that  speech  cannot  generally  be  supressed  in  ad-
vance,  and  may  only  be subject  to  sanction,  if ever,  after  its
harmful  effects,  if any,  can  be assessed:

“The  presumption  against  prior  restraints  is
heavier  —  and  the  degree  of protection  broader
—  than  that  against  limits  on  expression  imposed
by criminal  penalties.  Behind  the  distinction  is a
theory  deeply  etched  in  our  law:  a  free  society
prefers  to  punish  the  few  who  abuse  rights  of
speech  after  they  break  the  law  than  to  throttle
them  and  all  others  beforehand.  It  is always  dif-
ficult  to  know  in  advance  what  an  individual  will
say,
and
line  between
illegitimate  speech  is  often  so  finely  drawn  that
the  risks  of  free  wheeling  censorship  are  for-
midable.”’  Southeastern
Ltd..  vy.
Conrad,  43 L.  Ed.  2d 448,  459  (1975).

In  a  very  real  sense,  Section  608(e)  is premised  on  the  same
impermissible  basis.  Because  of  a  fear  that  some  in-
dividuals  might  use  their  independent  expenditures  as  a
loophole,  the  law  chooses  ‘“‘to  throttle  them  and  all  others
beforehand.”’

46

VI.

Appellees  Have  Not  Demonstrated  That  the
FECA  Limitations  on  Campaign  Contributions
Are  Constitutional.

A.  Wholesale  Limitation  on  Contributions  Violate  the

Constitution.

We  argued  in  our  main  brief  that  wholesale  limits  on
contributions  to  political  candidates,  parties,  and  commit-
tees  could  not  be  constitutionally  justified  (A.  Br.  116-27).
As  with  expenditure  limits,  appellees  misstate  our  argu-
ment.  We  do  not  contend  that  Congress  is without  power
to  impose  any  limitations  on  contributions.  To  the  con-
trary,  our  main  brief listed  a number  of regulable  instances
—  e.g.,  contributions  made  too  late  to  be fully  disclosed  to
the  voter  —  of contributions  carrying  a  high  danger  of im-
proper  purpose  (A.  Br.  75-78).  Indeed,  we  were  at  pains  to
show  that  less  intrusive  regulations  of  contributions  are
available  and  in  fact  would  be  more  effective  remedies  for
the  abuses  specified.  Thus  we  argued  that  United  States  v.
O'Brien,  391  U.S.  367  (1968),  in  no  sense  justifies  the  in-
discriminate  inhibitions  of the  FECA,  which  in fact  permits
the demonstrated  abuses  to continue.

Appellees  in  effect  contend  that  there  is  no  limit  on
Congress’  power  to  regulate  contributions;  that  con-
tributions  simply  have  no  constitutional  protection  (AG
Br.  II  62).  The  reasons  advanced  for  this  conclusion  are
patently  insufficient.

One  argument  is  that  contributions  are  not  necessarily
used  for  speech  purposes:  “‘[slome  will  go to  pay  rent,  some
to  conduct  polls,  some  to  pay  travel  expenses  and  salary,
and  some  to  cover  the  cost  of public  communications”  (SK
Br.  55S).  But  all those  activities  are  essential  to political  com-
munication  and  are  therefore  constitutionally  protected.

47

Every  campaign  expenditure  is  devoted  either  to  direct
communication  with  the  voters  or  to  ancillary  purposes
necessary  to make  political  speech  effective.

It is also  argued  that  a  limit  on  large  contributions  will
provide  access  to  many  presently  excluded  from  political
participation  (IA  Br.  67-74).**  This  argument  is hardly  con-
sistent  with  the  attitude  appellees  exhibit  toward  the  im-
portant  function  large  contributions  play in providing  seed
money  to  newcomers  (id.  120-29).  Appellees  are  perfectly
content  to  see  those  who  would  otherwise  have  had  an  op-
portunity  to  expose  their  ideas  to  the  public  fully  silenced
and  excluded.  Indeed,  large  contributions  themselves  are
frequently  indispensable  to  public  participation  in  cam-
paigns.  For  example,  the  1968  McCarthy  campaign  in  New
Hampshire  involved  the  widespread  participation  of many
volunteers.  Yet  it  is clear  from  the  record  that  if Senator
McCarthy  had  not  been  enabled  by large  contributions  to
wage  a viable  campaign,  such  participation  would  not  have
occurred.  (See  A.  Br.  127-38.)  Similarly,  the  Scott-Kennedy
brief,  in defending  18 U.S.C.  § 608(e),  would  prohibit  those
who  now  sign  advertisements  paid  for by others  which  sup-
port or  attack  candidates  from  that  form  of participation.

In no  sense  does  a limitation  on  contributions  enlarge  the
access  of others  to  political  activity.  Rather  it silences  the
contributor  and  all  those  other  participants  whose  op-
portunities  would  have  been  enlarged  by the  contribution.
Large  contributions  increase  political  communication  and
thereby  increase  participation  of citizens  in politics.  (See  A.
Br.  119-20.)

**  But  evervone  is  excluded  from  such  participation  in  general-
election  presidential  campaigns  by the  federai-subsidy  provisions.  See
pp.  56-S7,  infra.  And  the  interests  appellees  represent  are  trying  to  ex-
tend  that  exclusion  to  congressional  elections  as  well.  See.  e.g..  SK  Br.
SO-S1  n.  41.

48

49

The  argument  that  ‘‘equality”’  is somehow  promoted  by,
and  thus  justifies,  the  FECA,  is an  impermissibie  rationale
because  it is not  “‘unrelated  to  the  suppression  of free  ex-
pression,”’  United  States  v.  O'Brien,  supra,  391  U.S.  at  377.
Rather  it falls  within  the  admittedly  unconstitutional  exam-
ples  mentioned  by amici  (SK  Br.  29),  such  as  limiting  the
amount  of  newsprint  which  might  be  bought  in  order  to
equalize  the  volume  of  news  coverage,  the  number  of
readers,  or  the  amount  of influence  the  newspaper  exerts.
Indeed,  Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  supra,
involved  the  classic  example  of a statute  designed  to  redress
inequality  in  the  ability  to  engage  in  political  com-
munication  resulting  from  aggregated  wealth;  this  Court
held  the  statute  unconstitutional.

Appellees’  final  argument  relates  to  the  obligations  large
contributions  are  thought  to  create  (IA  Br.79-81).**  To  the
extent  such  obligations  involve  an  illegal  quid  pro  quo  or
other  corruption,  appellants  have  demonstrated  in  their
main  brief  that  far  less  intrusive,  and  far  more  efficient,
means  are  either  already  available*®  or  could  be  fashioned
(A.  Br.  75-78).

Appellees  also  appear  to  argue  that  any  large  con-
tribution  creates  a  political  obligation  so  substantial  as  to
require  regulation  (IA  Br.  128).
Indeed,  it  is  suggested
that,  if  a  candidate  received  seed  money  from  large  con-

**  Nothing  in the  record  supports  intervening  appellees’  insinuation
(IA  Br.  46)  that  large  contributors  are  “‘generally”’  not  disinterested.  In
fact.  quite  the  opposite  is true:  money  tends  to  follow  philosophical  in-
clinations.  See  A.  Br.  65-66.  The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  is candid  enough
to  concede  that  even  more  narrowly  drawn  statutes  would  largely  ban
“transactions  which  would  be  seen  as  wholly  innocent  if all  the  facts
were  known"  (SK  Br.  52).

**  For  example,  if a jury  believed  Herbert  Kalmbach’s  version  of the
dairy  industry's  1972  contributions,  IA  Br.  48-49.  it  would  convict
those  involved  under  the  “‘unlawful  gratuity”  statute  applied  in  United
States  v.  Brewster.  408  U.S.  SO1  (1972).

tributors  and  was  successful  in  his  campaign,  he  will  ‘‘be
forever  obligated  to  them”’  id.”’  This  argument  not  only  is
overdrawn  and  basically  anti-democratic,  but  also  distorts
the  political  process.  Politicians  seek  votes,  not  money.
They  employ  contributions  in  order  to  communicate  with
voters  and  to  persuade  them  to  vote  for  them.  Their
obligations  upon.election  are  in every  sense  to  those  voters,
and  acts  inconsistent  with  those  obligations,  or  perceived  as
inconsistent,  will  be at a political  price.

What  is necessary  is that  the  voters  have  full  and  complete
information  as  to  the  major  sources  of a  candidate’s  sup-
port  so  that  candidates  will  be  fully  sensitive  to  their
primary  obligation  and  cannot  covertly  act  inconsistently
with  the  interests  of their  constituents.**  Those  represen-
tatives  who  fail  to  act  in  the  interests  of  their  constitu-
ents  can  be  penalized  through  democratic  means.  The
FECA  assumes  that  the  voters  are  incapable  of  making
rational  decisions,  and  that  those  who  know  better  can  by
law  change  the  outcome.  The  First  Amendment  is  a
bulwark  against  such  self-interested  paternalism.

B.  The  Particular  Limitations  on  Contributions  Have

Not Been  and  Cannot  Be Constitutionally Justified.

The  limits  on  contributions,  like  those  on  expenditures,
are  not  even  facially  evenhanded  (A.  Br.  127-38).  They  do
not
finance
congressional  perquisites  every  year.  Nor  do  they  regulate

of  dollars

millions

which

affect

the

*?  The  same  reasoning  would  require  prohibiting  newspaper
editorial  endorsements  of  candidates.  which  may  have  far  more  in-
fluence  with  voters  than  the  communication  permitted  by  a  con-
tribution,  and  thus  on  appellees’  theory  create  a far greater  obligation.

**  The  suggestion  that  “since  the  turn  of the  century”  (AG  Br.  II 32)
disclosure  has  been  tried  and  found  inadequate  is  ludicrous.  There
were  no  even  remotely  effective  disclosure  requirements  until  the  1971
FECA.

50

51

or  limit  the  large  undisclosed  contributions  which  presently
go  to  congressional  “office  accounts’’  and  which  have
political  value.  Again,  appellees  ask  this  Court  to ignore the
political  environment  into  which  the  challenged  legislation
has  been  placed.”®

Beyond  that,  the  limits  on  individual  contributions  are
highly  discriminatory  and,  in  effect,  a  regulation  of  the
content  of speech  in  view  of  18  U.S.C.  § 608(bX2),  which
permits  political  committees,  tailored  to  the  needs  of
organized  economic  interest  groups  and  susceptible  of
unlimited  proliferation,  to  contribute  up  to  $5,000  to  can-
didates.  So too,  rigid  limits  on  contributions  by individuals
and  less  well-organized  groups  serve  to  regulate  content
when  more  generous  giving  by unions  and  corporations  is
permitted.  18  U.S.C.  §§  610,  611.  See  Police  Dep't  of
Chicago  v.  Mosley,  supra.  While  contributions  and  ex-
penditures  by union  and  corporate  “‘segregated  funds”  are
subject  to the  limits,  Sections  610  and  608 (e) (2) (B) exempt
from  all  limits  ‘“‘communications  by  a  corporation  to  its
stockholders  and  their  families  or  by a  labor  organization
to  its  members  and  their  families  on  any  subject.’’  Thus
(1)  spend  unlimited
such  favored  organizations  can
amounts  to  try  to  persuade  their  members  to  vote  for  a
specific  candidate,  and  (2)  spend  unlimited  amounts
to
seeking  to  induce
segregated  funds.  Both  types  of expenditures  are  illegal,  if
in  excess  of the  limits,  when  made  by anyone  except  those
favored  groups.

their  members  to  contribute

Finally,  appellees  have  no  answer  to  our  contention  that
the  stringent  limits  on  individual  contributions  —  not  to be

2?  Intervening  appellees  falsely  accuse  us  of  misrepresenting  the
ABA  Special  Committee's  position;  they  do  so  by directly  misquoting
what  we  said  (IA  Br.  88  n.  99;  see  A.  Br.  75 n.  7).  As  to  contributions,
the  ABA  Committee  endorsed  ‘‘reasonable”™  limits  but  did  not  conclude
that  the  FECA  limits  are  reasonable.

adjusted  for  inflation  like  the  expenditure  limits,  an
anomaly  appellees  make  no  attempt  to defend  —  will  make
it  far  more  difficult  for  newcomers  to  enter  the  political
process.  Under  the  FECA  the  wealthy,  as  the  Court  of Ap-
peals concluded  (op. at  1533),  will  have  little  trouble  raising
“seed  money,”  since  the  amount  which  a family  can  put  to
political  use  on  behalf of a  family  member  far exceeds  that
which  can  be  put  to  use  on  behalf  of  other  candidates.
Compare  18  U.S.C.  § 608  (a)  with  id.  § 608  (b)  (1).  In-
cumbents,  moreover,  also  have  substantial  resources  to
serve  as  seed  money,  including  cash  on  hand  from  previous
campaigns,  mailing  lists  and  the  ability  to  raise  funds
ahead  of a  campaign.  New  York  Times,  Oct.  28,  1975,  at
10, col.  2. (See A. Br.  130-31.)

Those  who  are  effectively  silenced  are  less  wealthy
newcomers  who  will  be  unable,  given  the  difficulty  and  ex-
pense  of raising  small  contributions  through  direct  mailing,
to  get  started.  The  record  demonstrates  two  propositions
beyond  doubt.  First,  ‘‘seed’”’  or  “‘up front’’  money  is essen-
tial  to  a  viable  candidacy  (A.  Br.  131-38).  Second,  the
raising  of small  contributions  through  means  such  as  direct
mail  is extremely  expensive  and  costs  at  least  40 percent  of
that  which  is  raised,  a  figure  based  on  experience  when
fewer  candidates  were  utilizing  that  method  and  which  may
now  therefore  be too  low.  Findings  II, B-SO,  p. 203;  App.  II
B 582.  The  effect  of the  contribution  limits,  therefore,  is to
make  the  expenditure  limits  even  lower  because  fund-
raising  costs  are  included  within  them  and  the  20-percent-
figure employed  by the  FECA  is woefully  inadequate.

Thus,  in the  case  of Senator  McGovern,  upon  which  ap-
pellees  continue  to  rely,  the  initial  mailing  was  financed
largely  by contributions  which  would  be  illegal  under  the
FECA.  By the time  of the  New  Hampshire  primary  only the
amounts  raised  in  large  contributions  over  and  above  his
mailing  permitted  him  to  wage  a  vigorous  campaign.  (See

, a

$2

53

A.  Br.  135-36.)  The  only  example  arguably  to the  contrary
is Ramsey  Clark  (a losing  candidate,  who  received  38 per-
cent  of the vote).  Mr.  Clark  had  advantages  not  available  to
the  typical  challenger.  He  was  well  known  and  had  a  sub-
stantial  constituency  in  New  York  State.  The  fact  that  he
had  severe  difficulties  in budgeting  and  raising  money  itself
demonstrates  what  a  heavy  burden  the  limits  on  con-
tributions  will  place  on  challengers  to  incumbents.  (See  A.
Br.  134-35.)

Nor do the records  of the candidates  for the Presidency  in
1976  help  appellees,  who  list  the  amounts  those  candidates
have  raised  to  date  (IA  Br.  122-23.)  In  the  chart  below  we
list  what  they  raised  and  their  present  cash  balances  as  of
September  30: *

Washington  Post.  Oct.  15,  1975,  at  A3.  col.  |

At  this  stage  of the  campaign,  a  candidate  would  normally
be  accumulating  a  war-chest,  not  dissipating  his  money
almost  as  fast  as  he  receives  it  in  a  desperate  attempt  to

raise  more  small  contributions.  Clearly  the  contribution
limits  impose  an  onerous  burden  even  on  well-known  can-
didates.  Informed  comment  indicates  that  even  the  receipt
of federal  subsidies  will  not  save  many  or  most  of the  can-
didates  from  being  driven  out  of the  race  at  an  early  stage
by  financial  starvation  resulting  from  the  contribution
limits.  Washington  Post,  Oct.  19,  1975,  at  A  1, col.  6; id..
Oct.  20,  1975,  at A 1 col.  4; New  York  Times,  Oct.  25,  1925,
at  44 col.  2. Every  indication  is that  the  new  law  is making
money  more  important  in  campaigns,  not  less  so  as  it was
supposed  to do.

Finally,  the  Scott-Kennedy  brief  seeks  to  justify  grossly
discriminatory  provisions  permitting  extensive  use  of family
wealth  on  the grounds  that  the  “emotional”  concerns  differ
as  between  persons  who  are  related  to candidates  and  those
who  are  not  (SK  Br.  72-73).  Such  a distinction  seems  to  im-
ply  that  for  some  unstated  reason  the  First  Amendment
values  genealogy  over  philosophy  or  ideology.  This  statute
on  its  face  disadvantages  poor  candidates  by limiting  their
ability  to  seek  funds  in  sizable  amounts  while  permitting
the  extensive  use  of family  funds  by  wealthy  candidates.
This  is not  only  blatantly  in  violation  of the  First  Amend-
ment  but  totally  inconsistent  with  appellees’  rationale  that
this  statute  will  open  access  to the  political  process.

Both  on  its face  and  in its operation,  therefore,  the  FECA
discriminates  against  newcomers  and  challengers  to  in-
cumbents.  Indeed,  the  discrimination  is such  as  to  amount
to  a  regulation  on  the  content  as  well  as  the  quantity  of
political  communication.  Police  Dep't  of  Chicago  v.
Mosley,  supra.

54

Vil.

Appellees  Have  Failed  To Demonstrate  Any Basis
For  Sustaining  the  Federal  Subsidy  Provisions  of
Subtitle  H.

A.  Funding  Campaign  Activities  by Direct  Payment  to

Candidates  or  Parties  Is Unconstitutional.

We  contend  that  no  form  of direct  federal  payments  to
political  parties  or  candidates  is  constitutional  because
such  payments  are  contrary  to  the  “general  welfare”  and
because  of the  constitutional  perils  inherent  in establishing
the  existing  two  major  political  parties  (A.  Br.  145-52).°*°
Appellees  assert  that  such  federal  funding  is not  offensive
to  the  First  Amendment,  and  that  Subtitle  H  neither
establishes  political  parties  nor  excessively  entangles  the
government  in  political  affairs  (IA  Br.  186-93;  AG  Br.  II
63-67).  They  argue  that  because  the  government  has,  in cer-
tain  isolated  instances,*'  attempted  to  foster  expression  by

*°  Ifeither  the  contribution  or  expenditure  limits,  or  both,  are  held
unconstitutional,  the  federal-subsidy  provisions  must  fall  even  apart
from  the  arguments  made  in this  section.  The  interrelationship  between
expenditure  limits  and  federal  subsidies  was  well  understood  by
Congress.  Senator  Pell,  Chairman  of the  Senate  subcommittee  that  con-
sidered  S. 3044  and  one  of the  Senate  floor  managers  of the  legislation.
stated  that  the  two  sets  of provisions  were  like  ‘‘the  warp  and  the woof.”
120  Cong.  Rec.  S. 4439  (daily  ed.  March  26,  1974).  The  relationship  is
equally  close  between  contribution  limits  and  federal  subsidies.  (See
AG  Br.  1 101.)

''  Intervening  appellees  cite  “tax  incentives  to organizations  devoted
to  developing  and  disseminating  ideas”  (IA  Br.  190)  as  one  such  in-
stance.  However,  tax  credits  and  deductions  in such  circumstances  sup-
port  appellants’  analogy  to  the  case  law  construing  the  First  Amend-
ment’s  prohibition  against  establishing  religion.  Tax  exemptions  for
religious  organizations  have  been  upheld,  Walz  v.  Tax  Commission,
397  U.S.  664  (1970).  even  while  direct  subsidization  of sectarian  schools
has  been  struck  down,  Meek  v.  Pittenger.  421  U.S.  349  (1975).  See
Bittker.  “Churches,  Taxes  and  the  Constitution.”  78  Yale  LJ.  1285
(1969).

private  citizens,  the  government  is  also  permitted  to  in-
tervene  massively  in the  electoral  process  through  Subtitle
H.

and

Balancing

Citing  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641  (1966),  ap-
pellees  argue  that  because  Subtitle  H is “‘ameliorative’’  its
restrictions  should  be  subject  to  a  standard  of review  less
strict  than  that  for which  appellants  argue  (IA  Br.  193-94).
They  are  incorrect.  As  Professor  Ely has  said:  ‘“The  old  saw
that  the government  can  ‘deal  with  one  part of a problem  at
a time’  surely  has  no  application  in first  amendment  cases.
See,  e.g.,  Police  Dep't  v.  Mosley,  408  U.S.  92 (1972).”’  Ely,
“Flag  Desecration:  A  Case  Study  in  the  Roles  of
First  Amendment
Categorization
Analysis,”’  88 Harv.  L.  Rev.  1482,  1503  n.  83 (1975).  Where
government  action  trenches  on  an  area  explicitly  protected
by a  constitutional  restraint,  the  certainty  with  which  that
intervention  could  be  characterized  as  ‘‘ameliorative’’  is
simply  not  present.  Thus  in Katzenbach,  supra,  this  Court
declared  that  Congress,  in  the  Voting  Rights  Act  of  1965S,
adopted  a  measure  to  enforce  the  guarantees  of the  Four-
teenth  Amendment,  384  U.S.  at  651  n.  10, but  emphasized
that  Section  5  of  that  Amendment  gave  Congress  ‘“‘no
power  to restrict,  abrogate,  or  dilute  these  guarantees.”’  /d.
Here,  the  First  Amendment  bars  an  abridgment  of
freedoms  of  speech  and  association  whether  or  not  the
statute  is labeled  ‘‘ameliorative”’  or  anything  else.

in

Appellees  incorrectly  imply  that  we  have  argued  that
establishment-clause  inhibitions  should  be  automatically
applied  so  as  to  keep  government  out  of the  entire  area  of
communication  and  opinion  to  the  same  extent  that  the
First  Amendment  keeps  it out  of religion  (IA  Br.  190,  AG
Br.  II  64).  We  make  no  such  contention.  We  do  contend
that  the  similarities  between  partisan  politics  and  religion
are  so  great  that  the  same  concerns  that  protect  religious
liberty  should  also  protect  political  liberty,  and  that  the
necessity  of  avoiding  governmental  dominance  and  en-

56

57

tanglement  is likewise  comparable  as  between  religion  and
partisan  political  activity.  Politics,  like  religion,  involves
deeply  held  beliefs  about  the  nature  of society.  Politics,  like
religion,  can  be  and  often  is  a  source  of divisiveness  and
dispute.  Politics,  like  religion,  involves  the  competition  of
ideas  and  philosophies.  Since  a  time  well  prior  to  the
adoption  of the  Constitution,  political  disputes  have  been,
and  are,  far  more  likely  than  religious  conflict  to  lead  to
unrest,  disorder  and  even  revolution  if government  is per-
ceived  as  preventing  free  and  open  popular  choice.  Sub-
sidizing  some  points  of view  but  not  others  violates  elemen-
tal  fairness,  and  in  effect  inhibits  the  rights  of  the
disfavored  groups  to  freedom  of  speech  and  association,
just as  much  in the  political  area  as  in the  religious.

For  ali  these  reasons  and  those  set  forth  in  our  opening
brief  (pp.  145-52),  we  think  that  the  First  Amendment,  the
general-welfare  clause,  the  Fifth  Amendment,  and  indeed
the  provisions  of Articles  I and  II  which  require  free  and
fair  elections,  all  demand,  together  if  not  separately,  a
holding  that  Congress  may  not  establish  political  parties.

Appellees  insist  elsewhere  in  their  briefs  that  they  are
concerned  about  improper  appearances  and  the  adverse  ef-
fects  such  appearances  have  on  public  confidence  in  govern-
ment  (IA  Br.  105-07).  When  they  come  to  federal  sub-
sidies,  however,  their  concern  for  appearances  abruptly
vanishes.  They  ignore  the  obvious  fact  that  in politics,  as  in
religion,  even  the  taint  of suspicion  that  government  is not
acting  evenhandedly  must  have  the  most  devastating  im-
pact  on  the  public  perception  of government's  integrity.

Likewise,  one  of appellees’  principal  justifications  for ex-
penditure  and  contribution  limitations  —  that  they open  up
the  political  process  and  make  elections  more  democratic
by compelling  candidates  to  ‘‘invite  the  ordinary  citizen  to
take  an  active  part  in  the  campaign’?  —  falls  by  the

wayside  when  the  federal-subsidy  scheme  is  added  to  the

money,  the  opportunity  for  all  citizens,  ordinary  or  not,  to
participate  financially  and  thus  to  implement  their  views  is
reduced  to  zero.  As  Senator  Robert  Kennedy  testified  in
1967:

“To  aid  campaigns  by  direct  subsidy  from  the
Treasury  would  only  further  separate  the  in-
dividual  citizen  from  the  political  process  —  in-
sulating  the  party  organization  from  any  need  to
reach  citizens,  except  through  the  one  way  com-
munication  of  television  and  advertising.  The
political  parties  would  talk  to  the  citizen;  but  the

effectively
Financing

Indeed,  public  financing  could  well  induce  candidates,
relieved  of all need  to arouse  the  intense  public  interest  that
stimulates  contributions,  to engage  in a race  as  bland,  non-
controversial  and  noncommittal  as  possible  —  knowing
that,  no  matter  how  low  voter  interest  and  participation
became,  a  majority  of one  among  whoever  turned  out  on
election  day  would  be  enough  for  victory.**  Such  a  result
would  be recognizably  similar  to the conformist  apathy  and
sluggishness  that  have  frequently  characterized  religious
life  in countries  with  established  churches.  In  the  political
arena,  like  the  religious,  establishment  is the  antithesis  of
free,  healthy  democratic  life.  Apathy  on  the  one  hand,  and
on  the  other  hand  sullen  unrest  perhaps  amounting  to
violence  from  those  excluded  from  the  establishment,

As  amici  admit,  the interests  represented  by  appellees  are  now
vigorously  seeking  extension  of federal  subsidies  to  congressional  elec-
tions  (SK  Br.  SO-SI  n.  41).

*  The  Attorney  General  recognizes  this  as  a distinct  possibility  (AG

Br.  | 94).  We think  it highly  probable.

58

59

would  be  an  ironic  consequence  of political  ‘“‘reform.’’  The
political  equivalent  of  religious  life  in  Czarist  Russia  or
19th-century  Spain  may  be  appellees’  idea  of freedom  and
health;  it is not  that of the  Constitution.

In  attempting  to  deal  with  the  effects  Subtitle  H would
have  had  in  past  elections,  appellees  unwittingly  reveal  the
dangers  of  entanglement  between  the  government  and
political  parties  in  this  supposedly  ‘‘ameliorative’’  statute
(A.  Br.  149-S2).°*  Appellees  argue,  for  instance,  that  “‘i]f a
Bull  Moose  phenomenon  occurs  in  1976,  it  will  be  time
enough  then  for  Congress  to  make  any  necessary  ad-
justments  for  1980.  ..”’  (IA  Br.  207).  It is precisely  because
of the  continuing  problem  of congressional  reappraisal  of
‘who  gets  what.  when  and  how”’  from  Subtitle  H that  ex-
cessive  federal  entanglement  is  an  insoluble  problem  so
long  as  direct  payments  to  candidates  or  parties  are  em-
ployed.  Nothing  in any  of the  briefs  defending  Subtitle  H in
any  way  rebuts  this  proposition.

The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  poses  the  dilemma  of a  major-
party  schism  (SK  Br.
100).
It  offers  as  the  “best”
solution  giving  both  wings  of  the  split  party  full  federal
financing,  a solution  nowhere  suggested  by Chapter  95, but
concedes  that  if such  a  solution  is  not  authorized  “..  .
Congress  will  have  to  deal  [with  the  gap]  if the  occasion

petite  grows  by what  it feeds  on.”  /d.  at 477.

.

should  arise.”’  Jd.  What  worse  possible  time  could  there  be
to  call  an  intensely  partisan  Congress  back  into  session  to
“deal”  with  or  about  such  a  problem?  The  Scott-Kennedy
example  plainly  demonstrates  why  this  Court  should  con-
sider  the  issues  now,  and  why  it should  avoid  continuous
and  massive  government  entanglement  in  politics  by
declaring  Chapter  95 unconstitutional.

All  appellees’  briefs  are  conspicuously  silent  as  to the  spec-
tre  posed  if only one  party  should  receive  over  25 percent  of
the vote,  as  almost  happened  in 1860,  1912  and  1924  (A.  Br.
153  n.  10).  The  resulting  gross  disparity  in federal  funding
for  the  next  election,  which  obviously  would  tend  strongly
towards  creating  a  permanent  one-party  system,  would  hard-
ly be  likely  to  be  ‘“‘corrected””  by  a  Congress  presumably
composed  heavily  of that  very  party.’*  This  further  confirms
the  danger  of governmental  entanglement  in  the  partisan
political  process.  It  also  highlights  the  significance  of ap-
pellees’  concession  that  just  such  continuing,  ad  hoc
congressional  entanglement  is  the  only  way  in  which  the
Act’s  obvious  unacceptability  as  applied  to various  perfect-
ly foreseeable  situations  could  be relieved.

B.  Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Demonstrate  That  Chap-
ter  95's  Operation  Is  Not  Discriminatory  Against  Minor
and New  Parties  and Independent  Candidates.

We  make  no  contention  that  any  scheme  of  election
financing  would  have  to  treat  every  announced  candidate

Nor,  if appellees  prevail  here,  would  it appear  that  the  “not  while
this  Court  sits”  rationale  (SK  Br.  87) could  be  used  to  strike  down  the
statute  at  that  time.  If a  bias  with  respect  to federal  funding  in favor  of
two  “major”  parties  as  against  all  others  does  not  violate  the  First
Amendment  for  reasons  analogous  to  those  behind  the  establishment
clause,  it is hard  to  see  how  a  different  result  could  be  arrived  at  on  a
principled  basis  merely  because  the  number  of established  parties  as
defined  by the  statute  had  been  reduced  by popular-vote  developments
from  two  to one.

60

61

with  mathematical  equality.  Nor  do  we  deny  the  real
benefits  of a  two-party  system  or  contend  that  a  financing
scheme  is unconstitutionally  discriminatory  unless  it makes
other  parties  better  off than  they have  been  in the  past.  We
do  think  it  is  clear  that  Congress  may  not,  through  dis-
criminatorily  allocated  subsidies,  entrench  the  two  major
parties  in  ways  going  beyond  the  perfectly  legitimate
dominance  they  have  won  in  a  free  system  through  their
own  efforts  and  performance.  Yet  that  is  what  the
legislation  does.

.

Intervening  appellees  claim  that  “(t]he  statute  in no  way
enshrines  any  particular  parties  .
.””  (IA  Br.  200).  In-
tervening  appellees  appear  ambivalent  as  to whether  Chap-
ter  95 offers  two  alternative  routes  (IA  Br.  210)  or  only one
(IA  Br.  213) to obtain  effective  federal  subsidies.  If they are
arguing  that  Chapter  9S provides  two  paths,  they are  wrong
for the  reasons  stated  at  A.  Br.  157-61  and  AG  Br.  I 100.  If
they  are  arguing  that  Chapter  95  provides  only  one  route
(presently  available  only  to  candidates  of the  Democratic
and  Republican  parties),  they  are  most  certainly  correct.  If
they  are  arguing  further  that  the  government  has  thereby
met  its  constitutional  responsibilities,  they  have  misper-
ceived  the  correct  standard  of review.  Cf. Shapiro  v.  Thomp-
son,  394  U.S.  618  (1969);  Memorial  Hospital  v.  Maricopa
County,  415  U.S.  250 (1974).

fact

remains.

The  weaknesses  of  appellees’  argument  are  apparent
when  they  belittle  the  means  less  intrusive  than  Subtitle  H
by which  federal  subsidies  might  be  allocated  (e.g.,  IA  Br.

*?  On  the  basis  of Williams  v.  Rhodes,  it was  predicted  as  early  as

1969
Parties  under  the  American  and  German  Constitutions,”
Supreme  Court  Review  271,  284.

**  In support  of the  five-percent  requirement,  appellees  cite  Jenness
v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431  (1971)  (IA  Br.  208-09).  As  the  Attorney
General  has  pointed  out,  the  five  percent  required  here  is actually  far
higher  than  that  in Jenness,  since  it must  be  accumulated  from  among
a much  smaller  portion  of the electorate,  i.e.,  those  not  voting  for other
candidates  (AG  I Br.  102).  Moreover,  the  five-percent  figure  cannot  be
viewed  in  isolation,  but  must  be  considered  as  one  element  in  the
totality  of discriminatory  provisions  adversely  affecting  new  and  minor
parties  and  independent  candidates,  Williams  v.  Rhodes.  393  U.S.  23.
34  (1968).  This  totality  includes  not  only  the  other  discriminatory
features  of Chapter  95,  but  also  the  adverse  effects  of contribution  and
expenditure  limits  and  sweeping  disclosure  requirements  (A.  Br.  94-
106,  127-38,  178-84).  Moreover,  the  statute  upheld  in  Jenness  per-
mitted  a petition  drive;  that  decision  can  hardly  support  a statute  which
aliows  no  such  drive  but  unblinkingly  denies  new  parties  any  way  to
escape  the  dead  hand  of prior-election  statistics.  Finally.  being  denied
federal  funding  is  worse  than  being  left  off  the  ballot;  write-in  can-
didates  sometimes  win,  but  unfinanced  candidates  never  do.  See  A.  Br.
158.

Ce ed

62

63

which  their  non-appearance  in four-year  old clection  results
could  be overcome.  /d.  They  give  no  reasons,  but  cite  only
to  the  Court  of  Appeals  opinion.  That  opinion  states
merely:

“Evidencing  support  by petition  signatures  may
provide  an  objective  and  more  current  measure
but again  it would  siphon  off considerable  cam-
paign  energies  into a petition  drive”  (op. at  1564).
There  are  several  answers.  First,  independent  candidates
like  Senator  McCarthy  and  third  parties  often  now  must
engage  in petition  drives  to  obtain  a  place  on  state  ballots:
their  petition  drives  for  federal  subsidies  would  at  most  be
no  more  than  an  adjunct  to the  ballot  drives.  Indeed  tl.ere
seems  no  reason  why they should  not  be allowed  to use  their
state-ballot  petitions,  monitored  and  checked  by state  of-
ficials  (who  are  unlikely  to  be  over-indulgent*’),  to  qualify
for federal  funding.

Second,  if federal  subsidies  are  as  highly  desirable,  and
private  fund  raising  as  suspect  and  burdensome  as  ap-
pellees  contend,  one  would  think  they would  prefer  to have
new  parties  and  independent  candidates  concentrating
“considerable  campaign  energies”  on  a  petition  drive
rather than  concentrating  those  same  energies  exclusively
on  private  fund  raising.

Third,  no  party  would  be obliged  to  conduct  such a drive:
surely  the  decision  as  to  whether  the  party’s  interests  lie
there  or  elsewhere  is  best  made  by the  party  itself  rather
than  by Congress  in denying the alternative  altogether.

Fourth,  how  burdensome  such  a  drive  would  be  would
depend  entirely  on  the  number  of  signatures  Congress
