required;  if its  judgment  was  that  one  signature  obtained
on  a  petition  is more  burdensome  to  obtain,  and  thus  in-
dicates  greater  seriousness  and  popular  support,  than  one
vote  in the  previous  election,  it could  of course  set  the  per-
centage  of signatures  required  at  a  figure  lower  than  the
five  percent  required  for  prior-election  performance.  Thus
appellees’  objections  to  a  petition  drive  as  an  alternative
way  to  demonstrate  ‘‘seriousness”  for  Chapter  95 purposes
turn  out  to be wholly  chimerical.“°

Appellees  point  out  that  public  funds  need  not  be
distributed  to every  citizen  who  chooses  to declare  himself  a
presidential  candidate;  we  agree.  Appellees  leap from  this
statement  of  the  obvious  to  the  conclusion  that  Congress
may  draw  an  arbitrary  line  between  “major”  and  “minor”
political  parties  at  25  percent  of the  popular  vote  (IA  Br.
210-11).  It may  be true  that,  as  they suggest,  once a line  is
necessary  some  arbitrary  effects  must  be tolerated.  But  ap-
pellees  never  ask  why a line  dividing  major  and  minor  par-
ties  must  be  drawn  at  all.  The  party  with  the  highest  vote
total  could  be  given  the  full  $20,000,000  subsidy,  and  all
other  parties  exceeding  some  initial  threshold  (either  by

“©  Equally  illusory  are  appellees’  objections  to  a tax  check-off  system
whereby  taxpayers  designate  the  party  or  candidate  to  whom  they
want  their  tax  dollar  to  go  (AG  Br.  II  66;  IA  Br.  218-19).  Appellees
argue  that  taxpayers  will  not  know  who  the  presidential  nominees  are
when  they  check  off  on  their  tax  returns.  Aside  from  the  fact  that
precisely  the  same  problem  affects  the  existing  tax  check-off  system.  it
is  in  fact  easily  remediable.  The  check-off  form.  which  could  be  sent  to
taxpayers  only  in  presidential  election  years  rather  than  annually.  and
which  could  be in the  amount  of $4  rather  than  $1  (as  I.R.C.  § 6096 now
provides),  could  be  returnable  for  transfer  of money  into  the  subsidy
fund  until  some  point  after  the  presidential  nominating  conventions
take  place.  Separating  the  check-off  form  from  the  income  tax  return
would  also  overcome  another  of  appellees’  objection,  that  political
preferences  should  not  be  known  to  persons  examining  tax  returns  (IA
Br.  219).  These  relatively  simple  changes  would  alleviate  immense  con-
stitutional  deficiencies.

prior-vote  record  or  by petition)  given  amounts  equivalent
to the  ratio  of their  candidates’  votes  to that  of the  highest

the  same  treatment  of  major  parties  where  that  is  ap-

‘Such  a

solution

could

well

entail

severe

con-
stitutional  problems;  our  basic  point  is that  no  method  of
allocating  federal  funds  among  political  parties  avoids  such
problems.  (See  A.  Br.  150.)  The  choice  Congress  made  un-
fairly  disadvantages  minor  parties  vis-a-vis  the  two  majer
parties;  the  alternative  would  run  the  risk  of further  en-
trenching  the  most  popular  party  by giving  it more  federal

no  matter  how  structured.  Cf  Miami  Herald  Publishing
Co.  v.  Tornillo,  supra.

Appellees’  briefs  are  replete  with  historical  inaccuracies
and  mistaken  conclusions.  Intervening  appellees  attempt  to
rebut appellants’  reference  to  Abraham  Lincoln’s  grave  as
“Just another  burial  plot  in the  cemetery”  by pointing  out
that  in  the  1856  election  the  Republicans  received  one-third
of the vote  (IA  Br.  206;  SK  Br.  97).  That  response  completely
misses  the  point.  The  crucial  effects  of  public  financing
would have  occurred  in  1856,  when  John  C.  Fremont,  run-
ning  as  the  candidate  of the  new  Republican  Party  would
have  been  denied  public  funds.  Chapter  95  would  have
completely  funded  the  Democrats  and  the dying Whigs,  but
would  have  given  not  a  pre-election  penny  to  Fremont.  In

ee er

all  probability,  that  would  have  aborted  his  candidacy  and
the  Republican  Party in that year.**

The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  goes  to  considerable  length  to
demonstrate  how  beneficial  Chapter  95 would  have  been  in
1972  to the  American  Independent  Party  and  its candidate,
John  Schmitz  (SK  Br.  98-99).  The  effort  merely  furnishes  a
present-day  example  that,  as  it would  have  done  with  the
Whigs  in  1856,  Chapter  95 irrationally  preserves  third  par-
ties  after  their  normal  life  expectancy.  The  dissent  below
noted  this  element  of capriciousness  (op.  at  1606,  dissent).
See  A. Br.  152-53.“

We  submit  that  legislation  producing  such  arbitrary,
irrational  and  discriminatory  effects  cannot  be  sustained
under  either  the  First  or the  Fifth  Amendment.

*'  Such  artificial  preservation  of the  Whigs  for  at  least  one  election.
is
with  little  public  support  but  rich  sinecures  and  revenues.
reminiscent  of  the  status  of  the  established  Anglican  churches  of
Ireland  and  Wales  —  rich  but  empty  —  for  generations  after  their
popular  support  had  virtually  disappeared.

*?  The  Scott-Kennedy  brief  erroneously  states  that  the  only  newly
formed  party  to have  surpassed  established  parties  was  the  Republican
Party  (SK  Br.  97).  The  Whigs  did  the  same  thing  in  the  elections  of
1836  and  1840.  The  brief  also  states  that  the  “Progressive  Socialists”
(presumably  meaning  the  Progressive  Party)  “would  have  received  17
percent  in arrears”  after  the  1924  election,  id. ; in fact,  the  Progressives
would  have  received  almost  41  percent  of  what  the  major  parties
received  had  Chapter  95 been  in effect,  and  an  equal  amount  in  1928.
though  they were  not  an  on-going  party  —  yet  another  example  of how
the  dead  hand  of the  past  would  distort  the  political  process.  The  brief
further  errs  in stating  that  only four  candidates  of new  or  minor  parties
have  ever  received  over  five  percent  of the  popular  vote.  /d.  In fact  five
other  such  parties  have  exceeded  that  threshold:  National  Republican
(1828),  Anti-Masonic  (1832),  Free  Soil  (1848),  Constitutional  Union
(1860),  and  Socialist  (1912).  Findings  I, 85 pp.  38-43;  App.  I] A 35-42.
Each  of these  parties  would  have  had  no  pre-election  federal  subsidies
in the  years  when  they  had  substantial  popular  appeal.  but  would  have
received  subsidies  in  subsequent  years  when  their  support  had
vanished.

66

Intervening  appellees  note  that  neither  of the  two  major
parties  challenges  Chapter  95  (IA  Br.  199).  Why  should
they?  The  Republican  Party  is even  now  preparing  to take
full  advantage  of the $22,000,000  federal  subsidy  by raising
large  sums  of  money  that  no  longer  need  be  used  in
presidential  campaigns  and  applying  it  in  congressional
races.  Broder,  ‘The  GOP’s  Financial
‘Loophole,’  ”’
Washington  Post,  Sept.  14,  1975,  at  B 6, col.  3. Minor  and
new parties  and  independent  candidates  who  do  not  share
in  the  Chapter  95  bonanza  are  thus  disadvantaged  at  the
congressional  ‘level  as  well  as  in the  presidential  campaign
itself.

Appellees’  contention  (e.g.,  IA  Br.  215)  that  minor  and
new  parties  and  independent  candidates  will  be  better  off
under  Chapter  9S  than  under  the  traditional  system  is
belied by the fact  that  eight such  parties*?  and  candidates“
or  interests  affiliated  with  them**  are  attacking  the  statute
here, either  as  appellants  or  as amici.  Not one  such  party or
candidate  has  come  forward  to  support  these  provisions.
They  are  better  judges  of their  interests,  surely,  than  ap-
pellees.

Appellees  concede  sub  silentio**  that,  as  we  pointed  out
(A.  Br.  154 n.  12), post-election  subsidies  are  available  only
to  the  extent  that  their  expenditures  have  been  made  with
borrowed  money.  This  is  a  crippling  restriction,  and  a
wholly unnecessary  one:  why should  the  qualifying  party  or
candidate  not  receive  his  full  entitlement,  to  be  used  for

67

general  political  purposes  and  preparation  for  the  next
election?  As  Senator  Metcalf  cogently  demonstrates  (Met-
calf  Br.  23-25),  under  Chapter  9S  such  parties  and  can-
didates  are  penalized  for  raising  private  contributions,  since
only  if they fail  to  raise  them  but  instead  rely  on  credit
can  they become  entitled  to any  post-election  funds.  This  is
utterly  irrational,  and  in  stark  contrast  to  the  rewards
bestowed  by  Chapter  96  on  major-party  candidates  for
raising  private  funds  in the pre-convention  period.*’

Finally,  appellees’  argument  (IA  Br.  223-225)  that  in-
dependent  candidates  such  as  appellant  McCarthy  are  not
denied  any  general-election  financing  is  wholly  incorrect.
Even  aside  from  the  unlikelihood  that  such  a candidate  will
have  a prior-vote  record,  and  the  statutory  denial  to him  of
any  way  to  qualify  by  petition,  Chapter  95  expressly  ad-
ministers  the  coup  de  grace  by denying  him  funding  merely

*”  Moreover,  the lack  of access  of third  parties and  independent  can-
didates  to substantial  credit  will  usually  reduce  their  “entitlement”  to a
nullity  as  a practical  matter.  The  notion  that  banks  will  accept  public-
opinion  polls  as  an  indication  of minor-party  strength,  on  the  basis  of
which  they will  lend  money,  SK  Br.  94,  is so  fanciful  that  it hardly  de-
serves  comment.  Third-party  election-day  performance  is  frequently  con-
siderably  different  from  what  the  polls  have  indicated.  See,  e.g.,  Maz-
manian,  Third  Parties  in Presidential  Elections  9  (1974).  Banks  would
be furtner  discouraged  by the  complete  uncertainty  as  to  whether  even
a  party  which  qualifies  for  post-election  funding  will  in  fact  receive  it.
since  the  Secretary  of the  Treasury  may  not  reserve  adequate  (or  any)
funds  for that  purpose. (See pp.  70-73,  infra.)

Intervening  appellees’  argument  that  “ordinary  bank  loans  are
available  without  regard  to  the  contribution  limits  in  Section  608."  IA
Br.  214,  is disingenuous.  3ank  loans  that  are  endorsed  or  guaranteed
are  counted  as  contributions  by the  endorsers  or  guarantors,  18  U.S.C.
§591(eX1).  Any bank  obviously  desires  repayment  of the  loans  it makes
political  campaign
nature
and,
organization,  will  almost  certainiy  seek  assurances  to  that  end.  Bank
loans,  therefore,  will  be subject  to  the  same  severe  limitations  as  direct
monetary  contributions,  with  precisely  the  same  harmful  effects  on
minor  and  new  parties  and  independent  candidates.

because  he  is not  the  candidate  of a  party.  fhe  argument
that  the  Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency-
McCarthy  76  could  qualify  as  a  “‘party’’  is simply  wrong.
The  definition  of party  on  which  appellees  rely (IA Br.  224)
expressly  requires  that  the  candidate’s  name  ‘‘appears  on
the  election  ballot  as  the  candidate  of’  the  group  sup-
porting  his candidacy.  McCarthy  has  no  intention  of allowing
any  party’s  name  to  appear  on  ballots  in connection  with  his
name;  that  would,  in his view, vitiate  what  he believes  to be the
advantages  and  attractions  of a  genuinely  independent  can-
didacy.  Why  that  choice  should  operate  to  deny  him  federal
funds  passes  understanding.

C.  Appellees  Have  Failed  to  Demonstrate  That  Chap-

ter  96 Does  Not Discriminate  Against  Certain  Candidates.

Appellants  have  demonstrated  that  Chapter  96 of the  In-
ternal  Revenue  Code  discriminates  against  independent
candidates  by its failure  to offer  them  any  federal  subsidies
during  the  same  period  when  candidates  for  party
nominations  for the  presidency  are  pursuing  that  goal,  and
that  the  primary  matching-grant  formula  of Chapter  96  is
invidiously  discriminatory  (A.  Br.  164-68).  Appellees  at-
tempt  to  justify  matching  grants  on  the  ground  that  they
‘‘allow  individual  citizens  to  indicate  by their  contributions
which  candidates  are  most  worthy  of  federal  assistance”
(IA  Br.  228).

Such  a  response  is  flatly  inconsistent  with  appellees’
position  on  Chapter  95’s  funding  system  for  general-
election  campaigns  —  which  purposefully  excludes  such
citizen  participation  —,  as  is the  quotation  from  Professor
Rosenthal  they offer  in support  of their  position  on  Chapter
96  (IA  Br.  230).  To  defend  the  matching-grant  system,  ap-
pellees  fall  into  the  same  inconsistency  as  the  courts  below
(see  A.  Br.  168):  they  are  forced  to defend  the role  of private
contributions.  an  exercise  which  is  directly  contrary  to
almost  every  page  in the  other  sections  of their  briefs.  They

are,  moreover,  forced  to  depart  from  the  “‘one  man,  one
vote’’  exhortations  earlier  in  their  brief  (IA  Br.  67-74).  If
they truly  believed  “‘one  man,  one  vote”’  principles  are  ap-
plicable  to  the  regulation  of  campaign  financing,  they
would  be  arguing  that  federal  subsidies  should  be  dis-
pensed  based  on  the  number  of contributors  to  a  candidate
(e.g.,  $5  of  government  subsidy  per  contributor  without
regard to  the  size  of the  contribution),  rather  than  on  the
size  of the  contribution.  Finally,  appellees  argue  that  the
“20-state  requirement  has  invigorated  the  early  stages  of
presidential  campaigns”  because  candidates  are  now  spend-
ing  extensive  amounts  of time  raising  funds  (IA  Br.  233).
Earlier  in  their  brief,  however,  intervening  appellees  were
decrying  the  extensive  amounts  of  time  that  candidates
spent  in precisely  the same  activity (id.  ,  72-73).

Chapter 96 has not  produced  the  effects  its  supporters
claim.  Reporting  on  the  contest  for  the  1976  Democratic
presidential  nomination, one  skilled  political  reporter  has
written:

‘When  Congress  provided  public  financing  for
1976  presidential  candidates,  through  federal
matching  grants  for  small  contributions,  the
belief  was  that  it would  erase  the  competitive  ad-
vantage  of the well-off candidate.

at

“But

this

stage

of  the

Democratic
nomination  race,  the  greatest  disparity  between
the  contenders  is financial.  Two  are  affluent  and
the  rest  are  struggling.  And  a growing  number  of
participants  in  the  race  believe  that  the  dollar
Squeeze  may  narrow  the  Democratic  field  faster
than  any  other  force.’  Washington  Post,  Oct.  19,
1975,  at  A 1, col.  6.

*
a one.

Experience  is  rapidly  demonstrating  that  the  FECA  con-
tribution  limits  are  so  crippling  that  even  candidates  who

70

71

qualify for matching  funds  will  not be able to survive unless
they win  one  or  more  of the very  earliest  primaries.  See  also
Washington  Post,  Oct.  20,  1975,  at  A  1,  col.  4.  What
“beneficial’’  effects  Chapter  96 has had  are  thus dubious  in
the  extreme.  Its  discriminatory  effects,  on  the  other  hand,
are  plain and substantial.

All  of the  pulling  and  tugging  by appellees,  and  all  the
inconsistencies  in  their  arguments,  indicate  the  severe
problems  inherent  in attempting  to justify Chapter 96 (and
Chapter  95  as  well).  There  need  be  no  hesitation  by this
Court  in  striking  down  so  constitutionally  defective  a
scheme.  Presidential  campaigns  now  under  way could
readily  adapt  to  a  political  world  without  Subtitle  H.  No
funds  have  yet  been  distributed,  and  none  will  be  until
January  1, 1976,  at  the  earliest.  On  the  other hand, almost
unimaginable  practical  problems  could  arise  if the  Court
took  appellees’  advice  and  sustained  this  legislation,  only to
with  emergency
a  few A  years  hence
to  hasty
perhaps  in  mid-election  year,
challenges,
congressional  tinkering  with  the  statute  in response  to such
a development  as  a major-party  split which  appellees  admit
the existing  statute  could  not  accommudate.

confronted

D.  Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Refute  Our  Contention
That  the  Federal  Subsidy  Provisions  Vest  Impermissible
Discretion  in the Secretary  of the  Treasury.

Appellees  admit  (e.g..  IA  Br.  185)  that,  if the amount  in
the  Fund  were  inadequate  to satisfy  the  full  entitlements  of
eligible  candidates,  problems  of projections  and  priorities
would  arise.  These  must  be resolved  by the  Secretary  of the
Treasury,  a  political  appointee  of a  major  party,  who  must
look  into  a  crystal  ball  many  months  prior to  the  election
and  prophesy.  for  example,  whether  any  minor or  new  par-
ties  or  independent  candidates  will  be  entitled  to  post-
election  funding  on  the  basis  of  their  election-day  per-

A

formance  (I.R.C.  §§ 9006(d),  9037).  If he  guesses  wrong,
they  can  receive  nothing  even  though  clearly  entitled  to  it.
He must  make  his decision  during  or  before  January  1976,
since  that  is  the  month  in  which  the  matching  grants  to
candidates  for nominations  become  payable,  and  they have
the  /owest  priority  under  the  statute,  1.R.C.  § 9037;  they
should  not  be  paid  in  full  if funds  will  be  inadequate  to
meet  higher-prior  entitlements,  which  include  post-election
payments  to  qualifying  minor  and  new  parties  and  in-
dependent  candidates.  Thus  the  Secretary’s  decision  must
be made  long before a realistic  assessment  is possible  as  to
whether  any  significant  third  parties  will  compete  in  the
election,  let  alone  the  likelihood  of their  exceeding  five  per-
cent  of the vote.  Even  if the designated  prophet  were  wholly
apolitical,  such  diaphanous  projections  would  be  fraught
with  perils  of  error  and  injustice.  The  fact  that  the
Secretary  of the  Treasury  is not  apolitical  surely  does  not
improve  the situation.

Appellees’  only  response  is to  cite  a  recent  estimate  that
the  Fund  will  contain  $100  million  before  the  1976  election
and  that  this  amount  will  be  adequate  to  meet  all  en-
titlements  (IA  Br.  185).  There  are  three  answers.

First,  even  if this  were  so,  it would  provide  no  guarantee
that  acute  problems  of discretion  and  prophecy  would  not
arise  in future  years.  While  it is true  that  the  present  trend
in exercise  of the tax-return  check-off  is upward,  that  trend
is not  necessarily  permanent  —  especially  if, as  we  predict.
the  public  becomes  disenchanted  with  federal  subsidies,  and
indeed  with  the entire  statute,  as  their  effects  in practice  are
experienced.

Second,  the  Fund  presently  stands  at  only  $60  million
(New  York  Times,  Oct.  16,  1975,  at  24, col.  1).  Fully 40 per-
cent  of the  “‘estimated’’  $100  million  is yet  to  be  checked
off;  the  assumption  is  that  check-offs  will  rise  from  $32
million  in  1975  to  $40  million  in  1976,  but  there  is  no

Oe Re ae ee .

—_—

72

73

assurance  that  this  will  happen,  and  there  will  be  none  in
January  1976  when  the  Secretary  must  act.  While  the
present  projection  may  be  as  reasonable  as  the  cir-
cumstances  permit,  it  is  perilous  to  permit  crucially  im-
portant  matters  of  political  equality  and  fairness  to  be  af-
fected  with  such  uncertainties  and  hazards  for mishap.

Finally,  even  the  projected  $100  million  is not  necessarily
adequate.  Of that  amount,  more  than  $44  million”  must be
set  aside  for  the  nominating  conventions  and  the  major-
party  general-election  subsidies.  That  leaves  less  than  $56
million.  At  present  there  are  ten  serious  candidates  for  the
Democratic  nomination  and  two  for  the  Republican.  If all
twelve  candidates  should  qualify  for  the  maximum  $5
million  matching  grant,  $60  million  would  be  required  for
their  federal  payments;  moreover,  additional  candidates
may  enter  the  race.  If the  Secretary  accepts  appellees’  view
that  sufficient  funds  for all entitlements  will  be available  in
1976,  he  will  pay  all  these  requisitions  in  full  as  they  are
submitted,  without  waiting  to see  what  the total  will  be. The
result  could  be  that  no  post-election  funds  would  be
available  if one  or  more  third-party  independent  candidates
qualified  for  them.  If only  one  such  candidate  did  qualify
(as  is  hardly  out  of the  question  if,  for  example,  George
Wallace  chooses  to run) and  obtained  15 percent  of the vote
with,  say,  40 percent  as  the  average  vote  of the  major  par-
ties,  then  he  could  be  entitled  to  as  much  as  $7.5  million,
making  the  total  demands  on  the  Fund  more  than $111.5
million,  as  compared  to  the  very  generous  projection  of
$100  million  for  what  it will  contain.  If the  Secretary  exer-
cises  his discretion  by not  setting  aside  substantial  funds  for
the  contingency  of a  third-party  entitlement,  the candidate
will  simply  go  unpaid,  and  the  gross  injustices  of  the
statute  would  be even  further  compounded.

**  That  figure  will  have  to  be adjusted  upward  for inflation.

Here  again,  it is minor  and  new  parties  and  independent
candidates  who  bear  the  major  brunt  of the  uncertainties
and  the  discriminatory  and  otherwise  bizarre  features  built
into  this  legislation.  The  established  two  major  parties  are
most  carefully  protected,  at  least  so  far  as  the  nominating
conventions  and  the general-election  period  are  concerned.

In  the  pre-nomination  period,  the  discretion-and-
prophecy  mechanism  also  presents  the  possibility  of
discrimination  among  major-party  candidates.  If,  again,
the  Secretary,  accepting  appellees’  view  that  the  1976  Fund
will  be  adequate  to  meet  all  entitlements,
pays  all
requisitions  in full  as they are  submitted,  and  if he proves  to
be  wrong  and  the  Fund  runs  out,  nothing  will  be  left  to
meet  later-presented  entitlements.  This  runs  the  further
risk  of  penalizing  candidates  who  enter  the  race  late  —
perhaps  in  response  to  new  developments  or  issues  —  or
who  begin  to  make  an  impact  later  than  their  competitors.
The statute  contemplates  this  invidious  possibility  with  per-
fect  equanimity,  providing  that  the Secretary  ‘“‘shall  take  in-
to  account,  in seeking  to  achieve  an  equitable  distribution,
the  sequence  in  which  such  certifications  are  received”
(Section  9037(b)).*°  This  is only one  of the  many  features  of
the  challenged  legislation  which  have  greatly  extended  the
time  span  of the  presidential  campaign,  thus  placing  ad-
ditional  heavy  burdens  on  all  candidates  and  reducing  the
flexibility  of  our  political  system  to  respond  quickly  to
changes  in circumstances.

*  If, as  seems  probable,  ihe  Secretary  is simply  te  pay  requisitions  in
the  order  they  are  submitted,  this  is  like  providing  that  income-tax
refunds  or  welfare  payments  shall  be  made  from  a  limited  fund  to  the
first  applicants  until  the  fund  is exhausted,  with  late-comers,  otherwise
equally  entitled,  to get  nothing.  On  the  other  hand.  if the  Secretary  has
power  to  make  any  adjustments  he deems  “equitable.”  his discretion  is
unconstitutionally  broad,  especially  considering  his  political  bias.

74

Vill.

Appellees  Have  Failed  To  Justify  the  Fun-
damental  and  Sweeping  Overbreadth  of  the
FECA’s  Disclosure  Provisions.

A.

The  Blanket  Disclosure  Requirements  Concerning
Contributions  to  Candidates  for  Federal  Office  Have  Not
Been  Justified.

Appellants  argue  that,  although  in  principle  disclosure
requirements  serve  a  valid  public  purpose,  the  FECA’s
particular  disclosure  requirements  (1)  sweep  overbroadly
across  protected  First  Amendment  associational  privacy
rights (A.  Br.  170-77),  (2) serve  no  compelling  governmental
interest  when  applied  to  third-party  and  independent  can-
didates  for  federal  office  (A.  Br.  178-84),  and  (3) establish
thresholds  for  reporting  and  disclosure  that  lack  a  sub-
stantial  nexus  with  the  governmental  interests  alleged  to
justify  such  thresholds  (A.  Br.  184-92).  Appellees  respond
that  third-party  and  independent  candidates  must  be  sub-
ject  to  precisely  the  same  standards  as  major-party  can-
didates  (IA  Br.  176-81;  AG  Br.  II  29-31),°°  and  that  the
disclosure  thresholds  are  not  too  low (IA  Br  174-76;  AG  Br.
II  31).  Appellees  never  come  to  grips  with  the  substantial
overbreadth  of the  FECA’s  disclosure  provisions.  Instead,
they  seek  to  justify  the  Act’s  blanket  disclosure  require-
ments  by offering  a few  hypothetical  examples  of situations
where,  they assert,  disclosure  of the  identity  of contributors
to  third-party  and  independent  candidates  would  be
necessary  to  achieve  compelling  governmental  purposes.

‘©  Amici  Scott  and  Kennedy  state  that  we  ‘apparently  concede  the
constitutionality  of disclosure  laws  as  applied  to  the  two  major  political
parties’  (SK  Br.  76).  This  statement  is, of course,  incorrect  to the extent
it implies  that  we  agree  with  the  reporting  and  disclosure  thresholds  of
the  FECA.

75

But  these  examples  cannot  controvert  the  fundamental
point  that  the  universal  sweep  of  the  FECA  does  sub-
stantial  harm  to  such  association,  while  at  best  achieving
minimal  gains  for  any  countervailing  governmental  pur-
poses.

The  appellees’  own  statistics  demonstrate  that  the  role
played  by most  such  parties  and  candidates  in  the  overall
picture  of federal  campaign  financing  is marginal.  (See  A.
Br.  182;  SK  Br.  12.)°'

Our  point  is precisely  that  the  vital  role  which  the  more
unorthodox  third  parties  and  candidates  have  played,  see
Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354  U.S.  234,  251  (1957),
demonstrates  that  the  application  of systematic  disclosure
requirements  to  them  is both  destructive  and  purposeless.
As the amicus  Socialist  Labor  Party  has  candidly  declared,
it views  its  presidential-campaign  activities  as  a  soapbox,
‘another  means  of education  of the  public  regarding  their
political,  social,  economic  and  philosophical  beliefs  ..  .  .”’
In short,  its candidates  run  for office  not  to achieve  govern-
mental  power,  but  to  communicate  their  dissident  views  to
the  public.  Disclosure  in  that  context  cannot  consti-
tutionally  be justified.

Federal  appellees  in  particular  have  misread  the  ap-
plicable  precedents  concerning  freedom  of  association.®?

*'  The  opposing  briefs  are  replete  with  references  to  the  “stalking
horse”’  justification  for  imposing  disclosure  requirements  on  minor-
party  and  independent  candidates.  But  only  three  actual  examples  are
cited  (IA  Br.  179-80,  n.  210),  and  none  involved  the  actual  secret
funding  of  any  candidate  for  federal  office.  Nor  does  the  extensive
Statistical  record  show  any  excessively  large  or  special-interest  con-
tributions  to  any  third-party  candidate.  The  stalking-horse  problem  is
more  illusory  than  real.

*?  Federal  appellees  spend  several  pages  rebutting  an  argument  ap-
pellants  do  not  make  —  that  the  First  Amendment  provides  an  ab-
solute  right  to  associational  privacy  (AG  Br.  II 38-41,  43-44).

ee el

——

76

77

They  argue  that  “‘it  is a strained  concept”’  to say  that  those
who  support  a  candidate  “are  thereby  joining  in  a  new
‘association’  "’  (AG  Br.  II 45),  and  that  such  association  “‘is
very  distant  kin  to  the  right  of  assembly  which  the  First
Amendment  protects”  (id.  46).  Such  arguments  are  in flat
conflict  with  numerous  decisions  of  this  Court  involving
political  association.  See,  e.g.,  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,
354  U.S.  234,  250  (1957);  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51,
56-57  (1973);  Cousins  v.  Wigoda,  419  U.S.  477,  487  (1975);
Pollard  y.  Roberts,  283  F. Supp.  248 (E.D.  Ark.  1968),  aff'd
per  curiam,  393  U.S.  14  (1968).  If  the  most  effective
argument  in  defense  of  the  disclosure  requirements’  in-
trusion  into  freedom  of  association  is  that  political  cam-
paigns  do  not  involve  “‘association,”’  then  the  case  in  favor
of the  FECA’s  disclosure  provisions  is weak  indeed.”?

53  Similarly  unpersuasive  is the  novel  argument  of amici  State  Elec-
tion  Commissions  that  association  which  takes  the  form  of  a  con-
tribution  to  a  party  or  candidate  is not  protected  by the  First  Amend-
ment  (Br.  14-20).  Asserting  that  the  NAACP  cases  involved  mem-
bership  lists,  amici  argue  that  contributors,  as  opposed  to  members,
have  no  associational  privacy  rights.  The  premise  is inaccurate  and  the
conclusion  unsound.  First,  this  Court  has  never  suggested  any  such
constitutional  distinction  between  members  and  contributors  to  an
organization.  Indeed,  many  of the  NAACP  cases  involved  a government
demand  for  records  of contributors  as  well  as  for  membership  lists.  In
Bates  v.  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516,  519 (1960),  the challenged  ordinance
“would  have  required  disclosure  of  the  names  of the  organizations’
members  and  contributors’  (emphasis  added).  Gibson  y.  Florida
Legislative  Investigations  Committee,  372  U.S.  539,  542  (1963);
NAACP  v.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449,  461  (1958).  Moreover,  the
association  doctrine  has  been  applied  to  protect  the  identity  of  pur-
chasers  of  books  from  a  lobbying  organization,  United  States  v.
Rumley,  345  U.S.  41  (1953);  United  States  Servicemen’s  Fund  v.
Eastland,  488  F.2d  1252(D.C.  Cir.  1973),  rev'd  on  other grounds,  421  U.S.
491  (1975),  and.  of course.  contributions  to  a  political  party,  see  Pollard  v.
Roberts,  supra.  Freedom  of association  is applicable  to writing  a check  to
the  candidate  of one’s  choice  no  less  than  becoming  a  member  of an
organization  which  shares  one’s  beliefs.  We  have  earlier  suggested  that
(continued)

Nor do prior decisions  of this  Court  relied  on  by appellees
preclude  our  challenges  to  these  disclosure  provisions.  We
have  indicated  why neither  Burroughs  v.  United  States,  290
U.S.  534  (1934),  nor  United  States  v.  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612
(1954),  is controlling  here  (A.  Br.  186-87).°*  Nor  do  other
decisions  involving  different  disclosure  requirements  in
other  contexts  supply the proper  rule.**

**  (continued)

appropriate  lines  governing  disclosure  can  be drawn  (A.  Br.  184).  The
point  is  not  whether  any  particular  line  should  be  drawn,  but  rather.
that  Congress  failed  to draw  any  such  lines  at  all.

In  California  Bankers  Association  y.  Schultz,  416  US,  21.  78
(1974),  the concurring  opinion  of Justice  Powell  joined  by Justice  Black-
mun  made  clear  that  financial  contributions  are  eligible  for  con-
stitutional  protection:  ‘Financial  transactions  can  reveal  much  about  a
person's  activities,  associations,  and  beliefs.  At  some  point,  govern-
mental  intrusion  upon  these  areas  would  implicate  legitimate  ex-
pectations  of privacy.”

“  Burroughs  did  not,

as  the  intervening  appellees  contend,
“foreshadow”  First  Amendment  analysis  (IA  Br.  170).  Rather,  the
opinion  accorded  extensive  deference  to  legislative  choices  of means  to
achieve  particular  ends,  a  deference  which  in  the  First  Amendment
area  is no  longer  accepted.  See,  e¢.g..  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.
258 (1967).

In Harriss,  the court  did observe  that  the “indirect”  restraints  of self-
censorship  “comparable  in  many  ways  to  the  restraint  resulting  from
criminal  libel  laws,”’  were  too  remote  to  warrant  striking  an  otherwise
valid  enactment.  Yet,  in  subsequent  cases,  the  court  has  placed  great
emphasis  on  the  need  to  guard  against  self-censorship  in  the  areas  of
speech  and  association.  See,  ¢.g..  Speiser  v.  Randell,  357  U.S.  513,  526
(1958);  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  supra.

Appellees  wholly  disregard  the  various  standards  pro-
posed  by appellants  to  determine  which  parties  and  can-
didates  should  be  subject  to  disclosure  of contributions.”
(Compare  A.  Br.  184 with  IA  Br.  177-80  and  AG  Br.  II 30-
31.) The examples  appellees  offer —  the  “More  Republican

**  (continued)

condition  imposed  on  receipt  of  a  government  benefit,  insured  the
privilege  was  used  only  for  mailing  valid  publications,  and  were
justified  by  congressional  power  to  classify  categories  of  mail.  New
York  ex  rel.  Bryant  v.  Zimmerman,  278  U.S.  63  (1928),  upheld
disclosure  of “‘oath-bound”  organizations  such  as  the  Ku  Klux  Klan,
premised  on  the  need  to  deter  illegal  and  violent  activities.  Viereck  v.
United  States,  318  U.S.  236  (1943),  involved  the  Foreign  Agents
Registration  Act,  but  did  not  squarely  present  a  constitutional  attack
and  in  fact,  reversed  the  conviction  because  the  lower  courts  had  read
the statute  too  broadly.

Finally,  the  decision  in  Communist  Party  v.  Subversive  Activities
Control  Board,  367  U.S.  1 (1961),  is not  dispositive  here.  The  Court's
decision  there  was  predicated  on  the  rationale  that  the  Communist  Party
was  the agency  of a foreign  power  and  posed  a threat  to the government
itself.  The  government  interests  identified  in that  case  are  of a different
magnitude  than  the  prevention  of corruption  in  campaign  financing.
Moreover,  two  years  later,  this  Court  indicated  that  applying  the
rationale  of that  case  to groups  other  than  the  Communist  Party  would
raise  grave  constitutional  issues.  Gibson  v.  Florida  Legislative  In-
vestigation  Committee,  372  U.S.  539,  547 n.  2 (1963);  see  also  Boorda  v.
Subversive  Activities  Control  Board,  421  F.2d  1142  (1969), cert,  denied.
397  U.S.  1042 (1970).

©  Amici  State  Election  Commissions  state  that  it  is  ‘unclear’
whether  appellants  believe  that  only  candidates  not  entitled  to
automatic  ballot  placement  should  be exempted  from  compliance  with
the  disclosure  provision,  or  whether  the  Mississippi  Republican  Party
should  also  be exempt  (Amici  Br.  24).  The  answer  is simple.  We do not
contend  that  the  Mississippi  Republican  Party  should  be exempt  from
disclosure  as  a  third-party.  but  only  one  facet  of appellants’  argument
rests  on  the  chilling  effect  of  disclosure  to  third-party  and  independent
candidates.  (See  A.  Br.  178-84.)  Appellants  also  contend  that  the
FECA’s  requirements  are  generally  overbroad  as  to all political  parties
and  candidates,  particularly  with  regard  to  the  disclosure  thresholds.
(See  A.  Br.  170-77,  184-89.)

Party”  and  an  independent  Wallace  campaign  (IA  Br.  178-
79) —  ignore  the  possibility  that  these  hypothetical  cam-
paigns  might  in fact  be covered  even  under  more  narrowly
drawn  statutes.  This  disregard  for  constitutionally  more
permissible  modes  of  disclosure  demonstrates  appellees’
inability  to  meet  the  “‘less  intrusive  means”  test  required
when  First  Amendment  rights  are  at  stake.  United  States
v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  265-66  (1967);  Aptheker  v.
Secretary  of State,  378  U.S.  500,  512-14  (1964).

Appellees’  discussion  of  the  $10  and  $100  triggering
thresholds  for  reporting
is  similarly
inadequate.  They  nowhere  attempt  to  justify  Congress’
failure  to  provide  a  cost-of-living  adjustment  for  those
thresholds.  As  inflation  continues,  in  effect,  smaller  and
smaller  contributions  are  required  to be disclosed.*’

Moreover,  appellees  ignore  the  Attorney  General's

seriously  lack  proportionality  in  terms  of  varying  ex-
penditure  levels  and  potential
for  influence  on  office-

8]

lines  should  have  been  drawn,  but  the  fact  that  more
precise lines  were  not.”

Appellees  also contest  the quantum  of evidence  adduced  by
appellants  to  support  their  position  against  the  FECA’s
disclosure  requirements  (IA  Br.  172-73;  AG  Br.  II 47).  Ap-
pellants  have  already stated  that  the record  in this case  does
not  reflect  the  same  persistent  and  concentrated  forms  of
harassment  found  in  cases  like  NAACP  v.  Alabama,  357
U.S.  449  (1958)  (A.  Br.  173).  Nonetheless,  the  right  of
associational  privacy  does  not  turn  on  a harassment  index,
requiring  that  a  certain  minimum  number  of people  lose
their  jobs,  receive  threats  or  suffer  acts  of violence  before  a
statute  will  be stricken  down.  It also turns  on  an  assessment
of  the  need  for  disclosure.  Indeed,  the  court  below

5®  Amici  State  Elections  Commissions  express  concern  that  the
ruling  appellants  seek  would  eliminate  all  state  disclosure  statutes.
That  is  not  necessarily  true  with  respect  to  the  disclosure-thresholds
argument,  since  campaign  spending  for  federal  elections  is generally
much  higher  than  spending  in comparable  state  and  local  campaigns.
Nor  would  a decision  requiring  more  precise  line-drawing  where  minor-
party  or  independent  candidates  are  concerned  necessarily  invalidate
the variety  of state  disclosure  statutes.

The  Commissions  also  suggest  that  it woald  be constitutionally  un-
fair  and  politically  difficult  to exempt  some  candidates  and  committees
trom  disclosure  requirements.  However,  at  least  two  states,  Minnesota
and  Washington,  provide  a mechanism  for doing  so.  In Minnesota,  pur-
suant  to  the  Minnesota  Ethics  in  Government  Act  of  1974,  the  Min-
nesota  Ethics  Commission  is  statutorily  empowered  to  grant  exemp-
tions  from  disclosing  the  identity  of  contributors  to  political
associations  where  disclosure  would  expose  individuals  to  “economic
reprisals,  loss  of employment  or  threat  of physical  coercion.”  M.S.A.  §
10A.20.  sub  8.  Pursuant  to  that  mechanism.  the  Minnesota  Socialist
Workers  1974  Campaign  Committee  has  received  such  an  exemption.
See  Doe  v.  Martin,  _.____-*F.  Supp.  ___  (D.D.C..  Oct.  20.  1975.
Civil  Action  No.  75-0083)  (involving  a  minor-party  challenge  to  the
disclosure  requirements  of  the  1974  District  of  Columbia  Campaign
Finance  Reform  and  Conflict  of Interest  Act.  Pub.  L.  93-376,  88  Stat.
446).

recognized  this  in  striking  down  2  U.S.C.  § 437a,  relat-
ing  to  disclosure  of  contributors  to  “‘issue  oriented”’

harm  which  would  be suffered  by the NYCLU,  in the course
of  the  Court  of  Appeals’  extensive  discussion  of  Section
437a  (op. at  1549-60),  only one  footnote  (op. at  1551  n.  130)
was  devoted  to  describing  that  harm,  and  the  Court  was
silent  on  the  harm  suffered  by Human  Events,  Inc.  Instead
the  court  quite  properly  focused  on  the  utter  lack  of  any
justification  for the section.*’

To  demand  voluminous  proof of the  “chilling”  effect  of
the  challenged  legislation  can  only  result  in  a  self-fulfilling
prophecy  —  minor-party  and  independent  candidacies
will  be  aborted  at  their  inception,  there  will  be  no  con-
tributors,  and  hence  there  will  be no  “proof”  of harassment
to  such  would-be  donors.  As  Chief  Judge  Bazelon  said  in
dissent,  “*.  .
.  minor  parties  would  wither  and  die  on  the
vine  awaiting  the evidence  required  [by the majority  below].
The  spectre  of reputations  biotted  and  lives  ruined  by the
release  of  membership  lists  is  still  too  vivid  to  claim
ignorance  of the  danger  disclosure  poses.  ‘All  others  see
and  understand  this.  How  can  we  properly  shut  our  minds
to it?’

(Op. at  1594-96).°°

°°  One  of  the  minor-party  amici  notes  the  irony  that,  under  the
ruling  below,  various  issue  organizations  are  now  free,  and  necessarily
so,  to  have a significant  impact  on  the  political  process  without  being
subject  to  disclosure  requirements,  while  minor  parties  which  seek  to
have  a similar  impact  are  subject  to the  full  thrust  of this  statute  simply
because  they  run  candidates  for  office  as  part  of their  effort  to  com-
municate  to the public  (Socialist  Labor  Party  Br.  10-13).

°°  Moreover,  the  record-keeping  burdens  themselves  pose  added  ob-
stacles and  difficulties.  The  disclosure  requirements  not  only  burden
associational  rights,  but  also  strain  “the  minor  party's  hard-pressed
resources,  financial  and  human.  to  interpret  the  provisions.  compile  the

Appellants  have  argued  that  2 U.S.C.  § 434e),  requiring
that  all  persons  who  make  expenditures  under  18 U.S.C.  §
608(e)  in  excess  of  $100  per  calendar  year  file  detailed
reports  of  such  expenditures  with  the  FEC,  is  con-
stitutionally  deficient  (A.  Br.  189-92).  Appellees  are  all  but
silent  on  the  specific  issue,  have  in  no  way  rebutted  this
contention,  and  have  failed  to  distinguish  the  one  case
squarely  on  point,  Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.  60 (1960).
(See  IA  Br.  173 n.  202,  and  AG  Br.  II 40-41.)  Section  434e)
is uniquely  chilling  since  its reporting  requirements  fall, not
on  political  organizations,  but  on  private  citizens,  engaged
in  what  they  would  reasonably  regard  as  normal  private
conduct,  and  who  will  most  probably  be  unaware  of the

IX.

The  Commission  Is Unconstitutionally  Constituted;  Its Im-
permissible  Powers,  Its  Domination  by Congress,  and  the
Intolerable  Vagueness  of the  Statute  It Administers  Make  It
the Most  Inappropriate  and Dangerous  Means  Imaginable  for
Massive  Regulation  of the Political  Process.

Since  the  members  of the  Federal  Election  Commission
are  officers  of the  United  States,  under  Article  II, § 2, cl.  2
of  the  Constitution  they  must  be  appointed  by  the  Pres-
ident,  subject  to  Senate  confirmation.  Since  the  current
scheme  provides  for appointment,  without  presidential  par-
ticipation,  of four  members  of the  Commission  by the  two
Houses  of  Congress,  the  Commission  is  improperly  con-
stituted,  and  its  existence  shouid  be  held  unconstitutional
by this  Court.

In response,  the  FEC  argues  that  the principal  challenges
to the  makeup  of the  Commission  are  not  ripe and  that  ap-
pellants  lack  standing  to  make  those  challenges.  The  Com-
mission  also  defends  most,  but  not  all,  of  its  statutory
powers  on  the  merits.*?  Amici  Scott  and  Kennedy  do  not
defend  the  Commission’s  powers  with  regard  to  civil  and
criminal  enforcement,  SK  Br.  at  109,  restricting  their  sup-
port  of the  Commission  to  its  other  powers.  The  Attorney
General  and  the  United  States  argue  that,  if the  questions
with  respect  to  the  Commission’s  powers  are  ripe,  many  of
upon _ the
those
prerogatives  of  the  executive  and  should  be  held  un-
constitutional  (AG  Br.  I 110-20).

unconstitutionally

impinge

powers

*?  Even  the  Commission  makes  no  attempt  to  justify  its  power  to
disqualify  candidates  for  federal  office.  as  it does  with  respect  to  every
other  power  it  admits  to  having  (FEC  Br.  42  n.  36).  Such  a  power  is
clearly  unconstitutional.  Powell  v.  McCormack.  395  U.S.  486  (1969).
and  appellants  have  a  direct  and  immediate  personal  stake  in  the
legitimacy  of penalties  which  they and  their  candidates  may  face.  Baker
v.  Carr,  369  U.S.  186 (1962).

lr ee a ee Re ee ER Te Se

86

87

The  questions  respecting  the Commission  are  ripe for ad-
judication,  and  appellants  do possess  the requisite  personal
interest  tc  assert  claims  of  unconstitutionality.  Congress
has  endowed  the  Commission  with  powers  which  neither
the  Congress  nor  an  “‘arm  of Congress,”  which  the  Com-
mission  admittedly  is,  may  constitutionally  perform.
Finally,  these  powers  are  integral  to  Congress’  conception
of the  FEC,  and  they  cannot  be  “excised”  from  the  FECA
without  violating  the  evident  and  clear  intent  of Congress.
The  proper  judicial  course  is to  hold  the  Commission  un-
constitutional  in  its  inception,  permitting  the  Congress  to
create  a  truly  independent  agency  to  enforce  those  restric-
tions  which  it  may  constitutionally  impose  upon  the
political  process.

A.  The  Constitutional  Issues  with  Respect  to  the
Legitimacy  of the  Federal  Election  Commission  Are  Ripe
and Are Properly  Raised  by Appellants.

The  1976  elections  are  one  year  away.  Campaigning  for
federal  offices  at  all  levels  is  already  in  progress.  James
Buckley  will  likely  run  for  re-election  to  the  United States
Senate;  Eugene  McCarthy  is  running  for  the  Presidency;
William  Steiger  will  either  run  for  re-election  to the  House  of
Representatives  or  seek  election  to  the  Senate,  and  each of
the other  appellants  is a direct  and  active  participant  in  the
political  process.  The  Commission’s  presence
federal
astride  that  process  is monumental  and  its  impact  thereon
overwhelming.  Its  already-exercised  powers,  including
rulemaking  and  issuance  of  advisory  opinions,  constitute
principal  means  of enforcement  of the  FECA.  They  present-
ly inhibit  the  freedom  of action  of each  of the  appellants
and  their  supporters,  members,  contributors,  and  can-
didates.

Appellants  are  directly  subject  to  FEC  regulation;  most
of them  fall  into  the  special  class  of persons  whom  the  Act

recognizes  as  most  intensely  impacted  by its  provisions  by
giving  them  the  right  to  seek  advisory  opinions  from  the
Commiss‘on,*?  2 U.S.C.  § 437f.  Congress  has,  moreover,
provided  that  defined  classes  of  persons,  which  include
appellants,  may  bring suit  challenging  the  FECA  including
the  provisions  relating  to  the  Commission.  2  U.S.C.  §
437h(a).*  Appellants  plainly  have  “such  a  personal  stake
in the outcome”’  of this  lawsuit  as  to grant  them  Article  III
standing.  Baker  v.  Carr,  369  U.S.  186,  204  (1962).  The
Commission  presently  is operating  with  its full  powers  and
is thereby  impinging  upon  the  rights of appellants,  who  are
bound  by its actions.  Cf. Regional  Rail  Reorganization  Act
Cases,  419  U.S.  102,  143 (1974).  To equate,  as  appellees  do,
appellants’  interest  here  to that  of any citizen  or  taxpayer  is
ludicrous.

Appellees  contend  that,  even  if the  Commission  is  im-
properly  constituted,  appellants  are  not  seriously  affected
because  it makes  no  difference  to  them  who  enforces  the
FECA  (FEC  Br.  8, 10;  see  also  SK  Br.  109,  AG  Br.  I 107).
First,  we  note  that  the  Court  of  Appeals  held  that  ap-
pellants  had  standing  and  proceeded  to  decide  on  their
merits  the certified  questions  concerning  the  method  of ap-

**  The  Scott-Kennedy  brief,  SK  Br.  21,  is wholly  incorrect  in stating

that  the Commission  is required  to give “‘anyone”’  an  advisory  opinion.

**  See  Warth  v.  Selden,  43  U.S.L.W.  4906  (June  25,  1975).  The
passage  from  the  Conference  Report  which  appellees  attempt  to  con-
Strue  as  excepting  Commission-related  issues  from  this  provision  (FEC
Br.  11)  cannot  possibly  bear  that  construction.  The  Report's  phrase
“related  to  the  activities  regulated  by the  Act”  is patently  a  shorthand
description  of the  criminal-code  provisions  enumerated  in  2 U.S.C.  §
437h(a)  and  does  not  limit  the  unqualified  right  of action  with  respect
to  ‘any  provision  of this  Act.”

As  to  the  Commission-related  issues,  Section  437h(a)  is  effectively
equivalent  to  the  statutes  in many  jurisdictions  which  authorize  specific
classes  of private  persons,  defined  with  reference  to their  interest  in the
question,  to bring quo  warranto  proceedings.  74 C.J.S.2d  222-23.

88

89

pointment  of the Commission  and  the validity  of those  of its
powers  which  the  court  believed  ripe  (op.  at  1572,  1582,
1585).

Appellees’  argument  is  ironic  in  view  of  their  general
defense  of the challenged  legislation  as  a ‘‘reform”’  measure
designed  to  restore  public  confidence.  It  is  hardly  con-
sistent  with  such  a goal for the entire  political  process  to be
dominated  by an  agency  whose  constitutionality  is subject
to  the  gravest  doubts  if there  exist  no  prompt  and  effective
means  for  resolving  those  doubts.  Even  the  Attorney
General,  while  agreeing  that  the  constitutional  questions
are  ‘of the  first  magnitude,”’  AG  Br.  I 10S,  asks  this  Court
to refrain  from  deciding  them.  Fortunately,  the  argument  is
without  merit.

of  these

the  administration

The  separation  of  powers  is  not  designed  for  the  sole
benefit  of federal  officeholders.®*  It is of vast  importance  to
appellants,  who  are  immediately  affected  by the  operation
and  enforcement  of the  FECA,  that  the  persons  entrusted
con-
with
stitutionally  be  so  empowered.  Where,  moreover,  the  in-
terests  appellants  represent  are  for  the  most  part  political
“outs’’  —  challengers  to  incumbents,  third  parties,  in-
dependent  candidates  and  the  like  —,  our  contention  here
that  the  Commission  is so  constituted  that  it will  disfavor
such  interests,  and  is impermissibly  under  the  thumb of in-
cumbent  Congressmen,  is  one  that  plainly  affects  ap-
pellants’  most  vital  interests.

statutes

can

“  The  selective-service  cases  cited  at  FEC  Br.  10  n.  6,  are  wholly
inapposite;  they  turned  on  special  features  of selective-service  law,  t.¢.,
preinduction  vy.  postinduction  remedies  and  the  interpretation  of  a
regulation  that  draft  board  members  shall  “‘if  at  all  practicable,  be
residents  of the  area  in  which  their  local  board  has  jurisdiction.”  F.g..
United  States  v.  Chaudron,  425  F.2d  605,  611  (8th  Cir.  1970).  None  of
these  cases  was  dismissed  for  lack  of standing.

This  Court  has  long been  committed  to ‘maintaining  the
constitutional  plan  of separation  of powers.’’  Glidden  Co.
v.  Zdanok,  370  U.S.  530,  536 (1962)  (opinion  of Harlan,  J.).
The  Court  has  vigorously  exercised  its obligation  to  protect
citizens  from  a Congress  bent  on  enforcing  its will  as  well  as
enacting  it.  Watkins  v.  United  States,  354  U.S.  178  (1957):
Quinn  y.  United  States,  349  U.S.  155  (1955);  Marshall  vy.
Gordon,  243  U.S.  521  (1917);  Kilbourn  v.  Thompson,  103
U.S.  168  (1881).  In  cases  impacting  the  separation  of
powers,  standing  has  not  been  limited  to  the  official  par-
ticipation  of the  branch  of government  arguably  aggrieved,
but  has  been  extended  to those  having  a direct  and  concrete
interest  in  observance  of  the  constitutional  principle  in
question.  Coleman  vy.  Miller,  307  U.S.'433  (1939);  Mitchell
v.  Laird,  488  F.2d  611  (D.C.  Cir.  1973);  Kennedy  v.  Samp-
son,  S11  F.2d  430  (D.C.  Cir.  1974).  The  appellants  plainly
meet  that  test  here.

The  substantive  context  in  which  the  FEC  operates
militates  strongly  in  favor  of this  Court's  jurisdiction  over
the  constitutional  challenge  to  the  Commission's  existence
and  powers.  The  Commission  enforces  statutes  which  at-
tempt  to regulate  the  most  fundamental  of our  liberties:  the
right  to  speak.  Even  assuming  that  some of the  substantive
provisions  appellants  challenge  are  constitutional,  it  is
essential  that  the  enforcement  responsibilities  under  the
FECA  be  in  the  proper  constitutional  hands.  Cf.  Freed-
man  v.  Maryland,  380  U.S.  51  (1965);  Lamont  vy.  Post-
master  General,  381  U.S.  301  (1965).  As  this  Court  held  in
Miami  Herald  Publishing  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241,  247
n.  6  (1974),  “[wJhichever  way  we  were  to  decide  on  the
merits,  it would  be  intolerable  to  leave  unanswered,  under
these  circumstances’’  an  important  issue  which  goes  to  the
very  core  of appellants’  freedoms  under  the  First  Amend-
ment  and  the  doctrine  of  separation  of  powers.  See  also
Mills  vy.  Alabama,  384  U.S.  214,  221-22  (1966)  (Douglas,  J.,
concurring);  Organization for a  Better  Austin  v.  Keefe,  402

90

9]

U.S.  415,  418  n.  (1971).  Under  well-established  principles
of constitutional  adjudication,  this Court  possesses  jurisdic-
tion  over  appellants’  challenge  to the  FEC.

B.  Congress  May  Not  Usurp  the  Powers  of  Ad-
ministering  and  Enforcing  Laws  Regulating  the  Federal
Electoral  Process.

The  Commission  asserts  that  (1)  Congress  has  special
responsibility  over  the  federal  electoral  process;  (2)  this
special  responsibility  implies  that  Congress  may  establish
and  enforce,  without  Executive  participation,  regulations
controlling  the  conduct  of Members  and  non-Members  of
Congress  as  that  conduct  may  affect  the  federal  electoral
process;  (3)  this  right  of  control  may  be  devolved  by
Congress  on  its  delegates;  (4)  the  FEC  is  an  arm  of
Congress;  therefore,  (S)  the  FEC  can  properly  administer
and  enforce  the  FECA  and  Subtitle  H.

There  is no  real  dispute  that  the  FEC  is in fact,  by virtue
particularly  of  the  appointment  of  four  of  its  members
solely  by  Congress  and  the  retention  by  Congress  of  the
power  to  veto  its  actions,  an  arm  of Congress,  an  integral
part  of the  legislative  branch  of the  government.®*  All  par-
ties  appear  to  agree  that,  if it is not,  or  if the  nature of its
powers  means  it cannot  be,  the  manner  of appointing  it is
patently  unconstitutional.  Constitution,  Art.  II,  § 2, cl. 2;
see  Myers  v.  United  States,  272  U.S.  52 (1926).  Departing
from  that  common  ground,  however,  the  argument  for  the
Commission's  preservation  breaks  down  irretrievably.

**  Apparently,  only  amici  Scott  and  Kennedy  view  the  Commission
otherwise  (SK  Br.  119).  The  Commission  believes  itself  to  be  an  arm  of
Congress,  FEC  Br.  36,  as  do  the  Attorney  General,  the  United  States.
the  Court  of Appeals,  and  appellants.  See  AG  Br.  1110.  The  Congress
appoints  a  majority  of the  Commissioners  and  uses  the  legislative  veto
as  a  means  to  “control|  | its own  instrumentality.”  /d.  at  112.

A a he te

Crucial  to  the  Commission's  position  is  the  argument
that  Congress  has  uniquely  broad  administrative  respon-
sibilities  with  regard  to  the  federal  electoral  process.  In
fact,  Congress’  authority  in the  area  has  been  narrowly  con-
strued  by this  Court  and  can  in no  way  support  the massive
intrusion  upon  Executive  prerogatives  attempted  through
the establishment  of the  FEC.

for  Senators

Article  I, § 4, cl.  1 provides  that  Congress  may  “by  Law”
make  or  alter  regulations  pertaining  to  the  ‘Times,  Places
and  Manner  of  holding  elections
and
Representatives.’’
This  involves  merely  the  power  to
legislate,  and  in no  sense  authorizes  Congress  to  act  outside
of its  traditional  legislative  role,  subject  to  the  President's
veto  power.  See  Oregon  v.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  117
(1970)  (opinion  of  Justice  Black).°?  The  Commission  also
relies  upon  Art.  I, § S, cls.  1 and  2. The  first  clause  provides
in  relevant  part:  ‘‘Each  House  shall  be  the  Judge  of  the
Elections,  Returns  and  Qualifications  of its own  Members  .
...'  The  second  clause  provides  in  relevant  part:  ‘Each
House  may  ..  .  punish  its  Members  for disorderly  behavior,
and,  with  the  Concurrence  of two-thirds,  expel  a Member.”

The  jurisdiction  of each  House  of Congress  to  “Judge”
the elections,  returns,  and  qualifications  of its Members  at-

rights  of membership.”  Barry  v.  United  States  ex  rel.  Cun-

I,  §  5,  was

That  the  power  granted  in the first  clause  of Art.  I, § S, is
to  be  strictly  limited  was  made  plain  in  Powell  v.  Mc-
Cormack,  395  U.S.  486  (1969).  The  Court  there  held  that
the  House,  in judging  the  qualifications  of petitioner  Powell
to  the  standing
under  Art.
qualifications  prescribed  in  the  Constitution.”  /d.  at  S50.
The  Court  was  particularly  concerned  with  protecting  the
Constitution’s  intention  ‘‘to  require  a  two-thirds  vote  for
expulsion,”  id.  at  548,  and  concluded  “that  the  Con-
stitution  does  not  vest  in the Congress  a discretionary  power
to deny membership  by a majority  vote.”  /bid.

“limited

Powell  vy.  McCormack  involved  an  attempt  to  exclude,
not  expel,  a  Member.  The  power  to  expel  is derived  from
the  second  clause  of Art.  I, § 5, which  speaks  of the  power
of Congress  to  punish  and,  by a  two-thirds  vote,  expel  ‘‘a
Member.”  The  grant  of power  is specific  and  quite limited.
The  purpose  of the  grant  of power  is to  protect  Congress’
‘interest  in  preserving  its  institutional  integrity.”  /d.  at
548.  It is employed  ‘‘in  extreme  cases,”’  id.,  and  can  be  in-
voked  only  by a vote  of  two thirds  of the  House  in question.
Whether  there  are  additional  substantive  or  procedural
limitations  on  this  power  was  a  question  left  open  by the
Court  in  Powell  vy.  McCormack,  supra,  395  U.S.  at  S07  n.
27,  n.  30;  but  there  is no  question  that  the  power  may  only
be  exercised  with  respect  to  Members,  that  it  requires  a
two-thirds  vote,  and  that  it  may  not  be  exercised  with
respect  to other  actors  in the  political  arena.

Far  from  endowing  Congress  with  a  broad  mandate  to
regulate  the  electoral  process  without  participation  of the
Executive,  these  sections  of  the  Constitution  do  no  more

i oe ee — -

Re a tt a CE lI as ee Nl a a

than  carve  out  certain  narrow  areas  in which  Congress  may
regulate  itself  and  police  the  conduct  of its own  Members.
And  these  special  areas  where  Congress  may  act  have  been
carefully  circumscribed  by this  Court.  To  attempt  an  ex-
trapolation  from  such  narrow  areas  to  the  extraordinarily
broad  powers  Congress  would  assume  for  its  delegates
through  the  FEC  has  beer  charitably  described  by the  At-
torney  General  and  the  United  States  as  “a  simple  non-
sequitur”  (AG  Br.  I 119).  The  bald  statement  of such  a
position  by  the  Commission  at  p.  26  of  its  brief  merely
demonstrates  the  flight  of legal  fancy  the  Commission  in-
dulges  in to protect  its existence.”

The  Commission  relies  on  what  it argues  is a long history
of Congressional  entrustment  of powers  to  its  delegates  in
policing  the  electoral  process  (FEC  Br.  29-30).  Appellants
do  not  contend  that  Congress  acted  illegally  in  the  past  in
establishing  certain  of its  officers®®  as  receiving  agents  for
campaign-financing  reports,  and  if the  Commission  were
merely  to  act  as  a depository  for  such  information  it might
not  impinge  unduly  upon  Executive  functions.”

**  Even  if Congress  had  the  powers  the  Commission  claims  for  it in
overseeing  all  aspects  of federal  elections,  it is clear  that  such  powers
could  not  be delegated  by Congress  to  any  other  body.  Exclusion  or  ex-
pulsion  of a  Member  is an  action  which  only  Congress  can  take.  See.
e.g..  Powell  v.  McCormack,  supra.

**  Of course,  Congress  can  appoint  officers  of its own  to  assist  it in
the  performance  of  its  tasks.  While  they  are  officers  of  the  United
States,  Art.  II, § 2, cl.  2 does  not  require  that  they be presidentially  ap-
pointed,  for  the  clause  expressly  exempts  from  that  requirement  of-
ficers  whose  appointment  is ‘otherwise  provided  for.”  Appointment  of
congressional  officers  is provided  for by Art.  I, § 2. cl.  5; § 3, cl.  5.

But  see  pp.  104-06,  infra.  The  enforcement  powers  were  the  functions
considered  by Congress  most  crucial  to  the  concept  of  a  separate  Com-
mission  to  participate  in  the  regulation  of the  political  process  under
the  FECA.  That  the  Commission  may  have  other  powers  which  stand-
ing alone  might  not  violate  the  separation  of powers  does  not  save  the
Commission  from  invalidation.  See pp.  108-11,  infra.

94

95

The  Commission  makes  numerous  references  to the  role
of  the  Comptroller  General  in  the  administration  of  prior
campaign-finance  legislation.  See,  e.g.,  FEC  Br.  31, 32, 33,
34,  40.  Whatever  role  the  Comptroller  General  has  had  in
past  regulation  of politics  (never  considered  by the courts)  is
totally  unhelpful  to the  Commission.  As Judge  MacKinnon
points  out  in  his  dissent  below,  op.  at  1615,  and  as  ap-
pellants  pointed  out  in their  opening  brief,  A.  Br.  205,  the
Comptroller  General  is appointed  by the  President  in con-
formity  with  Article  II,  § 2, cl.  2 —  a  fact  nowhere  men-
tioned  by the  Commission.  The  Comptroller  General  and
the  General  Accounting  Office  are  in fact  and  substance  an
independent  agency,  appointed  for  a fixed  term  and  acting
“without  direction  from  any  other  officer,’”’  31  U.S.C.  § 44,
not  a subservient  “‘arm  of Congress”  like the  FEC.”

Finding  a paucity  of evidence  in the  area  of regulation  of
politics  to  support  its  contention  that  Congress  may  appoint
officers  of the  United  States  who  perform  executive  func-
tions,  the  Commission  turns  to  a  variety  of  historical
‘‘precedents”  which  include  the  American  Printing  House
for the  Blind  and  the  National  Asylum  (FEC  Br.  at  18).  It is
difficult  to  imagine  what  relevance  these  incongruously
minor  situations  (never  considered  in the courts)  have  to do
with  the  massive  and  unprecedented  invasion  of executive
prerogative  attempted  in the formation  of the  FEC.

’"'  Even  so,  it  is  as  true  today  as  it  was  in  1939  that  “the  con-
stitutionality  of  the  Comptroller  General's  position  has  been  much
debated,  to  no  conclusive  result...  .  A decisive  answer  waits  upon  the
.  .”
pronouncement  of an  authoritative  trilunal.  None  such  has  spoken  .
Mansfield,  The  Comptroller  General  74 (1939).

United  States  v.  Stewart,  234  F.  Supp.  94,  99  (D.D.C.),  aff'd.  339
F.2d  753,  (D.C.  Cir.  1964),  expressly  held  that  with  respect  to  his  ad-
ministrative  functions  the  Comptroller  General  is  a  part  of  the
executive  branch,  and  that  he  is a  legislative  officer  on/y  with  respect  to
the  conduct  of investigations.

ee ee ee
et,

on

The

also

relies

Commission

congressional
designations  of named  persons  to particular  military  ranks.
In  Wood  v.  United  States,  107  U.S.  414,  417  (1882),  this
Court  held  that  a military  officer  can  be appointed  only  by
the  President  pursuant  to Article  II, § 2, cl.  2, but  that  once
an  officer  had  been  appointed  by  that  means,  Congress
could  change  his  rank  or  pay,  since  ‘‘this  is not  to  appoint
him  to  a  new  or  different  office.’  The  practice  thus  offers
the Commission  no  solace  here.

The  two  principal  alleged  precedents  on  which  the  Com-
mission  relies  are  the  presidential  succession  acts  and  a
statute  authorizing  the  reduction  of the  national  debt  (FEC
Br.  17-19).  Reliance  on  the  succession  acts  is astonishing  in
view  of Congress’  express  constitutional  power  (Art.  II, § 1,
cl.  6, now  supplemented  by the  Twenty-Fifth  Amendment)
to provide  by law  for the  presidential  succession.  Obviously
appointment  to that  office  is ‘‘otherwise  provided  for,”’  Art.
II, § 2, cl. 2, with  a vengeance,  and  thus  is not  subject  to the
presidential-appointment  requirement  of that  clause.

The  1792  national-debt  statute,  1 Stat.  281,  provided,  in
sections  6 and  7, that  the  President  of the  Senate,  the  Chief
Justice,  the  Secretary  of  State,  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury,  and  the  Attorney  General  should  be  com-
missioners  any  three  of whom,  “‘with  the  approbation  of the
President  of the  United  States,’  were  authorized  to  pur-
chase  and  redeem  the debt  of the  United  States.  1 Stat.  282-
83.  The  power  to  reduce  the  debt  was,  in  effect,  given  to
three  Executive  appointees,  and  the  Commission  could  not
act  without  the  “‘approbation”  of the  President.  When  the
statute  is read  in  the  full  context  the  Commission  did  not
choose  to  provide,  it  offers  no  support  whatever  for  the
right  of the  Commission  to  exist  under  the  constitutional
scheme  of separation  of powers.”

”*  The  Chief Justice  who  was  designated  as  a commissioner  was  John
Jay.  who  while  Chief  Justice  also  negotiated  the  treaty  which  bears  his

C.  The  Commission's  Principal  Powers  Are  Con-
stitutionally  Incapable  of Being  Exercised  by an  Arm  of
Congress.

The  FECA  grants  to the  Commission  powers  which  can-
not  be  performed  by  Congress  directly  or  through  its
agents,  but  only  by officers  of the  United  States  appointed
pursuant  to Article  II, § 2, cl. 2.

Rulemaking.  Integral  to  the  enforcement  of the  laws  is
the  power  to  make  rules  and  regulations  in interpreting  the
meaning  of  the  laws.  Interpretation  of  laws  through
rulemaking  is not  a legislative  function.  Hampton  &  Co.  v.
United  States,  276  U.S.  394,  406-11  (1928).  Substantive
of  ad-
rulemaking
ministration,  id.,  but  if it is to  be exercised  by Congress  or
its agents  such  rules,  amounting  to new  legislation,  must  be
subject  to  presidential  veto.  The  Commission  —  subject  to
congressional  veto  —  has  both  kinds  of rulemaking  power,
and  both  are  bad.

is  likewise

a  matter

primarily

government  may  invoke  the  action  of the  other  two  branches
“in  so  far  as  the  action  invoked  shall  not  be  an  assump-
tion  of the  constitutional  field  of action  of another  branch.”
Id.
In  that  context  the  Court  stated  that  Congress
has  often  found  it  necessary  to  authorize  by  statute  the
Executive  officers  ‘“‘to secure  the exact  effect  intended  by its
acts  of legislation,  by vesting  discretion  in  such  officers  to
make  public  regulations  interpreting  a  statute  and  direc-
ting  the  details  of its  execution,  even  to  the  extent  of
providing  for  penalizing  a  breach  of  such  regulations.”
Id.  (emphasis  added.)  See also id.  at 409.

It is plain  that  the  powers  to  “formulate  general  policy”’
under  the  FECA  and  the  parallel  sections  of the  criminal
code,  2  U.S.C.  § 437d(aX9),  and  to  prescribe  rules  and
regulations,  §§  437d(aX8),  438(aXi0),  are  executive,  not
legislative,  in character,  and  cannot  be appropriated  by the
Legislature  for  itself  or  for  a  legislative  agency.  The  At-
torney  General  agrees  (AG  Br.  I 116-19).

The Hampton  case  involved  the so-called  ‘‘flexible  tariff”
provision  of the  Tariff  Act  of  1922  which  authorized  the
President,  after  investigation,  to  alter  tariffs  levied  by the
United  States  to  equalize  tariff  penalties  between  it  and
competing  foreign  countries.  The  Court  held  that  Congress
had  not  unlawfully  delegated  any  of its legislative  power  to
the  President.  The  Court  first  noted  that  “‘it  is  a breach  to
the  National  fundamental  law  if  Congress  gives  up  its
legislative  power  and  transfers  it to the  President,  or  to the
Judicial  branch,  or  if by law  it attempts  to invest  itself or  its
members  with  either  executive  power  or  judicial  power.”
Id.  at  406.  The  Court,  however,  stated  that  one  branch  of

*  (Continued)

name  with  Great  Britain.  Presumably  not  even  the  Commission  would
argue  that  it would  today  be considered  proper,  on  the  basis  of any  such
precedent,  for  the  Chief  Justice  to  negotiate  a  treaty  with  a  foreign
nation.

While  it  is  settled  that  certain  types  of  rulemaking
powers  may  be  vested  in  an  independent  agency  whose
members  are  appointed  by  the  President,  see  Humphrey's
Execuuor  vy.  United  States,  295  U.S.  602  (1935),  it does  not
follow  that  Congress  can  reserve  such  powers  to  itself
through  the  vehicle  of an  agency  totally  subject  to  its  con-
trol.  If the  Commission  can  validly  make  rules,  those  rules
have  the  force  of  law  and  are  binding  on  the  population.
Since  Congress  has  reserved  to itself (and  is vigorously  exer-
cising)  the  power  to  veto  such  rules,  2 U.S.C.  § 438(c),  the
rules  become  effective  essentially  as  actions  by Congress  it-
self.  As such  —  that  is, as  legislative  acts,  which  is the  basis
on  which  the  Commission  tries  to defend  them  (FEC  Br.  54)
—  they  are  fatally  defective  because  the  constitutionally
required  participation  of  the  President  in  the  legislative
process  (Art.  I, § 7. cl.  2) has  been  excluded.  No  legislative
act  by  Congress  can  acquire  the  force  of  law  unless  the

Commission's
rulemaking  power  with  respect  to the  criminal  code  and  its
mandate  (2  U.S.C.  § 437d(aX9))  to  “formulate  general
policy”  with  respect  to  the  enforcement  of  criminal
statutes;  such  powers  trench  too  sharply  on  the  con-
stitutional  responsibility  of  the  Executive  faithfully  to
execute  the  laws  of the land  (Art  II, § 3). The  Commission  is
unwilling  even  to  attempt  a  defense  of the  FECA’s  vesting
of  such  powers  in  an  “arm  of  Congress.”  Indeed,  and
astonishingly  considering  its  own  actions  to  the  contrary,
the  Commission  baldly  tells  this  Court  that  it has  not  been
given  authority  to  promulgate  rules  governing  the  Title  18
criminal  provisions  imposing  expenditure  and  contribution
limitations  (FEC  Br. 49, 55).

Special  counsel  have  apparently  not  yet  heard  of  the
regulation  promulgated  by the  Commission,  and  rejected
by  the  Senate,  which  expressly  provided  that  certain
amounts  contributed  to  and  expended  from  so-called  ‘‘of-
fice  accounts”  of  Members  of  Congress  were  to  be  con-
sidered  contributions  or  expenditures  “‘subject  to  the
limitations  of 18  U.S.C.  §§ 608,  610,  611,  613.  614  and  615,”
(emphasis  added),  which  of  course  are  the  criminal
provisions  in  question.  Sen.  Rep.  No.  94-409,  94th  Cong.
Ist  Sess.  (October  6, 1975)  at  5-7,  9.”

>  The  Commission  ignores  the  fact  that  the disclosure  and  reporting
provisions,  as  to  which  it does  assert  rulemaking  authority,  are  them-
selves  criminal  provisions.  with  violations  punishable  by up to  one  year
in prison.  2 U.S.C.  § 441 (a).

Curiously.  the  Commission  never  published  these  rules  in  the
Federal  Register.  A  notice  of  proposed  rulemaking.  containing  the
same  language  quoted  above,  appears  at  40  Fed.  Reg.  32951.  § 113.2
(1975).

+ a ore eee.

Similar  failures  of  communication  between  the  Com-
mission  and  its special  counsel  have not  been  unusual  in the
course  of this  litigation.  (See  A.  Br.  203  & n.  7.)  Such  in-
consistencies  indicate  that  any  disclaimers  by  the  Com-
mission  with  respect  to  what  powers  it posseses  should  be
treated  with  the  utmost  caution.  The  Commission  should
be judged  on  the basis  of the powers  it has been  given by the
FECA,  or  at  least  on  the  basis  of the  powers  it is presently
exercising,  and  not  on  the  basis  of those  powers  its counsel
feci  willing to defend  before  this Court.’4

Nor  is  the  rule  mentioned  above  an  isolated  example.
The  “‘interim  guidelines”  published  by the  Commission  on
August  21,  1975,  to  govern  the  special  New  Hampshire
Senate  election,  40  Fed.  Reg.  40668  (1975),  consist  of
detailed  instructions
and  others
specifying precisely  what  conduct  is lawful  and  what  would
be criminal,  with  explicit  references  to the  relevant  sections
of the  criminal  code.  Many  of these  directions  are  not  even
interpretative,  but  substantive  and  administrative:  for
example:

Even  purely  interpretative  rules  nominally  limited  to  the
disclosure  provisions  are  beyond  the  power  of an  “arm  of
Congress”  for  the  reasons  stated  above  and  at  pp,  104.96,
infra.

The  Commission  has  also  begun  to  issue  rules  im-
plementing  the  federal-subsidy  provisions,  e.g..  40  Fed.
Reg.  40671,  47688,  41933  (1975).  These  are  largely  sub-
stantive  rules  governing  administration,  rather  than  in-
terpretative  rules.  Merely  as  one  example,  candidates  seeking
federal  funds  are  required  to  keep and  submit  photocopies
of checks  for  all  contributions,  id.  at  47689,  § 131.2  (cX2),
an  obligation  nowhere  suggested  in  the  statute.  I.R.C.  §
903H%b),
Commission
rulemaking  under  Subtitle  H,  plainly  permits  substantive
as  well  as  interpretative  rules.  But  administration  of  the
federal-subsidy  provisions  is no  more  a  permissible  activity
for  an  “arm  of Congress”  than  administration  of the  tax  laws
or  the  nation’s  foreign  affairs.  It is flatly  unconstitutional.

102

103

Advisory  Opinions.  The  Commission  has  already  issued
many  advisory  opinions;  it is preparing  to  issue  hundreds
more.  They span  every  conceivable  aspect  of the challenged
legislation  and,  therefore,  of  the  American  political
process.  The  Commission  is  explicitly  asserting,  and  vi-
gorously  exercising  in these  opinions,  the  right  to  construe
the  limits  provisions;  no  pretense  is  made  that  only  the
disclosure  requirements  are  in question.  And  the  most  cur-
sory  sampling  of these  opinions  and  of requests  for  them
confirms  the extraordinary  vagueness  of the  legislation,  the
host  of questions  they  leave  unanswered,  and  the  virtually
unfettered  discretion  of the  Commission  in filling  the  gaps.
E.g.,  40  Fed.  Reg.  29792,  31316,  34084,  36242,  36746,
44040,  45292  (1975)  (opinions);  40 Fed.  Reg.  26660,  28044,
28944,  30258,  31878,  36530,  49066  (1975) (requests).

These  advisory  opinions  effectively  make  new  law.  No
prudent  candidate  or  other  person  will  dare  to ignore  them;
any  candidate,  especially,  is acutely  aware  of the  crippling
impact  even  a threat  of enforcement  could  have  on  his  can-
didacy,  wholly  without  regard  to  what  precise  legal  effect  a
favorable  opinion  would  have  on  scienter  when  he  stands
before  a judge  or  jury.  As  a  practical  matter,  at  least  until
its initial  rulemaking  effort  is completed,  advisory  opinions
are  the  Commission's  principal  enforcement  mechanism
and  chief in terrorem  power.

It  is  no  part  of the  business  of Congress  or  of ancillary
congressional  agencies  to  issue  such  opinions  in  any  area,
much  less  in  an  area  so  deeply  implicating  First  Amend-

”  This  provision,  which  refers  expressly  to  the  criminal-code
provisions,  is  ignored  by  the  Commission  in  its  attempted  demon-
Stration  that  its  enforcement  powers  embrace  only  the  disclosure
provisions  (FEC  Br.  49, 60-63).  Subsections  (aX1),  (2), (3) and  (4) of Sec-
tion  437g,  providing  for  Commission  complaints,  notification  of
violations,  investigations  and  hearings,  likewise  are  expressly  made  ap-
plicable  to the statutory  limits  on  contributions  and  expenditures.  Even
Section  437  g(aXS)  (civil  enforcement  actions),  on  which  the  Com-
mission  does  focus,  may  also  embrace  those  provisions.  The  footnote  to
Section  434  to  which  the  Commission  refers  (FEC  Br.  61) specifies  that
the  term  “this  Act,”’  as  used  in Section  437  and  elsewhere,  means  “‘the
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of 1971  .
.  as  amended
by  P.L.  93-443,  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of
1974,”  P.L.  93-443,  entitled  ‘Federal  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of
1974,"’  included,  as  Title  I,  the  criminal-code  expenditure  and  con-
tribution  limitations.  88 Stat.  1263  (1974).  In any  event,  the  distinction
the  Commission  tries  to  make  between  two  different  kinds  of  en-
forcement  power  is wholly  academic  as  a  practical  matter.  If the  Com-
mission  brings  a  proceeding,  for example,  to establish  that  a candidate
has  not  reported  what  it  considers  to  be  a  contribution  or  an  ex-
penditure,  its  success  in  that  proceeding  will  establish  that  the  unreported
acts  (if they  place  the  candidate  over  any  applicable  limit)  violate  the
limits,  since  the  definitions  in  the  disclosure  and  the  limitations  sec-
tions  are  the  same.

That  power  is exclusive  and  applies  to  both  the  criminal
and  civil  enforcement  of the  laws.  United  States  v.  Nixon,
418  U.S.  683,  693  (1974);  Confiscation  Cases,  74  U.S.  (7
