111

cause  we  believe  that  the  following  discussion  is
superfluous,  we  present  it  without  the  fullness  of
argument  that  the  subject  deserves. )

B.  The  Commission  Is  An  Arm  Of  Congress  Because
Its  Members  Are  Appointed  By  Congress  And  Its
Regulations  Are  Subject  To  Congressional  Control.

It appears  to  be common  ground  among  the  parties
and  the  court  of  appeals  (J.S.  App.  1573)  that  the
Commission  is  an  arm  of  Congress,  a  “legislative
agency,”  and  this  is scarcely  open  to  doubt.”

While  plaintiffs  contend  that  the  method  of appoint-
ment  of  the  Commission’s  members  does  not  conform
with  Article  II,  Section  2,  clause  2  of  the  Constitu-
tion,  that  lack  of  conformity  simply  demonstrates

6’  Four  of  the  Commission’s  six  voting  members  are  ap-
pointed  by  the  Houses  of  Congress;  the  Commission’s  ex
officio  members  are  the  Secretary  of the  Senate  and  the  Clerk
of the  House;  all six  of the  Commission’s  voting  members  must
be confirmed  by both  Houses  of Congress;  all  rules  proposed  by
the  Commission  can  be  disapproved  by  either  House  of  Con-
gress;  the  Commission  must  submit  proposed  legislation  to
Congress  before  circulating  it to  any  other  agency  or  execu-
tive  officer;  the  Commission  must  submit  its  budget  requests
directly  to Congress  at the  same  time  as  it submits  its  requests
to  the  Office  of  Management  and  Budget.  These  indicia  prop-
erly  led  the  court  of  appeals  to  accept  the  Commission’s  con-
cession  and  to  conclude  that  the  Commission  is  an  arm  of
Congress  under  the  active  influence,  if not  the  day  to  day  con-
trol,  of  Congress.  This  conclusion  is,  as  the  Commission  ar-
gued  in the  court  of appeals,  fortified  by the  fact  that  many  of
the  Commission’s  duties  relate  to  powers—such  as  Congress’
power  to judge  elections  and  the  qualifications  of its  members
—that  Congress  can  exercise  free  of  control  by the  executive
branch.

that  the  members  of  the  Commission  are  neither
“other  Officers  of  the  United  States”  nor  “inferior
Officers.”  But  there  is  no  reason  why  members  of  a
legislative  agency  must  be  “Officers”  as  Article  II
uses  that  term.”

In  any  event,  under  the  Necessary  and  Proper
Clause,  Article  I,  Section  8,  clause  18,  Congress  is
entitled  to  have  the  aid  of  others  in  the  performance
of its  legislative  duties  and  it is necessary  and  proper
for  Congress  to  appoint  the  persons  to  provide  that
assistance.

Plaintiffs  also  have  argued  that  the  “legislative  —

veto”  provided  for  in  Section  438(c)  is  unconstitu-
tional.
issues  might  be
posed  were  this  a  provision  for  a  one-house  legisla-
tive  veto  of  action  by  the  executive  branch,”  the

Whatever  constitutional

°°  The  Constitution  expressly  grants  to  Congress  the  right
to  appoint  its  own  officers.  Article  I, Section  2, clause  5 pro-
vides  that  “The  House  of  Representatives  shall  chuse  their
Speaker  and  other  Officers;  *  *  *.”  Article  I, Section  3, clause
5 extends  similar  power  to  the  Senate.  The  members  of  the
Commission,  an  arm  of  Congress,  reasonably  can  be  viewed
as  officers  of  the  bodies  that  appointed  them.  Although  Con-
gress  has  provided  that  the  nominees  of  the  House  must  be
confirmed  by  the  Senate,  and  vice  versa,  we  think  they  could
do  so  with  any  of  their  other  officers.

7°  There  has  been  much  scholarly  debate  over  the  constitu-
tionality  of  the  legislative  veto  and  similar  devices  for  con-
gressional  control  of  executive  action.  The  most  recent  and
thorough  study  concludes  that  such  devices  are  often  an  uncon-
stitutional  intrusion  into  executive  authority.  Watson,  Con-
gress  Steps  Out:  A  Look  at  Congressional  Control  of  the
Executive,  63  Calif.  L.  Rev.  983  (1975).  See  also  Ginnane,
The  Control  of  Federal  Administration  by  Congressional

112

Commission  is an  arm  of Congress,  so  that  the  “legis-
lative  veto”  established  by  Section  438(c)  is  simply
one  means  by  which  Congress  controls  its  own  in-
strumentality.  It  is  not  an  attempt  by  Congress  to
control  the  executive  branch  or  an  executive  officer,
nor  is  it  a  method  by  which  Congress  or  the  Com-
mission  can  change  the  law  without  presidential
participation.

As  we  see  the  problem,  then,  it  is  not  so  much  a
question  whether  the  composition  and  decisionmaking
structure  of  the  Commission  are  defective,  as  it  is
the  question  what  a  commission,  so  constituted  and
functioning,  is  permitted  by  the  Constitution  to  do.

C.  The  Commission  Can  Perform  Only  Those  Func-
tions  That  Congress  Or  One  Of  Its  Committees  Or
Employees  Could  Perform.

In  order  to  minimize  the  possibility  of  confusion,
we  set  forth  in  the  margin  the  Commission’s  powers
we  do  not  challenge.”  The  powers  of the  Commission

ions  and  Committees,  66  Harv.  L.  Rev.  569  (1953) ;
og &  Keaton,  Congress  and  the  Faithful  Execution  of
the  Laws—Should  Legislators  Supervise  Administrators ?, 41
Calif.  L.  Rev.  565  (1953);  Schwartz,  Legislative  Control  of
Administrative  Rules  and  Regulations,  30 N.Y.U.  L.  Rev. 1031
(1955);  Cooper,  The  Legislative  Veto  and  the  Constitution,
80  Geo.  Wash.  L.  Rev.  467  (1962);  Jackson,  A  Presidential
Legal  Opinion,  66  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1353  (1953).

™  Congress  has  substantial  powers  to  investigate and issue
subpoenas  in  order  to  collect  and  evaluate  information  neces-
sary  to  enable  it to  make  laws.  See  Eastland  v.  United  States
Servicemen’s  Fund,  No.  73-1923,  decided  May  27,  1975.  Just
as  a  committee  of  Congress  can  investigate,  issue  subpoenas,
report  to  Congress  on  its  findings,  and  refer  to  the  proper

113
that  are,  in  our  view,  unconstitutional  infringements
of  the  exclusive  power  of  the  President  faithfully  to
execute  the  laws  or  of  his  constitutionally  assigned
role  in  the  law-making  process  are  the  power  to  seek
civil  sanctions  for  violation  of  FECA,  the  power  to
disqualify  candidates,  and  the  power  to  promulgate
rules  binding  upon  the  executive  and  the  courts.

authorities  any  apparent  violations  of  the  law,  so  Congress
can  authorize  its  legislative  agency  to  perform  similar  func-
tions.  Congress  can  authorize  its  staff  to discuss  and  propose
legislation  for  consideration  by it; the  Commission’s  power  to
do  likewise  is  not  open  to  challenge.  Congress  can  authorize
its  staff  members  to inform  it of their  view  of the  meaning  of
legislation  it  has  passed,  and  to  make  those  views  public.
Similarly,  the  power  of  the  Commission  to  issue  advisory
opinions  is  simply  a  power  to  announce  an  opinion  on  the
meaning  of a  law;  it is not  a power  to alter  or  amend  the  law,
or  to  enforce  the  law  (see  J.S.  App.  1579).

We  would  have  greater  pause  if the  Commission’s  advisory
opinions  were  binding  upon  the  courts  or  upon  those  properly
charged  with  prosecuting  violations  of  FECA.  The  pertinent
provision,  2 U.S.C.  437f(b),  states  that  a  person  who  “acts  in
good  faith  in  accordance  with  the  provisions  and  findings
of  [a Commission]  advisory  opinion  shall  be  presumed  to  be
in  compliance  with  the  provision  of  this  act.”  If  this  means
that  the  Commission  can  confer  immunity  from  prosecution,
then  it  raises  serious  questions;  if  it  means  only  that  the
advisory  opinion  establishes a presumption  in  favor  of  the
interpretation  of  the  law  set  out  in  it, the  problems  are  less
serious,  for  such  presumptions  can  be  rebutted,  and  the  ad-
visory  opinions  will  not  bind  the  coordinate  branches  of  the
government.  The  court  of  appeals  held  the  question  not  ripe
for  resolution,  and  we  think  correctly  so.  “When  a  person
who  relied  in good  faith  on  an  advisory  opinion  seeks  to assert
that  good  faith  reliance  as  a  defense  to a  civil  suit  or  a crimi-
nal  prosecution,  the  issue  of  the  effect  to  be  given  to  an  ad-
visory  opinion  can  be  raised  and  fully  considered  by  the
courts”  (J.S.  App.  1579).

114

1.  The  Constitution  separates  the  executive  func-
tion  from  the  legislative  function,  reposing  each
in  a  separate  body.

Congress,  no  doubt,  was  apprehensive  when  it
enacted  FECA  that  the  President  and  Attorney  Gen-
eral  would  not  enforce  the  law  in  the  same  way
Congress  would  have  enforced  it  in  their  stead,  and
therefore  decided  to  provide  the  opportunity  for  ex-
ecuting  the  law  itself.  But  this  tension  between  de-
partments  of  the  government,  and  the  consequent
belief  of  one  department  that  it  would  be  wiser  for
it  to  undertake  all  of  the  functions  itself,  is  not  a
“flaw”  in  the  constitutional  system  but  rather  is  its
genius.  See  O’Donoghue  v.  United  States,  289  U.S.
516,  530;  J.  W.  Hampton,  Jr.,  & Co.  v.  United  States,
276  U.S.  394,  406.

Each  branch  of  the  government  is  charged  by  the
Constitution  with  the  task  of  preserving  its  own
powers,  lest  the  design  of  the  framers  be  frustrated.
Although  the  framers  concluded  that  the  broadly-rep-
resentative  Congress  should  possess  the  power  to make
the  laws,  they  gave  to  the  President  two  sorts  of
check  upon  Congress:  the  power  to  veto  bills  passed
by Congress,  and  the  power  to  execute  the  laws  (Art.
II,  Section  3  of  the  Constitution).  And  they  did  so
because,  as  Montesquieu  wrote  in  his  Spirit  of  the
Laws  (1748):

When  the  legislative  and  executive  powers  are
united  in the  same  person,  or  in  the  same  body  of
magistracy,  there  can  be  no  liberty,  because  ap-
prehensions  may  arise,  lest  the  same  monarch  or

115

senate  should  enact  tyrannical  laws,  to  execute
them  in  a  tyrannical  manner.

In  contending  that  the  Commission  cannot  exercise
executive  powers,  we  do  not  suppose  that  it  would
exercise  its  statutory  powers  unwisely.  But  that  is
not  the  point.  In  the  long  run  the  Commission  and
other  entities  like  it, if they  assume  executive  branch
functions,  could  seriously  deform  the  structure  of
government  established  by the  framers  and  upset  the
separation  of  functions  and  the  allocation  of  checks
and  balances.”  The  framers  “rested  the  structure  of
our  central  government  on  the  system  of  checks  and
balances.
For  them  the  doctrine  of  separation  of
powers  was  not  mere  theory;  it was  a  felt  necessity.”
Youngstown  Sheet  &  Tube  Co.  v.  Sawyer,  343  U.S.
579,  593  (Frankfurter,  J.,  concurring).  The  system
is  that  stated  by the  Court  in  Massachusetts  v.  Mel-
lon,  262  U.S.  447,  488:

The  functions  of  government  under  our  sys-
tem  are  apportioned.  To  the  legislative  depart-
ment  has  been  committed  the  duty  of  making
laws;  to  the  executive  the  duty  of  executing
them;  and  to  the  judiciary  the  duty  of  interpret-
ing and  applying  them  in  cases  properly  brought

72  The  separation  of powers  works  on a principal  of frustra-
tion  of power.  It presumes  “an  organization  which  may  delay
the  accomplishment  by any  group  of  its  will,  even  at  the  ex-
pense  of  efficiency.  Against  the  unprecedented  power  of  rela-
tively  popular  legislatures  the  Fathers  set  strong  execu-
tives  *  *  *.”  Sharp,  The  Classical  American  Doctrine  of “The
Separation  of Powers,”  2 U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  385,  386  (1935).

116

117

before  the courts.  The general  rule  is that  neither
department  may  invade  the  province  of the  other
and  neither  may  control,  direct  or  restrain  the
action  of  the  other.

2.  Law  enforcement  and  interpretive  rulemaking
are  functions  assigned  to the  executive  branch.

The  Commission  has  been  assigned  the  power  to
institute  and  defend  civil  suits  in  its  own  name  and
in  the  name  of  the  United  States  and  to  issue  rules
interpreting  FECA,  which  presumably  will  bind  the
Commission  and  any  other  agency  that  enforces
FECA.  It  has  also  been  given  an  administrative
power  to  disqualify  candidates  for  office,  subject  to
judicial  review.  Each  of  these  powers  is  subsumed
in  the  power  and  duty  faithfully  to  execute  the  laws;
each  is  part  of  the  power  reserved  to  the  executive
by Article  II  of the  Constitution.

A  long  series  of  cases  decided  by  this  Court  es-
tablishes  that  the  power  to  enforce  the  laws  in  the
courts—whether  civil  or  criminal—is  an  executive
power.”  In  Quinn  v.  United  States,  349  U.S.  155,

7’  The  first  of these  is  The  Confiscation  Cases,  7 Wall.  454.
The  Attorney  General  brought  libels  to  confiscate  several
privateers;  an  informer  was  entitled,  by  statute,  to  one-half
of  the  recovery.  When  the  Attorney  General  moved  to  dis-
miss  the  cases,  the  informer  objected.  The  Court  rejected  the
informer’s  claims,  holding  that  the  “[s]ettled  rule  is that  *  *  *
courts  will  not  recognize  any  suit,  civil  or  criminal  *  *  *
if prosecuted  in  the  name  and  for  the  benefit  of  the  United
States,  unless  the same  is represented  by the  district  attorney”
(id.  at  457).  The  Court  concluded  that  the  Attorney  Gen-

the  Court,  while  acknowledging  that  Congress  has
the  power  to  investigate  events  that  may  be  crimes,
cautioned:
“the  power  to  investigate  must  not  be
confused  with  any  of the  powers  of law  enforcement;
those  powers  are  assigned  under  our  Constitution  to
the  Executive  and  the  Judiciary”  (349  U.S.  at  161).
See  also  United  States  v.  Thompson,  251  U.S.  407."
We  think  it  clear,  then,  that  attempts  to  enforce
the  law  in  the  courts  are  at  the  heart  of  the  execu-
tive’s  exclusive  power  faithfully  to  enforce  the  laws;
these  actions  are  beyond  the  control  of  Congress,  and
likewise  beyond  the  control  of  its  surrogate,  the  Com-
Interpretive  rulemaking  also  falls  within
mission.
the  executive’s  prerogative  or,  depending  on  the  na-
ture  and  function  of  the  rules  (which  in  this  case
cannot  now  be  determined),  is part  of  the  legislative
process  from  which  the  executive  cannot  properly  be
In  regard  to  the  power  to  make  initial
excluded.
determinations  that  a  particular  candidate  shall  be
disqualified,  whoever  has  the  power  to  make  that
initial  determination  is  exercising  the  power  to  de-

eral  has  full  control  over  all  federal  litigation  (id.  at  458-
459).

In  Ponzi  v.  Fessenden,  258  U.S.  254,  262,  the  Court  de-
scribed  the  Attorney  General  as  “the  hand  of  the  President
in taking  care  that  the  laws  of the  United  States  in protection
of  the  interests  of the  United  States  in  legal  proceedings  and
in  the  prosecution  of  offences,  be  faithfully  executed.”

™  United  States  v.  Cox,  342  F.2d  167  (C.A.  5)  (en  banc),
certiorari  denied,  381  U.S.  935,  held  explicitly  that  the  power
of  prosecution  belongs  solely  to  the  executive  branch,  and
that  neither  Congress  nor  the  courts  can  interfere  with  it in
any  particular.

118

119

termine  how  the  laws  shall  be  applied  and  carried
out.  It  is  an  executive  function,  similar  to  the  de-
cision  of  a  prosecutor  to  seek  an  indictment."

In  the  court  of  appeals  the  Commission  conceded
as  much,  but  argued  that  the  Commission  is  simply
exercising  the  “quasi-executive”  and  “quasi-judicial”
powers  reserved  to  Congress  by  Article  I.  This  ar-
gument  turns  on  an  analysis  of  the  subject  matter
of  the  Commission’s  jurisdiction;  the  Commission
agrees  that  it could  not  constitutionally  enforce,  say,
the  antitrust  laws,  but  contends  that  it  can  enforce
FECA  because  Congress  has  broader  powers  over  the
subject  matter  of  elections  and  election  campaigns.
This  approach  fails  to  distinguish  between  the  two
sorts  of  power  over  elections  entrusted  to  Congress.
The  first,  which  is  unique  to  Congress’  jurisdic-
tion  over  elections,  gives  each  House  of  Congress  the
power  to  judge  the  qualifications  of  its  members,  in-
cluding  the  validity  of  elections.  So  long  as  Congress
excludes  a  member-elect  for  failure  to  meet  constitu-
tional  qualifications,  or  because  he  was  not  properly
elected,  it  can  exercise  its  power  without  the  ap-
proval  of  either  the  judicial  or  executive  branches.
Powell  v.  McCormack,  395  U.S.  486.  To  the  extent
Congress  has  delegated  part  of  that  power  to  the
Commission,  the  Commission  acts  free  of  executive
interference.

7’  This  would  be  a  different  matter  if  the  Commission  had
final  authority  over  disqualification,  but  it  does  not.  It  can
make  only  the  initial  decision  to  disqualify,  and  judicial  re-
view  follows,  just  as  the  judiciary  reviews  the  actions  of
executive  officers  or  of  other  quasi-executive  agencies.

The  Commission  is not  accurate,  however,  in  char-
acterizing  Congress’  remaining  power  over  elections
as  other  than  ordinary  legislative  power.  That  power
is  the  power  to  make  laws  with  respect  to  elections
and  is no  broader  than  its  power  to  make  laws  with
respect  to  the  common  defense,  the  issuance  of  coin
and  currency,  or  the  regulation  of  commerce.  The
power  to  make  laws  with  respect  to  these  subjects—
and  many  more—is  textually  committed  to  Congress.
But  what  is  textually  committed  is  the  power  to
make  laws,  not  the  power  to  enforce  them,  and  an
especially  broad  subject-matter  grant  of  law-making
power  does  not  lead  to  any  conclusion  about  the  scope
of  Congress’  law  enforcement  power.  To  argue,  as
the  Commission  apparently  does,  that  because  Con-
gress  has  both  broad  law-making  power  and a special
interest  in  the  subject  matter  of  elections,  it  there-
fore  possesses  some  manner  of  law-enforcement
power,  is a  simple  non-sequitur.

3.  The  Commission,  acting  as  an  arm  of  Congress,
has  no  greater  authority  than  does  Congress  to
enforce  the  laws  Congress  has  made.

If Congress  itself  cannot  execute  the  laws,  or  make
rules  binding  on  those  who  execute  the  laws,  then
it  cannot  deputize  a  legislative  agency  to  exercise
such  powers  in  its  stead.”  Congress  cannot  bestow

7  Congress  can,  of  course,  make  “rules”  binding  on  the
executive.  But  it can  make  such  rules  only  in the form  of law,
over  which  the  President  has  the  power  of  veto.  The  “rules”
made  by the  Commission  are  not  subject  to  Presidential  veto,
and  its  rules  therefore  cannot  be  analogized  to  laws.

120

upon  its  subordinates  powers  it  does  not  possess.
“Legislative  power,  as  distinguished  from  executive
power,  is  the  authority  to  make  laws,  but  not  to
enforce  them  or  appoint  agents  charged  with  the
duty  of  such  enforcement.  The  latter  are  executive
functions”  “[I]t  may  be  stated  *
*  as  a  general
rule  inherent  in  the  American  constitutional  system,
that,  unless  otherwise  expressly  provided  or  inci-
dental  to  the  powers  conferred,  the  legislature  can-
*  *.”
not  exercise  *
Springer  v.  Philippine  Islands,  277  U.S.  189,  201,
202.

*  executive  *

*  power  *

*

*

*

Springer  is  the  paradigm  for  Congress’  creation  of
the  Commission.
Congress,  which  itself  lacks  the
power  to  enforce  the  laws,  has  attempted  to  “‘appoint
the  agents  charged  with  the  duty  of  such  enforce-
ment”  (id.  at  202).  It  is  not  entitled  to  do  so.”

In  sum,  law  enforcement  and  interpretation  are
integral  parts  of  the  executive  power  to  execute  the
laws.  Congress  is  entitled  to  legislate  but  not  to
execute  the  laws,  and  Congress  has  no  power  to  ap-
point  others  to  execute  the  laws  when  Congress  can-
not  do  so  itself.  It  follows  that  the  Commission  can-
not  exercise  the  three  executive  powers  granted  to
it by  FECA.”

77See  also  Washington  v.  Clark,  84  F.  Supp.  964,  966

(D.D.C.).

2  \

7  Intervening  defendant  Common  Cause  filed  a  separate
brief  in  the  court  of  appeals  addressed  to  the  question  of  the
Commission’s  powers.  In  supporting  the  constitutionality  of
FECA’s  provisions,  Common  Cause  drew  upon  two  analogies
—suits  by private  attorneys  general  and  suits  by independent

121

CONCLUSION

The  position  of the  Attorney  General  as  a  party  to
this  case,  and  of  the  United  States,  is  that  the  court
of  appeals’  judgment  with  respect  to  certified  ques-
The  United  States  as
tion  8  should  be  affirmed.

regulatory  agencies—to  demonstrate  that  the  powers  en-
trusted  to the  Commission  are  constitutional.  These  analogies
do  not  support  Common  Cause’s  argument.

Private  citizens  can  be  plaintiffs  in  qui  tam  actions  and
sometimes  can  act  as  “private  attorneys  general.”  But  it  is
one  thing  to allow  private  individuals  to share  a  right  of  suit
enjoyed  by  the  executive,  and  quite  another  for  Congress
to  take  the  right  of  enforcement  to  itself.  It  is  still  a  third
thing  for  Congress  both  to  exclude  the  executive  and  to  in-
clude  itself  in  the  business  of  enforcement.  The  first  is  no
precedent  for  the  third.  The  separation  of powers  and  checks
and  balances  built  into  our  Constitution  establish  the  method
by which  two  inherently  antagonistic  centers  of  power  share
in the  governance  of the  country.  These  delicate  sharings  and
checks  upon  power  are  not  disturbed  by  private  suits.  They
are  disturbed,  to a considerable  degree,  if Congress  appropri-
ates  to  itself  a power  the  Constitution  places  in  a  coordinate
branch.

As  to  the  analogy  to  independent  regulatory  agencies,  the
argument  ignores  a  decisive  difference:  the  members  of  the
independent  regulatory  commissions  are  “Officers  of  the
United  States”  within  the  meaning  of Article  II,  Section  2 of
the  Constitution.  They  are  appointed  by  the  President  and
are  confirmed  by the  Senate.  In  consequence,  they  can  share,
much.  as  cabinet  officers  share,  in  the  power  granted  by
Article  II  to  execute  the  laws.  Members  of  the  Commission
are  not  “Officers  of  the  United  States”  and,  in  consequence,
possess  no  power  under  Article  II.

122

amicus  curiae  takes  no  position  with  respect  to  the
remaining  portions  of the  court  of appeals’  judgment.

Respectfully  submitted.

In the
Supreme  Court  of the United  States

Octroser  Term,  1975

No.  75-436
No.  75-437

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY,  et  al.,
APPELLANTS,

v.

FRANCIS  R.  VALEO,  et  al.,
APPELLEES.

On  Appeats  From  tue  Court  or  APPEALS
For  tHe  Disrricr  or  CoLtumpia  Circuit

BRIEF  OF  SENATORS  HUGH  SCOTT  AND
EDWARD  M.  KENNEDY,  AMICI  CURIAE

Interest  of  the  Amici

This  case  results  from  a  sweeping  constitutional  attack
upon  every  aspect  of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
of  1971,  86  Stat.  3  (1972),  and  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  Amendments  of  1974,  88  Stat.  1263  (1975).

2

3

The  thrust  of each  of  appellants’  major  contentions  is that
the  Congress  is  powerless  to  counter  either  the  pressure
on  candidates  in  federal  elections  to  raise  and  spend  more
and  more  money  from  contributors  expecting  later  govern-
mental  favors,  or  the  growing  public  belief  that  both  politi-
cal  campaigns  and  government  itself  are  a  giant  ¥ rip-off -
by those  with  large  sums  of money  to  give  to  candidates  in
order  that  the  candidates  may  ‘‘buy”’  elections  and  later
give  to  the  contributors  governmental  favors  in  return
for their  largesse.

Hugh  Scott  is  a  Republican  Senator  from  Pennsylvania
and  Minority  Leader  of the  United  States  Senate.  Edward
M.  Kennedy  is  a  Democratic  Senator  from  Massachusetts.
In  recent  Congresses  they  were  the  bipartisan  sponsors
of  the  major  bills  to  provide  for  public  financing  of  elec-
tions  for  President  of  the  United  States,  United  States
Senator  and  United  States  Representative.  Amendment
406  to  8.372  (to  amend  the  FECA),  93d  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.
(1973) ; $.2297  (to  amend  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Rev-
enue  Code),  93d  Cong.,  1st  Sess.  (1973)  ; Amendment  651  to
H.R.  11104  (the  Debt  Ceiling  Act),  93d  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.
(1973);  S.2780,  93d  Cong.,  1st  Ses»  (1973);  8.3044,  93d
Cong.,  2d  Sess.  (1974) ; 8.4196,  93d  Uong.,  2d  Sess.  (1974) ;
S.564,  94th  Cong.,  1st  Sess.  (1975).  The  substance  of  these
measures  and  much  of the  language  were  incorporated  into
provisions  of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amend-
ments  of  1974,  here  at  issue,  which  provide  for  public
financing  of  campaigns  for  nomination  as  a  candidate  for
election  as  President  and  for  the  election  campaign  itself.
Senator  Scott  and  Senator  Kennedy  also  played  active
roles  in  the  deliberations  of  the  Senate  upon  all  the  other
provisions  of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  and the
Both  became  thoroughly  familiar
amendments  of  1974.
with  the  evils  flowing  from  spiraling  campaign  costs  and
pressure  to  raise  the  necessary  funds  by  large  private

contributions,  and  with  the  problems  encountered  in  fram-
ing  remedies  and  the  reasoning  underlying  the  congres-
sional  decisions.  Both  were  members  of  the  Conference
Committee  whose  report  led to enactment  of the present  law.
The  Executive  Branch  is ambivalent  in  its  position  upon
the  issues.  The  Solicitor  General  has  signed  two  briefs,
one  calling for affirmance  and  the other  stating  that  he takes
no  position  upon  critical  corstitutional  issues.  Questions
concerning  the  respective  powers  of  the  Congress  and  the
President  are  at  stake  in  the  portion  of  the  case  relating
to  the  appointment  of  members  of  the  Federal  Election
Commission.  These  unusual  circumstances  make  it  appro-
priate  for  the  Court  to  consider  a  bipartisan  submission
from  two  members  of  the  Legislative  Branch  who  were
active  supporters  of  the  challenged  measure.

Questions  Presented

Twenty-eight  certified  questions  are  before  this  Court
under  the  special  statutory  procedure  (2  U.S.C.  § 437h)
which  calls  for  expedited  rulings  upon  the  constitutionality
of the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of 1971,  P.L.  92-225,
86  Stat.  3  (1972),  2  U.S.C.  (Supp.  III)  §§  431  et  seq.;
and  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of
1974,  P.L.  93-443,  88  Stat.  1263  (1975).  In  substance,  there
are  five  central  questions:

1.  Has  Congress  power  to  legislate  with  respect  to
financial  contributions  and  expenditures  in  political  cam-
paigns  for  election  to  federal  office?

2.  Do  the  restrictions  imposed  by the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  upon  the  size  of contributions  to  candidates
for  federal  office,  and  of  expenditures  made  by candidates
or  in  their  support,  violate  the  First  Amendment,  either
on  the  face  of  the  statute  or  as  its  operation  can  now  be
projected?

+

3.  Do  requirements  for  the  detailed  reporting  and  dis-
closure  of all  contributions  received  and  expenditures  made
in campaigns  for  election  to  federal  office  violate  the  First
Amendment,  either  on  the  face  of  the  statute  or  as  its
operation  can  now  be projected?

4.  Does  the  appropriation  and  expenditure  of  federal
funds  in  the  form  of  grants  to  defray  the  campaign  ex-
penses  of  candidates  for  the  office  of  President  of  the
United  States  violate  the  First  or  Fifth  Amendment,
either  on  the  face  of  the  statute  or  as  its  operation  can
now  be  projected,  where  the  grant  to  each  candidate  in
the  primary  elections  is  to  match  the  sum  he  raises  from
private  contributions  of  $250  or  less  and  the  grant  to
candidates  in  the  general  election  is  limited  to  $20,000,000
for  each  of  the  major-parly  candidates  with  a  ban  against
further  expenditures,  with  grants  to  minor-party  and  new-
party  candidates  according  to  formulas  reflecting  their
probable  importance  without  limiting  their  right  to  seek
private  contributions  and  thus  make  expenditures  up  to
a  total  of  $20,000,000?

5.

Is  the  establishment  of  a  Federal  Election  Commis-
sion  with  a  variety  of  investigative,  interpretative,  rule-
making  and  enforcement  powers  or  the  conduct  of  any
FEC  business  to  be  declared  unconstitutional  in  limine
because  two  of  the  eight  members  are  the  chief  clerks  of
the  Senate  and  the  House  of  Representatives  ex  officio,
two  are  selected  by  the  Speaker  of  the  House,  two  by  the
President  pro  tempore  of  the  Senate,  and  two  by  the
President  of  the  United  States,  with  all  six  approved  by
a  majority  of  both  Houses  of  Congress?

5

Summary  of  Argument

I

The  restrictions  which  the  FECA,  as  amended,  puts
upon  the  size  of  financial  contributions  and  expenditures
in  campaigns  for election  to  federal  office  serve  high  ob-
jectives:
to  preserve  the  integrity  of  federal  political
campaigns  and  elections,  and  public  confidence  therein;
to  safeguard  the  honor  and  integrity  of  the  conduct  of
government,  and  public  confidence  therein;  to  reduce  the
financial  barriers  to  the  candidacies  of  men  and  women
without  access  to  large  contributors  of  money  or  their  own
personal  wealth;  and  to  equalize  access  to  candidates  and
government  officials  as  among  wealthy  contributors  and
other  citizens.
Judged  in  the  light  of  these  pressing  in-
terests,  the  restrictions  are  well  within  the  power  of  the
Congress.  and  the  President  to  make  into  law  without
offending constitutional  limitations.

A.  Congress  has  ample  power  to  legislate  for  these
objectives,  not  only  by  virtue  of  Article  I,  Section  4  of
the  Constitution  but  in  the  exercise  of  an  inherent  power
to  preserve  the  general  government  against  destruction
by corruption.

B.  The  restrictions  are  consistent  with  the  First  Amend-
ment.  The  substance  of  political  debate  is  left  untouched.
There  is no  attempt  to  censor,  directly  or  indirectly,  either
the  ideas  that  may  be  expressed  or  the  vehicles  chosen  for
their  expression.  The  restrictions  deal  only  with  conduct,
with  the  transfer  or  use  of  money  and  other  material
resources.  The  distinction  has  critical  importance  despite
the  likelihood  that  the  restrictions  upon  the  use  of  money
may,  in  some  cases,  limit  the  volume  of  communication
with  the  voters  through  the  mass  media  —  an  important
form  of  speech  protected  by  the  First  Amendment.

6

7

A law  which  restricts  the  kinds  of  ideas,  information  or
feelings  which  may  be  expressed  or  the  verbal  or  pictorial
vehicles  of  expression,  or  which  discriminates  among  per-
sons  who  may  express  them,  can  be  justified  —  if at  all  —
only  in  terms  of  the  dangers  posed  by  the  ideas  or  their
expression.  The  constitutional  test  is  exceedingly  stringent
in  such  cases,  because  such  censorship  not  only  suppresses
information  essential  to  self-government;  it  also  carries
the  seeds  of  official  ideology  and  dangerous  discrimina-
tion.

The  constitutional  standard  is less  severe  when  the  statute
regulates  conduct,  one  of  the  consequences  of  which  is  to
enlarge  the  enjoyment  of freedom  of  expression  but  which
also  does  public  injury  unrelated  to  any  effect  of the  com-
munication  facilitated.  The  injury  may  require  a  measure
of  public  control  directed  to  the  conduct  without  regard
to  the  character  or  consequences  of  the  ideas  or  their  ex-
pression.  A law  which  regulates  such  conduct  evenhandedly
is  constitutional,  even  though  its  operation  may  in  prac-
tice  tend  in  some  degree  to  curtail  practical  opportunities
for  gaining  the  maximum  benefit  of  First  Amendment
freedoms,  if the  public  interests  which  the  measure  serves
outweigh  its  costs  in  curtailment  of  opportunities  for  ex-
pression.

In  the  present  case,  the  balance  is  on  the  side  of  con-
stitutionality.  The  restrictions  imposed  by  Section  608(c)
upon  aggregate  campaign  expenditures  leave  ample  op-
portunity  for  a  candidate  to  achieve  public  recognition  and
lay  his  case  before  the  electorate  even  if  he  begins  re-
latively  unknown.  Since  the  statutory  ceilings  are  closely
related  to  the  expense  of  past  campaigns,  their  chief  func-
tion  will  be  to  prevent  further  rise  and  cut  back  wild
extravagance.  The  magnitude  and  value  of  the  public  in-
terests  identified  at  the  outset  of  this  Summary  show  that
the  justification  for  any  limitation  of  opportunities  to  buy

use  of  media  of  mass  communication  which  results  from
the  ceilings,  is  ‘‘paramount”’  or  ‘‘compelling’’  in  the  con-
stitutional  sense.  These  purposes  cannot  be  accomplished
by less  restrictive  measures.

Section  608(e)  is  particularly  assailed  because  it  for-
bids  any  person  to  expend  more  than  $1,000  in  any  one
election  to  elect  a  candidate  or  defeat  his  opponent,  even
without  consulting  the  candidate.  In  its  broadest  reach,
this  limitation  appears  to  bar  an  outlay  of  more  than
$1,000  to  publicize  one’s  own  words.  The  problem  was  one
of drawing  lines.  Without  Section  608(e),  the  $1,000  ceil-
ing  upon  individual  contribution  and  the  over-all  ceiling
upon  expenditures  by  or  on  behalf  of  a  candidate  could
easily  be  circumvented  by  massive  expenditures  for  cam-
paign  advertising  and  other  forms  of  mass  communica-
tion  made in  support  of  a  candidate  without  any  provable
communication  between  the  supporter  or  group  of  sup-
porters  and  the  candidate  or  his  agents.  The  same  con-
siderations  that  sustain  the  constitutionality  of  the  ceiling
upon  individual  contributions  sustain  Section  608(e)  in
these  instances.  Given  the  need  for  limiting  some  kinds  of
ostensibly  independent  expenditures,  Congress  drew  the
line  between  truly  personal  activity,  on  one  side,  and
spending  money,  on  the other.

The  language  is  tightly  drawn  so  as  not  to  discourage
political  discussicn  oriented  towards  public  issues.  The
restriction  applies  only  to  publications
‘‘relative  to  a
clearly  identified  condidate’’  and  only  to those  ‘‘advocating
the  election  or  defeat’’
In
Section  591(f),  Congress  also  made  certain  that  Section
608(e)  would  not  interfere  with  an  individual’s  personal
activities  in  speaking,  writing  or  canvassing  for  a  candi-
date,  or  performing  other  services,  without  using  money
to  expand  the  reach  of  his  words.  Given  these  carefully
circumscribed  limits,  we  believe  that  the  contribution  which

of  that  particular  person.

8

9

Section  608(e)  makes  to  the  workability  of  the  entire  plan
justifies  restricting  even  the  ability  of  an  individual  to
buy  facilities  for  giving  the  maximum  publicity  to  his  own
speech  on  behalf  of  a  candidate.

This  last  question  is  too  hypothetical,  however,  for  de-
cision  in  the  present  case.  Neither  pleading  nor  proof
names  any  person  who  wishes  to  make  such  outlays  in
order  to  gain  a  wider  audience  for  his  own  words.  Any
facial  attack  upon  grounds  of  overbreadth  should  be  re-
jected  upon  the  ground  that  the  challenged  legislation  deals
with  conduct,  t.e.,  spending  money,  and  the  overbreadth,
if any,  is insubstantial  judged  in relation  to  Section  608(e)’s
plainly  legitimate  sweep.

B.  The  ceilings  upon  individual  contributions  and  can-
didates’  expenditures  do  not  discriminate  against  any  type
of candidate  or  party,  either  inherently  or  insofar  as  their
operation  can  now  be  projected.  The  ceilings  apply  alike  to
all  candidates  and  financial  contributors  without  regard  to
party  or  other  form  of  political  sentiment.  Neither  express
language  nor  euphemism  nor  disingenuous  circumlocution
introduces  any  form  of  differentiation.  The  ceilings  do  not
result  in  any  systematic  discrimination  always  favoring
incumbents  and  established  parties  as  a  class.

Appellants  complain  that  other  laws  and  facts  of political
life  result  in  discrimination  because  incumbent  Senators
and  Representatives  enjoy  methods  of  communicating  with
voters  and  bringing  themselves  to  public  attention  which
are  not  available  to  those  who  run  against  them,  such  as
congressional  staff,  franked  mail,  office  space,  and  like
responsibilities  and  perquisites  of  office,  including  the
ability  to  make  news.  Insofar  as  the  complaint  is  that  the
performance  of  public  duty  brings  greater  public  recogni-
tion,  which  may  yield  returns  in  a  campaign  for  reelection,
the  short  answer  is  that  the  Constitution  does  not  require
the  government  to  provide  challengers  with  some  offsetting

“method  of  attracting  ballots.  Insofar  as  the  argument  is
that  the  staff  and  facilities  provided  to  Congressmen  are
used  as  campaign  facilities  which  challengers  have  to  pur-
(i)  that  appellants  have  proved
chase,  the  answers  are:
no  abuse,  general  or  particular;
(ii)  that  any  such  dis-
crimination  flows  not  from  the  FECA  but  from  other  laws
which  should  be  atiacked  directly  in  an  appropriate  action
if  there  is  abuse;  and
(iii)  that  no  appellant  is  a  victim
of  the  alleged  discrimination  because  none  is  a  challenger
to  an  incumbent  or  alleges  that  he  wishes  to  make  such  a
challenge.

Appellants’  argument  that  in  practice  the  ceilings  will
discriminate  against  challengers  and  in  favor  of  incum-
bents  fails  for  a  number  of  reasons.  It  rests  upon  specula-
tion  about  future  events  as  to  which  the  Court  will  not
substitute  its  prediction  for  the  judgment  of  Congress.
It deals  only  with  the  challenger’s  supposedly  greater  need
for  big  spending  and  omits  the  probability  that  in  the
absence  of ceilings,  the  incumbent  may  have  an  advantage
in  raising  contributions,  and  thus,  in  making  expenditures.
The  argument  asserts  only  that  the  ceilings  upon  the  size
of  financial  contributions  and  a  candidate’s  aggregate  ex-
penditures  will  operate  in  practice  to  ‘‘discriminate  gen-
erally  against  challengers  and  generally  in  favor  of  in-
cumbents’’  (Br.  p.  94,  emphasis  added).  One  cannot  make
a  case  of  unconstitutional  discrimination  by  this  kind  of
statistical  averaging  and  prediction.  Most  important,  the
constitutional  ideal  of equality  does  not  require  the  govern-
ment  to  allow  the  otherwise  weaker  candidate  to  spend  the
most  money  in  order  to  offset  his  deficiencies.  Because
public  recognition  pretty  regularly  accompanies  ineum-
bency,  a  challenger  must  be  allowed  generous  opportunity
to  present  himself  to  the  public.  The  statutory  ceilings  are
more  than  ample  for  that  purpose.  Once  that  purpose  is
served,  equality  in  the  constitutional  sense  is  achieved  by

10

11

treating  the  rivals  exactly  alike  in  the  segment  of  their
activities  to  which  the  law  applies,  while  leaving  them
where  they  were  in  every  other  aspect  of  their  appeal  to
the  electorate.

I]

The  FECA  requirements  for  detailed  record-keeping,
reporting  and  disclosure  of  virtually  all  campaign  contri-
butions  and  expenditures  are  constitutional  upon  their  face
and  insofar  as  their  operation  is  projected  by  this  record.
(i)  to  provide
voters  with  relevant  information;
(ii)  to  guard  against
the  use  of  money  for  corrupt  or  dishonorable  purposes;
(iii)  to  deter  violation  and  facilitate  enforcement  of
and
the  ceilings  on  contributions  and  expenditures.

The  requirements  serve  three  purposes:

These  purposes  are  of  compelling  importance  in  the
case  of  minor  as  well  as  major  parties.  They  would  amply
justify  such  slight  discouragement  of  the  exercise  of  free-
dom  of  association  as  may  be  expected  to  flow  from  them
because  a  few  persons  prefer  to  keep  private  the  fact  or
extent  of  their  gifts  of  money  for  political  purposes.  There
is  no  evidence  that  any  appellant  or  other  contributor
risks  subjection  to  substantial  public  reprisals.  Such  spe-
culative  hazards  as  appellants  articulate  are  ‘‘too  remote
to  require  striking  down  a  statute  which  on  its  face  is
otherwise  plainly  within  the  area  of  congressional  power
and  is  designed  to  safeguard  a  vital  national  interest.’’
United  States  v.  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612,  626  (1954).

This  case  does  not  raise  the  question  whether  the  re-
porting  requirements  would  be  constitutional  as  applied
to  a  political  party  so  unpopular  that  disclosure  of  the
names  of  its  financial  supporters  would  subject  them  to
risk  of  widespread  public  reprisal.  Even  if  uneconstitu-
tionality  in  some  applications  be  assumed,  the  resulting

overbreadth  would  not  render  the  disclosure  provisions
unconstitutional  on  their  face.
The  direct  regulation  of
speech  is  not  involved  and  the  overbreadth,  if  any,  is
insubstantial  judged  in  relation  to  the  statute’s  plainly
legitimate  sweep.
Moreover,  the  statute  need  have  no
‘chilling  effect’?  upon  the  exercise  of  First  Amendment
freedoms  in  this  area  because  any  party  faced  with  such
unpopularity  would  have  standing  to  raise  the  issue  with-
out  risking  prosecution  of  either  itself  or  its  supporters.

Ill

Article  I,  Section  8  grants  Congress  power  to  make
appropriations  ‘‘for  the  general  welfare.’’  Providing  ade-
quate  public  financing  by  which  candidates  for  election  as
President  of  the  United  States  may  present  themselves
and  their  views  to  the  electorate  serves  such  a  purpose.
The  related  restrictions  upon  expenditures,  especially  the
$20,000,000  ceiling  upon  the  aggregate  expenditures  of
any  candidate  (18  U.S.C.  § GO8(c)),  is  within  the  legis-
lative  power  of  Congress  for  reasons  previously  discussed.
Even-handed  public  financing  of  the  cost  of  election
campaigns  is  consistent  with  both  the  words  and  spirit
of  the  First  Amendment.  The  financial  support,  viewed
as  a whole,  goes  not  to  any  particular  candidate,  or  party,
or  political  opinion,  but  to  promote  the  process  of  public
deliberation  prior  to  balloting.  Where  the  Establishment
Clause  and  history  conjoin  to  teach  that  the  government
shall  not  promote  religion,  because  the  domain  of  religion
should  be  set  aside  as  a  garden  in  the  wilderness,  neither
the  text  of  the  guaranty  respecting  speech  nor  historic  ex-
perience  denies  the  public  the  means  of  providing  for
that  fullness  of  political  debate  necessary  to  the  preserva-
tion  of representative  government.

Appellants  complain  of  unconstitutional  discrimination

12

13

because  in  general  Presidential  elections  the  public  finan-
cial  aid  available  to  the  candidates  of  minor  parties
and  new  parties  is  less  than  the  subvention  available  to
the  candidates  of  major  parties,  i.e.,  those  whose  candi-
dates  received  more  than  25  percent  of  the  vote  in  the
previous  election.  ‘‘Sometimes  the  grossest  discrimination
can  lie  in  treating  things  that  are  different  as  though  they
were  exactly  alike.’’
Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  441-
42  (1971).  Congress  was  not  required  to  ignore  the  mani-
fold  greater  significance  of  major  parties  in  terms  of
their  ability  both  to  win  elections  and  to  raise  campaign
funds.  No  presidential  candidate  nominated  by  a  minor
party  or  new  party  has  ever  been  elected.  Minor  party  and
new  party  candidates  have  not  come  close  for  sixty  years.
The  formulas  for  allocating  aid  provide  equal  treatment
to  all  candidates  and  groups  in  relation  to  their  electoral
needs  and  potential.

Indeed,  when  the  grants  of  financial  support  and  the
restrictions  upon  expenditures  are  taken  as  a  whole,  there
is  every  reason  to  believe  that  the  FECA,  as  amended,
will  improve  the  relative  financial  position  of  the  Presiden-
tial  candidates  of  minor  parties  and  new  parties.  Under
FECA  a  major  party  candidate  receives  $20,000,000  in
substitution  for  the  ability  to  raise  and  spend  several
times  that  sum.  The  minor  party  candidate  receives  a
payment  proportionate  to  its  probable  significance  mea-
sured  by  the  electoral  vote  in  the  last  election  plus  the
sums  which  it  can  raise  and  spend  from  private  sources
plus  the  guaranty  of  a  further  sum  after  the  election,  if
deserved,  in  order  to  make  its  subsidy  proportionate  to
its  actual  electoral  significance.
When  this  package  of
financial  opportunities  is  compared  with  the  $20,000,000
to  which  the  major  party  candidates  are  limited,  it  seems
highly  probable  that  any  minor  party  candidate  will  be
relatively  better  off  financially  than  in  the  absence  of

legislation.  New  parties  receive  no  advance  public  sub-
vention,  but  the  ceiling  upon  expenditures  should  improve
their  relative  position.  Any  slight  risk  that  a  minor  or
new  party  candidate  will  lose  financial  power  relative  to
a  major  party  because  of  some  unforeseen  circumstance
is  justified  by  the  interest  in  avoiding  wasteful  public
expenditures  serving  chiefly  to  promote  frivolous  candi-
dacies,  splintered  parties  or  unrestrained  factionalism.

The  FECA,  as  amended,  also  enlarges  the  opportunity
to  seek  nomination  for  election  to  the  Presidency  despite
lack  of  wealth  or  access  to  wealth  by  providing  matching
grants  for  candidates  who  show  that  they  have  received
contributions  aggregating  at  least  $5,000  from  the  resi-
dents  of  each  of  20  States,  counting  only  the  first  $250
The  eligibility  requirements  are
of  each  contribution.
designed  to  confine  the  federal  subsidy  to  those  candidates
who  have  such  serious  support  of  their  own  as  to  give
The
them  a  significant  chance  of  winning  nomination.
matching  payments  do  not  wipe  out  but  plainly  tend  to
reduce  inequalities  of  opportunity  due  to  differences  in
ability  to  raise  large  contributions.  The  imaginary  candi-
date  with  popular  support  but  no  ability  to  raise  funds
is unlikely  to  find  himself  worse  off  than  before.  At  worst,
he  loses  only  some  marginal  chance  that  he  might  have
won  the  nomination  instead  of  the  candidate  whose  success
is  attributable  to  funds  which  that  candidate  would  not
have  had  without  the  public  subvention.  A  reform  measure
that  moves  strongly  in the  direction  of eliminating  the  con-
sequences  of  economic  disparities  is  not  unconstitutional
merely  because  some  of  life’s  unfairnesses  remain  un-
changed.

14

IV

The  court  below  correctly  ruled  that  the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Commission  was  lawfully  established  and  its  members
constitutionally  appointed,  without  deciding  whether  the
FECA  provisions  purporting  to  give  the  Commission
power  to  institute  civil  actions  and.  recommend  criminal
prosecution  are  unconstitutional
invasions  of  Executive
prerogatives.  The  Commission  has  a  core  of  other  impor-
tant  powers  of  kinds  which  do  not  belong  exclusively  to
the  Executive  and  which  have  often  been  vested  in  agencies
deliberately  put  beyond  executive  control.
The  Commis-
sion’s  duties  to  conduct  studies  and  recommend  legisla-
tion,  to  conduct  audits  and  make  certifications  in  connec-
tion  with  public  financing  of  federal  elections,  to  serve  as
a  depositary  of  financial  reports,  and  to  issue  interpreta-
tive  opinions  and  rules  and  regulations  implementing  the
FECA  are  all  functions  which  may  constitutionally  be
assigned  to an  independent  agency.

The  Federal  Elections  Commission  was  designed  to  be,
and  is,  an  agency  independent  of  both  Congress  and  the
President  in  the  performance  of  the  bulk  of  its  duties.
There  were  unique  reasons  not  only  for  that  choice  but
more  especially  for  the  dividing  the  selection  of  the  six
voting  members  among  the  President  of  the  United  States,
the  Speaker  of  the  House  of  Representatives,  and  the
President  pro  tem.  of the  Senate.  The  incumbent  President
would  soon  be  seeking  nomination  and  election.  He  would
be leading  his  Party  in  the  1976  campaigns  for  Senator  and
Representative.
The  six  new  commissioners  would  be
rendering  interpretations  of  a  new  law  which  might  well
be  thought  to  injure  one  major  party  and  assist  the  other.
The  six  members  to  be  appointed  might  soon  be  required
to  certify  whether  the  incumbent  President  was  entitled  to
matching  funds  under  26  U.S.C.  64  9033-9036  in  seeking

15

nomination,  and  later,  to  determine  whether  his  Party
and  he, himself,  were  eligible  for  payments  under  26 U.S.C.
§§ 9003-9005,  9008.  It would  be  their  duty  to  make  careful
audits  of  his  accounts  and  obtain  repayment  if  found
due.  It  would  also  be  their  duty  to  receive  other  reports
from  him  as  a candidate,  to determine  how  to  publish  them,
and  to  scrutinize  them  for  violations.  No  argument  is
needed  to  show  the  unsoundness  of  providing  that  all  six
men  should  be  selected  by  the  chief  political  figure  with
whom  they  must  deal  and  whose  behavior  they  must
scrutinize.  Dividing  the  authority  to  appoint  the  six  voting
members  of  the  Commission  among  three  high  ranking
officials,  including  the  President  of  the  United  States,  was
calculated  to  remove  the  Commission  from  any  single
source  of  influence,  and  to  guard  against  any  single  set
of  mind.  Still  further  assistance  is  contained  in  the  re-
quirement  that  the  President  pro  tem.  of  the  Senate  and
the  Speaker  of  the  House  appoint  ‘‘upon  the  recommenda-
and  the  minority
tions  of  the  majority  leader
leader...  .”’

...

Nothing  in  the  words  of  Article  II  or  the  theory  of  the
separation  of  powers  bars  this  solution  to  a  new  and
unique  problem.
The  sum  of  legislative  and  executive
practice,  and  of the  rare  judicial  rulings,  is that  the  Presi-
dent,  generally  speaking,  exercises  both  the  power  to
appoint  and  to  remove,  but  that  deviations  from  the  prin-
ciple,  including  virtual  appointment  by  Congress,  will  be
permitted  when  special  occasion  requires.

The  Federal  Elections  Commission  will  occupy  a  unique
role  in  our  politics  and  government.  It  can  succeed  only
if  its  complete  independence  and  freedom  from  bias  or
predilection  is  trusted  by  both  rival  candidates  and  citi-
zenry.  Dividing  the  power  of  appointment  so  that  no  more
than  two  of  six  members  would  ever  owe  their  position
to  a  single  source  is  an  important  step  in  enabling  the
Commission  to  build  that  essential  trust.

16

Argument

Introduction

For  government  of  the  people  to  survive  public  office
must  be  kept  a  public  trust.  Corrosion  of  the  honor  and
integrity  of  official  decisions  will  destroy  representative
government  more  surely  than  conquest  by  force.  Aliena-
tion  of  the  citizenry  from  the  political  system  because  it
sees  the  system  losing  integrity  is  a  final  phase  of  the
fall.

In  the  United  States,  in  our  time,  these  dangers  are
neither  hypothetical  nor  remote.  Through  the  1960’s  and
into  the  1970’s  ever-more  staggering  sums  of  money  were
being  spent  in  each  new  federal  election  campaign,  as
candidates  either  sought  to  win  by big spending  or,  despite
their  distaste,  feared  to  let  their  rivals  outspend  them.
The  need  for  enormous  sums  of  money  sent  candidates
‘‘where  the  money  is,’’  seeking  ever  larger  individual  and
concealed-corporate  contributions.  Often  the  big  contribu-
tions  came  from  those  whose  business  interests  would  be
directly  affected  by  government  decisions,  for  the  expan-
sion  of  government  regulation  and  spending  has  brought
more  business  interests  within  the  impact  of  day-to-day
governmental  action.
Decisions  favoring  those  interests
then  flowed  from  officials  subject  to  pressure  from  the
successful  candidates  or
political  party  which  had  re-
ceived  such  largesse.  Cause  and  effect?  The  relation  is
seldom  clear.  But  other  businessmen  were  feeling  pressure
to  match  the  contributions  of  their  competitors.  Citizens
who  observed  the  course  of  events,  especially  young  men
and  women  into  whose  hands  the  Republic  would  soon  be
committed,  were  alienated  by  the  evidence  that  the  world
of politics  and  government  served  chiefly  the  ‘‘big  money”’
which  brought  political  success.

17

Congress  faced  the  danger  squarely,  with  evident  ap-
preciation  of  the  weight  of  its  responsibility.  The  debate
was  full;  the  deliberations,  mature.  Congressional  concern
with  the  improper  influence  of large  financial  contributions
See  United
goes  back  to  early  in  the  present  century.
States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567,  570-84  (1957).  Congres-
sional  deliberations  leading  to  the  challenged  legislation
began  in  1966  and  proceeded  at  a  high  level  of  interest  for
eight  years.  During  this  period  three  major  laws  were
enacted  and  later  revised.  Presidential  Election  Campaign
Fund  Act,  P.L.  89-908,  80  Stat.  1587  (1966) ; Federal  Elec-
tion  Campaign  Act  of  1971,  P.L.  92-225,  86  Stat.  3  (1972) ;
Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code,  P.L.  92-178,  85
Stat.  562  (1971);  and  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
Amendments  of  1974,  P.L.  93-443,  88  Stat.  1263  (1975).

19

tire  subject  in  1973  and  1974  after  observing  the  operation
of  earlier  laws  in  the  1972  elections,  there  were  three  care-
ful  Senate  reports,  two  reports  made  by  the  Committee
on  Rules  and  Administration  (S.  Reps.  93-310,  93-689)  and
one  by  the  Committee  on  Commerce  (S.  Rep.  93-170).  An
elaborate  report  was  also  filed  by the  House  Committee  on

debate  in  both  chambers  and  an  elaborate  Conference
.  did  not
Report  (S.  Rep.  93-1237).  Thus,  ‘‘[t]he  bill  .
steal  upon  an  unsuspecting  legislature,  and  pass  unob-
served.  Its  principle  was  completely  understood,  and  was
opposed  with  equal  zeal  and  ability.  After  being  resisted
first  in  the  fair  and  open  field  of debate,  and  afterwards  in
the  executive  cabinet,  with  as  much  persevering  talent  as
any  measure  has  ever  experienced,  and  being  supported  by
arguments  which  convinced  minds  as  pure  and  as  intelligent
as  this  country  can  boast,  it  became  a  law....  It  would
require  no  ordinary  share  of  intrepidity  to  assert,  that  a
measure  adopted  under  these  circumstances,  was  a  bold
and  plain  usurpation,  to  which  the  constitution  gave  no
countenance.’’  McCulloch  v.  Maryland,  4  Wheat.  316,  402
(1819).

.

The  remedies  provided  by Congress  in  the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Campaign  Act,  as  amended,  regulate  the  financing  —
and  only  the  financing  —  of  political  campaigns  for  elec-
tion  to  federal  office.  Many  provisions  are  long  and  com-
plex,  partly  because  experience  demonstrated  the  dangers
of  evasion  and  avoidance,  but  partly  because  concern  for
political  liberty  required  Congress  to  resolve  questions  of
degree  with  all  the  precision  that  language  permits.  The
five  central  themes  are  simple  and  direct:

(1)  The  size  of  individual  and  organizational  con-

tributions  is limited.

(2)

Ceilings  are  placed  upon  the  aggregate  of  ex-

penditures  a  candidate  may  make  in  seeking  elective
federal  office.

(3)  Detailed  reporting  and  disclosure  is  required
for  contributions  and  expenditures  within  the  stat-
utory limits.

(4)  Public  financing  is  provided  in  Presidential
elections  for  candidates  with  substantial  public  sup-
port.

(5)  A strong,  independent  and  permanent  commis-
sion  is  established  to  implement  these  measures  and
investigate  the  need  for  further  electoral  reform.

The  basic  issues  in  this  case  are  framed  by  appellants’
contention  that  Congress  has  no  power  to  prescribe  any
of  these  remedies  for  the  evils  corroding  our  national
political  life.  The  thrust  of their  argument  is that  Congress
may  do  nothing  to  limit  the  size  of  candidates’  financial
expenditures,  nothing  to  limit  the  size  of  the  gifts  of
money  which  candidates  may  accept,  nothing  to  require
disclosing  the  names  of  contributors  to  any  but  Democra-
tie  and  Republican  candidates,  and  nothing  to  substitute
public  financing  for  reliance  upon  the  private  contributions
which  are  the  source  of  such  grave  danger.

We  believe  that  the  Congress  is  not  so  impotent  in
dealing  with  a  national  crisis.
The  remedial  measures
adopted  are  within  the  basic  legislative  powers  of  Con-
gress  and  fully  consistent  with  the  words  and  underlying
values  of the  First  Amendment.  The  substance  of political
debate  is  left  untouched.  The  restrictions  upon  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures,  and  also  the  requirements  of  dis-
closure,  deal  not  with  speakers  or  ideas  but  with  conduct
—  with  the  giving  and  spending  of money  and  like  financial
resources.  Indirectly  they  will  probably  check  further  in-
-reases,  and  may  somewhat  reduce,  the  amounts  of  money
available  to  hire  media  of  mass  communication,  but  those
consequences,  even  though  enough  to  implicate  the  First
Amendment,  are  amply  justified  by the  Act’s  contribution

20

21

to  the  openness  and  integrity  of  the  political  system.  The
plan  for  public  financing  in  campaigns  for  nomination  and
elections  as  President  serves  the  same  high  purposes.  The
appropriations  promote  general  public  debate  upon  can-
didates  and  issues  without  favoring  any  candidate,  polit-
ical  party  or  ideology.  Minor  parties  and  new  parties
will  have  relatively  greater  spending  power  vis  a  vis  the
major  parties  as  a  result  of  the  total  package  of  restric-
tions  and  payments  from  the  Presidential  Election  Cam-
paign  Fund.

The  fundamental  issues  concerning  whether  Congress
has  power  to  restrict  the  size  of  contributions  and  ex-
penditures,  or  to  require  any  disclosure  of  the  identity  of
any  contributors,  or  to  provide  public  financing  in  any
circumstances  are  properly  before  the  Court  because  they
go  to  the  facial  validity  of  a  statute  touching  immediately
upon  the  interests  of  one  or  more  appellants.  Appellants
have  also  identified,  concretely  and  with  particularity,  some
activities  in  which  they  would  engage,  or  others  would
engage  for  their  benefit,  but  for  the  statutory  prohibitions.
The  constitutionality  of the  Act  as  applied  in these  concrete
instances  is also  properly  before  the  Court.  FECA  Section
315(a),  codified  as  2 U.S.C.  § 437h,  supplies  an  expedited
procedure  and  eliminates  any  question  concerning  appel-
lants’  standing  to  challenge  the  public  financing  of  costs
of  political  campaigns.  See  Flast  v.  Cohen,  392  U.S.  83,
120  (1968)  (dissenting  opinion).

Appellants  also  present  many  purely  hypothetical  ques-
tions,  which  have  not  taken  shape  and  may  never  arise.
In  our  view,  Section  315(a)  does  not  authorize  decision  of
such  questions.  Such  a  mandate  would  be  an  unconstitu-
tional  effort  to  enlarge  the  Court’s  jurisdiction  beyond
the  ‘‘cases’’  and  ‘‘controversies’’  to  which  the  judicial
power  under  Article  III  is  confined.  ‘‘Passing  upon  the

possible  significance  of  the  manifold  provisions  of  a  broad
statute  in  advance  of  efforts  to  apply  the  separate  provi-
sions  is  analogous  to  rendering  an  advisory  opinion  upon
a  statute  or  a  declaratory  judgment  upon  a  hypothetical
case.’’  Watson  v.  Buck,  313  U.S.  387,  402  (1941).

We  discuss  these  limitations  upon  the  scope  of  the  ques-
tions  before  the  Court  at  greater  length  in  the  appropriate
substantive  context.  It  is  enough  preliminarily  to  note
three  points:

First,  the  only  constitutional  questions  to  be  decided
in  this  case  are  whether  any  challenged  FECA  provision
is uncoxustitutional  on  its  face  or  as  applied  to  a  live  con-
troversy  growing  out  of  a  concrete  situation  presented  by
appellants’  factual  allegations  or  proof.

Second,  the  constitutional  questions  properly  before  the
Court  are  not  to  be  decided  for  all  time  regardless  of
major  changes  in  social,  economic,  or  political  conditions.
An  Act  of  Congress  which  is  constitutional  under  one  set
of conditions  may  be held  invalid  upon  sufficient  demonstra-
tion  that  conditions  have  critically  changed.  Chastleton
Milnot  Co.  v.
Corp.  v.  Sinclair,  264  U.S.  543  (1924);
Richardson,  350  F.  Supp.  221  (S.D.  DL  1972).

Third,  arguments  charging  unconstitutional  vagueness
because  of  borderline  questions  of  interpretation  should
carry  little  weight  in  this  case.  No  one  need  refrain  from
any  lawful  activity  or  trim  his  words  because  of  uncer-
tainty  concerning  the  meaning  of  the  Act.  The  Act  re-
quires  the  Federal  Elections  Commission  to  give  anyone
considering  a  specific  course  of  conduct  an  advisory  opin-
ion  in  writing.  If he  relies  upon  the  opinion  in  good  faith,
he  is  not  guilty  of  a  criminal  offense.  2  U.S.C.  § 487f.

22

I

Tue  Restrictions  Upon  THE  Size  or  Frxanciat  Contri-
BUTIONS  TO  CANDIDATES  FOR  FEDERAL  OFFICE  AND  Upon  Ex-

A.  Congress  Is  Empowered  ta  Legislate  Concerning
Financial  Contributions  and  Expenditures  in  Federal
Elections.

Congress  has  full  power  to  enact  legislation  for  preserv-
ing  the  integrity  of  federal  elections.  In  the  case  of  Sena-
tors  and  Representatives,  the  power  is  expressly  delegated
by  Article  I,  Section  4.  Ex  parte  Siebold,  100  U.S.  371
(1879);  United  States  v.  Mosley,  238  U.S.  383  (1915);
United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.S.  299  (1941).  In  the  elec-
tion  of  the  President,  the  power  is  inherent.
Ea  parte
Yarbrough,  110  U.S.  651  (1884);
Burroughs  v.  United
States,  290  U.S.  534  (1934).  In  the  latter  case  the  Court
declared :

To  say  that  Congress  is without  power  to  pass  appro-
priate  legislation  to  safeguard  such  an  election  [i.e.
a  Presidential  election]
from  the  improper  use  of
money  to  influence  the  result  is  to  deny  to  the  nation
in a  vital  particular  the  power  of  self  protection.  Con-
gress,  undoubtedly,  possesses  that  power,  as  it  pos-
sesses  every  other  power  essential  to  preserve  the
departments  and  institutions  of  the  general  govern-
ment  from  impairment  or  destruction,  whether  threat-
ened  by force  or  by corruption.  Jd.  at  545.

‘23

Congress  is  also  authorized  to  enact  legislation  securing
the  conduct  of  government  against  financial  corruption
and  other  forms  of  improper  influence.  The  Court  re-
cognized  the  existence  of  this  power  in  the  passage  just
quoted  from  Burroughs.  It has  consistently  sustained  such
legislation,  including  restrictions  upon  the  political  ae?  y-
ities  of civil  servants,  Ex parte  Curtis,  106  U.S.  371  (1882)  ;
United  States  v.  Wurzbach,  280  U.S.  396  (1930);  United
Public  Workers  v.  Mitchell,  330  U.S.  75  (1947);  Cwil
Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Carriers  Union,  413  U.S.
548  (1973),  registration  of lobbyists,  United  States  v.  Har-
riss,  347  U.S.  612,  625  (1954),  the  punishment  of  bribery
and  conflicts  of  interest,  Burton  v.  United  States,  202
U.S.  344  (1906);  United  States  v.  Brewster,  408  U.S.  501
(1972),  and  the  punishment  of  conspiracies  ‘‘for  impair-
ing,  obstructing  or  defeating  the  lawful  function  of  any
department  of  Government,’’  Haas  v.  Henkel,  216  U.S.
462,  479  (1910).  See  also  In  re  Coy,  127  U.S.  731  (1888).
The  legislation  here  challenged  is  within  the  ambit  of
the  delegated  powers.  Its  objects  are  to  preserve  the  in-
tegrity  of  federal  political  campaigns  and  elections,  and
public  confidence  therein;
to  safeguard  the  honor  and
integrity  of  the  conduct  of  government,  and  public  con-
fidence  therein;  to  prevent  the  violation  or  circumvention
of  earlier  laws  regulating  financial  abuses  in  election  cam-
paigns;  to  reduce  the  actual  and  apparent  influence  of
vast  financial  resources  on  the  outcome  of  elections;  to
reduce  the  financial  barriers  to  the  candidacies  of  men  and
women  without  access  to  large  contributors  of  money  or
their  own  personal  wealth;  and  to  equalize  access  to  can-
didates  and  government  officials  as  among  wealthy  contri-
butors  and  other  citizens.

The  power  to  legislate  for  these  objectives  being  clear,
the  choice  among  means  reasonably  related  to  the  ends
is for  Congress,  subject  only  to  inquiry  whether  the  means

24

25

selected  violate  a  guaranty  of  liberty  in  the  First  or  Fifth
Amendment.  Cf.  Burroughs  v.  United  States,  290  U.S.  534,
547  (1934).

B.  The  Ceilings  Upon  Contributions  and  Expenditures
Are  Consistent  With  the  First  Amendment,  as  En-
acted  and  as  Applied  to  Any  Situation  Ripe  jor  Ad-
judication.

The  restrictions  upon  the  size  of  campaign  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  do  not  deny  anyone  the  right  to
speak  or  to  associate  himself  with  others.  The  Act  does
not  prohibit,  punish,  or  attach  liability  to  any  form  of
utterance,  publication  or  communication  as  such.  The  sub-
stance  of  political  debate  is  left  untouched.  There  is  no
attempt  to  censor,  directly  or  indirectly,  either  the  ideas
that  may  be  expressed  or  the  vehicles  chosen  for  their
expression.  Moreover,  this  statute,  unlike  the  law  under
attack  in  United  States  v.  CIO,  335  U.S.  106  (1948),  and
United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567  (1957),  does  not  dis-
criminate  among  individuals  or  organized  groups  in  op-
portunities  to  express  their  political  concerns.  The  restric-
tions  here  at  issue  deal  only  with  conduct,  with  the  trans-
fer  or  use  of  money  and  other  material  resources.  Appel-
lants’  argument  ignores  this  simple  truth.

The  restrictions  upon  conduct  will  undoubtedly  have
some  practical  effects  upon  opportunities  for  enjoying
some  forms  of  freedom  of  political  expression  and  for
In
increasing  the  effectiveness  of  political  associations.
modern  society  money  gives  access  to  the  channels  of mass
communication:  radio  and  television  broadcasts,  newspaper
advertisements,  direct  mailing,  and  other  media  of  ex-
pression,  If  a  particular  candidate  has  the  capacity  to
raise  and  spend  such  large  sums  of  money  as  to  exceed
the  ceiling  upon  campaign  expenditures,  then  the  ceilings

will  probably  limit  the  number  of  people  reached  by  his
speeches,  advertising  and  slogans,  and  also  the  frequency
with  which  his  name,  virtues  and  views  are  brought  to
public  attention.
The  ceilings  upon  individual  financial
contributions'  may  have  similar  consequences,  either  be-
cause  the  candidate  cannot  raise  the  full  amount  he  is
allowed  to  spend  without  obtaining  some  gifts  exceeding
the  ceilings,  or  because  larger  contributions  are  needed
to  buy  the  means  of  mass  communication  at  the  time
when  they  will  be  most  effective.  The  restrictions  upon
individual  expenditures  on  behalf  of  a  candidate?  and
upon  a  candidate’s  expenditures  from  personal  or  family
funds*  may  have  similar  consequences,

Although  the  indirect  effects  of  the  ceilings  upon  op-
portunities  to  buy  use  of  the  media  of  mass  communica-
tion  are  enough  to  require  consideration  of  the  bearing  of
the  First  Amendment,  the  distinction  between  regulation
of  the  use  of  money  and  regulation  of  speech  itself  has
critical  constitutional  importance,  The  reasons  are  these:

A law  which  restricts  the  kinds  of  ideas,  information  or
feelings  which  may  be expressed,‘  or  the  verbal  or  pictorial
vehicles  of  expression,®  or  which  discriminates  among  per-
sons  who  may  express  them,®  can  be  justified  —  if at  all  —
only  by showing  either  the  existence  of some  overwhelming
public  exigency”  or  the  utter  worthlessness  of  the  mes-

* §§608(b) (1) and  (3).
2 §608(e).
3 §608(a).
*E.qg.,  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.  254  (1964) ;

New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713  (1971).

5 E.g.,  Cohen  v.  California,  403  U.S.  15  (1971)  ; Spence  v.  Wash-

ington,  418  U.S.  405  (1974).

® E.g.,  United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567  (1957).
7 E.g.,  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  United  States,  403  U.S.  713,  726,
730, 731  (1971).  In  a  few  instances  the  restriction  is limited  to  the
circumstances  of  a  particular  time  and  place  but  based  upon  the
dangerous  character  of  the  ideas  expressed.  E.g.,  Brandenberg  v.
Ohio,  395  U.S.  444  (1969).  In  such  eases  the  justifying  public

26

>  hed 27

sage.  When  the  government  attempts  to  deal  with  speech
directly,  the  case  for  regulation  must  be  made—if  it can  be
made  at all—in  terms  of the  danger  posed  by the  ideas.  The
constitutional  test  is  exccedingly  stringent  in  such  cases,
if  indeed  the  First  Amendment  ever  permits  such  regula-
tion  in  a  medium  of  unrestricted  access.  Such  censorship
offends  the  individual’s  duty  to  voice  the  truth  he  has
discovered;*®  it  may  deprive  the  people  of  information
essential  to  self-government;  it  also  carries  the  danger
of  discrimination  and  the  seeds  or  official  ideology.

A  generalized  regulation  of  conduct,  not  tied  to  any
particular  ideas,  speakers  or  forms  of  expression,  carries
none  of  these  dangers.  Conduct,  even  when  one  of  its
consequences  is  to  facilitate  the  exercise  of  freedom  of
speech  or  of  the  press,  may  work  public  injury  unrelated
to  any  effect  of  the  communication  facilitated.  The  injury
may  require  a  measure  of  public  control  directed  to  the
conduct  without  regard  to  the  character  of  the  ideas  or
their  expression.  Here,  the  constitutional  standard  is  less
severe.  A  law  which  regulates  conduct  evenhandedly  is
constitutional,  even  though  its  operation  may  in  practice
curtail  the  maximum  use  of  First  Amendment  freedoms,
if  the  public  interests  which  the  measure  is  really  caleu-
lated  to  serve  outweigh  its  costs  in  curtailment  of  the
volume  of expression.  Similarly,  a  narrowly-drawn  restric-
tion  upon  time,  place  or  channel  of  expression  is  constitu-
tional  if justified  by  opposing  public  interests  invaded  by
any  act  of  speaking  or  any  resort  to  a  particular  form  of
expression  at  the  particular  time  or  place,  without  refer-

necessity  need  not  be  as  overwhelming  as  total  suppression  of  a
piece  of  information  would  require,  but  because  the  proscription
turns  upon  the  particular  words  and  their  consequences  the  test  is
more  stringent  than  would  otherwise  be  required.

8 E.g.,  New  York  Times  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  376  U.S.  254,  279-80
(1964):  Miller  v.  California,  413  U.S.  15,  23-24  (1973);  Paris
Adult  Theatre  I v.  Slaton,  413  U.S.  49,  54  (1973).

°Cf.  Hocking,  Freedom  of  the  Press  96-97  (1947).

ence  to  the  ideas  expressed  or  the  identity  of  the  one
expressing  them.  The  accepted  test  of  constitutionality  in
all  such  cases  is  to  inquire  whether  the  regulation  serves
a  ‘‘compelling’’  or  ‘‘paramount”’  public  interest  which  can-
not  be  secured  equally  well  by  another,  less  restrictive
measure.  Branzburg  v.  Hayes,  408  U.S.  665,  682,  733-34
(1972)  ; United  States  v.  O’ Brien,  391  U.S.  367, 377  (1968).?°

Other  restraints  upon  freedom  of association:  Gibson  v.  Florida
