1973);  Broadrick

Cases  dealing  with  speeches  advocating  overthrow  of  the  gov-
ernment  by  force  or  similar  violence  are  especially  instructive.
The  present  rule appears  to  be  that  advocacy  of  the  use  of  force  or
law violation  is  punishable  only  ‘‘where  such  advocacy  is directed  to
inciting  or  producing  imminent  lawless  action  and  is likely  to  incite
or  produce  such  action.’’  Brandenburg  v.  Ohio,  395  U.S.  444,  447
(1969).  The requirement  of  ‘‘imminent’’  lawless  action  limits  the
proscription  in  time  and  place,  but  because  the  only  harm  is  the
consequence  of the particular  ideas  which  the  speaker  wishes  to
express,  the  limitation  must  pass  a  more  stringent  test  than  would
otherwise  be  appropriate.  Mills  v.  Alabama,  384  U.S,  214  (1966),
was  such  a  case  even  though  the  prohibition  against  newspaper
editors  attacking  or  favoring  a  candidate  or  ballot  proposition

28

29

The  Attorney  General  (Br.  pp.  27-28,  59-61)  confuses  the
issue  by  suggesting  that  in  order  to  uphold  the  constitu-
tionality  of the  FECA,  as  amended,  one  must  look  to  prece-
dents  sustaining  regulatory  measures  addressed  to  the  con-
tent  of speech.  E.g.,  Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.
FCC, 395
U.S.  367  (1969);  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter  Car-
riers  Union,  413  U.S.  548  (1973).  Compare  Miami  Herald
Pub.  Co.  v.  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241  (1974);  Mills  v.  Alabama,
384  U.S.  214  (1966);  Brandenburg  v.  Ohio,  395  U.S.  444
(1969).  In  each  of  those  cases  the  challenge  was  to  a  law
drawn  upon  the  theory  that  certain  ideas  would  have  un-
desirable  consequences  if  expressed  at  a  particular  time
or  place,  or  if  not  followed  by  opportunity  for  rebuttal.
The  constitutional  test  was  very  stringent  because  the  law
was  concerned  with  what  was  said  and  was  enacted  because
of  its  allegedly  undesirable  consequences.
Similarly,  in
Eastern  Railroad  Presidents  Conference  v.  Noerr  Motor
Freight,  Inc.,  365  U.S.  127  (1961),  the  complaint  charged
the  illegality  of  the  substance  of  the  truckers’  publicity
campaign,  an  allegation  central  to  the  railroads’  case.  The
The  FECA,  as
present  case
amended,  is  not  concerned  with  what  is  said,  how  often
it  is  said,  or  who  speaks,  except  in  the  very  marginal  case
where  Section  608(e)  calls  for  looking  to  the  words  to
ascertain  whether  they  relate  to  a  clearly  identified  can-
didate  and  urge  his  election  or  defeat.  The  evils  which
Congress  addressed  are  the  corrosive  influence  of  large
contributions  and  expenditures  of  money  —  dangers  in
nowise  resulting  from  the  ideas  expressed  or  the  volume  of

is  altogether  different.

was  limited  to  the  day  of  an  election.  The  statute  treated  the
expression  of  certain  ideas  as  contrary  to  the  public  interest;
it  was  concerned  with  the  character  of  the  words  and  not  with
conduct  and  consequently  failed  to  pass  the  very  stringent  con-
stitutional  test.  Red  Lion  Broadcasting  Co.  v.  FCC,  395  U.S.  367
(1969),  also  involved  regulation  based  upon  the  ideas  expressed.
The  regulation  was  upheld  because  of  technological  limits  upon
access  to  the  electronic  medium.

expression.  For  this  reason,  the  cases  above  which  sustain
First  Amendment  claims  are  readily  distinguishable,  and
the  Court  can—and  we  submit  should—sustain  the  constitu-
tionality  of the  limits  upon  contributions  and  expenditures
without  drawing  upon  such  cases  as  sustain  regulation  of
speech.  The  instant  case  requires  no  expression  of opinion
upon  whether  the Court  would  uphold  the  use  of a balancing
test  or  upon  what  test  the Court  would  apply  where  particu-
lar ideas  are  singled  out  for  regulation.

Appellants  (Br.  p.  47)  likewise  blur  the  critical  dis-
tinction  when  they  argue  that  under  our  view  ‘‘a  limita-
tion  on  how  much  newspapers  spend  on  publication  —  to
hire  reporters,  investigate  stories,  and  print  editions  —
would  not  be  a  regulation  of  the  newspaper  itself.’’  We
make  no  such  contention.  Such  a  limitation  would  violate
the  First  Amendment  if  the  restriction  rested  upon  a
legislative  notion  that  the  evil  to  be  remedied  is  the  size
of  each  edition,  the  volume  of  news  coverage,  the  number
of  readers,  the  amount  of  influence  the  newspaper  exerts,
or  any  other  consequence  either  of  what  is  published  or
Cf.  Grosjean  v.  American
of  the  breadth  of  circulation.
Press.  Co., 297  U.S.  233  (1936).  On  the  other  hand,  the  First
Amendment  would  not  prevent  limiting  expenditures  which
were  the  source  of some  substantial  injury  to  an  important
public  interest  without  regard  to  what  information,  ideas,
or  arguments  the  publisher  chose  to  print.  During  wartime
shortage,  for  example,  Congress  could  place  valid  limits
on  expenditures  for  newsprint,  even  though  the  conse-
quence  was  to  curtail  the  circulation,  the  number  of  edi-
tions  or  the  size  of  the  paper.  Similarly,  if  a  publisher
were  to  pour  money  into  a  newspaper  with  a  view  to  a
temporary  reduction  in  price  and  increase  in  circulation
which  would  destroy  competitors  and  yield  a  future  mo-
nopoly,  the  First  Amendment  would  not  be  a  defense  to
prosecution  under  the  Sherman  Act.  Cf.  Associated  Press

HO

31

v.  United  States,  326  U.S.  1  (1945);  Lorain  Journal  Co.  v.
United  States,  342  U.S.  143  (1951).

Thus,  the  opposing  arguments  cast  no  doubt  upon  the
proposition  that
a  law  which  regulates  conduct  even-
handedly,  because  of  the  injury  which  that  conduct  does
to  the  public  interest,  is  constitutional  if  the  public  in-
terests  which  the  measure  is  really  calculated  to  serve
outweigh  the  costs  in  curtailment  of  opportunities  for  the
full  enjoyment  of  freedom  of  expression.  Nor  do  they
show  that  the  question,  insofar  as  it  can  be  reduced  to
words,  is not  whether  the  regulation  serves  a  ‘‘paramount’’
or  ‘‘compelling’’  public  purpose.

We  make  this  submission  in  full  awareness  that  the
volume  of  political  debate,  the  size  of  a  candidate’s  au-
diences  and  the  frequency  with  which  they  are  reached
may  be  substantially  affected  by  the  amounts  of  money
that  he  is  allowed  to  raise  and  spend  for  campaign  pur-
poses.  The  link  does  not  require  a  more  stringent  constitu-
tional  test.  The  ‘‘compelling  interest’’  test  or  some  essen-
tially  similar  formulation  has  been  applied  when  the  link
In  United  States  v.
was  very  much  closer  than  here.
O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367  (1968),  the  act  of  tearing  up
a  Selective  Service  card  was  itself  the  means  of  expres-
sion;  no  other  means,  in  the  speaker’s  judgment,  would
have  spoken  so  dramatically  to  so  many  people.  Similarly,
in  Kovacs  v.  Cooper,  336  U.S.  77  (1949),  use  of  a  raucus
sound  truck  bore  a  direct  relation  to  the  size  of  the  circle
of auditors.  Restrictions  upon  the  time  and  place  of  speak-
ing  may  also  have  important  effect  upon  the  attention  a
speech  or  demonstration  can  attract  through  the  mass
E.g.,  Adderley  v.  Florida,  385  U.S.  39  (1966).
media.
Balancing  was  permitted  in  those  cases,  despite  the  direct
connection  between  the  prohibited  conduct  and  the  effective
use  of  speech,  because  each  law  focussed  upon  the  evils
flowing  from  the  conduct,  just  as  the  FECA,  as  amended,

focusses  upon  the  evil  consequences  of  private  political
financing  in  the  absence  of  ceilings  upon  the  size  of  con-
tributions  and  expenditures.

The  extent  to  which  a  regulation  of  conduct  curtails
practical  opportunities  for  the  enjoyment  of  freedom  of
expression  is one  prime  element  in the  constitutional  equa-
tion.  The  other  two  are  the  weight  of  the  public  interests
served  by the  measure  and  the  availability  of  less  restric-
tive  measures.  Accordingly,  we  turn  to  each  of the  elements
successively  in  order  to  show  that  in  the  present  case  the
balance  requires  the  conclusion  that  the  ceilings  on  con-
tributions  and  expenditures  are  constitutional,  not  only
on  the  face  of  the  statute  but  as  applied  to  any  concrete
situation  presented  by the  record.

1.  The  Ceilings  upon  Campaign  Contributions  and
Expenditures  Leave  Generous  Opportunities  for
Political  Activity,  Including  the  Exercise  of Free-
dom  of Speech.

The  restrictions  imposed  by  Section  608(c)  upon  a  can-
didate’s  aggregate  expenditures  leave  ample  opportunity
to  achieve  public  recognition  and  lay  his  case  before  the
electorate  even  if  he  begins  re'»tively  unknown.  For  the
statutory  ceilings  are  so  closely  related  to  the  expense  of
past  campaigns  as  to  demonstrate  that  their  chief  fune-
tions  will  be  to  prevent  further  rise  and  cut  back  wild
extravagance.

In  both  1972  and  1974  slightly  over  97  percent  of  the
election  campaigns  for  Representative  were  conducted  by
major  party  candidates  at  less  cost  than  the  FECA  ex-
penditure  ceiling.  Less  than  3 percent  of  the  major  party
candidates  in  House  races  reported  total  expenditures  ex-
ceeding  the  combined  primary  and  general  election  ceil-

32

33

ings.  Findings  ILA,  {718,  40,7  I]  J.A.  442,  448,  In  1972,
both  Congressional  candidates  exceeded  the  spending  limit
in  only  4  of  380  contested  elections.  Finding  IIA,  {3,  I
J.A.  269.  In  1974,  both  candidates  exceeded  the  limit  in
only  one  contest.  Finding  ILA,  95,  II  J.A.  326.

Spending  was  somewhat  heavier  in  1972  and  1974  elec-
tion  campaigns  for  Senator,  but  75  percent  of  the  can-
didates  in  each  year  spent  less  than  the  present  FECA
combined  expenditure  ceiling.  Sixteen  of  those  who  spent
more  than  the  present  ceiling  were  incumbents.  Six  were
challengers  to  incumbents,  four  of  whom  were  outspent
by  their  incumbent  opponents,  and  13  were  candidates  in
open  races,  Findings  IIA,  1928,  47,  II  J.A.  445,  450.”

Spending  in  past  Presidential  elections  also  indicates
the  reasonableness  of  those  ceilings.  In  1968  the  Republi-
can  candidate  raised  and  could  have  spent  $29,600,000;  the
1969  Congressional
Democratic  candidate,  $13,600,000.
Quarterly  Almanac  1200.  The  $20,000,000  limit  fixed  by
Section  608(c),  plus  the  two  cents  per  potential  voter
allowed  national  committees  (4608(f)(2)  ), will  set  a  ceil-
ing  of  approximately  $22,400,000.  which  is  little  above  the
average  of the  two.  Comparison  with  1972  is more  difficult
because  there  is no  reliable  way  of breaking  down  the  total
figures  for  the  primary  and  general  election  campaigns.

111972  and  1974  Congressional  and  Senatorial  candidates  were
required  to  report  their  campaign  expenditures  under  the  1971
FECA.  Primary  expenditures  were  not  then  segregated  from
general  election  expenditures  and  it  is  not  possible  to  make  that
separation  now  with  reasonable  accuracy.  Therefore,  we  have  com-
pared  total  expenditures  in  those  years  with  the  combined  FECA
limits  for  primary  and  general  elections.

12Qnly  one  of  439  minor  party  candidates  in  1972  and  1974
House  races  exceeded  the  combined  spending  limit.  That  candidate,
Allard  Lowenstein  of  New  York,  first  ran  in  the  Democratic  party
primary,  then  ran  in  a  court-ordered  second  primary,  and  after
losing,  ran  as  a  Liberal  in  the  general  election.  Findings  IIA,
919,  40-41,  II  J.A.  448-49.

13  No  minor  party  candidate  for  Senate  exceeded  his  State’s  limit

in  1972  or  1974.

President  Nixon  reported  spending  $55,200,000.  Senator
McGovern  reported  $38,200,000.  Under  the  FECA  ceilings
total  spending  of $36,200,000,  including  20 percent  for  fund-
raising,  would  be  permissible,  in  seeking  nomination  and
election.

The  relation  between  past  experience  and  the  present
ceilings  upon  a  candidate’s  aggregate  expenditures  is more
than  sufficient  to  show  that  the  ceilings  allow  generous
scope  for  purchasing  facilities
of  mass  communication
more  than  ample  for  very  wide  political  debate  and  pub-
licity.  Appellants  have  made  no  showing  which  undercuts
the  connection.  The  fact  that  there  was  heavier  spending
in  a  small  number  of  closely  contested  elections  shows
only  that  candidates  chose  to  spend  all  they  could  raise,
not  that  such  sums  were  necessary  to  full  debate  and
publicity.

The  effect  of  the  $1,000  limit  on  individual  contributions
is more  difficult  to  predict,  but  it is also  much  less  directly
linked  to  the  total  volume  of  political  activity.  The  scat-
tered  evidence  available  tends  to  indicate  that  candidates
will  continue  to  raise  sums  sufficient  for  wide  and  vigorous
campaigning.
For  example,  preliminary  reports  of  ex-
penditures  in  the  recent  special  election  of  a  Senator  from
New  Hampshire  indicate  that  both  candidates  raised  and
spent  very  close  to  the  statutory  limit.

It  is  also  worth  noting  that  the  link  between  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures,  on  the  one  hand,  and  the  volume
of  campaign  debate,  on  the  other  hand,  may  be  looser
than  appellants  ask  the  Court  to  suppose.  The  $1,000  limit
on  individual  contributions  and  other  ceilings  upon  sep-
arate  gifts  or  expenditures  obviously  do  not  directly  con-
trol  the  sums  spent  upon  the  media,  although  they  will
influence  methods  of  political  finance.  The  ceiling  upon  a
candidate’s  aggregate  expenditures  may  exert  greater  in-
fluence,  but  even  that  restriction  leaves  room  for  adjusting

34

39

campaign  practices  so  as  to  reduce  expenditures  in  areas
other  than  travel  and  hiring  media  of  mass  communica-
tion.  Less  money  might  be  spent  in  taking  polls,  for exam-
ple.  Candidates  might  choose  to  spend  less  on  pre ad-
vertising  agencies  and  public  relations  men  to  —
the  product,’’  and  rely  more  heavily  upon volunteers.
candidate  might  even  write  some  of  his own  speeches  ~
stead  of  paying  the  salary  of  a  professional.  More  4
may  go  into  organizing  volunteers  and  activities,  oan in  :
advertising  candidates  like  soap  and  egaretion,  he b
this  to  suggest  that  it is a  function  of  FECA,  as  amen  ~
to  induce  such  changes;  nor  do  we  argue  that they wou
be  good  or  bad.  Under  the  First  Amendment  it is  not  for
government  to  judge  the  relative  values  of  various  forms
of political  debate.  The  point  we  urge  is  that  the  opportun-
ities  for  changing  campaign  practices  show  that  there  is
no  fixed,  one-for-one  relationship  between  ceilings  yn
individual  contributions  and  aggregate  expenditures  an
the  extent  to  which  candidates  and  their  supporters  can
enjoy  the  exercise  of  First  Amendment  rights.

For  both  reasons,  we  conclude  that  the  ceilings  upon
contributions  and  expenditures  allow  every  candidate  very
generous  opportunity  to  use  all  facilities  of mass  com-
munication  in  enjoying  both  freedom  of  political  activity
and  freedom  of  speech.

2.  The  Ceilings  upon  Contributions  and  Expenditures

Promote  Compelling  Public  Interests.

A primary  function  of  the  ceilings  on  financial  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  in  political  campaigns  for  election
to  federal  office  is to  secure  against  the influence  of  money
the  honor  and  integrity  of decisions  in  both  the  Legislative
.
and  Executive  Branch.
Expenditures  in election  campaigns  had  been  growing  by

.

leaps  and  bounds.*

pressure  to accept,  and  even  to extort,  very  large  gifts  from
men  whose  personal  ambitions  or  business  affairs  would

be  directly  and  substantially  affected  by  government  deci-

The  larger  the  contribution,  the  greater  the  pressure  to

The kinds  of  advantage  sought  by  large  contributors
and  the  degrees  of  ‘‘bargain  and  sale’’  vary  over  a wide
range.  A  major  solicitor  of  large  contributions  in  1972
acknowledged  that  an  ambassadorial  assignment  was  sold
for  a  financial  gift.*  More  often,  perhaps,  potentially  large
contributors  wishing  to  be  ambassadors  have  received  only
the  euphemistic  and  possibly  lawful  assurance  that  after
the  election  their  names  would  be  included  on  Ge  os of
‘‘well-qualified’’  persons  which  the  campaign  official  ad
been  asked  to  submit  to  the  appointing  authority.  In
either  event,  the  danger  of  corrupting  influence  is plain.

Many  potential  contributors  of  large  campaign  gifts
have  business  interests  likely  to  be  affected  by  decisions
of  the  Congress  and  federal  agencies.  The  proportion  has

risen  sharply  as  federal  taxation,  regulation  and  spending
have  increased.  The  expectations  of  business  contributors
are  various.  A  contributor  of  $100,000  during  the  1972
election  campaign  testificd  that  he  was  seeking  not  so
much  to  help  in  the  election  as  to  secure  the  lifting  of  an
order  suspending  him  from  taking  construction  contracts.”
The  timing  of  other  large  gifts  suggests  similar  motiva-
tion.  The  Milk  Producers  Association,  for  example,  pledged
$2,000,000  to  the  1972  campaign  at  the  very  moment  at
which  the  incumbent  officials  were  deciding  whether  to
raise  the  support  price  for  milk.’*  Other  large  contributors
testified  they  were  moved  by  fear  that  their  competitors
would  fare  better  than  they  in  dealing  with  government
agencies  unless  they  matched  their  rivals’  contributions.”
Still  others  hope  only  to  obtain  the  entree  to  the  offices  of
high  government  officials  charged  with  decisions  affecting
their  business  interests  which  they  suppose  —  often  quite
erroneously  —  that  they  would  otherwise  lack,  preferably
an  entree  under  the  auspices  of  an  influential  figure  in
Congress,  the  White  House  or  a  political  party,  whose
pleasure  or  displeasure  could  affect  the  responsible  of-
ficial.2”  Although  no  one  can  define  the  effect  of  such  con-

One  corporate  executive  explained,  ‘‘I  think  all  we  were
attempting  to do was  to assure  ourselves  of a  forum  to be heard...  .
[We  felt  we  needed  something  that  would  be  sort  of  a  ealling
card,  something  that  would  get  us  in  the  door  and  make  our  ‘point
of  view  heard.’’  Id.  at  5442.  A  campaign  fund-raiser  testified,
‘‘[I]n  return  for  that  [$100,000]  contribution  it  would  be  pos-
sible  for  me  to  arrange  for  several  appointments  with  various
people  within  the  White  House....’’  Jd.  at  7583.  See  generally

39

tacts  with  precision,  the  risk  of  improper  influence,  con-
scious  or  unconscious,  is  hardly  open  to  question.

Section  608(c)  strikes  at  the  source  of  the  above  evils.
Putting  a  limit  upon  the  aggregate  size  of  each  candidate’s
campaign  expenditures  reduces  not  only  the  risk  of corrupt
practices  but  the  pressure  to  raise  huge  sums  from  con-
tributors  expecting  official  favor  as  their  reward.  Section
608(b)(1)  deals  even  more  directly  with  the  danger  of
undue  influence  by forbidding  financial  contributions  large
enough  to  be  corrupting.  The  other  ceilings  implement
these  basic  limitations.  Since  no  restriction  is  imposed
upon  individual  contributions  to  a  standing  political  com-
mittee  which  has  expenses  for  general  purposes,  such  a
committee  is  forbidden  to  contribute  more  than  $5,000  to
a  candidate  with  respect  to  any  single  election.  Section  608
(b)(2).  Because  experience  showed  that  the  proliferation
of  ad  hoc  political  committees  was  a  handy  device  for  cir-
cumventing  earlier  regulation  of  campaign  financing,”  the
privilege  to  give  a  candidate  up  to  $5,000  is  limited  to
committees  with  enough  permanency  and  scope  to  have
been  registered  for  six  months,  to  have  received  contribu-
tions  from  at  least  50  persons,  and  either  to  be  part  of  a
State  party  organization  or  to  have  made  contributions  to
five  or  more  candidates  for  federal  office.  Ibid.

The  ban  against  individual  contributions  in  excess  of
$1000,  standing  alone,  would  give  marked  adavantage  to
candidates  able  to  draw  upon  sizeable  personal  or  family
fortunes.  Section  608(a)(1)  counters  this  danger  by  lim-
iting  the  total  amount  a  candidate  and  his  immediate
family  may  ‘‘contribute’’  to  his  own  campaign  to  $50,000
in  the  ease  of  Presidential  or  Vice  Presidential  candidacy,
to  $35,000  in  a  Senatorial  campaign  and  to  $25,000  in
running  for Representative.

21  Finding  I, 9137,  II  J.A.  190.

The  ceilings  on  individual  contributions  to  a  candidate
and  on  candidate  expenditures  could  be  easily  evaded  if
wealthy  potential  contributors  were  allowed  to  make  un-
limited  direct  personal  expenditures  in  support  of  candi-
dates.  The  function  of  Section  608(e)(1)  is  to  close  this
gap  by  limiting  expenditures  by  a  person  or  group  ‘‘re-
lative  to  a  clearly  identified  candidate  .
.  advocating  the
election  or  defeat  of  such  candidate’’  to  an  additional
$1000.*?

.

The  magnitude  and  value  of  the  public  interests  served
by  these  interrelated  restrictions  show  that  the  justifica-
tion  is ‘‘paramount”’  or  ‘‘compelling’’  in  the  constitutional
sense.  Earlier  decisions,  while  not  precisely  in  point,  sus-
tain  this  conclusion.  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter
Carriers  Union,  413  U.S.  548  (1973),  held  that  the  pursuit
of  such  objectives  justifies  some  curtailment  of  freedom  of
association  even  when  personal  activity  rather  than  the
mere  transfer  of  money  is  restricted.  See  also  Broadrick
v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601  (1973);  United  Public  Workers
v.  Mitchell,  330 U.S.  75  (1947).  Burroughs  v.  United  States,
290  U.S.  534  (1934),  sustained  reporting  requirements  im-
posed  to  secure  the  integrity  of elections.  In  United  States
v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567  (1957),  the  dissenting  opinion  de-
livered  by  Mr.  Justice  Douglas,  in  which  Chief  Justice
Warren  and  Justice  Black  concurred,  urged  the  uncon-
stitutionality  of  a  statute  forbidding  political  expenditures
by labor  unions,  but  the  opinion  also  stated  that  Congress
has  power  to  impose  a  general  prohibition  upon  large
contributions  if  it  finds  that  they  create  risk  of  undue  in-
fluence.  352  U.S.  at  598,  n.  2.”

he Section  608(e)(1)  is  discussed  in  more  detail  at  pp.  54-63

Second,  the  ceilings  imposed  by  the  FECA  amendments
upon  political  contributions  and  expenditures  serve  the
purpose  of  preserving  public  trust  in  the  institutions  of
representative  self-government.  In  Civil  Service  Commis-
sion  v.  Letter  Carriers  Union,  413  U.S.  548,  565  (1973)
the Court  observed:

[I]t  is not  only  important  that  the  Government  and
its  employees  in  fact  avoid  practicing  political  justice,
but  it  is  also  critical  that  they  appear  to  the  public  to

the  failure  to  appreciate  the  full  integrity  of  both  candi-
dates  and  officials.  The  precise  extent  of  the  discrepancy
between  image  and  reality  is not  important  in  adjudicating
the  present  issue.  As  held  in  the  Letter  Carrters  case,
dispelling  both  the  danger  and  the  appearance  of corrosive
influence,  in order  to  preserve  confidence  in government  of,
by  and  for  the  people,  is  a  paramount  public  purpose.
“A third  compelling  public  purpose  of  the  interrelated
ceilings  is  to  open  up  the  political  process.”  An apparent
need  for  ever-heavier  spending  and  correspondingly  larger
contributions  would  discourage  the  candidacies  of men  and
women  who  lack  great  personal  wealth  or  access  to  large
contributors.  The  need  would  also  deter  individuals  who
might  come  forward  but  for  unwillingness  to  put  them-
selves  in  a  position  where  they  might  have  to  choose later
in  the  campaign  between  incurring  personal  obligation  to
a  questionable  contributor  whose  financial  help  was  neces-
sary  to  carry  the  day  and  letting  down  those  who  had
already  given  him  devoted  personal  assistance.  The  con-
stitutional  interest  in  removing  financial  barriers  to  can-
didacy  has  already  been  eloquently  stated  by  the  Court.

47

paign  contributors  sought  entree  under  powerful  auspices
to  executive  departments  and  administrative  agencies.”
The  Attorney  General  treats  these  interests  as  if  Con-
gress  were  attempting  to  reduce  the  speech  of  the  wealthy
in  order  to  make  more  room  for  the  speech  of  those  with
lesser  means  (Br.  pp.  52-54).  We  make  no  such  argument,
nor  does  the  constitutionality  of  the  statute  depend  upon
any  such  treacherous  ground.  It  is  not  for  Congress  to
judge  the  relative  values  of  different  forms  of  speech  or
the  relative  claims  of  different  speakers.  The  dangers  to
which  we  refer  are  the  pressure  upon  candidates  to  accede
to  the  wishes  of  wealthy  potential  contributors  in  order  to
obtain  their  money,  not  because  of  their  words,  and  also
the  pressure  to  arrange  meetings  with  government  officials
for  those  who  have  given  money  in  order  to  repay  the
political  debt.

In  our  view,  therefore,  the  ‘‘compelling’’  nature  and
‘‘paramount’’  value  of the  interests  served  by the  package
of  ceilings  on  political  contributions  and  expenditures  are
beyond  serious  doubt.

Appellants’  contention  that  the  ceilings  upon  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  have  no  ‘‘compelling’’  justification
is primarily  an  effort  to  reargue  the  facts.  Their  brief  (pp.
55-75)  seeks  to  minimize  the  incidents  brought  to  the  at-
tention  of  Congress,  denies  the  danger  of  serious  corrup-
tion,  and  attributes  the  enactment  of  the  legislation  to

37**(T)n  return  for  that  [$100,000]  contribution  it  would  be
possible  for  me  to  arrange  for  several  appointments  with  various
people  within  the  White  House...  .’’  Testimony  of  Herbert  Kalm-
bach,  Final  Report  of  the  Senate  Select  Comm.  on  Presidential
Campaign  Activities,  S.  Rep.  No.  981,  93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  593
(1974);  ‘‘I  think  all  we  were  attempting  to  do  was  to  assure
ourselves  a  forum  to  be  heard....  [We  felt  we  needed  some-
thing  that  would  be  sort  of  a  calling  card,  something  that  would
get  us  in  the  door  and  make  our  point  of  view  heard.”’  Testimony
of  Orin  Atkins,  Chairman  of  the  Board  of  Ashland  Oil,  Hearings
Before  the  Senate  Select  Comm.  on  Presidential  Campaign  Activi-
ties,  93d  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  5442  (1973).

hysteria  induced  by  the  Watergate  investigations.
The
argument  misreads  events.  Congress  has  proceeded  with
extraordinary  care  and  deliberation.  The  history  of  con-
gressional  concern  through  the  1950’s
is  recounted  in
United  States  v.  UAW,  352  U.S.  567,  570-84  (1957)  and
in  an  appendix  to the  opinion  of the  court  of appeals  (J.S.
App.  1589-93).  Between  1966  and  1975  the entire  subject  was
considered  and  reconsidered  with  a  high  level  of  public
attention.  During  this  period  three  major  laws  were  enacted
and  later  revised.  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund
Act,  P.L,  89-908,  80  Stat.  1587  (1966);  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  of  1971,  P.L.  92-225,  86  Stat.  3  (1972);
Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code,  P.L.  92-178,  85
Stat.  562  (1971);  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amend-
ments  of  1974,  P.L.  93-443,  88  Stat.  1263  (1975).  There
were  elaborate  hearings.**  When  Congress  revisited  the
entire  subject  in  1973  and  1974  after  observing  the  opera-
tion  of  earlier  laws  in  the  1972  elections,  there  were  three
careful  Senate  reports,  two  reports  by  the  Committee  on
Rules  and  Administration  (S.  Reps.  93-310,  93-689)  and
one  by the  Committee  on  Commerce  (S.  Rep.  93-170).  An
elaborate  report  was  also  filed  by the  House  Committee  on
Administration

(H.R.  93-1239).

49

debate  in  both  chambers  and  an  elaborate  Conference  Re-
port  (S.  Rep.  93-1237).  The  present  law  represents  the
considered  findings  and  deliberate  judgment  of  the  Con-
gress.

.

Appellants’  effort  to  reargue  the  underlying  questions
of  fact  and  redebate  the  wisdom  or  folly  of  the  measure
misconceives  the  function  of  the  Judiciary.  The  preferred
place  of  First  Amendment  freedoms  in  the  hierarchy  of
constitutional  values  requires  the  Court  to  examine  the
character  of the  purposes  assigned  to  any  legislation  which
may  operate  in  a  way  that  restricts  opportunities  for
gaining  the  widest  benefit  from  freedom  of  speech  and
association,  so  that  it  may  determine  whether  the  pur-
poses  are  ‘“‘paramount’’  or  ‘‘compelling.’’  See  pp.  24-31
above.  In  the  instant  case,  for  example,  it  is for  the  Court
to  judge  whether  securing  the  integrity  of  government  and
public  confidence  therein  is  a  sufficiently  high  objective
to  justify  some  interference  with  a  candidate's  enjoyment
of  opportunities  for  gaining  the  widest  benefit  from  the
exercise  of  freedom  of  speech.  Similarly,  it  is  a  function
of  the  Court  to  examine  for  itself  the  fit  between the  pro-
visions  of  the  statute  and  the  assigned  justifications.  See
pp.  50-54  below.  If  the  commands  which  may  result  in
curtailment  of  opportunities  for  expression  or  association
are  of  a  character  demonstrably  broader  than required  by
the  assigned  justification,  then  the  Court  will  judge  the  law

stitutional.

<a an requirements  are  satisfied,  the  judicial  role
is  limited.  Judges  do  not  sit  as  legislators  to  inquire  into
social,  economic  or  political  conditions  in  order  to resolve
questions  of  fact,  to  make  judgments  of  proximity  and
degree  upon  the  seriousness  of  the  threat  to  the  public
welfare  or  the  efficacy  of  the  remedy,  and  then  to decide
whether,  as  legislators,  they  would  vote for  or  against  the
measure.  Quite  possibly,  judicial  serutiny  of the  facts  must

in  which  the  Court  has  sifted

be  somewhat  closer  in  cases  involving  First  Amendment
freedoms  than  under  the  Commerce  Clause”  or  Fifth  and
Fourteenth  Amendments,”  in  order  to  guard  against
circumvention  of  First  Amendment  guarantees,  but  we
know  of  no  case
a
variety  of  data  for  the  purpose  of  deciding  whether  it
would  reach  the  same  conclusion  as  Congress  upon  the
quantitative  magnitude  or  urgency  of  an  acknowledged
danger,  or  upon  the  wisdom  of  the  congressional  remedy.
The  limit  of  the  inquiry  should  be  whether  the  judgments
attributable  to  Congress  rest  upon  an  honest  and  solid
foundation,  rather  than  subterfuge  or  mere  speculation.
The  only  direct  precedents  indicate  that  the  Court  will
not  review  congressional  findings  of  fact  even  in  cases
under  the  First  Amendment.  Communist  Party  v.  Sub-
versive  Activities  Control  Board,  367  U.S.  1, 94-95,  169-70
(1961).  See  also,  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  271,
285  (1967)  (Brennan,  J.,  concurring;  White  and  Harlan,
JJ., dissenting).

In  the  present  case,  in  our  judgment,  the  congressional
findings  of  fact  and  appraisal  of  the  degree  of  danger  to
compelling  public  interests  are  entirely  correct.  Even  if
that  conclusion  be  debatable,  the  congressional  assessments
surely  rest,  as  shown  above,  upon  a  solid  foundation  of

3°In  upholding  congressional  enactments  under  the  Commerce
Clause  and  Section  5 of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  the  Court  has
consistently  held  that  it  is  for  Congress  to  determine  the  facts
and  weigh  the  various  considerations,  subject  only  to  a  requirement
of  rationality.  Heart  of  Atlanta  Motel,  Inc.  v.  United  States,  379
U.S.  241,  258,  261-62  (1964)  ; Katzenbach  vy.  McClung,  379  U.S.  294,
303-04  (1964);  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641,  653,  656
(1966)  ; Oregon  v.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  133,  248  (1970)  (opinions
of  the  Court  and  of  Justices  Brennan,  White  and  Marshall  dis-
senting).
*°  The  same  rule  applies  under  the  Due  Process  Clauses.  Munn
v.  Illinois,  94  U.S.  113,  125  (1876)  ; MeLean  v.  Arkansas,  211  U.S.
539,  550-51  (1909).  See  also  McGowan  v.  Maryland,  366  U.S.  420
4°6  (1961).

50

51

testimony  and  experience,  to  which  both  Senate  and  House
gave  long  and  careful  consideration.

3.  The  Compelling  Purposes  of  the  Ceilings  upon
Campaign  Contributions  and  Expeditures  Cannot
Be  Achieved  by  Less  Restrictive  Measures.

The  FECA  and  FECA  Amendments  of  1974  strike  di-
rectly  at  the  threats  of  corrupting  financial  influence  upon
the  integrity  of  representative  government.  The  method  is
no  broader  than  the  dangers  to  be  met,  By  putting  a  limit
upon  each  candidate’s  campaign  expenditures,  Section
608(c)  stops  the  rising  spiral  of  campaign  costs  and  limits
the  pressure  to  raise  more  and  more  money  from  every
possible  source.  Section  608(b) (1)  deals  more  directly  with
the  danger  of  improper  financial  influence  by  forbidding
contributions  large  enough  to  be  corrupting.  The  ceiling  on
over-all  expenditures,  standing  alone,  would  not  be  sui-
ficient  to  eliminate  the  risks  of  undue  influence  flowing
from  gratitude  to  large  contributors,  The  ceilings  upon
individual  gifts  would  be  neither  workable  nor  credible
in  political  campaigns  characterized  by the  massive  spend-
ing  invited  by  the  absence  of  a  limit  on  over-all  expendi-
tures.  The  subsidiary  ceilings  supplement  the  principal
limits.  Appellants  suggest  no  adequate  method  of  achiev-
ing  the  statutory  purposes  which  would  have  less  practical
impact  upon  opportunities  to  get  the  maximum  benefit
from  freedom  of  speech.”

41  We  are  persuaded  that  substituting  government  appropria-
tions  for  private  financing  of  Congressional  campaigns  would  serve
the  public  interest  still  better  than  the  present  law  but  such  a
measure  would  necessarily  contain  restrictions  very  similar  to  those
of  which  plaintiffs  complain.  Amendment  406  to  8.372,  93d  Cong.,

Bribery  laws  are  obviously  inadequate  to  relieve  candi-
dates  of  the  pressure  to  raise  huge  campaign  funds  from
every  possible  source,  including  contributors  expecting
their  money  to  influence  legislative  and  executive  deci-
sions.  The  wealthy  financial  contributor  and  the  recipient
can  be  punished  for  bribery  only  if  the  one  pays  and  the
other receives  the  contribution  in  return  for  a  promise  of
specific  official  action.  The  dangers  to  which  the  present
legislation  is  addressed  are  more  subtle.  The  donor  may
seek  no  commitment  to  a  particular  course  of  action  or
even  to  some  unspecified  favor.  The  successful  candidate
may  nevertheless  come  under  heavy  pressure  from  his
sense  of  political  indebtedness  plu:  his  realization  that  he
will  need  financial  aid  again  in  the  next  election.  The  suc-
cessful  candidate  may  be  unwilling  or  unable  to  do  more
than  pass  word  of  the  econtributor’s  political  importance
along  to  other  officials.  At  that  point,  the  interplay  of

huge  sums  of  money  and  later  the  political  pressure  upon

A
of  corrupting  individuals,  politics  and  government.
public  office  is still  a  public  trust.  In  government  even  more
than  in  private  life,  there  is  need  to  protect  the  trustees
and  their  beneficiaries  not  merely  from  the  temptations
of  outright  bribery  but  also  from  the  complex  pressures
of conflicts  of interest.

The  suggestion  that  Congress  should  have  listed  only
specified  categories  of  contributions  which  create  danger
of  improper  influence  is  unworkable.  The  problem  of  ex-
haustive  definition  could  not  be  solved  without  requiring
investigations  and  proof  so  elaborate  as  to  defeat  en-
forcement.  Neither  candidate  nor  contributor  can  always
tell  what  demands  will  be  made  for  evidence  of  gratitude.
Any  workable  list  of  ‘‘improper’’  contributions  would
necessarily  refer  to  a  few  extrinsic  proofs  of  impropriety,
thus  branding  as  morally  reprehensible,  and  therefore  un-
lawful,  transactions  which  would  be  seen  as  wholly  inno-
Such  draftsmanship
cent  if  all  the  facts  were  known.
carries  its  own  dangers  of  discrimination  and  unfairness
in  relation  to  political  activity.  Congress  could  properly
conclude  that  there  was  less  danger  of  unfairness  in  the
simple  equality  of  putting  all  contributors  and  all  eandi-
dates  for  the  same  office  under  one  ceiling.

Appellants’  criticisms  of  the  exact  dollar  figures  set  by
Congress  ask  the  Court  to  substitute  its  judgment  for  that
of  Congress  upon  sundry  questions  of  degree.  That  is
not  a  judicial  function,  unless  the  limit  is  set  so  low  as  to
be  beyond  solid  defense.  The  ceilings  on  over-all  expendi-
tures  are  within  the  range  supported  by  recent  expe-
rience.  In  fixing  each  dollar  limit,  effectiveness  in  pro-
moting  the  congressional  purposes  had  to  be  balanced
against  the  risk  of  curtailing  political  debate.  In  some

42  See  pp.  31-34  above.

instances,  adjustments  had  to  be  made  in  the  interests  of
fairness  and  equality;  for  example,  in  setting  a  limit  upon
expenditures  by  a  candidate  and  his  immediate  family.
The  judicial  inquiry  into  whether  the  public  purposes  of  a
statute  regulating  conduct  could  be  adequately  served  by
some  measure  having  less  tendency  to  limit  the  enjoyment
of First  Amendment  freedoms  goes  only  to the  character  of
the  legislation  and  its  scope.  Where,  as  here,  the  public
needs  are  so  compelling  as  to justify  uniform,  all-embracing
restrictions  upon  the  donation  and  use  of  financial  power
in  political  campaigns,  it  is  for  Congress  alone  to  deter-
mine,  within  reasonable  limits,  the  exact  point  at  which
the  balance  should  be  struck.  See  pp.  48-49  above.

Accordingly,  we  submit  that  any  tendency  of the  ceilings
upon  campaign  contributions  and  expenditures  to  limit  the
enjoyment  of  opportunities  for  freedom  of  speech  and
association  is  amply  justified  by  the  ‘‘compelling’’  and
“‘paramount’’  public  interests  which  the  ceilings  serve  and
which  cannot  be  satisfied  by less  restrictive  measures.

Appellants  also  complain  (Br.  pp.  93-94)  that  the  ceilings
should  be  more  particular.  The  limit  upon  individual  con-
tributions,  for  example,  is  said  to  be  unconstitutional  be-
cause  it  is  uniform,  without  variation  according  to  the
office  which  the  candidate  is  seeking,  the  costs
of  the
particular  campaign,  the  identity  of  each  donor,  the  situa-
tion  of  each  recipient,  ete.  Similarly,  appellants  attack  the
fixing  of  a  uniform  formula  for  determining  the  ceiling
on  expenditures  in  all  elections  for  Senator,  and  of  a  uni-
form  ceiling  in  all  elections  for  Representative  in  States
having  more  than  one  Representative,  regardless  of  the
character  of  the  particnlar  district.  The  short  answer  is
that  the  Constitution  does  not  require  the  impossible,  nor
does  it mandate  the  substitution  of  sophisticated  complex-

4.  The  Limitation  Imposed  by  Section  608(e)  upon
Independent  Campargn  Expenditures  Not  Author.
ized  by  a  Candidate  Is  Constitutional  as Enacted
and  as  Applied  to  Any  Situation  Ripe  for  Ad-
judication.

When  the  balance  between  the  public  needs  served  by  a
regulation  of  conduct  and  its  restrictive  effects  upon  prac-
tical  opportunities  for  enjoyment  of  freedom  of  speech  is
otherwise  close,  it  may  be  important  to  examine  the
proximity  of  the  relation  between  the  conduct  regulated
—  here  the use  of  large  sums  of  money  —  and  the  truly
personal  enjoyment  of  liberty.

Most  of the  restrictions  upon  financial  contributions  and
expenditures  will  have  little  effect  upon  anyone’s  personal
speech  or  political  activity.  The  effect  of the  an ceiling
upon  the  candidate’s  speech,  or  his  spokesmen  s,  is  that
of  any  partial  restriction  upon  the  ability  of a  would-be
speaker  to  gather  a  vast  pool  of  money  with  which  to

1K

.

buy  greater  access  to  channels  of  mass  communication.
The  effect  upon  the  potential  large  contributor’s  personal
enjoyment  of  freedom  of  expression  is  even  more  remote.
The  co.tributor  seldom  determines  the  use  to  which  his
money  is  put,  Assuming  the  money  to  be  pooled  with  all
other  receipts,  some  will  go  to  pay  rent,  some  to  conduct
polls,  some  to  pay  travel  expenses  or  salaries,  and  some
to  cover  the  cost  of  public  communications.  The  contribu-
tor  seldom  chooses  the  medium  of  expression  or  the
character  of the  message.  The  words,  with  rare  exceptions,
are  not  his  words;  the  ideas  are  not  his  thoughts.  These
characteristics  of  large  financial  contributions  further
strengthen  our  submission  that  the  statutory  restrictions
upon  size  do  not  violate  the  First  Amendment.

In  some  of  its  possible  applications  Section  608(e)  pre-
sents  a  slightly  different  problem.  Section  608(e)  forbids
any  person  to  expend  more  than  $1,000  in  any  one  election
to  support  a  candidate,  or  to  defeat  his  opponent,  even
without  consulting  the  candidate.  The  language  is  tightly
drawn  so  as  not  to  discourage  political  discussion  oriented
towards  public  issues.  The  restriction  applies  only  to  pub-
lications  ‘‘relative  to  a  clearly  identified  candidate’’  and
only  to  those  ‘‘advocating  the  election  or  defeat’’  of  that
particular  person.  By  the  definition  of  ‘‘expenditure”’  in
Section  591(f)  Congress  made  certain  that  Section  608 (e)
would  not  interfere  with  any  individual’s  personal  actiy-
ities  in  speaking,  writing  or  canvassing  for  a  candidate,
or  in  performing  other  services,  without  using  money  to
expand  the  reach  of his  words.  Yet  even  with  these  limita-
tions,  Section  608(e)  covers  forms  of  conduct  which  are
diverse  when  measured  by  their  relation  to  personal  en-
joyment  of  freedom  of  expression.  At  one  end  of  its
range,  Section  608(e)  prohibits  a  wealthy  supporter  from
spending  more  than  $1,000  for  such  assistance  as  hiring
an  advertising  agency  to  promote  an  ‘‘independent’’  eam-

06

at

paign  for  a  candidate’s  benefit,  i.e.,  a campaign  for  which
the  candidate’s  authority  was  not  obtained  and  which,
therefore,  would  not  count  against  his  expenditure  ceiling
under  Section  608(e)(2)(B).  In  their  broadest  reaches,
the  words  of  Section  608(e)  appear  also  to  bar  an  outlay
of  more  than  $1,000  to  broadcast  one’s  own  speech  in
support  of  a  candidate,  or  of  more  than  $1,500  for  travel
expenses  incurred  in making  speeches.“*  The  former  restric-
tion  has  no  more  affect  upon  personal  enjoyment  of  free-
dom  of  speech  than  the  ceiling  upon  contributions.  The
latter  restriction  upon  the  individual’s  use  of money  would
proximately  affect  his  personal  enjoyment  of  opportunities
to  publish  his  own  thoughts.

The  court  of  appeals,  after  giving  Section

608(e)  a
narrow  interpretation,  sustained  it  as  ‘‘a  loophole  closing
(J.S.  App.  1530)  We  urge  that  the  ruling
provision.’”’
is  correct  in  terms  of  the  facial  constitutionality  of  Sec-
tion  608(e)  and  as  it  is  applicable  to  any  situation  pre-
sented  in  the  instant  case.  We  believe  that  even  the  hypo-
thetical  applications  would  be  constitutional  but  the  ques-
tion,  being  closer,  should  be  reserved  for  the  future.

The  court  of  appeals  rightly  discerned  the  function  of
Section  608(e).  Although  the  ceilings  upon  contributions
and  aggregate  expenditures  would  be  useful  by  them-
selves,  Congress  realized  that  their  efficacy  would  be  re-
duced  without  some  limitation  upon  a  variety  of  inde-
pendent  expenditures.  Experience  under  earlier  laws  reg-
ulating  the  financing  of  political  campaigns  had  con-
vincingly  demonstrated  the  danger  of  circumvention,
evasion  and  outright  violation.®  The  $1,000  ceiling  upon
individual  contributions  and  the  over-all  ceiling  upon  ex-

44 Section  591(e)  exempts  from  the  definition  of  expenditure
the  first  $500  of  an  individual’s  unreimbursed  travel  expenses.
In  addition,  an  individual  is  allowed  up  to  $1,000  of  independent
expenditures  on  behalf  of  a  candidate.

ts See  J.S.  App.  1512;  Findings  I, 19139,  139-43,  II  J.A.  190-92.

penditures  by  or  on  behalf  of  a  candidate  could  easily  be
circumvented,  if  left  to  stand  alone,  by  massive  expendi-
tures  for  campaign  advertising  and  other  forms  of  mass
communication  made  in  support  of  a  candidate  without
any  provable  communication  between  the  supporter  or
group  of  supporters  and  the  candidate  or
his  agents.
A  wealthy,  potential  contributor,  for  example,  instead  of
giving  $100,000  to  a  candidate  for  his  campaign,  might
spend  the  sum  for  newspaper  or  billboard  advertising,
the  broadcast  of  radio  and  television  ‘‘spots,’’  the  distri-
bution  of  campaign  literature,  ete.
If  the  expenditure
were  made  without  some  kind  of  advance  communication
between  the  candidate  and  his  benefactor,  its  effectiveness
might  be  reduced  because  of  the  lack  of  coordination  with
other  outlays,  but  no  great  amount  of  imagination  is
required  to  reduce  the  difficulty.  The  wealthy  supporter
might  copy  newspaper  advertisements  used  earlier  in  the
campaign  or  in  other  cities.  He  might  buy  television  fime
to  rebroadcast  earlier  ‘‘spots’’  or  film  clips.  The  benefac-
tor  might  even  let  the  candidate’s  manager  know  early  in
the  campaign  that  he  would  spend  his  $100,000  on  ad-
vertising  in  a  particular  city,  handled  by a  named  agency
with  political  experience,  yet  he  could  stiil  avoid  obtaining
the  authority  which  would  make  the  expenditure  attrib-
utable  to  the  candidate  under  Section  608(c)(2)(B)  by
loudly  asserting  that  he  would  pursue  this  course  regard-
less  of  whether  the  candidate  approved  or  objected  to  his
independent  undertaking.

Numerous  independent  expenditures  of  this  character
would  make  mockery  of  the  effort  to  limit  campaign
spending.  They  would  create  many  of  the  dangers  of  abuse
inherent  in  vast  expenditures  on  behalf  of  candidates  and
in  large  financial  contributions.  Members  of  the  public
might  well  doubt  the  bona  fides  of  claims  of  independence.
Opposing  candidates  would  feel  heavy  pressure  to  match

08

59

the  financial  outlay  lest  they  lose  the  election.  This  class
of  independent  expenditures,  moreover,  involves  no  more
personal  exercise  of  freedom  of  expression  than  contribu-
tions  for  the  same  purposes.  Congress  was  well  aware  of
the  danger  and  repeatedly  emphasized  the  need  to  close  an
obvious  loophole.  S.  Rep.  93-170,  93d  Cong.,  1st  Sess.  71
(1973) ; H.R.  Rep.  93-1239,  pp.  6-7  (1974);  S.  Rep.  93-310,
pp.  12-13  (1973);  S.  Rep.  93-1237,  pp.  56-57  (1974).

Given  the  need  for  limiting  some  kinds  of  ostensibly
independent  expenditures  and  the  desire  not  to  interfere
with  truly  personal  activities  (as  distinguished  from  finan-
cial  assistance),  the  problem  becomes  one  of drawing  lines.
Two  solutions  present  themselves:  (i)  to  distinguish  be-
tween  independent  expenditures  which  serve  esscntially  the
same  campaign  functions  as  contributions,  on  the  one  hand,
and,  on  the  other  hand,  independent  expenditures  which  are
incidental  to  the  individual’s  personal  exercise  of  freedom
of  expression;  or  (ii)  to  draw  the  line  between  truly  per-
sonal  activity,  on  one  side,  and  spending  money,  on  the
other  (except  for  incidental  expenses  so  small  as  to  pose
no  threat  to  the  basic  limitations).

Congress,  with  full  concern  for  the  questions  of personal
liberty,*®  chose  the  second  course.  The  definition  of  ‘‘ex-

‘6  ‘*  Admittedly,  expenditures  made  directly  by  an  individual  to
urge  support  of  a  candidate  pose  First  Amendment  issues  more
vividly  than  do  financial  contributions  to  a  campaign  fund.  Never-
theless,  to  prohibit  a  $60,000  direct  contribution  to  be  used  for  a
TV  spot  commercial  but  then  to  permit  the  would-be  contributor
to  purchase  the  time  himself,  and  place  a  commercial  endorsing
the  candidate,  would  exalt  constitutional  form  over  substance.
Your  Committee  does  not  believe  the  First  Amendment  requires
If  Congress  may,  consistently  with
such  a  wooden  construction.
the  First  Amendment,  limit  contributions  to  preserve  the  integrity
of  the  electoral  process,  then  it  also  can  constitutionally  limit
independent  expenditures  in  order  to  make  the  contribution  limits
effective.’’
S.  Rep.  93-689,  p.  __  (1974);  ‘‘The  committee  is
mindful  that  an  absolute  proscription  of  independent  campaign-
related  spending  may  well  offend  the  guarantees  of  the  First
Amendment  and  would  be  poor  public  policy  in  inhibiting  the

penditure’’  in Section  591(f)  excludes  all  personal  political
activities  such  as  speaking,  writing  or  canvassing  for  a
candidate,  or  performing  other  services.  In  order  to  facili-
tate  truly  personal  activity  Congress  provided  that  the
payment  of  incidental  expenses  for  food  or  travel  should
be  exempt  from  Section  608(e),  up  to  $500.  But,  with  this
exception,  Section  608(e)  reaches  all  outlays  sufficiently
tied  to a  specified  candidate  and  specifically  aimed  at  bring-
ing about  his  election.

The  congressional  choice  is sustained  by four  persuasive

considerations:

First,  it  is  extremely  difficult  to  draw  a  fair  and  clear-
cut  line  between  the  expenditure  of  money  in  connection
with  truly  personal  political  activity  and  the  mere  purchase
of  services  or  use  of  a  medium  of  mass  communication.
Buying  a  full  page  of  advertising  space  in  The  New  York
Times  to  publish  a  letter  appealing  for  the  election  of
a  named  candidate  may  fall  on  the  ‘‘personal”’  side  of  the
line.  Buying  television  spots  to  rebroadcast  film  clips  made
by the  candidate  is pretty  clearly  the  mere  expenditure  of
money.  But  the  full-page  advertisement  might  be  one  previ-
ously  used  by  the  candidate  and  slightly  revised  by  the
purchaser  of  advertising  space,  or  it  might  be  drafted  by
the  purchaser  and  thoroughly  rewritten  by  a  professional
writer  of  advertising  copy.  The  page  might  be  used  to
display  a  large  picture  of  the  candidate  together  with  a
few  slogans  chosen  by his  benefactor  from  the  candidate’s

free  expression  of  views  on  vital  issues.  However,  it  is  believed
that  a  reasonable  limitation  on  such  spending,  in  the  context  of
an  overall  effort  to  maintain  the  integrity  of  the  electoral  process
is  feasible  and  Constitutionally  permissible...  .  The  committee  is
convinced  that  this approach  makes  possible  the  adequate  presen-
tation  of  candidate-related  views  by  independent  groups  and  at
the same  time  safeguards  the  integrity  of  the  candidate  spending
limits.  In this  way,  the  bill  effectively  reconciles  the  interests  of
Congress  in  promoting  both  free  speech  and  the  effective  regulation
of  Federal  election  campaigns.’’  H.R.  Rep.  93-1239,  p.  6  (1974).

4

60

61

own  publicity.  Joint  expenditures  by  a  few  individuals
would  raise  other  borderline  questions.“7  The  mixture  of
‘money  purchase’’  and  ‘‘ personal  involvement’’  is suscep-
tible  of  infinxe  variation.  So  far  as  we  can  discern,  if
Congress  had  chosen  this  alternative,  the  draftsman  could
have  done  no  more  than  articulate  the  abstract  principle
and  leave  its  precise  application  to  case-by-case  decision.
Second,  the  danger  that  Section  608(e)  will  substan-
tially  restrict  personal  political  activities  seems  largely,
if  not  wholly,  hypothetical.  No  appellant  alleges  that  he
desires  to  spend  more  than  $1,500  to  promote  in  behalf  of
a  candidate  activities  of  the  kind  we  characterize  as  ‘‘per-
sonal.’?  None  shows,  in  concrete  instance,  that  he  will  lose
the benefit  of such  activity  and expenditure  if Section  608(e)
is enforced.  It is difficult,  moreover,  to  recall  any  significant
number  of  individuals  other  than  office-holders  or  prospec-
tive  political  candidates  who  have  ever  expended  more  than
£1,500  of  their  personal  funds  in  order  to  speak  for  or
against  a  clearly  identified  candidate  without  the  invitation
or  other  authorization  which  would  take  the  expenditure
out  of  Section  608(e)  by making  it chargeable  against  the
candidate’s  expenditure  ceiling.“*  Considerably  more  sup-

<7  It  is  not  clear  whether  a  number  of  individuals  who  combine
to  purchase  broadcasting  time  or  a  newspaper  advertisement  cost-
ing  more  than  $1,000  on  a  single  occasion  are  subject  to  Section
608(e).  Section  608(e)  prohibits  any  ‘‘person’’  from  making  any
expenditures  in  excess  of $1,000.  Section  591(g)  defines  ‘‘person’”’
to  inelude  any  ‘‘commission,  association,  corporation  or  any  other
It  would  seem  entirely  prob-
organization  or  group  of  persons.’’
able  to  interpret  the  words  ‘‘group  of  persons’’  under  these
circumstances  to  mean  only  a  group  having  some  more  lasting
existence  than  combining  resources  for  a  single  expenditure.  The
Federal  Election  Committee  has  had  no  occasion  to  rule  upon  this
question,  but  in  interpreting  the  discovery  sections,  it  has  tenta-
tively  ruled  that  coming  together  for  the  purpose  of  purchasing  a
single  advertisement  does  not  constitute  a  political  committee.
40  Fed.  Reg.  44701  (Sept.  29,  1975).

48  An  individual’s  expenditures  made  independently  of  a  candi-
date  are  not  charged  against  the  candidate's  expenditure  limit.
However,  under  Section  608(c)(2)(B),  an  expenditure  made  on

porters  buy  advertising  space,  but  here  the  element  of
personal  activity  is  inconsequential.

Third,  past and  present  office-holders  would  almost  surely
form  a  very  large  proportion  of  the  small  number  of  indi-
viduals  who  might  spend  more  than  $1,000  of  their  own
money  making  uninvited  speeches  in  support  of  another
candidate.  Such  speeches  increase  the  public  exposure  of
the  office-holder  or  prospective  future  candidate  who  makes
them.  The  chief  consequence  of  shifting  the  line  to  allow
independent  expenditures  in aid  of truly  personal  activities,
therefore,  would  be to  increase  the  advantage  of candidates
who  know  public  office-holders  with  large  personal  means,
and  of such  office-holders  in gaining  exposure  for  their  own
political  purposes.

Fourth,  even  the  limit  on  spending  more  than  $1,000
to  propagate  one’s  own  speech  or  writing  in  support  of  a
clearly  identified  candidate  is somewhat  less  of an  invasion
of  personal  liberty  than  it  initially  seems.  The  liberty  is
not  one  that  more  than  a  very  small  fraction  of  the  people
can  afford  to  exercise.  There  is no  restriction,  as  we  have
said,  upon  the  personal  activities  not  requiring  large  sums
of money,  which  are  open  to all.  There  is also  no  restriction
upon  expenditures  to  promote  the  widest  discussion  of
particular  political  causes  without  specifically  tying  the
advocacy  to  a  clearly  identified  candidate.

Under  these  circumstances,  it  would  seem  quite  wrong
to  attempt  to  tear  the  few  most  extreme  imaginable  appli-
cations  of Section  608(e)  out  of the  continuum  of spending
in  order  to  ask  whether  the  isolated  instances  would  carry
by  themselves  such  threat  to  the  public  welfare  as  to
justify  thus  restricting  the  ability  to  buy  facilities  for

behalf  of a  candidate  with  the  cai.didate’s  or  his
nt’s  authoriza-
tion  is  charged against  the  candidate's  ceiling.  If a  candidate

d_  tak

G2

63

gaining  the  maximum  benefit  from  freedom  of  speech.
The  contribution  which  the  extreme  instances  make  to  the
workability  of  the  entire  plan  and  the  fairness  of  drawing
the  line  at  the  point  chosen  are  major  additional  elements
of justification.  When  a  line  has  to  be  drawn  and  is drawn
at  the  point  which  Congress  could  well  judge  to  be  clearer
and  fairer  than  any  other,  the  critical  question  is  whether
the  purposes  served  by the  prohibition  of  the  whole  class
of  activities  justify  the  resulting  diminution  of  practical
opportunities  for  the  maximum  enjoyment  of  freedom  of
speech.  In  this  case,  the  overall  justification  is  more  than
adequate,  for  the  reasons  stated  at  pp.  34-50  above.  Con-
sequently,  if  the  question  is  here,  Section  608(e)  should
be  held  constitutional  in  its  farthest  conceivable  reaches.
But  that  question  need  not,  and  therefore  should  not,
be  decided  in  the  present  case.  Neither  the  pleadings  nor
the  proof  names  any  person  who  wishes  to  make  such  out-
lays,  much  less  describes  the  circumstances  and  mixture
of  financial  expenditure  and  personal  activity  involved.
Appellant  Mott  alleges  that  he  ‘‘has  made  expenditures  on
behalf  of  clearly  identified  candidates  but  independent  of
snch  candidates  in  excess  of  $4,000  and  intends  to  do  so
The  allegation  gives  no
in  the  future.”’
information  about  the  character  of  his  independent  activi-
ties.  His  reports  filed  under  the  earlier  law  indicate  that
he  expended  in  the  neighborhood  of  $17,000  in  publicizing
his  views  about  clearly  identified  candidates,  all  of  which
went  to  an  advertising  agency.  There  is  no  indication
of  how  the  copy  was  prepared.  Mr.  Mott’s  affidavit  gives
no  more  concrete  information  about  his  activities  or  inten-
tions.  At  this  stage,  therefore,  the  problem  raised  by some
possible  applications  of  Section  608(e)  is  hypothetical;
the  most  difficult  of the  imaginable  constitutional  questions
may  never  arise.

(I  J.A.  39).

Such  questions  do  not  have  to  be  decided  hypothetically

because  even  a  decision  against  constitutionality  in  these
instances  would  not  render  Section  608(e)  unconstitutional
on  its  face.  Section  608(e)  applies  to  a  broad  range  of
expenditures  involving  neither  speech  nor  other  personal
political  activities  on  the  part  of  the  spender.  In  most  of
the  applications  now  foreseeable  the  element  of  personal
speech  or  association  is  very  small.  Throughout  most  of
its  range,  therefore,  Section  608(e)  is  constitutional  for
the  reasons  stated  in  discussing  the  ceilings  on  individual
contributions  and  overall  expenditures  (pp.  22-54  above).
Under  these  circumstances  the  Court  should  reject  the
facial  attack  upon  grounds  of  overbreadth  and  avoid  the
unnecessary  decision  of  hypothetical  questions.  For  the
case  is one  where  conduct,  i.e.,  spending  money,  is  involved
and  the  overbreadth,  if  any,  is  insubstantial  judged  in
relation  to  the  statute’s  plainly  legitimate  sweep.  Broad-
rick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  615  (1973);  California  v.
LaRue,  409  U.S.  109,  116  (1972).

C.  The  Limitations  Upon  Financial  Contributions  And
Expenditures  Are  Not  Inherently  Discriminatory  In
A  Constitutional  Sense.

The  limitations  imposed  by  Section  608(c)  of  18  U.S.C.
upon  aggregate  campaign  expenditures  by or  on  behalf  of
candidates  for  federal  office  apply  alike  to  each  and  every
candidate  for  the  same  office.  Section  608(c)  draws  no
distinctions  between  persons,  political  parties,  social  or
economic  organizations,  or  other  groups.  It does  not  favor
or  disfavor  any  political  opinion  or  ideology.  In  terms,
the  provisions  imposing  the  ceilings  are  as  evenhanded  as
the  words  can  be.

Similarly,  the  limitations  imposed  by  Sections  608(a)
and  (e)  upon  individual  contributions  and  expenditures
apply  equally  to  each  and  every  candidate  for  the  same
office.  They  too  make  no  invidious  distinctions,  and  prefer

64

65

no  economic  or  political  opinion  or  group.  A few  special
categories,  such  as  expenditures  from  a  candidate’s  imme-
diate  family  resources  (Section  608(a)(1)),  were required
by  considerations  of  fairness  or  practicality.  Lines  such
as  those  in  Section  608(e)  had  to  be  drawn  between  the
prohibited  and  the  permitted  even  though  the  kinds  of
financial  practices  leading  to  dangerous  abuses  sometimes
shade  by very  gradual  degrees  into  desirable participation
in  self-government.*®  None  of  the  classifications  is drawn
in  terms  of  political  ideology  or  affiliation.  None  involves
hostile  or  invidious  discrimination.

Appellants  nevertheless  charge  many  varieties  of  uncon-
stitutional  discrimination.  We  deal  first  with  the  allegation
that  the ceilings  on  individual  contributions  and  candidates’
expenditures  discriminate  in  favor  of  incumbents  against
challengers  (pp.  64-71  below).  We  then  turn  to  the  other
claims  of unconstitutional  discrimination  (pp.  71-73  below).

1.  The  Ceilings  Upon  Individual  Contributions  and
Candidates’  Expenditures  Do  Not  Discriminate
Against  Any  Type  of Candidate  or  Party,  Either
Inherently  or  Insofar  as  Their  Operation  Can  Now
Be Projected.

The  ceilings  imposed  by  Section  608  apply  alike  to  all
candidates  and  financial  contributors  without  regard  to
party  or  other  form  of  political  sentiment.  The  ceilings
apply  equally  to  challengers  and  incumbents.  Neither
express  language  nor  euphemism  nor  disingenuous  circum-
locution  introduces  any  form  of  differentiation.  There  is
no  argument,  as  we  understand  appellants’  brief,  that
Section  608  results  in any  systematic  discrimination  always

49  Section  608(e)  is  discussed  in  more  detail  at  pp.  54-63  above.

favoring  incumbents  and  established  parties  as  a  class.
Rather,  the contention  is that  challengers  and  new  parties
should  not  be  subject  to  the  same  financial  rules  as  others
because  they  have  more  need  for  massive  spending  and
wealthy  contributors  in  order  to  win  election  through  the
power  of  money.

One  branch  of this  contention  is the  complaint  that  other
laws  and  facts  of  political  life  result  in  discrimination
because  incumbent  Senators  and  Representatives  enjoy
methods  of communicating  with  voters  and  bringing  them-
selves  to  public  attention  which  are  not  available  to  those
who  run  against  them.  Congressional  staff,  franked  mail,
congressional-recording  studios,  office  space,  the  ability  to
hold  hearings  within  the  constituency  and  like  responsi-
bilities  and  perquisites  of  office,  including  the  ability  to
make  news,  are  cited  as  examples.

These  facilities  are  provided  in  order  to  enable  Senators
and  Representatives  to  perform  their  duties,  including
the  duty  to  assist  constituents  in dealing  with  the  executive
departments  and  administrative  agencies,  provide  them
with  information  published  by  the  government,  and  keep
them  informed  about  the  conduct  of  public  business,  No
one  denies  the  propriety  of these  functions.  Staff  assistants
and  other  facilities  are  requisite  if the job is to be done  well.
It  is equally  plain  that  outstanding  service  as  a  Senator
or  Representative  brings  greater  public  recognition  and
helps  enormously  in  a  campaign  for  reelection.  The  per-
formance  of  official  duty,  whether  individually  or  through
official  staff  and  facilities,  inevitably  has  this  twofold
aspect,  Appellants  can  hardly  mean  to  argue  that  members
of Congress  must  blind  themselves  to  what  all  others  know,
or  that  the  Constitution  requires  the  government  to  pro-
vide  challengers  with  some  offsetting  method  of  attracting
ballots.

66

67

Rather,  the  implication  seems  to  be  that  the  staff  and
facilities  provided  to  Congressmen  are  not  really  means
of  performing  public  duties  but  campaign  facilities  over
and  above  the  ceiling  upon  aggregate  expenditures,  which
challengers  have  to  purchase.  In  framing  the  FECA  Con-
gress  took  steps  to  prevent  such  abuse.  Section  434(d)  of
2 U.S.C.  indicates  that  use  of  congressional  television  and
recording  studios  is  to  be  treated  as  an  expenditure  in
Chapter  32  of  39  U.S.C.  limits  the  use
election  years.
of franked  mail.

The  drawing  of  lines  between  the  proper  performance
of  public  duties  with  incidental  political  gains  and  the
abuse  of  facilities  for  political  purposes  is  undeniably
difficult.  Abuse  may  occur.  That  problem,  however,  is  not
before  the  Court  in  this  case  for  three  reasons:

First,  appellants  have  proved  no  abuse,  general  or
particular.  So  far  as  the  record  shows,  the  staff  and  facil-
ities  available  to  Congressman  have  been  used  only  in
the  performance  of  their  official  duties,  and  any  electoral
advantages  have  flowed  from  good  performance.

Second,  if  the  legislation  providing  the  frank  and  other
public  facilities  is  discriminatory  or  administered  in  a
discriminatory  fashion,  the  discrimination  should  be  at-
tacked  directly  in  an  appropriate  action,  Congress  was
required  to  treat  all  candidates  equally  in  the  FECA;
it  was  not  required  to  frame  that  Act  so  as  to  offset  all
other  advantages  or  disadvantages  of  particular  classes  of
candidates,  whether  proper  or  improper.

Third,  no  appellant  is  a  victim  of  the  discrimination
because  none  is  a  challenger  to  an  incumbent  or  alleges
that  he  wishes  to  challenge  an  incumbent.

The  second  branch  of  appellants’  argument  —  that  the
Constitution  forbids  applying  the  same  limits  upon  con-
tributions  and  expenditures  to  all  candidates  for  the  same
office  because  uniform  treatment  operates  to  the  advantage

of incumbents  —  is extrapolated  by economic  and  political
theory  from  statistics  showing  the  expenditures  in  the  1972
and  1974  elections.  The  Brief  for  Intervening  Appellees,
pp.  112-29,  143-51,  demonstrates  the  theoretical  and  factual
errors  in  this  analysis  and  then  shows  that,  if any  reliable
conclusion  can  be  drawn  from  the  figures,  it  is  that  the
ceilings  do challengers  to incumbents  more  good  than  harm.
The  court  below  concluded  that  any  forecast  would  be
much  too  speculative  to  warrant  invalidating  Section  608
(J.S.  App.  1540-41).  The  correctness  of  that  conclusion
seems  beyond  question  when  one  reflects  that  appellants’
predictions  are  drawn  from a  statistically  small  sample
in  almost  complete  ignorance  of all  the  important  variables
that  affect  the  outcome  of  elections.*®*  That  court’s  con-
clusion  also  implicitly  recognizes  the  sound  and  funda-
mental  principle  that  the  duty  of  judicial  review  does
not  call  upon  the  courts
to  conduct  elaborate  factual
inquiries  into  matters  proper  for  congressional  determina-
tion  in  order  to  draw  their  own  inferences  and  then  to
uphold  or  invalidate  the  act  of  Congress  according  to  the
preponderance  of the  evidence  concerning  its  future  effects.
See  pp.  48-50  above.  For  both  reasons,  we  content  our-
selves  with  three  short  answers  to  appellants’  contention:
First,  appellants’  claim  only  that  the  ceilings  upon  the
size  of  financial  contributions  and  a  candidate’s  aggregate
expenditures  will  operate  in  practice  to  ‘‘discriminate
generally  against  challengers  and  generally  in  favor  of
incumbents.’’  (Br.  p.  94,  emphasis  added).
The  word

4%  For  example,  in  1972  and  1974  House  races,  a  total  of  forty-
eight  incumbents  were  unseated  in  the  general  election.  Of  these,
fourteen  outspent  their  successful  challengers  by more  than  15  per-
cent;  nineteen  were  themselves  similarly  outspent;  and  in  fifteen
races,  the  losing  incumbent  and  the  winning  challenger  expended
approximately  equal  sums.  Findings  IIA,  993,  5, II  J.A.  269-318,
326-86.  We  submit  that  the  only  significance  of  such  figures  is  to
demonstrate  that  none  of  the  figuring  demonstrates  any  useful
conclusion.

68

69

‘‘generally’’  veils  the  wide  variations  in  the  probable  ef-
fects  of  the  ceilings.  Sometimes  the  ceilings  will  work  to
the  advantage  of  a  challenger.  On  other  occasions  they
may  benefit  an  incumbent.  This  much  is  undeniable.  In
any  given  case  in  which  the  ceiling  on  aggregate  ex-
penditures  is high  enough  to  allow  a  candidate  an  adequate
opportunity  to  present  himself  to  the  public,  the  effect  of
the  ceiling  will  depend  upon  two  factors:  (1)  the  relative
ability  of  the  candidates  to  raise  and  spend  money  in
(2)  the  relative  gain  to  the  can-
excess  of  the  ceiling;
didates  of  spending  each  additional  dollar.  In  any  given
case,  these  two  forces  may  coincide  or  they  may  cut  in
opposite  directions  with  the  net  effect  depending  upon
If  the  candidates  have  equal
which  is  the  stronger.
capacity  to  raise  money  for  expenditures  in  excess  of  the
ceiling,  the  restriction  will  bear  hardest  upon  the  one  who
would  gain  the  most  from  each  additional  dollar  expended.
If  the  candidates  have  equal  use  for  money,  the  ceilings
will  bear  hardest  upon  the  ene  who  could  otherwise  raise
and  spend  the  largest  sum.  Individuals  differ  very  widely
in  their  ability  to  raise  money  as  well  as  in  their  need  to
spend  it.  Assuming  that  appellants  are  right  in  asserting
that  the  average  marginal  utility  of  a  dollar  expended  is
greater  for  challengers  as  a  class  than  incumbents,  still
their  argument  proves  nothing  about  the  total  effect  of
the  ceilings  because  they  fail  to  show  that  the  challengers’
average  ability  to  raise  money  is  not  so  greatly  inferior
to  the  average  ability  of  incumbents  as  to  more  than  offset
any  greater  average  need  for  spending.

Second,  appellants  show  no  more  than  that  those  who
were  gaining  most  from  conduct  injurious  to  the  public
interest  will  lose  most  from  its  prohibition.  The  statute
sets  up  no  classification  except  the  prohibition  uniformly
applicable  to  all  persons.  Thus  far,  no  question  of  equal

protection  is  raised.  Appellants  then  divide  candidates
into  two  classes,  incumbents  and  challengers,  and  seek  to
show  statistically  that  somewhat  more  candidates  disad
vantaged  by  the  statute  will  fall  in  the  second  rom  then
in the  first.  But  one  cannot  make  a  case  of  unconstitu-
tional  discrimination  by this  kind  of  statistical]  prediction

of

somewhat  harder  upon  one  class  than  the  other.  Jefferson
v.  Hackney,  406  U.S.  535,  548,  575-76  (1972);  James  v
Valtierra,  402  U.S.  137  (1971).

person  affected.  An  increase  in  the  statutory  minimum
wage  may  put  a  firm  which  makes  intensive  use  of  labor
under  a  serious  disadvantage  in  competing  with  a  rival
manufacturer  who  has  invested  heavily  in  labor-savin

Third,  there  is  not  the  slightest  reason  for  measuring
equality  in  the  vonstitutional  sense  by  appellants’  theory
of  the  marginal  utility  of  a  campaign  dollar.  The  Sen-
ator  quoted  by  appellants  (Br.  p.  99)  who  said  that
to  allow  challengers  to  spend  more  than  incumbents  would
defeat  the  bill  doubtless  realized  that  such  a  measure
would  be  universally  perceived  to  be  unequal.  Translated
into  simple  words,  the  marginal  utility  argument  asserts
that  the  constitutional  ideal  of  equality  requires  the  gov-
ernment  to  allow  the  otherwise  weaker  candidate  to

upon  expenditures  must  allow  a  challenger  a  generous  op-
portunity  to  present  himself  to  the  public.  The  present
statutory  ceilings  are  more  than  ample.  Once  that  purpose
is  served,  discrimination  is  avoided  by  treating  the  rivals
exactly  alike  in  the  segment  of  their  activities  to  which  the
law  applies  while  leaving  them  where  they  were  in  every
other  aspect  of  their  appeal  to  the  electorate.
