2.  The  Other  Statutory  Distinctions  Are  Just  and

Reasonable.

Appellants  challenge  two  sorts  of  minor  statutory  pro-

vision  as  unconstitutionally  discriminatory.

(1)  One  set of charges  alleges  discrimination  in favor  of
candidates  with  the  support  of organized  political  commit-
tees  or  of  a  national  political  party,  as  opposed  to  candi-
dates  without  those  forms  of  organized  support.  Section
608(b)(2)  allows  a  political  committee  registered  for  six
months,  which  has  received  contributions  from  fifty  per-
sons  and  has  contributed  to  five  or  more  candidates  for
federal  office,  to  contribute  up  to  $5,000  to  any  candidate.
Section  608(f)  allows  the  national  committee  of  a  political
party  to  expend  two  cents  per  potential  voter  in  a  Presi-
dential  election,  and  the  national  committee  or  a  state
committee  to  expend  the  same  sum  in  elections  to  the
Senate  or  House  of Representatives.

Political  parties  and  the  organization  which  binds  them
together  play  major  roles  in  our  national  political  life.
They  provide  both  continuity  and  a  degree  of  national
coherence  and  unity.  They  furnish  means  of  reaching  con-
census  upon  major  policies  and  also  important  machinery
for  selecting  candidates,  even  conducting  through  State
and  local  organizations  the  caucuses  or  primaries  neces-
sary  to  choose  candidates  for  office.  See  e.g.,  Ray  v.  Blair,
343  U.S.  214,  220-222  (1952);  American  Party  of Texas  v.
White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974).

To  distinguish  between  organizations  that  perform  these
functions  and  groups  which  make  no  similar  contributions
to  our  national  political  life  is  merely  to  permit  party
organizations  to continue  to perform  their  traditional  func-
tions,  in  the  same  manner  as  in  the  past  but  subject  to
limits  upon  the  size  of  the  contributions  to  candidates  and
of  expenditures  in  their  behalf.  If  party  organizations

72

73

were  deprived  of  this  role  entirely,  they  might  shrivel
and  die.  Any  informal  group  is free  to  set  up  an  organiza-
tion  which  has  the  modicum  of  structure  necessary  to
qualify  as  a  national  party  with  a  national  committee,  or
to  qualify  on  a  smaller  scale  as  a  program  committee.
The  slight  restriction  imposed  by  Section  608(b)(2)  —
the  requirement  of  six  months  registration  as  a  political
committee,  contributions  from  50 individuals  and  support  of
five  candidates  —  is necessary,  as  the  Court  below  observed
(J.S.  App.  1536),  to  prevent  the  proliferation  of  dummy
committees  which  made  mockery  of  the  earlier  Corrupt
Practices  Act.  The  differentiations  resulting  from  Sections
608(b)(2)  and  608(f)  are  therefore  well  within  constitu-
Cf.  Jemness  v.
tional  limits  as  heretofore  expounded.
Fortson,  403  U.S.  431  (1971);  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  US.
724  (1974);  American  Party  of  Texas  v.  White,  415  U.S.
767  (1974).

Appellants  also  assert  that  Section  608(a)  introduces
an  unconstitutional  discrimination  by allowing  a  candidate
and  his  immediate  family  to  expend  much  larger  sums  in
an  effort  to  win  an  election  than  another  person  would  be
allowed  to  expend  or  contribute.°*  The  imposition  of  the
ceilings  brings  about  greater  equality  by  reducing  the
advantage  of wealth  as  among  persons  seeking  public  office.
In  the  absence  of  Section  608(a)(1),  there  would  be  no
ceiling  upon  the  contribution  which  a  candidate  and  his
immediate  family  might  make  to his  own  election.  To  distin-
guish  between  immediate  family  and  other  persons  is  to
acknowledge  a  distinction  in  emotional  concern  recognized
by all  persons.  Furthermore,  the  danger  that  a  successful

503  The  term  immediate  family  means  ‘‘a  candidate's  spouse,  and
any  parent,  child,  grandparent,  brother,  and  sister  of  the  candi-
date,  and  the  spouses  of  such  persons.’’  §608(a)(3).  The  ceiling
is  $50,000  in  the  case  of  a  candidate  for  President  or  Vice  Presi-
dent,  $35,000  in  the  case  of  a  candidate  for  Senator  or  Represen-
tative  from  a  State  which  has  only  one  representative  or  $25,000
for  Representative  from  any  other  State.

candidate  will  be  subject  to  improper  influence  from  mem-
bers  of his  immediate  family  because  of  their  expenditures
on  his  behalf  is much  less  than  in  the  case  of  contributions
or  expenditures  by  outsiders.  Not  only  is  the  distinction
reasonable,  but  it works  no  systemic  differentiation  for  or
against  any  class  of candidates  or  other  persons.  Appellants
rely upon  a  supposititious  comparison  between  a  candidate
whose  family’s  wealth  permits  an  expenditure  up  to  the
applicable  $25,000,  $35,000  or  $50,000  ceiling  and  a  candi-
date  who  enjoys  no  family  wealth  but  who  would  easily
obtain  that  sum  from  a  rich  contributor.  While  this  situa-
tion  could  conceivably  develop,  one  can  also  imagine  cases
in  which  the  candidate  with  family  wealth  would  also
have  much  greater  access  to  wealthy  contributors.  Cases
of  the  latter  kind  seem  far  more  likely  than  cases  of  the
former.  Such  abstract  speculation  does  not  make  out  a
case  of  invidious  and  therefore
dis.
crimination.

unconstitutional

Il

THe  RequrreMeNts  ror  Reportinc  anp  DIscLOSURE  OF
Campaign  ConTRIBUTIONS  AND  Expenpitures  ArE  ConstitVv-
TIONAL  ON  THEIR  Face  anp  INsorar  as  THEmR  OPERATION
Can  Be  Progectep  Upon  Tus  Recorp.

The  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  and  the
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974
tighten  earlier  federal  requirements  consolidated  in  the
Corrupt  Practices  Act  of  1925,  43  Stat.  1070  (1925),  with
respect  to  the  maintenance  of  records  and  the  disclosure
of campaign  contributions  and  expenditures.  Section  431(d)
now  defines  a  ‘‘political  committee’’  to  include  any  group
of  persons  which  receives  ‘‘contributions’’  or  makes  ‘‘ex-
penditures’’  exceeding  $1,000  for  the  purpose  of influencing

74

75

the  nomination  or  election  of  any  person  to  federal  office.
Section  432(f)  requires  every  candidate  to  name a princi-
pal campaign  committee.  Sections  432(c)  and  (d)  require
every  political  committee  to  keep  an  exact  record  of:

(i)

all  contributions  made  to  it  in  excess  of  $10
with  the  date,  amount  and  name  of  the  contributor
and,  if the  contribution  is more  than  $100,  his  occupa-
tion  and  principal  place  of  business.

(ii)

all  expenditures  made  by  the  committee  in-
cluding  the  date  and  amount  of  the  payment,  a  state-
ment  of  its  purposes  in  the  form  of  a  receipted  bill
containing  full  particulars  and  the  address  of  the
payee.

The  records  are  to  be  made  accessible  to  the  Federal
Election  Commission  which  is  given  the  duty  of  auditing
political  committee  reports.  § 438(a)  (8).

Section  434  requires  each  political  committee  and  each
candidate  to  file  with  the  Federal  Election  Commission,  at
specified  times,  detailed  financial  reports  showing  assets,
receipts,  transfers  to  or  from  other  political  committees,
and  expenditures.  The  reports  must  include  for  each  con-
tribution  in  excess  of  $100  a  statement  of  the  amount  and
date  of the  contribution,  the  full  name  and  mailing  address
of  the  contributor,  and  the  aggregate  amount  of  all  con-
tributions  received  from  that  contributor.  On  the  expendi-
tures  side,  the  report  must  include  a  statement  of  all  ex-
penditures  showing  the  amount,  date  and  purpose  of  each,
and  the  names  of  the  payee  and  of  the  candidate  on  whose
behalf  the  expenditure  was  made.  Finally,  the  reports  are
to  include  a  statement  of  debts  and  like  obligations  owed
by or  to the committee.

These  provisions  serve  three  purposes:  First,  the  dis-
closure  helps  to  provide  voters  with  information  relevant

to  their  ballots.  Voters  may  judge  that  the  sources  of  a
candidate’s  financial  support  tell  much  about  his  outlook
and  qualifications.  ‘‘Birds  of  a  feather  flock  together.’’
The  knowledge  that  a  number  of  underworld  figures  have
each  contributed  just  under  the  $1,000  ceiling  to  a  can-
didate  who  is  preaching  vigorous  law  enforcement  would
help  voters  appraise  his  platform.  Similarly,  a  candidate’s
attitude  toward  the  issuance  of  stricter  regulations  de-
signed  to  reduce  air  pollution  by  the  Environmental  Pro-
tection  Agency  might  well  be  appraised  in  the  light  of  the
contributions  he  was  disclosed  to  have  received  from  the
executives  of  large  manufacturers  of  cement  and  oil
refineries.  The  formal  reports  may  not  themselves  be  very
meaningful  to  voters,  but  their  availability  to  opposing
candidates  and  a  vigilant  press  will  keep  the  voters  advised
about  such  relevant  circumstances.

Second,  disclosure  of  receipts  and  expenditures  is  a
safeguard  against  the  use  of  money  for  corrupt  or  dishon-
orable  purposes.  In  some  instances  the  very  payments  en-
couraged  by  absence  of  a  duty  of  disclosure  might  be
unlawful,  as  in  the  case  of  bribery  of  voters  or  election
officials.  Cf.  Burroughs  v.  United  States,  290  U.S.  534,
545  (1934).  Other  expenditures,  though  not  illegal,  may
be  of  a  character  that  many  voters  would  regard  as  im-
moral  or  otherwise  offensive.  ‘‘Dirty  tricks’’  designed  to
embarrass  a  candidate  by  putting  him  in  a  false  light  or
to  disrupt  his  campaign  schedule  or  public  rallies  are  one
example ; political  espionage  is another.  The  record-keeping
and  reporting  provisions  discourage  such  activities  and
increase  the  likelihood  that,  if  they  occur,  they  will  be
exposed  and  voters  may  take  account  of  them.  It  is hardly
necessary  to  add  that  the  solicitation  and  funneling  of
political  contributions  to  Hunt,  Liddy  and  other  original
Watergate  defendants  would  have  been  more  difficult  to
accomplish  clandestinely  under  the  challenged  legislation.

76

77

Third,  the  record-keeping  and  reporting  requirements
will  deter  violation  and  facilitate  enforcement  of  the  ceil-
The  man  who
ings  on  contributions  and  expenditures.
might  otherwise  contribute  an  illegal  sum  (i.e.  in  excess
of  $1,000)  will  think  twice  when  he  knows  that  the  re-
cipient  will  be  under  a  duty  of  reporting  and  disclosure.
By  requiring  a  few  aggregate  figures  in  addition  to  those
specified  in  the  statute,  so  as  to  show  whether  initial  assets
plus  receipts  balance  with  the  expenditures  plus  assets  re-
maining,  the  Federal  Election  Commission  can  also  make
the  reports  an  effective  means  of  deterring  improper  ex-
penditures.

|

The  purposes  served  by  the  disclosure  provisions  are
plainly  of  such  high  public  value  as  to  make  it unnecessary
to  consider  whether  the  appropriate  yardstick  is  best  ex-
pressed  by ‘‘compelling  purposes’’  or  by some  less  demand-
ing  phrase.  The  constitutionality  of  requiring  disclosure
of  campaign  contributions  was  sustained  in  Burroughs  v.
United  States,  290  U.S.  534  (1934).  Although  Burroughs
was  decided  prior  to  the  recent  development  of  legal  doc-
trine  interpreting  the  First  Amendment,  the  continued
vitality  of  its  holding  was  reaffirmed  in  United  States  v.
Harriss,  347  U.S.  612,  625-26  (1954).

Appellants  apparently  concede  the  constitutionality  of
disclosure  laws  as  applied  to  the  two  major  political
parties  but  argue  that  the  First  Amendment  prohibits
their  application  to  minor  parties,  new  parties  and  inde-
pendent  candidates.  The  need  for  disclosure,  as  the  court
below  observed  (J.S.  App.  1547),  is  no  less  in  the  latter
instances.  Disclosure  is  a  means  of  providing  the  public
with  relevant  information,  of  deterring  corrupt  election
practices,  and  of  implementing  enforcement  of  the  ceilings
on  financial  contributions  and  expenditures,  in  the  case  of
independent  candidates  and  the  candidates  of  minor  par-
ties  just  as  much  as  in  other  cases.  There  is  obvious  fair-

ness  in applying  the  same  disclosure  laws  to  all  candidates
and  all  parties.  The  need  is  the  same  in  all  cases  (unless
minor,  new  party  and  independent  candidates  are  to  be
brushed  aside  under  the  maxim,  De  minimis  non  curat  lez).
Appellants’  argument  thus  comes  down  to  an  assertion
that  requiring  disclosure  by  new  or  minor  parties,  or  by
independent  candidates,  will  do  more  to  discourage  their
prospective  contributors  than  potential  Democratic  or  Re-
publican  contributors,  because  their  contributors
face
greater  risks of reprisal.

The  argument  is  sheer  guesswork.  The  few,  scattered
incidents  incompletely  described  in  the  record  do  not  ap-
proach  the  level  of  public  harassment,  much  less  the  kind
of  ‘‘uncontroverted  showing’’  of  ‘‘economic  reprisal,  loss
of employment,  threat  of phyiscal  coercion,  and  other  man-
ifestations  of  public  hostility’?
made  in  NAACP  vy.
Alabama,  357  U.S.  449,  462  (1958).
There  is  not  the
slightest  evidence  in  the  record  that  contributors  to  any
political  party  in  the  United  States,  major  or  minor,  new
or  old,  or  to  any  independent  candidate,  have  been  sub-
jected  to  substantial  public  reprisals.  Insofar  as  the  hypo-
thetical  danger  is  supposed  to  be  of  political  reprisal,
there  is  no  reason  to  suppose  that  one  who  is  enjoying
political  patronage  under  a  Republican  administration  will
suffer  more  if  he  is  discovered  to  be  contributing  money
to  the  Conservative  Party  than  if  his  gifts  go  to  the
Democratic  Party.  Anyone  who  engages  in  political  activ-
ity  risks  some  criticism  or  loss  of  friends.  Although  the
record  fails  to  show  that  any  appellant  suffered  from  such
reactions,  it  seems  proper  to  add  that  wider  and  more
damaging  public  reprisal  would  be  needed  to  invoke  the
First  Amendment.

The  Court  has  consistently  held  that  such  speculative
hazards  as  appellants  articulate  are  ‘‘too  remote  to  require
striking  down  a  statute  which  on  its  face  is  otherwise

78

79

plainly  within  the  area  of  congressional  power  and  is
designed  to  safeguard  a  vital  national  interest.”’  United
States  v,  Harriss,  347  U.S.  612,  626  (1954).  See  also  Lewis
Viereck  v.
Pub.  Co.  v.  Morgan,  229  U.S.  288  (1913);
United  States  v.
United  States,  318  U.S.  236  (1943);
(D.C.  Cir.  1974);
507  F.2d  1194
Finance  Committee,
Stoner  v.  Fortson,  379  F.  Supp.  704  (N.D.  Ga.  1974).  The
decisions  holding  disclosure  requirements  invalid  under
readily  dis-
the  First  or  Fourteenth  Amendments  are
tinguishable.  In  NAACP  vy.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958),
the  State  was  unable  to  assign  any  reason  for  the  en-
forced  disclosure.  In  Bates  v.  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516
(1960)  ) and  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479  (1960),  the
disclosure  required  went  far  beyond  anything  necessary
to  achieve  the  purposes  assigned to the  statute,  even  if
they  could  have  been  regarded  as  compelling.  The  dis-
closure  requirements  should,  therefore,  be  upheld  not  only
against  facial  attack  but  insofar  as  their  prospective  ap-
plication  can  be  projected  upon  this  record.

We  press  the  argument  no  further  than  the  record  re-
quires  because  it  is  possible,  here  as  under  a  few  other
sections,  to  imagine  situations  in  which  enforcement  of
the  disclosure  requirements  might  raise  a  more  difficult
constitutional  question.  Occasionally,  tiny  political  parties
are  founded  and  endure  to  support  candidates  without
hope  of  election,  as  a  means  of  voicing  grievances  and
seeking  support  for  causes  so  contrary  to  the  dominant
social  or  political  current,as  to  be  repellent  to  the  general
public.  The  First  Amendment  protects  such  groups.  Far
out  as  they  may  seem  in  the  beginning,  one  or  more  may
become  an  important  agent  of  reform.  It  is  possible  to
imagine  cases,  therefore,  in  which  the  unpopularity  of  a
political  party  could  be  so  great  that  disclosure  of  the
names  of  its  financial  supporters  would  subject  them  to
risk of widespread  public reprisal.

This case  does  not  raise  the  question  whether  the  re-
porting  requirements  would  be  constitutional  as  applied
to those  hypothetical  circumstances.  None  of the  appellants
makes  such  a  case.  There  is  no  reason  to  suppose  that
such  a  situation  exists  anywhere  in  the  United  States
today  in  respect  to  any  election  affected  by  the  FECA
as  amended.  Nor  is  there  evidence  of  any  likelihood  that
such  a situation  will  come  into  being.  Even  if it be assumed
that the  disclosure  requirements  would  be  unconstitutional
if  applied  in  such  circumstances,  the  resulting  over-
breadth  would  not  render  the  provision  unconstitutional
on  its  face  or  as  its  operation  is  projected  upon  this  re-
cord,  for  the  reasons  stated  at  pp.  62-63  above.

In  this  instance,  moreover,  the  statute  need  have  no
chilling  effect  upon  the  exercise  of  First  Amendment  free-
doms  in  any  debatable  area.  The  party  faced  with  such
unpopularity  would  not  have  to  risk  prosecution,  nor  would
its  potential  contributors  be  required  to  disclose  them-
selves,  in  order  to  raise  the  constitutional  question.  The
party  would  have  ample  standing  to  raise  the  issue  pro-
spectively  if  it  could  show  that  the  danger  of  reprisals
was  real.  NAACP  vy.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958).

III

Pusuic  Fixanctnc  or  Campaicns  For  Present  or  THE
Unrtep  States  Unper  tHE  FeperaL  Evection  CAMPAIGN
Act  Is a  ConstiTuTIONALLY  PERMISSIBLE  REMEDY  For  Grave
Pvsuic  Evis.

The  spiraling  cost  of  federal  election  campaigns  coupled
with  the  candidates’  need  to  look  to  private  sources  of
financial  support  created  grave  public  evils  described
earlier  in  this  brief:  the  pressure  upon  candidates  to
obtain  ‘‘big  money’’  from  contributors  expecting  repay-

SO

81

ment  in the  form  of governmental  favors;  the  loss  of public
confidence  in the  integrity  of the  Executive  and  Legislative
Branches  as  citizens  observed  large  contributors  obtain
official  rulings  and  other  action  beneficial  to  their  financial
interests;  the  narrowing  of opportunities  to  be  a  candidate
for  office  because  wealth  or  access  to  wealthy  supporters
seemed  necessary  to  mount  effective  political  campaigns ;
and  the  increasing  influence  of  big  contributors  upon  the
candidates.

Congress  prescribed  a  tripartite  remedy  for  these  and
related  evils.  One  part  imposes  ceilings  upon  the  size  of
contributions  and  expenditures  in order  to  reduce  the  actual
and  apparent  influence  of  money  (see  pp.  22-73  above).
The  second  part  requires  detailed  disclosure  of  contribu-
tions  and  expenditures  within  the  statutory  restrictions  in
order  to  discourage  improprieties  and  provide  voters  with
information  relevant  to  their  ballots.  The  third  aspect
is  the  substitution  of  public  financing  for  most  of  the
private  financing  in  Presidential  election  campaigns,  and
the  public  supplementation  of  private  financing  of  cam-
paigns  for  nomination.  Insofar  as  relevant  to  the  constitu-
tional  issues,  the  plan  operates  in  the  following  manner:
Section  9006  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  establishes
a  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  to  which  is  appro-
priated  in  each  fiscal  year  a  sum  equal  to  the  number  of
individuals  who  indicate  on  their  annual  income  tax  re-
turns,  in  accordance  with  Section  6096,  a  desire  to  have
$1.00  of  their  tax  liability  used  for  that  purpose.  In  1976,
the  fund  will  apparently  be  adequate  for  all  the  payments
described  below.

Election  Campaigns.  Upon  certification  of the  Federal
Election  Commission,  the  candidates  of  a  major  party  for
the  offices  of  President  and  Vice  President  are  entitled  to
draw  from  the  Fund  up  to  $20,000,000,  the  aggregate  ex-

penditure  limit  set  by  18  U.S.C.  §608(c).*  In  order  to
qualify  the  candidates  must  agree  to  incur  no  campaign
expenditures  in  excess  of  the  amounts  that  they  receive
from  the  Fund,  to furnish  information  and  submit  to audits
with  respect  to  their  expenditures,  and  to  accept  no  money
26  U.S.C.  49003.
or  loans  from  private  contributors.
A  major  party  is  defined  as  one  whose  candidate  received
at  least  25  percent  of  the  vote  in  the  last  Presidential
election.  26  U.S.C.  §9002(6).  Today  only  the  Republican
and  Democratic  Parties  come  within  the  definition.

Parties  whose  candidates  for  President  and  Vice  Presi-
dent  received  between  5 percent  and  25  percent  of  the  vote
in  the  preceding  Presidential  election  are  defined  as  minor
parties.  26  U.S.C.  §9002(7).  Each  minor  party  candidate
may  draw  from  the  Fund  a  sum  in  the  same  proportion
to  $20,000,000  as  the  ratio  between  the  vote  of  his  party
in the  preceding  election  bears  and  the  average  of the  votes
for  the  major  party  candidates.  26  U.S.C.  §9004(a)(2)(A).
In  addition,  the  candidates  of  minor  parties  are  permitted
to continue  to raise  and  expend  money  from  private  sources
without  sacrificing  public  financial  assistance.  In  further
addition,  after  the  election  each  minor  party  which  receives
a  larger  proportion  of  the  vote  than  it  received  in  the
previous  election  may  draw  the  additional  sum  due  from
applying  the  formula  to  its  actual  vote  instead  of the  votes
in  the  previous  election.  Minor  party  candidates,  in  order
to  be  eligible  for  such  payments,  must  agree  to  the  same
record-keeping  and  audits  as  major  party  candidates  but
the  only  restriction  upon  their  receipt  of contributions  and

51  The  sum  of  $20,000,000  is  to  be  adjusted  for  changes  in  the
Consumer  Price  Index,  18 U.S.C.  §608(d).  The  acceptance  of public
financing  up  to  the  ceiling  does  not  exclude  the  national  commit-
tee  of  a  political  party  from  raising  funds  by  private  contribution
and  making  expenditures  in  connection  with  a  Presidential  election
campaign  in  an  amount  equal  to  2 cents  per  person  of  voting  age,
1.€.,  approximately  $2,800,000.

$2

expenditures  is that  the  aggregate  expenditures,  including
any  money  drawn  from  the  Fund,  shall  not  exceed
$20,000,000.  26  U.S.C.  §49003(a)  and  (c).

A new  party  or  independent  candidate  is entitled  to  draw
from  the  Fund  according  to  the  same  formula  after  the
election  but  only  if  he  or  she  receives  at  least  5  percent
of the  votes  cast.  26 U.S.C.  §9004(a)  (3).

.

Conventions.

The  provisions  made  in Section  9008  for
public  financing  of  nominating  conventions  follow  lines
parallel  to those  laid  down  for  election  campaigns.  Section
9008(d)  limits  convention  expenditures,  in  the  absence
of  ‘‘extraordinary  and  unforeseen  circumstances,’’  to
$2,000,000.  Major  parties  may  draw  this  sum;  they  may
not  increase  their  convention  expenditures  by drawing  upon
contributions  or  other  resources.  26  U.S.C.  §§9008(b) (1)
and  (d)(1).  Minor  parties  may  draw  upon  the  Fund  for
convention  expenses  according  to  the  formula  used  in  com-
puting  their  entitlements  for  the  expenses  of the  election
campaign.  Minor  parties  are  also  free  to  raise  additional
funds  for  their  conventions  from  private  sources,  subject
to  the  universally  applicable  ceilings  on  the  size  of  each
contribution,  and  to  make  expenditures  up  to  the  same
$2,000,000  limit  applicable  to  all  conventions.  26  U.S.C.
.
§§9008(b)(2)  and  (d)(2).
Nominating  Campaigns.  Any  candidate  seeking nomi-
nation  by  a  political  party  for  election  to  the  Presidency
may  qualify  to  receive  contributions  from  the  Presidential
Primary  Matching  Payment  Account.”  26  U.S.C.  §9033
(b)(2).  To  qualify  the  candidate  must  show  that  he  has
received  private  contributions  in excess  of $5,000  (counting
only  the  first  $250  of  any  individual’s  total  contribution)
in  each  of  at  least  20  States,  26  U.S.C.  §§9033(b)(3)  and

.
