Fund.

83

(4);  certify  that  he  and  his  authorized  campaign  commitee
will  not  exceed  the  expenditure  limits  applicable  under
Section  608(c)(1)(A)*;  and  agree  to  keep  and  furnish
records  and  information  requested  by the  Commission  and
to  submit  to  an  audit,  26  U.S.C.  §9033(a).  Each  eligible
candidate  is  entitled  to  public  financing  equal  to  the  total
amount  of  private  contributions  which  he  has  received
(again  counting  only  the  first  $250  of any  individual’s  total
contribution).  §9034(a).  Candidates  are  eligible  for match-
ing funds  regardless  of their  party’s  nominating  procedure,
1.€.,  primary  elections,  conventions  or  caucuses.

We  submit  that  this  plan  for  public  financing  of  the  cost
of  political  campaigns  is  constitutional  in  every  respect.
We  show  below,  first,  that  Article  I, Sections  4  and  8  of
the  Constitution  empower  Congress  to  place  a  ceiling  upon
the  expenditures  that  may  be  made  by  a  candidate  for
election  to  federal  office,  and  also  to  provide  the  money  to
pay  for  all  or  part  of  such  costs.  Second,  we  demonstrate
that, contrary  to appellants’  argument  (Br.  pp.  145-152),  the
First  Amendment  does  not  bar  any  and  all forms  of  public
financing  for  election  campaigns.  Third,  we  turn  to  the
particular  program  enacted  by Congress  and  show  that  the
formulas  determining  eligibility  for  public  financial  assist-
ance  under  the  FECA,  as  amended,  satisfy  the  require-
ments  of the  First  and  Fifth  Amendments.

53  See  pp.  80-81  above.
*  These  contributions  must  have  been  received  on  or  after
January  1  of  the  year  immediately  preceding  the  Presidential
election  with  respect  to  which  the  candidate  is seeking  nomination.
For  purposes  of  this  section  and  Section  9033,  ‘‘contribution’’
includes  only  gifts  of  money  made  by  a  written  instrument  which
identifies  the  person  making  the  contribution,  26  U.S.C.  §9034(a).

84

85

A.  Article  1, Sections  4 and  8,  Grant  Congress  Power
To  Provide  Public  Financing  For  the  Cost  of
Federal  Election  Campaigns.

Article  I, Section  8 of  the  Constitution  provides:

The  Congress  shall  have  power  to lay and  collect  taxes
...  to  pay  the  debts  and  provide  for  the...  general
welfare  of the  United  States....

The  words  empower  Congress  to  appropriate  money  for
any  public  purpose  which  Congress  believes  will  serve  the
general  welfare.  The  ancient  dispute  over  whether  the
spending  power  extends  so  far,  or  is limited  by the regula-
tory  powers  of Congress,  was  resolved,  first  by history  and
later  by this  Court,  in  favor  of  the  broader  interpretation.
United  States  v.  Butler,  297  U.S.  1, 65-66  (1936);  Steward
Machine  Co.  v.  Davis,  301  U.S.  548,  586-587  (1937) ; Helver-
ing  v.  Davis,  301  U.S.  619,  640-641  (1937).  As  Justice
Cardozo  observed  in the  opinion  last  cited,  ‘‘The  line  must
still  be  drawn  between  one  welfare  and  another,  between
particular  and  general.’’  Providing  adequate  public  financ-
ing  by  which  candidates  for  election  as  President  of  the
United  States  may  present  themselves  and  their  views  to
the  electorate  serves  a  general  public  purpose,  especially
when  designed  to  replace  the  dependence  upon  private
contributions  which  threatened  the  integrity  of the  govern-
ment  and  the  confidence  of  the  people.*  Appellants’  argu-
ment  (Br.  p.  145)  that  the  appropriations  do  not  serve

55 See  Bulloch  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  148-49  (1972)  where the
Court  observed:  ‘‘Viewing  the  myriad  governmental  functions
supported  from  general  revenues,  it  is  difficult  to  single  out  any
of  a  higher  order  than  the  conduct  of  elections  at  all levels  to
bring  forth  those  persons  desired  by  their  fellow  citizens  to
govern.

the  general  welfare  because  the  plan  is bad  for  the  country
raises  only  a  legislative  question.”

Although  the  restrictions  upon  expenditures  do  not  de-
pend  upon  the  public  financing,  the  plan  of public  financing
is  keyed  to  the  restrictions  upon  expenditures,  especially
the  $20,000,000  ceiling  upon  the  aggregate  expenditures  of
any candidate  (18 U.S.C.  §608(c)  ). The  link  casts  no  doubt
upon  the  power  of  Congress  to  enact  the  combination
because  the  restrictions  are  within  the  legislative  powers
of Congress.  See  pp.  22-24  above.

Nor  does  the  unusual  mechanism  chosen  to  measure  the
amounts  of the annual  appropriation  to the  Fund  cast  doubt
upon  the validity  of the  plan.  Each  person  liable  for  income
tax  is  offered  the  annual  opportunity  to  designate  $1.00
for  the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund.  26  U.S.C.
§6096.  By Section  9006  of 26 U.S.C.  Congress  appropriates
to  the  Fund  for  each  fiscal  year  a  sum  equal  to  the  total
designations.  The  appropriation  is  like  any  other,  save
that  the  amount  is determined  by this  measure  of the  extent
of  public  support  for  the  expenditure.  The  appropriation
comes  from  the  general  funds  of  the  United  States.  The
money  spent  for  this  public  purpose  will  not  be  available
for  any  other.  The  burden  of  raising  additional  revenues
will  be  distributed  among  all  taxpayers.  Just  as  these
elements  require  the  Court  to  treat  the  plan  as  one  involv-
ing  the  expenditure  of  public  funds  rather  than  as  a
mechanism  for  distributing  taxpayers’  voluntary  contri-
butions,  so  in  our  view  should  these  elements  preclude  a
taxpayer  from  arguing  that  any  part  of  the  very  dollar
he  designates  will  go  to  a  party  or  candidate  to  whom

°°  The  contrary  Opinion  of the  Justices,  347  Mass.  797,  197  N.E.
2d  691  (1964)  rests  upon  the  peculiar  ground  that  an  appropri-
ation  must  be  spent  by public  officials.  It  is also  worth  noting  that
80  percent  of  the  money  the  Massachusetts  legislature  was  propos-
ing  to  appropriate  would  have  gone  to  one  political  party.  Recent
Case,  78  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1260,  1261  (1965).

86

87

he  is  opposed.  Even  if  this  were  incorrect,  the  taxpayer
\vould  have  no  constitutional  complaint.  As  the  court  below
observed  (J.S.  App.  1562),  the  taxpayer  may  freely  choose
not  to  make  the  designation,  without  incurring  detriment.

B.  Public  Financial  Support  for  the  Cost  of  Election
Campaign  Is  Consistent  with  the  First  Amendment.

Political  campaigns  are  essential  parts  of  the  electoral
process.  Elections  would  be lotteries  unless  candidates  were
able  to  make  themselves  known,  to  lay  proposed  programs
and  policies  before  the  electorate,  and  to  debate  personali-
ties  and  issues.  In  the  crowded  modern  world,  full  public
attention  requires  the  use  of vehicles  of  mass  communica-
tion.  Such  use  costs  money.  One  would  suppose,  but  for
the  vehemence  of  appellants’  argument,  that  any  appro-
priation  of funds  with  which  to  carry  on  the  public  discus-
sion  so  vital  to  the  conduct  of  representative  self-govern-
ment  was  plainly  consistent  with  both  the  words  and  spirit
of  the  First  Amendment,  provided  that  there  was  no
interference  with  what  was  said  or  not  said,  with  who
might  speak,  or  with  the  form  of expression.

The  public  financing  provided  by  FECA  cannot  be
faulted  upon  the  latter  ground.  There  is  not  the  slightest
tendency  to  influence  who  speaks,  what  any  speaker  says,
or  how  or  when  he  says  it.

Appellants’  suggestion  (Br.  145-46)  that  ‘‘such  pro-
grams  have  almost  always  been  accompanied  by  various
measures  of  control  and  surveillance,’’  quoting  Lemon  v.
Kurtzman,  403  U.S.  602,  621  (1971),  tears  out  of its  context
an  accurate  commentary  upon  the  history  of  subsidies  to
religious  institutions,  and  applies  the  words  to  a  different
area  in which  there  has been  no  such  experience.  As  applied
to  this  case,  the  suggestion  is answered  by the  plain  words
of  the  Act.  The  payments  are  nowise  tied  to  any  measure

of control  or  surveillance,  except  to  confine  reimbursement
to the costs  of an  election  campaign  for  which  private  funds
could  be  lawfully  expended.  Justice  Holmes  provided  the
short  answer  to the  claim  that  public  financing  will  lead  to
censorship  and  government  control  of  political  parties  so
inexorably  as  to  require  the  Court  now  to invalidate  financ-
ing  free  from  such  interference:  ‘‘Not  while  this  Court
sits.’  Panhandle  Oil  Co.  v.
Knox,  277  U.S.  218,  223  (1928)
(‘‘The  power  to  tax  is not  the  power  to  destroy  while  this
Court  sits.’’).  See  also  Cole  v.  Richardson,  405  U.S.  676,
686  (1972).

The  distinctions  between  this  case  and  the  cases  involving
aid  to  religion  begin  with  a  clear  difference  in  the  govern-
ing  words.  The  interdiction  respecting  religion  reads:
‘Congress  shall  make  no  law  respecting  an  establishment
of  religion,  or  prohibiting  the  free  exercise  thereof...  .’’
Under  the  Establishment  Clause,  ‘‘[n]Jeither  a  state  nor
the  Federal  government  can  set  up  a  church.  Neither  can
pass  laws  which  aid  one  religion,  aid  all  religions,  or  prefer
one  religion  over  another.’’  Everson  v.  Board  of Education,
330  U.S.  1, 15  (1947).  With  respect  to  speech,  the  First
Amendment  provides  only  that  Congress  shall  make  no  law
‘“‘abridging  the  freedom  of speech,  or  of the  press.’’  There
is no  Establishment  Clause  applicable  to speech  or  political
activity.  The  interdiction  against  abridging  freedom  of
speech  cannot  be  read  to  imply  that  the  government  shall
not  enlarge  freedom  of all speech  by promoting  its  exercise.
Second,  the  Establishment  Clause  and  the  decisions  giv-
ing effect  to  it derive  from  a  distinct  historical  experience.
From  persecutions  and  prosecutions  for  heresy,  from  trials
for  witchcraft  and  experience  under  State  religions,  the
Framers  of the  First  Amendment  learned  that  the  religious
beliefs  of men  and  women,  the  ways  in  which  they  worship
and  whether  they  worship—indeed,  all  aspects  of  religious
activity  or  inactivity—must  be  isolated  from  every  form

&&

&9

of  government  intervention  as  matters  of  wholly  private,
personal  concern.  See  Everson  v.  Board  of Education,  330
U.S.  1, 8-15  (1947).  ‘‘Render  unto  Caesar  the  things  that
are  Caesar’s  and  unto  God  the  things  that  are  God’s.’’
Political  election  campaigns  are  inseparable  parts  of the
processes  of representative  self-government.  Their  vitality
and  integrity  are  matters  of  profound  public  concern.
Where  the  Establishment  Clause  and  history  conjoin  to
teach  that  the  government  shall  not  promote  religion  be-
cause  the  domain  of  religion  should  be  set  aside  as  a
garden  in the  wilderness,  neither  admonition  nor  experience
suggests  that  the  public  may  not  provide  the  means  of
conducting  that  fullness  of  political  debate  necessary  to
the  preservation  of  representative  government.

History  is  filled  with  examples  of  federal  legislation
giving  financial  encouragement  to  the  exercise  of  freedom
of  expression.  For  many  years  ‘‘newspapers  and  other
periodical  publications”’  as  well  as  books  and  other  educa-
tional  materials  have  been  carried  at  subsidized  postal
rates.  39  C.F.R.  6132.2(a)(1),  formerly  39  U.S.C.  §4354(a).
Today  the  government  provides  financial  assistance  to  the
Corporation  for  Public  Broadcasting.  47  U.S.C.  $§390-96.
Professor  Zechariah  Chafee,  Jr.,  an  ardent  defender  of
all  First  Amendment  freedoms,  observed:

Just  as  unrestricted  freedom  of  locomotion  would  be
useless  without  streets  and  roads,  so  when  communi-
cation  passes  beyond  a  conversation  in  a  small  group,
somebody  must  provide  means  for  reaching  hearers,
readers,  or  other  consumers.  Often  the  state  or  some
other  governmental  agency  is best  fitted  to  supply  the
Chafee,  Government  and  Mass
essential  facilities.
Communications  479  (1947,  repr.  ed.  1965).

.

Appellants  slide  over  the critical  distinction  between  pub-
lic  aid  to  the  conduct  of  election  campaigns  and  public
aid  to  a  particular  party  or  belief.  We  accept,  for  the
purposes  of  this  case,*”  the  observation  in  the  second  flag-
salute  case  that  the  purpose  of  the  First  Amendment  is to
.  from  all
reserve  the  ‘‘sphere  of  intellect  and  spirit  .
official  control.’?  Board  of Education  vy.  Barnette,  319  U.S.
624,  642  (1943).  Similarly,  we  accept  the  views  expressed
by Justices  Black  and  Douglas  in Machinists  v.  Street,  367
U.S.  740,  778,  788-91  (1961)  and  Lathrop  v.  Donohue,
367  U.S.  820,  871,  882-83  (1961)  to  the  effect  that  the
individual  cannot  be  compelled  to  finance  the  propagation
of ideas  or  political  causes  with  which  he disagrees.  Neither
proposition  is  relevant  to  the  instant  case.  Unless  the
allocation  is biased,  the  FECA  financing,  viewed  as  a  whole,
goes  not  to  any  particular  candidate,  or  party,  or  political
opinion,  but  to  promote  the  process  of  public  deliberation
prior  to  balloting.  To  treat  public  financing  as  if  public
funds  went  only  to  one  candidate  or  to  a  succession  of
isolated  candidates  without  looking  to the  combined,  overall
function  of the  spending is  to  misconceive  both its  purpose
and  effect.

C.  The  Formulas  For  Determining  Eligibility  For
Public  Financial  Assistance  Satisfy  the  First and
Fifth  Amendments.

In examining  the  present  challenge  to the  particular  plan
for  public  financing  of campaigns  for  nomination  and  elec-
tion  of the  President  of the  United  States,  the  Court  should
be guided  by two  propositions:  first,  Congress  may  provide
such  aid  if  the  distribution  formula  draws  no  ideological
lines  and  is sufficiently  fair  and  general  to  constitute  sup-

57  At  least  one  scholar  has  criticized  the  observation.  See  Howe.

The  Garden  in  the  Wilderness  (1965)  110-115.

90

1

port  for  the  process  of political  debate  rather  than  particu-
lar  parties,  candidates  or  ideologies;  second,  any  rule  of
distribution  which  accords  every  party  or  other  political
group  substantially  equal  treatment  in relation  to  its  politi-
cal needs  and  potentials  violates  neither  the  First  nor  Fifth
Amendment.

1.  Presidential  Elections

In framing  the  FECA  provisions  relating  to the  financing
of the  Presidential  election  campaigns,  Congress  had  four
objectives :

(1)  to  provide  for-full  public  disclosure  of, and  debate
upon,  candidates  and  issues;  but,  once  that  was
assured,  to  stop  the  spiraling  expenditures  which
generated  personal  and  party  obligations  corro-
sive  to  the  integrity  of government ;

(2)  to  relieve  candidates  and  their  supporters  of  the
need  to  raise  funds  from  private  contributors  by
substituting  public  financing  insofar  as  practic-
able ;

(3)  to  avoid  wasting  public  funds  and  diverting  atten-
tion  away  from  the  serious  candidates  to  others
without  any  significant  chance  of winning  the  elec-
tion;  and

(4)  to  treat  all  candidates  equally  in  the  sense  that
the  allocation  of  public  financing,  when  coupled
with  other  parts  of the  statutory  program,  should
neither  aid  nor  handicap  one  party  or  candidate
in  relation  to  any  other.

It  is  probably  impossible  to  achieve  100  percent  of  all
four  goals  without  creating  other  more  serious  problems.
We  submit  that  Congress  achieved  enough  of  each  objec-

tive,  and  struck  a  fair  enough  balance  between  conflicting
objectives,  to  satisfy  the  most  exacting  constitutional  test.
The  first  step  in the  accommodation  was  to  subject  every
_  party  to the $20,000,000  ceiling  upon  expenditures.  18 U.S.C.
§608(c)(1)(B).  Each  major  party  is  then  offered  the
opportunity  to  give  up  its  opportunity  to  raise  campaign
funds  by  private  contribution  in  return  for  $20,000,000
from  the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund.  In  the
absence  of  any  statutory  restriction,  both  major  parties
would  have  power  to  raise  and  spend  an  additional
$10,000,000,  $20,000,000  or  even  $50,000,000.  The  major
parties  are  thus  subjected  to  the  same  constraints  and
receive  the  same  benefits.  Although  a  theoretical  argument
ean  be  constructed  to  the  effect  that  this  allocation  alters
what  would  be  the  relative  positions  of  the  major  parties
in  respect  to  campaign  finencing  in  the  absence  of  the
FECA,  history  shows  that  for  116  years  the  man  elected
President  has  been  nominated  by one  of the  major  parties.
There  have  been  many  close  elections  and  shifts  in  ascend-
ancy.  Under  these  circumstances,  it is surely  fair  and  equal
to  provide  the  same  public  support  to  both  parties,  thus
assuring  the  body  politic  that  greater  spending  power  will
not  determine  the  outcome  of  an  election.  The  considera-
tions  which  led  Congress  to  look  to  a  minor  party’s  earlier
vote  in order  to measure  its  fair  subsidy  are  not  applicable
under  these  circumstances.

58  See  p.  81,  n.  51, above.
59In  1972,  the  Republican  Presidential  candidate  spent  a  total
of  $55,000,000  in  seeking  his  party’s  nomination  and  running  for
reelection.
The  Democratic  candidate  spent  $38,200,000  in  his
primary  and  general  election  campaigns.  (Finding  II,  §  51,  II
J.A.  451.)
In  1968,  the  Republican  candidate  spent  $29,000,000
in  the  general  election  alone.  The  Democratic  candidate  spent
$13,600,000.  (1969  Cong.  Q.  Almanac  1200.)

6°  The  figures  used  in  discussing  the  popular  vote  in  past,  presi-
dential  elections  are  taken  or  computed  from  tables  in  Schlesinger
(ed.),  The  Coming  to  Power,  pp.  498-533  (1971).

92

95

The  difficulty  in  determining  what  financial  support
should  be  offered  to  minor  parties  and  new  parties  is  that
they have  never  approached  equality  with  the  major  parties
in  their  ability  either  to  raise  and  expend  campaign  funds
or  to  win  Presidential  elections.  Major  parties  have  mani-
fold  greater  significance  in  these  respects  because  of  their
relative  continuity,  their  strength  in  all  parts  of  the
country,  their  roles  in  State  elections,  and  their  vastly
larger  membership  and  more  numerous  supporters.  No
Presidential  candidate  nominated  by a  major  party  or  new
party  has  ever  been  elected."  Minor  party  and  new  party
candidates  have  never  come  close  since  1856,  unless  one
counts  the  elections  of  1860  and  1912  in  which  there  were
splits  in  a  major  party.”

Current  conditions  and  history  thus  conjoined  to  teach
the  inappropriateness  of  granting  identical  financial  sup-
port  to  all  parties.  ‘‘The  fact  is  that  there  are  obvious
differences  in  kind  between  the  needs  and  potentials  of  a
political  party  with  historically  established  broad  support,
on  the  one  hand,  and  a  new  or  small  political  organization,
on  the  other...  Sometimes  the  grossest  discrimination  can
lie  in treating  things  that  are  different  as  though  they  were
exactly  alike.’’  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431,  441-42
(1971).  Identical  treatment,  as  the  court  below  observed,
‘‘would  not  only  make  it  easy  to  raid  the  United  States
Treasury,  it would  also  artificially  foster  the  proliferation
of  splinter  parties’’  (J.S.  App.  1563),  by radically  altering
the  relative  positions  of  the  candidates  in  the  absence  of
This  Court  has  recognized  on  several
public  financing.
occasions  the  important  public  interest  in  protecting  the
political  processes  against  this  kind  of  distraction.  Storer

61  Political  organization  was  so  fluid  in  1824  as  to  preclude  any

generalization  about  ‘‘party’’  strength.

62  See  pp.  100-01  below.

v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724,  736  (1974);  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405
U.S.  134,  145  (1972).

At  the  same  time,  the  congressional  sponsors  of  the
FECA  were  fully  appreciative  of  the  importance  of  pre-
serving  the  potential  fluidity  of  American  political  life.
New  and  minor  parties  contribute  ideas  and  influence  the
course  of events  to  a greater  extent  than  mere  membership
rolls  might  indicate.  Change  and  growth  require  that  every
new  party  have  a  fair  chance  to  move  to  the  foreground
and  establish  itself  as  a major  influence,  as  did  the  Republi-
can  Party  in  1856  and  1860.  It  would  also  be  inconsistent
with  the  policy  of  the  First  Amendment  for  government  to
influence  more  than  marginally  the  relative  strength  of
political  candidates,  ideologies,  or  groups,  and  then  only  if
compelled  by  some  paramount  objective.

These  desiderata  pull  in  different  directions.  Various
accommodations  are  conceivable.  A  number  were  consid-
ered  by  Congress.®  The  solution  chosen  has  three  parts.
First,  Congress  left  minor  parties  and  new  parties  free  to
raise  campaign  funds  from  private  sources  and  spend  them
up  to  the  full  limit  of their  power,  subject  only  to  the  fair
possibility  that  one  might  reach  the  ceiling  of  $20,000,000.
Next,  Congress  increased  the  spending  power  of  every
minor  party  by providing a financial  supplement—not  a sub-
stitute  as  in the case  of major  parties—from  the  Fund.  As  a
minimum,  the  minor  party  is entitled  to  a  sum  bearing  the
same  proportion  to  the  $20,000,000  available  to  each  major
party  that  its  vote  in  the  last  Presidential  election  bears
to  the  average  of  the  votes  of  the  major  parties.
Further,  in  an  effort  to  provide  for  a  situation  in  which
some  minor  party’s  true  strength  in  a  new  election  is
substantially  greater  than  in  the  past,  the  statute  stipu-
lates  that  a  minor  party  which  secures  a  larger  percentage

$3  See  e.g.,  5.1760,  94th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.  (1975)  (voucher  plan)  ;

$.1954,  93d Cong.,  Ist  Sess.

(1973)  (petitions).

O4

9%)

of the  average  vote  of the  major  parties  in the  new  election
than  it  received  in  the  old  shall  be  entitled  to  additional
payments  from  the  Fund  up  to  the  sum  due  under  the
new  proportion.  Although  the  dollars  paid  after  an  election
in  proportion  to  votes  then  won  cannot  be  spent  in  the
effort  to  win  them,  any  major  increase  in  a  minor  party’s
strength  would  normally  Le  discernible  as  the  election  cam-
paign  progressed  and  the  party  should  find  it  easier  to
increase  its  spending  by  borrowing  or  obtaining  credit
against  the  prospective  payments.

New  parties  and  independent  candidates  receive  no  ad-
vance  payments  from.  the  Fund  but  do  receive  payments
after  the  election  whenever  their  respective  candidates  poll
more  than  5  percent  of  the  votes  cast.

These  provisions  create  no  inequalities.  So  far  as  the
future  can  be  predicted,  the  candidates  of  minor  and  new
parties  will  have  a  financial  power  under  the  FECA
which,  when  compared  with  the  power  of the  major  parties,
is  proportionately  greater  than  it  would  be  in  the  absence
Under  FECA  a  major  party  receives
of  legislation.
$20,000,000  in substitution  for  the  ability  to  raise  and  spend
much  more  than  that  sum.  The  minor  aprty  receives  a  pay-
ment  proportionate  to  its  probable  significance  measured
by  the  electoral  vote  in  the  last  election  pls  the  sums
which  it can  raise  and  spend  from  private  sources  plus  the
guaranty  of  a  further  sum  after  the  election,  if  deserved,
in  order  to  make  its  subsidy  proportionate  to  its  actual
Although  statistical  measurement
electoral  significance.
is  impossible  because  of  the  short  lives  and  trifling  signifi-
cance  of  most  minor  parties  in  the  political  history  of  the
United  States,  it  seems  quite  plain  that,  when  the  package
of  financial  opportunities  allowed  minor  parties  is  com-
pared  with  the  $20,000,000  to  which  the  major  parties  are
limited,  any  minor  party  will  be  proportionately  better  off
financially  than  minor  parties  were  before.

Nothing  in  the  First  or  Fifth  Amendment  establishes  a
rule  requiring  Congress  to  choose  between  retaining  the
system  of  private  financing  of  political  campaigns  and
giving  every  party  or  candidate  the  same  sum  of  money.
‘‘Sometimes  the  grossest  discrimination  can  lie  in  treating
things  that  are  different  as  though  they  were  exactly  alike.
..’’  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431, 442  (1971).  Congress,
like  the  State  of  Georgia  in Jenness,  was  free  to  take  into
account  the  ‘‘obvious  differences  in kind  between  the  needs
and  potentials  of  a  political  party  with  historically  broad
support,  on  the  one  hand,  and  a  new  or  small  political
organization  on  the  other.’’  Jd.  at  441.  There  is no  invidi-
ous  discrimination  in a plan  of regulation  and  public  finane-
ing  which,  so  far  as  can  be  predicted,  leaves  the  new  or
small  organizations  relatively  as  well  or  better  off  than
before.  Any  slight  risk  that  a  minor  party  will  lose  finan-
cial  power  relative  to  a  major  party  because  of  some
unforeseen  circumstance  is  amply  justified  by  the  interest
in  avoiding  public  expenditures  serving  chiefly  to  promote
frivolous  candidacies  and  splintered  parties  or  unrestrained
factionalism.  Cf.  Bullock  vy.  Carter,  405  U.S,  134,  145
(1972);  Storer  v,  Brown,  415  U.S.  724,  736  (1974) ; Ameri-
can  Party  of Texas  v,  White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974).

More  detailed  analysis  of  the  separate  elements  of  the
FECA  plan  confirms  the  foregoing  conclusions.  The  statu-
tory  formula  for  the  pre-election  allocation  of  public  funds
to  minor  parties  was  consciously  based  upon  the  Court’s
approval  in  Jenness  of  a  system  which  based  differential
treatment  upon  demonstrated  relative  strength  in  prior
elections.  S.  Rep.  93-689,  p.  9.  The  aim,  as  stated  above,
was  to  give  financial  support  in  a  way  which  would  most
nearly  reflect  the  candidate’s  actual  significance  as  an  elec-
tion  factor,  thus  minimizing  governmental  influence  upon
the  outeome  of the  election.  The  distribution  formula  takes
the  minor  party’s  percentage  of  the  vote  in  the  previous

1)

97

election  as  the  principal  determinant.  There  is  probably
room  for  rational  argument  about  whether  the  vote  secured
by  a  party  four  years  earlier  is  the  best  measure  of  a
candidate’s  current  significance.  The  difficulty,  as  the  court
of  appeals  recognized,  is  that  other  measures  are  either
subjective  or  more  chancy.  To  allow  a  government  agency
to  judge  any  political  party’s  strength  or  importance,
except  when  anchored  to  unmistakable  statistical  bench-
marks,  carries  obvious  dangers.
Polls  are  too  chancy,
_—especiallypotls  taken  early  in  an  election  year.  To  dis-
tribute  vouchers  to  be  assigned  by  individual  voters
might  possibly  prove  workable  but,  again,  the  risks  are
high;  How  many  voters  would  actually  make  assignments?
Hlow  many  would  make  assignments  in  time  to  finance  can-
paigning?  What  are  the  dangers  of  a  race  to  be  the  first
fund  raiser  for  a  candidate  to  make  a  personal  approach?
How  would  the  expenses  of  that  solicitation  be  met?  How
responsibly  would  voters  handle  such  ‘‘phoney  money?’’
What  are  the  dangers  of a  ‘‘black  market?’’  In  the  face  of
such  uncertainties,  it  is  impossible  to  say  with  any  assur-
ance  that  Congress  could  have  found  a  more  reliable  yard-
stick  for  measuring  a  minor  party’s  initial  entitlement  to
public  financial  support.

The  risk  of  error  in  thus  estimating  the  significance  of
minor  and  new  parties’  candidates  is  reduced  by  Section
9004(a)(3),  which  provides  still  further  post-election  pay-
ments  to  those  minor  party  candidates  who  obtain  at  least
5  percent  of  the  popular  vote  and  demonstrate  greater
electoral  significance  than  they  had  four  years  before,  In
such  a case,  the  total  payments  will  reflect  the  actual  propor-
tion  of  the  vote  won.  The  payment  cannot  be  spent  in
advance,  just  as  the  candidate’s  electoral  strength  cannot
be  known  in  advance,  but  any  substantial  increase  in
popular  support  should  be  at  least  partially  evident  early
enough  in  the  campaign  to  increase  the  financial  strength

of  the  new  or  minor  party  by  facilitating  borrowing  and
obtaining  credit.

The  value  of  the  potential  pre-election  and  post-election
payments  to  minor  party  candidates  and  of  the  potential
post-election  payments  to  new  party  candidates  is  not  to
be  judged  as  if they  were  such  candidates’  only  financing.
Where  major  party  candidates  must  forego  private  financ-
ing  in  exchange  for  the  smaller  $20,000,000  payment,  new
party  and  minor  party  candidates  may  use  any  public
financing  to  which  they  are  entitled  in  addition  to  private
financing.  Although  they  are  theoretically  subject  to  the
$20,000,000  ceiling,  there  is not  the  slightest  indication  that
any  will  ever  achieve  it.  The  ceiling  operates,  on  the  other
hand,  to  prevent  the  major  parties  from  enjoying  their  full
power  to  raise  and  spend  money  from  private  sources,
This  difference  greatly  strengthens  the  relative  financial
position  of  new  and  minor  party  candidates  under  the
FECA  as  compared  to  what  it  would  be  in  the  absence
of legislation.

It  is  difficult  to  find  historical  examples  by  which  to
demonstrate  these  generalizations.  New  parties  and  minor
parties  have  much  too  rarely  been  significant  factors  in
Presidential  elections.  However,  a  few  facts  are  worth
noting.

(1)  The  only  newly-formed  party  to  move  to  the
front  in  a  field  of  established  parties  and  elect  its  nominee
as  President  was  the  Republican  Party,  which  elected
Abraham  Lincoln  in  1860,  For  that  election  it would  have
been  a  ‘‘major  party’’  under  Section  9004(a)(1)  because  of
its  large  vote  in  the  1856  election.  In  1856  the  Republican
candidate  would  have  qualified  for  post-election  financing
equal  to  the  sums  already  received  by  the  Democrats  and
Whigs,  but  not  for  pre-election  payments  unless  the  Re-
publicans  could  have  claimed  as  successors  to  the  Free
Soilers,  who  would  have  qualified  as  a  minor  party  in  1848.

Gs

99

(2)  The  Populists  won  approximately  9  percent  of
the  popular  vote  in  1892,  The  Populist  candidate  would
have  qualified  for  just  under  10  percent  of  the  subsidy
available  to  the  two  major  parties  in  1896,  in  addition  to
his  private  financing.

(3)

In  1924  the  Progressive  Socialists  would  have
received  17  percent  in  arrears  and,  if  the  party  had  sur-
vived,  their  1928  candidate  would  have  been  entitled  to
that  payment  in  advance  of  the  election,  in  addition  to
his  private  contributions.

(4)  No  new  or  minor  party  ever  received  5 percent  of
the  popular  vote  in  any  other  election,  except  in  1968.
(5)  Beginning  with  the  1968  election  it is  possible  to
make  a  rough  comparison  of  actual  financial  support  with
a  projected  operation  of  the  FECA  plan.  In  that  Presi-
dential  election  campaign  the  Republican  candidate  actu-
ally  spent  $29,000,000;  the  Democratic  candidate  spent
$13,600,000;  and  the  American  Independent  Party  candi-
date  spent  $7,200,000  (1969  Cong.  Q. Almanac  1200).  Appli-
cation  of  the  FECA  expenditure  ceiling,  by  itself,  would
have  improved  the  relative  financial  position  of the  Ameri-
can  Independent  Party  candidate  vis  a  vis  the  Republican.
The  actual  expenditure  ratio  was  approximately  1:4,  the
ceiling  would  have  reduced  the  disadvantage  to  1:3.  The
ceiling  alone  would  not  have  altered  the  relative  spending
power  of the  Democratic  and  American  Independent  eandi-
dates.  The  American  Independent  candidate  would  also
have  been  entitled  to  an  additional  $6,200,000  after  the
election,  assuming  that  he  had  expended  such  a  sum,  thus
bringing  his  total  funding  to  $13,400,000—a  tremendous
gain  relative  to  both  the  major  parties.  In  actuality,  the
total  expended  could  hardly  have  been  so  high  because  it is
unlikely  that  the  American  Independent  candidate  could

have  borrowed  or  received  credit  against  the  full  sum  to
which  he proved  entitled  after  the  election,  Still,  even  if the
figure  is severely  discounted,  it seems  plain  that  the  FECA
would  have  greatly  improved  the  American  Independent
candidate’s  relative  financial  position.

Although  the  American  Independent  Party  did  not  nomi-
nate  a  widely-known  candidate  in  1972,  one  can  get  further
insight  into  the  probable  operation  of  the  FECA  plan  by
projecting  its  1968  record  into  1972.  In  1972  the  Republican
Party  spent  $55,000,000.  Since  there  was  no  real  contest
for  the  nomination  it  is  fair  to  assume  that  at  least
$40,000,000  was  spent  in  the  general  election  exclusive  of
the  cost  of  fund-raising.
If  the  American  Independent
Party  had  again  raised  the  $7,200,000  of  1968,  the  Republi-
can  Party  would  have  outspent  it  by  more  than  5  to  1.
Under  the  FECA  the  ceiling  imposed  by  Section  608 (c)
would  alone  have  cut  the  advantage  to  3 to  1.  Under  the
FECA  each  of  the  two  major  parties  would  have  received
$20,000,000  in  public  financing.  The  American  Independent
Party  candidate  would  have  been  eligible,  by virtue  of  its
1968  record,  to  $6,200,000.  In  addition,  the  American  Inde-
pendent  candidate  was  entitled  to  raise  and  spend  private
contributions.  The  ceilings  upon  gifts  from  a  single  source
might  have  reduced  the  aggregate  below  the  $7,200,000
raised  in  1968,  but  even  if  the  figure  were  as  low  as
$4,000,000,  the  American  Independent  Party  candidate
would  have  had  half  the  spending  power  of  a  major  party.
We  do  not  offer  these  figures  as  anything  more  than  an
effort  to  check  the  FECA  plan  against  such  examples  as
can  be  derived  from  history.  Such  hypothetical  compari-

100

101

sons  involve  assumptions  and  uncertainties,  Nevertheless,
even  when  allowance  is made  for  the  speculative  elements,
the  examples  confirm  the  conclusion  also  to  be  drawn  from
abstract  analysis—that  the  total  effect  of  the  FECA  upon
Presidential  campaigns  is to  improve  the  relative  financial
position  of  new  parties  and  especially  of  minor  parties,
partly  because  of  the  limit  upon  major  party  expenditures
and  partly  because  a  minor  party,  unlike  a  major  party,
receives  public  financing  in  addition  to  what  it  can  raise
by  private  contribution.

History  requires  us  also  to  add  a  word  of  caution,  The
elections  of  1860  and  1912  exhibit  situations  which  do  not
FECA  structure.  In 1860  the  Democratic
conveniently  fit the
Party  split  into  Northern  and  Southern  wings;  the  Consti-
tutional  Union  Party  perhaps  included  other  fragments.
The  Republican  Party  split  in  1912  in  every  substantial
sense,  although  the  Taft  wing  kept  the  party  apparatus
and  name,  and  the  Roosevelt  wing  became  the  Progressive
Party.  The  FECA  makes  no  express  provision  for  such
It  seems  unlikely  that  Congress  desired  to  have
events.
the  Federal  Elections  Commission  decide  in  such  cireum-
stances  which  wing  is the  true  party  and  give  its  candidate
the  entire  $20,000,000  while  providing  no  funds  to the  eandi-
date  of the  other.  Perhaps  the  problem  can  be  solved,  if it
should  ever  arise,  by a  ne’  rule  or  regulation  of  the  kind
that  the  Federal  Election  Commission  is authorized  to  pre-
scribe  under  Section  9039  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code.
The  best  solution  would  be  to  make  full  financing  available
to  both  wings  of  a  divided  party,  thus  avoiding  any  risk
of  unduly  influencing  an  election.
If  this  solution  is  not
authorized,  then  there  is  a  gap  in  the  statute  with  which
Congress  will  have  to  deal  if  the  occasion  should  arise.

In  dealing  with  reforms  of  great  magnitude,  neither  the
Congress  nor  the  Court  can  foresee  every  possible  eventu-
ality.  Conditions  may  develop  in which  further  amendment
of  the  FECA  is  essential  to  fairness.  Legislation  which
embraces  a  major  new  departure  because  of  pressing
dangers  under  the  old  regime  almost  always  requires  modi-
fication  or  refinement  as  new  facts  develop.  The  Court  is
not  required  to  test  the  Act  against  every  conceivable  situ-
ation;  it  rules  upon  constitutionality  in  the  cireumstances
shown  by  the  record.  Under  these  circumstances  and  any
which  can  be projected  for  the  immediate  future,  the  FECA
ceilings  and  provisions  for  financial  support  in  Presiden-
tial  election  campaigns  work  no  unconstitutional  discrimi-
nation.  Should  a  new  situation  arise  and  Congress  or  the
Federal  Election  Commission,  acting  within  the  scope  of its
delegated  authority,  fail  to  make  adjustments,  the  Court
can  rule  upon  the  case  then  presented.  The  decision  here
would  not  govern  the  constitutionality  of the  statute  under
the  new  conditions.  Chastleton  Corp.  v.  Sinclair,  264  U.S.
543  (1924).

*  Appellants  pose  on  pp.  153-54  of  their  brief  a  hypothetical
example  in  which  Party  A  received  50  percent  of  the  prior  vote,
Party  B,  25.1  percent  and  Party  C,  24.9  percent.  The  example
is  too  abstract  to  be  meaningful  because  we  are  told  absolutely
nothing  about  the  organization,  previous  history  and  present  cir-
cumstances  of  the  three  parties.  The  one  absolutely  certain  thing
is  that  all  political  history  shows  that  Congress  had  no  cause  to
concern  itself  with  so  theoretical  a  possibility,  unless  there  was
a  true  split  in  a  major  party.  Neither  a  minor  party  nor  a  new
party  has  come  near  to  25  percent  of  the  vote  for  more  than  a
century.  Should  the  improbable  occur,  Congress  and,  if  necessary
the  Court  can  face  the  new  condition.

In  appraising  not  only  the  25  percent  line  but  all  others,  it  is
appropriate  to  recall  Justice  Holmes’  observation  in  Louisville
(dissenting  opinion).
Gas  Co.  vy.  Coleman,  277  U.S.  32,  41  (1928)

102

2.  Conventions

The  court  of  appeals  correctly  upheld  the  constitution-
ality  of  the  allocation  of  public  funding  for  party  conven-
tions  according  to  the  same  formula  used  for  distributing
pre-election  funds  for  Presidential  election  campaigns.
Appellants’  statement  that  these  ‘‘federal  subsidies  will
help  widen  the  gap  between  major  parties  and  all  other
political  groups’”’  (Br.  p.  169)  has  no  foundation  in  fact.
Like  appellants’  other  attacks  upon  public  financing,  the
argument  makes  an  altogether  false  comparison  by  ignor-
ing  half  the  statutory  plan.

The  major  parties  will  each  be  eligible  for  $2,000,000  in
federal  funding.  26  U.S.C.  §9008(b)(1).  Since  each  is also
prohibited  from  spending  more  than  $2,000,000  for  its
convention,  the  subsidy  is  in  substitution  for  much  larger
private  spending.  Any  new  party  or  minor  party  is  free
to  raise  and  spend  private  funds  in  addition  to  any  public
funding  to  which  it may  be  entitled.  Contributions  for  this
purpose  will  not  even  be  subject  to  the  $1,000  ceiling  upon
individual  gifts.  18  U.S.C.  §608(b)(1).  A  degree  of  effort
which  is not  reducible  to  monetary  terms  may  be  required
to  raise  the  private  contributions,  but,  even  after  allowance
for  this  factor,  it  seems  quite  plain  that  the  net  effect  is
to  make  the  new  parties  and  minor  parties  relatively  better
off  in  their  ability  to  equal  the  convention  spending  of
major  parties  than  they  were  before.

When  a  legal  distinction  is  determined,  as  no  one  doubts
that  it  may  be,  between  night  and  day,  childhood  and
maturity,  or  any  other  extremes,  a  point  has  to  be  fixed  or
a  line  has  to  be  drawn,  or  gradually  picked  out  by successive
decisions,  to  mark  where  the  change  takes  place.  Looked  at
by  itself  without  regard  to  the  necessity  behind  it  the  line
or  point  seems  arbitrary.  It  might  as  well  or  nearly  as  well
be  a  little  more  to  one  side  or  the  other.  But  when  it  is seen
that  a  line  or  point  there  must  be,  and  that  there  is  no
mathematical  or  logical  way  of fixing  it precisely,  the  decision
of  the  legislature  must  be  aecepted  unless  we  can  say  that
it  is  very  wide  of  any  reasonable  mark.

103

3.  Campaigns  for  Nomination

The  FECA  greatly  enlarges  the  opportunities  for  candi-
dates  to  seek  nomination  for  election  to  the  Presidency
despite  their  lack  of  wealth  or  access  to  wealth  by provid-
ing  matching  grants  for  candidates  who  have  qualified  by
showing  that  they  have  received  contributions  aggregating
at  least  $5,000  from  the  residents  of  each  of  20  States,
counting  only  the  first  $250  of each  contribution.  26  U.S.C.
§9033(b).  Every  candidate  is  subject  to  an  aggregate
expenditure  ceiling  of $10,000,000.  18 U.S.C.  §608(c)(1)(A).
These  provisions  help  the  candidate  who  can  reach  the
threshold  in two  ways:  (1)  they  prevent  the  more  powerful
money-raiser  from  greatly  outspending  the  less  by  spend-
ing far  above  the  ceiling;  (2)  they  double  the  money  avail-
able  to  the  less  effective  money-raiser  in  contributions  of
$250  or  less.  The  entire  thrust  of  the  measure  is  thus  to
reduce  the  financial  barriers  to  effective  eandidacy—a  step
in keeping  with  constitutional  trends.  Cf.  Harper  v.  Board
of  Elections,  383  U.S.  663  (1966);  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405
U.S.  134  (1972).

The  eligibility  requirements  are  designed  to  confine  the
federal  subsidy  to  those  candidates  who  have  such  serious
support  of  their  own  as  to  give  them  a  significant  chance
of  winning  nomination.
Ability  to  raise  funds  and  to
attract  popular  support  is not  only  an  important  measure
of  a  candidate’s  potential  but  a  more  reliable  guide  than
any  other  available  (such  as  the  artificial  taking  of  polls).
To  concentrate  the  public  funds  upon  the  serious  candida-
cies  avoids  encouraging  ‘‘frivolous  or  fraudulent  candida-
cies’’  and  ‘‘splintered  parties  and  unrestrained  factional-
ism.’’  Bullock  vy.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  145  (1972);  Storer
v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724,  736  (1974).  The  principle  applies
with  greater  force  in  the  context  of  public  spending  than
in  the  regulation  of  the  political  system  because  its  appli-

104

109

cation  in  the  former  sphere  nowise  restricts  the  ability  of
the  minor  group  to  promote  its  ideas  and  interests  without
government  interference.

The  requirement  that  support  come  from  20  States  is not
invidiously  discriminatory  in  the  context  of  Presidential
elections,  for  it too  is a  rational  measure  of the  importance
of  a  candidacy.  Presidents,  unlike  State-wide  officials,  are
not  chosen  by  counting  equally  all  the  popular  votes.  A
Presidential  candidate,  in  order  to  have  a  serious  chance
of  election,  must  have  support  in  various  geographical  sec-
tions.  This  requirement  is likely  to be at least  as  important
in  obtaining  nomination  as  it  is  in  the  general  election.
Moore  vy.  Ogilvie,  394  U.S.  814  (1969),  is  distinguishable
not  only  upon  this  ground  but  because  the  evident  purpose
of  requiring  signatures  on  nominating  papers  from  50  of
102  counties  was  to  bar  formation  of a  new  political  party
without  ‘support  in  rural  counties  containing  less  than
7 percent  of the  voters.  The  present  20  State  requirement
is  but  a  modest  and  reasonable  measure  of  whether  the
candidate  has  more  than  local  political  recognition  and
support.

In  criticizing  the  statutory  method  of  gauging  the  rela-
tive  support  for  candidates  as  discrimination  in  favor  of
the  wealthy  (Br.  p.  167)  appellants  resort  to  semantic  and
statistical  confusion.  The  effect  of  the  progressive  income
The  criterion  of  eligibility  is
tax  is  irrelevant  here.
‘‘wealth’’  only  in a  very  special  sense,  because  the  reference
is  to  campaign  funds  raised  from  a  very  wide  circle  of
supporters.  Such  words  as  ‘‘the  wealthy’’  and  ‘‘wealthy
persons’’  are  also  misleading  because  they  divert  attention
from  the  fact  that  only  the  first  $250  of  any  contribution
counts  in determining  eligibility  and  the  amounts  of match-
ing  funds—a  limitation  that  links  the  matching  payments
to  the  number  of  supporters  serious  enough  to  give  modest
financial  assistance  to  the  candidate  of  their  choice.

When  the  adjectives  are  stripped  away,  the  fact  remains
that  the candidate  for  the  nomination  who  has  wide  popular
support  among  those  citizens  who  can  donate  $250  apiece
will  receive  aid  proportionate  to  that  support,  whereas  the
imaginary  candidate  who  is supposed  to  have  equal  present
and  potential  support  among  citizens  who  cannot  afford  the
contributions  receives  nothing.  The  difference  might  not  be
tolerated  in an  ideal  world  of  perfect  political  ‘‘equality,”’
where  equality  was  measured  without  regard  to  ability
to  raise  or  contribute  funds,  and  in  which  there  was
perfect  ability  to  test  such  suppositions  of  wide  political
support.  Given  the  existing  political  system,  the  matching
payments  do  not  wipe  out,  but  plainly  tend  to  reduce
inequalities  of  opportunity  due  to  differences  in  fund-
raising  capacity,  especially  the  inequalities  due  to  differ-
ences  in  ability  to  raise  large  contributions.  The  allocation
of  funds  according  to  the  statutory  formula  will  almost
surely  increase  the  proportionate  spending  power  of  the
serious  candidates  with  the  least  fund-raising  ability  in
relation  to  their  initial  popular  support—if  they  can  cross
the  threshold—because  candidates  with  large  spending
power  in  relation  to  their  initial  popular  support  obtain
a  good  part  of  their  funds  from  large  contributions.  The
imaginary  candidate  with  popular  support  but  no  ability
to  raise  funds  is  unlikely  to  find  himself  worse  off  than
before.  If  he  could  win  before,  despite  his  lack  of  funds,
he  can  win  without  them  under  the  statute.  He  is  better
off under  the  statute  relative  to the  ‘‘big  spenders’’  because
the  ceilings  limit  their  spending.  At  worst,  he  loses  some
marginal  chance  that  he  might  have  won  the  nomination
instead  of  a  candidate  whose  success  is  attributable  to
funds  which  that  candidate  would  not  have  had  without
the  public  subvention.

A  reform  measure  that  moves  strongly  in  the  direction
of  eliminating  the  consequences  of  economic  disparities  is

106

107

not  unconstitutional  merely  because  some  of  life’s  unfair-
nesses  remain  uncured.  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.
641,  657  (1966) ; Schilb  v.  Kuebel,  404  U.S.  357,  366,  372-73
(1971)  (majority  and  concurring  opinions);  San  Antonio
School  District  v.  Rodriguez,  411  U.S.  1,  54-5  (1973).
in  which  the  FECA  plan  might  increase
The  case
inequality  of  opportunity  because  of  differences  in  fund-
raising  ability  is  wholly  hypothetical.  Were  the  risk  real,
it  would  be  more  than  justified  by  the  public  interest  in
the  long  stride  the  plan  for  matching  payments  takes
toward  generally  greater  equality  of  opportunity  in  seek-
ing  nomination,  and  in  promoting  public  discussion  of
serious  candidacies.

IV

THe  FeperaL  Exvection  Commission  Was  EsTaABLISHED

ix  AccorDANXcE  Witu  THE  CoNsTITUTION  AND  May  Constirvu-

TIONALLY  PERFORM  THE  Functions  Tuus  Far  UNDERTAKEN.

The  establishment  of  a  permanent,  independent  Com-
mission,  charged  with  broad  oversight  of  political  cam-
paigns  for  election  to  federal  office,  is  an  institutional  de-
velopment  of  prime  importance  not  only  for  implementing
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act,  as  amended,  but  for
promoting  further  reforms.  The  Commission  will  not  only
bring  continuous  study  to  bear  upon  developing  problems
which  may  require  new  legislation  on  the  part  of  Congress
and  the  President.  It  will  have  certification  and  auditing
duties  in  publie  financing  of  Presidential  elections,  and
will  also  serve  as  the  depositary  of  the  FECA  reports
upon  contributions  and  expenditures  designed  for  the  in-
formation  of  the  public.  By  its  interpretations  and  regula-
tions  under  the  FECA  it  will  facilitate  compliance,  close

interstitial  loopholes,  and  avoid  unnecessary  unfairness  in
the  practical  operation  of  novel  legislation.

The  legal  challenge  to  the  establishment  of  the  Commis-
sion  rests  upon  the  assertion  that  the  method  of  appointing
its  n.embers  is  unconstitutional,  Section  437¢  of  Title  2
of  the  United  States  Code  provides  that  the  Commission
shall  have  eight  members,  two  of  whom  shall  be  the  Secre-
tary  of  the  Senate  and  the  Clerk  of  the  House  of  Re-
presentatives  ex  officio,  two  of  whom  shall  be  appointed
by the  President  of the  United  States,  two  by the  President
pro  tem.  of  the  Senate  upon  recommendation  of  the  ma-
jority  and  minority  leaders,  and  two  by  the  Speaker  of
the  House  of  Representatives  upon  the  recommendation
of  the  majority  and  minority  leaders,  but  the  last  six  con-
firmed  by a  majority  vote  of  cach  House  of  Congress.  Ap-
pellants  argue  that  the  members  of  the  Commission  are
‘‘officers  of  the  United  States,’’  and  that  the  foregoing
method  of  appointment  therefore  violates  Article  II,  See-
tion  2, Clause  2 of the  Constitution,  which  provides  that  the
President—

shall  nominate,  and  by  and  with  the  advice  and  con-
sent  of  the  Senate,  shall  appoint  ambassadors,  other
public  ministers  and  consuls,  judges  of  the  Supreme
Court,  and  all  other  officers  of  the  United  States,
whose  appointments  are
not  herein  otherwise  pro-
vided  for,  and  which  shall  be  established  by  law:  but
the  Congress  may  by law  vest  the  appointment  of  such
inferior  officers,  as  they  think  proper,  in  the  President
alone,  in  the  courts  of  law,  or  in  the  heads  of  depart-
ments,

We  agree  with  the  Attorney  General  (Br.  pp.  110-111)
that  the  issue  cannot  be  solved  as  a  problem  of  nomen-
clature.  From  the  beginning  the  approach  to  questions
concerning  the  separation  of  powers  generally,  and  the

108

109

has
appointment  and  removal  of  officers  in  particular,
been  flexible  and  pragmatic.  Note,  Power  of Appotntment
to  Public  Office  Under  the  Federal  Constitution,  42  Harv.
L.  Rev.  426  (1929).  New  institutions  of  government  have
developed  as  new  times  produced  new  problems,  subject
only  to  the  fundamental  necessity  of  maintaining  the  es-
sential  balance  of  the  three  departments  and  preventing
one  from  taking  over  functions  falling  exclusively  in  the
core  of  power  belonging  to  another,  As  Justice  Holmes
observed  in  Springer  v.  Philippine  Islands,  277  U.S.  189,
209-211  (1928)  (dissenting  opinion),  ‘‘The  great  ordinances
of  the  Constitution  do  not  establish  and  divide  fields  of
black  and  white.  Even  the  more  specific  of  them  are  found
to  terminate  in  a  penumbra  shading  gradually  from  one
extreme  to  another  ....  When  we  come  to  the  funda-
mental  distinctions  it  is  still  more  obvious  that  they  must
be  received  with  a  certain  latitude  or  our  government
could  not  go  on.  ...  It  does  not  seem  to  need  argument
to  show  that  however  we  may  disguise  it by  veiling  words
we  do  not  and  cannot  carry  out  the  distinction  between
legislative  and  executive  action  with  mathematical  preci-
sion  and  divide  the  branches  into  watertight  compart-
ee

Accordingly,  the  answer  to  the  question  whether  the
its
Federal  Elections  Commission  was  established
members  appointed  in  violation  of  the  Constitution  is to  be
found  in  study  of  the  functions  assigned  to  the  Commis-
sion  and  its  relation  to  other  arms  of  government,  the  oc-
cupants  of  other  offices,  and  the  political  system  as  a
whole.

and_

We  divide  the  inquiry  into  two  parts.  We  show,  first,
that  the  Commission  exercises  a  number  of  important
functions  not  belonging  exclusively  to  the  Executive  De-
partment  but  appropriate  for  au  independent  arm  of  gov-
ernment  exercising  quasi-legislative  power.  We  then  dem-

onstrate  that  the  Constitution  does  not  forbid  dividing
the  authority  for  selecting  members  of  this  truly  indepen-
dent  Commission  among  the  President  and  the  highest
officers  of the  Senate  and  House  of Representatives  in order
to  build  the  greatest  public  confidence  in  its  complete  im-
partiality  and  freedom  from  influence  in  performing  its
unique  duties  in  scrutinizing  the  political  campaigning  of
the  President  and  members  of  Congress,  and  in  disbursing
public  funds  in  Presidential  elections.

We  do  not  examine  a  number  of  duties  assigned  to  the
Commission,  such  as  making  recommendations  for  criminal
prosecutions  and  instituting  civil  enforcement  actions.
Whether  they  are  so  essentially  executive  in  character  as
to  belong  exclusively  to  the  Executive  Department  need
not  be  decided  in  this  case.  Since  the  Commission  was  es-
tablished  in  accordance  with  the  Constitution  to  exercise
a  core  of  power  which  Congress  and  the  President  could
lawfully  confide  in  its  members  by  enacting  the  measure
and  signing  it  into  law,  decision  upon  the  delegation  of
other  powers,  not  yet  exercised,  may  be  postponed.  It
cannot  matter  to  any  of  the  appellants  whether  enforce-
ment  involves  the  Commission  or  is  reserved  only  to  the
Executive.

A.  The  Commission  Exercises  Important  Functions  Not
Belonging  Exclusively  to  the  Executive  Department
But  Appropriate  for  an  Independent  Agency  With
Quasi-Legislative  Powers.

Although  there  may  be  room  for  controversy  over
whether  some  of  the  functions  assigned  to  the  Commis-
sion  are  properly  prerogatives  of  the  Executive  Depart-
ment,  its  major  duties  are  of  kinds  never  regarded  as
belonging  exclusively  to  that  Department  and  often  per-

110

111

formed  by  other  arms  of  government  free  from  Executive
control  and  variously  described  as  ‘‘legislative’’  or  ‘‘in-
dependent.’’

1.  IJnvestigations  and  Reports.

The  Commission  is
given  wide  power  to  conduct  investigations,  direct  studies
and  make  legislative  recommendations,  2 U.S.C.  § 487d,  26
U.S.C.  §§ 9009,  9039.  The  power  extends  beyond  the  finane-
ing  of  political  campaigns  to  ‘‘the  administration  of  elec-
tions.’?  2  U.S.C.  § 438(b).  Sinee  these  duties  might  be
delegated  to  an  independent  agency,  a  committee  of  Con-
gress  or  even  congressional  staff,  there  is  no  room  for  con-
stitutional  challenge  to  the  assignment  to  the  Commission.

2.  Depositary  of Reports  of Contributions  and  Expen-
ditures,  One  of  the  Commission’s  major  duties  is  to  serve
as  the  depositary  of  all  reports  required  of  contributors,
political  committees,  and  candidates  for  federal  office  dis-
closing  receipts  and  expenditures.  In  order  to  facilitate
compliance,  the  Commission  is  charged  with  developing
forms  for  reports  and  record-keeping.  It  is  also  charged
with  conducting  audits  and  investigations,  in  order  to
bring  apparent  violations  to  the  attention  of  the  Executive
Branch  and  to  inform  Congress  upon  the  operation  of
the  Act  and  any  need  for  amendment,  Beeause  a  primary
purpose  of  the  reporting  requirements  is  to  inform  the
public,  the  Commission  is  directed  not  only  to  make  the
reports  publie  but  to  develop  mechanics  for  filing,  indexing

and  copying  which  will  facilitate  publication.

History  and  reason  indicate  that  although  these  duties
could  properly  be  assigned  to  executive  officials  appointed
by  the  President,
allocable  outside  the
Executive  to  an  officer  or  agency  created  by  Congress,

as  depositary  of  reports  concerning  campaign
bilities
finance  have  been  vested  in  officers  of  the  Senate  or  House

of  Representatives  for  almost  three  quarters  of  a  century.
The  Corrupt  Practices  Act  of  1910,  36  Stat.  822,  required
political  committees  to  keep  records  of  all  contributions
and  expenditures  for  filing  with  the  Clerk  of  the  House
after  the  eleetion.  The  legislation  was  broadened  in  1911
and  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  was  given  duties  paral-
lelling  those  of  the  Clerk.  37  Stat.  25.  The  Corrupt  Prac-
tices  Act  of  1925,  43  Stat.  1070,  greatly  elaborated  the
reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  and  imposed  ex-
penditure  limitations  in campaigns  for  Senator  and  Repre-
sentative.  The  details  are  unimportant  except  to  point
out  that  once  again  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  and  the
Clerk  of  the  House  were  assigned  key  roles.

responsibility  for  enforeement  of  the  expanded  reporting
and  disclosure  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election  Cam-
paign  Act  of  1971,  86  Stat.  3, was  vested  in  the  Secretary,
the  Clerk  and  the  Comptroller-General.

The  foregoing  statutes  were  enacted  at  different  Con-
gresses  and  signed  by  a  number  of  Presidents.  The  con-

litigated  and  upheld  without  any  suggestion  of  constitu-
tional  impropriety  in  designating  officers  in  the  Legislative
Branch  as  depositaries  of  the  required  reports  even  in  the
case  of a  Presidential  election.  Burroughs  v.  United  States,

branches  of  the  government  have  acquiesced,  shapes  the
