There  is  an  additional  reason  for  not  regarding  the
receipt  and  scrutiny  of  financial  information  relating  to
campaigns  for  election  as  Senator  or  Representative  as
duties  belonging  exclusively  to  the  Executive  Department.

112

113

Article  I, Section  5 of  the  Constitution  makes  each  House
‘the  judge  of  the  elections  ..  .  of  its  own  members.’’  The
receipt  of  reports  concerning  financial  practices  is  closely
related  to  that  constitutional  duty.

3.  Duties  in  Public  Financing  of Presidential  Elections.
The  Commission  is  a  primary  administrator  of  the  public
funding  of  Presidential  election  and  primary  campaigns
under  Chapters  95  and  96  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code,
26  U.S.C.  6§  9001  et  seq.  The  Commission  certifies  to  the
Secretary  of  the  Treasury  the  names  of  those  who  have
complied  with  the  statutory  conditions  for  cligibility  and
the  amounts  due;  it  also  conducts  audits  and  sees  that
overpayments  or  improperly  spent  funds  are  returned  to
the  Treasury.

In  order  for  a  Presidential  candidate  to  receive  public
funds,  he  must  agree  in  writing  to  furnish  the  Commission
to  keep  and
with  evidence  of  his  campaign  expenses,
furnish  records,  to  submit  to  Commission  audit,  and  to
make  any  required  repayments  where  necessary,  26  U.S.C.
© 9003(a).  Presidential  candidates  of  major  parties  who
seek  federal  financing  must  also  certify  to  the  Commission
that  they  will  not  incur  campaign  expenses  in  excess  of
the  amount  of  federal  funding  reecived  and  that  they  will
not  accept  private  financing.  The  Commission  is  to  desig-
nate  the  time  when  such  certification  must  be  made.  26
U.S.C.  © 9003  (b)  and  (c).  If eandidates  submit  the  required
agreements  and  certifications,  the  Commission  is,  in  turn,
to  certify  their  eligibility  for  payment  to  the  Secretary
of  Treasury,  who  is  charged  with  the  actual  disbursement
of  the  authorized  funds.  26  U.S.C.  6§  9005,  9006(¢c).  The
Commission  has  no  role  in  determining  the  amounts  paid
to  eligible  candidates,  such  amounts  being  fixed  by  law.
96  U.S.C.  § 9004.

The  FECA  also  provides  for  federal  financing  of  Presi-
Presidential

conventions

nominating

and

of

dential

primary  campaigns.  The  Commission’s  certification  duties
with  respect  to  these  payments  are  similar  te  its  duties
relating  to  Presidential  campaigns.  26  U.S.C.  &  9008 ( g)
9033, 9036, 9037.

.

Once  payments  of  federal  funds  are  made  pursuant  to
the above  provisions,  the  Commission  has  the  responsibility
of  auditing  the  expenses  of  Presidential  candidates  and
others  who  receive  these  funds,  26  U.S.C,  §§  9007(a)
9009(b),  9038(a),  9039(h),  and  may,  to  this  end,  require
the  keeping  of  appropriate  records,  26  U.S.C.  64 9009(b),
9039(b).  If the  Commission  determines  that  any  Presiden-
tial  candidate  received  or  spent  more  than  was  allowable
under  the  applicable  statutes,  it  is  required  to  notify  the
recipient,  who  is  under  a  duty  to  repay  the  improperly
received  or  expended  amounts  to  the  Secretary  of  the
Treasury,  26  U.S.C.  ¢$  9007(b),  9008(h),  9038(b).  If  re-
payment  is  not  made,  the  Commission  may  institute  suit
to  recover  the  amounts  due  the  Secretary,  26  U.S.C.
$$ 9010(b),  9008(h),  9040(b),  and  it  may  generally  seek
injunctive  relief  to  compel  compliance  with  the  pertinent
statutory  requirements.  26  U.S.C.  66  9011,  9040(e).  Crim-
inal  sanctions  are  imposed  for  violations  of  the  federal
financing  statutes,  26  U.S.C.
§§ 9012,  9042,  but  the  Com-
mission  has  no  direct  role  in  respect  to  the  enforcement
of these  penalties.

The  Commission’s  role  in  the  administration

of  the
publie  financing  available  in  campaigns  for  nomination
and  election  as  President  of the  United  States  is essentially
the  same  as  the  function  of  the  Comptroller-General  in
settling  and  auditing  substantially  all  government  accounts,
and  in  certifying  amounts  for  payment.  31  U.S.C,  &§  41,
44,  47,  60,  67,  71,  72,  74.  Like  the  Federal  Elections  Com-
mission,  the  Comptroller-General  has  duties  in  recovering
overpayments,  31  U.S.C,  § 227.

114

115

outside

The  Comptroller-General  is,  of  course,

the
Executive  Branch.  31  U.S.C.  4 41.  His  duties  in  auditing
and  settling  accounts  are  not  functions  belonging  to  the
Executive  Department.  He  is  often  described  as  ‘‘a  part
of  the  legislative  branch  of  the  Government.’’  E.g.,  Reor-
ganization  Act  of  1949,  Section  7, 63  Stat.  205.  By analogy,
we  submit,  the  Commission’s  auditing  and  certifying  fune-
tions  are  closely  related  to  the  legislative  power  and  were
assignable,  under  the  Constitution,  to  an  ageney  created
for  that  purpose,  removed  from  Executive  dominance.

4.  Regulations  and  Advisory  Opinions,

The  FECA,

as  amended,  gives  the  Commission  broad  authority  —

to  prescribe  rules  and  regulations  necessary  to  carry
out  the  provisions  of  this  chapter  [14  of  Title  2 of the
United  States  Code  relating  to  financial  reporting  and
disclosure. |

Any  proposed  regulation  must  be  laid  before  Congress
and  may  be  issued  only  if not  disapproved  within  30  legis-
lative  days.  2  U.S.C.  ©  438.  The  Commission  is  given
similar  authority,  subject  to  the  same  condition,  to  issue
regulations  necessary  to carry  out  the  plan  for  publie  finane-
ing for  Presidential  candidates,  26  U.S.C.  §§ 9009,  9039.

Section  437f  of  Title  2  of  the  United  States  Code  em-
powers  and  requires  the  Commission,  upon  request  of  a
federal  office-holder,  candidate  or  political  committee,  to—

respect  to
render  an  advisory  opinion  ...  with
whether  any  specific  transaction  or  activity  by  such
individual,  candidate,  or  political  committee  would
constitute  a  violation  of  [any  law  relating  to  the
financial  aspects  of  political  campaigns. |

Subsection  (b)  provides :

Notwithstanding  any  other  provision  of  law,  any
person  with  respect  to  whom  an  advisory  opinion  is

rendered  under  subsection  (a}  who  acts  in  good  faith
in accordance  with  the  provisions  and  findings  of  such
advisory  opinion  shall  be  presumed  to  be  in  com-
pliance  with  [the  laws  relating  to  financial  aspects  of
political  campaigns }.

We  submit  that  the  foregoing  powers  are  not  integral
parts  of  the  President’s  duty  to  ‘take  care  that  the  laws
be  faithfully  executed,’’  under  Article  II,  Section  3.  On
the  contrary,  both  are  the  kinds  of  function  repeatedly
delegated  to  independent  agencies  deliberately  placed  be-
yond Executive  control.

Were  one  to  suppose  that  the  power  to  issue  regulations
must  be  characterized  abstractly  as  belonging  to  only  one
of  the  three  great  divisions  of  government,  it  would  fall
most  naturally  in  the  Legislative  Department.  Such  regu-
lations  are  described  as  ‘‘legislative’’  because,  when  within
the  agency’s  authority,  they  make  laws  for  the  future  in
general  terms.  Davis,  Administrative  Law  Text  (3rd  ed.)
§$§ 5.01  et  seq.  They  bind  the  courts  as  well  as  other  arms
of government.

History  and  practice  confirm  this  theoretical  analysis.
Although  the  power  to  issue  legislative  regulations  has
not  infrequently  been  conferred  upon  an  executive  officer
or  department,  the  result  is  nearly  always  described  as  a
‘‘delegation  of  legislative  power;’’  and  the  legal  question
is  whether  Congress  has  provided  adequate  standards  to
guide  the  delegate’s  action.  Panama  Refining  Co.  v.  Ryan,
293  U.S.  388  (1935);  Schechter  Poultry  Corp.  v.  United
States,  295  U.S.  495  (1935).  But  the  practice  is  by  no
means  uniform.  The  power  to  issue  legislative  regulations
is  often  conferred  upon  independent  agencies  such  as  the
Federal  Trade  Commission  and  Securities  Exchange  Com-
mission  which  are  ‘‘independent  of  executive  authority”’
and  exercise  ‘‘quasi-judicial’’  or  ‘‘quasi-legislative’’  power.

116

117

In  Humphrey’s  Executor  vy.  United  States,  295  U.S.  602,
628  (1935),  the  Court  said:

Such  a  body  cannot  in  any  proper  sense  be  character-
ized  as  an  arm  or  eye  of  the  executive.  Its  duties  are
performed  without  executive  leave  and,  in  the  con-
templation  of  the  statute,  must  be  free  from  executive
control.  In  administering  the  provisions  of  the  statute
in  respect  of  ‘‘unfair  methods  of  competition’?  —
that  is to  say  in  filling  in  and  administering  the  details
embodied  by that  general  standard  —  the  commission
acts  in  part  quasi-legislatively  and  in  part  quasi-
judicially.

Issuing  general  rules  without  fixing  legal  responsibility
upon  a  particular  person  is  plainly  a  ‘‘quasi-legislative”’
function.  It  follows  that  investing  such  authority  in  the
Federal  Election  Commission  does  not  violate  any  prin-
ciple  pertaining  to  the  separation  of  powers  even  if  the
Commission  were  closely  aligned  to  the  Legislative  Branch
rather  than  truly  ‘‘independent.’’

It  would  seem  to  follow  that  issuing  ad  hoe  opinions
and  other  interpretative  rulings  for  the  guidance  of  those
subject  to  a  statute  may  be  fixed  outside  the  Executive
Branch.  Such  interpretations  do  not  make  new  laws  be-
cause  they  do  not  bind  the  courts.  The  interpretations
cannot  bind  the  Executive,  partly  because  they  do  not
make  law  and  partly  because  the  Executive  cannot  be
subjected  to  the  control  of  another  department  or  agency
in  carrying  out  its  functions  under  Article  If.  On  the
other  hand,  there  is  nothing  inherently  ‘‘exeeutive’’  about
providing  an  informed  and  internally-consistent  body  of
expert  opinion  concerning  the  ‘‘intent  of  Congress’’  for
the  guidance  of  those  subject  to  a  complex  statutory  re-
gulation.  The  function  is very  similar  to  the  power  to  issue

legislative  regulations  giving  more  concrete  meaning  to
broad  statutory  terms,  and  also  to  the  power  of interpreta-
tion  vested  in  independent  agencies  under  a  wide  range
of  regulatory  legislation.  E.g.,  Gray  v.  Powell,  314  U.S.
402  (1941);  National  Labor  Relations  Board  vy.  Hearst
Publications,  Inc.,  322  U.S.  111  (1944).  The  Attorney  Gen-
eral’s  bald  assertion  (Br.  p.  117)  that  interpretative  rule-
making  falls  within  the  executive  prerogative  is  supported
by neither  his  own  reasoning  nor  the  citation  of  authority.
In  the  court  below,  Judge  MacKinnon  expressed  the
opinion  that  Section  437(b)  is  an  unconstitutional  inva-
sion  of  the  prerogatives  of  the  Executive  Department  be-
cause  it  provides  that  an  individual  who  ‘‘acts  in  good
faith  in  accordance  with’’  an  advisory  opinion  of  the  Com-
mission  ‘‘shall  be  presumed  in  compliance  with’’  the  rele-
vant  laws.  Judge  MacKinnon  reached  this  conclusion  be-
cause  he  read  Section  437(b)  as  an  attempt  to  give  the
Commission  power  to  grant  immunity  from  prosecution.
This  characterization  misconceives  both  the  purpose  and
effect  of  Section  437f(b).  Section  437f(b)  does  not  au-
thorize  the  Commission  to  give  immunity  from  prosecu-
tion.  The  discretion  to  prosecute  or  not  to  prosecute  is
vested  in the  Executive  alone.  The  effect  of Section  437f(b)
is  to  set  forth  in  more  detail  the  elements  of  the  crimes
defined  in  the  sections  of  the  Criminal  Code  and  Internal
Revenue  Code  listed  in  Section  437f(b),  particularly  the
element  of  criminal  intent.  In  substance,  Section  437f(g)
provides  that  one  who  does  an  act  prohibited,  or  fails  to
perform  a  duty  prescribed,  by those  sections  shall  be  guilty
of  a  crime  if  he  knew  that  he  was  violating  the  law  or
failed  to  obtain  an  official  opinion  from  the  source  provided
by  law.  Conversely,  the  accused  commits  no  crime  if  he
acts  in  reliance  upon  that  advice,  unless  he  acts  in  bad
faith  because  he  knows  the  advice  to  be  erroneous.  Section
437f(b)  delimits  the  scope  of  the  crimes  much  as  the  use

118

119

of  the  word  ‘‘willfully’?  would  have  circumscribed  them
if  Congress  had  used  the  word  and  the  courts  provided  a
definition  of  wilful  violation  along  with  lines  indicated.
E.g.  Hargrove  v.  United  States,  67  F.2d  820  (Sth  Cir.  1933).
It  provides  a  form  of  statutory  defense  which  the  best
opinion  supports  in  appropriate  cases.  LaFave  and  Scott,
Criminal  Law  (1972)  365  et  seq.  The  unfortunate  drafts-
manship  should  not  affect  the  constitutional  question.®
Legislation  is  to  be  given  an  interpretation  which  avoids
constitutional  doubts.  Civil  Service  Commission  v.  Letter
Carriers  Union,  413  U.S.  548,  571  (1973) ; Almeida-Sanchez
v.  United  States,  413  U.S.  266,  272  (1973);  Ashwander  v.
Tennessee  Valley  Authority,  297  U.S.  288,  348  (1936)
(Brandeis,  J.,  concurring).

B.  The  Constitution  Does  Not  Bar  Dividing  Among
the  President  and  the  Senior  Officials  of the  House
and  Senate,  the  Responsibility  of Appointing  Mem-
bers  of an  Independent  Commission  Having  Unique
a  ‘‘Watch  Dog’’  Over  the  Political
Duties  as
Activities  of the  President  and  Members  of Congress.

Two  related  constitutional  conclusions  flow  from  the
nature  of  such  of  the  Commission’s  duties  as  we  have
described.

First,  they  are  not  exclusively  ‘‘executive’’  in  any  sense
of  the  word;  their  assignment  to  another  arm  of  govern-
ment  does  not  threaten  the  independence  or  functioning  of
the  Executive  Branch.

” ’

65  The  House  Report  on  Section  437f  states  that  it  provides
for  a  rebuttable  presumption  of  compliance  which  would  not  fore-
close  executive  prosecution.
H.  Rep.  93-1239,  p.  9  (1974).  The
language  of  Section  437f(b)  seems  ill  chosen,  but  it  is  not  incon-
sistent  with  the  view  that  the  presumption  of  eompliance  can  be
rebutted  only  by  showing  that  the  defendant  did  not  act  in  good
faith  reliance  upon  the  advisory  opinion.
In  this  respect,  Judge
MacKinnon’s  interpretation  seems  entirely  correct.

Second,  the  duties  of  the  Conimission  discussed  above
are  plainly  such  as  might  be  constitutionally  performed
by  an  independent  agency  whose  members  are  appointed
by the  President,  subject  to  confirmation  by  the  Senate.

In  our  view,  the  Commission  is  an  independent  agency
removed  from  the  control  of both  the  Executive  and  Legis-
lative  Departments  in  the  performance  of  its  responsi-
bilities  (except,  possibly,  those  duties  involving  the  furnish-
ing  of  information  and  recommendations  for  new  legis-
lation).  We  do  not  understand  the  Attorney  General’s
casual  assumption  (Br.  p.  110)  that  the  Commission  is
‘‘an  arm  of the  Congress,  a  ‘legislative  agency.’  ’’  Neither
Congress  nor  the  President  has  any  right  to  direct  the
Commission’s  performance  of its  statutory  duties.  Neither
could  properly  attempt  to  exert  influence.
Neither,  we
infer,  has  a power  of removal.  Cf.  Wiener  v.  United  States,
357  U.S.  349  (1958).  The  Secretary  of  the  Senate  and  the
Clerk  of  the  House  have  no  votes  in  the  Commission.
The  Attorney  General  misreads  the  statute  when  he  states
(Br.  p.  110,  n.  68)  that  ‘‘The  Commission  must  submit
proposed  legislation  to  Congress  before  circulating  it  to
any  other  agency  or  executive  official.’’
Section  437d
explicitly  states  that  the  Commission  shall  transmit  a  copy
‘“‘concurrently.’’  Similarly,  budget  requests  are  to be  trans-
mitted  ‘‘concurrently,’?  2 U.S.C.  §437(d)(1),  and  the  Com-
mission’s  annual  report  is  to  be  made  ‘‘to  the  President
of  the  United  States  and  to  each  House  of  the  Congress.’’
2  U.S.C.  §437e.  There  is  no  suggestion  here  of  subordi-
nation  of  the  Executive.  Nor  can  we  follow  the  Attorney
General  in  reading  subserviency  to  the  Congress  into  the
reservation  of  power  to  either  House  to  disapprove  legis-
lative  rules  or  regulations  issued  pursuant  to  a  delegation
of legislative  power.  The  arrangement  substantially  paral-
lels  the  old  provision  for  disapproving  Presidential  re-
organization  plans  and  the  current  statutes  governing

120

121

5  U.S.C.  §906;  28  U.S.C.
rules  issued  by  this  Court.
§42072,  2075.  Senator  Scott,  who  initiated  the  plan,  and
other  members  of  Congress  repeatedly  described  the  FIXC
as  an  ‘‘independent  commission.’’*  The  words  with  which
the  Court  described  the  Federal  Trade  Commission  fit
precisely  :

The  commission  is  to  be  non-partisan;  and  it  must,
from  the  very  nature  of  its  duties,  act  with  entire
It  is  charged  with  enforcement  of  no
impartiality.
policy  except  the  policy  of  the  law.  Humphrey's
Executor  v.  United  States,  295  U.S.  602,  624  (1935).

The  question  thus  comes  down  to  whether  the  Constitu-
tion  imposes  a  rigid  rule  that  all  the  members  of  an
independent  commission  performing  duties  not  belonging
exclusively  to  the  Executive  Department  must  be  appointed
by  the  President,  even  though  his  personal  interests  and
partisan  loyalties  will  be  involved  in  much  of  the  Commis-
sion’s  business  and  his  sole  selection  of  all  the  members
must  tend  to  reduce  public  confidence  in  the  impartiality
of  decisions  favoring  his  interests.

The  problem  facing  Congress  was  far  from  abstract.  The
six  voting  members  of  the  Commission  were  to  be  named
in  1975.  In  November  1976,  a  President  and  Vice  President,
one  third  of  the  Senate,  and  the  entire  membership  of  the
House  of Representatives  would  be  elected.  The  incumbent
President  would  be  seeking  nomination  and  election.  He
would  be leading  his  Party  in all the  campaigns  for  Senator
and  Representative.  The  six  new  commissioners  would  be
rendering  interpretations  of a  new  law  which  might  well  be
thought  to  injure  one  major  party  and  assist  the  other.

*  See  120  Cong.  Ree,  818525  (daily  ed.  Oct.  8,  1974)  (remarks
of  Sen.  Seott);  Id.  at  1110328  (et.  10,  1974)  (Rep.  Hayes) ;
Id.  at  10331  (Rep.  Armstrong);  Id.  (Rep.  Frenzel);  Id.  at
110344  (Rep.  Heckler).

The  six  members  to  be  appointed  might  soon  be  required
to  certify  whether  the  incumbent  President  was  entitled  to
matching  funds  under  26  U.S.C.  §§9033-9036  in  seeking
nomination,  and  later,  to  determine  whether  his  Party  and
he,  himself,  were  eligible  for  payments  under  26  U.S.C.
§§9003-9005,  9008.  It  would  be  their  duty  to  make  careful
audits  of  his  accounts  and  obtain  repayment  when  found
due.  It  would  also  be  their  duty  to  receive  other  reports
from  him  as  a  candidate,  to determine  how  to  publish  them,
and  to  scrutinize  them  for  violations.  How  then  shouki  the
six  men  be  chosen?  No  one  free  from  constitutional  limi-
tations  would  suggest  that  the  soundest  course  would  be
to  have  all  six  chosen  by  the  chief  political  figure  with
whom  they  must  deal  and  whose  behavior  they  must
scrutinize.
Nor  would  anyone  argue  that  allowing  the
most  powerful  contestant  to  name  all  the  stewards  charged
with  enforcing  the  rules  would  be  the  best  way  to  build
public  confidence  in  the  stewards’  rulings.  To  face  such
ancient  truths  of  human  conduct  casts  no  aspersion  upon
any  individual.  The  unsoundness  of any  such  arrangement
might  appear  less  dramatic  if  a  time  were  to  come  when
an  incumbent  President  named  fewer  than  all  the  members,
but  the  inherent  weakness  would  remain.

The  method  of  selection  chosen  by  Congress  not  only
avoids  the  pitfalls  of  Presidential  selection  but  does  as
much  as  possible  to  build  confidence  in  the  Commission’s
independence  and  freedom  from  bias,  short  of the  probably
unconstitutional  step  of  providing  that  the  members  be
named  by  this  Court.  Section  437(c)(3)  includes  an  un-
usual  declaration  emphasizing  the  need  for  public  confi-
dence  in the members’  freedom  from  every  sort  of influence :

Members  shall  be chosen  on  the  basis  of their  maturity,
experience,  integrity,  impartiality,  and  good  judgment
and  shall  be chosen  from  among  individuals  who,  at  the

122

125

time  of their  appointment,  are  not  elected  or  appointed
officers  or  employees  in  the  executive,  legislative,  or
judicial  branch  of the  Government  of the  United  States.

Dividing  the  authority  to  select  the  six  voting  members
of  the  Commission  among  three  high  ranking  officials,
including  the  President  of  the  United  States,  was  caleu-
lated  to  remove  the  Commission  still  further  from  any
single  source  of  influence  and  to  guard  against  any  one
set  of  mind.  Still  further  assurance  is  contained  in  the
requirement  that  the  President  pro  tem  of  the  Senate  and
the  Speaker  of  the  House  select  ‘‘upon  the  recommenda-
.’’
tions  of the  majority  leader...  and  the  minority  leader  ..
It  surely  cannot  be  the  position  of  either  appellants  or
the  Attorney-General  that  the  constitutional  prerogatives
of  the  President  are  shaken  if  he  lacks  power  to  appoint
the  persons  who  will  be  charged  with  issuing  regulations
and  giving  interpretative  opinions  affecting  the  President’s
candidacy,  who  will  determine  the  President’s  eligibility
for  public  financing,  and  who  will  later  scrutinize  his
accounts  and  reports.  Few  Presidents  would  wish  so  un-
pleasant  a  duty.  In  the  end,  therefore,  the  constitutional
question  is  simply  whether  some  abstract  theory  prevents
an  unfamiliar  solution  to  a  new  and  unique  problem.

Nothing  in  the  theory  of  the  separation  of  powers  im-
poses  this  rigidity.  Eighteenth  century  students  of  Locke
and  Montesquieu  differed  in  their  understanding  of  the
doctrine,  but  the  prevailing  view  emphasized  that  the  doc-
trine  was  not  to  be  rigidly,  but  flexibly,  applied.  Sharp,
The  Classical  American  Doctrine  of ‘‘The  Separation  of
Powers,’’  2 U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  385  (1935).  Madison  himself
wrote  in  No.  47  of  the  Federalist  Papers  that—

His  [Montesquieu’s]  meaning  as  his  own  words  im-
part,  and  still  more  conclusively  as  illustrated  by the
example  in  his  eye,  can  amount  to  no  more  than  this,

that  where  the
WHOLE  power  of  one  department  is
exercised  by  the  same  hands  which  possess
the
WHOLE  power  of  another  department,  the  funda-
mental  principles  of  a  free  constitution  are  subverted.
The  Federalist,  No.  47,  Fairfield  ed.  1969,  p.  140.

Madison’s  appreciation  of  the  need  for  flexibility  ex-
tended  to  the  appointing  power.  He  did  indeed  observe,
as  stated  by  appellants  (Br.  p.  196)  that—

each  department  should  have  a  will  of  its  own,  and
consequently  should  be  so  constituted  that  the  mem-
bers  of  each  should  have  as  little  agency  as  possible
Jd.
in  the  appointment  of  members  of  the  others.
No.  51,  p.  159.

A  few  sentences  later,  however,  he  added  a  qualification
omitted  from  appellant’s  selection  which  we  think  should
govern  here:

Perhaps  such  a  plan  of  constructing  the  several  de-
partments  would  be  less  difficult  in  practice  than  it
may  in  contemplation  appear.  Some  difficulties,  how-
ever,  would  attend  the  execution  of  it.  Some  devi-
ations,  therefore,  from  the  principle  must  be  admitted.
(emphasis  added).  [bid.

This  pragmatic  approach  has  characterized  the  dispo-
sition  of  appointing  authority  from  the  beginning.  The
Chief  Executive  and  the  heads  of  executive  departments
make  most  appointments,  but  the  courts  have  upheld  dele-
gation  of  the  appointing  authority  to  the  Judicial  Depart-
ment  in  the  case,  not  only  of  judicial  functionaries  such
as  clerks  and  commissioners,  Jn  re  ITennen,  13  Pet.  230
(1839);  Rice  v.  Ames,  180  U.S.  371,  378  (1901),  but  of
executive  officials  such  as  supervisors  of  elections  and  a
temporary  United  States  Attorney.  Fx  parte  Siebold,  100
U.S.  371,  397-398  (1879);  United  States  v.  Solomon,  216  F.

124

125

Supp.  835  (1963).  Congress  has  repeatedly  imposed  a  wide
variety  of  restrictions  upon  the  President’s  freedom  of
selection,  even  confining  the  appointment  to  a  person  or
persons  nominated  by others.  See  Myers  v.  United  States,
272  U.S.  52,  264-275  (1926)  (Brandeis,  J.  dissenting).
From  the  time  of the  Second  Congress,  Congress  has  occa-
sionally  named  the  individuals  to  perform  important  func-
tions  by  assigning  the  functions  to  the  holders  of  another
office  even  though  the  new  duties  were  in  nowise  related
to  the  old.  E.g.,  Act  of  May  8, 1792,  1  Stat.  281,  creating
a  commission  consisting  of  the  Chief  Justice  of  the  United
States,  the  President  of the  Senace,  the  Secretary  of  State,
the  Secretary  of  the  Treasury  and  the  Attorney  General
‘‘to  purchase  the  debt  of  the  United  States.’’
See
for  a  collection  of  similar  instances,  Note,  Power  of  Ap-
pointment  to  Public  Office  under  the  Federal  Constitution,
42  Harv.  L.  Rev.  426  (1929).  Congress  has  also  named
individuals  to  public  positions  and  specified  ranks  in  the
Armed  Forces.  Ibid.  The  President  has  been  held  gener-
ally  to  have  the  sole  right  of  removal,  Myers  v.  United
States,  272  U.S.  52  (1926),  but  the  power  is  denied  where
the  duties  of  the  office  demand  a  high  degree  of  independ-
Iumphrey’s  Executor  v.  United  States,  295  U.S.  602
ence,
(1935);  Wiener  v.  United  States,  357  U.S.  349  (1958).  The
sum  of  legislative  and  executive  practice,  and  of  the  rare
judicial  rulings,  is  that  the  President,  generally  speaking,
exercises  both  the  power  to  appoint  and  to  remove,  but
deviations  from  the  principle  must  be  permitted  when  the
special  occasion  requires.

|

The  language  of  Article  2,  Section  2,  Clause  2  (p.  107
above)  will  bear  the  interpretation  which  time  and  experi-
ence  have  put  upon  it.  Initially,  there  was  debate  over
whether  the  Constitutional  Convention  should  adhere  to  the
method  of legislative  appointment  provided  by the  Articles
of  Confederation  and  many  State  Constitutions  or  should

give  the  appointing  power  to  the  President,
The  issue
seems  to  have  been  compromised  by  the  addition  of  the
proviso:  ‘‘Congress  may  by law...  ,’’ for  there  was  little
subsequent  discussion.

The  proviso  can  be  given  a  restrictive  interpretation
denying  Congress  the  power  to  make  appointments  even
though  it  permits  the  Congress  to  delegate  the  appointing
power  to one  of a  variety  of officials.  The  words  can  equally
well  be  taken  to  assume  that  Congress  may  of  course
exercise  itself  a  function  which  it can  vest  in  one  of  desig-
nated  officials.  No  question  of  principle  was  at  stake,
only  efficiency  and  convenience.  Although  Madison  argued
against  the  power,  other  members  of  the  Convention  held
that  it was  indeed  reserved  to  Congress.  1 Ann.  Cong.  472,
904,  513  (1789).  As  we  have  shown,  time  and  experience
have  given  the  words  the  latter  meaning.  The  power  of
Congress  was  also  sustained  in  the  only  judicial  ruling
to  come  to  our  attention,  United  States  v.  Cooper,  20  D.C.
104,  124  (1891).  See  generally,  Note,  Power  of  Appoint-
ment  to  Public  Office  Under  the  Federal  Constitution,
42  Harv.  L.  Rev.  416  (1929).

Under  Section  437¢  the  President  of  the  United  States,
the  Speaker  of  the  House  of  Representatives,  and  the
President  pro  tem.  of  the  Senate  make  the  initial  selee-
tions,  but  the  vote  of a  majority  in both  Houses  of Congress
makes  the  appointment  the  action  of  Congress  at  the  final
step.

We  address  ourselves  only  to  appointments  to  an  inde-
pendent  agency,  and  only  to  the  unique  circumstances  in
which  the  appointments  are  to  a  watch  dog  commission
which  must  serutinize  the  conduct  of  the  President  and
members  of Congress  as  political  candidates,  and  deal  with
them  at  arms’  length.  In  appointments  to  positions  in  the
Executive  Department  the  principle  stated  by  Madison
—that  the  members  of  each  department  should  be  as  little

126

agents  as  possible  in  the  appointment  of  members  of  the
other—supports  the  FECA  practice.  Madison  was  con-
cerned  with  the  dangers  of excessive  loyalty  to  the  appoint-
ing authority.  In  providing  for  FECA  that  danger  dictated
the  unique  division  of  authority  which  Congress  achieved.
In  creating  the  Federal  Elections  Commission  te  imple-
ment  one  of  the  great  measures  of  political  reform  intro-
duced  in  this  century  Congress  faced  a  special  occasion.
The  Commission  will  occupy  a  unique  role  in  our  politics
and  government.  It  can  succeed  only  if  its  complete  inde-
pendence  and  freedom  from  bias  or  predilection  is  trusted
by both  rival  candidates  and  citizenry.  Dividing  the  power
of  appointment  so  that  no  more  than  two  of  six  members
would  ever  owe  their  position  to  a  single  source  is  an
important  step  in  enabling  the  Commission  to  build  that
essential  trust.

Conclusion

The  judgment  should  be  affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted,

ArcuiBaLp  Cox

Counsel  for Amici  Curiae

October  1975

IN  THE
Supreme  Court  of the United  States
OcToBEeR  TERM,  1975

Nos.  75-436
75-437

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY,  ET  AL.,  Appellants
V.

Francis  R.  VALeo,  ET  AL.,  Appellees

On  Appeals  from  the  United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the
District  of  Columbia  Circuit  and  the  United  States
District  Court  for  the  District  of Columbia

MOTION  OF  UNITED  STATES  SENATOR
LEE  METCALF
TO  FILE  A BRIEF  AS  AMICUS  CURIAE

United  States  Senator  Lee  Metcalf  respectfully  re-
quests  leave  of  this  Court  to  file  the  accompanying
brief  amicus  curiae,  which  is  restricted  to  an  analysis
of the  public  financing  provisions  contained  in the  Fed-
eral  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  (Pub.  L.  92-225,
86  Stat.  3,  Codified  in  Titles  2,  18,  47,  U.S.C.—herein-
after  referred  to as  ‘‘the  FECA”’),  the Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974  (Pub.  L. 93-443,  83
Stat.  1263  Codified  in  Titles  2, 5, 18,  26,  and  47,  U.S.C.

i

—hereinafter  referred  to  as  “‘the  FECA  Amend-
ments’’)  and  Subtitle  H of the  Internal  Revenue  Code
of  1954  (Pub.  L.  92-178,  85  Stat.  562  as  amended  by
Pub.  L.  93-53,  87  Stat.  138,  as  amended  by the  FECA
amendments  26  U.S.C.  §§ 9001  et  seq.—hereinafter  re-
ferred  to  as  ‘‘Subtitle  H’’).  Said  brief  amicus  curiae
supports  certain  propositions  advanced  by  appellants
and  also  supports  at  least  one  position  expected  to  be
advanced  by appellees.  It  is  filed  in  accord  with  Rule
42(2)  of  the  Rules  of  this  Court.  Written  consent  of
counsel  for  all parties,  including  the  written  authoriza-
tion  of  the  Solicitor  General,  has  been  obtained  and
true  copies  of  said  letters  of  consent  have  been  filed
with  the  Clerk  of this  Court.

The  constitutional  issues  before  this  Court  in  the
instant  action  are  at  the  very  heart  of  our  democratic
process  in  that  they  touch  upon  almost  every  facet  of
political  participation  and  expression.  Among  these
issues  are  certain  questions  of first  impression  regard-
ing  the  procedures  by which  an  individual  becomes  a
candidate  for  Federal  elective  office  and  can  solicit  or
receive  public  monies  for  such  a  candidacy.  Movant
submits  that  his  legislative,  political  and  practical  ex-
perience  regarding  such  issues  can  considerably  assist
this  Court’s  deliberation  in the  instant  case.

Movant  is  a United  States  Senator  from  the  State  of
Montana  and  is a  citizen  of  the  State  of Montana  and
of the  United  States  and  is eligible  to  vote  in elections
for  the  office  of President  of the  United  States.  He  has
a long standing  personal  and  legislative  interest  in pub-
lic  financing  procedures  for  elections  which  dates  back
to  involvement  with  the  formulation  of  the  Presiden-
tial  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  of  1966,  Pub.  L.
89-809,  80  Stat.  1539,  and  introduction  of  one  of  the

first  comprehensive  public  financing  proposals  for  fed-
eral  elections  in the  Congress  (S. 1390,  90th  Cong.,  1st
Sess. )

Movant  took  part  in this  action  below  by submitting
a  brief  amicus  curiae  and  by  participating  in  oral
argument  through  counsel.  See  e.g.  Buckley  v.  Valeo,
No.  75-1061,  U.S.D.C.A.,  Aug.  15,  1975,  Slip  Op.  at
1505,  1561  and  1605.

While  Movant  believes  and  argues  that  the  public
financing  provisions  cited  above  are  unconstitutional,
Movant  has  a  special  concern  that,  if  this  Court  so
holds,  neither  the  holding  nor  the  Court’s  language
should  preclude  the  Congress  from  devising  and  en-
acting  ©  public  financing  reform  which  does  not  con-
tain  the  flaws  of  the  challenged  statutes.

For  the  above  reasons,  this  motion  to  file  the  accom-

panying  brief  amicus  curiae  should  be granted.

Respectfully  submitted,

Lee  METCALF

Statistical  Abstract  of the  United  States,  1974

IN  THE
Supreme  Court  of the United  States
OcTOBER  TERM,  1975

Nos. 75-436
75-437

JAMES  L.  BUCKLEY,  ET  AL.,

Appellants

Vv.

FRANCIS  R.  VALEO,  ET  AL.,

Appellees

On  Appeals  from  the  United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the
District  of  Columbia  Circuit  and  the  United  States
District  Court  for  the  District  of Columbia

BRIEF  FOR  AMICUS  CURIAE
UNITED  STATES  SENATOR  LEE  METCALF  ''

This  brief  is  filed  by  United  States  Senator  Lee
Metcalf  contingent  upon  the  Court’s  granting  the  fore-
going  Motion  for  Leave  to File  a  Brief  Amicus  Curiae.
His  interest  is set  forth  in  that  Motion.

*  Amicus  Metcalf  has  asked  leave  of  this  Court  to  address  only
the  constitutional  questions  presented  with  respect  to  the  public
financing  provisions  in  the  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and
Subtitle  H.

2

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

Representative  democratic  government  should  ensure
for  all  citizens,  to  the  maximum  extent  feasible,  equal
access  to,  and  an  equal  opportunity  to  participate  in,
the  process  by  which  officials  of  the  government  are
selected.  Public  financing  of  elections  is  a  potentially
effective  way  to  achieve  this  objective.  Procedures  can
be  enacted  within  the  bounds  of  the  Constitution  to
utilize  public  monies  to  significantly  reduce  the  dis-
proportionate  influence  of  certain  segments  of  society
in  the  nomination  and  election  of  public  officials.  Such
procedures  also  can  constructively  assist  in the  presen-
tation  to  the  electorate  of  diverse  viewpoints.

Although  Congress  has  the  authority  to  provide  for
public  subsidization  of  Federal  election  campaigns,
elements  of  the  statutes  challenged  in  the  instant  ac-
tion  substantially  abridge  certain  constitutionally  pro-
tected  rights  and  freedoms,  and  therefore  should  be
subjected  to  strict  judicial  serutiny.

Specifically,  the  public  financing  provisions  of  the
challenged  statutes  violate  First  Amendment  rights  of
freedom  of  speech  and  association.  Minor  parties,
new  parties,  and  independent  candidates  are  dis-
criminated  against  by  the  inclusion  of  eligibility  and
allocation  formulae  that  violate  Fifth  Amendment  due
process—equal  protection  requirements.  Less  affluent
members  of  society  are  invidiously  denied  an  equal
opportunity  to  participate  in the  electoral  process.  The
general  election  public  financing  provisions  of the chal-
lenged  statutes  give  the  major  parties  a  permanent
monopoly  over  the  presidency  by freezing  the  political
status  quo.  Such  unequal  intervention  by the  govern-
ment  runs  directly  counter  to  numerous  constitutional

3

safeguards  that  ensure  all  citizens  a  right  to  equality
of  opportunity  to  participate  in  the  political  process.
These  challenged  public  financing  provisions  are
neither  rationally  related  in  fact  to  the  permissible
objectives  sought  by their  enactment  nor  the  least  re-
strictive  means  available  to  accomplish  the  reforms
sought.

This  Court  should  reverse  the  courts  below  and  find
the  challenged  public  financing  provisions  unconstitu-
tional.

ARGUMENT

I.  CONGRESS  HAS  AUTHORITY  UNDER  THE  CONSTITUTION
TO  REGULATE  PRESIDENTIAL  ELECTIONS  AND  TO  ENACT
PUBLIC  FINANCING  PROCEDURES  FOR  SUCH  ELECTIONS

The  history  of involvement  of the  legislative  branch
and  the  judicial  branch  in  the  area  of  election  law  re-
form  and  regulation  is extensive.’

Article  I, § 4 of the  Constitution  states:

The  Times,  Places  and  Manner  of  holding  Elec-
tions  for  Senators  and  Representatives,  shall  be
prescribed  in  each  State  by the  Legislature  there-
of;  but  the  Congress  may  at  any  time  by  Law
make  or  alter  such  Regulations.  ..  .

This  Court  as  early  as  1880°  and  1884*  upheld  fed-

2See,  Congressional  Digest  (February,  1974),  The  Question  of

Federal  Financing  of  National  Election  Campaigns  at  36.

* Ex  parte  Siebold,  100  U.S.  371  (1880).

*  Ex  parte  Yarbrough,  110  U.S.  651  (1884).  It  is  interesting  to
note  that  this  decision  found  that  a  right  to  protect  the  election  of

4

3)

eral  statutes  forbidding  corruption,  fraud  and  violence
in Congressional  elections.

In  Smiley  v.  Holm,  285  U.S.  355  (1932)  this  Court
considered  the  scope  of Congressional  authority  under
Article  I, §4 with  Chief  Justice  Hughes  stating  for  a
unanimous  Court  that  the  words  ‘‘Times,  Places  and
Manner’’  as  found  therein  are:

compi  ‘.ensive  words  [which]  embrace  authority
to  provide  a  complete  code  for  congressional  elec-
tions,  not  only  as  to  times  and  places,  but  in  rela-
tion  to  notices,  registration,  supervision  of voting,
protection  of  voters,  prevention  of  fraud  and  cor-
rupt  practices,  counting  of  votes,  duties  of inspec-
tors  and  canvassers,  and  making  and  publication
of election  returns;  in short,  to enact  the  numerous
requirements  as  to  procedure  and  safeguards
which  experience  shows  are  necessary  in  order  to
enforce  the  fundamental  right  involved.°

The  Court  in  Smiley  further  concluded  that  Article
I, Section  4 gave  the  Congress  ‘‘a  general  supervisory
power  over  the  whole  subject  [of  congressional  elec-
tions].’’°

The  proposition  that  federal  control  exists  over  pres-
idential  elections  was  established  by this  Court’s  deci-
sion  in  Burroughs  vy.  United  States,  290  U.S.  534
(1934).  This  position  has  been  reaffirmed  in  succeed-
ing  decisions.  Oregon  vy.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  124
(1970).

federal  officials  could  be  sustained  as  an  inherent  attribute  of
sovereignty—at  666-667.

°  285  U.S.  355,  366  (1932).

*  Id.  367.

In  United  States  v.  Classic,  313  U.S.  299  (1941),  this
Court  overturned  Newberry  v.  United  States,  256  U.S.
232  (1921),’  and  held  that  Congress  has  authority  to
.
regulate  primaries.  An  eight-man  majority  held  “‘.  .
the  authority  of  Congress,  given  by  [Art.  I]  §4,  in-
cludes  the  authority  to  regulate  primaries  when,  as  in
this  case,  they  are  a  step  in the  exercise  by the  people
of their  choice  of representatives  in  Congress.’’  *

Considerable  authority  supports  the  power  of  Con-

gress  to  expend  public  monies  for  election  reform.
Article  I, § 8, Cl.  1 of the  Constitution  states:

The  Congress  shall  have  the  Power  To  lay and  Col-
lect  Taxes...  to...  provide  for  the...  general
Welfare  of  the  United  States.

As  found  by the  Court  below,  this  provision  does  not
restrict  Congress  to express  grants  of legislative  power
found  in the  Constitution.
Buckley  v.  Valeo  No.  75-1061
U.S.  D.C.A.,  Aug.  15,  1975  Slip  Op.  1561.  See  also,
United  States  v.  Butler,  297  U.S.  1, 66  (1936)  ; Steward
Machine  Co.  v.  Davis,  301  U.S.  548  (1937)  ; and  Helver-
ing v.  Davis,  301  U.S.  619  (1937).

Congress  has  wide  ranging  authority  to  determine
what  expenditures  will  promote  the  general  welfare.
Election  reform  and  control  is a  permissible  objective
for  expenditures  under  such  authority.  Bullock  v.
Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  148-149  (1972).°

*This  Court  split  four  to  four  on  whether  Congress  had  the

constitutional  power  to  regulate  primaries.

Accordingly,  there  is considerable  precedent  for  the
Congress  to  regulate  not  only  presidential  primary
and  general  elections,  but  also  to initiate  public  financ-
ing reforms.”

IL  THE  PUBLIC  FINANCING  PROVISIONS  INCORPORATED  IN
THE  FECA,  THE  FECA  AMENDMENTS  AND  SUBTITLE  H
VIOLATE  CERTAIN  FUNDAMENTAL  CONSTITUTIONAL
RIGHTS  iNCLUDING  THE  RIGHT  TO  EQUALITY  OF OPPOR-
TUNITY  TO  PARTICIPATE  IN  THE  ELECTORAL  PROCESS

The  process  by  which  a  democratic  government  se-
lects  its  public  officials  is  the  very  heart  of  its  legit-
imacy.  The  judicial  branch  has  assumed  a  particularly
important  role  in  guaranteeing  that  all  citizens  have
an  equal  opportunity  to  participate  in  the  electoral
process.

In  Williams  v.  Rhodes  393  U.S.  23  (1968),  this  Court
specifically  recognized  that  fundamental  rights  are  in-
volved  in varied  ways  in  the  electoral  process  and  that
a strict  scrutiny  test  should  be applied  to state  infringe-
ment  in  this  area.  This  Court  in  Rhodes  rejected  the
state’s  argument  that  it  had  a  legitimate  interest  in
limiting  the  number  of  parties  on  the  ballot  to  avoid
political  fragmentation  on  the  grounds  that  the  chal-
lenged  statute  made  it ‘‘virtually  impossible’  for a new
or  minor  party  to  gain  access  to  the  ballot.”’  In  invali-
dating  the  State  of Ohio’s  ballot  access  law,  the  major-
ity held:

“The  right  to  form  a  party  for  the  advancement
of political  goals  means  little  if a party  can  be kept

Professor  Albert  J.  Rosenthal,  in  Campaign  Financing  and
the  Constitution,  9  Harv.  J.L.  359  (1972)  makes  a  compelling
argument  that  the  commerce  clause,  Art  I, § 8, Cl.  3, and  the  nee-
essary  and  proper  clause,  Art.  I,  §8,  Cl.  18,  may  also  serve  as
authority  for  public  financing  of  elections.

*?  393  U.S.  23  at  24.

off the  election  ballot  and  thus  denied  an  equal  op-
portunity  to  win  votes.  393  U.S.  23, 31  (1968)

The  importance  of the  strict  scrutiny  test  in the  elec-
toral  area  has  been  recently  reaffirmed  in  Kusper  v.
Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51  (1973),  where  this  Court  struck
down  a  State  of  Illinois  statute  which  prohibited  an
individual  from  voting in a primary  of a political  party
if  he  had  voted  within  the  preceding  twenty-three
months  in  another  party’s  primary.  The  majority  in
Kusper  recognized  that,  as  in  Rhodes,  the  mere  show-
ing  of  a  legitimate  state  interest  (the  prevention  of
raiding  by parties  in  AKusper)  is insufficient  if a  statu-
tory  scheme  substantially  restricts  a  voter’s  freedom
of  association  and  ability  to  participate  effectively  in
the  political  process.

Application  of the  strict  scrutiny  standard  in the  in-
stant  action  will  clearly  show  that  the  challenged  stat-
utes  should  be  invalidated.

A.  The  Primary  Election  Public  Financing  Provisions  of  the
Challenged  Statutes  Invidiously  Deny  the  Less  Affluent
Members  of  Society  an  Equal  Opportunity  To  Participate
in  the  Federal  Electoral  Process,  and  Should  Be  Strictly
Scrutinized.

|W Je cannot  close  our  eyes  to  the  fact  ...  that  the
practical  influence  of the  choice  of candidates  at
the primary  may  be so  great  as  to affect  profoundly
the  choice  at  the  general  election...  .”

In  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  144  (1972),  this
Court,  in  invalidating  a  Texas  filing  fee  scheme,  held
that  legislative  classifications  which  deny  certain  per-
sons  an  equal  opportunity  to  participate  effectively  in
the  political  process  on  the  basis  of  wealth  must  be

2  U8.  v.  Classic,  313  U.S.  299,  319  (1941).

8

9

‘‘celosely  scrutinized,  and  found  reasonably  necessary
to  the  accomplishment  of  legitimate  state  interests  in
order  to  pass  constitutional  muster.’’  Similarly,  in
Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709,  716-18  (1974)  this  Court
in reviewing a filing fee system  similar  to that  reviewed
in  Bullock  indicated  that  legislation  which  classifies
candidates  as  serious  or  frivolous  solely  on  the  basis  of
their  ability  to  raise  funds  invidiously  denies  certain
voters  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  the  candidate  of
their  choice  and  should  be  subjected  to  strict  scrutiny.

Amicus  Metcalf  submits  that  the  primary  election
provisions  of Subtitle  H, like  the filing fees  invalidated
in  Bullock  and  Lubin  v.  Panish,  deny  the  less  affluent
members  of  society  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  the
candidate  of their  choice,  and  therefore  should  be  sub-
jected  to  strict  judicial  scrutiny.

Under  this  chapter  of  Subtitle  H™  any  individual
who  seeks  nomination  for  elections  to  be  President
and,  (a)  takes  the  action  necessary  under  the  law  of  a
state  to  qualify  for  nomination  for  election,  (b)  re-
ceives  contributions  or  incurs  qualified  campaign  ex-
penses,  or  (¢)  gives  consent  for  any  other  person  to
receive  contributions  or  to incur  qualified  campaign  ex-
penses  in his  or  her  behalf  is defined  as  a  candidate.  26
U.S.C,  § 9032(2)  (1975  Pam,  No.  1).

As  noted  infra,  the  Secretary  of  the  Treasury  is  di-
rected  to  maintain  a  separate  account  for  presidential
primaries.  After  a  determination  has  been  made  by the
Secretary  that  there  are  sufficient  funds  to cover  the  re-
quirements  of the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Act

'  Chapter  96,  the  Presidential  Primary  Matching  Payment  Ac-

count,  Pub.  L.  93-443,  88  Stat.  1297,  26  USC  § 9031  et  seq.

(the  general  presidential  election  fund),’*  payments
are  to  be  made  from  the  matching  fund  account  upon
certification  of  the  Federal  Election  Commission.  26
U.S.C.  § 9036  (1975  Pam.  No.  1).  The  Secretary  is re-
quired  to ‘‘achieve  an  equitable  distribution’’  of funds
when  making  payments  to candidates  of the same  party
considering  ‘‘the  sequence  in  which  such  certifications
are  received.’’  26  U.S.C.  §9037(b)  (1975  Pam.  No.  1)
Presumably,  therefore,  early  certification  is desirable.”

Assuming  sufficient  funds  are  available,  the  federal
government  will  match,  dollar  for  dollar  up to $250  per
contributor,  the  funds  an  eligible  candidate  raises  from
private  contributions  for  use  in primary  campaigns.  26
U.S.C.  § 9034.  To  become  eligible  for  these  matching
grants,  a  candidate  must  raise  $5,000  in  private  con-
tributions  in  each  of  20  states,’  or  a  total  of $100,000.

4  Pub,  L.  92-178,  85  Stat.  563,  as  amended  by Pub.  L. 93-443,  26

U.S.C.  §§ 9001  et  seq.

"The  amount  of  discretion  given  the  Secretary  of  Treasury,
undoubtedly  a  political  appointee  of  a  ‘‘major’’  party,  to  adminis-
ter  the  public  financing  provisions  of  FECA,  the  FECA  Amend-
ments  and  Subtitle  H  is considerable  and  gives  rise  to  serious  con-
stitutional  and  practical  questions  regarding  the  operation  of  this
statutory  scheme.  For  example,  how  is  the  Secretary  to  determine
what  amount  of  funds  should  be  set  aside  for  post  election  pay-
ments  for  new  parties?  How  is  the  Secretary  to  allocate  funds  for
primary  elections  if  insufficient  monies  are  accumulated  from  the
income  tax  return  check-offs  (26  U.S.C.  6096)  ?

****As  with  the  geographical  distribution  requirements  in  the
Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  [Chapter  95]  this  re-
quirement  appears  unconstitutional.  The  requirement  that  seed
money  be  raised  in  20  states  is even  less  rational  than  the  require-
ment  that  a  candidate  be  on  the  ballot  in  ten  states  [the  require-
ment  in  Chapter  95].  Certainly,  there  is no  relationship  to  ultimate
success  in  the  primaries.  .  ..  The  statutes  would  permit  a  candidate
to  obtain  matching  funds  if he  received  the  necessary  contributions
from  people  in  the  20  smallest  states  while  excluding  candidates

11

Id.  at  (9033.  The  total  amount  of  public  funds  such  a
qualified  candidate  can  receive  is $5 million.  26  U.S.C.
§ 9034  (1975  Pam.  No.  1), and  18 U.S.C,  608(¢)  (1) (a).
This  matching  grant  system  is designed  and  will  op-
erate  to  grant  some  candidates  substantially  greater
It prima  facie  dis-
subsidies  than  are  accorded  others.
criminates  against  candidates  supported  by  the  less
affluent  segments  of  the  population.

Assume  arguendo  that  X and  Y are  candidates  in an
early  presidential  primary,  and  that  in  this  primary
each  is  permitted  to  spend  $300,000.  Assume  further
that  to  date  candidate  X  has  been  able  to  solicit  con-
tributions  from  10,000  voters,  evenly  distributed  over
20  states,  none  of  whom  can  afford  to  contribute  more
than  $5 to  his  campaign.  Because  he  has  not  been  able
to  raise  at  least  $100,000,  X  would  not  qualify  for
matching  grants,  and  would  be  left  with  only  $50,000
with  which  to  continue  his  efforts  to  attract  increased
voter  support.

Assume  that  Candidate  Y,  on  the  other  hand,  has
solicited  support  from  1,500  voters,  evenly  distributed

who  obtained  contributions  in  the  19  largest  states.  Nevertheless,
balancing  the  interests,  since  it  is  in  the  area  of  primaries  where
the  greatest:threat  to  the  waste  of  public  funds  arises,  some  geo-
graphical  restrictions  might  be  appropriate.  It  is  submitted,  how-
ever,  that  these  restrictions  should  be  keyed  to  the  possibility  of
ultimate  suecess  at  the  election  with  reference  to  the  electoral
college  system.  A  redrafted  statute  which  contained  showing  of
interest  requirements  in  a  number  of  states  determined  as  a  func-
tion  of  a  low  percentage  of  electoral  college  votes  ( although  prob-
ably  politically  unacceptable  because  of  the  significance  in  any
such  formula  of  the  populated  states)  would  be  more  appropriate
and,  more  importantly,  would  more  adequately  protect  the  in-
terests  of  the  public  purpose  without  unnecessarily  interfering
with  voters’  rights.’’  Schwarz,  Public  Financing  of  Elections:  A
Constitutional  Division  of Wealth,  American  Bar  Association,  Spe-
cial  Comm.  on  Election  Reform  Publication  at  67-68  (1975).

over  20  states,  all  of  whom  can  afford  to  contribute
$100  to  his  campaign  fund.  Under  Subtitle  H,  eandi-
date  Y  is  eligible  for  matching  grants  and  would  be
entitled  to  a  $150,000  subsidy.  When  added  to  the
$150,000  he  has  received  from  private  contributors,
this  subsidy  would  give  him  the  maximum  amount
he  is  permitted  to  spend  in  the  primary,  or  $300,000.

In  short,  under  the  primary  election  provisions  of
Subtitle  H  candidate  X, who  has  more  than  five  times
the  voter  support  of  candidate  Y,  would  have  only
1/6th  the  amount  of  funds  available  to  candidate  Y
solely  because  of the relative  inability  of his supporters
to  contribute.  Thus,  on  the  basis  of the  relative  wealth
of their  supporters,  the  primary  election  provisions  of
Subtitle  H  will  operate  to  increase  candidate  Y's
chances  of  winning  this  early  primary,  and  of  ulti-
mately  securing  the  party’s  nomination,  while  corre-
spondingly  diminishing  candidate  X’s  chances.”

Amicus  Metcalf  respectfully  submits  that  it  is  thus
clear  that  the  primary  campaign  provisions  of  Sub-
title  H,  like  the  filing  fees  invalidated  in  Bullock  and
Lubin  vy.  Panish,  invidiously  discriminate  against  and
prima  facie  deny  the  less  affluent  members  of  this  so-
ciety  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  the  candidate  of
their  choice.”

17  See  infra  at  18-29  for  a  discussion  of  the  data  which  indi-
cates  that  differential  allocation  of  campaign  subsidies,  whether
according  to  electoral  results  or  pursuant  to  a  matching  grant
system,  denies  certain  candidates  an  equal  chance  of  winning,
which  in  turn  denies  their  supporters  an  equal  opportunity  to
elect  the  candidate  of their  choice.

**  Hypotheticals  much  more  extreme  than  the  above  certainly

are  possible  and  probable.  See  n.  19  infra.

13

Further,  such  a  discriminatory  matching  system  as
contained  in  Chapter  96  is  contra  to  the  equal  protec-
tion  ‘‘one  man-one  vote’’  principle’  developed  by
Baker  v.  Carr,  369  U.S.  186  (1962)  and  its  progeny.”
As  noted  by this  Court  ‘‘the  only  weighting  of  votes
sanctioned  by  the  Constitution  concerns  matters  of
representation,  such  as  the  allocation  of  Senators  ir-
respective  of  population  and  the  use  of  the  electoral
.  But  once  the
college  in  the  choice  of  a  President  .
class  of  voters  is chosen  and  their  qualifications  speci-
fied,  we  see  no  constitutional  way  by which  equality  of
voting  power  may  be  evaded.’’  Gray  v.  Sanders,  372
U.S.  368,  380,  381  (1963).

.

Amicus  Metealf  concedes  that  the  challenged  public
financing  statutes  in the  instant  action  only concern  the
offices  of president  and  vice  president  and that  the  elec-
toral  process  for  said  offices  does  involve  a  somewhat
weighted  selection  process  through  the  operation  of the
electoral  college.  Amicus  Metcalf  does  not,  however,
interpret  the  electoral  college  provisions  (Art.  IT,  $1
Cls.  1-4)  of the  Constitution  to permit  the  type  of dis-
crimination  contained  in  the  challenged  statutes  for
presidential  primary  elections,  which  are  not  men-
tioned  anywhere  in the  Constitution.

19  Ag  discussed  above,  the  $250  ‘‘triggering  mechanism’’  of Chap-
ter  96  is  highly  discriminatory.  For  example  an  individual  only
able  to  contribute  $1  to  a  candidate  would  be  able  to  generate  $1
of  potential  public  funding  (assuming  the  candidate  could  ever
meet  the  $100,000  threshold  of  contributions  of  $250  or  less  in  at
least  20  states).  An  individual  able  to  contribute  $250  to  a  qualified
candidate  would  be  able  to  ‘‘trigger’’  $250  in  public  money.  A
disparity  factor  of  250.

2°  See  og.  Gray  v.  Sanders,  372  U.S.  368  (1963),  Reynolds  v.
Sims,  377  US.  533  (1964);  Wesberry  v.  Sanders,  376  U.S.  1
(1964) ; Kirkpatrick  v.  Preisler,  394  U.S.  526  (1969)  and  Wells  v.
Rockefeller,  394  U.S.  542  (1969).

Even  if one  were  te  conclude  that  an  analogy  could
be  drawn  from  the  electoral  college  weighting  provi-
sions  for  presidential  general  elections  and  primary
elections,  Amicus  Metcalf  submits  the  type  of discrim-
ination  evidenced  by  the  challenged  statutes  is  of  a
higher  degree  as  it  affects  the  formation  of  political
parties  and  the  selection  of  candidates.  As  this  Court
held  in  Williams  v.  Rhodes:

The  right  to  form  a  party  for  the  advancement  of
political  goals  means  little  if  a  party  can  be  kept
off  the  election  ballot  and  thus  denied  an  equal  op-
portunity  to win  votes.  393  U.S.  23, 31  (1968)

Accordingly,  the  primary  matching  fund  provisions
of  Chapter  96  of  Subtitle  IT  should  be  strictly  seru-
tinized  and  found  to be violative  of the  Constitution.

B.  The  General  Election  Public  Financing  Provisions  of  the
Challenged  Statutes  Should  Be  Sirictly  Scrutinized.
1.  The  general  election  public  financing  provisions  of  the
challenged  statutes  substantially  abridge  and  unconsti-
tutionally  condition  the  First  Amendment  right  to  free
and  effective  political  association.

It is well  established  that  the First  Amendment  guar-
antees  individuals  a  right  to free  and  effective  associa-
tion  with  the  political  organization  or  party  of  their
choice.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  U.S.  51,  56-57  (1973).
This  Court  has  held  that  the  right  to  associate  for  the
advancement  of political  ideas  ranks  ‘‘among  our  most
precious  freedoms,’’  and  consistently  has  subjected  leg-
islation  which  burdens  or  inhibits  the  exercise  of  that
right  to  strict  scrutiny.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  supra;
Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23, 30  (1968).

Appellees  may  attempt  to  distinguish  this  line  of
precedent  on  the  ground  that  differential  allocation  of

14

15

campaign  subsidies  does  not  deprive  certain  candidates
of a  place  on  the  ballot,  or  otherwise  deprive  their  sup-
porters  of the  opportunity  to nominate  and  vote  for the
candidate  of  their  choice,  but  instead  simply  denies
them  equal  access  to  certain  government  benefits.  Such
argument  is  insubstantial  given  that  this  Court  has
often  held  that  First  Amendment  rights  are  protected
from  indirect  as  well  as  direct  abridgement.  Perry  v.
Sinderman,  408  U.S.  593  (1972);  Speiser  v.  Randall,
357  U.S.  513  (1958),  and  Frost  & Frost  Trucking  Co.
v.  Railroad  Commission,  271  U.S.  583,  593-94  (1926).

The  government  may  not,  absent  compelling  justifi-
cation,  condition  eligibility  for  benefits  in  a  manner
which  inhibits  the  exercise  or  requires  the  surrender  of
the  right  to  free  and  effective  political  association.
U.S.  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258  (1967)  ; Shelton  v.  Tucker,
364  U.S.  479  (1960).  See  American  Party  of Texas  v.
White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974)  (Denying  minor  party
candidates  a  place  on  absentee  ballots  is sufficient  cause
to  warrant  strict  scrutiny.)

This  Court  has  recognized:

[I]f the  government  could  deny  a  benefit  to  a  per-
son  because  of his constitutionally  protected  speech
or  associations,  his  exercise  of  those  freedoms
would  in  effect  be  penalized  and  inhibited.  This
would  allow  the  government  to  produce  a  result
which  [it]  could  not  command  directly.  Sperser  v.
Randall,  357  U.S.  513,  526.  Such  interference  with
constitutional  rights  is  impermissible.  Perry  v.
Sinderman,  408  U.S.  593,  597  (1972).

Amicus  Metealf  submits  that  the  general  election
provisions  of  Subtitle  H  unconstitutionally  condition
and  otherwise  impose  substantial  if  indirect  burdens

upon  the  First  Amendment  right  to  free  and  effective
political  association,  and  therefore  should  be subjected
to strict  judicial  scrutiny.

Most  significantly,  the  record  establishes  that  differ-
ential  funding  of major,  minor,  and  new  political  par-
ties  under  Subtitle  H is indeed  specifically  intended  to
deter  or  inhibit  the  exercise  by potential  presidential
candidates  of  the  right  to  free  and  effective  political
association.

Congressional  committee  reports  indicate  clearly
that  one  of  the  primary  purposes  of  the  general  elec-
tion  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  is to  discourage  or  penal-
ize  candidates  who  prefer,  for  ideological  or  other  rea-
sons,  to  affiliate  themselves  with  a  minor  or  new  polit-
ical  party  rather  than  a  major  party.  Commenting  on
the  eligibility  and  allocation  formulae  relating  to  gen-
eral  election  campaigns  contained  in  Subtitle  HI,  the
Senate  Rules  and  Administration  Committee  asserted
that  as  the  result  of the  operation  of these  formulae:

...  [alll  but  fringe  candidates  would  have  an  in-
centive  to  seek  a  major  party  nomination,  rather
than  run  as  a  minor  party  candidate,  so  as  to  be
eligible  for  the  full  level  of public  assistance  in the
general  election.  The  bill  would  thereby  have  a  co-
hesive  effect,  encouraging  different  factions  to
compete  and  work  out  coalitions  within  the  frame-
work  of a  basic  two-party  system.

If  a  candidate’s  supporters  clearly  constitute  a
mere  fringe  group,  with  no  prospect  of  appealing
to  a  large  mass  of  voters,  then  he  may  choose  to
run  as  a  minor  party  candidate.  But  it is unlikely
he  would  raise  many  matchable  contributions,  in
the  event  his  party  had  a  primary.  And  in the  gen-
eral  election,  he  will  only  be  eligible  for  a  grant
based  on  his  party’s  prior  voting  record.  Sen.  Rep.

16

17

No.  689,  93rd  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  at  8  (1974).  (Em-
phasis  added)

The  fact  that  Congress  specifically  intends  Subtitle  H
to  inhibit  the  exercise  of the  First  Amendment  right  to
free  and  effective  political  association  by discouraging
candidates  from  affiliating  with  a  minor  or  new  polit-
ical  party  clearly  warrants  strict  scrutiny  of the  provi-
sions  here  in question.

The  general  election  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  sim-
ilarly  inhibit  the  exercise  of and  unconstitutionally  con-
dition  the  first  amendment  rights  of the  individual  sup-
porters  of  minor  and  new  party  candidates.  Under
these  provisions,  unless  there  is  a  deficit  in  the  fund
from  which  campaign  subsidies  are  to  be  drawn,  the
government  provides  a  major  party  candidate  the  total
amount  he  is  permitted  to  spend.  Thus,  persons  who
support  a  major  party  candidate  are  effectively  freed
from  the  burden  of contributing  to his campaign.  Those
who  support  a  minor  or  new  party  candidate,  however,
are  denied  this  benefit  on  the  basis  of  their  political
affiliations.  Such  persons  must  be  willing  and  able  to
contribute  funds  for  campaign  purposes  from  their
private  resources  in order  to  ensure  that  the  candidate
or  political  party  of  their  choice  enjoys  financial  sup-
port  equal  to that  the  government  accords  major  party
candidates.

Subtitle  H  denies  the  candidates  and  supporters  of
minor  and  new  political  parties  equal  access  to govern-

*t  Although  Subtitle  H  was  substantially  modified  in  other  re-
spects,  the  particular  general  election  provisions  here  in  question
were  not  amended  following  the  submission  of  Senate  Report  93-
689.  Therefore  the  views  expressed  in  the  Report  are  relevant  to
the  issue  of  Congressional  intent  or  purpose  presented  by  the
instant  case.

ment  benefits  solely  because  of the  particular  ‘*choice”’
they  make  in  exercising  their  First  Amendment  right
to  free  and  effective  political  association.  The  basis  of
this  difference  in  treatment  is not  the  number  of  sup-
porters  evidenced  by  or  the  relative  size  of  a  candi-
date’s  party.  Subtitle  H  does  not  provide  for  a  strict
correlation  between  subsidies  awarded  and  the  current
level  of support  a  party  enjoys.

In conclusion,  the  eligibility  and  allocation  formulae
relating  to general  elections  embodied  in Subtitle  H are
specifically  intended  to discourage  or  inhibit  association
with  minor  and  new  political  parties,  and  to  channel
the  expression  of political  ideas  through  the  two  major
parties.  Unless  it  is  assumed  that  these  formulae  will
not  have  their  intended  effect,“  Subtitle  H  will  force
candidates  affiliated  with,  or  individuals  who  wish  to
support,  a minor  or  new  political  party  either  to forego
government  benefits  in  order  to  follow  the  precepts  of
their  political  beliefs,  or  to  abandon  those  beliefs  in
order  to  enjoy  the  fruits  of  those  benefits.  Such  per-
sons  will  be forced  by the  federal  government  to  pay  a
higher  price  than  the  supporters  of major  party  candi-
dates  to  support  effectively  the  candidate  or  political
party  of their  choice.”

Accordingly,  it is clear  that  the  general  election  pro-
visions  of  Subtitle  H  unconstitutionally  condition  and

=  It  should  be  noted  that  in  Speiser  v.  Randall,  357  U.S.  513
(1958)  this  Court  suggested  that  the  imposition  of  a  condition
upon  eligibility  for  government  benefits  similar  to  the  conditions
here  in  question  would  ‘‘inevitably’’  inhibit  the  exercise  of  First
Amendment  rights.  The  Court  also  emphasized  that  conditions  upon
government  benefits  which  operate  to  inhibit  the  exercise  of  First
Amendment  rights  cannot  be  sustained  absent  ‘‘compelling  justi-
fication.”’

*3  See  Discussion  infra  at  Part  II.C.

18

19

otherwise  substantially  inhibit  exercise  of  the  First
Amendment  right  to  free  and  effective  political  asso-
ciation.

2. The  general  election  provisions  of Subtitle  H deny  certain
individuals  an equal  opportunity
to elect  candidates  of
their  choice.

(a)  Subtitle  H  Denies  Minor  and  New  Party  Candi-

dates  an  Equal  Opportunity  of Winning.

In  numerous  decisions  this  Court  has  held  that  par-
ticipation  in  the  electoral  process,  including  exercise
of the  franchise,  association  with  a  political  organiza-
tion  or  party,  and campaign  activities  designed  to build
an  electoral  coalition  in  support  of  the  candidate  of
one’s  choice,  is  a matter  of  fundamental  importance  in
our  representative  form  of  government.  Williams  v.
Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968)  ; Harper  v.  Virginia  Board
of Elections,  383  U.S.  663  (1966);  Reynolds  v.  Sims,
377  U.S.  533  (1964).  See  also  Shakman  v.  Democratic
Organization  of Cook  County,  435  F.2d  267  (7th  Cir.
1970)  cert.  denied  402  U.S.  909  (1971),  modified  on
remand,  356  F.  Supp.  1241  (N.D.  Ill.  1972).  Courts
consistently  have  held  that  federal  or  state  action  which
imposes  substantial  and  unequal  burdens  on  persons
engaged  in  such  activities  must  thus  be  subjected  to
strict  judicial  scrutiny.
Kramer  vy.  Union  Free  School
Dist.,  395  U.S.  621  (1969);  Reynolds  v.  Sims,  supra.
In  particular,  courts  have  been  especially  diligent  in
strictly  scrutinizing  measures  which  deny  certain  per-
sons  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  the  candidate  of
their  choice  (for  example,  malapportionment  schemes
and  ballot  access  laws).

Under  Subtitle  H  of  Chapter  95  a  candidate  is  de-
fined  as  an  individual  who  has:  (1)  been  nominated  for

election  for  office  of  President  or  Vice  President  of
the  United  States  by a  major  party,  or  (2)  qualified
as a candidate  of a political  party  for  election  to either
office  in  ten  (10)  or  more  states.  If  the  Presidential
Election  Campaign  Fund  contains  sufficient  monies  *
major  party  candidates  are  entitled  to  receive  pre-
election  subsidies  for  use  in  the  general  election  cam-
paign  equal  to  the  total  amount  they  are  permitted  to
spend—$20  million  plus  periodic  adjustments  for  in-
flation.  26  U.S.C.  §§9002(6),  9004(a)(1);  18  U.S.C.
608(¢)(1)(B).  (1975  Pam.  No.  1)

*#**  As  noted,  the  definition  of  ‘candidate’  requires  that  a  candi-
date  of  a  minor  party  qualify  on  the  ballot  in  ten  or  more  states.
This  requirement  would  appear  constitutional  only  if  the  Court
tests  the  statute  in the  most  liberal  manner  by use  of  the  traditional
rational  relationship  test,  treating  the  legislation  as  an  ameliorative
act.

“*Even  under  the  traditional  test,  however,  there  would  not  ap-
pear  to  be  any  rational  relationship  between  the  number  of  states
chosen,  ten,  and  the  chance  of  suecess  of  the  candidate  in  the
election  through  the  electoral  college.  The  Act  does  not  require  that
the  ten  states  have  a  significant  number  of  electoral  votes.  Indeed,
the  statute  would  permit  a  candidate  to  receive  public  funding  if
he  were  on  the  ballot  in  the  ten  smallest  states  with  a  total  of  34
electoral  votes  but  exclude  a  candidate  who  had  obtained  ballot
position  in  the  nine  largest  states  with  a  total  of 245  electoral  votes.
“Moreover,  the  effect  of  funding  a  minor  party  candidate  who
had  obtained  ballot  position  in  ten  states  would  be  to  further
restrict  other  minor  parties  and  new  parties  since  once  funded  the
minor  party  could  then  use  the  funds  to  attract  voters  in  other
states  where  fledging  parties  were  attempting  to  gain  recognition.
It  therefore  would  appear  the  geographical  distribution  require-
ments  are  unconstitutional’’  (footnotes  omitted)  Schwarz,  supra
Also  see  Fleishman,  Public  Financing  of Election
n.  16  at  62-63.
Campaigns:  Constitutional  Constraints  on  Steps  Toward  Equality
of Political  Influence  of Citizens,  52  N.C.L.R.  349,  393-394  (1973).
*  See  pp.  25-26  infra  regarding  availability  of  monies  for  Sub-

title  H  distribution.

20

21

Candidates  of minor  political  parties  are  entitled  to
pre-election  subsidies  only  in an  amount  in  proportion
to their  party’s  vote  in the  preceding  presidential  elee-
tion  and  the  average  of the  votes  received  by the major
party  candidates.  26  U.S.C.  §§ 9002(7),  9004(a)  (2).
For  example,  this  ratio  would  be 4:1  if the  major  par-
ties  received  an  average  of forty  (40)  million  votes  and
the  minor  party  received  ten  (10)  million  votes.  Thus,
if  in  the  next  election  each  major  party  candidate  is
eligible  for  a  $20  million  subsidy,  the  minor  party’s
candidate  would  be  entitled  to $5 million.

The  candidate  of a  new  political  party  is not  eligible
for  any  pre-election  subsidization  for  campaign  ex-
penses.

Subtitle  H not  only  accords  a major  party  candidate
a  $20  million  subsidy,  but  entirely  frees  the  candidate
of  the  need  to  expend  substantial  amounts  of  time,
money,  manpower,  and  other  resources  in  fund-raising
drives.  Major  party  candidates  can  thus  devote  all
their  resources  to  the  effort  to  attract  voter  support.
Moreover,  the  record  indicates  that  the  availability  of
money  early  in  the  campaign  significantly  affects  both
a  candidate’s  decision  whether  or  not  to  run,  and  his
ability  to  launch  and  sustain  a  successful  campaign.
Findings  of fact,  ILB,  £1 13, 16.

In  contrast,  even  if  they  proceed  through  private
fund-raising  to  make  up  the  difference  between  their
subsidy  and  that  accorded  a  major  party  candidate,
minor  and  new  party  candidates  will  not  have  nearly
as  much  money  as  a  major  party  candidate  for  use  in
attracting  votes.  First,  the  most  a  minor  party  candi-
date  can  receive  under  Subtitle  H is approximately  $13

million.”  This  leaves  the  candidate  $7 million  short  of
the  major  party  entitlement.  Moreover,  even  if  the
candidate  is able  to make  up  that  difference,  he  inevit-
ably  will  have  $4 million  less  than  major  party  candi-
dates  to  spend  given  that,  on  the  average,  40%  of  the
funds  raised  from  private  contributions  must  be  used
to  cover  fund-raising  expenses.  Finding  of  Fact  IIB
50.  In  addition,  minor  and  new  party  candidates  will
have  to divert  a substantial  amount  of time  and  energy,
and  the  time  and  energy  of their  supporters,  from  the
effort  to win  votes  to the effort  to attract  contributions.
See  Findings  of  Fact  ITI  £113,  16.

The  record  establishes  that  the  relative  availability
of  money  has  some  effect  on  the  outcome  of  elections.
Finding  of Fact  I, £4.  Subtitle  H  is premised  on  Con-
gress’  judgment  that  the  relative  availability  of  time,
manpower,  and  campaign  resources  other  than  money
also  has  some  effect  on  electoral  results.  See  Senate
Rep.  93-689  at  6.  As  demonstrated  above,  under
Subtitle  TH  major  party  candidates  inevitably  will  be
able  to direct  a  substantially  greater  amount  of money,
time  and  manpower  than  minor  or  new  party  candi-
dates  to the effort  to attract  voter  support  as  opposed  to
the  effort  to  raise  private  contributions.

Defendants  below  concede  that  the  need  to  solicit
private  contributions  imposes  a  ‘‘demeaning  and  time-
consuming’’  burden  on  presidential  candidates.  ID  Br.

**If  the  major  parties  in  the  preceding  election  received  an
average  of  38  percent  of  the  vote,  and  a  minor  party  candidate
24  percent,  the  minor  party  candidate  in  the  current  election
theoretically  could  receive  24/38  x  20  million  dollars  or  approxi-
mately  $12.6  million  dollars.
Therefore  ‘‘at  best’’  a  minor  party
candidate  will  have  to  make  up  a  $7.4  million  difference.
See  p.
23-25  infra.
