23

at  36-37.  The  Court  of  Appeals  also  recognized  that
the  solicitation  of  private  funds  imposes  substantial
burdens  on  presidential  candidates.

Finally,  it  is  not  likely  that  legislation  designed  to
tip  t!e  scales  in  favor  of  major  party  candidates  in
orde)  to  discourage  minor  and  new  party  candidacies
will  leave  minor  and  new  party  candidates  ‘‘better  off”’
than  they  were  under  a  system  of  private  financing.
While  Subtitle  H
Cf.  Sen.  Rep.  93-689  at  6-8.
may  not  guarantee  a  major  party  candidate  electoral
success,  as  intended  it  does  substantially  increase  his
chances  of  winning,  and  correspondingly  diminishes  a
minor  or  new  party  candidate’s  chances.

(b)  The  Substantial  Competitive  Advantages  Subtitle
Major Party  Candidates  Are  Not  Offset

I] Affords
by Post-Election  Subsidies.

A  candidate  entitled  to  full  pre-election  subsidies
knows  from  the  start  how  much  he has  to spend,  and  is
thus  in  an  excellent  position  to  manage  his  funds  in
that  manner  which  best  serves  his  strategic  needs.
Minor  and  new  party  candidates  can  put  the  hope  of
post-election  subsidies  to use  only  by borrowing  money,
and,  in  order  to  do  so,  they  must  persuade  a  lender  to
assume  the  risk  that  they  might  not  receive  enough
votes  to  entitle  them  to  post-election  grants  to  repay
such  loans.

Post-election  subsidies  do  not  relieve  minor  and  new
party  candidates  or  their  supporters  of  the  burden  of
fund  raising,  or  of the  need  to  campaign  on  issues  that
will  attract  money,  as  opposed  to  votes.  Post-election
grants  thus  clearly  will  not  help  a  minor  or  new  party
candidate  take  his  case  to  the  voters.

In fact  minor  and  new  parties  may  never  receive  any

post-election  public  funding  under  Subtitle  H.

Section  9004  of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
provides  that  minor  and  new  party  candidates  are  en-
titled  to  post-election  subsidies  equal  only  to the  lesser
amount  of:  qualified  expenses  incurred,  less  contribu-
tions;  or  $20  million,  less  contributions  received.  26
U.S.C.  9004(b).  Additionally,  this  section  mandates
that  the  public  monies  received  can  only  be  used  to de-
fray  qualified  campaign  expenses  or  to  repay  loans  the
procecds  of  which  are  used  to  defray  such  expenses,
26  U.S.C,  9004(¢)  (1975  Pam.  No.  1).

As  Professor  Albert  J.  Rosenthal  has  noted  the  rea-
soning  behind  post-election  subsidies  is  inescapably
reminiscent  of  ‘‘Catch  22’’."  Both  major  parties  and
new  parties  are  presented  with  a  choice  whether  to
solicit  contributions  or  rely  on  public  financing.  The
only  risk  that  the  major  parties  run  in  relying  on  pub-
lic  financing  is that  there  will  not  be  enough  funds  in
the  public  coffers  for  them  to  receive  their  full  $20
million,  However,  for the  new  party,  the choice  is really
a  Hobson’s  choice.  The  new  party  shares  the  risk  that
there  will  not  be enough  public  funds  to  go  around,  but
also,  since  the  new  party  only  gets  public  financing  af-
ter  an  election  on  a  formula  tied  to  its  performance
during  the  election,  it must  seek  private  financing  if it
is to have  the  funds  necessary  to get the votes  necessary
to get the  public  financing.  The  ‘‘Catch  22”  of the  post-
election  financing  provisions  is  that  the  more  private
funds  the  new  party  obtains  in order  to get  votes  in or-
der  to get public  financing,  the  less  public  financing  the
new  party  gets.

27  Rosenthal,  supra,  in  n.  10,  at  414.

24

25

To  illustrate,  hypothesize  a  slightly  different  set  of
facts  from  those  stated  in  note  26  supra,  i.e.,  major
party  X’s  candidate  in the  current  election  receives  50
percent  of the  vote;  major  party  Y’s  candidate  26  per-
cent  of the  vote,  giving  a  major  party  vote  average  of
38 percent.  New  party  Z’s candidate  receives  24 percent
of  the  vote  in  the  election.  New  party  Z’s  candidate
would  appear  to  be  entitled  upon  a  cursory  glance  at
Subtitle  H  to  24/38  x  $20  million,  or  approximately
$12.6  million.  That  amount  is not  necessarily  how  much
new  party  Z’s  candidate  receives,  however.  Assume
new  party  Z’s  candidate  received  $10  million  in  con-
tributions  and  incurred  $15  million  in  qualified  ex-
penditures.  Applying  Section  9004  new  party  Z’s  can-
didate  only  receives  $5 million  [the  lesser  amount  of:
qualified  expenses  incurred  ($15  million)  minus  con-
tributions  received  $10  million)  =  $5 million;  or  the
maximum  presidential  spending  level  permitted  ($20
million)  less  contributions  received  ($10  million)  =
$10  million].  Keep  all  of the  above  factors  and  figures
constant,  except  increase  the  amount  of  contributions
received  by new  party  Z’s  candidate  to $12 million,  and
as  a  result  new  party  Z’s  candidate  receives  even  less
in  public  monies:  $3  million  [the  lesser  amount  of:
qualified  expenses  incurred  ($15  million)  minus  con-
tributions  received  ($12  million)  =  $3  million;  or
maximum  presidential  expenditures
($20  million)
minus,  contributions  received  ($12  million)  —  $8 mil-
lion. |

Thus,  the  difference  of  only  two  percentage  points *
between  major  party  Y’s  26  percent  and  new  party  Z’s

**  Amicus  Metealf  questions  whether  the  25  percent  threshhold
in  the  statute  is  based  on  sound  empirical  data  or  is  merely  an
arbitrary  figure  and  not  rationally  related  to  any  analytical  find-

24  percent  means  not  only  anywhere  from  $17  million
to $15 million  dollars  in  public  funds  prior  to the  elec-
tion  (major  party  Y  would  have  received  $20  million
prior  to the  election),  but  also  a  considerable  disadvan-
tage  to  new  party  Z’s  candidate  given  the  hard  work
and  time  required  to raise  contributions  even  to attract
24  percent  of  the  vote.  In  fact,  as  noted  above,  the
harder  new  party  Z’s  candidate  tries  to  raise  pre-elec-
tion  contributions,  and  is  successful,  the  greater  he  is
penalized  with  respect  to  availability  of  post-election
public  money.

In  addition,  Subtitle  H  confronts  new  and  minor
parties  with  the  distinct  possibility  that  even  though
they  might  technically  qualify  for  post-election  public
financing  there  may  be  no  monies  available.  After  a
number  of  contested  presidential  primary  elections,
the  Secretary  of the  Treasury—assuming  no  new  party
would  receive  five  percent  or  more  of  the  vote—may
have  reserved  only  enough  money,  $40  million,  for  the
two  major  parties.  Unless  taxpayer  participation  in
the  check-off  system  significantly  improves  *”  such  an
election  situation  will  confront  a  Secretary  of  the
Treasury  with  many  practical  allocation  problems.”
And  the  above  hypothetical  may  become a reality  next

ing  by  the  Congress.  Amicus  Metcalf  notes  that  a  recent  Gallup
Poll  shows  that  even  one  of  the  two  ‘‘major’’  parties  is  now  below
the  25  per  cent  figure.  American  Institute  of  Public  Opinion  (The
Gallup  Poll),  ‘‘GOP  Affiliation  Continues  to  Slide;  Independents
at  Peak,’’  Princeton,  N.J.;  Sept.  21,  1975.

26

27

year  given  the  past  level  of  support  for  the  American
Independent  Party,’  the  abundance  of  presidential
candidates  *  and  the  scarcity  of  funds  available  for
public  financing.*

The  solution  to  this  dilemma  for  new,  and  minor
party  candidates  who  want  to  avail  themselves  of pub-
lic  financing  monies  for  general  elections  evidently  is
to  go  into  debt,  not  concentrate  on  fund-raising  and
pray  that  money  is left  for  post-election  disbursement.
Such  a  solution  suggests  the  clear  result  and  effect  of
this  provision  of  Subtitle  H:  Foreclosure  of  public
financing  for  general  elections  to  all  but  candidates  of
the  existing  two  major  political  parties.

(c)  By  Denying  Minor  and  New  Party  Candidates  an
Equal  Opportunity  of  Winning,  Subtitle  H  De-
nies  Certain  Individuals  an  Equal  Opportunity  to
Elect  the  Candidates  of Their  Choice.

While  a  candidate’s  interest  in holding  elective  office
might  not  be deemed  sufficiently  fundamental  to trigger
strict  scrutiny  of  measures  which  deny  him  an  equal
opportunity  of  winning,  this  Court  has  often  recog-
nized  that  governmental  action  which  affects  candi-
dates  inevitably  has  a  correlative  impact  on  voters.”
This  holds  true  for  Subtitle  H.  By denying  minor  and
new  party  candidates  an  equal  opportunity  of winning,

*1  Finding  of  Fact  I,  7 85  indicates  that  Governor  Wallace  re-

ceived  13.5  percent  of  the  vote  in  1968.

**  As  of  September  23rd,  1975,  49  candidates  for  the  presidency
had  filed  with  the  Federal  Election  Commission  Information  Office.

33  Td.  n.29  supra.

*4  See  e.g.  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709,  716  (1974).

which  is demonstrated  supra,  the  general  election  pro-
visions  of Subtitle  H substantially  and  invidiously  deny
their  individual  supporters  an  equal  opportunity  to
elect  a  candidate  of their  choice.

Assume,  arguendo,  that  X, Y, and  Z are  presidential
candidates.  X  and  Y  are  major  party  candidates  en-
titled  to full  public  funding,  or  $20 million.  Z is  a minor
party  candidate  eligible  for  20%  of  the  major  party
entitlement,  or  $4 million.

Assume  further  that  at  the  time  these  subsidies  are
distributed  each  candidate  has  1,000  committed  sup-
porters,  and  that  the  remaining  7,000  of  a  total  of
10,000  eligible  voters  are  undecided.  X  and  Y not  only
received  a  substantially  larger  subsidy  than  candidate
Z, but  both  X  and  Y  and  their  supporters  have  been
entirely  freed  of  the  burden  of  raising  private  contri-
butions.  Thus,  Subtitle  H  accords  the  committed  sup-
porters  of  major  party  candidate  X  and  Y a greater
opportunity  than  the  committed  supporters  of  minor
party  candidate  Z to elect  the  candidate  of their  choice,
and  both  X  and  Y  clearly  will  have  a  better  chance
than  Z of  attracting  enough  of  the  undecided  votes  to
win.

This  proposition  might  also  be stated  in terms  which
more  closely  approximate  the  approach  the  courts  have
tended  to  take  in  malapportionment  cases.  See  e.4.,
Reynolds  v.  Sims,  377  U.S.  533  (1957).  In  the  three-
party  system  described  above,  it is likely that  more  than
one-half  the  votes  eventually  cast  will  be meaningless  in
the  sense  that  the  candidate  for  whom  they  are  polled
will  lose.  Yet,  because  minor  party  candidate  Z  has
been  denied  an  equal  chance  of  winning,  there  is  a
higher  probability  that  the  votes  of his  committed  sup-
porters  will  be meaningless  than  there  is that  the  votes

28

29

of X’s  and  Y’s  committed  supporters  will  be meaning-
less.  Thus,  by  differentially  allocating  campaign  sub-
sidies,  Subtitle  H in effect  places  a  higher  value  on  the
votes  of  those  who  support  a  major  party  candidate,
and  correspondingly  dilutes  the  value  of  the  votes  of
those  who  support  a  minor  or  new  party  candidate.

Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.  724  (1974);  American
Party  of  Texas  vy.  White,  415  U.S.  767  (1974),  and
Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  U.S.  431  (1971),  which  have
been  cited  by  defendants  below  in  support  of  the  as-
sertion  that  the  general  election  provisions  of  Sub-
title  H  are  not  invidiously  discriminatory,  are  readily
distinguishable.
In  those  decisions,  this  Court  care-
fully  emphasized  that,  although  it  treated  minor  par-
ties  differently,  the  state  did  not  require  minor  party
candidates  to  bear  burdens  ‘‘inherently  greater’’
than  those  imposed  on  major  party  candidates.  As
discussed  above,  the  record  in  this  case  demonstrates
clearly  that  unlike  the  measures  in  question  in
Storer,  American  Party,  and  Jenness,  Subtitle  H
does  require  minor  and  new  party  candidates  to  bear
burdens  ‘‘inherently  greater’’  than  those  imposed  on
major  party  candidates  in  order  to  attain  access  to
campaign  funding,  to  attract  voter  support,  and  to
achieve  electoral  success.  This  in  turn  invidiously  and
substantially  denies  the  individual  supporters  of minor
or  new  political  parties  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  a
candidate  of their  choice.  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709
(1974);  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968).

It  is  thus  clear  that  Subtitle  H  imposes  invidious
and  substantially  unequal  burdens  on  the  efforts  of the
supporters  of  minor  or  new  party  candidates  to  elect
the  candidate  of  their  choice,  and  therefore  should  be
subjected  to  strict  judicial  scrutiny.

3.  The  general  election  public  financing  provisions  of  the
challenged  statutes  give  two  particular  parties  a  perma-
nent  monopoly  over  the  Presidency,  freeze  the  political
status  quo,  and  otherwise  invidiously  discriminate  against
certain  discrete  and  insular  minorities.

*‘All  political  ideas  cannot  and  should  not  be
channeled  into  the programs  of  our  two  major
parties.  History  has  amply  proved  the  virtue  of
political  activity  by  minority  dissident  groups,
who  innumerable  times  have  been  in  the  van-
guard  of democratic  thought  and  whose  programs
were  ultimately  accepted  ...  The  absence  of such
voices  would  be  a  symptom  of grave  illness  in  our
society.”’  ®

The  record  indicates  that  Subtitle  H  is  specifically
designed  to  give  two  particular  parties—the  Demo-
cratic  and  Republican—a  permanent,  as  well  as  imme-
diate  electoral  advantage,  and  it is likely  to accomplish
precisely  that  result.

If  the  Subtitle  H  general  election  public  financing
provisions  had  been  in  effect  throughout  our  history  it
is  doubtful  that  the  Whig  Party  would  have  disap-
peared  between  1852  and  1856,  that  the  Republican
Party  would  have  been  able  to  elect  Abraham  Lincoln
in  1860,  or  that  Teddy  Roosevelt  could  have  hoped  to
outpoll  President  Taft  in  his  Bull  Moose  candidacy  in
1912.°  Under  Subtitle  H  George  Wallace  and  his
American  Independent  Party  would  have  received
nothing  in  1968,”  while  John  Schmitz,  the  Party’s
candidate  in  1972,  would  have  received  several  million

*  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354  U.S.  234,  251  (1957).

%6  Finding  of  Fact  I, {[ 85.

37  Id.

30

31

dollars  as  a  result  of his  receiving  only  1.3  percent  of
the  vote.”

In the  1976  presidential  race,  only  candidates  of the
Democratic  and  Republican  parties  will  be eligible  for
pre-election  publie  funding,  and  as  major  party  eandi-
dates,  they  will  be  entitled  to  the  full  subsidization  of
their  campaign  expenses.  Consequently,  and  as  Con-
gress  specifically  intends,  all  but  ‘‘fringe’’  candidates
will  be  strongly  encouraged  to  seek  either  the  Demo-
cratic  or  Republican  nomination  rather  than  run  as
a  minor  party  candidate.  See  Sen.  Rep.  93-689  at
8.  This  procedure  thus  increases  the  likelihood  that
the  Democratic  and  Republican  parties  will  field  the
‘strongest’?  candidates,  and  by a  wide  margin  outpoll
any  minor  party  candidates  who  do  decide  to  run.  The
cycle  would  then  be  repeated  in  1980,  and  thereafter.
Thus,  assuming  that  Subtitle  H works  the  results  Con-
gress  intends,  starting  in  1976  the  Democratic  and  Re-
publican  parties  will  enjoy  an  effective  and  permanent
control  over  presidential  elections,  and  the  public  fi-
nancing  system  to  fund  such  elections.

Whatever  disadvantages  the candidates  and  support-
ers  of minor  and  new  political  parties  have  borne  under
a  system  of  private  financing  of campaigns  have  been
the  result  of  private  decisions.  Under  Subtitle  H, the
government  has  taken  action  which  invidiously  denies
the  candidates  and  supporters  of minor  and  new  polit-
ical  parties  an  opportunity  to  participate  in  the  elec-
toral  process  on  an  equal  basis  with  the  candidates  and
supporters  of the  major  parties.

Decisions  previously  rendered  by  this  Court  docu-
ment  the  long  history  of  prejudice  against  and  official
efforts  to  diminish  the  role  of  minor  political  parties,

*  See  gencrally  Statistical  Abstract  of  the  United  States,  1974.

(see  Williams  v.  Rhodes  and  its  progeny)  and  recog-
nize  that  legislators  have  a  substantial  interest  in  and
may  be  naturally  inclined  to  enact  measures  which
tend  to  ensure  that  persons  of  like  persuasion  will
control  the  office  of  the  President.  See  Kramer  v.
Union  Free  School  District,  395  U.S.  621,  628  (1969).
This  Court  has  also  recognized  that  discrete  and  in-
sular minorities  are  particularly  dependent  upon  close
judicial  supervision  of  governmental  action.
See
United  States  v.  Carolene  Prods.  Co.,  304  U.S.  144,
153  n.4  (1938)  (dictum).

C.  The  Tax  Check-off  System  Incorporated  in  the  Challenged
Statutes  Is  Constitutionally  Defective  and  Should  Be
Strictly  Scrutinized.
26.U.8.C.  § 6096  establishes  presidential  election  cam-
paign  funds  to be administered  by the  Secretary  of the
Treasury.  Every  individual,  other  than  a  non-resident
alien,  whose  income  tax  liability  for the  taxable  year  is
$1 or  more  may  designate  that  $1 shall  be  paid  into  a
presidential  election  campaign  fund.”

Taxpayers  so  designating  contributions  to  the  fund
are  not  permitted  to  select  a  specific  political  party “

minorities,  and  which  may  call  for  a  correspondingly  more  search-
ing  judicial  inquiry.’’

The  Court  of Appeals  stated  in its  opinion  below:

‘‘The  Congress  has determined  that  providing  pub-
lic  funding  for  the  three  stages  of the  Presidential
selection  process  would  advance  the  general  wel-
fare  of the nation  by reducing  the reliance  of Pres-
idential  candidates  on  large  contributions,  thus  re-
ducing  their  influence  on  the  outcome  of  elections
and  on  the  operations  of government.  We  are  in no
position  to  quarrel  with  that  judgment.”’  Buckley
vy.  Valeo,  Slip  Op. at  1561.

Amicus  Metcalf  submits  that  implicit  in  the  Court  of
Appeal’s  acceptance  of  Congress’  determination  that
public  financing  of  Presidential  campaigns  advances
the  general  welfare  is acceptance  of  the  principle  that
persons  participating  in such  funding  process  by means
of  the  voluntary  check-off  system  (26  U.S.C.  § 6096)
receive  a  benefit  from  the  government.  c.f.,  Bullock  v.
Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  148  (1972).  This  is  of  funda-
mental  conceptual  and  practical  importance,  because
it is basie  that  the  means  chosen  by the  government  in
making  a  benefit  available  cannot  be such  that  the  utili-
zation  of those  means  requires  the  surrender  of consti-
tutional  rights.  United  States  v.  Robel,  389  U.S.  258
(1967)  and  Perry  v.  Sinderman,  408  U.S.  593  (1972).
Notwithstanding  this  well  established  constitutional

limitation  on  governmental  action,  the  Court  of  Ap-
peals  went  on  to state:

.  Congress  can  establish  a  system  which  gives
‘*.  .
the  taxpayer  some  choice—in  affecting  the  total
amount  of  public  funds  to  be  distributed  on  the
Congressional  formula—without  going  all  the  way
to  permit  each  taxpayer  complete  authority  over
the  disposition  of  his  dollar  of  tax  obligation.  If
the  tarpayer  wants  to  avoid  any  conception  that
some  of ‘his’  tax  money  will  benefit  candidates  he
does  not  favor,  then  he  can  simply  refrain  from
checking  off  his  dollar  for  the  Fund.  Buckley  v.
Valeo,  Slip  Op.  at  1561-1562  (emphasis  added).

Thus,  the  Court  of  Appeals  recognized  that  some
taxpayers  must  refrain  from  accepting  the  benefit
which  Congress  has  conferred  on  other  taxpayers  if
they  wish  to exercise  their  First  Amendment  rights  of
free  political  association.  But  the  Court  below  ap-
peared  to  be  oblivious  to  the  prohibition  against  the
government’s  creating  a  system  of benefits  that  is con-
ditioned  upon  a taxpayer’s  needlessly  foregoing  the  ex-
ercise  of First  Amendment  rights  of free  political  asso-
ciation.  And  it is precisely  because  of such  prohibition
that  the tax  check-off  system  incorporated  in 26 U.S.C.
) 6096  should  be  found  violative  of  the  Constitution
and  invalidated.”

First  Amendment  right  to  effective  political  association.

lll.  THE  PUBLIC  FINANCING  PROVISIONS  INCORPO-
RATED  IN  THE  FECA,  THE  FECA  AMENDMENTS.
AND  SUBTITLE  H  ARE  NOT  RATIONALLY  RELATED
TO,  OR  THE  LEAST  RESTRICTIVE  MEANS  OF
OBJECTIVES

ACHIEVING.

PERMISSIBLE

A.  The  Public  Financing  Eligibility  and  Allocation  Formulae
Contained  in  the  Challenged  Statutes  Are  Not  Rationally
Related  in  Fact  to  the  Permissible  Objectives  Sought  by
Their  Enactment.
The  pertinent  legislative  history  indicates:  1)  that
the  primary  purpose  of  public  financing  of  federal
campaigns  is to equalize  opportunities  for effective  par-
ticipation  in  the  political  process  by  minimizing  the
importance  of personal  wealth  as  a  determinant  of who
becomes  a  candidate,  who  wins  elections,  and  who  en-
(See  H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239,
joys access  to elected  officials;
93d  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  (1974)  at  13-14);  and  2) that  the
primary  purpose  of the  particular  eligibility  and  allo-
eation  formulae  here  in question  is to identify  and  pro-
vide  assistance  to candidates  with  potential  for  attract-
ing a  ‘“‘significant”’  level  of voter  support  without  sub-
sidizing  or  encouraging  ‘“‘frivolous  candidacies”’.  See
H.R.  Rep.  No.  1239  at  13.  See  also  Sen.  Rep.  No.  689,
93d  Cong.,  2nd  Sess.  at  6-8.  In  addition,  the  record
indicates  that  the  general  election  and  convention  pro-
visions  of Subtitle  H are  intended  to ensure  the  Demo-
cratic  and  Republican  parties  a permanent  monopoly
over  the  presidency,  and  to  otherwise  freeze  the  polit-
ical  status  quo,  by inhibiting  affiliation  with  minor  and
new  political  parties.  See  discussion  supra  at  29-31.

Amicus  Metealf  submits  that,  while  the  government
has  a  permissible  and  indeed  compelling  interest  in
ensuring  individuals  an  equal  opportunity  for  effective
participation  in  the  electoral  process  and  in  prevent-

ing  the  subsidization  or  proliferation  of  frivolous
candidacies,  it  is impermissible  for  the  government  to
attempt  to  inhibit  affiliation  with  minor  or  new  politi-
cal  parties  and  to attempt  to give two  particular  parties
an  effective  monopoly  over  the  presidency,  Williams  v.
Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23, 32  (1968)  or  to otherwise  attempt
to  freeze  the  political  status  quo.  Jenness  vy.  Fortson,
403  U.S.  431  (1971).

Accordingly, the issue  presented  in testing  the  differ-
ential  funding  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  against  the
‘‘rational  basis  in  fact”  test  **  is  whether  defendants

*8  Appellees  may  question  whether  the  court  should  apply  the
traditional  minimal  rationality  standard,  or  the  more  recently  de-
veloped  rational  basis  in  fact  test.  However,  it  seems  now  gen-
erally  agreed  that  the  rational  basis  in  fact  approach  is,  for  the
majority  of  equal  protection  cases,  the  preferable  analytical  tech-
nique,  see  e.g.,  Gunther,  The  Supreme  Court  Review—1971  Term
—Forward:  In  Search  of Evolving  Doctrine  on  a  Changing  Court:
A  Model  for  a  New  Equal  Protection,  86  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1  (1972)
and  recent  cases  suggest  that  this  Court  is committed  to  an  effort
to  apply  this  new  standard  in  lieu  of  minimal  rationality  wherever
feasible.
See  e.g.,  James  v.  Strange,  407  U.S.  128  (1972) ; Eisen-
stadt  v.  Baird,  405  U.S.  438  (1972).

Amicus  Metcalf  submits  that  the  rational  basis  in  fact  test  can
and  should  be  applied  when  testing  the  constitutionality  of  the
classifications  here  at  issue.  It  has  been  suggested  that  difficulties
may  arise  in  applying  this  ‘‘heightened  rationality’’  standard
where  alleged  differences  between  persons  within  and  without  the
classification  are  subtle  and  not  especially  susceptible  to  empirical
proof,  or  where  the  classification  is experimental  or  otherwise  of  a
character  such  that  data  as  to  possible  differences  among  the
various  classes  established  will  not  become  available  unless  the
statute  is  allowed  some  period  of  operation.  Gunther,  supra  at  47-
48.  But,  while  public  campaign  finance  legislation  is experimental
a  great  deal  of  data  regarding  the  character  of  major,  minor  and
new  political  parties;  of  candidates  able  to  solicit  private  contri-
butions  as  opposed  to  those  who  cannot;  and  of the  probable  effect
of  providing  certain  candidates  more  money  than  others,  is  pres-
ently  available.  See  e.g.,  Masmanian,  Third  Parties  in Presidential

37

have  shown  that  those  provisions  are  in fact  rationally
related  to the  government’s  legitimate  interest  in 1) en-
suring  equality  of opportunity  in the  electoral  process,
and  2)  identifying  and  providing  assistance  to  candi-
dates  with  potential  for  attracting  a ‘‘significant’’  level
of  voter  support  without  subsidizing  or  encouraging
frivolous  candidacies.

Amicus  Metcalf  submits  that  defendants  below  failed
to  meet  this  requirement,  that  the  record  affirma-
tively  indicates  that  there  is  no  rational  basis  in  fact
for  the  differential  funding  provisions  here  in  issue,
and  that  therefore  each  of  those  provisions  should  be
invalidated.

1.  Presidential  Primary  Matching  Fund  Account

In  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134  (1974),  and  again
in Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709  (1974),  this  Court  in-
validated  state  imposed  filing  fee  requirements  on  the
grounds  that  a  candidate’s  access  to  private  funds,  in
and  of  itself,  is  not  a  certain  test  of  whether  his  can-
didacy  is serious  or  spurious.  Under  the  primary  cam-
paign  provisions  of Subtitle  H, a  candidate’s  ability  to
raise  private  contributions  is  the  sole  determinant  of
both  his  eligibility  for  campaign  subsidies,  and  the  size
of the  grant he will  be awarded  should  he qualify  at all.

Elections,  (1974);  Adamany,  Financing  Politics,  (1969).  See  also
Hearings  on  8S.  1103,  S.  1954,  S.  2417  Before  the  Subcomm.  on
Privileges  and  Elections  of  the  Senate  Comm.  on  Rules  and  Ad-
ministration,  93d  Cong.  1st  Sess.  (Sept.  1973).  Moreover,  this  data
is  sufficiently  refined  and  pertinent  to  allow  this  Court  to  make
the  type  of  judgments  required  to  determine  the  extent  to  which
the  eligibility  and  allocation  formulae  contained  in  Subtitle  H  do
or  do  not  promote  the  actual  and  permissible  purposes  of  the
challenged  statutes.

Defendants  below  failed  to  prove  that  the  matching
grant  system  established  by  Subtitle  H  provides  any
more  rational  a  test  of  a  candidate’s  seriousness  than
the  filing  fees  invalidated  in  Bullock  and  in  Lubin.“
In  fact,  the  sole  argument  defendants  below  make  on
this  point  is  that  the  amount  a  candidate  can  raise
from  private  contributions  is  an  objective  measure  of
his  support  among  the  electorate.  ID  Br.  173.

This  argument  is  clearly  without  merit.  Indeed,  it

was  expressly  rejected  in  Bullock  and  in  Lubin.

In  addition,  the  record  in this  case  affirmatively  es-
tablishes  that  there  is no  rational  basis  in  fact  for  the
primary  matching  fund  provisions  of  Subtitle  H.
Matching  grants  will  often  increase  the  disparity  be-
tween  the  funds  available  to  a  candidate  supported  by
the  well-to-do  and  a  candidate  supported  by  the  less
affluent  members  of  the  society,  even  if  the  latter  has
a  substantially  greater  number  of  supporters.  See  dis-
cussion  supra  at  10-12,

Such  a  result  is  hardly  consistent  with  either  the
general  purposes  of  public  campaign  financing,  or  the
more  particular  purpose  of  providing  assistance  to
serious  candidates  without  subsidizing  or  encouraging
frivolous  candidacies.  Indeed,  the  matching  grant  ap-
proach  seems  only  to  compound  the  inequities  cam-
paign  subsidies  ostensibly  are  intended  to  overcome.

It  is clear  that  the  primary  campaign  provisions  of
Subtitle  H  fail  to  satisfy  the  rational  basis  in  fact  re-
quirement,  and  accordingly  should  be  invalidated.

“  Although  the  ‘‘strict  scrutiny  test’?  may  have  provided  the
basis  for  decision  in  both  of  these  cases,  they  are  controlling  here
to  the  extent  that  they  hold  that  a  state  may  not  classify  a  candi-
date  as  serious  or  frivolous  solely  on  the  basis  of  his  ability  to
raise  funds,  or  the  ability  of his  supporters  to  contribute.

39

2.  The  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act
With  respect  to the  general  election  public  financing
provisions  of  Subtitle  H,  defendants  below  have
argued:

1)  That  Subtitle  H  ensures  ‘‘that  insubstantial
eandidates  do  not  receive  the  same  amounts  of
federal  campaign  assistance  as  significant  candi-
dates.’’?  ID  Br.  at  155;

2)  That  each  major  party  ‘“‘is  a  vehicle  for
political  expression  by a  sizable  proportion  of the
population  and  attempts  to  appeal  to  a  majority
of  the  electorate.’’  ID  Br.  at  158-9;  and

3)  That  recent  decisions  establish  that  the  gov-
ernment  need  not  ‘‘finance  the  efforts  of every
nascent  political  group  seeking  to  organize  itself
and  unsuccessfully  attempting  to  win  a  place on
the  general  election  ballot.’”’  IDR Br.  at 60, citing
American  Party  of Teras  v.  White,  412  U.S.  767,
793-94  (1974)  (emphasis  added).

Except  as  applicable  to  new  political  parties,  these
arguments  fail  to  establish  a  rational  basis  in  fact  for
the  general  election  provision  of  Subtitle  H.”

Most  significantly,  defendants  below  have  failed  to
prove  that  with  respect  to  the  permissible  purposes  of
public  campaign  financing  minor  party  candidates  are
either  ‘‘insubstantial,’’  or  ‘‘less  substantial’’  than  ma-

*°  Amicus  Metcalf  concedes  that  the  case  law  indicates  that  Con-
gress  is  not  constitutionally  required  to  provide  campaign  sub-
sidies  to  candidates  of  parties  that  fail  to  evidence  a  ‘‘substantial
Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403
modicum  of  support  of  the  electorate.’’
He  further  believes,  however,  that  Congress  is
U.S.  431  (1971).
constitutionally  required  to  provide  candidates  alternative  means—
e.g.  current  petition  support  as  well  as  past  electoral  support—of
demonstrating  they  do  indeed  enjoy  a  specified  level  of  support.
See  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709  (1974).

jor  party  candidates.  In  particular,  defendants  below
failed  even  to  attempt  to  prove  that  a  minor  party
[which  Subtitle  H  defines  as  a  party  whose  candidate
in the  preceding  election  received  between  five  (5)  and
twenty-five  (25)  percent  of  the  total  vote,  26  U.S.C.
§ 9002(£)]  does  not  provide  a  vehicle  for  political  ex-
pression  by a  sizable  proportion  of the  population  and
does  not  attempt  to  appeal  to  a  majority  of  the  elec-
torate.

The  Court  of Appeals  expressly  stated  in upholding
the  applicability  of  disclosure  laws  to  minor  parties
that  ‘‘no  election  law  should  ever  be  premised  on  the
notion  that  non-major  parties  cannot  win  an  election.”’
Buckley  v.  Valeo  Slip Op. at 1546,  n.  114.  Surely,  then,
it  is impermissible  to  presume  that  minor  party  can-
didates  do  not  attempt  to  ‘‘appeal  to a  majority  of the
electorate.’’  Indeed,  the  fact  that  minor  parties  are
treated  exactly  like  major  parties  with  respect  to  dis-
closure  laws,  but  are  denied  equal  access  to  campaign
subsidies,  further  indicates  the  invidious  character  and
purpose  not  only  of the  particular  differential  funding
provisions  here  at  issue,  but  of the  general  regulatory
scheme  of which  those  provisions  form a part.

Moreover,  the ‘‘modicum  of support”’  test  announced
in Jenness  reflects  this  Court’s  determination  that  the
candidate  of a  party  which  evidences  the  support  of at
least  five  (5)  percent  of  the  electorate  does  provide  a
vehicle  for  political  expression  for  a  substantial  por-
tion  of the  electorate,  does  seek  to appeal  to a majority
of the  electorate,  and  therefore  is a  ‘‘serious  and  sub-
stantial’  candidate.  Significantly,  this  Court  consist-
ently  has  held  that  while  candidates  who  evidence  a
modicum  of  support  may  be  treated  differently  than
major  party  candidates,  they  may  not  be  treated  un-
equally.  See  e.g.  American  Party  of Texas  v.  White,

oe
se A ete

ee  Sa

40

41

supra.  (Candidates  of  parties  that  evidence  sufficient
support  to  qualify  for  the  ballot  are  entitled  to  treat-
ment  equal  to  that  accorded  major  party  candidates
with  respect  to  absentee  balloting.)  See  also  Jenness
y.  Fortson,  supra.  (Differential  treatment  with  respect
to  ballot  access  of  parties  evidencing  a  modicum  of
support  was  upheld  because  it  was  not  shown  to  be
tantamount  to  inherently  wnequal  treatment.)

The  record  and  the  pertinent  decisions  of this  Court
indicate  that  minor  party  candidates  seek  and  have  the
capacity  to  attract  support  from  a  majority  of the
electorate,  and  may  therefore  not  be  deemed  frivolous.
Buckley  v.  Valeo  Slip  Op. at  1546,  n.  114.  Accordingly,
neither  of  the  permissible  purposes  of  the  legislation
here  in question  is served  by denying  minor  party  can-
didates  access  to  campaign  subsidies  equal  to  those
accorded  major  party  candidates.”  There  is  no  ra-
tional  basis  in  fact  for  such  treatment,  and  therefore
said  provisions  should  be  invalidated.

3. Presidential  Nominating  Convention  Financing
Section  9008  of  Subtitle  H  (Chapter  95,  26  U.S.C.
§ 9008)  establishes  an  account  for monies  received  from
the  Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund  Act  (26
U.S.C.  §  6096)  for  Presidential  nominating  conven-
tions.  Disbursements  from  this  account  are  authorized
to defray  lawful  expenses  up to $2 million  for the nomi-
nating  conventions  of major  parties.  Minor  parties  are
entitled  to a proportional  amount  computed  in the same

©  Amicus  Metcalf  does  not  submit  that  a  major  and  a  minor
party  candidate  must  be  granted  exactly  the  same  amount,  but
rather  that  each  (and,  correlatively,  the  individual  supporters  of
each)  is  entitled  to  an  equal  opportunity  to  secure  campaign  sub-
sidies.

See  discussion  supra  at  18-29.

manner  as  payments  are  computed  for  minor  party
presidential  candidates  during  the  post  primary-con-
vention  period.

Precluded  from  receiving  any  monies  for  Presiden-
tial  nominating  conventions  are  all  parties  (new  par-
ties)  which  did  not  receive  five  (5)  percent  of the total
vote  cast  at  the  last  election,  independent  candidates
and  all  parties  which  do  not  have  a  national  conven-
tion.  26  U.S.C.  § 9008(b)  (2).  Section  9008(a)  specifi-
cally  requires  the  Secretary  of the  Treasury  to  create
‘fa  separate  account  for  the  national  committee  of each
major  party’’  (emphasis  added).

The  rationale  for  establishing  public  financing  for
nominating  conventions  evidently  is  based  on  the  ‘‘in-
fluence  or  potential  influence  of  big  money  ...  in  all
phases  of presidential  elections,’’  since  ‘‘major  political
parties  have  come  to rely  heavily  on  large  corporations
and  labor  unions  to  pay  for  advertisements  in  conven-
tion  programs  to meet  the  expenses  of nominating  con-
ventions.”’  H.R.  Rep.  93-1239  at  14.  Defendants  be-
low  cite  American  Party  of  Texas  v.  White,  414  U.S.
767  (1974)  in  support  of  selective  funding  for  such
nominating  conventions.

ID  Br.  at  170-172.

Amicus  Metcalf  contends  that  defendant’s  below  fail
to establish  a  rational  basis  in the  record  of this  action
to  support  the  above  findings.  In  fact,  the  record  be-
low  compels  the  conclusion  that  public  financing  of
nominating  conventions  is  not  rationally  related  to
election  law  reform.

Findings  of  fact  I 176,

177,  178,  and  179  respec-

tively  state:

Nomination  by  a  national  convention  of  a  po-
litical  party  is neither  a  constitutional  nor  a  legal

43

requirement  for  an  individual  to  be  a  candidate
for election  as  President  of the United  States.  Not
all  candidates  for  President  of  the  United  States
are  nominated  by  a  national  nominating  conven-
tion.  (176)

Some  national  nominating  conventions  have  far
fewer  major  or  controversial  issues  to resolve  than
others.  (177)

others.  (178)

!

Public  funding  of  national  nominating  conven-
tions  does  not  relieve  individual  candidates  for
federal  offices  of  reliance  on  private  financing.
(179)

The  purpose  and  effect  of  the  public  financing  of
nominating  conventions  is  to  grant  an  additional  sub-
sidy  to  the  political  parties  which  use  national  nom-
inating  conventions  (i.e.,  the  two  major  political  par-
ties).

Reliance  upon  American  Party  of  Tezxas  V.  White
is  misplaced.  That  case  did  not  involve  convention
expenses  as  such,  but  rather  the  cost  of holding  an  elec-
tion—ballot  counting,  ballot  machines,  ete.  As  stated
by  plaintiff’s  reply  brief,  below,  ‘financing  the  me-
chanics  of  election  day  activities  is  obviously  far  dif-
ferent  from  financing  political  conventions  (which  the
Texas  statute  did  not  do  for  any  parties),  which  are
often  little  more  than  exercises  in  political  publicity,
particularly  when  the  renomination  of  an  incumbent
President  is  involved.’’  PR  Br.  at  67.

B. Laws  Less Restrictive  Than  the Public  Financing  Provisions

Contained  in the Challenged  Statutes  Would
the Reforms  Sought  To  Be Achieved  by the Congress

“(Elven  though  the  governmental

Preventing  public  financing  procedures  (and  cam-

competing  constitutional  freedoms  and  restraints  is  a
most difficult  objective  to  achieve.  Any  public  financ-
ing  plan  must  be  continually  aware  of the  hazards  in-
volved  in  achieving  such  an  objective  while,  at  the
same time,  responding  to two  very  important  criteria—

As Os Ren

The  way  in  which  the  government  intervenes  in
the political  process  is  particularly  troubling,  because
it  is  difficult  to  keep  interveution  from  becoming  in-
extricably  intertwined  with  certain  parties  or  candi-
dates  and  thus  infringing  upon  the  rights  of  freedom
of  speech  and  association  of  others.

As  demonstrated  above,  each  of  the  eligibility  and
allocation  formulae  in  Subtitle  H  invidiously  denies
certain  voters  an  equal  opportunity  to  elect  the  candi-
date  of  their  choice.  In  addition,  the  general  election

wi Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479,  488  (1960)

(footnote

omitted).

**  See  for  e.g.,  Redish,  Campaign  Spending

,

ng

Law

Laws  and  the  First

}

Amendment,  46  N.Y.U.  L.R.  900  (1971);  Fleishman,
and  Rosenthal,  supra  n.  10.

s1
——

44

45

provisions  of  Subtitle  H  substantially  inhibit  exercise
of  the  First  Admendment  right  to  free  and  effective
political  association.  Accordingly,  whether  on  equal
protection”  or  First  Amendment  grounds,  each  of the
provisions  here  in question  should  be subjected  to strict
judicial  scrutiny.”

Classifications  subject  to strict  scrutiny  generally  are
invalidated  if  their  defenders  fail  to  prove  that  the
classification  represents  the  least  discriminatory  meth-
od of effectively  promoting  the  permissible  purposes  of
the  legislation.  See  e.g.,  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709

*?In  numerous  cases  this  Court  has  indicated  that  the  due
process  clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  guarantees  equal  protec-
tion  of  the  laws’on  the  federal  level,  and  incorporates  the  analyti-
cal  techniques  and  standards  of  review  developed  under  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  See,  e.g.,  United  States  Department  of
Agriculture  v.  Moreno,  413  U.S.  528  (1973);  Schneider  v.  Rusk,
377  U.S.  163  (1964);  Bolling  v.  Sharpe,  347  U.S.  497  (1954).  See
also  Shapiro  v.  Thompson,  394  U.S.  618  ( (1969).

°°  This  Court  has  suggested  that  if  it  is  established  1)  that  the
purposes  of  the  statute  in  question  are  benign  or  remedial,  and
2)  that  the  statute  operates  to  enhance  rather  than  abridge  the
enjoyment  of  personal  rights,  then  a  court  may  apply  a  rational
basis  standard  of  review  even  though  as  a  general  matter  strict
scrutiny  would  be  appropriate.  See  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384
U.S.  641,  657  (1966).  By  its  own  terms,  this  ‘‘remedial  purposes
exception’’  is applicable  only  when  strict  scrutiny  would  otherwise
be  based  on  a  suspect  classification  theory.  It  cannot  be  triggered
when  strict  scrutiny  is  based  on  the  theory  that  the  denial  of
equal  treatment  denies  constitutionally  protected  rights—the  rule
requires  both  that  the  purposes  of  the  legislation  be  remedial,  and
that  the  denial  of  equal  treatment  enhance  rather  than  abridge
the  enjoyment  of  personal  rights.  As  demonstrated  supra,  althoug?
the  ostensible  purpose  of  public  campaign  finance  legislation  i: to
promote  equality  of  political  opportunity,  the  particular  eligdility
and  allocation  formulae  here  in  question  will  operate  wit!  respect
to  certain  persons  to  abridge  rather  than  erhance  theenjoyment
of  personal  rights.  Therefore,  these  formulae  do  not  fll  within  the
remedial  purposes  exception.

(1974)  ; Shapiro  v.  Thompson,  394 U.S. 618, 634  (1969).
In  addition,  proponents  of  the  ‘sliding  seale”’  ap-
proach  to  equal  protection  analysis—whereby  the
strictness  of  the  standards  against  which  a  challenged
classification  is  tested  veries  according  to  the  ‘facts
and  circumstances  behind  the  law,  the  interest  which
the  state  claims  to  be  protecting,  and  the  interests  of
those  who  are  disadvantaged  by  the  classification’’—
seem  inclined  where  important  individual  rights  or
interests  are  at  stake  to  require  the  government  to
establish  at  the  least  that  there  is  no  method  of  effec-
tively  promoting  the  permissible  purposes  of  the  leg-
islation  that  would  be  less  discriminatory  than  the
method  under  review.  See  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.
724,  756-57  (Brennan,  J., dissenting) .”

As stated  by Professor  Joel  L. Fleishman  in a  recent
article  reviewing  public  financing  alternatives,  the
Courts  in  assessing  First  Amendment  infrineements
‘‘would  weigh  the  means  chosen  against  all  other
reasonably  available  means  in  order  to  be  certain  that
it  is  the  least  onerous  alternative.’’™

This  Court  has  emphasized  that  ‘‘the  First  Amend-
ment needs  breathing  space  and  that  statutes  attempt-
ing  to  restrict  or  burden  the  exercise  of  First  Amend-

Ye This  Court  has  considered  specific  statutory  alternatives  in
striking  down  statutory  provisions  that  infringe  upon  constitn-
tionally  protected  rights.  Martin  v.  City  of Struthers,  319  U.S.  141,
147,  148  (1943).
See  also  O’Brien  v.  Skinner,  414  U.S.  524,  534
(1974)  (Marshall,  J.,  concurring).

*?  Fleishman,  n.10  supra  at  413.

Also  see  United  States  v.
Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  267-268  (1967);  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  US.
479,  488  (1960),  and  Note,  The  Less  Restrictive  Alternative  in
Constitutional  Adjudication;  An  Analysis,  a  Justification  and
Some  Criteria.

27  Vand.  L.R.  971  (1974).

46

47

ment  rights  must  be  narrowly  drawn  and  represent  a
considered  legislative  judgment  that  a particular  mode
of expression  has  given  way  to other  compelling  needs
of society.’’  Broadrick  v.  Oklahoma,  413  U.S.  601,  611-
612  (1973).”

Amicus  Metcalf  submits  that  the  challenged  public
financing  statutes  do  significantly  infringe  upon  cer-
tain  fundamental  First  and  Fifth  Amendment  pro-
tected  freedoms  and  that  defendants  below  have  failed
to  prove  that  the  eligibility  and  allocation  provisions
of Subtitle  1 represent  the  least  discriminatory  meth-
od  of  effectively  promoting  the  permissible  objectives
of  the  enactments.  Further,  Amicus  Metcalf  contends
that  the  record  affirmatively  establishes  that  several
alternative  approaches  would  more  effectively  promote
the  permissible  objectives  of the  statutes  in a  substan-
tially  less  discriminatory  manner.

The  efforts  of  defendants  below  to  establish  that
the  differential  funding  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  rep-
resent  the  least  discriminatory  methods  of  effectively
promoting  the  permissible  purposes  of  the  legislation
are  limited  to  the  fourfold  assertion  that  a  voucher
approach  to  public  campaign  financing  is undesirable.
They  assert  such  a  procedure:

(1)  ‘‘eould”’  increase  the  power  of  groups  able  to

collect  vouchers  quickly  and  efficiently ;

(2)  ‘*eould”’  lead  such  groups  to  use  coercion  to  ag-

gregate  their  voucher  strength;

53  See  Roc  v.  Wade,  410  U.S.  113  (1973);  Dunn  v.  Blumstein,
405  U.S.  330  (1972);  Shapiro  v.  Thompson,  394  U.S.  618  (1969)
and  Talley  v.  California,  362  U.S.  60  (1960).
See  also  Dreas  v.
Myers,  535  P.2d  541  (Ore.  Sup.  Ct.  1975)  where  the  State  of  Ore-
gon’s  campaign  financing  laws  was  found  to  be  unconstitutional
based  on  the  less  restrictive  alternative  test.

(3)  ‘‘might’’  lead  to  the  development  of  a  black

market  in  vouchers;  and

(4)  provides  no  assurance  that  a  candidate  will  re-
ceive  voucher  funds  in time  to  conduct  a  mean-
ingful  campaign.  ID  R at 60-65.

These  arguments  are  without  merit.  The  first  three
are  not  only  highly  speculative,  but  could  easily  be
averted  by provisions  in  voucher  legislation  for  harsh
eriminal  penalties  for  such  conduct.  With  regard  to
the  fourth  assertion  it  suffices  to  say  that  a  candi-
date’s  decision  to  seek  vouchers  late  rather  than  early
in  the  campaign  would  be  to  a  significant  extent  a
function  of a  candidate’s  strategy—-which  presumably
is a  matter  over  which  the  candidate  enjoys  some  con-
trol.  Iii  aiiy  case,  surely  a  voucher  plan  gives  a  candi-
date  more  control  over  when  campaign  subsidies  are
allocated  than  the  post-election  grant  provision  of Sub-
title  H.

Defendants  below  clearly  have  failed  to  prove  that
Subtitle  H represents  the  least  discriminatory  method
of effectuating  the  permissible  purposes  of the  legisla-
tion.  For  this  reason  alone  the  provisions  here  at  issue
should  be  invalidated.  See  Storer  v.  Brown,  415  U.S.
724, 760-61  (Brennan  J., dissenting)  (1974).

It  is  not  difficult  to  demonstrate  that  Congress  can
select  from  among  several  procedures  for  allocating
campaign  subsidies  which  would  promote  the  permis-
sible  purposes  of the  legislation  more  effectively  than
Subtitle  H,  and  in  a  significantly  less  discriminatory
manner.

One  less  onerous  alternative  that  would  accomplish
campaign  financing  reform  objectives  without  infring-

48

49

ing  upon  protected  Constitutional  rights  is  a  voucher
plan  introduced  by Amicus  Metealf  in  the Congress,”
which  was  noted  by the  majority  and  in  the  dissenting
opinions  of  the  courts  below.  Buckley  v.  Valeo.  Slip
Op.  at  1505  and  1561.  Under  this  plan  all  taxpayers
who  checked  the  appropriate  box  on  their  income  tax
forms  would  receive  political  campaign  contribution
vouchers.  A  box  designated  for  ‘‘congressional  elec-
tions’?  would  appear  on  the  tax  forms  every  year;  a
separate  ‘‘  Presidential  election’’  check-off  box  would
appear  only  in  the  two  years  preceding  presidential
election  years.

Prior  te  each  non-presidential  election  year,  each
taxpayer  would  have  been  sent  two  vouchers  designated
for  use  in  congressional  races,  one  of  which  would
have  been  received  in  time  for  use  in the  primaries  if
the  taxpayer  so  desired.  Otherwise,  he  or  she  would  be
permitted  to  use  both  vouchers  in  the  general  election.

Prior  to  each  Presidential  election  year,  in  addition
to the congressional  vouchers,  the taxpayer  would  have
been  sent  two  vouchers  a ‘signated  for  use  in the  pres-
idential  race.  Again,  one  of these  would  have  been  re-
ceived  in  time  for  use  in  the  primaries  but  would  re-
main  available  for  use  in  the  general  election  if  the
taxpayer  so  desired.

To  avoid  abuses,  however,  these  vouchers  could  not
be  carried  over  from  election  to  election;  they  would
become  invalid  after  the  date  of  the  general  election
every  two  years.  Further,  criminal  penalties  are  pro-

**S.  1760,  94th  Congress,  Ist  Sess.,  Cong.  Ree.  (daily  ed)  S-

8445-8448.

vided  for  any  person  who  sells,  offers  to  sell,  or  pur-
chase  vouchers.

Each  voucher  would  be  redeemable  for  one  dollar
when  presented  to  the  Department  of  the  Treasury,
pursuant  to regulations  issued  by the  Federal  Election
Commission.  Qualified  candidates,  political  commit-
tees,  and  parties  would  be  eligible  to  receive  vouchers.
The  vouchers  would  have  no  value  for  anyone  else,  or
for  any  taxpayer  who  neglected  or  decided  not  to  use
them.  The  Congress  would  appropriate  sufficient  funds
to  cover  the  number  of  vouchers  requested  annually,
and  the  amount  not  redeemed  by the  end  of each  bien-
nial  campaign  would  revert  to  the  Treasury.

Such  a  public  financing  system  would  not  necessitate
an  artifical  or  discriminatory  funding  formula.  Greater
individual  participation  and  interest  in  election  cam-
paigns  would  be  fostered.  Vouchers  would  be  avail-
able  to  and  could  be  redeemed  by any  candidate  who
has  a ‘‘modicum  of support.’’  Therefore,  public  finane-
ing  would  be  provided  ‘‘serious’’  candidates,  based  on
their  current  level  of support.  But  the  voucher  system
would  not  operate  either  to  encourage  frivolous  candi-
dacies  by those  with  little  support  or  to  provide  con-
tinuing  support  for  candidacies  by  those  who,  while
enjoying  substantial  support  in  a  prior  election,  no
longer  command  significant  support.

Further,  placing  the  ultimate  decision  with  the  pub-
lie  as  to  the  share  of public  funds  each  party  or  candi-
date  would  receive  would  eliminate  the  danger  of
partisan  political  manipulation  of  such  publie  funds.
Finally,  since  each  individual  would  be  aecorded  con-
trol  over  the  same  value  of  vouchers,  which  he  would
make  available  to  the  candidate  or  party  of his  choice,
such  a  system  would  not  discriminate  against  an  indi-

50

51

vidual’s  right  to  an  equal  opportunity  to  participate
to  the  political  process,  nor  abridge  in any  manner the
right  of  a  citizen  to  free  and  effective  political  asso-
ciation.

Another  less  restrictive  alternative  with  elements
of the  voucher  system  has  recently  been  recommended
by  David  Adamany  and  George  Agree.  Based  on  an
extensive  analysis  of campaign  financing  conducted  for
the  20th  Century  Fund,”  the  Adamany-Agree  proposal
would  establish  a  mixed  voucher-flat  grant  program.
Their  proposed  procedure  is similar  in some  respects  to
Amicus  Metealf’s  voucher  plan  discussed  supra,  and
would  provide  for public  financing  of both  Presidential
and  Congressional  candidates  as  well  as  a  system  of
direct  grants  to  political  parties.

A third  type  of  less  restrictive  alternative  contains
a  petition  option  procedure  under  which  a  candidate
would  be able  to obtain  public  financing  by either  qual-
ifying on  the  basis  of a  past election  percentage  factor,
or  submitting  petitions  bearing  a  specified  percentage
of eligible  voters.”

These  and  other  less  restrictive  alternatives  to  the
challenged  statutes  have  been  widely  discussed,  and
have  received  substantial  support  among  public  officials
and  constitutional  scholars.”

55  Adamany  and  Agree.  Political  Money:  The  Strategy  for  Cam-

paign  Financing  in  America  (1975)  Chapter  11.

See  S.  1954,  93rd  Cong.  1st  Sess.,  introduced  by  Senators

Stevenson  of  Illinois  and  Mathias  of Maryland.

57  See  Adamany  and  Agree,  supra,  n.  55  at  Chapter  10.

Also
see  61  Am.  Bar  Assoc.  J.  1085  (August,  1975)  Report  of  the
American  Bar  Association  Special  Committee  on  Election  Reform,
adopted  August,  1975  by  the  House  of  Delegates  of  the  American
Bar  Association.

Undoubtedly  the  area  of campaign  reform  presents
difficult  problems,  but  these  are  not  so  complex  ™  that
they  foreclose  the  above  mentioned  alternatives.  In-
deed,  some  of the  alternatives  noted  herein  would  be  a
far  more  practical  way—in  the  sense  of  efficiency  and
convenience—of  achieving  electoral  reform  than  the
challenged  statutes.  Certainly  the  voucher  procedure
would  eliminate  the  practical  problems  confronting  a
Secretary  of  the  Treasury  in  administering  and  dis-
tributing  public  financing,  problems  which  will  be com-
pounded  if public  receptivity  to the  tax  check  off  sys-
tem  as  presently  structured  remains  relatively  low.

This  Court  in the  past  has  recognized  that  valid  al-
ternatives  to accomplish  a  permissible  objective  should
be  considered  in  scrutinizing  a  constitutionally  flawed
statute.  Indeed,  ‘‘if  a  solution  exists  that  is  equally
solicitous  of the  state’s  interest  ...  but  is more  solicit-
ous  of individual]  interests,  a court  certainly  should  not
be  precluded  from  protecting  the  individual.’  ™

Clearly  less  restrictive  alternatives  exist  for  public
financing  of  elections.  The  less  restrictive  means  test
should  be  invoked  in  this  case  to  preserve  equality  of
opportunity  to  participate  in  the  political  process—
surely  one  of  the  most  fundamental  rights  of  the  cit-
izen  “—and,  accordingly,  the  public  financing  provi-
sions  of the  challenged  statutes  should  be  found  to  be
constitutionally  defective.

°°  Id.  e.f.  San  Antonio  Indep.  Sch.  Dist.  v.  Rodriguz,  411  US.  1,

41  (1973).

°°  27  Vand.  L.R.  971  (1974)  supra  n.  52  at  1022.
In  fact,  such  a  right  is  a  combination  of  many  protections
under  the  First,  Fifth,  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  and,  taken
together,  are  the  essential  guarantors  of  political  freedom  in  our
representative  system  of  government.

52

CONCLUSION

Amicus  Metealf  respectfully  submits  for  the  reasons
herein  stated  that  the  public  financing  provisions  of the
FECA,  FECA  Amendments  and  Subtitle  H  should  be
found  to  be  violative  of  the  Constitution  and  that  the
decisions  of  the  Courts  below  upholding  said  statutes
should  be  reversed.

Respectfully  submitted,

INTEREST  OF  THE  AMICUS  CURIAE!

The  California  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  is
one  of  four  political  parties  qualified  for  ballot  status
in  California.

The  Los  Angeles  County  Central  Committee
of  the  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  is the  body  officially

;  Letters  of  consent  to  the  filing  of  this  brief

have  been  filed  with  the  Clerk  of  the  Court.

authorized  to  conduct  the  party's  campaign  in  Los
Angeles  County  under  California  Election  Code  Sec-
As  of  September  12,  1975,  5,883  voters
tion  8940.
were  registered  as  affiliated  with  the  Peace  and
Freedom  Party  in  that  county,  an  increase  from  4,071
in  January  1975.

Both  the  Los  Angeles  County  Central  Com-
mittee  and  the  State  Central  Committee  of  the
California  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  are  affiliated
nationally  with  the  People's  Party,  whose  1972  presi-
dential  candidate,  Dr.  Benjamin  Spock,  appeared  on
the  ballots  of  10  states,  including  California,  and  was
of  the  four  presidential  candidates  assigned
one
Secret  Service  protection  during  the  general  election
campaign.  The  People's  Party  expects  to  increase  the
number  of  states  in  which  it  is  on  the  ballot  in  1976
and  the  California  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  will  hold
a  presidential  primary  election  on  June  8, 1976.

Tite  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  has  a  dispro-
portionately  high  number  of  poor  members  and  in
1974,  34  of  its  44  candidates  for  partisan  state  and
federal  office  filed  under  Court  order  after
the
Federal  District  Court  in  the  Northern  District  of
California  certified  their  inability  to  pay  the  candi-
date
Being
classified  as  a  "new  party"  by  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Acts  of  1971  and  1974,  its  nominee  for
president  would  be  denied  advance  public  funding  in
the  general  election,  regardless  of  her  or  his  potential

time.

that

In  early  1973,  the  Los  Angeles  County  Peace
and  Freedom  Party  Central  Comittee  sent  a  mailing
to  all  of  its  members,  urging  them  to  utilize  the  $1.00
check-off  established  by  26  U.S.C.  6096,  designating
their  $1.00  for  the  sole  use  of  the  nominee  of  the
"California  Peace  and  Freedom  Party."
All  such
dollars  designated  by  its  members  solely  for  the  use

of  the  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  nominee  were
subsequently  appropriated  by  the  Congress  by  P.L.
93-53  for  the  use  of  other  parties.

The  Peace  and  Freedom  Party  has  been  in  the
forefront  of  efforts  to  open  up  the  political  process
to  more  effective  participation  by  the  poor  and  by
dissenting  political  minorities.  Its  efforts  in  this  area
include  numerous  lawsuits  in  both  the  state  and
federal  court  systems,  including  Lubin  v.  Panish,  415
U.S.  709  (1974),  sponsored  by the  Southern  California
ACLU  Foundation  at  the  request  of  the  Peace  and
Freedom  Party.  Donald  Paul  Lubin  was  a  member  of
the  Los  Angeles  County  Central  Committee  of  the
Peace  and  Freedom  Party.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

This  brief  focuses  on  the  First  and  Fifth
Amendment  challenges  to  the  federal  system  for
public  financing  of  presidential  elections  contained  in
certified  questions  5  and  6  before  the  Court  of
Appeals.
The  judgment  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  on
these  two  questions  should  be  reversed.

infirmities

of  the  matching  system

Extensive  attention  is  devoted  to  the  consti-
tutional
for
This  system  confers  a
funding  primary  elections.
discriminatory  advantage  on  candidates  with  wealth-
ier  supporters,  thereby  discriminating  against  other
candidates  and  their  supporters  in  violation  of  the
First  Amendment  and  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment.  It  is  noted  that  the  state  asserts
no  compelling  interest  that  candidates  with  wealthier
supporters  be  given  more  public  funds  than  candidates
with  poor  supporters.
Furthermore,  indigent  voters
are  absolutely  excluded  from  effective  participation
in  this  integral  new  portion  of  the  electoral  process
solely  because  of  their  inability  to  make  a  monetary
contribution.

4

5

The  eligibility

criteria  chosen  for  public
funding  in  both  the  primary  and  general  elections  are
remarkably  ill-fitted  to  the  asserted  state  interest  of
limiting  public  funding  to  "serious"  candidates.
The
primary  election
is  based  on  wealth,  a
criteria
"traditionally  disfavored"  and  "suspect"  classification,
and  violates  the  "constitutionally  protected  right  to
participate  in  elections  on  an  equal  basis  with  other
citizens."
The  general  election  funding  criteria  fails
to  accommodate  the  fluidity  of  American  politics,
excluding  serious  new  party  candidates  from  any
advance  public  funding,  regardless  of  their  strength,
thereby  infringing  on  the  associational  rights  of  their

he  ran  second  in  both  the  popular  and  electoral  vote.

Both  the  primary  and  general  election  public
funding  systems  infringe  on  basic  constitutional  rights
and  must  be  subject  to  "close  judicial  scrutiny"  and

The  Court  is  further  reminded  that  there  is  a
vital  public  interest--a  state  interest--in  maintaining
a  diversity  of  political  views  within  the  electoral
system  and  that  "right  conclusions  are  more  likely  to
be  gathered  out  of  a  multitude  of  tongues  than
through  any  kind  of  authorative  selection."

The  $1.00  check-offs  designated  for  specified
party  nominees  in  early  1973  were  misappropriated
for  the  use  of  other  candidates  by P.L.  93-53.

ACT  OF  1974,  BY  PROVIDING  PRI-
MARY  ELECTION  PUBLIC  FUNDING
BASED  SOLELY  UPON  THE  MATCH-
ING  OF  PRIVATE  CONTRIBUTIONS,

POOR

MENT

AND

THE

BY

A.

The  Matching  System  Confers  an
Advantage  on  Candidates  With
Wealthier  Supporters  in  Violation
of the  Due
Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment.

Chapter  96  of  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal
Revenue  Code  of  1954  is  known  as  the  Presidential
Primary  Matching  Payment  Account  Act,  hereinafter
cited  as  the  Primary  Matching  Payment  Act.
26
U.S.C.  9031-9042.  For  every  qualified  dollar  raised  in
the  Account  will  give  the
private  contributions,
candidate  one
The  Act
in  public  funds.
dollar
provides  matching  funds  only  for  the  first  $250
contributed  to  a  candidate  by every  individual  donor.
26  U.S.C.  9034(a).
However,  to  be  eligible  for  any
matching  payments,  a  candidate  must  raise  at  least
$102,000  in  such  qualified  matchable  contributions,
with  at  least  $5,000  required  to  be  raised  in  each  of
at  least  20  states.

6

7

This  system  cannot  be  sustained  just  because
it  is  "workable,"  as  asserted  by  the  Court  of  Appeals
in  the  opinion  below  at  page  1566  (West's  advance
sheets).  The  question  is  whether  or  not  the  system  is
constitutional.

Any  public  campaign  financing  system  basing
public  payments  on  the  matching  of  private  contribu-
tions  confers  a  distinct  advantage  on  the  candidate
with  wealthier  supporters.
The  candidate  whose
supporters  can  contribute  $250  each  will  receive  250
times  as  many  public  dollars  as  the  candidate  with
the  same  number  of  supporters  who  are  financially
able  to  contribute  an  average  of  only  $1.00  each.
With  their  public  funds,  then,  the  candidate  with  the
wealthier  supporters  can  purchase  250  times  as  many
radio  advertisements,  television  spots,  handbills,  pos-
ters,  billboards,  newspaper  advertisements  or  mass
mailings  as  the  candidate  with  the  poorer  supporters.
The  advantage  conferred  on  the  candidate  with  the
wealthier  supporters  is  neither  small  nor  subtle,  it  is
immense  and  blatant.

The  State  asserts  no  compelling  interest  that
candidates  with  wealthy  supporters  be  given  more
ublic  funds  than  candidates  with  poor  supporters.
The State  does  assert  an  interest  in limiting  payments
to  "serious"  candidates.  But,  as  a  matter  of  law,  one
can  no  more  say  that  a  candidate  lacks  "seriousness"
solely  because  that  candidate's  supporters  are  of
limited  financial  means  than  one  can  say  that  a
candidate  lacks  "seriousness"  solely  because  she  or  he
is  personally  unable  to  pay  a  monetary  filing  fee.
Lubin  v.  Panish,  415  U.S.  709,  716-717  (1974),  Bullock
v.  Carter,  405  U.S.  134,  145-146  (1972),  Georgia
Socialist  Workers  Party  v.  Fortson,  315  F.Supp.  Toss,
»  affirmed  on  other  grounds  as
-D.

fications  based  on  wealth  are  inherently  "suspect"  and
"traditionally  disfavored,"  McDonald  v.  Board  of

Ga.

Harper  v.  Virginia  Board  of  Elections,  383  U.S.  663,
(1966),  Douglas  v.  California,  372 U.S.  353  (1963).
668

The  granting,  by  state  action,  of  such  an
immense  advantage  to  candidates  with  wealthier
supporters  invidiously  discriminates  against  candi-
dates  with  poor  supporters  in  violation  of  the  Due
Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment.  As  stated
by  this  Court  in  Johnson  _v.  Robison,  415  U.S.  361
(1974)  at  pages  364-365,  note  4:

standard  of  judgment,  the  matching  system  must

B.

8

The  Conferring of a  Discrimina-
tory  Advantage  on  Candidates
With  Wealthier  Supporters  Dis-
criminates  Against  Voters  Sup-
porting  Other  Candidates,
Effec-
tively  Diluting  the  Weight  of
Their Votes  in  Violation  of  the
Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth
Amendment  and  Abridging  «ir
Right of Association  Guaranteed
by the  First  Amendment.

The  rights  of  candidates  and  voters

are
closely  intermingled.  As  this  Court  stated  in  Lubin  v.
Panish,  supra,  415  U.S.  709  (1974),  at  page  716:

voters  can  assert  their  preferences
only  through  candidates  or  parties  or  both,
and  it  is  this  broad  interest  that  must  be
weighed  in  the  balance."

The  matching  system,  by  placing  candidates
with  poor  supporters  in  a  disadvantaged  position,
abridges  those  candidates'  supporters'  rights  to  as-
sociate  effectively  for  the  advancement  of  their  own
political  beliefs.  See  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  U.S,  134,
143  (1972),  and  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23,  31
(1968).

has

this

Court

Furthermore,

continually
widened  the  scope  of  the  voter's  right  to  equal
protection  in  the  electoral  process  over  the  past
At  first,  this  was  expressed  in  the
fifteen  years.
requirement  that  each  voter  have  an  equal  vote.
v.  Sanders,
Baker  v.  Carr,  369  U.S.  186  (1962),  Grey

372  U.S.  368  (1963),  Wesberry  v.  Sanders; 376 U.S.
(1964).
Then,  the  Court  broadened  its  language  in
Reynolds  v.  Sims,  377  U.S.  533  to  declare  at  page  565
that:

9

"...
Full  and  effective  participation  by
all  citizens  in...  government  requires  ...
equally  effec-
that  each  citizen  have  an
tive  voice  in  the  election.  ..."  (Empha-
sis  added.)

<<

Finally,  in  Dunn  v.  Blumstein,  405  U.S.  330

(1972)  this  Court  stated  its  position  at  page  336:

"4.
In  decision  after  decision,  this
Court  has  made  it  clear  that  a  citizen
has  a  constitutionally  protected  right  to
participate  in  elections  on
an  equal
basis  with  other  citizens  in  the  jurisdic-
tion."

Under  the  Primary  Matching  Payment  Act,  a  poor
citizen  obviously  has  a  much  less  effective  voice  than
a  wealthy  citizen  in  assisting  the  candidate  the  poor
citizen  supports  to  qualify  for  public  funding  and  to
receive  the  full  amount  of  such  funding.
The  poor
citizen  may  still  participate  in  the  election,  but  she
or  he  certainly  does  not  do  so  on  an  "equal  basis"  with
the  wealthy  citizen  under  the  matching  system.

The  California  Supreme  Court  carried  the
United  States  Supreme  Court's  various  decisions  to
their  logical  conclusion  in  its  unanimous  decision  in
Gould  v.  Grubb,  14  C.3d  661  (July  7, 1975),  holding  at
page  670:

a  Statute,  ordinance  or  election
"4.
practice  which  reserves  such  an  advan-
tage  for  a  particular  class  of  candidates
inevitably  dilutes  the  weight  of  the  vote
of  all  those  electors  who  cast  their
ballots
for  a  candidate  who  is  not
included  within  the  favored  class."

The  Primary  Matching  Pay  nents  Act  "reserves  such

September  6,  i975  Los  Angeles  Times,  on  page  l,

California's  unemployment  rate  peaked  at  10.6%,  the

situation  has  followed  a  similar  pattern.

The  poor,  the  indigent,  and  the  unemployed
can  be  found  in  all  mass  political  parties,  whether
Democratic,
Independent,
Peace  and  Freedom,  Libertarian
or  independent.
They  may  not  be  easy  to  see.  Many  are  disheartened
and  have  ceased  to  participate.  As  noted  by the  New
York  Times  editorial  of  December  4, 1972:

Republican,

American

"There  is  a  direct  correlation
between  income  and  political  participa-
tion.
large
The  poor  do  not  vote  in
numbers.  ...  They  cannot  believe  that  if
they  vote,  it  will  make  any  difference
in  their  lives  or  in  the  nation's  course."

Previouly,  governmental  bodies  had  sought  to  encour-
age  the  poor  to  participate,  emphasizing  that  the
state,  in  every  official  respect,  would  weight  their
participation  equally  with  that  of  the  wealthiest
citizen  in  all  phases  of  the  electoral  process.  Now,

12

13

with  the  matching  system,  the  poor  see  Congress
reversing  this  direction  and  moving  officially  to
demean  their  participation  in  the  political  process
because  of  their  poverty.

This  Court  has  been  vigilent  in  defending  the
constitutional  rights  of  poor  citizens  to  participate
fully  in  the  political  process.
It  has  moved  relent-
lessly  over  the  past  decades  to  strike  down  state-
to  equal  citizen
imposed  wealth-related  barriers
Harper  vy.
participation  in  the  electoral  process.

wealth-re!ated  barrier  to  equal  participation  and,  in

The  Court  of  Appeals  in  its  opinion  below  in
the  instant  case,  upheld  both  the  matching  system

of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  as  furthering  the
State's  interest  in  "reducing  the  reliance  of  Presiden-
tial  candidates  on  large  contributors,  thus  reducing
their  influence  on  the  outcome  of  elections  and  on

the  operation  of  government."  West's  advance  sheets,

the  primary  election  on  a  matching  system  is  cer-
tainly  destructive  of  such  a  stated  goal.  In  the  words
of  one  legal  scholar,  Joel  L.

article  on  "Public  Financing  of  Election  Campaigns:

from  private  sources  a  fixed  amount  of

leaves  wealthier  citizens  in  the  position
of  determining  who  will  and  will  not
receive  public  campaign  funds.
If  the
ceilings  of  individual  contributions  were
sufficiently  low--ten  to  twenty  dollars--
it is conceivable  that  such  a  mechanism
might  be  held  constitutional.
Higher
contribution  ceilings,  however,  would
permit  the  wealthy  to  control  the  field
of  primary  candidates  and  then  to  enjoy
a  free
ride  at  the  expense  of  the
taxpayers  for  the  major  portion  of  the
campaign  costs  themselves.

"Requiring

a  money  payment
alone,  therefore,  would  be  unconstitu-
tional.  ..."

The  Primary  Matching  Payment  Act  attempts
to  sort  out  "serious"  from  frivolous  candidates  by

eligibility  criteria  expressly  solely  in  terms  of  dollars
contributed  in  each  state.

out  spurious  candidates  from  "serious"  ones  with
filing  fees,  however,  such  a  wealth-related  criteria  is
"extraordinarily  ill-fitted  to  that  goal"  and  infringes
on  constitutionally  protected  rights.
Bullock  v.
Carter,  supra,  405  U.S.  134,  146  (1972),  Lubin  v.

Under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act
("FECA"),  eligibility  for  advance  public  funding  and
the  level  of  such  funding  is  solely  dependent  upon  the
percentage  of  the  vote  received  by  the  party's
presidential  nominee  four  years  earlier.  There  is  no
alternative  method  by  which  a  new  party  nominee
may  prove  her  or  his  "seriousness."  despite  the  fact
that  this  Court  has  recognized  that  other  "obvious
and  well-known  means  of  testing  the  'seriousness'  of  a
candidacy,"  such  as  a  petition  requirement,  could  be
employed.  Lubin  v.  Panish,  supra,  415  U.S.  709,  718
(1974).
The  possibility  of  post-election  reimburse-
ment  for  a  new  party  nominee  does  not  save  the

18

19

system.  The  new  party  nominee  will  still  have  had  to
divert  70%  of  her  or  his  energies  away  from  cam-
paigning  to  fund  raising.

Judge  Tamm's  dissent  cites  how  poorly  the
eligibility  formula  would  have  applied  to  the  elections
of  1968  and  1972.

Theodore  Roosevelt,  quoted  from  at  length  in  the
opinion  below  at  pages  1511-1512  as  an  early  advo-
cate  of  public  campaign  financing,  ran  as  the  candi-
date  of  the  newly-formed  Progressive  Party  in  1912.
Roosevelt,  even  though  he  ran  second  in  both  the
popular  and  electoral  vote  that  year,  would  have  been
ineligible  to  receive  any  advance  public  funding,
under  the  FECA  criteria.
Was  Roosevelt  not  a
serious  candidate?
Or  are  the  criteria  for  advance
funding  drawn  in  such  a  way  as  to  exclude  some
serious  candidates?  Obviously,  the  latter  is  the  case.
to
Furthermore,  the  tendency  of  FECA  criteria
perpetuate  existing  established  parties  can  easily  be
deduced.  The  Progressive  Party  largely  disintegrated
as  an  effective  national  organization  prior  to  the
1916  elections,  having  a  candidate  on  the  ballot  in
only  three  or  four  states  and  receiving  barely  35,000
But,  under  the  FECA,
votes  (0.19%  of  the  total).
with  the  prospect  of  millions  of  dollars  in  advance
funds  coming  for  the  party's  1916  nominee,  to  be
funded  as  a  major  party  candidate,  the  party  would
have  been  invigorated  and  many  would  have  sought  its
nomination.  Many  people  might  even  have  deserted
the  Republican  Party,  whose  nominee  would  have
a  minor  party
been  funded  in  advance  only  as
candidate  under  FECA  in  1916,  to  join  a  party
destined  to  receive  greater  funding.  Such  a  senario  is
not  just  speculation,  it  is  the  deliberate  intent  of  the
act  as  expressed  in  Senate  Report  93-689,  issued
February  21,  1974,  to  accompany  the  FECA  bill  for
that  year.  Reprinted  in  United  States  Code,  Congres-
sional  and Administrative  News,  93rd  Congress--2nd
Session,  1974  Volume  3, Legislative  History,  this
document  states,  at  page  5544;

"All  but  fringe  candidates  would
have  an  incentive  to  seek  a  major  parity
nomination,  rather  than  run  as  a  minor
party  candidate,  so  as  to  be  eligible  for

20

the  full  level  of  public  assistance  in  the
general  election.  ..."

If  the  FECA  system  had  been  in  effect  in  the  1840's,
we  would  probably  stil!  have  the  Whig  Party  around
today.  There  can  be  no  doubt  that  the
FECA  will  act,
and  is  designed  to  act,  to  perpetually  establish  the
presently  prevailing  political  parties.  In  this  respect,
then,  the  FECA  is  similar  to  the  Ohio  Election  Code
provisions  struck  down  by  this  Court  in  Williams  vy.
Rhodes,  supra,  393  U.S.  23  (1968).

'...  It  is  to  be  expected  that  a  voter
hopes  to  find  on  the  ballot  a  candidate
who  comes  near  to  reflecting  his  policy
issues.
preferences  on  contemporary
