society.
As  stated  by  the  eminent  Circuit  Judge
Learned  Hand  in  a  freedom  of  the  press  context  in
United  States  v.  Associated  Press,  52  F.Supp.
362
(S.D.  N.Y.,  1943),  at  page  372,  the  dissemination  of
news  and  views  from  many  different  sources,  is:

it

is  not

",..  one  of  the  most  vital  of  all  general
interests  ...  /It/is  closely  akin  to,  if
indeed
the
interest  protected  by  the  First  Amend-
ment;  it  presupposes  that  right  conclu-
sions  are  more  likely  to  be  gathered  out
of  a  multitude  of  tongues,  than  through

the  same

as,

21

any  kind  of  authoritative  selection.  To
many  this  is,  and  always  will  be,  fo!'y;
but  we  have  staked  upon  it our  all."

Elections  are  not  just  the  means  by which  we
select  our  public  officers,  they  are  a  vital  forum  for
the  discussion  of  public  policy.
Third  parties  and
independent  candidacies  play  an  “important  part"  in
this  process  as  recognized  in  the  comprehensive
Harvard Law  Review  article, supra,  "Developments  in
the  Law--Elections,"  88  HLR  IIIT,  at  page  1123:

",..
Frequentiy  they  raise  issues  and
develop  policies  long  before  established
parties  are  prepared  to  act,  and  their
presence  on  the  general  election  ballot
and  participation  in  election  campaigns
permits  voters  to  demonstrate  support
Major
for  new  or  unorthodox  ideas.
party  nominees  may  respond  to  popular
support  for  other  candidates  by reform-
policies  and  programs.
ulating  their
Thus,  despite  a  general  lack  of  electoral
success,  minor  parties  and  independent
candidates  may  eventually  see  substan-
tial  portions  of  their  programs  imple-
mented.

"Minor  parties  and  independent
candidacies  may  also  serve  a  legitimiz-
ing  function,  by  providing  disaffected
voters  with  an  outlet  for  their  frustra-
tion  with  the  established  parties.  With-
out  this  alternative,  dissatisfied  voters
who  find  themselves  repeatedly  con-
fronted  with  unattractive  policies  and
candidates  may  come  to  doubt  the
legitimacy  of  the  entire  electoral  pro-
cess.
It  is  therefore  plain  that  the
importance  of  independent  and  minor

22

candidates
party
ability
to  capture

electoral

their
office."

Thus,  there  is  a  state  interest  in  maintaining  an  open
electoral  process  so  that  the  society  can  grow  and
Legislators  concerned  about  re-
consider  new  ideas.
election  may  be  slow  to  see
it,  but  it  is  there
nonetheless.

Considering  how  often

the  claimed  state
interests  in  regulating  access  to  the  electoral  process
are  stated,  and,  as  noted  in  the  Harvard  Law  Review
article  cited  above  at  page  1137,  perhaps  sometimes
overstated,  it  would  be  a  positive  step  for  this  Court
to  reassert  the  state  interest  in  maintaining  a  diverse
range  of  choice  in  the  electoral  arena.
This  case,
involving  legislation  highly  discriminatory  to  inde-
pendent  candidates  and  minor  parties,  might  provide
a  good  vehicle  for  just  such  a  statement.

THE  PIRACY  OF  THE  RESTRICTED
ACCOUNT  FUNDS  BY  SECTION  6(d)
OF  P.L.  93-53  CASTS  A  DOUBT  ON
THE  "VOLUNTARYNESS"  OF
MIL-
LIONS  OF  DOLLARS  PRESENTLY  IN
THE  PRESIDENTIAL  ELECTION  CAM-
PAIGN  FUND.

In  the  first  year  of  its  operation  under  the
FECA  of  1971,  the  presidential
$1.00  check-off  on
the  federal  income  tax  return  established  by  26
U.S.C.
6096  and  9006  permitted  a  taxpayer  to
designate  her  or  his  $1.00  to  go  to  a  general  fund  for
the  use  of  all  party  candidates  or  to  a  "separate
account"  for  the  sole  use  of  the  presidential  nominee
If  any
of  whatever  party  the  taxpayer  designated.
money  in  a  separate  account  remained  unused  by the

23

party  nominee  after  the  election,  it  would  revert  to
the  “general  fund  of  the  Treasury,"  as  26  U.S.C.
9006(b)  then  required,  but  under  no  circumstances
could  it  be  used  to  benefit  the  candidates  of  any
other  party.

With  this  understanding,  millions  of  American
taxpayers  checked  off  their  dollars  for  designated
“separate  accounts."  in  early  1973.

By  a  rider  to  the  Public  Debt  Limitation  Bill
of  1973,  P.L.  93-53,  however,  the  "separate  accounts"
were  abolished  and  the  moneys  therein  transferred  to
the  general  fund  for  the  use  of  all  party  nominees.
Section  6(d)  of  the  bill  stated  that:

"...  Any  designation  made  /heretofore/
«»  for  the  account  of  candidates  of  any
specified  political  party  shall
be
treated  solely  as  a  designation  to  the
Presidential  Election  Campaign  Fund."

...

Consent  was  not  asked  of  the  individual  taxpayers  to
‘his  change.  Furthermore,  if  a  taxpayer  objected  to
the  change  and  wanted  to  withdraw  her  or  his  check-
off,  the  taxpayer  found  that  it  was  not  possible  to  do
so.

The  Court  of  Appeals,  in  the  opinion  below  at

pages  1507  and  1562,  stated  that:

"4.  If  the  taxpayer  wants  to  avoid  any
conception  that  some  purt  of  ‘his'  tax
money  will  benefit  candidates  he  does
not  favor,  then  he  can  simply  refrain
from  checking  off  his  dollar  for  the
Fund."

This  statement  leaves  an  erroneous  impression  that
all  the  money  presently
has  been
voluntarily  collected.

in  the  fund

24

Basically,  the  check-off  device  is  nothing  but
window  dressing,  "a  bookkeeping  manipulation  with  no
practical  impact,"  in  the  words  of  the  Harvard  Law
Review,  supra,  88  HLR  1111,  1266,  note  176, designed
to  give  people  the  sembiance  of  participation  and
control  without  the  substance.

CONCLUSION

The  judgment  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  on

certified  questions  5 and  6 should  be  reversed.

Respectfully  submitted,

MARGUERITE  M,  BUCKLEY
1253  -  7th  Street,  Suite  101
Santa  Monica,  California  90401

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

The  Committee  for  Democratic  Election  Laws  (CoDEL)
represents  a  broad  cross  section  of  citizens  concerned  with
election  laws  which  impede  the  democratic  process.
Co-
DEL’s  activities  include  providing  a  legal  presentation  in
eases  challenging  such  election  laws;  providing  a  voice  for
its  constituents  before  legislatures  concerning  laws  dealing
with  the  electoral  process  and  in  providing  information  re-
garding  a  variety  of  issues  touching  on  the  electoral  pro-
cess,  such  as  efforts  to  obtain  bilingual  ballots  and  to  facili-
tate  voter  registration.

CoDEL  believes  that  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  of  1971  and  the  Amendments  of  1974  burden
the  First,  Fourth,  Fifth,  and  Ninth  Amendment  rights  of
the  Plaintiffs  in  this  action,  of  candidates  and  supporters
of  small  parties  and  of  independent  candidates.
In  addi-
tion,  the  effect  of  the  Act  is  to  institutionalize  the  two  ma-
jor  parties  and  block  access  to  the  electoral  process  on  the
part  of  third  parties  and  independent  candidates,

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

I.

The  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act,  hereinafter  referred
to  as  the  Act  or  FECA,  presents  two  fundamentally  differ-
ent  and  separable  approaches  to  the  problem  of  controlling
election  campaign  abuses.

The  first  approach  is  a  series  of  regulations  controlling
the  financing  of  federal  election  campaigns.  This  approach
consists  of  limits  on  contributions  to  a  campaign  and  on
expenditures  of  a  campaign  coupled  with  requirements  for
reporting  and  disclosing  certain  contributions  and  expendi-
tures.

The  second  approach  is  public  funding  of  presidential

campaigns.

»
-

3

The  rationale  for  such  sweeping  regulation  of campaigns
is  the  federal  government’s  interest  in  preserving  the  in-
tegrity  of  the  electoral  process.

Amicus  Curiae  contends  that  in judging  the  constitution-
ality  of  the  challenged  legislation,  this  important  govern-
mental  interest  must  be  narrowly  construed  as  an  interest
in  preventing  the  outright  corruption  of candidates  and  of-
ficials;  further  that  there  is  no  legitimate  governmental
interest  in  limiting  the  influence  of  wealth  in  election  cam-
Unlike  elections,  i.e.  voting,  campaigns  are  pure
paigns.
speech.
The  inequalities  in  exercising  First  Amendment
rights  which  flow  from  inequalities  in  wealth  are  beyond
the  reach  of  the  federal  government.

Viewed  from  this  perspective,  the  Sections  of  the  Act
regulating  the  private  financing  of  federal  election  cam-
paigns  are  overbroad  in  that  they  are  not  narrowly  drawn
to  prevent  the  evil  with  which  Congress  is  properly  con-
cerned.
Such  regulations  represent  significant  burdens
and  limitations  on  First  Amendment  activity,  especially
with  regard  to  minor  parties,  without  any  reference  to
the  possibility  that  such  activity  might  corrupt  public  of-
ficials.

The  reporting  and  disclosure  requirements  are  equally
overbroad.
The  reporting  requirements  constitute  a  sig-
nificant  strain  on  the  time  and  money  available  to  minor
The  disclosure  requirements  will  present  a  real
parties.
danger  that  the  right  of  association  will  be  chilled  for  all
those  who  hold  unpopular  views.  Again,  such  burdens  are
inflicted  without  regard  to  any  possibility  that  the  regu-
lated  activity  will  corrupt  public  officials.

Il.

The  FECA  taken  as  a  whole,  and  the  public  financing
provisions  in  particular,  are  an  unconstitutional  intrusion
by the  federal  government  into  the  arena  of  public  debate.
The  Act  will  significantly  alter  the  advantages  and  disad-
vantages  that  various  parties  may  have  in  federal  election
The  Constitution  forbids  the  federal  govern-
campaigns,
Further,
ment  from  taking  such  a  role  in  public  debates.

contrary  to  the  purported  intention  of  the  Act,  the  public
financing  sections  and  the  regulations  on  private  financing
further  disadvantage  those  candidates  not  possessed  of
great  wealth  and  therefore  constitute  an  unconstitutional
burden  on  the  right  to  contend  for  public  office.

ARGUMENT

I.

THE  FEDERAL  GOVERNMENT’S  INTEREST  IN
PREVENTING  THE  CORRUPTION  OF THE  ELEC.
TORAL  PROCESS  IS  NARROWLY  LIMITED  BY
THE  FIRST  AMENDMENT.

The  FECA  poses  a  fundamental  issue  regarding  the
In  its  most  basic  terms,
First  Amendment  and  elections.
the  issue  is the  relationship  between  free  speech  and  money.
The  Court  of  Appeals  in  deciding  that  the  Act  was  con-
stitutional,  found  that  Congress  had  acted  to  protect  the
electoral  process  from  the  undue  influence  of  wealth.

The  Court  adopted  the  observation  that  great  wealth  had
the  power  to  ‘‘unduly  influence  politics,  an  influence  not
stopping  short  of  corruption.’’  Slip  opinion  p.  1511

It  is  obvious  that  no  First  Amendment  issues  are  raised
by  the  use  of  money  to  bribe  public  officials.  The  dis-
tinction  between  legitimate  partisan  political  activity  and
bribery  is not  so  obscure  as  to  escape  common  understand-
ing.  U.  S.  v  Hvrriss,  347  U.S.  612,  74  S.  Ct.  808,  98  L.  Ed.
989.

The  difficulty  in  terms  of  First  Amendment  freedoms
arises  when  the  uidue  influence  of  wealth  does  stop  short
of  corruption.

The  approach  of  the  FECA  is  not  to  ban  political  activ-
ity,  but  to  limit  the  mount  of  such  activity  in  an  effort  to
equalize  the  opportunity  for  such  activity.
The  FECA
purports  to  eliminate  the  undue  influence  of  wealth  by
‘‘limiting’’  the  amount  of money  contributed  to  or  spent  by
candidates  in  federal  election  campaigns.

4

Because  of the  necessary  connections  between  money  and
political  activity,  limiting  the  influence  of  wealth  must
necessarily  involve  limiting  political  activity.

Because  of  this  inevitable  conflict  with  First  Amendment
activity,  we  are  faced  with  a  threshold  question.  Can  the
federal  government  regulate  First  Amendment  activity  so
as  to  reduce  the  influence  of  wealth.

This  issue  should  not  be  confused  with  cases  in which  the
limitation  on  First  Amendment  activity  results  from  a  fee
or  costs  imposed  by  the  government.
There  cau  be  no
doubt  that  the  government  cannot  so  limit  First  Amend-
ment  activity.  Harper  v  Virginia  Board  of Flections,  383
U.S.  663,  86  S.  Ct.  1079,  16  L.  Ed.  2d  169;  Bullock  v  Car-
ter,  405  U.S.  134,  92  S.  Ct.  849,  31  L.  Ed.  2d  92;  Grosjean
v  American  Press,  297  U.S.  233,  56  S.  Ct.  444,  80  L.  Ed.
660  (1936).

Here  we  are  confronted  with  an  attempt  by  government
to  correct  the  limitations  on  First  Amendment  activity
which  result  from  the  market  costs  of  such  activity  and  the
unequal  access  to  various  media  of  communications  due  to
disparities  in  wealth.

Aside  from  election  campaigns,  the  most  important  me-
dium  of ideas  in this  country  are  newspapers  and  television.
Because  the  airways  are  public  and  broadcasters  are  li-
censed,  television  is  not  analogous  to  election  campaigns.

Newspapers  clearly  represent  a  case  where  the  wealthy
have  undue  influence  on  election  campaigns.  Yet  that  im-
pact  may  not  be  limited.  Mills  v  Alabama,  384  U.S,  214,  86
S.  Ct.  1434,  16  L.  Ed.  2d  484  (1966).  Recognizing  the  lim-
ited  access  to  this  powerful  medium  of  all  but  the  very
wealthy,  this
Court  considered  a  very  limited  compulsory
access  statute  enacted  by  the  Florida  legislature  in  Miami
Herald  Publishing  Co.  v  Tornillo,  418  U.S.  241,  94  S.  Ct.
2831,  41  L.  Ed.  2d  730  (1974).  The  statute  in  Tormillo  al-
lowed  a  candidate  for  public  office  to  reply  to  newspaper
attacks  on  his  character  or  record.
Thus,  Turnillo  pre-
sented  a  classic  case  of  protecting  the  integrity  of  the  po-
litical  process  from  the  undue  influence  of  wealth.

Without  regard  to  the  benefits  from  such  legislation,  the
Court  found  that  the  First  Amendment  prohibited  the  gov-
ernment  from  enforcing  such  an  ‘‘access”’  law.

As  the  Court  observed;

‘*Tt  has  yet to  he demonstrated  how  governmental  reg-
ulation  of  this  crucial  process  can  be  exercised  con-
sistent  with  First  Amendment  guarantees  of  a  free
press  as  they  have  evolved  to  this  time.’’
U.S.  at  p.
...y  S. Ct.  at  p.  2840.

Given  the  fact  that  our  society  is  marked  by  great  dis-
parities  in wealth,  and  that  wealth  is often  a  prerequisite  to
the  effective  exercising  of  First  Amendment  rights,  we  are
Shall  we  bear
necessarily  confronted  with  a  dilemma.
the  social  costs  of  having  First  Amendment  rights  un-
equally  distributed  accordiing  to  wealth,  or  shall  we  have
governmental  regulation  of  the  exercise  of  those  rights  in
order  to  redress  this  inequality.  Needless  to  say  there  is no
precedent  for  such  regulation.  Moreover,  the  First  Amend-
ment  forbids  ‘‘governmental  restraint  from  the  arena  of
public  diseussion.’’  Cohen  v  Calif.,  403  U.S.  15,  29  L.  Ed.
2d  384  (1971),  91  S.  Ct.  1780,  1787,  88  (emphasis  added).

In  simple  terms,  our  system  of  government  opts  for  the
costs  which  comes  from  foregoing  government  regulation
of speech  as  being  less  costly  than  allowing  the  government
a  role  in  regulating  First  Amendment  Activities.

Therefore,  it is the  contention  of  this  brief  that  the  prop-
er  concern  of  the  government  is  with  that  undue  influence
of wealth  that  does  not  stop  short  of  corruption.

As  a  corollory,  the  various  sections  of  the  FECA  must
bear  a  direct  relation  to  the  prevention  of  such  corruption,
if  they  are  to  be  constitutional.

The  cases  of  Burroughs  d  Cannon  vy  U.S.,  290  U.S.  534,
54  S. Ct.  287,  78 L.  Ed.  484  (1934)  ; U.S.  v  Harriss,  347  U.S.
612,  74S.  Ct.  808,  98  L.  Ed.  989;  U.S.  v UAW,  352  U.S.  567,
77S.  Ct.  529,  1  L.  Ed.  2d  563,  relied  on  by  lower  Court
cannot  be  construed  to  broaden  the  government’s  legiti-
mate  interest  in  preventing  corruption  into  a  generalized
interest  to  limit  the  influence  of  wealth  on  the  political
process.

Burroughs,  supra,  was  concerned  with  the  reporting  and
disclosure  provisions  of  the  Corrupt  Practices  Act.  That
act  was  challenged  on  the  grounds  that  control  of  elections
was  vested  in the  states.  The  Supreme  Court  held  that  the

6

7

federal  government’s  interest  in  preserving  the  integrity
of the  electoral  process  created  a  basis  for  reg lating  cam-
paigns.

However,  the  Court  narrowly  defined  the  federal  govern-
ment’s  interest  to  protecting  the  electoral  process  from
‘‘violence’’  and  ‘‘corruption,’’  supra,  U.S.  p.  546,  S.  Ct.

the  disclosure  laws  violated  the  First  Amendment.

In  Harriss,  supra,  the  Court  took  pains  to  restrict  the
act  requiring  registration  of  lobbyists  so  as  to  apply  only
to  professional  lobbyists  dealing  directly  with  legislators.
As  a  result  the  Court  avoided  a  governmental  invasion  of
the  First  Amendment  right  to  petition  Congress.
The
Court  found  that  there  was  a  difference  between  petitioning
Congress  and  the  activity  of  professional  lobbyists  and
that  whereas  Congress  had  a  right  to  inquire  into  the  spon-
sorship  of  the  latter,  Congress  could  not  make  similar  in-
quiries  of  nonprofessional  lobbying  efforts  even  if  such
efforts  were  generously  financed.

The  lower  Court’s  reliance  on  U.S.  v UAW

is misplaced.
The  UAW  had  been  indicted  for  violating  the  Corrupt
Practices  Act  by  sponsoring  a  television  broadcast  de-
signed  to  influence  an  election.
The  District  Court  held
that  the  indictment  failed  to  state  an  offense  under  the
Statute.

Justice  Frankfurter  who  wrote  the  opinion  for  the  Court
analyzed  the  legislative  history  of  this  law.  The  purpose
of  the  analysis  was  to  determine  whether  or  not  it covered
the  actions  charged  in  the  indictment.

The  Court  found  that  the  purpose  of  the  act  was  to
‘‘avoid  the  deleterious  influences  on  federal  elections  re-
sulting  from  the  use  of  money  by  those  who  exercise  con-
tro]  over  large  aggregations  of  wealth,’’  U.S.
v UAW,  su-
pra,  U.S.  p.  585,  S.  Ct.  p.  538.  The  Court  having  found  the
indictment  proper,  the  case  was  remanded  for  trial.

However,  the  Court  specifically  refused  to  rule  whether
or  not  Congress  could  constitutionally  pass  such  legisla-
tion.  Supra,  U.S.  p.  591,  S.  Ct.  p.  541.

The  wisdom  of  restricting  the  province  of  the  federal
yovernment  to  protecting  the  political  process  from  cor-

ruption  as  opposed  to  the  ‘‘undue  influence’’  of wealth  was
best  expressed  by  Justice  Rutledge,  concurring  in  U.S.  v
CIO,  335  U.S.  106,  68  S. Ct.  1349,  92  L.  Ed.  1849:

The  Government  stresses  the  ‘‘undue  influence’’  of
unions  in  making  expenditures  by way  of  publication
in  support  of  or  against  candidates  and  political  is-
sues  involved  in  the  campaign  rather  than  corruption
It  maintains  that  large  expendi-
in  the  gross  sense,
tures  by  unions  in  publicizing  their  official  political
views  bring  about  an  undue,  that  is supposedly  dispro-
portionate,  sway  of  electoral  sentiment  and  official  at-
titudes.  In  short,  the  ‘‘bloc’’  power  of  unions  has  be-
come  too  great,  in  influencing  both  the  electorate  and
public  officials,  to  permit  further  expenditures  of  their
funds  in  directly  and  openly  publicizing  their  political
views.  And  the  asserted  evil  is to  be  uprooted  by pro-
hibition  of  union  expenditures  as  such,  not  by  regula-
tion  specifically  drawn  to  meet  it.

There  are,  of  course,  obvious  differences  between  such
evils  and  those  arising  from  the  grosser  forms  of  as-
sistance  more  usually  associated  with  secrecy,  bribery
But  it  is  not  neces-
and  corruption,  direct  or  subtle.
sary  to  stop  to  point  these  out  or  discuss  them,  except
to  say  that  any  asserted  beneficial  tendency  of  restric-
tions  upon  expenditures  for  publicizing  political  views,
whether  of  a  group  or  of  an  individual,  is  certainly
counterbalanced  to  some  extent  by the  loss  for  demo-
cratic  processes  resulting  from  the  restrictions  upon
The  claimed  evil  is
free  and  full  public  discussion.
not  one  unmixed  with  good.  And  its  suppression  de-
stroys  the  good  with  the  bad  unless  precise  measures
are  taken  to  prevent  this.

The  expression  of  bloc  sentiment  is  and  always  has
been  an  integral  part  of  our  democratic  electoral  and
legislative  processes.  They  could  hardly  go  on  witb-
out  it.  U.S.  p.  143,  S.  Ct.  pp.  1367,  1368.

II.

THE  FEDERAL  ELECTION  CAMPAIGN  ACT  IS
OVERBROAD  IN  THAT  IT  SUBSTANTIALLY
BURDENS  POLITICAL  ACTIVITY  REGARDLESS
OF  ITS  POSSIBILITY  OF  CORRUPTING  PUBLIC
OFFICIALS.

Amicus  contends  that  there  is  a  definite  and  easily  dis-
cernible  difference  between  payments  to  a  candidate  or  of-
ficial  in  exchange  for  a  specific  consideration  and  contri-
butions  which  further  a  candidate’s  campaign.  The  former
is  a  bribe,  the  latter  a  politica)  contribution,  The  fact  that
the  political  contributions  (and  votes)  are  frequently  given
for  venal  reasons  instead  of altruistic  ones  does  not  obscure
the  difference.

If  indeed,  the  line  between  these  two  phenomenon  is  too
vague  to  discern,  then  we  cannot  say  that  anyone  has  been
properly  convicted  of  bribing  a  public  official.
Nor  does
the  difficulty  of  proving  scienter  in  the  cases  of  bribery
obliterate  the  basic  differences  which  exist  between  the  two.
As  argued  above,  the  government  has  a  legitimate  inter-

est  in  preventing  bribery.

It  has  also  been  conceded  that  the  FECA  prescribes  a
significant  scheme  of  limitations,  regulations  and  public
financing  of  the  public  debate  called  election  campaigns.

Keeping  the  obvious  in  mind,  the  question  is  whether  or

not  the  FECA  is

‘*|..  narrowly  drawn  to  meet  the  precise  evil  the  leg-
islature  seeks  to  curb...

If  the  evil  is  taken  to  be  the  corruption  of  national
elections  and  federal  officials  by  the  expenditure  of
large  masses  of  aggregated  wealth  in  their  behalf,  the
statute  is  neither  so  phrased  nor  so  limited  ..  .”’

J. Rutledge  concurring  in U.S.  v  CIO,  supra,  U.S.  pp.

141,  142,  S.  Ct.  p.  1367.

The  FECA  does  not  limit  contributions  made  in  order  to
‘‘buy’’  a  public  official,  but  limits  contributions  of  every-

Sl

9

one,  limits  expenditures  of  all  individuals,  political  com-
mittees,  and  candidates.
Such  limits  are  enacted  without
regard  to  the  purpose  for  which  the  money  is  spent,  nor  to
the  context  in  which  it is spent.

Similarly,  the  FECA  imposes  a  reporting  requirement  on
all  contributions  over  ten  dollars  ($10.00)  and  a  disclosure
requirement  on  all  contributions  over  one  hundred  dollars
($100.00)  without  regard  to  the  purpose  for  or  the  context
of  such  contribution.

Surely  we  are  not  to  assume  that  all  such  contributions
and  expenditures  are  made  for  ‘‘corrupt’’  reasons,  nor  that
all  contributions  and  expenditures  have  the  potential  for
corrupting  the  political  process.

Nor  can  we  deny  that  such  limitations  have  a  necessary
effect  of  limiting  the  speech  and  organized  political  activ-
ity that  such  money  makes  possible.

In  this  regard  the  FECA  is constitutionally  defective  for
the  same  reasons  that  the  Supreme  Court  struck  down  the
subject  Legislation  in  U.S.  v  Robel,  389  U.S.  258,  88  S.  Ct.
419,  19  L.  Ed.  2d  508  (1967).
In  Robel,  the  legislation  in
question  made  it  illegal  to  work  in  any  defense  facility
while  belonging  to  a  communist-action  organization.  There
the  right  abridged  was  the  right  to  belong  to  a  political  as-
Membership  was  not  forbidden,  but  was  bur-
sociation.
dened  by  the  subject  job  restriction.
The  government’s
right  to  protect  defense  plants  against  sabotage  and  espi-
onage  was  recognized  as  being  of  fundamental  importance.
The  act  was  declared  unconstitutional  because  it  was  not
based  on  a  finding  that  the  burdened  association  was  re-
lated  to  the  threat  feared  by the  government.

‘*(The)  statute  casts  its net  across  a  broad  range  of as-
sociational  activity,  indiscriminately  trapping  member-
ship  which  can  be  constitutionally  punished  and  mem-
bership  which  cannot  be  so  proscribed...  Thus  (the
Act)  contains  the  fatal  defect  of overbreadth  because  it
seeks  to  bar  employment  both  for  association  which
may  be  prescribed  and  for  association  which  may  not
be  proscribed  consistently  with  First  Amendment
rights.’’  Supra,  S.  Ct.  pp.  424-425,  U.S.  pp.  265-266.

10

Similarly,  the  FECA  proscribes  contributions  and  ex-
penditures  which  could  be  proscribed  (bribes)  and  those
which  present  no  danger  whatsoever  of  corruption.

It  has  been  suggested  that  although  the  FECA  reaches
innocent  behavior  in  its  attempt  to  control  illegal  behavior,
its  impact  on  the  First  Amendment  rights  involved  in  that
innocent  behavior  is  incidental.

‘*|..  We  are  not  concerned  with  a  ban  on  political  ac-
tivity,  but  with  a  limitation  on  amount,  without  any
discrimination  based  on  party  or  orientation  of  speech
or  activity.’’  Slip  op  p.  1518.

Of  course  the  alleged  non-partisan  character  of  the  limi-
tation  would  be  completely  irrelevant  even  if  it  were  true.
The  First  Amendment  doesn’t  give  the  government  power
to  infringe  freedom  of  speech  so  long  as  it does  so  equally.
Based  on  this  criteria  the  complete  silencing  of  criticism
from  anyone  would  be  constitutional.

Obviously  the  salient  consideration  is  the  scope  of  the

limitation  and  not  its  purported  non-partisan  character.

The  limitation  involved  can  be  characterized  as  a  limita-
tion  on  the  right  to  engage  in  political  campaigns  either  as
an  individual  or  in  a  political  party.  The  limitation  arises
from  the  burdens  and  limitations  that  the  Act  creates  in
financing  that  activity.
Just  as  Congress  has  recognized
the  evils  of  money,  so  we  must  also  recognize  the  necessity
of money.

The  limitations  resulting  from  this  act  are  especially
heavy  on  independent  candidates,  poor  candidates,  new  par-
ties  and  minor  parties.

(Precisely  those  categories  of  individuals  who  represent
the  most  remote  and  hypothetical  danger  of  corrupting  the
political  process  by campaigning  for  office.)

Once  more  we  want  to  reiterate  that  it  is of  no  moment
that  the  burdens  placed  on  the  minor  candidates  are  shared
The  burden  to  a  minor  party  is  no
by  major  candidates.
less  a  burden  because  a  major  party  may  be  able  to  over-
come  an  even  greater  burden.

More  specifically  some  of  the  burdens  and  limitations  on

political  activity  resulting  from  the  FECA  are:

11

a.  The  costs  of  reporting  all  contributors  of  amounts
over  ten  dollars  ($10)  can  be  substantial  because  of
the  need  for  the  professional  services  of  lawyers  and
accountants.

b.  The  disclosure  of  the  identities  of  all  contributors
of  amounts  in  excess  of  one  hundred  dollars  ($100)
will  burden  the  effort  to  collect  funds  necessary  to  fi-
nance  a  campaign.  (discussed  below)

ce.  The  one  thousand  dollar  ($1000)  limit  on  contribu-
tions  require  a  greater  numerical  number  of  support-
(There  is  no  re-
ers  in  order  to  launch  a  campaign.
quirement  in  the  Constitution  that  political  activity  is
permitted  only  to  those  persons  who  are  popular)

d.  The  limit  on  expenditure  of  individuals  cuts  off
that  person’s  free  speech  at  an  arbitrary  figure  of  One
thousand  dollars  ($1000).

e.  The  one  thousand  dollar  ($1000)  limit  on  contri-
butions  restricts  an  individual’s  political  activity  re-
gardless  of his  intention  to  corrupt  public  officials  or
the  political  process.

f.  The  limit  on  expenditure  is most  likely  to  limit  de-
bate  at  its  crucial  point,  the  eve  of  the  election.

These  limits  and  burdens,  with  regard  to  all  candidates,
are  significant.  To  new  parties  and  minor  parties  they may
be  fatal.
The  combination  of  disclosure,  the  costs  of  re-
porting,  and  the  limitation  on  the  contributions  of  a  few
wealthy  supporters  could  mean  that  the  minor  parties  will
not  be  able  to  launch  a  campaign.

However,  even  if  they  do  overcome  such  hurdles,  their
First  Amendment  activity  will  be  seriously  curtailed  by
their  increased  costs  and  decreased  revenue.
Their  activ-
ity  will  be  so  curtailed  without  regard  to  whether  or  not
such  First  Amendment  activity  bears  any  relationship  to
the  danger  Congress  should  to  prevent.  Thus  the  FECA
is  overbroad.  Cohen  v  Calif.,  403  U.S.  15,  91  S.  Ct.  1780,
29  L.  Ed.  2d  284  (1971),  Branderberg  v  Ohio,  395  U.S.  444,
89  S. Ct.  1827,  23  L.  Ed.  2d  430  (1969),  U.S,  v  Robel,  supra.

12

Il.

PUBLIC  FINANCING  OF  POLITICAL  CAMPAIGNS
IS  FORBIDDEN  BY  THE  FIRST  AMENDMENT.

The  First  Amendment  forbids  governmental  involve-

ment  in  the  arena  of  public  discussion.

‘‘The  Constitutional  right  of  free  expression  ...  is de-
signed  ...  to  remove  governmental  restraint  from  the
Cohen  v  Calif.,  supra,
arena  of  public  discussion.’’
U.S.  p.  24,  S.  Ct.  at  1787,  1788.

Just  as  the  government  cannot  tax  nor  license  First
Amendment  freedom,  Grosjean  v  American  Press  Co.,  297
U.S.  233,  56  S.  Ct.  444,  80  L.  Ed.  660  (1936),  the  federal
government  cannot  establish  franchises  for  the  exercise
of  First  Amendment  rights.

Regardless  of  the  criteria  established  for  the  distribu-
tion  of  these  funds,  the  fact  remains  that  the  government
will  give  millions  of  dollars  to  some  candidates  and  not  to
The  fact  that  the  recipients  have  proven  them-
others.
selves  more  popular  is  no  justification  for  this  governmen-
tal  involvement  in  the  arena  of  free  speech.

When  the  government  sponsors  certain  ideas  or  candi-
dates,  it  infringes  the  rights  of  opposing  ideas  or  candi-
dates  by disadvantaging  them  in  the  market  place  of  idea.
The  fact  that  non-governmental  wealth  creates  similar  ad-
vantages  does  not  minimize  the  First  Amendment’s  injunc-
tion  against  such  governmental  interference.

Not  only  does  public  funding  give  certain  candidates  an
advantage  over  others  to  the  tune  of several  million  dollars,
but  the  contribution  limitations  contain  similar  inequali-
ties.

The  fact  that  a  wealthy  person  can  give  up  to  $25,000  to
himself,  means  that  a  wealthy  candidate  has  a  substantial
headstart  over  his  less  fortunate  rivals.
The  fact  that
private  forces  lead  to  the  same  result  is  no  justification  for
a  governmental  scheme  which  makes  it easier  for  the  weal-
thy  to  run  than  the  unwealthy.

13

Similarly,  the  one  thousand  dollar  ($1,000)  limit  on  con-
tribution  will  mean  different  things  to  different  candidates.
For  the  candidates  with  the  support  of  the  wealthy  minor-
ity, it will  mean  that  he  will  have  to  increase  the  number  of
his  contributors  x  number  of times.  For  the  candidate  with
few  wealthy  supporters,  it  will  mean  that  the  money  lost
from  his  few  wealthy  contributors  may  have  to  be  made
up  by one  hundred  times  x  number  of  contributors.

What  substantial  governmental  interest  can  justify  this

intrusion  in  the  area  of  First  Amendment  activity?

Public  financing  which  rewards  the  popular  has  no  di-

rect  relation  to  preventing  corruption.

Aside  from  the  cynical  prediction  that  popular  candi-
dates  will  be  corrupted  and  therefore  must  be  bribed  by
the  public  so  as  to  prevent  them  from  taking  private  bribes,
there  is nothing  but  a  hope  that  such  funding  will  curb  cor-
ruption.

Against  such  fond  hopes  lies  the  inescapable  conclusion
that  public  funding  gives  an  advantage  to  certain  candi-
dates  over  others.  This  intrusion  in the  arena  of  public  de-
bate  is  unconstitutional  regardless  of  how  the  fortunate
candidate  is  chosen  or  how  beneficient  was  the  motive  of
Congress  in  passing  such  legislation.

In  short,  public  funding  increases  the  hegemony  of  the
Republican  and  Democratic  parties.  The  importance  of new,
third  or  minor  parties  to  our  democratic  system  has  been
repeatedly  recognized.
Sweezey  v  New  Hampshire,  354
U.S.  234,  77  S.  Ct.  1203,  1 L.  Ed.  2d  1311  (1957),  Williams
v  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23,  89  S.  Ct.  5, 21  L.  Ed.  2d  24;  Jeness  v
Fortson,  403  U.S.  437,  91  S.  Ct.  1970,  29  L.  Ed.  2d  554.

Moreover,  their  right  to  contend  for  power  is  guaran-

teed  by the  First  Amendment.

A monopoly  of two  parties  is still  a monopoly.
Nor  does  the  fact  that  the  Act  does  not  forbid  or  ban
third-parties  answer  the  challenge  to  the  act.  The  govern-
ment  cannot  intervene  and  give  an  advantage  to  one  party
The  ban  on
over  another  in  their  competition  for  votes.
such  governmental  interference  is  complete  and  it  does
not  matter  how  ‘‘non-partisan’’  the  government  is  in  de-
ciding  who  is  the  beneficiary  of  public  funds.
It  is not  the
process  of allocating  public  funds  which  is here  in question,

14

15

it  is  the  necessary  and  inevitable  result  of  such  allocation
which  involves  the  government  in  aiding  one  (or  two)  par-
ties  against  their  challengers.

Cases  limiting  access  to  the  ballot  are  not  analogous  to
puvlic  funding.  Government  printing  of  the  ballot  is  a
duty  imposed  on  it by Art  I, 44(1)  of  the  Constitution.
It
follows  that  it has  a  duty  to  make  the  ballot  comprehensible
to  the  voter,

The  government  has  no  duty  to  finance  political  cam-
paigns.  The  connection  between  such  financing  and a legi-
timate  governmental  interest  is  remote  and  speculative.
The  impact  of  such  funding  on  the  competition  for  voter
allegiance  is  immediate  and  substantial.

Thus,  the  public  financing  sections  of  the  FECA  are  un-

constitutional.

When  the  public  funding  provisions  are  viewed  in  the
totality  of  the  legislative  scheme  of  the  FECA,  we  see  that
they  represent  the  very  danger  the  Court  was  concerned
with  in  Miami  Herald  vy  Tornillo,  supra:

‘Tt  has  yet  to  be  demonstrated  how  governmental  reg-
ulation  of  this  crucial  process  can  be  exercised  con-
sistent  with  the  First  Amendment  ..

.’’

IV.

THE  DISCLOSURE  REQUIREMENT  IS  OVER-
BROAD  IN  THAT  IT  SUBSTANTIALLY  CHILLS
THE  FIRST  AMENDMENT  RIGHT  OF  ASSOCIA.
TION  WITHOUT  REGARD  TO  THE  POSSIBILITY
THAT  SUCH  ACTIVITY  WILL  CORRUPT  PUB.
LIC  OFFICIALS.

The  FECA  requires  all  contributions  over  one  hundred
dollars  ($100)  to  be  disclosed.
The  Act  makes  no  excep-
tions  for  cases  where  the  contribution  goes  to  a  candidate
who  has  little  chanee  of  winning.
Nor  does  the  Act  dis-
tinguish  between  the  contributions  of  wage  earners  and
those  of  corporate  executives.

While  it  is  true  that  the  probability  of  the  disclosure  re-
quirement  having  a chilling  effect  and  the  degree  of  such

effect  will  depend  on  the  situation  of the  individual  contrib-
utor  and  the  popularity  or  unpopularity  of  the  candidate
receiving  the  contribution,  it is  inescapable  that  the  disclo-
sure  requirement  will  have  a  wide  reaching  chilling  effect
in  light  of  its  scope.

Further,  regardless  of  whatever  chilling  effect  it has,  the
disclosure  requirement  represents  a  new  and  precedent  set-
The
ting  invasion  of  privacy  by  the  federal  government.
disclosure  requirement  is  so  broad  and  applies  to  so  many
contributions,  that  it is  tantamount  to  allowing  the  federal
government  to  register  all  persons  who  wish  to  be  politi-
cally  active.  For  all  of  the  reasons  expressed  in  U.S.  v  Ro-
bel,  supra,  the  disclosure  provisions  are  overbroad.

Nor  is  the  case  of  Burroughs  and  Cannon  v  U.S.,  supra,
authority  for  the  constitutionality  of  such  a  broad  disclo-
sure  law.  The  Court  in  Burroughs  was  clearly  not  faced
with  a  First  Amendment  challenge  to  the  disclosure  require-
ment  of  Corrupt  Practices  Act  and  did  not  deciae  the  First
Amendment  issue.

With  regard  to  the  question  of whether  or  not  the  Court
should  consider  the  chilling  effect  of  the  disclosure  section
on  minor  parties  in  the  absence  of  such  party’s  participa-
tion  in  this  case,  Amicus  Curiae  relies  on  and  cites  to  this
Court  the  dissent  of  Justice  Bazelon  to  the  opinion  of  the
Court  of Appeals  on  this  point.

Not  only  is the  disclosure  section  overbroad  in  its  sweep-
ing  effect,  but  it  has  been  proven  historically  to  be  an  in-
adequate  and  ineffective  tool  for  preventing  corruption.

The  lower  Court  has  used  this  record  of  ineffectiveness
as  a  rationale  for  supporting  more  sweeping  regulations.
(Slip  op  p.  1519)

Normally,  we  would  agree  that  the  choice  cf  means  to
achieve  a  legitimate  governmental  purpose  is a legislative
However,  in  the  area  of  the  First  Amendment,
function.
the  Court  must  use  its  scrutiny  to  determine  if  the  means
are  ‘‘narrowly  related’’  to  the  purpose.

The  lower  Court’s  opinion  amounts  to  the  proposition
that  if a  minor  restriction  of  First  Amendment  right  is  ef-
fective,  wholesale  restrictions  are  justified.

This  rationale  is not  only  destructive  of  the  Constitution-
al  limitations  on  governmental  interference  in  the  public
debate,  but  is not  supported  by history  or  logic.

16

The  other  aspect  of  the  failure  of  reporting  require-
ments  to  stem  corruption,  is  the  success  of  our  democratic
institutions  to  limit  and  uncover  corruption  when  it  has
occurred.

The  least  drastic  means  to  insure  political  integrity  en-
visioned  by  the  Constitution  is  to  allow  the  challengers  to
those  in  political  office  to  have  free  reign  to  mount  their
challenge.

The  best  method  of  preventing  corruption  is  the  close
scrutiny  of  official  action.  The  most  careful  detectives  in
this  process  are  often  political  opponents.

The  federal  government  has  no  interest  in  restricting  or
burdening  the  political  activity  of  political  opponents  in
favor  of  a  committee  appointed  by incumbents.

When  the  federal  eovernment  becomes  the  watchdog  of
the  integrity  of  political  process  instead  of  the  citizenry
performing  that  process,  the  First  Amendment  has  been
turned  upside  down  and  should  be  amended  to  read:

Congress  shall  make  adequate  laws  respecting  freedom
of  speech,  of  the  press,  the  right  of  people  peaceably
to  assemble,  and  io  petition  the  Government  for  a  re-
dress  of  grievances,  so  as  to  protect  the  people  from
being  deceived  by  corrupt  politicians.

To  proceed  in  such  a  fashion  is to  grant  Congress  a  trust
which  the  framers  of  the  Constitution  warned  against;
making  the  Congress  the  guardian  of  our  political  free-
doms  is  to  trust  the  wolf  to  guard  the  sheep.

17

CONCLUSION

CoDEL  urges  that  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act’s
regulation  of  the  private  financing  of  election  campaigns
be  declared  to  be  an  unconstitutionally  overbroad  regula-
tion  of legitimate  political  activity ;

That  the  public  financing  provisions  be  declared  to  be  an
unconstitutional  intrusion  by  the  federal  government  into
the  public  debate;  and

That  the  disclosure  requirements  be  declared  an  uncon-

stitutional  abridgement  of  the  freedom  of  association.

Respectfully  submitted,
