In THE

Supreme  Court  of the Wnited  States

Octoser  Term,  1975

Nos.  75-436  and  75-437

James  I, BucKLEY,  et al.,

Appellants,

Vs.

Francis  R. Va_ £0,  SECRETARY  OF  THE  SENATE,  et al.,

Appellees.

On  Appeals  from  ihe  United  States  Court  of Appeals
for the  District  of Columbia  Circuit  and
the  United  States  District  Court
for the  District  of Columbia

Brief  Amici  Curiae

iNTEREST  OF  AMICI  CURIAE

In  1974,  the  voters  of  California  approved  by  approxi-
mately  a  two-to-one  margin  an  initiative  measure  known
as  Proposition  Nine,  which  enacted  the  Political  Reform
Act  of  1974.  The  Act  provides,  inter  alia,  for  disclosure  of
campaign  contributions  and  expenditures  of  $50  or  more,
and  for  limitations  on  the  expenditures  permitted  to  be
made  by  candidates  for  state-wide  office  or  in  respect  of

=

2
initiative  or  referendum  campaigns.!  The  Fair  Political
Practices  Commission  is  created  by  Chapter  3 of  the  Act,
and  is charged  with  “primary  responsibility  for  the  impar-
tial,  effective  administration  and  implementation”  of  the
new  law.  Cauir.  Govt.  Cope  § 83111.

The  Commission  is  joined  in  this  brief  by  three  other
states.  These  states,  like  California,  have  enacted  laws
compelling,  among  other  things,  disclosure  of contributions
and  limiting  election  campaign  expenditures.

It is apparent  that  recent  events  have  generated  renewed
and  enhanced  interest  in  election  reform  legislation.  In
some  instances,  the  result  has  been  more  vigorous  enforce-
ment  efforts  as  to  existing  (and  perhaps  theretofore  under-
enforced)  laws.  In  other  cases,  such  as  California,  new
legislation  has  resulted.  To  a  considerable  extent,  these
laws  are  now  to  be  administered  and  enforced  with  a  dedi-
cation—tempered,  we  trust,  by sensitivity  for  the  rights  of
political  freedom  and  expression—not  previously  experi-
enced.  Yet  the  present  case  represents  a  concerted  effort
to  obtain  a  broad  judicial  declaration  that  such  laws  cannot
be  squared  with  the  First  Amendment.

Amici  believe  the  federal  law  before  this  Court  is consti-
tutional.  Amici  also  believe  that  the  various  laws  enacted  by
the  states  represented  here  (and,  doubtless,  by other  states
as  well)  represent  valid  efforts  to  protect  the  integrity  of
their  political  systems.  They  are  concerned,  therefore,  that
the  effects  on  their  respective  experiments  to  remove  the
corrosive  effects  of  money  on  the  political  process  of  any
decision  herein  not  be  overlooked  and  to  that  end  file  this
brief.

1.  Appellants  are

in  error  in  stating  that  California  has
adopted  contribution  limits.  See  App.  Brief,  at  137.  California  law
imposes  no  such  limits,  and  this  emict  curiae  brief  does  not  address
the  constitutionality  of  that  aspect  of  FECA.

3
SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT
I
Appellants  seek  the  adoption  of a per  se  rule  condemning
all campaign  expenditure  limitations.  Such  limitations  have
been  adopted  by  thirty-seven  states,  ard  those  statutory
schemes  vary  considerably.  While  amici  believe  that  the
provisions  of  FECA  are  constitutional,  in  all  events  a  per
se  disapproval  of  expenditure  limitations  is not  warranted
and  care  should  be  taken  to  avoid  invalidation  of  statutes
not  presently  before  the  Court.

,

The  Constitution  does  not  in  principle  forbid  expendi-
ture  limitations.  They  serve  fundamental  and  compelling
governmental  interests,  including  the  reduction  of competi-
tive  pressures  upon  candidates  to  raise  large  amounts  of
money,  correction  of  the  denial  of  effective  access  to  the
political  system  to  those  without  substantial  wealth,  and
counteraction  of  the  impression  (perhaps  the  reality)  that
money  can  be  translated  into  votes.  It cannot,  of course,  be
denied  that  if  limitations  are  set  at  an  unreasonably  low
level,  serious  constitutional  problems  would  arise  to  the
extent  that  candidates  are  prevented  from  carrying  their
message  to  the  electorate.  But,  as  Appellants  admit,  “cam-
paign  money  is  subject  to  the  law  of  diminishing  marginal
returns.”  App.  Brief,  at  97.  Limitations  which  permit  ef-
fective  campaigning,  yet  diminish  the  disabilities  that  addi-
tional  amounts  of  money  inject  into  the  political  process,
should  be upheld.

Appellants  urge  that  the  even-handed  limitations  im-
posed  by  FECA  benefit  incumbents,  and  imply  that  since
the  statute  was  adopted  by a  legislative  body  whose  mem-
bers  benefit  by  such  legislation,  the  law  should  be  viewed
with  suspicion.  Many  of the  advantages  said  to  be  enjoyed
-  by members  of Congress  are  not  shared  by incumbent  state
officials,  who  do  not  have  the  franking  privilege  and  rarely

4
have  large  staffs.  Moreover,  some  state  laws  establish  a
lower  limitation  for  an  incumbent  than  the  limitation  ap-
plicable  to  the  challenger.  E.g.,  Cauir.  Govt.  Cope  § 85101.
Other  potential  advantages  of  incumbency  can  also  be  lim-
ited  by legislation.  E.g.,  Carir.  Govr.  Cope  §§ 89000-01.

Other  aspects  of  the  federal  statute  challenged  in  this
Court  are  treated  differently  in the  thirty-seven  state  coun-
terparts.  For  example,  Appellants  challenge  the  $1,000
limitation  imposed  by  FECA  on  expenditures  by  inde-
pendent  committees  and  individuals.  Some  state  statutes
handle  the  problem  of  independent  expenditures  differ-
ently.  In  California,  for  example,  any  independent  com-
mittee  or  individual  may  spend  up  to  $10,000  without
restraint,  and  in  some  circumstances  can  spend  as  much
as  an  additional  $140,000.  Sce  Cautir.  Govr.  Cong  § 85104.
In  short,  the  subject  of  campaign  expenditure  limita-
tions  has  been  seriously  addressed  by the  people  in  and  the
legislatures  of  a  number  of  states  in  recent  vears.  The
Court  should  hesitate  to  curtail  this  experiment  in  elim-
inating  the  excessive  role  played  by  money  in  political
campaigns.

II

Forty-five  states  require  disclosure  of  campaign  contri-
butions  and  their  sources;  of  these,  all  but  four  set  the

minimum  threshold  for  disclosure  at  $100  or  $101  or  less.
Appellants’  reliance  on  the  NAACP  line  of cases  is based
upon  the  faulty  premise  that  the  donation  of  money  to
political  candidates  is  “speech”  or  “association”  protected
by  the  First  Amendment.  The  First  Amendment  protects
communication  and  not  conduct.  The  giving  of  money,
while  it  may  facilitate  communication  by  the  recipient,  is
not  an  act  of expression.  Restraints  on  the  giving  of money
(and  it  must  be  remembered  that  in  this  case  the  alleged
restraint  is  the  indirect  “chill”  disclosure  is  said  to

5
achieve)  do  not  forbid  supporters  of  a  candidate  from
giving  speeches,  writing  articles,  distributing  pamphlets,
or  the  like.  Indeed,  membership  in  a  political  party  or
organization  is not  subject  to disclosure.  Only  the  exchange
of  money  has  that  consequence.  No  decision  of  this  Court
holds  that  the  contribution  of  money  is  an  act  within  the
umbrella  of  the  First  Amendment,  and  such  a  holding
would  require  a  fresh  appraisal  of  existing  laws  prohibit-
ing  contributions  by  federal  employees,  corporations  and
labor  unions  to  candidates  for  federal  office.

Even  if  the  making  of  political  contributions  were  pro-
tected  by the  First  Amendment,  Appellants’  constitutional
challenge  would  require  a determination  of whether  govern-
mental  interests  justify  the  indirect  infringement  of  those
First  Amendment  values.  Appellants  concede  that,  as  a
general  principle,  disclosure  requirements  are  constitu-
tional  and,  indeed,  are  “the  proper  solution”  to  many  prob-
lems  in  the  political  process.  App.  Brief,  at  171.  They  con-
tend,  however,  that  FECA’s  disclosure  is overbroad  in that
it  applies  to  all  contributions  in  excess  of  $100,  a  feature
which  it shares  with  some  forty-one  states.  The  fixing  of the
appropriate  threshold  is,  however,  a  legislative  matter,
and  in  this  instance  the  line  has  been  drawn  at  a  reason-
able  point.  Congress  could  reasonably  conclude  that  contri-
butions  of  $100  do  have  the  potential  of  earning  influence
or  access.  Moreover,  a  substantially  larger  threshold  would
permit  the  concealment  of  significant  illegal  corporate  or
labor  contributions  in  amounts  just  under  the  threshold.
Similarly,  those  bent  on  noncompliance  could  make  a  con-
tribution  of  just  under  the  maximum  amount  and  arrange
for  others  to  do likewise.  For  all  of these  reasons,  the  judg-
ment  of  Congress  should  be  respected.

Appellants  also  assert  that  the  Constitution  requires  the
exemption  from  disclosure  of  contributions  to  minority

6
parties  and  independent  candidates.  The  Court  of Appeals
properly  rejected  this  claim,  noting  that  the  present  record
contains  only  scant  evidence  of  a  “chilling  effect”  on  such
contributions.  Even  if  the  record  had  more  persuasively
supported  Appellants’  factual  claims  in  this  regard,  disclo-
sure  would  nonetheless  be  constitutional  because  it is  sup-
ported  by  compelling  governmental  interests  and  because
the  exception  which  Appellants  would  carve  is unworkable
and  undesirable.  In  the  first  place,  the  problems  of defining
those  candidates  and  parties  entitled  to  exemption  would
be  enormous.  Even  if  it  could  be  accomplished,  serious
questions  of  fairness  and  evenhandedness  would  be  pre-
sented.  Finally,  minority  candidates  can  affect  the  outcome
of an  election  even  if they  do  not  emerge  victorious.  Every
vote  which  a  third  party  or  independent  candidate  receives
is a  vote  which  otherwise  might  have  been  cast  for  a  major
party  candidate.  There  is, for  these  reasons,  a  substantial
public  interest  in  disclosure  of  the  sources  of  financing  of
all  candidates,  not  merely  those  with  the  greatest  possi-
bility  of success.

I
CEILINGS  ON  CAMPAIGN  EXPENDITURES  ARE  NOT  UNCON-
STITUTIONAL  PER  SE.  EACH  LIMITATION  STATUTE  MUST
BE  SEPARATELY  SCRUTINIZED  AND  UPHELD  IF IT IS JUSTI-
FIED  BY  A COMPELLING  GOVERNMENTAL  INTEREST
Amici  believe  that  the  expenditure  limitations  of  the
FECA  are  carefully  drawn  to  serve  the  compelling  govern-
mental  interest  of  minimizing  the  disproportionate  influ-
ence  over  politics  and  government  enjoyed  by  those  in-
dividuals  and  institutions  able  to  pump  large  amounts  of
money  into  election  campaigns.  We  believe  that  the  expendi-
ture  limitations  are  constitutional  and  should  be  upheld.

7
It is  not  our  purpose,  however,  to  address  the  specific
constitutional  merits  of the expenditure  limitations  imposed
by the  FECA,  a  task  which  can  better  be  performed  by the
parties.  Rather,  we  hope  merely  to  dissuade  the  Court
from  adopting,  either  explicitly  or  by implication  resulting
from  unnecessarily  broad  language,  any  sort  of  per  se  rule
regarding  the  constitutionality  of  campaign  expenditure
ceilings.

Although  Appellants  urge  a  finding  of per  se  unconstitu-
tionality  (App.  Brief,  at 79-86),  their  arguments  do not  sup-
port  such  a  conclusion  but  consist  essentially  of attacks  on
the  specific  provisions  of  FECA.  We  do  not  find  these
attacks  persuasive,  but  even  if they  are  accepted  the  Court
should  make  it clear  that  limitation  statutes  containing  dif-
ferent  provisions  and  applicable  in  different  political  con-
texts  will  be upheld  if under  scrutiny  they meet  the  requisite
constitutional  standards.

In  recent  years,  thirty-seven  states  have  adopted  statutes
limiting  campaign  expenditures.  See  “Analysis  of  Federal
and  State  Campaign  Finance  Law”  (Cong.  Research  Serv-
ice,  Library  of  Congress,  June,  1975),  at  31-43.  These
statutes  have  not  derived  from  a  common  model;  rather,
almost  without  exception  the  people  or  the  legislature  of
each  state  have  done  their  own  drafting,  in  light  of  local
problems  and  conditions.  It is probably  rare  that  in an  area
of such  significance  the  states  have  so  effectively  performed
their  roles  as  laboratories  of  social  reform  in  so  diverse  a
fashion.  To  be  sure,  the  state  legislation,  like  the  federal,
regulates  in an  area  which,  under  the  Constitution,  is highly
sensitive,  and  perhaps  some  of the  statutes  will  fail  to  sur-
vive  whatever  degree  of  scrutiny  the  Court  believes  is
appropriate.  But  when  not  only  Congress  but  so  many
states  have  found  that  legislation  is  necessary,  the  Court

8
should  hesitate  to  sweep  too  broadly.  As  the  Court  said
(echoing  a  theme  expressed  often  in  the  decisions  of  this
Court)  in another  context,  “the  judiciary  is well  advised  to
refrain  from  imposing  upon  the  States  inflexible  constitu-
tional  restraints  that  could  cireumscribe  or  handicap  the
continued  research  and  experimentation  so  vital  to  finding
even  partial  solutions  ...  and  to  keeping  abreast  of  ever-
changing  conditions.”  San  Antonio  School  District  v.  Rod-
riquez,  411  U.S.  1, 43  (1973).

Appellants  would  have  the  Court  believe  that  expenditure
limitations  are  premised  on  the  notion  that  there  is  such
a  thing  as  “too  much”  speech,  a  notion  that  is  said  to  be
inconsistent  with  the  First  Amendment.  App.  Brief,  at  80.
They  misstate  the  case.  The  problem  is  not  that  there  is
too  much  speech  in  campaigns  but  that  there  is  too  much
money.  And  the  ever-increasing  influx  of  money  has  pro-
duced  a  number  of  pernicious  influences  on  the  American
political  system.  It has  resulted  in intense  competitive  pres-
sures  upon  candidates  to  raise  large  amounts  of  money  to
finance  their  campaigns,  has  foreclosed  or  diminished  ef-
fective  access  to  the  system  to  all  but  those  that  can  afford
it, and  has  created  the  impression,  and  possibly  the  reality,
that  money  can  distort  the  electoral  process,

Spending  limitations  are  designed  to  counteract  these
deleterious  effects.  They  will  relieve  the  competitive  pres-
sure  to  raise  vast  amounts  of  money,  and  thereby  make  it
more  likely  that  candidates  will  be  able  (1)  to  reject  con-
tributions  which  threaten  their  independence;  (2)  to  spend
more  time  representing  their  constituents  by  studying
issues  and  governmental  problems  instead  of  attending
fund-raising  events  and  cultivating  contributions;  and  (3)
to  finance  their  campaigns  with  contributions  from  legiti-
mate  supporters  rather  than  special  interests  seeking  access

9
and  influence.  Furthermore,  spending  limitations  will  help
to  open  up  the  political  process  and  to  increase  participa-
tion  by those  who  have  not  been  able  to afford  it as  the  ante
has  increased.  Finally,  limitations  should  counteract  the
illicit  relationship  between  money  and  votes,  and  by  so
doing  should  restore  confidence  on  the  part  of the  electorate
that  public  office  cannot  simply  be  bought.

Unquestionably,  these  are  valid  governmental  objectives.
Nevertheless,  it  is  undeniable  that  limitations  could  be  set
at  so  unreasonably  low  a  level  as  to  prevent  candidates
from  carrying  their  message  to  the  electorate;  we  do  not
suggest  that  such  laws  should  be immune  from  First  Amend-
ment  attack.  Limits  which  are  in  fact  reasonable,  however,
do  not  suffer  this  infirmity  because,  as  Appellants  admit,
“campaign  money  is subject  to  the  law  of diminishing  mar-
ginal  returns.”  App.  Brief,  at  97.  Limitations  which  permit
effective  campaigning  yet lessen  the  competitive  pressure  to
raise  funds  should  be  upheld  because  they  serve  vital  gov-
ernmental  interests  and  infringe,  if  at  all,  only  minimally
and  incidentally  on  substantive  communications.

Accordingly,  the  task  for the  Court  is to scrutinize  closely
the  specific  provisions  of  FECA  in  the  specific  context  of
the  federal  elections  which  are  regulated.  That  no  per  se
rule  is  possible  and  that  the  Court  should  avoid  needless
generalizations  appears  affirmatively  from  Appellants’
arguments  purportedly  in  favor  of  a  per  se  rule.  For  ex-
ample,  Appellants  present  the  Court  with  a  hypothetical
state,  apparently  modeled  on  New  Hampshire,  in  which
they assert  that because  of particular  local  conditions  higher
than  usaal  expenditures  may  be  necessary.  App.  Brief,  at
79-80.  Appellants  cannot  seriously  deny  that  Congress  has
good  reason  to  impose  uniform  standards  in  federal  elec-
tions  in  light  of  the  serious  drafting  problems,  to  say
nothing  of equal  protection  considerations,  which  any  alter-

10
native  would  create.  Cf.  pp.  26-27,  infra.  But  however  that
may  be, surely  the  fact  that  peculiar  conditions  may  exist  in
New  Hampshire  has  no  relevance  for  the  statutes  adminis-
tered  by amici.  The  statutes  of each  state  must  be measured
against  the  circumstances  in that  state.  Indeed,  Appellants
themselves  strongly  assert  that  circumstances  relating  to
limitations  vary  greatly  from  state  to  state.  App.  Brief,  at
94.  Their  assertion  illustrates  the  impossibility  of a general-
ized  approach.

At  the  center  of  Appellants’  attack  on  the  FECA  ex-
penditure  limitations  is  the  contention  that  the  limitations
benefit  incumbent  members  of  Congress.  App.  Brief.,  at
67-68,  87-104.  Appellants  imply  that  since  the  members
had  a  conflict  of  interest  in  drafting  and  approving  the
legislation,  the  statute  should  be  viewed  with  suspicion.  A
more  probable  reading  of  recent  political  history  is  that
Congress  adopted  reform  legislation  against  its  members’
perceptions  of  self-interest  as  a  result  of  pressure  from
their  constituents.  However  that  may  be,  the  Court  cer-
tainly  cannot  establish  a  per  se  rule  based  on  an  assumption
that  thirty-three  state  legislatures?  acted  in the  self-interest
of  members  rather  than  in  the  public  interest.  State  legis-
lators  do  not  have  the  franking  privilege  or  large  staffs
which  appellants  assert  slant  even-handed  limitations  in
favor  of incumbent  Congressmen.  Moreover,  in many  states
expenditure  limitations  were  enacted  along  with  provisions
designed  to  equalize  some  of  the  advantages  enjoyed  by
incumbents.  In  California,  for  example,  the  Political  Re-
form  Act  establishes  a  spending  limitation  for an  incumbent
seeking  reelection  to  the  same  office  which  is  ten  percent
below  the  limitation  imposed  upon  a  challenger.  Catir.

2.  The  other  four  states  which  have  adopted  spending  limita-

tions  did  so  by initiative.

11

Govt.  Cope  § 85101.  Moreover,  an  incumbent  who  has  filed  a
declaration  of  candidacy  is  prohibited  from  sending  any
legislative  newsletter  or  mass  mailing  at  public  expense.
Cauir.  Govr.  Cope  § 89001.  In  the  same  spirit,  a  candidate’s
position  on  the  ballot  must  be determined  without  regard  to
whether  the  candidate  is  an  incumbent.  Cauir.  Govt.  Cope
§ 89000.

Appellants’  contention  that  expenditure  limitations  are
the  product  of  incumbent  legislators  seeking  electoral  ad-
vantage  also  is disproved  by the  fact  the  voters  of at  least
four  states—California,  Idaho,  Missouri  and  Washington
—adopted  some  form  of  expenditure  limitation  via  the  ini-
tiative  process.

Appellants’  proposed  per  se  rule  condemning  expenditure
limitations  should  also  be  rejected  because  the  specific
provisions  of  the  various  statutes  adopted  by  the  states
contain  significant  differences.  Many  of  the  state  statutes
apply  to situations  without  any  analogy  in the  federal  legis-
lation.  For  example,  there  are  no  federal!  ballot  measure
or  public question  elections,  but  such  elections  are  significant
in  states  and  localities  which  require  voter  approval  for
constitutional  and  charter  amendinents  and  bond  issues,  and
which  in  some  cases  permit  direct  legislation  by the  voters
in  the  form  of  the  initiative  and  referendum.  In  such  elec-
tions  the  disparity  in  spending  on  hota  sides  may  be  more
of  a  problem  than  the  total  spending,  and  laws  adopted  by
some  states  to  equalize  spending  (e.g.,  Carr.  Govt.  Copr
§§ 85300  et  seq.)  are  designed  to  enhance  rather  than  in-
fringe  First  Amendment  values.  Cf. Red  Lion  Broadcasting
Co.  v.  FCC,  395  U.S.  367,  375  (1969);  CBS  v.  Democratic
National  Committee,  412  U.S.  94  (1973).

Even  as  to  candidate  elections,  the  limitations  statutes
contain  significant  differences.  To  take  a  single  example,
Appellants  make  much  of  the  federal  $1,000  limitation  on
expenditures  by  committees  and  individuals  acting  inde-

12
pendently  of  the  candidate.  App.  Brief,  at  107-15.  This
limitation  is  a  justifiable  and  necessary  provision  to  pre-
vent  circumvention  not  only  of the  expenditure  ceilings  but
also  of  the  federal  limit  on  contributions.  But  if the  Court
believes  the  federal  provision  is  too  restrictive  it  should
take  care  to  reserve  judgment  upon  state  statutes,  many  of
which  approach  the  problem  of  independent  expenditures
differently.  See  Note,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1111,  1254-56  and
n.122  (1975),  which  describes  the  variety  of  state  statutes.
Again  looking  to  California  by way  of  example,  any  inde-
pendent  committee  (and  this  includes  individuals  as  they
can  be “committees”,  Catir.  Govr.  Cope  § 82013)  may  spend
up  to  $10,000  without  being  subject  to  the  limitations  pro-
visions  of  the  Act;  moreover,  they  may  spend  as  much  as
another  $140,000  (adjusted  for  cost  of living  increases)  pro-
vided  that  the  total  expenditures  by independent  committees
for  a  particular  candidate  do  not  exceed  the  latter  figure.
Cauir.  Govr.  Cope  § 85104.  The  effect  of these  provisions  is
to  prevent  circumvention  by  a  candidate  of  the  Act’s  ex-
penditure  limitations  without  any  real,  as  opposed  to a theo-
retical,  infringement  of  an  individual’s  opportunity  to
speak.  The  distinction  is  potentially  significant,  for  it was
the  failure  of the Washington  limitations  statute  to allow  for
independent  individual  expenditures  which  contributed
heavily  to  the  court’s  invalidation  of  it;  and  it  was  that
aspect  alone  upon  which  the  three  concurring  justices
rested.  See  Bare  v.  Gorton,  526  P.2d  379,  385  (Wash.  1974) .8
The  point  here,  however,  is  not  to  debate  the  constitu-
tionality  of  California’s  law,  or  that  of  any  other  state,  or
to  suggest  that  the  California  approach  is  superior  to  that

3.  The  concurring  justices  stressed  that  a  properly  drawn
limitation  statute  might  well  be  upheld.  7d.,  at  385.  Cf.  State  v.
Conifer  Enterprises,  508  P.2d  20  (Wash.  1973)  (upholding  pro-
hibition  of expenditure  of  funds  to  pay  initiative  petition  signature
solicitors)

13
contained  in  the  FECA.  Rather,  the  purpose  of  amici  is
to  illustrate  that  each  state  statute  must  be  carefully
examined  with  respect  to  its  particular  provisions,  and  the
context  in  which  it will  operate,  before  a  judgment  regard-
ing  its  constitutionality  can  be  made.  Compare,  e.g.,  Wu-
liams  v.  Rhodes,  393  U.S.  23  (1968)  with  Jenness  v.  Fort-
son,  403  U.S.  431  (1971).

Thousands  of  American  citizens  and  state  legislators
have  worked  hard  in  their  respective  states  to  formulate
expenditure  limitations  that  would  improve  the  democratic
process  without  infringing  on  First  Amendment  rights.  In-
deed,  the  purpose  has  been  to  enhance  First  Amendment
values  by increasing  meaningful  participation  in  the  polit-
ical  process.  In  a  few  states  millions  of  voters  have
directly  approved  such  legislation.  We  urge  that  this  Court
not  abort  these  efforts  by  per  se  rules  founded  on  broad
generalizations  that  blur  or  ignore  significant  distinctions
between  the  approaches  and  circumstances  of  the  various
states.  Accordingly,  whatever  the  Court’s  decision  regard-
ing the  federal  legislation—and  we  reiterate  our  belief  that
FECA  is constitutional—each  state  statute  is entitled  to be
judged  on  its  own  merits.

II
CAMPAIGN  CONTRIBUTION  DISCLOSURE  LAWS
ARE  CONSTITUTIONAL

In  the  previous  section  of  this  brief  dealing  with  cam-
paign  expenditure  limitations,  we  attempted  to  point  out  a
number  of points  of  rational  difference  between  the  FECA
and  various  state  laws.  In  view  of  the  breadth  of  the  chal-
lenge  to the  disclosure  requirements  of the  FECA,  however,
such  points  of distinction  are  not  readily  found.  The  broad
attack  on  the campaign  contribution  disclosure  laws  pressed

— ~ =

14
in  this  Court  would,  if  successful,  seriously  threaten  like  |
disclosure  requirements  in  at  least  forty-one  states.  Ac-
cordingly,  in this  portion  of our  submission  we  find  ourselves
compelled  to  address  the  merits  directly.

Appellants  vigorously  press  their  claim  despite  their
admission  that  in  general  contribution  disclosure  is an  ap-
propriate  and  effective  method  of deterring  political  corrup-
tion  and  improper  conduct.  See  App.  Brief,  at  171.  The
essence  of  Appellants’  challenge  to  the  federal  disclosure
requirements  is,  as  we  understand  it,  two-fold:  they  ap-
parently  believe  that  the  law  requires  too  much  disclosure
in  that  contributions  of  as  little  as  $100  must  be  reported
(id.,  at  184-89)  and  they  oppose  disclosure  of contributions
to “unpopular”  or  “minority”  parties  or  candidates  (id.,  at
178-84).

Our  submission  here  stresses  three  propositions:  First,
Appellants’  reliance  upon  the  NAACP  line  of cases  is based
upon  the  faulty  premise  that  the  donation  of  money  to
political  candidates  or  organizations  is  “speech”  or  “asso-
ciation”  protected  by the  First  Amendment  in precisely  the
same  way  as  is  membership  in  a  political  or  social-action
organization;  we  think  it is not.  Second,  compelling  govern-
mental  interests  justify  disclosure  not  only  of  extremely
large  contributions  but  of  lesser  ones  as  well.  Third,  it
would  be  impractical  and  undesirable  to  exclude  from  the
ambit  of  disclosure  laws  “unpopular”  or  “minority”  eandi-
dates,  and  the  Constitution  does  not  command  that  result.

A.  Contributions  of  Money  Are  Not  Communications  Protected

by the First Amendment.

The  cases  on  which  Appellants  rely begin with  N.A.A.C.P.
v.  Alabama,  357  U.S.  449  (1958).  None  of them  holds  that  the
giving  of  money  to  a  political  candidate—as  distinguished

15
from  joining  an  association  or  making  a  speech  or  distribut-
ing a  pamphlet—is  protected  by the  First  Amendment.  The
issue  in these  cases  was  whether  the  state  could  compel  dis-
closure  of  or  information  from  an  organization’s  member-
ship  lists  (e.9.,  N.A.A.C.P.  v.  Alabama,  supra;  Louisiana
ex  rel.  Gremillion  v.  N.A.A.C.P.,  366  U.S.  293  (1961) ; Gib-
son  v.  Florida  Investigation  Committee,  372  U.S.  539
(1963)  ), whether  an  individual  could  be  compelled  broadly
to disclose  his  political  associations  in order  to  teach  in the
publie  schools  (Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  U.S.  479  (1969)),  or
whether  an  individual  distributing  a  leaflet  could  be  re-
quired  to  affix  his  name  and  address  (Talley  v.  California,
362  U.S.  60  (1960)).  In  Shelton  v.  Tucker,  supra,  and  in
Bates  v.  Little  Rock,  361  U.S.  516  (1960),  the  challenged
law  required  disclosure  of  membership  and  amounts  con-
tributed;  significantly,  however,  in each  case  the  Court  dis-
cussed  the  issues  as  if all  that  was  at stake  was  the  require-
ment  that  the  names  of  members  be  disclosed.  Thus,  in
striking  down  the  disclosure  requirement  in  Bates,  the
Court  remarked  that  “compulsory  disclosure  of  member-
ship lists  ...  would  work a significant  interference  with  the
freedom  of  association  of  [the  N.A.A.C.P.’s]  members.”
Id.,  at  523-24.  Indeed,  this  entire  line  of cases  was  recently
characterized  by the  Court  as  establishing  that  “[w]hen  a
State  seeks  to  inquire  about  an  individual’s  beliefs  and
associations  a  heavy  burden  lies  upon  it  to  show  that  the
inquiry  is necessary  to  protect  a  legitimate  state  interest.”
Baird  v.  Arizona,  401  U.S.  1, 6-7  (1971)  (emphasis  added).
Tt has  long  been  held  that  the  First  Amendment  protects
communication  and  not  conduct.  See,  e.g.,  Giboney  v.  Em-
pire  Storage  and  Ice  Co., 336  U.S.  490,  498-503  (1949)  ; Cox
v.  Louisiana,  379  U.S.  536,  555;  379  U.S.  559,  563  (1965)
(picketing) ; Zemel  v.  Rusk,  381  U.S.  1, 16-17  (1965)  (right
of speech  and  free  inquiry  does  not  include  right  to  travel

16
to  foreign  country)  ; United  States  v.  O’Brien,  391  U.S.  367
(1968)  (draft  card  burning);  Saxbe  v.  Washington  Post
Co.,  417  U.S.  843  (1974)  (right  of  press  inquiry  does  not
preclude  limitations  on  press  access  within  prison) ; Pell  v.
Procunier,  417  U.S.  817  (1974)  (same).  The  Court  recently
observed  that  “the  states  have  greater  power  to  regulate
nonverbal,  physical  conduct  than  to  suppress  depictions  or
descriptions  of  the  same  behavior.”  Miller  v.  California,
413  U.S.  15, 26 n.  8 (1973).  In  Cohen  v.  California,  403  U.S.
15  (1971),  the  Court  carefully  distinguished  pure  com-
.  which,
munication  from  “separately  identifiable  conduct  .
on  its  face,  does  not  necessarily  convey  any  message  and
hence  arguably  cowld  be  regulated  without  effectively  re-
pressing  [petitioner’s]  ability  to  express  himself.”  Id.,  at
18  (emphasis  added).

.

The  distinction  between  the  standards  applicable  to laws
which  regulate  speech  and  those  which  regulate  conduct
was  clearly  drawn  in  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  supra,  at
376-77,  where  the  burning  of a  draft  card  was  defended  as
an  act  of symbolic  speech  reflecting  opposition  to  the  Viet-
nam  War:

We  cannot  accept  the  view  that  an  apparently  limit-
less  variety  of conduct  can  be labeled  ‘speech’  whenever
the  person  engaging  in  the  conduct  intends  thereby
to  express  an  idea.  However,  even  on  the  assumption
that  the  alleged  communicative  element  in  O’Brien’s
conduct  is  sufficient  to  bring  into  play  the  First
Amendment,  it  does  not  necessarily  follow  that  the
destruction  of  a  registration  certificate  is  constitu-
tionally  protected  activity.  This  Court  has  held  that
when  “speech”  and  “nonspeech”  elements  are  com-
bined  in  the  same  course  of  conduct,  a  sufficiently  im-
portant  governmental  interest  in  regulating  the  non-
speech  element  can  justify  incidental  limitations  on
the
First  Amendment  freedoms.  To  characterize

17

quality  of  the  governmental  interest  which  must  ap-
pear,  the  Court  has  employed  a  variety  of  descriptive
terms:  compelling;  substantial;  subordinating;  para-
mount;  cogent;  strong.  Whatever  imprecision  inheres
in  these  terms,  we  think  it  clear  that  a  government
regulation  is  sufficiently  justified  if  it  is  within  the
constitutional  power  of  the  Government;  if it furthers
an  important  or  substantial  governmental  interest;  if
the  governmental  interest  is unrelated  to  the  suppres-
sion  of  free  expression;  and  if  the  incidental  restric-
tion  on  alleged  First  Amendment  freedoms  is  no
greater  than  is  essential  to  the  furtherance  of  that
interest.

In  the  case  of undisclosed  contributors,  of course,  there  is
not  even  the  possibility—as  there  was  in  O’Brien—that  the
conduct  (there  the  burning  of  a  draft  card)  is  itself  in-
tended  to  communicate  an  idea,  There  is,  moreover,  no
assurance  that  the  money  contributed  will  be  used  for  pur-
poses  of  facilitating  speech.  Political  contributions  are
used  for  a  variety  of purposes  other  than  the  actual  expres-
sion  of  views  by  the  candidate.  They  may,  for  example,
be  used  for  further  fund  raising.  Unless  forbidden  by law,
they  may  even  be  used  by  the  recipient  to  defray  the  ex-
penses  of public  office.  Finally,  to the  extent  that  a contribu-
tion  is  used  for  the  expression  of  a  candidate’s  views,  the
donor  understandably  will  not  (and  surely  showd  not)  have
any  control  over  the  content  of  the  candidate’s  expression.
Thus  at  best  the  contribution  of  money  is  several  steps
removed  from  speech  itself.

The  friends  and  supporters  of  the  candidate  or  party
are  not  restrained  by the  disclosure  requirement  from  com-
municating  their  ideas:  they  may  give  speeches,  write
articles,  and  distribute  pamphlets  without  disclosure  under
the  Act.*  The  fact  of membership  in a  political  organization

4.  Disclosure  evidently  would  be  required,  however,  if an  indi-
vidual  directly  expends  more  than  $100  in  these  endeavors.  2
U.S.C.  §434(e).

18
is  likewise  not  subject  tu  disclosure.  It  is  only  the  giving
of money  or  other  things  of value  that  is the  subject  of the
required  reports.

It  is  not  surprising  that  the  Court  has  never  held  that
the  mere  exchange  of  money  is an  act  within  the  umbrella
of  the  First  Amendment.  Such  a  holding  would  require
the  fresh  appraisal  of a  number  of well  established  federal
laws.  For  example,  it  has  long  been  the  law  that  federal
employees  may  not  contribute  to  the  campaigns  of persons
seeking  election  to  federal  office  (18 U.S.C.  § 607),  nor  may
federal  officials  accept  such  contributions  (18 U.S.C.  § 602).
See,  e.g.,  Brehm  v.  United  States,  196  F.2d  769  (D.C.  Cir.),
cert,  den.,  344  U.S.  838  (1952).  The  constitutionality  of such
a  prohibition  was  upheld  in  Ex  Parte  Curtis,  106  U.S.  371
(1882);  accord:  United  States  v.  Wurzbach,  280  U.S.  396
(1930).  Subsequently,  the  Court  in  United  Public  Workers
v.  Mitchell,  330  U.S.  75,  97  (1947)  spoke  with  apparent
approval  of the  ban  upheld  in  Curtis,  adding:

The  conclusion  of  the  Court,  that  there  was  no  con-
stitutional  bar  to  regulation  of such  financial  contribu-
tions  of public  servants  as  distinguished  from  the  exer-
cise  of political  privileges  such  as  the  ballot,  has  found
acceptance  in the  subsequent  practice  of Congress...  .

Likewise,  federal  law  has  for  many  years  prohibited
corporations  and  labor  unions  from  making  contributions
in  federal  elections.  18  U.S.C.  § 610.  The  Court  has  never
decided  a  case  as  to  the  constitutionality  of  these  prohibi-
tions  although  the  lower  courts  have  upheld  them.®

5.

See  United  States  v.  Boyle,  482  F.2d  755  (D.C.  Cir.),  cert.
den.,  414  U.S.  1076  (1973).  United  States  v.  Pipefitters  Local
Union  No.  562,  434  F.2d  1116  (8th  Cir.  1970),  rev’d.  on  other
grounds,  407  U.S.  385  (1972);  see  also  Egan  v.  United  States,  137
F.2d  369  (8th  Cir.)  cert.  den.,  320  U.S.  788  (1943)  (upholding
prohibition  in  Publie  Utility  Holding  Act  of  1937  against  con-
tributions  by  registered  utility  holding  company).  It  is  true  that
some  members  of  the  Court,  in  separate  opinions,  have  expressed

19
Under  the  circumstances,  there  is  simply  no  basis  in  the
cases  decided  by the  Court  for  Appellants’  assumption  that
the  First  Amendment  protects  the  act  of  giving  money  to
a  candidate  or  political  party.  The  point  is  of  critical  sig-
nificance.  Appellants  contend  that,  under  cases  such  as
N.A.A.C.P.  v.  Alabama,  the  disclosure  of  contributions  is
like  the  disclosure  of  membership  and  must  be  justified  by
a  compelling  governmental  interest.  But  that  kind  of  rig-
orous  judicial  scrutiny,  designed  for  the  protection  of core
First  Amendment  rights,  is misapplied  to  the  exchange  of
money;  moreover,  in  this  phase  of  the  case  (as  distin-
guished  from  that  dealing  with  the  portion  of  the  Act
limiting  the  amount  which  an  individual  may  contribute),
the  attack  is necessarily  tangential  because  at  the  most  dis-
closure  can  have  only  an  indirect  effect  upon  contributions.
To  be  sure,  money  contributed  will  facilitate  speech.  It
can—although  need  not  necessarily—be  used  to  buy  pam-
phlets  or  television  air  time.  But  it is one  thing  to  say  that
the  candidate  has  a  right  to  speak  and  quite  another  to  say
that  a  supporter  has  the  right  to give  the  candidate  money.
The  supporter’s  rights  of  speech  and  association  are  not
restricted,  let  alone  “chilled”  by  the  threat  of  disclosure.
Private  support,  personal  assistance  (such  as  the  donation
of  supporters’  labor)  and  the  contribution  of  ideas,  posi-
tion  papers,  and  draft  speeches  are  not  infringed  nor  is
disclosure  required.

doubts  as  to  the  constitutionality  of Section  610,  but  those  concerns
went  to  the  restraint  of  that  section  upon  the  communication  of
ideas  (in  union-financed  publications  or  communications)  and  not
to  the  contribution  of  dollars  to  candidates  or  parties.  United
States  v.  C.I.0.,  335  U.S.  106  (1948);  United  States  v.  Interna-
tional  Union,  352  U.S.  567  (1957);  Pipefitters  v.  United  States,  407
U.S.  385  (1972).  The  argument  in  these  cases  simply  does  not
reach  the  contribution  of  money  to  a  candidate;  in  that  respect,  it
is significant  to  note  that  one  of the  dissenters,  Mr.  Justice  Douglas,
observed  in  International  Union  that  large  union  contributions
could  be  prohibited.  352  U.S.  at  598  n.  2.

20
The  Court  of  Appeals  recognized  these  principles  (Slip
Op.,  at  1516-17),  and  characterized  the  scope  of  review  as
defined  by  United  States  v.  O’Brien,  supra.  Its  opinion
suggests,  however,  that  in fact  it applied  an  even  more  rigor-
ous  standard  of review.  (See  td., at 1519).  We  agree  with  the
Court  of  Appeals  that  contribution  disclosure  legislation
will  in fact  survive  strict  scrutiny  and  that  compelling  gov-
ernmental  interests  support  it.  But  we  do  not  agree  that
strict  scrutiny—of  the  kind  which  concededly  would  be ap-
propriate  were  the  disclosure  requirements  applicable  to
membership,  association,  or  speech—is  required,

B.

Disclosure  of Political  Contributions  Above  a  Minimal  Amount
Is Justified  by Overriding  Governmental  Interest.

Even  if  the  making  of  political  contributions  were  pro-
tected  by  the  First  Amendment,  that  would  only  be  the
beginning—and  not  the  end—of  the  inquiry.  For  it would
also  be  necessary  to  determine  that  there  are  lacking  any
governmental  interests  which  justify  the  indirect  infringe-
ment  of  those  First  Amendment  values.  Thus  in  Bates  v.
Little  Rock,  swpra,  at  524,  the  Court  said:

Decision  in  this  case  must  finally  turn,  therefore,  on
whether  the  cities  as  instrumentalities  of  the  State
have  demonstrated  so  cogent  an  interest  in  obtaining
and  making  public  the  membership  lists  of  these  or-
ganizations  as  to  justify  the  substantial  abridgement
of  associational  freedom  which  such  disclosures  will
effect.  Where  there  is a  significant  encroachment  upon
personal  liberty,  the  State  may  prevail  only  upon
showing  a  subordinating  interest  which  is compelling.

21
The  opinion  of  the  Court  of Appeals  fully  demenstrates
(as,  doubtless  will  Appellees  in  their  brief)  the  various
important  public  interests  which  contribution  disclosure
serves.  See  Slip  Op.,  at  1511-16,  1543-48.  Indeed,  we  do not
understand  Appellants  to  question  either  the  constitution-
ality  or  decirability  of disclosure  laws  as  such:

[A]ppellants  believe  that  narrowly  drawn  disclosure
requirements  are  the  proper  solution  to  virtually  all
the  evils  Congress  sought  to  remedy  by  passing  the
challenged  statutes.  (App.  Brief,  at  171).

Appellants  challenge  the  disclosure  mandated  by  the
FECA  on  the  ground  that  it  overbroadly  applies  to  all
contributions  in  excess  of  $100°  rather  than  to  contribu-
tions  in  excess  of  some  presumably  larger—though  unspe-
cified—amounts.  See  App.  Brief,  at  184-89.  Because  in only
four  of  the  forty-four  states  which  require  campaign  con-
tribution  disclosure  is the  reporting  threshold  set  at  more
than  $101  (see  note  7, infra  and  Exhibit  “A’’)  this  aspect  of
the  attack  poses  a  serious  threat  to the  laws  of many  states,
including  amici.

In  the  first  place,  it is a matter  of judgment  as  to at what
point  political  contributions  become  so  unimportant  to  a
candidate  that  the  public  interest  in  knowing  of  them  is
minimal  and  the  possibility  of  purchasing  influence  and
access  is  avoided.  In  local  elections,  of  course,  a  contribu-
tion  of  $50  or  $100  may  be  highly  significant  to  a  cam-
paign;  but  even  in  federal  elections  contributions  of  $100
cannot  be  said  to  be  minimal.  Appellants  believe  that  the
line  should  have  been  set  at  some  other  point.  But  the
drawing  of lines  is a  legislative  matter.  Even  accepting  the
proposition  that  the  requirement  of  disclosure  must  be

6.

<A  second  ground  of  challenge—application  of  the  disclosure
requirement  to  minority  parties  and  candidates—is  dealt  with  in
Part  II(C),  infra.

22
supported  by a  significant  governmental  interest,  it cannot
seriously  be  argued  that  such  a  showing  must  be  made  to
defend  fixing  the  line  at  $100  rather  than  $397.  As  Justice
Stewart  said  in  Oregon  v.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112  (1970),
with  respect  to  the  suggestion  that  the  legislative  deter-
mination  of  an  age  of  majority  for  voting  purposes  might
be  tested  by “strict  scrutiny”  equal  protection  standards:
Yet  to  test  the  power  to  establish  an  age  qualification
by the  “compelling  interest”  standard  is really  to deny
a  State  any  choice  at  all,  because  no  State  could  dem-
onstrate  a  “compelling  interest”  in  drawing  the  line
with  respect  to  age  at  one  point  rather  than  another.
Obviously,  the  power  to  establish  an  age  qualification
must  carry  with  it the  power  to  choose  21  as  a  reason-
able  voting  age,  as  the  vast  majority  of  the  States
have  done.  (/d.,  at  294-95).

The  vast  majority  of  the  states  require  disclosure  of
campaign  contributions  in  a  fashion  comparable  to  the
FECA.  In  Exhibit  “A”  we  have  summarized  the  applicable
state  laws.  The  enactments  of  thirty  states  compel  disclo-
sure  of  contributions  over  $50,  eleven  set  the  line  at  $100
or  $101,  and  only  four  states  set  the  line  at higher  amounts."

7.  The  breakdown  is  as  follows:

23
Although  there  are  differences  between  the  federal  and
state  interests  and  circumstances,  the  wide-spread  percep-
tion—as  the  laws  of at least  forty-one  states  demonstrate—
of a need  for disclosure  of contributions  of these  lesser  mag-
nitudes  cautions  against  acceptance  of Appellants’  attempt
to  second-guess  the  judgment  of  Congress  in  this  regard.
There  are  other  considerations  of  an  intensely  practical
nature.  Even  if it could  be  said  that  contributions  of, say,
$500,  are  insufficiently  large  to  have  the  potential  of  im-
proper  influence  on  a  candidate  or  official,  limiting  disclo-
sure  to  such  contributions  could,  as  a  practical  matter,  ren-
der  the  disclosure  law  unenforceable.  Contributions  in large
amounts  could  effectively  be  concealed  by  the  use  of  a
variety  of  techniques;  for  example,  a  contributor  could
make  a  contribution  of  just  under  the  maximum  amount
and  arrange  for  others  to  do  likewise.  Similarly,  illegal
corporate  or  labor  contributions  could  be concealed  through
the  use  of  individual  “contributors”  making  contributions
of $499.  “‘[I]n  requiring  that  the  state  use  to a  proper  end
the  means  designed  to  impinge  minimally  upon  fundamen-
tal  rights,  the  Constitution  does  not  require  that  the
state  choose  ineffectual  means.’”  Pesario  v.  Rockefeller,
410  U.S.  752,  762  n.  10  (1973).

In  view  of  these  considerations,  the  Court  of  Appeals
correctly  concluded  that  it had  “no  basis  for  holding  that
this  approach  by  the  legislature  .
.  in  drawing  the  line
[at  $100]  exceeded  the  bounds  of  reasonable  latitude.”
(Slip  Opinion,  at  1545).  Accord:  United  States  v.  Finance
Committee,  507  F.2d  1194,  1200  (D.C.  Cir.  1974);  Stoner
v.  Fortson,  379  F.Supp.  704  (N.D.  Ga.  1974).  In Stoner,  the
court  said:

.

The  Georgia  General  Assembly  has  made a legislative
determination  that  $101  is  the  cut-off  point  above

24

which  disclosures  must  be  made  in  order  to  achieve
their  stated  purposes.  This  court  recognizes  that  a
cut-off  point  had  to  be  established  somewhere.  There
is no doubt  that  all  campaign  contributors  giving  $101
or  more  do  not  make  such  contributions  in  an  attempt
to  buy  influence  +  .d  to  corrupt  the  elective  process;
however,  for  the  legislation  to  be  valid,  there  need  be
no  showing  that  all  would  be  so  inclined.  [Citations]
This  court  views  the  $101  or  more  threshold  as  a
rational  attempt  by the  Georgia  General  Assembly  to
establish  a  level  of  contribution  that  will  attract  the
attention  of  the  candidate  and  thus  begin  to  satisfy
the  goals  of  the  statute.  This  court  notes  that  federal
judges,  under  court  rules,  disclose  gifts  over  $100,
that  Georgia  judges  must  do  the  same  under  the  Code
of  Judicial  Conduct,  that  the  Federal  Corrupt  Prac-
tices  Act  of  1925,  supra,  required  disclosures  of  con-
tributions  over  $100,  and  that  the  new  Federal  Elec-
tions  Campaigns  statute,  2 U.S.C.  431  et  seq.,  requires
disclosures  of contributions  over  $100.  (Jd.,  at  712-13).

C.  Minority  Parties  and Candidates  Are Not Constitutionally

Exempt
from Disclosure.

Appellants  contend  that,  in  all  events,  disclosure  laws
cannot  constitutionally  be  applied  to  “new  and  minor  par-
ties  and  independent  candidates.”  App.  Brief,  at  178.  It  is
not  entirely  clear  whether  Appellants  would  be  satisfied
to  see  exempted  only  those  parties  not  entitled  to  automatic
ballot  access  (see  #d., at  184)  or  whether  they likewise  would
exempt,  as  their  brief  implies,  the  likes  of  the  Missouri
Republican  Party  (see  id., at  174),  George  Wallace  (see  td.,
at  173)  or  Appellant  McCarthy.

The  Court  of  Appeals  rejected  the  claimed  constitution-
ally-based  exemption,  resting  in  part  on  its  determination
that  the  record  “presents  only  scant  evidence  tending  to
show a ‘chilling  effect’  resulting  from  the  major  disclosure
requirements,  and  only  a  skimpy  indication  of  harassment

25
of those  whose  contributions  were  disclosed  in the  1972  and
1974  elections.  .
.  .” (Slip  Op.,  at  1546).  Nonetheless,  the
balance  of  the  Court  of  Appeals’  analysis  persuasively
demonstrates  the  existence  of  compelling  governmental  in-
terests  of  the  kind  which,  even  if  a  showing  of  “chilling
effect”  could  be  made,  would  warrant  the  enforcement  of
a  uniform  disclosure  requirement.  Accordingly,  while  we
agree  that  the  judgment  can  be  affirmed  in  this  respect  on
the  ground  that  no  significant  “chilling  effect”  was  shown
on  the  record,  we  believe  that  the  logic  of the  Court  of Ap
peals’  governmental  interest  analysis  in  fact  answers  the
question  the  court  purported  to  reserve:

It  may  well  be  that  a  party  (or  candidate)  can
demonstrate  such  harassment  to  itself  or  to  its  sup-
porters  as  to  underpin  a  ruling  that  disclosure  in par-
ticular  circumstances  cannot  constitutionally  be  re-
quired.  Compare  NAACP  v.  Alabama  ez  rel.  Patterson,
357  U.S.  449  (1958).  Whether  the  Commission  through
its  regulations  or  its  advisory  opinions  can  appropri-
ately  address  this  situation  is  a  question  we  do  not
decide  in  the  absence  of  concrete  facts.  In  any  event,
courts  sit  to  act  in  such  cases  when  presented  with  a
proper  record.  (Slip  Op.,  at  1548).

Litigation  is  now  pending  in  the  lower  courts  in  which
minority  parties  or  candidates  claim  that  the  record  does
demonstrate  a  “chilling  effect.”  E.g.,  Central-East  Los
Angeles  Socialist  Workers  Campaign  Committee  v.  Pines
(Calif.  Ct.  App.,  2d  App.  Dist.  Div.  4,  Civ.  No,  46258)
(appeal  from  denial  of  preliminary  injunction  against  en-
forcement  of disclosure  provisions  of California’s  Proposi-
tion  Nine).  Because  the  record  in the  present  case  does  not
support  such  a  claim,  it  may  be  that  the  Court  will  wish
to  refrain  from  reaching  that  question  of  balancing  inter-
ests.  It  seems  clear  to  us,  however,  that  for  the  very  rea-

26
sons  developed  by the  Court  of Appeals  in  its  opinion,  the
interests  served  by  disclosure  are  so  compelling  that  an
exemption  for  “minority”  parties  is  unthinkable.  In  this
respect,  we  would  like  briefly  to  underscore  several  points
in  support  of  a  uniform  application  of  the  disclosure  laws.
First,  the  Constitution  does  not  command  miracles  of leg-
islative  draftsmanship.  The  task  of  drafting  an  exemption
for  organizations  which  are  both  burdened  with  enormous
community  hostility  and  whose  candidates  are  not  serious
contenders,  would  be  enormous.  Void  for  vagueness  chal-
lenges  would  be  inevitable.

Second,  even  assuming  that  careful  drafting  might  have
produced  a  statute  clearly  exempting  some  groups,  further
constitutional  problems  would  render  such  a  statute  vul-
nerable  to  challenge  by included  candidates  and  parties.  If,
as  Appellants  contend,  the  area  of campaign  contributions
disclosure  is protected  by the  First  Amendment,®  then  legis-
lative  lines  must  be  drawn  with  special  precision;  by  the
same  token,  exemptions  cannot  be given  absent  a compelling
justification.  For  where  legislation  regulates  the  exercise,
of rights  expressly  guaranteed  by the  Constitution,  the  “ra-
tional  basis”  test  of  review  is  inapplicable,  and  judicial
review  requires  the court  to adopt “an  attitude  of active  and
critical  analysis,  subjecting  the  classification  to  strict  scru-
tiny”.  Westbrook  v.  Mihaly,  2 Cal.  3d 765, 785  (1970).  Legis-
latively  specified  inclusions  and  exclusions  will  be  upheld
only  if  justified  by  a  “compelling  governmental  interest.”
Shapiro  v.  Thompson,  394  U.S.  618,  634  (1969)  (emphasis
added).  See  e.g.,  San  Antonio  School  District  v.  Rodriguez,
411  U.S.  1,  29-34  (1973);  Kramer  v.  Union  Free  School
District,  395  U.S.  621,  626-631  (1969);  Harper  v.  Virginia,
383  U.S.  663  (1966);  Skinner  v.  Oklahoma,  316  U.S.  535

8.  In  Part  II(A)  supra,  we  of  course  contend  to  the  contrary.

27
(1942).  Nowhere  have  the  courts  been  more  vigilant  in this
respect—and  properly  so—than  in  the  areas  protected  by
the  First  Amendment.  See,  e.g.,  Police  Department  v.
Mosley,  408  U.S.  92,  94-102  (1972);  Cox  v.  Louisiana,  379
U.S.  536,  581  (1965)  (Black,  J., concurring).  As  the  Court
put  it in  Mosley,  “government  may  not  grant  the  use  of  a
forum  to  people  whose  views  it finds  acceptable,  but  deny
use  to  those  wishing  to  express  less  favored  or  more  con-
troversial  views.”  Id.,  at  96.  The  point,  of  course,  is  not
that  an  attack  by  a  Republican  or  Democratic  candidate
would  necessarily  succeed;  surely  it  is  enough  that  those
who  drafted  the  statute  avoided  the  constitutional  difficul-
ties  which  would  have  arisen  had  exceptions  for  “unpopu-
lar”  or  “minority”  parties  been  written  into  the  statute.
Third,  in  the  same  vein,  there  is an  additional  virtue  in
the  refusal  of  Congress  to  exempt  any  group  from  the
requirements  of  the  law.  The  equal  protection  doctrines
just  discussed  reflect  a  widely  perceived  principle  of  our
jurisprudential  system—namely,  that  special  privileges
and  exemptions  in  the  political  process  are  disfavored.
The  FECA  broke  new  ground  in  numerous  respects;  in
some  instances,  in the  interest  of strengthening  and  purify-
ing our  democratic  processes,  it imposed  new  requirements
and  burdens.  The  efficacy  of  such  legislation—indeed,  vir-
tually  all  legislation—depends  on  the  citizenry  and  their
willingness  to  accept  and  adhere  to  the  requirements  of
law.  Exeeptions  to  any  law,  be it in the  tax  field  or  election
reform,  which  the  public  cannot  perceive  as  just,  threaten
publie  confidence  and  ultimately  the  Rule  of Law.

Fourth,  Appellants  argue  that  some  minority  candidates
or  parties  will  always  be  minorities,  and  therefore  repre-
sent  no  threat  to  the  integrity  of the  parties  process.  They
urge,  in  effect,  an  exception  for  candidates  and  parties

28
whcse  cause  is hopeless.  But  who  is to  say  that  a  candidate
who  seeks  public  office  is  not  a  serious  contender?  Appel-
lants  would  have  the  law  draw  a  line  the  ascertainment
of  which  is  rife  with  danger.  In  Oakland,  California,  for
example,  a  Black  Panther  candidate  for  Mayor  made  a
serious  challenge;  the  candidacy  of  Governor  Wallace  on
the  American-Independent  Party  platform  was  seriously
regarded  in  many  states;  and  recently  there  was  talk
of  the  formation  of  a  third  party  espousing  conservative
principles,  with  former  Governor  Reagan  as  the  Presiden-
tial  standard  bearer.  See  also  Note,  Developments  in  the
Law—Elections,  88  Harv.  L.  Rev.  1111,  1247  n.  75  (1975).
Candidates  and  parties  are  not  entitled  to ask  the  electorate
for  their  votes  while,  in  the  same  breath,  proclaiming  that
their  candidacies  are  not  “serious”.

Fifth,  even  a  candidate  who  lacks  a  realistic  chance  of
election  can  affect  the  outcome.  This  is  so  because,  as  the
Court  of
Appeals  recognized,  each  vote  that  a  third  party
candidate  receives  is  a  vote  which  otherwise  might  have
been  cast  for  one  of  the  major  party  candidates.  Slip  Op.,
at  1547.  For  this  reason,  no  candidate  is  necessarily  irrele-
vant  to  the  campaign  or  even  to  the  outcome  of an  election.

(And,  doubtless  for  this  reason,  there  have  been  instances
of  major  candidates  contributing  further  to  minor  party
candidates—a  tactic  which  disclosure  could  reveal;  like-
wise,  as  the  Court  of  Appeals  observed,  minor  parties  can
“he  deliberately  forced  to  take  a  stalking  horse  role.”  Jd.\.

29
For  all  of  these  reasons,  the  requirement  of  disclosure
should  not  be  riddled  with  a  constitutionally  compelled
exemption  of  uncertain  size  and  shape.

Dated:

October  16,  1975

Respectfully  submitted,

Exhibit  A

Minimum  Reportable  Amounts  Under
State  Campaign  Disclosure  Laws

The  following  listing  shows  for  each  state  the  smallest
reportable  contributions  under  campaign  disclosure  laws.’

Where  the  disclosure  laws  of  the  state  have  been  revised
in  the  last  two  years,  the  year  of  the  revision  is  stated  in
parentheses.
