INTEREST  OF  THE  AMICUS  CURIAE

This  brief  of  Amicus  Curiae  is  filed  pursuant  to.  Rule
42(2)  of  The  Rules  of  the  Supreme  Court  by  consent  of
the  parties.  Amicus  Curiae,  the  Socialist  Labor  Party,  is
an  unincorporated  association  established  in  1877,  the  re-
sult  of  a  merger  of  the  National  Labor  Union,  the  North
American  Federation  of  the  International  Workingman’s

2
Association,  and  the  Social  Democratic  Workman’s  Party,
and  was  first  known  as  the  Workman’s  Party  of the  United
States.

It  nominates  candidates  for  public  office  on  local,  state,
and  national  levels  and  engages  in  a  number  of  activities
other  than  those  generally  associated  with  a  political  party.
The  party  publishes  a  weekly  newspaper,  THE  WEEKLY
PEOPLE,  as  well  as  pamphlets  which  are  distributed  by
mail  and  on  street  corners  throughout  the  United  States.
It  further  holds  public  meetings  and  study  groups  and  en-
gages  in  radio  and  television  broadcasts  and  commentaries.
Though  it has  nominated  and  campaigned  for  candidates
for  congress  and  the  presidency,  it has  never  won  an  elec-
tion  on  the  national,  state,  or  local  levels.  It has  established
a  political  committee  to  accept  contributions  and  make  ex-
penditures  on  behalf  of  a  presidential  candidate  to  be
nominated  at  a  convention  convening  on  February  7, 1976.
The  nomination  of  a  presidential  candidate  and  his  cam-
paign  are  viewed  by  the  party  as  another  means  of  educa-
tion  of the  public  regarding  their  political,  social,  economic,
and  philosophical  beliefs  which  can  be  described  as  Marx-
ist-De  Leonism,  In essence  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  adopts
Marxist  Analysis  and  applies  this  concept  to  American
life.

The  Committee  submitted  a  registration  to  the  Federal
Election  Campaign  Committee  on  August  22,  1975.  As
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  subjects  them  to  re-

1.  Brissenden,  The  ].W.W.,  A  Study  of American  Syndicalism,
New  York,  Columbia  University,  1919,  pp.  46-47;  Madison,  Critics
and  Crusaders,  A Century  of American  Protest,  New  York,  Fred-
erick  Ungar  Publishing  Co.,  1959  ed.,  pp.  449-452.

2.  The  Weekly  People  has  been  found  to  be  a  non-commercial,
non-profit  publication.  Long  v.  City  of  Anaheim,  255  Cal.App.2d
191,  63  Cal.Rptr.  46  (1967).

3
port  and  disclosure  requirements,  the  committee  is  inter-
ested  in  the  outcome  of  the  challenge  of  the  Federal  Elee-
tion  Campaign  Statutes  because  disclosure  requirements
will  inhibit  their  ability  to  obtain  contributions  to  further
their  educational  campaign.  Secondly,  the  committee  has
concern  for  their  inability  to  obtain  funds  from  the Public
Campaign  Finance  Fund  while  the  major  parties  qualify
for such  aid.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

I

Minor  parties  are  required  to  disclose  the  source  of esm-
paign  contributions  under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act.  Disclosure  poses  a  threat  to  first  amendment  as  socia-
tional  rights  chilling  the  exercise  of  fundamental  libe:  ties.
The  threat  is posed  by economic  and  personal  reprisals  that
contributors  to  unpopular  causes  will  face  upon  disclosure,
The  opinion  of the  Court  of Appeals  recognizes  the  threat
and  considers  disclosure  from  minor  parties  as  an  infringe-
ment  of associational  right.  However,  the  Court  is unwilling
to  declare  the  disclosure  provisions  as  applied  to  minority
parties  unconstitutional  without  ample  evidentiary  basis  of
infringement.

The  evidentiary  basis  is  difficult  to  prove  and  the  sanc-
tions  are  subtle.  As  irreparable  harm  can  be  done  to  indi-
viduals  and  associations  prior  to  meeting  any  legal  tests,
the provisions  should  be declared  unconstitutional  as  applied
to minor  parties.

The  government  does  not  have  a  compelling  interest  to
require  disclosure  from  minor  parties  whose  role  in  the
political  process  is  educational  and  serves  to  validate  our
free  society  and  inform  the  electorate.  The  function  of  the
Socialist  Labor  Party  is  akin  to  issue-discussion  groups
exempted  from  disclosure  by  invalidation  of  provisions
‘applicable  to  their  exercise  of  first  amendment  righis.

4
The  abuse  aimed  at  by the  laws  is not  created  by or  with
the  aid  of a  minor  party.  Though  restriction  of first  amend-
ment  freedoms  may  be  permissible  because  of  the  interests
of  the  government  in  the  integrity  of  elections,  the  least
restrictive  means  was  not  employed  for  the  legislative
scheme,

Il

The  public  financing  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  excludes  minor  parties  access  to  public
funds.  The  standards  employed  to  allow  campaign  funding
are  suspect  classifications  drawn  by  incumbents  of  the
major  parties,  and  the  laws  favor  those  two  parties.

The  effect  is to  limit  the  access  of minor  parties  to  public
funds  and  thereby  unduly  burden  their  interest  in  the
availability  of  political  opportunity.  This  infringes  upon
the  associational  and  free  speech  rights  of  the  members
of  disadvantaged  minor  parties.

Though  reasonable  restrictions  can  be  placed  on  first
amendment  freedoms  the  factual  basis  of  legitimizing  the
restriction  must  he  provable.  The  Court  is obligated  to apply
strict  scrutiny  when  inquiry  of  competing  governmental
interest  and  first  amendment  interests  abut.  If  no  com-
pelling  interest  justifies  the  encroachment,  the  provisions
must  be declared  unconstitutional,

ARGUMENT

I.  The  Disclosure  Requirements  of the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act  Should  Be  Declared  Unconstitutional  as  Applied  to  Minor
Parties.

Under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  the  Socialist
Labor  Party  is  required  as  the  major  parties  to  disclose
the  names  and  addresses  of  contributors  to  their  campaign

5
through  audit  or  report.  2 U.S.C.  432,  454(B)(2),  488.  26
U.S.C.  9003(A).  The  disclosure  required  does  not  consider
the  nature,  purpose,  and  effect  the  Socialist  Labor  Party
and  other  minor  parties  have  in  election  campaigns.  The
purview  of  the  Act  does  not  recognize  the  position  of  such
a  minor  or  minority  party  in  the  political  spectrum,  and
the  consequences  of  disclosure  to  the  party,  its  members
and  sympathizers,  and  the  political  process.

A.

Disclosure  Poses  a  Sufficient  Threat  of Intimidation  and  Harassment  to  Minor
Party  Contributors  to  Recognize  Infringement  of  Their  Associational  Rights
Without  an  Evidentiary  Basis.

The  disclosure  requirements  pose  a  threat  to  the  party,
its  members,  and  democratic  principles  by  imposing  the
specter  of  harassment  and  intimidation  of  individuals  who
would  support  such  unpopular  causes.’  Accordingly,  indi-
viduals  are  inhibited  from  contributing  to  the  campaign
of the  spokesman-candidate  of the minor  party.

In  NAACP  v.  Alabama,  357  US  449,  460,  78  S.Ct.  1165,
2 L.Ed.2d  1488  (1957),  requiring  disclosure  of membership
lists  violated  the  rights  of  members  of  an  organization
to  engage  in  lawful  association  in  support  of  common  be-
liefs.  Although  there  was  no  contention  that  the  member-
ship  was  directly  suppressed  by such  disclosure  the  conse-
quence  of  suppression,  intimidation,  harassment,  physical
abuse,  and  loss  of  economic  sustenance  provided  the  basis
for  the  unconstitutionality  of  the  disclosure  in  the  absence
of an  overriding  valid  state  interest.

An  analogous  situation  exists  with  the  Socialist  Labor
Party  being  required  to  disclose  the  names,  addresses,  and

3.  The  harassment  and  intimidation  that  concerns  the  individual
is  not  from  the  major  parties  who  win  elections  nor  the  publie
generally  as  the  majority  opinion  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  suspects
but  that  from  neighbors,  landlords,  employers,  business  associates,
store  owners,  and  lenders  of money,  ete.  whose  sanctions  are  subtle.

suppression,

6
occupations  of  its  contributors.t*  Through  not  contention  of
intimidation,  harassment,  physical
direct
abuse,  and  loss  of  economic  sustenance  can  be  made  pres-
ently ;° protection  of first  amendment  rights  should  not  only
become  available  after  the  foreseen  consequences  have
crippled  individuals  and  associations  creating  a burden  that
endangers  the  efficiency  of the association,  and  the beliefs  of
its members  and  sympathizers.

The  recognition  of  the  threats  posed  by  disclosure  to
contributors  to  controversial  candidates  without  an  evi-
dentiary  basis  was  accepted  by  the  court  in  Stoner  v.
Fortson,  379  F.Supp.  704  (N.D.GA  1974).  However,  the
court  decided  that  there  was  a  compelling  state  interest
for  disclosure,  and  a  substantial  connection  between  the
disclosure  sought  and  the  purpose  for  which  disclosure
was  required  that  made  infringement  of  associational
rights  permissible.

In  Stoner  the  threatened  infringement  was  based  on
disclosure  of  contributors  to  a  controversial  candidate.
No  rational  demarkation  can  be  made  between  controver-
sial  and  noncontroversial  candidates  to  permit  decision
prior  to  evidentiary  findings  of  the  harassment,  physical  —
abuse,  and  loss  of  employment  feared  from  disclosure  that
would  provide  consistent  treatment.  A  standard  can  be

4.  Even  if  contributions  under  $100.00  are  not  reportable  as
the  Act  requires  recording  of  the  contributor  of  $10.00  or  more
which  is  subject  to  audit,  there  is  no  guarantee  of  confidentiality
for  the  contributor.

5.  The  Socialist  Labor  Party  has  a  policy  of  disclosing  in  their
paper,  The  Weekly  People,  contributions  unless  anonymity  is  re-
quested.  Though  no  evidence  can  be  presented  with  regard  to threats
of  FECA  disclosure,  the  Party  can  provide  evidence  of such  feared
activity  in  relation  to  contributors  generally  and  prior  to  this  dis-
closure.

7
established  when  a  line  is  that  between  major  and  minor
parties  thus  obviating  the  need  for  an  evidentiary  hear-
ing.®

The  evidentiary  basis  of  infringement  feared  from  dis-
closure  cannot  readily  be  proved.  In  order  to  do  so  the
party  must  require  members  to  publicize  that  which  will
foster  the  reprisals  they  seek  to  avoid.  It  is  difficult  to
prove  economic  or  other  repvisals  because  the  sanctions
are  subtle.  Prior  to  meeting  a  legal  test  to  justify  the  un-
constitutionality  of  disclosures  provisions,  great  injury
could  be done  to the  organization.’

B.

The  Role  and  Effect  of  Minor  Parties  in  the  Political  Process  Does  Not
Require  Disclosure  to  Insure  Valid  State  Interests.

United  States  v.  Harriss,  347  US  612,  74  S.Ct.  808,  98
L.Ed.  989  (1954)  supports  the  view  that  interference  with
first  amendment  freedoms  is  permissible  if  disclosure  re-
quirements  infringe  such  rights  as  long  as  valid  and  para-
mount  state  interests  indicate  the  choice  of  state  interests
over  first  amendment  freedoms.  However,  in  [Harriss  paid
lobbyists  were  required  to  disclose  the  source  of  their
funds,  or  in  other  words,  their  clients.  The  situational
context  was  commercial  and  distinguishable  from  contribu-
tions  to  a  candidate  for  public  office,  especially  a  candidate
whom  is unlikely  to be elected.

The  scrutiny  that  an  elected  official  will  receive  avoids
in  large  measure  the  need  for  disclosure  of  his  campaign
contributors.  It  is  unlikely  that  contributors  to  minor  par-
ties  will  be  doing  so  to  buy  any  favors.  Accordingly,  the

6.  Bazelon  Dissent,  Unpublished  Opinion,  Buckley  vs.  Valeo,

page  1593.

7.  Bazelon  Dissent,  supra,  Page  1595;  Harvard  Law  Review,

Vol.  88,  Number  6, April  1975,  Pages  1248-1249.

8
abuse  that  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  seeks  to
eradicate  is  not  present  in  the  case  of  a  minor  party  such
as  the  Socialist  Labor  Party.

The  abuse  is one  created  by the  major  parties  for  which
minor  parties  will  suffer  the  detriments  attendant  on  loss
of associational  freedom.  The  history  of the  Socialist  Labor
Party  demonstrates  that  no  candidate  nominated  for  any
elective  office  has  ever  won.  It  is  doubtful  that  the  abuse
aimed  at  by the  disclosure  provisions  of  the  Federal  Cam-
paign  Act  is operative  under  this  circumstance.

The  purpose  and  role  of  minor  party  candidates  are  pri-
marily  educational  in  the  context  of  our  polities.  Minor
parties  validate  our  free  society  and  inform  the  electorate.
Inspite  of  their  lack  of  fiscal  ability  they  provide  a  guide
for  major  party  candidates  of  minority  views  which  may
influence  the  candidates  programs.  This,  of  course,  mar
not  always  be  true,  and  recent  experience  has  indicated
independent  candidates  can  influence  elections.*  For  the
remainder  of  minor  parties  the  possibility  of  influencing
elections  is non-existent  because  of a  lack  of funds  sufficient
to  enable  them  to  gain  political  parity  with  the  major  par-
ties,

In  the  1972  presidential  and  vice-presidential  campaign
the  Socialist  Labor  Party  Committee  expended  contribn-
tions  in  the  amount  of  $106,261.41  on  behalf  of  their  can-
didates.*®  They  had  limited  access  to  media  during  the  1972
campaign  and  on  their  only  broadcast  with  an  ABC  radio
network  commentary,  only  six  of  the  networks  affiliates
carried  the  show.  The  Party  has  never  won  any  election
eampaign.  Accordingly,  the  campaign  is  regarded  as  edu-

8  e.g.  George  Wallace  Campaign  of 1968.

9.  This  amount  is  less  than  %  of  1%  of  the  funds  the  Act

authorizes  for  expenditures  on  a  presidential  campaign.

9
cational  activity  where  the  views  of  the  Socialist  Labor
Party  may  have  the  opportunity  to  enter  the  forum  of
public  debate.

C.  There  Is  No  Compelling  State  Interests  Sufficient  to Justify  Disclosure  by the

Labor  Party.
Socialist

The  Party  relies  upon  their  campaign  funds  to  carry
on  an  equally  important  educational  activity  to  the  pub-
lication  of  their  newspaper.  The  number  of  members  and
sympathizers  is  small,  and  when  limited  to  under  $100.00
to  avoid  disclosure  it is unlikely  if the  candidates  will  have
sufficient  funds  to  establish  an  organization  to  permit  ac-
tive  campaigning  in  a  meaningful  manner.  Traditionally
larger  contributors  to  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  candidates
own  businesses,  and  though  supportive  of  the  views  of
the  Party  realize  that  an  association  of  their  business  and
the  Party  will  bring  economic  detriment.  In  other  instances
partners  of  contributors  do  not  want  the  association,  and
consequently  contributions  from  these  sources  will  be  eur-
tailed  or  cease.

The  majority  opinioa  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  recog-
nizes  that  disclosure  may  prompt  reduction  of  contribu-
tions  to  minority  parties  and  that  so  far  as  unpopular
minor  parties  are  concerned,  disclosure  does  indeed  in-
fringe  their  first  amendment  freedom  to  associate  to  advo-
cate  unorthodoxy.  Citing  Sweezy  v.  New  Hampshire,  354
US  254,  77  S.Ct.  1203,  1  L.Ed.2d  1311  (1954).  However,
the  majority  views  the  disclosure  requirements  as  a  1¢2-
sonable  restriction  of  such  rights  validated  by  the  state
interests  in  avoiding  campaign  abuses  and  insuring  the
confidence  of  the  American  public  in  the  political  process.
Further,  they  present  the  “Stalking  Horse”  theory  in which
people  influence  the  election  of  their  candidates  by  eco-

10
nomically  supporting  exempted  minority  parties  who  will
theoretically  reduce  the  votes  of the  candidate  the  contribu-
tor  opposes.

Though  not  allowing  the  supposed  threat  of  infringe-
ment  of  first  amendment  freedoms  to  invalidate  the  dis-
closure  requirements,  the  majority  has  employed  the  sup-
posed  threat  of  the  “Stalking  Horse”  to  support  the  view
that  compelling  state  interest  necessitates  the  restriction
of  constitutional  liberties.  The  “Stalking  Horse”  theory
is  unrealistic  when  applied  to  the  Socialist  Labor  Party
because  of  their  limited  ballot  access  and  inability  to  reg-
ister  any  significant  voter  response.

When  government  restriction  stifles  the  exercise  of  fun-
damental  liberties  the  least  restrictive  means  to  satisfy
legitimate  interests  must  be  chosen  as  a  legislative  scheme.
Shelton  v.  Tucker,  364  US  479,  488,  81  S.Ct.  247,  5 L.Ed.2d
Judge  Bazelon  has  proposed  criminal  sanctions
231  (1960).
as  a  less  restrictive  remedy  for  the  state  without  infringing
constitutional  rights  to  stable  the  “Stalking  Horse”.  This
supposed  threat  could  be  remedied  by establishing  a  mone-
tary  ceiling  of  contributions  that  would  require  reporting
if  an  effectual  amount  of  campaign  funds  are  collected.”
In  order  for  the  “Stalking  Horse”  theory  to  become  mean-
ingful  the  amount  of  funds  a  candidate  and  minor  political
party  would  require  to  effect  an  election  would  be  substan-
tially  more  than  the  amount  of contributions  the  committee
of  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  will  receive;  which  they  hope
to  be  similar  in  an  amount  to  the  1972  campaign.  As  the

10.  Among  the  solutions  to  fashioning  disclosure  requirements
to  insure  associational  rights  is an  alternative  that  only  after  elec-
tion  would  disclosure  be  required.
Of  course,  a  definition  of  minor
or  minority  party  would  first  be  required.  See  Footnote  1 of Bazelon
Dissent,  Buckley  »,  Valeo,  p.  1593.

il
problem  does  not  exist  without  substantial  funds  it is dif-
ficult  to justify  infringement  of associational  rights  of non-
monied  parties.

The  opinion  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  has  declared
Section  437A  of  the  Act  unconstitutional  because  of  its
application  to  “issue  discussion”  groups  such  as
New  York
Civil  Liberties  Union  as  requiring  disclosure  of  their  non-
partisan  political  activity.  There  is  no  effective  difference
between  the  New  York  Civil  Liberties  Union  and  the
Sovialist  Labor  Party  in  terms  of  their  political  activities
other  than  the  nomination  of candidates  to  run  for  elective
office.  In  fact  the  Civil  Liberties  Union,  and  other  groups
such  as  Common  Cause  and  the  League  of  Women  Voters
may  have  more  influence  upon  elections  than  the  partisan
Socialist  Labor  Party  due  to  such  factors  as  their  funding,
organization,  and  media  access.  Minor  parties  share  more
characteristics  with  issue-discussion  groups  than  they  do
with  the  major  parties.

The  opinion  acknowledges  only  that  these  groups  may
be  subject  to disclosure  requirements  as  the  provision  could
exact  information  that  would  substantially  infringe  the
associational  rights  of the  members  of organizations  taking
public  stands  on  public  issues.  The  purpose  and  function
of such  groups  and  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  is educational,
and  though  the  party  nominates  candidates  for  public  office
its  effect  is no  different  than  issue-discussion  organizations
as  it has  not  won  any  election  in the  almost  100  years  of its
existence.  Further,  as  it  obtains  a  small  percentage  of  the
vote  it  is  unlikely  to  influence  the  election  of  any  other
candidate.

The  Party  through  its  newspaper,  The  Weekly  People,
will  besides  discussion  of public  issues,  public  officials,  poli-
tical  leaders,  and  candidates  endorse  its  candidate  for

12

president.  Due  to such  advocacy  the  Party  would  be required
as  Human  Events  to  report  under  Section  437A.  Further,
under  2  U.S.C.  Section  434(F)  the  Party  as  well  as  the
Committee  will  be  subjected  to  report  requirements  due
to such  advocacy  in their  newspaper.”

The  advocacy  there  involved  appears  no  different  than
the  advocacy  of  United  States  v.  National  Committee  for
Impeachment,  469  F.2d  1135,  18 ARF  925  (1972).  The  funds
of  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  and  the  Committee  will  be
segregated.  The  activities  that  the  Party  will  have  in  the
election  year  will  be  the  same  as  any  non-election  year.
The  only  exception  will  be  that  the  spokesman  at  activities
will  be  the  candidate  instead  of  the  Secretary  of the  Party
who  will  perform  the  educational  function  of  the  Party.

The  disclosure  requirements  present  a  burden  to  minor
parties  not  only  because  of  its  associational  infringement.
It  further  burdens  the  minor  party’s  hard  pressed  resources,
financial  and  human,  to  interpret  the  provisions,  compile
the  records,  and  obtain  legal  counsel  to  comply  with  the
reporting  requirements  of  the  law.  A  large  percentage  of
manpower  and  funds  is  already  consumed  by  attempts  to
qualify  for  ballot  access.  Thereby,  the  effective  advocacy
of  the  minor  party  position  is  endangered.  However,  this
problem  is  not  equivalent  to  the  burden  that  disclosure
creates  on  associational  right,  but  is  part  of  that  greater
burden.

In  essence,  the  justification  for  disclosure  from  minor
parties  is  circumspect  because  minor  parties  take  clearer
positions  than  the  major  parties,  and  their  role  is  such
that  a  list  of  contributors  is  unlikely  to  receive  publicity.
However,  contributors  will  feel  endangered  by  disclosure

11.

See  Buckley  v.  Valeo  Opinion,  p.  1551.

13
because  of the  unpopularity  of minority  party  views.  With-
out  funds  to  organize,  the  effectiveness  with  which  their
positions  can  be advocated  is compromised,  and  the  burden
may  stifle  the  unpopular  view.  Though  disclosure  of  the
major  parties  contributors  may  reduce  the  volume  of speech,
disclosure  of  the  minority  parties  could  eliminate  their
speech.’”

ll.  The Standards  to Obtain  Public  Financing  of Campaigns  Dis-
criminate  Against  Minor  Parties  in Violation  of the Consti-
tution.

The  public  financing  provisions  of  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act  were  passed  to lessen  the  influence  of private
money  and  therefore  large  donors  on  the  election  of  public
officials.  The  Act  is a  response  to  past  campaign  abuse  and
indiscretion,  and  as  no  solicitation  of  campaign  funds  is
necessary  the  corruption  and  favor  buying  should  be  dis-
couraged  if  not  eliminated,  Minor  parties  felt  that  the
public  financing  of  campaigns  would  facilitate  the  election
of  those  individuals  and  parties  without  substantial  funds
and  with  limited  ballot  access.  26  U.S.C.  9008(I).  Due  to
the  qualifiation  requirements  for  funding  no  minor  party
as  the  Socialist  Labor  Party  can  qualify  for  campaign
funds.

In order  to meet  the  definitional  requis‘tes  of a  candidate
for  president,  a minor  party  nominee  riust  have  his  name
or  presidential  electors  on  the  ballots  of  ten  states.  26
U.S.C.  9002(2).  In  1972  the  Socialis:  Labor  Party  did
qualify  for  ballots  in  eleven  states  via  the  presidential
elector  alternative.’®

12.  Harvard  Law  Review,  Volume  88,  Number  6,  April  1975

p.  1247.

13.  The  Socialist  Labor  Party  qualified  for  ballot  access  in  the
states  of  Colorado,  Illinois,  Indiana,  Iowa,  Michigan,  Minnesota,
New  Jersey,  New  York,  Ohio,  Virginia,  Washington,  and  Wisconsin.

14
It  would  appear  that  the  Party  will  be  able  to  satisfy
this  threshold  requirement  for  funding,  {i should  be under-
stood  that  numerous  minor  parties  will  not  be able  to satisfy
this  requirement.  The  Socialist  Labor  Party  has  been  able
to  obtain  ballot  access  in  the  past  by devoting  a  dispropor-
tionate  amount  of  manpower  and  finances  to  insure  this
limited  amout  of  inclusion  in the  political  process.

The  standards  to  obtain  payments  are  wedded  to  the
proportion  of  popular  votes  obtained  by  a  party  in  the
former  election  or  contribution  requirements  impossible
for  a  minority  party  to  meet.  26  U.S.C.  9004  (A)(2)(A).
The  standards  absent  factual  implications  are  not  objec-
tionable  per  se.  However,  when  it  is  understood  that  the
only  two  parties  that  could  qualify  for  full  funding  by
obtaining  in excess  of 25%  of the  popular  vote  is the  Demo-
cratic  and  Republican  parties,  and  the  only  other  party
to  qualify  for  proportional  funding  is  the  American  Inde-
pendent  Party  by  receiving  between  5%  and  25%  of  the
vote;  and  no  other  party  qualifies  for  funding  as  no  minor
party  received  over  5%  of the  vote,  the  standards  as  drawn
unfairly  exclude  minor  parties  in  violation  of  the  Fifth
Amendment.  Lubin  v.  Parnish,  415  US  709,  94  S.Ct.  1315,
39  L.Ed.2d  630  (1974).  26  U.S.C.  9002  (6)(7)(8).

Under  this  system  a  minor  party  is locked  into  a  second-
ary  role,  and  because  of  previous  lack  of  success  at  the
polls,  which  stems  partly  from  ballot  access  restrictions,
a  minor  party  will  not  be provided  with  the  monetary  ability
to  change  their  position.  Kusper  v.  Pontikes,  414  US  5,
94  S.Ct.  303,  39  L.Ed.2d  7  (1973).  Instead  the  parties  that
need  funds  the  least  will  be  receiving  the  benefits  of  public
financing.  This  discrimination  should  be  viewed  with  strict
scrutiny.  Chicago  Police  Department  v,  Mosley,  408  US  92,
92  S.Ct.  2286,  33  L.Ed.2d  212  (1972).

15
The  state  does  appear  to  have  a  valid  interest  in  main-
taining  the  integrity  of  its  elections,  Storer  v.  Brown,  415
US  724,  94  S.Ct.  1274  (1974);  Jenness  v.  Fortson,  403  US
431,  91  S.Ct.  1970,  29  L.Ed.2d  5541  (1971),  and  the  state
is not  obliged  to  finance  the  efforts  of  every  nascent  polit-
ical  group.  American  Party  of  Texas  v.  White,  415  US
767,  94 S.Ct.  1296,  39 L.Ed.2d  744  (1974).  However,  alterna-
tives  to  access  must  be  provided,  Bullock  v.  Carter,  405  US
134,  92  S.Ct.  849,  31  L.Ed.2d  92  (1972),  and  the  state  must
demonstrate  that  a  compelling  interest  requires  the  regula-
tions  imposed,  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  393  US  23,  89  S.Ct.  5,
21  L.Ed.2d  2124  (1968),  and  NAACP  v.  Button,  371  US 415,
83 S.Ct.  328  (1963).

The  standards  for  sharing  in  the  government  subsidy  of
election  campaigns  are  inherently  suspect  classifications.
The  standards  were  drawn  by  incumbent  members  of  the
major  parties,  and  the  laws  definitely  favor  those  two
parties.  Discrimination  against  minor  parties  occurs  he-
cause  the  governmental  interest  is not  achieved  in a  manner
that  does  not  unnecessarily  burden  a  minor  parties  interest
in the  availability  of political  opportunity.  Lubin  v,  Panish,
supra.  The  government  has  discriminated  against  minor
parties  by  framing  the  law  to  exclude  them  from  funds  it
appears  they  can  qualify  for  under  the  public  financing
sections  of  the  Act  in  derogation  of  associational  and  free
speech  rights.  Williams  v.  Rhodes,  supra.

The  analogous  ballot  access  cases  cited  are  determined
on  a  consideration  of  the  procedure  employed  to  regulate
or  provide  equal  treatment.  Depending  upon  how  much
factual  discrimination,  and  therefore  burden  is  placed  on
the  constitutional  litigant  the  interest  of  the  government
or  of  the  litigant  is  validated,

16

When  first  amendment  freedoms  are  involved  the  govern-
ment  may  not  only  have  to  permit  their  exercise  but  pro-
vide  facilities.  Healey  v.  James,  408  US  169,  92  S.Ct.  2338,
33  L.Ed.2d  266  (1972).  This  should  be  prescribed  because
the  public  financing  provisions  violate  constitutional  safe-
guards  by  the  discrimination  promoted  with  suspect  clas-
sifications,  and  the  undue  burden  of  access  standards  for
minor  parties.  Though  there  is  no  obligation  to  support
every  nascent  political  group,  the  laws  cannot  be  drawn
in  a  manner  that  instead  of  maintaining  the  integrity  of
elections,  maintains  the  tyo-party  system.

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons  your  Amicus  Curiae,  the
Socialist  Labor  Party,  respectfully  requests  that  the
judgments  of  constitutional  questions  6, 7(A),  7(B),  and
7(D)  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  be  reversed.

Tony  GeRAM

BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE  FOR
JAMES  B.  LONGLEY

INTRODUCTION

James  B.  Longley,  a  resident  of the  State  of Maine,  presents
this  brief  in  support  of  the  Plaintiffs-Appellants  with  the  con-
sent  of  counsel  for  all  parties.

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS

James  B. Longley  is Governor  of the  State  of Maine.  He  was
elected  in  November,  1974  in  spite  of the  fact  that  he  was  not

affiliated  with  a  political  party.
He  did  not  campaign  as  a
Democrat  or  as  a  Republican  but  rather  as  a  candidate  inde-
pendent  of both  political  parties.  In  the  1974  general  election,
he  was  the  only  candidate  in  the  nation  elected  to  a  major  of-
fice  without  political  party  affiliation.  Governor  Longley’s
successful  election  was  in  part  attributable  to  the  concern  of
thousands  of Maine  citizens  with  the  integrity  of the  two  party
He  shares  the  view  of  those
system  in  the  United  States.
Americans  who  believe  that  the  integrity  of  the  electoral
process  can  best  be  served  by subjecting  election  campaigns  to
the  rule  of  law.  He  does  not  share  the  view  of  those  who  be-
lieve  the  rule  of taw  should  be used  to deny  citizens  the  right  of
political  expression  except  through  affiliation  with  the  Demo-
crat  or  Republican  parties.
Certain  portions  of  the  Federal
Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  as  amended  by  the  Federal
Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of 1974  (hereinafter  cited
as  FECA)  require  such  political  affiliation  as  a  practical  con-
dition  to  effective  political  expression.  Accordingly  Governor
Longley  joins  the  Plaintiffs-Appellants  in urging  reversal  of the
decision  of  the  United  States  Court  of Appeals  for  the  District
of Columbia  Circuit.

ARGUMENT

FECA  represents  a  successful  bi-partisan  attempt  to  preserve
the  present  two  party  system  by placing  obstacles  in  the  paths
of candidates  of other  partjes  and  independents.  The  legislative
history  is replete  with  examples  of bias  in  favor  of the  Republi-
can  or  Democrat  parties.  Senator  Pastore  argued  in connection
with  the  check-off  system  “‘What  we  are  trying  to  do  is give
both  sides  the  same  amount  of money  ....’’  Cong.  Rec.  818891
(daily  ed.  Nov.  17,  1971).  In  the  public  financing  section  of
FECA,  non-major  party  candidates  and  independents  do  not  re-
ceive  full  funding.  If one  assumes  a  certain  amount  of altruism
on  the  part  of  Congress,  a  difficult  assumption  in  view  of the
legislative  record  pertaining  to  FECA,  one  must  further  assume
that  the  failure  to  place  non-major  party  candidates  and  inde-
pendents  on  an  equal  footing  with  major  party  candidates  was

prompted  by  the  supposed  unlikelihood  of their  election.  The
successful  Longley  candidacy  in Maine  contradicts  this  premise.
It is naive  to consider  his victory  as  a  political  aberration.  Third
party  efforts  have  admittedly  made  a  recognizable  impact  on
national  elections  in  the  past  decade,  and  the  Longley  victory
proved  that  a  third  party  candidate  or  independent  could  in
fact  be  elected  to  a  major  office  if such  a  candidate  could  at-
tain  political  viability.  Certain  of the  FECA  provisions  make  it
impossible  for  such  a  candidate  to  achieve  such  viability.

I.

THE  DISCLOSURE  REQUIREMENTS  OF  8434  ARE
UNCONSTITUTIONAL  WHEN  APPLIED  TO  NON-
MAJOR  PARTY  OR  INDEPENDENT  CANDIDATES.

In  part,  a  non-major  party  or  independent  candidate  must
depend  upon  obtaining  financial  support  from  contributors
who  demand  anonymity.  This  is not  a  concern  of  the  major
In  most  instances,  their  contributors  have  previously
parties.
acknowledged  their  political  views  by  registering  with  a  par-
ticular  party.
Such  contributors  are  not  concerned  with  the
privacy  of  their  political  beliefs.  Their  act  of political  registra-
tion  is  public  testimony  of  that  fact.  The  disclosure  of finan-
cial  support  for  a  particular  party  candidate  is  a  logical  and
innocuous  extension  of  their  political  affiliation.  Such  is not
the  case  with  those  who  might  be  inclined  to  contribute  to  the
candidacy  of  a  person  outside  the  two  party  system.  Such  a
potential  contributor  must  consider  the  effect  of acting  on  his
convictions  by  publicly  acknowledging  his  support  for  a  candi-
date  who  is  not  a  member  of  the  Democrat  or  Republican
parties.
‘epending  upon  the  extent  of  his  prior  political  in-
volvement  with  the  major  parties,  such  a  potential  contributor
must  pay  a  price  to  exercise  his  First  Amendment  freedoms.
This  price  could  range  from  mere  neighborly  concern  as  to  his
reasons  for  supporting  a  third  party  or  independent  candidate
to  loss  of  employment  or  worse  as  a  means  of  retribution.
Whatever  the  price,  it  must  be  paid  by  the  potential  contribu-
tor  who  decides  to  exercise  his  First  Amendment  freedoms  out-
side  the  established  two  party  system.  ii  is a  price  that  is not
exacted  from  potential  contributors  to  the  major  party  candi-

dates.  It isa  price  that  necessarily  poses  a  reasonable  danger  of
chilling  the  exercise  of First  Amendment  rights.

Chief  Justice  Warren  wrote  in  Sweezy v. New Hampshire,
354,  U.S.  234,  251  (1957)  “History  has  amply  proved  the
virtue  of  political  activity  by  minority,  dissident  groups  who
innumerable  times  have  been  in  the  vanguard  of  democratic
thought  and  whose  programs  were  ultimately  accepted.”  The
opinion  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  recognizes  the  historical  wis-
dom  of  Chief  Justice  Warren’s  observations.  Yet  the  decision
permits  a  law  to  stand  that  necessarily  poses  a  threat  to  the
political  viability  of  any  third  party  or  independent  candidate.
It  does  so  on  a  “technicality”.  It  does  so  because  the  record
does  not  contain  substantial  evidence  of possible  chill  and  har-
First  Amendment  freedoms  are  too  dear  to  be
rassment.
eroded  by  legal  “technicalities”.  The  Court  should  apply  its
most  exacting  review  in  this  instance  and  find  the  disclosure
requirements  of  8434  unconstitutional.

Il.  THE  CONTRIBUTION  LIMITATIONS  OF  8608(b)  ARE
UNCONSTITUTIONAL  WHEN  APPLIED  TO  NON-
MAJOR  PARTY  OR  INDEPENDENT  CANDIDATES.

Common  sense  requires  one  to  conclude  that  the  power  of
wealth  “unduly  influenced  politics,  an  influence  not  stopping
short  of  corruption  ....”  United  States v. UAW,  352  U.S.  567,
570  (1957).  Congress  correctly  responded  to  the  excesses  of
the  1972  election  by  proposing  legislation  designed  to  curb
obvious  abuses  of  our  electoral  process.  Congress  incorrectly
responded  to  those  excesses  by enacting  legislation  that  makes
non-major  party  participation  in  the  eléctoral  process  a  practi-
cal  impossibility.

FECA  is  premised  on  the  erroneous  assumption  that  a  non-
major  party  or  independent  candidate  cannot  be  elected  to  a
major  national  office.
The  election  of  James  B.  Longley  as
Governor  of  Maine  should  serve  to  dispel  that  illusion.  Given
the  same  financial  assistance  available  to  the  major  party  Presi-

dential  candidates  in  any  given  year,  it  is  probable  that  a
Longley  could  have  obtained  a  substantial  number  of votes  and
possibly  have  been  elected  President.  Had  such  a  hypothetical
election  occurred  at  an  appropriate  time  subsequent  to  the  dis-
closure  of  the  revelations  concerning  the  last  Presidential  cam-
paign,  the  election  of  a  Longley  would  have  been  more  than
possible  -

-  it  would  have  been  probable.

If  FECA  becomes  law,  the  erroneous  assumption  that  a  non-
major  party  or  independent  candidate  cannot  be  elected  to  a
major  national  office  will  no  longer  be  an  erroneous  assump-
tion.  The  Democrat  and  Republican  parties  will  have  success-
fully  legislated  any  third  party  or  independent  competition  out
of existence.  They  will  have  done  so  by subjecting  their  poten-
tial  competitors  to  their  bi-partisan  contribution  limitations
and,  at  the  same  time,  excluding  those  same  potential  competi-
tors  from  sharing  equally  in the  public  financing  section  of the
legislation.  The  non-major  party  and  independent  candidates
should  not  be  treated  as  the  equals  of  major  party  candidates
in  one  section  of  the  legislation  and  less  than  equals  in  other
sections.
If  the  non-major  and  independent  candidates  are  to
be  subjected  to  the  disadvantages  of  FECA,  at  the  very  least
they  should  be  allowed  to  benefit  equally  with  the  major  party
candidates  as  far as  the  advantages  of  FECA  are  concerned.

iil.  THE  PUBLIC  FINANCING  PROVISIONS  OF  FECA  DIS-
CRIMINATE  AGAINST  NON-MAJOR  PARTY  OR  IN-
DEPENDENT  CANDIDATES.

An  independent  candidate  who  does  not  participate  in prim-
aries  or  receive  the  nomination  of a  party  at  a  convention  is
obviously  ineligible  for  primary  and  convention  funding.  In
order  for  a  Longley,  for  example,  to  achieve  the  necessary
political  viability,  national  exposure  would  be  a  necessity.  A
primary  campaign  or  convention  enables  a  candidate  to  obtain
such  exposure.  The  independent  candidate  is then  denied  the
benefits  of  a  primary  campaign  or  a  convention  unless  the
candidate  chooses  to  follow  the  typical  major  party  route  to
election.  Much  of Governor  Longley’s  appeal  to  the  voters  of

6

Maine  was  based  upon  his  refusal  to  follow  this  path  in Maine.
He  maintained  that  the  major  parties  had  lost  touch  with  the
needs  of  the  people.  The  people  of  Maine  obviously  agreed
with  him.  Had  he  run  nationally  in  November  of  1974,  it  is
probably  safe  to  assume  that  a  great  many  people  in  other
states  would  likewise  have  agreed  with  him.

Other  provisions  concerning  public  funding  are  likewise
weighted  in  favor  of  the  established  parties.  The  treatment  of
a  non-major  party  candidate  is discriminatory.  However,  such
a  candidate  benefits  generously  compared  to  the  independent
who  desires  to  run  for  national  office  without  the  political
strictures  of a  party.  For  example,  not  only  is the  independent
denied  primary  and  convention  funding,  if he  wins  he  is not
allowed  to  receive  post  election  funding  unless  he  has  created  a
party.  The  Court  of  Appeals  seems  to  recognize  this  uneven
treatment  but  fails  to  find  it  fatal  because  the  appropriate
At
provisions  of  FECA  are  not  invidiously  discriminatory.
the  same  time,  the  Court  recognizes  the  necessity  for  all  con-
cerned  to  maintain  a  careful  scrutiny  as  the  provisions  are  im-
plemented.  The  Court  seems  to  be  suggesting  that  the  admit-
tedly  discriminatory  features  of FECA  will  be judicially  tested
at  such  time  as  a  real  rather  than  a  speculative  controversy
exists.  Such  an  occasion  will  probably  never  occur  because  the
discriminatory  features  of FECA  will  never  enable  a  non-major
party  or  independent  candidate  to  succeed  to  the  point  where
he  can  present  a  real  controversy  for judicial  scrutiny.

The  Supreme  Court  has  never  hesitated  to  strike  down  any
scheme  that  places  unreasonable  obstacles  in  the  way  of candi-
dates  or  parties  seeking  access  to  the  ballot.  Williams v.
Rhodes,  383  U.S.  23,  31  (1968).  The  failure  to  afford  non-
major  party  or  independent  candidates  equal  access  to  public
funds  places  an  unreasonable  obstacle  in  such  a  candidate’s
path  to  national  office.

Many  Americans  are  disenchanted  with  the  present  two
party  system  and  desire  change.  Many  Americans  desire  po-
litical  leadership  independent  of  a  party  system  they  distrust.

They  desire  to  vote  for  an  individual  and  not  a  party.  The  Pub-
lic  Financing  Provisions  of  FECA  perpetuate  the  two  party
system  at  a  time  when  many  Americans  are  seeking  an  alterna-
tive.  Through  FECA  the  two  major  parties  have  successfully
constructed  legal  fortifications  around  their  endangered  po-
sitions.  They  have  used  the  public  demand  for  reform  of the
electoral  process  to  frustrate  the  ever  increasing  independent
and  third  party  movement  in  the  United  States.  Each  major
party  sacrificed  equally.  In return  each  major  party  was  guar-
anteed  that  its  members  could  seek  elective  office  within  the
traditional  political  framework.  Each  party  willingly  accepted
the  inconvenience  of  FECA  in  return  for  the  convenience  of
seeking  elective  office  without  the  third  party  or  independent
threat.

IV.  ABSENT  REVERSAL  OF  THE  COURT  OF  APPEALS’
DECISION,  RESTRICTIVE  STATE  LEGISLATION  A-
GAINST  NON-MAJOR  PARTIES  OR  INDEPENDENT
CANDIDATES  IS  PROBABLE.

21  Maine  Revised  Statutes  Annotated  8849]  -  494  pertain  to
the  procedure  for  nominating  a  candidate  for  any  State  or
County  office  independent  of  a  political  party.  The  Legisla-
ture  of  Maine  amended  several  sections  of  Title  21  after  the
election  of  an  independent  candidate  as  Governor.  Previously
an  independent  candidate  for  Governor  could  gain  access  to
the  ballot  by  obtaining  voters’  signatures  equal  to  one  percent
(17)  of  the  number  of  votes  cast  for  Governor  at  the  last
gubernatorial  election.  One  of  the  amendments  increased  the
number  of signatures  required  to  three  percent  (3°7).  Previous-
ly  the  required  signatures  had  to  be  filed  with  the  Secretary  of
State  before  August  I5th  of  the  election  year.  One  of  the
amendments  altered  this  section  of  Title  2]  by requiring  such
Signatures  to  be  filed  on  the  date  of  the  primary  election.
Title  21  Maine  Revised  Statutes  Annotated  8448  designates  the
second  Tuesday  of  June  of  each  general  election  year  as  the
Finally  the  Legislature  enacted
date  of  the  primary  election.
an  amendment  requiring  an  independent  candidate  to  file  a

a,

8

declaration  of  candidacy  with  the  Secretary  of  State  by April
Ist  of the  election  year  in which  he  or  she  will  be  a  candidate.

These  changes  in procedure  obviously  make  a  potential  inde-
pendent  candidate’s  task  more  difficult  in Maine.  It is equally
obvious  that  these  changes  resulted  because  of  the  Longley
victory.  Unless  the  decision  of  the  Court  of Appeals  is revers-
ed,  it  is certain  that  other  state  legislatures  will  attempt  to  per-
petuate  the  two  party  system  by  placing  discriminatory  ob-
stacles  in  the  way  of  non-major  party  or  independent  candi-
dates.  FECA  will,  in  all  likelihood,  be  utilized  as  a  blueprint
for  ostensible  election  reform  by  state  legislatures  throughout
the  country.
The  Act  and  its  progeny  thus  will  effectively
sound  the  death  knell  for  non-major  party  and  independent
political  activities  in  the  United  States.

CONCLUSION

Amicus  urges  that  the  judgment  of the  Court  of Appeals  be

reversed.

Respectfully  submitted,
